title: 5 eMTB Motors Ridden & Rated for 2025: Bosch vs DJI vs Shimano vs SRAM vs Specialized
author: Matt Beer
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, videos, reviews, bosch, dji, shimano, specialized, sram, amflow
article_text: Testing and Ranking 2025's Best eMTB Motors https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/n7jejPcM Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 19:02 Welcome to the great eMTB motor debate. Which motor is the best one out there? It’s not as simple as picking the one with the most power or torque. Noise, efficiency, and useable power on the trail are larger factors to consider. As you know from our Field Tests, we can't test every option out there - before you ask, we tried to get our hands on the Pinion MGU, but it turns out those are a rarity in North America. The list was slimmed down to five popular motors: • Bosch Performance Line CX (4th gen.) • DJI Avinox • Shimano EP801 • SRAM Powertrain • Specialized Turbo Full Power 2.2 In order to test them head-to-head, we established some qualitative and quantitative tests to simulate regular riding scenarios that focus on trail performance and how quickly the motor drains the battery. The shootout tests include a Boost Mode Hill Climb Challenge, Perceived Noise Trial, and Motor Efficiency Test Loop. We’ll also dive into the user experience of each motor system in terms of tuning, app functionality, and user experience in this shootout. Contents Motor Specifications Test Setup Boost Mode Climb Challenge Perceived Noise Trial Motor Efficiency Test Loop Livability - Controls Livability - Apps Verdict Motor Specifications Bosch Performance Line CX (4th gen.) Details • Available bikes: Various • Motor weight: 2.9 kg • Peak power: 600 W • Max torque: 85 Nm • Number of assist levels: 4 • Controller/Display: Wired Kiosk and Kiosk Display or wireless Mini Remote and System Controller • bosch-ebike.com DJI Avinox Details • Available bikes: Amflow PL Carbon • Motor weight: 2.52 kg • Peak power: 850 W* • Max torque: 105 Nm* • *(120 Nm and 1000W for 30 sec in Boost) • Number of assist levels: 5 • Controller/Display: Wireless Controllers and OLED Touchscreen Display • dji.com Shimano EP801 Details • Available bikes: Various • Motor weight: 2.7 kg • Peak power: 600 W • Max torque: 85 Nm • Number of assist levels: 3 • Controller/Display: Wired SC-EM800 remote, SC-EN500 display, and/or Satellite power switch • mtb.shimano.com Specialized Turbo Levo 2.2 Details • Available bikes: Specialized only • Motor weight: 2.9 kg • Peak power: 565 W • Max torque: 90 Nm • Number of assist levels: 3 • Controller/Display: Wired Handlebar remote, Mastermind TCU • specialized.com SRAM Powertrain Details • Available bikes: GasGas, Propain, Transition, Berria • Motor weight: 2.9 kg • Peak power: 680 W • Max torque: 90 Nm • Number of assist levels: 2 • Controller/Display: AXS Ultimate Pod remote and Bridge display • sram.com Test Setup Now there are a few caveats with these tests. Since each bike has its own motor mount, it’s not possible to use a control frame and swap out motors (like we did with rear shocks for Shock Week ). For each test, the motor was placed in the highest assist level (we’ll call that boost mode throughout the test for simplicity’s sake) and set to its stock mapping. Each bike’s battery size also varied, so we calculated the amount of battery potential used over the vertical gain achieved on our set test loop. Over those five laps, the vertical averaged 484m. Download the Trailforks App This follows Car and Driver Magazine’s method for measuring fuel efficiency conducted in their reviews. It also allowed each bike to be ridden on the test loop on the same day for equal trail conditions, which were wet and cold. During the motor efficiency test, the cadence was kept to what I would consider a normal climbing cadence (between 80-90 RPM) although some variance in crank length and gear selection was noted. Another control we used for all three tests was a set of MX wheels with soft compound, gravity casing tires. Front tire: 29x2.4" Schwalbe Albert Radial Ultra Soft compound, Gravity casing, 25 PSI. Rear tire: 27.5x2.4" WTB Judge High Grip compound, SG1 Tough Casing, 27 PSI. Boost Mode Climb Challenge In this test, we’re looking to rank the real world functionality of the boost mode on a technical climb. If you haven’t spent a lot of time on e-bikes, you might think climbing the steepest or most technical hill is all about selecting the strongest mode, pointing the bike straight up and going for it, but there’s a lot more finesse needed than that. Again, all of the motors were set to the highest stock assist mode. It’s not necessarily how you’d want to tackle a technical hill, but it shows how useable, or unusable, those modes are on the trail. We recognize that all of these motors are highly tunable. Changing the parameters can be done through the corresponding apps. The purpose is to point out those characteristics like the overrun (how long the motor stays engaged), pick-up (re-engagement timing), and torque. Ranking: #5 - Specialized The Turbo 2.2 has the lowest peak power amongst the group, at 565 W, but still has a good amount of torque (90 Nm). While it builds power quickly, the drawback on this technical climb was that the overrun is too high to slow the bike down when you need to pause. Then, reengaging took more rotation from the cranks than anticipated. Again, it’s possible to tweak this in the app. When you are on the pedals, the traction is very manageable. #4 - DJI Wow does the Avinox stand out from the rest here. There’s a ton of torque to manage here as it builds momentum incredibly quickly. That means, when you’re ready, it’s possible to get up some big moves. Speed is your friend if you’re thinking ahead. This mode is quite hectic and not practical for tech climbing. It’s likely most riders will want to drop to “Trail” (which is more comparable to the rest of the motors' boost modes here) to navigate any sort of obstacles. Although the pickup is decent, there’s too much overrun. #3 - Shimano The EP801’s strength point is the natural pick-up on the pedals but it’s the how the torque is applied that makes managing grip from a standstill trickier. That makes engaging the motor again more manageable than the Specialized, but it may be worth toning down how quickly the power ramps up. #2 - SRAM I dig how smooth the manageable the power delivery is, but, as you’ll notice in the video, it takes more gusto to get the bike over the log and up the next rock. In those moments before each lunge move, the power doesn’t ramp up quickly. That does allow to find and manage traction in the highest boost, or “Rally” mode though, making the Powertrain ride more natural to an unassisted bike in it’s stock parameters. #1 - Bosch The Bosch motor might be the Goldilocks of power management. There’s a fast pick-up and the power rolls off smoothly. Bursting up the big moves applies a sensible amount of torque without carrying on out of control. This motor is one step above the Powertrain in terms of intuition. Perceived Noise Trial Now that we’ve got the hardest part out of the way, physically, it’s time to talk about the noise emitted from these motors while climbing. Overall volume isn’t the only aspect we’re judging here, but also the pitch or tone of the gears spinning inside there. Here we’re using a scorecard system to rank them. We decided against using a decibel meter because that doesn’t account for the pitch or whine emitted. We’re listening for how annoying the noise is - microphone shoe installed and away we go. The least annoying wins. For this test, all the bikes were placed in the highest stock boost settings and the 36-tooth cog on the cassette while keeping a relatively similar cadence. We also recognize that each frame can resonate differently. Without tearing apart each bike, this is how we perceived the noise level of each motor. Ranking: #5 - Shimano The EP801 might have other strengths, but a stealth operation isn’t a strong point. Aside from being the loudest, it’s also the whiniest. The tone has a distinct cry that fluctuates quickly depending on cadence and speed. #4 - DJI The Avinox motor falls somewhere in the middle of a hum and a whine. The overall noise level is less than the Shimano, but the tone of the motor is sharper than the rest. #3 - Bosch The tone of the Performance CX Line is what I would call squeaky. The noise level emitted is quite low, and less erratic than the Shimano or DJI. Overall, it’s liveable, but not as easy to forget about compared to the Specialized or SRAM motors. #2- Specialized The electric motor manufacturer Brose makes both the Specialized Turbo 2.2 and SRAM Powertrain units so it’s no surprise to see them rank next to each other. While an exploded view of the Powertrain was hard to come by, the Turbo 2.2 uses a belt. That could the key to the least annoying sound. Each one emits a quiet humming sound with a low tone. Neither sounds like they’re working as hard as the previous three motors mentioned. #1 - SRAM It was a close fought battle but the Powertrain edges ahead of the Turbo 2.2 in this pseudo-science challenge. Motor Efficiency Test Loop Now, there’s no perfect way to do this. This is the mountain bike industry after all, and standards are never quite “standards”. As we mentioned, every bike uses a different motor and battery size, so instead of fully draining the battery, we measured the percentage used over the vertical - the lower the figure, the more efficient the motor is. This removed the variability by which a given bike might encounter a steep climb, changing the overall efficiency. It’s similar to how Car and Driver perform their fuel economy test. As with the other tests, we used the same wheel sizes and tires across each bike and started with a fully charged battery. We took the same lines, kept the cadence and gearings as similar as possible and we only used the stock boost mode. The loop totalled a vertical gain of 484m up the infamous Debeck’s Hill here in Squamish. The route consisted of a paved commute to a mellow dirt single-track climb, followed by a steep gravel road climb. Then the trail dropped back to the valley bottom and we returned back to home base. Results: #5 DJI - 0.645 Wh/m There's a lot of buzz around this motor, and for good reason, but efficiency may not be at the top its list, at least not in the highest assist level. That may be because the Avinox has the highest peak power (800 W) and torque (105 Nm) out of these motors. It doesn’t stop there though, because remember, the Avinox can pump out 1000 W of power and 120 Nm of torque for a 30-second burst in the boost setting. You could draw a parallel between the Avinox and electric cars. Its acceleration is unmatched compared to what we’ve previously known for e-bikes. While those are impressive numbers, all Class 1 E-bikes are limited to 32 km/h or 20mph, and that includes the Avinox. Yes, its acceleration is lightening fast, but it will never exceed 32 km/h. It’s golden ticket is that it holds that peak power. When pedalling moderately hard, the motor churns out an intense amount of support right up to the speed limit. Zooming up steep road climbs is comical, however, it chews through the battery very quickly in Turbo. Here’s another plot twist though: the other four bikes took about 45 minutes to complete the test loop. The Avinox knocked ten minutes off that time. That might explain why the Avinox had the lowest efficiency in this test. Although that’s the goal of this test, it does show how incredibly powerful the Avinox motor is. In fact, the Avinox’s “Trail” mode is more comparable to other motor brands’ boost setting. The Trail mode also cuts off the wild Turbo burst, lowering the pace, but more importantly, conserving the battery. For what it's worth, we conducted a bonus lap in the lower Trail mode and result in efficiency score of 0.604 Wh/m. Remember, every bike was set to their stock motor parameters for this test. We’ll have a full-length review of the Amflow PL, the only bike on the market currently built around the DJI Avinox motor, coming soon. #4 Shimano - 0.621 Wh/m Judging by the riding range during Propain Sresh review, I had a sneaking suspicion that there would be more efficient motors in the group than the Shimano unit. The EP801 placed 4th with a rating of 0.621 Wh/m. #3 SRAM - 0.586 Wh/m The Powertrain motor nudged ahead to 0.586 Wh/m, although it was surprising to see it lag behind the Specialized motor by so much, since these two motors are made by the same company, Brose #2 Specialized - 0.492 Wh/m The final two motors fought a tight battle for the top step. The Specialized Turbo 2.2 motor was pipped by a hair in this test. #1 Bosch - 0.490 Wh/m From dishwashers to drills, Bosch is well known name when it comes to motors. As it turns out, they know a thing or two about efficiency. Although it squeaked into first place, my money was on the Performance CX Line based on total ride times with other eMTBs. It was satisfying to confirm that theory to rest with back-to-back laps on each motor. Liveability - Remotes and Displays This section covers the user experience with various remotes and displays. There's a balance between having just the basics and being overloaded with buttons and lights. Having functions visible in the display, like mode selection, speed, and battery percentage are worthwhile. After that, it gets distracting to look at while riding. An ideal remote should integrate with the most common handlebar components and not be too vulnerable in the event of a crash. Wires can than be cleanly routed. You can't argue with the stealthy look of wireless remotes, but you’ll have to remember to check the battery life of the remote. We’ve omitted charging times since this will depend on the size of the battery. The convenience of charging also depends on how the battery is integrated into the frame and where the charging port is located. Bosch Most of the bikes featuring the Performance CX Line motor tend to use the built-in top tube Controller unit and the wireless Mini handlebar remote. This is a slimmer package than the Kiox display and bulky remote we’ve tried in the past. The Controller lets you turn the bike on, change power modes, and sync to the app with Bluetooth connectivity. One downside here, versus the Kiox remote, is that you can’t see the battery percentage or other useful stats, like speed and vertical ascent. In order to see that, you’ll have to connect the bike to the app. Yes, it’s annoying to pull out your phone to see the battery percentage. We all get range anxiety once in a while. Most Bosch e-bikes use the built-in Controller built into the top tube along with the wireless Mini Remote. There's also the less common Kiox display and wired Kiox remote. DJI DJI has drawn on their expertise in building drones for the motor components, but also OLED touchscreens in cameras. This type of display is a first for eMTBs and enhances the user experience another notch. The 2” screen features automatic brightness adjustment and can still read fingertips if it’s raining. It’s a novel concept that I could see being developed even further, similar to what Stark motorcycles have done with their Varg device integration. The Avinox features not one, but two wireless remotes on the handlebars. The right-hand side toggles through the display screens while the left changes the assist level and engages walk mode. The screen toggling remote could be removed altogether since a button on screen will turn the bike on and toggle through the windows. The Avinox has one handlebar remote on either side: left toggles the assist, right shuffles through the screen display windows. Specialized and SRAM use frame integrated displays like this, but they're not touchscreens. Shimano When internal cable routing became a headache, some riders rejoiced when wireless drivetrains came about. But then when they arrived on e-bikes, people wanted to go backwards. Well, not necessarily backwards, but they figured, if I have to charge my E-bike, it should charge all of the components at once. Shimano has been down the electronic shifting road before. Their e-bike remote controllers and displays are wired to one main battery. The new SW-EN600-L switch has ergonomic, rubber-coated buttons that are easy to locate. On that switch is also a power button and other lights for the assist level and battery percentage. Although those are tiny, it makes the same stats display on the handlebar mounted SC-E8000 screen a bit redundant. Like the Bosch Controller unit, the Shimano display only shows the battery life remaining in bars. Surprisingly, the Shimano E-Tube Ride app doesn't take that any further. The SW-EN600-L assist switch packs a lot into a small area. Finding the rubberized up and down assist buttons isn't an issue. The SC-E8000 display tucks in next to the stem out of harm's way. All of Shimano's remotes and shifters connect with wires. If you want to utilize the Autoshift function of the EP8 motor, you'll need this shifter. Specialized Specialized can take credit for kicking off the built-in top tube displays, but now, DJI has surpassed them with their touchscreen display. Still, the Mastermind TCU is a clean attempt that doesn’t throw too many numbers at the rider. Likewise, the handlebar remote is slim and ergonomic, although not wireless or a hinge-clamp design. There are four buttons two for assist modes, one to toggle through display windows and another to activate Walk Mode. That button can unfortunately overlap with some dropper post levers and be tricky to hold. Compared to Bosch, DJI, and Shimano, Specialized offers just three assist levels. That should be more than enough for most riders, plus you can play with the tune of each mode. It's also possible to adjust the amount of assistance as a percentage in 10% increments, which can be helpful when trying to match your output with that of a rider on a different motor system. Specialized's wired remote is discrete in size. Unfortunately, the walk mode button can be tricky to push depending on the dropper post remote/lever position. The Mastermind TCU has just one button - hold the button to power up or down or press quickly to shuffle through windows. SRAM SRAM’s Powertrain is all about integration, from the built-in top tube display, to the full AXS ecosystem. The Powertrain system is wireless except for one optional piece of the puzzle - Automatic Shifting option. Oddly enough though, there are just two assist levels; Range or Rally. Should you want to turn the motor off for descending, you need an AXS Ultimate pod on the lefthand side of the handlebars. That’s fine if you also have a RockShox Reverb AXS. Otherwise, you can use two remotes, which can get a bit cluttered. The other option is to run a non-SRAM dropper lever and toggle through the assist modes on the Bridge Display. That's a clean look. All of the Powertrain controls are wireless and sit below the bar. Imagine wireless brakes. The top tube display has two buttons; one to turn the power on or off, and the other to change the assist and pair with the AXS app. There's a low chance you'll end up on an e-bike with a Powertrain motor without a RockShox AXS Reverb dropper post, but if you want a handlebar remote (AXS Ultimate Pod behind) to change the assist level, you'll have to get creative with how they integrate. If that's the case, you can ditch the AXS Pod and resort to pressing the top tube button. Liveability - Apps Bosch Bosch’s Flow app is fairly thorough but a bit overcrowded. Once you navigate to the area you’re looking for, making motor tune adjustments is simple. Locking motor functions, although not something to solely rely on, are sweet if you’re an e-bike owner. Bosch offers a free security step that the owner can choose to turn on, only allowing the bike to assist once permission is granted through their phone. Additional security and tracking services are available with the premium paid version of the Flow+ app. Those features are available for free from DJI and Specialized. Another minor quibble is that you can only connect one bike per app at a time, however, that’s less likely to be an issue for a consumer. DJI Connectivity is another area where the Avinox shines. Pairing the bike to a phone is done by capturing a QR code. The app covers everything from security, diagnostics, motor tuning, and an auto-assist mode. The Avinox also has the possibility to change the assist level from the motor automatically, based on the cadence and speed of the bike. Navigating through the motor parameters is straightforward with little redundancy on each page. There are four modes (Eco, Auto, Trail, and Turbo) to tweak with inputs including the amount of assistance, motor overrun, pickup and torque. The user can also optimize the motor for either a 27.5 or 29” rear wheel - there’s no need to bring it to a dealer to make that change. A highlight of the Avinox has to be its security, which includes a locking motor function, alarm and tracking device. Shimano For apps, Shimano has two; the E-Tube, which is more of a diagnostic tool, and the E-Tube Ride, to connect rider’s with the bike. The E-Tube Ride allows for the usual motor tuning and unit tracking. Included is a map function, however, there are no security features. Overall, the E-Tube Ride app has more of a Windows feel to it than iOS, meaning it’s not the smoothest to navigate. One thing that Shimano does offer though is AutoShift. For that, you'll need a specific shifter and wired derailleur. Specialized When browsing the Specialized app, I realized that it’s oddly similar to the one that DJI built. They both balance simplicity with functionality. Everything you’re looking to modify or activate is easily accessible. Like the DJI Avinox, the Turbo 2.2 motor and Specialized app includes a free flocking motor function, alarm and tracking device too. SRAM The AXS app is one of the more intuitive apps to use. Maybe too straightforward. It lacks the ability to change the motor overrun or pickup, granted, it worked well on our Boost Mode Challenge. When using two AXS pods, you can change the controls around in a variety of ways, including engaging Automatic Shifting. Verdict Winner: Each one of these motor systems is unique in its own way and neither one is perfect. Picking one winner wasn't easy, but if we'd choose just one, based on these tests, it would be the Bosch Performance Line CX. Why? Well, in our tests, the Bosch came out on top in efficiency and useable power. It also fared well for perceived noise. The only small downside to the Bosch unit is the rudimentary Controller. It's not about the big numbers. While the DJI Avinox had us giggling over its immense peak power and torque, it's still a Class 1 E-bike, and that limits it to a top speed of 32 km/h. The motor is also impressively light, but proved to be less efficient and not that quiet. Best power: The Avinox motor also has four distinct modes, one of which taps into an immense amount of power and torque. Thankfully, you can turn this “overdrive” off because it blows the doors off of any other motor here. Granted, those two higher-assist modes use a considerable amount of battery power, it’s a rocketship. It’s also worth highlighting that the most powerful motor, the Avinox, is also the smallest and lightest motor in the test. Should DJI liscence the Avinox to other E-bike brands, the slim size motor could allow for less constraints on suspension packaging. Best integration: This is a closely fought battle that I’d put in a two-way tie between the DJI Avinox and SRAM Powertrain. The Avinox’s touchscreen makes life easier. You don’t need to pull out your phone as often to read stats. It also works in the rain and with gloves on - that’s better than most phones. Plus, the handlebar remotes are wireless. SRAM’s marketing spiel might call it an “ecosystem” but when all of their parts are lumped together, it’s a very clean system. The lack of modes and tuning may be less appealing for some E-bike enthusiasts, but for a lot of riders, it simplifies the experience. A clutter-free app tops off the Powertrain drive unit - another motor that’s capable of shifting automatically. Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Videos Reviews Bosch Avinox Shimano Specialized SRAM Amflow Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: 7 of the Best Hydration Vests Ridden & Rated
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: In classic outdoors nerd fashion, the simple matter of how one carries stuff for a bike ride has snowballed into something of a debate. There are trends and stylistic shifts to account for, but its hard to ignore the predominance of the cycling hydration vest, which seems to be having something of a moment. As a narrow man myself, the hip pack has never been a perfect solution, and the traditional backpack feels like overkill for all but the most extreme use cases. Hence, a happy medium. I'd consider myself something of a pioneer in this field, boldly exposing myself to the bleeding edge of vest technology . There are an increasingly diverse set of options on the market, and on account of only having one back I haven't been able to try them all, but here is a collection of seven unique options that stood out. One key asterisk before we jump in - the quoted liter volumes for the various packs are as stated by the company, but the real world packing space can vary. All of the weights have been taken without the bladder, as the sizes and brands aren't consistent across the board. Contents Camelbak USWE San Util Osprey EVOC High Above Rhythmus Camelbak Chase Vest • Bladder zip, main zip, two stuff pouches • Two chest zips, one stuff pouch • 1.5L bladder • 4L storage • 1 size • Weight: 363 grams • Price: $126 USD • camelbak.com Camelbak's Chase Vest has taken many forms over the years, but has remained a staple of the category even as the competition has multiplied. Taking cues from running vests and adding some simple variations to better suit mountain biking, the Chase is an easy item to recommend, with one key caveat - I think this older model is the goldilocks. The newer versions omit the stuff pouches on the rear in favor of slimming down the overall size, but those are one of my favorite features on this old dog. Being able to shove a few extra items in the outer pockets feels key to the Chase's utility, and makes this small pack punch above its weight. I've abused this pack for years, and even though the main zipper has started to corrode from exposure, it still works smoothly. Unfortunately, the newer packs use smaller-gauge zippers that seem to be more frail, proven out by a friend's brand new Chase blowing a chest zipper almost immediately. The z-pattern strap on the side panel does a great job of snugging things up, and the stretch webbing on the lower of the two chest straps keeps the load tight even as you deplete the water and food stored inside the pack. This combination of factors is simple and effective, even if the stretch webbing won't survive as long as the static alternatives. The internal storage is simple and useful, and small features like a bungee in the main back stuff pouch make holding things like pumps or saws a bit more secure, even if they can't fit in the zippered pouch. The large dual-zip chest pouch can fit a camera or larger tool. The fabric is very breathable, and coverage is fairly minimal, making this a great hot-weather option. The body fabric isn't waterproof, but is thick and sturdy, taking a while to fully wet out. Pros + Older generation balances features and size well + Excellent fit system + Easy-access large chest pocket Cons - Newer packs omit features and quality - Stretch webbing breaks down eventually - Danny DeVito patch not included USWE Rush Vest • Bladder zip, main zip • Two chest zips, one stuff pouch • 2L bladder • 6L storage • 5 sizes • Weight: 336 grams • Price: $165 USD • uswe.com USWE's claim to fame is their unique sternum buckle that simplifies closure and promises a secure and jostle-free fit. The Rush Vest is a low-profile option with stretchy materials throughout, and a bungee closure on the side panel to hone in the fit. Sizing for this vest is more aligned with a clothing item than a backpack, so you'll have to make sure there's an option to suit your trunk - some folks I've chatted with have found the largest size uncomfortably tight, so try one out if you have a big torso. I've been wearing the size Large, and find it fits perfectly. Storage is ample and well-organized on the USWE, with huge chest pockets that can accommodate larger items like cameras and tools. The mesh fabric used throughout allows you to see the contents of the bag, making it easy to find any smaller items that may shift around during flight. Demerit if you're trying to hide something. The light and stretchy fabrics used on this pack keep things comfortable on hotter days, but can allow for a bit of jostling if you've really loaded the pack. Those looking for a weather proof option should go elsewhere, as this is very far from waterproof. The upside is it does dry quickly, as the mesh breathes very well. Pros + Snug fit without fuss + Good lightweight option + Single buckle is convenient Cons - Light fabrics don't hold weight as well - Fit is akin to a clothing item - Storage doesn't scale beyond pockets San Util Saguaro Vest • Bladder sleeve, main zip, bungee lash • Two chest zips, two stuff pouches • 2L bladder • 4L storage • 1 size • Weight: 390 grams • Price: $230 USD • sanutildesign.com San Util has a strong history in the bikepacking and ski touring worlds, offering frame bags, touring backpacks, and hip packs all made in their Colorado headquarters. The Saguaro Vest is the newest offering from the brand, targeting a similar use case as the rest of the options here while optimizing for simplicity and durability. One main compartment, accessed via the side of the pack, and a slide-in bladder sleeve is all you get for the main body storage. A daisy chain and bungee lash on the back panel allow for some extras to get strapped down when need be. Two things have set this pack apart from the rest: that side access main compartment, and the perfect chest pockets. In order to access your main storage, all you have to do is take one arm out and swing the pack around to your front, making the primary compartment way more useful for quick-access items. The chest pockets are symmetrical, large, and stretchy, so you can cram larger items in if need be, and know that they'll stay secure. The small stretch pouches are good for trash or cool rocks, and don't impede the utility of the main pockets. I think some people will be turned off by the idea of a zipperless bladder sleeve, but I'd question why that really poses an issue. The bladder can't come out unless you remove the top security lash, and in my experience no debris has collected in the sleeve, as it sits quite flush when on your back. It keeps the pack a bit lighter, and makes bladder access quick and easy for refills. The fit requires some re-working as the webbing breaks in, and occasionally takes adjusting as you take the fill from maximum to minimum. Like all of the packs that use rigid webbing, I'm curious how they'd perform with a stretchy replacement, like used on the Chase vest. Despite using durable materials, the San Util vest has been comfortable on very hot days, thanks to a fairly small coverage footprint and breathable face fabric. Pros + Best main compartment on test + Sturdy construction, still comfortable + Simple and effective chest pockets Cons - Rigid webbing and fabric - Bladder sleeve will be miss for some - Bungee is less useful than pocket Bungee lash has been removed. Osprey Escapist Velocity 3 • Bladder/main zip, bungee lash • Two chest zips, one stuff pouch • 1.5L bladder • 3L storage • 2 sizes • Weight: 355 grams • Price: $110 USD • osprey.com Osprey’s Escapist Velocity 3 is the least expensive option in this roundup, but it’s certainly not lacking when it comes to features. The main pocket, which is accessed via a ¾ length zipper that runs across the top of the pack and then partway down the size, holds a 1.5 liter bladder, and there’s a small zippered internal pocket to hold keys or other smaller items. If you’re the type of rider that likes strapping things to the outside of your pack, the Escapist comes with an elastic cord that criss-crosses down the center for storing a jacket or something similar. I ended up taking that cord off in order to keep it from snagging on branches - the brush can be thick where I live, and the fewer external straps the better. The two front zippered pockets are where I typically stuck a multi-tool and a tire plug for easy access. A phone will fit in either pocket, but I found that it ended up positioned a bit high for my liking, a little too close to my collarbone for comfort. There’s a clever little cutout on each side that’s super useful for stashing sunglasses - just slide one arm in and they’ll stay put on steamy climbs. The overall fit is nice and secure once the two side straps are snugged down, and the Velocity 3 gets high marks for the overall fit. I do wish those straps had a little bit of give to them - as it is, the pack can ride a little bit higher in steep terrain, especially when it’s at full capacity - the Evoc Hydro Pro is a more form fitting and less likely to shift in those situations. Pros + Not as expensive as other options + Convenient sunglasses storage + Easy to access front pockets Cons - Side straps aren’t elasticized - Pockets are a good size, but place items fairly high on the chest EVOC Hydro Pro 1.5 • Bladder/main zip • Two chest zips, two stuff pouches • 1.5L bladder • 1.5L storage • 1 size • Weight: 288 grams • Price: $130 USD • evocsports.com Evoc was an early entrant to the vest pack game, and the Hydro Pro 1.5 is still a great option. It’s the overall fit that helps it stand out - the elastic side straps help keep it securely in place no matter what type of contortions its wearer is doing, and the size is just right for carrying everything you’d need on shorter riders (or longer ones if you don’t need to carry a ton of gear or extra layers). The front pockets are placed in a good spot, and they sit a little lower than those on the Osprey Escape, which makes them more phone friendly. The stretchy outer pockets are good place for sticking snacks, whether that's a Snickers bar or a Gu packet depends on your ride fueling strategy, but either way the treats will be easy to reach. My only real gripe is with the velcro upper closure for the main pocket - when the pack is full this has a tendency to get pushed open. A zipper or a roll top would be an improvement on this design; a little sleeve for a mini-pump would be nice to see too. Oh, and the hydration bladder tube is black / grey, which for some reason looks less dorky than a clear tube, but it also makes it harder to see if anything weird is growing in there. Pros + Great fit, stays put very well + Good front pocket layout and location Cons - Main rear pocket design could be better - Limited storage capacity for pump or an extra layer High Above Supernatural • Bladder/main zip, bungee lash • Two chest stuff pouches • 2L bladder • 4L storage • 1 size • Weight: 445 grams • Price: $240 USD • highabove.net Despite High Above's long history making hip packs, the Supernatural is their first foray into the backpack or vest realm. This is one of the largest packs on test here, with a large main compartment that houses both the bladder and some organizational pockets in the waterproof shell. Externally, they've included a bungee lash, but no daisy chains, and have integrated a clever system that allows you to carry a full-face helmet on the climbs. The chest pockets are very simple, and neither features a closure beyond a simple top flap. This feels like a big miss to me, as riding can easily dislodge items from a stuff pouch, particularly if you're packing a tool, phone, or camera up front. The left flap closure can just barely close over a standard size iPhone, and I'd vastly prefer a zipper for peace of mind. I struggled a bit to get the fit dialed on the Supernatural, with the pack tending to sit higher on my back than I prefer. When I let out enough tension in the side straps to rotate it back further, the load would jostle around on descents. In this case, an over-shoulder adjustment like that used on the Rhythmus would be useful, or simply offering a larger fit for bigger torsos. Despite the aptly high-above fit, the pack is impressively secure once cinched into place. For how big the storage is, things don't dance around inside thanks to smart pockets and lash points for longer items. The simple and effective stretch webbing added to the chest straps allow the pack to move with you as you gyrate, taking the edge off the sturdy materials. Pros + Large main compartment with smart storage + Secure fit + Great weatherproofing Cons - Lack of effective chest pockets - Large storage sits high on back - Bungee is less useful than pocket Rhythmus RP 9L • Bladder/main zip, outer zip, two stuff pockets • Two chest zips, four stuff pockets • 2L bladder • 7L storage • 1 size • Weight: 653 grams • Price: $160 USD • rhythmus.equipment Rhythmus is a new player in this space, coming to market with the RP 9L as their sole offering. Focusing on durability and a highly tunable fit, this pack caters to the hyper-organized crowd, with a huge array of stuff pouches and internal separators. The main compartment houses the bladder and a large storage area, with a separator sleeve between the two. On the outer face of the pack, you have a vertical zip pouch with a magnetic lid to hold the contents in. On both sides of that, mesh pouches serve as overflow storage. In lieu of daisy chains, the base of the pack has two lash straps to hold bigger and more awkward items. Most of these elements make sense to me, save for the lack of a more robust closure on that vertical zip pouch. That area is the easiest access storage on the pack, and makes sense for smaller items like tools, keys, and spares, but the fact that the top is only enclosed by a flap is a bit disconcerting. I never lost anything during testing, but it's not hard to imagine some items ejecting during a crash or a particularly chaotic bit of riding. The chest pockets feel like they cater well to a specific type of user, but I don't think that person is me. They're all quite small and close together, bearing closer resemblance to an over-complicated billfold wallet than a simple grab-and-go pack. The largest pouch is just big enough for a standard size iPhone, so those with larger devices, cameras, or tools will have to keep those in the rear compartments. The Rhythmus has one of the largest footprints when worn, and lacks the mesh face fabric that others have employed. This may help with long term durability, but in the short term it does feel a bit warmer than the others. That might be reflected in the overall weight, as well, which tips the scales quite a bit higher than any of the other options. Pros + Plenty of room for hyperorganization + Lots of fit adjustments + Sturdy materials Cons - Over-complicated storage - Large surface area, warm - Rigid webbing requires refitting Conclusion Seven responses to the same prompt, and seven very different options for those looking to carry some extra water, food, tools, or layers on their next ride. I've come to enjoy having a pack for rides of any length, as I can just throw it over my shoulder and know that all the things I need are in one place, instead of cramming my pockets anew each day. Of the assortment on test here, my personal pick is the San Util Saguaro. The design packs a lot of utility into a simple form, and is comfortable on even the longest days. The chest pockets and main storage feel like the best executed versions thereof, and though I'd love to see some elastic straps for fluid fit, the rigid webbing holds fast once adjusted into place. If the fancy materials and US-made pricing of the San Util is too rich for your blood, then the Camelbak Chase Vest remains a stellar option. Though the newer packs seem to have pared down on features, the generation on test here has proven to be an excellent piece of kit, with plenty of storage and a great fit system. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Ridden And Rated Reviews Packs Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Allied Fitz Review: The XC Rider's Trail Bike?
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: The Fitz is Allied’s longest travel bike yet, the 140mm follow up to the 120mm BC40 that launched back in 2022. According to Drew Medlock, Allied’s CEO, the bike is aimed at the “backcountry rider who logs big miles and long climbs, prioritizes climbing and pedaling efficiency, but regularly encounters terrain too aggressive for an XC bike.” The Fitz’s frame weighs approximately 5 pounds without a shock, which allows complete builds to come in around 26-28 pounds. With bikes and components seemingly getting heavier rather than lighter each product release cycle, it’s nice to have a trail bike show up that’s comfortably below that 30 pound mark. Allied Fitz SRAM X0 Details • Travel: 140mm / 140mm fork • 29" wheels • Carbon frame • 64.5° head angle • Chainstay length: 440mm • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Price: $8,790 USD • Weight: 27.5 lb / 12.5 kg (size L) • Lifetime frame warranty • Frame weight: 5 lb / 2,268 grams (claimed, w/o shock) • alliedcycleworks.com There are three complete models available, all with the same carbon frame, and all with fairly high price tags - this probably isn’t the place to look if you’re trying to stretch those hard earned dollars as far as possible. I’ve been testing the $8,790 USD SRAM X0 build, which (not surprisingly) has an SRAM’s X0 T-type drivetrain, Motive brakes, an Industry Nine 1/1 Whisp Race Wheelset, and a OneUp dropper post. It’s possible to customize the final build on Allied’s website, where customers can choose things like crank length, dropper post length, stem length, and more. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take The Fitz is a totally fine, capable, and quite light trail bike, it's just lacking that extra bit of pizzazz to really set it apart from the pack. It’s like a bowl of plain oatmeal versus one that’s swimming in maple syrup and blueberries - they’ll both fill you up, but one will get you buzzing more than the other. Food-based similes aside, the Fitz will be a good match for riders who want a little extra travel for covering ground quickly. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The Fitz has all of the little details you’d expect to find on a modern trail bike - room for a long travel dropper post thanks to the straight seat tube, a secret storage compartment with a magnetic lid for a tube or snacks in the downtube, and a healthy dollop of chainstay protection. The storage compartment remained rattle free for entirety of the test period, which isn't the case with all of the various takes on this feature. I do like having the compartment situated below (rather than under) the bottle cage - this seems to reduce the stress on the door, and increases the chances that it'll stay silent for the long run. Speaking of water bottles, there's plenty of room for carrying one bottle of pretty much any size inside the front triangle. There’s really not much to pick apart when it comes to the execution - riders who run their rear brake on the left side may find themselves wishing for a slightly different cable port orientation, but other than that everything is taken care of. Overall, it’s a very straightforward, relatively simple design that’s free of any strange design choices. Okay, I mentioned before that I don’t really like the font and the kerning on the white logo, but that’s more of a me thing than an actual issue, and Allied does have a bunch of frame color and logo colors to choose from. Geometry There are five sizes of the Fitz available, small thru extra-large. The numbers are squarely in line with what you’d expect to see on a modern trail bike - the head angle is a relatively slack 64.5-degrees with a 140mm fork, the reach is 480mm on a size large, and the seat angle is a moderate 76.5-degrees. The chainstays measure a moderate 440mm for all sizes - Allied hasn’t gone down the size-specific chainstay route yet. Suspension Design In its stock form, the Fitz uses a 210 x 50mm shock to deliver 140mm of travel via a Horst Link layout, but that can be bumped up to 150mm by removing a travel spacer. During development, Allied considered going with a flexstay layout, which would have resulted in an even lighter frame, but decided that a Horst link layout was the way to go in order to achieve the suspension performance they were looking for. Specifications Price $8790 Travel 140mm Rear Shock Fox Float Factory Fork Fox 36 SL Factory 140mm Headset Wolf Tooth Performance IS 41/52 Headset Cassette SRAM X0 Crankarms SRAM X0 Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 T-type Shifter Pods SRAM AXS pod Handlebar OneUp Carbon Stem OneUp 35mm / 42mm length Grips OneUp Brakes SRAM Motive Silver Wheelset Industry Nine 1/1 Whisp Race Tires Vittoria Agarro Seatpost OneUp Dropper 31.6mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup The Allied showed up with a 210mm dropper post, a 42mm stem, and OneUp’s 35mm rise handlebars, which were all a great match for my personal preferences. I ran the stock Industry Nine Whisp carbon wheels and Vittoria Agarro tires for the majority of the test period, but I did do some experimenting with an alloy wheelset and Schwalbe’s Albert Trail radial tires to see what that did to the feel of the bike. I experimented with varying amounts of sag, between 25 - 30%. For general trail riding on rougher terrain 30% felt good, but dropping down to 26% made for a noticeably firmer rider that’ll appeal to riders who prioritize efficiency over comfort. Testing Info Testing took place mainly in Bellingham, Washington , and the surrounding area in the wide range of trail and weather conditions that accompany the tail end of winter in the Pacifc Northwest. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Allied Fitz review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/0wAtek6R Copied Live 00:00 08:08 08:08 Climbing The Fitz covers ground quickly, thanks in part to that relatively light weight. If you’ve been slogging around on a 35+ pound enduro bike and then switch to the Fitz it’ll feel like you somehow gained an extra lung. That’s said, it’s decidedly not an XC bike, or even an XCish bike like the Transition Spur for that matter. I had the Spur and the Allied on hand at the same time, and the Spur’s shorter travel and steeper head angle give it quicker, snappier handling, and I was more likely to find myself standing up and pedaling hard out of the saddle on the Spur, while the Fitz felt more like it wanted to settle into a steady (but still quick) seated spin session. Some bikes feel very region specific, reflecting the zone where they were developed, and the Fitz falls into that category - the born-in-Bentonville traits really come through, and I don’t mean that in a bad way. For more rolling terrain, the build kit will work well, and the angles strike a good blend between being aggressive enough for rowdier sessions and still remaining manageable while climbing. The Fitz’s head angle sits at 64.5-degrees, which is on the slacker side for a 140mm trail bike. On the climbs, that can make its handling feel a little less lively than something like the aforementioned Spur, or even the Revel Rascal SL, which has 130mm of rear travel, a 140mm fork, and a head angle that’s 1-degree steeper. It’s a decent climber, but not earth-shattering - there are other bikes in this category that are more adept when things get tight and tricky. The Fitz is fairly sensitive to rear shock pressure changes - at 25-27% sag it feels nice and efficient, without a ton of suspension movement during seated pedaling. That’s the setup I preferred, since this is a bike that feels best when pedaling is prioritized over sending it down the rowdiest trails around. When the shock is set to 30% sag the amount of grip increases, but so does the amount of bobbing when you’re hard on the pedals. Descending If Santa Cruz can call their Tallboy the “downhiller’s XC bike,” it wouldn’t be a stretch for Allied to call the Fitz the “XC rider’s trail bike.” That’s a good distillation of how it feels on the trail - rather than being a shrunken-down enduro bike, it rides more like a longer travel XC bike. For some riders, that’s going to be exactly what they’re looking for, something that’s has the geometry and extra travel to give it more comfort and stability compared to a true XC bike, while remaining light enough to occasionally get called into duty at a local cross-country race. The relaxed geo figures gives it the Fitz a good amount of stability at speed, but there’s still an edge to its handling that appears when things are really rough and fast. The Fox Float shock worked fine for this application, but the overall suspension feel is quite neutral - it’s not mind-blowingly sensitive off the top. There is a good amount of ramp up and support, and it wasn’t harsh when I did use all the travel, but this isn’t one of those bikes that feels like it’s actively encouraging you to go bigger and faster. As far as frame stiffness goes, I didn't run into any issues with undue swingarm flexing, despite the fact that there isn't a brace between the seatstay. Granted, I'm not the heaviest rider out there, but the overall amount of frame flex during hard cornering didn't give me any reason for concern. Yes, you could increase the stroke length and put a 150mm fork on the Fitz, but the handling in the stock configuration feels like the intended setup - it’s not a bike I’d rush out to stick heavier wheels and tires on. If I was going to upgrade one thing it’d be the SRAM Motive brakes in order to have more immediate stopping power, but I’ll go into that more later one in this review. The ideal trail for the Fitz would be one with a little bit of everything - feed it a mix of flowy turns with the occasional bit of chunk and it feels right at home. I could see this being a good bike for riders in Colorado, or areas with similar terrain, zones with bigger backcountry loops that aren’t ridiculously technical, but still warrant having a bike with a little extra travel. The Fitz’s neutral manners are both a blessing and a curse. It’s nice to have a bike that doesn’t do anything out of the ordinary in most situations, but it also means that it doesn’t really have one defining characteristic, other than that light weight. Allied Fitz Revel Rascal SL How Does It Compare? I mentioned the Transition Spur earlier, since I had it and the Fitz on hand at the same time. Pitting a 120mm bike versus a 140mm bike isn’t the typical matchup (and yes, Transition has the Smuggler as their trail bike) but in reality they’re both designed for similar terrain. For me, the Spur had a stronger pull. Yes, there’s not as much travel, but I like the feedback that the Spur delivers - it feels like you’re getting away with something when cleaning a spicy section of trail, and it’s an overall more energetic feeling bike. The Revel Rascal is a closer competitor to the Fitz, with 130mm of rear travel and a 140mm fork. If you’re counting grams, the Fitz takes that point - its frame is a pound or so lighter than the Revel's. On the trail, where grams don’t matter as much, the Rascal’s suspension is more comfortable - it has better small bump sensitivity than the Fitz, and feels like it really makes the most out of its travel. I’m more of a cat person, but if I had to make some dog-based analogies I’d say the Fitz is more of a high-strung greyhound and the Rascal is a easy-to-love labrador. Which Model is the Best Value? There are three complete bikes in Allied’s lineup, and they’re all pricey - even the base model GX AXS build is $7,020, so it’s tricky to say any of them pose a particularly good value. Going the frame-only route could be the way to build up a slightly more affordable option, especially if you went with a cable-actuated drivetrain and did some smart shopping for other components. Technical Report SRAM Motive Brakes: There’s still a Code-sized gap in SRAM’s lineup between the XC-oriented Motive’s and the DH-oriented Mavens. The Motives are fine on XC bikes, and they’re waay better than the Levels that they replaced, but I wouldn’t put them on a bike with more than 130mm of travel - they feel very underpowered on longer descents. Swapping to HS2 rotors helped a little, but these still wouldn’t be my first (or second) choice for a trail bike, even a light one like this. Tire Choice: The stock Vittoria Agarro tires match the bike’s intentions well, and that’s the style of tire I’d recommend - the Agarro or a Maxxis Forekaster have enough grip for most scenarios, but still roll quickly enough to avoid negating the benefits of the bike’s light weight. I did stick some Schwalbe Albert Trail tires on for a handful or rides to see how the bike felt with a more aggressive radial tread. The softer radial casing felt great in chunkier, rootier sections, taking the initial edge off those sharper impacts. They do come with reduced rolling speed compared to the Vittoria tires, and overall I’d say the Fitz is better suited to faster rolling rubber - it’s not really a bike that’d I’d rush out to try and turn into a burly short-travel machine. It’s better to embrace its light trail designation and keep the quickness. Fox 36 SL fork: I’ve praised this fork before, and I’ll do it again here - it’s a great choice for a light-but-capable trail bike like the Fitz. Once it was set up I didn’t need to fuss with it, and stayed smooth and quiet for the entirety of the test period. Pros + Very reasonable weight without too many compromises + Modern geometry works well in a wide range of scenarios + Good stability considering its light weight Cons - No matter the build, all of the options are on the pricier side. - More powerful brakes would be a better match for the bike’s capabilities. - It's a decent climber, but there are livelier options out there. Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Allied Cycle Works Allied Fitz Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: SRAM Maven Brake Review: The Current Benchmark for Accessible Power
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, big-brake-test, reviews, brakes, sram, sram-maven
article_text: SRAM Maven Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/SrCozbsb Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 01:08 SRAM Maven Ultimate SRAM's introduction of the Mavens was something of a catalyst for this test, as they seemed to have set a benchmark for accessible power in a mountain bike brake. Since their release, the Mavens have become a staple of stock builds and World Cup race bikes alike, establishing them as one of the big brakes to beat. Maven Details • Mineral oil • Adjustments: Reach, pad contact • 2 pad options • A1: 2x 19.5mm, 2x 18mm pistons • B1: 4x18mm pistons • Price: $580 USD (Silver, pair) • Brake weight: 367g (front) • Rotor weight: 199g (200mm) • sram.com BUY AT: Thunder Mountain The massive caliper and lever assembly give them a noticeable presence on the bike, and the Van Halen graphics on the Ultimate calipers doesn't exactly blend into the background. Despite a completely novel architecture, they feature the adjustments typical to SRAM's other gravity brakes like the Code. One critical difference is the move from DOT fluid to mineral oil, allegedly with longevity and maintenance ease in mind. Setup The Mavens mark SRAM's departure from DOT fluid, now using a mineral oil made for them by Maxima. The bleed fittings may be different, but the procedure is identical to that of the Code, Level, Guide - any Bleeding Edge aficionado should feel familiar. The stock bleed procedure is consistent and easy, with only one special trick required to get a consistent feel. That trick is what SRAM calls the piston massage - essentially just a cycling of the pistons to get them all firing in tandem. The included pad spacer is all you need to perform the task, and you're off to the races. Reach adjustment is tool-free, thanks to the prominent knob on the pivot of the lever. It's easy enough to adjust while pedaling, with noticeable detent clicks between each position. The Pad Contact feature modifies the free stroke at the lever, and offers a very wide range of adjustment relative to other options. SRAM offers two different pad compounds for the Mavens: Metallic and Organic. They both have their merits, but given the higher power output of the metallics, we opted for those. There are also two rotor options: HS2 and Centerline; the 2mm thick HS2's larger braking surface made it the logical choice for those looking to get more braking power out of the system. Livability: Easy bleed procedure, best-in-class ergonomic adjustability thanks to Pad Contact and reach dial. Piston massage is a slightly annoying extra step, but is critical for performance and consistency. Feel SRAM's Swinglink cam drives the piston in a way that feels intuitive and easy to adapt to. The force required to pull that lever does seem to differ from copy to copy on the Mavens, with some actuating very easily, and others requiring more force to break into the stroke. The lever itself is very neutral in shape, and feels usable at the minimum and maximum of the reach range. There's no extreme shaping to it, but the flare at the end is more than enough to reef on. Depending on where you set up your Pad Contact adjuster, the free throw on the Mavens can be quite far, or as minimal as any option on test. In either extreme, the power is quick to ramp up, and requires some rider calibration to adjust to. Once you have, the bite point can be effectively feathered, and a short pull past that point gives you access to full power. Feel Ranking: Far from the lightest pull on test, but very consistent and easy to intuit. Lever force differs set to set, without much indication as to why. Water Bottle Pull Test: 898 g pull force (8th overall, performed with A1 lever) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power Power is the name of the game here, with two 18mm and two 19.5mm pistons per massive caliper. The amount of wheel-stopping force these can generate is immense, and only matched by a few of the most extreme options on the market. Those looking to temper that power can do so via rotor selection, with HS2 rotors providing a claimed 7% more power than Centerlines. Each 20mm increment in rotor diameter increases the mechanical advantage by 14%, so starting with a smaller rotor might suit some riders. Power Ranking: Top spot, followed by the Shimano Saint, the Hope Tech 4, then the Trickstuff Maxima. Enough stopping force to change the way you ride, in a good way. Honda Fit Stop Test: 25.0 m / 82 ft (2nd overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. Original (A1) = red. New (B1) = gold. Updates Since we conducted the bulk of this test, SRAM went and released an updated version of the Mavens. The new brakes look very similar, but feature a new SwingLink and caliper to achieve a lighter feel and similar power. The new B1 brakes take the same lever body as the original A1, and use a SwingLink with higher leverage to move through the stroke more easily. To compensate for the increase in power, the caliper now has 4x18mm pistons, instead of 2x18mm and 2x19.5mm. You can read our in-depth impressions here , but the short story is this: the lever action is much lighter, the freestroke is a bit larger, and the power isn't as massive at the wheel. They still have a ton of bite, but easing into that max force is a less aggressive ramp, yielding a softer initial feel. Generally, the B1 feels like an easier to use brake that will suit trail bikes, with the A1 befitting longer travel gravity bikes. Pros + Massive power + Consistent, wide range of adjsutment + Good ergonomics Cons - Power ramp too aggressive for some - Extra setup steps are key to performance - Lever actuation differs brake to brake Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more .
