With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Check Out: Lighter, Grippy Tires, Wet-Weather Gear, Coffee in a Tube, & More
author: Matt Beer
article_text: CHECK OUT A lot of gear comes across our desks here at Pinkbike. Check Out is an occasional roundup of everything our tech editors have gotten their hands on. Sometimes it's products we're doing long-term tests on, other times it's stuff we're stoked on but don't have time to fully review. And, sometimes it's crazy shit someone sent us unsolicited, and we're having a laugh. Check Out January '26 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/wH89udMB Copied Live 00:00 09:35 09:35 Continental Kryptotal Fr Enduro, SuperSoft tire Features • Size: 29x2.4" • Front-specific tread • Enduro casing • SuperSoft compound • Weight: 1,220 g (actual) • MSRP: $91.95 USD • continental-tires.com SuperSoft rubber, Enduro casing. No 275s in this combo yet. When Continental relaunched its mountain bike tire lineup, there were some holes in the specification sheet. If you wanted the Super Soft compound, you had to pay the weight penalty for a downhill casing tire. Slowly, they’ve been introducing new combinations, including what I imagine will be one of the most popular choices, the 29” Kryptotal Front, SuperSoft compound and Enduro casing (Kryp Fr SS EN). I measured the weight of the Kryp Fr SS EN at 1,220g, almost 100g lighter than the downhill casing. The ride characteristics are starkly different between the two because of the lighter casing. I wouldn’t call it crazy bouncy, but it is much more flexible, closer to a Maxxis' EXO+ trail casing, rather than their DoubleDown enduro casing. Otherwise, the predictable grip across the top of the Kryptotal Fr tread remains. The bite in soft terrain is a hair less than the Kryptotal Rr tread but that doesn’t mean it can’t be a decent rear tire too, if you’re running a dual 29” wheel program. Another tire now available in the Enduro casing and SuperSoft rubber is the intermediate Argotal tire, which has shown to excel in soft dirt and mud. Again, that one is only available in the 29x2.4” size. In due time, I suspect more options from Continental will come to market in that lighter, sticky tire combination. 6D ATB-2T helmet Features • Virginia Tech 5-Star Rated: #41 • Patented Omni-Directional Suspension Technology (ODS) • Integrated Sunglass Dock • Ionic+™Antimicrobial Comfort Liner • Fidlock™ Magnetic Closure • Sizes: XS/S, M/L, XL/XXL (53-62 cm) • Exceeds NTA 8776 (E-Bike), EN1078, CPSC, AU/NZS 2063 Safety Certifications • Weight: 469 g • Price: $229.95 USD • 6dhelmets.com ODS = Omni-Directional Suspension. Quality trim. 6D designs its helmets differently. They’ve been onto the idea of dissipating rotational energy for a long time, but they don’t use Mips technology. Instead, they use their own Omni-Directional Suspension (ODS). This absorbs energy in a 3D manner, instead of just rotational forces. 6D calls the ATB-2T a 3/4 trail helmet, but it does not emulate the coverage of something like the Fox Dropframe RS or Bluegrass Jetro. This is a traditional half-shell with extra volume. That's because it uses 6D's Omni-Directional Suspension safety technology, just like their full-face helmets. It receives a 5-star rating on the coveted Virginia Tech helmet scoring system (#41 to date). As a result, the high-volume shell isn’t the lightest. It reads 469g on my scales, which is about 65g heavier than the POC Cularis, currently sitting in second place on Virginia Tech's charts. On the trail, that extra weight can be felt, yet it's well-balanced. Air flow is hampered by the extra weight, and sweat is absorbed by a quality Ionic+ liner. The vents also act as a home for your sunnies, while the visor has 3-positions. Finishing off the features is a Fidlock closure for one-handed installs. I'd call the fit normal, and even better than their Alterra lightweight full-face, although the M/L circumference is actually closer to most other brands' size medium. WTB Peacekeeper Features • Application: Trail and XC • Size: 29x2.4” • Rubber: TriTec, Fast Rolling and High Grip avail. • Sidewall: single-ply, 60 & 120 TPI • Colors: black and tanwall • Weight: 741-1,024 g • Price: $80.95 • wtb.com Big volume, ...low rolling resistance. WTB’s Peacekeeper tire is said to be the do-it-all tire for short travel and even enduro bikes. While that’s stretching the imagination, it does complement a meatier front tire on trail bikes, or double up well on downcountry bikes. It fits in next to competitors like the Maxxis Forekaster and Specialized Ground Control, which work well in variable conditions without dragging down your pace. The shoulder knobs are strong enough to corner aggressively within the confines of a short-travel bike. In the center, marginally shallower and smaller lugs are spaced out. This helps the tire clear softer soil while still rolling fast on hardpack tracks. I love how simple WTB makes it to understand their tire specifications. Words like “light” and “tough”, or “fast rolling” and “high grip” help filter out the applicable tire for your needs. The Peacekeeper only comes in the lighter casing and a 29x2.4” size. Across the board, the TriTec rubber formula is used to balance grip versus wear. Breaking it down further, there are two types of “Light” casings, 60 or 120 TPI. The latter being more supple, but less robust. The weight ranges from 741 g to 1,024 g, depending on the casing and amount of tackiness. And yes, there’s a tanwall option, too. Shimano SH-GE900GTX shoes Features • Gore-Tex materials • Boa Fit System dial and Velcro strap • Upper ankle cuff • Sizes: 38-48 • MSRP: $300 USD • shimano.com Shimano’s old MW7 wet-weather shoe was a favorite. Bringing that up to date with a Boa Fit System is the GE900GTX. The dual-action of a Velcro strap up top and a Boa Fit System across the forefoot allow for fine-tuning the pressure throughout. The sizing is true, and the width is decent Underneath, the rubber isn’t the grippiest, but then again, these aren’t for flat pedals. The sole isn’t brutally stiff, which does make uphill walks marginally easier, but be mindful if you’re hiking up slick rocks or across logs. Some riders may wish for more support underfoot depending on the size of their pedal platforms. The cleat positioning moves reward enough, but I would mind even further positioning behind the ball of the foot. Dario and I both spent time in the flat pedal version of this shoe and enjoyed them for their grip and dexterity, however, the design flaws are mirrored on the clip version, too. Despite bearing the Gore-Tex trademark and claiming to be “waterproof”, your feet will get wet in these shoes. A low cuff height is partially to blame for that. I’d also call out the materials as being splashproof at best. Given the high price point, I’d imagine most riders will want something 100% waterproof, or just stick to their regular shoes. Otherwise, they are a quality pair of shoes for anything besides blistering hot summer rides. And if the burnt orange isn’t your style, there’s a North Shore-approved black color available too. NoNormal Coffee paste Pocket coffee. Features • Mixes into hot or cold water • Light or dark roast flavors • 20 cups per tube (Sweet Black) • 45 mg caffeine per serving • Made in Switzerland • Price: $24 USD per tube / $64.80 for 3 • nonormal.com 30 cups per tube of the Classic Black flavor. Mix it in hot or cold water. Coffee goes hand in hand with cycling. In the past, I’ve relied on dehydrated coffee packets from boutique roasters, but that can get pricey. NoNormal’s approach is about half the cost. The beans are sourced from Colombia and made into the syrup-like substance in Switzerland. They say that you get 20 cups of coffee from a tube of the light roast Sweet Black, light roast flavor, or 30 cups from the Classic Black dark roast flavour. All it takes to mix up is 1 tsp stirred into hot or cold water. The Classic Black is sugar-free, whereas the Sweet Black is pre-sweetened with organic Swiss beet sugar. In NoNormal’s blog, you can even find some recipes to blend it into baked goods. This unique coffee concentrate comes in a convenient aluminum tube that packs down well. I see it to be most fitting for bikepacking adventurers, but also keep a tube in my truck in case I start to get drowsy on a roadtrip or just a long commute home. Akta Trail Vest Vests - so hot right now. Features • Two-way zipper • Self-packing pocket w/frame strap • 20k/20k waterproof material • Bluesign approved • Colors: grey • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • 132g (size MD) • Price: $142 USD • aktamtb.com Inside the outside. The pocket inverts into a carrying case with a bungee attachment. Why would you need a waterproof garment that doesn’t cover your arms? If you live in a coastal climate, the precipitation can roll in waves. Once those ominous clouds have unleashed a downpour, the water can stick around long after the sun has come out. Basically, you want a waterproof strip to cover the middle of your body, but some breathability under the arms. That’s where the Akta Trail Vest fits in perfectly. I’ll admit it seems a bit specific, but it’s highly useful for muddy downhill laps, even through the shoulder seasons. The back panel has a vent, while the front uses a two-way zipper, should you need even more airflow. At the same time, the material works well to block wind and covers your neck. If you get too hot with the vest fully unzipped, you can fold the vest into the single chest pocket and strap it to your frame. Rest assured, the 20k/20k material is waterproof and gets the Bluesign tick of approval. The Trail Vest comes in five sizes from XS-XL. I found the size medium to be loose enough to fit a light insulating layer underneath, without it flapping around on top of a jersey. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Check Out Reviews Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Check Out: Magnetic Glasses, Tacky Grips, Handguards, Protection From YT, & More
author: Matt Beer
product_name: Magnetic Glasses, Tacky Grips, Handguards, Protection From , & More
article_text: A lot of gear comes across our desks here at Pinkbike. Check Out is an occasional round up of everything our tech editors have gotten their hands on. Sometimes it's products we're doing long-term tests on, other times it's stuff we're stoked on but don't have time to fully review. And, sometimes it's crazy shit someone sent us unsolicited and we're having a laugh. Smith Shift Mag XL Glasses • Magnetic frame/lens • Polarized & clear lenses inc. • Various frame and lens colors • Dual-position rubber nose bridge • MSRP: $305 USD • smithoptics.com You only get two eyes, and you need both to get depth perception, which is why I’m all about eye protection. The Smith Shift Mag XL glasses are just that - the frame and lenses are enormous for maximum coverage, but a smaller version of the Shift Mag exists too. The term “mag” in the name comes from the magnet hinge-lock that allows for the lenses to be quickly swapped. Included is a solid carrying case that makes it convenient to carry the second lens on rides that start or end when the sun is blasting down on you. This gloss black frame comes with one clear lens and one photochromic lens to automatically adjust how much light passes through. That’s perfect for overcast days or trails that dart in and out of the trees. I found them to fit more loosely, similar to other straight armed glasses like the 100% Speedcraft and less like the face hugging POC Devour. The frames also sat a little further off of my face and let air flow under the lenses. If you’re plagued by your glasses fogging up, these might do the trick. Rubber grommets on the ear pieces and a two-way adjustable nose piece keep them in place. Those also grasp onto the slots in Smith helmets well, but are flexible enough to poke into vents in other helmets. Ergon GDH Team Grips • Raised palm pad • Waffle pattern on the under side • Soft and tacky rubber • Single lock-on clamp w/ 3mm allen bolt • MSRP: $39.95 USD • ergonbike.com There’s no denying the GDH Team grip looks awfully familiar. It’s not a direct copy of the popular ODI Elite Pro but there are similarities, such as the raised palm pad, waffle pattern on the underside and the flange on the inside. The main thing is that the GDH Team is extremely comfortable. Ergon derived an extra soft Gravity Control Rubber that is much softer than their other grips. On the eco-friendly scale, they score an SGS certification for being free of PBBs and PBDEs and are manufactured in Germany. A wide, single clamp locks down on the handlebar with a 3mm allen head bolt. On the outer edge, there’s a press-in plug to shrug off dirt naps. There’s only one rubber and color choice, black and gunmetal. For the dimensions, the diameter varies but the highest portion in the middle measures 32mm and the thinnest is 30mm. Length-wise, they take up 135mm of space on the handlebar plus the extra 5mm plug sticking out. Measured to the outside of the flanges, the rubber grip surface itself is 122mm wide. Abit MTN Shorts • Adjustable waist cams • 4 zippered pockets • Mid-weight fabric with DWR coating • Long, short version and athletic cut available • MSRP: $115 USD • Abitgear.com Abit Gear’s MTN Short is a mid-weight riding short that is versatile and comes in heaps of sizes with three fits, including two length options. The Regular fit seen here is offered in black or grey colors and sizes 28-38. I’m wearing a size 32 and found the standard length and fits my 80cm inseam nicely. There’s also a long version of the shorts that comes in the Regular fit or an Athletic cut with more volume in the glute area. Sizes range from 30-40 in both fits for the MTN Long shorts. Both versions are cut from the same 4-Way stretch, 90% nylon/10% elastane and are Bluesign approved. The material seems thin, but they’ve withstood serious abuse and are still light enough for XC days. DWR spray adds a thin spray shield without locking in moisture. They’re also covered by a lifetime warranty against defects. A regular jeans-style button and zipper fly closure can be cinched in at the waist by cam-lock on either side. Two thigh and two hand pockets on each side use YKK zippers and are well-positioned to access while riding. The lower thigh pockets double as vents and have a small sleeve to hold a device in place. Lewis Prosafe Handguards • Composite shroud • Hinged, aluminum clamp with 3mm allen bolt • 14cm wide shroud • 2 lateral positions • MSRP: $37.90 USD • Lewisbike.com Handguards aren’t just for channeling your inner moto spirit - they serve valuable protection purposes and you don’t have to be racing to utilize them. The Lewis Prosafe Handguards shrug off small branches that might whip your fingers or unexpectedly grab your brake levers. Tripling up on protection, it’s possible they could reduce the chance of a bent brake lever too. Lewis designed the aluminum brackets with a useful split, pivoting 22mm clamp, like Shimano and SRAM brake lever mounts. The allen bolt drops in from the top for quick installation and removal. The composite, bolt-on shrouds are adjustable and replaceable too. The shroud is 14cm wide and a flip-chip allows the shroud to move laterally, but they are fixed 10cm from the bar. That makes them slightly smaller than the Sendhits Nock V1s and there isn’t a cushion on the inside of the shroud. At just 108g, their weight is insignificant for the performance gains. YT Trail Knee Pads • D3O Ghost protection • Elastic calf strap • Silicone-backed cuffs • Highly breathable and flexible • MSRP: $59.99 USD • yt-industries.com YT Bikes have expanded their wearable goods from apparel to protection, which includes two styles of gloves and a lightweight knee pad. The small logos and neutral colors are subtle enough that you won’t be guilted for not riding a YT bike either. If you’re looking for a slim and subtle knee pad that will take the edge off mild crashes and allow you to pedal unobstructed, these could be the ticket. They’re seriously lightweight, comfy and breathable. The key to the breathability is the Ghost D3O orange energy-dissipating foam insert. Unlike regular D3O pads, this pad has a bunch of vented triangular shapes printed on a material backing for ultra mobility and air flow. It’s not as substantial as other solid D3O pads and receives a Level 1 safety certification. The pads rely on silicone-backed, elastic cuffs and one non-adjustable strap around the calf to hold them in place. There are no velcro straps - just slide them on. An abrasion-resistant material in the main front panel covers the D3O insert down a decent length of the pad, whereas the sides are a lighter lycra material with a vented portion behind the knee. Four sizes run from S-XL and come in just plain black. I managed to stretch into the medium size, which was surprising, as I usually wear large knee pads. A thorough size guide is available on the product page . YT Trail and Gravity Gloves The Trail glove is a lightly padded glove with no velcro cuff or padding. • Light outer material and palm • Elastic cuff • Black or grey outer material color choices • Smart phone compatible • MSRP: $19.99 USD • yt-industries.com Whereas the Gravity glove has added D3O knuckle and palm padding with a velcro wrist closure. • D3O knuckle and palm padding • Durable plam and outer fabric • Smart phone compatible • Velcro wrist closure • MSRP: $29.99 USD • yt-industries.com Matching the knee pads are a set of Trail gloves. An elastic cuff leads into a simplistic palm and thin outer material that offer up a choice of grey or solid black. There’s also the burlier Gravity glove with reinforcements throughout. That earns you D3O knuckle and palm padding, as well as a velcro wrist closure. I noticed that the knuckle padding was a bit off center and the wrist cuff runs tight and short. Both the Trail and Gravity gloves feature fingertips and have a silicone gripper to operate a smartphone screen. The glove sizes range from XS-XXL and I found the medium size in both models to fit comparably against other brands like Fox. Visit the high-res gallery for more images. title: Field Test Bonus: Can We Unlock the Rocky Mountain Instinct's Potential?
author: Henry Quinney
tags: reviews-and-tech, field-test, reviews, shocks, trail-bikes, rockymountain, fox-float-x
article_text: Rocky Mountain Instinct Compression Testing https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/AhanWGpz Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 20:02 The Rocky Mountain Instinct in this year's Field Test was a bit of a head-scratcher. On paper, it ticked all the boxes: geometry, features, and adjustment, all at an impressive weight, but when riding the bike it quickly became apparent that the shock tune wasn't to our liking. A shock tune is much like an opinion - what works best for you might well spell trouble for somebody else. At Pinkbike, we do try and be self-aware of our habits. A good rule of thumb is that if we're nearing the limit of the compression range on either end, that doesn't necessarily mean something is wrong - some testers generally prefer more compression damping, while others prefer less. Rider weight and riding style plays a role in this equation as well. But if we're at the limit of the range of adjustments, and still not near our preference, then that will be mentioned in the review. The Instinct is a $160 shock rebuild from being a very well-rounded bike. This is largely how it played out with the Instinct review, only my desire for more compression damping was universal across the testers. Yes, you could increase the spring rate, but then you had difficulty accessing the latter stages of the travel. It was frustrating, as there are a lot of good things about the Rocky, but, as we all know, you don't ride spec sheets or CAD drawings. Bike riding happens in the real world, and that's where the Instinct loses its edge. Kaz and I decided to embark on a day of testing to see how much we could improve the Rocky Mountain, or at least bring it closer in line with our own preferences, by tuning the compression alone. We had three tunes: the CS30, CS40, and CS50. Our test was simple: blind testing, the same air pressure with the same shock volume, and back-to-back laps. Kaz could then inform us of his preferred tune, as well as let the audience know what he liked, too. Of course, nobody is immune from innate bias and it's impossible to completely safeguard any run-and-gun test. That said, it represents something that anyone could do should they also have the opportunity to try different tunes. Trails like this are on the upper limit of what a trail bike will be ridden on. However, we also felt the bike was underdamped on a whole variety of different trails. Kaz, after a day of test laps, preferred the CS50 - which was the firmest tune. After seeing how much fun it was, I went home to Squamish, put some different color cable ties on the shocks, some tape over the serial numbers, took the o-rings off, and repeated the test. My findings aligned with his. It felt more supported and, as he put it, less "busy." When testing bikes, especially in a group test, it's important to review the bikes we receive and not see them as a platform to build out, change, alter, and adjust endlessly until they are perfect. Simply put, that's not replicating a lot of people's experience when buying a bike, nor is it reasonable to expect to have to change fundamental aspects just to make it the performance product that it's intended to be. Especially when you factor in the thousands of dollars you may have parted with for your brand-new mountain bike. The cost in Canada for Fox to service and re-valve your shock would be $230, or $160 in the US. That's not to mention there are many independent tuners that would also be able to help. Any tune is just a preference, and it's not to say that if you enjoy the light rebound compression tune on your bike then there is some good reason why you shouldn't. But it is also worth noting that on the same trails, on the same hill, and often on the same day, not all the bikes on this year's Field Test came under fire in the same way, and that when you have a tune that suits your preference, it will give you more confidence and control when descending. The 2025 Trail Bike Field Test is presented by Rapha & Schwalbe Posted In: Reviews and Tech Field Test Reviews Shocks Trail Bikes Rocky Mountain Fox Float X Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Field Test Review: 2024 Commencal Meta 5 SX - Supercross for a Reason
author: Henry Quinney
tags: reviews-and-tech, videos, field-test, reviews, enduro-bikes, commencal, commencal-meta-sx
article_text: Versatile Freeracer: Commencal Meta SX V5 Review | 2023 Enduro Bike Field Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/ZD5s3LWB Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:38 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST REVIEW Commencal Meta SX Words by Henry Quinney; photography by Tom Richards The Commencal Meta is one of the tried-and-true lineages of bikes. Although, for some reason it doesn't have the same brand-power as something such as the Enduro, the Genius or even the Trance, perhaps it should. Where those bikes have had one layout or design often underpinning the bike (FSR, kooky shocks, and Maestro suspension respectively) the Meta has hopped around. In its nearly 20-year history it's had various linkage designs, some confusing names and has featured all of the main wheel sizes. This V5 features a new twin link suspension system, it's called the SX to denote its mixed wheels (the standard Meta V5 is full 29"), and it's all-aluminum. The top-line V5 SX Signature we've tested has a retail price of $6,900 USD, and there are solid builds that start at $4,500. Commencal Meta 5 SX Details • Travel: 165mm / 170mm fork • Aluminum frame • 64° head angle • 77.5 seat angle • 442 (S/M) or 445mm (L/XL) chainstay length • Reach: 480mm (L) • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Weight: 36.7 lb / 16.7 kg • Price: $6,900 USD • More info: commencal.com The Meta SX is an alloy bike that offers balanced geometry, rather than extreme geometry. The 64-degree head angle is adequately slack and when paired with its high stack height (643 mm for the size large we tested) that means there is little to no risk of you ever feeling like you're going to get sent out the front door. These elements are balanced by comparatively long chainstays to help shuffle to rider's weight forward a little, all while reaping the descending ability of something slack and tall. The frame has some clean lines and neat features, but not all of them landed. The extra bosses on the underside of the top tube to store tools are nice, but what would have been nicer still is correctly placed water bottle mounts. In their place, the bottle contacts the shock. I first assumed that the reason it was so high, and so in the way, was because of the way the linkage moves. I figured it would probably foul the bottle if it was lower. However, after fettling with a shock pump and some cable ties I found a position that neither fouls the linkage or the shock. In fact, while we couldn't fit a small 22oz (650ml) in the stock position, with cable ties even a 26oz (760ml) bottle can sneak in. Why Commencal couldn't achieve this, I don't quite know. There are other dashes of pragmatism though, all of which are thankfully better executed. There are ports for easy internal routing, as well as internal headset routing through the headset. The headset itself has blanking plates installed but, should you have an interest, you could pop them out and run them through. The new suspension at the bike's heart is called the Virtual Contact System. It uses two small links that connect the swingarm to the front triangle, the upper of which also drives the shock. Where some bikes that use a dual link suspension layout have been at pains to keep the rear triangle and at least one link behind the seat tube, the Meta has both links in front. This isn't an issue, but it does mean the swingarm takes on some extra width as it navigates around the seat tube. Thankfully, this bike does have decent geometry because there is no adjustment to any key dimensions such as the head angle or rear center. We tested a size large, which has a reach of 480 mm and a rear center of 447mm. For the small and medium sizes, that rear center is reduced to 442 mm. The spec is solid, although the $6,900 price tag doesn't offer as great of a value as Commencal was known for in the past. Highlights include a Fox Factory X2 and 38, SRAM GX T-type drivetrain, Shimano XT brakes and solid DT EX1700 wheels. My only real bugbear would be that the 175mm seat post is too short for a bike with a seat tube angle approaching 78 degrees. With Maxxis DHRII DoubleDown tires installed our test bike weighed in at 36.7 lb. FIELD NOTES Climbing Climbing the Commencal is definitely something that could happen. It's definitely possible, it's just you'll end up thinking of reasons why you really don't want to. It is a big burly enduro bike, but there are plenty of big burly enduros that feel a lot snappier and responsive. It does provide an adequate if not spectacular platform for climbing, with good but not great levels of grip - it's not particularly willing to break into its stroke at slower speeds. On technical trails, it could definitely be a little more active, and perhaps has slightly too much anti-squat to feel like it can really find the traction no matter what you're riding over, but then again you'll also find yourself reaching for the climb switch on tarmac roads or pavement. Maybe that means it's in the sweet spot, but I'm not so sure. Truthfully, there are better climbing bikes out there. The rear suspension does some things very well, and I'll touch upon them in all their glory in the next section. The geometry and balance of the bike is good. Its high front, long rear center, and steep seat tube mean that there is plenty of weight on the front while still being very comfortable. You don't have to muscle up through tight sections because your weight is already in a good place. In regards to both climbing and descending, Commencal seem to really understand how to use a bike's geometry to the rider's advantage, it's just all the other things that hold this bike back when going up. Descending This bike is a very confidence-inspiring descender and thrives on trails that are fast and committed, and needs your position to be up and ready, looking out for what's next. The faster you go the better this bike is. Everything about it wills you - the suspension feel, the geometry, and, to some extent, the weight that you've just lugged to the top of the hill. It's true heavy metal. Thanks to that long rear end, you'll find yourself committing hard to banked turns, and feeling the confidence to transition from edge to edge at will. You only need to ask nicely, apply some weight into your inside hand and you'll be amazed at how this bike corners - especially considering how tall that front is. That said, you will also notice elements of flex creeping in. For me, it's passable, and I enjoyed the comfort it brought with it, but I certainly wouldn't want it to be any flexier through high-speed berms. When the suspension is in its stroke and moving it provides genuinely fantastic performance. It's responsive, with great support and bottom-out resistance. That said, it sometimes felt unwilling to use the initial part of its travel. Once that was overcome, however, it's one of the best systems I've personally ridden on enduro bikes and manages to offer a great blend of traction and support. On trails that don't work the bike so hard, I don't think it quite has the suppleness in the rear to really shine in terms of tracking. This obviously isn't just down to rider speed but also elements like steepness and how you brake, but I think the Commencal does do a more effectiive job at finding grip on medium to high speed trails. This bike, which I could get full travel out of in the right situations, is not one to tiptoe around the problem. Instead, it wills its rider to tackle everything head-on and deal with the consequences. That's in part down to the suspension but also the upright and balanced geometry. The shock does track well at higher speeds and gives a great feeling of stability, but more often than not I wish it would have been more supple at slower speeds. For an enduro bike, this is a very good descender and thrives on bike park trails. It has loads of pop, and in some ways is the freeride bike reimagined, only now you truly can pedal it. Technical Report Sensible Parts Kit (except for the seatpost): If we can forget about the dropper that is too short, and the bottle that doesn't fit, the Meta does have all the parts that you'd want. In fact, if it came with the 200mm length Fox Transfer, as it should do, it would be really smartly speced. The small touches of the alloy Renthal cockpit, alloy DT wheels, ODI Grips, and a SRAM GX T-Type drivetrain and Shimano XT brake pairing cover pretty much all the bases. It's almost exactly as I'd have it straight from the shop. It was a breath of fresh air compared to the silly carbon one-piece bars, horrible grips, and brake hoses that rubbed on the bars you could find on some of the other bikes. Fox Factory Suspension: In the game of RockShox versus Fox I think Fox has the slight upper hand currently. While the Zeb is a good fork, it can feel somewhat dull compared to the precise and honed tracking of the 38 GRIP2. Both are very good, but I was impressed not only by the frame's suspension design but also the way the Fox shock and fork kept pace with it. Noisy Bearings: If you heard this bike coming, you'd think it had done at least 3 seasons in the bike park. From day one this thing creaked, cracked, and clicked its way through the test. If it were my bike, I'd be interested in seeing what decent after-market bearings could do. That said, quality bearings aren't always the solution, and it's actually the facing and machining of the frame itself that can prove to be an issue. However, I would imagine and hope the former is more likely. Pros + Very capable descender + Great, balanced geometry + Comfortable + Shock performance is truly impressive on mid to high speed trails Cons - Water bottle didn't fit - On the cusp of being slightly too flexy - Frame developed creaking from pivot / bearing area fairly quickly The 2023 Enduro Field Test is presented Bluegrass Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos Field Test Reviews Enduro Bikes Commencal Commencal Meta Sx Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Field Test Review: 2025 Specialized Stumpjumper 15 Alloy
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, field-test, reviews, trail-bikes, specialized, specialized-stumpjumper-15-alloy, pinkbike-field-test
article_text: Specialized Stumpjumper https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/7aBFghTX Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 22:07 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST REVIEW Specialized Stumpjumper 15 Alloy Words by Dario DiGiulio; photography by Max Barron & Tom Richards The Stumpjumper is Specialized's now ubiquitous do-everything trail bike, with adjustability and adaptability integral to the bike's character. This year's update to the 15th iteration of the bike wasn't some massive overhaul, but more of a refinement, with very similar geometry to the outgoing Stumpjumper EVO, and the introduction of the Genie shock. While not necessarily required, this shock is core to the bike's purported improvement in suspension performance, and does achieve some impressive marks on paper. The Stumpjumper 15 was initially released as a carbon-only bike, which divisively only allowed for wireless shifting drivetrains. With the release of the alloy Stumpy came the option to run a cabled drivetrain, with all other features kept the same. There are an impressive six sizes to the Stumpjumper range, with significant geometry adjustments on tap for the end consumer. Stumpjumper 15 Comp Alloy Details • Aluminum frame • 145mm frame travel, 150mm fork • 29" or MX wheels • Horst link suspension • 64.5° head angle • 76.5° seat angle • Reach: 400, 425, 450, 475 (tested), 500, 530mm • Chainstay: 430, 432, 435, 435 (tested), 445, 445mm • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 (tested), S5, S6 • Weight: 16.3 kg / 36 lb • Price: $4,500 USD • More info: specialized.com Head tube angle, bottom bracket height, and fit figures are all editable via a flip chip on the chainstay of the bike and a series of quick-change headset cups up front. If the full 29" setup isn't to your liking, the bike can be set up MX via the use of a different rocker link and rear wheel. Worth noting is the fact that the S1 and S2 bikes are mixed-wheel only, meaning the 29" rear wheel option is eliminated to keep geometry and fit more consistent. The geometry of the Stumpjumper scales nicely over the size range, with 25mm jumps in reach matched by a healthy growth in stack height (608, 618, 627, 640 tested , 654, 667mm) to keep the fit consistent. The chainstay length grows through the range as well, with essentially three rear center lengths splitting the range. This Stumpjumper 15 Comp Alloy is the least expensive bike in this test by a healthy margin, ringing up at $4,500 USD. For the price, you get a well-chosen parts spec, and the same adjustments and suspension performance as the highest-end models in the range, with prices nearly triple that of the bike we tested. As is often the case with bikes that hit impressive budget targets, the aluminum Stumpy isn't terribly light. Tipping the scales at 16.3kg / 36lbs, this is also our heaviest bike on test - but you'll need to read further to see how that shook out on trail. FIELD NOTES Climbing The Stumpjumper 15's updated suspension design touts big-bike travel feel, which isn't always good news for those who value climbing performance above all else. That said, the Stumpy's ability to hum through rough and technical terrain is very impressive, thanks in part to that active suspension feel. Rear wheel traction is plentiful, meaning you can focus on putting down power without worrying about spinning out when things get tricky. As terrain smooths out and climbs hit a more consistent gradient, the Stumpjumper isn't the most energetic of the bunch, due more to the active suspension and slack seat angle than the weight of the Comp Alloy bike we had on test. The climb switch will be a friend to some, though most of us opted to climb with the bike open in most circumstances. Those looking to maximize the pep in the new Stumpjumper platform would benefit from adding more spacers to the Genie, to reduce the extra volume and add some support through the main portion of the stroke. There's a tradeoff with grip, but that's to be expected. Descending The Stumpjumper can be a hard bike to pin down for a simple review given the breadth of adjustments and alterations you can make to the platform. That said, it seems to shine brightest in its stock setup, with the neutral headset orientation and high bottom bracket setting. No painting yourself into a corner, this is simply where the Stumpy feels most adaptable and ready to tackle varied terrain. The adjustments go deeper on the Stumpjumper 15 though, as shock setup plays a critical role in the dynamic ride quality of the bike. Put plainly, the Genie's wide setup window allows you to take the suspension feel all the way from taut and energetic all the way to deep and planted, all via the air can spacers. As above, too many variables to comment on all at once, but yet again the bike seems to shine in the stock setup. With one or two volume spacers in the extra volume chamber, the Stumpy has a very deep and gluey feeling suspension, much like a bike with a decent bit more travel might. The geometry and corresponding handling of the bike feels natural, intuitive, and well rounded, which lends itself to the varied riding one might expect to do on a capable trail bike. A few of us regularly referred to the bike as "safe" feeling, thanks to an easygoing nature that managed to extend even into some pretty gnarly sections of trail. With some work on the adjustment front, and after careful setup of the shock, I think the right rider could find great success racing this bike. As to that "right" rider, it can be hard to define, but a light touch seems to accentuate the bike's talents well. In this way, the Stumpjumper should work well for new riders and experienced folks alike, growing with that skillset and adapting well to changes in terrain. Technical Report Genie Shock: The Genie is a novel approach to shock design, relying on a large volume main air chamber, which effectively becomes a smaller volume at 70% of travel. While this transfer point is not adjustable, the volume of the two chambers is, meaning you can alter the spring curve of the bike as it goes through travel. That alteration requires some time, a shock pump, and ideally a repeatable section of trail where you can bracket through all the spacer settings to get a sense of which you prefer. This isn't required by any means, but like the other adjustments one can make to the bike, the shock setup is worth playing with in order to optimize the Stumpjumper for you. Specialized Alloy Wheelset: The components on these bikes suffered a hard two weeks in Whistler, wheels chief among them. The aluminum wheelset on the Stumpjupmer felt comfortable and held tension fairly well through testing, but wasn't quite as bombproof as others, suffering one nasty dent that required some attention. That said, considering the abuse the wheel suffered, this wasn't too big a deal, and remained rideable for the rest of the test. TRP Trail EVO Brakes: These brakes essentially flew under the radar throughout the test, but don't let that come across as a detraction of any sort. They were plenty powerful, with impressively consistent lever action and light feel. Though they're not the biggest brakes in TRP's lineup, they'd be one of the last components I'd opt to swap off this bike. What's it Like to Live With? The Stumpjumper fairs well in this category, with easy adjustments, very large in-frame storage, and a build in tool to make quick trailside fixes as need be. The flip chip procedure can be done trailside, and the head tube angle adjustment is an equally easy procedure, though you may need to swap in a different cup if you want to go with the ±1° option. We noticed a few creaks in the rear suspension linkage, but found that an occasional check to the bolt tightness kept that at bay. Pros + Suspension is impressively sensitive + Extensive adjustment to alter geometry, wheel size, and shock performance + Can descend like a bigger bike Cons - Quite heavy for a trail bike, due primarily to frame weight - Genie shock warrants extended setup testing - Can be hard to balance sensitivity and support The 2024 Trail Bike Field Test is presented by Rapha & Schwalbe Posted In: Reviews and Tech Field Test Reviews Trail Bikes Specialized Specialized Stumpjumper 15 Alloy Pinkbike Field Test Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Field Test: Rocky Mountain Instinct - Geometry Vs. Suspension?
author: Henry Quinney
tags: reviews-and-tech, field-test, reviews, trail-bikes, rockymountain, rocky-mountain-instinct, pinkbike-field-test
article_text: Rocky Mountain Instinct Review | 2025 Pinkbike Field Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/Y6dHafAV Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 17:32 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST REVIEW Rocky Mountain Instinct Words by Henry Quinney; photography by Tom Richards & Max Barron The Rocky Mountain Instinct was bred for the very hills that we got to test it on, or near enough. Based in Vancouver, the Canadian brand certainly isn't short of the terrain where a bike like the Instinct shines. BC, in fairness, by its sheer variety, has something for most bike categories. However, when it comes to backcountry riding and pedal loops, riding the mid-travel aggressive bikes here can be the perfect companion for some of the lesser-known gems, as well as some of the modern classics. The Rocky has a lot going for it and hits all the high notes on the spec sheet one might expect from a mid-travel aggressive bike. The adjustment, the geometry, and the low weight will all prove popular for somebody who wants their 140mm bike to do just about anything. Instinct C70 Details • Travel: 140mm rear, 150mm fork • Carbon Frame • 29" wheels (27.5" for XS & S) • Head angle: 63.5 - 64.3° • 76.4° seat-tube angle • Reach: 480mm (L neutral) • Chainstay length: 437 or 447mm • Weight: 31.3 lb / 14.2 kg • $6,499 USD • More info: bikes.com The Instinct pairs a 150mm fork to that rear travel. The bike uses 29" wheels in all but its smallest sizes and three different ways to adjust its geometry. It now also includes a storage compartment. The internally routed cables can be run in either orientation, and are clamped on the entry to try and reduce rattling. In terms of geometry, our size large bike was built around a 480mm reach. That can be increased or decreased by 5mm thanks to the supplied headset cups. A nice touch is that the cups can be pressed into the frame by hand, so you could theoretically do it trailside. The bike also has a chainstay adjustment of 10mm. That said, that's not with a UDH hanger. If you do want to run a T-Type mech, you would be forced to run the bike in the 447mm rear-centre option. Since this is a Rocky, it of course has a multi-directional flip chip at the lower eyelet. In past years, there could be as many as nine different settings. Now, riders must make use of just four. Thankfully, that's ample. All of the adjustments mean that there are many, many different options in regard to geometry. The head tube angle can be as slack as 63.5 degrees or as steep as 64.3 degrees; 64 degrees is the middle, neutral position. In that same neutral position, the seat angle is 77 degrees. FIELD NOTES Climbing The Instinct was the second lightest bike on test, and that alone makes it feel sharper on the climbs than some others. That said, the low weight isn't the overriding characteristic when riding this bike. Off the geometry sheet alone, the Instinct has a lot going for it. The front wheel feels planted and secure when climbing, and the bike has the feeling of being in range and maneuverable despite the longer rear center. The beauty of the adjustment is, of course, that with that light frame, it could just be a canvas to paint your ideas of what a trail bike should be onto. The bike is very progressive, meaning it tends to sit quite deep and feel quite soft under power. It's certainly not a spritely feeling when putting the power down. It's not often I make use of climb switches, but with the Instinct it's something I was reaching for. When using it, you did get a bike that tracked worse, even if it was more efficient, and it subsequently struggled to find grip over edges or bumps. In the open mode, it can deliver a lot of grip, but you are presented with two very opposing choices with the Instinct should you reach for the switch. When open, the low ride height combined with a low bottom bracket height makes pedal strikes something you have to be cognizant of. Descending Reviewing the Rocky is tricky. I'm going to break it down into two parts: the suspension and the geometry. "I need a bazillion more PSI in here, maybe a bazillion and two." While I was shouting this over shakey GoPro footage, it should be worth noting that I still only used 75% of the stroke. Early in the test, we removed all the volume spacers of the shock to try and give a bike that offered more support, but even then, the bike just felt far too progressive. The compression tune on this bike is simply too light. While I do have a track record with running more compression damping than most, in this instance, I think Rocky has got it wrong. All of the testers found it to be underdamped. The Instinct uses the start of its stroke very easily and was incredibly reluctant to use the end of the stroke. If you dropped the spring rate, you might get closer to bottom out, but would have something that felt very unstable to ride. If you found something that was more supportive, you would bounce off the ramp in progression. All in all, it was a very difficult bike to really get to grips with. Various testers found it better in the shorter chainstay position to reduce leverage over the shock. I personally ran it with a slightly softer spring rate and with the climb switch on. Is this the desired, best, or an adequate long-term solution? Absolutely not, but it did showcase how well the Rocky could ride if only it offered more compression damping. In some ways, the Instinct is its own worst enemy because it gives riders the geometry that will encourage them to thrash and charge, which directly undermines the shock's abilities. The geometry is balanced and suitable, and it wants to let the rider descend with confidence. The adjustment gives riders a lot of options, but at its core, in the neutral setting, the Instinct is fundamentally a well-proportioned bike. 636mm stack, 480mm reach, and a 77-degree seat tube angle are exactly where I would want those key dimensions, and when combined with the 480mm reach and 448mm rear end, it has the bones to be right at the sharp end of what a trail bike is in 2024. It's just a shame the shock tune can't cash the cheque. Overall, the bike does have decent angles and a sharp feeling underneath you that is direct without feeling harsh. If this were a long-term review, I would have experimented with a firmer compression tune and maybe even a coil shock, but sadly, our time frame during the test was slightly too condensed to do it in a meaningful way where I could then come back to you with a tune that I thought really let the bike shine. Technical Report Fox Suspension: The Fox Performance Elite 36 is as high-end as you could ever really need from the brand. Yes, it might not be shimmering gold, but it has all the key parts you would want. Fox does make some really great shocks; the tune on the Float X was to blame in this instance for the bike not delivering the performance it should have. Adjustment Galore: If a rider knows what they want their bike to feel like, Rocky has you covered. If you don't, then through simple experimentation you can quickly learn what works for you thanks to the adjustments on offer. They're not completely hassle-free but represent as near to a seamless process as you get on a mountain bike in 2024 for changing key dimensions. What's it Like to Live With? The frame storage on the bike is well sorted, with one of the largest openings. It also has an AirTag fitting on the underside of the door, which is a nice touch. The adjustments of the bike will make it a fan favorite with die-hard riders, but it should also hopefully make it fit everyone better. The +/- 5mm adjustment on the headtube is simple yet effective. Plus, bikes using the standard hanger will also benefit from the easy chainstay adjustment, which can not only affect the whole balance of the bike but can even be a way to fine-tune the shock feeling. The Ride4 adjustment is good, but it does feel somewhat ironic that even with four different settings, three of which are there to provide meaningful changes to both the shock and geometry, there isn't any setting that makes the shock feel good, and the most meaningful and useful geometry adjustments happen elsewhere on the bike. The bike happened to be quite noisy and was one of the loudest bikes on the test - it seems to be the housing rattling against the inside of the frame, something that some foam or heat shrink tubing could potentially fix. Pros + Balanced geometry + Very adjustable + Light + Well designed storage Cons - A shock tune that undermines the geometry - T-type compatibility issues in short chainstay setting - Noisy The 2025 Trail Bike Field Test is presented by Rapha & Schwalbe Posted In: Reviews and Tech Field Test Reviews Trail Bikes Rocky Mountain Rocky Mountain Instinct Pinkbike Field Test Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Field Test: Spot Mayhem 140 - Conservative Geo, Liberal Amounts of Fun
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, field-test, reviews, trail-bikes, spot, spot-mayhem
article_text: Spot Mayhem Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/sNYV6f5Q Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 17:17 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST REVIEW Spot Mayhem 140 Words by Matt Beer; photography by Tom Richards The Spot brand of bikes may be best recognized for introducing a leaf spring to a few of their full-suspension bikes. One of those is the new Mayhem 140, which was designed to be both stronger and lighter than their previous offerings. The unique suspension design still remains, with a small titanium leaf spring that runs from the downtube to the swingarm. Lying at the shorter end of the trail bikes here on test, the Mayhem 140 has, you guessed it, 140mm of rear wheel travel. Our bike was built with a 150mm fork, but those focused on descending will be happy to know that the Mayhem can handle a 160mm fork as well. Spot Mayhem Details • Carbon frame • Dual 29" wheels • 65.2° head angle • 77° seat angle • Reach: 415, 445, 475, 505mm • Chainstay: 435mm • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Weight: 14.1 kg / 31 lb • Price: $9,499 USD • More info: spotbikes.com The carbon frame rides on 29” wheels and is the only bike in the bunch that doesn’t offer any geometry adjustments. Also absent is an In-frame storage compartment - another box not ticked for the Mayhem. Aside from that, the cables are internally routed, the chainslap protection is looked after, and a standard water bottle fits inside the front triangle on its unique mounting bracket. Geometry figures like a 65.2-degree head tube angle (with a 150mm fork, as tested) and 435mm chainstays don’t exactly paint this bike as a downhill brawler, however, numbers don’t always translate as how we expect them to on the trail. The Mayhem surprised us in two of our introductory challenges; one up and one downhill. Our “6-Star” build from Spot spares no expense and makes the Mayhem the most expensive bike on test at $9,499 USD. This includes RockShox Ultimate suspension, a SRAM XX1 Transmission drivetrain, and carbon wheels from Industry Nine. FIELD NOTES Climbing Whistler’s valley trails (those outside the bike park) will swiftly point out a bike’s flaws due to their steep and tricky climbs. The Mayhem proved to be a brilliant climber in areas such as riding position, traction, and efficiency. Being the lightest bike on test probably didn’t hold it back either. The head angle is in the steeper zone, the stack is slightly higher, and the chainstay is short, but you know what? When linked up with supportive suspension like the Mayhem’s, it all equates to an exceptional climber. On paper, those 435mm chainstays might lead you to believe the Mayhem would be prone to looping out on steep climbs. That’s not the case though. Whatever is happening underneath you with that funky leaf spring, well, there’s some magic to it. That may be due to how the anti-squat rises towards the sag point, similar to Propain’s Pro 10 suspension design, and hence, where Spot’s “Inversion“ moniker comes from. That doesn’t diminish from the traction on tap either. The wheel hugs the ground impeccably well over small bumps without detracting from the forward momentum you put into the cranks, making the Mayhem light on its feet. Another bonus is how the system holds the rider forward and upright on the bike. The Mayhem doesn’t lag around tight switchbacks or stumble in those stop-start instances. Pedal strikes aren’t an issue and that allows the Mayhem to scoot through tight cruxes with ease too. Descending Surely then, the best climber must be the worst descending bike? That’s not always the case, nor is it true of the Mayhem. This is a bike that wants to pump and jump, rather than tip in and hang on, though. As we saw in our “Perceived Time” test, it’s a bit of a live wire. The Mayhem nabbed the fastest time but took a good amount of focus to keep it pointed straight. You get the sense of riding on top of the bike, rather than settled down in between those big wheels. It’s also snappy and reactive, helping it navigate tight, slow trails in a flash. The downside is that the front wheel can tend to tuck or twist when dropping into steppy corners. Once the grade of the trail really falls away, the front wheel can have the effect of riding underneath your hands. Reaching the limit of the Mayhem happens sooner than the longer, lower, slacker bikes, such as the Canyon Spectral CF. And what of that Living Link leaf spring? It is simply another spring, meaning, it resists and restores energy in combination with air shock. You can read more in depth about the influence it has on the leverage curve here . Coincidentally or not, it provides a springy, light reaction to the suspension. The initial break away isn’t as smooth as the Specialized Stumpjumper, but then again, nothing else on test came close. There is, however, a lot more support earlier on in the travel that keeps the Mayhem from floundering in the middle of its travel. Frame stiffness has become an increasingly popular topic of discussion amongst us tech editors. Spot tout the improved stiffness on the new Mayhem 140 over the outgoing model and that can certainly be felt on the trail, although it’s not over the top. The front end has a solid feeling, similar to the Santa Cruz Bronson. Out back, it's toned down a degree. The Mayhem offers a solid ride without any harshness when going down rough trails. While the Mayhem won’t encourage you to chase friends on enduro bikes down fall line descents, it will leave them feeling deflated on fast-paced trails where a dynamic rider is rewarded with bursts of speed. Nothing is lost in the suspension, for better or worse. Technical Report Tire Clearance: Whether it’s a constraint of the linkage design or the robust appearance of the chainstay yoke, the gap between the frame and tire is incredibly close. Our 29 x 2.5 Schwalbe Alberts don't have the most aggressive tread out there either (they measure exactly 2.4" in width, although they are on the taller side when measured from the rim sidewall to the top of the tire). Periodically, we experienced instances while climbing where the rear wheel would lock up as the tire wedged debris against the rubber protector. The swingarm also crunched a few rocks against the bottom bracket area too. According to Spot, part of the reason for that tight clearance is the thickness of the rubber frame protection. From the sounds of things, they're working on an update, but for now there's not a ton of room around the tire. RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate: This shock hasn’t let me down on any bike I’ve swung a leg over. That holds true for the Mayhem as well. There’s a wide berth of adjustment given by each turn of a clicker. On the ending stroke, the built-in hydraulic bottom out control is handy to have as well since the Mayhem leverage rate isn’t overly progressive. Industry Nine EN325 Carbon: These carbon rim-alloy spoke wheels are as unique as the bike they’re mounted to. The alloy straight pull spokes have a count of 24 per rim. At the center, the Hydra rear hub doesn’t wait around. With just 0.52-degrees of engagement, they're as close to instantaneous as you’ll get for a pawl system. Ease of Ownership Despite different design methods, there’s not much to critique the Mayhem on. The layout of the frame is clean, aside from the interruption of the reinforcing Slaybar. The frame doesn’t require any proprietary components or have strange standards. There’s tidy internal cable routing, full seat post insertion, and a water bottle mount inside the front triangle, and on the lower link. The leaf spring also reduces the number of bearings used in total on the frame, at least, compared to a dual-link bike. Not all cages will hold the bottle tight enough to keep it from tapping against the shock though, as we found out with our Elite Prism model. A small issue also arose earlier on in the First Ride when the linkage started to creak, although that escalated into a secondary issue. Torx 30 bolt heads aren’t commonly found on a multi-tool either, so you’ll have to be prepared to tighten loose hardware trail-side, should that occur (we didn’t have any issues during the Field Test). Once extracted and greased, the Mayhem withstood our thrashing. Pros + Great suspension feel + Excellent climbing performance + A trail bike through and through Cons - Very limited tire clearance - T30 frame hardware is annoying - No in-frame storage (although there's plenty of room for a frame bag) Note: In the time since this review aired, Spot has taken steps to increase the amount of tire clearance. Here's the update from Spot: What we know: • With the current rubber protection and stock tire (Maxxis Dissector 2.4WT EXO+), we measure 6.5mm of clearance. The Aggressor 2.5WT shows the same clearance. • With the protection modification we’re implementing, clearance increases to 9mm, providing significantly more room to accommodate a wider range of tires. • The new Schwalbe Albert used as the control tire is 2.4” wide but is taller than other 2.4” tires, highlighting the reduced clearance. What we’re doing: • We’ve updated the chainstay protector to maximize clearance. The rubber still extends across the chainstay bridge but doesn’t duck into the wheel well. • This new part will be included on all future bikes and sent to existing customers who want the additional clearance. We'll also stock the updated protector as a replacement part. When we’re doing it: Now. We’re implementing this change starting immediately. Existing owners can reach out for the new protector. The 2025 Trail Bike Field Test is presented by Rapha & Schwalbe Posted In: Reviews and Tech Field Test Reviews Trail Bikes Spot Spot Mayhem Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Field Test Review: 2023 Unno Burn - You Know You're Curious
author: Henry Quinney
tags: reviews-and-tech, videos, field-test, reviews, enduro-bikes, unno, unno-burn
article_text: Y'know You're Curious: Unno Burn Review|2023 Enduro Bike Field Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DUIOJGH2 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:28 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST REVIEW Unno Burn Words by Henry Quinney; photography by Tom Richards Unno is a brand whose reputation precedes them. As a mountain biker, I seem to know a lot about the brand, whether it's their inception under the leadership of designer Cesar Rojo, their initial forays into domestic carbon fiber production or their boutique bikes that seem to have little regard for convention. Quite honestly, it was an exciting prospect to ride one of these sought-after machines. Unno says they "started again from zero" to update their enduro platform, and the Burn is nothing if not out there . To look at it, there seems to be elegance mixed with brutish features, pragmatism mixed with absurdity. Unno Burn Details • Travel: 160mm rear, 170mm front • Carbon frame • Mixed wheels • 64° head-tube angle • 76.5° seat-tube angle • Reach: 470mm (S2) • Chainstay length: 445mm • Weight: 33.4 lb / 15.1 kg • $8,897 USD • More info: unno.com The Burn features its own novel suspension layout and it's not just radical in its looks - Unno suggests running this at around 30 to 40% sag. They can do this because the bike has a very progressive kinematic curve. This level of progressivity will ensure that there are no harsh bottom-outs. Although much of the progression happens in the beginning portion of the stroke (one of the reasons for the Burn's sensitive initial travel), a kinematic like this can make it difficult to use full travel other than during very large impacts. This might not be a bad thing if you demand lots of initial activity and want to save the rest of the travel for a rainy day, but there is a reason why most bikes don't offer this level of change throughout the stroke. Our test bike, which was a size S2, was the Race model and retails for just less than $9,000 USD. There are frame-only options, plus an entry point Elite and Factory model to sit on either side of the Race in their pricing. Our bike featured an SRAM GX AXS drivetrain, Formula Cura 4 brakes, Fox Factory suspension, alloy Crankbrothers Synthesis e-bike wheels, and a Duex enduro one-piece bar. The bar itself is an interesting prospect. Despite it looking quite wild, dimensionally it's nothing too out-there. Its flat shape obscured its actual rise, which is achieved by the sleeve of the stem extending quite a long way down the steerer. The bike features in-frame storage, a universal derailleur hanger, and through-the-headset cable routing. The geometry of the S2 impressed us with its sheer balance. Its high stack, middling-to-short 470mm reach, and 445mm stays all played their role in this. Although the reach might not be in the same vein as other bikes meant for those around 175 to 185cm in height, which might typically have 480-485 mm of reach, it proved a hit with us. There was one significant outlier though - the seatpost and seat tube. At 460mm the seat tube is very long. This in itself isn't the end of the world, and you can get it low enough to give enough clearance. The plot thickens, though, because the seat tube sits directly above the shock - it's not only very high but also with a short insertion depth. This means that while the post is high, you also can't fit in a long dropper (our test bike had a 150mm post when something over 200mm would have been preferred). With the saddle dropped it was too high, and if you lowered the seat post itself to an appropriate length for a descent it would then be too low for pedaling when at full extension. It's further exacerbated by the fact that it doesn't use a seat tube collar but rather a wedge to clamp against the post. FIELD NOTES Climbing The Unno offers a great platform for climbing. Due to the fact that it's so happy to get into its stroke its tracking is very good, and, even with anti-squat values of around 120 to 90% depending on where in the sag range of 30 to 40% you are, the grip is good and there is a great degree of composure in terms of how the rear wheel handles the terrain. It's very happy to follow the contours of the ground and also give a decent degree of efficiency. It's also the joint lightest bike on test. The 76.5-degree seat angle isn't the steepest, but the climbing position itself was comfortable and upright, thanks to the high stack. On the steepest climbs the tall front end did occasionally make the front end feel a bit light, but otherwise the Unno was a capable, efficient climber, as well as being one of the lightest bikes in this roundup. Descending The Burn looks fast, and in the right situations (steep, rough tracks) it comes to life. The squat links that connect the front triangle to the swing arm are neat and provide a taut, stiff platform to really drive the bike. The geometry plays in that too. It made for a bike that had lots of the rider's weight going through their feet, and it meant that you could focus on applying weight with your hands when you want to initiate a turn or pump the bike, rather than ever being pulled forward or having large amounts of weight in your hands by default. The geometry cuts a sensible balance, and one that feels like it's been honed in on during real-world riding and years of reflection. It's interesting to see something so balanced with a suspension system so extreme. There are other examples of both virtues and vices juxtaposing one another. For instance, the frame storage sits beneath a panel that holds the water bottle. It works well and is both simple and effective. Yet, it leaves the bottle rattling on the underside of the top tube. The suspension gives a feeling that thrives on sleep-slow tech, but then the seat tube is so long, with such short insertion depth, that it means you'll struggle to get the saddle out of the way for the very trails the bike was meant for. It's a fascinating bike and they've clearly compromised some elements or dimensions to grant the real estate to make their own suspension platform, and it poses a question right at the heart of the issue - is any suspension platform good enough for these compromises? Probably not. Is this suspension system a game-changingly wonderful take? I wouldn't say so. It's okay in certain situations, such as the steeper technical trails, but it also struggles because while you may never hit bottom out, you do often hit that wall of support. During my testing, I experimented between 140-210 psi, which is a huge swing and didn't get bottom out in either. In fact, the 210 psi felt better if only because it gave more initial support, and I just learned to accept that I would largely be using less than full travel. All these problems, and the intense ramp-up, mean that on flat-out fast trails, this bike can be fatiguing. You're not bouncing off the bottom out, but you may as well be. It can also lead to a hanging-up sensation in some instances that will not only punish mistakes but also your wheels. After our test period, the rear was in a sorry state. Technical Report Finishing Kit: The bike isn't cheap, so it's frustrating that you'd want to immediately explore options in terms of low-stack seat posts. Plus, if it were my bike, I would take the bars and grips off too. They're just a kook too far for me. That's not even to mention the E13 cassette and YBN chain in a combination that didn't offer as good a shift as I have come to expect from a full complement of SRAM parts. Formula Brakes: The Foruma Cura 4 Brakes offered good power, even if the feel didn't give as much bite as I would personally like. That said, there was plenty of modulation and adjustment on offer, although sadly the latter wasn't tool-free. Pros + Good grip offered at slower speeds + Solid, balanced geometry + Climbs well in terms of suspension performance Cons - Water bottle rattles on underside of top tube - Odd finishing kit - Seat tube is too long and seatpost too short - Arguably too progressive The 2023 Enduro Field Test is presented Bluegrass Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos Field Test Reviews Enduro Bikes Unno Unno Burn Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: First Ride: 2025 Rocky Mountain Element
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: First Ride Rocky Mountain Element Words: Henry Quinney Photos: Margus Riga Are we there yet? Mountain bike geometry has gone from huge strides to measured paces, and now we're at the stage where new bikes seem to be about nips-and-tucks rather than page-one rewrites. The new Rocky Mountain Element typifies this. There have been some changes to the suspension layout and some small refinements to the geometry, but largely, it's a further evolved bike. And that's no bad thing. If you're going to gently update anything, it may as well be something as highly regarded as the incumbent Element. When released in 2021, it was met with near-universal praise as it sat front and center of the downcountry trend. Rocky Mountain Element Details • Wheel size: 29" • Travel: 120mm, 130mm fork • Carbon frame • 65.5° head angle (neutral) • Chainstay length: 435mm (S-XL) • Sizes: XS (27.5"), S, M, L, XL (29") • Weight: 26lb 14oz (L Carbon 70 w/o pedals) • Price: $4,499 - $11,999 USD • More info: bikes.com Frame Details The Element doesn't forget its roots, and still has water bottle mounts aplenty. On most frames, there are two on the downtube, then another on the underside of the top tube to store tools. Rocky has done a solid job of trying to maximise each size's ability to carry bottles while also having to operate within the obvious constraints. The XS frame has to make do with one 650ml bottle, the small can handle a 650ml plus a Fidlock 450ml bottle and the other sizes can handle two 770ml bottles. It's not perfect, but it's good to see them try and tailor the best solution for each size. The bike has fully enclosed cables, which can be run either way meaning that everyone will be able to get tidy cables. Interestingly, there isn't frame storage on the Element. It has similarly slender tubes as the outgoing Element, and I can only speculate it wasn't worth ballooning the downtube, while also making it presumably heavier or weaker, or perhaps both. Suspension From afar this looks to have the same suspension layout also. However, if you look closer you'll see that there is no longer a bearing near the rear axle. This saves around 350 grams, which is not to be sniffed at. The rear triangle is now a swingarm that relies on a flex pivot. Rocky calls this their Smoothlink SL suspension and says that they aim for it to behave like the four-bar equivalent. All shocks are made with size-specific tunes. That said, they all use the same rear-end size. So, while the tune might be specific, Rocky hasn't gone all in like some brands have to tune the frame size to both the kinematic and the shock. Geometry It wouldn't be a Rocky without a geometry adjusting flip chip, in this case the Ride-4 configuration. With it, riders can choose between 4 different settings that alter geometry and shock feel. Models Element Carbon 99 Fork : RockShox Sid Ultimate Flight Attendant, 120mm travel, Auto-Adjust Flight Attendant Damper, 42mm offset. Shock : RockShox SIDLuxe Ultimate Flight Attendant, 190x45mm, size-specific tune. Brakes : SRAM Level Ultimate Stealth 4-Piston. Shifters/Derailleur : SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Wireless. Crankset : SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Carbon, 32T, Power Meter, DUB Spindle. Wheelset : DT Swiss XRC 1501 Carbon, 29” for all sizes except XS, which is 27.5”. Tires : Maxxis Rekon WT 3C MaxxTerra EXO Tubeless Ready, 2.4. Seatpost : RockShox Reverb AXS Wireless Post. Element Carbon 70 Fork : Fox 34 Float Performance Elite, 130mm travel (120mm for XS), GRIP X Damper. Shock : Fox Float Performance Elite, size-specific tune. Brakes : SRAM Level Bronze Stealth 4-Piston. Shifters/Derailleur : SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Wireless. Crankset : SRAM GX Transmission, 32T, DUB Spindle. Wheelset : Race Face ARC 27, 29” for all sizes except XS, which is 27.5”. Tires : Maxxis Rekon WT 3C MaxxTerra EXO Tubeless Ready, 2.4. Seatpost : Fox Transfer Performance Elite Dropper. Element Carbon 30 Fork : Marzocchi Z2 Float EVOL Rail, 130mm travel, 44mm offset. Shock : Fox Float Performance, size-specific tune. Brakes : Shimano MT4120 4 Piston. Shifters/Derailleur : Shimano XT. Crankset : Shimano Deore, 32T, 24mm Spindle. Wheelset : WTB KOM Team i27 TCS 2.0, 29” for all sizes except XS, which is 27.5”. Tires : Maxxis Rekon 2.4 WT EXO Tubeless Ready. Seatpost : X Fusion Manic Composite. Element Carbon Frameset Rear Shock : Not included, rider's choice. PRICES Element Carbon 30 $5,599 CAD | $ 4,499 USD Element Carbon 50 $6,699 CAD | $5,299 USD Element Carbon 70 $8,499 CAD | $6,999 USD Element Carbon 90 $12,499 CAD | $9,999 USD Element Carbon 99 $14,499 CAD | $11,999 USD Element Carbon Frameset $ 3,599 CAD | $2,999 *Element C50 and Element C90 models are not available at launch. They will be released early in 2025. Ride Impressions Riding the Element felt like something a journey back in time, if only because I rode the exact same singletrack loop that I rode 3 years ago for the last Element's first ride. How does it compare? This bike is, broadly speaking, very similar. There is also three years difference between those rides, and memory can only do so much. The big difference that I felt was in the shock. The previous Element felt like you had to run a very high spring rate to control the shock through bigger compressions. This newer model feels a shade more progressive with more compression damping available from the shock. I can run it a lot softer, meaning it does an admirable job of hunting for grip while, thanks to the compression damping, it still retains that rocketship characteristic that made me love the other Element so much. I mainly rode it in the open compression setting, with the butterfly dial set to the firmest of the three possible positions. The Element climbs very well, and you can really plumb along smoother trails. The geometry is balanced, and gives you confidence without lethargy. My only small gripe would the Fox Grip X damper. I'm yet to get on one and settle on a setup, and it feels inferior to the outgoing, and still excellent, Grip2 damper. It was the Grip2 equipped 34 that I rode three years ago that got my nod for suspension component of the year, something it duly won. I would take that fork over the Grip X 34. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Rocky Mountain Rocky Mountain Element Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: First Ride: Auckland Cycle Works Marra Prototype with KOLARP Suspension
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: The Auckland Cycle Works Marra is a dual short-link enduro bike created by bike shop owner, Gary Ewing. The unusual linkage layout arises from an atypical, back-to-basics approach to bike design; rather than making use of any computer aided design, Gary chooses instead to crack out the Lego to visualize and model linkage layouts. That can be an incredibly useful exercise, at least in gaining some level of understanding as to how different linkages deliver different axle paths; Gary's video of this process is worth a view, if only to grasp just how aggressive the rearward axle path of his KOLARP suspension design is. However, it's worth pointing out that, at least to my knowledge, no other modern day suspension bike on the market has been designed in this way, i.e. without the use of a computer and the precision that comes with conventional suspension analysis tools. Marra V2 Details • Steel frame • 165mm travel, 180mm fork • Mixed wheels • Size tested: 451mm reach • Weight: 38 lb / 17.28 kg (w/o pedals) • Head angle: 64° • Seat tube angle: 76° • BB height (unsagged): 350mm • Rear-center length (unsagged): 416mm • Rear-center length (sagged): 441.5mm • Rear-center length at bottom-out: 476mm • Price: £5,500 (frame only, with custom geometry) • More info: aucklandcycle.works The Marra V2 The Reiver Outcomes of Gary's design process were on display at Bespoked in Dresden; the Marra and the Reiver are nothing if not head turners. Both run versions of the Kind of Like a Rearward Pivot URT design, though the Reiver is slightly more out there, given that it houses the BB on a moving link. The Marra is now available to pre-order for the lofty price of £5,500 for the frame. Tested here is the V2 prototype. Testing of prototypes isn't something we make a habit of, but it's nice to make an exception every once in a while. Also, having followed Gary's development story over the last few years, and upon learning that he doesn't know how he could possibly make the KOLARP suspension design any better than it already is, the temptation to give it a shot was all too much. Frame Details & Suspension Design The Marra V2 prototype is constructed from steel tubing, welded to Gary's specifications by Coal Bikes owner, Gavin White. Its suspension takes the form of a short link four-bar layout, with an idler pulley mounted directly to the lower link, such that its position relative to the chainring moves as the rear wheel is displaced. This does indeed fall under the scope of the I-Track patent. Sequence 04 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DoDpiQEc Copied Live 00:00 00:38 00:38 Through compression, the lower link rotates counter-clockwise for most of the travel, until it stops at 147mm and begins to rotate clockwise until bottom-out. The upper link rotates counter-clockwise throughout compression, driving the shock via an extender, which is unusually long in the context of the 65mm stroke shock that it's mounted to. The resulting axle path is aggressively rearward; unsagged, the rear-center is 416mm (as tested), extending to 441.5mm at sag, and 476mm at bottom-out.
