It's not 100% rearward; the inflection point comes at around 113mm travel, at which point the rear axle has moved rearwards along the horizontal by a substantial 32mm from the unloaded state - that's a bigger increase than we see on some of the highest single pivot enduro bikes, like the Forbidden Dreadnought , but not quite as much as the 45mm rearward movement exhibited by the wild-looking Aper KOMPace . There are two points of adjustment on this frame. Firstly, the rear-center length can be adjusted through 30mm, in 5mm increments, by virtue of the sliding dropout - Gary's unique version of which is T-Type compatible. That's a pretty generous range of adjustment. Lengthening the stay does of course increase the axle's leverage over the shock. Going from the shortest position to the longest position, the increase in leverage is enough to warrant around an 18 psi increase in shock pressure to achieve equivalent sag. Then, there's the forward shock mount. Though Gary's original intention was to have use of a full range of adjustment here, in practice, only a few of the furthermost forward positions are viable, owing to the startlingly high leverage ratios that the rearward positions give. More on that shortly. Ride Impressions I rode the the Marra in the 416mm chainstay position, with a 230mm x 65mm Cane Creek Kitsuma air shock mounted in the furthermost forward position, and a 180mm travel Fox 38 fork. That gives the bike its lowest possible bottom bracket height of around 350mm, which is pretty high in the grand scheme. Positioning it further back raises the BB, lengthens the reach, shortens the rear-center, and steepens the head and seat tube angles. Gary's recommendation was to run sag between 20% and 25%, with both high- and low-speed compression and rebound damping close to fully open. Weighing just 59 kg, that's how I would normally run the dials of any shock, to get it as active as possible at the relatively low pressures I would normally run. That said, that sag range is very much on the firm end of the spectrum, especially with such a long travel bike. For context, a conventional enduro bike would normally run around 30% sag, while a reduced sag of 20-25% is normally reserved for much shorter travel bikes, such as 100-120mm travel XC race bikes. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 31 Height: 5'4" / 163cm Weight: 130 lbs / 59 kg, kitted Hopping onto the Marra, the first thing I noticed was how tall the bike is. Relatively little sag combined with 155mm cranks from Hope, and the increased saddle height they require, gave me a center of gravity that felt substantially higher than I am accustomed to. I felt perched atop the bike, which took some getting used to, though the seat tube angle felt plenty steep enough to give an upright climbing position, with the BB tucked underneath my hips in a comfortable position. On the whole, the bike felt very inefficient on fire road climbs. I didn't feel as though the suspension was to blame here, owing to it feeling impressively neutral under pedaling; there really was little to no pedal-induced bob to speak of, even with the shock's damper settings fully open. I'm more tempted to apportion blame to the bike's 17.3 kg (38.1 lb) heft, and the extra drivetrain drag introduced by two extra pulleys; the tensioner pulley and the idler. I didn't do a great deal of technical climbing, but on the odd occasion I did, the rear end felt quite muted over bumps, and traction was not lacking. The bike has a reach of 451mm. That is approaching (but not exceeding) the upper end of the range that I'd choose to ride, but with a 35mm stem it was well within the realm of manageable. I was able to maneuver the bike around to hit my usual lines on well-known enduro trails without undue aggression. What I wasn't able to do was hit those lines with any of the usual pace I'm accustomed to. On the mellower gradient trails, littered with small gaps over furrows and root clusters, I found I was failing to make the backside of features that are normally very achievable. The bike felt sluggish through these twisty, busy sections of track, and was not able to carry good speed. The rear end felt harsh, as though it wasn't efficiently absorbing bumps, delivering a distinct lack of composure. Figuring out where to position my weight between the two wheels proved difficult, and it often felt as though I was getting rocked back and forth, never quite getting a good, consistent body position where the bike felt predictable. There were a few glimpses of things starting to work, but they were few and far between. I did, by and large, stick to Gary's recommendations of around 23% sag. Curiosity did get the better of me, however, and I did do one run with a more standard 30% sag just to see how that felt; sadly, it did not improve matters, and the bike simply felt as though it was folding in compressive corners, with marked tendency to understeer. To get some consistent runs, I completed seven shuttle-assisted laps of a well-established downhill race track. After a couple of sighting laps, I incrementally adjusted sag from around 25% down to 20%. With more air in the shock, the bike actually felt more supple off the top, with a less raw feel to it over the smaller bumps in the track. Still, it felt harsh on successive hits, jarring almost, and that feeling of the rear wheel getting easily hung up did not go away. That said, there was no discernible pedal kickback to speak of, even under firm braking through rough terrain. The harshness instead felt to be coming up through the bike, as though the rear wheel wasn't able to get up and out of the way over bumps, particularly while on the brakes over braking bumps. Throughout the day, I developed an enhanced appreciation for the benefits of not braking in corners - a rule of basic riding technique that can help one carry more speed through corners. This is of course something I've been familiar with for many years, but the benefits of rigidly applying that rule felt particularly important while riding the Marra, if only to make it more predictable through corners. In higher speed, well-supported corners where I could stay off the brakes, the Marra felt at its most predictable. The geometry felt well preserved throughout the apex of the turns, and it was easy enough to hold a consistent line on berms. As the number of laps increased, and the sag reduced, the riding experience improved as I subconsciously adapted to how the bike behaves. Dan Roberts ( of Behind the Numbers fame ), did a spot of suspension analysis on this one to help put some numbers to what's going on here. To be perfectly clear, I refrained from looking at any kinematic graphs until I had ridden the Marra on several occasions. The above account was written up in advance of seeing the outcome of the suspension analysis. The Marra V2 leverage curve, as per the configuration tested Kinematic The graphs are illuminating, to say the least. The leverage curve goes some way to explaining the harshness of the Marra's ride quality. The leverage, or the mechanical advantage that the rear axle has over the shock, is incredibly high initially at 3.8. That is something that can and does give a very supple feel on touch down. However, that leverage ratio falls incredibly sharply, with more leverage ratio change in the first 90mm of travel than other bikes have over their whole travel range. Within 90mm travel, the ratio has already dropped to its lowest value of around 2.34, and from thereon becomes regressive to bottom-out. With the Marra's leverage curve being so progressive through that initial portion, the suspension actually uses quite a lot of its rear wheel travel relative to sag at the shock. A sag of 20% at the shock gives a rear wheel travel sag of 39.5mm (24% sag), meaning the bike has less positive travel available to use as compared to bikes that see less progression through the early stroke. The Cane Creek Kitsuma on the Marra has a stock off-the-shelf tune, not custom to the kinematic of the Marra. In terms of tuning suspension for such a leverage curve... well, manufacturers don't tend to work with such high starting ratios. As an example, RockShox recommend that rear suspension designs have an average leverage ratio that sits between 3.0 and 2.1, and the Marra does conform to that with an average leverage ratio of 2.54. The difficulty comes with the huge variation in leverage ratio throughout the travel; tuning a shock to offer effective damping over such a range is, I am reliably informed, not terribly viable, and a tuner would effectively have to make a decision on which portion of the travel to tune the damper for, leading to big compromises either way. While I felt as though much of the bike's unpredictable ride feel could be attributed to the dramatic changes in rear-center length, the high leverage ratios throughout the first portion of the bike's travel could also have been a contributory factor. High leverage ratios deliver a relatively slow shaft speed, with little damping force generated as a result, something that may have resulted in faster rebound speeds than desired. That'd be compounded by the fact that a high leverage ratio requires relatively more shock pressure to achieve the same level of sag (131 psi gave 23% sag). Auckland Cycle Works Marra magnified rear axle path The Marra's dramatic drop in leverage ratio is occurring while the rear-center length is increasing significantly. At the point at which the rear axle stops tracking rearwards, the rear-center length has grown by a whopping 48mm from the unloaded state, which is around twice as much growth that a conventional bike sees throughout its entire travel. Meaningful changes, both to how much support is offered up by the rear suspension, and how long the rear-center length is, are occurring at a portion in the travel that the bike spends most of its time oscillating about. This unusual set of characteristics is likely responsible for the lack of predictability I experienced while riding the Marra. Marra pedal kickback at three gear ratios There is minimal cross-talk between chain forces and suspension movement while riding in the smaller sprockets of the cassette, as one does while descending. The positioning of the idler pulley on the moving lower link, as per the i-track patent, keeps chain growth to a minimum in these gears. In fact, in the 10T sprocket, pedal kickback values drop into negative values, meaning the required length of chain between the cassette, idler pulley and chainring actually decreases. In the absence of a chain guide, such a design would likely have a tendency to cause the chain to come away from the teeth of the idler or the chainring thanks to the addition of unnecessary chain links along those top spans of chain. Meanwhile, for the larger cogs of the cassette, pedal kickback is much greater. In the 52T, the pedal kickback value sits at 7° at sag, doubling to 14° at just over half way into the travel. It's worth pointing out here that this is higher than we see for other idler-equipped enduro bikes, and actually comparable to more conventional Horst pivot designs. All that said, I honestly can't say I was able to discern that while riding. If anything, I'd say the pedaling action felt pretty smooth over rougher ground, and traction wasn't an issue. To be fair, more noticeable than anything was the change in cadence that comes with very short (155mm) cranks. This was distracting enough that it was difficult to notice much else about the pedaling feel. Auckland Cycle Works Marra anti-squat As you might expect, anti-squat is at its highest in the climbing gears. Though it is remarkably high at 179% (for 32-52T) in an unloaded state, it drops sharply to 102% for 23% sag. That's a value that many manufacturers aim for, as it delivers a bike that doesn't sink into its suspension travel under pedal-induced accelerations. This is the silver-lining of the Marra's suspension design. However, those desirable anti-squat values are only present through a small window around sag, and only really apply to the easiest gears. The story is quite different when moving to the smaller cogs of the cassette. In fact, the anti-squat drops into negative values in the 10T cog. In the real world, that meant the bike wallowed while sprinting in harder gears, making it feel mushy and inefficient. No suspension design is immune to variation in anti-squat through different gears of the cassette, but the Marra's KOLARP suspension design brings about a much wider variation than most. Auckland Cycle Works Marra anti-rise A major contributor to the harsh ride feel of the Marra is how the suspension behaves under braking. Anti-rise is the metric we use to describe suspension's tendency to either rise (extend) or compress (squat) under braking. Many designers look to keep anti-rise around about or south of 100%, which theoretically would give a suspension platform that remains neutral, or even extends a little, under braking. It essentially aims to keep the suspension active under braking, so that the rear wheel can continue to move up and out of the way of consecutive bumps on the trail. The anti-rise of the Marra is inordinately high, sitting at 179% at around 23% sag. Later on in the travel, anti-rise will only drop as low as 97%, which is higher than many bikes will start at. In real life, that translates to the suspension being forced into compression under braking, pushing the bike to a much lower leverage ratio and a higher spring rate, where the suspension is less able to effectively absorb consecutive hits. Having listened to Gary's thoughts on suspension design, and having read the information on his website about the KOLARP kinematic , these high anti-rise values were very much intentional. His interpretation however, is that the suspension stays at sag under braking. Parting Thoughts To some, it may seem as though the many of the mainstream mountain bike brands are are lacking in creativity when it comes to suspension design. Many of the big players are sticking with comparatively plain-looking, conventional designs like the linkage-driven single-pivot or the four-bar Horst link. While these aren't especially visually exciting, they are well-understood designs that can be tweaked subtly to deliver a ride feel that is very predictable, and easy to ride for a wide range of bike handling competencies. Suspension designers can wade in here and tell me i'm wrong, if they like. But, at least to my mind, we are past the point of reinventing the proverbial wheel when it comes to linkage layouts. There are so many tried and tested designs out there that do an excellent job of providing good support, while balancing pedaling efficiency and maintaining a good, safe-feeling geometry under braking. That's not to say that there aren't some innovative new suspension designs on the horizon. Specialized and Norco spring to mind, here. Even they aren't reinventing the wheel, however. Both platforms (still not commercially available) are about finding ways to independently adjust kinematic parameters (anti-rise, axle path, leverage curve), with a view to fine-tuning these elements to suit different terrain types (or more specifically, World Cup race tracks). I guess what I'm trying to say here, is that I'm not surprised that some of Gary's claims around how the KOLARP suspension design works haven't materialized in the Marra. The Auckland Cycle Works website reads, "KOLARP is a new patented suspension system that delivers better performance uphill, downhill, pedaling and braking." It would be surprising if anyone with a fraction of the budget of these companies could, without the use of modern computer aided design and suspension analysis tools, design a bike that out-performed incumbent, race-winning designs. Sadly, the Auckland Cycle Works Marra prototype has not hit the mark. In reality, many of its suspension characteristics are not desirable, which is why you'll struggle to find a modern day bike with a kinematic that comes close to that of the Marra. The Production Marra The Marra V2 tested here is just a prototype, after all. We reached out to Gary to find out what changes the production Marra will be subject to. His response was as follows: "The prototype bike that you rode had an issue which required swapping out rear shocks, this ended up raising the BB height by 10mm and putting the bike outside the window where the various kinematic forces are balanced. This would also prevent you pushing the bike through corners with your feet. Since the front centre shortens and the rear centre lengthens by very similar amounts, standing centrally in the bike is a technique which helps Marra perform at its best. I offered to remedy the adjustment range by fitting a shorter shock yoke. My own further testing with the shorter yoke highlighted the importance of this adjustment and puts the shock back into the centre of the range of adjustment. The rider has the opportunity to fine-tune the BB height to their exact preference. I’m aware that all my kinematic values are outliers, but there is a balance to be found between the highly curved axle path and the equally curved leverage curve. The raised BB means that this balance was missed during these test rides. The next iterations of Marra and Reiver has a number of changes, maintaining adjustability and reducing weight. R&D is ongoing in both CAD and Lego!" Pricing & Availability In 2024, Auckland Cycle Works will produce only 25 Marra frames. Pre-order is open now, with a 50% down payment of £2,750 required to secure a spot. In that £5,500 total price, Gary is offering custom geometry, the choice of any color imaginable, and those hyper-adjustable dropouts. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Auckland Cycle Works Marra Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 401 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: First Ride: Formula's New Josh Bryceland Edition Selva Fork
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Josh Bryceland with his signature fork. Photo: Formula. It's no secret that professional mountain bikers often have something a little different going on with their suspension, the benefits of having access to unreleased parts or a suspension technician that can build a custom shim stack to meet their specific needs. With the new Josh Bryceland-edition Selva fork, Formula is trying to give the general public a taste of that customization. Each fork comes with a Compression Tuning System (CTS) valve that was created specifically for Bryceland, who asked Formula's engineers for something with good grip at the beginning of the stroke and then plenty of ramp up for bigger hits. Bryceland Selva Details • Travel: 170mm • Wheel size: 29" • 43mm offset • A2C: 585mm • 35mm stanchions • Custom Compression Tuning System valve • Includes pump, tools, travel spacers, 2 additional CTS valves • Weight: 2020 grams • Price: 1514 EUR, 1279 GBP, 1750 CAN • rideformula.com The CTS system is novel in that it only takes a few minutes to swap a valve – if a rider ends up purchasing this limited edition fork (only 100 are being produced) and then realizes that they don't ride like Josh, it's a quick procedure to install one of the two standard CTS valves that are also included. The Bryceland-edition Selva is available with 170mm of travel for 29” wheels, and is priced at 1514 EUR. My test fork with the thru-axle installed weighed in at 2020 grams. The Selva is covered by a 2-year transferable warranty, as well as Formula's 10 year support promise, which says that every product they make will have parts, spares, and support available for 10 years. The fork has custom Rat King stickers, and a laser etched signature on the crown Fork Details Along with the custom CTS valve, the air-sprung Selva has a new larger volume positive air chamber that's intended to give the fork a more linear spring curve. This version of the Selva uses a three-stage coil negative spring underneath the positive air chamber, where the springs get stiffer towards full extension in order to prevent any noticeable topout. Formula's CTS valves and Neopos volume spacers are the two ingredients that ensure the fork doesn't bottom out harshly. The Selva air spring and three stage negative spring. For those who aren't familiar, Neopos are volume spacers made of foam rather than that hard plastic that's typically used. That foam compresses as the air pressure inside the positive chamber increases, which creates a more gradual end stroke ramp. It still acts like a volume spacer, though, so removing it may still be necessary to achieve full travel depending on the fork setup. The rebound knob is located in the typical location, on the underside of the right leg, and there are 18 clicks of adjustment. The tool-free quick-release lever on the thru-axle is removable; once it's taken off the axle can be tightened or loosened with a 5mm Allen wrench. The blue dial adjusts the compression, and the gold lever is a lockout. The lockout force is adjustable at the knob to the left of the lever. . Formula's Neopos foam volume spacer. It's the number of ports in each valve that differentiates them from one another – the amount of oil flow as the fork compresses is what alters the damping curve. This chart shows the different curves that each CTS valve creates. Josh Bryceland putting his fork to work. Photo: Formula Ride Impressions I started off with the Bryceland CTS valve installed and the fork inflated to 65 psi as per Formula's recommendation. I headed out on one of my usual test loops, a ride that includes a good mix of jumps, drops, berms, and some steeper, more natural trails. In other words, a mountain bike ride. Not that I had any doubts before, but that ride reinforced the fact that no, I'm not Josh Bryceland. Formula definitely achieved the goal of having a soft, supple initial stroke and then tons of support after that, but the extreme ramp up meant that I was only using around 140 millimeters of travel, and that was on a ride with plenty of hard drops and G-outs that would have used almost full travel on my typical setup. The softer initial part of the travel meant that I wasn't getting bounced around, but the ramp-up deeper in the travel was very, very noticeable – it was like running into a padded wall, and no matter how hard I pushed I couldn't get through it. Time to swap the CTS valve for something better suited to a mere mortal. I tried both the orange and gold valves, eventually settling on the gold valve with no spacers as my preferred all-round setup. As it turns out, Formula says that valve is what 75% of their customers prefer. If I was on an e-bike, or knocking out high-speed bike park laps I could see going with the orange valve, due to the extra mid- and end-stroke support it provides. With the gold valve, the fork had excellent grip off the top, and the support built nicely throughout the travel. Formula's CTS system makes it easy to experiment with different setups, and it's a clever way to give riders a wide range of noticeably different fork configurations. With the Bryceland Selva, I can't help but wonder if this the final push for this model before something new is released. Don't get me wrong, it works very well; other than the fairly loud rebound noise I don't have any complaints about its performance, it's just partially overshadowed by the newest offerings from Fox and RockShox. The Selva's stanchions still measure 35mm, and while the chassis was certainly stiff enough for my 160 lb weight, I could see bigger riders gravitating towards the burlier 38mm options out there. The flip side is that the Selva weighs around 300 grams less than a Zeb or 38, which is a significant weight savings - something to keep in mind for riders who are tired of their enduro bike weighing as much as a downhill bike. Bleeder valves would be a nice touch, and I also don't think the lockout lever is necessary on a long travel fork. I wouldn't consider any of those deal breakers, though, and for riders interested in trying something a little different the Selva is worth considering. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Forks Formula Formula Selva Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: First Ride: Revel Rascal V2
author: Matt Beer
article_text: With a name like the Rascal, you know that Revel Bikes designed their 130mm travel trail bike to be a sharp handling, playful bike. The Rascal V2 continues to use Canfield’s Balance Formula, dual-link suspension design and roll on 29” wheels. Shape-wise, it looks virtually identical, so what’s new? Revel has updated the rear triangle for UDH compatibility, stretched the geometry, and shed frame weight while increasing stiffness. They’ve added a XXL frame size for riders up to 6'8" as well. There’s no shortage of ways to build a Rascal. A bright metallic mustard or pinot purple are available with Fox and RockShox air suspension starting at $3,599 USD. Six complete packages start at $5,999 USD and break the five-figure price tag when SRAM's T-type wireless shifting and Revel’s own Fusion-Fiber carbon wheelset are added to the mix. Rascal V2 Details • Full carbon frame • Wheel size: 29" • 130mm rear travel, 140mm fork • CBF dual-link suspension design • 65.5° head angle • 76° seat angle (MD) • 436mm chainstays • Weight: 14.2 kg / 31.30 lb • Sizes: S, M, L, XL, XXL • Price: $5,999 - $10,499 USD • revelbikes.com Canfield's Balance Formula (CBF) uses a dual-link design. Carbon links on the top and bottom. This cable routing hasn't been an issue yet but is mildly concerning. Titanium hardware all around. The rear fender protects the lower link well. Frame Details On the outside, the Rascal V2 looks identical to the original, however, there are a ton of changes besides the UDH update and geometry alterations. At 2,810g for a size medium frame, excluding the shock, the Rascal V2 saves 150g from its predecessor. Impressively, Revel states that the frame stiffness has been beefed up by an extra 20%. To go along with a stiffer chassis, the frame hardware and bearings are said to last longer too. Small details to shed mud and reduce noise are always welcomed. A finned guard aims to keep flailing chains from causing a racket and fully-guided internal cable routing is finished with a secure clamp under the bottom bracket shell. A tiny fender hides etween the vertical braces in the rear triangle and a rock guard shields debris from entering the lower link as it articulates. Suspension Design CBF stands for Canfield Balance Formula, so before you make any judgements about how Revel bikes may look like a Canfield, they’ve licensed the dual suspension platform. The bread and butter of CBF suspension is keeping the instant center rotating around the chainring, or center of curvature in this case, through 100% of its 130mm of travel. The Rascal's leverage ratio starts relatively high to give initial small sensitivity and ramps up gradually. The leverage ratio begins at 2.95 and moves down to roughly 2.35 in a smooth arcing line. The anti-rise starts at 110% and dips to 90% at the end of the travel which should make the Rascal very neutral under braking, but possibly not the most sensitive. On the flip side, the forces induced by pedaling, the anti-squat value, begins at 140% and stays high throughout, barely dipping below 100% by the end of the travel. Based on these values, it’s easy to see how Revel prioritizes the Rascal ability to be highly responsive and efficient on the trail. Geometry The main talking point here is the addition of the fifth frame size, an XXL, which is directed at riders who tower at 6‘3“ to 6‘8“ tall. On the low end of the size range, the small frame is still aimed to fit riders anywhere from 5’1” to 5’6”. This opens up choices for riders to size up or down with smaller jumps between frame sizes now. On top of the larger size, the rest of the geometry has been slightly tweaked as well. Minor tweaks like a slacker, head angle and longer reach, should Increase the Rascal's downhill capabilities. Those numbers have only shifted by .5° now sitting at 65.5 and 471 versus 464mm, however, they’re welcome to changes. The seat angle for the size large has also increased to 76° and the chain stay has been the lengthened to 436mm up from 433. The length doesn’t change per size, but the seat tube angle does get slightly steeper, rising up 0.5° incrementally on the next two frame sizes. The two top Rascal models come with Revel's Fusion-Fiber carbon wheels. Models and Pricing The bike we are reviewing is the X0 build and normally costs $7,999 USD with a SRAM X0 Transmission shifting, Code RSC brakes, and a RockShox Ultimate Lyrik and Super Deluxe shock. Revel's RW30 Fusion-Fiber carbon wheels add on an extra $1,200 USD. Those weigh 1850g, have 28 spokes per wheel and are laced to Industry Nine Hydra hubs, then wrapped in Continental Trail tires. Rounding out the cockpit components are carbon bars from Trail 1, a 180mm Bike Yoke Revive post with a Wolf Tooth lever, and SDG Radar saddle. That brings this build to $9,199 USD but there's the XX kit which costs $10,499 USD and includes the RW30 wheels. Those wheels are also available on the next component level down for $8,199 USD. This package includes Fox 36 and Float X Factory series suspension, a full Shimano XT brakeset and drivetrain, plus the rest of the bits listed above. Revel is still finalizing the exact build specifications on each price point, starting at $,5999 USD, but the Rascal is only available with carbon frame construction. That means the frame kits start at $3,599 with the Super Deluxe Ultimate shock and $3,749 with the Fox Float X Factory shock. A 65.5-degree head tube angle, shorter 44mm-offset fork and 40mm-stem make for twitchy handling. Ride Impressions “Little bikes” are a hoot to blast around on less frequented singletrack and backyard jumps in the wintertime when the snow closes out more serious terrain. The Rascal fits in this category perfectly with peppy handling and dual 29-inch wheels to keep it rolling rapidly. Soon after the first ride, I started thinking of the Rascal as the dirt jumper's XC bike. The Rascal can be summed up in three words: light, low, and short. The handling is particularly quick and great if that’s what you’re looking for, but at higher speeds and bouncing down steeper trails, it has its drawbacks. A lot of that comes down to what’s going on up front and when you look at the spec, it all makes sense. First, there’s the 20mm rise bar and 40mm stem that are mounted to a short, 44mm offset fork. Typically that’s not too out of the ordinary, but when you pair that with a 65.5° head tube angle, it makes for some twitchy steering. After swapping out for 35mm rise bars and 50mm stem the handling settled down and became much more predictable. I’ve had the most fun blasting low angle singletrack, and mellow jumps on the Rascal, always looking for side hits along the trail. Loading up the suspension and getting energy out of the bike is extremely rewarding. It’s not just while descending that you feel like you’re on a live wire either, for better or worse. Climbing the Rascal is a breeze and it can change direction on a dime. Since the Rascal is sticking around for a longer-term test, it’s tempting to extend the fork to 150mm travel and slacken it out another .5°, since that move is still within the frame's specifications. I imagine that's how most riders in my neck of the woods might set it up. That would then match the travel figures of Forbidden's Druid V2. Then we can see just how much further I can push the Rascal on the downhills because there's more muscle to tap into with the right setup. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Revel Bikes Revel Rascal Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Orbea's 2026 Rallon Enduro Proves Long-Travel Enduro Bikes Aren't Dead
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, dh-bikes, enduro-bikes, orbea, orbea-rallon, matt-beer
article_text: Orbea Rallon Enduro Review Final https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/NBQD0Q4n Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 19:23 Product photos: Roo Fowler Action photos: Dave Smith Review: Matt Beer Orbea’s all-new Rallon Enduro is undeniably one of the hottest bikes of the year. I don't think there was one negative comment when this bike first launched. It’s sleek, light, and feature-packed. Oh, and it uses the same frame to transform into the downhill model. To both the manufacturer and the consumer, it makes a lot of sense; two bikes in one. [Note: see my full review of the Rallon in DH mode here . It's a very different machine.] Will anyone take the time to strip down one bike and swap out frame parts, plus various other components, to take advantage of this product design? Probably not. But I wouldn’t concern myself with that tedious conversion. In its enduro form, keeping up with downhillers on the Rallon isn’t a far-fetched idea. Compared to the outgoing Rallon , the 2025 Rallon EN is a totally different bike. For starters, the travel has been bumped up 10mm at either end. The 7th-generation Rallon now rides on 170mm of travel with a 180mm fork. This bike is a beast, yet it’s relatively light for how much travel it has. Add in the fact that it can hug the ground like the DH version, yet responds quicker, and you have a true long-travel, go-anywhere machine. Rallon EN Details • 170mm travel, 180mm fork • Carbon frame • Dual 29" or MX wheels • Concentric Boost 2 suspension design • Downtube and water bottle storage • Gravity Link bracket with optional weight • 63.2 - 64.8° head angle • 442 or 450mm (two options, non-adjustable) • Sizes: S, M, L (tested), XL • Lifetime frame warranty • Weight: 15.88 kg / 35.00 lb (size LG, E-Team spec, MX wheels, no weights) • Frame weight: 3.1 kg / 6.83 lb (size LG, no shock, no weights) • Pricing: $5,699-9,999 USD • Frame kit: $4,299 USD (with Fox Float X2 shock) • More info: orbea.com Contents Introduction Frame Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take There are very few bikes that can take on such a wide array of trails and terrain. I was equally content taking the Rallon out for 8-hour pedal days as I was rolling up to extra spicy slabs and scary chutes. — Matt Beer Hover mode. Nature's horns amongst the fog. You don't need to wrestle the Rallon to move it around. Storage compartment: check. The Enduro Gravity Links are specific to the rear wheel size. An auxiliary link can be purchased for $55 USD. 395g of factory lead is included too. Internal cable routing runs through the downtube, or headset, if you're a glutton for punishment. The Rallon Enduro also showed up with a downtube shuttle guard. Frame Details The frame features found on the Rallon Enduro are the same as the Rallon DH, which I reviewed a couple of weeks back. In case you missed that, both Rallons include downtube storage under the water bottle mount, completely internal cable routing, and a hidden multi-tool in the rocker link pivot. The full carbon frame uses design aesthetics similar to the Wild, Orbea’s full-powered E-bike, too. The lines aren’t just for looks, though (okay they might be largely for looks, but it does look great). An aggressively low top tube, unique seat tube alignment, and extra mass near the BB add up to a low centre of gravity, a trait unsurprisingly shared with the Rallon DH. Hiding in the lower shock mount zone is the shapeshifting Gravity Link - the component responsible for transforming the Rallon frame from downhill to enduro modes. There is one gravity link for the DH version of the bike, which requires a different length shock, and two versions for this enduro model to account for different wheel sizes. Onboard the Rallon Enduro Gravity Links is an extra weight bracket, but not as much as the DH version. Where the DH link has weights on the side, the enduro links have a 4-way adjustable flip-chip instead. This flip chip yields the option for high or low shock progression as well as two head angle/BB positions. That’s on top of the angle-adjusting headset, making it possible to rake the Rallon Enduro slacker than the DH version. Boost Concentric 2: Orbea's single-pivot linkage-driven suspension. Technically, you could run the DH Gravity link and long shock to get 200mm of travel (with a 27.5" rear wheel only), but the anti-squat decreases significantly. 0 seconds of 29 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/wLCrD49J Copied Live 00:00 00:29 00:29 Here's the DH setup it slo-mo action. Suspension Design Calling on the same front and rear triangles as the Rallon DH, as well as the rocker link, something has to change in order for the travel to be reduced. Nothing about the function of the Boost Concentric suspension design changes, aside from the shock length and where it mounts to the mainframe. It’s the manipulating Gravity Link that dictates where the shock sits. The Rallon Enduro requires a 205x62.5mm Trunnion-mounted shock - disregarding the shock stroke limitation here would result in frame damage. 170 millimeters of rear wheel travel seems to come with less of that diving mid-stroke characteristic than the Rallon DH had. I suppose, if you were tempted for more travel, the DH Gravity Link and a 225x75mm shock could be installed. You'd definitely want a climb switch in that case, as the anti-squat drops significantly (see graph here ). Keep in mind, you'd be limited to a 27.5" rear wheel if you started out on a 29er. What results from those pieces is an anti-squat value starting close to 100%, much higher than the Rallon DH, but a nearly identical leverage rate and anti-rise figure - just over a shorter shock eye-to-eye and stroke. The leverage rate in the more progressive setting. Geometry If you checked out the Rallon DH review, you might remember that Orbea only makes these elegant carbon frames in four sizes. While I think that might be tolerable in the downhill department, they’re leaving out riders on the upper end of the size range. The max reach for an XL Rallon Enduro is 502mm. The number of geometry adjustments is plentiful. In terms of angles, you have three positions for the headset cups, and two more settings down in the Gravity Link. Those take the head tube angle from 64.8 degrees all the way down to 63.2, a hair slacker than the DH version. Towards the middle of the bike, the seat tower stands out. Not for height, because that’s kept to a minimum, but the angle - it’s one of the steepest around. In the slackest setting, it’s still 78.8 degrees. We like steep seat angles, but has this one gone too far? We’ll talk about that below in the “Climbing” section. Two chainstay lengths are possible: 442 or 450mm. This is achieved through non-adjustable entire rear triangles. Customers need to choose their preferred chainstay length at the time of purchase. Orbea says they will make both chainstay options available aftermarket, but no pricing or availability is public as of this review. Unlike the mixed-wheel-only Rallon DH, the Enduro can use a 29” rear wheel with either chainstay length. Specifications Price $14999 Travel 170 Rear Shock Fox Float X Live Valve Neo Factory Trunnion 2-Pos Evol LV Kashima custom tune 205x62.5mm Fork Fox 38 Float Factory 180, Grip X2, QR15x110, Kashima Headset Orbea Alloy 1-1/2", Black Oxidated Bearing Cassette SRAM X0-1295 Eagle 10-52t 12-Speed Crankarms SRAM X0 Transmission 165mm w/ 32T ring Chainguide e*thirteen CL55 30-36t black Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA 73mm Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 Transmission 12-Speed Chain SRAM X0 Transmission 12-Speed Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar OC Mountain Control MC10 Carbon, Rise 20mm, Width 800mm Stem OC Mountain Control MC11 Alu SL, 0º Grips Fizik Comfortfeel grips 32mm Brakes Shimano XTR 9220 w/ 203mm Galfer Shark rotors Wheelset Oquo Mountain Control MC32LTD Tires Maxxis High Roller III 29x2.40", 3CG/EXO+ front, Maxxis Minion DHR II 2.40" 3CT/DD rear Seat Fizik Terra Ridon X5 145mm Seatpost Fox Transfer Factory 240mm, Shimano I-Spec lever Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC The world is your oyster when building any Orbea. Their MyO custom configurator allows you to pick and choose exactly what component and paint scheme you’d like. When it comes to the stock Rallon Enduro builds, there are three to choose from. The most expensive E-LTD model, seen here, is supplied with an electronic shiftset and rear shock. Our test bike was supplied with aluminum Oquo wheels instead of the carbon option. We also requested a Fox Float X2 in addition to the Float X Live Valve Neo to better understand their strengths and limitations. RIDING THE Rallon EN Test Bike Setup To say I was excited to get this bike back on home soil was an understatement. It had tons of travel, weighed less than expected, and felt more intuitive to ride than the Rallon DH. My pick on frame options was a size large, 450mm chainstay length and 27.5” rear wheel since I’d be focusing on freeride moves, bike park laps and exploring beyond. For that reason, the headset stayed in the slackest setting, but I did rotate through all four lower shock mount positions. If I were more focused on enduro racing, I’d stick with the size large, but likely opt for the bigger wheel and shorter rear triangle, increasing roll over capabilities and maneuverability in tight turns. I mentioned how tall the stack height is on this bike. After my first shakedown ride, I dropped the stem height to just 10mm above the headset. Finding the sweet spot for the suspension came easier than with the Rallon DH. The Fox Float X2 was set to 186 PSI with 0.6 cubic inches of volume spacers. The Float X Live Valve spent the most time with the “Shore Tune” loaded up and sat at the same pressure. The fork pressure landed at 100 PSI with one volume spacer installed. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 38 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Testing Info The versatility of the Rallon Enduro became most apparent during Crankworx in Whistler. It handled everything in the bike park without hesitation. The Rallon was also my bike of choice for the SRAM Mega Ride, which requires riders to cover every corner of the Whistler valley, with some help from the Peak Chair. A logical choice was to throw on some Continental Enduro-spec casing tires and the Fox Live Valve shock to make this long-travel rig cope with the gruelling day. On another outing with two brothers going by the last name Atherton, we headed up to the Alp-X Heli Trail. Despite Gee making all of the climbs on his downhill bike and Dan dropping everyone on his Atherton S.150 enduro bike, the Rallon like the perfect middle ground bike for the lengthy alpine descent. Download the Trailforks App Big day out with the Athertons during Crankworx. Photo: Moonhead Media The Rallon was well matched to a big heli epic. Photo: Moonhead Media Climbing One of Orbea’s main priorities when building the Rallon DH was to minimize pedal kickback - it was intended to be a downhill race bike first and an enduro bike second. With suspension layouts like this, pedal kickback is generally proportional to anti-squat, or the suspension’s ability to resist squishing under pedalling forces. I did wonder what effect this would have on the climbing ability of the Rallon Enduro. On paper, the anti-squat is lower than most long-travel bikes, but don’t let the numbers fool you. There’s something about that ultra-steep seat tube angle that holds your weight forward and deters the bike from bobbing with each pedal stroke. In the real world, the Rallon Enduro pedals on par with, or better than, other bikes in the super enduro category. And I say all of that in regards to the Fox Float X2. If you choose the E-LTD build, with the Float X Live Valve Neo, or decide to add that to your custom build, it’s an even more positive story. But more on that later. As great as that steep seat tube angle is, there could be downsides for taller riders. When positioned in the slacker head tube angles, the theoretical top tube length could become very short in comparison to the reach. In other words, the seated position could be cramped on an otherwise stretched standing position. Mix in a tall stack, and it certainly creates a new school position for climbing enduro bikes - a hunched-over XC attack position it is not. It’s not right or wrong, but it is very upright. Some riders can find that it puts unwanted pressure on their wrists while climbing. Again, that can be dependent on your bar height, too. On the size large, however, I didn’t personally find that to be an issue, even on the longest rides. Descending Whatever you want to call it, a super enduro, big mountain, freeride, or gravity bike, the Rallon covers all of those bases. Sadly, for riders who pedal to the gnarly trails, this category is being eaten up by eMTBs at some other brands. The Orbea's proud front end and low centre of gravity make handling bike park speeds less sketchy, and a lot of riders, including myself, favour the response and wheelbase of an enduro bike for jump trails. It’s crazy to think that a bike with this much travel is so versatile, especially considering how well it descends. There’s no question that the bolt-on Gravity Link weights add stability to the bike. I favoured those on the downhill bike 99% of the time. On the Rallon Enduro, it was more of an even split. For the bike park or shuttling, it makes total sense. It’s not necessarily more work to spin causally uphill, but I wouldn’t say it’s as beneficial for everyday pedal laps. That’s the beauty of the singular 395g weight for the Rallon Enduro, though; spinning those two bolts on is a 2-minute job, based on where you’re riding on the day. On mellow trails, there are certainly more responsive, shorter travel options out there. That said, the Rallon EN is manageable due to its respectable weight. The tall front end and deep stack take more time to get the bike tipped into turns. That’s not necessarily a disadvantage, because tall stack heights and large BB drops go hand in hand with stability, which the Rallon had boatloads of. The other side of the coin here is that it simply takes more time for a taller object to tip from side to side with the same force, so the rider will have to be more aggressive to change direction. Luckily, there are a multitude of ways to configure a Rallon, but some options are set in stone, or carbon rather, like the chainstay. I made my choice with the 450mm rear triangle and stuck to a 27.5” rear wheel for increased maneuverability and body clearance. If I were 100% focused on racing enduro on this bike, I’d lean towards the shorter chainstay and bigger wheels to roll over objects easily while keeping the rear center length in check. That’s based on my past experience with bikes that have the capability to flip between rear wheel sizes. Orbea says that they designed the Rallon platform around the DH version. I enjoyed that bike, but I did feel the suspension could use more support, and the frame made an awful lot of noise. Thankfully, the Rallon Enduro didn’t possess either of those issues. It was more composed and much quieter. Possible reasons for that could be the higher anti-squat values and the wireless drivetrain with a stronger derailleur clutch. Riders focused on pure gravity performance should look to the Float X2 (or potentially the standard Float X, but I didn’t try that option), while more enthusiastic pedallers may appreciate the Fox Live Valve technology on the Float X Neo shock. The Float X2 offers more control through damper adjustments and doesn’t have as binary a feel as the Float X Neo. The high progression shock setting translates to excellent grip down spicy trails where traction under braking is mandatory. This also sets the rider down further into the bike, behind that tall stack, exuding confidence. It’s especially beneficial on highly technical, but slower-speed trails that demand big travel and attacking angles, yet without the sluggish nature of a full-on downhill bike. I’ll die on the “travel doesn’t weigh anything” hill, meaning I will happily pedal to alpine ridges or secret stashes when others will have to push a downhill bike or be “under-biked”. That’s where the Rallon Enduro stands out as being one of the best in the big-mountain bike world. Online and ready to pedal. Is the seat post long or is the frame low? Both, but the "steep and deep" seat tube handles a 240mm post no problem. Technical Report Fox Float X Live Valve Neo: The inclination-detecting brains of the Live Valve expand the Rallon into a true “all-mountain” machine. That does come at a small cost for raw downhill performance. There isn’t the same level of damper tuning to be had as on the Float X2, and the Live Valve doesn’t feel as intuitive. At times, and depending on the tune applied, the ride can be firmer than expected. But it does feel amazing on the climbs. Is that extra $1,000 worth it? If you’re more concerned with pedalling performance than flat-out descending control, then perhaps so. Audio levels: Compared to the Rallon DH, the noises caused by the usual suspects are decibels lower. The Rallon Enduro isn’t totally silent, but they’re acceptable. The wireless drivetrain does erase that cable rattle inside the frame. Despite not having a lower chainguide pulley, there’s flapping in that area, too. A new noise not heard on the DH bike came to life. The water bottle knocked against the underside of the top tube. Once again, the trusty mastic tape saved the day, padding that area. Dropper Post Length and Standover Height: Orbea managed to make a 240mm dropper post slam all the way into size small Rallon frame. 240mm of drop may be impractical if you want to use the seat to steer, but that’s the way it should be with all frames; the rider should have the freedom to choose whichever post height and drop they want. Coupling with the seat clearance, the top tube is low slung - tack on that extra weight and flip the geometry to the slackest settings, and you can really tuck into this bike. Which Model is the Best Value? Three complete builds are separated by a few thousand dollars. Without an aluminum frame option, the barrier to hopping on a Rallon starts above $5,500 USD. That’s no small sum; however, the E10 is topped off with reasonably capable components. At the other end, just a dollar short of five figures, the top-of-the-line E-LTD receives all of the electronic toys (other than a motor), like the Fox Float X Live Valve Neo shock and a SRAM X0 Transmission groupset. In the middle of the pack lies the $7,199 E-Team, my pick of the litter. Mainly, this build stands out as the highest value for me due to the premium suspension and stronger brakes. Otherwise, if you’re looking to equal that level of performance, you’d blow the $7,200 mark, according to my rough math. Let’s say you bought the E10 and decided to upgrade the 38 Grip damper to the Grip X2. That’d cost roughly $420. Then, stronger brakes, like the SRAM Maven Silver, would cost $265 apiece. That’d leave $550 to find a burlier rear shock. Minor advances are also made up the drivetrain totem pole when splurging for the E-Team. There is always the fully custom bike builder from Orbea, called MyO. Choosing your own paint scheme will only cost you in waiting time. Orbea Rallon EN E-LTD: $9,999 USD Transition Spire Carbon X0 AXS: $5,499 USD (sale) $6,999 How Does It Compare? It would be rude to sit here discussing lightweight, carbon super enduro bikes and not mention one of the leaders in that category, the Transition Spire (Henry's punching air right now). It’s long, light, and slack, yet it can happily handle rides below its pay grade, and that's a good thing. The Rallon Enduro ticks that same box. As versatile as the Spire may be, the frame adjustments are simpler and more limited than the Rallon. Then again, we’re talking about a bike that debuted back in 2021. Features like internal frame storage weren't as common outside of Specialized bikes, and bolting extra weights onto the frame would have been blasphemy. 29-inch-wheeled enduro bikes were the play back then - if you remember, even downhill bikes trended towards double wagon wheels. Transition doesn’t recommend the Spire to be fitted with a 27.5” rear wheel, or the handling would go out the window on lower-angle trails. There’s also only one geometry adjustment on the Spire, and that comes at the lower shock mount, changing the head tube angle/BB height, as well as the seat tube angle. Although they do make two chainstay lengths, they’re size specific (446mm on the S, M, L and 452mm on the XL and XXL) and do not have any further adjustment, such as flip-chips. What does that all mean on the trail? The Spire sits slacker in most modes, but it isn’t as ground-hugging. At speed, the Rallon hunkers down with greater intent. Suspension-wise, they aren’t too far apart. The Spire offers more support uphill on the pedals and downhill when pushing into the bike. Pros + Suspension travel-to-weight makes it far more versatile than expected. + Dialled, aggressive geometry (with sensible adjustments). + Extremely low stand over height and dropper post insertion. + Predictable suspension performance matches the bike's capabilities. Cons - Bottle fitment is tight, even on the size large. - Not cheap, no aluminum option. - Taller riders may be limited by sizing options. Pinkbike's Take Just when long-travel enduro bikes seemed to be falling out of fashion, Orbea bucked the trend and knocked it out of the park with the Rallon EN. The overall low weight and adaptable geometry can transform it for sendy Saturdays in bike parks to Sunday slogs in the alpine. There are undeniable similarities to the 200mm-travel downhill version of the Rallon, but the bike feels most at home in this enduro mode. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews DH Bikes Enduro Bikes Orbea Orbea Rallon Matt Beer Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: 5 Anti-Kickback / Chainslap Devices Ridden & Rated
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, ridden-and-rated, drivetrain, ethirteen, ochain, rali, rimpact, stfu-bike
article_text: 5 Chain Devices Tested https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/fpOtTtRX Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 32:28 There are plenty of nerdy debates in the world of mountain biking, and this is certainly one of them. Whether you call it pedal kickback, chain slap, chain flail, or some other cryptically vague fourth option, some folks have taken issue withe the impact the drivetrain can have on suspension performance. Encapsulated by the rare but undeniably lovely chainless run, there's certainly something to the concept of trying to mitigate just how much effect the chain can have on the rest of your ride - and that sensation is exactly what these companies are trying to offer. In an attempt to get down to exactly what each of these can offer on your next ride, we went out for a day of repeat laps in order to see which devices worked as intended, and which ones didn't quite make the grade. Pinkbike's tech editors have also been using these devices over the past six months in order to form longer term impressions. Dig into the video for all the nitty gritty hems and haws, or read on below to get a feel for which device shook out on top. Contents STFU Trail Rali Chain Mag Ochain R Rimpact Chain Damper e*thirteen Sidekick Climbing Descending Noise Service & Livability Conclusion The influence of the chain on a given bike's suspension characteristics varies significantly, but noise reduction is a common denominator that all bikes can benefit from. STFU Trail • Fits 9-13spd drivetrains • Trim to fit, no adhesives • Dual compound rubber • DH 7spd, 10spd, and singlespeed variants • Weight: 62g • Price: $40 USD • stfubike.com One of the first and arguably the simplest in the game, STFU's chain damper is a clever way to isolate the chain's movement from the rest of the bike. By capturing the chain's range of motion within its soft rubber hoops, the STFU prevents the chain from contacting other surfaces, and keeps the overall flail to a minimum. As it doesn't have any mechanical complexity or direct involvement with the drivetrain, the STFU falls into our passive category. Rali Chain Mag • 5 size options • Trim to fit, sticky tape • Neodymium magnet and rubber fins • Holds chain when not pedaling • Weight: 93g • Price: $33 USD • ralibike.com Rali's Chain Mag is essentially a stick-on chainslap protector with a trick up its sleeve. That trick comes in the form of a strong magnet, housed within some soft plastic towards the front of the stay guard. The idea here is to hold your chain down while coasting, to prevent it from bouncing up and down on the chainstay. Once pedaling force is applied, the chain lifts up and does its thing, unaffected. Though the Rali does try to hold the chain down for part of the ride, it's still essentially a passive device. Ochain R • Active chainring spider • 4 deadband options & lockout • 7 crank interface options • Chainring rotates backwards under chain load • Weight: 218g w/ 34t chainring • Price: $415 USD • ochain.bike Ochain's lineup of devices have become a common sight on the World Cup DH circuit, with a variety of offerings rounding out their lineup. On test we have the Ochain R, which is their most current offering, with 4 positions of externally-adjustable deadband, as well as a lockout to get a sense of the baseline. The external dial allows you to select between 4°, 6°, 9°, and 12° of deadband, with the active spider clocking backwards as the chain tugs on the chainring. When you push the pedals forward to accelerate or climb, you take up that 4-12° rotation and bottom out the springs, where things feel firm and normal. Rimpact Chain Damper • Active chainring • 30-36t chainrings • 5 crank interface options • Chainring rotates backwards & forwards under chain load • Weight: 242g, 32t variant • Price: £280 / $371 USD • rimpactmtb.com Like the Ochain, Rimpact's Chain Damper is housed in the architecture of a chainring, but Rimpact has taken the integration one step further. Instead of packaging the active portion of the device into a spider onto which you mount a chainring, the Chain Damper is itself a chainring, with all the active bits housed inside. There are various size and fitment options, but the motion within is non-adjustable - you get 8.6° of rearward rotation, and 4° of forward rotation. When you put enough power into the pedals, the springs bottom out, and you're back to a firm pedaling rotation as with a standard chainring. e*thirteen Sidekick • Anti-kickback rear hub • Boost, Superboost, DH spacing • 3 adjustable deadband options • Freehub rotates under chain load • Available as hub or full wheel • Weight: 424g, Boost XD variant • Price: $500 USD • ethirteen.com e*thirteen's Sidekick is a hub-based system, integrating all the complexity and monkey-motion into an existing component, as opposed to introducing complexity elsewhere. The Sidekick has three positions of deadband adjustment, with 12°, 15°, and 18° to choose from. Adjustment requires the removal of the wheel, but is otherwise quite easy. In order to keep that deadband position consistent, the "pawl pusher" resets itself as you coast via its own single pawl, with all the drive-pawls disengaged. As you pedal forward, that pusher engages the drive system, and the bike kicks into motion. Climbing All five devices target bikes that go up and down hills, so it makes sense to discuss their climbing performance before digging into their descending characteristics. While the passive devices don't really make themselves known, there are quirks and compromises to the active devices that are worth touching on. As the passive devices in the test, the Rali and STFU devices should have zero effect on climbing performance, so long as they're installed correctly. Your bike will pedal as you're used to, with the chain now traveling through a rubber hoop or near a magnet. The Sidekick has a noticeably deadband before engaging the drive pawls, producing a feeling similar to that of a low-engagement hub. Unlike a typical hub, that deadband and slop in the pedaling characteristic is very consistent and therefore easier to intuit once you've gotten used to the feel. There is no soft catch on the pedal input, so once you've engaged the drive pawls, it's a firm application of power. I can imagine the 18° option being quite annoying in just about any technical climbing situation, but the lower figures are easy enough to live with. Those who climb a lot will probably find the Ochain to be the easiest active device to abide by, as the springs suspending the spider are light enough to bottom out under fairly little power output, with elastomers to clutch the pedaling force as you engage the hard stop. Due to the external adjustability and short-throw deadband options, you could even tailor the Ochain to suit a more climbing-biased riding characteristic. I never chose to lock the device out for climbs, but in theory one could do so, just as you would a shock. In an even more touchy-feely sense, there are situations where the rubber bump stops can feel like a slip clutch - namely when you're really mashing on the pedals, and traction is limited. The standout device on the climbs is the Rimpact , but sadly not in a positive sense. Their Chain Damper uses very heavy springs to suspend the chainring, requiring quite a bit of consistent force through the pedal stroke to keep them bottomed out. When you're not applying that force in a perfect cadence, the sensation is similar to having a rounded-out square taper bottom bracket, with the cadence feeling notchy and awkward. I was able to get used to the sensation on some bikes, on well-graded roads, but when riding with friends or wandering around it's harder to maintain the required pedaling form. After a healthy amount of feedback to this end, Rimpact iterated on the concept and released what they've dubbed the Trail Spring kit, a lower-force pair of coils that feel much easier to pedal. I found these fairly easy to get used to, but others still struggled to adapt when compared to the other active devices on test. Descending Each of the devices on test have some influence on the bike's noise level while descending, but they don't all play a role in changing the dynamic feel of the suspension. Again that's a realm primarily dealt with by the active devices, where drivetrain forces are met head-on. Like the climbing performance, the Rali has essentially no effect on the dynamic feel of the bike when braking or hitting bumps. It's stuck to the chainstay, doing its thing, letting the chain move as it may. In our experience the magnet never held the chain through serious compressions, doing nothing to counter any flail or tug that occurs. The STFU does more to manage chain flail, and can calm some of that sensation before reaching the feet, quieting the feel of the bike down through chatter and heavy compressions. On some bikes, the STFU loops might be the only intervention needed to optimize the descending feel. Ultimately, both of these are meant to tackle noise more than feel, so more on that later. On all three active devices, the shared descending sensation can be summed up in a few points. When braking through rough terrain, the feet feel more even-keeled and the chassis seems to pitch less as a result. Compressions and pre-loading the bike moves you through more of the stroke before chain force pushes back against you, reducing some of the effect of anti-squat. In a sense it can feel like you're using more travel more often - whether that's a benefit or not ultimately depends on the use case. Riding the Rimpact up hills may not be for everyone, but it does an admirable job of quieting chain forces on the descents. The stiff springs keep chain flail at bay, and the fixed deadband handles most small and medium compressions before you feel the drivetrain engage. There is a reduction in the pedal feedback when pumping the bike, but in a way that feels less free-floating than the other active devices. Where the Sidekick can simulate the feeling of your chain being gone entirely, the Rimpact is more of a quieting of the system, with a quiet tug still apparent. Even on the descents, I preferred the Chain Damper with the lighter trail springs installed, as the float felt a bit smoother, and the pedaling performance wasn't as noticeably effected when grabbing some cranks out of a corner or into a feature - with the heavy springs, I found myself shifting up one or two gears to push through the spring resistance and get a firm pedal feel. As alluded to above, the Sidekick profoundly changes the feeling of the bike's rear suspension, providing a chainless sensation for however far you set the deadband's travel. The effect of this hub is so significant, I found myself taking a few runs to get used to the altered dynamic feel of the bike, adjusting weight bias and braking points to account for the different feel. When the deadband is set to the right adjustment point, you can effectively move through the entire stroke of travel without feeling any chain tug, so it can take some adaptation when pumping, cornering, and jumping the bike. Because the cassette can still clock forward quite freely, you occasionally feel the clunk of it reaching the end of the deadband, but that's more relevant to the noise section coming up. The Ochain has a firmer feeling deadband than the Sidekick, but with reduced overall travel and a smoother end-stroke stop. Those looking to delete all chain feedback through the entire stroke will probably be better served by the Sidekick, but the Ochain's smaller deadband options pair nicely with shorter travel bikes and situations where you want to reduce chatter through the feet and still retain some of the normal anti-squat feel of the bike and be able to get on the gas fairly quickly. I found the Ochain easy to adapt to, predictable through all riding situations, and adjustable enough to suit almost all bikes. Noise Rapid fire here, because thankfully noise is a simpler problem. The Rali does almost nothing to help chain slap in our experience. The magnet block is quite hard, and while the magnet can hold the chain down in some ideal scenarios, it lets go quickly in most real-world riding and allows the chain to slap around as normal. On some bikes, we found that it added noise, as the magnet block's fairly hard surface generates quite a bit of impact noise as the chain flails about. STFU was one of the first devices to market to combat chain noise, and it remains one of the best. Almost all chain slap noise is eliminated, save for the harshest compressions where the chain impact the underside of the chainstay. When used in conjunction with one of the active devices, true nirvana can be achieved. Though the stiff springs can be challenging to adapt to, the Rimpact does do an admirable job of quieting chain slap. That said, the lighter Trail springs are equally effective in almost all situations, save for the hardest impacts. The long-travel of the Sidekick's deadband takes up a lot of slack when the chain is flailing about, but plenty of noise is still generated when slamming the rear end into holes. Because there isn't any soft internal catch in the hub, once the cassette bottoms out at the end of the deadband, the chain continues to clock and smacks around. Most smaller-impact noise is eliminated through, and combining the Sidekick with the STFU dampers manages to eliminate just about all noise. Thanks to some rather soft elastomers and light spring force, the Ochain keeps almost all chain noise at bay, especially in the longer deadband settings. On all of our test bikes it ended up being the best standalone option for noise reduction. Service & Livability Folks looking to keep their bikes simple and maintenance-free will be best served by the Rali and STFU options, as installation is essentially the first and last thing you have to do with them. The Rimpact is fairly simple to service, with internal markings and stops to keep the springs in check when putting the device back together. Luckily so, as it has a tendency to creak after fairly little use, requiring cleaning and lubrication more often than the other active devices. One other note is the overall width of the body, which had a tendency to trap rocks between the inner plate and bashguard or bike frame. This happened often enough on a couple bikes to warrant a mention, though it won't be an issue on all platforms. Ochain also features guides and internal marking to try to ease service, but in our experience it's significantly less pleasant a job. The adjustability adds some complication to the task, and loading the four internal springs and ancillary parts can be quite a frustrating task until you've completed it a few times. Luckily the service interval is fairly long, with creaking cropping up only after many months of riding. A note on the device as it comes stock - the provided chainring bolts are flimsy at best, and should be replaced by a more robust option. This may change now that Ochain and SRAM are one in the same, but having had a few of the stock bolts crack it's worth a mention. With the active element integrated directly into the rear hub, the Sidekick's maintenance intervals and ease follow suit. Cleaning and lubrication are ultimately the only tasks to complete, and luckily the assembly procedure is very easy. Concerns of durability were common upon release, and I've been putting a massive amount of mileage on my original hub to see how well it would hold up. For a full year of minimal maintenance, it survived and continued to work perfectly, then one day that abruptly ended. Pedaling uphill, I felt a sudden catch in the hub's freewheel motion, and when I went back to pushing on the cranks, the freehub spun forward and never engaged again. It seems that somehow the pusher pawl got crimped and was unable to extend, leaving the drive pawls unengaged. One warranty later and I'm back on the hub - I like to assume this was a fluke, but we'll see how the second one holds up. After sending the Sidekick hub back to e*thirteen, they found that the issues was due to a pre-production steel ratchet ring, and they haven't seen any failures of production ratchet rings in the field. You can read the full statement from e*thirteen here . Conclusion Favored by all our testers, the Ochain felt like the best combination of predictability and performance, with the added bonus of doing a very good job of keeping the bike quiet. The Sidekick offers the most dramatic feel difference, and the noise it allows can be addressed by adding some STFU loops to the equation. The Rimpact is a solid option for park bikes and DH riding, but the stock stiff springs make pedaling too awkward to recommend to anyone. The lighter Trail Springs are far more usable, and have most of the same benefits. If adjustability doesn't appeal, and you're not sensitive to an altered feel while pedaling the bike, it could suit. Sadly the Rali Chain Mag fell flat on all of our test bikes, despite even trying to optimize setup to make it work. The magnet fails to meaningfully retain the chain, and in many situations just added noise. Add to that the incompatibility with certain bikes that have too large a chainstay-to-chain distance, and it feels like a second pass is warranted. The STFU might just be the people's champ in this test, with longevity, easy installation, and serious effect on the chain noise while riding. It's cheap, requires no maintenance, and makes a real difference. If a friend asked - and many have - I'd recommend the Ochain or the STFU for most applications. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Ridden And Rated Drivetrain Ethirteen Ochain Rali Rimpact Stfu Bike Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Intrusive Thoughts: The Hudski Dualist - Is This a Mountain Bike?
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, intrusive-thoughts, reviews, xc-bikes, hudski, hudski-dualist
article_text: This is Shadow Dario. He does not adhere to manufacturer recommendations. He follows the whims of his impulses, however misguided they may be. Such ideas do not constitute advice, nor should they be taken too seriously. Seen here are the results of those fever dreams and runaway ideas, those intrusive thoughts. A bike is a bike, but not all bikes are mountain bikes. I've done my fair share of underbiking, pushing the line of what makes logical sense for the goal at hand, and though it's not always pleasant, it's usually very fun. Embracing that ethos is Hudski, a brand committed to making a bike that does just about everything pretty damn well. With no suspension to speak of, but the ability to run mountain bike components from tip to tail, the Hudski Dualist begs the question of what it really takes to be called a mountain bike. Dualist Details • 0mm travel, 0mm fork • Aluminum frame, carbon fork • 29" wheels, or 27.5"+ • 67° head angle, 74.5° seat angle • 415-495mm reach, 570-645mm stack • Chainstay and fork offset adjustment • 4 sizes available • Pricing: $2,700 complete, $1,600 frameset • Weight: XXX • hudskibikes.com Frame Details Speaking to Brian Szykowny, one of the chief minds behind Hudski, it's clear that he has a deep love for mountain biking, with a resume that reflects plenty of time in the industry. Taking the principles of MTB and applying them to a multipurpose chassis is simple on paper, but deciding which details are important becomes a unique challenge. On face value, the Dualist is a hybrid, a touring bike, an ATB, a rigid mountain bike. In keeping with those categories, the frame is covered in bottle bosses, accessory mounts, and additional anchor points for racks and other accessories. If you're looking to carry a ton of shit on a bike, boy do I have a solid option for you. Many, many mounts. Fork included. Maybe you could strap some mass dampers to this puppy to take the edge off. Considering the utilitarian nature of the Dualist, I'm a bit annoyed at the fact that it uses internal routing for the derailleur and both brakes. It makes for a clean look, but robs the bike of some of the parts-bin-magic that it exudes otherwise. Of course, most folks aren't swapping brakes on a regular basis, so this is just a picky gripe. One detail that sets the Dualist apart from many other hybrid-esque framesets out there is the fact that it uses Boost spacing front and rear. This opens up a wide array of aftermarket options, and means that you may be able to share a wheelset between your mountain bike and this rigid ripper. The Dualist frame and fork have also been tested to ASTM 3 standards, so you can treat it like the little mountain bike is just might be. Hudski implemented some very clever frame adjustments, in the form of front and rear dropout flip chips, allowing the user to modify both the rear center and fork offset of the bike. Both chips are self-contained, and don't require any spare parts storage. Swapping can be done trailside, should you feel so inclined. Two-position rear dropout, with UDH compatibility in both. Two fork offsets to choose from. Geometry The Dualist's geometry is adjustable, but none of the changes are going to alter the roomy and upright fit of the bike. Reach figures are in line with typical bikes of that size, and stack heights are tall, especially considering the riser bars that come stock. The 67° head angle may look a bit steep, but with no fork travel to compress and steepen, it feels less drastic than you might expect. Same goes for the 74° seat angle, which remains consistent and comfortable even on steeper climbs. Steering geometry can be changed by altering the fork's offset, with 44mm and 54mm positions available to those that want to experiment. 50mm risers keep you nice and tall. Hudski's clever UDH flip chip allows you to alter the chainstay length by 15mm, opting between 435mm and 450mm. The stability and handling tradeoffs are exactly as you'd expect, with the shorter option likely working best for the smaller two sizes in the range, and the longer catering to the lankier folks. Dominant headtube. As the Dualist isn't suspension corrected, you'd be smart to stick with the Hudski carbon fork up front. There are other rigid forks with a similar axle-to-crown measurement out there, should other mounting options or features appeal to you. Suspension Design Hope you're feeling supple, because there's not much to take the edge off here. Tire pressure matters a whole lot more on a rigid bike, and can drastically alter your experience. In the past, I've had luck with heavier casing tires run at low pressures, and may even recommend inserts to folks looking to rip the Dualist down rough trails on a regular basis. For riding dirt, I had luck with around 22-23psi up front, and 25-26psi out back. The EXO casing tires definitely do some wiggling, but you're thankful for it when things get chattery. Being that this is an Intrusive Thought, I also ran some stupid tire combos, with the Schwalbe Albert Gravity radials front and rear standing out as a surprisingly comfortable option for those looking to add some serious heft. Long dropper opens up a world of possibilities. Resin-only brakes close some of those doors. Who What Where Why?
