I didn’t have too many rock strikes with the stock 165mm cranks (I live in a region where you’re more likely to hit a root than a rock in the middle of a trail), but dropping down to 155 or even 150mm cranks would create more ground clearance and make it easier to half pedal and keep the motorized momentum going. For the record, I’m not as convinced by super short cranks on non-motorized bikes, since at a certain point it can feel pretty strange spinning those little circles, but on an eMTB it’s a different story, and going short makes a noticeable improvement. Descending The differences between the new Wild and the version I previously reviewed are fairly subtle when it comes to descending performance. The extra 10 millimeters of rear travel is nice to have for those not-quite-perfect line choices, and it still has that punching bag-like quality I enjoyed with the last one - it does a great job of absorbing hits without getting rattled. Speaking of rattling, the new motor is nearly silent while descending, and overall the Wild is an extremely quiet bike, motorized or not. Some riders aren’t bothered by extra clacks and rattles, but I’m not one of them - I want the bike I’m on to be as quiet as possible. I took the Wild off some sizeable drops (at least for me), and it took them all in stride, free of any harsh bottom outs or nerve wracking noises. The suspension does feel a little more eager to initiate travel, and that improved small bump sensitivity was nice to have for finding grip on wet, slippery trails. It doesn’t have quite the same bump-erasing ability as the Trek Slash+, but I’m not sure that trait would be possible to replicate without a high pivot suspension layout. The overall handling is in line with what I’ve come to expect from a modern enduro bike, which makes sense when you look at the geometry numbers. Its handling is very neutral, which is great news if you’re looking for a bike that’ll get up to speed and do what you want it to without any quirks, but it also makes it a little trickier to hone in on one standout ride characteristic. That’s a problem for me, the reviewer, rather than the rider, though; there’s something to be said about a bike that just does exactly what it’s supposed to. It is good to see the mixed wheel option added to the menu; that was one of the requests I had after testing the last one. Of course, it would be nice if a different rocker link wasn’t required to make the change, but at least it’s possible. I found the smaller rear wheel improved the downhill cornering performance, at least when it comes to tighter, steeper turns - it was a little easier to tip the bike in and out of corners with the mixed wheel setup. You've heard of the loam shelf, now how about a loam cupboard? What’s It Like to Live With? Overall, the Wild has held up reasonably well to the elements, but there are a few issues that have cropped up. The first has to do with the design of the bash guard / motor protector. That plastic cover has holes in it that allow for air flow to keep the motor from overheating, but those holes also let mud and other debris makes its way in. That wouldn’t be a big deal if the dirt could also escape the same way it came in, except that there are raised plastic portions inside the guard that do their best to hang onto as much soil as possible. If you’d like to take home soil samples from every area you ride in, this design is perfect. Otherwise, be prepared to remove four bolts (they’re not all the same size either - you’ll need two different hex keys) to take off the guard and clean things up. The charging port is another area that wants to suck in dirt - it didn’t always fully seal, and over time it became more difficult to pull it out and rotate the cover out of the way. Plus, if you do use the range extender the area around the port is more exposed, which gives dirt even better access. Orbea Wild Norco Range VLT How Does It Compare? The competition is heating up, but the Wild still holds its own against other contenders in the full power enduro eMTB category. Compared to the Norco Range VLT, it’s significantly lighter, and the geometry works better for my 5’11” height. On the Range I fall in between sizes, while with the Wild the size L is a good fit. The Range is bike that prefers to smash and plow its way down the trail, while the Wild doesn’t require the same level of aggression to get the most out of it. Which Model is the Best Value? For riders on a budget that’s big enough to afford an e-bike, but not big enough to drop $12,000 on one, the aluminum H10 is the one I’d go with. It’s still not cheap, at $7,300, but you do get the same Bosch motor found on the carbon models, and a 750 Wh battery. It’s also possible to upgrade select components at check out; personally, I’d go with the 600 wH battery and range extender, and maybe consider the X2 shock over the Float X for the increased level of adjustability. Technical Report Range extender: I used the range extender for, duh, extending the range of my rides. It’s easy to install, although as I mentioned it does leave the charging port more exposed to the elements. I also wish it was possible to charge the main and the auxiliary battery at the same time from one charger. As far as I can tell, there isn’t any sort of split cord available to make this possible. SRAM XX Transmission derailleur: The Wild’s XX Transmission is wired into the main battery, which should mean there’s one less thing to remember to charge. Except that I ended up on a ride where the wire stopped working, leaving me with a really expensive derailleur that could only be shifted by pushing on it and ignoring the loud crunching noises. Once I got home I removed the wire and installed an AXS battery, and everything worked like it was supposed to. It turns out the extension cord retails for $190, so hopefully if that issue cropped up it would be covered under warranty. Shimano XTR brakes: The XTR brakes with Galfer rotors provided enough stopping power to keep the Wild from running away with me, that is, after I swapped out the organic pads for metallic ones. Organic pads don’t last very long in the rain, especially not on heavy e-bike. Pros + Calm and composed handling + Very quiet while descending + Excellent power delivery while climbing Cons - Still has thru-headset cable routing - Even shorter cranks would be nice to see - Motor cover is a mud magnet. Pinkbike's Take The previous Wild was (and still is) a great bike, so it makes sense that the new one received only a slight revision rather than a dramatic overhaul. Gripes about headset cable routing aside, the Wild strikes a very good balance when it comes its handling and suspension performance on the climbs and descents. It's a bigger, full power ebike that hides its weight and wheelbase length relatively well, which means you don't need to have the absolute rowdiest trails to still have a good time on it. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Orbea Orbea Wild Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: 2025 Trek Top Fuel - More Capable & Adaptable Than Ever
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Trek Top Fuel Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/xam3vKbZ Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 13:54 The Trek Top Fuel continues to roll closer to trail bike territory with each revision, and what was once a purebred XC race machine is now mixed wheel compatible, and can be set up with 130mm of rear travel and a 140mm fork. In its stock configuration, the Top Fuel has 120mm of rear travel, a 130mm fork, and 29” wheels. Trek mentions the word 'downcountry' on their website a few times, but I think that term's time in the sun has come and gone – this is a short travel trail bike through and through. Yes, some of Trek's XC racers did use a slightly modified version at select World Cup races this year, but even though the frame is 220 grams lighter than before it's still not as light as other brands' flexstay-equipped, racing-oriented frames. Trek Top Fuel 9.9 XX AXS Details • 120mm travel, 130mm fork • Carbon frame • 29" wheels (MX compatible with 140mm fork) • 65.5° head angle (low geo setting) • 440mm chainstays (L) • Sizes: S, M, M/L, L, XL • Price: $10,499 USD • Weight: 28.2 lb / 12.8 kg (size L, 9.9 XX AXS) • trekbikes.com For a trail bike, though, the weight is reasonable, especially considering the number of adjustments, the in-frame storage, and, the part that really matters, how capable this bike is out on the trail. I've been working my way through the various configurations over the last few months in order to figure out which settings stood out from the rest. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The suspension remains calm under harder pedaling, while retaining enough compliance to keep chunkier climbs from feeling jarring. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The overall look of the Top Fuel is almost identical to the previous version, other than the new flip chip design at the lower shock mount. That chip provides a high and low geometry setting along with two different progression settings, either 14% or 19%. Oh, and there's no more Knock Block steering limiter – you can crank the handlebar as far as you'd like now without any hard stop. The in-frame storage door has been updated to improve its weather sealing, and the opening is a little larger to make grabbing that windbreaker or tool bag easier. Other details include generous downtube and chainstay protection, room to run a longer travel dropper post, and a threaded bottom bracket. The flip chip alters the geometry as well as the amount of shock progression. There's a little more room and better weather sealing under the storage door. Geometry We've seen geometry changes slow down across the board, and the Top Fuel is no exception - this time around it's a story of smaller changes, like a slightly taller head tube, and different chainstay lengths depending on the size. The head and seat angles come in at 65.5 and 75.9-degrees respectively with a 130mm fork; switching to a 140mm fork will put the head angle closer to 65-degrees, and the seat angle around 75.4-degrees. That's not the steepest seat tube angle around, but remember, this is a 120mm bike, so that number won't change as much at sag as it would on a longer travel bike. Reach numbers range from 417mm on the size small (that size is only available with 27.5” wheels), up to 507mm on the XL. The S and M sizes have 435mm chainstays, M/L and L have 440, and the XL's measure 445mm. The seat tube lengths have been shortened compared to the previous version, providing more room for running longer dropper posts. Suspension Design As I mentioned earlier, in the flip chip's forward position there's a 14% leverage rate change, and in the rear, more progressive setting that increases to 19%. A 185 x 50mm shock is spec'd on all models, but the frame is also compatible with a 55mm stroke shock, which increases the travel to 130mm. Now that the Top Fuel is approved for use with a 140mm fork, it's also possible to run it with mixed wheels – a 140mm fork and running the shock in the high position keeps the bottom bracket high enough to make that possible. Specifications Price $10499 Travel 130mm Rear Shock RockShox Deluxe Ultimate RCT Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate, 130mm Cassette SRAM Eagle XS-1299, T-Type, 10-52, 12 speed Crankarms SRAM XX Eagle Rear Derailleur SRAM XX SL Eagle AXS Chain SRAM XX Eagle Shifter Pods AXS pod controller Handlebar Bontrager RSL Integrated handlebar/stem Brakes SRAM Level Ultimate 4-piston Wheelset Bontrager Line Pro 30 Carbon Tires Bontrager Gunnison / Montrose, 29" x 2.4" Seat Verse Short Pro Seatpost RockShox AXS Reverb Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE TOP FUEL Test Bike Setup As you'll see shortly, I did all sorts of experimentation with the Top Fuel, so that meant there were multiple setup changes. I did get in a bunch of rides in the stock configuration before beginning to tinker, in order to have a base line for comparison. 165 psi in the RockShox Deluxe shock put me right at 30% sag, and I ran 71 psi in the 130mm Pike. I ran two click of HSC from full open on the Pike, and the compression dial in the middle of its range, adding or subracting one or two clicks depending on trail conditions. I ended up running a 200mm PNW Components Range dropper post for a good portion of the test period, and I also tried a slightly higher rise bar that wasn't as brutally stiff. And then there are the brakes - after having one too many moments where I didn't have the stopping power I wanted I swapped out the Level 4-piston brakes for Code RSCs. We try to avoid swapping brakes when possible, since that's not something most people will be doing, but after doing my due diligence with the stock setup it was nice to have less fade and more power from the Codes. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington, over the course of the summer and into the early fall. That meant the bike saw the full range of conditions, from wet and sloppy to dry and dusty, and everything in between. The loop above would be a good one on the Top Fuel, but realistically almost any combination of the trails in that area would be fun on this bike due to its very versatile nature. Climbing It's easy to obsess over a bike's actual weight, but there's something to be said about how a bike feels out on the trail. With the Top Fuel, it feels fast and eager to accelerate, even with tires and wheels that aren't all that light. I never used the climb switch, not even on my biggest ride on the bike, a 62 mile loop that included 10,800 feet of climbing. The Top Fuel manages to feel quick and efficient without any harshness, a feat that's not easy to accomplish. The suspension remains calm under harder pedaling, while retaining enough compliance to keep chunkier climbs from feeling jarring. The geometry is nicely balanced too – the chainstay length and overall wheelbase have grown compared to the last model, but if anything I'd say that improves the bike's handling while climbing and descending. On the climbs, it helps create a more centered position, which makes it easier to maintain traction on steep sections, or to pause for a brief trackstand in the middle of a particularly tricky jumble of roots. I'm a big fan of technical climbs, and I'll still regularly stop and throw myself at a tricky section until I make it through without dabbing. On the Top Fuel, there weren't many dabs at all – I'd be tempted to call this a climber's trail bike, except that it works really well on the descents too. I tried almost every possible setup option on the Top Fuel, starting with the stock 120 / 130mm setup, and switching things out until I arrived at the 130 / 140mm configuration. I was a little concerned about the seat tube angle getting even slacker with the longer fork, but I was able to achieve a comfortable position with a higher rise bar and sliding the seat a little more forward on the rails. On that topic, it would be nice if the seat angle got steeper as the sizes got larger, rather than the other way around. Descending It's pretty wild to look at the geometry of the 2016 Top Fuel compared to this version. That bike had a 70-degree head angle, and that's in the low setting. Needless to say, things have progressed dramatically over the last decade, and the current Top Fuel feels much, much less pointy than those older models did. It's not that they weren't fun to ride, it's just that they didn't have as much room for error. With the Top Fuel Gen 4, the handling is quick without feeling sketchy – it's right at home on rolling, meandering trails, and it does a great job of maintaining speed on descents that require pumping and pedaling to avoid getting hung up. Just like on the climbs, there's support for those moments when an extra pedal stroke or two is required, and enough small bump sensitivity to keep the wheels gripping on slippery, awkward sections of trail. I mainly rode with the shock in the higher progression setting in order to have a little more end-stroke ramp up for the jumps and drops that are all over my local trails. The tune on the RockShox Deluxe felt very well matched to the bike – I didn't have any harsh bottom outs, and the instances when I used all of the travel were all very warranted. Configuration Quick Hits 29” 120 / 130: The Top Fuel's out of the box setup is ideal for quickly covering ground, with enough travel to handle just about everything save for super rough, steep descents. Toss on some lighter, faster rolling tires for the occasional XC race, or go with some even meatier rubber and maybe a higher rise bar and a shorter stem for more technical trails. 29” 120 / 140: This setting, which is achieved by swapping out the air spring in the RockShox Pike, give the Top Fuel geometry and travel numbers that are very similar to the 2020 Norco Optic, a bike that still holds its own, even though a new high pivot version has since been released. On the Top Fuel, I'd recommend increasing the rear travel at the same time, but the 120 / 140 setup it very useable – that rear shock does a commendable job in rougher terrain. 29” 130 / 140: The longer travel setting ended up being my favorite configuration, especially when I installed some silly-light Roval XC wheels and Maxxis Rekon tires. As it turns out, combining the rolling speed of an XC race bike with a little extra travel for those techier sections is a recipe for a really good time. The extra suspension travel wasn't a hindrance at all on the climbs, and it was very nice to have at the tail end of that big ride I mentioned, when my line choosing skills weren't quite as sharp as they'd been earlier in the day. Honestly, I think Trek could do a slight reshuffle of their lineup. Call this bike the Fuel, and use the 130 / 140mm configuration as the stock setup. That would open the door for a lightweight 120/120 flexstay thing that's truly deserving of the Top Fuel moniker. Mixed wheel 130 / 140: I prefer mixed wheels for longer travel bikes, but I'm not as sold for shorter travel options – you lose some of the rollover benefits when climbing, and on a bike like this that's a large part of the equation. This setup was entertaining, especially when cornering and jumping, but it didn't feel like the most optimized setup. Trek Top Fuel Transition Spur How Does It Compare? If downcountry was a real thing, the Transition Spur would be the poster child. The geometry is similar between it and the Top Fuel, but the Top Fuel has the more versatile frame thanks to the geometry and shock adjustment possibilities, plus the in-frame storage. The Spur excels in its category, and I found it worked best with a 120mm fork to match the 120mm of rear travel. It's not really a bike that I'd try to beef up to turn into more of a trail bike. With the Top Fuel, it's a slightly different story – it's more of a light trail bike, and it works well with a slightly longer fork. Even in the 120mm setting, the Top Fuel feels like it gets more out of its travel – the ramp up is less noticeable, which can make it feel like there's more travel on tap. Another contender in this category is the Santa Cruz Tallboy. The Tallboy still feels like more bike than the Top Fuel – it has both wheels in the trail bike category, and has a more muted, slightly less lively nature than the Top Fuel. Which Model is the Best Value? Not surprisingly, the $10,499 Top Fuel XX AXS doesn't pose the best value. If you're shopping for a higher end bike but don't want to unnecessarily spend money, I'd recommend looking at the 9.9 XTR model, which retails for $7,000. That model has cable-actuated Shimano XTR drivetrain, along with top-of-the-line RockShox suspension, and carbon wheels. For riders on a tighter, more reasonable budget, I'd suggest going the aluminum route. Yes, it'll be heavier, but it'll also leave more money in your bank account. The Top Fuel 8 is priced at $4,000 USD, and would be a great place to start. The drivetrain is a mash-up of XT, SLX, and Deore level components, and there's Fox 34 fork with a Rhythm damper. Technical Report SRAM Level brakes: This is fast becoming a tired refrain, but the Level 4-piston brakes simply don't have enough power for more aggressive riding. Swapping to Codes comes with a small weight penalty, one that's more than worth it for the increased power and reduced brake fade. Integrated bar: I don't mind the rise, sweep, and roll of Trek's integrated bar / stem combo, but it's still too stiff for my liking, and that only increased if you trim down the bars. Bontrager Gunnison / Montrose tires: I was pleasantly surprised by the new Gunnison / Montrose tires. They're a little narrow, measuring 2.3” rather the the claimed 2.4”, but the rear tire is fast rolling, and the rubber compound is grippier in the wet than Bontrager's previous attempts. The retail price is $70, which is a good amount cheaper than similar options from Maxxis or Schwalbe. RockShox Reverb AXS Post: 170mm of dropper post travel is okay on a bike like this, but 200mm would be even better. RockShox still hasn't released a 200mm version of their fancy $645 wireless dropper, so I swapped this one out for a $199 cable-actuated PNW dropper post and was much happier. Livability: The Top Fuel has a very straightforward frame design, and I didn't run into any creaks or loosening hardware during the test period. The internal cable routing works well – when I swapped the rear brake I fed the line from the rear of the bike to the front and it popped right out, no fishing around required. The shock and linkage bolts are easy to access, and overall I'd rank it high on the 'ease of use' scale. Pros + Great mix of pedaling and descending capabilities + Wide range of effective adjustments + Clean looking frame with all the modern amenities Cons - Not as light as you'd expect considering the price - Underpowered brakes Pinkbike's Take At its core, the Top Fuel is a truly excellent bike, a prime example of how well-rounded a modern trail bike can be. Sure, it's not as light as it once was, and there are a few spec choices that could be improved, but the ride quality and sheer number of possible configurations help make up for that. For riders on the hunt for a bike they can customize to perfectly suit their favorite local trails, the Top Fuel is well worth considering. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Trek Trek Top Fuel Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: 2026 Devinci Spartan - A Canadian-Made Aluminum Beast
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: The Devinci Spartan’s history dates all the way back to 2013, when Stevie Smith rolled up to the DH World Champs in Pietermaritzburg, South Africa, aboard a new bike designed specifically for that extra-pedaly course. Three generations of the Spartan have come and gone since that time, which brings us to the fourth iteration that launches today. The 2026 Spartan no longer has the high-pivot layout found on the previous version (or a carbon frame for that matter). Instead, it uses a Canadian-made aluminum frame to deliver 165mm of rear travel via a Split Pivot suspension design. While the prior models were aimed at enduro racing, this one is much more of a “find steep, gnarly stuff and try to ride down it” type of bike - call it freeride or big mountain, no matter the term this is one long, slack machine. Devinci Spartan Details • Wheel size: Mixed (29" compatible) • Travel: 165mm, 170mm fork • Aluminum frame (made in Canada) • 63.5 or 63-degree head angle • Chainstay length: 450 or 457mm (size L) • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Weight as shown: 37.8 lb / 17.1 kg (size L, no pedals) • Price: $5,999 USD / $7,499 CAD • devinci.com The stock builds come set up with mixed wheels, but a flip chip at the lower shock can be used to run a 29” rear wheel. The chainstay length is also adjustable by 7 millimeters, thanks to another set of flip chips at the front of the seat stays. There are three complete builds and a frame only option - I’ve been testing the GX AXS model, which retails for $5,999 USD. Component highlights include a Fox Factory 38 fork and Float X2 shock, SRAM Maven Silver brakes, GX AXS T-Type drivetrain, Race Face wheels, and a Maxxis DHF / DHRII tire combo, both with the thicker DoubleDown casing. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Frame Details Devinci’s Quebec headquarters are located in a region that’s known as the Aluminum Valley, so it makes sense that the Spartan uses a 6061-T6 aluminum frame. There won’t be a carbon version either - Devinci decided that aluminum was the way to go with this model, citing durability and sustainability as two of the driving factors behind that decision, although I’m sure the lower cost and ease of production were also parts of the equation. The frame’s lines share some similarities with the shorter travel Troy , including the distinctive kink in the middle of the downtube. Personally, I like the looks; you get the sense that this is a bike with a purpose, where function trumps fashion. All of the bolts are easy to access, and there aren’t too many nooks and crannies for mud to accumulate in. The Spartan uses Devinci’s “Shed” in-frame storage system, which provides some room for stashing the essentials inside the downtube. The door itself feels pretty rudimentary, and it’s not the quickest to re-install after it’s been removed, but it didn’t rattle or fall off during testing, so that’s a start. The cables are routed internally, through ports in the head tube, and two clips in the storage door area keep them snugged up against the inside of the frame to prevent any annoying rattling. Other details include 12 x 148mm rear spacing (Devinci ditched SuperBoost when the Troy was released in 2024), a threaded bottom bracket, and three ISCG tabs for running a full strength bashguard. Devinci's Shed storage compartment. Two flip chips, one for the wheel size and the other for the chainstay length and head angle. Geometry For years, every bike that launched seemed to be getting longer and slacker than the previous version. That trend has started to slow down a little as the limits of what’s comfortably rideable are approached, but compared to the previous Spartan this new model is significantly longer and slacker. The head angle now sits at 63.5- or 63-degrees depending on the orientation of the chainstay flip chip. That flip chip also changes the chainstay length, which measures either 450 or 457mm on the size large and XL bikes, and 440 or 447mm on the small and medium frame sizes. The reach numbers range from 445 on the small to 505mm on the XL, and increase in 20mm increments between sizes. If all those numbers make your head spin, the overall takeaway is this: the new Spartan is a very long, slack bike, with a wheelbase that’s nearly 30mm longer than the outgoing model. Suspension Design The Spartan uses a 205 x 65mm stroke trunnion mounted shock for its 165mm of travel. Like the rest of Devinci’s full suspension lineup, it uses a Split Pivot layout, where the rearmost pivots are located around the rear axle, and the brake is mounted on seat stay. The Spartan has a linear, progressive leverage ratio, which allows it to work with both air and coil shocks. Specifications Price $5999 Travel 165mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Factory Fork Fox 38 Factory Grip X2, 170mm Headset FSA Orbit Cassette SRAM XS 1275 10-52 Crankarms SRAM GX Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle AXS T-Type Chain SRAM GX 12-speed Shifter Pods SRAM AXS pod Handlebar Race Face Era carbon Stem Race Face Turbine Grips Devinci lock-on Brakes SRAM Maven Silver Hubs Race Face Vault Rim Race Face ARC 30 Tires Maxxis Minion DHF MaxxGrip DD / DHR II MaxxTerra DD Seat SDG Bellair 3.0 Seatpost SDG Tellis 31.6mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup The Spartan arrived with its SDG Tellis dropper post out of the bike, so I slapped on a little grease, stuck it into the seattube opening, and… nothing. It wouldn’t fit. I double checked that the post was the right size (it was) and then did some grumbling. It turns out there was an issue with some of the early Spartan samples, and the seat tubes weren’t reamed properly. (Maybe that was the day Yoann Barelli and Steve Vanderhoek were working the factory floor ). Devinci assured me the issue has been resolved, and even sent over a photo of the reamers they’re using to make sure it’s corrected for good. Other than the seat post struggles the rest of the setup process was very straightforward. I trimmed the bars to my preferred 770mm, set the Fox 38 to my typical pressures, and by the end of the test period was running the Float X2 at 180psi, which resulted in 28% sag. Conditions were extra-wet for much of the test period, so I did swap out the Minion DHF tire for a Highroller III. The Minion was my go-to for years, but these days, if I was picking from the Maxxis catalog, I’d rather run a Highroller III or an Assegai. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Devinci Spartan https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/oabOus3m Copied Live 00:00 06:36 06:36 Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington , and the surrounding area, with conditions typically on the wetter side of things, although there were some perfect-fit days in the mix. Climbing The Spartan falls into a similar category as the Trek Fuel LX, or the Raaw Madonna - they’re stout, aluminum framed bikes that don’t exactly leap up the climbs, but they’re also not bad places to be while you’re working your way uphill. On the Spartan, the climb lever for the Float X2 is easy to access, which meant that I was much more likely to use it than if it was buried down near the bottom bracket. I’d typically flip it closed for long gravel road slogs or spinning on pavement to a trail head, and open it up for more technical sections. I’ve said it before, but a slack head angle and long chainstays don’t automatically create a bad climber. Sure, the handling is more sluggish than a shorter, steeper bike, but the extra-long footprint of the Spartan helps give it loads of traction and stability. It isn’t the snappiest pedaler, but it doesn’t feel overly wallowy either - on rougher climbs with the shock open there was plenty of grip, and I was able to get it up chunky climbs without that much fuss. It’s no lightweight, but the overall level of heft is close to what I’d expect for this style of bike - these days it seems like most aluminum enduro sleds are coming in around the 36 - 39 pound mark. Descending My first few rides on the Spartan didn’t really work out the way I wanted. Sometimes a bike will show up and I’ll click with it almost instantly, while others require an adjustment period. That’s how it was with this green machine. I’ve ridden plenty of long, slack bikes over the years, and I’ll always have a soft spot for burly aluminum enduro/freeride bikes, but at times this one felt a bit longer and slacker than I wanted - I had a few front end washouts, and a few moments in the steeps where I didn’t feel like I was totally in charge. I ended up dropping the handlebars down a little lower, and running less air pressure in the fork to get my weight in a better spot over the front wheel. That helped, and things started to settle into place, although I can’t say I ever felt completely at home on the Spartan. Some of that has to do with the overall wheelbase - it’s almost 1300mm, a number that's around the limit of what feels comfortable for me on an enduro bike, at least for my 5”11 height on the steeper, tighter trails that I prefer. The elusive M/L size would likely be the ideal fit for me - given the Spartan’s chainstay length and head angle I think a shorter reach number would help make things feel more manageable. The longer / slacker setting does reduce the reach by 5mm, but then the wheelbase gets even longer. I tried that setting just to see, and while it’s entertaining to have a bike with geometry lifted straight from a DH bike, it makes the bike even more terrain specific - the extra-slack setting definitely isn’t the way to go for anything other than the steepest terrain, and ideally those trails have room to let the bike run. In zones with higher average speeds - a bike park, for instance - the Spartan’s length wouldn’t feel as cumbersome, and I’d have no qualms about using it as a daily driver in the Whistler Bike Park. It has more of a muted, stuck to the ground nature, but once it’s airborne it has the lazier, stable handling that’s more typically associated with a downhill bike. As far as suspension performance goes, the Spartan does a good job with maintaining grip on small-to-mid size bumps, and it breaks into the initial portion of its travel quite easily. On bigger hits it feels like you’re smacking the ground with a dead blow hammer - it’s quite muted, and the hit is dissipated quickly, although there’s not as much to push against when those impacts come in quick succession - that first part of the travel is gobbled up pretty quickly before the shock ramps up. I ended up running the rebound almost all the way open on the Float X2, and could have still gone quicker in order to help the bike recover from those repeated impacts. The Spartan is coil shock compatible, and I’d strongly consider going that route in order to gain a little more support, and bump up the level of traction even further. Devinci Spartan Raaw Madonna How Does It Compare? Many of the Raaw Madonna’s geometry numbers are quite similar to the Spartan, but all of the small differences add up to create two bikes with very different on-trail characteristics. On the Raaw, the lower bottom bracket, taller stack, and slightly steeper head angle make it easier to have an upright position and steer from your feet - it’s almost like riding an off-road scooter, except that it’s way cooler. With the Spartan, the bike feels more sprawling, and it takes more physical effort to push it through corners or get through slower speed chunky sections. They’re both what I’d consider “big” bikes, in that they do their best work in steep, gnarly terrain, but the Raaw has a wider window of happiness, where the Spartan really needs room to run. The Trek Fuel LX / MX falls into the burly aluminum bike category too, and it can be set up to be slightly slacker than the Spartan depending on the shock links that are used. The Fuel LX’s chainstay length and reach are both shorter than the Spartan, and as I wrote in my review of that bike, “Other than that slack head angle, the other dimensions aren’t outliers - the moderate reach and chainstay length feel well matched, and help keep the bike manageable in twistier sections of trail.” I’d classify the Trek and the Spartan as freeride bikes in the more old-school definition of the term, bikes meant for seeking out gnarly maneuvers deep in the woods, but the Trek is a better match for the slower speed jank. Which Model is the Best Value? For the price, the top of the line GX AXS build I reviewed does a good value - other than potentially swapping the front tire, everything else is well suited to the bike’s intentions. $6,000 is still a lot of money, though, which is why I’d suggest the next model down, the Spartan 90, for riders who are trying to get the most bang for their buck. That model comes with SRAM's cable-actuated Eagle 90 drivetrain, SRAM Maven Base brakes, plus a RockShox Select+ fork and Vivid air shock, all for $4,799 USD. You can save even more by dropping to the $3,899 USD Spartan Deore model, but the SRAM DB8 brakes and RockShox Domain Gold fork are going to be a noticeable step down from the spec on that Spartan 90. Technical Report Chainslap protector: The Spartan’s chainslap protector is okay, but not amazing. It’s on the firmer side, and doesn’t mute the chain noise as well as some of the better options out there. Overall the Spartan is a pretty quiet bike; running some softer chainslap protection tape or an STFU would make it even more stealthy. SRAM Maven brakes: It’s old news by now, but the Mavens are very, very powerful, and they were a good match for the Spartan. Riders that want the lighter lever feel that the recently released B1 levers deliver will need to shell out an extra $90 to purchase an upgrade kit. For some riders, the brakes will be totally fine in their stock form, but I will say that the revised pivot kit does give them a really nice and light action, which I prefer. Fox 38 fork: My test bike had a 2026 Fox 38 fork, but full production models will come with the brand-new 2027 Fox 38. You can read all about the fork here . Frame storage door: The Spartan’s storage door doesn’t like to sit flush, and it can be a little finicky to get back in place once it's removed. I’d think of it more of an emergency storage compartment, rather than something you’ll want to fiddle with on every ride. Pros + The answer for riders who think most bikes aren't long or slack enough + Very stable, especially at high speeds + Adjustable chainstay length that actually makes a difference Cons - There's such thing as too long and slack - Some of the finishing touches (storage compartment door, chainslap protection) could use refinement Pinkbike's Take The new Spartan is a lot of bike to handle, and realistically, for most riders the 150mm Troy is going to be the better option. That bike is a little shorter, a little steeper, and still very capable. That said, for riders who want a big brute of a bike, one that works best on more open, steep, and higher speed trails, the Spartan fits the bill - it’s fast and stable, just make sure you have terrain that warrants a bike like this. — Mike Kazimer Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Devinci Devinci Spartan Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: The New Pivot Firebird  is Well Balanced & Refined
author: Seb Stott
article_text: V5 Pivot Firebird review.mp4 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/TTPAPn0t Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 06:04 “Don’t mess it up!” That was the brief from Pivot’s enduro team for the V5 Firebird. The new bike is more about evolution than revolution, with none of the suspension or drivetrain weirdness developed for their Phoenix downhill bike. It has tweaked geometry and suspension, plus impressive adjustability and storage features that Pivot hopes will take their race-winning enduro platform up a notch. The Firebird is designed to be a capable enduro race bike, with 165 mm of rear travel and a 170mm fork. But it’s more versatile than those numbers would suggest thanks to impressive pedalling efficiency and balanced geometry that make it happy to cover ground and rewarding on flatter trails. It’s also got room to carry more snacks and tools than most of its rivals. Pivot Firebird Details • Wheel size: 29" or MX compatible • 165 mm rear travel, 170mm fork • 63.8° head angle, 77° seat angle • Size specific & adjustable chainstay • Three bottle bosses & downtube storage • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • Claimed frame weight from 6.18 lbs / 2.8 kg • Actual weight as tested: 16.1 kg / 35.5 lb • Lifetime original owner frame warranty & bearing replacement • Price: $6,499 to £13,699 ($8,699 as tested) • pivotcycles.com While the V5 Firebird isn't a radical redesign, the subtle refinements seem to pay off on the trail. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Everything from the suspension to the geometry feels well-balanced and refined. — Seb Stott Frame Details The most obvious change to the frame is the addition of storage under the bottle cage. The downtube is large enough to swallow a regular inner tube plus a pouch for tools and snacks. The door is particularly well-designed with a secure yet easy-to-use latch that doesn’t rattle even with a full-sized water bottle attached. There's also a magnet to hold a master link on the underside of the door. There are also three bosses under the top tube that are intended to carry tools but, at least on the XL frame, can accommodate a Fidlock water bottle in addition to a full-size bottle on the downtube. There are two more bosses under the downtube plus a small door in front of the bottom bracket that is to assist with cable installation but could accommodate an emergency gel or two. This is one of the most adjustable enduro frames on the market. A clever flip chip at the rear axle allows the chainstay length to be increased by 8 mm. It’s one of a small number of frames that offer this feature while retaining SRAM’s UDH and Transmission compatibility. Interestingly, Pivot made use of a patent they had for a similar swinging dropout used on their fat bike. Even though the drive side dropout rotates when the chainstay length is extended, Pivot say there’s no need to adjust the B-tension. However, they do recommend using a chain with one more link with the longer setting to stay within SRAM and Shimano’s chain length recommendations. That makes it more of a set-and-forget feature than something to chop and change. The chainstay length is also size-specific, with 7 mm separating a small and XL. This difference is accommodated with the front triangle only – the pivot locations change slightly relative to the bottom bracket – so the rear triangle and links are common to all sizes. This is nowhere near enough to maintain the ratio of front-centre to rear-centre across the sizes. But combined with the adjustability, there is a 15 mm difference between the smallest and longest combination, which is enough to notice. More specifically, the chainstay length in the small sizes is particularly compact, a plus for short riders who struggle to fully unweight the front wheel. The long setting in the XL size (tested) is not especially long. A second flip chip adjusts the bottom bracket height by 5 mm and the frame angles by 0.4 degrees. This isn’t enough to keep the geometry the same with 29” or 27.5” wheels, so it is lower and slacker with a mullet setup. Pivot say either setting can be used with either wheel size, but the high setting is strongly recommended with MX wheels. Yet another flip chip at the lower shock mount alters the leverage curve for a more linear or progressive suspension feel. As if that weren't enough, the oversized ZS56/56 headset makes it possible to install an aftermarket reach adjust or head angle adjust headset. While the rear triangle is the same for all sizes, the front triangle’s carbon layup is "tuned" so taller riders have a stiffer frame and smaller ones get more flex. Cables are clipped to the inside of the downtube rather than running through tubes within the tube; this makes it easier to change brake routing depending on which side of the handlebars you want the rear brake to go. As you would hope, the cables come out of ports beside the head tube and not through the headset. It's fair to assume that the downtube storage and flip chips will increase the frame's weight compared to the outgoing model. The 2022 version weighed 1 kg less when we tested it , albeit with some lighter parts and a smaller frame size. Fair to say the new Firebird isn't the lightest, but it's hardly heavy by modern enduro standards. Out back, Pivot are sticking with the SuperBoost 157 mm rear axle standard, which they say makes a 30% stiffer and more reliable wheel. An obvious downside of this is that there are fewer replacement wheel options than the 148 standard, but on the other hand, the SuperBoost wheel is generally interchangeable with those on DH bikes, which could come in handy for those who own a DH and enduro bike. The rear triangle is shaped to provide maximum heel clearance, and Pivot say this is actually easier to achieve with a SuperBoost rear end. It also uses a press-fit bottom bracket. According to Pivot’s Chris Cocalis, “Press fit doesn’t creak; poor tolerances creak.” Pivot say their manufacturing process ensures reliably precise alignment. There are three colors to choose from: White Gold, Yolo (yellow) and limited edition Purple Galaxy. The frame is covered by a lifetime first-owner warranty and a lifetime bearing warranty – if the pivot bearings wear out they’ll send a new set for free (fitting not included). Suspension Design Pivot did consider using a six-bar suspension layout like their Phoenix downhill bike for the Firebird, but decided any marginal benefits on the descents were more than offset in terms of climbing and complexity. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Pivot Firebird V5 suspension by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 2,749 Faves: 1 Comments: 0 The DW4 design offers consistent levels of anti-squat, which helps the bike to pedal with minimal unwanted bob or slouching across a wide sag window. It also provides consistent anti-rise levels, which could make braking behaviour more consistent. The overall result is a bike that resists pitching whether pedalling or braking, so the chassis remains stable and centred in its travel. Anti-squat. Anti-rise. The flip chip at the lower shock mount offers two options for frame progressivity: 32% when the eyelet is mounted towards the rear and 27% towards the front, when measured from 0 to 100% of travel. The more progressive setting is similar to the old Firebird and is more progressive than most without being an outlier. This is the setting I used most and found the balance of support and bottom-out resistance to be about right with the single green volume spacer that comes installed with the shock. Pivot say the suspension travel is barely affected by the progressivity fip chip or the high/low setting, but switching to the longer chainstay will increase travel by a few millimetres. I measured the real-world travel on my test bike at a shade over 170 mm in the long setting. Geometry & Sizing Compared to the previous Firebird, the new model is 5 mm lower, 0.2 degrees slacker and with a slightly steeper seat tube in the smaller sizes. The charts above show the geometry in the stock configuration: the low setting with 29” wheels and the high setting with MX wheels. By my measurements, the seat angle at my pedalling height measures 76.5 degrees in the low setting. That's slightly slacker than claimed, but shorter riders will get a steeper position because of the offset seat tube. Note the XS is available as a mullet only. Pivot's sizing guidelines are as follows: X-Small: 4'11"–5'4" | 1,50–1,63 m Small: 5'2"–5’8” | 1,57–1,73m Medium: 5'9"–6' | 1,73–1,83m Large: 5'11"–6’4” | 1,80–1,93m X-Large: 6'1"+ | 1,85m+ At 6’3” / 191 cm, I found the XL to be a great fit, but, interestingly, Pivot's guidelines wouldn't rule out the large. Bear in mind that the reach can be adjusted by +/- 5 mm with an aftermarket headset . Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $8699 Travel 165 mm Rear Shock 2026 Fox Factory Float X2 Fork Fox Factory 38, GRIP X2 - 170mm Headset Pivot Precision Sealed Cartridge 56/56 Cassette Sram X0 1295 Eagle Transmission 10-52t Crankarms Sram X0 Eagle DUB 32t - 165mm (XS-SM), 170mm (MD-XL) Chainguide Pivot upper guide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB PF92 Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur Sram X0 Eagle Transmission 12-Speed Chain Sram X0 Eagle Transmission Flattop Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods Sram AXS Pod Controller Handlebar Phoenix Team Carbon - 20mm Rise 760mm (XS), 20mm Rise 780mm (SM), 35mm Rise 780mm (MD-XL) Stem Phoenix Team Enduro V2- 32mm (XS), 40mm (SM-LG), 50mm (XL) Grips Phoenix Factory Lock-On Brakes Sram Maven Silver, 200 mm rotors Wheelset DT Swiss XM1700 - Mixed or 29” Hubs DT Swiss 350, 36t Star Ratchet Spokes DT Swiss, 32 Rim 30 mm, alloy Tires Front: Continental Kryptotal-F 29” x 2.4” Soft Compound Enduro Casing Rear: Continental Kryptotal-R 27.5” or 29” x 2.4” Soft Compound Enduro Casing Seat S, SM: Phoenix WTB Pro High Tail Trail MD-XL: Phoenix WTB Pro Volt Seatpost Fox Transfer Factory, 125-150mm (XS), 155-180mm (SM), 185-210mm (MD-LG), 215-240mm (XL) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC One less-obvious spec highlight is Pivot’s own carbon bar, which they say matches the benchmark compliance of OneUp’s carbon handlestick. Test Bike Setup Testing kicked off with four full days of shuttle laps in Finale Ligure, followed by a handful of rides on familiar soggier trails in Scotland’s Tweed Valley. Pivot provides a handy sag indicator, which makes initial setup much easier than measuring and calculating sag with a non-RockShox shock. This corresponds to 33% shock sag, but I soon added 10 psi to the shock, which left me with 19 mm (30%) sag. I also removed one of the two volume spacers from the fork to balance out the suspension feel and stuck with the single green spacer in the shock. After playing around with most of the geometry adjustments, I settled on the longer chainstay, low flip-chip setting and more progressive suspension. At 190 cm / 6’3”, the XL size fit me well. I did swap the 50 mm stem for a 40 mm, but I was happy with either on this bike. Tire pressures were 23 psi & 27 psi in Finale and 22 psi and 26 psi in Scotland. Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 32 Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 37" / 93cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted Testing Info I rode Little Champery in Finale Ligure seven times thanks to shuttling from Finale Bikers. This allowed me to compare most of the Firebird's features (BB height, chainstay length and stem length) back-to-back. It's also a trail where the bike felt particularly impressive, both in the sweeping turns up top and the rough rock gardens at the bottom. Climbing The Firebird is particularly stable and efficient when pedalling. The suspension is remarkably still whether spinning or sprinting, but it feels especially good when accelerating out of the saddle. It's not the supplest when pedalling over bumpy terrain, but it’s not terrible; the suspension does a decent job of conforming to the terrain while still staying on top of its travel. My only criticism is the seat angle. It’s designed to avoid putting the rider too far over the front wheel for riding the rocky traverses around Pivot’s Phoenix HQ, but for me, the position feels a touch off the back for the brutally steep, but not especially complicated climbs we have in the Tweed Valley. With the saddle slammed all the way forward it’s not far off my ideal position, and the bike still climbs well because the suspension stays high in its travel, but I think the XL could do with having the seatpost a little more upright. After all, how many people have their saddle slammed all the way backwards ? Descending The stable suspension platform continues on the descents. The bike feels sturdy and solid, perhaps helped by a stiff wheel and frame package, and the suspension is well-damped and measured. It doesn’t give away travel unduly and never wallows. There’s excellent cornering support and a responsive feel that belies the travel and works particularly well in supportive turns. At the same time, the suspension responds well to small bumps and tames the most hectic terrain. It feels like there's plenty of travel in reserve and gets more impressive at higher speeds and on harder hits. It just doesn’t cosset and cushion you as much as some enduro bikes (the Deviate Claymore comes to mind). Having said that, there is a lot of scope to adjust the suspension feel with damping, volume spacers and the frame’s flip chip, so if you want a more pillowy feel that's what it's there for. In the stock setup with one volume spacer and 28-30% sag, I found the end of the travel only on the biggest hucks-to-flat and sometimes felt the bottom-out bumper doing its thing towards the end of the stroke. But it’s not a dramatic or harsh ramp: it’s more a soft catch for when you’re getting silly. The handling is similarly well-judged. The XL’s wheelbase is stable without being overly stretched out, which retains some of the agility you want for enduro-style riding. But the generous stack height and 35 mm bar offer a roomy cockpit out of the box. I started with the shorter chainstay setting and found it to be easy to manoeuvre without feeling out of touch with the front tire in loose, open corners. Swapping to the longer setting isn’t the night and day difference some might imagine. In a back-to-back test, the difference is subtle, but it adds a little confidence in long-radius turns. The change to the suspension is probably more noticeable if the shock isn’t adjusted to compensate for the extra leverage - it felt slightly more supple. It takes a little more force to initiate a manual, but to me this isn’t a downside so much as a difference. The high setting with 29" wheels offers extra clearance for tight awkward sections and doesn’t detract from cornering confidence when you commit to the turn, but to me it made the bike feel more nervous on fast, steep sections. Pedal clearance is ample even in the low setting. Again, it's a relatively subtle change. The Firebird is a solid-feeling bike in rough and technical terrain yet remains responsive and efficient on flatter sections. It’s more on the sporty rather than squishy side, but it’s never harsh or nervous. It feels like an appropriate tool for a wide range of riding from DH shuttles to trail rides. Don't say there aren't enough options. What's the Best Value? It's hard to justify spending $2,200 more than the starting price as the "best value" option, but I think if value-for-money is your primary concern you'd be better served elsewhere. The Firebird is an unashamedly bougie bike whichever model you choose. With that in mind, I think the Pro X0 Eagle Transmission build that I tested makes the most sense relative to its rivals. It has the top-spec suspension components that you want in a posh enduro bike without the Live Valve or carbon cranks this bike doesn't need. As if there aren't enough options in the chart above (I had to shorten the model names from Transmission to TR to fit them all in), the Pro and Team models can be upgraded to Reynolds Blacklabel Enduro Pro carbon wheels for an additional $1,200. But I wouldn't bother with that. All in all, there are 64 combinations of spec options, colour and wheel sizes available. Also, Europeans will have a choice of Newmen wheels instead of DT or Reynolds. Pivot Firebird Scott Ransom How Does It Compare? The Firebird fits into an elusive category of big-hitting enduro bikes that are happy to climb and don’t feel out of place on mellower trails. Some other bikes on that list would be the Merida One-Sixty, Canyon Strive, Orbea Rallon and Scott Ransom. The Pivot wins practicality points for its non-headset cable routing, impressive warranty, superior storage options and helpful adjustability. It’s probably the best-pedalling bike on that list with the shock left open too, although the Merida’s steeper seat tube gives it an edge when the climb points skyward. When descending, the Pivot feels most like the Scott, with a stout-feeling frame and well-controlled suspension. I would say the Pivot’s suspension is more consistent and predictable, perhaps thanks to the 2026 X2 shock. On descending performance alone I would put the Firebird a hair ahead of that particular pack, although the Merida and Canyon are considerably more affordable. Technical Report Fox Transfer Seatpost: My test bike came with a 210 mm seatpost which was a little shy about returning to full height. It works fine if the lever is pressed fully and the saddle is unobstructed but if the saddle catches on your shorts or the lever is half-pressed it gets stuck a few centimetres from top-out. This is a little disappointing for a brand-new high-end post. SRAM Maven Silver brakes: While the power and lever feel are excellent, the rear brake on my bike had an inconsistent bite point out of the box that persisted even after multiple re-bleeds. Following SRAM's piston massage procedure has since solved this (at least for now). Continental Enduro casing tires: I’ve spent a lot of time on the DH casing Kryptotals but the enduro casings impressed. They stood up to four days of shuttling in Finale and provided predictable grip on Italian rocks and Scottish mud alike. They do squirm noticeably more than the DH version, but they are appreciably lighter and faster-rolling. I would prefer the SuperSoft compound up front though. Pros + Well-balanced overall ride and handling rarely feels out of place + Superb pedalling efficiency adds verstaility + Impressive frame storage and adjustability + Lifetime frame and bearing warranty Cons - Expensive - Seat angle could be steeper, at least for the XL size - Super Boost rear axle limits wheel options Pinkbike's Take Mountain bikes are all about fine-tuning and subtle tweaks these days. The new Firebird isn’t a game-changer that’s head and shoulders ahead of its rivals; nor is it radically different to its predecessor. But it does ride like an extremely well-oiled machine. Everything from the suspension to the geometry feels well-balanced and refined. It also offers exceptional storage and adjustability which give it a USP. Its impressive pedalling efficiency makes it a good choice for those who want a capable enduro machine that’s got your back on the toughest terrain but doesn't sap the life out of mellower trails or punchy climbs. — Seb Stott Photos: Carlo De Santis Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Pivot Pivot Firebird Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: 2026 Trek Fuel LX / MX - The Secret Menu Option
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: reviews-and-tech, videos, reviews, enduro-bikes, trek, trek-fuel-lx
article_text: 2026 Trek Fuel LX More Videos From Pinkbike https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/VFt88Y6W Copied Loading ad Live 00:00 00:00 00:00 The Fuel has long been the meat and potatoes, bread and butter, or whatever your favorite food-based analogy is in Trek’s MTB catalog. Over the years, though, it’s started to nudge its way into longer travel territory. Back in 2020 it had 130 millimeters of travel, which increased to 140 millimeters in 2022, and now that’s been bumped up to 145 millimeters, and that’s just the starting point. Trek has decided to turn the Fuel into a bike that can be transformed from an all-round trail bike into a burly enduro / freeride machine by swapping out some links and suspension components. The number of possible combinations can seem overwhelming at first, but there are essentially three base models, and then the offshoots of those models. Trek Fuel LX 9 EA90 Details • Wheel size: 29" or mixed • Travel: 160mm rear / 170mm fork • Aluminum frame • 63.4-degree head angle • Chainstay length: 442mm (L) • Sizes: S, M, L (tested), XL, XXL • Weight: 38.6 lb / 17.5 kg • Frame weight: 10.1 lb / 4.6 kg • Price: $5,200 USD / Frame only: $1,999 USD • trekbikes.com The Fuel EX is the bike that Trek expects to be the most popular, with 145mm of rear travel, a 150mm fork, and 29 inch wheels. It’s essentially a slightly updated version of the bike that we named ' Bike of the Year ' back in 2022. The next model is the mixed-wheel Fuel MX, which uses a different rocker link and lower shock mount hardware for its 150mm of rear travel. The final model is the Fuel LX, which uses its own rocker links and the same lower shock links as the EX. This model has 29” wheels and 160mm of travel - it’s essentially a Slash without the high-pivot. For riders that start with one model and get the itch to turn it into something else, rocker link kits will be available for $100, and the lower shock mount kits are $20. I’ve been putting the miles in on the aluminum-framed Fuel LX 9 EA90, which has a very smart parts spec considering the $5,200 USD price tag. That gets you a 170mm Fox 38 Factory fork, DHX2 factory coil shock, SRAM Maven Bronze brakes, and a cable-actuated Eagle 90 drivetrain. Of course, I ended up getting sucked into the configuration rabbit hole, and my current setup is slightly different from stock, but I’ll get into that in a bit. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take No cable rattling, no chain slapping - just point it downhill, hang on, and enjoy the sound of silence. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The Fuel LX's frame shape starts out fairly similar to the Gen 5 Slash - it even still has that slight hump in the top tube. Yes, I wish it was straight too, purely for aesthetic reasons, but it does provide extra room for a big water bottle, and it’s not noticeable when you’re actually riding the bike, so I’m okay letting it slide. The Fuel now uses a standard zero stack headset, which opens the door for riders to install an aftermarket reach or head angle adjusting headset if the wide array of stock possibilities aren’t enough. It’s at the seat tube where the frame shape starts to deviate from prior iterations - the seat tube is much steeper, and it's straight, which makes it possible to run longer travel dropper posts - even the size medium will fit a post with 200mm of drop Other details included an updated frame storage hatch with better sealing, and a revised chainslap protector that works very, very well - this is one of the quieter bikes I’ve been on in recent memory. Geometry In its stock configuration the Fuel LX has dual 29” wheels, a 63.4-degree head angle, and a 473mm reach for a size large. Chainstay lengths vary depending on size - they measure 437mm on the small and medium, and then increase by 5mm for the large, XL, and XXL sizes. Trek adjusted their sizing for the Fuels, ditching the size M/L and then altering the reach figures to ensure that there was a reasonable progression between each size. The biggest change is the increased amount of stack for the larger sizes - the size large has 17mm more stack than the prior model, thanks in part to a 125mm head tube length. More stack makes it easier to achieve an upright, modern riding position without needing to resort to super high rise bars or large stacks of spacers. Suspension Design Trek decided to go with different links for each model rather than just swapping shocks in order to ensure the suspension performance matched the bike’s purpose. For that reason, the longer travel models have more progression, giving them a softer initial portion of the travel and more ramp up to help handle those bigger hits. There are two positions on the lower shock mount, which changes the amount of progression by around 5%. For instance, on the LX shown here, the choice is between 19.1% or 24.2%. Higher progression amounts typically work best with coil shocks, and the slightly lower amount should work well with air, although it still comes down to rider preference - it only takes a couple minutes to adjust the progression, so on-trail experimentation is an easy process. The LX uses a trunnion-mount 205 x 65mm shock for its 160mm of travel. The Fuel MX and Fuel EX both use a 205 x 60mm stroke shock for their respective 150 and 145mm travel amounts; increasing the stroke length to 65mm without swapping any links results in 163mm of travel for the MX and 156mm for the EX models. Specifications Release Date 2026 Price $5200 Travel 160mm Rear Shock Fox DHX2, 205mm x 65mm Fork Fox Factory 38 170mm Cassette SRAM Eagle XS-1275, T-Type, 10-52 Crankarms SRAM Eagle 90, DUB, 30T steel, T-Type, 55mm chainline, 170mm length Rear Derailleur SRAM Eagle 90 Shifter Pods SRAM Eagle 90, 12 speed Handlebar Race Face Turbine, alloy, 35mm, 40mm rise, 780mm width Stem Bontrager Elite, 35mm, 0 degree, 45mm length Brakes SRAM Maven Bronze Wheelset Bontrager Line Comp 30 Tires Maxxis Assegai MaxxGrip / DHRII MaxTerra EXO+ Seat Verse Short Comp, steel rails, 145mm width Seatpost Bontrager Line Dropper, 200mm travel, Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Fuel Test Bike Setup My first few rides were with the Fuel LX set up with dual 29” wheels, the way that it showed up. It wasn’t long before I decided to swap to a 27.5” rear wheel - these days I’m a big fan of mixed wheels for longer travel bikes - it makes a lot of sense in the steep terrain that I prefer. Trek’s official recommendation for turning this into a long travel, mixed-wheel bike is to swap both the lower shock mount links and the upper rocker links; that’ll keep the geometry fairly similar. There’s also the bonus menu option, that involves running the MX lower shock mount links while keeping the LX rocker links. That creates a very slack 62.9-degree head angle and lowers the bottom bracket substantially. That ended up being my favorite setup, for reasons I’ll go into shortly. For riders that decide to go that route, I’d recommend 165mm cranks (I wish Trek had spec’d them to begin with on this model). Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Other than rear wheel and geometry tinkering I didn’t need to change much in the way of components. I put a DoubleDown casing Maxxis DHRII on the 27.5” rear wheel I used, a step up in puncture protection from the stock EXO+ casing tire, and I swapped out the house-brand grips for ones that made my hands happier. The bars were trimmed to my preferred 770mm length, and I tried a 40mm stem and the stock 45mm stem, eventually settling on the stock length. A 450 lb spring put me at 27% sag, which worked well for the harder packed, blown out conditions that prevailed - I'd go with a slightly lower spring rate for slippery, softer conditions. I ran 85 psi in the Fox 38 with one volume spacer and the rebound and compression set to my liking. Testing Info Most of my testing took place in Bellingham, Washington, and the surrounding area, including a trip up to Hope, BC, to ride the new First Blood trail. I’m always a little skeptical about trails that seem to receive an inordinate amount of internet hype, but I’d say it’s justified in this case - the amount of work that went into carving that trail out of a very steep hillside is impressive, and hopefully it's the start of even more purpose-built trails in the area. The climb is a bit of a grind, but it's not that bad, and I'd way rather be pedaling than crammed into a vehicle with a bunch of other riders. Climbing I’m going to avoid harping on the Fuel’s weight too much, but there’s no getting around the fact that this is definitely no lightweight, and that’s with the stock EXO+ tirese - add on some beefier rubber and you’re looking at a 39+ pound bike, and that’s without pedals, tools, or water. The carbon frame version does weigh nearly 3 pounds less, but it’ll also leave your wallet $1,000 lighter. Heft aside, the Fuel LX stays calm while climbing, and the only time I reached for the climb switch was on paved road segments, and even then that was mainly to have something to do. The higher rise bars and steepish seat angle create a nice spot to settle into for those long grinds. For typical winch and plummet / groan and grin type riding, where a steep dirt road leads to even steeper descents the Fuel isn’t hard to live with - as long as the descent is worth it, the extra weight isn’t the end of the world. It’s on flatter, more meandering approaches that the extra effort to get the bike moving is noticeable, but that’s also related to the geometry and build kit - the LX model isn’t what I’d recommend for cruising around on blue trails. On slower speed, technical approaches the Fuel remains very manageable - even when I put it into the extra-slack and low mixed-wheel setting I didn’t need to fight with it to navigate tighter switchbacks or get up rooty steps. Again, all of this is in the context of it being an enduro bike - I don’t want to make it seem like you’ll be snagging KOMs on the climbs, and I also don’t want to undersell the point that it does take extra effort to lug this thing around. That didn’t stop me from taking it on multiple rides with over 4,000+ vertical feet of climbing - I can handle some big big slogs if there's a reward at the end. Descending I rode the Fuel in three different configurations - the stock version, with dual 29” wheels, the mixed wheel setup with the MX rocker link and lower shock mount, and in the secret menu option, which entails using the LX rocker link and the MX lower shock mount. If you’re considering this bike and are planning on using it for steeper, rowdier trails, maybe with some bike park laps thrown into the mix, I’ll cut right to the chase: mixed wheels with the LX upper / MX lower links is the way to go. The lower bottom bracket height and smaller rear wheel makes ripping corners even easier, and the slack head angle gives it a surefooted, stable feel in the steeps. On paper, 62.9-degrees is quite slack, but somehow the combo of those higher rise bars and a 45mm stem makes it much more manageable than I expected, even on slightly tamer terrain. I tried a 40mm stem for a few rides, but ended up going back to the 45, since it felt like that shifted my weight forward just enough to prevent any front wheel washouts. The increased stack compared to Trek's prior models is a welcome change, especially in the steeps - that puts you in a good attack position above the bike, rather than feeling like you're getting sucked down into the front wheel. The weight that I griped about in the climbing section isn’t an issue at all while descending; if anything, it helps give the Fuel LX/MX an extra helping of stability. Other than that slack head angle, the other dimensions aren’t outliers - the moderate reach and chainstay length feel well matched, and help keep the bike manageable in twistier sections of trail. It's essentially a freeride bike, at least depending on your definition of that term - to me, freeriding involves rock rolls, jumps, drops, and plenty of steep chutes, and it's on those types of rides that the Fuel LX/MX feels most at home. I'm a big fan of bikes that you can chuck into nasty webs of roots or piles of rocks without worrying too much, and the Fuel LX/MX certainly fits that bill. I mentioned the low decibel level of this bike earlier, but it’s worth noting again - I was really impressed by how little noise it emitted. No cable rattling, no chain slapping - just point it downhill, hang on, and enjoy the sound of silence. O-Chains and other chain flail / pedal kickback devices have been getting a good deal of attention lately, but the Fuel LX isn't a bike that's immediately calling out for one of those devices to be installed - it's nice and calm underfoot, and even awkward stairstep-like sections didn't unsettle it. As far as overall suspension performance goes, it was free of any surprises - there’s plenty of ramp up for handling bigger hits, and I only had one harsh bottom out, and that was fully warranted - it happened after an extra-nasty drop into a hole. I wasn’t on the extremes for either the fork or shock, which is a good sign, since it means there’s room for adjustment depending on the trail conditions. Trek Fuel LX Raaw Madonna V3 How Does It Compare? I’m very familiar with the Raaw Madonna, so a comparison between that aluminum machine and the Trek seems appropriate. When it comes to geometry, both bikes are very adaptable - both can be set up mixed wheel or dual 29”, and can accept angle or reach adjusting headsets. The Raaw’s chainstay length is longer, which I’d say is the most noticeable trait out on the trail. The Madonna has a centered, carvey feel to it; it’s almost like you’re turning it like you would on skis. The Trek’s chainstays are a little shorter, which can make it easier to perform quick direction changes in the steeps, but the flip side is that there isn’t quite the same locked-in feeling when cornering. For flat out, high speed trails I’d give the slight edge to the Madonna when it comes to stability, but realistically neither bike has much of a speed limit. Price is where the Madonna falters - it’s around $2,500 USD for the frame only, without a shock; building up a complete Raaw will end up being a good deal more expensive than a Trek. The Raaw’s not exactly a lightweight, but the Trek is even chunkier. Which Model is the Best Value? Out of all the alloy Fuel LX models, the base model one easily poses the best value - paying more will get you wireless shifting, but realistically that’s about it. The same goes for the carbon version - the $6,199 USD has essentially the same build as the alloy model here, other than the switch to a carbon bar and carbon-railed seat. It also weighs 4 pounds less… Is it worth spending $250 for each pound saved? That’s between you and your wallet, but no matter what, the base model of each frame material gets my vote. Technical Report Maxxis Tires: Trek’s bikes have typically been shod with Bontrager tires, with mixed results. Some versions were decent, others were quite sketchy in the wet and had to be swapped out almost immediately. For that reason, it’s good to see the proven Assegai / DHRII EXO+ tire combo, with MaxxGrip rubber up front and MaxxTerra out back. A DoubleDown casing would have been nice to see, at least for the rear. SRAM Eagle 90 Drivetrain: The Eagle 90’s shifter feel isn’t anything to get too excited about, but it does the job, and the drivetrain works well - the shifting is very consistent, and even after multiple smacks into rocks and roots the derailleur is still hanging tough. 165mm cranks would also be on my wish list, at least for the LX model, since the bottom bracket height starts getting pretty low if you explore the secret geometry menu options. Fox DHX2 Coil Shock: The DHX2 received a substantial update earlier this year, and now uses a monotube design with externally adjustable high and low speed compression and rebound. There's no hydraulic bottom out feature, but the Fuel LX's level of progression and the shock's foam bumper kept things feeling nice and smooth, even on bigger hits. The rebound knobs do need to be removed to swap out the spring, which is a minor inconvenience, but most riders will likely only be doing that once or twice. Pros + Super solid and composed in technical terrain + Great parts kit for the price + Frame can easily be adapted for different uses + Very quiet - no cable rattle or chain slapping Cons - Geometry or wheel size changes require purchasing different links or headset parts - Heavy frame Pinkbike's Take The new Fuel lineup might be a little more complicated than it needs to be, but if you sort through the geometry charts and possible builds there are some real gems to be found. The Fuel LX EA90 is no lightweight, but I’ve been consistently impressed by its downhill performance - at its core this is a no-frills freeride / enduro bike that won’t hold anyone back. — Mike Kazimer Photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos Reviews Enduro Bikes Trek Trek Fuel Lx Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: 3 of the Latest Burly Tailgate Pads from Dakine, Fox, & Send It
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, bikeracks, dakine, foxracing, send-it
article_text: Indisputably, the most convenient way to load your bike in a pickup truck is by resting it on the tailgate using a specifically crafted pad, although I’ve seen everything from folded cardboard bike boxes, blankets, and floor mats used as improvisations. Tailgate pads are enticing due to their short loading time, plus they cost less and allow for a better departure angle than hitch-mounted racks. Of course, they reduce the truck bed’s cargo capacity, but tradeoffs exist with every type of bike carrier. As these seemingly rudimentary bike cushions have improved, truck bodies have evolved in shape too. They now feature rounded-edge profiles, back-up cameras - even built-in ladders have been introduced. Heavier, bulkier e-bikes add stress to their own frames and the tailgate edges too. No doubt, all of those factors have caused frustrations for tailgate pad designers. Here are three of the latest and greatest tailgate pads from Dakine, Fox / Race Face and Send-It, which incorporate deluxe materials and shapeable functions to solve such headaches. Dakine Universal Pickup Pad Tropical patterns and colorful stripes have been dropped in favor of just one matte black finish with a straightforward grey logo.
