DETAILS As the name suggests, Dakine’s Universal Pickup Pad has been designed with those shapely tailgate profiles in mind, as well as maintaining a sleek fit for straight profiles too. The main panels are connected by velcro strips to alter the contour of the tailgate edge and adjustable straps cinch tight when the preferred shape is met. Behind the exterior panel lies a fleecy material to avoid rubbing the truck’s paint job. Universal Pickup Pad Highlights • Bike capacity: 5 mid-size, 7 full-size • Metal security eyelet • Velcro inside pocket • Flip-up backup camera window • Drop down hooks connect main straps externally • Price: $295 USD / $425 CAD • dakine.com Like most of the tailgate pads out on the market, there are two sizes; one for mid-size trucks that holds 5 bikes, and one for full-size trucks to carry 7. In terms of coverage, the mid-size measures 140cm wide and 40cm (55.1 x 15.7”) for the mid-size pad and 155 x 40cm (61 x 15.7”) for the full-size. PRICE Kick me for saying it, but inflation seems to have hit the truck tailgate market too. $300 ain’t cheap. $295 USD / $425 CAD will get you either a mid or full-size Pickup Pad. A two-year limited warranty applies to Dakine’s tailgate pads. Granted, the performance has been increased with features like the rubberized cradles, a velcro pocket, security eyelet, and metal hooks on the straps are welcomed, but all of that is reflected in the higher price tag. Pads and panels aplenty. Grippy rubber bumpers inside and out. Long velcro downtube straps with plenty of mounting points. FIT/INSTALL Dakine reversed the orientation of the strap system securing the pad to the truck for a speedy lace up - just throw the pad over the tailgate opened at a 45-degree angle and let the dead end of the straps fall through the gap between the bed and tailgate. Next, close the tailgate, position the pad accordingly and feed the metal hooks on the strap ends through the eye on the bottom, exterior edge of the pad. This means that you don’t get covered in dirt during installation either. A slick, spring loaded strap cinch buckle takes up the excess length and an elastic loop cleans up the folded, excess strap length. Strapped up to a 3rd generation Tacoma, the Dakine Universal Pickup Pad falls a bit short on coverage from left to right. It took a bit of wrestling for it to sit flat since the Tacoma’s tailgate pad has just a small curved lip on the upper edge of the tailgate. Adjustments can be made to straighten out the wrinkles and increase the camera window coverage by repositioning the velcro connection points but this will likely take a few tries. Similarly, the camera window has a short overlap too, so if you find that a bike’s fork or tire lands in the area, be sure the flap is positioned accurately and strapped securely. FUNCTIONALITY Looking back to the upgrades Dakine implemented, the resting area for the downtube is well protected with a thick rubber bumper for E-bikes. Lengthy downtube straps are held in place by velcro and can be placed at various degrees to line up with the contours of any frame shape. Similarly, the outside to edge features the same bumper for the fork area, although you’ll want to pay attention to its position; sitting on the stanchion is not ideal. Through the review period, I found that the rubber surfaces gripped better and caused less chafing than the other pads. That could be because the rubber is tacky yet smooth, helping to shed sand and grit best.Those rubber bumpers inside the bed are specifically placed to cradle the downtube which does limit how confidently you can squeeze an extra bike into the truck, although that’s not recommended. One minor inconvenience about the Universal Pickup Pad is that the downtube straps must be fed through a tight fabric loop. The Race Face T3 pad uses plastic loopholes which expedites the loading process. There is one metal loophole for locking it down and a velcro pocket for tools, beers, or where 99% of people will stash their keys while riding. Spring-loaded cinch straps and elastic keepers for the dead ends. More overlap on the backup window would be appreciated. Mind the chafing on the paint. Pros + Full-size pad holds 7 bikes securely + Rubber fork and downtube bumpers don’t chafe surfaces as easily as fabrics + Easy install: main straps connect via hooks on the outside Cons - Coverage width-wise is on the narrow side for popular mid-size trucks - Downtube straps take time to feed through fabric loops versus plastic eyes - Expensive Send It High Roller Tailgate Pad Big blocks cover the entire tailgate. Sender approved. DETAILS Like the rest of the bunch, the High Roller comes in just black and holds 5 or 6 bikes depending on truck size. Following the others’ lead, that choice won’t cost you more either. The branding isn’t subtle but the High Roller does its intended job well, protect your trucking and hold your bikes in place. Hopefully you can take constant verbal abuse because you can count on people badgering you with the phrase, “Did you send it bro?” High Roller Tailgate Pad Highlights • Bike capacity: 5 mid-size, 6 full-size • Expanding downtube pad for universal fit • Sliding backup camera window cover • Metal security eyelet • Zippered inside pocket • Drop down hooks connect main straps externally • Price: $225 USD / $313 CAD • senditco.com Built into the top edge are accordion-like rows of expanding padding to contour to any tailgate shape. The 45-degree panel creases on the outside add to the universal fit and take up slack without extra straps to cinch it tight. The front side is that same heavy duty rubberized-nylon while the flip side is covered entirely in the commonly-used fleece. You’ll also find 5 or 6 velcro straps on the top edge. Four straps loop from the inside of the bed out and connect via metal hooks on the exterior of the tailgate. That may not seem like a huge deal, compared to the reverse fashion of feeding them up through the gap in the bed, but for those that remove these bike-carrier systems frequently, you’ll appreciate the reduced frustration of this attachment method. The mid-size measures 144cm wide and 53cm tall (56x21”) (exterior vertical panel) while the full-size is 160x60cm (63x23.5”). PRICE The High Roller comes in at $225 USD / $313 CAD, making it a little cheaper than the two other long-time players' goods. Send It also backs up their product with a lifetime warranty and 100% customer satisfaction guarantee. Plush pads everywhere. The expanding accordion-style top edge padding. FIT/INSTALL The High Roller fit the Tacoma tailgate perfectly, running wider than both the Fox /Race Face and Dakine. Each pad and panel is thick and tough - there are virtually no thin spots for your bike to bash into the tailgate. There’s minimal messing around involved when installing the High Roller pad, no matter what profile your tailgate has. Those accordion-like pads stretch out where needed and negate getting tangled up with any velcro strips or extra straps. Like Dakine’s offering, Send It’s tailgate pad attaches with those handy hooked straps. Just drop them through the gap between the bed, hook the strap loop and tighten. FUNCTIONALITY Next to the funky, expanding ribs, the High Rollers stand-out feature is the back-up camera window which slides up and down. It’s a novel concept but I found the panel didn’t drop low enough to get full view out of the camera. Rotating the pad down the tailgate further didn’t help as it wanted to curl under the truck’s panel, behind the bumper. More coverage is better for a universal product versus not enough, though. Durability, serious padding, and universal fit are the key takeaways with the High Roller. A slight miss is the narrow, rectangular downtube strap eyelets. Larger or rounder slots would be less tedious when strapping the bikes down. The sliding window is trick but it didn't slide down far enough for a fullscreen view with the backup camera. The metal hooks drop into place from the inside of the bed and attach quickly - no jammed fingers or floppy ends here. Narrow plastic eyelets take some fidgeting to thread the velcro ends through. Lock'er down. It's got it in its pocketses. Pros + Accordion-style top edge covers most truck shapes and frame angles + Substantial padding throughout + Easy install: main straps connect via hooks on the outside Cons - Camera window didn’t slide down far enough for full view - Narrow plastic eyelets for downtube straps to feed through - Loud graphics Fox Mission / Race Face T3 Tailgate Pad I bet they had some extra forks lying around to design those cradles with. DETAILS Adaptability and custom fit is what the new Mission pad from Fox (or T3 in the case of Race Face) is all about. Three separate PVC tarpaulin panels feature fresh materials and are laced together with four main straps. The width on the mid-size comes in at 134cm and the height has a wide range of adjustment. All of that seems a bit confusing when unboxing the Mission / T3, but thankfully, Fox / Race Face have put together an installation video to ease that process. The end result is a pad that becomes tailor-fitted to any tailgate profile. Mission / T3 Tailgate Pad Highlights • Bike capacity: 5 mid-size, 6 full-size • Highly adjustable fit • Hinged downtube pad for tonneau cover fitment • Cushioned fork cradles • Metal security eyelet • Price: $259 USD / $269 CAD • ridefox.com Highlighting the security side of the redesigned tailgate pad is the monstrous downtube pad that hinges out of the way to completely close a tonneau cover, and the soft, yet durable fork bumper grooves. Choices in color are limited to black, but both brand names are available in either truck size. The mid-size carries 5 bikes and the full-size officially fits 6. PRICE Both brands sell the Full and Mid-Size for $259 USD / $269 CAD, which includes the comical strap installation tool. You can buy those separately for $4 to lace up previous models. For those who might need space for vehicle accessories or camp gear, there’s a 2-bike option for $130 USD / $145 CAD as well. Is your tailgate bowed from hauling around a snowmobile? The Mission is probably adjustable enough to still fit elegantly. This sliver of orange plastic pulls the main straps through the gap in the bed. Check out the video below to watch the installation process. FIT/INSTALL The Mission / T3 does tighten down well without bulking up but it’ll take some trial and error to perfect that. Between all of the straps, velcro panels, and installation tools, there’s a lot to manage. You’ll want to give yourself time to dial in the fit and coverage. The camera window is quite large, which is why three extra pieces are provided to close the gap. Velcro strips match up behind the main panels, all of which are backed with micro-brushed fleece lining. Oddly enough, like the Dakine Universal Pickup Pad, the Fox Mission / Race Face T3 is on the short side when fitted to a mid-size Toyota Tacoma. This is where taking time to stack the bikes in and properly place the downtube straps is necessary. Depending on which direction you turn the front wheel of the bike will dictate how bikes are spaced, so it’s worth checking their positions with a full rack of bikes before thinking you’re ready to rock. FUNCTIONALITY There’s no arguing the Mission / T3 is a tad narrow, but it does hold the bikes securely and gently. When positioned perfectly, the generous downtube straps have no trouble holding even the most chonky frames solidly in place. Adding to its stability, the fork cradles do a swell job to keep the bikes from twisting, but I’d still like that piece of mind knowing the entire tailgate panel is covered. Another point in the coverage department is the camera window; the personalized panel placement does the job, but compared to a flip up or sliding door, they’re not the most convincing. They did stay in place during the review and I never lost any pieces, however, larger sections of velcro holding them in place would be more assuring. Finally, it may have come to a trade off to design an adaptable fit or reduce the price, but the Mission / T3 pad still uses the annoying bottom-up lacing method - aluminum buckles are a nice touch since they’ll outlast plastic bits. If you only install this once a season, it’s not a huge ordeal, but seeing how other brands are catching on, I would have guessed Fox / Race Face would have followed suit. The downtube straps have plenty of slots to arrange them across the generously thick and dispersed padding. Tonneau cover friendly. Eye spy. The Mission includes three of these panels. Pros + Downtube pad folds into bed for a flush fit with tonneau covers + Well padded in the critical areas (downtube and fork contact points) + Fork cradles help to keep bikes from twisting Cons - On the narrow size for popular vehicles - Lots of straps for universal fit mean tedious setup - Main straps feed up through the bed/tailgate gap Overview As one of the originators of the tailgate pad carrying system, Dakine’s latest version is burly and installs quickly - just keep an eye on the coverage around the camera window and outside edges. Plus, for full-size owners, they can confidently squeeze in a 7th bike, whereas the others state 6 is the max. Fox / Race Face’s offering is slightly more complicated to install but that’s made up for in its long list of features and thick, yet retractable protection. Newcomer, Send It, slides in with a solid contender in a busy market. Simple installation backed by ample padding gets the job done swiftly, barring the slightly narrow downtube strap rings. All of these tailgate pads do an admirable job of transporting your bike gently and securely on your truck. Each one has their own unique benefits but also small niggles which may not fit your needs, or truck. None are inexpensive, but then again, what’s the price of protecting your prized possessions? They’re more dialed in and less smelly than an old piece of carpet too. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Bikeracks Dakine Fox Factory Send It Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Reviewed: 6 of the Best Lightweight Elbow Pads for 2024
author: Henry Quinney
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, ridden-and-rated, apparel, fox-clothing, gform, ion, leatt, poc, troy-lee-designs
article_text: Ridden & Rated 6 Light MTB Elbow Pads By Henry Quinney I'm Fed Up With Crusty Elbows If you've gone mountain biking recently, you might have noticed riding past a litany of things you don't want to drag your skin along or smash your body into. For years I've just ridden in a t-shirt, knee pads and then changed between an open or full face helmet depending on whether I was riding something uplifted or not. Even then, more often than not unless I was on a downhill bike I wouldn't bother with a full face helmet. I don't know whether it's from getting older but I've made a conscious effort to wear more body protection and pads in recent years. It started with getting some larger knee pads that weren't a thin slice of foam in a sleeve, and then I started trying to wear thicker gloves, downhill-rated helmets, chest-protection and long sleeves. There was one final level that I never quite got to, though - elbow pads. My experience with elbow pads when I tried them for the first and only time was that they were massive, looked awful, ran hot and either slipped down or caused arm pump. In the decade since though, surely they must have changed? When conducting this review, I looked for the lightest and most comfortable pads a particular brand offers. Although bruising and breakages shouldn't be dismissed, it's the constant loss of skin during bike park season that irks me. I tested these pads during the hot months and into fall to find out whether there are finally options for something discrete, comfortable, and secure. For reference, my forearms have a circumference of 28cm at their middle, and my biceps measure 36cm. Contents • Fox Enduro Elbow • G-Form Pro-X3 Elbow • ION E-Sleeve AMP Elbow • POC VPD Air Flow Elbow • Leatt ReaFlex Elbow • Troy Lee Speed Elbow • Top Picks Fox D3O Enduro Elbow The Fox D3O Enduro features a removable pad for easy washing, a wide silicone gripper on the upper and a slightly looser fitting forearm piece. The size medium on test had a suggested size of 27.9-30.5 cm at the bicep and 26.7-27.9 cm at the forearm, so they should have provided a snug fit. They're some of the bigger pads in the test, and are probably alongside the Leatt as the bulkiest, even if the Fox does just edge it out as the slightly bigger. However, they were also very comfortable, and offered decent moisture wicking. Fox D3O Enduro • D3O padding • Silicone grippers • Weight: 160 grams (set M) • MSRP: $74.95 • foxracing.com While they are a nice pad, I would say that Enduro doesn't really fit their application. Although the moisture is wicked well, the pads cover a lot of real estate. They felt secure around my bicep but would fall down on long-sweaty runs in the bike park. Not only did they look the worst through a jersey thanks to their size, but I also didn't like how much they moved, and didn't appreciate the constant hoisting up required to keep them where you want them. Overall, these are a pad of seemingly good quality and might offer more protection than some of the lighter thinner ones thanks to their D3O padding. I would, however, definitely try before you buy and consider sizing down. Pros + Great coverage + Flexible pad and stretchy material made removal very easy Cons - Larger pad can be warm - The least secure pad in terms of fit G-Form Pro X3 Elbow G-Form makes pads as light as they are distinctive. The exoskeleton look is a little strange, and all the labels of NFL this or that, aren't going to help. Let us be honest, no elbow pads look particularly good but I think the G-Forms fly even closer to the sun than most. On the flipside, these Pro X3 pads can be nearly hidden under a long-sleeved jersey. G-Form Pro-X3 Elbow • Smartflex pad hardens on impact • Silicone grippers • Weight: 120 grams • MSRP: $59.99 • g-form.com Their pads are typically light, with an emphasis on flexibility and comfort. While this might not be what I want for my knee protection, for elbow pads it all begins to add up. The light, shape-hugging fit means that not only was this the most flexible pad on test but also the best fitting. The medium pads have a suggested size of 32-34.5 cm and 21.5-24cm at the bicep and forearm respectively and fit me very well. The longer grippers help with comfort around the bicep and stop the pads from feeling like they're cutting into my arms. The way the sleeve holds your arm also means that it doesn't need to be particularly tight in any one place; instead, there's a reasonable tension all over. The forearm uses a dotted-silcone surface to provide grip without being restrictive. The pads were secure, comfortable, and breathe well. The pad grippers are very effective, meaning they can rotate the pad over time if they're out of joint, but they were also probably the best at hugging the shape of your arm on the test while also being easy to get on and off. Pros + Very comfortable + Secure and stayed in place + Light and breathable + Very discreet Cons - Looks are divisive, should you care ION E-Sleeve AMP Elbow The Ion pads use inserts to make washing easy, and a mesh material throughout. The size chart suggests 28-30cm and 21-23cm at the bicep and forearm respectively. I tested the medium size and although they were comfortable they left some things to be desired. ION E-Sleeve AMP Elbow • 3-directional pad • Weight: 182 grams • No sew abrasion • MSRP: $85USD • ion-products.com These are good pads, and the way they fit is pretty good. They did shuffle around a little but largely stayed in place. ION's problem is not getting the pad to stay on though, largely it's getting it off again. The material of the sleeve is just too inflexible. You find yourself rolling, and sliding it as you try and get a purchase. Throw in some of the creak-tick of threads snapping and it becomes a frustrating experience. Every time I removed them I thought I would tear them. I was above the size chart recommendation, which shouldn't be ignored, but I also wouldn't have wanted these pads looser while riding. They were comfortable, and far more comfortable than you might think from the somewhat stern material, but I think there are better options. While not quite bulky, they're certainly on the verge of it. They do hide under a jersey well and offer good coverage. Pros + Stay put + Comfortable + Good coverage Cons - Very hard to get off POC VPD Air Flow Elbow The POC VPD M had a size suggesting a bicep of 28-32cm and a forearm of 26-30cm. While these pads felt like a high-quality item with some nice features, getting them to stay up was an issue. They were probably the least secure on test. That's also despite the way the pad cups your elbow, which is both comfortable and supportive. I don't think that the sleeve is too big so much as the grippers seemed somewhat ineffective. Around the bicep, it just didn't feel as if the silicone grippers got any real purchase. POC VPD Air Flow Elbow • Certified to EN1621-1, Level 1 • Concave pad w/ silicone bicep grippers • Weight: 215 grams • MSRP: $130 USD • pocsports.com The pads might offer slightly more protection than some and are one of the few to have certification reported in their copy, and you would hope so too for nearly double the weight of something like the G-Form. The pads are very very comfortable, and assuming they stayed up would be the ones I'd happily crash in, but getting them to do so was just too difficult. They're not the most discrete either thanks to the pad extending beyond the elbow quite a bit. That said, they're on par with the Fox pads. I think that the POC is a nice pad, but if it's going to be bigger and heavier I want it at least to stay up consistently. Thanks to the lack of gripper on the forearm the whole sleeve fits quite tight, and I definitely wouldn't want it any tighter. That said, it makes it a real shame that they wouldn't stay in place on long runs. Pros + Very comfortable + Certified Cons - Would fall down - Heavier than others Leatt ReaFlex Elbow The ReaFlex guards only give a sizing suggestion around the bicep, and it's quite wide too at 24-28cm. The pads feature the same style of padding as the G-Form, however, it's more of a hybrid between the more classic style where the is one large pad compared to the G-Form which seems to be a lots of pads connected together via gel-sections. The pad itself is very concave and hugs the elbow. There is a cutout in the forearm to help both air and blood flow and overall they're a thin material, even if they feel less premium than some other pads due to the absence of a soft-stretchy sleeve. Leatt ReaFlex Elbow • EN1621-1 certified • Upper gripper • Weight: 180 grams • MSRP: $89 • leatt.com The pads are very good though, and they stayed up, in place and comfortable over long runs. I like the concave pads as they feel like they're going to stay in place better and rotate less on impact. That's helped further by a textured gripper where your elbow will sit. When riding, they felt like around the joint they fit a bit looser and subsequently ran cooler. All in all, they're a good pad but sadly suffer from the same bad looks as the G-Form, but unlike the G-Form they do without the ability to hide under a jersey seamlessly, should you care. Pros + Comfortable and cool running + Certified + Stayed put Cons - Slightly bulky - Lower opening is large and could be tapered more Troy Lee Designs Speed Elbow The Troy Lee pads have a wafer-thin 4mm pad, and looking at them they were very much in line with what I was looking for. Minimalist and discreet, yet with enough coverage to stop the loss of skin. The pads feature a gripper around the bicep and rely on a good deal of stretch to hold the pad securely. The medium pads suggest a lower forearm of 6.5-8 (16.5 - 20 cm) inches and a bicep of 10-12 (25.5 - 30.5 cm). They also give weight recommendations of 63-82 kg. I didn't factor the weight factor in too much, as surely there is just so much more information needed but I suppose it might help somebody. TLD Speed Elbow Guards • D3O pads • CE EN 13688:2013 Certified • Weight: 136 grams • MSRP: $49USD • troyleedesigns.com The pads are the most discreet on test, and also look subtle in short sleeves - they don't look overly different from a base layer. Sadly, I just couldn't get them to stay up. I ended up riding an XS/S pad and, while my arms are by no means big I was disappointed that even that success was hit-and-miss. It wasn't drastic, but over several runs, they would move down. They felt good in terms of fit, but just wouldn't stay where I wanted them. Pros + Very thin + Discreet + Probably the best looking pad Cons - Fell down occasionally - Sizing is off Top Pick My goal in this test was to find something so comfortable and minimalist that I didn't see any reason not to wear them. With that in mind, the G-Form Pro X3 Elbow is the clear winner. Comfortable, secure, and breathable all while being the most discreet. When working at the World Cups, I'd often see riders wearing these hidden away under their sleeves, despite what the sponsor read on the outside of their jersey, and that's not without good reason. I really liked the pads, and they're the only ones I feel really deliver in every regard. If this test has shown me one thing is that elbow pads still aren't quite there in terms of fit, and just getting them to stay up was more often than not a difficulty. In fairness, it was on long hot, and sweaty runs of the bikepark. However, that's not to say I wouldn't expect more when spending what can often be a decent chunk of change on protective equipment. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Ridden And Rated Apparel Fox Clothing G-Form Ion LEATT POC Troy Lee Designs Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: 6 Clipless Trail / DH Mountain Bike Shoes Ridden & Rated
author: Seb Stott
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, shoes, crankbrothers, endura, fizik, fox-racing, northwave, shimano
product_name: 6 Clipless Trail / DH Mountain Bike Shoes Ridden & Rated
article_text: Clipless enduro shoes group test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/qHCKNMAa Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 16:04 Picking the right pair of riding shoes for your needs can make a big difference to your riding. Shoes must find a balance of stiffness, weight, durability, protection, comfort and traction - both on and off the bike. Each shoe will have different biases and each rider will have different priorities. With so many options on the market, we set out to find which shoes offer the best all-round performance. We're looking at shoes aimed at anything from trail to enduro and gravity riding here, so don't expect any carbon soles or toe studs. These shoes would typically be used with 140 mm travel bikes and up. They should all work for everything from enduro and downhill racing to casual trail riding and the odd bit of hike-a-bike. I've been testing six pairs of clipless shoes over the last year to help you pick out the best options. If flat pedals are more your thing, Jessie wrote a roundup of those recently. Tester: Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Height: 6'3" / 191cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted Shoe Size: UK 11 / EU 46 Preferred riding style: Trail, enduro & eMTB Preferred pedal: Crankbrothers Mallet E or Mallet DH What to look for Shoe stiffness is a major talking point and a big differentiator between XC-style shoes and the more gravity-biased ones we have here. But a recent study suggests a stiffer shoe doesn't necessarily help with power delivery or pedalling efficiency after all. Stiffer shoes can feel more comfortable on long descents, especially when used with a smaller pedal platform, whereas more flexible soles may make it easier to locate the pedal mechanism when clipping in and make life easier when walking. In general, I'd suggest a stiffer sole if you favour smaller platform pedals or a more flexible one for broader pedals. If you plan to carry or push your bike over uneven ground, a flexible sole is your friend. For gravity-focussed riders, the position of the cleat is important, with more room to adjust cleat position fore and aft and laterally being a bonus. A recent poll revealed that a significant fraction of Pinbkbike readers are setting their cleats as far back as they'll go for trail and enduro riding. Fortunately, the current crop of gravity shoes all make it possible to adjust the cleats much farther back than many shoes did a few years ago. Most people should be able to put their cleats where they want them with all of these shoes. The closure mechanism is an important consideration too. You want something that holds the foot securely while remaining comfortable and adjustable. Boa dials, Velcro straps and good-old-fashioned laces all have pros and cons, with the former generally being the most expensive and most fragile, while laces are cheap and replaceable but aren't as good for fine-tuning the fit. The outsole (tread) should offer plenty of grip when walking over soft and hard ground, or even when riding unclipped over rough terrain. But the tread mustn't get in the way when it comes time to clip back in. Fit is another consideration. I have relatively normal-proportioned feet and I didn't have any issues with comfort or shape with any of these shoes. If you have unusually wide or narrow feet there's no substitute for trying before buying. How the shoes were tested To keep things simple and fair, I tested all six shoes with the same pedals - Crankbrothers Mallet DH. I have used several of these shoes with Shimano pedals too, and generally, what works with one will work with another. Even a Shimano shoe can be used with a Crankbrothers pedal and visa-versa. It's not like Apple and Android. I've had several of these shoes on test for over a year, with plenty of regular ride time. I've also tested them head-to-head on the same day to make the differences more apparent, even riding with different shoes on each foot for the most direct comparison possible. I also measured what I'm calling the cleat channel width (left) and cleat slot length (right) to put some objective numbers on the adjustment range of the cleat position. You can also get a sense of this by looking at the photo above underneath the video, which shows the cleats in my preferred position. If you buy through our links, we may earn an affiliate commission. We do not accept money for editorial gear reviews. Read more about our policy. Because you're all busy people, I'll order these reviews in ascending order of preference and summarise my findings at the end. Review Contents • Fizik Gravita Versor • Northwave Enduro Mid 2 • Fox Union Boa • Endura MT500 Burner • Crank Brothers Mallet Trail Boa • Shimano GE9 Fizik Gravita Versor BUY AT: MIKE'S BIKES (USA) The Gravita is designed to cater to everything from trail riding to downhill, but the shoes feel more casual and less secure than the rest. The cut is low and the upper material is thin, making the ankle and upper foot more exposed to rock strikes and debris. The ripstop fabric feels hardwearing despite these being the lightest shoes on test, but there's noticeably less padding around the upper foot. Fizik Gravita Versor Details • Closure type: laces • Weight: 1005 grams (EU 46) • Sizes: 36-48 EU • Cleat channel width: 41 mm • Cleat slot length: 32 mm • MSRP: $149.99 USD • More info at fizik.com The laces do a decent job of securing the foot for sprinting on the bike, although there is some heel lift when walking up steep slopes and the foot feels less locked-down than other shoes. There is a version with an extra Velcro strap (the Gravita Tensor), which should resolve this issue. The cleat slot isn't the longest but it has just enough adjustment to get the cleats where I wanted them. The sole's low-profile tread and tapered cleat channel make it easy to clip in and out, and the shoes grip the pedals nicely when not yet clipped in. Walking on hard surfaces is very comfortable, although the tread is not well suited to mud. The sole is on the flexier side, which makes it easier to feel what's going on under your feet, but adds to the casual feel. Pros + Lightweight + Decent grip on pedals and hard ground + Flexible sole is good for walking and clipping in Cons - Low cut and thin upper leave feet exposed - Laces allow for some heel lift Northwave Enduro Mid 2 BUY AT: BACKCOUNTRY (USA) In contrast to the Fizik, these shoes feel robust and secure, with exceptional ankle protection and a padded upper. This lends them to people who ride in loose, rocky terrain. By the same token, these are the heaviest shoes on test - over 200 g heavier than the lightest. That's not especially noticeable on the bike but it can be when walking, and it represents a weight difference that would be considered substantial when comparing components. Enduro Mid 2Details • Closure type: SLW X-Dial and Velcro strap • Weight: 1213 grams (EU 46) • Sizes: 38-48 • Cleat channel width: 40 mm • Cleat slot length: 37 mm • MSRP: $197.99 USD • More info at northwave.com The Northwave SLW X-Dial works similarly to the more common Boa system; it holds the foot securely alongside the Velcro strap and allows quick and easy adjustments. It's not covered by a no-questions-asked warranty like the Boa system, but at £11 for a pair of dials, it's not expensive to replace if needed. There's minimal heel lift and the tread grips well in the mud. Unfortunately, the rubber compound is slightly slippery on hard, wet surfaces like roots and rocks. The sole is stiff by the standards of this test and quite thick too; this creates a numb feeling when walking on uneven ground or when locating the pedal to clip back in. The cleat channel allows the cleats to be set very far back in the shoe, but the rubber sole gets in the way of the pedal when the cleats are set all the way back in the slot, making it harder to clip in. Nevertheless, I was still able to get the cleats in the position I wanted them (as pictured) with no issues getting clipped in. There is a slightly concave section under the ball of the foot and a slightly raised arch in the middle, which feels strange at first but I found the fit very comfortable. Pros + Secure closure system + Robust and protective upper + Tread grips well in soft ground Cons - Hard compound and stiff sole aren't ideal on slippery hard surfaces - Heavy Fox Union Boa BUY AT: COMPETITIVE CYCLIST (USA) These shoes are the most race-focussed here. They have a stiff sole and firm, unyielding upper; with dual Boa dials allowing for a particularly snug fit. The cleat channel is wide and long, making it possible to get the cleats very far back without any issues getting clipped in. It's hard to measure this precisely, but I'd say the cleats can be set further back on these than any other shoe on test - much further back than I would want them. They have a tough toe box and some extra protection around the side of the shoe, although the inner ankle bone is more exposed than with some other shoes. Fox Union Boa Details • Closure type: Dual Boa • Weight: 1148 grams (EU 46) • Sizes: 37-47 • Cleat channel width: 42 mm • Cleat slot length: 36 mm • MSRP: $249.95 USD • More info at foxracing.com They're heavy and stiff and the tread isn't optimised for walking, so there are better options if you plan to mix your DH racing with more casual riding, especially if a hike-a-bike is on the cards. While Boa dials are replaceable and covered by a generous guarantee, they are prone to damage in my experience and having four dials per pair increases the chances of damage. Moreover, my pair developed a tear in the sewn-in lace guide on the opposite side of the shoe from the ratchet. The mechanism still works, but this part isn't replaceable if it wears through altogether. The lace-up version (£160), which has a thick Velcro strap for a secure fit, is likely a better bet for durability and value. Pros + Super secure fit + Lots of protection in the toe and side Cons - Dual Boa closure may invite repairs and mine are starting to wear through - Stiff sole is not ideal or walking - On the heavy side Endura MT500 Burner BUY AT: BACKCOUNTRY (USA) These shoes come with two sets of laces. This could be used to fine-tune the fit in the front and rear parts of the shoe separately, but the second set is really a spare or an alternate option to choose between a muted or funky colourway. They can be tucked away under the Velcro strap, which also helps to secure the fit after the laces are tied. This is a practical and reliable system which holds the foot securely, although it takes a bit longer to get the fit just right and stash the laces neatly when compared to Boa dials. Endura MT500 Burner Details • Closure type: Dual laces and Velcro • Weight: 1092 grams (EU 46) • Sizes: 38-47 • Cleat channel width: 47 mm • Cleat slot length: 40 mm • MSRP: $159.99 USD • More info at endurasport.com The fit is snug, with no heel lift and a locked-in feel once you've got the laces sorted. There's generous padding to keep your ankle bone protected, yet they remain impressively mid-pack weight-wise. The cleat channel is especially roomy, making it easy to position the cleat where you want it. In fact, there's more room to adjust cleat position than any other shoe here, although like with the Northwave, the tread gets in the way with the cleat set as far back as it goes. There's still more than enough space to get the cleat in a very rearward position where it's easy to clip in and out. The tread offers excellent grip on all surfaces and the sole is a nice balance of stiffness and flex, making it easier to locate the pedal underfoot. The ergonomic EGM insole feels odd at first. The raised metatarsal button in the middle of the shoe feels a bit like there's a lollipop stick running laterally across the middle of the shoe. Endura have big claims for these insoles but I found that while I got used to it after a while and found the shoes comfortable, I didn't notice any particular benefit. You could always swap them for an ordinary insole if they bother you. Pros + Secure foot-hold once laces are sorted + Good ankle protection and walking grip + Flexible sole and big cleat slot make it easy to clip in + Well-priced (even for a lace-up shoe) Cons - Ergonomic insole feels strange at first - Laces take longer to adjust than Boa dials Crankbrothers Mallet Trail Boa BUY AT: EVO (USA) While Crankbrothers' latest clipless shoe is nominally aimed at trail riders, it is in many ways an improvement over the original Mallet DH and Enduro shoes. They are slightly lighter, though there's only 68 g in it on my scales, and they are noticeably stiffer, but this doesn't seem to detract from walking comfort or on-pedal feel. Mallet Trail Boa Details • Closure type: Boa and Velcro strap • Weight: 1018 grams (EU 46) • Sizes: 37-49 • Cleat channel width: 41 mm • Cleat slot length: 34 mm • MSRP: $219.99 USD • More info at crankbrothers.com The tread offers extra grip for walking at the toe and heel, but this doesn't affect the pedal area so clipping in is just as slick. The cleat channel is even wider and the cleat slots run far enough back that gravity riders should still be able to get the cleat where they want. The Trail shoe's upper has a slightly less padded feel, making it easier to get a firm hold, but there is still some ankle protection. The shoes feel comfortable and secure on or off the bike, but being fussy, the Velcro straps are a bit too long and stick out when tightened down firmly. They come with Crankbrothers cleats pre-installed, which speeds installation and makes the value proposition a little sweeter for Crank Brothers pedal fans. However, I still prefer to add an extra cleat spacer underneath for easier engagement, as I do with most of the other shoes. I can confirm they play nicely with Shimano Saint pedals, too, just as Shimano's shoes do with Crankbrothers pedals. These shoes are still expensive, and I broke the Boa dial by catching it on a protruding root. You can get a replacement for free under Boa's lifetime guarantee , but it'll take longer to fix than laces. A lace version is available but, at £170, it's still expensive and lacks a Velcro strap for a secure hold. Pros + Plenty of room to adjust cleat position and easy access with CB or Shimano cleats + Good walking grip and pedal feel + Secure hold + Lightweight Cons - A Boa dial broke on my shoes Shimano GE9 BUY AT: COMPETITIVE CYCLIST (USA) These are the kind of shoes you happily forget about while riding. They don't have any strange features or quirks; they just work. They have a relatively flexible sole and soft upper padding, but not so soft that they feel vague. They have a decent amount of protection but they're respectably light. Shimano GE9 Details • Closure type: Boa and Velcro • Weight: 1034 grams (EU 46) • Sizes: 38-48 • Cleat channel width: 41 mm • Cleat slot length: 27 mm • MSRP: $225 USD • More info at shimano.com The flexible sole and tread make them especially comfortable for walking, with good grip in all conditions and minimal heel lift. When pedalling and sprinting on the bike, I didn't notice any downsides when compared to the stiffer-soled shoes on test. The cleat slot is the shortest on test but it's well-placed. There's enough room to move the cleat back for gravity-focussed riding and I can't imagine many people will want to position it further forward than the slot allows. The extra-long channel running the length of the sole makes it easy to find the pedal mechanism - even with pedals from Shimano's rival pedal brand. The cut-out in the middle of the foot also offers somewhere for the "eggbeater" mechanism to go when your foot lands too far forward on the pedal, making it easier to push on during one of those "ride it out" moments. They are expensive and Boa dials are a potential point of failure (though I had no issues in this case), but the cheaper GE7 is available for those who prefer laces, and it retains the velcro strap for secure fit. Pros + Supremely comfortable yet secure-feeling + Grippy and compliant sole, whether on the pedal on the ground + Easy to locate and engage the pedal mechanism Cons - Small (but well-positioned) cleat adjustment window What did we learn? At the risk of sounding like an industry shill, all these shoes are pretty good. One of my first jobs as a bike journalist in 2015 was a shoe group test and I remember drilling out cleat slots and cutting the rubber around the cleat channel with a Stanley knife to get them to fit. These days, most shoes in the trail/gravity category work great out of the box - even if you like to set your cleats quite far back as I do. The only thing you may need to play with is cleat spacers and/or pedal pins to get the contact force between the pedal and shoe to your liking. That's not to say there's nothing separating them. A common theme is the choice of closure system. Boa-style ratchet dials are the quickest to take on and off or adjust tension, but two of the shoes in this test had issues with them. One required a replacement dial, which was free under Boa's lifetime warranty but isn't quick or easy to replace, while another suffered non-fatal but non-repairable damage to the lace guide opposite the dial. Laces may be the smart-money choice, ideally in combination with a Velcro strap to quickly cinch up the uppers on the fly. Some of the shoes on test have cheaper alternatives like this. The low-cut Fizik Gravita feels exposed around the ankle and upper foot. The Northwave Enduro Mid 2 is heavy and a little slippery underfoot on hard, wet surfaces. The Fox Union is great for DH duties but its stiff sole and lack of toe tread mean it lacks versatility, and mine developed some damage on the Boa lace guide too. The Endura MT500 Burner is quirky with its ergonomic insoles, but you can replace these if they don't suit you. If you can live with laces it's a great all-round shoe with good protection at a fair price. The Crankbrothers Mallet Trail Boa is light, stiff and secure on the foot, without losing any of the downhill capabilities of the Mallet DH shoe. But the Boa dial broke on my set. The lace-up version is still expensive and lacks a Velcro closure. That leaves the Shimano GE9. It's a similarly impressive all-rounder to the Mallet Trail but with a slightly softer upper and more compliant sole, which makes it great for walking with little or no compromise on the bike. The GE7 is probably the smart money choice as it swaps the Boa for laces but keeps the Velcro strap. The Shimano GE9 would be my top pick, but any of the top three are well worth considering. The Endura MT500 Burner would be my value choice, as it's both well-priced and should be reliable with its doubled-up laces instead of ratchet dials. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes CrankBrothers Endura Fizik Fox Racing Northwave Shimano Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: 6 Months with the New DT Swiss 240 DEG Hubs
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, hubs, dt-swiss, dt-swiss-deg, sea-otter-2024
article_text: DT Swiss rarely reinvents their product lineup, with the original design standing tried and true for a long while before they introduced the updated EXP series. While those hubs suffered some initial hiccups, they were quickly brought back to DT's reliability standard. Their newest product hopes to build upon that, with a burly construction and features geared towards increased serviceability. That new product comes in the form of the Ratchet DEG hub, the highest-engagement hub DT has ever designed. With 90-tooth ratchet rings, the DEG hubs achieve a scant 4° engagement angle, all while using a very similar design to the DT internals we're accustomed to. 240 Ratchet DEG Hub Details • 28 or 32 spoke count • Aluminum hub shell • 4° engagement • 6-bolt rotor mount • 90 tooth steel ratchets • Freehub: XD or Microspline • 15x110mm / 12x148mm spacing • Weight: 270g (rear), 140g (front) • Price: $499.90 USD / €376.90 (rear hub only) • www.dtswiss.com Engagement is not without compromise. The DEG hubs incur a 90 gram weight penalty when compared to the equivalent EXP hub. The weight comes from a few factors: two springs instead of one, larger diameter ratchet rings, and a larger hub shell to fit the increased ratchet size. The DEG system is coming exclusively in these 240 hubs for now, striking a middle ground in their overall lineup when it comes to cost, weight, and finishing touches. While the EXP family remains the lightest in the lineup, the DEG really is just focused on increased engagement above all else. There is one serviceability detail worth pointing out, and that's the ease of bearing removal when that time comes. Due to the ratchet size, you don't have to remove the threaded drive ring to punch out the driveside bearing. This means no special tool required, one less step, and an even more field-serviceable product. Teeny tiny teeth. Service is still as easy as can be. The construction is visually different from preexisting DT designs, but the principles are almost identical to the original Ratchet concept. That should be welcomed news to anyone concerned with durability, as the originals have a pretty bombproof reputation, regardless of discipline or rider level. E-bikes bring a more destructive level of power into the mix, though, and as DT didn't have explicit information regarding the DEG hubs use on eMTBs, I inquired and got the following answer: A good question! We do not specifically recommend the DEG hubs for e-bke but the use is not excluded. As e-bikes do have a additional freewheel system in the motor a high engagement in the rear is not really needed. At the other hand we know that high engagement comes usually together with higher service demands. At the moment we see most of the e-bike users searching for less service and therefore we invented the specifically reinforced DT Swiss Hybrid hubs (240 and 350) for their needs. The reinforced Hybrid hubs do probably make more sense for eMTB usage, and I personally only used the DEGs on meat powered bikes. For those who want something a little more exclusive, there's a limited release of candy red 240 DEGs accompanying the release. Only 240 sets of these will be in circulation, so you'll have to be quick if you're after the trailside clout. For those who want to stand out. The layman's offering was good enough for me. Ultimately the DEG hubs are a fairly simple update, in keeping with the DT Swiss approach to hub design. Reducing parts, complication, and potential failure points is welcomed to me, but anything new has to be put through the paces. Ride Impressions I've been riding these new DEG hubs laced to some EX511 rims since last October, so suffice to say they've seen their fair share of mileage. Most of that riding took place in the wet Bellingham winter, but they also accompanied me on a trip to the dusty and dry summer in Chile. A wide array of conditions, and more than enough time to get a feel for the hubs. In my book, hubs should be a bike part you absolutely never think of, except for the rare occasion when you lube the drive mechanism or replace the bearings. I prefer quieter hubs to the screamier options out there, and want something that will work season after season with little fuss. For those reasons, I've really enjoyed DT's original Ratchet hubs, and rarely wanted for much more. However, there are upsides to increased engagement, as the 36-tooth stock ratchet does leave some room in the drivetrain before things catch. In this sense, the DEG hub has been a massive improvement, with very tight engagement and the secure-feeling ratchet drive feel. Technical climbing, sprinting out of corners, and small power inputs on the pedals all feel snappier with less room between engagement points. The amount of drag feels decently low, though it is increased a bit over the lower engagement alternatives from DT. I'm not ultra-sensitive to the pedal feedback you feel between a low-engagement and high-engagement hub, but do notice some extra feedback on certain bikes as the engagement decreases. By no means have these 4° DEGs made bikes unrideable, but those of you who get up in arms about pedal kickback and high engagement hubs will probably want to stick with the good ol' Ratchets. As to durability, I haven't had a single issue over the past 6 months, with some wear beginning to show in the bearings but nothing extraneous. Winter in the Northwest is hell on bearings, so a refresh will probably be due by the beginning of the next slop season. The ratchets are good as new, the grease internally still looks fairly nice, and the fact that you can clean and regrease them in about two minutes makes the lifespan that much better. For those looking for a high-engagement hub in a very durable and time-tested package, I think the DEG is a worthy contender, and a welcomed addition to the DT Swiss lineup. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Hubs DT Swiss Dt Swiss Deg Sea Otter 2024 Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: 6 of the Latest & Greatest Downhill Full-Face Helmets
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Dh Helmets Master https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/xy0TfKd5 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 14:15 Whether you’re racing downhill, sending big features, or regularly riding a bike park, a downhill rated full-face is the best option for protecting your noggin. Lightweight enduro full-face helmets are a decent alternative if you are just occasionally riding one of these disciplines, but a downhill full-face offers the most protection from the higher speeds with increased padding, stronger materials and a more secure fit system. We’ve been bouncing downhill with six of the latest and greatest full-face helmets for the whole summer, putting them to test and seeing how they compare. Contents Fox RPC Bell Full 10 Spherical POC Coron Air MIPS Troy Lee Designs D4 Polyacrylite Abus HiDrop Sweet Protection Fixer 2Vi Pinkbike's Take Fox RPC Details The Fox RPC or Rampage Carbon Pro is a long-standing premier helmet in Fox's lineup. In fact, we reviewed this helmet and its current form three years ago. It’s unchanged except for the colors, yet it’s one of the most popular choices you’ll see in the lift line—and for good reason. The safety features are abundant. Our RPC helmet has a carbon fiber, shell, MIPS liner and dual density Varizorb EPS protective foam. There’s also an Ionic liner with quick remove cheek pads and a breakaway visor in case of an accident. A D-ring system at the chin strap locks you in. It comes in four sizes ranging from 55 to 62 cm in circumference, weighs 1250 g for the size large, and costs $499 USD. RPC Details • MIPS safety system • Carbon fiber shell • Breakaway visor design • Dual-density Varizorb EPS foam liner • X-Static liner • Quick-remove cheek pads • Sizes: S, M, L, XL (55-62 cm) • Colors: various • Weight: 1258 g (LG, actual) • Safety certifications: CPSC, EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063, ASTM 1952-15 downhill • Price: $499 USD • foxracing.com In terms of fit, the one caveat to that high volume shell is that you’ll likely want to size up with the RPC. Otherwise, there is a lot of cushion inside the helmet which can make the fit snug and secure. I never noticed any pressure points and compared to Fox's helmets, I find the fit to be rounder around the forehead. Compared to the other helmets on test, the RPC does feel more substantial on your head for better or worse. More material generally means more energy dissipation, but it also makes it on the heavier side. The ventilation is adequate, but wearing it all day for an enduro race might be challenging. All in all, it’s easy to see why the RPC is an attractive premium helmet. The price is on average with other high-end contenders - you’ll just want to make sure you’ve got the right fit for you. Pros + Very secure yet comfortable fit + Well constructed, with high-end features Cons - Traditional size is small - On the heavier side Bell Full 10 Spherical The Bell Full 9 seemed to have been around as long as the American company has been making helmets for. Its successor, the Full 10, looks drastically different and packs a lot into a very lightweight and vented helmet. The futuristic looking Full 10 seems to have all the bells and whistles, no pun intended, and it is better because it costs $650 USD. What makes it so expensive? Well for starters it has a carbon shell that makes it super light. The Bell Full 10 is the lightest helmet we have here today. It weighs 1080 g for a size medium. It comes in four sizes to fit head from 51 to 63 cm. There’s also a lot of technology packed into this extremely vented and angular helmet. It features the MIPS Spherical system, which is made up of two bowl-shaped layers of progressive EPS foam. This helps to dissipate energy from crashes of all sizes and angles. Full 10 Spherical Details • Carbon shell • MIPS Spherical safety system • Progressive layering EPS • Adjustable, Flying Bridge visor • Quick-release magnetic cheek pads • Thermal Exchange Airflow System • Camera mount • Sizes: XS/S, M, L, XL/XXL (51-63 cm) • Colors: various • Weight: 1080 g (MD, actual) • Safety certifications: NTA 8776 (e-bike), US CPSC, CE EN 1078:2012, ASTM F1952-15, DH ASTM F2032-15 BMX • Price: $650 USD • bellhelmets.com Bell has also designed what they call the Flying Bridge visor that uses a bolt on each side, but no center post. It’s a method that they say increases protection by allowing for material in that area. The visor itself also incorporates a breakaway function. The Full 10 is hands-down the most breathable helmet we have here. It looks like someone has taken a hacksaw to it. This promotes cool air in and exhausts hot air out the back - something Bell calls the Thermal Exchange Airflow System. On top of that the liner material is made from Ionic+ fabric which is soft but also top notch at wicking away sweat. There are also pull-away cheek pads and titanium D-rings for the chinstrap closure, plus a trick magnetic clasp for the end of the chinstrap. The fit of the Full 10 is also spot on. There is a huge field of vision, and like the Sweet Fixer, it reduces the chance of hitting it on your stem or bars in extreme scenarios. Don’t let the size or the shape of the Full 10 for you, this is a helmet that could be worn for high speeds, big stunts and all-day enduro racing. Yes, it is expensive, but it has some of the best helmet technology going right now and the workmanship to back it up. Pros + Best venting on test + Lightweight + High degree of safety features Cons - Highlights are reflected by the pricetag - Styling may not be for everyone POC Coron Air MIPS Being the safe Scandinavians they are, POC does things a little differently when it comes to helmets and protection. Round shapes and more elastic properties are part of their designs. The Coron Air MIPS cover heads from 51 to 62 cm, however, POC uses just three shells across that range. Additional cheek pads are included, which helps bridge those but compared to the Troy Lee Design D4, it’s not as tailor-fitted. It’s available in black, white or silver and costs $330 USD. Highlighting the safety features on the Coron are a MIPS evolve system, pull-away cheek pads and a multi-impact EPP liner. What exactly does that mean? Well, the formula of EPP that POC uses is softer and malleable compared to other helmets. For example, when you push into the foam with your fingernail, it has the tendency to absorb and rebound to its original shape. Coron Air MIPS Details • Fiberglass shell • MIPS safety system • Multi-impact EPP liner • Quick-release chin strap clasp • Pull-away cheek pads • Sizes: S, M, L (51-62 cm) • Colors: black, white, silver • Weight: 1165 g (MD, actual) • Safety certifications: CPSC Safety Standard, EN1078:2012+A1:2012, ASTM F1952, NTA 8776, • Price: $330 USD • poc.com The Coron is also very round and smooth which has been theorized to reduce impacts by glancing off of objects, but it doesn’t have as many vents as other competitors. POC has also integrated a breakaway visor and designed their own release, magnetic chin strap clasp. The interior of the helmet is comfy for wearing on longer rides for two reasons. First it has decent airflow due to the air pockets throughout the liner and secondly you can hear very clearly due to the sizeable voids around the ears - something that POC specifically focused on. One thing I did notice was that the depth of the helmet feels more shallow along the top and forehead areas in comparison to the other equally sized helmet here. Overall, the Coron Air was a surprise performer in terms of ventilation in overall comfort. It’s definitely geared towards the side of gravity side of riding. If you're focused exclusively on enduro, you might be better off with their lighter Otocon option. Pros + Quick-release chin strap is convenient + Unique safety features Cons - Three shell sizes means a less tailored fit - Breathability could be better Troy Lee Designs D4 Polyacrylite If you haven’t seen a Troy Lee Designs D4 helmet before, then you must be living under a rock. This is their most recent evolution of the D-series helmets. It comes with the same styling and safety features as the more expensive carbon version, but in a more affordable package. At 1150g (claimed), the D4 polyacrylite is only 150g heavier than the carbon version, but it costs $300 less. Inside, you’ll find the MIPS C2 rotational system and collarbone suspension system. The liner features antimicrobial padding with quick release cheek pads. For the strap closure, there’s the classic double D-ring loop for peace of mind Fit has always been one of the highlights of TLD helmets and the D4 comes in a wide range of sizes from XS to XXL. That aligns with head circumferences from 53-63 cm. With six sizes, you’re more likely to find a snug, yet comfy fit versus other brands that may only have three or four. D4 Polyacrylite Details • MIPS C2 safety system • Polyacrylite shell • Front and side air mesh vents • Breakaway visor screws • Collarbone Suspension System • Quick release cheek pads • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL, 2XL (53-63 cm) • Colors: Various • Weight: 1198 g (MD, actual) • Safety certifications: CPSC 1203, CE EN1078, ASTM F1952-DH certified • Price: $299.99 USD • troyleedesigns.com Behind the aggressive styling of the D4 are numerous vents along the top, side and rear of the helmet for excellent air flow. I’d put it in second place, just behind the Bell Full 10 by a slim margin. This is definitely a helmet that won’t boil your brain on long descents in the heat of the summer. The TLD D4 Polyacrylite may not have the pizzazz of the carbon version, however, you still receive the main features, fit and safety that make the D4 a crowd favorite. Pros + Widest range of sizes + Excellent venting Cons - Padding can occasionally become folded when pulling the helmet on - Longer jaw piece compared to competitors Abus HiDrop Abus is another new player in a full-face helmet game and the HiDrop is their most protective offering. At just $200 USD you’d think that they would have to cut some corners, but it still receives the ASTM rating for downhill racing. The HiDrop weighs just 1053 g and comes in four sizes. Those run from 55 centimeters up to 62 cm in circumference. The shell comes in for neutral colours and the styling is a mix between the POC Coron and Sweet Fixer. The outer shell is ABS and it is said to make the helmet impact resistant - as it should be - I think what they mean is the plastic is more resistant to cracking or denting on small impacts. Under the shell, there is EPS and EPP foam to handle the major forces. In addition to those features, the chinstrap uses a double D-ring, and the visor has a breakaway safety feature to reduce neck trauma. HiDrop Details • Breathable cheek pad inserts • Break-away visor • Double D-ring buckle system • ABS shell • EPS & EPP foam core • Sizes: S, M, L, XL (55-62 cm) • Colors: black, white, blue, green • Weight: 1053g (MD, actual) • Safety certifications: EN1078, ASTM F1952, ASTM F2032, CPSC, AS/NZ • Price: $200 USD • abus.com One thing absent from the HiDrop is a MIPS liner and that may deter some riders from the helmet. Opting around the technology does keep the price of the helmet down though. Compared to some of the more luxurious helmets on test, the liner does feel a little rougher. With that said, it still keeps the air flowing well through the vents. The goggle port is plenty wide and the shell doesn’t have any pressure points. I would add that it does fit slightly larger for a medium than most of the other helmets we have here. Other than that, Abus has hit the mark well with a breathable, comfortable helmet that doesn’t break the bank. Pros + Lightweight + Superb value Cons - Padding material is reflected in the price - Absence of MIPS liner may deter some riders Sweet Protection Fixer 2Vi Details Sweet Protection may not be the first name that comes to mind when you think of gravity-oriented full-faces. It’s been some time since they made one with a dedicated chin bar, but their new Fixer 2Vi looks to change that in all the right ways. There are two versions, but this is the carbon which comes in at an impressive 1104 g for a size medium. It comes in two colors, white or black and costs $600 USD. There are five sizes, each which use their own individual shell mold. That’s not something you’ll find most helmet manufacturers do for such a wide size range - they might resort to inserting thicker or thinner padding to squeeze two sizes out of one shell. Alongside that carbon shell is a two-layer MIPS system, which is where the “2Vi” name comes from. That liner uses EPS and EPP segments strategically placed to take any incoming impacts. Fixer 2Vi Carbon Details • 2-layer MIPS safety system • Carbon fiber pre-preg shell • Multi-density EPS impact shields • Adjustable (two-position) break-away visor • Emergency removal cheek pads • 11 penetration resistant vents • Titanium double D-ring buckle • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL (53-62 cm) • Colors: black/carbon, white/black • Weight: 1104 g (MD, actual) • Safety certifications: EN1078:2012m, A1:2012, CPSC 1203, ASTMF 1952-15, NTA 8776 • Price: $600 USD (available Spring 2025) • sweetprotection.com Large vents in the front of the helmet bring in a moderate amount of cool air. The big story is how the pads are placed that improves the acoustics inside. The cheek and neck pad also form space around your ears and hug the back of your head moreso than any other helmet I’ve tried. If you have claustrophobic feelings about full-face helmets, this could be the answer for you. In addition to the space inside the helmet, the goggle port is very large and the chin bar is tucked in out of sight as well. Pros + Snug but spacious fit + Wide field of vision Cons - Not the most sweat absorbent padding - On the pricier side Pinkbike's Take Best Performer: Bell Full 10 Spherical The looks may not be everyone's taste but the performance of this lid is impossible to ignore. The weight to protection ratio, as well as the venting to protection, is as good as it gets. A large size range, adjustable fit, and premium liner round out the inside. Plus, that MIPS Spherical and GoPro mount haven't made a peep. Best Value: Troy Lee Designs D4 Polyacrylite Yes, it's $100 more expensive than the Abus HiDrop, and 100g heavier, but hear me out. It's the fit and function that gives the D4 Polyacrylite the Best Value title. It has the same safety features as the D4 Carbon, including the MIPS C2 slip plane - no doubt a big miss for many riders considering the HiDrop. Add in the wide size range and incredible venting, only 2nd to the mega-expensive Bell Full 10. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Ridden And Rated Helmets Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: 6D's New Alterra Enduro Full Face Helmet
author: Matt Beer
article_text: 6D Alterra helmet review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/rW8VOhop Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 03:32 6D is best known in mountain biking for their downhill full-face helmets. Until now, they’ve had a gap between those moto-inspired helmets and an open-face trail lid, the ATB-2T. This new Alterra helmet from 6D is big on safety and venting, but low on weight. Although it looks like an enduro full-face helmet with a removable chin-bar, it is fixed in place. The chin strap incorporates a Fidlock buckle and the adjustable visor stows goggles for on-trail conveniences. The carbon chin-bar, along with all of the other tech built into this helmet, shows how 6D prioritizes safety on all fronts. If you’re familiar with 6D’s patent Omni-Directional Suspension (ODS) system, which is used throughout their helmet line, then you won't’t be surprised to find it in the Alterra too. 6D Alterra Details • Fidlock chin strap closure • Omni-Directional Suspension (ODS) • EPP and EPS construction • Weight: 719 g (actual, size M/L) • Sizes: XS/S, M/L, XL/XXL (52-62 cm) • 6 color choices • Price: $395 USD • ASTM F1952 DH certified • 6dhelmets.com The recess and padding on the lower edge aims to save clavicles. From the back, the helmet has a bit of a sloping, wide Porsche coupe appearance to it. Safety Features and Certifications This helmet is burly and not the slimmest enduro full-face, despite being fairly light. That means the Alterra helmet exceeds the CPSC/EN1078/AU/NZS 2063 safety standards. Plus, it meets the NTA 8776 E-Bike standard, ASTM F1952 (DH), and ASTM 2023 (BMX) standards. It's currently the top-ranked full face helmet in Virginia Tech's ranking list, although the list of competitors who have also submitted full face helmets for testing is still fairly small. That said, it's still in the eighth spot even when half shell helmets are added into the mix. The ODS is an attractive safety feature. It is said to function differently than any MIPS slip-plane, including the Split Integra system found in the Fox Proframe RS. Two foam layers (EPP and EPS) make up the inside of the Alterra. Those are said to handle impacts of varying forces and are connected via the ODS. Those pieces work as a suspension of sorts to absorb both linear and angular accelerations. On top of that, there’s the multi-impact EPP liner on the inner cup of the shell, and the EPS outside of that. Those two foams are protected by a polycarbonate shell. Up front, an in-molded carbon fiber chin bar adds peace of mind. The Alterra hides one more safety feature; sternum and clavicle crash pads. These are made from a softer EPP foam in hopes of not causing damage to your bones. Performance Fit The Alterra comes in three main shells to span a typical range of heads 53-62 cm in circumference. The XS/S covers 53 to 56 cm, while the M/L from 56 to 59 cm, and the XL/XXL fits 59 to 62 cm. Included with each helmet are three sets of cheek pads, where the medium thickness is installed, but only two head liners and two neck rolls. All of those pads are made from the popular Ionic+ antibacterial fabric. There are also two strips that cover the chin strap as well. I found the stock pad set on the M/L to fit comfortably without squishing my face. Instead, they sit further forward and hold the helmet in place well. Some other lightweight full-faces I’ve tried have gotten this place wrong by placing them on the jaw bone hinge, which can be tiring and even painful after a long day. In terms of shape, the volume inside the Alterra is larger than a Fox Proframe RS, especially towards the top/rear of the head. In some way this is a good attribute because the helmet feels snug and further down over your head. Along the temple area, the Alterra even has a relief for sunglasses if you’re averse to goggles. The padding on the chin strap moves out of the way and the buckle can rest against your jaw bone. There's no debating the convenience of a Fidlock helmet buckle. Adjustment The M/L is without a doubt the correct shell size for me. It’s snug in terms of circumference, but without an occipital retainer, a third and larger volume neck roll included, the fit could be dialled in further for more stability. Up top, the visor can be set to three positions with the middle looking the most balanced. The highest position allows goggles to sit on the brow of the helmet. From there, the goggles never snapped down on my face while climbing or traversing and the strap didn’t slide out of place on the shell. Metal D-rings offer extra security and could be warranted for downhill race speeds, but for enduro riding, the Fidlock buckle closure makes total sense. It’s strong and foolproof. The tension never backed off, which annoyingly, can’t be said for all plastic buckles. One small improvement 6D could make in this area is on the padding around the plastic buckle. When pulled tight, the padding can slip out of the way, leaving the buckle pressing around the jaw bone. It’s not the end of the world and revising the locations of the loops could be a running change. Vents galore. Ventilation Lightweight full-face helmets all seem to have plenty of cut-outs, but there’s something special about the way the air flows into the Alterra, particularly around the forehead and temples. In fact, I’d argue that it’s better than some half-shell helmets. While the daily temperatures haven’t been setting summertime record highs, it has peaked above 20 degrees C a few times. I also noticed that placing the goggles below the visor, or reversing them, didn’t cause them to fog either. Price Historically, 6D helmets haven’t been cheap and the Alterra doesn’t break that mold either. At $395 USD, it’s more expensive than the Fox Proframe RS and POC Otocon by $40, and the Troy Lee Stage by $80. At that price, you also receive a helmet carry sock and those additional pad kits mentioned. Additionally, the Alterra does come with a 3-year limited warranty on manufacturing defects too. For what it’s worth, the Alterra comes in six colorways as well; Gloss Black/White, Matte Military Green, Matte Slate Blue, Matte White/Green, Matte Black, and Matte Sand. Showing off that carbon weave. The brow vents bring in a substantial amount of fresh air. Weight 6D helmets haven’t always been the lightest, but the Alterra is very respectable at 719 grams. Considering the safety security offered by the ODS system, they’ve done well to drop the weight below a top competitor, the Fox Proframe RS (820 g). The Troy Lee Designs Stage is still 35 grams lighter and the Specialized Gambit is in another league at just 620 grams. The visor is small and narrow in comparison to the rest of the helmet volume. The visor smoothly snaps up a notch or two to stow goggles. Pros + Impressive venting + ODS system doesn’t squeak Cons - Chin strap padding slides to side - Additional padding may be needed to refine fit Pinkbike's Take 6D has sparked my interest in lightweight full-face helmets with the Alterra. Its Omni-Directional Suspension system jockeys for top spot amongst other multi-directional and variable force impact designs. Plus, the weight and price are very competitive in that field. Some experimentation with pad kits might be needed to find the ideal fit though. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets 6d 6d Alterra Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: 7iDP's New Project 21 Open Face Helmet
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: 7iDP have brought to market a new open face helmet. While this isn't their first foray into this style, this is a higher end prospect than the current M1 model. The naming convention looks to bring it inline with their Project 23 full face helmet that we reviewed several years ago. The 21 includes some of the same design cues as well as technologies. The Project 21's claimed weight of 402 grams (+/- 10g) is around 50-80 grams heavier than some of the lighter weight trail helmets available. My test M/L helmet weight 406g. Although 7iDP say the name is derived from the amounts of vents, it stands to reason, and a bit of guesswork, that there might be a Project 22 3/4 helmet at some point. Project 21 Details • 7iDP Rear Dial Adjuster • ERT (Energy Reduction System) • Conehead Foam • 21 vents • Crash Replacement Policy • Weight: 406 grams (M/L, actual) • Sizes: XS/S, M/L, XL/XXL • Meets CE, CPSC, AS Standards • MSRP: $229.99 USD • www.7idp.com The helmets 7iDP's ERT (Energy Reduction Technology) system. This is the same tech that used to be called SERT. This is used to reduce rotational forces transitioning from the helmet to your head. It also uses Conehead foam, which uses two layers of foam that intersect with each other via a lot of corresponding cone shapes, with a decoupling layer in between. These cones then break or crush under the impact, which is claimed to reduce harmful forces to a rider's head. Lastly, the helmet uses a three-position visor designed to release in the case of an impact. The brand also uses size-specific visors for aesthetic reasons. For the record, I think this is a great-looking helmet, so maybe there's something in it. The helmet has 21 vents, with large ones crossing just above the rider's brow. The Trail Pro model on test comes with a zipped-carry case that more typical of a full face helmet. (I've actually been looking for one of these for a while. While flying, I normally like to take my helmet as a personal item in the cabin. The bag conveniently fits a laptop in the base, and can mean you have a bit more wiggle room in terms of weight). Project 21 uses different size pads to help fine-tune the fit. The strap uses a magnetic Fidlock buckle and a ratchet dial to hold your head securely. It is available in sizes XS/S, M/L, XL/XXL in their Black Hologram Colourway. There is also a crash policy, where if you crash in a helmet, you can buy a replacement at 50% off. Fit and Function This is a really deep feeling helmet, and reminds me of the TLD A3. It has that comfy-bucket feel. In some helmets, especially deeper ones such as the POC Kortal, I find that they come too low, and I can almost see the brim through my eyebrows. This isn't the case with the Project 21, even when the ratchet is at its second highest position, meaning the front of the helmet is angled down more. I am typically on the cusp between Large and Extra Large helmets, and have a head size of 59cm. I found the M/L to be an excellent fit, with plenty of room for adjustment and all while using the thicker pads. While not heavy, it does feel more sturdy on your head. The weight is noticeable compared to my favoured Bluegrass Rogue, with the 7iDP helmet feeling a lot deeper around the back. I would say that it's not the most ventilated helmet, though, especially compared to something like the Bluegrass. All in all, this is a great-looking helmet that is both reassuringly sturdy, very comfortable and with a lot of coverage to boot. Helmet reviews are tricky because we never want actually to test them in terms of safety, that's not to say that some don't feel more robust than others. My only gripe is that I found it hard to find secure and easy eyewear storage in the vents with glasses that have slightly bent arms. They could be made to fit, but not easily enough to do one-handed while riding.
