title: Canfield Lithium V3 Review: Long Travel Without the Lethargy
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Canfield is one of those brands with a fierce following and a dedication to creating bikes a little outside the norm. With one of the more distinct suspension designs on the market, they aim to offer a novel suspension feel that other layouts don't quite mirror. At a time when many bikes feel like they're converging on a common design, Canfield's approach is very much their own, and the ride quality achieved is certainly worth experiencing. The Lithium is on its third iteration, and has seen some nice updates to modernize things - having ridden the previous version, I'd say the improvement is significant. Lithium Details • Travel: 165mm / 170mm fork • 29" or MX wheels • 7005 aluminum frame • 64.5° head angle, 76.7° seat angle • 425-500mm reach, 635-649mm stack • 427-443mm chainstay length • Frameset price: $2,500 USD • Complete price: $3,999-$7,499 USD • Weight: 36.82 lb / 16.7 kg • canfieldbikes.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take In a world where many long-travel bikes are starting to feel alike, the Lithium stands out as a versatile and fun ride, regardless of how gnarly your terrain might be. There are some weak points and details that could be refined, but boy can it rip a corner. It's not the most capable bike in all-out rough and steep terrain, but embodies that all-mountain ethos of doing a good job in most places. — Dario DiGiulio Lithium V3 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/JZNd8zAq Copied Live 00:00 19:30 19:30 Frame Details The raw aluminum frame of the Lithium hides some complication you wouldn't notice on first glance. Where the front triangle is fairly simple, and heavily oriented around the suspension layout, the dropouts hide some unique adjustments. Wheel size and chainstay length are adjustable via an on-board flip chip dropout system, allowing the user to convert the Lithium from MX to 29, short chainstay to long. Each change takes only a few minutes, and is best done in a bike stand, though you could carry the task out trailside in a pinch. Adjustable chainstay length and wheel size. Well integrated and self contained. The cable routing is very barebones, with unguided cables channeled through bolt-in gates at the front of the bike. Though it comes without any silencing measures, some foam tubing goes a long way in quieting the bike down - a fairly critical step in my experience. At the risk of sounding like a broken record, I’d probably give the frame external routing, but that’s just me. The cable ports themselves are simple, and unfortunately one of the female threads (frame side) on my bike arrived stripped, making it challenging to tighten that port down completely. Some electrical tape took care of things for the duration of the test. Seatpost insertion for the 30.9mm post is quite deep, though the seattube lengths aren’t particularly short. I was able to run a 240mm length post in my XL bike, and conveniently that fully slammed post landed right at my perfect pedaling height. The frame hardware is simple and easily accessible, though a long-handled tool is useful for reaching the desired torque specs. The frame uses 6902 Max bearings throughout the linkage, to keep replacement simpler. I had some creaking pop up after a couple weeks of riding, but a quick regrease and torque check took care of that noise. Despite the complication, the rear dropout adjustment proved reliable, with no loosening or noise to report. Geometry The Lithium's geometry chart is relatively simple, with few adjustments on tap for the front half of the bike. You get what you get, regardless of wheel size or chainstay length adjustment. The 64.5° head angle is a bit steeper than the typical enduro sled, but plays into how nice the bike can feel through a corner. The 76.7° seat tube angle is on the slack side by modern standards, with the actual seat angle pitching the rider back as the saddle height gets higher. The chainstays are adjustable via a bolt-on dropout and integrated flip chip, with three options straddling the two wheel sizes. 29" long mode gives you a 443mm chainstay, 29" short and 27.5" long yield a 435mm length, and 27.5" short shrinks down to 427mm. Options for everyone, within a fairly standard range. At 350mm, the bottom bracket height is on the higher end for similar bikes, but the dynamic position puts you lower than the on-paper figures might indicate. Combined with stack heights that sit right in the middle of a bell curve, and you're left with a nice rider area that feels roomy enough, with a higher rise bar adding some front end height for those that prefer that position. Suspension Design The Lithium’s frame revolves around the Canfield Balance Formula (CBF) suspension layout, which looks to isolate the rider from drivetrain feedback and any kinematic changes that may incur through travel. In my experience, it allows for a wider range of shock setups than some other systems, with anti-squat staying consistent through travel. CBF optimizes for a consistent instant center at the top of the chainring, preventing chain tension from disrupting the suspension action through travel. The Lithium’s 165mm of rear-wheel travel is delivered via a 230x65mm shock, and is compatible with a wide range of coil and air shocks. My test bike was built around a Fox Float X2, with a base tune that generally suited the bike quite well. The only hitch I encountered on shock setup came down to the rebound tune - I found myself running the low-speed rebound fully open, and the high-speed close to that point. Though I found a happy place with this adjustment, there’s always the curiosity for what more you could get eke out with a different base tune or wider range of adjustment. Lighter riders may require a lighter rebound tune, but I can’t speak to that experience. Specifications Release Date 2026 Price $5999.99 Travel 165mm Rear Shock Fox Factory X2 230x65mm Fork Fox Factory 38 170mm Headset Cane Creek Cassette Shimano XT Crankarms Canfield Bottom Bracket Shimano Rear Derailleur Shimano XT Di2 Chain Shimano XT Shifter Pods Shimano XT Di2 Handlebar e*thirteen Base Stem e*thirteen Base Grips Lizardskins Brakes Shimano XT Wheelset DT Swiss E1700 Hubs DT Swiss Spokes DT Swiss Rim DT Swiss E1700 Tires Maxxis EXO+, Assegai 2.5" MaxxGrip, DHRII 2.5" MaxxTerra Seat Canfield Bel Air Seatpost OneUp V3 240mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Lithium V3 Test Bike Setup Canfield has a rather extensive build menu, with six stock options and plenty of smaller component choices to make about small parts and details. Crank length, chainring size, and stem length are all moving targets, so you'll be able to hone in on those select details if you please. The only stock part I fully swapped out was the e*thirteen Base handlebar, which was a bit low-rise for my taste, and has a sweep that feels off to me. Otherwise, I tuned up the XT brakes as I always do, with thick rotors (in this case, some 2.3mm Hope discs) and TRP's blue brake pads. The CBF suspension system works well with a very wide sag range, but I found myself served best by the standard 30% mark. As mentioned, I ended up at the extreme of the rebound damping range, with LSC all the way open - compression was more central in the range, and felt rideable at the extremes, though you'd certainly notice a dynamic difference. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 84 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario As mentioned, the Lithium V3 comes with chainstay length and rear wheel size adjustability - long or short, big or small. I tried a few of those options, and ultimately found that the long chainstay mode and full 29" setup were my favorite for the bike's overall character. Testing Info My time on this bike started in Whistler and Squamish, both on pedal trails and in the bike park, before heading home to flog it on some familiar trails in Bellingham . Conditions ranged from foul to perfect, and the Lithium saw a very wide variety of trails - loamers to A-Line, mellow to heinous. Climbing Long-legged enduro bikes have gotten progressively heavier and stickier with time, adding capability and composure to the descents at some cost to the climbing pep. That’s not to say that big bikes climb poorly, they just err on traction and a comfortable body position (ideally) to keep you moving up the hill. The Canfield Lithium bucks that trend a bit, offering a more energetic climbing characteristic than I’m used to experiencing with bikes in this travel bracket. 165mm may indicate a sluggish feel, but that’s not the case here. You do lose out on some of the slow-speed grip when compared to some other options, but I’d say the balance struck is ideal for most use cases. The bottom bracket isn't particularly low, so pedal strikes are less of a concern in awkward technical terrain, allowing for a more consistent cadence when you're trying to scrape up something goofy. My only complaint about the climbing performance of the Lithium comes down to the seat angle, which feels a touch slacker than some contemporary bikes. Some riders will prefer this, but I found myself consciously weighting the front wheel on very steep climbs, where a steeper seat angle can naturally keep you centered and equally weighted between the wheels. I’d place the weight of the Lithium as a standard middle-of-the-road, with an all-aluminum parts kit and medium weight EXO+ tires balancing out to a package that’s easy to live with on long days out. Those tires might seem a bit under-gunned for the travel, but they survived my abuses. The massive built-in carry handle helps when it’s time to portage the bike over obstacles, should you want to get adventurous. One detail that might not pair so well with long days out is the lack of a bottle mount inside the front triangle. You do have provisions for a cage beneath the downtube, but cow poop, clearance, and aesthetics might prevent that from being an ideal option. Descending Most people seem to overestimate their bike needs when purchasing a new model. More travel has a certain appeal to it, and the promise of sending in on some gnarly line is a perfectly valid reason to size up on capability. The Lithium presents as a bike in the big bruiser category based on its travel numbers (165mm/170mm), but the character of this thing is far more adaptable than expected. The suspension quality of the Lithium is impressive, so long as you fall into the right category of rider. For those looking for something athletic and energetic, that generates speed well in compressions and corners, this is worth a look. Those looking to plant their heels and plow might find the bike (at least in this setup) provides a bit more feedback and pop than you'd like. I found myself getting along with the Lithium very well, both on natural, bony terrain, and flowier, more carved riding alike. It seems that a big part of the Lithium's character here comes from the even-keeled nature of the CBF suspension, which alters in feedback very little as you push through the travel. Small and large compressions have a similar level of feedback, keeping the bike predictable through corners, even when chopped out and uncertain. The frame has a nice level of flex to it, giving as you push into the bike, but never feeling unnerving, even in the most high-g corners. The cornering performance was probably the single standout feature of this bike, and the head angle is partly to thank for that. 64.5° is a bit steep by modern standards - at least for a long-travel bike - but the precision and front wheel grip on offer here is worth whatever assumed challenges you might have in steeper terrain. I rode some of the steeper trails I've hit so far this year aboard the Lithium, and never found that front end figure to be unsettling; it's nice to be able to change direction quickly. In comparison, I rode the previous Lithium a fair bit, and never really got along with the cornering performance. The chainstays were much shorter on that bike, and while the V3 model's aren't especially long on paper, they do feel like a better balance with the front end of the bike, helping out that cornering equation. Things aren't all rosy, as there are some sore points on the Lithium's descending performance. The main culprit here is noise. As I touched on in the frame details, the cable rattle on this bike is intense, and needs to be dealt with when building the bike up. I added some after my first ride, and found that things did improve a bit. Chain slap is also quite aggressive, but the Shimano XT Di2's lack of clutch may be partly to blame there. Canfield also went with a very low-profile slap protector, which does little to damp the noise. Finally, I'll revisit my first point, as some riders might not jive with the more all-mountain feel of the Lithium. It's an efficient, lively bike, not a super damp truck. Some people just want a machine to plow on, and this feels more at home railing a corner and popping over a root mat, rather than rattling through them. Canfield Lithium V3 Specialized Stumpjumper 15 How Does It Compare? I dug back through my mental archives searching for a bike that felt similar in disposition to the Lithium, and the match came as a bit of a surprise. I think the Stumpjupmer 15 is a close match for the overall capability and handling feel that the Lithium offers, though the way they achieve that is very different. Aligned in head angle, these bikes are quite different when it comes to their vibe. The Stumpy punches well above its weight class in terms of rear-wheel travel, and the Lithium has a sharper quality to it than most long-travel enduro bikes. Both corner very nicely, and feel like good tools for long days out. Both have slacker seat angles than I'd like, and also feature some serious adjustability. The Stumpjumper offers a bit more in that aspect, for those keeping score. Small frame details are very well sorted on the Specialized, where the Canfield is a little rough around the edges. Ultimately, this comparison is as much for me to make the point that you can achieve a similar feeling with drastically different bikes, so long as a few of the ingredients align. For those looking to split them apart on suspension feel, I'd say that the Canfield is more predictable through travel, where the Stumpjumper does an excellent job of erasing the bumps you occur along the way. One other bike that comes to mind is the Propain Spindrift . A very long travel bike on paper, the Spindrift has a more do-everything quality than you'd expect, and felt to me like more of a leggy trail bike than an enduro rig meant to smash through terrain exclusively. The Lithium's suspension maximizes consistency, where the Spindrift's support is the defining characteristic. With some similar adjustments on the table, it could hit a similar mark to the Lithium, for riders shopping around. Which Model is the Best Value? With six stock options to choose from, Canfield has done an excellent job of offering a strong value for the price with the Lithium V3. I'd recommend the Core build I rode to just about anybody, as you get plenty of options between the brands that tend to polarize people. Fox or RockShox, Shimano or SRAM, you can have it your way and enjoy a bike with very nice suspension, a solid drivetrain, and good brakes. I'd probably upgrade that latter element to the off-market spec that I'm running, but that's a smaller detail and price tag. Technical Report Shimano XT Brakes: I've said it before I'll say it again: these brakes massively benefit from a thicker rotor. Be it a Hope or TRP offering, I'v really enjoyed how Shimano brakes feel with less deadband and a more reliable disc, so the 2.3mm options on the market feel like ideal pairings. Add to the mix some good aftermarket brake pads (I've had good luck with blue TRP pads), and you've got yourself some mighty fine stoppers. Tire Choice: The EXO+ tires that come stock on all builds of the Lithium are a potential weak point for riders pushing the bike in the bike park and on gnarly terrain. They survived my test period, but that was relatively brief in the grand scheme of things, and I did take care to not demolish them on any dumb moves. If you can manage the thinner casing, the lighter weight is definitely welcomed. DT Swiss E1700 Wheels: I'm having a bit of an aluminum wheel renaissance, thanks to drying trails and the faster, rougher speeds that come along with those conditions. A little more compliance is more than welcomed in those scenarios, and the E1700s offer that in a package that feels reliable and simple to live with. Not bombproof, but sturdy, these should handle plenty of abuse with reasonable maintenance. Pros + Energetic, predictable suspension performance + Geometry provides good handling on varied terrain + Excellent value for complete builds Cons - Very noisy frame, as stock - Not the most composed through rough terrain - Geometry could be improved for climbing Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Canfield Bikes Canfield Lithium Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Cannondale Habit Carbon LT1
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: REVIEW Cannondale Habit LT WORDS: Henry Quinney PHOTOS: Tom Richards Personally, I've always had a soft spot for Cannondale. I'm too young for the 90's heyday, but I do have fond memories of the exciting new world of enduro and Jerome Clementz riding in a way I think we all wish we could. The Waves, or whatever they're called, are all a bit much for me. Amazing riders, yes, showcasing the best of a brand like Cannondale? Maybe not. I want to see downhill bikes, racing, and something different. In fact, that two-shock downhill bike was the coolest thing the brand has done in a very long time, especially accompanied by the "This is a Test" tagline. It's such a shame they canned it to pursue cooler projects. Habit Carbon LT1 • Wheel size: 29" • Travel: 140 frame / 150mm fork • Carbon frame • 64.7 degree head tube angle • 440 mm chainstays (size large) • Sizes: XS - XL • Large w/o pedals 14.25 kg/31.4 lbs • Complete bike: $5,499 USD • cannondale.com When I covered the release of the Habit it struck me just how sensible and conventional it was. There was no quirkiness to it, at least on the surface, and everything about it seemed to be pragmatic and well-judged. That's not to say there wasn't some clever design involved, or that this bike isn't a very solid execution, but rather it was akin to seeing David Bowie talk eloquently and expressively about philosophy, and then remembering that he too was Ziggy Stardust. Yes, you knew he had it in him, but you're kind of taken aback by how down-to-earth he is. Maybe the freeride kids know what they're talking about, after all. Maybe, in fact, two shocks and one fork leg is exciting, but not what I would personally wish to ride every day. Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Frame Details There is something about the Habit that has people pointing out how good it looks. Working for Pinkbike, I get to ride several different bikes over quite intense periods. Turning up to my local cafe and bike shop, Ride Hub, is often a good acid test for whether a bike is desirable or not. Oftentimes, they hardly even blink, or worse yet exclaim what-on-earth?! but there was something about the Habit where everyone immediately agreed it was a very good-looking bike. I'm not sure if it's just the paint or the styling, but I would say that it might have something to do with its simplicity, which will become a theme when we get onto the riding characteristics. The Habit also comes in a non-LT version, which sports 10mm less travel in both the fork and the shock. It's essentially the same frame and linkage but with a different stroke shock. Often, shocks with a certain eye-to-eye size will cover more than one stroke length, and the difference is taken care of with a limiting spacer. This spacer can be removed if you rebuild the damper. You could then, depending upon which fork you have, change the air spring on the fork to match, too, if you wanted to make the wholesale change from Habit to Habit LT. Speaking of the suspension, it should be noted that Cannondale uses what they call "Proportional Response System". What this references is that there is sometimes a one size-fits-all approach to both geometry and suspension layouts, and that can sometimes be expressed in different ways. For instance, some brands might have an extra-large with longer stays but the stay itself is the same length - however, where they hang the bottom bracket off the front triangle is different. So in some ways, much like with seat tubes, there can often be a difference between effective and actual - both with their limitations and nuances. Before you get too excited though it should be noted that this proportional system is only available on the carbon bikes, plus it's only three different layouts over five sizes, meaning the extra small, small, and medium still share the same rear end. It's also hard to test in that I ride a large. So while I can say how this bike performs it would be impossible for me to test a bike for somebody of smaller stature, and vice versa. The frame has some nice subtle features, though. There is a rubber cover to stop your chainstay from gobbling up rocks, non-guided non-headset (hoorah) routing that is made very easy to live with thanks to a removable panel on the downtube, ISCG threads, and a tried-and-true threaded bottom bracket. There is also a second water bottle mount should you want to go full-enduro and fit the bike with tools or tubes. Geometry The Habit LT's geometry, to my mind, screams modern, sensible trail bike. It feels as if every three years we all say " Finally , trail bikes have actually got decent geometry!" But this time we must surely mean it. Of course, the Habit isn't the first 140 mm travel bike to get the best of enduro and downhill - far from it in fact - but it does, to my mind at least, offer very well-rounded geometry and capitalizes and improves upon all of the nearly-great trail bikes of years past. In fact, the only thing I would take aim at would be the seat tube length. It's short enough, but only just. The size large that I had on test featured a 440mm rear center, a 475 mm reach, and a very healthy 644 mm stack. How a bike fits you is down to both the size of the bike, and the size of your body. For me, at 183 cm or six feet, it fits perfectly. Everything was in range, comfortable and easy to affect. I think 475 mm reach, for me, is the sweet spot, especially on a bike like this. The relatively large stack height is then combined with a 30 mm rise bar. While plodding around Squamish I ran the stem sometimes lower on the steerer. For the fast, steep and sequential turns of the bike park though I was happy to have the option to run it higher. Suspension Design Cannondale use a four-bar, Horst Link system. As many brands do, they've renamed it something fancy but four-bar is what it is. So, what sets it apart, if anything at all? Firstly, this bike has a comparatively low anti-squat value. This will be bad news in terms of all-out efficiency on smooth roads and, in my experience, good news if you plan on your suspension actually suspending you on actual mountain bike trails. A value of around 100% should give a platform that neither extends or contracts under acceleration; above 100% and the suspension will want to extend, which could increase efficiency, and below it'll be far happier to break into its stroke. Extending, firm suspension is good but when you're off-road and pedaling I find that I'm demanding the bike to not only provide support my efforts but also grip and track. A lower anti-squat will aid this because the bike isn't resisting going into its travel as you pedal. The Habit has a value of 80-90% at sag depending upon the gearing. This is leaning towards my preference, but it is a game of opinion. Anti-rise is relatively middling to high at around 50% at sag. This is related to the mass transfer and suspension extension under braking. Again, it's a personal thing. A lower value will help keep braking forces independent and stop the wheel locking. A high value will make the bike squat more and keep geometry more consistent. The leverage is also reasonably linear as it progresses from a ratio of 2.9 to 2.4 in a relatively consistent and smooth curve. This should give a bike that is happy to use the initial part of its stroke and then steadily ramp up the resistance the shock and offer as you go deeper into the stroke. RIDING THE Cannondale Habit LT Test Bike Setup The Cannondale is an easy bike to just get on and ride. That's not to say it doesn't have some creases in the spec that need ironing out though. Firstly, the SRAM Codes are a perfectly decent brake - and are very appropriate for a bike like this - but there is a vast gulf in power and feel from the Code Rs to the RSCs. Yes, I know that nothing comes for free, and wanting top of the line brakes is just the start of wanting top of the line everything else, however, I would gladly forgo a carbon bar for some more powerful options to slow me down. To really enjoy this bike, I decided to put on some RSC levers that I had at home. Save for the piston material it makes the brakes the high-end option, and for me is a great way to add some extra pep to a bike's spec. Henry Quinney Location: Squamish Age: 31 Height: 183 cm / 6' Inseam: 82 cm / 32.5" Weight: 77 kg / 169 lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None The Rockshox Lyrik Select+ is a great fork, as is the Super Deluxe shock. Both were not only easy to set up but also easy to make small and consistent adjustments too. They have all the important bits you'd want from the higher-end kit - namely the excellent damping, although they do go without one or two features. The fork does without the Buttercups, and the shock doesn't have any external compression adjustment. That said, the compression damping it did offer from stock was just where you'd want it to be. The rebound damping was effective and within a useable range, too. The cockpit, save for the 170 mm post's awkwardly shaped remote lever, was all correctly proportioned and fit for service, as were the tires. Yes, there are gripper, beefier tires than the Exo+ Dissector and Minion combination, but there are also burlier and beefier enduro bikes to fit them to. If this were my bike would I change the tires for something gripper and thicker? Perhaps - but I would run these into the ground first. Climbing The Habit LT's geometry offers a quite upright position that, when combined with that amply steep 77.1 degree seat tube angle, gives you the front wheel tracking you might want from a steeper bike, with all the control and comfort of a bike with a higher stack. Of course, as stack height increases, especially on slacker bikes, a wayward and floppy front end can come into play as your weight lurches. However, the Habit has tempered this balance well to give you the best of both worlds. It also opens up its remit as an all-round trail bike because there isn't an excessive amount of weight in your hands and wrists. Of course, it's no XC bike, and there are bikes that will climb faster than this 140 mm travel bike, but the Habit does offer a very well-rounded performance. It's comfortable on undulating terrain thanks to that higher stack, planted on steeper switchbacks, and the suspension tracks and grips well. I would sometimes make use of the climb switch for hard-pack fire roads or tarmac stretches, but if there was anything remotely rough I set it to open and let it track. Again, balance is the right word, and the bike does strike a great balance between tracking and efficiency. It might not be the most suited to out-of-the-saddle stamping, and if you're somebody that throws your weight around the bike then maybe the Habit won't be the firmest feeling, but if you're happy to stay seated and spin it out then the suspension does a very admirable job. I found that there was enough mid and end-stroke support when descending to really be able to enjoy the suppleness of the shock on the climbs and reap the benefits of all the grip it offered. For a bike so capable on the descents, this climbs just about as well as you could hope. Descending Cannondale has made some weird bikes in the past. Hell, they're even making some kind of weird bikes now. They're still sticking to the Lefty, they're still making the Jekyll which, at best, could be described as "different", much in the same way one might describe a pug with its tongue too big for its mouth and that has trouble breathing as "cute". The Habit, however, the Habit is a different story. Not only is it simply a good-looking bike, but it's also a good-looking bike because it looks clean and simple. That ethos tracks through to how it descends, too. It doesn't feel like it's trying to redefine the category or disrupt the industry with a lukewarm take. Instead, it seems that it is merely simple good quality fresh ingredients cooked with care. We review a lot of trail bikes, and there are many great ones out there, but each has its foibles. It's almost as if Cannondale has looked at the complaints people have made about this last wave of great trail bikes and just thought "What if we make a bike that isn't trying to be this boundary-breaking, genre-defining, world-changing thing but instead just avoids a lot of the criticisms other 130 or 140 mm travel bikes come in for?" It's extra-regular, and all the better for it. The geometry is balanced - for me, this bike is near perfect in its proportions. The ever-so-slightly shorter reach and the high front just make everything feel so in range. The shorter reach means that also, proportionally speaking, the 440 mm rear end puts more weight on the front, as it's representative of a greater proportion of the bike's total length. Yes, your bike with a 430 mm rear center may well have ridden very well several years ago, but it was also combined with a reach of a similar value. So, although one tends to think of shorter rear ends as giving a more poppy, rear weighted, and playful bike, one could argue that the generation of short-stay bikes that enjoyed its heyday in 2015-2018ish oftentimes had more weight on the front than the long stay-bikes of today. The Habit LT is weighted on the front, balanced on most trails, and able to handle very steep trails because of and not in spite of this well-proportioned and pragmatic geometry. I'm not a believer in massive reaches, especially when combined with short rear ends. This Habit, and the grip it achieves on any manner of trail is a testament to this. The suspension is well-damped while also offering solid tracking. For a bike that is so smooth off the top the mid-to-end stroke support is very very good. In fact, this bike doesn't really have a blind spot when it comes to descending. Sure, it can hang up a little of square chunks if you don't have speed on your side, but it's all in all a very well-executed four-bar. I tended to run mine soft and fast, and it felt like the bike could reset between impacts. That, coupled with the sure-footed-yet-sensitive nature of the Lyrik, felt like a bike where the main components were all reading from the same page. Its spec is largely sensible, where it really counts anyway. The Lyrik Select+ with the Charger 3 is a good fork, and the Super Deluxe shock is also a welcome addition. If you want a bike that can genuinely hang with enduro bikes on bike park laps and also has big climbs and all-day trail rides well within its remit, you could do a lot worse than the Habit LT. Cannondale Habit 140 Canyon Spectral How Does It Compare? Canyon and Cannondale are in some ways polar opposites, and in others seem to try and be doing similar things. Both have a strong foundation in cross-country. Both employ a slew of freeriders and cool-kids to showcase their bikes and both have enjoyed success in enduro at the highest level. The Spectral is slacker and longer than the Habit, with 10 mm more travel in both the frame and the shock, but I would say what the Habit comes short on in travel it comes good on with stability and balance. The Spectral is a solid bike, it really is, but it feels in some ways like a conservative bike that has included certain progressive dimensions, all while being a very strong pedaller, whereas the Habit feels like a progressive bike that's been tempered to hold a greater relevance on the trails it will actually ride. The Canyon is longer at the front and shorter and the back. It also has a lower stack. When going very fast, it comes alive, especially on smoother trails. Its lower front also plays into this. It's a bike for picking apart trails and nailing turns, more than a bike for letting the brakes off and hoping for the best. While the suspension does a great job, it feels simple yet effective rather than particularly well-damped or refined. That does come back to the rider in some ways though, and it offers lots of support under and pop under load. The Cannondale, while shorter on travel, gives you more to brace against with the higher front, meaning you're more tempted to let off the brakes and really let it run. Despite it being less travel, I would say the shock offers more composure on repeated hits. It does track well and offers excellent grip when climbing, but there isn't so much of a platform when putting load through the bike. That said, it will also struggle to get close to the Canyon in terms of all-out value for money. Prices inUSD. Which Model is the Best Value? Which of the bikes would be the one to go for? Well, the LT1 that I tested could do a lot worse in terms of value, but it could also do better. In fact, what would I personally prefer, GX or SLX? Code Rs or SLX brakes? For both instances it would be the latter. From there, my only point of concern would be losing the reliable DT Swiss rear hub in exchange for the Shimano model. Shimano's hubs have got better in recent years, but DT Swiss still are superior in my opinion. The side-ways-grade to the smaller Pike wouldn't be an issue in terms of stiffness or capability - and it might even align better with what your riding than the heavier Lyrik. However, I would have one eye on upgrading the damper in the future. Outside of that, I would put the money saved towards a longer dropper should you have it. This bike isn't just its suspension - what else caught my eye? Technical Report SRAM Code R Brakes: This bike deserves better brakes, or at least bigger rotors. 180 mm on the rear is fine - we all know that it is - but 200 mm is better, especially when trying to make the most of the Code Rs. Although they might look the same, the architecture of the lever is very different between the R and RSC, and the difference in power is drastic. Cannondale Cockpit: The bars feature an oval center, and are relatively comfortable. However, while a carbon bar is very exciting I would rather have an alloy one and better brakes. I know it's not that simple but I would prefer a higher spec where it counts. Dropper post: This is a great bike... that's been fitted with a dropper that's too short. 170 mm for a large just is not enough. Furthermore, the lever is clunky, and it's hard to find a good placement with the SRAM brakes. It's either too far away or rubbing your thumb. Wheels and Tires: The Exo+ casing tyres are good, and the Minion DHF/Dissector combination offers plenty of grip for something that rolls so fast. The wheels, which include a nice DT rear hub, do track well, and I think the low profile WTB rims are to thank. That said, they did detension several times, and are a bit more oval now than when I started. If longevity isn't something you struggle with though, I would say these rims actually offer a really great level of compliance and help smooth out the rough stuff and chatter to break it down into smaller bites for the bike to handle. Pros + Balanced, proportional geometry + Suspension strikes a balance between support and tracking + Sensible frame features, including no headset routing + Covers a lot of bases in climbing and descending Cons - Some spec choices miss the target - Not the most efficient climber on smooth roads Pinkbike's Take Extra-regular is no bad thing. This is a very well-executed bike that simply works very well. Balance, composure, maneuverability, and comfort can oftentimes seem like ideologies that are diametrically opposed to one another in bikes, and you can merely have two or three out of the four at best. The Habit LT makes a very strong argument that, as riders, we might just be able to have it all. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Cannondale Cannondale Habit Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: Cannondale Moterra SL - Full Power Without All the Weight
author: Seb Stott
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, cannondale, cannondale-moterra-sl
article_text: REVIEW Cannondale Moterra SL WORDS: Seb Stott PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson The Moterra SL promises to let you have your cake and eat it too. It's a full-power eMTB weighing no more than many 'SL' (Super Light) e-bikes. We got a first taste back in February, but now we've had time to see how the full-power featherweight handles on home trails. The Shimano EP801 motor provides the full 85 Nm of oomph to send you up steep hills and the 601Wh internal battery is big enough to do long rides without skimping on assistance. Cannondale wanted to get this bike under 20 kg, and it probably limbos under that number in the smallest sizes, but my extra-large test bike (the lightest and priciest model) weighed in at 20.4 kg / 45.0 lb. Moterra SL Details • Wheel size: Mixed, dual 29" compatible • 142-149 mm rear travel (size-specific), 160mm fork • 62.5-63.7° head angle, 77° seat angle • Shimano EP801 motor, 601 Wh batttery • Weight: 20.4 kg (45.0 lb) as tested, XL • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Lifetime original owner frame warranty, 5-years for the swingarm (inc. flex pivot) • Price: $7,000 to $14,000 as tested (USD) • cannondale.com That's still impressive considering many eMTBs with less power and smaller batteries weigh about the same. What's more important, though, is how it rides. The Moterra SL isn't just impressive on paper but delivers when tires meet the dirt. It's not without quirks, and there are a few setup suggestions that I feel get the most out of it. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Moterra Sl Review Fixed Audio https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/zIbLSMrm Copied Live 00:00 13:03 13:03 The kilograms saved compared to other full-power e-bikes pay dividends in complicated sections, particularly when maneuvering the bike through tight trees, sharp corners or off-camber roots. While still not quite the same as riding an analog bike, it's a hell of a lot more intuitive and engaging than a traditional eMTB. — Seb Stott Frame Details Cannondale worked hard to save weight with the Moterra frame, most obviously with the flex-stay suspension system, which does away with chainstay pivot hardware in favour of a carefully engineered flexible carbon layup. The enclosed downtube also helps save weight compared to a removable battery, but at the expense of convenience as it prohibits off-bike charging or battery swapping. For some, this will be a major consideration when comparing the Moterra Sl to other (heavier) full-power eMTBs. The top-spec frame (tested) only offers cable routing through the headset, but the less expensive builds also get cable ports in the frame. Cannondale say they omitted the frame ports in the Lab 71 spec for aesthetic reasons, rather than to save weight. The second-tier build is only around 200 g heavier as a complete bike, according to Cannondale, despite less fancy components. There is an impressive amount of geometry adjustability on offer, with a headset that alters the head angle by around 1.2 degrees, plus a flip chip on the seatstay that lowers the BB for use with a 29" rear wheel. The low setting with a 29" wheel offers similar geometry (just a few mm higher) to the stock setup with a 27.5" wheel in the high setting. However, the bike is so low and slack that you can use the high mode with a 29" wheel, resulting in a BB height that's about 10 mm higher and a head angle around 0.7 degrees steeper. Word to the wise: when changing the flip chip, it's all too easy to end up chasing after all the spacers in the pivot assembly around your workshop floor. This is because the flex stays cause the seatstay to spring upwards when the bolts are removed, allowing the spacers to drop to the ground. Take it from me - getting them all back in place is a fiddly process. To avoid this, hold the seatstay in place over the pivot (ideally have a friend do this or use a toe strap) while you remove the bolts and reposition the flip chips. I wouldn't recommend attempting this trailside. There is room for a full-size water bottle and you don't have to give that up to fit a range extender. There's decent tire clearance too, even with a 29" rear wheel. Motor and Battery The Shimano EP801 offers the full 85 Nm of assistance in Boost mode, and the assistance modes are custom-tuned to Cannondale's taste. Apparently, the second level of assistance is designed to replicate the feel of mid-power systems found on other SL e-bikes - I would call that sandbagging mode. The assistance settings are configurable with Shimano's app if you want to change them, but I spent most of my time riding in Boost mode. The custom battery is another key to lightness. With 601 Wh of energy and a claimed weight of 3.1 kg (6.8 lb) it has a pretty high energy-to-weight ratio (194 Wh/kg), although Bosch's latest batteries are slightly better (200-205 Wh/kg). Shimano will handle support and warranty on the battery (along with the rest of the drive unit) in North America and Europe. Suspension Design Cannondale's flex pivot suspension replaces the conventional chainstay pivot bearings and bolts with a vertically flexible region in the carbon fiber layup. The main advantage of this is weight savings, but it also means two sets of pivot bearings don't ever need to be replaced. Flex pivots are common among cross-country bikes but rarer among long-travel bikes, especially e-bikes. The skinny chainstays raised some skeptical comments when the bike was first released, but Cannondale say the flex pivots offer "near infinite fatigue life" and they're covered under the swingarm's 5-year warranty. That isn't something you can say about many chainstay pivot bearings. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Cannondale Moterra Scientific Squish Test by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 4,448 Faves: 0 Comments: 0 Unlike most flex-pivot suspension designs, the flex pivot is on the chainstay (not the seatstay) making this a Horst-link layout. That basically means the engineers have more control over the anti-rise (braking behaviour) than they would in a single-pivot design, where there is no pivot between the rear axle and the mainframe. Perhaps the most interesting thing about the Moterra's suspension is that, while the one-piece rear triangle is common to all frame sizes, the locations of the pivots are tweaked to provide size-specific suspension kinematics. In short, this means that larger frames get more anti-squat and anti-rise than they otherwise would. This is to help prevent pedal bob and brake dive, respectively, which are more problematic with a higher centre of gravity. Bigger frames also feature a more progressive leverage curve to help resist bottom-out - and more travel too, going from 142 mm for a small to 149 mm in XL. Whether bigger bikes need more progression is debatable in my view, but the change in travel between sizes is probably more noticeable. It's hard to see a good reason for that, so it's likely just a consequence of the other changes. The overall progression is middle of the road. Cannondale says it's not designed to take a coil shock, mostly because few will fit. The leverage curves for all four frame sizes. Note bigger frames have more leverage, hence more travel (first column in the table). Frame progression is usually quoted in terms of the change in leverage ratio from 0-100% travel, which varies between 23.4% (S) to 26.4% (XL). In either case, that's not especially progressive but not overly linear either. Anti-squat is a bit more complicated. Bigger frames get more anti-squat in most gears but slightly less in the biggest sprocket. Generally, anti-squat levels are well-balanced so the suspension doesn't bog down when accelerating but the suspension can still move freely over bumps under power. Anti-rise levels are nice and constant throughout the stroke, helping the chassis to resist pitching when braking hard without the suspension stiffening up too much. Geometry & Sizing Cannondale managed to tweak the pivot locations to achieve size-specific chainstay lengths as well as suspension. I imagine that getting all those characteristics to line up simultaneously with one rear triangle was a bit like solving a Rubix cube.
