Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Moterra SL Sizing Changes MP4 by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 1,876 Faves: 1 Comments: 0 The rear-center length is the same between small and medium frames (449 mm) but grows by 4 mm when you go to a large and 5 mm from large to XL. Those differences aren't exactly going to revolutionise bike handling , but in theory, compared to a bike with the same chainstay length throughout the size range, it means the L and XL sizes will have a slightly more even weight distribution (benefiting flat corner traction) at the expense of requiring more effort to lift the front wheel. The head angle is super slack in the stock configuration. Cannondale quoted 62.5 degrees but I measured my test bike at 61.8 degrees. It's not unusual for bikes to measure slacker than advertised due to shock sag, tire size differences and other factors. Fortunately, you can make it 1.2 degrees steeper (63.7 degrees on paper) by rotating the headset cups. In my view, if you're going to offer adjustability, you might as well go to the extremes. Alternatively, the Moterra accepts a 29" wheel and while there is a geometry flip chip to compensate for this, I enjoyed the handling with a bigger wheel and the chip left in the high setting. This steepened the head angle to 62.8 degrees (by my measurements) and raised the bottom bracket from 333 mm to 343 mm. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $14000 Travel 160mm (Front), 142-149mm (Rear) Rear Shock Fox Float X Factory, 2 pos adj, EVOL LV, 210x55mm Fork Fox Float Factory 36, 160mm travel, Grip2 Damper, Kashima coating, 44mm offset Headset Acros Adjustable Angle (1.2°) Cassette SRAM XX Eagle, T-Type, 10-52, 12-speed Crankarms ethirteen espec Race Carbon - Gen 4, 165mm, 34T Chainguide N/A Bottom Bracket Shimano EP801 Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM XX Eagle AXS, T-Type Chain SRAM XX Eagle, T-Type, Flattop Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM Eagle AXS T-Type Ultimate Pod Controller Handlebar OneUp Carbon Ebar, 35mm clamp, 35mm Rise, 8° sweep, 5° rise, 780mm Stem OneUp Stem, 6061 Alloy, 1-1/8", 35mm, 0° Grips Cannondale TaperRidge Brakes SRAM Code Ultimate Stealth, 4-piston hydraulic disc, 200mm HS2 rotors Wheelset DT Swiss XMC 1501, carbon, 30mm inner width, hookless, TSS tubeless ready Hubs (F) DT Swiss 240, 15x110mm / (R) DT Swiss 240 Ratchet EXP 36, 12x148mm Spokes DT Swiss Competition Race Rim DT Swiss XMC 1501, carbon, 30mm inner width, hookless Tires (F) Maxxis DHF, 29x2.5", 3C, EXO+ / (R) Maxxis Dissector, 27.5x2.4", 3C, EXO+ Seat Fizik Terra Ridon X1, 145mm, Wingflex, Carbon Mobius rail 7x10mm Seatpost RockShox Reverb AXS, 31.6, 150mm (S), 170mm (Other Sizes) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Moterra SL Test Bike Setup I found myself looking for a bit more sensitivity and traction from the Moterra so I set the shock with 30% sag and preferred the compression damping towards fully open. In the stock setup with mullet wheels, I also struggled with fork settings, ending up a few psi softer than I normally run in a Fox 36 to improve comfort on small bumps and steps. With a 29" back wheel and the flip chip left in the high setting, the suspension felt more impressive all-round, with less harshness front and rear. I returned to my go-to pressure in the Fox 36 (95 psi) and increased shock pressure slightly too (220 psi). I still preferred the compression damping open. This setup would use all the travel on the biggest compressions but didn't bottom out harshly. Given the Moterra SL has less travel than most full-power eMTBs, I felt the need to trade off bottom-out resistance in favour of suspension sensitivity, which benefits both climbing and descending. Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 32 Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 37" / 93cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted I set the handlebars high (25 mm of spacers under the 35 mm rise bar), swapped the Cannondale grips for my preferred ODI's and dropped the 170 mm travel seatpost another 50 mm for serious descents. Testing Info Testing took place in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, at Glentress, Cademuir and Innerleithen. The Moterra SL impressed the most on trails with steep tech, nice corners or high-speed sections, but not so much when things got super rough. Climbing The combination of a full-power motor and mid-power weight might sound like a recipe for the ultimate climbing e-bike, but unless you're against the clock, I don't think there's a noticeable advantage compared to heavier (~25kg) eMTBs with the same motor. On the other hand, when compared to other SL e-bikes with less power, it is in another league. Recently I tested the Canyon Spectral:ONfly , which weighs about the same but uses the TQ-HPR50 motor with half the peak power (admittedly the Canyon cost half as much as the Lab 71 Cannondale). I tried and failed three times to get up a steep rocky section that I cleared consistently on the Cannondale. If two well-matched riders went out on these two bikes it wouldn't be much fun for either unless the Cannondale rider remained in the first two assistance modes (and gave the TQ rider a push home when their battery died). Range On the subject of range, I managed 1,000 m of height gain riding only steep off-road climbs in Boost mode before the assistance started dropping off dramatically. Surprisingly, I managed another 200 m before the power cut off completely, although much of that was almost unassisted. While pedalling any push bike feels like you're going nowhere after cutting about in Boost mode all day, riding a 20 kg eMTB without assistance isn't as bad as you might think. In short, I could bank on 1,000 m of climbing and I'm sure you could stretch that beyond 2,000 m with subtler assistance. When it comes to technical climbs, the Moterral SL wasn't perfect in its stock configuration. The super-slack head angle isn't a problem when climbing, but the low BB height (333 mm) requires care to avoid pedal strikes - I'd prefer shorter cranks than 165 mm. The seat tube angle is slacker than I would like when attacking the steepest climbs too, and the stock 27.5x2.4" Dissector rear tire struggled for grip in slippery conditions. Also, longer travel e-bikes with softer suspension provide a little extra comfort and grip when pedalling over rough ground. Don't get me wrong, it still rips when pointed uphill, but when things get tricky it's not as impressive as you might expect out of the box. Swap to a 29" rear wheel (without flipping the geo chip) and the bike levels up. With more ground clearance and a more upright pedalling position, plus a slightly smoother ride over bumps thanks to the bigger wheel, it's more surefooted on technical pitches. Throw in a stickier tire (Schwalbe Tacky Chan, soft compound) and it becomes the ultra-capable climbing machine it ought to be. Descending Once again, I wasn't thrilled with Moterra's handling as it comes out of the box. With the mullet wheels and slack headset setting, the head angle is so slack that it starts to introduce noticeable downsides, both in terms of handling and suspension performance. The steering feels like it wants to keep doing what it's doing; if you're going straight it wants to keep going straight and if you're turning it wants to keep turning. There's a lot of wheel flop at slow speeds and the front wheel feels a long way out in front when committing to tight, loose turns. I don't want to exaggerate here: these traits are subtle compared to many modern eMTBs, some of which are barely a degree steeper, and they're certainly something you can get used to. But I don't perceive any real benefit to going this slack when compared to a head angle in the 63-64 degree range, so it's worth pointing out. Also, the combination of the raked-out fork and relatively skinny (for a 20 kg bike) stanchions seemed to affect suspension performance. The fork sensitivity on small bumps, steps and slap-down landings felt less plush than I was used to. I ended up backing off the compression and reducing pressure relative to my usual Fox 36 settings, which I put down to the slack fork binding due to the mismatch in angle between the load on the tire and the fork stanchions. If you want to stick with the mullet setup (or just want to avoid paying for a new back wheel) I'd recommend trying the headset in the steeper setting. I had a spare 29" wheel from Cannondale so I simply installed that without changing anything else. This steepened the head angle by around a degree while raising the bottom bracket by 10 mm. While still slack, the bike handled more neutrally, with more intuitive steering in particular. The fork sensitivity seemed to improve too, and I returned to my default settings. I didn't notice any downsides in terms of twitchiness and the higher BB didn't make it feel like I was perched above the bike. The rear suspension also felt a little on the firm side at first - not to the point of being harsh, but not as sensitive as I would have liked. I backed off the compression damping and, after experimenting with different combinations of air pressure and damping, ended up with the compression fully open and the air pressure at 220 psi, giving me bang on 30% sag. This is a fairly soft setup for the amount of travel, helping get the traction and comfort I was after, but the suspension would bottom out fairly regularly. The Float X has a decent bottom-out bumper, so this didn't bother me. With an eMTB, suspension sensitivity is important for climbing and descending, so it's worth running a softer setup in my view. With less than 150 mm of travel on tap, this resulted in a compromise to big-hit control. It's worth noting that smaller sizes have less travel than the XL tested here. With the suspension dialled in and the bigger wheel fitted, the bump performance was impressive. While still not the plushest, it has a fairly damped and predictable feel that deals with small to medium bumps sensitively without feeling too soft or getting lost in the travel. The flex-pivot suspension doesn't throw up any unusual quirks. After a couple of rides, I found the Moterra SL easy to ride hard on technical downhills. While weight can be an advantage when plowing through chunder, the kilograms saved compared to other full-power e-bikes pay dividends in complicated sections, particularly when maneuvering the bike through tight trees, sharp corners or off-camber roots. While still not quite the same as riding an analog bike, it's a hell of a lot more intuitive and engaging than a traditional eMTB. Slowing down on steep sections is one area where full-fat eMTBs can struggle, and many SL eMTBs sabotage any advantage with light-duty brakes or tyres. The Moterra's Code brakes with HS2 rotors provide enough power, but it's worth upgrading the rear tyre to help with stopping. The geometry of the XL is decidedly stable, with a long wheelbase, slack head angle and long chainstay, especially in the Xl size. This calms things down when riding rough and fast terrain, and helps the bike carve through fast corners. Yet the Moterra is still easy enough to hop and manual when you need to; it doesn't feel too stuck to the trail. I'd put that down to the generous stack height as well as the modest weight, which makes it easier to pick up the front wheel and move your weight around the bike. That said, when pulling a manual on flat terrain, it takes noticeably more effort than some SL eMTBs with shorter chainstays. Overall, the Moterra SL strikes a good balance between stability and maneuverability, especially when compared to full-fat eMTBs. What's the Best Value? The Lab 71 model that Cannondale sent me to test is frankly obscene. The SL1 spec is essentially identical in terms of suspension, motor, battery and more, plus it comes with into-frame cable routing. Cannondale says it only weighs 200 g more, yet it's over five grand less expensive. Relative to similarly spec'd carbon eMTBs, it's decent value. The base model has identical suspension save for the gold coating and is less expensive again, if by a smaller margin. It's still a lot of money for a bike with a (mostly) Deore drivetrain and brakes, but is worth considering if electronic shifting isn't your thing. Notably, all models come with 170 mm seatposts (150 mm in small). Cannondale Moterra SL Whyte Elyte 150 How Does It Compare? In my review of the Whyte Elyte , I described it as "one of the best handling e-bikes" and I stand by that. The bottom bracket is nearly identical to the Cannondale in the stock setting, and the Whyte can be made even lower, while I chose to make the Cannondale higher. Fortunately, the Whyte has 155 mm crank arms that makes this work (most of the time). On paper, both motors have 600 W peak power, but the Whyte's Bosch SX motor is comparatively gutless and stalls if the cadence drops. They both weigh about the same, but there's no doubt the Cannondale is a better climber (especially when set up as a 29er). On the descents, the Cannondale's extra suspension travel (10 mm at the front and 7 mm at the rear) is noticeable, especially when negotiating big holes and drops, but neither is a self-propelled DH bike. The big difference is the geometry. The Whyte is at least a degree steeper even in its low setting, at 64 degrees. It also has a slightly shorter chainstay and 20 mm less wheelbase. The result is that the Whyte is more neutral handling and agile, but without becoming twitchy. Both bikes are noticeably heavier than any pedal-powered MTB and so the Whyte's extra maneuverability was, on balance, a benefit for me. Technical Report SRAM Code brakes: While overshadowed by the Mavens, the Codes still get the job done with 200 mm HS2 rotors. You could always upgrade to 220 mm rotors if need be. Reverb AXS Seatpost: The 170 mm dropper simply isn't long enough these days - at least, not for an XL. I dropped the seatpost by another 50 mm before each proper descent, which feels ridiculous on a $14k bike. Also, while the twin-button remote is potentially configurable to new technologies, it seems odd to have two buttons to do one job (activating the seatpost). In the heat of the moment, I sometimes confused the twin dropper buttons with the ergonomically similar mode selector just above them, which never happens with a traditional single lever. Pros + Low weight, high power combo is not only impressive but great fun to ride + Adaptable geometry can be radically slack or more conventional + Lightweight spec doesn't make too many compromises for the scales + Stable geometry and (relatively) low weight make for confident handling on tech trails Cons - Potentially too low & slack in the stock configuration - I think it rides better as a 29er, but you can't buy it like that - Non-removable battery and no range extender limits options (though it's still rangier than most SL eMTBs) - 170 mm seatpost is not ideal for bigger sizes - Insane price as tested (though the SL1 spec is much better value) Pinkbike's Take The combination of a high-torque motor and ~20 kg weight is genuinely game-changing in the eMTB world. The Moterra makes other 'SL' e-bikes look limited in range and technical climbing abilities, powering up steep scrambles where mid-power motors struggle and stall. At the same time, it offers a refreshingly "normal" ride compared to full-fat eMTBs when tackling technical descents. The best of both worlds? Pretty much. Perfect? No. The model tested is insanely expensive, though the other builds are more reasonable. My biggest gripe is that in many ways it rides better as a full 29er and for that, you have to source your own back wheel. But if you can live with that and afford the $7k starting price (ignore the most expensive model), it's a serious laugh. — Seb Stott Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Cannondale Cannondale Moterra Sl Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: Cannondale's 2025 Scalpel 1 is a Capable, Modern XC Bike
author: Sarah Moore
article_text: REVIEW Cannondale Scalpel 1 WORDS: Sarah Moore PHOTOS: Tom Richards The latest version of the Cannondale Scalpel was launched in March of 2024, keeping in sync with the cycle of updating it every four years to align with the Olympics, and it had a fantastic first season under the former Cannondale Factory Racing crew. Alan Hatherly won an Olympic bronze medal, became the 2024 XCO World Champion, and took the 2024 World Cup XCO overall title on board it. It also won the XC Marathon World Championships under Mona Mitterwallner and Simon Andreassen. For 2025, Jolanda Neff has signed with Cannondale Factory Racing and will be competing on the Scalpel. Scalpel Details • 120 mm travel rear, 120 mm fork • 29" wheels • Carbon frame • 66.6° degree head tube angle • Size specific chainstays • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • 1780 g frame (Lab 71) / 1980 g (Series 1) • 11.15 kg (25.35 lb) as tested, size Medium • Price: $4,000 - $14,000 USD • cannondale.com The Scalpel has been in Cannondale's lineup for more than 20 years now, and the new version has 20mm more travel front and rear compared to the previous 100mm Scalpel that I tested as a part of the 2021 Field Test . There's also a slacker head tube angle, a longer reach, and a shorter seat tube length in order to accommodate longer travel dropper posts. All models have 29" wheels. While the exact lineup varies from country to country, there are five different models available in North America, with prices ranging from $4,000 to $14,000 USD. All models now come with dropper posts. While the higher-end European models use the single-sided Lefty fork and all models in Europe have a TwistLoc remote lockout, in North America there aren't any lockouts or Leftys to be found, other than on the highest end Scalpel Lab 71 model. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Modern cross-country bikes are incredible and the Cannondale Scalpel is one of the best there is. It feels like a rocket ship on the climbs as you would expect, but descends with a composure that makes it a fun choice even when you aren't toeing a race line. — Sarah Moore Frame Details There are two different carbon frames for the Scalpel, with the top of the line Scalpel Lab 71 model getting the Ultralight Series 0 Carbon frame and the Scalpel 1 I rode getting the slightly heavier Series 1 Carbon frame. The frame weighs 1980 grams for the Series 1 frame without shock and 1780 grams for the highest end Lab 71 frame. For comparison, a Scott Spark SL weights 1870 grams, an Orbea Oiz is 1750 grams, and the new Specialized Epic 8 is 1965 grams for the regular version, and 1795 grams for the S-Works version. While the previous generation of the Scalpel used what Cannondale called Asymmetric Integration, a concept that involved moving the hub and cassette 6mm towards the driveside, which Cannondale claimed created a stiffer wheel, a better chainline, and allowed for shorter chainstays, the new version uses a 55mm chainline which is becoming the new norm and doesn't require you to re-dish a brand new wheel to fit the frame. The Scalpel has also switched over to a threaded bottom bracket from the PF30 standard that was used on the previous Scalpel. While those two updates will make things a little easier for mechanics, they might not be quite as excited about the thru-headset cable routing that all models of the Scalpel now use. The higher end models receive Cannondale's new SystemBar XC-One, a one-piece carbon cockpit with a mouth-like port at the front that the brake, dropper, and remote housing run through. It might not be the easiest to work on, but it sure is sleek looking, especially in the red version on the Scalpel 1. It also comes in a decent 760mm width with the stem coming in a 60mm length on the small and medium bikes and 75mm on the large and XL bikes. The dropper post is 31.6mm and the size small gets a 125mm drop while medium through XL get 150mm of drop. All models have ample room to fit two water bottles. Other details include 2.4” tires on 30mm internal width rims, a threaded bottom bracket, and an integrated wheel sensor that delivers speed, route and distance info, registers your bike, keeps you on top of maintenance, and more, all through the Cannondale App. Geometry The Scalpel's updated geometry puts it right in line with the latest modern cross-country machines. The head tube angle has been slackened by one degree from the model I rode in 2021, putting it at 66.6-degrees. The seat tube angle is a degree steeper at 75.5º, and the reach numbers have grown by 10 to 20 millimetres depending on the frame size, with the size medium getting a 450mm reach, a full 15mm longer than the previous model. Unlike the previous model, there are now size-specific chainstays, starting at 434mm on the size small and going up to 446mm on the XL in 4mm increments. In theory, that should help maintain a similar balance between frame sizes. For the sake of comparison, here are a couple of other modern XC bikes and their head angle / seat tube angle / reach (size M). Specialized Epic 8: 65.9° / 75.5° / 450mm Orbea Oiz: 67°/ 76.5° / 450mm Scott Spark RC: 67.2° / 76.6° / 441mm Yeti ASR: 66.5° / 75.5° / 445mm Suspension Design Back when we tested the previous generation Scalpel in 2021, there were two models to choose from, the 100mm race bike or the 120mm Scalpel SE which we dubbed the "downcountry" version. Now, all race models get 120mm of front and rear suspension, so there is no "SE" model in the lineup anymore. The Scalpel uses a single pivot flex stay with a linkage driving the 120mm shock through its stroke to keep weight at a minimum. Cannondale says that they customize the kinematics on the Scapel by frame size with their Proportional Response Suspension system. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $9500 Travel 120mm Rear Shock Fox Float SL Factory EVOL SV, Kashima Fork Fox Float Factory 34 SC, Kashima Headset Acros ICR Cassette SRAM XO Eagle, 10-52, T-Type, 12-speed Crankarms SRAM XO T-Type, 34T Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA 73mm MTB Wide Rear Derailleur SRAM XO Eagle AXS, T-Type Chain SRAM XO, T-Type, 12-speed Shifter Pods SRAM AXS T-Type Handlebar SystemBar XC-One Flat Stem SystemBar XC-One Flat, carbon integrated bar/stem Grips Cannondale XC Silicone Brakes SRAM Level Silver Stealth, 4-piston hydraul Hubs DT Swiss 240 Spokes DT Competition Race, straight pull Rim DT Swiss XRC 1501 SPLINE ONE Tires (F) Maxxis Rekon Race WT, 29x2.4", EXO Protection / (R) Maxxis Aspen WT, 29x2.4", EXO Protection Seat Prologo Dimension NDR Seatpost Fox Transfer SL Factory, Kashima Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Cannondale Scalpel 1 Test Bike Setup The Cannondale Scalpel 1 comes with Cannondale's integrated SystemBar XC-One Flat bar and stem combo which has a 760mm width and 60mm stem on the size medium. It has a 5° upsweep, 8° backsweep and -6° stem and I was happy to get along with it out of the box. Most of the testing took place on the Maxxis Rekon Race and Maxxis Aspen WT that came on the bike, but I also rode the bike with a set of Schwalbe Rick XCs towards the end of the test period. I rode the bike with 185 psi in the Fox Float SL Factory EVOL SV rear shock, which put me right at 30% sag, and I ran 82 psi in the Fox Float Factory 34 SC fork. Tire pressures were 19 psi front and 21 psi rear. Sarah Moore Location: Squamish, BC Age: 34 Height: 5'7" (170cm) Weight: 160 lb (72.5kg) Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @smooresmoore Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place over the course of the summer and into the fall in Squamish, British Columbia. Trail conditions were dry for the most part, but I got a couple of really rainy days, including a fun race, towards the end of the testing period in October. Squamish's technical cross-country trails are perfect for the Scalpel and the trails on this loop are where the Cannondale feels at home. While I received the bike after this race took place, the course from our local cross-country marathon race, the Haulback , is a perfect example of terrain well-suited to the Scalpel. Climbing The climbing position on the Cannondale Scalpel felt comfortable right out of the box and I took it on a much longer ride than I would usually recommend right off the bat since I just felt at ease on it immediately. The extra length compared to the previous model makes it feel like you're in a more aggressive climbing position and have more weight over the front end of the bike despite the extra 20mm of travel on the fork. That being said, it doesn't feel like you're too stretched out by any means and my lower back agreed with the position, which can't be said for all cross-country bikes (I'm looking at you, Pinarello Dogma XC). On tight singletrack, the 66.6º head tube angle meant that steering was a bit less quick than on some other cross-country bikes I've ridden, but it never felt like I couldn't maneuver the bike exactly where I wanted to and I actually enjoyed the more composed feel of the steering. As for pedalling, the suspension platform feels very efficient and the bike really does feel like a rocket ship on the climbs. Unlike many cross country bikes, the North American version of the Scalpel 1 does not have a lockout or electronic suspension, but it really didn’t feel like that was something it was lacking, even on paved roads and smooth gravel climbs. The European models do come with a lockout, but I prefer having a clean handlebar set up and fewer controls to worry about when I'm redlined, so it was great that the suspension felt firm and efficient without a lockout even when doing hard efforts out of the saddle. When climbs got more technical and required more than just pure power to get up and over obstacles, the Scalpel had ample traction and I never felt like I was bounced off line. The Scalpel made it enjoyable to go out and find technical climbs and challenge myself on them. The bike also felt comfortable over small bumps and trail chatter which made long rides less tiring on my upper body overall. The Scalpel 1 isn't beating any cross-country weight records at 25 lbs (11.34 kg) without pedals in the size medium I rode, but it's still a very respectable race weight and very responsive. For a lighter frame and build, you'll have to look to the $14,000 USD Scalpel Lab 71, which uses the same frame that the Cannondale Factory Team races on. Alan Hatherly's Scalpel in size large came in at 22.5 lb (10.23 kg) when he won the Andorra World Champs on it. If I'm nitpicking, I would have preferred a different dropper post since there are only two positions on the 150mm Fox Transfer SL that the Scalpel 1 comes with so you can't just lower your dropper post a couple of centimeters in rolling terrain. I also found the 34 tooth chaining a bit aggressive, and I prefer it when cross-country bikes have 32 -tooth chainrings, but it’s appropriate for the intended use. Some longer grinds were done at a slightly lower cadence than I would have preferred due to the 34T, but I'm also someone who generally rides with quite a fast cadence. Cadence is quite a personal choice and a chainring is an easy swap to make, in fact many racers change it depending on what race course they're competing on. Descending The Scalpel is a weapon on the climbs, as cross-country bikes have to be to be competitive, but it's also really impressive when things point downhill. That's in part due to the geometry with the Scalpel's 66.6º head tube angle and long reach and partly due to the mix of components. The bike comes with 760mm handlebars, a 150mm dropper post, 4-piston brakes with 180/160mm rotors, and 2.4" tires. The Maxxis Rekon Race and Maxxis Aspen WT tires that come on the Scalpel might look like scary tires with their low-profile tread, but they did a great job of holding traction in a variety of conditions. Descending on the new Scalpel feels more intuitive than it was on the previous model with its longer reach and slightly lower bottom bracket. It especially feels much more intuitive to corner and it maintains traction evenly throughout the corners. The suspension on the Scalpel is supportive and firm without feeling like it beats you up too much at the end of a long descent. Obviously, it won’t be as comfortable as descending on something with more travel, and my triceps and hands definitely got more sore on the Scalpel than they would on a longer travel bike. However, I think the main talking point is that you’re actually having a fun time trying to find flow and pop off of things. You don't have to be "fully on" and concentrated on the descents and you can take a minute to recover from your climbing effort instead of white knuckling your way down descents on the easiest line possible. It also makes the bike more versatile and I found myself reaching for it for more rides as a result. The Scalpel is what we're calling a modern cross-country bike, but I do think it eclipses what we were calling a "downcountry" bike at the XC/DC Field Test just four years ago. There’s a reason why the Scalpel SE is no longer in the lineup, and that's because the race version of the bike is a more capable climber and a more fun descender than that bike was with its updated geometry and 120mm of travel. The one thing I disliked on the descents is that the Fox Float Factory 34 SC fork is quite noisy and there's a distinctive noise that I can only describe as "squelching" that comes from it every time it's compressed. It was so noisy that even the people I was riding with noticed it when I popped out of the exit of a trail. It was silent on the climbs, but quite noticeable on the descents when it moved through its travel. Otherwise, the bike was quiet on the downhills. It's also worth noting that it's not an issue you would have if you live in Europe, since the Scalpel comes with the Lefty fork there. Cannondale Scalpel 1 Specialized Epic 8 S-Works How Does It Compare? Both the Specialized Epic 8 and the Cannondale Scalpel were released in 2024 and have 120mm of travel, fancy integrated one-piece handlebars, and several World Cup podiums apiece. The geometry of the two bikes is also quite similar, with both having a 75.5° seat tube angle and a 450mm reach on the size medium. The Epic 8 gets a slightly slacker head tube angle at 65.9° compared to the 66.6° of the Scalpel. The Epic 8 has 435mm chainstays across the board, while the Scalpel's chainstays range from 434mm on the size small up to 446mm on the XL. Both bikes thoroughly impressed me and it's really no surprise that they've been so successful on this year's World Cup circuit. As far as climbing position and geometry of the bikes, they're very similar and both maintain excellent traction on tricky sections of technical climbs. The frame weights are also almost identical if you're comparing the Lab 71 version to the S-Works version and the Scalpel 1 to the regular Epic 8. On the descents, both are also really fun to ride. I'd say that the Epic 8 felt slightly more forgiving and left me less tired at the end of long descents, but the Scalpel felt sturdier and a touch more stable at speed. I rode the S-Works version of the Epic 8 and the second-tier level of the Scalpel 1 so it's not quite apples to apples if we talk components, but I will say I preferred the AXS dropper post on the Epic 8 to the Fox Transfer SL. I also thought that the Flight Attendant system on the Epic 8 was a bit distracting with the noises that it makes on the climb, but that was preferable to the squelching noise that the fork on the Scalpel 1 made. This is perhaps one of the more difficult comparisons I've done since both bikes are remarkably similar and the choice between one and the other will largely come down to brand preference and which components you'd like to ride. Both are excellent choices for cross-country racers and riders who just want to ride really light and fast bikes. As far as pricing goes, Specialized offers more models, but the Cannondale starts at $1,000 USD less with a $4,000 USD option. Which Model is the Best Value? Out of the five version of the Scalpel that Cannondale offers in the United States, it's the $6,500 USD Scalpel 2 that I think offers the best value. That model has a SRAM GX Eagle T-Type wireless electronic drivetrain, RockShox SID Select+ suspension both front and rear, and Cannondale's HollowGram XC-S 27 carbon wheelset. The frame is the same Series 1 Carbon frame as the $9,500 USD Scalpel 1 that I rode, although it's undoubtedly a bit heavier without the premium components that the Scalpel 1 is specced with. That being said, since it's exactly the same frame for $3,000 USD less, so there's the opportunity to pick and choose which components you upgrade and save some weight. Scalpel Lab 71 / $14,000 USD Scalpel 2 / $6,500 USD Scalpel 3 / $5,500 USD Scalpel 4 / $4,000 USD Technical Report Maxxis Rekon / Rekon Race tires: I'm a fan of the Rekon tires. They look scary with their minimal tread, but somehow manage to hold grip much longer than you think they should. I preferred them over the Schwalbe Rick XCs I put on the bike later on in the season since I felt they maintained traction better in both dry and wet conditions. Fox Transfer SL dropper post: The Transfer SL post saves weight by only having two positions – all the way up and all the way down. I didn't have any issues with it from a reliability standpoint, but it's not as intuitive to use as a regular dropper and it's quite loud when it changes position, letting your fellow riders know exactly when you've pressed your dropper post lever. Sizes medium through XL get 150mm of drop and with that amount of drop, I found myself wishing there was a middle setting available, especially for rolling terrain. SRAM Level Silver Brakes: Many of Pinkbike's editors have found SRAM's Level 4-piston brakes to be underpowered, even for XC usage, but with the set on the Scalpel 1 I always felt like I had enough power to slow down on the descents. The 180mm front and 160mm rear rotor might have added a couple of grams to the weight of the bike, but it's definitely worth it. Pros + Comfortable and efficient climber + Capable and fun descender + More versatile than most cross-country bikes Cons - Noisy fork and two position dropper Pinkbike's Take Cross-country race bikes of the past were steeds that I would only hop on if I was committed to training for a big race since I felt like they compromised the fun factor of mountain biking overall and really restricted the terrain that I was excited to ride. The Cannondale Scalpel might be classed as a cross-country bike and have the accolades to back that claim up, but it's also a fun bike to ride. It still feels extra speedy on the climbs, so you can do more climbing in a ride than on any other type of non-motorized bike, but once things are pointed downhill, you're not just holding on for dear life until the trail points upwards again; you're actually able to find flow and enjoy the descent. Sure, your arms and hands will get tired more quickly than on something with more travel, but not at the expense of having fun. There's a reason why the "downcountry" category has gone out of fashion and I believe it's because cross-country bikes like the Scalpel have eclipsed them on the fun and the fast. — Sarah Moore Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Cannondale Cannondale Scalpel Author Info: Sarah Moore Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 30, 2011 1,624 articles Pinkbike Content Manager

title: Review: Canyon Lux Trail - Mellow Marathon Machine
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: REVIEW Canyon Lux Trail WORDS: Dario DiGiulio PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio / Eric Mickelson We last touched on the Canyon Lux Trail our Downcountry Field Test, where it didn't fare as well as the competition. Held back by compromised geometry and a more XC-focused spec than others in the category, it irked some testers in the group. Canyon seems to have taken that feedback to heart, with the new Lux Trail sporting a host of changes that seem to address some of those problems. With revised sizing, kinematic, and categorization, the Lux feels a bit more at home in its skin. The focus still feels decisively bent towards efficiency and speed, but read on to see just how things shook out. Lux Trail Details • Carbon frame • 29" wheels • 115mm travel, 120mm fork • 67° head angle • 76° seat angle • 412-500mm reach, 480mm size L • 435mm chainstay length • Measured weight: 28.5 lb / 12.9 kg • US Price: $3,599 - $6,199 USD • canyon.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take It feels like a bike made for very long days in the saddle, covering as much ground as possible at a quick clip. While you might have to skirt around some of the gnarlier features and trails, you'll probably make up for it when you're pinned on the road transfers. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details Canyon took the opportunity presented by reworking the Lux Trail's front triangle and really ran with it. The level of integration here is impressive, with in-frame storage, a hard-mounted multitool, and two water bottles to boot. The bike feels like it's been accessorized with long days in mind, and the quick-access tools and stashes make for a speedy fix should something go awry. There are two frame trims to choose from, with the differences essentially coming down to weight. For an average size Medium frame, the CF models are claimed to weigh 2101 grams, with the more expensive CFR frames clocking in at 1936 grams. With equivalently light parts kits fit to each, there will definitely be some weight-weenie friendly builds in there. The Lux now features a UDH rear end, making all your hard-shifting Transmission dreams come true. Front and rear lockout mean you can turn your mountain bike into a road bike at a moment's notice, and might make some post-ride pumptrack sessions more fun. Quickdraw CO2 holster. Quite a bit of cargo capacity down there. While the improvements are many and welcomed, there are still some vestiges of the compromised designs of prior generations still at play here. The main two that come to mind are the flat-mount rear brake (this is for road bikes, please keep it out of the MTB world), and our favorite enemy, the through-headset cable routing. Both details make for a bike that requires specialized components that usually don't perform at the level of equivalently light or convenient designs. Geometry & Sizing In the name of keeping things quick (a theme with this bike), here are the beats: the seat tube angle is 1.5° steeper, landing at 76° across all sizes. The head tube angle of 67° is half a degree slacker than the prior generation, while the reach, stack, bottom bracket drop, and chainstay length remain essentially unchanged. This addresses a primary complaint about the prior gen bike, which was just how stretched out the seated position was, given the relatively long reach and slack seat tube angle. The new Lux Trail's numbers are more in line with typical trail bike sizing, giving it both a more comfortable climbing position and confidant descending geometry. I've been riding a size Large, and found the seated position quite comfortable. The stock handlebar is a bit flat for my taste, but a little rise improved the ride quite a lot. Suspension Design The Lux Trail retains the linkage-driven single pivot layout of the prior model, but implements a hanging rocker meant to improve the small-bump performance. The 115mm of rear suspension is much more progressive than the Lux World Cup, with a left-hand lockout setup that allows for quick changes in suspension characteristic. The Open mode is full fat, letting things move as much as your setup allows for; Pedal mode firms things up, and holds the suspension higher in travel; Locked means locked, expect no movement. Behold, chart. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $6199 Travel 115mm Rear Shock RockShox SIDLuxe Select+ Remote, 210x50mm Fork RockShox SID Select+ Remote, 120mm Headset Canyon Integrated Cassette SRAM GX T-Type Crankarms SRAM GX T-Type Chainguide Canyon Integrated Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM GX T-Type Chain SRAM GX T-Type Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM GX T-Type Handlebar Race Face Ride, 760mm, 10mm rise Stem Race Face Ride, 35mm clamp, 50mm length Grips SRAM TwistLoc Ultimate Brakes SRAM Level Silver Stealth, 4p front 2p rear Wheelset DT Swiss XRC 1501 Spline S12 Hubs DT Swiss 240 EXP Spokes DT Swiss Competition, straight pull Rim DT Swiss XRC 1501 Tires Maxxis Rekon / Rekon Race 3C EXO 2.4" Seat Ergon SM10 Pro Seatpost RockShox Reverb AXS, 31.6mm, 100-150m Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Lux Trail Test Bike Setup The highly integrated nature of the Lux frame leads to a bike that feels like more of a single unit than an assembly of parts. Obviously all the same rules of bike setup still apply, but the Canyon shows up pretty close to ready to ride out of the box, in an attempt to make that user experience a little bit easier. It helps that many of the components on this build are familiar, so there was a little less to dial in over time. The Maxxis EXO tires with low-profile Rekon and Rekon Race treads work best at lower pressures - 22 front and 23 rear felt optimal to me. You're less liable to peel a tire on a little bike like this, plus the added grip pays dividends on tricky terrain where the geometry might not be helping out too much. The one component I swapped out for a long period was the stock handlebar, which felt too low and narrow for me. 760mm is manageable, but the 10mm rise wasn't my cup of tea - I added 20mm to that and things felt more balanced. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info This bike feels particularly well suited to backcountry exploration, where distance and scenery take precedent over burliness of terrain. The suspension lockout and climbing geometry mean you'll be comfortable in the saddle for a long day out, and the many on/in bike conveniences mean you'll be well provisioned for any mishaps far from the trailhead. There are many routes that fit this bill, but this one came to mind as a good use of a bike happy to put in the miles to get somewhere beautiful. Climbing The Canyon Lux Trail is a cross-country bike, no matter what the marketing lingo may lead you to believe. To me, this is a point of pride, not a demerit, as the speed an XC bike can carry on climbs and flat sections of trail is second to none. While the total weight of this bike may be a few pounds over race-focused XC machines, you hardly notice on long days in the saddle. The pedaling platform feels consistently supportive and energetic, especially with the added efficiency from the 3-position lockouts on the fork and shock. I found myself in Pedal mode the most, splitting the middle between a more traction-rich Open and the completely rigid Lock. Pedaling the bike in Open mode is far from frustrating, as the kinematic seems to keep things from bobbing too much under power, while still keeping decent grip at the rear wheel. By no means is it the stickiest short travel bike I've ridden of late, but there's enough traction to keep you from burning out any time you leave the saddle. The Lux Trail feels particularly well-suited to seated pedals over mellow gradients, where climbs come gradually and overall speed is rather high. The low front end keeps you in an aggressive position (and a rather uncomfortable one, unless you're used to the more road-style fit), and the 76° seat angle puts you in a nice spot above the pedals. With a higher rise bar installed, overall pedaling comfort increased for me, but your mileage may vary. With the bike in Lock mode, the bike feels more than ready for some mad-dash sprints on pavement, or pinner climbs up very smooth paths. Ultimately it's a bit of an extra third mode, unless your rides regularly contain long stretches of smooth paved pedaling. Descending The climbing performance of a bike doesn't have to come at the expense of descending prowess, but it seems that somewhere along the way here sacrificed capability in service of efficiency. The Lux Trail is quick to remind you that it's an XC bike in all the ways you'd hope to forget, with touchy steering and a nervous feeling over technical sections of trail. You can get down some decently challenging bits of trail, but you have to do so carefully and more slowly than you might like. Flat, somewhat rough sections feel quite good, on the other hand, thanks to suspension that happily moves through the middle of travel to keep the wheels from getting too hung up on obstacles. The wheelbase of the Lux is quite short, making it feel plenty agile when you're trying to hook the bike through squiggly turns and tight sections of trail. The downside is overall stability is rather low, compared to other new-age XC bikes that are starting to rake the front out to near trail bike figures. If you're in pursuit of a more traditional feeling XC bike, with some added comfort from an increase in travel, then I think this could easily fit the bill. The fairly steep 67° head angle corners nicely on smooth trails and flatter turns, but can quickly become a hazard if you're putting the fork deep into travel while trying to negotiate the bike. Anything too technical can overwhelm that delicate composure and have you looking for a safer line to veer towards, though the brakes might not slow you down enough to get there. I'll touch on it further in the Technical Report, but the Level 2-piston brakes are a poor match to a bike that could otherwise be a fast and efficient off-road traveler. When things line up nicely and you start to push the Lux Trail into some heavier compressions, the rear suspension does a solid job of keeping you from slamming into the bottom of travel, though the huge bottom-out bumper in the SIDLuxe shock is probably partly to thank for that. When the brakes are biting as well as they can, the bike does give a good amount of rear-wheel traction, allowing you to cut speed without blowing through the minimal tire tread out back. The bike has a tendency to pitch forward under heavy braking in steeper terrain, but ultimately it feels like the Lux Trail is meant more for carrying speed through rough sections rather than navigating steeps. Canyon Lux Trail Specialized Epic 8 How does it compare? These two XC machines are currently in my regular rotation, and both represent a modern take on the genre, so it felt worth comparing them while fresh on my mind. Put simply, the Epic blows the Lux Trail out of the water when it comes to on-trail capability and well-roundedness. For traditional racing, both have their merits, though given the increasingly technical nature of XC courses I'd probably still err towards the Epic's more evolved geometry. The Lux offers more creature comforts in the form of frame integrations, though the Epic's in-frame storage has a much larger opening and still allows for two water bottles. Climbing capabilities are similar between the two, with the Canyon erring a bit more on the side of efficiency and sacrificing a little grip at the rear wheel. For those looking solely to put long days in the saddle, where the technicality of the trails are incidental, the Canyon may be the better choice. Finally there's the matter of price. Two of the Lux Trail's complete builds are less expensive than the standard Epic frameset, and the most expensive bike in the Lux lineup is essentially the same price as the S-Works frameset. In other words, the Epic is a very expensive bike, and the Lux is not. Performance might not be the same on the descents and tech climbs, but it's worth considering your use case before throwing down the cash. Which Model is the Best Value? Canyon's direct-to-consumer business model allows for some pretty staggering prices on complete bikes, and the Lux Trail is no exception. Nothing on this list is dirt cheap, but when you consider the quality of the components hung on the frame, the aftermarket cost starts to get a bit daunting. My pick for value would be the CF8 model, sporting a Shimano XT drivetrain and brakes, Fox Performance Elite suspension, and DT Swiss carbon wheels. All for $4,999 USD (currently on sale for $500 less), you get a swath of some of the best components for the category. Technical Report RockShox 3P TwistLoc: The cockpit cleanliness of this system when used in tandem with the AXS dropper is impressive, keeping things tidy and tucked away. That said, I can't help but wish that it could be done with a standard lever arrangement, so you could run standard grips and toggle the lockout with a quick jab of the thumb. Minor difference in the scheme of things, but I think some users would appreciate the option. Luckily, the TwistLoc function is consistent and unobtrusive for my hand size and position, it just took some getting used to the slide-on grips. SRAM Level Silver 2-Piston Brakes: While the 4-piston Levels feel appropriate for XC bikes, the 2-piston variants feel pretty awful regardless of the context. Part of this falls on Canyon for deciding to keep the flat mount rear brake standard on this bike - despite the fact that flat mount has no place in the mountain bike world - but SRAM didn't really run with the prompt either. The brakes feel underpowered and slow to bite, and the 160mm rear rotor doesn't help matters much. Metallic pads (same shape as SRAM Red road brakes) marginally help the situation, but not enough to justify the choice. RaceFace 35 Handlebars: These are the only other spec dud, though the frustration is far less potent than with the Level 2P brakes. For a bike focused on weight, efficiency, and all day comfort, a handlebar seemingly made of solid aluminum bar stock feels a bit out of place. The weight of these is impressive, and translates directly into a jarring feeling when compared to just about anything else I swapped in. I ended up running a carbon riser bar for a while, mostly for fit reasons, but dropping a significant bit of weight helps as well. Pros + Efficient and comfortable pedal platform + Good geometry for mellow pursuits + Clever, well-integrated frame details Cons - Poor performance in technical terrain - Size-specific rear center would be nice Pinkbike's Take If you're in pursuit of a more traditional feeling XC bike, with some added comfort from an increase in travel, then I think this could easily fit the bill. What the Lux lacks in downhill capability is somewhat made up for in pedaling efficiency and amenities for a long day out, making it a solid choice for someone who wants a comfortable cruiser to explore mellow terrain. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Canyon Canyon Lux Trail Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Canyon's New Tempr CFR Cross-Country Shoes
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Canyon are dipping their toes into the shoe world with the launch of the new Tempr CFR. No, I'm not sure where the second 'e' from that word went – the vowel shortage seems to be continuing over at Canyon. In any case, the new shoes are designed for XC riding and racing, a high end option that's already seen success on the World Cup XC circuit. For now, there are only two models in Canyon's lineup – the Tempr CFR Road, and the Tempr CFR Off-Road version that I've been using for the past few months. The shoes are essentially the same, other than the soles – the Off-Road version gets a lugged sole made from Vibram's Megagrip rubber. Tempr CFR Details • Two Boa Li2 fit adjust dials • Full length carbon inner plate • Vibram Megagrip outsole • Colors: black, grey, white • Sizes: : 37 - 48, 1/2 sizes available from 40.5 - 46.5 • Weight: 340 grams (size 45) • MSRP: $329.95 USD • canyon.com The CFR Tempr is priced at $329.95, and is available in sizes 36-48 with half sizes from 40.5 to 46.5. There aren't women's or men's specific models – Canyon claims their fit system allows the shoes to work well with both high and low volume feet. Black, grey, and white are the color options - this black and white scheme was part of a limited run. Boa Li2 dials are used to adjust the fit. The branding is fairly minimal, just a small logo on the outer heel. Details The Tempr CF has the looks you'd expect from a top level XC shoe, with a full carbon inner plate to help ensure they're stiff enough for those hard pedaling efforts. The shoes have a sock-like tongue that's fully integrated into the upper, and then two straps wrap over the top (Canyon call the system PerformFit), connected to two aluminum Boa Li2 dials. The idea is that the positioning of the straps helps to really lock the heel in place, eliminating any unwanted lifting. The upper has a pressure relief zone located around the widest part of the forefoot (right around the base of the pinky and big toes), which is designed to alleviate hot spots or pressure points that can develop. There's less material in those locations, making it easier for the shoe to conform to a rider's feet. The shoes also come with custom insoles that Canyon developed in conjunction with Solestar. As mentioned, Vibram takes care of the sole's rubber with their Megagrip compound. The lugged pattern is fairly open, in order to keep it from getting clogged up with mud on sloppy days. There aren't any screw-in toe spikes, but that feature doesn't seem to be quite as prevalent as it once was with XC shoes. Performance Out of the box, the overall fit of the Tempr shoes felt spot-on for my size 45, medium-width feet. The heel cup is nice and defined, and with the Boa dials snugged down the Tempr has a very locked-in fit. Adjusting those dials on the fly is easy too – all it takes is rotating them in one direction to tighten, the other to loosen, or pull outwards to release all of the tension at once. My initial rides were in the 1.5 – 2.5 hour range, and for that amount of time the shoes worked great. They're stiff, snug without being uncomfortable, and simple to adjust. I'd happily use them in an XC race, or for a fast post-work spin. However, that comfort started to diminish on longer rides – even with those pressure relief zones the shoes started to hurt on the outside of my pinky toe, no matter how loose or tight the Boa dial was set. That sentiment was shared by my colleagues over at Velo about the road version. Riders with narrower feet might have better luck, but for me, the Tempr worked best for shorter rides – these aren't the shoes I'd grab for a big all-day adventure. Speaking of adventures, they work okay for the occasional off-bike scramble, but at the end of the day this is still a very stiff, racing-oriented shoe, and not what you're going to want to be wearing for any extended hike-a-bike sections. Ventilation It's been hotter than usual here in the Pacific Northwest (and yes, I'm sure there's someone in Phoenix who will tell me I don't know what real heat is), which meant I've had multiple days to test the Tempr's ventilating abilities. In that department, I'd say they're about average. I didn't find my feet getting hotter than expected, but they also aren't as airy as something like the Fizik Vento, which uses a screen-like material that provides great ventilation. The Tempr's tongue fabric is light and breathable, but the fit system's two straps end up covering most of it. The outer fabric has started to tear around the inner ankle bone area. Durability As far as durability goes, the outer fabric layer is starting to show some signs of wear around inner ankle area, the spot that occasionally rubs against cranks. There's another layer underneath that seems to be more durable, but for the price I'd like to see a tougher material. Speaking of material toughness, the outer heel cup is made from a very hard plastic. That's good for foot support and protection, but it can do a number on a frame's paint job if you're not paying attention – I found that out after an especially wet ride where the hard plastic + mud combo left some permanent scratches on a carbon frame's chainstay. Pros + Boa Li2 system is very easy to use + Excellent heel retention, very secure fit + Very comfortable on sub-2.5 hour rides Cons - Comfort diminishes on longer rides - Average ventilation - Material around inner ankle showed signs of wear sooner than expected Pinkbike's Take Canyon's entry into the shoe market is slightly unexpected, but they're certainly not the first brand to decide to broaden their line of bike-related accessories, and it'll be interesting to see what other models are added in the future. The Tempr is a solid first step (sorry, shoe puns are just too easy), although riders with wider feet may still want to look elsewhere for comfort on longer rides. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Canyon Canyon Tempr Cfr Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Chris King's New US-Made MTN30 Aluminum Wheels
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Chris King has been producing American-made bicycle components for nearly 50 years, and have been based out of Portland, Oregon, since the early 2000s. In 2022 they launched their first mountain bike wheelset, which used carbon rims made in Utah by FusionFiber. Now, an aluminum wheelset has been added to their catalog, also with US-made rims. Designed for trail and all-mountain usage, the MTN30 AL wheels are available in 29”, mixed, and 27.5” configurations, with either Centerlock or 6-bolt disc mounting. (Side note: as someone who prefers 6-bolt mounting, it’s great to see that Chris King has returned to offering both options after going Centerlock-only for a bit). MTN30 AL Details • Wheel size: 29", 27.5", or mixed • US-made aluminum rims • Internal rim width: 30mm • 32 Sapim Race spokes. 3-cross • 5-degree engagement • 6-bolt or Centerlock hub options • 2-year rim warranty, lifetime hub warranty • Rim weight: 29” 595g / 27.5” 565g • Weight: 2162 grams (mixed wheelset) • Price: $1,250 USD • chrisking.com The rims are laced to Chris King’s MTN hubs with 32 Sapim Race J-bend spokes and brass nipples. My mixed-wheel setup weighed in at 1035 grams for the front and 1127 grams for the rear, for a total of 2162 grams. The wheels are priced at $1,250, and come with a 2-year warranty against defects for the rims, and a lifetime crash replacement program where a new rim can be purchased at a discount. Those terms aren’t as generous as what we’ve seen from companies like Reserve or Race Face recently, where their rims are covered by a lifetime warranty. The MTN 30 wheels do have a lifetime warranty on the hubs, though. The rims have a slight external lip that's intended to help prevent pinch flats. DESIGN Chris King hasn’t disclosed exactly who is making their aluminum rims (it’s not Velocity), but they were designed in-house, rather than being chosen from a catalog of existing options. The numbers are fairly typical for modern mountain bike wheels, with a 30mm internal width and a 19mm height. The rim bead has an external hook that’s designed to help ward off pinch flats - a wider surface is less likely to cause a puncture when a tire is smashed against it. The rims have a matte black finish, but the hubs are available in 10 different hues for riders that want to add some color to their ride. As for those hubs, they’re the same Mountain Hubs that Chris King has been producing for years, with a proven design that uses a RingDrive mechanism (pictured above) with 72 points of simultaneous engagement. That equates to 5-degrees between engagement points, putting them in the 'quick but not crazy quick' engagement category. A special tool is required for a full service, but a basic bearing maintenance doesn’t require anything out of the ordinary. Even the hub bearings are manufactured in-house. Chris King's ring drive has 72-points of simultaneous engagement. A T10 torx is used to adjust the preload ring on each hub that pushes against the angular contact bearings. PERFORMANCE I’ve had these wheels on either a Santa Cruz Bronson or a Raaw Madonna for the last three months, and they’ve seen more than their fair share of wet conditions. After all those muddy miles the wheels are still spinning smoothly; impressively smoothly in fact - get them spinning on the bike or in the truing stand and they’ll just keep going and going. There aren’t any dents or dings to speak of, and I haven’t had to true them at all - they’re still properly tensioned and free of any hops or wobbles. I did need to snug down the preload collar on the rear wheel after a couple of rides, a quick procedure that eliminated any side-to-side play, and I haven't had to touch it since. As for ride feel, I’d place the MTN30 wheels on the stiffer side of the spectrum. They’re stout and solid, the type of wheels that you don’t give a second thought about smashing into corners as hard as possible. They’re billed as being for trail and all-mountain usage, but I don’t see any reason they wouldn’t work well for even more aggressive riding. They’re noticeably stiffer than Santa Cruz’s Reserve HD wheels, and I wouldn’t call those wheels especially soft to begin with. For bigger riders that don't like the vague feeling that can occasionally come along with more 'compliant' wheels this will be a positive attribute, or really for anyone that places a high priority on wheels that are responsive and precise. On the flip side, these wouldn't be my first pick if I was searching for wheels that were on the more comfortable side of the spectrum. Harsh is too strong of a word to describe the MTN 30 wheels, but they also don't mute that much trail feedback. It wouldn't be a wheel review without mentioning the hub sound. With these wheels, I found the signature angry bee sound was fairly subdued, and I didn't find it to be obnoxious at all. As for the functionality, there was zero skipping or slipping from the freehub, and the 5-degrees between engagement points was quick enough that I never thought twice about it, even when working my way through slower speed, more technical climbs. Comparison Chris King hasn’t made it easy for themselves in the comparison department - there are currently multiple options that offer a better price and weight ratio. Reserve’s 30|HD aluminum wheels are the most obvious contender - those are 100 grams lighter, $550 cheaper, and now come with DT Swiss' DEG hub, which has an oversized 90-tooth ratchet ring for 4-degrees between engagement points. There are even carbon wheels for less than the Chris Kings - Roval’s Traverse HD wheels come to mind; those are 1830 grams laced up to Industry Nines 1/1 hubs. Along with being lighter, they have an excellent ride feel, and a very wide rim bead that does a good job of preventing pinch flats. Of course, for some riders the fact that the rims and hubs are made in America is going to be part of the appeal. I'm going to avoid wading too deep into the political sea, since it's looking pretty polluted at the moment, but there is something to be said about purchasing items that are manufactured close to home, no matter where you live. Reserve HD: 2036 grams / $699 USD Roval Traverse HD (carbon) : 1830 grams / $1,110 I9 hubs Chris King MTN30 AL: 2162 grams / $1,250 Play Video Play Current Time 0:00 Loaded : 0% Progress : 0% Duration Time 0:00 Mute 1080p 720p 480p 240p Quality 480p Fullscreen This is a modal window. Realistically, it’s the made-in-USA angle that’ll steer some riders towards these wheels, and Chris King’s hub quality shouldn’t be downplayed either - those are very nice hubs, and with occasional maintenance should last a lifetime. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images.
