Easy access - all air valves and tool-free damper adjusters are located on the upper end of the eyelet; main spring, bladder pressure, LSC, HSC, and rebound. 1, 1, 2, 3, 5... not all clicks have an equal effect. Descending Is the bladder as squishy and supple as you’d hoped to hear? Well, yes and no. On one hand, the buttery transition in shaft direction rivals the monster-sized Vivid and Float X2 shocks. Unfortunately, there was a pesky bit of top-out. When the bike became unweighted, a slight bump could be felt as the shock returned to full travel. In the case when the rear wheel was on the ground, the shock functions extremely smoothly. And what about the air spring performance? The Topaz X produced a generic air spring ride that took a touch more effort to ease into the travel than the rest of the bunch, although I wouldn’t say it wallows by any means. Altering either air spring volume wasn’t the play here as progression was spot on. A similar level of support was found on the Marzocchi. These two shocks provided a softer mid-stroke with a later, more aggressive ramp-up. No negative spacers were installed to begin with, therefore no actions could remedy the initial force required to break it into the travel. Sitting in the middle of the price bracket, the Topaz X provides value with DVO’s vault of tuning arrangements and the on-the-bike external adjustments, even if they are finicky. Another win is that no special tools are required for regular air can servicing which comes up at 150 hours of ride time, or about once a year. Are those new external features and moderate $550 USD pricing enough to hold onto the fans of DVO’s bladder-type dampers? Considering that three other high-end shocks give you equal, if not more adjustability and climbing functions for roughly $200 more, they’ll be hard to look past. Changing clip-on volume spacers only involved releasing the air spring pressure and the help of a strap wrench to twist off the air can. Pros + Changes in shock shaft directions are very smooth + Tool-free LSC, HSC, and rebound adjusters Cons - We observed a small clunk or top-out as the shock returned to full travel - LSC and HSC adjusters do not produce equal changes per click - Increase in LSC adjustments is forfeited in favor of T3 lever (climb switch) from Topaz 3 Stay tuned for more Shock Week content, including a roundtable discussion of all the air shocks we tested. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shock Week Shocks DVO Suspension Dvo Topaz X Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Endura MT500 Burner Flat Pedal Shoes
author: Matt Beer
article_text: The Endura MT500 Burners - where scuba gear meets flat pedal shoes. If there’s a brand that knows about wet weather, it’s the Scottish company, Endura. They entered the footwear market roughly one year ago and have more recently developed a waterproof flat pedal shoe, the MT500 Burner. Endura's MT500 line of apparel caters towards demanding conditions and these shoes are no different. Wrapped up by a neoprene ankle cuff and lace cover, the flat pedal Burner shoe uses a speed lace forefoot closure and a velcro strap to lock your foot in place, exactly like the clipless version that appeared first. The exterior materials appear smooth and durable, yet the inside is soft and cushioned. Endura MT500 Burner Details • StickyFoot Grip and Dura rubber sole • Speed Lace system & Velcro strap • Lace cover and ankle gaiter • Reinforced toe and heel box protection • Sizes: EU 38-47 / US 6-13 / UK 5-12 (half sizes 6.5-10.5) • Weight: 442 grams (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $189.99 USD / £159.99 GBR • endurasport.com On the underside of the sole lies a dual-tread design which uses the StickyFoot Grip and Dura compounds. The lugs on the heel and toe use the harder wearing Dura rubber to dig in while hiking, while the Grip compound is placed in the pedal contact zone and features a tighter-spaced tread. The shoes come in black or an extremely-not discrete bright orange and retail for $189 USD / £159 GBR. In terms of the size range, the limited to 38-47 EU, but there are half sizes available between 39-43 EU. No matter how impervious the rest of the shoe is to water, having adequate overlap between the cuff and pants is the key to staying dry. The MT500 Burners have plenty of real estate in that area without being restrictive or tricky to slid into. I thought the material was pilling up after the first ride, but later discovered that these raised bumps were intended. As you'd imagine, they're tingly and mildly annoying to stand on. FIT Plush isn’t a word you’d typically use to describe bike shoes, but there’s an element of that when you step into the Burners, again drawing back to that surf bootie feel. Fitting them does require some finagling to keep the velcro bits from sticking together, however, that’s a small price to pay for keeping mud and water out. In terms of length and width, I’d label them as true to size in both directions, giving my 42/EE foot more than enough space. The volume is plentiful as well, even with a wool riding sock. I needed to pull the speed lace fully closed, nearly touching the two sides together. It’s worth noting the arch height is similar to Shimano, or on the lower side of Specialized shoes. The closures and fit ward off any heel lift, primarily due to the forefoot strap that locks it down. Similar to the Shimano GF800GTX shoes, the shallow heel feels slightly vulnerable to rolling and would prefer that it rests deeper in the shoe. If cinched down tightly, that strap can cut into the forefoot when hiking up steep grades; mind you, I never had any issues when pedalling in the Burners. Locked and loaded; the MT500 Burners stay tight and are well-shielded. The tread has proper traction on the heel and toe for hiking. The pedal platform has a tighter, lower profile design for consistent grip, albeit, not the tackiest. PERFORMANCE Throughout the test period, I spent days hiking, digging, and pedaling in the Burners and found them to be grippy on and off the bike, comfortable to wear for extended periods of time, and very sufficient for cold, wet rides. Claims of being waterproof and breathable are often overshot, but I never found the Burners to turn on the clammy, greenhouse effect. Through the worst soakings, the Burners keep your feet warm, similarly to a wet suit. Even during a -10°C cold snap, I returned with toasty toes. That extra layering, combined with a stiffer sole and tough exterior makes them slightly duller underfoot. For some riders though, they may be looking for this type of freedom to move around the pedals. With extra long pins the StickyFoot Grip is soft enough for extra long pins to sink into for a secure hold. Off the bike, the tread pattern and rubber is reassuring when hiking through mud, snow, or dense brush, which is one downside to Five Ten’s dotted sole. Shimano GF800GTX Five Ten Trailcross GTX PRICING AND COMPARISON Finally, after what seems like forever, there is a worthy selection of waterproof flat pedal shoes on the market and some of the best come from the usual suspects. The Shimano GF800GTX and Five Ten Trailcross GTX have been favorites in the past, but their price points are $10 and $30 USD above the $189 Endura MT500 Burners. To be fair, Burners design and construction are much different. They’re far more robust in terms of coverage and support with their high ankle cuff and dual closure system. They aren’t as vulnerable to splashing through deep river crossings and are considerably warmer. It really takes a full dunk for the Burners to get wet, and even then the materials hold heat impressively, which can’t be the same for the GF800s. The grip doesn’t latch on like Five Ten’s Stealth Rubber and is still less than the GF800GTXs. Granted, not everyone is looking for maximum grip. Staying warmer and drier may be a trade off some riders are willing to sacrifice for a marginal loss in traction. Compared to the two competitors mentioned above, the sole is a tad thicker and stiffer. The downside there is a less connected feel with slightly less control. DURABILITY Winter took a turn from being mild and soggy to dry and cold around the New Year here in Squamish. Post-ride clean ups have led to the Burners being hosed down and placed on the boot dryer regularly. On the exterior of the shoe, all of the seams are holding solid while the velcro tabs and speed lace closures haven’t lost their bite. The shell materials have staved off the odd nick from pedal pins and given a helping kick to some ground tools while tuning up the trails. Inside though, the material used to line the foot bed has small bumps which feel odd, especially when wearing thin socks. So far, the sole has held up well to even the nastiest pins from the Tectonic Altar pedals . Should anything go wrong with the Burners, Endura offers a 90-day guarantee on any MT500 series product. Pros + They do a great job of keeping water out + Should they get fully submerged, the Exoshell FW neoprene material keeps your feet warm + Excellent grip for hiking/pushing the bike Cons - Not the stickiest sole rubber - Bumps on footbed are annoying - Some riders may want a more flexible shoe for improved control/feel Pinkbike's Take Endura hasn't messed around when they set out to build solid footwear for harsh environments. For flat-pedal enthusiasts, the MT500 Burners may not have the same dexterity or tackiness as their competitors. When paired up with ultra-grippy pedals, though, they're still sticky enough to charge confidently and are excellent at staving off the winter elements. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Endura Endura Mt500 Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Every Pair of MTB Shoes I've Worn This Year
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shoes can be an annoying investment to make, as they're one of the bits of kit that sees the most abuse on a regular basis. I've been guilty of forcing shoes well past their point of retirement, as once you've found a good pair it can be hard to move on. My collection ballooned this year, and left me with a bit of a pile to pick from on a daily basis. I ride just about every day, so these all got a fair bit of use, though the favorites ended up seeing more time on the pedals than others. Read on for a breakdown of each pair, who I think they'd be good for, and finally which ones came out on top. Some notes about me before we get into it, just for a frame of reference. All of these are a size 45 or 45.5, which is my standard street shoe size. I have a fairly wide forefoot, a narrow heel, and a high arch - that said I think arch support is a hoax to keep your feet soft. I've spent a lot of time pedaling in all of these, and each pair has seen a fair bit of hiking around in the woods. My preferred cleat position is about as far rearward as a typical channel allows, with the cleat as far as it can go outboard to keep my feet close to the cranks. Crank Brothers pedals unless otherwise noted. So Many Shoes https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/Or9DMd1n Copied Live 00:00 18:43 18:43 Contents Crankbrothers Mallet Trail First Degree Fuse AC Ride Concepts Hellion Clip Shimano GE9 Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Clip Fox Union Canyon Scampr Giro Chamber III Shimano GF8 GTX Vans SK8-High BMX Crankbrothers Mallet Trail Mallet Trail Details • Boa dial, hook & loop strap • TPU-reinforced toebox • Ripstop upper • Fiber-reinforced shank • Sizing: 35-49 • Price: $240 USD • Weight: 490g / shoe • crankbrothers.com The Mallet DH sold me on Crankbrothers shoes, and while the Mallet Trail is quite different, they uphold a positive legacy. The upper construction on these is lighter than you'd expect, but feels like it offers plenty of protection where it counts - the toe, heel, and side of foot are all well guarded. The gaiter is well executed, and keeps larger debris from working its way in. Crankbrothers touts their Match concept as being an ideal soul-bonding of shoe and pedal - while I do use Crankbrothers pedals, and like how these shoes interface, I wouldn't say it's drastically different than any of the other options here. You'll have to use some shims to space the cleat out, but once that's done it does lock into the pedal nicely. I've found the soles of these get fairly chewed up, but they remain useable even after plenty of time on the pins. High quality materials. Gumshoe gets a bit torn up. The stiffness is fairly neutral on these, suiting heavy-duty usage without sacrificing walkability. My only real complaint is that they feel a bit tall underfoot, as if you're further separated from the pedal you're standing on than with some of the other options here. Not a big issue, but a detail I noticed when switching back and forth. Pros + Durable, high-quality construction + Comfortable balance of stiffness and give Cons - Simple fit adjustments - Tall-feeling sole First Degree Fuse AC Fuse AC Details • Lace up, hook & loop strap • Agressive outsole • Seamless welded upper • Foam-reinforced tongue • Sizing: 36-47 • Price: 175 € • Weight: 492g / shoe • firstdegree-mtb.com I was initially quite excited about these, as the details, aesthetics, and feel all added up to a shoe that seemed pretty ideal for my use case. Sadly, one detail kept them from working for me: the cleat channel. For some reason, the cleat channel on these is very far forward in the shoe, rendering even the rearmost position a bit awkward feeling to me. I could deal with it and get used to the feel, but the rearward cleat position that all of the other shoes on test deliver was sorely missed here. The other annoyance with the channel is how deep it is relative to the sole - you'll have to run quite a few spacers to match these up with Crankbrothers shoes, and even Shimano pedals required some tall standoffs. Besides that detail, the shoes are quite nice. The stiffness is similar to the Crankbrothers, and the uppers are robust thanks to a smooth welded construction. The recessed laces feel safe from snags, and stay in place nicely once you've snugged up your fit. Speaking of fit, the forefoot on these is nice and wide, without adding too much bulk - points for comfort. Bungee for lace storage. Bummer about the cleat position. Thanks to the fairly aggressive outsole on these, their pedal contact is very strong. Perhaps too much if you're someone who likes to break free easily, in which case you'll have to space the cleat out even further. I liked the locked in feel, and found these to be some of the grippiest shoes for hiking and scrambling around, should you find yourself in those situations. Pros + Nice details, great fit + Grippy sole Cons - Cleat channel needs redesign - Many cleat spacers required Ride Concepts Hellion Clip Boa Hellion Details • Boa dial • D3O padding throughout • Cordura upper • Nylon shank • Sizing: 39.5-47, women's available • Price: $220 USD • Weight: 586g / shoe • rideconcepts.com Ride Concepts overhauled their lineup earlier this year, introducing a few models under the Hellion banner. I've been using the more gravity-focused Hellion Elites, and these: the Hellions. As the more trail-focused version of the two, these shave down on some features in pursuit of simplicity and lighter weight, but ultimately they just feel like the lesser of the two options. The sole, cleat positioning, and rubber compound are all great, offering solid connection to the pedal and great support for the foot. The main upper material is very robust, and offers great protection for the foot. Where these fall short for me is in the smaller details, such as with the Boa loops that started fraying almost immediately. It seems like brushing through undergrowth has been enough to cause these to fray quite a bit, and though they're holding strong so far, the design feels a bit flawed to me. Not quite as burly as their Elite counterpart. Same great sole and shank though. Luckily the general fit of these is quite comfortable, with a generous forefoot and tight heel cup. I've found that the single Boa dial isn't adequate for tightening the whole shoe down in one go, requiring you to either pull the lacing through or flex your foot around to distribute tension as you crank things down. Compared to the Elites, which use two Boas, these feel a bit less secure. A note: these and the Elites have the furthest back cleat channel of any of the shoes on test, so far rearward that I actually moved my cleat up a bit in the channel - a first in quite a while. Those who like a cleat at the forward tip of the shoe (why?) might not love this feature, through I really do. Pros + Comfortable fit, good pedal connection + Protective and generally durable Cons - Weak point details - Boa tension is hard to distribute Shimano GE9 GE9 Details • Boa dial, hook & loop strap • Torbal flexible midsole • Debris collar at ankle • Fiber-reinforced shank • Sizing: 38-48 • Price: $250 USD • Weight: 507g / shoe • ride.shimano.com I've put this pair through the wringer, partially because they were the only shoes I had on hand for a little while, partially because they are very comfortable. If you have wide feet, these are plenty accommodating, with a big toebox and a fairly loose fit. One the flip side, if you like a locked-in ski boot feel this probably won't be your ideal shoe. The material feels soft and pliable, with a fair bit of extra padding throughout. While that helps with protection and some comfort, it makes these feel a bit warmer and more prone to getting soggy. I also found that I had to alter my cleat position to keep the inner portion of the shoe from rubbing on the crank too much, due to how wide they are. Lotta' rubbin'. Nice hiking tread. Due to that rubbing, I ended up sawing through the buckle that retains the hook-and-loop strap in very little time, necessitating the addition of a little DIY slot in the upper material. That did the trick, and they've continued to work despite. The plastic Boa lacing loops are great, and assure that the laces tension up the shoe every time, despite grit, grime, and wear. Overall, the feel of the upper and sole of these is softer and more malleable than most of the other shoes on test, which makes them very walkable but takes away some of the racy stiffness some folks want. If you do want more stiffness, Shimano released the GE900HS , which is stiffer and a little slimmer than these shoes. Pros + Wide and comfy + Nice lacing, good tread traction Cons - Soft and baggy feel isn't for everyone - Strap durability Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Clip Boa Hellion Elite Details • Two Boa dials • D3O padding throughout • Cordura upper • Nylon shank • Sizing: 39.5-47, women's available • Price: $262 USD • Weight: 596g / shoe • rideconcepts.com As mentioned earlier, these are essentially the bigger, better version of the Ride Concept Hellion - hence the name. The fit felt a bit potatoey at first, but after a short break-in period they started cinching in to my foot shape and feeling like home. The sole is plenty stiff without being ridiculous, and the overall fit feels comfortable enough for all-day use. The heavy weight on the scale luckily translates to impressive durability in real world use, as these have held up remarkably well to my abuses. The laces are pulling smoothly, the upper fabric is intact, and the sole is still functioning well despite getting chewed up by the pedals. In my book, a bit more weight feels like a reasonable tradeoff for a shoe that lasts longer than just one season. Boa bumpers. Holding strong despite a tone of use. That weight also manifests in a very protective shoe, one that has certainly saved my toes on a handful of occasions. The D3O padding seemed a bit gimmicky initially, but after having some talus rocket into the top of my foot I became convinced of its utility. Same goes for the plastic toecap, which deflects nicely off of trailside obstacles. The Hellion Elites are warm and lack much breathability, but they're not unbearable even through the summer months. These have proven to be a pair that I reach for first, thanks to the well-rounded design and functional details. A note: as I mentioned about the Hellions earlier, these have the furthest back cleat channel of any of the shoes on test, so far rearward that I actually moved my cleat up a bit in the channel - a first in quite a while. Pros + Very protective + Good balance of comfort and utility Cons - Warm, not very breathable - Heavy Fox Union Boa Union Details • Two Boa dials • Welded and reinforced toebox • Ripstop upper • D3O insert, two arch support options • Sizing: 37-47 • Price: $275 USD • Weight: 506g / shoe • foxracing.com The Unions are one of the stiffer pairs in this lineup, with a sole that gives very little even under massive loads. They feel precise, with good pedal contact and sticky enough rubber to lock in. The rigid last doesn't hike very well, but does the job when need be. For me, the Unions have one critical flaw: the material of the upper comes way too far up the ankle, pushing into the tendon at the front of the ankle (tibialis anterior). The rubbing was at its worst on climbs, where the repetitive motion of pedaling really ground things down. After trying to break them in naturally, I resorted to a bit of user modification, opening up that corner with a pair of snips. This helped quite a bit, but didn't completely alleviate the issue. Comfort modifications. Rigid. Fit-wise, the Unions are on the narrower side of things, but they don't feel too uncomfortable for me. The rubberized toe cap keeps things from opening up with use, but my feet had enough room for long rides. The dual Boa dials keep things tight and well-adjusted, and while they lack some of the dial protection that other shoes feature, I haven't had any damage occur. Pros + Stiff and precise + Nice fit Cons - Annoying cuff detail - Not great for hike-a-bike Canyon Scampr Scampr Details • Boa dial • Knit upper • Tri-panel tension fit • Solestar insole, Vibram sole • Sizing: 36-48 • Price: $160 USD • Weight: 451g / shoe • canyon.com Canyon first introduced these shoes to the gravel / adventure cycling market, but from the jump they struck me as a potentially ideal trail bike shoe. The defining feature here is the knit upper, which feels like a durable sock with some structure to it. The single Boa dial cinches the whole thing together, and you're off to the races - or the graveling or adventuring, whatever. Thanks to that upper construction, the Scampr is breathable and light, with a somewhat disconcertingly airy feel. Great on hot days, but not so great when it comes to impact protection. That's entirely not the point here, so it feels like an unfair complaint, but don't expect these to protect your upper foot from much. There is a solid toe cap, and the sole offers plenty of support for real descending. These are my favorite shoe for short-travel bikes where I know I won't be pushing the limits, and for longer days in hot temps. Extreme sports sock. Stiff enough where it matters. The stretchy fit of these should accommodate a wide variety of foot shapes, and felt very comfortable to me. The fit system Canyon implemented here is excellent, hugging your foot tight without any faff. Even if the Boa were to break, the shoe's cuff is sturdy enough to keep them on your foot, in a pinch. My one complaint comfort-wise comes down to the Solestar insole, which has an odd bump right in the middle of your forefoot, and never felt quite right to me - I swapped it out for a random insole that fit, and I've been happy. Pros + Very comfortable and breathable + Excellent fit system Cons - Very little protection in upper - Solestar insole Giro Chamber III Chamber Details • Lace up, Velcro strap • Padded microfiber upper • Low-profile tongue • DH-focused shank • Sizing: 36-48 • Price: $200 USD • Weight: 538g / shoe • giro.com The Chamber III is on par with the Fox Unions as the stiffest two pair of shoes on test here, but the Giros take things a step further with a tighter and more aggressive fit and feel. The last of these is quite narrow, and might not work with those with wider feet as a result. If that doesn't disqualify them for you, then they provide a very locked-in feel, with plenty of fit adjustment thanks to the traditional lacing system and a Velcro strap to cinch things down. Giro reduced the heft and thickness of these quite a bit compared to the last iteration of the Chamber, and we're left with a shoe that feels nicely pared down, while still offering enough protection and durability. The protection here feels more like padding than a shell, especially when compared to shoes like the Hellion Elite - both work, but the shell method seems to withstand a bit more abuse. As you'd expect with a very stiff shoe, the hiking performance isn't amazing, but Giro did give these sticky enough rubber to keep you upright on all sorts of dumb scrambles. Padded faux leather upper. Real stiff, quite narrow. I found the narrow fit required some lace adjustment here and there over long days, opening up slightly with enough time. The lacing feels a bit fiddly compared to the Boa dials featured on most of the other shoes, but you can get a very secure and stay up on your bunny ear technique. As a package, these are a pretty ideal choice for those looking to put a ton of power into the pedals, and get little to no feedback from those efforts or impacts. Pros + Stiff, locked-in feel + Precise fit system Cons - Narrow fit - Fussy to adjust Shimano GF8 GTX GF8 Details • Boa dial • Gore-Tex liner • Tall elastic cuff • Low-temperature sticky rubber • Sizing: 38-48 • Price: $220 USD • Weight: 513g / shoe • ride.shimano.com Shimano's GF8 is the only dedicated mountain bike flat shoe I'm including here, because it's my favorite that I've used this year. They are far from perfect, but the important things are close to my ideal. They used a softer, low-temperature sticky rubber for the sole, which hooks to pedals remarkably well, no matter how foul the weather. The extra traction is welcome, and luckily hasn't some at the expense of a sole that lasts - these still look good despite plenty of use. The stiffness also feels well-suited to my preference, with a bit of give to allow for good pedal feel, without sacrificing impact protection. Yes, they're also waterproof, but that feels far less critical for me, despite living in a notoriously wet place. The upside is a dry foot through splashes and light rain, the downside is a permanently wet foot as soon as any water gets in past the cuff. Waterproof means it keeps just as much in as it lets out. I'm willing to live with that tradeoff, as the boot fit is nice and secure, albeit annoying to get on and off. Weak materials and a very tight cuff. Excellent rubber. The biggest complaint I've had about the GF8 GTX comes down to durability - the liner material has proven to be far too light-duty for the job. Because the cuff is so tight, you have to really yank on these to get them on (or get a shoehorn), and the very thin heel area is prone to tearing as a result. I've continued to use these well past that patch giving up the ghost, but I'd like to see better design on a pair of shoes that otherwise ticks all the boxes. Pros + Great pedal feel + Very sticky rubber, even in cold Cons - Poor durability - Frustrating to put on Vans SK8-Hi BMX SK8-Hi Details • Lace up • Duracap high-wear reinforcement • PopCush footbed • Vulcanized rubber • Sizing: 34.5-47 • Price: $90 USD • Weight: 519g / shoe • vans.com These are just a good old pair of Vans. There's nothing special and mountain bike specific about them, they just happen to be one of my favorite shoes for the job. If protection is important to you, look elsewhere, but if grip and pedal feel are your main goals, then the classic high top is a pretty compelling option. I picked these up to see if the BMX moniker actually improved anything in terms of durability and usefulness for the bike, and so far they've impressed. It's no mystery that Vans durability has been pretty horrible of late, but the rubber and uppers has held up on these so far. Though they lack any sort of upper foot protection, the UltraCush insoles do take the sting off any bottom-of-foot strikes. Crankarm protection. Classic. I don't love the pointed-toe fit of Vans, but after a long break in they do start feeling like home. A simple lace-up fit keeps you locked in if you want it, though these are anything but stiff. The beauty of Vans is how much pedal feel you get, and how well the sole holds onto pedals. They're really not optimized for mountain biking, but that hasn't stopped them from being a great option for the right use cases. Pros + All the pedal feel + Great grip Cons - Lacking protection - Pointed fit Final Choices For those looking for a great all-rounder shoe, I'd recommend the following: 1. Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Boa Clip 2. Crank Brothers Mallet Trail 3. Giro Chamber III For shorter travel bikes and lighter use, the Canyon Scampr is my top choice. For pure downhill use, the stiff and precise feel of the Giro Chamber III would be my pick, with a nod to the Fox Union despite some issues with the design of the upper. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: EXT Aria Air Shock
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Jockeying for top spot in the air shock markets means brands need to bring something innovative to the table. Modern expectations such as plush operation, reliability, and high and low-speed compression adjusters are expected at the least. The Italian suspension manufacturing, EXT, aimed to do exactly that with their air sprung damper, the Aria. Translating simply to "air" in Italian, the Aria sets EXT apart from the usual suspension brands with new features that haven’t been seen before in an air shock. Taking a similar approached from their Era fork, the Aria shock uses two adjustable air springs: the main self-equalizing positive/negative spring and a ramp up chamber to change the progression. In other words, the mid-stroke support and progression can be tuned more independently, without having major effects on the initial breakaway force. The Aria is one of the few air shocks on the market that features a hydraulic bottom out control as well, but it's not slim in shape or inexpensive. Aria Details • Metric sizes: 210x50-55, 230x57.5-65, 250x67.5-75 mm • Trunnion sizes: 185x50-55, 205x57.5-65, 225x67.5-75mm • Independently tuneable main and ramp up air chambers • Hydraulic bottom-out control (HBC) • 5 damper adjustments: high and low speed compression, rebound, HBC, lock-out (Lok 2.0) • Weight: 659 grams (205x65mm) w/o hardware • MSRP: €1,060 EUR / $1,150 USD (includes high pressure shock pump) • extremeshox.com How the Dual Air Chamber System Works EXT calls their dual positive air chamber AS3 technology. This unique patented design uses two dynamic seals to separate three different chambers. The main air chamber indicated by the “+” symbol on the black air valve features a self-equalizing negative air chamber while the nickel coloured “++” valve controls the progression. Unusually high resting pressures are required for the Aria’s ++ chamber to operate compared to standard air shocks. The ++ chamber can be pumped up to 600 PSI for maximum progression, or as low as the main air spring pressure for less ramp up. Due to those high pressures, a proprietary analog pump is needed and included with the Aria. The major benefit of the system combines two individually tuneable air springs to give a much more linear leverage curve versus a traditional air shock which can fall from the beginning to middle of the stroke, often referred to as “wallowing”. By allowing for independent changes in the air chambers, you can see on the how white line of the AS3 spring produces a straighter line versus the red dashed line of a typical air spring. Increasing the + pressure equates to a more linear change affecting the mid stroke while increasing the ++ pressure has a more noticeable effect on the end stroke progression. This leads to fewer compromises versus a traditional air shock that uses volume spacers to increase the progression, such as a high stored spring force. Furthermore, the Aria requires a strict and tedious set up procedure. The instructions are clear but there's a lot of numbers floating around. An air pressure chart shows the recommended starting + and ++ pressures, as well as the equivalent coil spring weights. It’s critical to follow these steps closely and give yourself time to allow for the initial set up. Damper Adjustments The Aria uses the same damper design as EXT’s heavy-duty E-Storia coil shock, however, slight modifications have been made to account for the dynamics of the different spring types. Externally, there are five adjustments on the Aria: a single rebound dial, the hydraulic bottom out control (HBC), high and low-speed compression, plus a Lok 2.0 climb switch lever. The high and low-speed compression adjusters have 14 clicks of adjustment, while the rebound has 16. On the HBC, there are 12 clicks. The Lok 2.0 switch can be set anywhere in its range, but firms up quickly once it nears the closed position after 90 degrees of rotation. Those compression circuits are said to be independent of one another, as is the rebound control. Each click or two does have a noticeable change on the operation of the shock, although that could vary depending on the bike’s kinematics. To change the low-speed compression and HBC, a 4mm allen key is needed. For the high-speed compression adjuster you’ll have to reach for a 12mm socket or spanner. Only the rebound dial can be turned by hand, but 3D-printed caps that snap onto the high-speed adjuster are out there. The position sensitive adjustable HBC slows the compressive force towards the end of the stroke. This can be more desirable than increasing the ++ pressure since the air spring return force remains unchanged. Price and Weight Made in Italy, EXT’s Aria offers adjustments like no other air shock on the market. Unsurprisingly, that makes it the most expensive shock on the market too. At 1060,00€ / $1,150.00 USD, it’s nearly double the price of the $699 Rockshox Vivid Ultimate, which also features hydraulic bottom-out resistance. Weighing 659g, the Aria is 79g lighter than the Vivid (738g). Ironically, that’s as much as EXT’s Storia LOK V3 coil-sprung damper, with a 475# coil. Setup Nukeproof Giga 297 Test Bike Details Rear travel: 180 mm Shock stroke: 65 mm Average leverage ratio: 2.87 Overall progression: 25.5% Fork: EXT Era 170 Rider weight: 78 kg Main air spring pressure: 250 psi (24% sag) Ramp chamber pressure: 400 psi Rebound: (from closed): -8 High Speed Compression: -12 Low Speed Compression: -10 Hydraulic Bottom Out: -10 EXT provided a few Aria dampers over the course of this drawn out review. The first was tuned to the progressive nature of a Nukeproof Giga. Further down the road, I also installed another Aria on the Ibis HD6 with a specific tune as well. Neither of those starting points are special treatment though - EXT has a catalog of tunes to match your bike’s kinematics. Unboxing the Aria reveals the proprietary, high-pressure pump, mounting hardware and a detailed setup guide. The guide lays out the inflation procedure, suggested damper settings, and a chart that correlates the air spring pressures with the equal coil spring rates, however, that is only helpful if you know which one you would use on said bike. Otherwise, you’ll need to make a guess-timate, check the amount of sag, and fine tune from there. To begin the inflation procedure, high-pressure ++ (nickel) air valve is first inflated to the prescribed pressure. Next, the + (black) valve is pumped up in 50 psi increments. It’s important to note that these increments are counted after cycling the shock and the pressure has been equalized each time. Installing and inflating the shock took about 20 minutes - much longer than a standard air shock. In order to reach the 250 psi in the + chamber, the equivalent to the 500# coil spring on the Nukeproof Giga, this procedure required eight cycles of installing the pump and equalizing the shock. Once inflated though, the dual air chambers mean that altering the progression doesn’t require removing the air can or extra parts, like all other air shocks. In the case of the Giga, EXT’s guide correlates a 500# coil spring to recommended pressures of 250 and 480 psi in the + and ++ chambers. That provided roughly 24% sag - less than the coil spring achieved (30%). I’d go on to drop the ++ pressure down to 450 psi to decrease the progression though. EXT’s damper setup guide includes a baseline of clicker adjustments. For the given spring rate on the Giga, I toggled between 7 and 8 clicks from closed on the rebound, 10 and 12 out on the low and high-speed compression. Due to the combo of an air spring and the Giga’s progressive leverage rate, I rarely found the bottom of the travel, even with the HBO open, so that stayed 10 clicks out from closed. Bikes with less progression are where the advantages of the Aria’s ramp chamber and HBO make the most sense. With that said, I was able to dial back the ++ chamber from the recommended 480 psi to 400. That toned down a firm ramp up to make the second half of the travel closer to the Storia coil shock that it replaced, but brought along the benefit of support early on in the stroke too. The HD6 was spec'd with a Fox Float X2 and the pressure hovered close to 180 psi , depending on the type of riding I'd plan to tackle. Not knowing the equivalent coil spring rate, I choose 200 and 390 psi to sit at 30% sag, the equivalent of a 400# coil spring. At those numbers, the HD6 felt choppy and reluctant to break into the stroke, so I dropped the + pressure down to 185 psi and that’s when the Aria’s dual air springs started to work their magic. Performance The Aria isn’t your average air shock and that’s evident after navigating the first few features on the trail, however, it brought a few surprises along the way. While there were good days aboard the Aria, there were also some let downs, but let’s start on a positive note. Set to the pressures listed above, the Aria provided an unmatched level of mid-stroke support that no other air shock attained on the Giga. The damper performance is equal to EXT’s coil versions, which both provide exceptional control with slightly heavier hydraulic sense, even when the compression adjusters are wide open. This was most apparent on the HD6 and for the majority of the time I ran the Aria’s high and low-speed compression adjusters close to fully open (HSC -12, LSC -10) on that bike. With the dual air chambers, the Aria has the capability to be tuned more linearly than other air shocks - an attempt to have a consistent “coil-like” feel throughout the stroke, and while that’s true on the trail, the sensitivity isn’t all that I hoped it would be. As for the noise level of that damper action, the Aria is louder than a Vivid or Float X2. Throughout the test, I tried multiple Aria dampers, some with re-designed seal layouts. The production version uses three “notches” in which the chambers equalize. One shock in particular had what I’d describe as a “stepped” feel. Near 30% travel on both shocks, a bump could be felt as air moved in its internally intricate ways, most noticeable on low-speed compressions. Each shock broke into the travel smoothly though. On three occasions, the Aria developed a top-out problem, eventually leading to the main positive air chamber failing and the shock would become stuck at full travel. Discussions with EXT confirmed the inflation procedure was performed correctly and after three attempts to resolve the issue (with new shocks), we arrived at the same result. It’s unclear if the trunnion mount-style shock of the Giga caused that issue, but I never ran into those problems with any other shock on that bike either. Fast forward a few months and onto a new test bike, the Ibis HD6. During the Aria's time on the HD6, it worked impeccably and showed no signs of air pressure changes, top out dilemmas, or inconsistencies. That leaves me with mixed feelings about the reliability of the Aria. I do have to tip my hat to EXT for designing that clever dual-air design. The dual air chambers and HBC offer the advantage of balancing mid-stroke support, progression and bottom-out control without a harsh ramp up or springy kickback of too many volume spacers. However, there are proven competitors out there that are more dependable for a lower cost. EXT Aria Ohlins TTX2 Air How Does It Compare? We would have loved to include the Aria in our Shock Week group test last year, but at the time the air valves didn’t clear the shock tunnel on our baseline bike, a Santa Cruz Nomad. EXT has solved the clearance issues, although it does requires a unique air can for Santa Cruz bikes. Given the various shocks I’ve installed on the Nukeproof Giga, I would rank the Aria as one of the best-performing dampers out there. The TTX2 is smoother throughout the travel but finding additional mid-stroke support comes with a compromise in which the initial stroke firmed up. For adjustments, the Ohlins TTX2 Air houses rebound, high and low-speed adjusters, but with fewer clicks, potentially letting the user arrive at a sweet spot sooner. Progression is first chosen by way of two different size air cans, depending on the bike’s leverage rate, and then adjusted internally using volume spacers. There’s no hydraulic bottom-out control either. Again, the Aria’s most impressive feature, the dual air chambers were able to be tuned in a way to provide support in the middle of the stroke without firming up the start of the travel- something that other air shocks can’t deliver in the same fashion. When switching back and forth between the two, it was clear how the mid-stroke of the Aria allowed for more mid-stroke support - the ability to push into the bike to make dynamic movements without diving deeper into the travel - compared to the TTX. Pros + Dual positive air chambers can be tuned individually to work with frames of all leverage types + One of a few air shocks on the market with hydraulic bottom out control + Mid-stroke support is unrivalled by any other air shock Cons - Tedious setup procedure the requires proprietary high pressure pump (incl.) - Inconsistencies and failures in some air springs - High price point Pinkbike's Take In the past, I've always appreciated the performance and reliability of EXT's coil shocks. Unfortunately, that wasn't totally true in the case of the Aria. Initially, the Aria didn't meet our expectations of being buttery smooth. On multiple occasions, the air spring began to top out. It then proceeded to fail, resulting in the shock becoming stuck at full travel. Further down the road, EXT sent another Aria damper, which was fitted to a different test bike. That specific shock didn't let us down after that. When the Aria was functioning normally, it provided brilliant mid-stroke support and bottom out control that can't be matched by other air shocks. I can live with the lengthy setup procedure and proprietary pump given how well this shock can perform but for the price, reliability shouldn't be on your mind. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shocks Ext Ext Aria Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: EXT Arma V4 - One of the Best Shocks Gets Even Better
author: Matt Beer
article_text: EXT Arma V4 Shock Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/vwGFc92e Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 10:45 Extreme Suspension is a serious brand name, but that’s because their mountain bike suspension products are too. The high-end Italian dampers are built on years of engineering and race experience, which makes evolving their goods ever more ambitious. The Arma V4 is the latest iteration of their premium, gravity-oriented damper. Along with addressing top-out concerns through a newly developed hydraulic mechanism, EXT has improved the user experience on the Arma V4 by reshaping the adjusters to use common MTB tools. It's a little thing, but it makes things easier out on the trail. Arma V4 Details • Adjustments: low-speed compression, high-speed compression, rebound, bottom-out control • 14mm DLC-coated steel shaft • Position sensitive rebound (PSR) • Spring rides on thrust bushings • Locking preload collar • Weight: 448g (250x75mm, w/o spring) • Made in Italy • MSRP: $1,155 USD / €1,069 EUR • extusa.bike The Arma V4 is a mono-tube shock with an oversized 14mm, diamond-like carbon (DLC) coated steel shaft and other low-friction components throughout. Positioned primarily for downhill riding, the Arma V4 offers four damper adjustments: rebound, low-speed compression (LSC), high-speed compression (HSC), and hydraulic bottom-out control (HBC) - the function that popularized EXT shocks. The 4mm allen key head for the low-speed compression adjuster lies inside the 5mm high-speed compression dial (black). The hydraulic bottom-out control also uses a 4mm (silver head). The 14mm DLC steel shaft and slimmer bottom-out bumper. Arma V4 Details From the outside, the Arma looks to have the same functions as the 3rd generation, but inside, there are two dramatic changes at play. In the reservoir, the internal floating piston (IFP) separating the nitrogen gas and oil has been replaced by a bladder design. The low pressure of 55 psi is said to avoid cavitation and have low hysteresis (the lag as oil flows between compression and rebound). The flexible bladder is also said to lower friction in the system by eliminating the dynamic seal previously found in the IFP. It also eliminates the need to change IFP lengths per shock travel, making production or after-sales changes simpler. The biggest news may be the position-sensitive hydraulic system developed to eliminate top-out when the shock rebounds. A one-way valve increases the rebound damping force as the shaft returns to full travel in the final few millimeter of stroke. At the other end of the travel, EXT still applies a hydraulic bottom-out control, activating through the last 20% of the shock stroke. This is internally adjustable for the corresponding stroke length, so you don’t lose HBC when travel is reduced. Externally, it can be tweaked to your liking with 10 clicks of adjustment. Two smaller, yet welcomed additions are the removal of the plastic debris-clearing washer on the shock shaft and the high-speed compression adjuster. The HSC is now turned with a 5mm allen key head instead of a socket. The spring preload collar is fixed in place by a small C-clip that can be removed with your fingernails or a small pick. There's also a set screw on the collar to keep the preload from backing off as the spring is compressed. Sizes and Weight As we mentioned, the Arma is largely a gravity-oriented shock, so it’s funny to see that EXT offers (or can build) it in smaller sizes that you’d normally find on a trail or XC bike. You’d probably want a climb switch, which the Arma doesn’t offer. In any case, the Arma V4 comes in standard sizes from 210x47.5mm to 210x55mm, 230x57.5mm to 230x65mm, and 250x67.5mm to 250x75mm. Then there’s the trunnion sizes ranging from 185x47.5mm to 185x55mm, 205x57.5mm to 205x65mm, and 225x67.5mm to 225x75mm. On the scales, our 250x75mm shock weighed 475g with the hardware (448 w/o), which is 108g lighter than the 2026 Fox DHX2 (583g). EXT claims their springs are amongst the lightest on the market. The EXT 475 lb spring weighed 392g, whereas the 450 lb Fox SLS weighs 406g. It’s also worth pointing out that EXT spring will fit most dampers on the market with the help of their adaptive thrust bearing spacers. Setup I began with the Arma V4 installed on an Intense M1, and then later in the season installed it on the new Scott Gambler. I was curious to see how this pair worked together since one of Scott’s engineers happened to be running the same shock on his Gambler. We discussed the stock tune, which happened to be the same as the M1, however, we both agreed that the compression damping was a bit firm for our liking. I reached out to Alba Distribution for assistance. The lighter compression tuned opened up much easier on small chatter and square-edge bumps. The range of adjustment remained wide enough to dial in the compression, closer to what I’d prefer if I were racing, where shorter laps with higher control would outweigh all-day bike park comfort. Setup Details Rider weight: 80 kg/176 lb (with gear) Spring: EXT 475, 1.5 turns of preload Sag: 30% Bike: Scott Gambler w/long chainstay, low BB, 30% progression shock mount Travel: 210mm Average leverage ratio: 2.8 LSC: -7/14 (7 clicks from closed, 14 in total) HSC: -8/15 R: -5/-14 HBC: -3/10 A major win for the Arma V4 is the move to allen-key head compression dials. Considering that the Scott Gambler houses a multi-tool in the shock tunnel cover, making changes on the trail is even more convenient. The LSC adjuster is turned with a 4mm allen key, while the HSC uses a 5mm. Compared to before, adjusting the Arma’s compression dials would mean returning to your staging area and hauling out the toolbox each time. The HBC is still adjusted with a 4mm allen key and the rebound remains tool-free. Performance EXT has always leaned into firm or heavy damping, and that’s still prevalent in the Arma V4. The original tune was deployed for the Intense M1, but also aligned with the Scott Gambler since it has a similar leverage curve. I found the compression damping, particularly the HSC, to be very firm in the middle position. Unless that was set to wide open, square-edge hits weren’t absorbed well and the impacts were transmitted through the bike. After chatting with EXT and Scott, Ben Arnott at Alba Distribution sorted out a lighter compression tune, and we were in business. With the new tune installed, my preferred setting landed near the middle of the damping range. Finding that magic combo wasn’t difficult. Feeling the difference between two, or sometimes just one click of the rebound, is discernible on the trail. Each click is mechanically well-defined and felt by hand. Top-out, that clunk experienced when the shock shaft returns to full stroke, has to be one of my greatest pet peeves when it comes to an otherwise perfectly functioning suspension component. I wasn’t bothered by that on the previous Arma, but it has plagued other EXT shocks I’ve ridden, as well as other high-end dampers like the Ohlins TTX2M. The new position-sensitive rebound is a surefire solution to remove any chance of top-out from occurring, even if you like super-fast rebound. How Does It Compare? In talking about rebound, Ben pointed out that EXT’s HBC circuit doesn’t have any effect on the rebound damping due to the oil flow pathway. That can’t be said for every shock that has a hydraulic bottom-out control. I’m not making a direct comparison in terms of ride quality, but dollar for dollar, the Vivid Coil is a sweet deal at nearly half the price. Otherwise, if you’re willing to shell out for a premium shock, the Arma V4 holds some unique selling features that separate it from the rest of the bunch. Like the Vivid, the Arma only has one rebound adjuster, compared to the Fox DHX2, which has both high and low-speed rebound. This separation can be exceptionally useful for balancing the rebound of a bike with a highly progressive leverage ratio. Although I don’t doubt some suspension wizards could navigate that with further setup or tuning, having two rebound adjusters within arm's reach is convenient. On the other end, the Arma’s HBC is now matched with a slimmer bottom-out bumper. For how simple they are, elastomers do wonders for stopping that clang at the end of the travel, but they’re a spring, so the restoring force is not damped. I found the larger bottom-out bumper on the DHX2 to have slightly more kick-back than the Arma when using full travel. Prices (shock + spring): User Experience One small quibble I’ve come to notice, and maybe it’s just bad luck, but EXT’s hardware seems to fall on the low side of frame dimension tolerances. More than once now, I’ve noticed that the reducing sleeve sits loosely in the shock mount location. When setting the sag, the preload collar turns smoothly and can be locked in place with the nylon grub screw. It’s a small nuisance, but I’d like to see the puny flat-head screw replaced by one with an allen key head to match the other damper adjusters. Trail-side convenience is also a factor since most multi-tools typically don’t house a flat-head of that size. If you’re not paying attention when installing the spring, you may misjudge seating the thrust bearing, only to find out after mounting the shock on the bike that it was misaligned, which in turn causes the spring to sit loosely in place. The thrust bearings are totally worthwhile. I never once experienced any binding from the spring on the shock body. The Arma is stocked with a nylon guard on the shock body for peace of mind. It may come down to the fact that EXT makes quality springs as well. On the topic of noises, I’m happy to report for all of you riders that focus on the sounds your shock makes, rather than the performance it brings, the Arma V4 is the quietest EXT shock I’ve ridden. And not just because it was hidden in the Gambler’s downtube either. In the past, the debris-clearing washer on the shock shaft of EXT dampers has also caused some rattling (an easy solution was to glue that in place). Now, the DLC steel shaft offers a high level of protection without the need for that plastic washer, which may have been overkill anyhow. Pros + Position-sensitive redbound eliminates top-out. + Compression adjusters are more accessible + Quieter hydraulic operation (for those that care). Cons - Premium performance comes at a price. - Competitor offers high and low-speed rebound controls for lower cost. Pinkbike's Take EXT has packed unique features into the new Arma V4 while also addressing small bothers found on the previous model. Together, those changes lift the performance of the Arma even higher than before, making it one of the best, but also the most expensive, coil dampers on the market. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shocks Extreme Racing Shox Ext Arma Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: EXT ERA V2.1 Fork
author: Seb Stott
article_text: EXT made quite an impression with their first MTB fork back in 2020. Yes, it was eye-wateringly expensive, but the performance was enough to give Fox and RockShox a bloody nose. That's not something you can say for every brand trying to break into the MTB suspension game - Öhlins is one of the biggest names in motorsport suspension yet it took several years before their MTB forks were on par. The latest iteration is called the Era V2.1. It's still aimed at the enduro market; with 36 mm stanchions and up to 170 mm travel, it weighs in at 2,344 g, which is very similar to a RockShox Zeb or Fox 38. If you want more travel, there's the ERA V2 LT, which offers 180 or 190 mm. Updates over the original include a whole new air spring with a larger negative chamber for improved EXT ERA V2.1 Specs • Intended use: enduro • Travel: 140-170 mm (internally adjustable at service centre) • Adjustments: High- & Low-Speed Compression, Rebound, dual air pressure • HS3 hybrid coil & air spring with 2 positive air chambers & self-equalising negative chamber • 29" only, 44mm offset, 36mm stanchions • Torque cap compatible • Weight: 2,344 grams (actual, 215 mm steerer) • MSRP: 1,480 € + Vat • extremeshox.com mojo.co.uk off-the-top sensitivity. New lower legs feature a floating axle to help reduce friction and are fitted with new slotted bushings for the same reason. There's also a lighter rebound tune and top-out bumper in the secondary positive air chamber. Our complaint with earlier versions of the ERA was a tendency to top out when pulling on the handlebars. For a fork this expensive, that's pretty hard to overlook, so we wanted to see if EXT have it sorted. Technology & features Air spring EXT use a three-chamber air spring much like Öhlins and Manitou. There's a regular positive chamber above the piston to resist compression, with a self-equalizing negative chamber below it, helping ease the fork into the first part of its travel. The extra third chamber, which EXT call "++", controls the mid-to-end stroke progression. The "++" chamber is set to a higher pressure than the regular "+" chamber. As the fork compresses into the travel, the air in the + chamber is compressed until it equals the pressure in the ++ chamber. From then on, the floating piston that separates the two positive chambers moves freely upwards, and the volume of the positive spring effectively expands to include both chambers. The upshot is that the air spring acts like a low-volume air spring in the first part of the travel, but a high-volume air spring in the second part. It's like fitting a RockShox fork with several volume tokens to improve mid-stroke support, but those tokens disappear at some point in the mid-travel so the end-stroke doesn't get too firm to use all the travel. In other words, you have a more linear (coil-like) spring curve, with more mid-stroke support but less end-stroke ramp than a regular air spring. Another advantage is that there's no need to fiddle around adding or removing volume spacers to adjust the progression. Just use a shock pump to adjust the pressure in the ++ chamber at the trailside. The more pressure in the ++ compared to the + chamber, the later in the travel the floating piston will start moving and the more progressive the spring curve will be - see the graphs above from EXT. Play Video Play Current Time 0:00 Loaded : 0% Progress : 0% Duration Time 0:00 Mute 1080p 720p 480p 240p Quality 480p Fullscreen This is a modal window. Caption Settings Dialog Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done EXT ERA V2 coil spring by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 3,890 Faves: 0 Comments: 0 In addition, there's a small coil spring housed on the end of the air shaft where it connects to the lower legs. Much like RockShox's Buttercups, the idea is to make it easier for the fork to start compressing on small or high-frequency bumps, by allowing the fork chassis to slide a few millimeters independently of the air spring, or by compressing in tandem with the air spring at the beginning of the stroke, thereby making the overall spring less stiff. EXT say this improves the "off-the-top" feel and small-bump sensitivity. Unlike RockShox Buttercups, if you remove the lowers and compress the spring by hand (as shown in the video), you can see the coil spring compressing by several millimetres over the first 10 or 20 mm or so of the air spring's stroke. At the beginning of the travel, this will soften the overall spring stiffness by acting as a pair of springs in series. Chassis The lower legs have been upgraded to include a floating axle, much like Fox and Öhlins use. The axle has a step that clamps the hub against the left leg first, then the right leg clamps onto the axle with a pinch bolt. The idea is that the legs can self-align and remain perfectly parallel - independent of hub width variation, wear or dirt - and this keeps friction to a minimum. The crown extends upwards around the base of the steerer tube. This means the crucial connection between the crown and the steerer benefits from a longer connection surface (about 5 cm instead of 3 cm), helping to improve stiffness and reduce the chances of creaking at the crown. Pretty clever. Damper The damper is relatively conventional compared to the spring, with a single-tube architecture with a spring-backed IFP. Rebound and compression flow are fully checked to stop back-flow through the rebound valve during compression (and visa-versa), so the rebound setting shouldn't affect the compression forces (and visa-versa). There's low-speed and high-speed compression adjustment (16 and 14 clicks, respectively) plus 20 clicks of rebound. The rebound range has been made lighter/faster for V2.1. Bushings EXT say a new bushing material allows for more accurate sizing, resulting in more consistently low friction. A slot in the bushing allows oil to flow up through it when the fork compresses (as shown), helping with lubrication. Servicing EXT recommends performing a lower leg service every 25-50 hours. Unlike when the fork was first launched, this can now be done at home following the instructions in the video above or the manual here . For a full service, which is recommended every 50-100 hours or every 6-12 months, you'll need to send it to an EXT service centre. Setup and performance I rode the V2 fork briefly before sending it away to Mojo Rising (EXT's UK partner and service centre) where the V2.1 updates were installed. Before the updates, the rebound was relatively slow when fully open, yet there was some top-out when pulling hard on the bars to hop over trail obstacles. After the updates, the rebound was a little faster, though still not super fast, and the top-out issue was gone. In contrast to Matt's experience with the original ERA, I found the suggested settings printed on the fork leg to be pretty much spot-on. At 85 kg, I settled on 70 psi in the main spring and 110 psi in the ++ chamber. I kept the rebound fully open (20 out of 20 clicks from closed) and added a few clicks of low-speed (8 out of 16 clicks from closed) and high-speed compression (6 out of 14 clicks from closed) to add support in big holes and steps that litter the Tweed Valley where I ride. That was all I needed to do. If you do need help or advice with setup, the people at Mojo Rising live and breathe this stuff and can talk or walk you through it if you're in the UK. There are no bleeder valves to release trapped air pressure in the lower legs, but Chris Porter from Mojo Rising suggested using a clean, lubed zip tie to release air via the wiper seals. He also suggested doing this with the fork partially compressed to create a partial vacuum in the lower legs when the fork is at full extension. Sensitivity at the start of the stroke is good even without this hack, but the vacuum trick makes it just that little bit better - at least for a while. Of course, you could do this with any fork and it's easier with bleeder valves, but I wouldn't say the ERA needs this to offer good sensitivity. It certainly helps though. How does it compare? I tested the EXT back to back against a Fox 38 Factory, which I consider to be the best in class among enduro forks . As well as having plenty of background ride time on each, I did a back-to-back test, riding them both several times on the same track, same bike, same day. I strongly believe this is the only way to get a true sense of how a fork stacks up. In this case, there isn't much to separate them. In terms of sensitivity, both are class-leading. When it comes to small bumps near the start of the travel, an area where Fox has outshone all its other rivals, I think the EXT might have a very slight advantage. I only felt this when going very slow (usually when climbing) - once up to speed on a rough trail, both offered similar levels of comfort and a reassuring feeling of consistent connection to the trail. Also, bear in mind this is while using Chris Porter's zip tie trick to create a vacuum in the lowers; the Fox had regular atmospheric pressure in its lowers. With the setup I was using, I felt the Fox 38 was riding higher in its travel and recovering faster from big compressions. The rebound was fully open on the EXT, but a few clicks away on the Fox. I'm not saying the EXT was too slow for me, but it's nice that the Fox has a bit more room to manoeuvre in the faster direction, especially for lighter riders. On the other hand, the EXT's compression adjustment range feels more intuitive to me. A few clicks make a noticeable difference and you can add meaningful hydraulic support without running into too much harshness. Whereas on the Fox GRIP2 damper, the compression adjustment range is narrow and it's easier to get lost in the many barely distinguishable clicker settings. But unlike some other reviewers, I had no issues with the 38 holding itself up in the travel under braking or big holes, even with minimal compression damping. In terms of chassis stiffness, I couldn't separate them, and I deliberately sought out big holes and heavy landings. Perhaps the biggest difference on the trail, however, is the axle to crown length. I measured the EXT at 582 mm (EXT states 582+/-5 mm) whereas my 170 mm Fox 38 measures 590 mm. For added context, I measured a RockShox Zeb at 585 mm. This could partly explain why I felt the Fox 38 rides higher in its travel (I think the faster rebound is a factor too) and the need to add compression damping to the EXT to hold it up higher in holes. A shorter axle-to-crown length is neither good nor bad, but it's important to be aware of this when choosing which travel to go for, especially since the ERA maxes out at 170 mm. If you want a longer fork/higher ride height you'll need to go for the ERA V2 LT, which covers 180 and 190 mm. It's a shame that neither option can cover 170 and 180 mm, which are probably the most common travel options in the enduro category. The Fox 38 can be set to 160, 170 or 180 mm. Which would I choose? I'd likely stick with the Fox 38 if for no other reason than the quick-release axle makes wheel swaps easier, although for some riders that's a very minor point. The option to run faster rebound and the ability to run 160-180 mm air springs makes the 38 more versatile too. But the real difference is the price. This will vary by market but there's no doubt the ERA is pricier. 1,480 € + Vat translates to £1,590 in the UK. The Fox Factory 38 retails for £1,399 ($1,159 in the US). It feels pretty strange to be suggesting a four-digit fork as the "value option", but less expensive versions of the Fox 38 are available with very similar performance and there are plenty of deals online. Pinkbike's Take EXT are still relatively new to the MTB market but they've cooked up a product that offers genuinely top-drawer performance. The blend of sensitivity with support is outstanding (possibly class-leading), the setup guide is spot-on and the compression adjustment is effective and intuitive. Some riders may want faster rebound options though. It's also important to be aware that the axle to crown height is slightly shorter than other rivals, and if you want to compensate by boosting the travel to 180 mm, you'll need a whole new fork. That brings us to the price, which is on the higher end compared to its similarly performing rival, the Fox 38. For those looking for a boutique fork and aren't afraid to splash the cash, the ERA V2.1 won't disappoint. — Seb Stott Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Suspension Fork Extreme Racing Shox Ext Era Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: The EXT Vaia USD Fork is Extra Adjustable, But Lacks Consistency
author: Matt Beer
article_text: EXT Vaia Fork Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/OaNwOdjW Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 16:37 When EXT started prototyping their first dual-crown fork, there weren’t many brands producing USD forks, barring the Manitou Dorado and some models from Intend. It was a gamble for a smaller brand to choose this layout, but for EXT, the benefits were clear. From a design standpoint, USD forks don’t require the same investment to cast lower legs. They’re also known for their supple ride qualities, which can come from multiple aspects, as we’ll explain. Now, that decision aligns with current trends and other major manufacturers. Fox brought the Podium to market in 2025, which was also their first USD (single crown) model, and silenced the naysayers. The Vaia is a gravity fork that looks like it came straight from the moto world with massive tubes and exquisite CNC work. It’s suited to downhill, freeride, and eMTBs, providing 170-200mm of travel. Along with that comes EXT’s highly tunable air spring, three damper adjustments, and a built-in adjustable offset feature. Vaia Details • Travel: 170-200mm • Adjustments: rebound, low-speed compression, high-speed compression • HS3 hybrid air spring • 38.5mm stanchions • Designed for 29" wheels • Adjustable offset: 44 or 48mm • 200PM brake adaptor • 15 or 20mm axles available • Two fender options • Weight: 3,375g (3,195 claimed) (w/ axle, lever, steer tube) • Price: $2,145 USD • More info: extremeshox.com All in, the Vaia costs $2,145 USD and weighs 3,374g. If it's expensive and heavy, then it had better be damn impressive to ride. Pinkbike's Take The Vaia is a high-end, complex fork that will attract expert riders looking to fine-tune their ride, but also those who like to tinker. Compared to a traditional right-side up fork, such as a Fox 40, I don’t perceive the amount of torsional flex to be an issue for most riders. In that area, it is stiffer than a Manitou Dorado Pro, and that’s reflected in the weight. I want to like this fork because the Vaia offers enticing features, such as the highly tunable air spring and dual-offset crown positions, but unfortunately, the consistency and performance across multiple samples have not lived up to the price tag. — Matt Beer The keyed titanium axle comes in 15 or 20mm options. A flip-chip in the upper crown provides two offsets; 44 or 48mm. Chassis Design Hype isn’t the reason that EXT chose the USD design method for the Vaia. The layout provides greater fore and aft stiffness, partially due to the larger upper tubes, but also because of the bushing placement. The Vaia features sliding or “trombone” bushings, where one is placed on the top of the inner stanchion, and the other in the seal head of the outer tube. In the static position, it gives 240mm of bushing overlap, but that distance grows larger as the fork compresses, increasing the stiffness further. USD forks also allow the bath oil to stop against the dust wipers, keeping them constantly lubricated. Overall, they function to reduce bushing bind and seal friction. The caveat is that USD forks battle torsional stiffness or twisting without an arch connecting the lower legs. How much flex in that area is a personal preference and can even depend on wheels, tires, and grip. Taking a page out of Mojo Rising’s MORC crown , EXT implemented a flip-chip to adjust the offset between 44 and 48mm. Down at the other end of the fork, the self-extracting titanium axle can be switched between 15 and 20mm Boost spacing. Fenders are also another piece of the USD puzzle. EXT offers two choices: one that bridges the lower leg guards, or the motocross method, where the fender sits under the upper crown. The moto-style fender has no trouble on the Whistler Bike Park chairlift, but doesn’t fit a North Shore Rack, since the rack prongs capture the upper crown. The opposite is true of the lower fender approach. The entire steer tube is machined offset to match the top piece. The headset crown race slides right without a press fit. The lower crown uses a pinch bolt to hold the steer tube in place. The Vaia uses sliding bushings; one in the seal head at the bottom of the upper tube, and the other 240mm away at the top of the stanchion. They grow farther apart as the fork is compressed. The HS3 hybrid air spring uses a coil and two air chambers. Air Spring What’s the best way to make an air spring break into the travel like a coil? Use a coil spring, of course. EXT’s hybrid spring (HS3) system does just that. A small coil helps initiate movement. Then, two air chambers allow for mid-stroke tuning. On top of that, the Vaia features positive and negative volume spacers to further adjust the initial breakaway and end-stroke ramp. Air volume spacers for both ends; positive on the left, negative on the right. Damper EXT carried over the same damper technology found in their Era single crown fork. They preach high turbulent flow, and if you like heavy damping, the Vaia has barrels of it. There are XX numbers of clicks, which do make the fully open settings more approachable. Like the Era, the Vaia also features three adjustments: rebound, high-speed compression, and low-speed compression. One click of high-speed compression has a noticeable effect on the trail - Rebound and LSC, a little less so. High and low-speed compression are up top, rebound is on the bottom. The red buttons on both tubes release air that naturally builds up over time. The main "+" and the "++" ramp/mid-stroke air chambers. Volume spacers can be added to both the positive and negative side of the air spring. Installation Fitting the Vaia has a few more bolts than a standard fork, but the instructions are well laid out. All of the bolt threads have laser-etched torque values on them, as they should be. Every structural bolt is titanium, which feeds into aluminum surfaces. Anti-seize paste should be applied to these. The second fork I received didn’t arrive from the factory with this and caused issues, as I’ll explain. Furthermore, the base of the floating steer tube is only chamfered at one end, meaning you can slide the crown race on by hand. I was a little skeptical of this, wearing or wiggling the headset loose, but that was never the case. It is important to note the direction in which the offset is clocked as you preload the start nut. The front wheel can be trickier to fit between the lower legs, since those can rotate independently, but that’s not partial to the Vaia - all USD forks have that small issue. Switching between the 15 and 20mm axles not only requires that piece of the puzzle, but also a proprietary tool to remove the self-extracting axle nut. Two upper crowns are available for the Vaia: a 10mm drop for 107-136mm head tube lengths or a 30mm drop for 127-156mm frames. When set to 200mm travel, the axle to crown length can slide between 604 and 614mm. Setup Dual-rate air spring forks, like some Manitou and Ohlins forks, have a specific inflation process. The ramp, or ++ chamber, as EXT calls it, should be inflated first, followed by the main + chamber. EXT provides a basic start guide for the Vaia, as well as a more complex document describing the air spring functionality. I spoke directly to EXT for setup advice. They suggested 144 PSI in the + chamber and 185 in the ++ chamber, which provided 19mm sag. This provided a low breakaway force and appreciable dynamic ride height, without hard bottom outs. Initially, the Vaia was installed on an Intense M1, but then later on a Scott Gambler. The setup didn’t change much besides a couple of clicks of LSC added for the Gambler. Base settings: + chamber: 144 psi ++ chamber: 185 psi (19 mm sag) Rebound: -18/18 (open) LSC -12/16 HSC -8/10 Performance The Vaia is a brute of a fork. It’s heavy, heavily damped, and rides strong. You can really charge into objects and expect a lot of support from the damper, even when the air spring is set to a lower, softer ride height. The air spring is the beauty of this fork. It’s very customizable for each and every rider or track. I mainly stuck to the stock setup of 4 spacers in the positive and 2 in the negative. When asked about increasing the sensitivity of the fork, EXT suggested adding a third spacer to the negative volume, which helped, but I preferred the previous ride height. If you’re a fan of heavy compression damping, tune into EXT suspension components: their stock tunes are very supportive. Lowering the spring rates and adding compression damping is another way to achieve front-end support on larger impacts. I found that near open, the HSC could become overbearing very quickly. I wouldn’t say that equated to less traction, but it did make the bike much harder to hold onto. I much preferred increasing the ++ chamber. This added much more of a platform earlier in the travel without the harsh feeling of the HSC. Lately, RockShox has focused on making its suspension operate with a minimum noise level, and they’ve succeeded. EXT dampers, on the other hand, have been known to make loud hydraulic noises while in operation. That’s not necessarily a performance negative, unless you’re distracted by that sound. The Vaia isn’t as loud as their other rear shocks, but it’s miles away from the silence of the Boxxer. We’ve come full circle on flex being a positive characteristic with inverted forks leading the charge.
