Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Five Ten Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Fizik Nanuq GTX Clipless and Flat Shoes
author: Matt Beer
article_text: FIzik's Terra model line of mountain bike shoes serve specific functions for extreme conditions and aren't just part of gorp-core fashion. The Nanuq GTX shoes are the best option in the Terra series when it comes to blocking out wet and cold elements. The Nanuq is based on the Artica X2 but will replace that model as the waterproofing material has been updated to a Gore-Tex membrane. There's more at play than just a new name though. Fizik has also added a Nanuq GTX flat pedal version which is identical in construction, excluding the sole. The Nanuq GTX Flat uses a Vibram XS Trek rubber sole underneath and both models feature a zippered cuff and BOA dial closure, to avoid dealing with muddy laces. Fizik Nanuq GTX Details • X2 outsole w/ Ripstop fabric • Fleece-lined insulation • Gore-Tex Koala membrane • Boa Fit System L6 dial & zippered ankle cuff • Vibram XS Trek tread • Stiffness index 3 • Sizes: 36-48 w/ half sizes 37-47 • Colors: black/grey • Weight: 456g - flat, (size 42) • MSRP: £289.99 GBR / $299.99 USD / €279 EUR • fizik.com Both versions of the Nanuq retail for £289.99 GBR / $299.99 USD / €279 EUR and come in sizes 36-48. Half size increments are available between size 37-47 too. Vibram Trek XS soles are somewhere in the middle of the road for grip when hiking and clinging to pedal pins. The sole is one of stiffest I've tried in a long time for a flat pedal shoe. Gore-Tex outside + fleece inside = dry and warm feet. Sliding on the Nanuqs no longer requires breaking a sweat thanks to the finger loop and a roomier cuff. Fit The Nanuq looks identical to the Artica X2 but there are more than just a few material changes at play. One of the major complaints with Artica X2 shoes was the difficulty of pulling them on. Fizik has added a finger loop at the top of the cuff to make this easier. In addition, the cuff doesn’t have the exact same tight grasp that the Artica X2 did, making these boots a breeze to pull on - no more shoehorns needed. Inside, the shape and feel of the lower boot also appears to have a touch more volume. The width is generous as well for a size 42 and the length is true to fit, compared to most other similar size shoes. The fleece interior is cozy, however, there is an abrupt change in comfort and flex at the transition from the upper cuff to the lower shoe body, especially when flexing the foot. A stiff, thin lower material, notably around the rim of the heel cup can be felt due to its prominent edge. The top lace of the Boa can still produce a bit of unwanted pressure when you really crank them down. Plenty of room to move the cleats behind the ball of the foot. The sole on the clipless version is firmer than the flats, but only slightly. "X2 Stiffness 3" A Note Performance - Nanuq GTX Clipless With such a similar appearance to the Artica, I knew what I was getting into with the Nanuq before even trying them on, but I hoped that the change in material improved the breathability slightly, and the longevity, but more on that later. When it comes to the most important job for wet weather footwear, the Nanuq is one of the best at keeping your feet warm and dry. Fizik has definitely taken a robust and expensive route to get there, but they would be my number one choice for longer rides in absolute downpours. When the temperature really drops, especially with the clip version, you’ll want thick wool socks, as the cleat bed area acts as a heat sink, but that's not isolated to the Nanuq clip. The stitched seam around the inside of the toe box was a long-term concern for a past model, but this one is holding strong. Durability One of the major issues that I had with the Artica X2s was the seam on the inside of the toe box, which let go after a few months of hard riding in torturous conditions. I wasn’t alone in this dilemma, and other riders encountered the same issue. For the price, I expected the Artica X2s to hold up longer. Thankfully, Fizik has a solid warranty program and took care of those customers. In the case of the Nanuq, our bizarrely dry winter hasn’t called for their action quite as heavily as in previous winters, but they have still seen their fair share of abuse. That seam is still holding strong and not showing any signs of letting go. This could be down to the new construction methods and the ripstop material holding strong. It’s worth adding that if you are a rider who tends to polish their cranks with their feet, these may not be the shoe of choice for you, as you will likely encounter some rubbing in the heel area from the zipper, or even the inside face of the shoe. Underneath, the Vibram rubber still looks brand new even when paired up with the tallest pedal pins on the Nanuq GTX Flat version. Fizik Nanuq GTX Flat - $299.99 USD Endura MT500 Burner Flat - $189.99 USD Comparison - Nanuq GTX Flats On the flat pedal version, they are more similar to the Endura MT500 Burner in their stiff shell design versus the more flexible and sticky soled options from Shimano and 510. The latter would be the better option if you are gearing towards all out flat pedal performance versus lasting all day in abysmal weather. The Vibram XS Trek rubber soul provides decent traction, but is no match against the Shimano GF800GTX or the Five Ten Trailcross GTX grip. It’s not just the rubber that makes up for a difference in grip overall though. The Nanuq has a stiffer soul and even the shape is concave in the lateral direction which gives a vague, disconnected feel before totally weighting your foot. Jumping back to cross-compare, both the Burners and the Nanuqs are exceptionally impervious to water. When your pants are no longer waterproof and the shoes eventually soak through, the scuba gear-like material of the Burners insulates your feet substantially better. Price-wise, they're one of the most expensive waterproof shoes on the market. The Nanuq is at least $80 USD more than any of the other three shoes mentioned here. What would the ideal flat pedal waterproof shoe look like then? There are elements in all four of these mentioned shoes that could be combined to make an exceptional set of sh!t kickers. The Five Ten Trailcross GTX is an extremely close example of this, however they don’t have the same impervious qualities of the Nanuq, a Boa dial like the GF800GTX or the robust shell of the Burners. The earth isn't flat, but pedals are. The curvature of the rubber never broke in, adding to the clunky operation of the Nanuqs. Pros + One of the best shoes (clip version included) for keeping your feet warm and dry. + Durability has been increased. + Excellent coverage (and now easier to pull on). Cons - The sole could be more flexible for increased control. - The singlular Boa dial closure can cause a minor pinch point on the medial ligament. - High cost Pinkbike's Take Fizik have taken what they've learned from their previous resilient wet-weather shoes and applied it to the Nanuq GTX Flats. There aren't many other shoes I'm confident will stay dry and block out chills like the Nanuq GTXs - these are a true winter mountain biking shoe. The durability has increased and adding a finger loop to the heel makes them much easier to pull on. With all of those functional materials and features though, they don't have the level of dexterity or connectivity that some flat pedal riders will want. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Fizik Fizik Nanuq Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: The Forbidden Dreadnought V2 Has a Need for Speed
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Review Forbidden Dreadnought V2 WORDS: Matt Beer PHOTOS: Thomas Renborg The allure of high-pivot bikes are strong, and Forbidden is a brand that's built all of their bikes around that suspension design. They can melt away small bumps and give a sense of having more rear wheel travel than stated, yet that design can come with caveats such as complicated drivetrains and uneasy shifts in rider weight bias. Forbidden’s second generation Dreadnought follows the evolution of their high-pivot suspension design, which moves to a multi-link design and touts other widgets. This enduro battleship has been as much of a mainstay between race tape as it has in bike parks, often with a dual-crown fork. Dreadnought V2 Details • Carbon frame • Wheel size: MX or dual 29 • 160mm rear travel, 170mm fork • 4-bar, high-pivot suspension design • 63.1° head angle (63.5° w/ dual 29") • 77.1° effective seat angle • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 • Reach: 430, 450, 471, 491mm • Chainstays (+/-10mm): 431, 446, 460, 475mm • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 • Weight: 15.6 kg / 34.3 lb (S3, actual) • Price: $8,499-11,999 CAD, $6,499-9,299 USD • forbiddenbike.com For 2024, they’ve also increased the rear wheel travel from 154mm to 160mm and revised the kinematics considerably. They’ve also introduced modular drop-outs to carry either a 27.5” or 29” rear wheel and various chainstay lengths. Being constructed from carbon only, the price tag keeps the entry point higher than other aluminum enduro bikes on the market. The component selection doesn’t spare any expense either. Ranging between $6,499-9,299 USD ($8,499-11,999 CAD), all of the builds use RockShox suspension, Maxxis tires, Crankbrothers wheels, and T-type shifting from SRAM. Contents Introduction Frame Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take For every action, there’s a reaction. The Dreadnought is a prime example of Newton’s Third Law of Motion. In some scenarios, it can descend wide-open trails at a frightening pace, yet at other times it can be a handful to navigate at slower speeds. — Matt Beer Frame Details From afar, the Dreadnought V2 bears a similar shape to the original, but get up close and you’ll notice a lot more bolts. Interchangeable dropouts give riders the option to change between a 27.5 or 29” rear wheel, although that can be decided when ordering. Savvy thinking allows a single 200mm post mount brake adapter to be used; the axle feeds through the adapter allowing it to pivot, acting as one mounting point while a series of three bolt holes on the upper portion account for the varying dropout lengths. Smart use of the boxed shaped-frame above the bottom bracket continues to provide a gear storage solution, at least enough for a spare tube. Under the top tube is an accessory mount and a full size water bottle fits inside the front triangle. Cleanly routed internal cables are clamped down by the grommets and ample rubber on the inside faces of the swingarm keep the chain very quiet. Part of that calm, quiet ride is due to the chain security. That includes a substantial cover on the idler pulley, skid plate to protect the chainring, and a lower jockey wheel. You’ll notice that the Druid V2 doesn’t require this extra pulley since the frame has less travel and therefore less chain growth. Forbidden uses an 18-tooth idler pulley wheel positioned slightly off-axis to reduce friction when cross-loading the chain in the larger cogs. During the First Ride, we noticed an increase in drivetrain friction compared to the Druid. We experimented by removing that pulley and expanded on that further down in the Technical Report. Dreadnought squish https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/UsnZVO5Z Copied Live 00:00 00:11 00:11 The Dreadnought is built around a 205x60mm trunnion shock and can handle air or coil sprung dampers. Suspension Design High-pivot suspension is defined by the main swing arm pivot’s height above the chainring. In the case of the Dreadnought, that main pivot is located well above the chainring, slightly offset from the pulley. This moves the rear wheel away from the front triangle through the majority of the travel. To visualize this, pretend the swing arm is the hand on a clock. A high-pivot’s rearward axle path would move from 7-9 o’clock through the suspension travel. Bikes like the Dreadnought require a secondary chainring, or pulley, near the main pivot to account for the increased chain growth as the rear axle moves further away from the bottom bracket through its travel. Now with a multi-link design, the Dreadnought V2's kinematics can be controlled with greater independence between one another. The leverage ratio start and end point values have been increased substantially, which should help lighter-weight rider’s opting for a coil-sprung rear shock find the correct spring rate. The curve has also been smoothed out considerably for more consistency on the trail. The anti-squat is considerably higher than before (136% at sag vs. 116%) and should reduce pedal bob further. Under braking, the anti-rise remains similar aside from a reduction later in the travel. Another change is the axle path. It still moves rearward for the majority of the travel, but towards the end of the travel, the axle moves vertically compared to the V1’s single high-pivot which is rearward through 100% of the travel. Geometry Stability largely relies on wheelbase and that’s something that the Dreadnought has heaps of. If you look at the reach and head angle numbers, they’re pretty typical of an aggressive enduro bike (471mm for a size S3 and 63.1° heat tube angle), but it’s the rear end that really adds to the overall length. And that just continues to grow up larger, up to and beyond the sag point. Forbidden calls the balance between the front and rear centers One Ride geometry. Across the four frame sizes, the front to rear center ratio remains the same. The smallest of those has virtually an equal reach and rear center, or chainstay length, of about 430mm (431mm chainstay in MX mode). That doesn’t sound too wild until you consider the chainstay length of the S3 frame. Most brands typically align their standard size “large” to hit a 480mm reach with chainstays under 450mm in length or less. Forbidden shifts both of those numbers by about 10mm. That equates to the S3 frame having a 471mm reach and 460mm chainstays (0mm offset dropout). Regarding setup and my choice of frame size, its unusual for me (I’m 178cm for reference) to want anything shorter than that in terms of reach at least on this style of bike. But given the 1288mm wheelbase, I can understand why two of the Forbidden Synthesis team races, who are taller than I am, went with an S2 frame. Both Alex Storr and Rhys Verner chose S2 frames with dual 29” wheels and the 0mm offset dropout, landing the chainstay at 446mm (Editor's Note: Since writing the review, both riders have moved to the +10mm setting) . The 29" wheel dropout also steepens the head angle by 0.4 degrees and increases the reach by 4mm. Due to frame clearance concerns, there is no -10mm dropout option for the 29" rear wheel, regardless of the frame size. If you’re unsure on sizing, I’d advise scaling down. Test riding a Dreadnought if possible would be a smart move too, or at the very least swinging a leg over one in a parking lot. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $9299 Travel X0 Eagle Transmission Rear Shock RockShox Vivid Air Ultimate Fork RockShox ZEB Ultimate 170mm, Charger 3.1 RC2 Headset Cane Creek Hellbender 70, ZS44/ZS56 Cassette X0 Eagle Transmission Crankarms X0 Eagle Transmission, 165, 32t Chainguide MRP custom Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA 73mm Rear Derailleur X0 Eagle Transmission Chain X0 Eagle Transmission Flattop Chain Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar Burgtec Ride Wide Enduro Carbon, 800x30mm Stem Burgtec MK3 42.5mm, 35mm Grips Burgtec Bartender Pro Greg Minnaar Signature Brakes SRAM Maven Ultimate, 200mm F/R, Organic pads Hubs Crankbrothers Synthesis Enduro Std Spokes Sapim CX Ray/Sprint Rim Crankbrothers Synthesis 11 Carbon Tires Maxxis Assegai 29x2.5" 3C MaxxGrip EXO+ F/Minion DHR II 27.5x2.4, 3C MaxxTerra DD Seat Fizik Alpaca X5, 145mm width Seatpost OneUp V2 Dropper, 31.6mm, 180mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Dreadnought Test Bike Setup In the Geometry section, I explained why I chose the S3 Dreadnought. I started with the stock MX wheel configuration but later switched to the -10mm dropouts (450mm chainstay). Since the bike already required a lot of input to change direction, I never went to the longest 470mm setting or experimented with a 29” rear wheel. The 30mm rise handlebars were cut to 765mm with 20mm of spacers under the stem. Regarding suspension setup, guidelines are clearly laid out on Forbidden’s website: set the rear shock sag to 28-30% sag while seated and turn the clickers as directed. On the first outing, the Vivid Air was pumped to 197 PSI to provide 30% sag. The shock arrives with 2 volume spacers from the factory. I’d go on to increase the pressure to 205 PSI, but eventually revert to 197 and add a third volume spacer. The HBO adjustment was kept fully closed. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 37 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r The digressive rebound tune (R85) was an interesting choice and meant I ran the red dial fully open. We’ll dive into those choices in the Technical Report below. On the compression side, I settled on placing the high and low speed compression dials at -3 and -2 clicks from closed, not far off of Forbidden’s recommendations (HSC -2, LSC -2). For the 170mm Zeb Ultimate, I ran the pressure lower than usual (around 78 vs. 84 PSI - both with 1 volume spacer) as it helped to keep more weight on the front of the long wheelbase through corners. Trails in the Dreadnought’s repertoire were a mix of fresh and old in the Diamondhead area in Squamish, as well as visits to the North Shore and Whistler Bike Park. Even a few high-speed laps on Harper Mountain in Kamloops were added to the mix to really let the Dreadnought open up. Download the Trailforks App Climbing During the Dreadnought’s First Ride, I immediately picked up on the increased drag from the lower pulley, which isn’t part of the Druid’s high-pivot equation. Extended climbs are where this is most prevalent, as well as in the largest cogs when the rear wheel is turning slower. It’s not terribly impeding compared to other bikes with idlers. In fact, it’s on par or better in terms of perceived efficiency, yet clear as day after riding other non-high-pivot bikes back to back. The stretched out geometry makes the footprint of the Dreadnought lengthy. The rear wheel takes some coaxing to pull around tight switchbacks. We’re not quite into Grim Donut V2 territory, but it’s a long ship to swing around 180-degree turns. Looking ahead and anticipating how wide you need to take those corners is mandatory. However, once understood and anticipated, that length aids its technical climbing ability. Worrying about the cranks bumping obstacles isn’t as much of a concern as I first thought. A high anti-squat value and 165mm cranks help the bike to maintain decent ground clearance and continue pedaling over lumpy ground. A central rider position and rearward axle path make blasting up rocky steps and over logs a breeze as the bike keeps moving forward. 27.5” rear wheels can hamper some enduro bikes’ uphill performance, but that’s where the Dreadnought differs. Although I didn’t try the larger wheel I can imagine how those characteristics would be exploited further, based on past experience. Descending Make no mistake, this isn’t just a long-travel Druid. They both share that same trait of seemingly offering more travel than stated, however, on our First Ride, some aspects of the bike blew me away. Once we towed the Dreadnought through more varied trails, some quirks started to show. Smashing high speed trails are where this beast comes to life. Point it straight and it will gobble up everything in sight. There is a catch though: repetitive square-edge bumps. Being a high-pivot bike, I expected the Dreadnought to erase those stutter bumps but it never gave that "wow"-facter like the Norco Range or Devinci Chainsaw did. Part of that reason may be down to the shock tune on the Vivid Air (R85 C26). Forbidden found a digressive rebound tune to handle the returning spring force best. The shock tune on the Dreadnought V2 is R85 C26, with 2 Volume Spacers. What that translates to is a digressive rebound tune and a light compression tune. This shock tune was chosen based on the changes made to the leverage ratio and the shape of the leverage curve. Compared to the V1 Dreadnought, the V2 has more support in the mid, less overall progression (25% vs 31%) and less end stroke progression (less ramp at the end of the curve). This works better with large volume air springs, like the Vivid. It also takes advantage of the hydraulic bottom out control seen on many shocks these days. Overall, this gives a more predictable feel to the bike throughout its travel. — Forbidden Bikes This reduces that kicking or bucking motion as the rear wheel returns from repetitive, medium-size hits. I can see why that makes sense in some instances. Some riders find high-pivot bikes can push their weight forward in those instances, particularly on jumps. I found the Dreadnought to avoid putting me in that unwanted situation and that could be where that shock tune comes into play. I also had the chance to briefly try a Fox DHX2 coil shock (digressive rebound/light compression tune) on the Dreadnought and found that to break into the travel and handle each consecutive impact more gently and consistently. On descents that best replicate mogul skiing, bouncing from one loamy lump to the next, the Dreadnought provides serious traction by splatting itself to the ground. This can make the bike more involved to turn, especially in steep zones where braking and changing direction needs to happen simultaneously (think Val Di Sole for enduro bikes). I wouldn’t say that this was an issue of the bike being too big for me, though it did require more consideration than most other bikes when it comes to how you weight the bike as it moves through the travel. The balance on each wheel can change considerably. Moving to the -10mm dropouts made a world of difference in regard to maneuverability. Back in the open, where sight lines are much further ahead, that elongating chainstay brings boat-loads of stability. The bike glues itself to the ground - not so much that you can’t pop over obstacles, flick a Scandi turn, or pull a manual. The Dreadnought encourages you to let it fly on high speed trails. As is the case with most high-pivot bikes, it’s a catch twenty-two situation. They can light up in certain scenarios and struggle more than say a Horst link suspended bike. Thankfully, the Dreadnought has enough parameters that could be changed to suit those devoted to the ways of a high-pivot bike. Remember, there’s still two wheel size options, three dropout settings and the possibility to run a coil shock too. Downsizing to a smaller frame and opting for a coil shock would be something to consider, based on how tall the rider is and the terrain they most frequently ride. Technical Report RockShock Vivid Air rear shock: We've been impressed with the Vivid's performance on other bikes but we had some hiccups with one particular shock when the IFP lost pressure. On top of that, the suppleness never seemed to be equal to the Vivid we tested on a Nomad during our Shock Week last summer . Although our time with a Fox DHX2 on the Dreadnought was short, a coil shock would be our preference for the Dreadnought. SRAM Maven Ultimate brakes: It’s been a long time since we saw prototypes of SRAM’s comically large prototype brake calipers, but I’m glad they’re here. Maven Ultimates are a major improvement over the Code RSCs in terms of power, especially when you're on the brakes for considerable lengths of time. The Dreadnought came with organic pads and 200mm front and rear. These give much less bite than the metallic option yet don't fade out as quickly as they would on a Code. MRP chainguide: Another component that I thought may interfere with the small bump sensitivity was the added tension from the lower pulley. I removed that for a few rides and didn’t notice a major improvement - just more noise from the chain. In actuality, the derailleur clutch could be a bigger culprit as it flaps around unpredictably giving inconsistencies throughout the travel. It's no elbow to MRP because the pulley rolls smoothly, but it's still an extra component that isn't used on most other suspension designs. Which Model is the Best Value? If you’re seriously looking at a Dreadnought, then expect to drop a bit of coin. Carbon-only frames do push the entry point higher than competing aluminum enduro bikes. The cream of the crop X0 RS U build doesn’t cut corners but it's the RS S base model that seems the most sensible. It largely has the same components as the RS S+, including the GX AXS T-type drivetrain. The main differences are the damper used in the ZEB chassis (Charger 3 vs. the older RC) and adjustments on the Vivid Air. Other than that, I wouldn’t worry about the Maven Bronze brakes as they are still plenty powerful and have a crispy feel. Upgrading the fork damper to the Charger 3.1 and replacing the rims with something burlier could be considerations down the road. Forbidden Dreadnought V2 Santa Cruz Nomad How Does It Compare? A hot competitor in the world of smashy enduro bikes is the Santa Cruz Nomad. No, it’s not a high-pivot, but for riders looking to lap bike parks, send it off of ridiculous things, and race enduros, these two bikes are built to tackle such endeavors. Neither one would ever win an XC race, however, they are designed to pedal everywhere on the map. The Dreadnought actually wins out in climbing effectiveness by riding a touch higher in the travel and setting the rider in a more upright, centered position on the bike. Efficiency-wise though, as in just spinning up a road, the Nomad’s traditional drivetrain is less draggy over the course of a full day. Point them downhill though, and you can get wild. The Dreadnought is 10mm shorter on travel out back but makes up for that in some sense with the rearward axle path. Both bikes have a noticeably high amount of tension from the drivetrain as the rear wheel breaks into the travel. The shock tune on the Dreadnought could be the culprit but a device like the O-Chain Active Spider remedies a lot of the clanging on the Nomad. At the end of the stroke, the Nomad handles bottom outs best from a more consistent ramp up. Each frame is stiff with aggressive geometry that can be ridden hard by riders at the top end of the sport. At the same time, the Nomad doesn’t need to be pushed to go fast or muscled as hard to turn. For that reason, it’s less demanding to ride than the Dreadnought. As for maintenance, the Dreadnought doesn’t have as many bolts as something like Commencal’s Supreme V5 downhill bike but the Nomad is hands down simpler. There’s no pulley wheels to keep running smooth and the links have grease ports to quickly remedy any creaks. Santa Cruz also offers a lifetime supply of replacement frame bearings for the original customer too. When it’s time to slap down the credit card, neither one is cheap, or available in aluminum. A Nomad frame and shock (Fox Float X2) costs $4,049 USD, $250 less than a Dreadnought frame kit. Looking at the premium build kits, they’re very comparable with Forbidden choosing RockShox Ultimate suspension and Santa Cruz going the Fox Factory route. There’s also carbon rims, an X0 T-type drivetrain, and Maven brakes, stalemating at a cool $9,299 USD. Pros + Calm and quiet ride. + Centered, upright seated position gives a helping hand on technical climbs. + Comes alive at higher speeds. Cons - Not the small bump performance expected from a high-pivot bike. - Long chainstays that continue to grow through the travel can be a handful on steep trails. - Digressive shock rebound tune has a peculiar ride characteristic. Pinkbike's Take After being blown away with the capabilities of Forbidden's 130mm-travel Druid, I thought the Dreadnought would be all that and more, but it didn't eat bumps like the magic carpet ride I dreamt about. Convincing this beast to turn means being forceful in where you point the bike and when to lean it in. With that said though, when you get the Dreadnought on high speed trails and optimize the shock setup, there aren't many other enduro bikes that will unlock the same level of speed. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Forbidden Forbidden Dreadnought Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Forbidden Druid - Travel is Just a Number
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: REVIEW Forbidden Druid WORDS: Henry Quinney PHOTOS: Tom Richards Forbidden are a very busy brand. In the last year or so we’ve seen a new Druid, a new Dreadnought and a long-teased World Cup downhill bike. Of their crop of current bikes, the apple doesn’t seem to fall far from the tree, and they all share common ground both in terms of their fundamentals and aesthetics. While the Dread and now Supernaught are enjoying many outings underneath their pro riders at the highest level of downhill and enduro racing, it’s worth remembering which of the current stable claimed the brand's first, and currently only, elite level World Cup win - the iddy-biddy-tincy-weeny Druid. How did this happen? And is the fact a 130mm bike won an Enduro World Cup a comment on the terrain that we race on or the bike’s capabilities? Druid V2 Details • Carbon frame • 130mm travel / 150mm fork • 29" or mixed wheels • 65° head tube angle • 77º seat tube angle • Reach: 440, 460, 480, 500 • Chainstay: 423, 437, 452, 466 • Weight: 14.6kg (32.2lbs) • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 • Price: $6,199 USD - $8,899 USD ($7,799 CAD - $11,199 CAD) • Frame kit: $3,799 USD / $4,999 CAD • Forbiddenbike.com A woman stopped me outside the supermarket to say the bike was a beautiful colour. This doesn't happen normally. Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take It’s a fun, incredibly capable bike that defies whatever you think you know about what 130mm can get you. — Henry Quinney Industrial-like features give way to gentle touches. Frame Details The Druid is a far better-looking bike than the previous version. Gone is the long seat tube, towering over the rest of the frame like a mast over its ship. The whole bike is a huge step forward in terms of looks, and each line complements and flows into the next. The bottom bracket shell is cradled in a triangle of carbon that is aligned with the chain stay, and the gentle edge of the seatstay sweeps into a top tube of similar profile and shape. The aforementioned space around the bottom bracket also serves a purpose beyond prettiness: it’s a very stealthy way to hold a tube. The front triangle also has two sets of mounts for a full-size bottle and a tool. The cable ports lock the cable to the frame for a quiet ride, and can be replaced with blanking plates. This is good for both left and right rear-hand brakers, or if you want to remove cables on a wireless setup. The bike on test here is the full 29” version, but this bike is also available with a smaller rear wheel. However, keen Forbiddenists will note a lack of Ziggy link, which is what the brand named the link that could maintain geometry when switching to a smaller wheel in the past. This bike instead uses different chainstay lengths for the different wheel sizes. Another key area of development was the positioning of the main pivot and idler. While it’s still high, it’s noticeably lower and more rearward than the V1. This not only means that chainring benefits from more wrap, which subsequently means the bike doesn’t need to run a second chain-idler beneath the cranks, but also that the chain now goes through the stay, rather than running over the top. The design team focused not only on the position of the idler but also the size. The size is increased from 16 to 18 teeth and is made from steel for extra longevity. Not only that, but the angle is offset to 0.69 degrees to better align with the middle of the cassette. This is optimized for both 52 and 55mm chain lines. The frame uses collet-style hardware on oversized bearings and a trunnion shock. It’s arguably a good use of the technology here where the packaging is so tight, and the inversed four-bar should give something stout enough not to stress shock internals too much. I love the mixture of flat and round surfaces. Sneaky storage is a great touch. Geometry The Druid V2 uses size-specific rear-center lengths to try and give the bike a consistent feel and balance, regardless of size. For instance, the very short 423mm stays on the S1 size were made with the 435/440mm reach in mind. Whereas if they had used the same dimension stay across all sizes, the reach of the S1 could have been dwarfed by the 452mm stays of the S3, for example. This is great to see, even for a long-stay fan such as me. Speaking of which, the 466mm stay of the S4 is… large. The stack height plays off the foundation laid by the long rear end. As a general rule of thumb, bikes with higher stacks require longer stays to keep the front weighted. Bikes with shorter rear ends need lower fronts to stop feeling like the weight balance is too rearward. The bikes have a 64.5 or 65-degree head angle for the MX or 29” versions, respectively. This seems to be where a lot of trail bikes are settling. Slack enough to get away with riding some committing and fast trails but steep enough to stop the wheel from flopping around at slower speeds. Again, the seat tube angle is tempered by being aware of the intention of what we want our trail bike to do. 76.7 or 77-degrees, depending on wheel size, is steep enough, but it will feel more relaxed on flatter terrain compared to something even a degree or two steeper. Suspension Design The Druid uses an inverted four-bar. What is this? And why does it matter if it’s inverted or not? Compared to the linkage-driven single pivot of the V1, a four-bar allows for more tuning. However, if it wasn’t inverted, the pivot would still be relatively low, much like you see on many brands that use the layout, and Forbidden wouldn’t be able to incorporate the rear axle path that is so key to their design. By running it inverted, they can increase the height of the instant-center (the thing the rear axle pivots around) and get the tunability of the four bars with the axle path they want. Forbidden Druid Suspension Cycle https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/gzLxUs3k Copied Live 00:00 01:16 01:16 Anti-rise is lower in the V2, but still very high compared to many other bikes. Because of the axle path being more reward, high pivot bikes tend to go hand in hand with higher value of anti-rise. When a bike uses a high value of anti-rise, it means that braking, and the subsequent shifting of mass, provides a compression force to the shock. This can lead to a slightly firmer suspension feel under heavy braking, but it can also help preserve the bike's geometry by keeping the rider's mass centered, rather than pitching forward and weighting the fork. Anti-squat is also high. This, for me, is one of the main benefits of a high-pivot. You can have a high anti-squat bike that is still happy to grip and go into its stroke because the chain doesn’t inhibit as you're driving the bike forward. Counterintuitively, high-pivots can be about having more of the climbing attributes you want. Specifications Price $8899 Travel 130 Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate Fork RockShox Lyrik Ultimate Headset Cane Creek Hellbender 70 Cassette SRAM X0 T-Type 10-52T 12speed Crankarms SRAM X0 Eagle 32T - 165mm Chainguide Forbidden Trail Guide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Threaded BSA Wide Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 AXS T-Type 12speed Chain SRAM X0 Transmission Flattop 12speed Shifter Pods SRAM Pod Ultimate Handlebar Burgtec Ride Wide Enduro Carbon Stem Burgtec OE MK3 Enduro Grips Burgtec Bartender Pro Brakes SRAM Code Stealth Ultimate 4-Piston Wheelset Crankbrothers Synthesis 11 - Carbon Tires Maxxis MaxxTerra Assegai EXO / Forkaster EXO+ Seat Fizik Terra Alpaca X5 Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Forbidden Druid Test Bike Setup The test Druid was a mix of SRAM and RockShox parts, with Ultimate-level suspension and brakes. While drool-worthy in its quality of spec, the bike is not perfect. Henry Quinney Location: Squamish Height: 183cm / 6' Inseam: 82 cm / 32.5" Weight: 79 kg / 174 lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @henryquinney The 180mm dropper is too short for a large. Not least because the OneUp models can be specced down anyway. Why not spec the 210 and leave it to the rider to shorten to taste? They’re buying a large for a reason. The 180mm option is short enough to go fully flush inside the frame, but I think that would be a poor basis to spec a length of post. Secondly, as we’ll get into as we go into the descending portion of the review, everything about this bike wills you to go faster. However, slowing down could often prove tricky. A Dissector or DHR2 tire would not have only provided better braking traction in a wider variety of conditions but also more consistency when leaning heavily on the edge knobs. The Forekaster is just too XC for a bike that is this confidence-inspiring. Couple that with a 180mm front rotor, and it felt like it was on the ragged edge all too often. I will expand upon this further later in the review. Lastly, I know bikes are expensive, and industry price points have to be hit yada-yada-yada but I would contend a MaxxGrip tire on the front would have been a nice touch. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place in the spring of this year. In Squamish, the dirt is awful with a bit of moisture. The trails aren’t dreamy mixes of loam-flow. The grip on offer doesn’t leave even the most relaxed rides turn into a hell-for-leather test of mettle. Honestly, it’s awful. I think the place is probably not worth visiting this time of year. Don’t come. The seated position ensures a well-weighted front. Climbing Napoleon was of below average height, you can see the Great Wall of China from space, and high-pivot bikes climb badly. These are some of the most often quoted misconceptions. Some high-pivot bikes can, surely, but only in the same way a four-bar can be bad, or a single-pivot can. I don't think there are any unifying combinations of layout and attributes. Trends, definitely. But even with the definition of a layout, there will always be better and worse executions. On technical terrain, I don't think I've ridden a better climber (stock XC-ish tire not withstanding). There are similarities across high-pivot bikes, but they're not always equally severe in their attributes. Typically, the main attributes of high-pivot bikes are a large amount of grip and excellent tracking. Some, such as the Nicolai Nucleon that we featured during the Enduro Field Test, almost feel grippy to the point of detriment. The Druid is not like that. To put it simply, the bike tracks incredibly well and lets you put the power down when and where you want. The rearward axle paths particularly shine on square edges or any situation that could provide a hanging-up sensation. The long rear and high front also play into this, giving a bike that is easy to maneuver and comfortable, yet with a front that is very well weighted. Bikes have got a lot better for this in recent years, and weight biases in our seat positions have shuffled forward. On some bikes, even now, though, there is sometimes a feeling that one isn’t so much climbing the bike as wrestling it up a hill. This is not only more difficult but also more tiring. The Druid has the tracking and weight distribution to let you sit and spin for most of your climbing. While it might not have the efficiency stamp some riders will want for a 130mm bike, and its relaxed nature and position are far from sporty, it is a very adept technical climber and adequately efficient on smoother sections of pavement. The idler proved to be quiet and didn’t suffer any derailments. If you're searching for a sharper-feeling bike, the Druid just won’t be for you. However, although the idler will be the culprit that takes the blame for this, I would put it down more to its high-stack value than how the chain is routed. Descending The Druid is as equally easy to ride as it is hard to define. 130mm trail bikes tend to come in different flavors and are one of the categories that offer the most genuine variety. On one end of the spectrum, you have the Canyon Neuron, which is alive almost to a fault. It’s light, responsive and sharp. Then you have your Commencal Tempo and Transition Smugglers, which, although the geometry is similar to the Neuron, are a bit more trail-focused and have suspension that feels a bit less about efficiency and a bit more about coping with big hits. The Druid makes those descending-focused trail bikes feel thoroughly undergunned. This bike has both grip and pop that would make some 150mm bikes jealous. The long rear end, the slacker, higher front and the 10mm longer fork mean it can hit things unbelievably hard and offers stability at speed that was as impressive as it was eye-widening. The suspension plays a huge part in this, too. It constantly had me asking a question - is this the best descending 130mm bike I have ever ridden, or a 150mm travel bike that just so happens to have less travel? At some point, the numbers become arbitrary It’s remarkably stable while also being easy to maneuver with small body weight adjustments. I make no secret that I typically enjoy the high-yet-weighted fronts delivered by this style of geometry. It feels like you just have so much of your weight being placed onto the axles from your feet, and then you can add to that with your hands to affect the bike. It gives a bike that takes high-speed sections in its stride while also being fun and agile when on trails of less gradient. The geometry combines with stiffness well too. It's a bike you can really drive in turns, and there is absolutely adequate stiffness to do that, while never feeling overly harsh. This bike with a longer dropper, a grippier rear tire and a bigger front rotor is trail-biking heaven. It’s not perfect, of course. While everything about this bike enables you to feel you can go as fast as you want, what you do when you get there is a bit more nuanced. Firstly, the shock is damped enough, but perhaps only just. I think for heavier riders, or riders who really charge, they might find the compression on the light side. I enjoyed where it took me in the travel and it offers an impressive mix of tracking, support and bottom out, but I sometimes felt like I got through the stroke a bit quickly, which had a tendency to mean the bike felt like it got bullied by the trail. However, this tended to happen on trails that you wouldn't expect to ride a bike with this amount of travel that is so light down, and that shouldn't be overlooked. The axle path is solely rearward, meaning that hangups are basically non-existent. The tracking is sublime for 130mm, and it makes you realize that travel is just a number. Sometimes, when riding high-pivot bikes, you can hit the inflection point (where the axle path goes from rearward to forward) and it can deliver stern hangups, or feel like your weight shifts forward in certain situations. I wonder if, in some ways, limiting the travel of the Druid to 130mm meant they could avoid this altogether and, in some ways, give a better all-around performance. Secondly, I do know that if I bought it, it would see some changes immediately: a bigger front rotor and probably thicker casing tires with a bigger tread on the rear. I would love to see Forbidden offer some divergence in their bikes, maybe something like the BC-editions that Rocky sometimes does. The Druid isn’t a bike for somebody who is after a bit more travel or extra comfort for their XC rides. It’s for people who want to make trail and enduro trails a bit more lively while not smothering all the life out of them. There is a narrow band of trails where this is the absolute best bike, and truthfully, it’s easy to get distracted by bigger, badder trails here in Squamish. Should you happen to find yourself on them, you will be able to ride this down absolutely anything, and it's no surprise to say it can feel overwhelmed, because the geometry and suspension write checks that the 130mm of rear wheel travel and small fork can sometimes find it hard to cash. This bike is as good as it is, but it has to be defined. Forbidden Druid Orbea Occam LT How Does It Compare? It occurred to me while testing the Druid that in some ways it is the exact polar opposite to the Orbea Occam that I tested earlier this year. The Druid feels like a true enduro bike, with everything save for the required travel, whereas the Occam gave a suspension feel that felt like it was a lightweight trail bike that just gave you more margin for error thanks to the sheer volume. If I had to race an enduro race tomorrow, which would I pick? The Druid. In a heartbeat. If I had to do a stage of the BC Bike Race? Probably the Occam. It just goes to show that travel is but an indication of the bike's intentions but isn't always the be all and end all. The high-front, long rear geometry of the Druid makes it feel like a bigger beast than the Occam, for both the good and the bad. Prices in USD. Which Model is the Best Value? Between the three bikes, I would be torn between the entry-level GX AXS RS S and the mid-level bike that features a similar spec, save for more adjustment on the suspension and better brakes. $800 USD is a lot of money, but I would try to go with the mid-level bike if I could. It'll be ready to ride (save for the dropper, which will be too short). The bike has a great spec, save for some key dimensions Technical Report RockShox Suspension: The Lyrik really is a very good fork. RockShox forks tend to feel like they track the ground better, sucking up any bump they come across, whereas sometimes Fox feels like they sacrifice some sensitivity to drive the wheel into the ground harder. I think there are few places the distinction in feel is so obvious as the Lyrik and 36. The 36 feels more like a true enduro fork, and the Lyrik is more trail-focused. For that reason, it feels more appropriate on a 130mm bike. It can still be pushed incredibly hard, but for me, 35mm legs are plenty. Rotors, Tires and Dropper: All of these parts, on their own, are excellent. It's just a shame that they ended up either being the wrong size for a bike of this nature or with too much of an eye on being lightweight. Bikes aren't cheap, and the idea of spending the best part of $10,000 dollars on a bike, only to then have to spend more money on filling in some key component gaps, seems utterly mad to me. Carbon Touches: I don't often ride Burgtec bars if only because it seems they don't come on the test bikes I get to review. Similar with Crankbrothers carbon wheels. That said, both were great during this test, providing comfort and low weight. Pros + Makes you question what travel really means + Grip, pop and consistent suspension really shine + Geometry is on the cutting edge of the new school + Efficient and grippy climber on technical terrain Cons - It doesn't do what you'd want a 130mm bike to do - Not as lively as some 130mm bikes - Descent focused geometry won't be for everyone Pinkbike's Take I don’t really know what the Druid is. Well, that’s untrue. I know it’s an amazing feat of engineering, but maybe better yet, I don’t really know what it’s for. It’s a fun, incredibly capable bike that defies whatever you think you know about what 130mm can get you. The catch is that if the Druid doesn't excel on the trails you might buy a 130mm bike to ride to begin with. It's a trail bike for those who love descents and want something livelier and more responsive than a classic enduro rig. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Forbidden Forbidden Druid Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: The Forbidden Reya Throws Conventional XC Geometry Out the Window
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Forbidden Reya Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/T57cVls6 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 22:15 A few years back, while visiting Forbidden Bikes’ HQ, I saw one of their riders rolling around on a high-pivot frame shod with a full XC trim. It caught me off guard because I was there to check out the Druid V2 (Forbidden’s 130mm-travel trail bike). Was this just a goofy experiment or were they testing the XC waters? Surely, even Forbidden’s marketing lore wouldn’t be enough to sway watt counters and calorie counters over to the dark side of a high-pivot. The bike in question turned out to be just a Druid V1, however, Forbidden was about to enter the world of XC. For the 120mm-travel Reya, Forbidden decided to go for a “regular” suspension design. It may have a carbon frame, two 29” wheels, and 120mm of travel, but there’s very little that’s regular about this XC bike. Reya Details • 120mm travel, 130mm fork • Full carbon frame • Dual 29" wheels • Four-bar, Horst-Link suspension design • Sizes: S1, S2, S3 (tested), S4 • Size-specific chainstays (S3: 449mm) • Weight: 12.9 kg / 28.5 lb (T2 model, S3 size) • Price: CAD $7,699 - 12,999 / USD $6,299 - 10,499 • Frame kit: CAD $4,199 / USD $3,399 / UK £3,199 / EU €3,799 • forbiddenbikes.com Forbidden has applied its Trifecta geometry theory to the Reya where the front-to-rear center ratio holds across all frame sizes. You can read more about that on the Forbidden website, but the Cole’s Notes is that the Reya’s geometry is extreme, particularly the rear half of the bike. Find me another 120mm travel bike with a 65-degree head tube angle and 449mm chainstay length (S3 frame size). Contents Introduction Pinkbike's Take Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Technical Report Pros & Cons Forbidden threw conventional XC geometry out the window with the Reya. This isn’t your mom or dad’s awkward 29er from 2015 that tries to pitch you over the bars the moment it encounters the smallest pile of rocks. It's long, low, and slack - a conversation we've heard over and over with enduro bikes. Except this is an XC bike - an XC for the new-school, where the downhill pace is just as important as the climb. What's more is that the stability and grip that the Reya delivers exceeds some longer-travel bikes I've ridden. Make no mistake, this isn't a bike you can blindly huck into oblivion. If you treat it nicely, you'll be rewarded with a lighter, freeing sense on almost any trail. — Matt Beer Frame Details Aesthetically, the Reya is an eye-pleasing bike. The slim, round tubes flow straight from the rear axle to the head tube, almost reminiscent of a hardtail. Those play into the structural elements and qualitative findings of the frame too. Where have we seen small, round tubes before? Oh yeah, steel frames. They’re known for their damped ride qualities. Although it’s a totally different material, the Reya’s tubing is comfortable in the right zones without being a limp noodle. Forbidden decided to pass on internal frame storage. It adds complication to the frame design with extra pieces, and ironically, cutting a hole in the downtube adds weight, since more material is needed to reinforce the area. That said, the Reya (2,270g, S2) is far from the astoundingly light Scott Spark RC (1,427g, size MD). Then again, the Reya isn’t intended to be an XCO race bike. Another consideration that they tossed out was fitment for a second water bottle. I suppose, if you really wanted to, you could rig up your own on the underside of the downtube. Otherwise, a small hydration vest is your other option. The final details aren’t skimped either. Cables and hoses don’t take the tourist route through the headset, but there is one unusual feature. Forbidden added a second route through the downtube for a remote shock lockout. Bikes have been ditching cables for batteries at a staggering pace, but this is one extra line that I think could come in handy on the Reya. Suspension Design The first thing you probably noticed about this Forbidden full-suspension bike is the lack of a high-pivot and idler wheel. Building the suspension platform around a 4-bar, Horst-Link layout defined an axle path better suited to this style of bike. As much as Forbidden has done to reduce drag on idler wheels, I could only imagine the moaning from watt-counters and the efficiency loss. Overall, the Reya’s suspension is very progressive. A small, carbon rocker link starts at a fairly high leverage rate around 3.5, or possibly higher (it’s not clear based on the graph provided). The leverage curve, combined with a bearing eyelet, helps the 45mm stroke air shock break into the travel effortlessly. Towards the end, the rate drops to around 2.0, ramping up the progression. We’re working with 120mm of travel, so bottom-outs do happen; however, they can be anticipated. In general, the Reya’s suspension is a world away from some wooden-feeling XC bikes, aimed specifically at racing. Wallowy would be an overstatement, but I’d err on the high side of shock pressures, as the geometry can be swayed quickly by just a few psi difference, but more on that below in the Setup section. Geometry The numbers behind the Reya make it very unique, almost polarizing. It may split riders into two camps. On one side, riders accustomed to the fit of a traditional XC bike (short wheelbase, steep head tube angle, slacker seat tube angle) may find the fit and handling foreign. Others, coming from the gravity side of the sport, who are used to stretched wheelbases and slack head tubes, will rejoice, “An XC bike that feels like my downhill bike!” At 65 degrees, the Reya is as slack as the legendary Iron Horse Sunday. Taking the effective top tube length into consideration is another important factor. The seat tube angle is steep for this category, at 77 degrees. That’s close to most modern enduro bikes, which have become increasingly steeper to hold your weight forward as the bike sags into the plentiful travel. The Reya only has 120mm, though. For that reason, I would anticipate most riders won’t need to slam the seat forward. As always, trying before you buy is a wise decision. Like all of Forbidden’s bikes, the chainstays make up a large portion of the Reya’s wheelbase. They are sized proportionally, according to their ideal front-to-rear center ratio, so the larger the frame, the longer the chainstay. The S3 uses a 449mm rear center, putting the Reya in a unique spot for this travel bracket. It’s long, at least on the S3, but not unmanageable. Where things get crazy is the 464mm chainstay on the S4. The reach on the S3 is 475mm, 5-10mm shorter than most brands’ size Large frames. I’d suggest studying the wheelbase of other bikes that you’ve been fond of and using that as a gauge more than the reach. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $7699 Travel 120 Rear Shock RockShox Deluxe Ultimate, LinearXL Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate, 130mm travel, Charger 3.1 RC2 damper Headset Cane Creek 40 ZS44/ZS56 Cassette SRAM XS-1275 Crankarms SRAM S1000, 165mm, SRAM 32T Alloy Chainring Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA Rear Derailleur SRAM GX T-Type Chain SRAM GX T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM Pod Rocker Handlebar OneUp Alloy, 20mm rise, 800mm width Stem BikeYoke Barkeeper, 45mm length Grips Forbidden lock-on, 128mm length Brakes SRAM Motive Silver, organic pads, 180mm Centerlock F&R Wheelset DT Swiss 350 Spline Tires Front: Maxxis Forekaster, 29x2.4" 3C MaxxTerra, EXO, Rear: Maxxis Rekon, 29x2.4" 3C MaxxTerra, EXO Seat Fizik Terra Ridon X5, 145mm Width Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post V3 31.6mm | Size Specific Travel S1:150mm / S2:180mm / S3:180mm / S4:210mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Action photos by Joe Wakefield. Test Bike Setup Air pressure on the Reya is key. Forbidden provides a comprehensive suspension setup guide . The advised range is between 32% sag (measure while seated) for the soft side, while 29% is the recommended firm setup. I preferred 28% sag to find support in the rear suspension a little sooner in the travel. That higher air pressure lined up with the firmer air pressure in the fork. Essentially, I set the bike up for someone who weighs closer to 88 kg (190lb). Admittedly, I’m at the lower height limit for the S3 frame. I factored in the effective top tube length, as well as reach, and have been happy with that decision. I did need to swap the 210mm dropper post out for a 180mm so I could lower the post and still make use of the full travel. The saddle wasn’t completely slammed forward either. A few other changes I made were chopping the bars down to 760mm, and eventually even narrower to 750mm. By doing this, my arms had more range of motion to move back and forth, and weight the front or rear wheel when needed. The stock grips were immediately banished to the storage bin, as were the organic brake pads. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 39 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Testing Info: Putting miles in on the Reya was very enjoyable and made lugging an enduro bike out of the garage a bit demoralizing. I kept the gradient and severity of the trails in check and mainly aimed at faster, low-angle singletrack. Many of those routes have been part of race courses throughout the decades of XC racing in and around Squamish, such as Lower Powersmart, Word of Mouth, and Powerhouse Plunge (all of which have seen recent work - thanks, trail builders). Springtime moisture didn't last long, but luckily, a few showers kept dry spells to a minimum. Download the Trailforks App Long, low, and slack - for the unorthodox XC rider. Climbing If you’re coming from a traditional XC bike with a short 435mm chainstay and a steep, 68-degree head tube angle, expect a polarizing ride. The Reya won’t be for everyone who regularly rides in Lycra. Forbidden knows this and has leaned into it: the Reya is supposed to be different. The first switch-up is the climbing position. The seat tube angle is 2.5 degrees steeper than that of a Specialized Epic 8 Evo and even a full degree steeper than that of a Transition Spur (more on that comparison below). Combined with the long chainstay, it may actually mean shifting some weight back to get traction. You'll have no worries about lifting the front wheel. Secondly, the leverage rate is more akin to, again, an enduro bike. Generally, XC bikes start firm and then settle into a softer zone. The Reya throws that concept out the window and is plush from the get-go. This invites you to stay seated more often while maintaining traction over fickle little roots, but it can have some negative effects on the geometry when you start to jump on the cranks. When the sensitive suspension compresses, it further slackens the head angle and changes the balance of the bike quickly. This takes some core work to keep the Reya’s front wheel pointed straight when climbing steeper grades or rounding super tight switchbacks. Through undulating terrain, when you're moving in and out of a seated position or pumping the ground to build speed, it can feel almost too active. Wisely, Forbidden anticipated this with a workaround: the frame features cable routing for a remote shock lockout. Adding an extra cable seems like blasphemy on such a polished-looking frame, but I think it would be a worthy and highly useful feature. The Reya also has a low BB. Forbidden accounted for this by specifying 165mm cranks. Say what? Short cranks are becoming the norm, even for some World Cup XC racers. It may take some learning when and where to time your pedal strokes between obstacles. As you might expect, many of those points are forgotten about when it's time to descend. The Reya moves uphill quickly. I'm not one to record many rides, but I clocked a few PRs during this review. What it's all about on the Reya: unlocking singletrack that would be otherwise boring on an enduro bike. Descending The Reya’s suspension delivers traction and support like a bigger travel bike. It lets you ride the way you intended to, instead of succumbing to the limits of shitty suspension and geometry designed only to climb. The only problem is, you can easily send it too hard. Many riders will reach for burlier casing tires, more robust wheels, and bigger brakes, but that’s the wrong move, if you ask me. The intent of the Reya is so that you can feel like you’ve pressed the fast-forward on flatter trails. Don’t be dumb. Don’t try to build the Reya into something it’s not. Be smart, and you’ll have an absolute blast carrying speed on trails where your enduro bike left you feeling bogged down. Composure is not always found in short-travel bikes, but the Reya says differently. The sensitive, yet supportive suspension stays active under braking. For 120mm of travel, it does well at keeping the wheels on the ground through chatter and even handles the big chop nicely. Hitting small features or dropping into large holes in the trail can touch the bottom, but it’s not painful. When it does occur, it’s usually something you can predict and brace for. The long wheelbase and slack head tube angle help out massively in that regard, too. You don’t feel like you’re riding a short rocking horse, about to get bucked over the bars. It’s part of the reason why you can ride so fast and confidently on this “XC” bike. Across high-speed marbles and dusty machine-made tracks, you feel grounded and safe when cornering. Usually, the speed and frequency of this type of trail can make a short travel bike feel like a shopping cart. Even with thinner, speedy little tires, the Reya remains very quiet. There’s a sense of calmness to the frame that I haven’t found on other carbon bikes. It could be the round and slim tubing I mentioned earlier. Beating down those high-speed washboard trails comes with less shuddering than expected. It’s just another reason why the Reya handles speed with ease. Finding weak points in the Reya’s descending ability is honestly a challenge. One zone where it takes some planning and a bit more finesse is wiggling around tight, steeper corners where you can’t lean the bike over as much. The longer wheelbase might mean a Euro-hop or keeping tabs on derailleur clearance, but that’s a tradeoff to be expected. Not afraid to get going over the marbles with this wheelbase. What's the Best Value? The top model in the Reya lineup caters towards a lighter, racier component trim - you can tell by the tires alone. The gucci gold Reya T1 has a Fox 34 SL Factory fork, DT Swiss XRC 1500 carbon wheels, and a full SRAM XX Transmission drivetrain. As usual, when the weight drops, prices take a hike. The T1 build weighs in at 11.74 kg / 25.88 lb (S2 size) and costs $10,499 USD. Dang! Our review T2 model is considerably less expensive than the T1 at $7,699 USD and only 1.25 kg heavier. A high-performance build remains with a RockShox Pike and Deluxe of the Ultimate variety. Then there’s also SRAM’s GX Transmission, Motive Silver brakes, alloy DT Swiss 1500 XR wheels, and knobbier tires. Not bad. The winner for value in my books is the $6,299 USD T3 option. This one only gains a few hundred grams, but maintains solid suspension, wheels, and brakes. All of the tradeoffs are worth the savings, if you ask me. Reya T3: CAD $7,699 / USD $6,299 / UK £6,099 / EU €7,299 // RockShox Pike Select+ fork, RockShox Deluxe Select+ shock, SRAM Eagle 90 T-type drivetrain, SRAM Motive Bronze brakes, DT 1900 X wheels w/370 Hubs, Forbidden cockpit, Maxxis Forekaster (F)/Rekon (R) 3C Maxx Terra EXO tires. Weight: 13.34 kg / 29.4 lb (S2 size) Forbidden Reya T2: $7,699 USD Transition Spur XT Di2: $7,499 USD How Does It Compare? The cage match you've been waiting for: the Forbidden Reya versus the Transition Spur. The Spur is one of the bikes that helped spark this new-school approach to geometry for bikes in the 120mm travel bracket. It all starts with a carbon frame that uses a flexstay suspension layout, eliminating the need for bearings at the dropout pivot. At 5 lb flat (2.27 kg), the Reya wins out by 0.59 lb (270 g), possibly due to its slim tubing and lack of downtube storage. What matters more than a cup of water in weight is their ride qualities on the trail. The Spur’s chainstay is shorter, and the head tube angle is steeper, even in the low mode. That makes it slightly reserved and sensitive on the downhills, yet it's still able to rally. The Spur's seated position is more relaxed with a slacker seat tube angle, which puts more weight over the shorter back end. The result is a longer cockpit feel that, at times, calls for a slight weight shift forward to keep the front end down. Ground clearance is another area that stands out. Although both bikes rock shorter 165mm cranks, navigating pedal strokes through rocks, stumps, and other tricky pieces of climb trails is breezier on the Spur. Suspension-wise, the Spur is still adequately supple, but isn’t as eager to move into the travel, for better or worse. The sensitivity is good, but not as fluttery as the Reya. Where it wins out is sprinting and pumping. The Spur has a slight edge in response here. All of these differences seem small on the screen in front of you, but it’s the sum of the parts that makes the Reya profoundly unique on the trail. When it's time to throw down cash, it's a toss-up: both bikes have excellent parts kits with only $200 between the two of them. If in-frame storage or geometry adjustments are really important to you, then the Spur has those. It may come down to your taste in suspension, brakes, and drivetrain: the $7,499 Spur is a full Shimano/Fox dealio, where the Reya uses the SRAM/RockShox tag team. 20mm of spacers below a 20mm bar, and I could go higher. SRAM's Motive brakes are decent, even around the steeps of Squamish. Swapping to metallic pads made a considerable difference. Technical Report OneUp Alloy 20mm rise bar and Forbidden grips: On paper, a 20mm-rise bar seems appropriate, but I maxed out the stem overlap with the steer tube. Swapping for a higher-rise bar wouldn’t be an unreasonable proposition. As a result, a fair bit of weight is transferred into the grips. I went for a cushy pair of SDG Thrice Open Core from day one. The stock grips aren't very thick or soft, plus they have an odd tapered shape. Your hands will thank you later. Maxxis Forekaster/Rekon: Don’t underestimate this duo. The Rekon’s rippled tread is more predictable than downright grippy. But that allows it to adhere to a variety of soil and rock. Braking, climbing and cornering are trustworthy in any scenario. Up front, the Forekaster performs best in looser dirt. I wouldn’t rule out something with more supportive sideknobs, given the corners you can carve with the Reya. SRAM Motive brakes : Dario isn’t afraid to put Mavens on his XC bike, and you’re welcome to do so too; however, I think the Motives are a fine fit for the Reya. The one stipulation is the pad compound. Switching to metallic pads will boost braking effectiveness on extended or steeper descents. Just remember, once you commit to one pad material, those rotors will be matched to that type best. It's a long bike, but side hits are possible. Pros + New school geometry opens up much more terrain for an XC bike + Unbeatable suspension performance in the 120mm-travel category + Quiet and muted frame Cons - Handling is a bit wonky across undulating, low-speed trails - No geometry adjustments (if you care about that) Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Forbidden Forbidden Reya Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: 2025 Forestal Siryon - Balanced Handling With Some Practical Pitfalls
author: Seb Stott
article_text: Forestal Syrion Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/oJngOn8g Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 10:37 Note: When asked about the rattling water bottle mount and downtube protection, Forestal claimed these are indeed pre-production issues. Most brands were making pedal-powered bikes for years before adding e-bikes to their range. For Forestal, it's the other way round - this is their first bike without a motor. It takes its lead from Forestal's e-bikes, with a distinctive elevated swingarm, linkage single pivot suspension and a full-carbon frame. It has 170 mm of travel and a 29" wheel at either end. There is no mullet option or a flip-chip to adapt to a 27.5" rear wheel. The Diōde: build we have on test costs a cool € 8,999, but there is a Halō model for € 6,999 or a frame kit for € 3,999. Forestal Siryon Details • Wheel size: 29" only • 170 mm travel f&r • 64.1° head angle, 76° seat angle (measured) • 445 chainstay • Downtube storage • Sizes: S/M, L, XL • Actual weight as tested: 16.4 kg / 36.2 lb (XL, no pedals) • 7-year frame warranty • Price: € 8,999 (Diōde: model tested) • forestal.com The Syrion offers a well-rounded ride feel, but it has a few bugs to bear in mind. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Forestal is well-mannered and versatile on the trail, thanks to its long-travel yet efficient suspension and rounded geometry. — Seb Stott Frame Details That one-piece swingarm connects to a pair of links that drive the shock via a sizeable shock extender. That long extender increases the buckling load on the shock, and this is widely considered to contribute to shock wear over time. The shock is nestled between two carbon struts that tie the seat tube to the top tube. These are designed to stiffen the frame, but make it awkward to access the shock to measure sag or adjust the air pressure. The downtube offers a handy storage compartment, and the hatch includes a Fidlock water bottle mount. But it's a relatively narrow opening, and the door on my test bike rattles when riding over rough terrain with a full water bottle attached. Forestal say this will be fixed for full production bikes. A full-size 800 ml Fidlock bottle fits fine, but there are no additional accessory mounts like you see on many modern frames. While I'm complaining, the rubber downtube protection on my test bike started peeling off after a handful of rides. Again, Forestal say there is a different bonding tape for production bikes, which hopefully will fix this. All cable routing goes through the headset - there's no other routing option, which could be a headache when it comes to servicing the upper headset bearing. The headset also has a steering limiter to protect against crash damage. There's a small plastic fender to protect from debris. The 2.6" tires pictured are not production spec; they were fitted by Forestal due to supply issues and left too little room for mud or stones to pass through without scraping. There isn't that much room, even with the production-spec 2.5" Schwalbe Albert tires I replaced them with, which measure up narrower than many 2.5" tires from other brands. There is an integrated upper chain guide and ISCG tabs, but no bash protection as standard. On a positive note, the bottom bracket is threaded. Suspension Design The swingarm connects to the middle of a short aluminum link, the top of which connects to the shock extender and the bottom connects to another short link, which then connects to the seat tube. It's a classic linkage-driven single-pivot affair, much like the old Saracen Myst, just packaged in a more tucked-away fashion. Play Video Play Current Time 0:00 Loaded : 0% Progress : 0% Duration Time 0:00 Mute 1080p 720p 480p 240p Quality 480p Fullscreen This is a modal window. Caption Settings Dialog Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Forestal Syrion suspension squish by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 2,492 Faves: 1 Comments: 0 According to Forestal, the Syrion's leverage ratio starts very high, meaning less force is needed to move the shock into the start of its travel. The leverage ratio drops by 37% throughout the travel, making it one of the most progressive bikes out there, on paper. However, the all-important change in leverage ratio between sag (about 50 mm wheel travel) and bottom-out isn't too extreme. That means there's plenty of support to resist bottom-outs without making it impossible to reach full-travel. There's a decent amount of anti-squat to hold the bike up in its travel. At sag, this ranges from 100% in the 52-tooth sprocket up to 118% in the 10-tooth sprocket. However, anti-squat drops off throughout the travel, so there will be less support the further the suspension compresses. Being a single-pivot, anti-rise starts high and drops off slightly deeper in the travel. This means the suspension will sit deeper in its travel than most while braking, but may be less comfortable when braking over choppy ground. Geometry & Sizing There are only three sizes to choose from, but the 29"-only strategy may not be ideal for short riders anyway. Recommended heights for each size are as follows: S/M, 160 cm - 177cm / L,177 cm - 187 cm / XL, > 187 cm. At 191 cm / 6'3", the XL fit me well. The reach is generous, but the stack height is relatively short, and the head angle is steeper than most bikes in this travel bracket, leading to a front-center length that's a little more compact than average. I needed a higher-rise handlebar to get the grips high enough. I measured the effective seat angle at my pedalling height at 76 degrees - considerably slacker than advertised. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $10178 Travel 170 mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Factory 2-Position, 230x65mm Fork Fox Factory 38, GRIP X2 - 170mm Headset Acros Integrated ZS 56/56, internal cable routing, stainless bearings Cassette SRAM X0 T-Type, 10-52T Crankarms SRAM X0 T-Type DUB, Length: 165mm Chainguide Integrated top guide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB, threaded Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM X01 Eagle AXS T-Type Chain SRAM X01 Eagle T-Type Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM Eagle AXS Controller Handlebar Forestal Oxydon C 780mm, 31.8mm clamp, 25mm rise Stem Forestal Oxydon 45mm Grips Production Privée CR35 Brakes Formula Cura 4 Carbon 203mm/180mm Wheelset Crankbrothers Synthesis Enduro I9 Alloy 29" Hubs Industry 9 6-bolt Spokes Sapim E-Light (f) / Sapim Strong (r) Rim Synthesis enduro alloy, 31.5 mm (f) / 29.5 mm (r) Tires Schwalbe Albert Gravity 2.5 - UltraSoft (f) / Soft (r) Seat Fizik Aidon Seatpost OneUp V3, 31.6mm, Length: 150-160mm (S/M) / 170-180mm (L, XL) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC I upgraded to this 230 mm dropper from the first ride. Test Bike Setup I was happy with the suspension support with just under 30% sag and stock volume spacers. Because sag is tricky to measure on this bike, I only measured sag for the initial setup, then went off pressures alone thereafter, ending up with 235 psi. I set the Fox 38 to my usual settings, with 98 psi when used with the Grip X2 damper, one volume spacer, and compression and rebound fairly open. My test bike was shipped with 2.6" Schwalbe tires, which are not the correct spec. I quite liked how they handled, but they were too big for the frame's clearance. I swapped them out for 2.5" Schwalbe Albert tires after the photos in this article were taken. I also swapped the stock 180 mm rear rotor for a 200 mm one; the 25 mm rise handlebar for 40 mm, and the 180 mm-travel dropper (160 mm in S/M size) for 230 mm. While I think the front end is too low for an XL, you could chalk the handlebar up to personal choice; the rotor and dropper travel are simply too small for a modern enduro bike in my view. With the 2.6" Schwalbe Radial tires with the Gravity casing, I was able to run pretty much standard pressures - 23-24 psi front, and 26-27 psi rear. But with the 2.5" size and Trail casing, these went up a couple of psi. Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 32 Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 37" / 93cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place in the Tweed Valley, on familiar trails at Glentress, Innerleithen (pictured) and Cademuir. I also rode at Comrie Croft. The DH trails at Innerleithen that we recently used for our tuned mass damper testing are where the strengths and weaknesses of a bike are most exposed. Climbing The Syrion pedals well for the most part, with enough support to feel efficient for its travel when sprinting or spinning, while dealing with bumps under power nicely. However, the real-world seat angle is a little slack, making the seated position too far back for attacking the steepest climbs. The front wheel is prone to go light, and this caused me to stall on technical climbs more than once.
