This means that while it does more force to get the bike to move through its stroke at later stages, the change the leverage undergoes is relatively consistent. Giant Trance X Suspension Cycle https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/nl9MeLGs Copied Live 00:00 00:53 00:53 Much like the geometry, it's eggs-over-easy. Although there might not be any strange outliers, I think that's part of the charm of both the Trance and the Maestro 3 system. It's carefully considered and controlled. I also like the fact that its anti-squat values are lower than what some brands might be tempted to incorporate into their mid-travel trail bike's suspension. At 30 lb, this bike was never going to be sluggish on the climbs, but the slightly lower anti-squat values set it up to be a good technical climber too. Specifications Price $8000 Travel 140 Rear Shock Fox Float X Factory, 185/52.5 Fork Fox 36 Factory, 150mm, GRIP 2 damper Cassette SRAM XO, T-Type, 10x52 Crankarms SRAM XO, T-Type, 30t Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB, press fit Rear Derailleur SRAM XO Eagle AXS, T-Type Chain SRAM XO, T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Giant Contact SLR Trail Integrated Stem Giant Contact SLR Trail Integrated Brakes Shimano Deore XT BR-M8120, [F]203mm, [R]180mm Wheelset Giant TRX WheelSystem Tires Maxxis Minion DHF, 29x2.5, 3C MaxxTerra, EXO / Dissector 29x2.4, 3C MaxxTerra Seat Giant Romero SL Seatpost Giant Contact Switch Adjustable Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Giant Trance X Test Bike Setup The test bike was a mixture of SRAM X0 T-type, Shimano brakes and a healthy slew of Giant's own brand parts. That's not to say it wasn't without its quirks. While I have always enjoyed the XT brake pads, the one-piece steel rotors did seem to manage heat noticeably worse than the more expensive IceTech range. While they're still respectable, this bike isn't exactly cheap. Henry Quinney Location: Squamish Height: 183cm / 6' Inseam: 82 cm / 32.5" Weight: 79 kg / 174 lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @henryquinney The Giant own-brand kit was, across the board, well spec'd and proportioned. The integrated handlebar might put some off, but thanks to its adjustments in both roll and effective stem length, it was easy to get into a position that suited. Typically, I run my bars relatively rolled forward, and I found that there was ample adjustment to replicate this position. The dropper post, which was 200mm in length, could also be reduced by 30mm. I didn't make use of this, and found 200mm to be ample. The EXO casing tires were suitable for a 140 mm bike. That said, while the DHF is a good tire, there are better options for an all-rounder front tire. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Similar to my suggested test route for the Druid I reviewed recently, looking out for SORCA's race loops is always a good bet. Something like this would be a great day on a bike in the woods like the Trance. The low weight of the Trance makes it feel very fast. Climbing Different trail bikes climb in different ways, and luckily enough, there are enough different flavours that anyone can surely find their preference. There are stomp-heavy diehards with enough anti-squat to give you something very efficient, even if perhaps not particularly supple over roots and edges. There are the honey-I’ve-shrunk-the-enduro-bike types which have a shortened stroke but are essentially the same bike as their longer travel cousins, which can suffer from particularly steep seat tube angles and seem more about giving an enduro bike more pop and support than about making a bike that thrives in rolling terrain. Then there are outliers like the Forbidden Druid and Norco Optic, which try to combine low weight and a high pivot to give a bike that is a lot of fun, even if hard to define. There is also grip aplenty, with easy tracking over bumps and lumps. Then there are bikes like the Trance. True trail bikes that somehow feel pure in their remit. Bikes such as the Trance feel like they’re about being the absolute best at riding singletrack and natural tech terrain, all while doing so with efficiency and balance. A huge part of that is how the bike climbs on mountain bike trails, rather than worrying how it climbs on pavement or on steep fire roads. The Trance offers very well-balanced geometry, which excels on flowing trail climbs. The seat tube is amply steep yet slack enough that you feel you can canter along without having too much weight channelled through your hands. However, at the heart of the Trance’s climbing composure is the Maestro 3 system. Whereas typically, a trail bike might have an anti-squat value in the range of 110%, the Trance's is much lower. In fact, the only part of the Giant's travel where the anti-squat is higher than 100% is at the very start, which isn't somewhere the rider spends a lot of time. Furthermore, the high-leverage rate at this same point should help to offset any harshness felt should the drivetrain try to inhibit suspension movement. Where the rider spends most of the time when climbing, usually around 15% on either side of sag, is far closer to 80-85%. This change might not seem like much, but the effect in the real world is large. The Trance offers excellent tracking while climbing, all while being efficient and being able to handle surges in power. And how does it handle pavement? Well, a 30 lb 29er is never going to be exactly sluggish. While it might not offer a massive platform when compared to some other bikes in the category and suits a rider who spins more than one who stamps, it is a very quick and spritely bike. Besides, should you be faced with a long climb, you always do have the option of the lockout lever. I never found the need, though. Descending The Trance has two things at its core - balance and consistency. Everything about this bike is well-measured, and it's a bike happily bereft of any quirky geometry features or strange sizing dimensions. It's seen Giant climb down from the larger reach of the previous version and also knows which key dimensions have worked in the past. At 439mm, the stays are slightly longer than many of its competitors and combine well with the 628mm stack, which is also slightly higher than what one might expect from a trail bike from even a year or two ago. This doesn't seem like much, but placing emphasis on balance for these dimensions (the previous Trance X, in its shortest slackest setting, had a reach of 486mm). This gives a bike that is very composed and able to build on the concept of modern geometry instead of trying to reinvent it. All this, and you still get the headset cups to add or subtract a further 5mm of reach. For the record, I'd personally be more likely to go for 475mm than 485, and I found this bike rode best with the stem in the 50mm setting. Flow-loam is my, and the Trance's, favorite type of trail currently. Whether plumbing along seated traverses, turning on a dime, or pointing and shooting through rough and committing chop, the Trance is well executed and composed. Again, the predictable nature of the suspension plays into this. There is no ramp severe ramp-up to fettle and tune or a fear of falling through the midstroke when really pushing. Everything about the platform is consistent, easy and settled. Nothing is perfect, though, and it could sometimes hang up on square edges that took the bike deeper into its travel. That said, it didn't seem to provide any particularly adverse pedal feedback. On faster chatter, whether the bike was taking small yet high-frequency hits, you could get initial feedback through your feet as the bike broke into its stroke. That said, it was largely quite calm, and it didn't come to the fore on the singletrack trails that it was designed for. All around, I'm a big fan of the Maestro 3 system for shorter travel bikes. Packing as much as possible into 140mm is no easy task, and I think Giant has chosen their features well - it's consistent, efficient and blends great support with very adequate small bump sensitivity. In regards to stiffness, the bike feels direct and stiff, all while being comfortable. This stiffness, coupled with the carbon wheels, which do an excellent job of keeping the bead of the tire locked in place, can mean the tires really take the brunt of any lateral flex. During testing, I often rubbed the tire on the stay. While burping a tire is no good thing, I do wonder if there could have been a better compromise of rim flex or give on the bead to stop the tire from leaning over so much. Better yet, you would just make the frame with more tire clearance. The Trance is a bike that can really handle being pushed hard. And what about the handlebars that look like a prop from Stargate SG1? Well, retrieve your sick from out of your cornflakes because they're actually okay. They have a very middle-of-the-road feel and have enough adjustment to make their weight benefits well worth it, to my mind. That said, I think my curiosity for carbon bars has long since died a death, and I am always happy with alloy. The stem does come with neat integrations, though, be it for Garmin or action cameras. In some ways, the bar represents the entire bike - light, adjustable, well proportioned, and far more suitable for your average rider than you might initially think. Giant Trance X Advanced Forbidden Druid How Does It Compare? In the pink corner, we have the ridiculous kit car of your dreams, and in the blue corner, we have your parent's retirement present to themselves - a Volvo saloon that is as pristine as it is sensible. Well, when you get out on the open-trail, these bikes aren't so different after all. Both are rad . Whatever that means. The Giant is a better trail bike, if only because it's better at doing what a trail bike does - riding single-black trails and dark blues with confidence and precision. The Druid is a more exciting prospect... but better? That's harder to say. There is a section of riders, who want enduro-features without enduro-travel, that the Druid will suit better. However, if I want the classic trail bike, albeit very well executed, I think the Trance Advanced X has a far wider potential audience and would probably be what I went for myself. It just feels sharper on mellower terrain and can still handle some very wild sections of trail. Prices in USD. Which Model is the Best Value? Although Giant is a huge company, its presence in North America is somewhat checkered, and only three models of the Trance X are available in the country. Realistically, however nice the carbon bike is, $8,000 is such a huge amount of money. I would go for the sensibly specced Trance X1, and take several life-changing mountain bike holidays with the spare change. Shimano SLX is more than enough for me, and the alloy frame has the same features as the carbon one. Giant parts have gotten a lot better in recent years and shouldn't be sniffed at. Technical Report Giant Finishing Kit: Yes, you read that correctly. Giant has done a very good job not only with the bike but also with the whole ensemble. Everything from the adjustable stroke dropper, bar, stem, and wheels didn’t put a foot wrong during the test period. Mismatched Spec Comes Good: Some people might be irked by the mix of SRAM and Shimano, although it personally doesn't bother me too much. I would happily have XT brakes over Codes RSC. In fact, I would rather have a mechanical XT groupset than a T-type, but that's maybe a story for another time. All that said, the cheap RT66 rotors seem a little low-end on a bike, so expensive. Adjustment and Frame Tech: Giant made some ripples with the last generation of the Trance by offering a flip chip that actually offered a serious swing in the numbers. They pale in comparison to what's offered on the new bike, though. If I was picky, it would be great to see them integrate the same brake-mounting system from the Glory that enables easy chainstay adjustment. However, that would probably also entail an increase in weight. Either way, the more adjustments on offers that go beyond 17-way flip chips that are give with one hand and taken away with the other, the better. Pros + A true trail bike, at a low weight + Neutral and consistent suspension inspire confidence + Geometry that is the product of years of refinement + Plenty of adjustment. Cons - North America doesn't see the full range of models - Can hang up deeper in the stroke Pinkbike's Take The Trance is both extreme and extremely sensible. The suspension does a fantastic job with the 140mm on offer, combining grip and support that could make longer travel bikes blush. The damped support will let riders push hard, and the geometry is proportioned, adjustable, and balanced enough to keep up. Its features and sheer performance represent everything a rider could want. Plus, it's a 30 lb trail bike that, for once, actually weighs 30 lbs. I thoroughly enjoyed riding the Trance X, and came away very impressed. Your parents' Volvo, it is not. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Giant Giant Trance X Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: Giant TRX Carbon Wheels
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Giant TRX Carbon Wheel System Giant may not be the first brand that comes to mind when you mention carbon wheels, but their TRX series has been available on complete bikes, as well as aftermarket for a few years. In a somewhat confusing naming scheme, the new "TRX Carbon Wheel System" replaces the outgoing TRX 2 model. The revised version boasts features built for heavy-duty trail and enduro riding scenarios. A key factor in the rim’s resilience is thanks to a technology that Giant calls their “Wide Guard” sidewall design which they says is extremely strong and reduces flat tires. That’s not limited to carbon rims though. An alloy version shares the same design. At the center, the TRX HE (high-engagement) hub features a 6-pawl design and cartridge bearings. There's also an even more robust eMTB specific version available too. TRX Carbon • Wheel size: 29" or MX • Intended use: trail/enduro • Rim material: carbon fiber • Rim dimension: 30mm (internal) • 5mm Wide Guard rim sidewall • 28 straight-pull spokes per wheel • Hubs: 10x110 front, 12x148 Boost w/XD or MS freehub (HG avail.) • eMTB version uses steel axle and freehub • Weight: 1813g (MX w/XD) • MSRP: €1448 EUR / $1998 CAN / $2650 AUS • More info: giant-bicycles.com The TRX Carbon wheels come in a 29” or MX (29” front, 27.5” rear) set. They’re spec’d on select 2024 Giant bikes but also available aftermarket for €1448 EUR or $1998 CAD. DESIGN Starting with a 30mm-wide, boxed cross-section rim, you get a sense what these wheels are all about: durability. Both front and rear 28-hole rims are identical in shape. Their height almost gives the perception that the wheel is smaller in diameter than they appear. The thickness in the profile lines up with the sidewall of a 2.5” Maxxis tire. Along the external edge of the rim bed is where you’ll find the Wide Guard technology, a 5mm wide, hookless bead. Basically, Giant has thrown a ton of carbon material at this area to reduce failures, so much so that they mention in their copy that tire inserts aren’t necessary to reduce rim failures. The hubs use a straight-pull design and ride on sealed cartridge bearings. Double-butted Sapim Laser spokes form a 2-cross pattern and thread into brass Double Square nipples. Giant states that the hub uses a 72-tooth design, but the drive ring fixed to the hub shell has 36 teeth. On the freehub, there are 6 spring-loaded pawls. The freehub gives off an extremely loud buzz and provides a moderately quick engagement. Inside the TRX HE hub, an alloy axle is used, however a heavier, yet stronger steel one is used in the E-TRX HE hub. In terms of specifications, the TRX HE hubs only come in 110x15mm spacing for the front wheel and 148x12mm for the rear. For brake interfaces, 6-bolt rotor mounts are the lone option. Freehubs are available for Shimano MS or HG cassettes as well as the threaded SRAM XD driver. All of the TRX Wheel Systems come in MX or dual 29" diameters. An internal width of 30mm is now the norm. The hub spacing is only available in 15x110mm up front and 12x148mm for the rear wheel. PRICE AND WEIGHT The pricing varies depending on the region of sale. In Canada, the TRX Carbon are on par, or slightly above, other premium aftermarket carbon wheel brands. Unfortunately, the TRX wheels aren’t available in the USA on their own, but are specified on the Trance X Advanced 0 and 1 bikes. Compared to Reserve’s 30|HD, they’re roughly the same weight (1,860g - 30|HD w/ 1/1 hubs), however, the cost is significantly less ($1,399 USD w/ 1/1 hubs). The 30|HDs are backed by a lifetime warranty versus Giant’s traditional 2-year warranty against manufacturing defects. Outdoing both of those brands in the insurance category are Race Face’s Era wheels, which boast a “no questions asked” replacement policy. Our TRX Carbon MX wheelset came in at a respectable weight of 1,813g. The 29” front and 27.5” rear wheels check out at 941g and 872g, respectively. That includes the valves, rim tape and an XD driver. A 29” set is claimed to weigh 1,846g while the E-TRX Carbon make it under the 2,000g mark at 1,971g. Brass nipples are much less likely to round out compared to alloy. SETUP Whether it was a tight ERTO measurement or the low-depth of the center rim channel, fitting various folding bead enduro tires required some muscle. I don’t often reach for a lever to install tires but having one on hand is worthwhile with the TRX Carbon. Once the tire was on there, though, they form a tight fit making inflation possible with just a 100cc hand pump. They also retained all the sealant inside the tire, even when deflated, which led to a much cleaner install. Wide Guard Technology - a 5mm thick rim edge designed to reduce flats and withstand high impacts. PERFORMANCE Compliance is a word frequently tossed around in carbon wheel marketing. That can also be translated to another word - flexibility. The TRX Carbon wheels are not that. While most other carbon rims manufacturers are leaning towards forgiveness, the ability to track through rough terrain, Giant may have overshot the stiffness campaign in favor of durability. They’re rigid laterally which could work best for eMTBs, or riders looking for a firmer feel. In terms of how those impacts translate through the bike vertically, they aren't uncomfortable, however, they could be too much for lighter riders. The rim tape also withstood multiple tire changes and still looked brand new after a full run on a flat tire. It’s not the most romantic selling feature, but it’s a nuisance when you need to refit a new strip of tape every time you change a tire, or ride a flat down an entire mountain. Metal shavings where they shouldn't be is usually concerning. 6-pawls give off a loud buzz. A 36-tooth drive ring. DURABILITY These carbon wheels are frickin’ strong. I put them through hell and back by riding down the infamous Wairoa Gorge on a flat tire, without an insert. It's also worth looping back to the tight fitment of the tires - the rim held the tire in place while it wasn't even inflated. Of course, that isn’t standard practice for a wheel review. You can read more about that experience and the whole bike setup from the NZ MTB Rally here . As for the claims of reducing pinch flats, the TRX Carbon rims also shook off multiple impacts that left me wincing. That one particular tire failure I mentioned came in the form of sidewall slice - not a rim strike. Throughout the review period, the wheels required little attention, even after those heinous rim smacks. The spoke tension remained high and even, needing just a turn here and there after the first two rides. The bearings are rolling smooth and haven’t become pitted despite seeing their fair share of washes after being subjected to winter conditions in Squamish and dusty trails in New Zealand. The main seal took care of holding the elements at bay as the grease inside the freehub is fairly clean. On the other hand, the promoted lab results didn’t leave me fully convinced of the freehub's durability. After far too many “pop” and “bangs” while putting in pedal strokes, it was the pawl teeth that left us with mixed feelings of the TRX's lifespan. I never experienced any failures or slipping per se, but metal shards were found inside the freehub area. To rule out any user error, the TRX HE hubs don't use an adjustable bearing preload system and the noises occurred while the frame's rear axle was torqued correctly. After speaking with the product team, we’ve determined that the hubs you have in hand are early production models and that the material covering the pawls is indeed shaving off ever so slightly at the tips. This is the reason for the shards you find in the hub as well as the “pop” you might hear when the pawl doesn’t engage completely on the ratchet tooth, as the pawl edge has become rounded ever so slightly. The product team is looking into the issue. — Giant Product Team Giant TRX Carbon Reserve 30HD HOW DO THEY COMPARE? The TRX Carbon wheels remind me most of the Reserve 30|HDs. They share a box-shaped rim profile and ride similarly on the trail. Impacts resonate through with a dull shock rather than a sharp ping. Both give a sense of a strong, stiff wheel though. In terms of the specifications, I’m a fan of J-bend spokes, such as the ones found on the Reserve wheels. Although they’re more timely to replace versus the straight pull type of the TRXs, they are far easier to track down. Neither wheelset includes spare spokes in this case. I’ve had positive experiences with the performance and durability of the Industry 1/1 hubs in the past, which are an option on the Reserve wheels. They don’t have the quite same high degree of engagement as the TRX HE (you’ll have to upgrade to the Hydra hubs to out rank them in that department), however, I’d rest easier if the Giant branded hubs avoided making those skipping sounds. Pros + Extremely durable rims + Stiff (if that's what you're looking for) yet do not transmit too much vibration Cons - Not the greatest value - other brands offer more enticing warranties - Unwanted freehub noises, metal shavings Pinkbike's Take Giant has undeniably built a resilient rim in the TRX Carbon Wheel System. They aren’t the most forgiving or the cheapest option out there, but if you’re looking for pure bombproof rims, these could be the ticket. After far too many “pop” and “bangs” while putting in pedal strokes, it was the hub that left us with mixed feelings in terms of durability. Although we never experienced any failures or slipping, metal shards were found in the freehub area. For that reason, I’d be inclined to choose the heavier e-bike version with the steel hub internals. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Wheels Giant Giant Trx Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Giro's New Coalition Lightweight Full Face Helmet
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, helmets, giro, giro-coalition, mike-kazimer
article_text: A sizeable wave of lightweight full face helmets has hit the market over the last few years, likely fueled by increased eMTB sales, and the fact that more riders than ever are moving quickly through technical terrain where a little extra protection certainly doesn't hurt. Giro's new Coalition helmet is the latest addition to this fast growing segment. It checks in at a competitive weight of 787 grams for a size medium, and uses Giro's Spherical Technology, where two two separate layers of foam are connected by elastomers that allow them to move independently, a design that's intended to reduce the amount of impact force that reaches the brain. Giro Coalition Details • Spherical Technology system • 23 vents • Weight: 787 grams • Colors: black, white/blue, metallic coal/mineral, dark shark dune • Sizes: XS/S, M/L (tested), XL/XXL • Certifications: CE/CPSC/ASTM DH/BMX/NTA • MSRP: $325 USD • giro.com There are a total of 25 vents, with 13 of those located in the front, and the rest spread over the top, side, and rear of the helmet. There's also internal channeling that's meant to help air find its way towards the back of the helmet, preventing riders from feeling like they're in a skull-shaped sauna. Unlike some lightweight full face helmets that use a ratcheting dial to adjust the fit, the Coalition works like a traditional full face, relying on the thickness of the check pads (25 and 30mm thick options are included). Three different shells are used to accommodate six sizes, from XS to XXL The Coalition is available in four different colors, and retails for $325 USD. DETAILS Lightweight full face helmets have something of a checkered reputation when it comes to chin bar strength – the thinner profile and additional venting means they're not usually as strong as you'd find on a dedicated downhill helmet. To address that, Giro came up with what they're calling the 'Halo System', where a bushing system integrates the chinbar into the rest of the helmet, a design that helps reduce the amount of force that's transferred during an impact. According to Giro, the result is a chinbar that's almost twice as strong as what's required to meet the ASTM chin bar deflection testing standard. Other features include an adjustable visor that uses three breakaway bolts, a Fidlock magnetic buckle, and Ionic+ antimicrobial padding. The two different foam layers are joined with elastomers that allow them to rotate independently. The Coalition uses a Fidlock magnetic buckle on the chin strap. FIT Giro's Insurgent downhill helmet fits my size medium, ovalish head shape extremely well, so I was glad to find that the Coalition was just as comfy. The padding isn't quite as thick, and there's more room around the ears compared to the Insurgent, but the helmet is just as secure, even when worn without goggles. That's important, since there's no ratcheting fit system to snug things down. That's not the end of the world, especially since plenty of full face helmets do a great job without one, but it does mean that the fit around the head can't be fine tuned to the same extent as it can be on a helmet like the Fox Proframe RS. VENTILATION It's still supposed to be winter here in the Pacific Northwest, even though the temperatures lately have been downright springlike. That means I haven't been able to test the Coalition in fully sweltering conditions, but I was impressed with how well the helmet breathes on long, slow climbs – I never got that stuffy, almost claustrophobic feeling that can arise when there's not adequate ventilation. A full face will always be warmer than a half shell, but the vents in the chin bar help prevent hot air from blowing back at your face, and the vents around the ears make it possible to hear what's going on around you, rather than muffling the outside noises. The X shape in the chin bar does make it a little trickier to cleanly spit through it or squirt water from a bottle, but if that's the price to pay for extra strength I'm will to adapt. WEIGHT & PRICE The Coalition falls in line with many of the helmets in this category – there are multiple options in the 800ish gram weight bracket, and with a price between $300 - $350. A few of my favorites are listed below (the Coalition is now on my list of favorites too), and you can also check out Henry Quinney's recent round-up article for even more options. Fox Proframe: 820 grams / $359 USD Bluegrass Vanguard: 776 grams (large) / $350 USD POC Otocon Race Mips: 750 grams / $420 Pros + Comfortable, secure fit + No creaking or squeaking from the liner + Well ventilated for a full face helmet Cons - Lack of a retention dial means that fit is reliant on padding thickness Pinkbike's Take The new Coalition helmet is an excellent addition to Giro's lineup. It's comfortable, well ventilated, and comes in at a reasonable weight for this category. The design of the Spherical system makes a lot of sense, and while I luckily didn't put it to the test, it's good to know it's there if things don't go as planned out on a ride. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets Giro Giro Coalition Mike Kazimer Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Giro's New Montaro III Helmet
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: The Montaro is one of the most popular helmets in Giro’s lineup, which undoubtedly put a little extra pressure on the design team to get things right for version 3.0. The newest iteration is still intended to be an all-round trail helmet, but the overall look has changed; the profile is more angular, and the lines of the two position visor flow a little more naturally into the rest of the helmet. There’s secure sunglasses storage in two places for riders that stash their shades on the climbs (my hand is raised), and a Fidlock magnetic buckle to make the already-easy process of buckling your helmet even easier. A dial at the rear of the helmet is used to fine tune the fit, with three different vertical positions to adjust how low the helmet sits. Montaro III Details • MIPS Evolve liner • Two-position visor, sunglasses storage • Weight: 445 grams (medium) • Sizes: S, M, L • Colors: black, white, sage, stone • CPSC, CE, and NTA8776 certified • MSRP: $215.95 • giro.com The Montaro III is CPSC and CE certified, and it also meets the NTA e-bike certification. Unfortunately, it doesn't have a five-star rating from Virginia Tech. Virginia Tech recently reset their safety threshold , which meant that over 100 formerly 5-star helmets were bumped down to four stars, including the Montaro III. According to Virginia Tech, "This is not because their performance has worsened, but rather the bar for top-tier performance has risen." FIT & FUNCTION Most of my rides with the Montaro III have been on warmer, humid summer days. The fit is excellent for my oval shaped head - it’s fairly deep feeling, but it’s nice and secure even when blasting down rougher trails. The glasses storage works great too - I typically use the rear vent holes to slide the glasses arms into, where they’re held very securely in place. On paper, the weight is on the heavier side, but that wasn’t too noticeable on the trail - the depth of the helmet helps keep it from feeling too tall and tippy. It's worth playing around with the vertical height adjustment feature in order to make sure the helmet sits in the right spot. There are three positions, and a quick push or pull on the mechanism is all it takes to slide it into a different position. The overall amount of ventilation is good, and even on hot days the helmet didn't feel over stifling. My main gripe has to do with the thin padding around the brow - it doesn’t take much sweat for it to become saturated and start dripping, usually right onto my glasses. As I mentioned, it’s been pretty humid lately, but even still, the Montaro III doesn’t do the best job of dealing with sweat - I’ve been tempted to find some sort of stylish headband to help deal with the persistent perspiration, and that's not something I've had to do with other helmets. WEIGHT & PRICE The Montaro III is on the heavier side of the spectrum for a trail helmet, weighing in at 445 grams for a size medium. The $216 price tag is on the higher end as well, although everything seems to be getting more expensive these days, and there are plenty of examples of pricier mountain bike helmets out there. Fox Speedframe RS: $279 / 408 grams Giro Montaro III: $215.95 / 445 grams Mavic Deemax: $200 / 395 grams Specialized Ambush: $180 / 360 grams Lazer Coyote: $109 / 340 grams Specialized Tactic: $110 / 380 grams POC Tectal: $180 / 352 grams Giro Source: $130 / 351 grams Pros + Good coverage, comfortable fit + Very effective glasses storage Cons - On the heavier side - No 5-star Virginia Tech rating - Thin brow padding Pinkbike's Take I don’t want it to seem like I’m being overly nitpicky, but there are a lot of very good helmets on the market right now, and it’s the little things that help one stand out from another. The Montaro III gets high marks for its overall comfort and secure sunglasses storage, but the lack of a 5-star Virginia Tech rating and sub-par sweat mitigation take it down a notch. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets Giro Giro Montaro Iii Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Stinner Romero MT - A US-Made Steel Trail Bike That's Better With Mixed Wheels
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Stinner Frameworks started as a one-man operation in Aaron Stinner’s garage back in 2011, and in the years since the company has grown into something much bigger. They're still based in Santa Barbara, California, but Stinner recently moved into a new facility that’s allowed them to increase their manufacturing capacity and shorten turnaround times. That means riders eager to purchase a mountain, road, or gravel frame won’t have as much of a waiting period before being able to put tires to dirt or pavement. The Romero is the first full-suspension model from Stinner, with a unique design that pairs a steel front triangle with a 6061 aluminum back end. The bike uses Ministry Cycles’ 3VO dual-link suspension layout, something of a departure from the single pivot models that are more commonly offered by smaller steel frame builders. Stinner Romero MT Details • Travel: 140mm / 150mm fork • Wheel size: 29" or mixed • Steel front triangle, aluminum swingarm • 65° head angle • Chainstay length: 435mm (M/L) • Sizes: XS, S, M, M/L, L, XL, XXL • Price as tested: $7,750 USD • Weight: 36.3 lb / 16.5 kg(size M/L) • stinnerframeworks.com/ There are two version of the Romero, the Mid Travel (MT) or the Long Travel (LT). The MT can be set up with either 130 or 140mm of rear travel, and the LT can have either 150 or 160mm. I’ve been spending time on a 140mm MT with a 150mm fork. US-made bikes aren’t typically known for being affordable, but Stinner’s prices aren’t totally out there. The frame only (without a shock) is $3,000 USD, and complete bikes start at $6,200 USD. I’ve been spending time on the Eagle build spec’d with Fox suspension and Stoic carbon wheels, a combo that puts the total price at $7,750 USD. Going with aluminum wheels would drop the price down to $7,000. Stinner Romero MT review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/YBLIVJOD Copied Live 00:00 08:23 08:23 Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take The Romero is an interesting bike, and the more I rode it the more it started to grow on me. It’s the sort of bike that feels best if you’re not in a hurry, a companion for those rides that meander up and down decently technical trails without any purpose in mind other than exploring and having a good time. It’s not the lightest, or the most energetic, but it does possess a calm, composed characteristic that’s very enjoyable. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The Romero’s frame is refreshingly straightforward - the cables are externally routed, there’s plenty of room to run a longer travel dropper post, and there’s room for a water bottle inside the front triangle. There isn’t much in the way of adjustments, but theoretically it’s possible to purchase a different dropouts to increase the chainstay length by 10mm. The front triangle is made from Velospec steel, and the swingarm is constructed from 6061 aluminum with 7075 aluminum hardware. The whole thing is powdercoated, and so far the finish has proven to be quite resilient. The fact that the lower shock mount is removable also leaves the door open for future changes. When I mentioned to Stinner that I'd been experimenting with running the Romero as a mixed-wheel bike without swapping the dropouts, Chris, one of the designers, had this to say: "What I don't think we anticipated is how many riders, as a percentage of the whole of our orders and inquiries, want to deviate from stock. We're a somewhat set-it-and-forget-it crowd over here (after finding the right setup, of course), and so the volume of requests we've gotten for mullet setups, mix-and-matched shock chips, angle adjust headsets, and the like, has been very interesting. Because of the volume of interest in all those tweaks, we're working on a guide for what all the various changes will do to the geometry of the bike, and what we'd recommend, or not recommend. Likewise, we're making sure that we'll have plenty of 27.5" dropouts. At the end of the day, part of the beauty of bikes is that you get to customize them to your unique wants, needs, and desires; intentions of the designers be damned. By the same token, we'll need to help guide folks on that journey so they don't take themselves down the wrong path." One detail that’s slightly out of the ordinary, at least in the mountain bike world, is the use of a T47 external bottom bracket. T47 was introduced around 10 years ago as a solution to the creaky PF30 bottom brackets that were all too common, especially on road bikes. The Romero uses a 73mm wide bottom bracket shell, but the inner dimensions are larger than they would be if it was designed for a BSA bottom bracket. Don’t worry, though; while it may not be as familiar of a standard for mountain bikers, there are plenty of high quality bottom bracket options on the market from most major manufacturers. Geometry Stinner offers seven sizes of the Romero: XS, S, M, M/L, L, XL, XXL. That’s an impressive spread, especially from a relatively small company. At 5’11” I went with the size medium/large, which has a 480mm reach, 65-degree head angle, and 435mm chainstays in its stock configuration. The seat tube is quite steep, at 78.4-degrees, and the stack measures 651mm, which is on the taller side. For comparison, the Propain Tyee Trail I recently reviewed has a stack of 634mm, and that’s with a 160mm fork. Suspension Design If someone mentions a steel full suspension bike, my mind gravitates towards some version of a single pivot. That’s one of simpler suspension layouts to construct, and if you’re a smaller builder oftentimes simpler is better. Stinner went a slightly different route, and chose to use Ministry Cycles’s 3VO suspension design. Designed by Chris Currie, the dual link layout has a progressive leverage ratio that allows for the use of coil or air shocks, a moderately high level of anti-squat for efficient pedaling, and anti-rise values that stay between 100 - 110% for the entirety of the travel, which should keep the bike from having any massive geometry shifts during heavy braking. Specifications Price $7750 Travel 140mm Rear Shock Fox Float X Factory Fork Fox 36 Factory 150mm Cassette SRAM Eagle 90 Crankarms SRAM Eagle 90 165mm Rear Derailleur SRAM Eagle 90 Chain SRAM Eagle 90 Shifter Pods SRAM Eagle 90 Handlebar Race Face Aeffect R 20mm rise Stem Race Face Aeffect R 35mm Grips Race Face Getta Grip 33mm Brakes SRAM Maven Base Wheelset Stoic Carbon Tires Maxxis Assegai 29 x 2.5 / Maxxis Dissector 29 x 2.4 Seat WTB Silverado Seatpost Race Face Aeffect R Dropper 31.6mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup I didn’t immediately change anything on the Romero, other than swapping the 180mm Race Face Aeffect post for a 210mm OneUp. With such a steep seat angle, having plenty of drop is even more important, since the seat doesn’t move away from you when it’s lowered the way it would on a bike with a slacker seat angle. After a handful of rides on the bike my curiosity got the best of me and I installed a 27.5” rear wheel. Like I mentioned earlier, there aren’t any flip chips, so as expected that had a fairly significant impact on the geometry, dropping the bottom bracket to 335mm, and slackening the head angle to 64-degrees. I’ll talk more about how it handled later on, but the general takeaway was that the bike felt much, much better than I expected after making the swap, almost like this should be the stock configuration. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 165 lb / 74.8 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer As for suspension settings, I ran 185psi in the Float X to achieve 27% sag, and 80 psi in the Fox 36 with the clickers set to my typical numbers, which are very close to Fox's recommended base settings. I rode the bike with several different tire combinations, including the stock Maxxis Assegai / Dissector configuration, as well as with dual Schwalbe Romy radial tires (review on the way), and with Specialized's new radial Butcher and Eliminator tires. Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington , with nearly perfect trail conditions for a good portion of the test period. Spring time in the Pacific Northwest is something special. Climbing I’m not going to harp on the Romero’s weight too much in this review, but there’s no getting around the fact that it’s not the lightest trail bike out there. Faster rolling tires can help put some pep in its step, but no matter how you slice it 35+ pounds doesn’t feel that fast off the line. Out of all the various climbing scenarios, the Romero felt most at home on rougher, looser climbs. The rear wheel does a good job of digging in and finding grip as opposed to staying high in and travel and spinning out, and the steep seat angle creates a comfortable, upright position. It’s the sort of bike that you can chuck at an uphill obstacle and it’ll stick, providing a little extra time to get composed and put more power through the pedals to keep moving upwards. Nearly every suspension design is claimed to achieve that magical / elusive characteristic of being able to absorb bumps while also resisting pedaling forces. Some do it better than other, and with this iteration of 3VO I’d say it’s skewed a more towards the traction rather than efficiency side of the spectrum. If you’re seated and pedaling there’s not a ton of suspension movement, but it is pretty sensitive to weight shifts - get up and out of the saddle and the bike will go into its travel relatively easily, even when running less than 30% sag. It’s a different experience compared to something like the Propain Tyee Trail, which has a much more supportive, snappy suspension feel, albeit at the expense of some small bump sensitivity. For riders who want the lightest, snappiest, most efficient feeling trail bike out there, the Romero isn’t it (and it’s not really trying to be). It’s more of a tractor, one that’s capable of crawling its way up all sorts of things; it feels like it wants to cruise onwards at a nice medium pace instead of zipping around frenetically in search of uphill KOMs. Descending My first handful of rides were on the Romero in its stock configuration, with two 29” wheels. With that setup it felt okay, and there was plenty of speed available, but something was missing. I was perched a little higher than I wanted to be, and I didn’t quite click with the bike’s handling. The weight and overall muted nature made it feel like a longer travel bike, one that you would except to shine on steeper trails, but then the tall BB and not-so-slack head angle didn’t give it the handling to match. On a whim I decided to swap the rear wheel for a 27.5” one, just to see if it was even rideable in that configuration. On many 29ers if you swap the rear wheel without doing anything else the resulting bottom bracket can be too low, and the head angle too slack, but with the Romero the geo numbers weren’t that outlandish. I ended up with a 64-degree head angle, 335mm bottom bracket height, and a 77.5-degree seat tube angle. Yes, the bottom bracket height is a little low, but this bike does come with 165mm cranks, so pedal strikes were less of a concern. In the mixed-wheel configuration it felt like I’d unlocked the door to the ride feel I was looking for. It still climbed well, and all the climbing traits I mentioned earlier carry over, but now the descents were so much more enjoyable. Cornering was more intuitive, it felt more at home in the steeps, and the overall character of the bike felt more cohesive. I know, talking about a bike’s character can be a little cringy, but there is more to the Romero than meets the eye. Something about it made be think of the era when the UK scene was going off, and every week there’d be videos of a bunch of riders drifting through some muddy, leaf covered track. The Romero’s ride feel is as muted as Miles Davis' trumpet - it’s not the most lively and playful, but once up to speed it does an outstanding job of filtering out trail chatter. It stays on line extremely well, and there’s plenty of stability, especially considering this is a 140mm bike, not something with 10 or 20 millimeters more travel. Propain Tyee Trail Santa Cruz Bronson (with some fancy bits installed that will be reviewed soon). How Does It Compare? I recently reviewed the new Propain Tyee Trail, which I’d place in the same category as the Romero MT, although the bikes are very different out on the trail. The Propain has longer chainstays (446 vs 435 for a size large), a lower stack, and the ability to swap wheelsizes without changing the geometry. I was able to get along with both wheelsize configurations on the Propain, while mixed wheels felt much better to me on the Romero. The Propain is much snappier and eager to accelerate - it generates speed very well on rollers and berms, and it’s an ideal option for zones where the trails resemble pump tracks in the woods. The Romero does best in chunkier, slightly steeper terrain, or at least terrain without too many sudden steep uphills. It’s more comfortable and planted when faces with a section of high speed chunk; the Propain gets knocked around a little more, and transmits more feedback to the rider. Which Model is the Best Value? Stinner offers the Romero with three different suspension options (Marzocchi, RockShox, and Fox), two different drivetrain options (cable-operated Eagle 90 or battery-powered XO Eagle), and either DT Swiss aluminum wheels or Stoic carbon wheels. Going with the Marzocchi suspension, Eagle 90 drivetrain, and DT wheels adds up to a price of $6,200 USD. That's still not what I'd call a budget-friendly price tag, but remember we're talking about a bike from a smaller US company here. Stinner also sells the Romero with just the frame, shock, and fork; for rider's looking to get the most bang for their buck that's likely the way to go - with some smart shopping it'd wouldn't be too hard to put together a solid build for less than the cheapest complete option that Stinner offers. Technical Report Eagle 90 drivetrain: I like the fact that the Eagle 90 drivetrain doesn't need batteries, and I've had good luck with the overall durability of the derailleur, but I still wish the shifter felt a little more refined. I've been spending some time on an older SRAM XO shifter on a different bike, the one with the adjustable paddle angle, and that's reminded me of just how good that shifter worked. It has a snappier lever feel, and doesn't feel as cheap as the Eagle 90 shifter. I said it in my original review, but it still holds true - a higher end shifter for the cable-actuated Transmission derailleurs would be a great addition to SRAM's lineup. Fox 36 Factory fork: The last few bikes I've reviewed have had a Fox 36, and each time setup has been straightforward and the performance has been excellent. Compared to the new RockShox Lyrik I'd say the 36 transmits just a touch more feedback; it doesn't muffle the trail chatter quite as much as the Lyrik, but there's still great sensitivity and grip when things are extra-slippery. Maven Base Brakes: I'm still a fan of the Maven Base brakes , although the introduction of the updated lever for the 'regular' Maven Silver and Ultimate brakes does mean that, if you don't mind paying more, you can now have that lighter lever combined with bearings instead of bushings at the lever blade and a pad contact adjust dial. Chainslap protection: The Romero arrived with almost no chainstay protection, just a very thin strip of mastic tape. I'd recommend budgeting for something more effective, like STFU's Stay Guardp . Pros + Very muted, calm ride characteristics + Good stability at speed, especially for 140mm bike + Lots of traction while climbing and descending Cons - On the heavier side for a trail bike - Not the most energetic or lively thing - US-made comes with a higher price tag. Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Stinner Stinner Romero Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,980 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Goodyear Newton MTR & MTF Tires
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Goodyear is a familiar name in the automotive market, but they've had a pretty minimal presence in the bike world - especially when it comes to dirt. They've had a solid little lineup of tires for a few years now, but early issues with the performance of their mountain bike tires may have slowed their entry into the market. Looking to change that, they've been hard at work making incremental changes to the Newtons, optimizing their compound, construction, and pattern to best suit gravity riding. It's a common complaint, but the mountain bike market has a tendency to be pretty myopic when it comes to tire choice. To be fair, Maxxis and Schwalbe do a damn good job making their lineups perform well and cover all the bases, but there are plenty more options out there that do the job just as well. After a summer of riding, I think the Goodyear Newtons belong on the list of capable competitors. Newton MTF & MTR Details • Intended use: Enduro, Downhill, Trail • Front- and rear-specific patterns • Claimed weight: 1280g (F), 1130g (R) • Configuration tested: 29 x 2.5" & 2.4", Enduro casing, Grip3S • Measured width: 2.53” and 2.41" • Measured weight: 1409g (F), 1244g (R) • MSRP: $70-80 USD • goodyearbike.com Newton MTF - Size options: 27.5x2.5, 29x2.5 - Casing options: Trail, Enduro, Downhill - Compound options: Trail2, Grip3S Newton MTR - Size options: 27.5x2.4, 27.5x2.6, 29x2.4 - Casing options: Trail, Enduro, Downhill - Compound options: Trail2, Grip3 Details The Newton MTF and MTR are designed to work well in a "broad range of conditions from loose dust to wet loam ... at home on challenging terrain where high levels of control are required." This pretty much spans the gamut of what you'd expect of an all-round gravity tire, and that's certainly what the Newton aims to be. Up front, the fairly open and pronounced front tread is meant to clear well, with reinforced knobs that should resist any serious fold-over while cornering. There are wide sipes that only go about halfway into the knob, adding surface are without effecting the structure too much. The MTR is a more standard affair, resembling a cross between a Maxxis DHR II and a Schwalbe Big Betty. The knob pattern is similar to the former, with the structure and casing shape more like the latter. Both tires feature Goodyear's Enduro casing, which utilizes two 120 TPI plies to provide stability and durability. The MTF utilizes the Goodyear Grip3S compound, which is their stickiest and softest offering. The 40/42/60a density combination is meant to offer a very slow rebound and provide the most grip possible. In the hand, it feels similar to MaxxGrip or Conti's Super Soft rubber, with fairly slow rebound and easy deformation. The MTR uses the slightly stiffer Grip3 rubber, which has a triple-density 40/50/60a layup, meant to balance durability and grip. Big, well-supported edge knobs up front. And smaller, more flexible knobs in the rear. The tread of the MTR looks a lot like the Michelin Wild Enduro, to the degree that they may share some lineage. Goodyear's early attempts (2018-2019) at the mountain bike tire game were a little unsatisfactory, with hard rubber produced by a third-party manufacturer. According to some people on their team, they made a concerted effort to move away from that supplier, moving production to their own facility in Nantou, Taiwan. Good quality products take time and resources to develop and produce. As a key component to a tire, the compounds (3 different types in the Newton) were at the top of our priority list; finely balancing grip, and longevity. At this point we have lab and real word tested hundreds of compound mixtures to get to where we are today. Unfortunately, we can’t learn too much from the automotive world when it comes to compounding as the gross vehicle and rider / driver weights are off the chart in comparison to bicycle. — Ben Evans; Commercial Director, Goodyear The Enduro casing on test here uses a dual-ply 120 TPI construction, which manages to be quite supple for how durable and supportive it can be. If you're after something stiffer, the Downhill casing is a dual-ply 60 TPI, while the Trail tires use just one 60 TPI casing. For my purposes, the Enduro strikes a nice balance, and feels similar to other tire casings I tend to like, such as Maxxis' DoubleDown and Continental's Downhill tires. Setup-wise, I was able to run quite low pressures with the Newtons without running into any stability or durability issues. In the dry, I was running 22 or 23 psi in the front, paired with 25 or 26 in the rear. When things got a little loose or wetter, I dropped down to 21psi front, 24psi rear. Generally a couple lower than an equivalent DoubleDown casing, for reference. It's worth noting a strange issue I had with the very first set of these I was sent. They were nearly impossible to get seated on the rim due to a loose fit, requiring me to resort to some old janky tricks from the early tubeless days. Instructions, should you ever encounter this issue: Put a tube in the tire, inflate that until the bead is seated, pop only one side of the tire off the rim, install the tubeless valve, then inflate the whole thing with a compressor or charger pump. I reached out to Goodyear to inquire about the issue, and they assured me that the set was probably part of a non-production batch, or some sort of anomaly. After mounting up another 2 sets of the Newtons, I had no similar issues, so I do think that loose fit was some sort of early batch issue. Ride Impressions Pretty much every time I hop on a new pair of tires, I expect a few sketchy moments to occur in the adjustment period, as I get used to the boundaries and edges of grip. After fitting up the Newtons, I kept waiting for those moments to occur, but the tires never really delivered a terror-inducing slip. That's not to say there's no end of grip on these new Goodyears, but it's well past the point of commitment, meaning they hook up quite well when you're pushing them hard. I received my test set a few weeks before the start of Crankworx, and had some time on them at home before deciding that bike and tire combo would be the one I took with me for two weeks of Whistler laps and lots of pedaling. Long story short, I'm quite happy with my choices. More on the bike side of things later, for now let's stick to the tires. The Newton MTF has a really nicely rounded profile, a feature I've come to look for in aggressive tires, as the smooth lean angle you get with a nice round casing means they're less likely to break away on sketchier terrain. If you're only riding in deep soil, you can get away with something edgier, but when loose conditions are present, round feels correct. Out back, however, I like the fairly flat-top profile of the MTR as it serves as a great braking anchor when needed, plus it breaks away before the front, a sensation that I tend to prefer. That shape profile is matched with the slightly different rubber compounds in the front and rear tire, which biases towards sticker and slower rebound up front, and firmer and faster rolling in the rear - again, a combo that makes sense to me. The Grip3S front rubber is very soft, without feeling too wiggly on rocks and roots, and holds its own in wet conditions. Same goes for the rear, though it's ever so slightly worse in the wet. One of the longer days of Crankworx was an all-day pedal ride with a bunch of industry folks that happened to coincide with a dawn to dusk downpour that only let up as we were finishing our day up back at the Village. This was the first truly wet weather I'd ridden the Newtons in, and I really came away impressed. They stuck to muddy slabs nearly as well as the MaxxGrip boots everyone else was wearing, and though they might've been ever so slightly slipperier on wet roots, I never felt like they were holding me back. Luckily, most of the weather through Crankworx was much more warm and pleasant, meaning there were plenty of dry park laps to take the Goodyears down. Despite the soft rubber and prominent knobs, they really held up well, only showing minimal wear after plenty of downhill miles. I've continued to ride the same set in Bellingham (where tires do seem to last a very long time), and they're still going strong. I'm now running three pairs on different bikes, and haven't had any flats or unusually quick wear. One area where the Newton MTF falls a bit behind other aggressive tires like the Specialized Cannibal, Maxxis DHF, or Schwalbe Tacky Chan is in very hard-pack corners, where you'll have to lean the Goodyears a little bit farther than you might want to really get side knob engagement. I didn't find them too sketchy in these settings, but I'm also relatiively light at 180 lbs, so bigger riders might be stressing those fat side knobs a little more than they can handle in huge bike park berms. The only real downside I can think of with the Newtons is the weight. At 1200-1400 grams for the Enduro range, they're in keeping with other competitive options, but the measured numbers are a good bit higher than the stated weights. To me this wasn't really an issue, but I know there are plenty of people who'd rather not roll around on a few kilos of rubber. Those people probably don't want tread this aggressive though, so they'll likely keep their distance. Pros + Excellent mechanical grip in loose and challenging conditions + Sticky rubber that works fine in the wet + Front and rear combo compliment each other nicely + Impressive durability Cons - A good bit heavier than claimed weight - Not the absolute best wet-weather grip, but quite good Pinkbike's Take After subjecting them to a wide range of conditions, plenty of miles, and quite a few bad lines, the Goodyear Newtons have really impressed me. I'll admit some initial skepticism given their earlier offerings, but the sticky rubber, robust yet supple casing, and well-rounded tread patterns won me over. I'd feel confident throwing the Newtons on any sort of gravity-biased bike, knowing the grip will be there even when things are loose. — Dario DiGiulio Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires Goodyear Goodyear Newton Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Haro Greer Alloy LTD 2
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: REVIEW Haro Greer LTD 2 WORDS: Dario DiGiulio PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio / Eric Mickelson Haro has a long and storied past in the BMX world, but their forays into the mountain bike market have seen some ups and downs. The brand recently reinvigorated their MTB lineup, with two full suspension models - the Greer and the Daley - and an aggressive hardtail to boot. On test here is the Greer, their enduro-focused long travel machine, with a limited-release raw aluminum finish. The LTD 2 designation reflects the bike's build kit, which is the less expensive of the two raw models, retailing for $4,599 USD. Haro Greer Details • Mixed wheels, 27.5/29" • Aluminum frame • 160mm frame travel, 170mm fork • 63.8° head angle, 78° seat angle • 430-510mm reach, 615-642mm stack • 435mm chainstays • 4 sizes • Actual weight: 39.2 lb / 17.8 kg (size L) • Price: $4,599 USD • harobikes.com Greer Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DN5JVa4j Copied Live 00:00 16:05 16:05 Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Ultimately, the Greer is a good bike that doesn't really have any one standout feature, but does the job pretty well. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details The Greer is a satisfyingly simple bike, with no flip chips or adjustments to speak of. Nice details for those who want them, but having nothing to fuss with can be a liberating thing - all you need to do is get out and ride. Despite the overall simplicity, some of the frame details do add a bit of annoyance. For starters, the cables are run internally but lack any sort of guide or sound mitigation, making for a decent bit of rattling and fuss. The clamps are a fiddly two-sided cover, with a tiny bolt holding the arrangement in place. I've seen the same design used on some older Transition models, so at least the hardware is common enough to be replaceable should you lose one of the pieces trailside. Room to roost. Could use some improvement. The Greer errs on beefiness above all else, with plenty of bracing in the rear triangle, a one-piece rocker link, and sturdy frame hardware all around. The cables are routed internally for the most part, with a few external segments that snake under the lower shock hardware. Haro has integrated some robust chainslap protection to the stays, with large fins to keep things quiet. There is an ISCG 05 mount at the bottom bracket, though the bike doesn't come with a chainguide or bashguard. Finally, Haro specced the Universal Derailleur Hanger at the dropout, allowing for easy replacement and the potential to run a Transmission drivetrain. Smooth bends, small holes. More iffy hardware. Geometry & Sizing The Greer's geometry fits the enduro mold pretty squarely, with aggressive angles and fairly roomy front triangles. The 63.8° headtube angle feels bang-on for hard riding of any sort, keeping thing stable and safe without getting too sprawled out on flatter terrain. A 78° seat tube angle keeps the climbing position upright and comfortable, and the 30mm bottom bracket drop makes for a roomy rider area. Stack heights aren't very high per reach, but they're not unusually low. The bike comes with a nice 40mm rise bar, putting the hands in a good position. The rear center of the bike is short by the most modern standards, with a 435mm chainstay across all sizes. This should feel reasonably well balanced with the smaller two sizes, but starts to feel a bit mismatched as you scale to the Large and Extra Large. Generally, the jumps in sizing are fairly large, but most people should be able to find a happy place between the two extremes, even if the final fit does require a bit of work to dial in. Suspension Design The Greer uses a simple Horst link suspension design, with no bells, whistles, or weird acronyms to mention. The 4 bar linkage drives a 205x65mm shock, delivering 160mm of rear wheel travel. Haro went with a lower leverage rate on the Greer, with 23.5% total progressivity through the full stroke. At sag, anti-squat sits at 101%, making for a nice even feeling pedaling platform while seated. At the same sag point, anti-rise is calculated at 61%. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $4599.99 Travel 160mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Performance Elite Fork Fox 38 Performance Elite, 170mm, Grip X2 Damper Headset FSA ORBIT 1.5E ZS/ NO.57E tapered, 44/56mm head tube Cassette Shimano XT 12-speed Crankarms Shimano XT, FC-M8120-1, 165mm Chainguide N/A Bottom Bracket Shimano MT501 BSA Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur Shimano XT 12-speed Chain Shimano M8100 Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods Shimano XT 12-speed Handlebar Race Face Turbine, 35mm OD, 40mm rise, 800mm wide Stem Race Face Aeffect R, 35mm ID, 40mm reach Grips WTB Wavelength 130mm lock-on Brakes TRP Trail EVO 4-piston, 203mm rotors Wheelset DT Swiss M1900 Hubs DT Swiss 370 Ratchet LN Spokes DT Champion straight-pull Rim DT Swiss M1900, 30mm internal Tires Front: Vittoria Mazza 2.4", enduro G2.0 120TPI / Rear: Vittoria Martello 2.4" enduro G2.0 120TPI Seat WTB Volt Medium, chromoly rails Seatpost Kind Shock Rage-I dropper, 34.9OD, sizes: Small 150mm, Medium 170mm, Large 190mm, X-Large 190mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Greer Test Bike Setup I had a fairly easy time getting the Greer set up for my liking, with no baked-in adjustment on tap and a fleet of components I'm very used to. The cockpit, contact points, and suspension all felt suited to the bike's use case, requiring no modifications or tweaks during the test period. Kudos to the product manager on this front. I settled on 105psi in the fork, with one volume spacer, and 196psi in the shock, with no volume reduction. One detail that took some adjustment were the Vittoria tires, as it had been a while since I'd ridden their Enduro casings. For the uninitiated, expect a stiff-feeling sidewall, and play with pressure to match that feel. For the soft and slippery conditions we're currently experiencing, I was happy with 22 psi in the front, and 25 psi in the rear, for most circumstances. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info I found the Greer most fun on jump trails, and in natural terrain where you're rewarded for generating speed and getting off the ground to skip through rough sections. I can imagine a lot of people gravitating towards the Greer for bike park use, but more on that later. Climbing The Greer does a good job of offsetting its high weight with a supportive pedaling platform, and a fairly steep seat tube angle that keeps your weight centered on mellower grades. Steep climbs feel a bit more effortful, as you're consciously shifting weight forward to keep the front wheel planted and tracking. Generally, the big Haro feels suited more towards spinning up road grades at a consistent pace, rather than punching up anything terribly technical. The grip at the rear wheel is fine - not outstanding - but the supportive feel of the suspension keeps you up in travel even if you're pushing hard on smoother pitches. One important yet invisible detail in the climbing department is the weight, and this bike comes with plenty of it. Tipping the scales at nearly 40 lbs / 18 kilos without pedals, the Greer is far from a lightweight. I'm often one to espouse the benefits of ignoring bike weight in the right applications - and some of that logic does apply here - but it's hard to ignore that heft when it's up to you to haul it to the top of the hill. Generally, I'd say some heavy bikes do a better job of masking their mass than others, and the Haro wears it proudly on its sleeve. Descending That weight isn't all bad news, as it gives the Greer a planted, muted feel when plowing down the trail. The frame feels stout, stiff, and unbothered by bad lines and sideways landings. The suspension feel matches that characteristic, with a firm and consistent platform to push against to generate speed, take the edge off landings, and pop off lips. That firmer feeling can get a bit awkward on slower speed moves and when trying to keep the wheels on the ground through rougher sections, leading to a ride style that caters towards faster, smoother terrain. I was able to reach shock settings that gave the rear wheel a bit more purchase in those more awkward, slow bits of trail, but the Greer then felt a bit less composed on those higher speed hits where it was otherwise quite comfortable. In the end I ended up with a compromise that suited most situations well, but didn't necessarily shine anywhere. That said, don't read that as overly negative, as the Greer was still good fun at high speeds. The Haro felt happiest when popping between smoother patches of trail, aiming for positive contact and airing over anything too chopped up. Those who err on a heels-down plow style might find the rear wheel feedback to be a bit jarring when trying to push at speed, at least with the stock setup. The shorter rear center could also be an element here, as the bike loses some stability compared to other similarly positioned enduro bikes with better front/rear balance. I think the Greer could pair well with a coil shock, to get a bit more top-end grip out of the suspension platform when you're not digging in, but with enough rider input the stock setup can deliver plenty of traction. With the air shock, the bike feels great on smoother trails with big jumps and one-off hits, leading me to think of it as a good contender for folks looking for a dependable and robust park bike. The frame is stout, the hardware hasn't given me any issue yet, and with some work it could be made fairly quiet. Sadly, in the stock setup, the cables do rattle loudly in the frame, given the lack of insulation or guiding in the front triangle. Haro Greer Santa Cruz Nomad How does it compare? In my mind these bikes are targeted at similar demographics: people who are either seeking out pedal-accessed gnarly terrain, or who want a single crown rig that's more than happy to spend a day in the bike park. The geometry is similar if not identical on a few fronts, and differs only a little in a couple areas. The Haro's reach is a bit longer per size, and has a lower bottom bracket across the board, though the slightly longer-travel Nomad (170mm) bring the dynamic positions closer together. Santa Cruz's size scaling has a leg up on the Haro when it comes to stack and rear center, giving a more balanced feel across the board. The Greer is less expensive than the Nomad due to the use of an aluminum frame vs. carbon, although Santa Cruz does have remaining 2024 Nomads on sale, which narrows that price gap significantly. In terms of ride quality, the differences are pretty significant. The Nomad feels more forgiving and capable in just about every trail situation, delivering plenty of traction and support without having to compromise on shock setup. The weight plays a role on the climbs, but generally I'd say the nature between the two is similar: not in a rush, but comfortable and supportive enough to go all day. Which Model is the Best Value? Haro's four build options of the Greer span the gamut of price and spec level, with the top-end build sporting just about everything you could want on an enduro bike for only $5,700 USD. That said, I'd opt for the LTD 2 that I've been testing - the performance of nearly all the components is up to the task, and you could upgrade the bike to full potential for less than the price difference to the LTD 1. The main functional difference to me is in the brakes, as the TRP Trail EVOs fall short of the DHR EVO's strength and fade resistance. Technical Report Cable Management: I'm of the opinion that if a bike is going to route cables internally, it must be silent once done, and ideally require no faff to get them from A to B during installation. The Greer fails on both fronts, with rattling, uninsulated cables that use flimsy hardware to keep them somewhat in place on entry and exit. If I were the owner of this bike, I'd wrap the cables in some foam tubing inside the frame, or figure out how to securely mount them to the outside of the frame and avoid the hassle entirely. TRP Trail EVO Brakes: In comparison to TRP's top-spec DHR EVO brakeset, the Trail EVOs are a bit underwhelming. They do a good job of remaining consistent over long runs, maintaining a consistent bite point, and accessing a similar level of adjustment to the DHRs, but the overall power and ease of reaching that max point fall behind. I'd consider upgrading these if long, steep runs were in your future, or if your riding regularly involves full-stop power braking as opposed to scrubbing speed. KS EXA Form Dropper: I was initially confused by the spec of this dropper post, as I'd never heard of the model before, and because it differed from the KS-branded post that was listed on the spec sheet. As far as I can tell, this is the same internal and external design as the KS Rage-I dropper Haro specs, but with different laser-etched branding. Regardless, the performance was pretty underwhelming, with a slow return that required consistent upkeep, and a total length of 190mm, which falls a bit behind the longer-travel norms. Pros + Supportive suspension, stable at speed + Solid build spec for price + Burly frame and fun handling make for a good park bike Cons - Very heavy stock bike - Compliance and grip don't come easy - Noise from internal cable routing Pinkbike's Take Ultimately, the Greer is a good bike that doesn't really have any one standout feature, but does the job pretty well. The suspension feel is on the firmer side, but rewards strong input. While the handling isn't quite as balanced and forgiving as some other bikes in this category, it does feel fun on jumps and on trailside hits. It's not perfect, but there are some good elements and I look forward to seeing what Haro does with the platform down the line. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Haro Haro Greer Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Hayes Dominion A4 Brake Review: Ultra-Light Lever Action and Plenty of Power
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, big-brake-test, reviews, brakes, hayes, hayes-dominion
article_text: Hayes Dominion Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/E2wMU6Qd Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 00:46 Hayes Dominion A4 In the late 90s and early 2000s, the Hayes Mag brake was heralded for its immense power. The purple and champagne color brakes were one of the most popular choices for downhillers. In that era, fully kitting out your bike with Hayes brakes likely included buying aftermarket CNC'd Dangerboy or Razor Rock levers, too. The hype slowly faded when Hayes built some less successful brake models, and other competition came along from Hope, Shimano, and Avid. After a hiatus from the gravity side of the sport, Hayes launched the Dominion A4 back in 2018. It would be fair to say that they were met with some skepticism. That was short-lived - positive reviews began to emerge and Hayes rebuilt a loyal fan base with the Dominion A4s. Two factors that make the Dominions stand out to gravity riders are their extremely light lever action and the manner in which they quickly build power. In terms of structure, the ambidexterous master cylinder houses a reach adjustment screw and a tool-free bite point dial built into the lever. At the caliper end, a couple one-of-a-kind setup aids can be found. Another bonus for consumers is that Dominions include a set of semi-metallic and sintered pads in the box. Brake Details • DOT 5.1 fluid • 4x17mm pistons • Pad contact adjustment • Tool-free lever reach adjustment • Crosshair caliper alignment feature • 2-stroke caliper bleed feature • Semi-metallic and sintered pads included • 160, 180, 203, 220mm rotors avail. • Bronze, silver, and purple colorways • Weight: 303g front, 189g rotor • Price: $499.98 USD (pair) • hayesbicycle.com BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist Setup Hayes chose DOT 5.1 fluid for the Dominions, a decision that’s becoming less common with brands like SRAM and Formula moving away from the caustic oil. The bleed process uses two syringes to move the fluid through the system (available separately). Hayes has shared a step-by-step procedure of how to bleed the entire Dominion brake , or just the caliper end of the system using the 2-Stroke dual bleed ports. Each half of the caliper has its own bleed port to move fluid in isolation from the rest of the system. This can be handy to purge the smallest amounts of air. Another unique feature found on the Dominions is the Crosshair alignment system. Two set screws help to keep the caliper centered over the rotor while tightening the mounting bolts. Each complete brake comes with a 180cm length hose. Although I haven’t experienced it myself, the kevlar reinforced hose is larger in diameter than most and can be stubborn to feed through some tube-in-tube carbon frames with internal routing. Hayes offers four rotor sizes ranging from 160mm to 220mm in a six-bolt mounting pattern only. At 1.95mm, they aren't nearly as thick as some of the other hefty discs out there. Liveability: I've heard of some riders breaking a Dominion master cylinder clamp in a crash, but they've survived any lay-downs I might have had using these brakes. With the exception of easily accessing the pad contact adjuster, working on the Dominions is generally headache-free. Feel The ultimate brake test is to walk over to any bike and grab hold of the lever. In all seriousness, when you do this with a set of Dominions, you’ll immediately notice how freely the lever moves inward - that showed in our lab experiment too. In fact, the lever action is so light that it can take some getting used to modulating the brake while trying to hold a wheelie. The lever is shorter in length compared to the Trickstuff or Hope brakes on test, and the pivot is further away from the bar too. A bevelled edge positions the hook of the lever comfortably under your finger.
