In terms of climbing, the Spire feels a bit punchier but the Knolly offers out and out more grip on technical climbs. Plus, the lack of cross-talk between drivetrain and rear-wheel travel make for a bike that is very adept at climbing stepped technical sections. Which Model is the Best Value? Looking at which bike is best value, Knolly's pricing does guide you through incremental gains rather than giant leaps. That said, it still does feel a little strange to suggest the most expensive model. Even though it costs the most, in terms of value, it does represent the most bang for your buck. I believe that Shimano XT is superior to GX in terms of longevity and have always enjoyed the M8000 series of brakes. If it wasn't for the XT Factory model, I would suggest the Deore version. However, once you're getting to $5,400 for the GX the return does get considerably better to spend more and upgrade to a better drivetrain, components and fancy-factory suspension, if only to keep the eventual resell value higher. The Knolly has a sensible build kit... but there are gaps. 180mm rotors, a lack of chain guide and the stock Exo+ tyres that can't keep pace with the rest of the bike. Technical Report Fox Suspension: The 38 is a fantastic fork, as is the Fox X2 that came on the bike. Moving to a coil shock was game changing though, and the DHX2 offered a great level of grip, adjustment and support. The highly progressive bike and my high-chosen spring rate did make for a lot of top-out noise though, but it was easy to tune out and still offered a rebound speed that was amply fast enough for me. Shimano XT Drivetrain and Brakes: If ever somebody at SRAM comes across my reviews they must just put their head in their hands. SRAM makes amazing wireless drivetrains and now arguably the most powerful mass-produced brakes in the world. What would I buy with my money? And what do I want on my own bikes? A mechanical drivetrain that's been out for nearly 5 years and do-it-all four pot brakes. I just love that mechanical XT is always good. No charging, good ergonomics and a solid shift. What else is there? Enduro Light Build Kit: The build kit isn't bad - and is of a very good quality, but if you make a bike that can be ridden this hard it needs those final touches to complete the job. Thicker tires for both damping and puncture resistance, 200mm rotors all round and a chain device aren't things a rider should expect to have to source themselves when buying a brand-new enduro bike in 2024. The green room. Pros + Excellent tracking with little feedback through your feet + Balanced geometry + Ultra long seatpost insertion + Muted and calm ride through fast chundery trails + Will hunt out grip on technical climbs Cons - It felt too progressive for me when riding with the air shock - Punishes rear wheels on square edges - Not particularly snappy under accelerations - Spec could be more suited to the bike's capabilities Pinkbike's Take The Chilcotin is a very good bike and might even offer enough to persuade more people to become diehards. The tracking off the brakes is fantastic, and the muted feel it gives you through your feet can genuinely be compared to bikes that feature an idler. The geometry also bleeds into that, with a ride quality that is both stable, fun and responsive, all with plenty of grip. Just like rearward axle paths have their benefits and drawbacks, so too do bikes with more vertical or even forward ones. While it might show itself more when riding rough trails, I think that aspect, combined with the high progressivity of the rear suspension, means it will suit riders who are in tune with both the trail and setup of their bike and possibly punish those who are not. If you can embrace the setup and learn to ride it in a more engaged manner, you can negate some of these drawbacks, and should you do so, you will end up with a bike that is exceptional in some areas. The setup wasn't easy and took a lot of experimentation. A coil shock is also worth trying, and, quite frankly, if this were a review of the bike with an air shock, it wouldn't be anywhere near as positive. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Knolly Knolly Chilcotin Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: 2025 Knolly Warden - Pushing the Limits of Long, Low, & Slack
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, freeride-bikes, knolly, knolly-warden
article_text: Has Extreme Geometry Gone too Far? | 2025 Knolly Warden https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/SkvNXDK4 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 14:19 Bike design is a holistic process, with many equally important details to balance and factor in. How one goes about chasing those details and optimizing things for a given purpose can yield drastically different results, and is part of what makes the mountain bike world so interesting. Knolly is a brand that has always approached things differently, with some radical geometry figures and a novel suspension design. Billed as the most composed descending bike in their lineup, the Warden aims to take on the gnarliest trails around. I've been putting it to that test for the past few months - read on for the long-term impressions. Warden Details • 170mm travel, 170mm fork • Aluminum frame • MX 27.5/29" wheels • 62.5-63.25° head angle • 431-533mm reach, 605-637mm stack • 434-446mm chainstays • 5 sizes • Pricing: $5,799-7,299 USD ($3,599 frame) • Weight: 36.02lb / 16.3kg (size large) • knollybikes.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Warden is well suited to folks who want to point straight down something steep and hold on through the rough - just be sure to grab a set of high-rise bars first. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details The straight top tube might be the most apparent update to the Warden's frame, but there are a host of details to highlight besides the new design language. Each size features room for a water bottle, with an additional pair of mounts on the underside of the toptube for tools or spares. BC tough. Rear cable orifice. Dropper post insertion is quite good on the bike, with maximum insertion depths ranging from 260mm on the XS to 420mm on the XL. That means even the smallest bike in the lineup should be able to run a 170-190mm dropper post. Cables are routed internally through the front triangle, and external on the rear stays. While they do run unguided and require some fishing to snake through the frame, the sharp bends that the routing requires keeps them from rattling and causing a ruckus. Good clearance. Hard to forget which bike you're riding. Geometry Knolly describes the Warden's geometry as "freeride-focused," with the lengths and angles landing at some fittingly extreme figures to match the expected terrain. Located at the lower shock mount, the bike features a Neutral/Slack geometry adjustment, with the Slack position dropping the head angle to a very raked out 62.5°. The higher mode is still fairly extreme, landing at 63.25° on all sizes. That flip chip also affects seat angle and bottom bracket offset - the seat angle lands at either 77.75° in Neutral or 77° in Slack, and the BB drop is either 0.5mm in Neutral or 9.5mm in Slack. Knolly's approach to the relationship between stack and reach is a bit unusual, and worth digging into. You'll note that reach numbers are quite long, especially if aligned with other brand's named sizes - though I'd generally caution against buying a bike purely based on the t-shirt size. Those long reach numbers are paired with very short stack heights across the board, verging on the extreme in the larger sizes. The logic here is that one would run a long stack of spacers underneath the stem to reach the desired stack height, while simultaneously bringing the reach in a bit as those bars get closer to you. In practice, there's only so much adjustment on tap, as steer tube length, aesthetics, and the eventual tradeoff of reach reduction come into play. My setup ended up relying on a full stack of stem spacers and a rather high riser bar, but more on that later. Suspension Design Knolly implements their unique Fourby4 suspension design in all of their full-suspension bikes, with distinct implementations to cater to each use case. Principally, the design is a Horst link layout, with an additional link driving the shock. The outputs aren't drastically different than a standard 4-bar on paper, but feel quite unique in application. The 205x62.5mm shock delivers 170mm of travel, with 28.3% progression in the Neutral geometry setting, and 29.8% in the Slack position. I reached out to Noel Buckley, Knolly's founder and chief engineer, to get a sense for the kinematic outputs they settled on for the Warden. On anti-squat: Obviously somewhat suggestive based on rider height, frame size, cassette cog, chainring size, etc... Easy to fudge as well to get a "determined result". And... I think my opinion on using anti-squat as the definitive "Be All, End All" in terms of describing pedaling efficiency is pretty well known: "I.e. It might be useful on a rear suspension road bike with a rigid fork, climbing on pavement at a fixed grade in ideal weather." We like a bike that's active and able to follow terrain. We're not afraid to say that you can use the lockout lever on the rear shock if you're riding and climbing a ton of pavement. The reason for this is quite simply, if you build too much anti-squat into the frame, you can't remove it and it will negatively affect your traction and pre-load your rear suspension due to increased chain tension. So, we aim to take a balanced approach to non-technical terrain, technical terrain and difficult terrain when pedaling, while allowing the suspension to do its job with terrain following. Anyway - some numbers... 95% - 108% static dependent on cassette cog. About 5% less at sag. On anti-rise: We like a bike that's active under braking. We do like to have some fork dive offset, but we also want to ensure that the rear of the bike is free to follow the terrain and not pack up due to excessive braking forces. What some riders call "brake jack" is actually the rear suspension unloading when the rear wheel breaks contact with the ground: we want to ensure that this never happens to our customers. Our philosophy has always been to ensure that the more serious the trail conditions, the better the ability of the bike to respond to those conditions. It's easy to make a bike that works well when things are calm; it's when things are going sideways that you want your bike to be performing at its maximum and this is especially true for bikes like the Warden which are designed explicitly for aggressive riding. Around 67% static Around 70% at sag. Trending towards 100% at full compression. On rear travel and long-stroking: The Warden ships stock with a 205 x 62.5mm stroke shock, which generates 170mm of travel. The bike can use the full 65mm stroke of the shock which generates about 180mm of rear travel, which is the maximum travel that the bike's rear suspension is capable of. On the front end: forks from 170 - 203mm travel are allowed. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $7299 Travel 170mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Factory 205x62.5, CL RM Fork Fox 38 Factory 29" 170mm, 44mm offset Headset Cane Creek 50 ZS44/56 Cassette Shimano SLX M7100 12spd, 10-51T, Hyperglide+ Crankarms Shimano XT M8130 30T OR 32T, 170mm Chainguide N/A Bottom Bracket Shimano MT800 BSA, threaded Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur Shimano XT 12spd M8100 Chain Shimano XT 12spd Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods Shimano XT 12spd M8100 Handlebar Spank Vibrocore 35mm x 820mm, 25mm Rise Stem RaceFace Aeffect R 35mm x 40mm Grips Spank Spoon Brakes Shimano XT 4 Piston M8120 Wheelset Spank 359 HEX J-Bend 32h MX (MS) 29/27.5" Tires Maxxis Assegai 29" 2.5WT 3C MaxxGrip EXO+ / Maxxis DHR2 27.5" 2.4" 3C MaxxTerra EXO+ Seat SDG Bel-Air V3 Seatpost SDG Tellis (S1-S2: 170mm, S3-S5: 200mm), 31.6 Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Warden Test Bike Setup The purple size large Knolly came with familiar parts tip to tail, making most of the setup fairly easy. That said, the bike still required a fair bit of puzzling to get dialed into a place where I felt comfortable. One key facet was shock setup, which was more of a drawn-out process than I'm used to, due to the bike's unusual suspension characteristics. Though the numbers seem fairly standard on paper, the ride feel says otherwise - more on that later. I started with 30% sag, but found myself incrementally increasing pressure until I settled around 225psi, putting me in the mid-20% range for sag. I stuck to a fork setup that felt comfortable, with 105 psi and close to centered LSC and HSC. Shock compression was more regularly adjusted, depending on terrain. I found that I generally preferred the support a firmer LSC provided, but avoided closing things up too much so not to choke it out - opening things up unsurprisingly helped the bike sit deeper on steep terrain, where grip was paramount. HSC was more neutral, and sat at 3-5 clicks for the most part. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario The most critical change I made was to the bike's cockpit, in order to compensate for the very low stack height. I tried a few different bar/stem combinations, but ultimately settled on a 50mm rise OneUp Carbon Bar as the best option for this bike. Getting my hands higher helped the handling massively, and felt like a pretty critical change for my riding style and preference. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info The Warden does best on natural terrain where gravity does a lot of the work, and where controlling speed is the key to success. I took the Warden on many such rides, where a patient pedal up yields some fantastic raw descending. There are many good long descents in this world, but Whistler's Top of the World to Creekside is pretty hard to beat. Climbing Put simply, this is a lockout bike. I've had quite a few 170mm enduro bikes this year, and of the bunch this is the most lethargic to pedal. You can achieve a more supportive and energetic feel, but doing so requires a shock setup that compromises some of the bike's descending performance. As mentioned in the setup section, I ended up settling on a firmer shock setup to achieve the feel I wanted. You can get the bike to a place where you're not reaching for the lock switch on every sustained climb, but I think it's okay to just accept that step if you find yourself climbing sustained roads on a regular basis. Singletrack climbing benefits from the grip provided by the rear wheel, even if the overall efficiency isn't great. I found the Warden easy enough to get up and over some weird technical obstacles, thanks to the active suspension and very high bottom bracket. One geometry element that can work against you on the climbs is the combination of the head angle and stack figures. The Warden sports a very slack head angle at either 63.25° or 62.5°, and the stack height sits at a very low 628mm in the size Large / S4. Where a low stack can feel racy and powerful on cross-country bikes, that feel pairs better with the steering angles typical to that category, not those more often seen on downhill bikes. The combination here makes for a front end that wants to wander and flop, unless you're actively keeping the bike in line. Once you get a feel for how the bike wants to be ridden, you can get along just fine, but I wouldn't call the handling intuitive. Despite that, you can pedal through all the jank, thanks to a higher than normal bottom bracket and traction-rich suspension. Descending The mountain bike world is full of unique and purpose-built creations, and the Warden strikes me as one of the more niche. The geometry of the bike is unusual and challenging in many situations, but might make sense for some people in some places. Specifically, I think that place might be the North Shore, where steep rolls, awkward tech, and generally rough trail beds are predominant. I found the Knolly hard to ride well in most trail settings, but when riding on its home turf things made the most sense. Still, I think there are some points to pull apart here, and discuss how the geometry and suspension work together in a unique way. First, the suspension - traction-heavy and even-keeled. I'm very impressed by how little feedback reaches your feet through the roughest and most chopped out trails, even under hard braking. Knolly's Fourby4 does offer something special here, though you will have to spend more time on shock setup to reach an ideal balance. As mentioned in the Climbing section, some compromises are required to get the bike to offer the support required to make speed on some trails, but if you're indexing more heavily on the compliance and grip end of things, you should be happy with a fairly standard setup. I was willing to give up some of that traction for more support through the stroke, but not everyone will want to make the same compromise. The geometry of the Warden might make sense for some specific moves and situations, but overall I'd say it's the bike's weak point. Given the low stack, long reach, and high bottom bracket, the body position is very much over the bike, as opposed to the fuzzy in-bike feel that tends to be a goal for designs in this category. Bringing the cockpit up to the highest point on the steer tube (as cut by factory) and adding a 50mm rise OneUp bar did help, but ultimately those are unusual setup constraints many other bikes don't require. Generally, I felt that the bike was more stable and easy to handle in the Slack geometry setting, mostly due to the lower bottom bracket height. Unfortunately, that also gives you the more extreme head tube angle, sitting at a static 62.5°. This requires a conscious weighting of the front wheel through corners, but does provide plenty of stability at speed. I'd be curious to try the bike in the Slack position with a +1° headset to bring the steering back into a more multi-purpose position, but that's a bit beyond the purview of a typical review. Overall I'd say the Warden is well suited to folks who want to point straight down something steep and hold on through the rough - just be sure to grab a set of high-rise bars first. The Warden is a quiet bike, thanks to the general lack of chainslap and simple-yet-effective cable management. I'd still prefer the cables to be externally routed, but despite the required fishing and fuss, the internal routing that Knolly implemented does at least keep things from rattling. One small complaint about that routing comes down to the rear orifice where they go into the frame, which essentially acts as a catchment for all the water and debris that your rear tire kicks up. Water can easily follow the cables into the frame, and the bolt showed rust after only a couple months. Knolly Warden Frameworks Enduro How does it compare? These bikes have similar goals, but go about achieving things in very different ways. Both sport 170mm travel front and rear, both aim to provide a stable ride and composure through rough terrain, but they differ massively on the geometry front. Where the Knolly achieves wheelbase via a slack headtube angle and long reach, the Frameworks adds length to the chainstay - 18mm, to be exact. As a result, the Knolly necessitates more conscious weighting of the front wheel in corners and through awkward tech, where the Frameworks allows you to keep your weight a bit more central and neutral, freeing up some body English to navigate things. The other stark difference between the two geometries comes down to the stack height. The Frameworks boasts 50mm higher stack than the Knolly, and in my experience that benefit comes without any negative tradeoffs. As you'd expect, the riding position is far more upright both up and down the hill, with plenty of rider area to work with. Though climbing isn't the main attraction with either of these rigs, I'm going to give the nod to the Frameworks on that front as well. A very steep seat tube angle and supportive-enough pedaling platform make for a bike that is far easier to pedal all day, with a comfortable upright seated position to boot. Which Model is the Best Value? Of the three complete builds available, I'd opt for the middle of the range Eagle 90 build. The frameset options will probably be compelling to some, but for those that want to pack as many of the best products on one build, the Eagle 90 kit is probably the one I'd recommend. The Mavens are vastly better brakes than last-gen Shimano XT or Deore, and the RockShox supension suits the Warden's nature - namely the extra-firm lockout on the shock. Beyond that, the builds are mostly the same, so one could just buy the Deore build and upgrade bits here and there as they see fit. Technical Report Shimano XT 4-Piston Brakes: One of these brakes was plagued with issues since the get-go, exhibiting some unusually sticky lever action, and the typical wandering bite point. I was able to get them feeling decent with a good bit of work, but ultimately treated it as an opportunity to spend time on the new Magura Gustavs - birds and stones. Maxxis EXO+ Tires: The Assegai/DHRII tire combo is a modern classic for good reason, but this casing choice makes no sense on a bike built for freeride lines and gnarly trails. You can run high pressures and hope for the best, but inevitably a flat or rim dent is sure to occur if you're riding this bike in the intended terrain. My guess is this was a spec choice made purely for build weight, so hopefully you have a shorter travel bike you can rotate the light tires onto. Fox X2 Shock: The last-generation X2 that I've been riding on this bike has done a good job of managing forces and surviving the test unscathed. That said, it seems the bike is now shipping with the updated monotube version of the shock, which should alleviate any lasting longevity concerns one might have about Fox's fattest air shock. I think the tune works nicely with the bike, and though the suspension took a good bit of fussing to get dialed, it was helped by the many adjustments you can make to the X2's damper. I haven't ridden the Warden with the Vivid, but given the progressive nature of the bike, I'd be curious how the hydraulic bottom out interacts with that end of stroke travel. Pros + Suspension feel is quiet and composed + High quality frame + Dual crown & long-stroke potential Cons - The low stack, slack head angle, and long reach can cause awkward handling. - Delicate setup to balance traction and support - Climbing performance dependent on shock lockout Pinkbike's Take Knolly's unique suspension design offers an impressive amount of grip and composure through rough terrain, but the Warden's geometry feels compromised and challenging in most situations. You can make setup alterations to help the bike feel more well-rounded, but ultimately it feels like a purpose-build tool for a very specific niche. As I mentioned earlier, it's going to work best for riders whose typical trails are steep and rough, just be prepared to do some cockpit and suspension adjustments to find the sweet spot. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Action photos: Stefan Licko Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Freeride Bikes Knolly Knolly Warden Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: KOM Xeno Hubs - Reinventing The Wheel
author: Seb Stott
article_text: Reinventing the wheel may be a stretch, but KOM's Xeno hubs represent a radical redesign of bicycle hubs. Rather than the hub shell and freehub body sitting next to each other on the hub axle, the freehub spins inside the hub shell and runs the whole width of the hub. It's kind of like the two components are doing a firm handshake instead of a fist bump. Why would you want that? According to the British company, their patented design makes for a much stiffer hub, with less chance of breaking an axle, longer bearing life and less stress on the pawls. The design is also claimed to reduce drag while pedalling because only two bearings are spinning at any time, compared to four or five with a standard hub. The downside? This layout forces the freehub ratchet to the brake side, which requires proprietary rotors and potential clearance issues. KOM Xeno Hub Details • Performance claims: Reduced drag, longer bearing life, stronger axle • Straight-pull spokes • Only compatible with KOM disc rotors • Available with all major freehub and axle standards • Modular, serviceable design • E-bike approved • Customizable colours • Price: £550 (rear hub) / £275 (front); £1,175 (wheelset with DT Swiss EX51 rims) - prices include rotors • Claimed hub weight: 260 g (rear hub), 140g (front) • Wheelset weight: 2,079 g (effective) • Lifetime Warranty on axles and hub shell • More information: k-o-m.co.uk From a first principles engineering perspective, I'm convinced the KOM design is superior. The question is, is it worth making the switch to a new way of doing things? Six large pawls are housed inside the ratchet ring on the brake side. The ratchet ring sits inside the rotor, but is separate from it. Design All hubs have an axle, a sleeve that fits concentrically around the frame's thru-axle. Most regular hubs have a hub shell that connects to the spokes, with two bearings between it and the axle. Next to this, there's the freehub body that holds the cassette, with two or three bearings between it and the axle. A cutaway showing the KOM freehub axle inside the hub body and the hub axle inside that. A cutaway of a relatively standard hub. The main hub axle that goes around the thru-axle, with the bearings on which the freehub axle spins. The freehub axle, which connects to the cassette on the right and the rotor and ratchet on the left, goes concentrically around the hub axle. The hub body spins on the two large-diameter bearings. In the KOM hubs, the freehub fits concentrically around the full width of the axle, with two bearings between it and the axle at either end. The hub body goes concentrically around this freehub, with two large-diameter bearings between it and the freehub, allowing it to spin independently while coasting. This approach has three main advantages: • The full-width freehub adds stiffness to the system, reducing flex in the axle, which reduces the risk of axle fatigue and failure, bearing misalignment and bearing wear. • The two bearings that connect the freehub to the axle are as outboard as possible, further reducing stress on the axle and the bearings. • While pedalling, only the two inner bearings between the freehub and the axle are spinning, reducing drag compared to conventional hubs, where the bearings in the hub body and freehub all spin while pedalling. (During coasting, it's the two larger-diameter bearings that are rotating, but this is the same number of bearings as a conventional hub.) • The stiffer hub and brake-side ratchet sidesteps the risk of uneven loading of the pawls due to axle flex, which can damage freehubs in conventional hubs with radial ratchet designs (this is less of an issue with axial ratchet rings like DT Swiss hubs). Force diagram from Sheffield Hallam University's hub stiffness test. The results: the KOM hubs showed significantly less vertical deflection with a load applied to the hub. We have proof that the KOM hubs are stiffer thanks to an independent test from Sheffield Hallam University . They tested the KOM hubs against four other high-end MTB hubs with a 4,000 Newton (408 Kg or 900 lb of force) vertical load and measured the deflection. The Xeno hubs deflected about a half to a third as much vertically as the other hubs in this test, and a tenth as much angularly (bending). So we know they are stiffer, but whether that matters is an open question - even in the least stiff hub, we're talking about a millimetre of vertical deflection under nearly half a ton of load. To achieve this layout, the ratchet is moved to the brake side (I'll not call it the non-drive side to avoid confusion). This required KOM to produce their own disc rotors to fit around the ratchet ring. They offer these rotors in all common sizes: 160mm,180mm, 200mm, 203mm, 220mm & 223mm. At £50 each, they're not extortionate compared to other rotors, and your first set is included with the hubs. On the plus side, this layout has an added benefit of allowing more space for the ratchet, so KOM use six oversized pawls engaging a large-diameter ratchet ring simultaneously, resulting in even less stress on the pawls for a given input torque. This could be particularly relevant to e-bikers. The ratchet ring is also easily replaceable, so you could swap it out for more or fewer points of engagement (the standard is 120 POE) or if it wears out. In fact, the whole hub is designed to be modular and easy to service with basic tools. KOM say spare parts will be available for sale "for decades." Loads of room here, but the rotor bolts can catch the frame or brake mount on certain frames. Compatability The Xeno hubs are available with interchangeable freehub bodies (XD, Microspline and HD); in 142, 148, and 157 mm widths, and with all rotor sizes from 160 mm to 223 mm. However, the four-bolt brake pattern is a larger diameter than a standard 6-bolt or centerlock rotor, and this won't fit with every bike. KOM has a list of compatible bikes on their website , and instructions to measure your bike to make sure. But if you're planning to keep the wheels for longer than your bike, there is a risk they won't fit future frames. I've had no issues with a Forestal Siryon and Crossworx Dash 290 , but a Privateer 161 wouldn't fit without modifying (filing down) the brake mount. KOM offer matching front hubs with the same brake interface, but while they claim their bearing layout minimises axial preload to further reduce friction, the rear hub is the real innovation here. So if you're not concerned about matching aesthetics, you could consider a rear wheel only. Cost and weight comparison For the bare hubs, KOM claim 260 g for the rear and 140 g for the front. For comparison, DT Swiss's premium 240 straight-pull hubs weigh a claimed 207 g rear and 118 g front. So a set of 240 hubs is 75 g lighter according to official figures. The 240 hubs cost £319.99 (rear) and £159.99 (front). So the KOM hubs are £230 more expensive for the rear alone, or £345 for the pair. That price includes the KOM rotors of your chosen size. Spare or replacement rotors cost £50 each, which is similar to a standard rotor. My 29" KOM wheelset with EX 511 rims, 28 spokes on the front and 32 on the rear, weighs 2,515g - that's including the KOM 200 mm rotors, which are 2 mm thick. A set of 200 x 2 mm SRAM HS2 rotors weighs 410 g, and a set of twelve rotor bolts weighs 26 g; so taking that off, the " effective " wheelset weight is 2,079 g. For comparison, a set of DT Swiss EX 1700 wheels (with the same rims on their mid-range 350 hubs) weighs 1,985 grams. The complete KOM wheelset tested with DT Swiss EX 511 rims costs £1,175 (+ £50 for custom colours), compared to £760 for a DT Swiss EX 1700 wheelset. So there's a £415 price difference for a wheelset, but those KOM hubs include rotors. You can also get the KOM wheels with Reserve rims, from £2,275. I tested the wheels on a Crossworx Dash 290 and a Forestal Siryon Performance KOM claim their hubs offer noticeably less coasting drag, but I'm not convinced. Measuring such things in the real world is incredibly hard, but in one simple test, I spun up the KOM wheel and a DT Swiss E 1700 wheel to the same speed and timed how long it took to stop. While I can't guarantee the initial speed was identical every time, I tried this crude test three times and got the same result: the KOM stopped first. I also made sure that neither brake was rubbing by retracting the pads. This test is imperfect because it doesn't take into account the vertical load on the wheel while riding, and perhaps the KOM system's stiffer axle reduces bearing drag when the wheel is loaded, but you'd need a controlled indoor test rig to measure that. KOM haven't provided any evidence to support this claim except for this video showing a rider on KOM hubs pulling away from his mates, which could be explained any number of ways. When I tried doing roll-down tests (timing how long it takes to coast down a gentle hill), the times were all over the place, even with the same wheelset, making this claim impractical to test in the field. When pedalling, the KOM hubs could have an advantage since they're only spinning two bearings instead of four, which could reduce drag. But a lab test conducted by Classified found that a DT Swiss 240 hub only had 0.2% power losses when unloaded, so there's not much room for improvement there. Again, the potential gains could be greater when the hub is loaded, but according to this Hambini article , the losses in a typical hub are around 1% when loaded, so even if the KOM hubs were friction-free, this would be too small to measure in the real world.
