I can't notice any difference in stiffness either. The rim and spokes will no doubt dominate wheel flex when compared to any hub. I can report that the KOM brake rotors offer good braking performance, easily on par with the in-house rotors from Hayes, Formula or Magura, whose brakes I tested with. The 120-point ratchet is relatively quick to engage, never skipped in testing, and makes a satisfying whirr. But realistically, the hubs fade into the background like any other. I've had these wheels for six months, but it's too soon to say if they are any more reliable than conventional hubs. I have several wheelsets that have years of use without issues, so it's impossible to know if these will last any longer. Pros + Hubs are proven to be stiffer by science, which may increase with bearing life and reliability for hard use cases. + Backed by warranty, modular design, and spare parts promise Cons - Requires specific rotors, but these are included, are available in all sizes and perform well. - Large rotor bolt diameter won't fit every frame. - Expensive. - Heavier than some other premium hubs. Pinkbike's Take The ground-up redesign of the KOM rear hub is something that engineers are sure to get hot under the collar about. I'm convinced it's a superior layout. But it's solving a problem that isn't exactly ubiquitous. While broken ratchets, snapped axles, and premature bearing wear aren't unheard of, and some hubs were notorious , these problems are far from universal among conventional hub designs. That said, if you're a big rider or e-biker looking for a set of wheels to last, they're worth considering. The proven stiffness, oversized ratchet, warranty, modular design and spares should offer confidence that they'll go the distance. The proprietary rotors needn't be a dealbreaker - they're available in all sizes, they're reasonably priced and perform well with multiple brake brands. Besides, rotors are a wear item. The lack of universal frame fitment is a concern if you change frames regularly but want a wheelset to last for years. But, if you're building a custom "forever bike," they have appeal. — Seb Stott Note: since this article was first published, KOM have pointed out that a rotor (of any size) is included with each wheel or hub. The article has been updated to reflect this. - Seb Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Wheels Kom Cycling Kom Xeno Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: 2025 Kona Hei Hei G10
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Hei Hei Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/xkD6ZlPW Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 18:36 The Kona Hei Hei first entered this world in 1991 as a titanium hardtail, but things have clearly evolved quite a bit since then. Now pushing out of its traditionally XC-focused heritage, the Hei Hei is toying with the trail bike realm, with travel and geometry pushing towards capability rather than all-out race speed. There's still plenty of get-up and go left in the chassis, but the platform has been designed more as a malleable multipurpose bike as opposed to an ultra-focused singular one. Hei Hei G10 Details • 120mm frame travel, 130mm fork • Carbon fiber frame • 66° head angle, 76° seat angle • 424-509mm reach, 606-639mm stack • 435mm chainstay length • Weight: 30.6lb / 13.9kg, XL no pedals • Price: $6,299 USD / $7,499 CAD / 6,499€ • konaworld.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The expectations for cross-country and trail bikes have come a long way in the past few years, and while the Kona Hei Hei feels like it's a bit behind that wave, it's still fun in the right terrain. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details The Hei Hei sports a carbon front and rear triangle, with internal routing through both. There's a press-fit bottom bracket, a 31.6mm seat tube with good insertion depth, and nine bottle bosses for all your stuff carrying pleasure. The bike takes a 180mm rear rotor at the minimum, and can fit a 38t chainring. For the most part, Kona did a good job of speccing burly hardware throughout the Hei Hei's frame, save for the 3mm collet bolts in the pivot axles - I'm always wary of hardware that small on a relatively high-torque interface. Dropper insertion per size is as follows: SM 225mm / MD 255mm / LG 290mm / XL 330mm. The Hei Hei uses a 44/56 headset, and features an ISCG 05 mount at the BB. Kona quotes their frame weight for a medium frameset with a shock at 2.7kg / 5.95lbs. Geometry Slacker longer and steeper, the Hei Hei has been modernized quite a bit when compared to its previous iteration. The 66° head angle is close to some of the most cutting edge cross-country bikes out there, but definitely keeps things sportier than more descent-focused trail bikes in this hard-to-define category. Reach numbers jump quite a bit per size, making for a large sweep across the range. The stack heights don't grow much relative to reach, keeping the body position a bit more XC-aggressive than upright. The seat tube angle hits an average effective figure of about 76°, but the actual angle is quite a bit slacker at 71°. This makes for a fairly long top tube, so factor that in when it comes to choosing a size. Chainstay lengths across all four sizes land at 435mm, with a bottom bracket drop of 31mm. There aren't any flip chips or geometry adjust features to monkey with, so you're set with what you see here, barring any component changes. I puzzled over the sizing on this bike a bit, and while I could certainly ride a Large without issue, the XL is the size that should fit me for this category of bike. I fit squarely in that position on the geometry chart, and the XL is more in line with other 120mm bikes I've been reviewing recently. Ultimately, I think I'm a bit caught between sizes, and would probably elect for something between the two. Suspension Design Moving away from the flex stay design they'd previously used, Kona went with a linkage driven single pivot for the 10th generation of the Hei Hei. Their Swinger suspension relies on a seatstay pivot and toptube-mounted swing link to drive the 190x45mm RockShox Deluxe shock. The Hei Hei feels quite supportive and efficient at the top end of travel, and was easy to hone in for the initial setup. My experience so far hasn't warranted any use of the shock lockout, and I don't foresee many folks needing it unless you're really pushing the bike in a race scenario. The Deluxe rear shock sports a 3-position compression adjustment, and I've generally found the middle position to suit my use case well. RockShox's new Deluxe. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $6299 Travel 120mm Rear Shock RockShox Deluxe Ultimate Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate Charger 3.1, 130mm Headset FSA No. 57E Cassette SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Crankarms SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Press-Fit Rear Derailleur SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Chain SRAM GX T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Handlebar Kona XC/BC 35 Stem Kona XC/BC 35 Grips Kona Key Grip Brakes SRAM Motive Bronze Wheelset WTB KOM Team i30 Hubs DT Swiss 370 Spokes Sandvik Stainless, 2.0/1.8 Butted Rim WTB KOM Team i30 Tires Maxxis Dissector, 29x2.4", EXO, MaxxTerra Seat WTB Volt Seatpost TranzX Dropper +RAD, 31.6mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Hei Hei G10 Test Bike Setup The Hei Hei is one of those bikes that never really deviated from my initial setup, thanks to a simple layout and familiar components. I settled on 93psi in the Pike, 200psi in the Deluxe, and the rest was a matter of fine tuning fit to best suit my preference. The seattube angle necessitated some saddle slamming, and I did prefer the bike with a higher rise handlebar, but other than that things were cut and dry. I mentioned a couple small changes I'd make down in the Technical Report, but I'll reiterate them here for good measure. Those looking to get the most out of the Motive brakes should swap out the stock Organic pads for their Metallic alternatives, and consider ditching the Centerline rotors for some HS2s. I'm not a big fan of the outgoing Dissector as a front tire, but have run it enough to feel fine leaving it on long-term. Those who don't like a large gap from center to edge knobs should consider a swap. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info The Hei Hei moves through the woods efficiently and keeps things fun, making it a solid trail bike for areas with a good mix of climbing, traversing, and flowy descending. It doesn't encourage much riding above the expected use case, but is happy to get out for long days at a decent pace, so long as the fit suits your preferences. Climbing The Hei Hei offers and efficient and stable pedaling platform, with a little noticeable bob and just enough traction to keep the rear tire planted on steeper climbs. That traction breaks free under harder efforts, but that's often the cost of having an even-keeled level of support underneath you. Despite that composure and efficient feel, the 120mm Kona feels quite far away from the XC race bikes I've ridden recently, erring far more on the trail bike end of the spectrum. It lacks the energetic snap that true race bikes offer in spades, instead sporting more of a comfortable long-day-out feel. Kona will cite Cory Wallace's 24-hour wins on the platform, but I'd note the vastly different build kit on his bike versus the one on test here. Evolutions in geometry are often centered around descending performance, but we've seen just as many advances in body position and comfort for the climbing side of the equation. On that front, I found the Hei Hei to be a bit behind the times, awkwardly split between more comfortable trail bikes and better-proportioned XC bikes. The spectrum of options in the 120mm travel bracket is a bit dizzying, but I'll use two bikes I've ridden extensively and spoken about a lot to paint the picture. On one end sits the Specialized Epic 8, with fairly cutting-edge geometry for a World Cup XC bike; on the other end sits the Santa Cruz Tallboy, not an XC bike, but a vey capable 120mm trail bike that still feels lively and efficient. Compared to both of those, the Hei Hei features a much higher bottom bracket, slacker-feeling seat tube, and much longer top tube, resulting in a bit of a stretched-out pedaling position. That fit geometry suits some folks, and can feel less compromised in certain sizes in a given range, but I can only speak to the XL size that I've ridden for all three platforms. Small details matter when you're trying to put your all into the pedals on a hard climb, and on that front I found the Kona's geo to be a bit wanting. Luckily, you have a comfortable suspension platform underneath you to keep humming along as you put in the miles, but ultimately it's the combination of factors that makes a bike. Descending Kona took the Hei Hei out of its longstanding cross-country pedigree with this bike, with the 120mm rig now straddling the line between worlds. To me, it feels more like a trail bike than a lightweight XC whip, despite the generally supportive and efficient feel of the platform. That said, certain choices with the geometry are more in line with traditional XC figures, and will still cater to those who think the category has gone too far in recent years. As someone who's nothing but elated at how capable the newest crop of XC bikes has been, I'm a bit disappointed Kona didn't push things further with their platform. Despite tipping the scales at over 30 pounds, the Kona still feels a little nervous on technical trails, thanks to a fairly forward-biased body position as dictated by the geometry, and a suspension platform that errs on pop and support over composure and grip. I've been able to put the bike in tricky situations and make it work to my liking, but it requires a heavier hand than I'd prefer, and feels more tiring for it. I tend to prefer a bike that situates the rider in a more stable area between the pedals and the bars, and the Hei Hei takes a decent bit of body English to get your center of gravity in a spot that feels similar to my top choices. The Kona's weight pays dividends in how well it holds up to the abuse of daily trail riding, with a stout feel that takes hard corners and bad landings well. None of the gingerly XC tiptoeing required, you can simply ride it like a trail bike. This comes in handy, considering the terrain where the bike felt best: flow trails with loads of support and jumps where pop is rewarded. I had a great time on the Hei Hei on local trails that feature plenty of that sort of riding, as the bike carries and generates speed well when you're not navigating too much tech. I think the Hei Hei will be a solid tool for those who prefer a more stretched out body position, want to optimize for efficiency, and ride fairly rolling terrain that rewards pumping for speed. Kona Hei Hei G10 Santa Cruz Tallboy 5 How does it compare? Two bone-colored 120mm mountain bikes, similar in intent but quite different in execution. The Tallboy is a bike I'm very familiar with, and the Hei Hei is one I've grown to know over the course of this review. If I had to choose between the two, there's no question that I'd go with the Tallboy. The geometry is far more balanced, and feels better-suited to both climbing and descending on natural and sculpted terrain alike. Do I have complaints about some of the Tallboy's details? Yes, but despite those I think the bike still performs better as a trail bike / overbuilt XC than the Hei Hei. The longer reach and shorter stack of the Kona can feel racier if a low front end body position suits your fit, but takes away some of the easy handling that comes with the Tallboy. Add to that a 10mm higher bottom bracket and a shorter rear center, and the Hei Hei ends up being more reactive and imbalanced than the alternative. Frame details are another matter, and more of a personal decision - bottle bosses vs. in-frame storage, press-fit vs. threaded bottom bracket, no flip chip vs. one. The one design decision that might limit some folks' ability to get the most out of the Kona is the length of the seat tube, which extends a full 30mm further than the Santa Cruz. Suspension performance is significantly better on the Tallboy, with a more composed descending quality and less feedback to the rider, while still maintaining a similar level of efficiency. All-out support goes to the Hei Hei, but at the cost of grip, bump absorption, and comfort. If XC racing is more the focus, and you want all the frame mounts, non-flex stay suspension, and efficiency that the Hei Hei could offer, I'd put the Salsa Spearfish on your list. Though I'd still opt for the Epic 8 as a favorite XC race bike, that bright green Salsa offers a pretty compelling option for those interested in something a little out of the norm. Which Model is the Best Value? Being that there's only one build available for the Hei Hei, this is a fairly easy decision. The discussion then becomes more about how good a value the one build kit really is, and that's ultimately a choice for the customer. For $6,299 USD, there are a lot of budget-focused parts on this bike. Luckily, the suspension is top notch, and the brakes and drivetrain do the job quite well. I'd love to see a nicer wheelset with lighter weight and higher engagement on a bike like this, and would definitely opt for a different cockpit if this were my personal ride. For $2,950, you can get a frame and shock, which does come in lower than competing bikes like the Spur and the Tallboy. If the features and geometry suit your use case, and you're keen to build up a unique bike from parts, then that's an option I'd strongly consider. Technical Report SRAM Motive Bronze Brakes: I've been happy with SRAM's new XC brake offering, as they've greatly increased the power output and managed to keep the lever feel light and predictable. As specced here, the Motives are essentially in their weakest possible configuration, with Organic pads and 180mm Centerline rotors. Folks looking to get the most out of this brake platform should swap to Metallic pads and HS2 rotors. Maxxis Dissector (Gen 1) Tires: The initial Dissector has never been my favorite tire, feeling just fine out back and fairly sketchy as a front tread. The large gap from the center lugs to the edge can be learned and appreciated, if only those outer lugs were better supported and didn't feel sketchy to commit to. I'd probably just run the Dissector as a rear on this bike, and install something a little more predictable up front. RockShox Pike / Deluxe Ultimate: The completely silent operation of the newest RockShox product isn't typically the thing I fixate on when riding, but on a bike that is otherwise completely silent, it's a nice detail. Beyond the lack of noise, the Pike feels well-suited to this bike, and is a fork I've been getting along with quite well across a few other review platforms. Pros + Silent on trail + Well-appointed frame details + Keeps things fun Cons - XC geometry, trail bike intentions - Heavy build weight - Lacks composure in rougher terrain Pinkbike's Take The expectations for cross-country and trail bikes have come a long way in the past few years, and while the Kona Hei Hei feels like it's a bit behind that wave, it's still fun in the right terrain. Though the combination of factors doesn't put this bike on my short list, I've still had a great time ripping around on flowier terrain, on a platform that's easy to get back to the top of the hill for a second lap. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Kona Kona Hei Hei Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: The Kona Process 134 CR DL is a Comfort Zone Companion
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: Kona Process 134 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/AVvcV9G4 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:43 Cut from the same cloth as its longer-travel sibling, the Process 134 is Kona's answer to the needs of the modern day trail rider. Now in its 3rd generation, it rolls up with a new-found versatility with capacity to run a 29" or MX wheelset, with a subtly modernized set of geometry figures that don't oversell its intentions. Lo and behold the bike possesses 134mm of rear wheel delivered via the same linkage-driven single-pivot platform seen on the Process 153, and the Process X enduro bike. The range-topping 134 CR DL reviewed here is a carbon-framed affair priced at $4,499, with one more affordable carbon option, and two budget-oriented aluminum options, too. They share the same wheel size versatility as the carbon frame. Process 134 G3 Details • Carbon frame • 134mm travel / 140mm fork • 29" & MX capable • Head angle: 65.5º • 76.6 - 76.9º seat tube angle • Reach: 430 - 515 mm • Chainstay: 435 mm • Weight (actual): 14 kg / 30.8 lbs (Size S, stock) • S - XL • Price: $4,499 USD • Warranty: 3 years • konaworld.com Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take It's not hard work to enjoy riding the Process 134, but it will reward an aggressive and precise riding style. Its firm ride feel and quick handling through turns can make you feel pinned on smooth bike park terrain, but it's easy to come undone when carrying that speed into looser, steeper terrain where a little more suppleness on- and off the brakes would make things a little more comfortable and predictable. — Jessie-May Morgan Four holes in the front allow for either-or cable routing Frame Details The 134 CRDL has a carbon frame, and weighs in at 14 kg (30.8 lbs) in a size small. That's a little portly for a carbon bike in this travel category, but that has to be considered alongside the component specification and asking price before a fair judgement can be passed. The bike has a respectable build spec (though it is a little outdated), and its most expensive variant is clear of a thousand dollars cheaper than the low-end build options from competitors like Pivot. Personally, I am a huge fan of the frame shape and the contouring of its carbon tubes. It almost looks as though it has been sculpted by an artist at the seat tube and along the stays. Those lovely shapes can't possibly be born purely born out of function, can they? Love letter penned, let's turn to what matters. A trough, for when you run out water on a rainy ride The dropper cable makes a small appearance before entering the seat tube All sizes can accept a water bottle in the front triangle. On the small, I had to make use of an offset mount from Apidura to shift the bosses up the downtube such that the shock piggyback would clear the bottle. Accommodation made, I can fit a 600 ml bottle in there with a side-entry bottle cage - if I wrestle it in. Such a palaver might not be experienced by anyone riding the entry-level alloy Process 134 as it runs an in-line shock. Cable routing is internal and guided throughout by carbon tubes for easy cable replacements. The CR/DL model I tested has a SRAM Transmission AXS drivetrain, but there are ports for routing a cable should you ever want to go back to a mechanical drivetrain. Close to where the cable would exit the front triangle, there is a clamp for securing it on the main pivot so the cable shouldn't move and rub the carbon as the suspension articulates. Only the routing of the brake hose is external along the chainstay. Tube-in-tube cable routing, a dropped stay and comprehensive chain slap protection make for a very quiet bike. Is there anything more joyful? Some final important frame details: an ISCG 05 mount is there for the mounting of a chain guide and bash guard, and the maximum chainring size is 36T. The rear brake mount is sized for a 180mm rotor direct mount, though most models get a 20mm post mount adaptor for the 200mm rotor. Last but not least, the Process 134 runs a press fit bottom bracket. Geometry The Process 134 goes 5mm longer than the predecessor, matching the burlier 153 with its reach numbers. The four sizes span a generous range with reaches of 430mm, 455mm, 480mm and 515mm, paired with short seat tubes. Kona do not afford each front triangle its own appropriately sized chainstay length; all four sizes share the same rear triangle with a 435mm chainstay. Though insertion depth of the seat tube isn't quite to the center of the earth, it is respectable. The 175mm Reverb spec'd on the S is wholly sufficient for that frame's intended rider height. Drop length increases to 200mm on the M-XL sizes, and many a rider will want to swap it out for something longer. To that end, these maximum seat post insertion depths should be kept in mind: S ; 270mm, M; 270mm, L; 300mm, XL; 320mm. The head angle sits at 65.5°, ball-park for a bike of this travel bracket. The seat tube angles are steeper than those of the predecessor, but still a little off-the-back in regard to where the rider is positioned, and relative to some of its competitors. In a size large, it's an effective 76.7°. Given the all-round trail riding intentions, I can see why Kona are a little conservative on this front. I certainly wouldn't expect it to have the extra-steep, wrist-loading seat tube angles we see on longer-travel bikes, but a touch steeper wouldn't hurt, in my opinion. Head tube length increases in 11mm increments as we look up through the size range, so taller riders may not have to make too many accommodations to bring the bars up to a comfortable ride height. The flip-chip at the rocker-stay interface is a geometry correction tool to be used when switching out the rear wheel size. It corrects for the otherwise reduced bottom bracket height, and in doing so increases the rear axle's mechanical advantage over the shock, increasing the average leverage ratio. As such, you'll need to run a little more air in the rear shock when switching to the 27.5" rear wheel if you want to run the same sag. Suspension Design The Process 134 delivers its rear wheel travel via a linkage-driven single pivot, with a 185mm x 50mm trunnion mount shock. Its leverage curve is consistently progressive. In its stock configuration with a complete 29" wheelset, travel is its namesake 134mm, and the leverage curve starts at around 3.0, finishing up at just over 2.4. The leverage ratio is slightly increased with the MX wheelset, starting out just north of 3.1, and travel increasing to 140mm. All models ship with an air shock, though Kona state the kinematic works well with coil, too. Anti-squat is relatively high, sitting just north of 100% around sag. That indicates the bike's suspension should feel agnostic of pedaling inputs at this portion in the travel when winching up inclines in the easier gears. Specifications Price $4499 Travel 134mm Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate RCT Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate RC2 Charger 3, 140mm Headset FSA Orbit 1.5 E ZS Crankarms SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Kona XC/BC 35 Stem Kona XC/BC 35 Grips Kona Key Grip Brakes SRAM G2 RSC, 200mm Centerline Rotors Wheelset WTB KOM Team i30 TCS Hubs DT SWISS 350 Tires (F) 29" x 2.5" Maxxis Minion DHF EXO / (R) Maxxis Dissector EXO 27.5" x 2.4" (S) Seat WTB Volt Seatpost RockShox Reverb (170mm, S) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup I'm 163 cm, and I tested the Process 134 CRDL in a size small with a reach of 430mm paired with a 50mm stem. Sizes M-XL are shipped with a complete 29" wheelset, but the small arrives as a mullet. It can still accommodate a 29" rear wheel, however I didn't try it myself. I cut the 20mm rise bar to my preferred 740mm, and rode the bike with the shock sitting at 25% sag (128 psi). I set the fork to the pressure recommendation from RockShox. I have spent a lot of time on the Charger 3 damper. As a rider who sits on the lighter end of the spectrum, I am familiar with the need to run its compression and rebound damping dials open, the latter to get the fork to recover its travel sufficiently from repeated hits. To those interested, the Charger 3.1 is a marked improvement on that front, allowing for much higher rebound speeds at low spring pressures and better traction as a result. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Aboyne, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 127 lbs / 57.5 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan Download the Trailforks App Testing Info I rode the Process 134 on a variety of terrain; bike park and more natural, granite rock singletrack, mostly in dry conditions. It was the preferred option over my go-to enduro bike for rides on sculpted bike park-esque trails, and rides that involved very long stretches of climbing on punishingly steep fire road to access some of the more iconic mountain-top descents on this eastern edge of the Cairngorms. I also took a liking to grabbing it for quick lunchtime laps of the local hill that is riddled with several easily-lapped hand-cut descents of mellow gradient. I rode it both within, and outwith its intended comfort zone to find where it excels, and where it leaves you wanting. Climbing The Process 134 isn't as spritely a climber as its travel numbers and fast-rolling Maxxis Dissector tire might have you think. The suspension is relatively neutral while pedaling on smooth fire roads. The bike doesn't feel particularly inefficient in that regard and I didn't feel the need for a lock-out. It's not so supple on bumpy singletrack climbs though, and I found it a challenge to prevent the rear tire from breaking traction over rocks, roots, as well as softer ground. The seated pedaling position is a little too far off the back for my liking. I have come to appreciate the upright position afforded by steeper seat tube angles, and the Kona is a little slack in that regard. I chose to push the saddle to its extreme forward position to alleviate some of the discomfort I felt in seated pedaling, but it didn't solve the problem entirely. On very steep sections of singletrack, the slack angle had me inching my way up the nose of the saddle in a bid to keep weight down through the front tire. Those riding flatter or more undulating terrain are less likely to experience an issue here. Descending The Process 134 is reasonably adept in descending most terrain. It got on well with most challenges I sent its way, though with varying degrees of finesse. With just 134mm of rear wheel travel, it would be naïve of anyone to expect the bike to slay on the most demanding technical singletrack there is, but also wrong to assume it isn't at least capable of managing without drama. I would generally steer clear of judging a bike too hard on its travel numbers alone, as it can often be rather misleading. I was very comfortable riding the Process 134 on technical, flowing singletrack from the get-go. Stood up on the pedals, the cockpit is a little on the compact end of the spectrum for me, but I quite like that in a trail bike. It maximizes the bike's capacity to feel playful, and can be helpful in preventing its rider from getting too over-confident in steeper sections of trail. The 65.5° head tube angle is a long way off from feeling twitchy, but when pointing the bike down the fall line, one can't help but feel a little vulnerable. I did benefit from raising the bar by around 20mm (relative to the position shown). The bike felt better to climb with the bar in the lower position in which it arrived, but for descending I preferred the more carefree riding style that was afforded by the higher bar. The suspension feels relatively firm off the top. I wouldn't go so far as to call it harsh, but on the whole the bike does have a certain firmness to it. For context, I have ridden more supple-feeling 120mm travel bikes. To begin with, I ran the rear suspension at 25% sag with high and low-speed compression wound to their open positions. On some bigger compressions and small hucks I was blowing through the travel and coming up against a hard bottom-out. I remedied that with an extra volume spacer, and a few clicks of HSC. This made the rear considerably more resistant to bottom-out, and considerably more supportive through the mid-stroke. With these settings, the bike came alive. It was a joy to ride on fast, flowing singletrack, and felt utterly wonderful to push and pump through berms and rollers. The rear end tracks very well in most situations, but under braking the suspension noticeably stiffens up. It caught me off guard on the first few rides, but I soon adapted, taking care to pick my braking zones effectively. It's not always possible of course, but when the opportunities arose, I felt it beneficial to brake before an array of braking bumps, and to roll through them off the brakes. It's not hard work to enjoy riding the Process 134, but it will reward an aggressive and precise riding style. Its firm ride feel and quick handling through turns can make you feel pinned on smooth bike park terrain, but it's easy to come undone when carrying that speed into looser, steeper terrain where a little more suppleness on- and off the brakes would make things a little more comfortable and predictable. The $5,499 USD Kona Process 134 CRDL - sale price $4,499 The $6,999 USD Pivot Trailcat LT Ride GX Eagle Transmission How Does It Compare? The recently-launched Pivot Trailcat LT has many similarities with the Kona Process 134, being MX- and 29" capable with a carbon frame and the same quantity of rear wheel travel. They deliver that travel through very different suspension layouts, but otherwise the two platforms share similar intentions. While the Pivot is considerably lighter (claimed weight of 13 kg in a medium, XTR), the $4,499 USD asking price for the Kona comes in well under the price of Pivot's most affordable Trailcat LT model. I won't do an in-depth analysis here, but know that you are getting a lot more for your money with the components on the Kona, as compared to the Trailcat LT. There are two sides to every coin, though; while the Pivot is sold with a lifetime warranty, Kona sell the Process 134 CRDL with a three-year warranty. The Process 134 is considerably more capable in my hands, up and down the hill, than the Pivot Shadowcat , a 27.5" wheel 140mm trail bike, despite that bike being equipped with a 160mm travel fork - versus the Kona's 140mm affair. On the flip-side, I recently rode a 120mm travel Santa Cruz Tallboy which, despite its XC bracketed travel numbers and 29" wheelset, takes the top spot in my mind as the most capable, playful bike of those mentioned here. The most relevant performance comparison comes with the Pivot Trailcat LT that I have in for review just now; it is the more capable of the two bikes, especially on steeper trickier trails and would therefore be my top choice if money were no object. Which Model is the Best Value? Of the four Kona Process 134 models, the most expensive $4,499 USD CR DL one that I tested will be the lightest build. However, dependent on exactly what you value, the one may not be the best option for you. One rung down the ladder, you can pay $3,199 USD for the 134 CR. It has the same carbon frame as the CR DL, but a subtly different build spec. It switches out the Rockshox Reverb for a TranzX RAD+ dropper (the more adjustable and reliable post, in my experience). Process 134 CR - $3,199 USD It also switches out the SRAM G2 RSC brakes for the G2 R brakes. They have the same 4-piston caliper and lever architecture, and thus the same capacity for stopping power, but they lack the bite point adjustment. Sure, it is a nice to have feature, and can help reduce intervals between bleeding. The other major differences lie in the suspension components and drivetrain. While the CRDL runs the previous generation upper-end suspension components from Rockshox, the CR model gets mid-range options from Fox. The CRDL has an electronic T-Type derailleur, while the CR model runs the SRAM GX mechanical option. The two bikes roll on the same Maxxis tires on the same WTB KOM Team i30 rims, but the CR model uses DT 370 hubs, as compared to the CRDL model's faster-engagement DT 350 hubs. If I was committed to one of the carbon models, I'd have to go for the more affordable CR model given it is a whopping $1,300 USD price difference. Process 134 DL - $2,399 Process 134 - $1,599 If carbon gives you the boke and you don't mind a weight penalty then you have two aluminum models to choose from, though both are equipped with more budget-oriented componentry. Generally, you have to look to more boutique brands if you want to find top-level componentry on an aluminum frame. Both models are going to be considerably heavier than the 14 kg CRDL model tested here, and the cable routing is completely external, but importantly they are sold with a lifetime warranty. It has to be said, $1,599 USD for a complete bike, sold with a lifetime warranty from a well-established brand is undeniably excellent value in 2025 - cheaper than some frame only options from other brands. You'll want to swap out its SRAM Level two-piston brakes almost immediately, but you'll have plenty of cash to spare for a quality upgrade. Technical Report SRAM G2 RSC Brakes: These brakes are reasonably powerful. With the 200mm Centerline rotors I was never left wanting, but my heavier colleagues think these should be thrown in the trash and replaced with the more powerful SRAM Code, or something with a similar amount of power. RockShox Reverb Dropper Seat Post: Early on the post developed undesirable sag. In its top-out position, its sags by around 20mm under my weight. Similarly, in its low position, it extends up by around 20mm, reducing clearance. This is a common issue with the mechanical Reverb, but is very quickly rectified with the Rockshox Vent Valve Tool that is supplied with the bike. Maxxis Dissector: On the few wetter rides, I felt a notable lack of bite from the Maxxis Dissector rear tire on soft climbs, with a similar notable lack of braking traction. Its tread pattern is relatively shallow and the centre tread blocks don't provide much modulation in that regard, tending to lock up with little encouragement. Kona Key Grip: The rubber compound is very firm and uncomfortable on long descents. I also disliked the raised bump at the end of the grip, as it prevents me from comfortably running my hands at the end of the bar. FSA Headset: All sizes of the Process 134 get this Orbit 1.5 E ZS headset with a 20mm top cap that raises the minimum stack height unnecessarily. Pros + Loves a flow trail + Quiet + More affordable than many of its direct competitors Cons - Seat tube likely to feel very slack for taller riders - On the heavier end of the spectrum for a carbon trail bike - Not the most proficient climber - Spec is a little outdated, likely owing to Kona's sale and inventory issues Pinkbike's Take The Kona Process 134 didn't blow me away in any department, but that's not to say I didn't have a good time riding it. Its slack seat tube angle and spec from a bygone era hold it back from greatness, and it left me wanting more predictability and comfort on rougher descents, but it was a fine companion for flattering, flowing singletrack. The value is great though, making this a solid option for those watching their pennies. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Photos: Pete Scullion Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Kona Kona Process 134 Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Kona Process 153 - Predictable, Rewarding, & Sharp
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: Kona Process 153 G3 WORDS: Henry Quinney PHOTOS: Tom Richards There aren't that many times that the entire Pinkbike comment section bands together in agreement. However, when Kona looked to be in real trouble as rumors of them going under swirled at Sea Otter in the spring, it felt like most people agreed that it would be sad to see them go. Uncertainty seems to have stalked the brand for a few years since they were sold to Kent Outdoors in 2022. Eventually, founders Dan Gerhard and Jake Heilbron bought back the brand two years later, signaling what it's hopeful is a real fresh start. As a brand, Kona has touched most things in gravity mountain biking and had some bikes that have been nearly synonymous with a movement, type of riding, or a certain philosophy. Process 153 Details • Carbon frame • 153mm travel / 160mm fork • Full 29" as stock • Head angle: 64.5° • 76.6 - 76.9º seat tube angle • Reach: 430 - 515 mm • Chainstay: 435 mm • Weight: 32.5 lb / 14.74 kg (size L, stock) • S - XL • Price: $5,499 USD • konaworld.com When you think Stinky, you think freeride. When you think Stab, you think floating brake arm; when you think of the Process 111, you think of one of the first genuinely fun short travel bikes... and the Process 153? You think of a bike with a short rear, balanced geometry, and a platform that offered a huge amount of versatility when it was introduced a decade ago. Folklore and business acumen to one side, what's up with the new 153, and more importantly, is it a fresh start or throwback? Kona Process 153 G3 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/jhcNcYQd Copied Live 00:00 15:19 15:19 Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take On its trail, it's predictable, rewarding, and sharp. It's a set of carving skis or a hot hatchback. Yes, it has its limits, and in some instances, when pushing hard, you can feel the front begin to wash or the rear hang up, but all in all, the Process has made good on its potential, and is a fun bike, even if not the out-and-out most capable. — Henry Quinney Four holes in the front allow for either-or cable routing. Frame Details The Process has an angular, chiseled look that I personally quite like. Some elements threaten to be almost brutalist, as flat surfaces combine with sharp edges. This is mainly apparent on the rear of the bike, where the swingarm looks overbuilt with a bridge sitting on the underside of the seat stay and the chainstay being asymmetric, with a twist as it nears the main pivot. There are some smart features, too. My personal favorite is the tubing. There is a y-junction, where two ports at the headtube lead to one exit port. This means that whichever way you like your routing, Kona has you covered. This is what a sensible, functional, and well-executed routing feature looks like, and I would have it over internally routed headsets any time, any place. The bike came as a full 29" but has a flip chip where the stay meets the rockers, which offers enough offset to go between wheel sizes. There are two bottle mounts for both a cage and tool, plus plenty of downtube protection for both trail-litter and tailgates. Geometry The geometry is largely the same as the alloy 2022 Process 153 DL that I reviewed a few years ago. There have been some small tweaks, though, and the alloy bike was absent of some of the features I've already mentioned, like the wheel-size flip chip or tool mount on the underside of the top tube. The large size that I have on test uses the same 480mm reach, 64.5-degree head angle, 435mm stays, and 625mm stack. In fact, apart from a few millimeters on the headtube, the only significant change is the 30mm reduction in length on the seat tube, which is very welcome. If it's not broken, don't fix it, right? Well, I quite enjoyed the geometry of that last bike; the issue was a little more related to the shock. The geometry of this bike is a true-trail bike, meant for going both up and down, without being so biased to steep climbs or descents that it undermines the balances on flatter sections or more gentle gradients. Suspension Design The bike uses a linkage-driven single pivot. The main pivot is intersected by the chainring in a bid to mute pedal feedback, all while giving something that is responsive and alive . This is something I often like about single pivots. They don't do everything well, but can be really rewarding to ride in certain instances, all while giving decent midstroke. Anti-squat starts at between 110 and 120% depending on gearing and drops to around 100% around sag. The bike is progressive in a smooth, linear fashion. Specifications Price $5499 Travel 153 Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate Trunnion Fork RockShox Lyrik Ultimate RC2 Charger 3 DebonAir+ w/ButterCups 160mm Tapered 110mm Spacing Headset FSA Orbit 1.5 E ZS Cassette SRAM GX Eagle Transmission 10-52 12spd Crankarms SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB PF92 Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Shifter Pods SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Handlebar Kona XC/BC 35 Stem Kona XC/BC 35 Grips Kona Key Grip Brakes SRAM G2 RSC Hubs DT Swiss 350 Spokes Stainless Black 14g Rim WTB KOM Trail i30 TCS Tires Maxxis Assegai / DHR2 Exo+ Seat WTB Volt Seatpost Rock Shox Reverb w/1x Remote Lever 31.6mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Kona Process 153 G3 Test Bike Setup The bike's spec was sensible in some regards and silly in others. I loved the improved shock, with far more damping available. I also enjoyed the WTB alloy rims on DT Swiss 350 hubs, the 200mm dropper, decent Maxxis tires, and, since the advent of DUB, I can even get on board with the press-fit bottom bracket. However, some pills weren't so easy to swallow. Henry Quinney Location: Squamish Height: 183cm / 6' Inseam: 82 cm / 32.5" Weight: 79 kg / 174 lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @henryquinney The Guide G2s do not belong on a bike with a 160mm fork and a 64.5-degree head angle. Hell, I wouldn't want them on my XC bike. I get it. SRAM is a big brand, and makes huge OE plays. That said, if the Guide family gets demoted to hardtails and commuter bikes, I wouldn't bat an eyelid. Less power, more hand fatigue... and that's not even mentioning the underpowered soft organic pads that seemingly did their best impression of damp tissue paper. Please, product managers, please... please stop. Testing Info This bike was a great companion for late summer rides in Squamish. After a heady summer scaring myself silly in the bike park on downhill bikes, it was nice to come back to Squamish and get back to enjoy more natural mountain biking on the amazing trails we have here. Typically, this bike would see single-blacks and fast, dark blue trails. Ditch Pig is perfect for a bike like this. That said, there are so many choices and diversions into unmarked trails coming off it; I wouldn't blame you if you considered Ditch Pig to be more of an area than a trail in itself. Climbing The Process' suspension feel is consistent and muted, both going up as well as down. It's a bike that pedals well yet doesn't feel particularly spritely. However, thanks to the adequate damping on the shock, you can run a fairly middle-of-the-road setup, which means the bike is comfortable and offers decent grip. That said, it's not something that gives a great deal of initial sensitivity. However, once at sag and beyond, it is relatively supple. The climbing position on the Kona is something some will love, and some will feel slightly alienated by it. The 76.7-degree is steep enough, but only just. Should you be somebody who runs a lot of exposed seatpost, you might begin to find it too slack. I'm not opposed to bikes like this, with a weight bias that isn't about winching to the top of the steepest fire roads but more about finding balance on undulating trails and not overloading your wrists on flatter terrain. I would say that the setup of the Process is 1-degree slacker than where I would want it for a bike like this, though. The seat tube angle is countered somewhat by the 625mm stack. However, it does feel long and unwieldy in some spots, with the seat tube and low stack combining to give some that are balanced if only stretched. On technical climbs, it would be nice if it had just a little bit more weight on the front wheel. While the cockpit is in reach, it makes that long position feel slightly longer as you try and claw back grip on the front. With the same angles as the previous model, I would say it largely reflects similar feelings that I had those years ago. However, I would say that my preferences and how I want a bike to be weighted have changed slightly, with me now preferring a bike with a slightly more forward weight bias on climbs. In the meantime, I've started running clips as opposed to flats, which means I run a higher effective saddle height. I wonder if this has just taken me over the edge of what I now deem a comfortable seat tube angle. Descending I still feel the same way about the geometry as I did in 2022, except now Kona has given a shock feel that actually lets you enjoy what the bike can do. In short, my liking for damped shocks has only grown, and it's a mark of how far the shock tune on the Process has come that, even with more compression than ever the Kona delivers. This bike rides far, far, far better with a shock that offers a decent level of compression damping. The short rear end, much like the seat tube angle in the climbing section, is offset by the lower front. This is a balanced bike that absolutely rips on tighter, medium-paced trails. That said, the short geometry can begin to feel a little overwhelmed when things get particularly rowdy, but everything has a blind spot. Some riders don't agree with my personal preference of longer rears and higher stacks. In some ways, this bike is for them. It's not the new frontier of geometry and ultimately isn't as capable as the fresh-faced new schoolers, but then again, what are you buying a trail bike for? This bike is a product of the old-school... but perhaps could be described as a better execution of a more limited concept. It's smooth through turns, and the oversized bridges on the stays give something that is firm underfoot and resists twisting forces well, all while being compliant enough. The single pivot gives less chain feedback than you might think, and for a single pivot is almost quite a neutral feeling. Through turns, the shorter wheelbase really rips and combines well with the taut feeling delivered by the single pivot. On its trail, it's predictable, rewarding, and sharp. It's a set of carving skis or a hot hatchback. Yes, it has its limits, and in some instances, when pushing hard, you can feel the front begin to wash, or the rear hang up, but all in all, the Process has made good on its potential, and is a fun bike, even if not the out-and-out most capable. Kona Process 153 CR/DL Giant Trance Advanced How Does It Compare? The Trance and the Process are two heavy hitters of the category, and 10 years ago, the argument was between the alloy Trance X or the original 153. Both are fun bikes, that will suit somebody who has a wide remit of trails and types of riding they want to do, even if they'll be most suited to single-blacks and challenging but not heinously technical terrain. The Trance seems to me to be the best of modern geometry, and it feels all that more capable for it. The Process just feels that bit sharper, for both good and bad. Through smooth turns or medium-speed tighter trails, I would prefer the Process. I would say the Trance is better rounded, though, and is ultimately more capable while being a better climber with a suspension system that offers more grip on both climbs and descents. Prices in USD. Which Model is the Best Value? In a world of extreme prices, the higher end bikes aren't bad value by any stretch. On the other end, the Process 153 range is offering some real value too, with the alloy bikes starting at $2,499. I do feel there is a slight gap, though, in that, I would love to see a higher spec on one of the alloy bikes. I don't really care whether the frame is carbon or not, and I remember the alloy bike I tested in 2022 having a comfortable and amply direct feel. With that in mind, I think the entry-level carbon bike would be the one I would go for. The Shimano build, which features a patchwork quilt of several different tiers, will be easy to live with, and the Fox suspension will do all you need it to and plenty more. I still think Shimano has a significant edge on SRAM when it comes to anything other than the highest-level electronic parts. Technical Report SRAM Makes Some Great Parts...: This bike has a very SRAM build. RockShox suspension and dropper work well, and even though I am something of a Luddite, I have to acknowledge that lots of people do enjoy running wireless drivetrains even if I don't. Everything worked well. The RockShox suspension was the star of the show. The Ultimate level Super Deluxe and Lyrik really are very, very good. And Some Truly Terrible Brakes: I get somewhat irked by people bagging on the Codes. They aren't perfect, but they serve their purpose. If, however, all that ire and frustration was aimed at the G2, I would consider it entirely justified. Underpowered, soft with a mushy feel, and outgunned by every other performance brake I can think of. I really, really, really, really dislike these brakes. Product Manager Kudos: Apart from a momentary suspension of reason with the choice of brake, the PMs for this build got everything else right at least for my tastes. I like the 200mm seatpost, the suspension and wheel, the tires, and the hub choices. If we take the brakes out of the equation, save for some very personal things such as bars and stems, this bike is ready to rip. Pros + Well balanced on tighter, medium gradient trails + Great suspension feel at low-medium speed + Well executed single pivot + Some neat frame features Cons - Geometry and suspension begins to feel overwhelmed at higher speeds - Slack seattube will punish taller riders - Underpowered brakes Pinkbike's Take The Kona Process 153 G3 isn't a bike from the future, but it's a bike built on the lessons from the past. If you've checked out of new school geometry and shake an angry fist in the face of long rear ends and bikes that are most rewarding when ridden hard, the Process is a welcome tonic. It's balanced, fun, and will shine on the things you want a long-travel trail bike to excel on, and it isn't distracted by trying to be an enduro bike that just so happens to have less travel. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Kona Kona Process Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: KS Lev Circuit Wireless Dropper Post
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: KS is the latest company to enter the wireless dropper fray with their $699 Lev Circuit. It's available with up to 200mm of drop, which is 30mm more travel than the longest Reverb AXS. Now, this isn't the first time we've seen a wireless dropper from KS – a prototype was on display at Eurobike all the way back in 2016 , but it wasn't until late last year that that a production version hit the market. There are 31.6 and 30.9mm versions of the Circuit, with travel set at 125, 150, 175, or 200mm. I've been testing the 31.6mm, 200mm version for the last three months, enough time to subject it to plenty of rain, mud, and generally messy conditions. Lev Circuit Dropper Details • Travel amounts: 125, 150, 175, 200mm (tested) • 30.9 or 31.6mm diameters • Adjustable air pressure to alter return speed • IP67 water resistance rating • MSRP: $699 USD • Weight: 739 grams / remote: 45 grams • More info: kssuspension.com DETAILS The internals of the Lev Circuit are almost identical to the non-electronic version, it's just that the orientation is flipped – the air valve that can be used to adjust the return speed is located at the bottom of the post, and a small motor at the top opens a valve to actuate the post when the remote is pushed. The Lev Circuit is powered by a 450mAh rechargeable battery that slides in just behind the seat clamp. On their website, KS touts the benefits of a rechargeable battery over a disposable one, saying that it allows riders to “contribute to a greener approach and help reduce their carbon footprint.” You know what's even greener than a rechargeable battery? Not using batteries at all... The main battery is said to provide up to 8 weeks of run time under normal use, and it can be recharged with the included charger in 4.5-hours. The wireless dropper remote uses a CR2032 battery, and has an LED indicator that changes from green to red when the battery needs to be replaced, typically after 8 months of use. A single bolt clamps the seat rails in place, and then the angle is adjusted via a 3mm Allen key at the front of the post. The battery slides into the back of the post's head and is locked into position with the latch at the bottom. INSTALLATION Getting the Circuit up and running is dead simple – insert the post and clamp it down, install the remote, and then pair the system by pushing the button on the top of the post and the smaller recessed button on the remote. Honestly, the only slightly challenging part was figuring out that a cap hides the saddle angle adjustment screw – I was used to the configuration of a Reverb AXS, so it took me a couple of extra minutes to get things sorted out. Installation is when all the measurements start to matter, and this is one area where the longer overall height of the Circuit could become an issue. Similar to the the TranzX post I reviewed in September, the Circuit's length below the collar is fairly short at 291mm for a 200mm post, but the motorized mechanism adds around 15mm to the overall height. That means if you had a Circuit and, say, a OneUp dropper, the Circuit would need to be inserted 15mm further into the frame than the OneUp in order to achieve the same saddle height. For riders on bikes with short seat tubes and plenty of insertion room that won't be an issue, but it's something to keep in mind if you're trying to fit a post with the maximum amount of drop. The return speed can be adjusted via the valve at the base of the post. KS Lev Circuit Wireless Dropper Post https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/BeiLlQVw Copied Live 00:00 00:17 00:17 PERFORMANCE The Circuits remote lever is large and easy to reach, and the response time is very quick. The amount of force required on the saddle is fairly light, and the post slides smoothly out of the way without much effort. It'd put it on par with the RockShox AXS Reberb post as far as how much (or little) effort it takes to get the seat out of the way, and ahead of the TranzX EDP01 – the post didn't operate as smoothly as the Circuit. When it's time to raise the seat for a climb, holding down the the remote returns it to full extension. This is where the AXS Reverb has the edge – that post extends much more quickly than the Circuit. I inflated the Circuit to 180 psi, the max recommended, and even then I'd call the return speed adequate but not amazing. On rides with more rolling terrain where the seat is constantly being adjusted the difference between the Circuit and the AXS Reverb is noticeable. The same goes when comparing it to a Fox Transfer – the Circuit is a little less eager to fully extend. Of course, not every one wants a super-speedy post, and I wouldn't call the Circuit slow, it's just not the fastest out there. I didn't have the time (or patience) to test out KS' claim that the Lev circuit will get 12,000 actuations out of a single charge, but I can say that the run time is more than adequate. I never had the post die out on a ride, and I'd usually charge it every 2-3 weeks depending on how much I'd been riding, or whenever the thought crossed my mind. One final note on performance is that if you have the post compressed and go to lift up a bike by the saddle it'll extend slightly, a trait that I've only experienced on KS posts. I know, you're technically not supposed to pull on a seat with the dropper lowered, but it happens, and WTB even has a saddle with a built-in handle. It's a fairly minor detail, but one that's worth a mention since I haven't experienced it on any other posts in recent memory. It's not pretty, but it works surprisingly well. This is the fix I came up with after accidentally breaking the remote lever. DURABILITY / SERVICE Part way through testing I accidentally broke the thumb paddle off the remote. I'd laid my bike on the ground and was stepping away from it when my shoe snagged on the plastic lever and ripped it off. That's 100% user error, but it did illustrate that the lever can break fairly easily. The quick fix I came up with involved putting a ferrule over the actuator button, and covering it with mastic tape. It's not pretty, but it actually works very well, and I'm not really sure why KS went through the effort of creating a thumb lever – a simpler push button design would have been easier to execute, and would probably be more crash resistant too. Opening up the post to give it a refresh is as easy as removing the nut at the bottom of the post and unthreading the collar to slide the stanchion out of the lower. There was still plenty of grease when I pulled the post apart after 3 months, but it was clear some mud and grit had made it way past the wiper seal. A quick cleaning and a fresh coating of grease took less than five minutes, and the entire procedure is a sub-15-minute affair even when working at a leisurely pace. PRICE / WEIGHT At $699 the Circuit sits between the $861 Reverb AXS and the $499 TranzX EDP 01. Of course, there's no 200mm Reverb AXS, so riders looking for that length will be choosing between the TranzX and the KS. The KS does lower more easily than the EDP01, and the battery lasts longer, but $200 is a pretty substantial price difference. When it comes to weight, the 739 gram Circuit is just 28 grams lighter than the TranzX, so I'd call it a draw. Even without the weight of cable and housing, adding a battery and a motor to a post creates a heavier product - a 210mm OneUp post weighs 592 grams, and even after adding in 60 grams for cable and housing you're still looking at an 87 gram weight difference. Pros + Smooth & light action when dropping seat + Long battery life + Easy installation Cons - Middle of the road return speed - Remote is a little flimsy - Expensive Pinkbike's Take For me, wireless dropper posts still fall into the 'neat, but not necessary' category. There's no shortage of cable-actuated options that are reliable, affordable, and don't require remembering to charge a battery, and that's the direction I'd point most riders. That said, for those who want a post that's easy to install (or swap from bike to bike), and creates a clutter-free cockpit, the Lev Circuit is worth a look. I wouldn't mind a slightly faster return speed and a more robust remote, but the long battery life and the fact that there's a version with 200mm of drop does make it a good option. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Seatposts Ks Ks Lev Circuit Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Kuat Huk Tailgate Pad
author: Matt Beer
article_text: When it comes to transporting mountain bikes the tailgate pad is one of the simplest methods, provided you have a truck. Kuat’s Huk improves that experience by speeding up the installation process and polishing the aesthetics while hosting a flap for backup cameras. On tap for sizing are five Huk models. The mid-size heights come in 51 and 54” pads that carry five bikes, plus there’s also a stubby 25”-width Huk that holds just two. For full-size truck owners, a 61” width model has a six-bike capacity with two tailgate profile options, straight or curved. The Huk product page includes a fit guide to select the right pad for your vehicle if you're unsure which best suits your whip. Huk Details • Bike capacity: 2 or 5 for mid-size trucks, 6 for full-size • Sizes: 25, 51, 54, 61, 61" curved • TPU outer/ fleece inside material • Fold up camera window • 10 downtube strap loop positions • Price: $298 USD • kuat.com The two-bike carrier costs $149 USD, but that price doubles to $298 USD for full tailgate coverage. Each Huk tailgate pad comes with straps to secure the bikes’ downtubes and a three-year warranty. INSTALLATION Installing the Kuat Huk is a breeze thanks to hooked straps that secure to the outside of the tailgate. This method may be more costly to produce but is incredibly sensible - no more fumbling fingers to feed the straps up through the tailgate gap or covering your clothes in mud while holding the tailgate pad in place. I started with the straps laced near the end of their length on the inside facing buckle, placing the pad on the open tailgate, and lining up the folds. Next, lifting and closing the tailgate lets gravity do the work, pulling the metal hooks into the gap at the end of the bed. After fishing out the straps and placing the hooks in the eyelets, center the pad and cinch it up. The creases and edges of the 54" Huk were likely modeled around a 4th generation Toyota Tacoma because they contour almost perfectly to this tailgate. I haven't tried this on all mid-size pickup trucks, but the Huk could be stretched a few centimeters on either side for this vehicle. BIKE RETENTION The Kuat Huk retains bikes moderately well, but although no shuttle scaring occurred there could be a few improvements. The strap system is fairly basic. On this 54" Huk pad, there are 10 loops to feed the downtube security strap through. Placing the strap through two loops spaces the bikes evenly but shortens the strap length, something to consider for e-bikes or unconventional frame shapes. That means you'll need to offset the strap to one side to make the most of the length, which could clash with the rest of the fleet's spacing. When not in use, it's critical that the straps are closed or they'll go by the wind. No extras are provided. The loops that the straps feed through could also be placed closer to the top edge of the tailgate to maximize the reach, but the plastic buckles never lost tension. DURABILITY One of the primary concerns when using a tailgate pad is potential damage to your truck's paint. The Huk ensures there's no rubbing on the inside of the pad thanks to the soft fleece material. On the outside, there are fewer edges on the Huk which is where tailgate pads typically wear first. The TPU material has stood up to a summer of intense UV rays, showing no signs of wear or holes. While the foam underneath the inside edge of the tailgate does a decent job at padding the frame, thicker foam would provide extra protection against rough road impacts. When wrestling camp gear and multiple bikes into the bed, I'd often lower the tailgate, place the bikes on and then close it up. This led to lifting the velcro flap frequently which prematurely wore out the clingy material. I never rolled up the camera cover but that made me question how long the velcro would last in this area if you frequently used this feature. Lined up against the Race Face T2 tailgate pad, the Kuat Huk offers a sleeker appearance and discrete logo. If you're into colorful styles though, you're stuck with plain black as the only option for the Huk. COMPARISON The standout feature of the Kuat Huk is the reverse straps and alloy hooks, making installation significantly faster and cleaner. It does falls slightly short when it comes to downtube strap placement and access compared to the Race Face. The straps can be a bit challenging when multiple bikes are loaded, especially compared to the convenient velcro loops on the Race Face tailgate pad. At the moment, the $298 USD Huk is three times the price of the Race Face T2 (marked at $99, down from $209). If you set it and forget the tailgate pad for the season, then it's tough to justify the extra cost of the Huk. The downtube straps of the Race Face T2 Tailgate pad lie closer to the top edge of the truck bed for easier access. The camera flap folds out of the way more elegantly than the velcro punch-out style of the Race Face T2. Pros + Fast install that doesn’t leave you covered in dirt + Snug fit and low profile Cons - Downtube straps and placement could use refinement - Plenty of cheaper options exist with a similar function Pinkbike's Take The main takeaway from the Kuat Huk tailgate pad is the simplified installation process with its additional hooks on the outside, however, that does come at a high price. While it has a few minor quirks, the functionality and aesthetics deliver a reliable solution for hauling bikes on your truck. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles.
