In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Modifying the TRP DH-R Evo Brakes with Aftermarket Levers
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, brakes, freedom-coast, oak-components, trp, trp-dh-r-evo
article_text: Aftermarket Levers https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/HoOFh2zY Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 05:20 Brake choice is a rather personal matter, and while there are objective measures that play into the decision, much of it comes down to little details and subtle differences. Ergonomics are a big part of this calculus, and are generally the hardest part of a system to tune - you're typically stuck with whatever the initial designer laid out, and left to work around those constraints. That element is changing though, as we enter another era of aftermarket brake parts to tune your ride. I've been a big fan of the TRP DH-R Evo brakes for a while now. They offer excellent power that feels easy to control thanks to a light lever action, and they're impressively consistent over long descents and in foul weather. I don't have too many gripes with the stock levers and ergonomics, but a common complaint revolves around the limited reach adjustment offered by TRP's design. Folks with smaller hands and those who prefer a close bite point often comment on the relatively far distance from grip to lever, even at the extreme of the adjustment range. As a response to this, more and more aftermarket levers are popping up to offer different ergonomics to the stock offering. Freedom Coast and Oak Components have both come to market with hooked designs that bring the bite point closer to the bar, while maintaining the same adjustment and leverage as the stock brakes. I've been riding both of those as well as the stock levers to get a sense of which best complements these excellent brakes. Oak TR Lever Details • 10-35mm adjustment range • Compatible with TRP DHR, Slate Evo, Slate T4, Trail Evo, and Quadiem master cylinders • Four color options • Manufactured in Bavaria • 20g per lever blade • $119 USD per set • oakcomponents.de The Oak levers have a square and sturdy body with a tapered and rounded hook at the finger, with dimpling machined into the catch for increased grip. They've been extensively machined to remove excess material, in an attempt to reduce weight and visual presence while remaining stiff under hard braking. All cut edges have been chamfered for looks and comfort, and there are 4 finish options available to match your brakes. Their adjustment range sits 10 to 35mm away from the grip edge. Maximum reach. Minimum reach. Freedom Coast Lever Details • 7-32mm adjustment range • Compatible with all TRP EVO models • Ten color options • Manufactured in California • 24g per lever blade • $119 USD per set • freedomcoast.net Freedom Coast's levers feature a very aggressively hooked shape, topographical machining marks for grip, and a wild array of color options. They too have some material reduction for weight savings, but not quite to the level of the Oaks. The blade is much flatter and wider, making for a larger feel and form factor in use. The outer edges have been rounded, and the blade tapers outwards towards the finger contact, where it is at its widest. Their adjustment range sits 7 to 32mm away from the grip edge. Maximum reach. Minimum reach. TRP Stock Lever Details • 21-46mm adjustment range • Compatible with the brakes it comes on • One color option • Manufactured in Taiwan • 19g per lever blade • $Free / embedded cost • trpcycling.com TRP's stock levers have a long flat section with a hooked end, with cut holes at the tip for grip. There is also dimpling the length of the blade, which might come in handy should you want to want to brake with more than one finger for some reason, or find your self choking up on the lever in some circumstances. They're rounded, smooth, and have a nice pebbly texture to them. Their adjustment range sits 21 to 46mm away from the grip edge. Maximum reach. Minimum reach. Conclusion If you don't currently have any issues with the fit and function of your TRP brakes, then I wouldn't spring to pay for either of the aftermarket options here. Both are quite expensive, and though their manufacturing quality and bling factor may warrant the price for some, they don't offer quite the same performance upgrade as something like a new set of tires or a fork service. Barring reach issues, I never found the stock levers disappointing when swapping back from the aftermarket options. That said, if you find that the stock TRP brakes don't work for your hand size, or simply feel wrong per how you tend to set up brakes, then both the Oak and Freedom Coast levers offer some benefits to optimize these already excellent brakes. For me, the choice comes down to two differentiating factors: ergonomics and reach. For me, the Oaks win out on ergonomics, with a much more comfortable blade and finer attention to detail throughout. The rounded grip portion makes a difference when you're reefing on the levers, and feels like an upgrade over the stock option. For those that want the absolute minimum reach on their brakes (think bite point in the meat of the grip), then the Freedom Coast levers do get a little bit closer at minimum reach. That isn't my metric for success, but some folks love that to-the-bar brake feel and will appreciate the extra few millimeters. It's worth noting that the lever blade is quite wide and flat, so those that like a rounded feel might want to look elsewhere. My ultimate choice for the TRP DH-R Evos on my personal bike are going to be the Oak TR levers, thanks to their excellent ergonomics and stiff feel. The hooked end mates up perfectly with my preferred bite point (±30mm from the grip), and the finish quality and shaping keep them feeling comfortable and precise. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Brakes Freedom Coast Oak Components Trp Trp Dh R Evo Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Motion Instruments System 2 Data Acquisition Kit
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: Data acquisition is becoming more commonplace, and you'd be hard-pressed to find a serious racer who hasn't experimented with it. For your more casual rider, though, there have been barriers to overcome before benefiting from using it. Motion Instruments has tried to make acquisition more accessible to more riders by making a cheaper, user-friendly system that's easier to live with. System 2 Details • Waterproof tracers • Bluetooth Operation • Electronic position sensing fork tracer • Rear sensor uses link rotation to plot axle position • $499 USD • motioninstruments.com The Sensors System 2, which uses two independent tracers that run on rechargeable batteries and are read via a Bluetooth connection to your mobile phone, put the emphasis on ease of use and simplicity. The fork tracer is a very tidy bit of kit and does without the membrane roller system that tends to attract dirt or wear. Instead, it uses electronic position sensing with no wearing of electromechanical components. Simply put, a flat, plastic rod passes through a slot attached to the lower. This has advantages not only in longevity but also in terms of alignment and cleaning. Both tracers are 100% waterproof, which means not only you can ride in the slop but also wash your bike care-free afterwards. The top of the tracer mounts using a custom air valve cover to clamp down on the sensor on to the top of the fork. Should you be using dual-crown forks, there is another clamping style. While the front sensor is a reimagined version of the rods that you're familiar with, the rear sensor is something else altogether. It's a clever device that manages to avoid some of the pitfalls of normal systems. That said, it is also compromised in certain aspects. The rear tracer can be fitted with several different sizes of Allen key fitments to secure to the centre of your frame's main pivot. You then clamp down the wing of the sensor on the rocker itself, set the position at 0% travel, and again at 100%. Once the system knows these two extreme positions, it can work out where you are in the stroke. There are limitations, however. Firstly, this won't work on bikes where the main rocker is concealed behind another piece of the frame, or at least it will work less effectively. For best accuracy, the link with the most rotation will be best. Secondly, because you set the end position, it's limited by the stiff elastomers that you can find in some shocks. However, every system has drawbacks, and not worrying about alignment makes for a very quick and easy install. Motion Instruments also say that this solution is stop gap, and they hope to one day have a bank of bikes with all the leverage curves saved, meaning you can just fit the part, select the bike and you're good to go. System 2 also does without external cables or a central box. Again, this makes installation far easier. The tracers can be used by themselves, but for a system that puts so much emphasis on balance, it might undermine it to an extent. The Software The MotionIQ app gives riders plenty to go on. Used via a mobile app on iPhone, iPad, Mac or Android, it shows you all you could need to know regarding position, balance, axle speed. Whenever I use data acquisition, how much learning I have left to do always strikes me. Maybe that feeling never goes away. That said, I think the data-led approach will work better for people who already have a decent understanding of different types of graphs and technical language. While the app does a good job of breaking down the information for the more casual user, I think it's best to go into using it, anticipating that it will be a new skill to learn and not something that confirms things you already know. System 2 also uses the GPS function of your phone. That means you can drop in as soon as the sensors are connected. The recorded data is then overlayed on a map. This is particularly useful if only to remember which run is which. The software is free when purchasing the kit and can be upgraded to the Pro version for an additional subscription of $99 per year. The Setup The app focuses on getting a balanced bike and is far less prescriptive than the BYB Telemetry system . The setup that you'll get to isn't so much about what's fastest but rather what balances front and rear. This isn't bad, but it feels like it's missing a certain piece. Data acquisition is inherently limited because it only goes off the data that it's been fed. What is balanced on one track might be out of balance for another. With that in mind, I think it would be helpful if that app gave some guidance regarding axle speed and how to interpret it. The idea seems to be much less rigid regarding setup and takes a more pragmatic approach. Not everyone will like a certain attribute, but everyone can benefit from a better-balanced bike, even if they then bias it in a certain direction. I often rode with the system recording data, but using it to reflect upon my setup after I'd already established how I felt about it. Almost always, when I thought the bike felt best was when it was most balanced according to the data. That said, I am very happy to not have the deepest events equal, especially on the front. Often, when using data to setup my bike, I don't pursue using full fork travel as I would rather have something spare for extreme hits. It can only read the data it's fed, after all, and I try not making a habit out of bottoming my fork out on every single run. That said, the app does a very respectable job of giving users a very solid interface that allows them to cut sections out of the track for more accurate readings and interpret the data in a variety of graphs. It explains, expressed as a plus or minus, whether the shock is compressing faster or slower than the fork. From there, you can tune it to be as near to equal as possible. When you have a clear idea of what you want, this is easy because you can read what is closest to the speeds you want in the data. For beginners, though, it would be good to get some ratings or suggestions to guide them a little. What's it Like Compared to Other Systems? The system takes literally minutes to put on, and is so easy to live with. You don't have to worry about crashing on it, or having to bend it back when you do. I loved how robust and clutter-free it was. In some ways, this feels like a cross between the all-out tech of BYB's system and the user-friendly and hardy nature of ShockWiz. I feel like it will bring data acquisition into the realm of more riders, which is a good thing. That said, for ultimate accuracy and guidance I think that BYB's system is going to cater to the people wanting to extract the most out of their bike. There were two occasions that the Motion Instruments linkage sensor felt loose in its Allen-key fitting and was giving a dead zone to the travel. After reclamping it, it worked well, but all in all, it just felt less precise. BYB uses data collected from various riders to give you prescriptive instructions on what to do. While this isn't the end goal, it does help guide users to a solid base setting. From there, they can familiarise themselves with the data and inputs to add their own flair to the suggestions. Pros + Waterproof + Ease of installation + Software is fast and easy to connect + Focusing on balance rather than suggesting a particular setup Cons - Elastomer bumpers shocks limit rear tracer - Focusing on balance rather than suggesting a particular setup - Data takes time to learn how to interpret, and what works for you Pinkbike's Take Motion Instruments have done a very good job with System 2. While it isn't perhaps as high-performance as BYB's system, it also does without much complication thanks to its wireless functions and lack of data rods on the rear shock. That said, it does mean that it can never be quite as accurate, on the rear at least. If I had to throw one on a bike for an hour, it would be the System 2 that I would use. If I were to spend a day getting increasingly granular, it would probably be the BYB. System 2 is waterproof, easy and vastly cheaper than most other systems. Bike nerds, rejoice. If you want to tinker with your suspension, there's probably never been a better time than now. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Accessories Motion Instruments Motion Insturments System 2 Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: The New Orbea Rallon RS Almost Isn't An eMTB. Is That What Riders Want?
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, orbea, orbea-rallon-rs
article_text: Rallon RS https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/LznjXj7w Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 15:56 “You might not like it at first.” That’s not the way a bike is typically introduced at a press camp, but the new Orbea Rallon RS isn’t your typical e-bike. In fact, it’s barely an e-bike - its TQ HPR40 motor and 290 Wh battery provide a subtle amount of assistance, as opposed to acting like an electric shuttle truck. While the fastest, most powerful e-bikes on the market essentially drag riders to the top of a hill, the Rallon RS feels like someone is lightly pushing on your back. And I mean lightly - Orbea’s description of it feeling like a tailwind isn’t far off. The Rallon RS is more than just a lightly powered eMTB, though; Orbea are using it as a way to showcase a level of electronic integration that hasn’t really been present in the marketplace until now. Yes, “integration” is one of those words that runs the risk of feeling like it was lifted from a mid-level manager's LinkedIn post, but it’s accurate in this case - the dropper post, shock, derailleur, and motor are all powered by the same battery. Rallon RS Details • Travel: 170mm, 180mm fork • Mixed wheels • Carbon frame • 64.25° head angle • Chainstay length: 445mm • TQ HPR40 motor, 290 Wh battery • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Price: $10,199 - $14,999 USD • Weight: 40.1 lb / 18.1 kg (Large) • orbea.com On top of that, they communicate with each other - the shock can be set to open up when the dropper post is lowered, and the level of motor assistance can be reduced at the same time. Before diving into all the extra-techy details, let’s take a moment to go over the basics. The Rallon RS is essentially a motorized Rallon, which means it has 170mm of rear travel, a 180mm fork, and can run either a 29” or 27.5” rear wheel by swapping out the upper shock link. There are only two models of the Rallon RS, and they’re both expensive. The Rallon RS Team models is 10,199 USD, and the Rallon RS LTD is a whopping $14,999 - that price difference is mainly due to the cost of the Fox Neo electronic suspension components. Total weight for my size large review bike was a svelte (for an e-bike) 40.1 lb / 18.1 kg. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The 40 pound weight is noticeably easier to maneuver than the heavier 50+ pound full-power e-bikes out there - jumping feels very natural, and steep corners are handled with ease. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The small form factor of the TQHPR 40 motor and the 290 Wh battery size make it hard to tell that the Rallon RS is an e-bike - it’s really only the flap covering the charging port on the top of the downtube and glow of the LED on the handlebar remote that let curious onlookers know there’s something else going on here. The HPR40 motor weighs just 1170 grams, and offers 200 watts of peak assistance and 40 Nm of torque. There are two modes, Range and Rally, plus an additional mode called Ultra that delivers an even more subtle level of assistance. Orbea says that riders can expect to get 1200 - 1800 meters of vert out of a single charge, or around 2-3 hours of ride time (I was able to get even more than that during my testing). If the battery does run out of juice it’s not that bad pedaling the RS without assistance - there are non-motorized enduro bikes on the market that are about the same weight. There's no screen to stare at - lights on the remote indicate the battery perccentage and motor mode. The small size of the HPR40 motor allowed Orbea to keep the suspension layout almost identical to that of the non-motorized Rallon. To help with the integration that I mentioned earlier, Orbea developed a gateway, a small box that all of the elements plug into so that they can communicate with each other using the CAN bus protocol. It also leaves the door open for adding on more electronic accessories in the future - for example, lights that are also powered by the battery could be an option. The Smart Controller remote on the left side of the handlebar allows riders to choose their suspension mode and motor mode without pulling out their phone, or looking at any screen at all. Just below those two buttons is a small lever that activates the electronic dropper posts. The Smart Controller and dropper are hardwired to the battery, which reducea the number of extra batteries to keep track of charging. The short, uninterrupted seat tube makes if possible to run the longest dropper posts currently available - the size small and medium frames can fit a 210mm post, and the L and XL a 240mm. All models use Orbea’s own electronic dropper, the MC10-RS; the post has an optoelectronic Time-of-Flight sensor which relays the position of the post to the suspension. Depending on how the bike is set up, fully lowering the dropper can be used as the signal to fully open the suspension. Geometry The Rallon RS’ geometry is almost identical to that of the non-motorized Rallon EN. The head angle sits at 64.25-degrees, the seat tube angle is a steep 79.25-degrees, and the chainstays measure 445mm for all sizes. The lower shock mount has a flip chip that’s used to change the bottom bracket height by 7mm, and the head angle by .5-degrees. There’s also an angle adjusting headset that can be used to alter the head angle by +/- .75-degrees. There aren’t really any outliers on the geometry chart - Orbea did their homework, and the result is a bike that gets it right when it comes to putting the rider in a good position for steep climbs and descents. Suspension Design Thanks to the motor and the electronic shock, the Rallon RS doesn’t need to have excessive levels of anti-squat in order to make it an efficient climber, although the numbers aren’t as low as what you’ll find on the DH bike. The anti-squat percentage sits around 89% at sag, and drops as the bike goes through its travel. Anti-rise begins around 67% and drops slightly deeper in the travel, but overall remains fairly consistent, free of any large changes that would be noticeable while riding. Specifications Price $14999 Travel 170mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Live Valve Neo Factory Fork Fox 38 Float Factory 180, Grip X2 Cassette Shimano CS-M9200 10-51t Crankarms e*thirteen Helix Race alloy 32T Bottom Bracket TQ-HPR40 / 290 Wh battery Rear Derailleur Shimano XTR M9260 Chain Shimano CN-9100 Shifter Pods Shimano XTR M9250 Handlebar OC Mountain Control MC10 Carbon Stem OC Mountain Control MC11 Alu SL, 0º Brakes Shimano XTR 9200 Hydraulic Disc Wheelset Oquo Mountain Control MC32LTD Tires Maxxis Highroller III / DHR II Seat Fizik Terra Ridon X1 145mm Seatpost OC Mountain Control MC10, 31.6mm, Electronic Dropper Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Rallon RS Test Bike Setup Other than swapping out the Fizik grips for ones that felt better in my hands (Peaty’s Monarch grips in this case), I tested the bike in its stock configuration. The Maxxis Highroller III / DHR II tire combo worked well in Spain and at home, a good choice for late season rides that have a mix of trail conditions. Test Locations I was able to test the Rallon just down the road from Orbea's headquarters in the Basque Country, as well as at home in Bellingham, Washington. The trails in the Navarra region reminded me a little of the East Coast of the US - the birch trees put on a show with their golden fall colors, and the trails were a fun mix of fast and swoopy with some steeper, techier sections to keep things interesting. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 165 lb / 74.8 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Download the Trailforks App Eremua mountain biking trails Climbing As Matt Beer mentioned in his review of the regular Rallon, the climbing position of the Rallon RS is very upright thanks to the tall stack and steep seat tube angle. For riders coming from a bike with more relaxed geometry it may feel a little strange at first, but it makes a lot of sense for steep climbs, and even on flatter approaches the tall front end helps prevent it from feeling like there’s too much pressure on your hands. As far as how much assistance the motor provides, that’s where the Rallon takes a different turn compared to most e-bikes. Orbea calls it a “rider dominant” design, as opposed to “motor dominant,” which describes most eMTBs on the market. In other words, it doesn’t feel like the motor is doing all that much. I’d equate each motor mode as the rough equivalent of shaving 4 pounds of weight off the bike. So in Ultra mode it feels like a 36 pound bike, in Range it becomes a 32 pound bike, and in Rally it's a 28 pound bike. Compared to a full or even mid-powered eMTB there’s never really the sensation that you’re getting towed up the hill - no matter the mode, you’ll still be doing at least some work. The upside is that the 290 Wh battery lasts a really long time, since it’s not being called upon to support massive wattage or torque outputs. On one ride I tallied up close to 6,000 vertical feet of descending over the course of 30 miles without fully draining the battery. The HPR40 motor uses a single clutch design, which makes it possible to mount a chainring directly to the cranks, unlike most mid- and full-power motor designs, where the chainrings are mounted separately. That makes it handy for lubing the chain, since rotating the cranks backwards springs the chainring backwards the same way it would on a non-motorized bike, but there is a noticeable downside to the design. On rolling, more technical terrain, the type where you might need to take the occasional half pedal stroke, there’s a slight delay between when the rider stops pedaling and when the motor stops applying force to the cranks. This results in a strange sensation at the pedals - it’s almost like skid-stopping on a fixed gear bike, where you can still feel the force of the chain pushing against your feet. It doesn’t happen all the time, and it’s more noticeable in the Rally motor mode, but when it does it’s very disconcerting. If you’re riding winch and plummet style trails that go straight up and then straight down it won’t be all that apparent, but on rolling, technical terrain I’d call it a substantial drawback. It’s detrimental enough that I made sure I’d turned the motor to the ‘off’ position before descending to avoid feeling what I can only describe as the nastiest pedal kickback imaginable. The kickback sensation aside, Fox's Neo electronic suspension system does a great job of making the Rallon RS into a very efficient climber - it firms up the shock automatically, providing a good platform to push against, and keeping the bike from sagging too deep into its travel. The motor output may not be extreme, but it does provide enough assistance to tame steeper, more technical climbs, although, once again, it doesn’t really hold a candle to a full power bike - you won’t be scaling vertical faces, or seeking out strange, seemingly impossible ascents with this level of output. Descending This is hands down one of the best descending mountain bikes I’ve been on recently. The tall stack and the weight centered around the bottom bracket give it an incredibly confident presence on the trail - it’s very, very good. That shouldn’t come as a total surprise, though, since its non-motorized counterpart snagged the 2025 Bike of the Year award. There are some positioning similarities to the Raaw Madonna, a bike whose descending performance I’ve had high praise for in the past. The tall front end plays an important part in the equation, along with enough bottom bracket drop and moderately long chainstays. The motor weight and battery weight are low in the frame, and the overall system weight feels ideal for high speed, rough downhill descents. The upright descending position can create some really entertaining moments - changing directions is less about turning the handlebars and more about tipping the bike slightly. I’ve used the analogy before, but there were moments on the Rallon RS where it felt like I was carving on skis; this bike can rail corners with the best of them. The 40 pound weight is noticeably easier to maneuver than the heavier 50+ pound full-power e-bikes out there - jumping feels very natural, and steep corners are handled with ease. The suspension feels more supportive than the Rallon DH bike, something that Matt mentioned in his review of the Rallon enduro bike as well. There’s a predictable platform to push off of, and the 170mm of rear travel is very well managed - it’s accessible when necessary, but there isn’t any wallowing or diving sensation in the initial portion of the travel. We've seen enduro bikes drop in travel slightly over the last couple of seasons, but I'm a fan of the Rallon's 180mm fork and 170mm of travel. It helps boost the confidence levels when blasting into a big mess of rocks, and while those higher speeds are where it shines, on maneuvers where you need to slow to a crawl and get lined up to thread the needle down a slimy rock roll it never felt like a handful. On top of the actual handling characteristics, the Rallon RS is a very quiet bike - there's no cable rattle, and no motor clanking. There's a little chainslap from the XTR wireless derailler, but otherwise it's a nearly silent ride. How Does It Compare? At the moment, there really aren’t that many bikes in this category, at least not with this much travel. The Specialized Kenevo comes to mind, but even that motor received a bump up to 50 Nm of torque a couple of years ago. I’d say that the Trek Slash+ comes the closest when it comes to overall descending performance, but you’ll be able to get back up to the top much faster on it thanks to the TQ HPR60 motor system. Both bikes do an excellent job of smoothing out the rough stuff, but I'd give the point to the Slash+ and its high pivot design when it comes to just how well it erases obstacles. The flip side is that the Slash+ feels a little more glued to the ground; the Orbea is slightly peppier when it comes to popping off those bonus side hits. Who Is the Rallon RS For? This is a tough one. I’m sure there are a few riders out there who think this is the perfect bike for them, and it very well may be, but there are a few things to keep in mind. • If you think you’ll be able to keep up with riders on full powered e-bikes, you won’t. There’s simply no way to easily bridge the gap between the max output of the TQ HPR40 motor and something like a full-power Bosch or Avinox motor. The on-trail difference between 40 Nm and a motor with 100 or 120 Nm of torque is huge, and you’ll quickly be left in the dust. • This could be a good way to make up for differing fitness levels if the fitter riders are on non-motorized bikes. I can see a parent or a coach using this bike to keep up with a hyperactive group of groms. Both riders will still get a workout, but the one on the e-bike won’t be quite as shelled at the end of a big ride. • It does make steep climbs easier, just not by a massive amount. The difference the motor makes is similar to what you’d experience by running XC tires instead of DH casing tires - it’s noticeable, but not earth-shattering. During the test period I did the first half of a ride with the motor off, riding with friend on non-motorized bikes. After those initial laps, which totaled 2,300 vert of climbing, I turned the motor on and headed out on my own to repeat what we’d just done. The thing is, that second lap didn’t feel all that different. Yes, it was easier, but not that much easier. That’s the part of this bike that’s tricky to wrap my brain around; the motor assistance is subtle enough that I didn’t miss it all that much when it wasn’t on. Technical Report XTR brakes: Compared to Mavens, the XTR brakes don’t offer the same amount of power, or the level of adjustment. I’d call them adequate, but not exceptional for the Rallon’s capabilities. The Galfer rotors help bump up the stopping power a little, but for a downhill-oriented, 40 pound bike I’d go with Mavens. Fox Neo Float X2: I’m still not really sold on electronic suspension, but I will say that the Neo system does work quite well. It’s nearly invisible - the bike firms up when climbing, and opens up while descending without any odd delays. It’s also very tunable, and diving into the options on the app makes it possible for riders to really customize the setup. Pros + Top notch descending capabilities + Impressive amount of tuning potential thanks to connected motor, dropper, and shock. + Excellent range considering battery size Cons - Very low amount of assistance from motor - Pedal feedback due to HPR40 motor design on rolling terrain - Expensive, especially considering how similar it feels to the non-motorized version. Pinkbike's Take The Rallon RS is going to be a polarizing bike, and I’d put myself in the camp of riders who think it sort of misses the mark. There are aspects of it that are highly commendable - running all of the electronics off of one battery is something I hope catches on, and the dropper post / suspension interaction is a feature that’s been on my wishlist for over a decade. The fact that this is an e-bike that doesn’t really feel much like an e-bike is what I haven’t been able to fully come to terms with. For the record, I’m a big fan of TQ’s HPR60 motor - Yeti’s MTe, or the Trek Slash+ are bikes that nail it when it comes to delivering the ride experience I’d expect from a lightweight e-bike. Those bikes are easier to handle than their full-powered counterparts, and they have enough “oomph” that it feels like the motor is actually doing something. At the end of the day, I wish that Orbea had gone with the HPR 60 on the Rallon RS. If weight was a primary concern they could have stuck with the 290 Wh battery and offered the option to run TQ’s 160 Wh range extender. As it is, the HPR40 puts this bike in a pretty odd middle ground. Sure, it’s only 5 pounds heavier than the non-motorized Rallon, but that sentiment goes the other way too - it’s 5 pounds heavier and thousands of dollars more expensive without really bringing that many benefits to the table. As a proof of concept, I’m very interested to see where the ideas that are showing up on the Rallon RS go in the future, but the underpowered motor makes it a bit of a non-starter for me, no pun intended. More than anything, my time on the Rallon RS left me scheming ways to get my hands on the regular, non-motorized version, since this is one of the best descending enduro bikes I’ve been on in recent memory. — Mike Kazimer Action photos: Roo Fowler Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Orbea Orbea Rallon Rs Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: The 2026 Norco Revolver 130 is Ready for Modern XC Riding
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: No, the new Norco Revolver doesn’t have 32” wheels - we’ll have to see what the next version looks like a few years down the line. What it does have is fully updated geometry, a lightweight carbon frame, and two different versions to choose from - the Revolver 130 or the Revolver 120. The two options share the same frame, but, not surprisingly, the 130 comes with a 130mm fork and has 120mm of travel, while the 120 has a 120mm fork and 115mm travel. The 120 is aimed more at riding between the race tape, while the Revolver 130 is aimed at a broader swathe of riding conditions and styles. That’s why you’ll find a Maxxis Forekaster tire up front, a Fox 34 SL fork, and comfortable wheels that aren’t the absolute lightest, parts that help make it more surefooted on technical climbs (and descents) compared to its even shorter travel sibling. There’s also no lockout on this model, although the frame does have that as a possibility. Norco Revolver 130 Details • Wheel size: 29" • Travel: 120mm, 130mm fork • Carbon frame • 66-degree head angle • Chainstay length: 430mm (S3) • Sizes: 1,2,3,4,5 • Weight: 25.8 lb / 11.7 kg • Price: $7,899 USD • norco.com As tested, the Revolver C1 retails for $7,899 USD, and weighed in at 25.8 lb / 11.7 kg for a size S3. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Revolver eats up those back-to-back turns, snapping from the exit of one to the entrance of another with a satisfying quickness. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details Norco absolutely nailed it when it comes to the overall look of the Revolver’s carbon frame. The lines are sharp and clean, free of any strange humps or lumps. It’s one of those bikes that looks fast standing still, which is exactly what you want from an XC speed machine. Claimed weight for an S3 frame and a RockShox SIDLuxe shock is 1900 grams, which is right in the mix for a modern XC bike. For comparison, the Specialized Epic 8 has a claimed weight of 1795 grams for the S-Works version, and 1965 for the standard layup. It’s a similar story with the Orbea Oiz - the OMX frame is 1740 grams, and the OMR is 1940 grams. There’s room for two water bottle inside the front triangle, although there isn’t any in-frame storage to be seen. That’s not too surprising, since it’s not found on any of Norco’s other models, and it’s also not as common in the XC world. The Specialized Epic has it, but most companies have decided to save a few grams by skipping the addition of gummy bear terrarium. Other frame details include two ISCG tabs for running a little chain device if desired, a threaded bottom bracket, and titanium suspension hardware. Geometry There are five sizes of the Revolver, with reach numbers ranging from 422mm all the way up to 522mm. The head angle sits at 66.5 degrees with a 120mm fork, and 66-degrees with a 130mm fork; that’s around .5-degrees steeper than a Specialized Epic with the same fork length, and the same head tube angle as a Yeti ASR. Chainstay lengths change by 4mm per size in order to help preserve the balance of the bike, at least partially, starting at 422mm and going to 438mm. The seat angle sits at 75-degrees, which is .5-degrees slacker than the Revolver 120 due to the additional fork length. Suspension Design Flexstay suspension layouts have become the norm in the XC world - it’s a good way to save some weight, and there are still ways to tune the design to give a bike different ride characteristics. In Norco’s case, they preload the seatstays slightly, which makes it easier to get into the first 30% of suspension travel, since the shock isn’t fighting against as much friction / resistance. Additionally, Norco says that the leverage curve is designed to work best with the smaller volume air can of an XC shock, with enough ramp up later in the stroke to keep it from using all of its travel too often. Specifications Price $7899 Travel 120mm Rear Shock Fox Factory Float SL, 190x45mm Fork Fox 34SL Factory Float, Grip X, 130mm Headset FSA No 42/48ACB Cassette Shimano Deore XT CS-M8200-12, 10-51T, 12sp Crankarms Shimano Deore XT FC-M8200, 32T Rear Derailleur Shimano Deore XT Di2, 12-speed Chain Shimano Deore XT CN-M8100 Shifter Pods Shimano Deore XT Di2 Handlebar Race Face ERA35, 760mm (S1,S2,S3) / 780 (S4,S5), 20mm rise Stem Alloy CNC, 50mm Length, 35mm Clamp Grips WTB Wavelength Brakes Shimano Deore XT BL-M8200 Wheelset Zipp 1Zero Hitop S Tires Maxxis Forekaster / Rekon, EXO, 29 X 2.4" Seat Fizik Terra Ridon MG Rail Seatpost TranzX YSI36, 31.6mm, 120mm (S1, S2) -150mm (S3) -170mm (S4) - 200mm (S5) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Revolver Test Bike Setup We’ve said it dozens of times over the years, but it continues to be true - Norco’s Ride Aligned suspension setup app is one of the best around. Plug in your weight and height and it’ll spit out numbers that more often than not are right on the money. I ran a 40mm rise bar for a portion of the test period, and that’s what I’d run if this was my personal bike due to the more upright riding position. The 50mm stem and 760mm, 20mm rise bars are totally appropriate, and I put in a bunch of miles with the bike in its fully stock configuration, I just happen to prefer a little less stretched out riding position. I also ran a 210mm Fox Transfer dropper post for a good chunk of the test period. In this case too, the 170mm post that comes stock on the S3 isn’t out of the ordinary, and for racing I’d stick with the shorter post. For more all-around trail riding, and getting into some not-quite-XC terrain, the extra drop of the 210mm post was a benefit. The nice thing about the frame design is that there’s plenty of insertion room thanks to the uninterrupted seat tube. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Norco Revolver Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/RLV5Oi1j Copied Live 00:00 06:38 06:38 Testing Info Testing began in Whistler, BC , and continued in Bellingham, Washington , with conditions ranging from dry and dusty all the way to slightly frozen and a lot sloppy as winter set in. Climbing Modern XC bikes are converging on very similar frame shapes and suspension layouts, which isn’t exactly surprising - there are only so many ways to accomplish the goal of making a very light frame that also holds a couple water bottles. Despite all the visual similarities, there are distinct differences in the way the rear suspension doles out its travel. On one side of the spectrum you have bikes like the Orbea Oiz, which is very firm initially, even in the fully open setting, prioritizing snappy acceleration over just about everything else. On the other end you have a bike like the Yeti ASR, which leans heavily on its lockout lever for climbing support, and then delivers tons of traction when the shock is fully open. The Revolver falls in the middle of those two possibilities, with what I’d call the Goldilocks position of being ‘just right’ for most instances. That meant I could leave the Float SL shock in the middle of the three compression settings and not give it a second thought for the entire ride. That setting gives the bike plenty of support for out-of-the-saddle sprinting, while still remaining comfortable for bigger hits and choppy sections of trail. At 5’11” I went with the size S3 Revolver, which has numbers that are pretty close to a size large from most brands. The 468mm reach, 66-degree head angle, and 75-degree seat tube angle are all pretty similar to those of the Specialized Chisel I’ve put a ton of miles in over the past two seasons, so choosing the S3 rather than the S4 made the most sense, and I know the 493mm reach of the S4 and the longer top tube would feel too stretched out for me. I wouldn’t have minded if the seat tube angle was just a touch steeper (the 130mm fork slackens it even further compared to the Revolver 120), but with the stock 50mm stem and the seat slid forward a bit on the rails I didn’t feel overly stretched out. The other geometry request would be for slightly longer chainstays. It’s great to see size-specific chainstays (something Norco has been doing longer than most), but there were a few instances when the front end felt a little light during steep, seated climbs, and I had to either stand up or make a conscious effort to really get my weight forward. The S3 Revolver’s 430mm chainstays are on the shorter side of things; for comparison, the Yeti ASR has 439mm chainstays, and the Cannondale Scalpel has 442mm chainstays. Now, I should stress that these are fairly small geometry gripes - at the end of the day the Norco is a good, quick climber, and it’s in line with many modern XC bikes. Descending My first ride on the Revolver 130 took place on some not-exactly-XC trails in Whistler , which was a great introduction to its capabilities - the brakes are strong enough and the suspension is dialed enough that it can hold its own on spicier sections of trail without making you question your bike (or trail) choice. Keep in mind, it’s still very much an XC(ish) bike - sticking a 130mm fork on a race machine doesn’t magically transform it into an enduro bike built for the steepest trails around. That said, the Revolver’s 120mm of travel is very well managed. Like I mentioned in the climbing section, I kept it in the middle compression setting for most rides, since that gave it enough support for generating speed on flatter sections of trail without feeling harsh. On the instances that I did flip it to the open setting the difference was noticeable, although it’s not a night and day change - it’s just a little softer off the top, and a little easier to initiate travel. The short chainstays do make navigating tight corners a very enjoyable experience - the Revolver eats up those back-to-back turns, snapping from the exit of one to the entrance of another with a satisfying quickness. At higher speeds and on longer, swoopier corners there were a few occasions when the Revolver’s back end felt a little chattery - the stiffer frame and short rear center means that it doesn’t carve through corners the same way as the Yeti ASR, for example. The sweet spot feels slightly harder to find; by that I mean that the Revolver isn’t quite as forgiving if you get your weight a little too far forward or back. Longer chainstays aren’t the answer to all the world’s problems the way some riders seem to believe, but in this case I do think a slightly longer back end would help on the climbs and the descents without taking away any overall speed. Specialized Chisel Yeti ASR How Does It Compare? I get to ride a huge variety of bikes over the course of a year (that’s definitely the best part of my job), but I try to always have a short travel option on hand. They’re great palate cleansers, a way to pedal away the sins of riding a full-power e-bike, or to feel like you’re getting places way, way faster than you would on a 40-pound enduro sled. Compared to my personal Specialized Chisel, the Revolver is a couple pounds lighter thanks to its carbon frame, and it has a slightly snappier feel under power. It also has 10mm more rear travel, travel that’s easier to access than the Chisel’s, which makes it a bit more forgiving in rougher terrain. The geometry numbers are pretty similar between the two, but I prefer the Chisel’s higher stack and slightly longer chainstays. How about compared to the Yeti ASR? Realistically, it's the Revolver 120 that would be the more direct comparison, since it too has 115mm of rear travel and a 120mm fork, but I was really impressed with how usable the ASR’s 115mm of travel was. The ASR is a very comfortable bike in the fully open setting, although it is a bike that benefits from the use of a lockout in order to gain climbing efficiency. Ready for some more made-up categories? I’d call the ASR a comfortable-country bike, while the Revolver has a stiffer, more robust feel that pushes it more towards the light trail bike side of things. And finally, the Transition Spur. That bike has had an excellent run - it’s crazy that it’s been over 5 years since its debut, and it still holds its own - for many riders it’s what they think of when the term “downcountry” gets mentioned. It has a longer reach, slacker head angle, and steeper seat tube angle than the Revolver, and while none of the differences are drastic, they do add up to give each bike its own flavor. The Revolver has a racier feel - it’s a little quicker out of the gate, and its frame is around a pound lighter than the Spur’s, while also being noticeably stiffer. For the rider that has more than a couple XC races on their calendar, the Revolver would be the better choice, but the Spur is still a very fun, very capable short travel XC/DC machine. Which Model is the Best Value? Norco has done a good job with the parts spec of the Revolver, and even $3,999 C3 model doesn’t make too many concessions, with a Shimano Deore cable-actuated drivetrain, Deore two-piston brakes, a RockShox Reba fork with a Motion Control damper, and a SIDLuxe Select+ shock. The one downside with that model is the weight - it's 30 pounds, which is a whole 3 pounds heavier than the C2 model. That $5,399 C2 model is the one I’d gravitate towards if I was shopping with my own money. It has a Shimano XT Di2 wireless derailleur and shifter paired with a Deore crankset and cassette, Deore 4-piston brakes, and a Fox 34SL with a Grip damper. The claimed weight is a reasonable 27 pounds, a number that would be easy to reduce by upgrading to a lighter cassette, cranks, or wheels when the time comes. Out of the box, though, there’s really nothing to change right away; I’d take a good look at this one if you’re trying to find the best blend of price vs performance. Fox 34 SL fork. Zipp 3Zero HiTop S carbon wheels. Technical Report Zipp HiTop S wheels: At 1535 grams, Zipp’s HiTop S carbon wheels certainly aren’t the lightest option out there; this would be an easy area to save a couple hundred grams, at least if you’re willing to cough up the cash required to do so. On the trail, the HiTop S wheels fall on the softer side of the spectrum by design, although they feel much more “normal” compared to the extra-compliant 3Zero Moto trail / enduro wheels. Other than the occasional twang from the spokes when the wheels were really loaded up the Moto S wheels faded nicely into the background - they stayed true, and I didn’t have any flats while running them. Frame Rattle: Thankfully the Revolver doesn’t have thru-headset cable routing, something that’s still all-too common on XC bikes, but there’s still room for improvement. The dropper and brake lines run through hard plastic sleeves, and those sleeves can rattle against the inside of the down tube. I was able to quiet down the racket by taking the fork off and shoving a big piece of foam just behind the head tube junction, but I’d rather not spend my time pulling apart a fancy new bike while hunting for silence. The more typical foam tubing seems like it would have been a simpler solution, or maybe an updated version of the down tube ports that the older Optic had, where zip ties could be used to snug the housing down against the inside of the downtube. Shimano XT Di2: I really like the speed of Shimano’s new Di2 drivetrains, along with the shifter ergonomics and the excellent battery life. The chainslap is really the only downside - on bigger compressions the cage returns to its starting position with a loud “thwack.” It was a little less pronounced on this bike, perhaps due to the shorter travel, but it’s quite noticeable in certain situations. Fox 34 SL: The 34 SL worked well for the entire test period, free of any strange squelches, creaks, or squeaks. I didn’t need to do much adjusting to it either, thanks to Norco’s Ride Aligned settings - those were pretty much identical to my final setup. I could see some riders contemplating going with a 36 SL, which is a great option, although that 265 gram weight penalty is something to keep in mind. It’d make sense if you were trying to make the Revolver more of a short travel trail machine, but stick with the 34 if you don’t want to deviate too far from the XC realm. The 34 SL is still stiff enough for the vast majority of trails where a bike like this makes sense. Pros + Quick and responsive handling + Very well balanced suspension feel + Parts spec helps it shine on more technical trails without losing its XC nature. Cons - Dropper and brake housing can rattle inside frame - Chainstays are pretty short, even with size specific geometry Pinkbike's Take The Revolver ticks almost all the boxes when it comes to how a modern cross-country bike should perform. Norco did a great job with the overall suspension feel, creating a bike with a very good blend of efficiency and grip. There’s some room for improvement with the in-frame housing design, and I wouldn’t say no to slightly longer chainstays, but those are fairly small gripes - on the whole, the Revolver 130 hits the mark, a cross-country bike that can still hold its own on more technical trails. — Mike Kazimer Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Norco Norco Revolver Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Norco's New Sight VLT TQ - High-Pivot Meets Mid-Power
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, trail-bikes, norco, norco-sight
article_text: The new Norco Sight VLT TQ brings the total number of models bearing the Sight name up to three. On one side of the lineup you have the non-motorized Sight , the all-mountain machine that came out in 2024, and on the other side there’s the Sight VLT CX , the full-powered eMTB that uses Bosch’s Performance CX motor. The new VLT TQ splits the difference, putting out 60 Nm of torque and a maximum of 350 watts of assistance thanks to TQ’s HPR60 motor. The Sight rolls on mixed wheels, and has 150mm of rear travel paired with a 160mm fork. There are three complete models, with prices starting at $6,599 for the C3, and going up to $10,599 USD for the C1 reviewed here. Build kit highlights of the C1 include a 160mm Fox Factory 36 fork, Crankbrothers Synthesis carbon wheels, Shimano's wireless XT Di2 drivetrain, and 4-piston XT brakes. Norco Sight TQ VLT Details • Travel: 150mm, 160mm fork • Mixed wheels • Carbon frame • 64° head angle • Chainstay length: 435mm (445mm at sag), S3 • TQ HPR60 motor, 580 Wh battery • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 • Price: $10,599 USD • Weight: 44 lb / 20 kg (S3) • norco.com The smaller motor and 580 Wh battery help keep the Sight's weight down to a fairly reasonable number - my size S3 test bike weighed in at 44 lb / 20 kg. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Frame Details The VLT TQ (every time I write those initials it feels like I’m just typing random letters) has a similar but slimmer frame profile compared to the full-power VLT. All three TQ models have full carbon frames, and they’re all equipped with mixed wheels. 150mm of rear travel is delivered by Norco’s VPShp design (hooray, more initials), which stands for Virtual Pivot Suspension high pivot. There’s a small display situated in the top tube, and a two-button remote to switch between motor modes on the left side of the handle bar. The charging port is located partway down the top of the downtube, which helps keep it from getting absolutely blasted by mud and grit - I prefer e-bikes with charging ports in this general area, rather than down low in the splash zone by the bottom bracket. There’s room for a water bottle inside the front triangle, and there are two bolts on the underside of the top tube for storing a tube or tool. As for the motor itself, the TQ HPR60 puts out - you guessed it - 60 Nm of torque, and up to 350 watts of peak power. For comparison, the full-powered Sight VLT with Bosch's CX motor has 100 Nm of torque, and 750 W max power. It's essentially the difference between jogging up a hill versus sprinting up it as fast as possible. There are three main motor modes that can be customized in the TQ app. The Sight ships with custom modes that Norco selected, which means there's room to make the bike even peppier by opening the app and bumping up the output. I was happy with the stock settings, but just know that it's possible to give the bike a little more juice by making some adjustments in the app. Of course, that'll also reduce the range, which is why I kept things close to stock. Geometry The Sight TQ’s geometry numbers are identical to those of the Sight VLT CX - Norco call it all-mountain geometry, and I’d say that’s accurate. Key numbers include a 64-degree head tube angle, a 77-degree seat tube angle, and a 436mm chainstay length for a size S3. That chainstay length may appear to be on the shorter side, but don’t forget that it increases as the bike goes through its travel - at sag it measures 445mm. Norco was one of the first companies with different rear center lengths for different sized bikes, and that's still the case here. It's not really a massive difference, but it is more than zero - there's an 8mm difference in rear center length between the S1 and the S4. Reach numbers range from 422mm for the S1 up to 522 for the S5. Suspension Design The Sight TQ VLT uses the same high pivot Horst link layout found on the non-motorized Sight and on the more powerful Bosch-equipped Sight VLT. This design has the idler pulley located on the chainstay, separate from the main pivot shaft, which allowed Norco’s engineers to fine-tune the amount of anti-squat. The design also requires paying a licensing fee to I-Track, who hold the patent for the design. For riders looking for a little more squish, running a 230 x 65mm shock instead of the stock 230 x 60 will increase the travel to 162mm. The bike is spec’d with a 160mm fork, but running a 170mm fork is totally acceptable. Specifications Price $10599 Travel 150mm Rear Shock Fox Factory Float X, 230x60mm Fork Fox 36 Factory Grip X2, 160mm Cassette Shimano Deore XT CS-M8200-12, 10-51T, 12sp Crankarms e*thirteen Helix Race Alloy Bottom Bracket TQ HPR60 / 580 Wh battery Rear Derailleur Shimano Deore XT RD-M8250, SGS, 12sp Chain Shimano Deore XT CN-M8100 Shifter Pods Shimano Deore XT Di2 Handlebar Race Face Era carbon Stem Norco Alloy, 40mm Length, 35mm Clamp Grips WTB Wavelength Brakes Shimano XT M8220 Wheelset Crank Bros. Synthesis Enduro Tires Continental Kryptotal-F, Enduro, Soft, 29" x 2.4" / Xynotal, Enduro, Soft, 27.5" x 2.4", Seat WTB Rocket Fusion Seatpost TranzX YSI08 RAD+ Travel Adjust Dropper Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Sight VLT TQ Test Bike Setup Norco's Ride Aligned online guide serves as a good place to start when it comes to suspension settings - just plug in your height, weight, and select your riding position and it'll spit out fork, shock, and even tire pressures. Norco was still fine-tuning the outputs when I received the bike, but by the time you're reading this everything should be dialed in and ready to go. I've spent a ton of time on the latest version of the Fox 36 fork, so it was a quick process to get it set up to my liking. My settings were as follows: 83 psi, 6 HSR, 8 LSR, 6 HSC, 10 LSC. I never totally got along with the Float X shock, as you'll read later on in this review. By the end of the test period I was running it with 205 psi, which equated to 25% sag, and the low-speed compression dial fully closed. I also added the a slightly larger volume reducer to increase the bottom out resistance. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 165 lb / 74.8 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer As mentioned, testing took place during some seriously wet conditions, and I ended up running a Maxxis High Roller III / DHR II tire combo for a good portion of the test - the stock Continental tires are fine for most conditions, but aren't the stickiest when it's really wet and slimy - they both use Continental’s Soft rubber comping with the enduro casing, rather than the SuperSoft compound. I also ran a dual Magic Mary Radial combo, but the more compliant casing combined with the softer suspension feel made things feel too vague for my liking. Test Locations My first lap on the Sight TQ took place on Burke Mountain, located just up the road from Norco's BC headquarters. After that, I put the remaining miles in on the trails surrounding Bellingham, Washington. Conditions tended to be on the wetter side of things, but January did deliver a solid two weeks of perfect riding conditions. Riding the Norco Sight VLT TQ https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/lgyxoGnI Copied Live 00:00 07:10 07:10 Climbing There’s a meme floating around poking fun at the inclusion of a climbing section in eMTB reviews, and it’s not totally wrong - after all, we are talking about a bike with a motor. Still, not all e-bikes are equal, and in the case of the TQ-powered Sight it makes its way uphill in a more moderate manner compared to the full power rocketships on the market. I really enjoy the way that the HPR60 delivers its power - it’s very natural feeling, and almost completely silent. The amount of assistance is enough to make most climbs much easier, but it’s not so dramatic that you’ll be spit off the trail if you don’t pay attention. It strikes a nice balance, where it still clearly feels like you’re on an eMTB due to the average speed, but the handling and overall ride sensation is closer to that of a regular mountain bike. I mainly rode with the motor in the full power mode, only dropping down to the two lower power settings when I was trying to maximize my range. As far as range goes (no pun intended), 3,500 vertical feet of descending and 20 miles of distance felt like the sweet spot - that usually equated to about two hours of ride time, and I’d end up back home with 10% or less battery remaining. Below 10% battery the amount of assistance is reduced, a feature I found myself annoyed with more than once - I’d rather be allowed to completely drain the battery as fast as I wanted rather than need to finish a ride at a reduced pace. I did find that the battery life didn’t quite match that of the Yeti MTe that I reviewed earlier this year. I wasn’t able to standardize for all variables, but on several rides with a fellow tester with a similar weight to myself on the MTe they were finishing with more battery remaining, and we both stayed in the same mode for the entire ride. Descending These days it seems like most full-powered eMTBs weigh around 47-50 pounds, and the mid-powered options end up hovering around 42 - 45 pounds. That’s a broad generalization, of course, and depending on the battery size and parts spec those numbers can change by a significant amount. That said, the handling difference between a 50-pound bike and a 44-pound bike is very noticeable - the handling of the Sight TQ is much closer to that of a non-motorized bike in the way that it feels while cornering or maneuvering through tighter sections of trail. You’ll still feel the extra heft when bunnyhopping at slower speeds, but other than that it’s a very intuitive bike to ride, one that doesn’t require extra time in the gym to handle. The geometry gives the bike a very neutral feel - it’s not super slack, or super long, at least in the S3 size that I was on; I’d likely be singing a slightly different tune if I was trying to pilot the S4 and its 497mm reach. The rearward axle path from the high pivot design isn’t extreme, and I didn't notice any odd handlinq quirks from it while cornering or landing off drops and jumps, moments where the wheelbase of the bike extends. I did try the bike in a longer travel configuration, removing the 5mm travel reducer from the shock and swapping the fork for a 170mm Fox 38. After trying both setups, I ended up preferring the stock configuration - the bike is a little more nimble, and the bottom bracket’s a little lower, two traits that keep it engaging in mixed terrain. Even in that longer travel configuration the Sight TQ doesn’t really feel like an enduro smasher - its handling is more on the all-mountain side of things; it’s a bike for exploring, for riding a wider range of terrain, as opposed to something that shines on the steepest, nastiest trails around. Other than a couple gripes about the XT brakes lacking power compared to SRAM’s Mavens, and the not-that-sticky front tire, it was the rear shock that left me slightly frustrated. Despite plenty of experimentation, I wasn’t able to get the Float X to feel exactly the way I wanted. In some ways, its behavior reminded me of the 2020 Sight - that bike tended to move through its travel quickly, and the same thing was happening with this one. Even at 25% sag and with the low-speed compression dialed all the way in I still found myself wanting more support - the bike is very eager to go through its travel, and while the off-the-top sensitivity is commendable, the lack of anything to push against in the mid-stroke was frustrating at times. That lack of support makes the bike ride deeper in its travel, and it’s more active over small to medium hits than it needs to be. Norco went with a CS40 tune, and I’d say they could have gone firmer, or spec’d a Float X2 for a wider range of compression adjustment. How Does It Compare? The Yeti MTe is a good point of comparison for the Sight. Yeti’s comparable build kit is more expensive than the Sight due to the carbon wheel spec, but even without those it’s still pricier, so for value-oriented riders the Sight gets the point. As far as ride characteristics go, the point goes to the MTe - even though it has 5mm less travel than the Sight it doesn’t feel like it, and it does a better job of managing its travel. It’s calmer while climbing without sacrificing traction, and on the descents it feels like it’s always in the sweet spot of the stroke. The on-trail differences between the two was a good illustration that just because two bikes share the same suspension components doesn't mean they'll behave the same way. On the MTe, I never worried about using all the travel, and it remained supportive and responsive even on chewed up sections of trail. With the Sight TQ, that lighter compression tune didn't give much to push against, causing the bike to feel less responsive than the MTe. The geometry numbers between the Norco and the Yeti aren't worlds apart, but I do prefer the slightly longer reach of the MTe - the 480mm reach with 449mm chainstays felt nicely balanced to me. Sight VLT TQ C2 / $8,999 USD Sight VLT TQ C3 / $6,999 USD Which Model is the Best Value? Out of the three bikes in the Sight VLT TQ lineup, it's the C2 model that stands out. It has a nearly identical build kit to the C1, but the aluminum wheels instead of carbon and the Fox Performance Elite suspension instead of Factory lowers the price by $1,500. Yes, nine grand is still a boatload of money - I'm not about to try and justify e-bike prices - but the C2 does have a solid built kit for the price, one that doesn't need any immediate upgrades (other than maybe a stickier front tire). The Kryptotal / Xynotal tire combo works for most conditions, but a softer compound front tire would have been appreciated for those wet winter rides. Shimano's XT Di2 derailleur was a quieter operator with the Sight's high-pivot, elevated chainstay design. Technical Report Shimano XT Di2 drivetrain: I’ve complained a fair bit about the chainslap on the new XT and XTR Di2 derailleurs, but thankfully that wasn’t an issue on the Sight TQ. The elevated chainstay and idler pulley helped keep things nice and quiet, and I don’t have any noise-related gripes about this bike. Shimano XT brakes: I ended up swapping out the rear 180mm rotor for a 200, but there were still several times when I found myself wishing for more braking power. I’ve been spoiled by all the e-bikes that have been coming with SRAM’s Maven brakes over the last year or so - the Mavens have much more power, and feel more appropriate for heavy or even slightly heavy e-bikes. Dropper post: At 5'11" I’m right in between frame sizes, at least according to Norco's chart, and going with the S3 meant that the bike arrived with a 170mm dropper post. A 200 or 210mm post that could have its travel reduced would have been nice to see; as it is, riders that are in a similar boat as myself may need to budget for a longer dropper. Thankfully the uninterrupted and fairly short seat tube means the frame isn’t limiting the options. Pros + Motor is nearly silent + Easy handling on a wide variety of terrain + Lower weight improves maneuverability Cons - Goes through travel very easily - a firmer shock tune would help - Doesn't really stand out in any one specific area Pinkbike's Take The mid-power eMTB category is an interesting one. For a time it seemed like it was going to go extinct, erased by bikes that were powerful and light, but we really haven’t seen that happen yet. For now, TQ’s HPR60 motor is one of the best options in this category, allowing brands to create bikes that are nearly silent and a decent amount lighter than their full-powered compatriots. The Sight VLT TQ doesn’t really break any new ground, but it does slot nicely into Norco’s lineup, where it’s a good option for riders looking for a true all-mountain e-bike that’s versatile and easy to handle; just keep in mind that the stock shock tune may not be ideal for some riders. — Mike Kazimer Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Norco Norco Sight Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Northwave Overland Plus Clipless Shoes
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Back in the 90s, Northwave’s red and yellow Integral XC shoes were unmistakable. Today, the colors are a little more subdued in the clipless Overland Plus model, but functionality and performance are still high on the list of objectives list for this all-around trail shoe. The Overland Plus looks to hit the right marks of a solid trail or enduro shoe. There’s a Vibram sole, breathable yet tough outer material, and a stiff sole capped with an EVA footbed. Other highlights include Northwave’s patented SLW3 dial-closure system and a Velcro strap to balance out the tension. Northwave Overland Plus Details • Vibram Fort Augustus rubber sole • EVA midsole • SLW3 lacing dial • Velcro strap • Colours: black and green • Sizes: EU 37-48 • Weight: (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $175.99 USD • northwave.com For such a feature packed shoe, you’d expect the Overland Plus to have a hefty price tag, but at $175.99 USD, it lands below most other brands premium models. They’re not as svelte as their modern XC shoes, the Rebel 3 , but they come in at 894g, a respectable number when compared against similar style shoes. Fit Northwave shoes seem to run a bit longer than normal. I typically go for a size 42, but in this case the fit is on the longer side, and unfortunately the Overland Plus comes in full sizes only. Those span from 37 through to 48. The width aligns with most other performance clipless shoes. Although the side panels of the shoe are flexible and the toe box is roomy, they may fit a slender foot best. A high arch also meant wincing through a ride or two before the shoe molded to my foot. Between the Velcro strap at the top and the SLW3 dial around the toes and midfoot, balancing the tension was convenient. A long and cushioned tongue provides ample overlap under the Velcro strap, which meant no pinching at the forefoot. Performance What would make the ideal clipless trail shoe? It would likely have a slim profile that is well vented and be stiff under foot. Offering enough adjustment to suit a variety of foot shapes and pedal cleat positions would be expected too. In the case of the Overland Plus, it nearly checks all of those boxes. Up top, the lightweight material is both breathable and abrasion resistant while the dual closure system holds the foot in place while moving the bike around. It’s flexible enough, but not too squirmy. I really dig this style of closure system; a strap at the top and lace dial in the middle/bottom. The Velcro strap locks your foot in without cutting off circulation, like some other shoes are notorious for. There’s generous padding in the tongue without feeling squishy and vague. Across the lower foot, the SLW3 dial takes in the slack and doesn’t create any pressure points. The SLW3 dial can be loosened, or tightened by a single click, which makes easing the tension a snap during longer rides. Underneath, I have mixed feelings about the sole of the Overland Plus. The stiff shank is preferable for power transfer, however, it lands with a clunky feel while hiking in rocky or rooty terrain. The smooth Vibram rubber doesn’t offer much purchase in soft organic dirt especially in the wet. The cleat box also had a forward bias. For my cleat position preference, I couldn’t move the cleats rearward enough. I felt as if I was standing on my toes while descending, leading to fatigue through the foot and shins (tibialis anterior muscle). Pricing and Comparison Another high-performance clipless shoe that isn’t too far from the style of the Overland Plus is the Fox Union Boa. The latter is slightly more uniform in materials but has an equally stiff sole, comparable venting, and uses a dial closure. Next to the lacing systems and materials, the largest difference is the size of the cleat box. The cleat position can be moved much further back on the Union shoe, even behind the ball of the foot. At $175.99 USD, the Overland Plus is considerably cheaper than the Union Boa but no less durable. If something were to go wrong with either dial system, you’d be more likely to find a replacement for the commonly used Boa at a shop, depending what region of the world you’re in. With that said, online ordering from either brand has never made it easier to make repairs. One small inconvenience of the SLW3 system from Northwave compared to a Boa is that you have to hold the dial’s latch in the open position to unwind the wire and open the shoe. Conversely, the SLW3 can be loosened one position at a time, whereas the Boa used on the Union can only open completely. Both systems can be closed one click at a time. I prefer to pedal uphill, but I’m not adverse to a hike-a-bike. In this case, neither shoe is amazing for walking uphill due to their stiff soles. The Overland Plus locks you foot in for taking on steeper grades whereas the Union Boa can produce some heel lift and chafing. In terms of grip though, the Union’s moderately deep lugs give better traction across all conditions. Durability I’ve been using the Overland Plus on everything from downhill to gravel bikes. Given their on-the-bike benefits, they’ve been treated to more pedal-focused rides and less adventures traipsing through the forest. They’ve been fully submerged on shorter winter rips and haven't been suffocating on longer rides through progressively warmer spring temps. All of the stitching, seams and bonded areas are holding up well. I did manage to snag my toe while climbing and nicked the rubber but that hasn't caused the material to lift any further. So far, the breathable materials on the upper sections haven’t been compromised by any wayward sticks or rocks poking into the trail. All of the stitching and seams are holding strong, plus the Velcro is still grabbing tightly. The SLW3 dial has been trouble free and thanks to its position out of harm’s way, it never saw any impacts either. Pros + Dual closure system allows for fine tuning fit + Dial can be opened one notch at a time for on the move adjustments Cons - Cleat positioning is limited to a forward bias - Lacks grip for hiking Pinkbike's Take Northwave’s Overland Plus is so close to being the ideal mid-weight trail shoe. The dual closure system is one of the best I've tried; a Velcro strap and SLW3 dial can hone in on a personalized fit that locks in your foot without cutting off circulation. Underneath though, it's a different story. Gravity riders will likely wish the cleats could be positioned further back. The stiff sole could benefit from a deeper tread and take the shoe one step further in soft conditions. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Northwave Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: One Year on the Liv Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1
author: Sarah Moore
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, pbwmn, enduro-bikes, liv, liv-intrigue-lt
article_text: REVIEW Liv Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 WORDS: Sarah Moore PHOTOS: Tom Richards Liv introduced the Intrigue LT early in 2023, first in an aluminum version and then in the 'Advanced' carbon model that I've been riding for the past year. This is an entirely different Intrigue to the one I tested back in 2020, with the 'LT' designation calling out the 150mm of rear travel compared to the shorter travel version's 125mm of rear travel. Earlier this spring, Liv added the Intrigue X to the line, which slots into the range between the 125mm Intrigue 29 and the 150mm Intrigue LT as a 140mm travel affair. The Intrigue LT features an integrated downtube storage compartment, has adjustable geometry thanks to the 3-position flip chip, and comes in four sizes. The extra-small and small come in a mixed-wheel configuration, while the medium and large bikes come with 29" wheels, although they are also compatible with a 27.5" rear wheel. Liv Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 Details • Wheel size: Mixed - XS, S / 29" M, L • Carbon frame • Travel: 150mm / 160mm fork • 65.1° head angle (mid setting) • 77.6° seat angle (mid setting) • Chainstay length: 442mm • 3-position flip-chip • Downtube storage compartment • Sizes: XS, S, M, L • Price: $7,000 USD • liv-cycling.com With the Hail no longer in the lineup, the Intrigue LT is now the most aggressive bike that Liv offers and is the same frame that Rae Morrison raced to several top-10 finishes during the 2023 Enduro World Cup season. Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take I felt immediately at ease on the Intrigue LT Advanced 1. It's got a ton of traction both on the climbs and the descents and it has a predictability to its handling that made me feel like it had my back when venturing onto new trails. — Sarah Moore Frame Details The new Intrigue LT Advanced is the first bike in Liv's line up to get in-frame storage and uses the same system as Giant's Trance and Reign models. When you turn the latch on the downtube 90 degrees, an internal compartment that contains a water-resistant bag is revealed. The door stayed secure and didn't rattle for the duration of the test, but I didn't find the compartment as useful as it could have been had it had a larger opening. I still ran my pump alongside the water bottle cage and my windbreaker strapped to my handlebars. I was slightly disappointed to find that you can only fit a small 21 ounce (620 mL) water bottle on the size medium frame, but at least the medium frame fits a standard sized small water bottle. While there are water bottle mounts on all frames sizes and Liv says that you can put a water bottle on the two smaller sizes, they will only fit a 13.5 ounce (400 mL) bottle. While the shorter-travel Intrigue has a dual-position link that allows you to choose between a high or low position, the Intrigue LT Advanced has three positions.
