This is actually accomplished with two different flip chips – a combination high/low and a dedicated mid-position chip. The adjustment on the upper rocker arm of the rear suspension changes the head tube angle, seat tube angle and bottom bracket height. It's worth noting that the low position is not permitted for medium and large frames when set up as a mixed-wheel configuration. I was impressed at how durable the top coat is and how good the frame continues to look after more than a year of riding and not always been treated gently. There are a couple minor chips in the clear coating when you look closely, but overall, it's very tough and the raw carbon finish looks great. There are serious rubber protectors along the downtube to keep it safe when rocks fly up from the trail or when you're shuttling and there is a thick ribbed chainstay protector to keep chainslap to a minimum. I did wear through the rear housing cable initially since it pops out of the rear triangle very close to the chain behind the chainring. Under chainslap, the part that isn't covered by a rubber protector got frayed. Once I secured the cable in place with zip ties, it didn't happen again however. Luckily, since routing the cable through the rear triangle proved to be more challenging than it should have been since there was no tube in tube. The frame has integrated cable ports and is compatible with Fox Live Valve. Liv uses a SRAM DUB press fit bottom bracket on the Intrigue LT and the rear triangle is compatible with SRAM's UDH. Most of the pivot bolts have the torque spec and rotation direction listed on them. The frames uses Boost hub spacing and the max tire size that fits in the frame is 2.5". The biggest chainring you can ride is a 32t. Geometry & Sizing Frame sizes Small and XS are solely designed for an MX configuration with a 29" wheel up front and a 27.5" wheel in the rear. Frame sizes Medium and Large come with 29" wheels stock front and rear but can accommodate a 27.5" wheel in the rear if you choose to purchase one separately. The geometry chart for the Intrigue LT is more complicated than most, with the three flip-chip positions making for three different head tube, seat tube and bottom bracket heights per size. In addition, Liv shared the geometry chart for the medium and large sizes in an MX configuration, which adds more numbers to the mix, although the low position isn't actually permitted for frame sizes M and L when set up as a mixed-wheel configuration due to safety standards. I rode the size medium frame and settled on the low setting with the 29" wheels, which gave it a 64.8-degree head tube angle, a 77.3 degree seat tube angle, a 439mm reach, 1208mm wheelbase and 35mm bottom bracket drop. Using the flip-chip will alter the angles by about 0.4 degrees in either direction and move the reach 4mm in either direction. The reach numbers vary from 398 in the Low position on the XS, up to 467mm on the Large frame in the High position. Chainstays are the same regardless of frame size, varying from 443mm in the Low position to 440 in the High position. Suspension Design The Liv Intrigue LT uses the same Maestro suspension design that Giant bikes do which uses four pivot points and two linkages that all work together to create a single floating pivot point. Maestro's upper rocker arms feature trunnion shock mounts and a high-pressure molding process is used to produce the Advanced Forge Composite upper rocker arm. Liv says it is lighter, stiffer and stronger than aluminum. A 185x55mm Trunnion mount shock handles the 150mm of rear wheel travel and the pivot bearings are double sealed. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $7000 Travel 150/160 Rear Shock Fox Float X Performance Elite 185x55 Fork Fox 36 Performance Elite, 160mm, Grip damper Cassette SRAM GX 10x52 Crankarms SRAM GX, 30t, 55mm chainline Chainguide MRP AMg V2 chainguide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB, press fit Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle Chain SRAM GX Shifter Pods SRAM GX Eagle Handlebar Giant Contact SLR TR35, composite, 20mm rise, 35mm Stem Giant Contact SL, 35mm Grips Liv Tactal Pro Single Lock-On Brakes SRAM G2 RSC, hydraulic, SRAM CenterLine rotors [F]200mm, [R]180mm Hubs [F] Giant MTB alloy, 6-bolt, [R] Giant MTB alloy, 3-pawl driver, 6-bolt Spokes Sapim Rim Giant TRX 2 29 WheelSystem, composite, 30mm inner width Tires [F]: Maxxis Minion DHF 29x2.5, 3C Max Terra, EXO, TR, tubeless [R]: Maxxis Dissector 27.5 (XS+S) 29 (M+L) x2.4, 3C Max Terra, EXO, TR, tubeless Seat Liv Sylvia Seatpost TranzX travel-adjustable dropper, remote Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC The spec is pretty dialled for the $7,000 USD price tag of the Liv Intrigue Advanced Pro 1 that I rode. You get a carbon frame, wheels, bar, and a Grip 2 damper in the Fox 36 fork. The carbon parts are “house” branded, which makes it easier for Liv to accomplish this at this price tag than it is for other brands. The only house-branded component I had issues with was the hub, which started to make a racket relatively early on in testing so I swapped the rear wheel out for another wheel for the second half of the test. Standouts for me were the narrower handlebars than are found on most unisex bikes at 760mm (XS), 760mm (S), 760mm (M), and 780mm (L). It's a small thing, but it was nice not to have to cut my bars down to my preferred 760mm. While the lever placement wasn't easy to dial in and I felt it sat a bit too far inwards because of the clamp placement, I did appreciate that the Intrigue LT has decent dropper lengths, something that Liv has struggled with in the past and when I rode the 125mm Intrigue. The TranzX travel-adjustable dropper comes in 125mm travel (XS), 120-150mm travel (S), 150mm travel (M), and 150mm travel on the size Large. Touch points can be a personal preference, but I'm a fan of the Liv Sylvia saddle and swapped out the Liv Tactal Pro Single Lock-On grips for something with a bit more cushion. Otherwise, a SRAM GX drivetrain, Shimano XT brakes, and Maxxis tires round out the parts package. RIDING THE Liv Intrigue LT Advanced 1 Test Bike Setup I usually ride a size medium bike and the Liv Intrigue was no exception, with the medium frame being suited to rider 5'5" (164cm) to 5'9" (175m). Dialling in the suspension and fit was relatively straightforward on the Fox Float X Performance Elite rear shock since it just has rebound and low speed compression adjustments. I varied the sag between 28-30% based on the riding location. As for the Fox 36 Performance Elite fork, it has high and low speed compression and rebound adjustments and I rode it with one token and 20% sag. Other than swapping out the grips for something with a bit more cushion, I got straight to ride with the stock bike. I spent a fair bit of time in all three geometry settings on a variety of trails, including everything from flowy XC trails to bike park descents. Sarah Moore Location: Squamish, BC Age: 33 Height: 5'7" Weight: 160lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @smooresmoore Testing Info Testing took place in Squamish, British Columbia and Revelstoke, British Columbia over the course of a year. Conditions varied from dusty blown out summer riding in Revelstoke to slippery, wet days in the Squamish winter green room. The Intrigue LT was my one bike for the year I was on maternity leave and I did everything on it, including cross-country trails, alpine epics, bike park, and black diamond tech on it. The Trailforks loop shown here is one of my go-to favourites, which I did several times on the Intrigue LT. Download the Trailforks App Climbing The climbing position of the Intrigue LT is well balanced and it felt natural from day one. I was comfortable climbing it on a wide variety of terrain, from steep fire road climbs, to twisty single track. The seat tube angle is steep enough that, even in the low position, I didn't have to jam the saddle forward on the rails to get over the pedals and I found it easy to keep the front wheel on the ground on steeper sections of trails. In the high position, the bike is definitely a bit more of an aggressive pedaler and gets you around tight corners a bit quicker, but even in the low position, it's easy to wind up tight corners and pedal strikes weren't common. The bike is also relatively lightweight for its 150mm of rear travel, weighing in right around 30 pounds (13.6kg) without pedals on thanks to lots of house-branded carbon bits including its Giant TRX 2 carbon wheels. There's no two ways about it, a lightweight bike definitely makes the climbs more enjoyable. There's also a 30T chainring on this bike that, especially when you're riding the bike in its full 29er set up, is a great size for being able to stay seated and keep a quicker cadence on steeper climbs. Chainring size is a bit of a personal thing depending on whether you like to power up climbs or spin up them seated and it also depends on where you live and how much of a slog your climbs are, but I do think it is a good choice for this bike. I didn't find the Maestro suspension to be the firmest of pedalling platforms; there's a slight bob when you push into the pedals, or the most lively, so I did find myself adding a bit more air to the rear shock, and ended up running a bit less sag (28%) to help counteract that. The active suspension does mean that it has oodles of traction when you do get to technical climbs, and you don't get bumped off line very often. It feels like the Intrigue LT can just keep finding grip and making its way over uphill obstacles long after you think you're a goner. Descending When things point downhill, the Intrigue LT hits a good middle ground between feeling fun to ride on lower angle terrain, while staying, composed when it gets pushed into steep, black diamond trails. Liv calls it their enduro race bike, and it's more than capable of racing, but it is on the lighter end of what we now see from that category. Even in my preferred low position, it isn't an all-out brute that will forgive any bad line or allow you to huck blindly into messes of roots and rocks. That being said, it is a confident descender and it has a predictability to its handling that made me feel like it had my back when venturing onto new trails. The traction that I appreciated on the climbs was also there on the descents, and the Intrigue LT felt controlled whether I was trying to creep down sections of trail or hit loose, high-speed corners. The Intrigue LT tolerates blue flow trails, but never felt like a rocket ship through them. The suspension really comes to life when things get rough. Even at higher speeds, the bike remained composed on chunky sections of trail. Occasionally, on larger drops, I felt like the bike went through its travel faster than I would have expected it to, but overall it was a predictable and balanced ride. I would say that some of the spec choices felt like they were made with weight in mind and not descending prowess. While the bike's light weight made for a more nimble climber, I put beefier tires on and I would have liked bigger brakes. The EXO casing tires might be lighter, but they are squirmy and much more likely to get burped on fast corners and more puncture-prone than EXO+ tires. Other than the brakes and tires, the components held up well to a year of abuse, including several bike park days. Technical Report SRAM G2 Brakes: I absolutely cooked the G2 brakes on the long descents in Revelstoke and would have next to no power left at the bottom of runs, just when my hands were most tired. I also had to change my pads more frequently than if this bike had come with Code brakes. That being said, it looks like Liv has made a spec change for 2024 and the Intrigue LT Advanced 1 now comes with Shimano XT brakes, although both models are currently still available for purchase. TranzX Dropper Post: The lever position of the TranzX dropper is clunky and I found it a bit hard to reach, but the post itself worked very well despite zero maintenance throughout the year. It's also nice to see a 170mm travel post on Liv's medium-sized frame since that was something they struggled to fit in the past. That being said, the seat tube could be a bit shorter since my 27" inseam is the bare minimum to ride the bike with its 170mm dropper. Liv Sylvia Saddle: Touch points are personal, but I'm pretty picky with saddles and one of the first things I usually do on a new bike is switch out the saddle. However, I didn't feel the need to swap out the Liv Sylvia saddle and found the shape of it very comfortable. It also shows no signs of wear after a year of use. Ibis Ripmo How Does It Compare? Another bike that I've spent quite a bit of time on recently is the Ibis Ripmo. Both bikes have 150mm of rear travel and a 160mm fork, but feel very different out on the trails. If we compare the Intrigue LT in the low position to the Ripmo on paper, the two bikes have near-identical seat tube angles and head tube angles, but the Liv sports chainstays that are 8mm longer than the Ripmo and a reach that's 20mm shorter at 439mm compared to 460mm. The wheelbase on the Ripmo is slightly longer at 1219mm compared to the Intrigue's 1208mm. While the reach numbers look very different, the Intrigue doesn't feel as short as the reach number would suggest and both climbing positions are comfortable. However, on the climbs, no one will be surprised to hear that I prefer the Ibis with its DW-link. It just feels much more responsive when you push into the pedals, despite having the same amount of rear travel. For alpine epics and climbing, there's no doubt that the Ripmo is the tool for the job. When things point downhill, it's not as straightforward. The Ripmo is a livelier bike on the downhills as well, but I'd say that the Intrigue LT feels more capable of plowing through rough sections and requires less rider input to makes its way down the hill. On rough, high-speed trails and on bike park days, I would choose the Intrigue LT. Lining up the spec sheets, the Ibis Ripmo XT touts Shimano XT brakes and drivetrain and a Fox 38, compared to the Liv Intrigue LT Advanced 1's SRAM GX drivetrain, GX brakes and Fox 36. Normally, the Liv Intrigue LT would take the price war by a mile, but currently the Ibis Ripmo XT is on sale for $6,524 USD ($8,699 USD regular price) with carbon wheels, while the Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 that I tested is on sale for a comparable $6,600 USD ($7,000 USD regular price). The version of the Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 that comes with Shimano XT brakes is not currently on sale and sits at $7,000 USD. Pros + Finds traction easily on the climbs and the descents + Composed on rough terrain + Lightweight build Cons - Not the most supportive suspension platform - You'll need to make a couple spec changes to make it an enduro race rig - XS/S doesn't fit a normal waterbottle Pinkbike's Take The Liv Intrigue LT is a well-rounded bike that has tons of traction whether you're heading uphill or downhill. It's not as beefy as some enduro bikes we see these days, but that keep it relatively lightweight for climbing. While it still might not be the liveliest of climbers due to its pedalling platform, the suspension really comes to life when things get rough on the downhills and with a couple of spec tweaks, it can definitely hold up to all-day enduro racing. — Sarah Moore Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews PBWMN Enduro Bikes Liv Liv Intrigue Lt Author Info: Sarah Moore Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 30, 2011 1,624 articles Pinkbike Content Manager

title: Review: One Year With the High-Pivot Canyon Sender CFR DH Bike - Fast & Refined
author: Stephane Pelletier
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, dh-bikes, canyon, canyon-sender, stephane-pelletier
article_text: Sender Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DAmGGUrH Copied Live 00:00 12:22 12:22 Canyon’s Sender has been a fixture on the World Cup circuit since its debut in 2016. A lot has changed since then, but two things have remained true: legendary racer Fabien Barel has been instrumental in the bike’s development, and it’s always been engineered with the pinnacle of downhill racing in mind. The latest CFR redesign is the most significant overhaul yet, marking the biggest visual and technical changes across the bike’s three generations. Developed in close collaboration with the Canyon CLLCTV team and tested over a full season of World Cup racing, the new Sender brings a high-pivot suspension platform, added adjustability, and a full-carbon chassis built for speed. Sender CFR Details • 200 mm travel, 200 mm fork • Carbon frame • MX wheels • High Pivot MX Link suspension platform • 62.7°- 63° head angle • 438mm rear center (all sizes) = 455.5mm at sag • Sizes: S, M , L(tested), XL • 6-year frame warranty • Weight: 17.92 kg / 39.4 lb • Pricing: €4,999 - €5,999 / $5,110 - $7,779 USD • More info: canyon.com It’s already proven itself on the big stage with wins from Tahnee Seagrave, Aletha Ostgaard, and Troy Brosnan, but how does it stack up outside the tape for a rider who just loves downhill laps? After months of testing on everything from slick clay ruts to blown-out bike park lines, the Sender CFR has shown itself to be one of the most dialled DH bikes on the market, though not without a few quirks. The most obvious miss? No size-specific rear ends. More on that later. Canyon offers two carbon-framed models: the Underdog build at C$6,349, and the CFR Team model, a no-expense-spared replica spec with SRAM suspension and DT Swiss wheels for C$8,399. That’s not the cheapest, but it’s still strong value considering the kit. Low-slung race geometry, smooth kinematics, and powerful brakes form the foundation of a bike built to go as fast as possible. And Canyon doesn’t mince words in their own marketing: “Our goal was to build the best and fastest DH bike on the planet, no excuses.” A nice baby blue frame sure does pop in the lush woods. For the more low-key riders, a blacked-out version also exists. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Performance Value How Does it Compare? Technical Report Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take The Sender CFR is the most refined Canyon DH bike to date, quiet, fast, and impressively adjustable. It’s not just a race bike for pros; privateers and park riders will appreciate its versatility and calm handling too. Whether you’re chasing seconds on the clock or stacking laps at the bike park, the new Sender feels like a bike built to win. The only notable aspect holding the Sender back on faster-pace tracks would be the slightly shorter rear center. That of course will play in your favor on steeper tracks or if you're a freerider looking for responsive 200mm travel rig. — Stephane Pelletier The High Pivot MX layout looks futuristic and tidy, with all the linkage tucked neatly inside the bottom bracket area. Tube-in-tube management is nice and tidy for those running their rear brake on the right side. One downside to a split pivot system is that it makes size-specific chainstays a real struggle. The Sender has one relatively short 438mm rear center length, though it grows to around 455mm at sag. Construction & Features The Sender’s frame is a tidy package of race-focused details. The carbon chassis uses Canyon’s polymer-reinforced HIT Technology for impact protection, paired with a full rubber armour kit that shields everything from the downtube to the rocker link. Internally routed cables, a skid plate, ISCG tabs, and integrated fender coverage ensure a quiet and user-friendly experience. Frame weight comes in at 4,680 grams. The most noticeable update is the new high-pivot MX suspension layout, Canyon’s first foray into the high-pivot world. The frame has a futuristic, clean look, with a low-slung bottom bracket, long and slack geometry, and a linkage-driven single pivot that manages to pack plenty of complexity into a surprisingly tidy design. Another standout feature is the K.I.S. steering stabilizer, which adds a subtle centering force at the bars for extra stability on high-speed straights. It’s easy to adjust, or remove entirely if it’s not your thing. Out back, Canyon opted for 148mm Boost spacing instead of the DH-standard 157mm, a different approach to most, but one that allows for wheel swaps with trail and enduro bikes in a pinch. Since the goal was to make a bike that’s race-ready straight out of the box, Canyon built in plenty of adjustability. There’s ±8mm of reach adjustment via tool-free headset cups, flip chips that tweak BB height by ±5mm and progression between 32.7–37%, plus easy-access linkage hardware for quick pit servicing. Importantly, all adjustments are said to work independently, so you can fine-tune one aspect without throwing off another. Overall, Canyon has clearly sweated the details, aiming to make this race-ready machine as straightforward to live with as possible, whether you’re a factory pro or a privateer. 0 seconds of 34 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/e5WR8smA Copied Live 00:00 00:34 00:34 Suspension Design At the heart of the new Sender is the High Pivot MX Link layout. A concentric BB pivot and split pivot at the axle create a rearward axle path, with up to 23.5mm of rear-center growth that helps the wheel move up and back through square-edge hits. If you’re wondering about the “MX” name, it doesn’t actually refer to wheel size, although the Sender is now mixed-wheel only. Instead, it comes from “motocross linkage,” an adaptation of suspension layouts used in motocross. This isn’t entirely new to Canyon either; the Sender has run a version of this linkage since its inception, as have several other DH bikes on the market. Anti-rise sits high early in the travel (around 130%), which means the bike should stay composed under braking without pitching forward, a characteristic shaped by feedback from Fabien Barel and the Canyon CLLCTV team. Pedal kickback remains impressively low thanks to the idler placement, and the leverage curve can be fine-tuned via a flip chip to run either more supportive or more supple. Canyon designed the frame around coil shocks, though it’ll run air without issue. The Sender uses a 250 x 75 mm coil shock, with stock spring rates tuned per frame size (S = 350lb, M = 400lb, L = 450lb, XL = 500lb). In practice, the kinematics strike a sweet spot between traction and support, one of the Sender’s most defining traits. You can read a very in-depth article about anti-rise written by Jessie-May Morgan here . A breakdown of the geometry, comparing the 2nd gen to 3rd gen models. Geometry & Sizing With a 63° head angle and a low bottom bracket (347–352mm depending on setting), the Sender is every bit as aggressive as you’d expect from a race-bred DH bike. The 438mm chainstay length looks short on paper, but it extends to 455.5mm at sag, long enough to deliver stability without feeling like a bus. Unlike the previous Sender, the new version uses a single rear-center length across all frame sizes. The old bike had an axle flip chip to fine-tune chainstay length, but with the move to a split pivot at the axle, that option disappeared. Manufacturing different rear triangles for each size would also add cost and complexity. Canyon says they tested multiple rear-center lengths with their World Cup riders, who agreed this was the best compromise, but I can’t help feeling that true proportional sizing would improve the ride for a broader range of riders. There’s 5mm of BB height adjustment via a flip chip in the upper shock mount, and reach can be shifted ±8mm with tool-free headset cups, making it easy to tweak fit for different tracks. Four frame sizes cover most riders, with a low-slung top tube that provides plenty of clearance when muscling the bike through rough terrain. Reach values range from 443mm on the Small up to 518mm on the XL. Interestingly, the jumps aren’t consistent: 25mm from Small to Medium, 27mm from Medium to Large, and 23mm from Large to XL. Stack numbers run from 626mm to 639mm. Overall, the Sender feels roomy across the range, and the ability to fine-tune reach and stack means riders can adjust between a shorter, more upright position for steeper technical tracks, or a longer, lower setup for flat-out speed. In my case, at 6’3” (191cm), I went with a Large even though I sit between Large and XL. I usually size down for more nimble handling in the bike park. I ran the +8mm reach setting with 10mm of added stack, which put me in a comfortable, versatile position. It worked well for both fast technical DH tracks and Whistler Bike Park laps. For pure racing, though, I’d be curious to try the XL. That said, bigger riders may find the single-size rear end on the short side. One note: if you want to run a 29” rear wheel, you’re out of luck; the frame is 27.5-only in the back. Personally, I’m a big fan of mixed-wheel DH bikes; they strike a great balance between plowing through rough terrain and keeping the back end maneuverable. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $7799 Travel 200 mm Rear Shock RockShox Vivid Coil DH Fork RockShox BoXXer Ultimate Headset Acros Cassette SRAM X01 DH 7-Speed 11-25t Crankarms SRAM X01 DH Carbon Chainguide MRP SXG Bottom Bracket SRAM BSA DUB Pedals n/a Rear Derailleur SRAM X01 DH Chain SRAM PC 1110 11s Shifter Pods SRAM X01 DH Handlebar Canyon G5 30mm Rise Stem Canyon G5 45mm Grips Ergon GDH Team Brakes SRAM Maven Silver Stealth / SRAM HS2 200mm rotors Wheelset DT Swiss FR1500 Hubs DT Swiss 240 Rim DT Swiss FR1500 Tires Maxxis Assegai MaxxGrip DH 2.5" / Maxxis Minion DHR II 2.4" Seat Ergon SMD20 Seatpost G5 Seatpost 34.9mm Compare to other DH Bikes As a consumer-direct brand, Canyon has built its reputation on value-packed build kits, and the new Sender stays true to that formula. The lineup is simple with just two models: the budget-friendly Underdog at $6,349 CAD (not available in the U.S.), and the high-performance Team at $8,399 CAD / $7,779 USD—the version I’ve been riding. The Underdog gets a well-rounded mix of proven parts: Fox Performance suspension front and rear, DT Swiss F1900 rims laced to 350 hubs, SRAM Maven Bronze brakes, and SRAM GX DH shifting paired with alloy cranks. The Team spec takes things up a notch with components you’ll find on top racers’ bikes: RockShox Ultimate suspension, a SRAM X0 DH drivetrain with carbon cranks, Maven Silver brakes, and DT Swiss FR1500 wheels on 240 hubs. Both builds roll on the same Maxxis tires and come with Canyon’s in-house G5 cockpit. The bar-and-stem combo has a quality finish and comfortable sweep, with 30mm rise bars. I do wish bar rise varied by frame size, but it’s easy enough to fine-tune stack height with spacers. Ergon grips and saddles round out both builds, adding a nice finishing touch across the range. RIDING THE Sender Test Bike Setup I was fortunate to have Canyon’s Factory Race Team helping with the initial setup, which meant a lot of the fine-tuning most riders would do at home was guided by their mechanics and engineers. I started in a neutral setup: 0mm reach, linear progression, low BB setting, and the stock 450lb spring. After a few laps on Windrock Bike Park’s Tennessee Nationals course, I made some key changes. I switched to the +8mm reach headset cup, went to the progressive shock mount, and bumped up to a 525lb spring. That progressive mount allowed a slightly more supple initial stroke, but also gave me more mid- and end-stroke support at speed and through big compressions. I also raised the BB setting; the low setting felt too close to the ground and sketchy over rough terrain with pedal strikes. Even in the higher setting, the bike still felt plenty low. Stephane Pelletier Location: Cumberland, BC Height: 6'3" / 191cm Weight: 230 lb / 104 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None This setup put me at about 30% sag, right where I’d typically be on a DH bike. Shock settings landed at 2 clicks LSC, 2 clicks HSC, and 11 clicks rebound, while the fork was at 185 PSI, HSC fully open, and 7 clicks LSC. Back home in Canada, riding Whistler Bike Park and Mount Washington, I tweaked things again, slightly stiffening the fork for jumps and adding 10mm of stack height with spacers for a more comfortable position. As for the K.I.S. steering stabilizer, I experimented with different levels but ultimately wasn’t a fan. It felt unwieldy and unpredictable, so I kept it at the lowest setting for most of my testing. Testing Info Download the Trailforks App Mt. Washington Alpine Resort mountain biking trails I first picked up the Sender at Windrock Bike Park at the end of March. Since then, it’s been my go-to for park and shuttle laps—everything from greasy early-season tech at Windrock, to blown-out Dirt Merchant laps in Whistler, to rough bike park tracks at Mount Washington, plus a good dose of loamy, compression-filled trails closer to home. The bike has seen a lot of riding in a short time and, so far, hasn’t skipped a beat. Performance Right away, the Sender felt like it was asking me to push harder. The low-slung geometry and high-pivot design make it incredibly confident through rough terrain; it floated over chatter and square-edge hits like a magic carpet. It did take some adjustment to feel balanced. At first, the relatively short rear end threw me off, but once I switched to the +8mm reach cups, things felt more natural. From there, the more I pushed, the more the Sender rewarded me: braking later, carrying more speed, and trusting the bike to smooth out the nastiest lines. One thing that stood out immediately was just how composed the Sender feels when braking hard. The bike settles into a neutral position under load, which allowed me to stay calmer, brake later, and push harder than I usually would in steep or blown-out sections. On high-speed, rough trails, the suspension platform shines. On flatter terrain, however, it takes a bit more effort to keep momentum, and the slack, low geometry can feel twitchy. Cornering was a highlight, the rear-center growth lengthens the bike mid-turn, giving me a really centred and stable position. Surprisingly, the Sender handled jump trails in Whistler better than expected. It’s not the most playful park bike, but the mixed-wheel setup and raised stack put me in a stable, comfortable position in the air. Manuals were less intuitive; like many high-pivot bikes, the balance point felt vague, almost like balancing on a ball. Mechanically, my test bike has been almost flawless. Aside from regular chain lube, I didn’t need any mid-season maintenance. Bearings stayed smooth, pivots tight, and the frame shrugged off some nasty rock strikes thanks to its HIT-reinforced armour. Issues were minor: the chain hopped out of the hardest gear twice, and the headset cups developed some creak during peak dust season, but both were easy fixes. My only ongoing gripe is chainstay length; size-specific rear ends make sense on DH bikes, and the Sender’s single rear-center dimension feels like a compromise for larger riders. Serviceability has been excellent. After a year of heavy use, the bearings are still solid, and disassembly was moderately straightforward, though not as easy as VPP or 4-bar layouts, since the linkage is all cocooned in the BB area. I was impressed to find Canyon utilized rubber gaskets around all pivot hardware, which seemed to do a remarkable job of keeping the grime out. After a year of riding as much as possible, the bearings were still smooth, and even still had cleean, factory direct grease packed in there. Canyon also offers a deep catalogue of replacement hardware, so keeping the bike running long-term should be painless. What's the Best Value? Both build levels are strong options. The Team spec is race-ready right out of the box, with high-end suspension, drivetrain, and wheels. That said, the Underdog punches well above its weight. Performance-level suspension has really stepped up in recent years, and the difference between Maven Bronze/Silver brakes or GX/X0 drivetrains isn’t as dramatic as the price gap suggests. Pair that with solid DT wheels, Maxxis rubber, Ergon touchpoints, and Canyon’s G5 cockpit, and the Underdog delivers a ton of performance for the money. How Does It Compare? Canyon Sender CFR Team: $7,799 USD Commencal Supreme DH V5 RockShox: $7,800.00 USD. The Commencal Supreme DH V5 is a natural comparison; both are consumer-direct, podium-proven, high-pivot race bikes. Geometry is nearly identical, though the Commencal runs a touch longer and lower. The biggest difference comes at the rear end: the Supreme offers size-specific chainstays plus an axle flip chip, whereas the Sender sticks to a one-size-fits-all solution. That adjustability makes the Commencal more versatile, especially for taller riders. Spec-wise, Canyon edges out the Commencal. Both share RockShox Ultimate suspension, but the Sender upgrades to an X0 drivetrain, Maven Silver brakes, DT FR1500 wheels, and Maxxis tires versus the Supreme’s GX drivetrain, Maven Bronze brakes, DT F1900 wheels, and Schwalbe rubber. Ergon touchpoints are another nice detail on the Sender. At 39.5 lbs, it’s also lighter than the 41.2 lb Supreme. Both bikes are clearly capable of winning World Cups. If value is the deciding factor, the Canyon’s build kit tips the scales in its favour. Technical Report Maxxis tires: Both builds come with sticky DH casing Maxxis rubber, a proven choice that’s race-ready out of the box. SRAM Maven Brakes: The new benchmark for power and consistency. The Silvers offer full adjustability, while the Bronzes on the Underdog drop contact point adjustment but retain the same power. My arm pump noticeably decreased with Mavens; less death-grip braking, more energy for holding lines. Ultimate Suspension: No surprises here. High-end suspension that’s buttery and controlled, exactly what you want on a DH race bike. Carbon X0 Cranks: Although good on paper, thanks to their lighter weight, I find carbon cranks to be a long-term durability concern. Maybe it's a me thing as a flat pedal rider with some sort of an instep, but that level of wear in a year is concerning. I'd take the alloy version's weight penalty for durability any day. Pros + Race level spec right out of the box + Extremely composed under heavy braking + Tons of adjustability to suit most riders Cons - Taller riders or racers may want a longer chainstay - Headset creak developed during dusty bike park laps - Carbon cranks can be a durability concern. Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Riding Photos: Matt Wonbon Regions in Article Mount Washington Bike Park Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews DH Bikes Canyon Canyon Sender Stephane Pelletier Author Info: Stephane Pelletier Cumberland, BC, Canada Member since Sep 30, 2008 461 articles Based in Cumberland, BC, Stephane Pelletier is the Content Manager at Pinkbike, overseeing events, tech, media, and gear reviews. He got his start in bike shops at age 14 and competed in slopestyle before working in industry brand marketing roles. With 18 years of experience and an unrelated Geology degree from the University of Calgary, he pairs deep tech knowledge with a passion for the sport. Off the clock, he’s usually riding, surfing, snowboarding, or on his moto.

title: Review: The O'Neal Trailfinder Helmet With Quin Sensor Can Call For Help If You Hit Your Head
author: Seb Stott
article_text: The O'Neal Trailfinder may look like a generic trail helmet, but it houses some pretty promising tech. Alongside the O'Neal Blade Polycarbonate full-face helmet, it's compatible with the latest Quin Pod sensor, which uses gyroscopes, accelerometers and algorithms to assess head impacts in real-time. If the algorithm thinks you've hit your head badly and you don't respond to a prompt on your smartphone, it can alert your emergency contacts, and even the emergency services, letting them know your status and location. The concept is potentially life-saving, and could offer peace of mind to those of us who ride alone, but it's an idea that's been tried before and has so far remained niche. Quin says their latest sensor developed with O'Neal adds a gyroscope and updated algorithms, helping it to distinguish between a serious crash and a helmet drop, thereby virtually eliminating the annoying false-positive alerts which may put people off such systems. So the question is - does it work? O'Neal Trailfinder With Quin Details • Quin sensor is integrated into the helmet, detects impacts and communicates via an IOS or Android app • Includes 2 accelerometers and a gyroscope • IP67 dust and waterproof rating • Helmet with adjustable visor • Weight: 445 g (L/XL, including sensor) • Sizes: S/M (54cm - 58cm) or L/XL (59cm - 63cm) • Colour options: Black, White/grey, White/purple • MSRP: £81/€89,99 (helmet) + £116/€129,99 (sensor) = £197/€219,98 • oneal.eu The Quin System The Quin Pod is the sensor unit that clips into the structure of the helmet behind the liner. It contains two accelerometers and a gyroscope to detect acceleration and rotation of the helmet up to 6,600 times per second, and communicates with your phone via Bluetooth and an app (Android or IOS). If the algorithm determines that an event could be serious, an alert is sent to the phone. You then have ten seconds to cancel the alert if you're okay. Otherwise, it will send a text message and email to your chosen emergency contacts (you can register up to three), letting them know your location with a Google Maps link, and prompting them to call you. The app can also contact emergency services in thirty-two countries if you sign up for the Plus ResQ membership for $7.99 per month. With this service, Quin will also try to contact you to find out if you're okay. To be clear, you don't have to pay anything for the app to message your own emergency contacts. Quin seems to have had the story of the boy who cried wolf in mind when designing their latest sensor. Compared to the system they released in 2021 with Abus , it has 2.5 times the sampling rate and adds a gyroscope to more accurately measure what's happening to your head, helping to prevent false alarms while detecting real events. For example, if you drop your helmet onto a hard surface, the linear acceleration (G-force) can be similar to a real crash. But an empty helmet will tend to keep rotating after impact, while a helmet that contains a rider's head can't do that (unless you're an owl), so the algorithm looks at how the rotational velocity changes over time to distinguish an empty-helmet drop from a crash. If the helmet hits the deck hard, the app will register this as a helmet drop and prompt you to check for damage. The sensor is designed specifically for each model and size of helmet, taking into account the shape, structure, and weight to more accurately determine whether an impact is deemed serious. To be clear, you can't mount the Quin sensor to any helmet. The app also stores data on the severity of the impact (rotation and linear acceleration), which Quin says could be helpful for rehab in the event of a concussion. Quin claims this has helped in the recovery of one sponsored motocross athlete, but they acknowledge that head injuries need to be treated based on symptoms rather than impact forces. The sensor is easy to take in and out of the helmet. It charges via USB-C in about two hours, and the battery lasts for 40+ hours of ride time and months of standby time. You don't have ot plug it in after every ride. It's IP67 rated, meaning you don't have to worry about it getting wet or dusty, unless maybe you drop it in the sea. The app is easy enough to use. It offers ride tracking, but this is less useful than other apps like Strava. I just use the Quin app to make sure the sensor is connected and charged. Does it work? It's hard to say for sure how the system would respond if the proverbial hit the fan (I haven't been unlucky enough to "need" the system yet), but I can say it does a good job of eliminating false positives. Maybe too good. Simply dropping the helmet on the ground either doesn't register at all or is logged as a helmet drop. Not wanting to put my own head at risk in the name of science, the best I could do was to ride with the helmet in one hand and hit it into a tree. But even this doesn't usually trigger a response, or it registers as a helmet drop. I tried this literally dozens of times with different configurations of impact speed, angle and surface, but I couldn't "trick" the system into registering a crash and messaging my emergency contact (don't worry, Quin had disabled the emergency services feature on my device). After talking to Quin, and making many more attempts, I managed to trigger an alert by filling the helmet with a bag of gravel to simulate the inertia of a head, which reduces the rotational acceleration a bit like a real head, then running with the helmet into the base of a tree. This triggered a loud alarm in the app almost immediately, and after ignoring the notification, it sent a message to my emergency contact (whom I had informed of what I was up to), showing her my correct location on a map. Success! So, yes, it can work. But it's hard to know how reliably it would work for real, as I only triggered an alert once despite many attempts. The Bluetooth connection between the helmet and the phone would drop out occasionally, too, despite having all app permissions switched on. To be useful, the sensor has to correctly recognise a real impact, then send the signal to the phone. This requires the phone to remain functional after the crash, at least for a little while, and the Bluetooth connection to remain uninterrupted. You'll also need a phone signal for it to be able to reach your contacts or the emergency services if you've paid for that service. I'm not saying that chain of events is unlikely, but it's far from guaranteed. Quin's efforts to eliminate false positives make it hard to trigger the system without hitting the deck for real, and that makes it hard to know if it would work when it counts. I spoke to Quin about this at length, and they claim that they have detected thousands of crashes between their motorbike and cycling helmets, with "less than a handful of false positives and false negatives" being registered. Quin also say that while they've worked hard to avoid false alarms, the algorithm is tuned to trigger a crash alert if there's ambiguity. I'm not going to put my own head on the line to test that out, and there is a lack of testimonials or reviews saying that it does work when it counts. Needless to say, don't think of it as an excuse to do stupid solo stunts, but as a last line of defense in case things don't go to plan. O'Neal Trailfinder Helmet My main issue with this implementation is not the Quin sensor - it's lightweight, unobtrusive, and relatively inexpensive in the grand scheme of things, so why not have it? My concern is with the O'Neal Trailfinder helmet it's fitted to. It doesn't offer the best passive safety credentials, so there is a tradeoff in this case. While it complies with European EN1078 and American CPSC safety standards for bicycle helmets as you'd expect, it's not listed on Virginia Tech' s independent and comparative safety rankings, and it doesn't have MIPS or any other device designed to create a slip plane to prevent rotational forces from affecting the rider's head. All the top-scoring helmets on Virginia Tech's list have technology like this. It's only available in two sizes, which may not suit everybody. Personally, I found the fit a little narrow and tight above the ears. The Trailfinder is otherwise a perfectly acceptable budget helmet (it costs just £81/€89,99 without the sensor), but if the appeal of Quin is to maximise safety, it's a shame the helmet isn't already one of the safest on the market. As it is, it's hard to know if the Quin sensor makes up for the Trailfinder's unproven safety credentials. For the combined price of the helmet and sensor (€220), you could have your pick of many of the safest helmets according to Virginia Tech, including the top-ranked Canyon Deflectr RLS ($160). Personally, I use WhatsApp live location when I'm riding alone, along with Garmin's Incident Detection feature. This is far more basic than Quin's system, and Garmin is awful for false alerts (it's never going to be as sensitive when it's not connected to your head), but at least they're easy to cancel without pulling out your phone with the Garmin on your handlebars. More importantly, the Garmin can be used with any helmet, and I'm not sure the Quin sensor offers an edge over the Garmin system that justifies being tied to a relatively basic helmet. Pros + Quin sensor can automatically alert emergency contacts if your head takes a hit + Doesn't cry wolf Cons - No guarantee the sensor will work when it's needed most - requires phone signal to be effective - Trailfinder helmet lacks a Virginia Tech safety ranking and MIPS liner or similar Pinkbike's Take The idea behind the Quin sensor is appealing for those who ride alone. If I were to end up unconscious at the side of the trail, I'd want people to know where I was as soon as possible. But it's hard to know if the device would alert your emergency contacts when it came to the crunch, as a few things have to line up for that to happen: impact detection, Bluetooth connection & phone signal. It's a small and unobtrusive device, and the combined cost of the helmet and sensor is reasonable, so why not have it just in case? Unfortunately, the O'Neal Trailfinder helmet that the sensor is made for isn't the most recommendable if safety is paramount. It doesn't have a Virginia Tech ranking, nor does it have a slip-plane system like all the top-ranked helmets do. It's a budget-priced helmet with fairly basic passive safety features. Personally, I'd feel safer wearing a top-rated helmet without a sensor, especially when combined with more basic means of sharing your location. — Seb Stott Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets Oneal Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: OneUp Clip Pedals
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: OneUp Clip Pedal Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/auyxOXpP Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 06:24 OneUp has had flat pedals in their component lineup for nearly a decade, but it wasn’t until earlier this year that they began offering an option for riders that wanted to clip in. The Clip pedals are intended to work for everything from XC to DH and all the riding categories in between. The pedals use an SPD-compatible mechanism, with adjustable spring tension and four removable pins on each side. They have 5-degrees of float and a 12-degree release angle. The aluminum platform is 95mm long and 80mm wide, and the cleat mechanism itself is 26.8mm thick. OneUp claim it’s the thinnest clipless pedal in the world at 14.4mm, and so far I haven’t found anything to dispute that claim. Clip Pedal Details • SPD compatible • Chromoly steel axle • 90 x 80mm aluminum body • Internals: bushing + 3 cartridge bearings • 4 removable pins per side • 8 platform color options • Weight: 416 grams with pins / 406 grams without • MSRP: $160 USD • oneupcomponents.com For comparison, Shimano's XTR pedals are 17.2mm thick. Keep in mind that the ground clearance difference between the two is only 1.4mm, so it's not going to be a drastic change - you'll still probably keep hitting the same rock you hit on every ride no matter which brand pedal you're on. The pedals are priced at $160 USD, and are available in eight different colors, including purple, orange, green, and bronze. Cleats are included, along with a plastic cleat spacer to fine tune the amount of room between pedal and shoe. A pedal nut bearing tool is also provided, which can be used to remove the axle for a quick re-greasing. Terminology Talk Clipless pedal reviews inevitably result in a semantics debate breaking out in the comments section, and somewhat understandably so. I’m old enough to have started riding with toe clips and straps, so the clipless terminology makes total sense to me - ditch the toe clips for a pedal with a binding mechanism and presto, you have clipless pedals. I also get that lots of riders barely even know what clips and straps look like ; for that contingent, calling these pedals “clipless” is a head scratcher. For this review, I’m sticking with clipless, partially because I’m stubborn, and partially because I like the incongruity of the term. Now back to the review. Pins I’m still not convinced that pins are necessary, or even helpful at all, on a pedal with a fixed clip-in mechanism. I don’t want my shoes to hang up when I’m trying to un-clip, just like you wouldn’t want a random screw preventing or hindering your release on a ski binding. After a couple rides with the pins installed I took them out completely and never looked back. Could they help with traction when you’re not clipped in and resting a foot on the pedal body? Potentially, although I didn’t have any issues slipping off the pedal during those half in / half out moments - the pedal body is nice and wide, and the grooves in it help add some traction when things are slippery. Retention My first round with these pedals was cut short when OneUp issued a stop sale notice - OneUp said that “in rare and specific conditions, certain riders may experience difficulty unclipping from the pedal.” I was one of those riders, and at that point I’d already had several instances where the cleat would hang up, delaying the moment of release, and at times making it almost impossible to get out of the pedal depending on the orientation of my foot. The solution was a revised binding plate (the metal bar that sits in front of the rear portion of the clip-in mechanism). I sent my pedals back to OneUp for the change, and restarted the review process when the updated pedals arrived. With the updated plate in place, it was much easier to get in and out, enough that I was able to increase the spring tension closer to the middle of the range. The performance has remained consistent, and I haven’t had any further release issues. I've used OneUp's cleats and Shimano's SH51 cleats with good results. The Shimano cleats seem to provide a slightly easier entry and exit, even though visually the shape is nearly identical. The combination of the wide platform and the shape of OneUp clip-in mechanism creates a very secure interface - there's minimal up and down or fore-aft movement once you're clipped in, which make the pedals feel extra secure. OneUp vs Shimano I really like the Clip pedal's platform shape and the additional support it provides - I did a ride with an XTR pedal on one side and a OneUp pedal on the other and the difference was noticeable. Of course, a shoe’s stiffness and sole design plays a part in this equation, but for shoes with a flatter soles the OneUp platform provides a nice perch to rest on. In fact, the platform is wide and supportive enough that it may allow some riders to go with a slightly softer shoe than they'd normally run. The OneUp pedals also offer a higher maximum spring tension - that’ll be a plus for riders who still pop out of Shimano’s pedals even when they’re cranked all the way down. I’m usually in the middle of the spring tension range with Shimano pedals, so that’s not an issue for me, but it’s worth a mention When it comes to price and weight, Shimano’s XT pedals are $10 less and weigh 14 grams more. XTR pedals are 16 grams lighter, but they’re also $60 more expensive at their full retail price. Shimano’s pedals have a two year warranty, versus one year for OneUp. Durability wise, I’ve had very, very good luck with Shimano's pedals over the years, especially the XT model. With XTR, the little rubber dust seal does have a tendency to pop out, but both models can go a really long time without needing service. When it is time to service them, fresh grease is usually all that’s required. For the OneUp Clip pedals, service is fairly simple, and the bushing removal / installation tool is only $10. My pedals don’t have any play in them yet, and there was plenty of grease on the axle when I pulled them apart, so things are off to a good start. When it comes to color options, OneUp handily takes that one - there aren’t any purple pedals or green pedals in Shimano’s lineup. At the end of the day, my overall recommendation goes to Shimano’s XT M8120 pedals due to the slightly smoother entry and exit, lower price, and proven longevity, but OneUp’s Clip pedals put up a good fight - they’re a solid contender in the sea of SPD-compatible options. Pros + Wide platform provides extra support + Very competitive weight + Good range of spring tension Cons - Pins don't provide much benefit - Entry and exit isn't quite as smooth as Shimano (but it's close) Pinkbike's Take OneUp’s Clip pedals aren’t a dramatic departure from what’s already on the market, and that’s okay. After all, the SPD design has been around for 35 years at this point - it’s withstood the test of time, so it makes sense to offer an option with a few refinements, like a wider, thinner platform, rather than completely starting from scratch. I ran into a few hiccups at the beginning of the test period, but since then the performance has been much more consistent, and I’m not in a rush to switch over to something else. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Pedals OneUp Oneup Clip Pedals Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: OneUp V3 Dropper Post - Still the One to Beat
author: Seb Stott
article_text: OneUp V3 240 mm Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/9VljEq6H Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 04:29 The OneUp V3 is arguably the best all-around droppers on paper . It’s one of the longest-travel, shortest stack, lowest weight and least expensive on the market. But I wanted to see how it fared in the real world. It's no longer top-of-the-class in every area, as more affordable long-travel options have come to market recently, but it's still the benchmark because it scores well in every area. The V3 came out in early 2024, claiming to be more reliable, smoother and 60-70 g lighter than the V2, but the comparison is a little more complicated against the new competitors. After eighteen months of use on two different bikes in its most extreme 240 mm length, it’s time to give the verdict. In short, it’s still the one I'd recommend. OneUp V3 Dropper Details • Travel: 90mm / 120mm / 150mm / 180mm / 210mm / 240mm (tested) • Total Length: 295mm / 350mm / 415mm / 480mm / 545mm / 610mm • Travel can be reduced by 10 or 20 mm • Compressed Stack Height: 30mm (25mm for 34.9) • 27.2 / 30.9 / 31.6 / 34.9mm diameters • 2-year warranty • MSRP: $269.99 USD / $364.99 CAD / €291.99 / £291.99 ($302.37 with remote) • Weight: 612 g (actual, 240x31.6 mm, post only) • More info: oneupcomponents.com How does it compare? Until recently, the 240 mm OneUp dropper was the longest travel dropper available. That crown has since been stolen (marginally) by the 242 mm Wolf Tooth Resolve rev2 and the 250 mm RockShox Reverb AXS , and equalled by the cable-operated Fox Transfer , but the V3 remains one of the longest options, and I suspect more than ample for most riders. Below is a selection of droppers with more than 210 mm of travel to see how the specs stack up. While the V3 is no longer the longest, it's still the lightest and the lowest stack height, if only by a little. The compressed stack height is measured from the bottom of the collar to the centre of the saddle rails with the post fully compressed - this metric can be compared between droppers with different travel. If you're limited by the length of external space between the seat clamp and the saddle rails at pedalling height, a shorter compressed stack will allow you to run a longer travel dropper post (the V3 can be reduced in travel by 10 or 20 mm if it's too long). The total length is important if it's the internal insertion depth that limits your maximum seatpost length, but that's harder to compare between seatpost with different travel. The 240 mm V3 is just 1 mm shorter than the 240 mm Fox Transfer, but 23 mm longer than the Wolf Tooth Resolve V2. OneUp has a calculator to determine the maximum length of dropper that will fit your frame. The OneUP V3 is no longer exceptional in any area, but the combination of traits makes it exceptionally well-rounded. Servicing OneUp recommends a 120-hour service interval for a clean and grease & 350 hours for a full rebuild. They say this takes less than ten minutes using standard tools: a 14mm wrench, 5mm hex, and 2mm hex. I've not attempted the full rebuild yet, because my post doesn't need it and I want to test its longevity. The fully sealed cartridge design was a concern to me, given that other posts need occasional adjustment of the air pressure to maintain return speed, but the V3 has shown no signs of slowing down over the 18-month test period. OneUp says that doing without a Schrader valve eliminates the main source of leaks in refillable systems, and using pure nitrogen maintains pressure better than air, too. Worst case, you can get a replacement cartridge (along with all the other spare parts) from OneUp for $41.99. How much travel do you need? Not everyone needs a 240 mm seatpost, but at 191 cm / 6'3" tall, I find having beyond 200 mm of drop to be a distinct advantage. It allows me to move around the bike, absorb compressions and ride more fluidly. I don't think this is a result of steep seat tubes so much as that modern bikes require a more centred riding position, whereas with old-school bikes, you were swinging off the back behind the saddle. I also think modern enduro bikes are so capable that we're riding trails that used to be called DH tracks with 150-170 mm travel, so the extra room between your legs is appreciated. Performance There isn't too much separating modern cable-operated dropper posts in terms of performance, since the details are largely ironed out by now. Nevertheless, the V3 returns swiftly, even in grotty conditions, and makes an audible top-out so you know it’s fully extended before sitting down. If anything, the V3 post was a little slower to return when it was new. I gave the seal a quick grease about a year ago, and it's been enthusiastic ever since. After eighteen months without a proper service, my 240 mm version is sometimes slightly sticky at full extension, and benefits from a little forward scoot to start it compressing smoothly, but it takes minimal effort to overcome this. I have a 213 mm Bike Yoke Revive on test, which is noticeably smoother to get moving in a butt-to-butt test, but that's not a fair comparison as it's shorter travel and much newer. While you can make it stick by pushing your weight back in the saddle, this is a non-issue on the trail and likely results from my deliberate withholding of TLC. There's about a millimetre of squish when the post is set to mid-travel, but this isn't noticeable, and the post is solid at full extension, with no sagging and minimal play. The dropper remote is particularly ergonomic and neat, with its rubber thumb pad and cable-end holder. All this should be expected these days, but some droppers still fail to return swiftly and consistently, especially in cold weather or after a few months of use, or at least require adjustment of the air spring pressure to ensure reliable return speed. The fact that the V3 has maintained its smooth and swift performance even in the 240 mm length makes it more impressive. Pros + Still a great balance of cost, weight, stack and travel + Healthy return, audable topout + No play or squish after eighteen months Cons - PNW & SDG offer alternatives with similar travel for less money, while the Wolf Tooth Resolve V2 has a shorter insertion depth - Slight sticking from full extension if you abuse it Pinkbike's Take While OneUp's V3 post is no longer in a class of its own, it's still the benchmark. It's not just light and compact for the travel, it also performs solidly and has stood up well to an extended test period. — Seb Stott Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Seatposts OneUp Oneup V3 Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: Orange Switch 7 - A Clashing of Worlds
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: Orange Switch 7 Q&A https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/x3jutkpn Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 10:04 Review: Orange Switch 7 Words: Henry Quinney Photos: Tom Richards Two decades ago, Orange were at the height of their powers. World Cup wins, some of the sports' biggest stars and a design that seemed of the time . That said, as the sport has grown and changed, and bike design has changed drastically in the intervening years. The geometry of Oranges, it has to be said, has never been scared of being progressive or cutting edge. The design of their bikes, however, has often been low-hanging fruit for references to filing cabinets, step ladders, cutlery drawers and camper vans. Despite this, Orange are sticking to their guns. This bike still looks like an Orange and has all the hallmarks one might expect of the brand - large profile tubes, simple lines, a shock held horizontally in the frame and a complicated relationship with the water bottle. However, bikes aren't for looking at, they're for riding. And could the reason be found in the madness? Switch 7 Details • Aluminium frame w/ CNC lugs • 170mm travel / 170mm fork • 29" front wheel, 27.5" rear • Head angle: 63° • 76º seat tube angle • Reach: 458 - 502 mm • Chainstay: 448 mm • Weight: 34.4 lb / 15.6 kg (size L, stock) • S - XL • Price: £5800 - £7400 / Frame w/shock: £3500 • orangebikes.com When flicking through the model pages on the website, something will occur to you: there are about a dozen times as many Oranges as you realised. It's a bewildering mess of bikes with the same amount of travel, design and intention but with different wheel sizes and model names. There are around 30 mountain bikes. To make things more complicated, there will be some names you will remember, some you will have forgotten, and some you never even knew existed. The Switch 7 is a bike I have wanted to ride for a long time, and this summer, I got the opportunity to put it through its paces. Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Ultimately, this bike truly shines on the trails you dream of riding. — Henry Quinney Frame Details The Switch 7 is a mixed-wheeled bike that has 170mm travel front and rear. A criticism levelled at Orange in recent years is their inability to fit a water bottle. Well, they've remedied that... sort of. This bike does have water bottle mounts on the underside of the toptube. That said, it also doesn't quite have the clearance to take anything other than a small FidLock. So, better but still not great. There is another bottle mount on the underside of the downtube should you particularly enjoy giardia, or just somewhere to put extra tools or a tube that's nice and low. Speaking of muck and grime, there aren't too many bearings that would need changing, but if you do, there is a 5-year warranty for the original owner. This is a really nice touch, and it's good to see another brand offer this. Orange have used FEA (finite element analysis) to tune compliance, deflection and fatigue. The swingarm is asymmetrical to aid these goals and give a bike that aims to give compliance and tracking. Naturally, it being an Orange, this bike started and sheets of aluminium before being folded into shape. They then seam-weld the joins and CNC the suspension. The bike has healthy chainstay and downtube protection, and a UDH hanger. There isn't a flip chip, but there are aftermarket links available to tweak the geometry ever so slightly and give the option of a 5mm higher bottom bracket and a half-a-degree steeper head tube angle. Geometry There is no one perfect geometry for perfect mountain biking for perfect people in perfect places. It doesn't exist, and if you think there is, you've been putting something a bit extra in your Yorkshire tea. Geometry is exciting and rewarding to discuss because it's about compromise. The important thing, for me, is considering what the bike aims to do, and from there, consider elements of weight distribution, rider confidence, comfort and ease of use. This is a big, metal, burly enduro bike. To my mind, that means it should be stable, confidence-inspiring and able to rip steep, fast trails while also rewarding riders on slower, techier sections. The Orange has a very healthy stack of 646mm. This puts it on the upper end of the mainstream in terms of height. That front end has a 63-degree head angle. These two dimensions will give the rider a high and stable front at speed but could threaten to give something that suffers from a handful of slop at slower speeds and struggles to feel positive on the front when leaning the bike. To counter this, the bike has a 448mm chainstay. This will help claw back some of the weight onto the front wheel. That said, any time we think about weight distribution, it's worth considering that more important than any geometry figure will be the gradient of the trail we are riding. The bike has a 485mm reach on the size large I tested. A consequence of this, especially when coupled with the 76-degree seat tube angle, is that it has a large effective top tube. While stack can offset the sensation of the bike getting too long to a degree, it is worth looking out for should you like a compact seated climbing position. Suspension Design Although this isn't the first multi-link bike from Orange, it's still got a large swingarm that's hard to ignore; at first glance, it might appear to be the same design that you've become most accustomed to in recent years. However, if you look a little closer you will see their Power Link Driven Suspension. This is essentially a linkage-driven single pivot. As the bike goes into its travel, the swing arm is connected to the middle of a lever. That lever, at one end, is attached to the frame and, on the other shock eyelet. There is something so satisfying to watch and enjoy about the subtle design, which almost seems to open up as you cycle the bike. Something less satisfying, however, is the difficulty you'll have in bolt-checking the hardware. This design allows them more freedom in choosing how they want the suspension to feel, as well as giving less drivetrain interaction. The bike has a relatively low pivot to try and keep the rider's feet neutral. Anti-squat numbers for the Switch 7 in the 32/52t gearing start at 130%, falling to 105% at full travel. This bike could be a good home for something like an O-Chain if you find yourself riding high-speed, rough trails and want a lesser degree of influence on the suspension. That said, the 120% at sag will aim to give a bike that is pedal-efficient, at least. In terms of the leverage ratio, it reduces from 2.85 to 2.10, which equates to 26.3% progression, a number that should allow the bike to work with both air and coil shocks. Specifications Price $8300 Travel 170mm (rear), 170mm (front) Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Factory 205x65 Fork Fox Factory 38 Float 170mm 29 Headset FPD 44mm / 56mm zerostack Cassette Shimano XT 10-51T 12spd Crankarms SRAM GX Eagle 32t Chainguide E13 Vario Compact Guide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Rear Derailleur Shimano XT 12spd Chain KMC 12spd Shifter Pods Shimano XT 12spd Handlebar Renthal FatBar M35 800mm Stem Burgtec Enduro Black 42.5mm Grips Strange Grappler Lock-On Brakes Shimano XT Trail 203/180mm Wheelset Stans Flow Mk4 + Hope Pro 4 (Black) 29 + 27 Hubs Hope Pro 4 (Black) Rim Stans Flow Mk4 Tires Maxxis Minion DHF 2.5 3c EXO+ 29 / DHR II 2.4 3c EXO+ 27.5 Seat SDG Strange Bel Air III Seatpost Fox Factory Transfer 175mm Drop Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Orange Switch 7 Test Bike Setup This bike featured a mixture of components that worked well but might be a little odd job for those who enjoy having complete drivetrain or cockpit setups from one brand. That said, all the dimensions, save for the seat post, were well chosen and really melded well with the geometry to give a bike that was easy to feel comfortable, safe and fast on right out of the box. Henry Quinney Location: Squamish Height: 183cm / 6' Inseam: 82 cm / 32.5" Weight: 79 kg / 174 lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @henryquinney One thing that I didn't like, however, is the tires. I'm sorry, internet, I won't let this go. Somebody who spends this much money on an enduro bike shouldn't be happy they survive with 900-gram tires. The tire choice should also be about unlocking and playing into the performance of the bike. Should all bikes come with the heaviest tires in the world? No. But Maxxis make DoubleDown casing tires for a reason. Appropriately, I sliced a tire the second time riding the bike, which left me as the main course for the midge's supper as I walked back to the pits in Fort William at the World Cup. As mentioned previously, the 170mm post was too short. I installed a 210mm in size large frame, slammed it to the collar and was very, very happy. Again, this expense shouldn't fall on the customer. Testing Info This test bike started its time with me in Europe when I went to the UK. After that, it's been thrashing about Squamish and Whistler. It's been the home for many testing products, including the embargoed wheels that you see in the photos that we thought would be released by now. This bike has been the ride I would often reach for over these summer months and was the perfect bike for the Zeb vs. 38 video earlier in the year. That said, my highlight of the test period may well be riding this bike in Scotland on Top Chief at Fort William. The rock-strewn trail just felt so aggro and matched the character of the Orange. Climbing As mentioned previously, the Switch is a big bike to hop onto. You get used to it, and fast, but I did find myself shuffling the saddle forward slightly, Should you run a high saddle height, the long seatube that continues all the way to the BB will mean that you're not exponentially punished with a slack seat tube angle like you are with some bikes that have very slack actual angles. At 183 cm tall, the 645 top tube fit, but only just. Going between test bikes, it often felt that bit larger than anything else I would ride. The bike is firm underfoot when you're putting power through the pedals. That said, it still has a degree of bob. Although I didn't, you could well find yourself reaching for the climb switch if you wanted to calm the rear down on smoother roads. Although the anti-squat is fairly high, the high progression gives a bike that is, in some ways, caught between two minds. At high cadence, it pedals well, and if you can time your strokes and be smooth with your inputs, you'll also have a bike that is an adequate technical climber. That said, if you want to throw your weight around the bike, the soft initial stroke undermines that energetic feel and sends you into the travel. There does feel to be a lot of bike in front of you, and even though the long rear-end offers a good degree of stability, it doesn't have that glued-to-ground feeling on the front wheel that some shorter bikes with steeper effective top tubes can offer. Descending Descending on the Orange, in a word, is raw. Raw, unfettered fun. It offers this strange collision of old and new school that is charming in its own way. It's got the noise, the tautness and the energy a lot of bikes would be envious of, but it also has the forgiving and useable geometry that represents the new school of bike progression. The suspension gives so much support while still offering a great deal of tracking and grip. For those that want the single most small bump compliance, this isn't it. For those that want something that forces its wheel into the ground as opposed to sticking to it, you'll be very happy. That does come undone, to some extent, on braking on steep, rough chutes. The wheel begins to interfere with your feet a lot, and you can feel in real-time the wrestling match between the contact patch wanting to find grip and your feet resisting the suspension's cycle. This interaction also comes to the fore on fast bike park chunder. Medium-speed natural trails? Not so much. Holding on for your life as your bike skims over the top of endless high-frequency compressions? Yes, you're going to notice that. For that reason, I would say that this bike isn't the best for bike parks, but you do get used to it very quickly and I wouldn't cross it off your list for that alone. When braking hard through compressions, your wheel can come into harm's way. It seems like deep in the stroke on repetitive large hits; the energy has a tendency to go to the rim instead of pushing you yet further into the travel. It doesn't feel like you hang up, but your wheels may well pay the price. Ultimately, this bike truly shines on the trails you dream of riding. It matches the character of the 38 perfectly, especially the new GripX2 damper. There's something about this bike that just manages to offer a huge amount of composure, and while not the single most sensitive, it is probably one of the most predictable and manageable suspension feels you could hope to find. It never seems to get overwhelmed, and the more you give it, the more it thrives. In corners, this bike has a surprisingly strong turn-in, and thanks to that high front, it gives you that PMBI-approved stance - level feet, eyes up, elbows pointed and your mass square in the middle of the bike. When driving hard, the level of flex through the bike is also in a really nice range. It's not so stiff that it isolates the swing of your weight away from the turn, but at the same time, it's not so soft that you feel like you're falling through the bike. It's very consistent and tracks cambers nicely. When charging hard, the large tubes do seem to carry some vibration through the bike, especially at the front. While not feeling the vibrations at the rear, you'll certainly be hearing them. In fact, so will your friends and anyone within a nearby town. This thing is loud, and the noise seems to use the metal frame as a megaphone to announce your approach to the world. All in all, I loved riding this bike, and in a world of mass-produced carbon, it's refreshing to ride a bike with so much character. Orange Switch 7 Atherton S.170 How Does It Compare? Two British mountain bikes. Both are metal, both have a swingarm, and both will set hearts ablaze if you grew up with MBUK and celebrated the introduction of Sealskinz socks with genuine earnestness. That might be wear the similarities end, though. The Atherton feels like a sophisticated suspension layout packaged into undeserving geometry. The Orange has great geometry and, in fairness, very good suspension feels, yet it is undermined by the single pivot's age-old enemy - the bicycle chain. On medium-speed trails, that can give you a taut, energetic feel that combines with the geometry to give something that feels more creative and responsive than the Atherton, despite it being a slacker. That said, when trying to conserve wheels or slow down on steeper, rougher chutes, the Atherton's DW-link ultimately delivers far more performance. Which Model is the Best Value? This bike is handmade in the UK, that said, it's still prohibitively expensive. I loved riding this frame, and I find it hard to recommend anything based on value, with their propositions of full builds being either £7,400 or £5,800. I would explore options of buying the frame only and imagine you could get a whole new bike from a direct-to-sales brand for less than £4,000, which to donate parts on before selling the new unused frame. Unorthodox, but I genuinely don't know what else to say. The bike has a great spec, save for some key dimensions Technical Report Tires and Dropper: This is the other bike that recently inspired an op-ed where I couldn't understand or justify the poor spec choices. Again, short droppers and thin tires have no place on bikes this capable or gravity focused. I put a 210mm OneUp post on for testing and was all the happier for it. Shimano XT Drivetrain & Brakes: I'm always happy whenever I get a mechanical groupset on an enduro bike. The shift of the XT is still fantastic, and I love the feel of the brakes. The rear rotor could be larger, but will give enough power and heat management for most people. Fox 38: The 38 has an amazing ability to mix precision with tracking and sensitivity with a high-ride height. This matched the character of the Orange perfectly. The bike felt so matched front-to-back, which only means it inspires more confidence. Editors Note - the photographed spec doesn't feature the stock wheels, tires or dropper. This is down to a mixture of personal preference, as well as the bike serving as a test mule for other parts. Pros + Balanced + Capable descender + Superb suspension feel + Excellent Geometry Cons - Not the most efficient at braking - Noisey - Pedal feedback Pinkbike's Take The Orange Switch is the old-school answer to a modern question: how to make the enduro bike that is both fast and easy to just get on a ride? The high front is stable, and the slack head angle gives you a lot of security, but it's the balance that this bike achieves that will really put a smile on your face. The effective top tube can give a stretched climbing position, and it doesn't feel the most efficient, but if you want to charge the descents with a suspension platform that will reward you for your hard work and inputs, then this should be on your list. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Orange Bikes Orange Switch 7 Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: 2023 Orbea Oiz M-Pro - Don't Call it Downcountry
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: REVIEW Orbea Oiz M-Pro Orbea's marketing materials for the Oiz make it clear that it wasn't designed to be a downcountry bike or a trail bike. It's a purebred, modern XC machine, where outright speed is the overarching goal.
