Compared to the EP8 motor, the EP801 is said to be improved in nearly every way, except the weight, which remains unchanged at 2.7kg. Those improvements are claimed to be better reliability and heat management, as well as more responsive and natural engagement. Shimano also says that when the motor is activated, the noise emitted is lower. Like nearly all motors, the EP801 can be tuned with an app. In this case it’s the Shimano E-Tube. It’s fairly straightforward to navigate and there’s no shortage of adjustments to tweak. On the trail, I did find increased motor sensitivity and power delivery compared to the EP8, however, the claimed difference in the noise level is less apparent. The clutch still rattles while descending, but that too has been toned down marginally. Suspension Design Propain’s Pro10 suspension design is a compact configuration of counter-rotating links that compresses the shock from either end. Before diving into the kinematics of this arrangement, it’s worth noting that this allows for a steep, uninterrupted seat tube, low standover height and frees up space inside the front triangle for a water bottle. Propain’s bikes are known for pedaling well with high levels of anti-squat that are retained through to the sag point. The Sresh is no different as that value remains above 100% for the majority of the travel, at least with the 34-50-tooth gear combo listed. The kinematics curves look unorthodox but the ride quality is surprisingly normal, in a positive way. A 210x55mm standard mount shock is used on the Sresh. It’s both air and coil shock compatible too, as displayed across the build kits. The leverage rate produced starts on the linear side but ramps up gradually towards the end. As for the axle path, it’s basically vertical when you zoom out. It moves just a couple millimeters rearward early on and then returns forwards at bottom out. Geometry & Sizing Four frame sizes is a standard spread for high-end carbon bikes. By Propain’s suggestions, each frame would fit a rider as such; small: 158-172 cm, medium: 168-182, large: 178-192, and extra large: 188-202. It makes sense why Propain kept the angles of the Sresh more conservative - it’s not meant to be a big bruiser. For that, there's the Ekano model with 170mm of rear wheel travel and a 64-degree head tube angle. A 65-degree head tube angle gives hints at the downhill capacity of the Sresh. There’s also a steep 78.5-degree seat tube angle and 447mm (450 - 29”) chainstays across all sizes. Aside from the flip-chip to toggle between rear wheel sizes (which keeps the geometry virtually identical), there are no further adjustments. Specifications Price $7529 Travel 150 Rear Shock RockShox Vivid Ultimate RC2T Air Fork RockShox ZEB Ultimate 160 mm Charger 3 Headset Acros Cassette SRAM XG-1275 Eagle Crankarms Shimano FC-EM600 165mm 34t Bottom Bracket Shimano EP801 Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle 12 spd Chain SRAM GX Eagle 12 spd Shifter Pods SRAM GX Eagle 12 spd Handlebar SixPack Millenium 805 Stem SIXPACK Vertic 50 Grips Ergon GD1 Evo Slim Brakes Formula Cura 4, 220 F/ 203 R rotors, organic pads Wheelset Newman Evolution E.G.30 Tires Maxxis Assegai 29x2.5", MaxxGrip, EXO+ / Maxxis DHR II 27.5x2.4", MaxxTerra, DoubleDown Seat Ergon SM Enduro Comp Men Seatpost OneUp Dropper V3 180mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Sresh Test Bike Setup Finding my ideal setup for the Sresh seemed involved at first glance but turned out to be pretty easy. The thought of messing with the stem height, given the internal routing in that area, seemed involved to say the least. Thankfully, Propain’s clever two-piece spacer system means you don’t have to mess with any of the cables or housing. Taller riders may be concerned with the low-rise bar and the steer tube being cut on the short side, however, you could always track down a taller bar. Setting up the suspension didn’t involve anything out of the ordinary, however, the wide sag range could misguide some riders. Propain suggests setting the sag anywhere from 20 to 30%. I settled on the larger number, preferring to let the shock sit deeper in the travel for a more relaxed ride on steeper trails. That meant 205 psi in the Vivid Ultimate. Up front, I ranged between 83-85 psi in the 160mm Zeb fork. Using all 150mm of travel seemed to happen more frequently than I liked and closing the hydraulic bottom out (HBO) took the sting out of those impacts but was too abrupt. Adding two tokens and backing off the HBO two clicks balanced out the firmness of the end stroke. The air pressure remained the same while the rebound was set to -17 (from closed). On the compression side, the high-speed compression adjuster was set to 1 and the low-speed to 0 or -1 (0 being the middle position on this damper type) depending on the trail type and grade. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 38 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r We opted for a size large frame with a 27.5” rear wheel. I did try the 29" configuration, and as expected, that improved stability and bump-roll over capacity. Reverting to the MX wheel combo for the majority of the test improved the maneuverability, especially when needing to move more weight over the back of the bike. I was tempted to bump the fork travel to 170mm in order to take more weight off of the front wheel, but that’s not within the Sresh’s limit - 160mm is its maximum fork length. Download the Trailforks App Colder, wetter days in autumn are prime e-bike season. Typical rides would involve a road climb to access the upper Diamondhead trails in Squamish for longer descents when conditions were dry and dusty at the end of August. Mid-September brought ideal dirt for hitting fresher trails, but then heavy rains ensued. Then, touring the maze of rock features in the Alice Lake zone seemed like the wisest plan. Climbing Assistance aside, the Sresh is a very competent climber. The bike eggs you on to drive through uphill corners and doesn't send you to a back seated position. Unwanted wheelies can happen in the blink of an eye with some e-bikes, but the Sresh naturally avoids those with a steep seat tube angle of 78.5 degrees and a mellow 65-degree head tube angle. The low stock bar height promotes keeping that weight forward and the rider more engaged. Propain is onto something with their Pro10 suspension, particularly for e-bikes. A lot of that comes down to the geometry, but the suspension also plays a large helping hand, so much so that the climb switch was rarely used. The Sresh rarely faults on technical climbs due to how the suspension stays active (that’s with the climb switch open) without caving under power. This allows the rear wheel to hug the ground and the bottom bracket to remain high enough such that crank strikes aren’t an issue. A typical ride would start with a gravel road climb on Boost mode for the flatter sections, then switch to Trail mode for the steeper grades, and limp home with the remaining battery life in the single digits. I found that to be the best method to conserve battery. It was rare that I’d squeeze out more than 1100m of vertical accent out of the 626Wh battery. If you’re looking to extend your ride, you’ll have to purchase a secondary main battery ($599.99 USD) - currently the Sresh is not compatible with any range extenders. Descending You might think that the lower weight and medium amount of travel might make the Sresh a more maneuverable bike, and it is, as long as the wheels can stay on the ground. The seat tube height isn't the shortest and the steep angle can make it difficult to move the bike around in the air. Getting the front wheel in the air takes a fair bit of effort. In fact, the chainstay feels longer than they are and that tends to push the front wheel down. That can be a good thing though, since understeering rarely happens on shallow-angle berms. Plunking down lines usually tackled on larger enduro bikes takes a bit of commitment, but can be done with precision. Moving behind the bike in safe position while descending and on rough fade-away corners, you have to hold on tighter to keep the wheel straight. Thankfully, the Sresh’s geometry isn’t disrupted under braking because placing more weight further forward wouldn't be desirable. The rear wheel grips exceptionally well on off-camber sections of trail and handles small impacts without hanging up. It also buffs out larger, consecutive hits fairly smoothly too. You’ll know when you use all of the travel though. That can happen a little sooner as the ramp up can be abrupt. Modifying the spacers in the Vivid Air helped reduce that “clunk” to a “thud”. I also installed a Cane Creek Tigon - the coil shock with a pneumatic chamber - which worked equally as well. On one hand, Propain has made the Sresh fairly light and MX wheel compatible, which makes you think it might be ideal for playing about on mellow terrain, but it’s difficult to feel like you’re not kissing the stem. If they wanted a faster handling bike, limiting the rear wheel to a 27.5" and shortening the chainstays might have been a wiser move. I'm not opposed to the 65-degree head tube angle but having the option to run a 170mm fork would make the Sresh less nervous on rowdier trails. What's the Best Value? Despite the carbon frame, this isn’t a super-light e-bike and that means you’ll appreciate parts that can cope with that extra weight. In that case, I’d look at the Ultimate Enduro or Factory builds, mainly for their stiffer fork chassis. We’re looking at value, though, and that’s where the Ultimate Enduro package wins out. It’s substantially less, yet doesn’t skimp on performance. When comparing the slimmer Sresh builds, a RockShox Lyrik is a sensible choice for a non-assisted all-mountain bike, but an eMTB? I’d rather take the Zeb’s stout chassis. It’s down for whatever the Sresh can handle. Relatively, you’re not adding much weight - we’re talking about about 430g difference in the comparative suspension packages of the Base and Ultimate Enduro. You’ll also receive stronger brakes and more adjustable suspension over the Base model. That includes the hydraulic bottom out support from the Vivid Ultimate shock. Propain Sresh CF Devinci E-Troy GX LTD How Does It Compare? A close contender in the 150mm rear wheel travel range is the Devinci E-Troy. Its travel figure, maximum torque, and battery size are matched but that’s about where the similarities end. One of the largest numbers to swallow is the 6-pound difference in their overall system weights. Devinci’s pledge to build Canadian-made aluminum frames may factor into that. The E-Troy is confined to a 27.5” rear wheel, whereas the Sresh can also accept either rear wheel size. Neither bike has further geometry adjustments though. On the flip side, the E-Troy frame is rated for either a 160 or 170mm fork. The longer option is actually included in the GX LTD model. Their trailside manners and riding stances are polar opposite to each other as well. The Sresh’s forte is rolling terrain and flat corners as the rider is coaxed into a low, forward position. Its supportive pedaling platform rewards dynamic inputs on flow trails and tricky uphills. Contrasting those habits, the E-Troy doesn’t flinch at the sight of a rock roll or steep, rutted corner. Simply push the bars into the right line and the rest of the bike will follow. Tech climbs didn’t come as effortlessly as on the Sresh though - you'll need to mind where the front wheel ends up due to the slacker head angle, while also watching out for pedal strikes from the less supportive suspension. EP8 and some updates make this into the EP801 motor. That brings along more assistance and improved motor responses. Newman wheels, carbon or aluminum, have yet to let us down. Straight-pull spokes do take some patience to true. These Formula Cura 4 brakes came with large, thick rotors (220 and 203mm) for the demands of an e-bike, but the organic pads had to go. In terms of noise pollution on the trail, it is a shame that the brake pads and motor caused some distractions because the Sresh is otherwise a very quiet bike. The inside of the rear triangle is well padded against chain slap, the battery is bolted down, and the cables use solid, albeit, interesting routing. Shimano have not completely worked out how to rid the motor of that knocking clutch though. Technical Report Formula Cura 4 Brakes: It’s not often that you see Formula brakes arrive on a complete bike. We gave them a positive review in 2023 . Specifying thicker, large diameter rotors was a smart choice for an e-bike. In order to optimize the power on tap and reduce fading, we switched from organic to metallic pads. Unfortunately, we couldn’t eliminate the pads rattling in the calipers (something we didn’t experience when we reviewed them separately). Upgrading to their “Two-Piece” lever will also give a more progressive power delivery and ergonomic lever blade. Newman Evolution E.G.30 Wheels: Another less common sight, at least in North America, are these Newman wheels. Their hubs have very low drag and are nearly silent while coasting. The aluminum rims have shaken off some cringe-inducing hits, however we did have one particular spoke lose tension a few times. That wasn’t such a big issue - it was more the aggravation of the spoke spinning with the nipple while trying to re-tension the wheel. Shimano EP801 Motor: Critiquing eMTB motors can be a can of worms to navigate. There are so many factors to consider when comparing their range; tires, conditions, gear selection, etc all come into play. I was surprised to rarely squeeze out more than 1100m of climbing on the EP801 motor and a 626Wh battery. To compare, the Specialized Levo SL with a range extender could pull off the same lap, although at a slower climbing pace. Overall, the motor pick-up and audible assistance are considerable upgrades from the EP8. There’s still that pesky rattling from the motor clutch though. Pros + Climbs exceptionally well + Excellent value + Secure, yet removable battery Cons - Seat tube angle and length may limit maneuverability - Strong forward weight bias brings hesitation on steep trails - Rattling motor clutch and brake pads detract from ride quality Pinkbike's Take Propain’s Sresh is a full-powered eMTB that checks in at a reasonable weight without breaking the bank. The handling will suit someone who wants a more even-keeled ride rather than one solely focusing on descending. All that comes in a sharp looking, neatly laid out frame (maybe too neat for some). Most importantly, the external battery is neatly and securely bolted into the downtube. Although some functions have been improved with the EP801 motor, Shimano is still playing catch up to other major players. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos eMTB Reviews Trail Bikes Propain Propain Sresh Cf Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Propain Sresh SL Review: A Mid-Power eMTB That Doesn’t Ride Like a Tank
author: Matt Beer
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, trail-bikes, propain-bicycles, propain-sresh-sl
article_text: Propain Sresh SL Final https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/NwrCUUjS Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 17:21 Mammoth-sized e-bikes can rip up hills with batteries that outlast most riders’ agendas, but there’s something more familiar about the handling of a lighter “SL” eMTB. That’s where the Propain Sresh SL fits in perfectly - it’s able to tag along with full-powered rigs, yet doesn’t ride like a tank. With a lightweight carbon frame, 150mm of travel, and a choice of either rear wheel size, its user selling points position it as the perfect happy-medium e-bike. At the center of that equation is TQ’s motor, the HPR60. It embodies the quiet operation and natural power delivery that we’ve come to appreciate from the HPR50. As a consumer-direct brand, Propain offers complete and semi-custom build kits. This includes picking out your own paint and logo colors, but also modifying suspension, drivetrain, and battery sizes. Their sales model is appealing with aggressive pricing, however, you can go wild by choosing boutique parts, like the Trickstuff Dirrettissima brakes on our $10,193 USD package. Sresh SL Details • 150 mm travel, 160-170 mm fork • Carbon frame • Motor: TQ HPR60 - 350 W peak power, 60 Nm torque • Battery: 360, 580 Wh (160 Wh range extender avail.) • MX or dual 29" wheels • Dual-link suspension design • 63.6-64.1° head angle (64.5° w/dual 29") • 77.1-78° seat tube angle • Chainstay length: 450 mm (MX) • Sizes: S, M, L (tested), XL • Weight: 20.1 kg / 44.3 lb (as tested) • Price: $10,193 USD (as tested) • More info: propainbikes.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Motor System Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Technical Report Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The low noise and sensible power of the TQ HPR60 make the Sresh SL one of the most pleasant e-bikes I’ve ridden. — Matt Beer Like the Sresh, but less power and weight. The only frame adjustment on the Sresh SL. Fun fact: you can actually ride the 27.5" rear wheel in the 29" setting for a super slack setup. The housings can go through or around the head tube. Frame Details Bearing the same base name, the Sresh SL has the same trail manners as the full-powered Sresh - each bike has 150mm travel, MX wheel capability, and is only available in carbon fiber, even the geometry numbers are only a shade apart - the motor and battery size are primarily what distinguish the two. With those weight-reducing components, the Sresh SL positions itself as a competitive player in the mid-power e-bike category. Pound for watt-hours, it's on par with the Specialized Levo SL. Granted the Sresh SL can be built up lighter, the full-powered Amflow PL Carbon weighs a few hundred grams less than the bike we have in for review. It begs the question, what's the point of these so-called "SL" e-bikes? A few years ago I would have told you that SLs descend better, but since Avinox (DJI) debuted their industry-leading power-to-weight ratio motor on the Amflow PL Carbon, I'm not so sure. Propain rates the Sresh SL as a category 4 frameset, greenlighting it for enduro riding and mid-size bike park features. The rest of the Sresh SL carries many of Propain bike’s features along for the ride, such as the Pro 10, dual-link suspension design, optional internal headset routing, and a flip-chip at the upper seatstay pivot for changing rear wheel diameters. When writing up the First Ride report, I found that my position rode heavily on the front wheel. After chatting with Propain, they suggested installing a 170mm to raise the front end, an option that they approved in the frame specifications later on. Now, when you scroll through their online build configurator, you can select burlier enduro forks like the Rock Shox Zeb and Fox 38 in that burlier travel bracket. TQ's juicy little HPR 60 motor. With the turn of a bolt and one latch, the battery slides out of the downtube. There's no need to unbolt the motor. A sleek and simple screen. Motor System Building on the success of the TQ’s first E-bike motor, Propain chose the quiet and compact HPR60. The torque has been bumped up to 60 Nm and 350 W of power, while maintaining virtually identical packaging to the original HPR50 model (50 Nm, 300 W). Molded into the top tube is TQ's own LED screen. A single button controls the power; a quick press turns the motor on or adjusts the assist mode, while a longer five-second hold shuts the bike down. The motor assist can also be adjusted from the wired handlebar remote. Although it’s slim in nature, the angle at which the wire exits the remote caused conflict with the Trickstuff brake lever. One of the largest factors that dictates a e-bike’s weight is the battery. Propain offers the size MD, LG, and XL frames with two battery options, 360 and 580 Wh. Due to the length of the downtube, the 580 Wh battery won't fit in the size small frame. To make up the difference, you can attach the 160 Wh range extender. The custom mount replaces the water bottle cage, so you’ll have to find other ways to carry H2O. If you really want to minimize the weight on board, you could remove the downtube battery and use just the range extender. Battery Weights: 580 Wh = 2,695 g 360 Wh = 1,835 g 160 Wh Range Extender = 960 g Suspension Design Pro 10 is the sole suspension design used across Propain’s mountain bikes (excluding their kid’s bikes). The solid rear triangle rotates on two short co-rotating links. The swingarm has one vertical brace on the non-driveside and allows for easy shock removal. A cable guide attached to the lower link keeps the brake line articulating smoothly. The Sresh SL meshes with a variety of air or coil shocks from Fox, Ohlins, and RockShox. Some of Propain’s longer travel bikes use a trunnion mount, but the Sresh SL runs on a 210x55mm stroke shock. There are flip-chips to alter the travel or leverage rate on the frame, though. Most suspension designs feature a leverage rate curve with an upwards-facing parabola, but the Propain bikes have a downwards-facing parabola. Those links move in such a way to produce a moderately progressive rate, but more recognizably, an increase in anti-squat partway into the travel. The Sresh SL's leverage curve. 0 seconds of 12 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/ybmkcwaQ Copied Live 00:00 00:12 00:12 Four frame sizes, three geometry settings, two wheel sizes, and one flip-chip. Geometry & Sizing Propain sticks to a certain identity across all its bikes. They’re following along with the trend of lengthening rear center numbers, pushing seat tube angles forward, and increasing stack heights. This promotes ample front wheel grip and an upright climbing position. Typically, Propain builds four frame sizes, each separated by 30mm jumps in reach. The Sresh SL is available in SM, MD, LG, and XL, but the gaps between frame sizes are not evenly distributed. The LG comes in with a reach of 473mm (high MX position) and the MD at 448mm. Luckily, the small is still “small” at 423mm, but super-tall riders may be left in the cold as the XL tops out at 498mm. All chainstays measure in at 450mm, regardless of frame size. At the top of the seatstay lies a flip-chip in the pivot to fit a 27.5 or 29” rear wheel, but be careful. The same geometry won’t be retained. With a 27.5” rear wheel installed in the high mode, the head tube angle sits at 63.8. The effective seat tube lands at 77.4 while the chainstays stretch to 450mm. Putting the Sresh SL in the low setting kicks the head angle back to 63.6 degrees. A 29” rear wheel in the low setting results in the steepest head angle possible at 64.5 degrees. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $10193 Travel 150 Rear Shock Fox Float X Factory, 210x55mm Fork Fox 36 Float Factory, 160mm, GripX2 Headset Acros ZS56 / ZS66 Cassette SRAM X0 Eagle Transmission Crankarms Praxis EC-3 Carbon 160 mm Bottom Bracket TQ HPR 60 Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM X0 Eagle Transmission Shifter Pods SRAM AXS POD Controller Handlebar OneUp Carbon 35 mm Rise Stem Sixpack Millenium 35 Grips Ergon GDH Team Brakes Trickstuff Diretissima, 203mm rotors F&R Wheelset DT Swiss EX 1700 Tires Schwalbe Magic Mary 29x2.5", UltraSoft, Radial Gravity front, Schwalbe Albert 27.5x2.5", Soft, Radial Gravity Seat Ergon SM Enduro Comp Seatpost Fox Transfer Kashima 180mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC I'd prefer to have the motor assist remote rotated further down, but the lever reach dial from the Trickstuff brakes interferes. SRAM Transmission drivetrains are ready for your poor shifting habits. Test Bike Setup Propain sent over this custom-built Sresh SL with a SRAM Transmission shiftset, Fox Factory suspension, DT Swiss EX 1700 mixed wheels, and Schwalbe Radial Gravity tires. Overall, it’s quite extravagant with the shiny Trickstuff brakes, however, I did run into a few issues outlined in the First Ride. A 2026 Fox 36 resolved the air spring issues with the previous fork and allowed me to leave more steer tube for a higher bar height. A good chunk of time was spent on the 160mm fork before switching to a 170mm travel fork as advised by Propain. A Formula Selva V fork, which was also in for a review, fit the bill in terms of travel. Pressures in the rear shock hovered around 190 psi. That number gave a dead even 17mm of sag, or 30%. The shock tune in the Float X provided was CMSSB024/RM002 with a 0.1 cubic inch volume spacer installed. The rebound was set five clicks from closed and the compression only four from closed. I mainly rode the 180m post with 50mm of usable post exposed. I don’t need a 200mm dropper post, but I think an extra 20mm would have helped lower the saddle out of the way in some scenarios. Aside from that, the 35mm rise handlebar was cut to my usual 770mm width with 20mm of spacers under the stem. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 39 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Download the Trailforks App I enjoyed riding the Sresh SL in the High motor assist setting the most, and found that it didn’t chew up the battery overly enthusiastically. 1.5-2 hour rides in that mode would get me home with 20-40% battery left. Most of the routes I picked off would be considered Squamish classics, without going too far into the deep end of the stunt pool. Climbing I was beginning to get worried there for a minute; we hadn’t seen much activity from TQ since they made a splash with their HPR50. Thankfully, they’ve stuck it out and are still setting the bar high. The HPR60 retains a compact size and silence under power. It glides up the hill, undetected by anyone not listening closely. On paper, the boost in power and torque to the HPR60 doesn’t sound massive, but on the trail, it’s noticeable. Putting the HPR60 in the highest assist still won’t match the capabilities of most full-powered e-bikes, but that’s not the HPR60’s full intent. Instead, it’s all about smooth, natural power delivery. In that respect, it never puts out an overwhelming burst or cuts the power early. I’d give it a gold star for being top of its class. A pesky groan somewhere in the motor’s bearings developed early on. It only creaked occasionally under extreme side loads or max pedal efforts, though. I inspected the motor mounting bolts and crank arm interface, but never got to the bottom of it. That experience hasn’t been consistent with my time previously spent on TQ equipment or other riders I have spoken with, so I’d still rate the overall experience high. TQ does stands behind their product with a two-year warranty on both the motor and battery systems. As far as the Sresh SL geometry and suspension go, the ride is a familiar space after spending time on other Propain models. The seat tube angle is steep, and the chainstays are long enough to keep the front wheel from lifting on any climbs possible with the Sresh SL. I find the seated position and rear end length settle down the need to redistribute body weight when you point up a steep pitch. Another plus is that the front wheel doesn’t understeer when turning into switchbacks. Sometimes a steep seat tube angle can put unwanted pressure on the rider's hands, but that’s not a major concern on the Sresh SL - the angle isn’t too extreme. A 120mm head tube length on the size large frame and 35mm bar option helps the case here. Under power, the suspension holds itself up in the travel well. Like all of Propain’s bikes designed for pedalling uphill, the shock orientation places those handy lockout levers close to the lower link. Flipping that on the fly means pulling off a yoga stretch, or more safely, stopping and waiting for spinning components to come to a stop. Range When it comes to the overall experience of riding e-bikes, I believe we’re getting close to an ideal power-to-weight ratio. The 580 Wh battery matches the other efforts of the Sresh SL. It doesn’t ride like there is a bag of bricks strapped to the downtube, and there’s enough juice to clock 2000m of climbing, or more, if you can resist using the higher assist modes. If you’re keen to ride for an extended time, there’s always the 740 Wh battery option (excluding the size small frame) or the range extender. Descending Overall, the Sresh SL checks a lot of boxes for what I look for in a mid-travel, mid-power eMTB. The motor is light, the weight distribution feels dialled, and it descends quietly. Between the battery and rear-wheel options, its adaptability is high. Propain made a wise choice to extend the fork limits. As expected, a longer 170mm fork and taller 50mm bars helped retract some of my initial impressions. It’s hard for me to put pen to paper to describe why I didn’t totally get along with the descending characteristics of the Sresh SL. Mainly, I found the rear suspension to dive into the first half of the travel. Even after bumping up the air pressure to achieve 26% sag, and slowing down the compression, an underlying wallowy trait followed. Effectively, it can help absorb small bumps and give excellent grip when you aren’t forcibly weighting the bike. The downside is that the suspension suddenly pushes through the travel when trying to pump through rhythms on the trail or boost extra high off of jump faces. To me, that “hinging effect” the Pro 10 suspension exhibits governs the dynamic balance of the bike. It’s similar to how I’d describe other FSR bikes with a top-tube-mounted swing link, a la Rocky Mountain or Ari, handle. The knock-on effect of that is when riding super steep trails, the pitch of the bike can change. Maybe it’s just a “me” problem, but I never felt 100% confident nosing into trails that you need the utmost confidence for. With that said, while riding more casually or on less daunting trails, the Sresh SL levels out nicely and is pleasant to ride. The rear triangle is on the softer side and follows the contours without getting knocked offline too easily. I’d also like to point out that there’s minimal chainslap and the motor clutch doesn’t rattle whatsoever. One smaller spec-check is the dropper post insertion length. The tube tapers near the bottom to give clearance in order for the shock to clear as the links compress towards each other. The Sresh SL arrived with a 180mm dropper and worked for me, just. Slamming that post leaves 40mm exposed. I’d wager that most riders choosing a size large would appreciate a 200mm dropper, which may pose limitations for riders seeking more drop. What's the Best Value? Consumer direct brands can sometimes offer better value. Where you live can influence the model availability and the landed cost, so watch for those added fees. More to the point, the Sresh SL only comes in two models in North America, versus the five options available in Germany. On our side of “the pond”, building your own custom Sresh SL starts at $5,999 USD, then the Signature Spec 1 and 2 add another grand each time. I figured I could ditch the SRAM GX AXS shifting for a Shimano XT mechanical combo and use the cash to include a longer 170mm Zeb Ultimate fork. The savings earned on the Magura MT5 brakes allow for a Fox DHX2 coil shock to be added to the mix. All in, the custom build sits at $7,759 USD, marginally cheaper than the Signature Spec 2. My custom build: RockShox Zeb Ultimate 170mm, Fox DHX2 Factory coil shock, Crankbrothers alloy Enduro MX wheels, Magura MT5 brakes, Shimano XT mechanical drivetrain, and a 580 Wh battery. Propain Sresh SL: $10,193 USD Specialized Levo SL S-Works: $14,000 USD How Does It Compare? The carbon finish and travel numbers are about all these two lightweight e-bikes have in common. They’re intended to cover the same types of trails, but their ride dynamics are very drastically different. The Sresh SL and most Propain bikes in general force the rider’s weight on the front wheel. It takes an assertive effort to ride further back on the bike and not let your weight influence the steering too much. Whereas a Levo SL takes the opposite approach: shorter chainstays require the rider to lean forward and push weight onto the bars wheel when riding low-angle trails, or else the front wheel can start to slide. The benefit is that the Levo SL is a whole lot easier to handle in tight corners and more relaxed on steep, surfy trails. Even though they’re both light for eMTBs, Sresh SL is much harder to play on when the trail does provide defined jumps. Again, I’d point partly towards the balance of the bike, but also the suspension. I found there to be a shade of wallowyness which can sap some energy when trying to press into rhythms on the trail or gaining some extra boost on jump faces. Comparing the weight-to-range is tricky because the Sresh SL’s stock battery is bigger (580 vs. 320 Wh). Ripping up to the top of the Diamondhead trail network gives me a solid reference point. On the Levo SL, I’d have to tone down the assist level to “Trail” in order to muster out 1000m, whereas the Sresh SL can almost double that in the “High” setting. A weight penalty of 2.3 kg (5.07 lb) has to be paid, but considering you can climb faster on in the “High” setting too, it makes the HPR60 motor a compelling player in the SL game. Trickstuff's Dirrettissima are smooth as hell, but tiring to live with. "Turn" of the Millenium problems. Solid rims, less dependable hub internals. It’s dead simple to work on, easy to find parts for, upgradeable and isn’t too buzzy. The rims have been stellar, but a stripped set of ratchets left me jaded, and stranded way out in the middle of a tech XC maze with a useless e-bike. I slapped in a 54-tooth ratchet ring after I limped my way out. If I were spec’ing a e-bike, I’d be looking for alternatives. Trickstuff Direttissima brakes: These are bougie brakes. They add $700 USD to your custom-built Sresh SL versus a set of SRAM Maven Silver brakes, but I’m not convinced they’re worth the extra cost. My main concern is that they’re challenging to keep running consistently. Storing the bike vertically will likely introduce air into the system. They also lack adjustment beyond lever reach, so setting the left and right lever to feel the same is nearly impossible, given how difficult they are to bleed. Fox 36 Factory Grip X2 fork: This is my first hoon on a 2026 36 fork, and boy does it ride swell. The level of grip is more than noticeable between the new and the old; it is drastic. The change from rebound to compressive is much less sticky, as is the breakaway into the travel - give the new Glide Core “floating” air spring a round of applause for that performance gain. Sixpack Millenium 35 stem: On two occasions, the stem twisted on the steer tube. It overlapped the steer tube completely and was tightened to spec with a torque wrench. Was it bad luck with the steer tube and stem tolerances added up on the low side? Who knows. Luckily, I wasn't moving fast during both of those instances. I swapped it out for another stem after that second scare. Pros + The TQ HPR60 is a standout performer in every way (quiet assistance, no rattles downhill, simple app). + System weight is impressive for its ride time. + Descends like an unassisted bike. Cons - Ride dynamics put a lot of weight on the front wheel. - Not the most supportive rear suspension. Pinkbike's Take Propain has summed up all the options that riders could want in an SL e-bike and packaged it nicely. Their Pro 10 suspension characteristics that bring a defined sense follow the Sresh SL, where comfort overrides support. If that floats your boat, then the TQ HPR60 make the Sresh SL a hot contender in the mid-power eMTB space. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review.
