title: Review: 2023 Propain Tyee CF
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: REVIEW Propain Tyee CF WORDS: Mike Kazimer ACTION PHOTOS: Eric Mickelson The Tyee has been in Propain's lineup for over a decade, where it sits as the brand's all-mountain / enduro option, positioned between the Hugene trail bike and Spindrift freeride machine. The most recent version launched earlier this year with revised geometry and frame updates designed to increase its strength and stiffness. Propain's online configurator means that there are numerous ways to build up a Tyee, but no matter what the final result will have 160mm of rear travel courtesy of Propain's PRO10 dual-link suspension design. The wheel size options vary depending on the frame size – sizes S – M are available with dual 27.5” wheels, and sizes M – XL are available with either a mixed-wheel or full 29” setup. Tyee Details • Wheel size: 29" or mixed (tested) • Carbon frame • Travel: 160mm (r) / 170mm fork • 64º head angle • 77.1º seat tube angle • 445mm chainstays (size L) • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • Weight: 33.9 lb / 15.4 kg (size L) • Price: $6,614 USD • propain-bikes.com For this review I was aboard the mixed wheel, carbon-framed option, with a 170mm fork. Build kit highlights include a RockShox Zeb Ultimate, a SuperDeluxe Ultimate shock, SRAM Code RSC brakes, a SRAM X0 T-type drivetrain, and Newmen Evolution SL A30 wheels. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take I'd describe its trail manners as energetically neutral - it's peppy enough to get airborne off smaller obstacles without a struggle, but the overall handling feel is calm and balanced. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The overall look of the Tyee hasn't changed much from the previous version – the shock still sits in front of the seat tube, where it's sandwiched between two short links. The main changes to the carbon frame were increased tire clearance, a lower standover height, and thru-headset cable routing. Two out of those three details are improvements, and the third, well, I'm still confused as to why it's a thing. Propain's take on thru-headset cable routing has the housing running underneath the front of the stem and through a rubber grommet; compared to the versions with ports in the headset top cap this should help keep water out. Stainless steel headset bearings are used to further reduce the amount of maintenance the headset will need. The frame's pivot bearings are also well sealed, with a secondary rubber grommet in place to prevent mud and grit from getting in. Bolts have been added to the underside of the top tube to hold a tube or tool, although it'll need to be a pretty tiny attachment, otherwise it'll hit the water bottle. New chainslap protection has been added that has raised hollow rubber rectangles to keep any noise to a minimum. The cable routing around the bottom bracket area was also updated – the derailleur and brake line now run next to the lower shock link, rather than underneath the frame. Geometry & Sizing The Tyee's multiple possible configurations means there are several different geometry accompanying geometry charts. The majority of my time was spent with the flip chip in the high position, the recommended setting for a mixed-wheel setup. That gives it a 64.1-degree head angle, 77.1-degree seat angle, and a 475mm reach for a size large with 445mm chainstays. Suspension Design The Tyee has a progressive leverage ratio curve that allows it to work well with either an air or coil shock. It's slightly less progressive than before, with a lower starting leverage ratio that puts the percent change at 21.9% rather than the 25.9% found on the previous model. The anti-squat level has been lower slightly too, and is now 113% at sag. That number increases for the first half of the Tyee's travel before dropping off sharply. Specifications Price $6614 Travel 160mm Rear Shock RockShox SuperDeluxe Ultimate Fork RockShox Zeb Ultimate 170mm Headset Acros ZS56 / ZS56 Cassette SRAM X0 Transmission, 10-52 tooth Crankarms SRAM X0 T-Type Rear Derailleur SRAM AXS X0 T-Type Chain SRAM X0 T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Handlebar Sixpack Millenium 805 (20mm rise) Stem SixPack Millenium 35 ICR Brakes SRAM Code RSC Wheelset Newman Evolution SL A30 Tires Maxxis Assegai / DHR II, DD rear, EXO+ front Seat SQLab Seatpost RockShox AXS Reverb, 170mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Tyee Test Bike Setup It's possible to build up the Tyee to be more of an all-mountain machine by selecting a 160mm fork and a lighter parts kit, or to push it more into the enduro realm with a 170mm fork. That's the option I went with, knowing that there were bike park laps and plenty of rough trails in its future. I tried the Tyee with both a SuperDeluxe air and coil, and ended up preferring the feel and adjustability of the air shock. 185 psi put me right at 30% sag. I ran 55 psi in the RockShox Zeb with half of a Bottomless Token installed for that Goldilocks 'just right' level of bottom out resistance. Propain included a 35mm and a 50mm SixPack stem for me to try, but unfortunately there's no 40mm option in SixPack's line, and the special headset spacers under the stem mean that it's not possible to swap it out for another brand's stem. I ended up switching out the headset top cap in order to be able to run the stem I wanted, a procedure that required undoing the rear brake line, further cementing my conviction that thru-headset cable routing is silly. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer I've had the Tyee in regular use for the last three months, and it's accompanied me on dry dusty laps in Pemberton, multiple days in the Whistler Bike Park, and on numerous rides around Bellingham, Washington. Climbing The efficiency of Propain's Pro10 suspension design is one of its hallmarks, and that trait continues with the Tyee. Propain did reduce the anti-squat slightly compared to the previous version, but that's done little to reduce its climbing abilities. There are enduro bikes out there that deliver more climbing traction (the new Ibis HD6 comes to mind), but there aren't many 160mm bikes that have the Tyee's level of climbing support. Even with a coil shock the back end remains impressively calm, and there's really no need to reach for the climb switch, unless you're looking for something to do while spinning up a smooth road. As far as the actual climbing position goes, the steep actual and effective seat tube angles create an upright and centered position. It seems like the race to create the steepest seat angle possible has subsided, with most long travel options settling in the 77-78 degree range, and for good reason. That number keeps the seated climbing position from feeling too stretched out, while still remaining rideable on mellower terrain. I have a fairly flat gravel road approach to my local trails and never felt like the Tyee's geometry was putting too much pressure on my hands. On tight, awkward climbs the Tyee remains manageable. It has the calmer handling you'd expect from an enduro bike, but it doesn't feel overly sluggish or lethargic. The 34-pound weight is quite reasonable, especially considering that that's with mainly aluminum components, a DoubleDown rear tire, and a Zeb – it wouldn't be hard to shed some weight with a slightly less DH-oriented build. Descending Even with a 170mm fork the Tyee's geometry is fairly middle-of-the road for this style of bike. It's not super long and slack, or overly steep and short. I'd describe its trail manners as energetically neutral - it's peppy enough to get airborne off smaller obstacles without a struggle, but the overall handling feel is calm and balanced. I never felt like I had to fight to place it where I wanted, no matter whether I was picking my way through jumbles of pointy rocks in Pemberton, or logging some air time in Whistler. Compared to the Commencal SX that we tested last year, another mixed wheel bike in this travel bracket, the Tyee doesn't require as much effort to manuever, likely due to the steeper head angle and shorter reach, figures that give it a 20mm shorter wheelbase. Some of the extra-efficient feel that the Tyee demonstrated on the climbs bleeds over onto the descents, where it's not quite as beneficial. It can handle big hits with ease, and it has a nicely planted feel when touching back down to earth, but this is a bike that feels more poised than plush – it doesn't have ton of sensitivity at the beginning of its travel. I experimented with running more sag, and ran a SuperDeluxe coil shock for a while, but no matter the setting the Tyee never transformed into a plush, ultra-sensitive machine. The Tyee's flip chip is mainly designed to allow the use of a 29” or 27.5” rear wheel while preserving the geometry, but it can also be used to create slack and very low option – with a 170mm fork the head angle drops to 63.2 degrees, and the bottom bracket feels like it's hovering just above the ground. I took a handful of laps in the Whistler Bike Park with the Tyee in configuration and found it to be very enjoyable on high speed jump trails. The increased stability is noticeable, and the low bottom bracket height made it even easier to push into tall bermed turns. The downsides to the setting become evident almost immediately when venturing into more technical terrain – with 170mm cranks I had a number of semi-scary pedal strikes. I'd say that it's a usable option, but shorter cranks are highly recommended unless you're content to always coast on technical trails. Propain Tyee Santa Cruz Nomad How does it compare? The Propain Tyee and the Santa Cruz Nomad aren't that far apart when it comes to geometry figures like reach and chainstay length. A size large Nomad in the low setting has a reach of 472.4mm, and the Tyee checks in at 475mm. The chainstay length is 443mm on the Nomad, and 445mm on the Tyee. The Nomad does have 10mm more rear travel, and a head angle that's a half degree slacker. It also has a much shorter seat tube length, which makes it easier to run a long travel dropper post. On the trail, both bikes are good climbers considering how much travel they have. The Tyee has slightly less suspension movement when climbing, but the flip side to that is the Nomad has an easier time finding traction when things are slippery. The Nomad also does a better job of dealing with chattery sections of trail. Both bikes fall onto the more supportive side of the suspension spectrum, but the Nomad has the edge when it come to smoothing out repeated high speed hits. When it comes to price, the Propain handily takes that match-up. The carbon Tyee frame with a Fox DHX2 coil shock is $3089 vs. $4089 for a Nomad frame with a Fox X2 shock. Which Model is the Best Value? If you're sticking with Propain's pre-configured parts packages, the Price2Ride option is a good deal at $4,199. That price gets you a SRAM GX drivetrain, Formula Cura 4 brakes, and a Formula suspension package. Personally, I'd go with the alloy frame to save some money (and to not have thru-headset cable routing). With a few clicks it's possible to put together a bike with top-of-the-line RockShox suspension, Code RSC brakes, and a long travel dropper post for $4,300 US. No matter what, the level of customization Propain offers is impressive, and it's possible to put together a very good bike for the price. Technical Report Dropper post: The 170mm RockShox AXS electronic dropper post is quick and effective, but it's also too short for a bike like this. There's still no longer travel option either; I'd suggest going with the longer travel cable-operated options from Propain's online congfigurator and saving a couple hundred dollars at the same time. Keep in mind that the Tyee's seat tube length isn't that short, and the insertion depth is limited. I was able to run a 200mm post, but I have fairly long legs – some riders may find themselves running out of frame clearance. Newmen SL A30 wheels: These aluminum-rimmed wheels have held up well, and they haven't even needed a trip to the truing stand yet. The freehub ratcheting noise is very subtle, which complements the Tyee's overall quiet ride nicely. X0 Transmission: I encountered some shifting issues on my first ride on the Tyee, which ended up being caused by the derailleur rotating backwards and sitting in the incorrect position. It turned out the mounting bolt hadn't been torqued to the correct 35 Nm spec, and smooth, consistent shifting returned once I tightened it down properly. It's worth double checking that bolt if you're building up a Tyee at home to avoid any on-trail trouble. Pros + Very adaptable – wide range of build options. + Calm and quiet. + Efficient pedaler, well supported suspension Cons - Not that plush in the rough stuff - Taller, tapered seat tube may limit dropper options - Thru-headset cable routing adds inconvenience Pinkbike's Take The Tyee's adaptability is its strong suit - this is a bike that can be built up for enduro racing just as easily as it can be configured for pedal-heavy missions. It has a calm and stable trail presence, and it'll handle a wide variety of terrain without much fuss. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Propain Propain Tyee Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: 2026 Propain Tyee Trail - Does Less Travel Really Make a Difference?
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: The Tyee Trail is a new addition to Propain’s lineup, but it’s technically a modified version of the Tyee Enduro that launched a few years ago. By swapping out the upper shock link, Propain were able to reduce the travel to create a 145mm travel bike that’s more of a do-everything / all-mountain bike, rather than being an enduro smasher. The Tyee Trail is equipped with a 160mm fork, and can be run either with full 29” wheels, or with a mixed wheel setup - a flip chip on the seatstays helps preserve the geometry. Propain has two complete builds available for $4,049 and $5,999 USD, but it’s their online configurator that’s especially appealing. By clicking a few buttons you can swap out things like brakes, suspension, or dropper post length to create a custom build kit of your own. Propain Tyee Trail Details • Travel: 145mm / 160mm fork • 29" or mixed wheels • Carbon frame (alloy available) • 64.2° head angle • Chainstay length: 446mm (L) • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • Price as tested: $6,094 USD • Weight: 33.1 lb / 15 kg (size L) • propain-bikes.com The bike I reviewed would retail for approximately $6,094 USD, with a build kit that includes a Fox 36 fork and Float X Factory shock, Shimano XT drivetrain, SRAM Maven Base brakes, and DT Swiss XM 1700 wheels. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take I was initially a little skeptical about the Tyee Trail - could less travel and a little lighter build kit really make that much of a difference compared to the Tyee Enduro? As it turns out, the answer is a resounding “yes.” The more supportive suspension feel is a better fit for the Tyee Trail, a bike that feels right at home on trails with a mix of jumps, berms, and tech. It’s not the absolute plushest, but its snappy, engaging character makes it a very fun, versatile machine. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details There’s been plenty of internet chatter lately about how all bikes look the same, and while I don't agree, I do understand the basis of the complaint, especially considering the ubiquity of the Horst link suspension design. The Tyee is one of the exceptions to the rule, and Propain’s Pro 10 suspension design gives it a look that stands out from the crowd. Personally, I like the frame shape - it’s different enough without being visually jarring, and that’s true for both the carbon and aluminum versions. The Tyee received ports in the head tube in 2025, which means riders are no longer forced to go with thru-headset cable routing. It’s still possible for the masochists out there, but everyone else can run their brake and derailleur lines inside the frame, but on the outside of the headset bearings. My gripe about the seat tube length that I mentioned in 2023 still holds true. It’s on the taller side, and the insertion depth is limited due to the angle and the way it tapers, which means shorter riders may have issues trying to fit a longer travel dropper post. I have pretty long legs, so I was able to run a 210mm OneUp post, but that’s not going to be the case for everyone; I would have been out of luck if I wanted to run a Fox 210mm Transfer post for example. Propain does have a chart that helps make it easier to figure out exactly what posts will work depending on a rider's inseam. There’s plenty of room for a water bottle, plus accessory mounts under the downtube to mount a tool or other accessory holder. The chainslap protection is robust, and does a decent job of keeping the bike quiet - I’d put in in the middle of the pack as far as the overall decibel level out on the trail goes. A flip chip makes it possible to run either a 29" or 27.5" rear wheel while preserving the geometry. The Tyee's bearings and even the seatpost collar have an extra rubber seal to help keep water at bay. Geometry Not surprisingly, the Tyee Trail's geometry is pretty close to that of the Tyee Enduro. The head angle is just .3-degrees different due to the difference in fork travel (the Trail version runs a 160mm fork, the Enduro a 170). The Tyee Trail's upper shock link reduces the leverage ratio slightly, drops the travel down to 145mm, and lowers the bottom bracket. The chainstay length is the same 446mm on the M, L, and XL sizes, and it's 430mm on the S and XS sizes. Those two smaller sizes also roll on dual 27.5" wheels. Suspension Design Propain’s Pro 10 suspension design uses two co-rotating links, with the shock “floating” in between them. On the Tyee Trail, a 210 x 50mm shock delivers 145mm of rear travel. The hallmark trait of Propain’s bikes is the fairly high anti-squat values, which increase slightly as the bike goes through its travel before dropping off for the last 30% of the shock’s stroke. The Tyee Trail has a progressive leverage curve, with a change of around 19% - that’s a number that’ll work well with air shocks, but running a coil isn’t totally out of the question. Specifications Price $6094 Travel 145mm Rear Shock Fox Float X Factory Fork Fox 36 Float Factory 160mm Headset Cane Creek Cassette Shimano XT 12-speed Crankarms Shimano XT Rear Derailleur Shimano XT Chain Shimano XT Handlebar OneUp Carbon V2 Stem SixPack Grips OneUp Brakes SRAM Maven Base Wheelset DT Swiss XM1700 Tires Schwalbe Albert Radial Trail Seat Ergon SM10 Enduro Seatpost Fox Transfer Kashima Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup Propain’s online configurator offers all sorts of options, allowing riders to choose everything from stem length to brake model, which hopefully means there’s not too much parts swapping required when the bike arrives. I ended up running a 210mm OneUp post instead of the 180mm Fox Transfer that the bike arrived with, and switched the 35mm SixPack stem out for a 40mm stem to suit my personal preference. I started off by running the bike in full 29” mode when we were filming the Value Bike Field test down in Sedona. I wanted to see how the bigger wheels worked on the chunky desert terrain, and the same goes for the Albert Radial tires. More recently, I’ve been running the Tyee Trail with mixed wheels and Specialized’s new radial Butcher and Eliminator tires. Those tires are pretty hefty, but the MX setup is a great for hitting jumps and riding steeper trails without getting buzzed. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 165 lb / 74.8 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington , and the surrounding area, with trail conditions ranging from muddy to slightly dusty and everything in between. In Sedona the sun was shining and the trails were bone dry, providing a nice reprieve from the winter sogginess at home. Tyee Trail https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/xJrdegP0 Copied Live 00:00 06:00 06:00 Climbing Propain’s bikes have historically been snappy pedalers - even the 180mm Spindrift feels much, much more efficient than what you’d expect. That’s the story with the Tyee Trail as well, although the Schwalbe radial tires do help take some of the edge off that initial firmness, in a good way. There was a time when “climbs like a hardtail” was the highest level praise for a full suspension bike, but that’s no longer the case - modern riders have come to expect their bikes to take the edge off when climbing and descending, all without bobbing too wildly or feeling too mushy. With the Tyee Trail, it has a very satisfying surge when you step on the pedals - it stays higher in its travel, and feel quicker out of the gate than other bikes in this category. That trait is great on smoother climbs, but it can be a little less beneficial when things get really chunky. Take the trails in Sedona, for instance. Many of them contain rocky, stairstep-like sections that require the right amount of power and speed to successfully clean. With non-radial tires, the Tyee Trail borders on feeling too firm off the top; it doesn’t get into its travel as easily as something like the Specialized Stumpjumper, or even the Santa Cruz Bronson, another dual-link bike. Add in those softer radial casing tires, though, and the Tyee Trail feels excellent. The influence of radial tires hasn’t really been discussed all the much in the context of suspension feel, but in some cases it can make a bike feel like it has a lighter compression tune. After all, tires are the first thing to encounter an obstacle, and in this case they do a great job of muting the initial impact, and pair very nicely with the firmer suspension feel earlier in the stroke. As far as the actual climbing position goes, the geometry feels very similar to other bikes in this category - the 77-degree seat angle and 64.2-degree head tube angle are fairly typical, and it didn’t take long to get comfortable with the Tyee’s overall handling. The fact that it tends to sit a little higher in its travel gives it a little quicker handling than the geometry chart might suggest - it’s certainly not sluggish, and even at slower speeds it’s still very manageable. The overall weight isn’t ridiculously light, but it’s also not ridiculously heavy, and gram-obsessed riders could shed some grams by throwing more carbon parts (and money) at it. Descending When the Tyee Trail was announced I was curious about just how much difference a different link and build kit would actually make compared to the Enduro version I’d already reviewed. As it turns out, the change in ride feel is significant, and I’d say its for the better, at least for riders looking for a bike that’s more of an all-rounder. For the trails close to my home in Bellingham, Washington, the Trail version is pretty ideal - it has enough travel for just about anything, and it feels very responsive. It’s best on trails that feel like oversized pump tracks, where there’s free speed available if you find the right rhythm. It’s a great jumper too - there’s a nice poppy nature to it, even with those moderately long chainstays. The geometry numbers are very similar to what you'd find on many enduro bikes, so it's really the amount of travel on tap that makes the difference - the head angle is slack enough that the steepness of the trail doesn't really pose much of an issue, and the overall wheelbase provides a good amount of high speed stability. Yes, more travel would make it easier to go faster on extra-chopped up sections of trail, but for trails that are a touch less rowdy the Tyee Trail feels like the better match. It feels zippier, to use the technical term, and rather than being an enduro bike that feels a little closer to a trail bike like the Tyee Enduro, it’s a trail bike that feels like, well, a trail bike. (Feel free to swap the phrase “trail bike” with “all-mountain” bike if that makes more sense for your personal bike categorization rules). Similar to what I mentioned in the climbing section, radial tires help take the edge off the choppier sections of trail, and give the Tyee better small bump manners than it would otherwise. It’s not the plushest mid-travel bike out there, but even on the chunky Sedona trails it held its own - the Float X is a good match, and the new, larger bottom out bumper helps keep the bike from making any bad noises or rattling its rider’s bones when the end of the travel is reached. Which rear wheel size is better - 29” or 27.5”? These days I prefer mixed wheels for enduro and DH bikes, and 29” wheels for XC bikes. In the mid-travel category I’d say I’m wheel size agnostic - it really depends on the terrain and what type of ride characteristic I’m chasing. On the Tyee, the dual 29” setup offers better rollover when climbing, and it feels faster once it’s up to speed while descending. Somewhat counterintuitively, I preferred the Tyee Trail's cornering performance with the dual 29” wheels: it felt like I could load them up and really rip them around a turn better than I could with the mixed-wheel setup. For steeper terrain or jump-filled trails I’d go with the mixed wheels - there’s more butt-to-tire clearance, whether you’re tipping it into a steep chute, or getting airborne off of a lippy jump. Tyee Trail Santa Cruz Bronson (with some fancy bits installed that will be reviewed soon). How Does It Compare? The geometry numbers of the Santa Cruz Bronson or Hightower and the Tyee Trail are pretty similar - they both have a 64(ish) degree head angle, and 440-something millimeter chainstays. The suspension feel is quite different between the two, though; the Bronson has more traction, and gets into its travel more easily than the Tyee trail. Yes, the Bronson has 5 millimeters more travel than the Tyee Trail, but the difference feels greater than that. The Tyee is a snappier pedaler; it feels closer to the way that Santa Cruz’s bikes felt a couple of generations ago, with less suspension movement under hard pedaling. The Bronson’s stack height is 10mm higher, which comes in handy on steeper terrain - the Tyee Trail is a good candidate for a 40mm rise bar in order to match that front end height. For rougher terrain the Bronson is more comfortable, but on smoother, more rolling trails the Tyee has a firmer platform to push against when pumping through rollers. When it comes to features, the Tyee has the ability to run either wheel size, while with Santa Cruz you’ll need to choose either the Bronson or Hightower depending on which way you want to go. The Bronson gets the point for overall frame finishing details - the tube-in-tube routing makes brake or derailleur housing swaps easier compared to the Tyee. The Bronson also has in-frame storage, which isn’t as much of a necessity now that tiny backpacks are back in fashion, but it is nice to have a spot to hold a tube and mini-pump just in case. Which Model is the Best Value? Going with an aluminum frame is always the way to go for riders who want to save some coin and don’t mind a little extra weight. The aluminum frame is around a pound heavier, and $800 less expensive. Propain’s configurator makes it easy to spend where it makes sense, and save where it doesn’t. Personally, I’d go with a mechanical drivetrain, aliuminum wheels, Maven Base brakes, and then splurge a little on nicer suspension - that’d put the price around $4,000 for a well spec’d aluminum all-mountain bike that shouldn’t need any upgrades for a while. Schwalbe Albert Radial tires Shimano XT mechanical drivetrain Technical Report Shimano XT drivetrain: It’s nice having the option to built up a bike without batteries. Shimano’s XT drivetrain still works well, and it actually has an adjustable clutch, unlike its wireless counterpart. Schwalbe Albert tires: I’ve probably already written more than enough words about how radial tires improve the feel of the Tyee Trail, but a new tire has recently become available that I’d recommend over the Albert - the Schwable Romy. The Albert is a good general use tire, but it can get overwhelmed in muddier conditions. The Romy has a more open tread pattern, and has a great blend of rolling speed and grip. I’d recommend an Albert up front and a Romy in the back, or going with a dual Romy setup. DT Swiss XM1700 wheels: These aluminum wheels held up very well, and even after spending time being smashed into desert sandstone they're still spinning true. The newer version available in Propain's configurator has DT's DEG DF hubs , which allow riders to chose how many degrees the freehub rotates before engaging the star ratchet in order to reduce the amount of pedal feedback. That hub should be a good complement to Propain's suspension layout. Pros + Great pedaling performance, ideal for riders who want a snappy, efficient feeling bike + Wide range of parts spec options Cons - Not the plushest mid-travel bike out there; may not suit riders looking for maximum traction and comfort - Longer and tapered seat tube limits maximum dropper post lengths Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Propain Propain Tyee Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Push Nine One V2 Inverted Fork - Plush & Pricey
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Push Nine One V2 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/HBaNuxKO Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 13:54 Just like baggy jeans and Nirvana t-shirts, upside down forks are experiencing something of a resurgence. Fox, Intend, and Push have all released enduro-oriented options, and Cane Creek has teased a prototype USD fork of their own. Is it a sign of things to come, or will the USD concept remain something of a fringe option in a sea of right-side-up forks? I’m leaning towards the latter, but time will tell. Push first revealed the Nine One at Sea Otter 2023 , but it took some time before it finally hit the market. Once it did, the Colorado-based company didn’t waste any time before rolling out version 2.0, which adds on some clever features not found on the initial version. Push Nine One V2 Details • Coil sprung • Travel: 140-170 mm (internally adjustable) • 15 x 110mm thru-axle • Adjustments: high- & low-speed compression, rebound, Sub Chamber, hydraulic bottom out • 29" or 27.5" wheel • Torque Cap compatible • Weight: 2,950 grams w/ axle • MSRP: $2,250 USD • pushindustries.com Details Let’s start with the basics. The Nine One is coil sprung, with external rebound, high and low-speed compression adjustments. There’s also adjustable hydraulic bottom out, and a feature Push calls the Sub Chamber, which can be toggled on or off to add end-stroke progression. HBO The hydraulic bottom kicks in for the last 70mm of travel. It's almost like a shock within a shock - it uses a monotube layout with its own main piston, plus an adjustable internal floating piston (IFP) that can be inflated up to 40 psi. As the chart below illustrates, thanks to its speed sensitive design it provides much more bottom-out resistance at higher velocities than the ABS (air bump stop) feature found on version 1.0 of this fork. Sub Chamber The Sub Chamber is another very clever feature - it uses the ambient air trapped in the fork to create additional mid-stroke support. Push uses the following analogy to describe how it functions: " Take a plastic water bottle and empty the water from the bottle. Now squeeze the bottle. With the cap removed the bottle is very easy to crush in your hand. Now, expand the bottle, tighten down the cap, and squeeze the bottle again. With the cap installed ambient air has been trapped inside the bottle causing resistance or creating a basic air spring. " That's what the Sub Chamber does - when it's closed off the air is trapped in the fork, adding that extra support. The Nine One's upper tubes measure 44mm in diameter, and the stanchions are 36mm. The Nine One uses a 15mm x 110mm thru-axle, and is compatible with Torque Cap hubs (that fact will be more pertinent later on in this review). The axle is secured by four pinch bolts. The amount of travel can be set between 140 - 170mm in 10mm increments, a procedure that can be performed at home without the need for any additional parts. It does require basic mechanical skills, and it’s not the quickest task, but still, it’s nice not needing to shell out any extra dollars for another spring assembly. The two-position Sub Chamber dial. Low-speed compression and high-speed compression are adjusted on the right side, with nice and distinct clicks with each turn. Price & Weight On my scale the Nine One V2 weighed in at 2950 grams with the axle installed - that’s 255 grams heavier than a Fox Podium, and it’s also heavier than a RockShox Boxxer or a Fox 40, which are dual-crown DH forks. As for the price, that’s on the higher side of things too - it’ll set you back $2,250 USD, and you’ll likely want to shell out another $35 for the fender. Push Nine One V2: 2950 grams | $2,250 USD. 140 - 170mm travel Fox Podium : 2695g | $2,000 USD. 150-170mm travel. Fox 38 : 2381g | $1,249 USD. 150-180mm travel. Intend Flash : 2350g | $2,263 USD. 160-190mm travel. RockShox Zeb : 2324g | $1,159 USD. 150-190mm travel. Setup I’ve had the Nine One V2 in my test rotation for the last four months, and during that time it’s been installed on a Trek Fuel LX, a Specialized Levo 4, and a Yeti MTe. To me, those represent the trio of bike styles that I envision potential customers installing this fork on - a burly enduro bike, a full power eMTB, and a mid-power eMTB. Testing began in the Whistler Bike Park during Crankworx, where the fork was subjected to plenty of deep braking bumps and harsh compressions, and continued on the trails surrounding Bellingham, Washington. The fork arrives with a handy little setup guide, with a section that has the base settings written down, and the signatures of the employees that assembled and inspected the fork. There are a total of seven spring weights available, from 30 to 60 lb/in in 5 pound increments. For my 160 lb weight I went with the 45 lb spring, and started with the low and high speed compression dials at 15 clicks out from closed, and the rebound at 12 clicks out. The IFP for the hydraulic bottom out feature was inflated to 30 psi (40 psi is the max). On the Trail Damping The extra-sensitive initial stroke is the trait that first sticks out, no matter whether you’re climbing or descending. Now, the climbing performance of a fork isn’t usually a big topic of conversation, but the ease that the Nine One goes into its stroke is worth a few extra lines of electronic ink. Some forks are plush enough to make you want to actively run into things just to see what’ll happen, and the Nine One falls into that category. Small undulations in the trail are erased, and larger obstacles, whether that’s an anaconda-sized root or big jumble of chunky rocks, are easily dispatched with a ker-squish , ker-squish (that’s the sound of the damper doing its thing - the Nine One isn’t the quietest fork). That smoothness (and the audible damper noise) carries over onto the descents - straightline plowing is when the fork feels the best. I experimented with both Sub Chamber settings, and found that for the most part I preferred to have it on, due to the extra support it provides deeper in the fork’s travel. For maximum bump-suckability, turning it off gives the fork the fully linear, classic coil spring feeling, right up until the last portion of the travel when the hydraulic bottom out is activated to prevent any clunks or clangs. I ended up pretty much in the middle of the compression settings, and I still had 6 more clicks to go before being fully open on rebound, which means there should be a good range of options for riders to play with in order to achieve their ideal setup. Stiffness Rough, twisty trails, the type that Whistler’s Creekside zone is full of (Miss Fire, Ride Don’t Slide, and BC’s Trail for example) start to bring the Nine One’s main weakness to light - the lack of stiffness in those slower speed, heavy front end movement scenarios. At times it felt like the wheel wanted to wander, as if it was grabbing at the edges of the trail when all I wanted to do was keep going forward. After my first few rides where the amount of front end flex felt unexpectedly high, even for a USD fork, I contacted Darren Murphy, the owner of Push, to see if he had any tips. His suggestion? A different hub. It turns out that some hubs are better than others at resisting the independent leg movement that comes with an upside-down fork design; in this instance he suggested Industry Nine’s Hydra hub, or at the very least using Torque Caps, the oversized end caps that are available for some hubs. I happened to have a Forge & Bond (RIP) wheel with an I9 hub and Torque Caps, so I made the swap and started putting in the miles again. That hub change made a more noticeable difference than I expected - the amount of deflection was reduced, and the stiffness was closer to an acceptable level, although there were still scenarios where the handling was less precise than I wanted. The fork felt very good when the trails were greasy, instances when the best tactic is to avoid overly-aggressive corrections, but when things were drier the extra front-end movement was more noticeable - even with a different hub the Nine One didn't have the level of precision I was looking for. Weight The high weight of the Nine One is noticeable, especially on a bike without a motor. On the Trek, it made the front wheel feel like it wanted to stay glued to the ground, and the bike as a whole felt less eager to get airborne. Granted, it was already pushing 40 pounds before installing the Push, but the extra heft of the fork definitely didn’t help. The plushness, which is great for traction, adds to that glued-down sensation too. On the e-bike, especially the Levo, the weight was a little less noticeable, since that bike is already pretty heavy as it is, but it's not doing it any favors in the maneuverability department. Push Nine One V2 Fox Podium How Does it Compare? The most obvious contender to pit against the Nine One V2 is the Fox Podium . Both forks are inverted, they’re both expensive ($2,250 for the Push and $2,000 for the Podium), and neither one is particularly light (2950 grams for the Push, and 2695 for the Podium). The Podium is air sprung, though, which does save some weight, and it uses a 20mm steel thru-axle, which helps in the stiffness department. Out on the trail, the Nine One wins in the initial sensitivity department. The Podium is no slouch, and it’s extremely smooth, but in this air vs coil battle the Nine One snags the plushness point. Push also takes the point when it comes to bottom out resistance. Yes, the Podium can be tuned with volume spacers to increase the amount of ramp up, but it’s much easier to achieve a smooth end-stroke ramp up on the Nine One thanks to the Sub Chamber and the hydraulic bottom out features. The Podium pulls ahead when it comes to stiffness and precision. It’s not as torsionally stiff as a 38 (and it’s not claimed to be), but I found the Podium’s handling to be more predictable than the Nine One, especially in those aforementioned steep, rough, slower speed twisty trails. With the Nine One, there were times when it was a little hard to discern what the front wheel was doing. It may seem odd to want more feedback from a fork, but just like it’d be hard to climb a steep sport route while wearing thick wool socks, it can be harder to navigate a trail when the sensations reaching your hands are extra-muted. Dario DiGiulio also spent time on both forks, and his findings mirrored mine - he found the chassis flex to be the biggest differentiator between the two. He’s 185 pounds vs my 160 pound weight, and for him the flex in the Nine One was even more excessive in tight corners and hard compressions. His takeaway? "Though the top-end feel of the fork is very impressive, I can’t think of any real-world applications where I’d rather ride this fork than something like a 38, Zeb, or the Podium." Personally, I’m not totally sold on either upside-down option. I’d pick the Podium over the Nine One, since it’s stiffer and quieter, but I’d pick the 38 over either one - I prefer the precision and lighter weight of that fork, even if it’s not quite as plush off the top. It’s also almost half the cost, and to me the performance difference isn’t worth an extra $1,000, no matter how cool an upside down fork looks. Pros + Very smooth and plush thanks to coil spring and USD design + Sub Chamber feature is very effective + Optional fender works and actually looks decent Cons - Noticeable flex in certain scenarios - Heavy - Expensive Pinkbike's Take I’ll always root for the underdog, especially when a small company is trying to go up against the bike industry behemoths. This time, though, David’s flying rocks aren't enough to take down Goliath. The Nine One is as plush as it gets, and it’ll absolutely plow through the rough stuff, at least if you’re going in a straight line, but the lack of stiffness holds it back on slower speed, tighter trails, especially ones that require heavy braking. That chassis flex is the main sticking point - the 15mm thru-axle has its work cut out for it, and there’s a distinct lack of front end precision in certain scenarios. There’s also the extra weight to contend with, and the sky high price. For riders that are enamored with the looks and traction that the Nine One delivers those points can be overlooked, but for most riders going with a right-side-up, less expensive option will be the best bet. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Suspension Fork Push Push Nine One Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Push SV Eight Coil Shock
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Push released the SV Eight shock earlier this year, a made-in-Colorado offering that comes in at a slightly lower price point than their flagship ElevenSix shock. While the ElevenSix has two separate damping circuits, essentially allowing riders to toggle between two completely different setups with the flick of a lever, the SV Eight has a more standard layout, with just one circuit. It's still highly adjustable, though, and it has a hydraulic bottom out feature that kicks in for the last 15% of the travel. The SV Eight has 24 clicks of high speed and low speed compression damping, along with 18 clicks of rebound. Push uses US-made Hyperco springs for their shocks, which are available in 25 pound increments. Other coil springs are compatible too, as long as they have a 35mm internal diameter. Push SV Eight Details • Adjustments: low speed compression, high speed compression, rebound • Melonite QPQ high strength steel shaft • Hydraulic bottom out piston • eMTB compatible • Weight: 423 g (without spring, 205 x 65mm) • MSRP: $865 without spring • pushindustries.com Rather than being available in a wide range of tune options, the $865 (without spring) SV Eight only comes in two configurations – an A version designed for riders using spring weights between 300 – 500 lb/in, and a B version for spring weights between 525 – 700 lb/in. That's a different approach compared to what Push does with the Elevensix, their $1,600 (with spring) flagship model – that shock is tuned specifically for the bike it's going to be installed on. That said, if a rider does need a different setup than what's possible with the A or B tune, Push offers a custom tuning option for $170. Other Details Hydraulic bottom out: The SV Eight's hydraulic bottom out system kicks in for the last 15% of the shock's stroke, assuming the stroke length hasn't been limited. A secondary piston sits on top of the main piston, where it's pushed into a smaller chamber as the end of the shock's travel is reached. That constriction in the oil path increased the amount of force required to compress the shock, preventing a harsh bottom out. Push do still use a standard sized bottom out bumper, so there are essentially two systems in play to eliminate any jarring impacts. And yes, if you shorten the shock's stroke the bottom out resistance will happen later in the shock's travel; a 62.5mm stroke shock would have that hydraulic bottom out resistance kick in for the last 11% of travel. Unlike option from RockShox and EXT, Push's hydraulic bottom out feature isn't externally adjustable - the amount of force is fixed from the factory. Warranty: Each SV Eight comes with a 1-year warranty, which is extended by 1 year each time the shock is sent in for its annual service. That service is priced at $190, and includes all new oil seals, wiper seals, and o-rings, along with new fluids. The dials are easy to turn, and have clearly defined clicks. Play Video Play Current Time 0:00 Loaded : 0% Progress : 0% Duration Time 0:00 Mute 1080p 720p 480p 240p Quality 480p Fullscreen This is a modal window. Caption Settings Dialog Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done SV Eight ASMR by mikekazimer Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 1,968 Faves: 0 Comments: 1 Performance The SV Eight was installed on my Raaw Madonna, which has been an excellent test bed for evaluating various shocks. A 475 lb spring put me at the recommended 26% sag. As per Push's instructions, I started off with the dials close to the middle of the range, with 12 clicks of LSC, 12 clicks of HSC, and 9 clicks of rebound, all counted from closed. Those adjustments don't require any tools, and each click is very well defined. A good chunk of my testing took place in the Whistler Bike Park, where I was able to take dozens of runs and experiment with the extremes of the compression range. Even with the low speed and high speed compression fully closed the SV Eight was still rideable – it's obviously a much firmer ride, and not something I'd want to run all day, but it also wasn't overly harsh or jarring. The same goes for the fully open settings – the shock was too active for my taste in that configuration, with more movement and less support than I wanted, but once again it wasn't so extreme as to be unusable. In the end, I only deviated a couple clicks from Push's recommended starting point – I sped up the rebound by a click, and opened up the low speed a few clicks to get a little more traction in the wet conditions. There's no climb switch on the SV Eight, but Push claim that the shock has pedaling efficiency built in thanks to a bridged piston design. That may be so, but I didn't notice much of a platform while climbing compared to other shocks. The Raaw has fairly calm pedaling manners, so I didn't miss having a climb switch, but I do think that's a feature some riders will still want, especially those on bikes with less efficient suspension layouts. Up to two 2.5mm spacers can be added underneath the bottom out bumper to reduce the shock's stroke. The SV Eight has an excellent ground feel, meaning it smooths out impacts very well, but not so much that you lose track of what the rear wheel is doing. It delivers all the grip and small bump compliance you'd hope to get from a coil shock, while also remaining supportive enough for bigger jumps and drops. Those laps at Whistler helped illustrate its well balanced nature – I was able to go from the chunkier, more natural trails like Whistler DH to the freshly spruced up A-Line without feeling like I needed to adjust any settings. It's the high level of traction the SV Eight delivers that impressed me the most – it's very, very supple, quickly getting up and out of the way of even the smallest obstacles, a trait that's even more useful on wet, slimy days where you want your rear wheel hugging the ground as much as possible. The hydraulic bottom out does its job, and I didn't experience and harsh clunks or clangs when full travel was reached. Of course, since there's also a bottom out bumper it's hard to suss out exactly which feature is coming into play, and I should also mention that I didn't have any harsh bottom out events when using the Ohlins TTX22, which doesn't have that feature. There is a small amount of top out, but that was only noticeable when bunnyhopping or jumping at slower speeds; in those instances I could feel a slight 'twunk' as the shock reach full travel, but it was fairly subtle. In the bike park that sensation wasn't noticeable at all. Push SV Eight Ohlins TTX22 How Does It Compare? The SV Eight had a less damped feel than the Ohlins TTX22 I'd been on before; the difference is similar to that of wearing a thicker, insulated glove (Ohlins), compared to a thin silk glove (Push). Both shocks accomplish the same goal, but go about it in a slightly different way. In back-to-back testing, I ended up preferring the feel of the Push; it seemed to provide more traction in wet, slippery conditions, even at slower speeds. Keep in mind that both shocks can be re-tuned to suit a rider's specific needs - a lighter compression tune on the TTX22 could make it feel more like the SV Eight, and vice versa, but I will say that, overall, the TTX22 does tend to feel more damped than its competition. The Ohlins is a top tier shock, and really comes alive as speeds increase and the impacts get harder, but it does have a more heavily damped feel, even in its most open setting. The TTX22 does have a three position HSC lever that can be used as a climb switch, something the Push is lacking. As for price, the SV Eight is $40 more than the Ohlins, at $865 vs $825. For further comparison, a RockShox SuperDeluxe Coil is $550, and a Fox DHX2 is $679. All of those prices are without a spring, so don't forget to factor that into your equation. Pros + Good range of usable adjustments + Great traction and overall feel + Made in USA Cons - Hydraulic bottom out isn't adjustable - Pricey (but it's a lot less than an ElevenSix) - Some riders may want a climb switch Pinkbike's Take I'd be curious to see what the introduction of the SV Eight does to the sales of Push's even-fancier ElevenSix shock. Personally, if I was deciding between the two I'd go with the SV Eight. The dual valve chamber system of the ElevenSix is novel, but I don't think it's all that necessary, especially considering just how well the SV Eight works for hundreds of dollars less. At the end of the day, the SV Eight is a high end shock with performance that goes a long way towards justifying its price – its overall feel left me thoroughly impressed. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images.
