article_text: Rossignol Heretic Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/fwMtKaPs Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 15:40 Starting from scratch takes commitment, and that's exactly what Rossignol has done with this redesigned Heretic. A new alloy tube set, robust bearing hardware, and adjusted kinematics make this bike an entirely different beast from the old version. The travel figures have gone up as well - the rear end now sits at 165mm, and the fork is properly outfitted for enduro racing or stunts at 170mm. Rossignol stands by the dedication to 29" wheels, however, the frame is now MX-compatible. Given that this is an alloy frameset, it doesn't land on the light side at 16.5 kg (36.3 lb), but it's not a total tank either. Heretic Details • 165 mm travel, 170 mm fork • Aluminum frame • 29 or MX wheels • Horst-link suspension • 64-64.5° head angle (63.6° MX) • 437/442 mm chainstays • Sizes: S, M (tested), L, XL • Pricing: €6,200 EUR / $6,200 USD / $8,000 CAD • Weight: 16.5 kg / 36.3 lb (MD) • 3.2 kg frame (MD, w/o shock) • rossignol.com With four models in the lineup, the Heretic offers great value at nearly all levels. This GX model costs $6,200 USD and is laden with crowd-favorite components all around. After giving the Heretic a thumbs up in our First Ride, we spent the better part of the summer ragging on this very purple machine to find out how the frame hardware and components would hold up over the long run. Contents Introduction Frame Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Heretic surprised me by being quick to initiate turns, yet still staying planted through the rough. Maybe there's something to these 29" enduro bikes after all. — Matt Beer Frame Details Rebuilt and oversized, the Heretic pivot hardware is solid. A large main pivot with a locking collet-style axle keeps everything where it should be. On top of that, the welded 3-piece rocker link is sturdy-looking. You won’t find internal frame storage in the downtube, but if you look under the BB, you’ll notice the rubber protector doubles as a home for a multi-tool and tire lever. It can be a bit of a nuisance to remove, and it'll get caked with mud, so save it for emergencies. Thankfully, it never rattled or wiggled loose. Further supplies can be bolted to the underside of the top tube, plus there’s room for a water bottle inside the front triangle on all sizes. Cable routing runs through the downtube and into foam tubing, then it's locked in place by bolt-in guides. The rear triangle looks well guarded, and the cable routing is neatly laid out. A rubber mud flap on the chainstay yoke does the trick without any squeaks, but the chainslap protection stops short of the dropout pivot and higher along the seatstay. It's a minor woe and nothing some mastic tape can't solve. A slick mud flap and double-sided bash guard. The rocker link and pivots are seriously beefed up compared to the previous Heretic. The timeless 4-bar, Horst Link suspension design. 0 seconds of 1 minute, 49 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/pmEOtrHV Copied Live 00:00 01:49 01:49 Suspension Design Rossignol stuck with a 4-bar Horst Link design, but made considerable changes to the kinematics. The most notable is the lower starting leverage rate with a more gradual increase in progression. That's said to jive well with either air or coil 205x65mm shocks. Although I didn't experiment with a coil shock, Rossignol equips their top-of-the-line "Super" Heretic model with a RockShox Vivid Coil Ultimate. The anti-squat and anti-rise values aren't huge deviations from the old Heretic, but I found the new bike to pedal and brake with much more stable, neutral handling. The old Heretic (pink line) was much more progressive and tended to push through the initial part of the travel too easily. The dashed orange and purple lines show the anti-squat of the new Heretic in a 50-tooth cog. Anti-rise is a shade lower than the old Heretic, yet still higher than some other Horst link bikes. Geometry The reach of Heretic frame sizes is skewed slightly compared to most enduro bikes out there. Each size is about 10mm longer in that aspect than what I’d call the average fit. That puts the medium at a 464mm reach in the high, 29 geometry position. This seemed more appropriate for my height (178cm) and how intended to ride this park-duro bike. In regards to the flip-chip, the high setting has a contemporary 64.5-degree head tube angle. Flipping that dropout pivot chip will cut the head angle down to 64 degrees. Rossignol actually calls these two positions Flow (high) and Attack (low). The flip-chip will cut down the reach by 5mm when dropping it into the low position. If you’re tempted to run a 27.5” rear wheel, I'd take a close look at the geometry table. Doing so chops off the reach measurement by 8mm and puts the BB drop down to -35mm. The head angle also rakes out to 63.5 degrees and the seat tube angle tips back further again. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2022 Price $6200 Travel 165 Rear Shock FOX Float X2 Factory Kashima, 205X65 Fork FOX 38 29" Factory, 170mm, GRIP2 Headset Acros ZS49/ZS49 Cassette SRAM XS 1275 T-TYPE 10-52 Crankarms SRAM GX EAGLE POLAR 2 GUARD 32T-TYPE Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA Rear Derailleur SRAM GX T-TYPE AXS Chain SRAM GX T-TYPE Shifter Pods SRAM AXS POD Handlebar BURGTEC RIDE WIDE ENDURO ALLOY 800MM X 30MM RISE Stem BURGTEC ENDURO MK3 STEM BLACK Grips Burgtec BARTENDER PRO GRIPS Brakes SRAM MAVEN BRONZE, 200mm Centerline rotors F&R Wheelset DT SWISS E1900 SPLINE 30 Tires Maxxis Assegai, 2.5, 3C, EXO+, TR front, Maxxis Minion DHR II , 2.4, 3C, EXO+, TR rear Seat SDG BEL AIR V3 Seatpost SDG TELLIS 2.0 Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC The Rossignol Heretic GX, size medium in the low geometry setting with dual 29" wheels. RIDING THE Heretic Test Bike Setup I didn’t have to puzzle much in order to feel comfortable when I first hopped on the Heretic in the Utah desert. The components listed were well-proven and familiar. My only complaints were the short 150mm dropper post and light-duty tires. With the length of the seat tube, there’s no reason why a 180mm option couldn’t be fitted to the size medium frame. I swapped out for that longer post and some more durable casing tires, along with a softer compound up front. For the suspension, I set the Fox 38 to 100 psi and added a second volume spacer from the stock setup. The rebound was set to the faster side of Fox’s recommendations, and the compression was more open. The rear shock arrived without any volume spacer inside, and that meant increasing the air pressure to avoid bottom outs. Some small bump compliance was given up, so once back on home soil, I added a volume spacer. That allowed for the top end to stay supple while ramping up and took the sting out of heavy landings. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 38 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r I started out with the sag at 25%. I settled around 190 psi, which equated to 21% sag, which is a much lower sag number than you usually hear of. The more I rode the Heretic, though, the more air I kept adding to the rear shock, and the better the bike handled. The low-speed compression was set to -11 clicks from close, and the high-speed to -8. On the rebound side, the low-speed was usually -11 or -12, and the high-speed stayed at -4. Testing Info Download the Trailforks App Throughout the summer, I put the Heretic through every kind of ride: all-day, big mountain pedal missions around Squamish, and lift-assisted laps down A-Line. While its first instinct isn't to win the race to the top, it will happily plod up a road and come alive when it's time to have fun on feature-filled trails. Climbing Let’s not kid ourselves. The Heretic isn’t ever going to be a fast bike uphill. It’s a respectable weight for an alloy enduro bike, and competitive with others in its class, but climbing isn’t the Heretic’s forte. The slacker seat tube angle sets the riding position further back into an overly relaxed position. Sliding the seat forward helped improve the situation, but only by a small margin. The climb switch gives a helping hand here, and also keeps you from smashing the cranks into the ground. Realistically, the Heretic, like some other enduro bikes that advertise they can cope with both rear wheel sizes (such as the Giant Reign) is best left as a 29er if you’re planning on pedalling to the top. The MX mode may work for a gravity-focused rider with an uplift nearby, but if you are dead set on swapping out the rear wheel for a 27.5” option, sourcing shorter cranks would be advised. Another element that detracts from its ability to clean challenging trail ups is the 18-tooth Star Ratchet hub. Unless you’re able to keep the cranks spinning through rock gardens, there’s a large lag in the engagement. That means you'll have to push the pedals further around before transferring power to the rear wheel. Luckily, that’s a simple upgrade the DT Swiss wheel, but that doesn't come at an insignificant cost. Descending You know what? For a full 29er, I dig how quickly the Heretic handles. It’s just as maneuverable as some other MX enduro bikes I’ve bounced on. It’s compact, yet balanced. The chainstay length in the low mode is a sensible 442mm. That also allows it to bob and weave through jumpy bits of trail without a laggy sense. At the same time, the 29-inch wheels and deeper standing position give it security when mobbing high-speed sections of the bike park. Instead of relying on a super slack and floppy head tube angle, which can feel sluggish to pump through jump tracks or twisty turns, the balance of the geometry and big wheel combo gives it a grounded sensation. Although the MX setup can work in the right gravity-based conditions, it’s a stretch for everyday trails or enduro racing. The bike becomes a totally different ride. You’ll have to bump up the air pressure in the shock as the dynamic geometry sways to a rearward weight bias as the angles seriously relax. Timing your pedal strokes perfectly is still challenging, and switching out for short cranks would be a smart move. That does leave me wondering, who would want a compact 29er bike? I’d say someone who appreciates the roll over and grip of 29-inch wheel, yet still wants to rip bike parks with confidence. It spells success for enduro racing with big travel and brawn, but it’s all within a reasonable wheelbase. When rallying through the bike park trails, you will notice feedback from the chain in two areas: one is noise, and the other is the chain feedback through your feet. In a few areas, the frame protection falls short of where the chain hits. Adding a few pieces of mastic tape helped quiet down those “tings”. In order to aid the Heretic on technical climbs, I installed that 54-tooth Star Ratchet in the hub I mentioned earlier. Funny enough, I noticed how that upgrade also reduced the number of heavy kicks the cassette transferred into the pedals. An O-Chain will aid in that sense too, which is another component I tacked on for a short period of time. As expected, that calmed down the kickback, too. A pleasant surprise was the little amount of upkeep I had to do on the hardware. The Heretic lived up to the hype of being durable and reliable. After the initial break-in and a quick bolt check, the pivots stayed tight, smooth, and creak-free. I tip my hat to the team behind the full redesign, as this bike suffered its fair share of braking bumps and a couple of deep sends. Technical Report Maven Bronze brakes: Nowadays, most SRAM brakes come with the HS2 rotors. Rossignol has chosen the thinner Centerline rotors and organic pads, which slightly reduces the amount of available braking power. In addition, the organic pads don't have the surge in power halfway through the lever stroke that the metallic pads do. I installed metallic pads (with fresh Centerline rotors) for part of the review to tap into that extra power the Mavens are known for, especially on extended fall line trails. This combo could be the sweet spot for riders who are currently using HS2 rotors but would prefer a smoother transition to peak power. Maxxis EXO+ tires: Ask any rider who’s tried the Assegai-DHR II tread pairing - they’re exceptional, but in this case, these aren’t the optimal specifications. The Heretic can motor downhill, and it isn’t in a hurry to go back up, so it would make sense to take the hit for a couple hundred more grams. Selecting Double Down casing tires front and rear, as well as a softer front tire compound, would make the most of the Heretic’s descending capabilities. SDG Tellis V2 post: Another solid product, but the wrong spec. The medium frame has a long enough seat tube to accommodate a 180mm post slammed in the frame. 150mm is too short for the rider height recommendation set out for the size medium frame. Which Model is the Best Value? As a tech editor, I admittedly get drawn towards bikes and components on the higher end, mostly for performance reasons, but sometimes for weight. The Heretic Deore 12 build kit breaks that thinking. At $3,300 USD, it’s a heck of a deal with a workhorse of a drivetrain and buttery Bomber suspension. There’s also no carbon frame higher up the ladder to grasp for - the base model uses the same frame as the most expensive model. The Deore brakes may lack the power of Mavens, and you’ll need to keep an eye on the wheel tension for those WTB i30 rims to last as long as possible, but all things considered, it’s a fair price in an ever-inflating market. Rossignol Heretic GX - $6,200 USD Raaw Madonna V3 frame kit - $2,603 USD How Does It Compare? It seems as though the 4-bar, Horst link suspension is becoming “the way” to build a mountain bike, and rightfully so. You can achieve a smooth leverage curve, build burly pivots, and still have room for a water bottle inside the front triangle. One of the people’s choice bikes using that design choice is the Raaw Madonna. It’s also aluminum, can handle both rear wheel sizes, and has similar travel and geometry numbers. One factor that separates the two bikes is the sizing chart. The Madonna is available in 5 sizes, so the reach might align with your ideal number. The head and seat tube angles are in the same realm, as are the chainstays; 445mm on the size M Madonna, and 442mm on the Heretic (low position). Some nifty setup changes can tailor the Madonna further to your liking, such as the chainstay length, but also the rocker link - yes, Raaw offers two rocker links to suit different rider weights. Running a 27.5” rear wheel comes without any drawbacks, too. By switching out the lower shock mount, you can retain the same geometry laid out for the 29” rear wheel. Considering the Madonna can be tuned to a finer degree, you can expect to pay more for the frame. For example, the Heretic SLX 12 model is the same price as the Raaw Madonna rolling chassis (that excludes brakes and the drivetrain). Although it’s built darn tough, the extra oversized bearings do increase the weight of the Madonna - a size medium clocks in at 3.9 kg (8.6 lb) without a shock. Pros + Solid parts choice for GX model, and still at a decent value. + Finds the sweetspot of being playful and composed. Cons - Dropper post is too short on all size frames. - MX setting is too low and too slack for enduro riding and racing. Pinkbike's Take Rossignol have come a long way with redesigning the Heretic. From day one, I've enjoyed pushing this bike into burly terrain and sending sizeable hits. The MX mode doesn't convey that same sensation, as the full 29er package rides best. Unfortunately, that may turn away a fair number of riders from an otherwise solid enduro deal. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Rossignol Rossignol Heretic Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Salsa's New Spearfish - The Bright Green Race Machine
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, xc-bikes, salsa, salsa-spearfish, sea-otter-2025
article_text: Spearfish https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/KxBp4Mvn Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 13:51 The Spearfish hasn't seen an update since 2019, and serves as a solid time capsule for how much cross-country bikes have changed in only a few years. Reach figures have grown by about 30mm per size, the head angle is a degree and a half slacker, and the travel has been increased by 20mm. With progressive numbers across the board, and frame details that cater to folks looking to push distance and speed, the Spearfish jumps into modernity and keeps XC fun. Spearfish Details • 29" front and rear • Carbon frame, Deluxe or standard layup • 100-120mm frame travel, 100-130mm fork • 66.3° head angle, 77.3° seat angle • 431-510mm reach, 589-629mm stack • 430mm chainstays • 5 sizes • Actual weight: 27.3 lb / 12.4 kg (size L) • Price: $3,999-10,999 USD • Frame Price: $3,499 • salsacycles.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take For how balanced and supportive it felt on the climbs, I expected the descending to suffer some drawbacks, but the Spearfish continually impressed with what it could handle downhill. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details The Spearfish is an all-carbon affair, no matter which trim level you select. Just how light that carbon is depends on the build kit you select though, as Salsa is rolling out a two-tier catalog for the bike. "Deluxe" models will come with a lighter frame, shaving about 255g off the standard frame weight, while maintaining the same ride characteristics and finish details. The frame has moved from SuperBoost to the far more common Boost 148 rear axle, which is nice to see. Frame weights for a size Medium are as follows, with the previous generation bike thrown in for good measure. Deluxe 4.0 1940g | Carbon 4.0 2195g | Current 3.0 2482g Yo dawg I heard you like bottle bosses. I decided to shorten the cables up after this, as the gangly look was bugging me. Every Spearfish frame comes with a huge array of threaded mounts, for carrying bottles, tools, and even hard-mounted bags if your ride warrants it. All sizes can carry at least two water bottles, while the Large I've been testing can fit 3 if you get a little creative. There's an additional pair of mounts on the toptube for a feed pack or computer mount. There's a minimal strip of chainslap protection and a clean little bolt-on chainguide mount, but no provisions for a bashguard. As a bike erring on the XC side of things, I don't think this is a miss - especially with the recent resurgence of chainring-mounted bash protectors. Cable routing requires some fishing - fitting of the name - and has both the derailleur and brake snaking through the chainstays, with all 3 cables popping out at the left side of the headtube. That exit point will make routing a bit simpler, as you can reach the exit holes with the fork removed. Downtube protection and a nice little chainguide. Itsy bitsy mudflap. Geometry & Sizing As mentioned in the intro, the Spearfish features far more modern geometry than its predecessor, which was fairly traditional on the whole. The updated frame features a high/low flip chip at the lower shock mount to alter things slightly - I'll be referring to the Low position unless otherwise noted. The 66° head angle is quite close to the cutting edge in the XC world, with only a few bikes sporting slacker figures at this point. More unusual is the 77° seat angle, which is significantly steeper than most other race-oriented short travel bikes out there. Both of those can be steepened up by 0.3° in the bike's High setting, should you want to draw the riding position forward a bit. The front end geometry is fairly long per size, with 20mm gaps in reach between almost all 5 sizes - the smallest two are a bit closer together. Stack grows by about 9mm per size, giving the bikes a decently scaled front end as you scale up. One figure that doesn't scale at all is the rear center, which sits at a short 431mm in all sizes. 35mm bottom bracket drop is a bit higher than some ultra-modern XC bikes, but the dynamic fit feels a bit lower than the numbers indicate. Suspension Design Salsa implements the Split Pivot patent on their bikes, which places a pivot at the rear dropout, concentric to the rear axle. This linkage-driven single pivot has a fairly high starting leverage, but the shock tune allows you to add enough compression to avoid moving through travel too readily. I found the balance of grip and support impressive, but more on that later. Anti-squat is high across the gear range, making for an efficient pedaling platform even with the shock open. That efficient feel in combination with the steep seat angle puts you in a fairly high and upright position while pedaling, but luckily the bike opens up nicely when turned down hill. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $6499 Travel 120mm Rear Shock Fox Float SL LV Performance Elite Fork Fox 34 SL Performance Elite, 120mm, Grip X Headset FSA ORbit 1.5E ZS No.57E Cassette SRAM GX AXS T-Type Crankarms SRAM GX AXS T-Type Chainguide MRP 1x SL TR2 Direct Mount Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM GX AXS T-Type Chain SRAM GX AXS T-Type Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Race Face Turbine Stem Salsa 35mm Guide Trail, 50mm Grips WTB Wavelength Lock-On Brakes SRAM Motive Bronze Wheelset WRB KOM Light Hubs WTB Frequency 28h Rim WTB KOM Light i30 TCS 28h Tires Teravail Camrock 29x2.4", Light Trail casing Seat WTB Silverado, Medium, Chromoly, DNAx Seatpost TranzX YS108 RAD+ w/ GL Stealth Lever; XS: 120mm, S-M: 150mm, L-XL: 170mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Spearfish Test Bike Setup I fiddled with the Spearfish quite a bit, but not necessarily because I had to. It was fairly easy to find a happy spot with the stock setup, particularly when it came to the suspension. 100psi in the 120mm 34SL felt optimal through the winter, and 195psi in the Float SL shock was pretty spot-on for my 13.5mm sag target. The fiddling came into play as I toyed with how capable you can make the bike feel, given the decently stable geometry and active suspension feel. I found the stock Teravail Camrock tires to be far too minimal for wintery conditions, as they lacked bite in any sort of loose dirt and weren't too friendly with roots. I can see them working well in desert conditions, and on summer hardpack, but I found a Specialized Ground Control far more comfortable up front for the majority of the winter testing. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info The Spearfish is definitely a cross country bike, but don't let that designation fool you. I found the bike comfortable on a wider variety of terrain than you'd expect of a light and efficient 120mm bike, pushing into fairly proper trail bike territory without getting too scared. Still, efficiency and covering ground are the name of the game, so a good mix of effort and excitement seems to be the ideal. Climbing The Spearfish has an energetic feel to it, balanced with a surprising amount of grip at the rear wheel. It's not going to compete with a long travel bike when it comes to keeping the wheel stuck to the ground, but for the travel and intended purpose it does an impressive job of digging in when you put down power. Balance is key here, as the bike manages to stay high in travel despite this characteristic. Helping matters is the very steep seat tube angle, which is by a margin the most upright on any XC bike I've ridden. This detail may make for some quirky bike fit considerations, but I had little issue getting the seated position dialed for my preferred feel. That upright body position means you'll use some big-bike techniques when navigating technical climbs, with a slight drop of the seat going a long way to make sure you can shift your weight around and load the bike through funky terrain. I'd happily choose the Spearfish as a race bike, be it for longer endurance racing or something a bit more pinner. The overall weight isn't going to blow minds, but carbon bars, cranks, and wheelset are all that separate the build I have from something optimized for top-level racing. For my more mortal purposes, the Spearfish strikes a really nice balance of comfort, efficiency, and traction through real trail riding, which encourages the speedy riding I'm looking to do a light little bike. Descending At some point while recording the video for this review I described the Spearfish as surprisingly capable, and I think that holds true through my long term experiences aboard the bright green race machine. For how balanced and supportive it felt on the climbs, I expected the descending to suffer some drawbacks, but the Spearfish continually impressed with what it could handle downhill. I played with a couple different shock setups - primarily just toying around with sag and compression damping - but ended up on a pretty straightforward 30% sag and medium compression setting. The LSC dial on the shock has a fairly noticeable effect on how high you ride in the travel, and for chopped-up natural terrain I preferred the open mode - folks riding/racing smoother buff trails will probably prefer the feel of things with that batwing adjuster closed. The components feel very well suited to the Spearfish's goals, with strong brakes, stout and tunable suspension, and a drivetrain that'll deal with whatever you throw at it. Definitely not the lightest collection of parts, but that pays back a bit on the descents, as the bike doesn't feel too nervous in hard corners and in technical sections. 27957922 The one detail I'd wish to change on the Spearfish would be the rear center length. 430/431mm feels too short for the longer sizes of this bike, and starts to take away from the stability in higher speed section of trail. Sure, it does play a positive role when whipping the bike through tight turns and squiggly pedaling bits, but I haven't felt a big detraction on that front from bikes with slightly longer rear ends. All told, you could beef the Spearfish up into a little trail bike, or keep it lively and light as a long-legged XC machine. Either way, it should do well, as the suspension has a nice balanced character to it, and the geometry generally backs that up. Salsa Spearfish Specialized Epic 8 How does it compare? XC bikes are improving in leaps and bounds right now, and I'd say these two have stood out the most from the recent crop I've ridden. Both feature cutting-edge geometry, light frame weights, and provisions for cargo and long-haul rides. The Spearfish allows for a bit more wiggle room on frame and fork travel, but the Epic could probably be short-stroked to similar effect. To me, the biggest differentiators come down to suspension performance and weight. Though I haven't had the opportunity to ride them back to back, I'd give points to the Salsa on the suspension front, as the combination of pedaling efficiency and traction is quite nice. The Epic does well here, but gives up a bit in the name of efficiency and weight with the flex stay rear end. There are lighter XC frames out there, but the Epic's frame weight is remarkably light for what that bike can handle on trail. The Spearfish certainly wins on price, though only the highest end model gets carbon wheels, so things even out if you factor in that variable. The Epic's geo is a bit more stable feeling, thanks to a lower bottom bracket height, longer rear center, and ever-so-slightly slacker head angle. Not a massive difference, and a gap that is closed a bit by how the Spearfish dynamically rides, but worth noting. Either way, you're stoked - cross-country bikes are rad right now. Which Model is the Best Value? While the frame weight of the Deluxe model is an impressive 250g lighter than the standard layup, I imagine most people will end up on the non-Deluxe bikes for monetary reasons. The Spearfish C Deore 12 being only $500 more than a Deluxe frame is reason enough to give it a closer look, but the spec choices are solid throughout. The Deore is great, the SID is an appropriate fork for the bike, and even the M4100 brakes can be made quite good with some nicer rotors and metallic pads. Those looking for the Deluxe experience won't be disappointed with the GX AXS bike I have on test, as there aren't any weak points in the spec, save for little details that will come down to user preference. Tires, bars, etc. are ultimately up to your tastes, but major items like brakes, suspension, and drivetrain are great. Update: Since publication, Salsa has had to increase the pricing of the Spearfish to make up for the increased cost of business due to the new tariffs levied by the United States. As it stands, the new pricing is as follows. Spearfish XO: $10,999 | Spearfish GX $6,999 | Spearfish 90 $5,399 | Spearfish Deore $3,999 Technical Report SRAM Motive Brakes: I've already spoken about these at length, but the Motives have proven to be a massive improvement over their previous Levels. I wouldn't be taking these off this build anytime soon, thanks to power that feels ideal for a bike like this and lever action that is light and dead consistent. Teravail Camrock Tires: The Camrocks didn't blow me away, as they seemed to prioritize rolling speed above all else. The chemical grip was lacking, as the rubber felt plasticky even on dry roots and rocks, but at least they roll quite quickly. I'd happily run these in the dry summer months, or in grittier desert conditions where rolling speed takes precedence over how well a tire bites into soil. Fox 34SL Grip X: Though I'm confused by the Grip X damper's position in the Trail and Enduro worlds, it does very well in the 34SL's XC application. The rebound is quite noisy, and will probably annoy silence purists out there, but the damping feels appropriate for what is becoming an increasingly capable fork category. Pros + Well balanced climbing character + Lively and capable descender + Feels speedy without needing lockouts + Overwhelming number of bottle bosses Cons - Stock build isn't very light - Steep seat angle will be a tricky fit for traditional XC folks - Linkage bolts have awkward access Pinkbike's Take The Salsa Spearfish has been a killer little bike, with a speedy and efficient feel while pedaling and surprisingly fun traits when you point things downhill. Though it's billed as an XC bike, this frame could be many things depending on how you build it. For the most part, the geometry feels forward-thinking, and the suspension performance is quite good for the intended purpose. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Salsa Salsa Spearfish Sea Otter 2025 Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: 2025 Santa Cruz Bullit eMTB
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, santa-cruz-bicycles, santa-cruz-bullit
article_text: Bullit Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/HrRZJzGg Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 13:41 Last year, Santa Cruz made their most significant departure from the VPP suspension platform with the release of the Vala, their all-mountain eMTB. This year's release of the Bullit builds on the Vala's precedent, pushing the Horst-link platform to the 170mm mark. The bike may share an identical motor and battery spec with its' smaller sibling, but the intended use case is definitely catered towards a narrower band of riders. Carbon fiber, mixed wheels, and 170mm of travel is the case across all 5 sizes of the Bullit, regardless of the build kit you choose. All the bikes come in under 50 pounds in the Medium size as well, which is pretty impressive considering the power and travel on tap. Bullit GX AXS Details • 170mm frame travel, 170mm fork • Mixed wheels 27.5"/29" • C carbon frame • 63.6° head angle • 600Wh battery • 85 Nm torque, 600 W max power • High/low and progression flip chips • Price: $9,949 USD • Weight: 50.0lb / 22.7kg (XL bike) • santacruzbicycles.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Motor & Battery Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Santa Cruz did a great job updating the Bullit to suit riders looking to lap the gnarliest terrain around. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details Santa Cruz is offering the Bullit in both C and CC frame options, which differentiate in weight and the ability to run a cable-actuated drivetrain. The latter may bug some, but luckily the C level frame is functionally identical and just as available. Other frame details are as well integrated as you'd expect of the brand, with an easily accessed geometry flip chip at the seatstay-rocker link pivot point, and a small progression flip chip at the lower shock mount. All of the hardware is easy to nip up with a multitool, though I've had no issues with bolts coming loose over the test so far. Thanks to the move to a Horst link suspension layout, dropper insertion is massive on the bike, with a 240mm easily fitting in the XL I've been testing. Water bottle fitment is also a breeze, with room for either a large bottle or a Bosch PowerMore bolt-on battery pack. That boost pack will give you an extra 250Wh of juice, if the stock 600Wh isn't enough. Otherwise, the frame is pleasantly simple. The 600Wh battery is hard-mounted, and doesn't feature any hot swap potential, but is also perfectly quiet and feels solid. The Bosch CX motor is similarly quiet, with a pretty light whine compared to most of the other full-power options out there. The 85Nm max torque feels accessible and easy to control, and the 600W max power gets you up some pretty ridiculous pitches with ease. I'm a big fan of the controls integration Bosch created, with a Bluetooth remote that's easy to access without being in the way of your dropper or grip. Geometry Like just about every Santa Cruz out there, the Bullit features a high/low flip chip to adjust the geometry of the bike. I'll refer to things in the high mode, as that's where I've preferred things recently. The head angle sits at 63.6°, and the seat angle at 78.7°, with an effective angle of around 77.4° for the average pedaling height. Reach figures range from 435mm on the Small to 525mm on the Extra Large, with 20mm gaps through the middle three sizes. Stack heights range from 622-670mm, with larger jumps as you climb up the size range. Rear center lengths grow by 3mm per size, from 440-452mm. I've been riding the XL bike, and have been pretty pleased with the fit overall. I'm running the stem up at the top of the steer tube to gain a bit more stack, but otherwise haven't had to fuss with much. One detail worth noting that feels geometry adjacent is the stock crank length on the bikes, with stubby 155mm arms across the board. As the bike dynamically rides pretty low, this makes a big difference in how easily you can pedal the hog up tech climbs, and allows for some pretty goofy uphill maneuvers. Stubby 155mm cranks. Flip chipper. Suspension Design The big news came with the Vala, but it's just as significant on this front. Santa Cruz chose to forgo their long-synonymous VPP suspension layout in favor of the more common Horst link design, primarily due to packaging constraints and desired kinematic outputs. The engineers at Santa Cruz have been able to eke out some impressive performance from the VPP system, but there are some inherent benefits to a 4-bar design like the one used here. The performance while braking feels optimized for the use case, and the different pedaling characteristic suits the eMTB context nicely. Folks who want to toy with suspension feel will be able to adjust the progression via a simple flip chip at the lower shock mount, with a Hi/Lo progression mode to suit different terrain, riding styles, and shock choices. I generally preferred the Lo progression mode, as the sensation of support felt more consistent in the top end of travel, with enough progression to avoid any harsh bottom-outs. The Hi progression mode is noticeably softer initially, and had me firming compression at the shock to find a happier balance, with sag set at the same level. Though none of the builds come with a coil shock, the higher progression mode will probably suit that linear shock feel, though really I don't think the bike needs help with the sensitivity you get out of a coil shock. I reached out to Kiran MacKinnon, Santa Cruz's PM of Suspension and Ride Quality, to get a sense of how the Bullit stacks up to their broader lineup of bikes. The Bullit's leverage has a familiar Santa Cruz feel to it, being quite straight and progressive. The curve has a small amount of what we call "belly" in the shape which gives the bike increased ride height without building too much feedback once the bike is deeper in the travel. We enjoy consistency, so whatever shapes we employ to the leverage curve are intentional and used pretty sparingly. We believe in low anti-squat values to allow the suspension to move with limited interference from the drivetrain, both for descending and climbing traction. The Bullit has a low and regressive anti-squat figure to help the bike remain stable in pedaling segments of travel, but also reduce drivetrain interference on bigger hits. In taller gears on the cassette, the Bullit intentionally produces lower anti-squat values to further descending sensitivity compared to shorter gears, which produce higher values for climbing support. Anti-rise is very consistent as the Bullit uses travel. We targeted a 70% AR value which strikes what we think is a nice balance between sensitivity and geo preservation. When tuning anti-rise, we're also looking for a value high enough to provide a platform for the rider to push into when slowing the bike quickly. — Kiran MacKinnon One-piece link. Progression flipper. Motor & Battery Details The Bullit relies on Bosch's CX motor and 600Wh battery to propel you up the hill, with no alternative battery size available. This detail is sure to frustrate some, but was settled on deliberately to try to balance weight, maneuverability, and range. I found the bike's range to be adequate for my usage, netting about 4000-5000' of gain over rides in the mid-20 mile range. You can strap a PowerMore battery to the bottle cage mount, adding 250Wh of juice to the equation. The mount is fairly secure, but can wiggle around a bit on very rough descents. Bosch's motor is tunable via their app, which connects easily to the bike via Bluetooth. I never found the need to alter my settings from the stock setup, as their pre-loaded modes offer a great array of boost profiles. The Turbo and eMTB modes were my most often used, and despite the meaningless naming scheme, the colors make it easy to know where you sit in the range. The wireless remote keeps the cockpit relatively clutter-free, and never had issue connecting to the bike's brain to make mode changes. Bosch did a good job of keeping their head unit simple, with battery life and mode indication that don't distract and provide unnecessary fluff. Some might want to see more details, but I prefer the basic display. Red means fast. Wireless remote for a cleaner cockpit. I had the opportunity to ride Bosch's updated power profile on the Bullit, which takes the stock profile (85Nm torque, 600W power, 340% assistance) and kicks things up a notch (100Nm torque, 750W power, 400% assistance). The increased power is noticeable and impressive, but doesn't make the bike feel too outlandish - it's just an appreciable increase in the speed you can carry up steep sections of trail and road. I think the stock settings are better for my use case, as I'd rather save a bit of battery for more vertical gain, rather than spend it on the uphill speed to get there. Regardless of modes or settings, the Bosch CX motor is impressively quiet, with only a soft whine to let you know that you're being augmented by the machine. The motor and battery are both silent on the descent, and I had no issues with any components coming loose over the course of the test - Santa Cruz definitely packaged thing nicely on this front. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $9949 Travel 170mm Rear Shock FOX Float X Performance Elite, 205x65 Fork Fox 38 Float Performance Elite, Grip X2, 170mm Headset Cane Creek 50 IS Integrated Headset Cassette SRAM XS 1275 Eagle T-Type, 10-52t Crankarms SRAM GX Eagle Cranks, 155mm Chainguide OneUp Chainguide Bottom Bracket N/A Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle AXS T-Type, 12spd Chain SRAM GX Eagle AXS T-Type, 12spd Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar OneUp Aluminum Bar, 35mm Rise, 800mm Stem OneUp Stem, 42mm Grips Santa Cruz Bicycles House Grips Brakes SRAM Maven Bronze Wheelset Reserve 30|HD AL 6069 Hubs E13 SL E-Spec, 6-Bolt, 32h Rim Reserve 30|HD AL 6069 Tires Maxxis Assegai / DHRII, Double Down Seat SDG Bel-Air V3 Max Lux-Alloy Atmos Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post, 31.6, 240mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Bullit Test Bike Setup Santa Cruz does a very good job of recommending a baseline suspension pressure for just about every bike in their lineup, and I tend to find that they work quite well for me. On the Bullit, I ended up a shade over the recommended pressure, running 207psi in the Float X shock. I'll dig into this later, but I generally preferred the bike in the Lo leverage setting, despite playing with pressures and volume in both chip settings. High and Low geometry modes both felt usable to me, but the High suited my preferred handling characteristics a bit more, so I ended up erring towards that setting more often. The only component I changed through the testing period was the handlebar, opting for a 40mm rise option to get a bit more front end height than the stock 35mm bar. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info I think the Bullit feels best when pushed hard, be it on big features or rough natural terrain. I can imagine a lot of people using the bike to replace a shuttle truck to get a few laps of proper descents, and do some woodsy exploring to boot. Climbing First a note on the Bosch CX motor, as that's obviously core to the function of this bike. I've had the opportunity to ride the Bullit with Bosch's new higher-power settings update, which increases the max torque to 100 Nm and the power to 750 watts, and found the extra boost impressive, but not critical to performance. I get along very well with the stock power output you can achieve with the motor set to eMTB+ and Race modes, and prefer to preserve a bit of battery if possible. You don't need to go through any weird relearning process to hit the right cadence for speed, and the power output feels natural and intuitive. The Bullit's uphill handling feels balanced and easy to control, even when rallying the bike up the stupidest climbs. Where the Vala felt nimble and easy to bop around on standard technical climbs, the Bullit feels more tuned to scrape up steeper, looser, and less bike-optimized climbs without requiring too much body english. A longer rear center and steeper seat angle both go a long way in achieving this, with the added bonus of some stubby 155mm cranks to help you keep spinning even as the walls of the ruts close around you. Like any well-tuned Horst link bike, the Bullit provides a good amount of grip at the rear wheel under power, and still provides enough support to keep you from getting too backseat when things tip up. I had an easier time getting this bike up unfriendly climbs than just about any other eMTB I've ridden, thanks in large part to the predictability and neutral body position. 170mm of travel and a motor doesn't hurt either. Descending The Bullit is an impressively comfortable bike. The well-tuned suspension and forgiving frame flex are both to thank here, with a nod due to the components spec, specifically the wheels and tires. The bike takes big hits very nicely, and stays composed through very rough terrain without requiring too much input to maintain a line and set up for the next corner. I'd strongly recommend playing with the geometry and suspension settings to figure out which you prefer, as the ride feel is distinct depending on where you end up in those settings. I found myself consistently preferring the High geometry position and the Lo leverage setting, even on very steep and feature-heavy trails. The handling of a slightly steeper head angle and forward weight bias made the bike a bit easier to wrestle through tricky lines, even though I did prefer the rider area and stack height of the Low geo position. High with a taller handlebar was a solid half measure, and worked well for me. As to the suspension settings, I erred on the Lo mode due mostly to how supportive and predictable it felt. The two settings aren't worlds apart, but the Hi mode feels like it uses more travel more often, giving good passive grip and a nice dynamic sag position for those not actively pressing through the pedals in most compressions. On the other hand, the Lo mode felt more like you could generate grip with dynamic riding, pressing the bike through corners and compressions without sacrificing stability or ride height quite as much. Again, not a drastic difference, but I'm trying to characterize the two in a distinct way. Even if you were to swap a coil shock in place of the well-tuned stock option, I'd consider trying both modes to see which leverage option plays best with your riding style. Santa Cruz tends to do a very good job of delivering a neutral geometry that handles nicely and maintains a balanced feel, and that holds true with the Bullit. The bike feels intuitive and easy to get up to speed on, and does a good job of hitting figures that should be comfortable for a wide variety of people and places. Personally, I'd love to ride a version of the bike that pushed some of the figures a little further, but I didn't struggle to get comfortable with the bike as is. More on that geo in the Comparisons section, if you want to peek down the rabbit hole. Though nearly all my time was spent on the GX AXS build you see here, I did have the opportunity to ride the highest-end build kit option, lovingly known as XO AXS RSV. The major differentiator of that build in my experience was the Float X2 shock, and Reserve carbon wheel spec. The X2 allows for more adjustment fidelity, namely the ability to control high-speed compression and rebound - both useful on a bike that you can put into some pretty high-amplitude situations. I think most people will be well served by the Float X that shows up on all other builds, but some might like having the extra control and damping on offer from the X2. The carbon wheels offer a more energetic ride feel, damping less of the chattery inputs on trail but giving more energy back in corners and heavy compressions. As noted later on, the aluminum wheels held up admirably well, but a burly set of carbon hoops can keep on trucking remarkably well. It should come as no surprise that the Bullit isn't terribly exciting on mellow trails - it's simply not meant for that. I'm sure it'll be seen all over the map, but in my mind this is a bike for the special places where pedal-accessible terrain really warrants all the bike you can get. You know who you are. Vala vs. Bullit From my review of the Vala: Part of the reason I ride e-bikes at all is to try to improve skills through the extra riding you can get, so in a way the Vala's sharper handling was a boon to this goal. I realize that's not everyone's intent with getting an eMTB, but hopefully you have a fairly accurate sense of why you're purchasing a very expensive motorized bike before you do so. If all-out capability is the goal, and you're looking to drag brake down the steepest trails around, Santa Cruz's new Bullit is probably the better option. I think the Bullit is a bit more location dependent, where the Vala will probably suit a wider swath of riders and terrain. Despite the longer travel, the Bullit doesn't add much weight, but the geometry is a bit more sprawled out, and the suspension feel biases more towards terrain that can really push a bike. Adding to that, I think the Bullit is ultimately more of a niche offering than the Vala - or so it should be. The Vala will better serve more people in more places, while the Bullit should be reserved for trails where you can really tap into the bike's potential. This is somewhat true of most long-travel bikes, but in the Bullit's case it feels underwhelming on mellower trails and smoother terrain. For those looking to maximize fun over capability, I'd definitely lean towards the Vala. Santa Cruz Bullit GasGas ECC 6 How does it compare? The GasGas ECC 6 was a very unique bike, and though it had some weak points as a do-everything long travel eMTB, there were moments of greatness that feel worth comparing here. That bike's stability at high speed and ability to generate grip are phenomenal, and make up for the awkward and demanding slower speed handling. I think the Bullit falls behind on all-out stability and front wheel grip, but does a far better job of remaining balanced across the spectrum of riding situations. Those looking for a bike that feels like a bike will probably be happier more often on the Bullit, but if you have fast trails and a penchant for speed, the GasGas is worth a look. Though the Bullit's geometry feels like a solid package for most riders, there are some elements of the GasGas I'd be curious to integrate on the Santa Cruz to see how much further you could take things. The ECC 6 has a 20mm taller stack height than the Bullit, and features a 20mm longer chainstay - both giving the rider more room to move in the chassis without upsetting the balance and weight bias between the wheels. Though not motorized, the Frameworks Enduro Bike I've been riding a lot errs closer to the GasGas in this way, and also manages rider input in a way that I prefer. The extreme geometry of the GasGas combined with a 29" rear wheel made for a full package that was hard to handle in the steepest trail situations, but I do think a mixed wheel setup would improve things greatly on that front. The less-supportive suspension feel of that bike also made for some unusual handling, where the Bullit manages its travel very nicely. Overall, I think the Bullit with a higher bar (as I've been riding it) is a great middle ground, and keeps the handling easy for more riders in more situations. My curiosity is more to do with the upper limit potential of the bike, where there's always room for improvement. Which Model is the Best Value? With only four models on tap, the choice is pretty easy here. The Bullit 90 is essentially identical to the GX AXS build I rode, but with a mechanical Transmission drivetrain and Maven Base brakes. Those Base model brakes' light lever action might make them better than the Bronze option for some riders, and the shifting performance of the Eagle 90 drivetrain is functionally the same as its wireless counterpart. As you'll see below, the 30|HD AL wheels have been impressive, and complement the ride feel of the Bullit nicely. I wish there was a cheaper build that featured the Performance Series Elite version of the Float X2 shock, but ultimately I don't have too many complaints about the Float X that all but the nicest kit include. Being able to control high-speed rebound and compression allows for some marginal gains on composure and comfort, but the complication might not be worth the cash for most. Technical Report Maxxis DoubleDown Tires: I can imagine some folks complaining about the lack of a DH casing on at least the back of this bike, but I'm here to give the DoubleDowns some defense. I ran these tires through the duration of the test, and found that the casing was plenty sturdy for the majority of riding I did on the Bullit. Beyond that, I think they add something when it comes to compliance and comfort, which when paired with the alloy wheels and less-than-rigid chassis, makes for a very comfy package. Reserve 30|HD AL Wheelset: I expected these wheels to show some serious wear by the end of the test period, but they continued to impress through to the very end. The rims held true, tension remained consistent, and they provided a nice ride feel that balanced sturdiness with a bit of give in rougher bits of riding. Beyond some tensioning here and there, they were maintenance free, and held up remarkably well. The only complaint I had was due to the front hub, which had a tendency to creak and ping while rolling along. The sound was similar to the settling noise you get from spoke nipples, but never went away and seemed to be emanating from the spoke-flange interface. It didn't yield any riding issues, just some annoying noise if you were listening for it. Pros + Stellar suspension performance + Impressively reliable frame and electronics + No-fuss build kit + Impressive climbing abilities Cons - Battery capacity will disappoint some (but a range extender is available) - Taller riders may want even more stack & longer chainstays Pinkbike's Take Santa Cruz did a great job updating the Bullit to suit riders looking to lap the gnarliest terrain around. The suspension is adaptable and offers a great balance of support and grip, and though the geometry could be taken a shade further, it still offers an intuitive ride that feels well suited to steep and feature-heavy trails. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review.
