With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Santa Cruz V10 - Hard to Ignore
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, field-test, dh-bikes, santa-cruz-bicycles, santa-cruz-v10
article_text: Santa Cruz V10 Review: Hard To Ignore | 2024 Downhill Bike Field Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/XHV8B8V7 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 12:00 The latest version of the Santa Cruz V10 looks a lot like the one it supersedes. When you dive into the details and listen to feed from the team riders, though, you quickly realize this is a seriously new and evolved frame. High pivots and idlers were experimented with, but veteran test riders dismissed any of those benefits in favor of acceleration and maneuverability. There’s also a twist when it comes to wheel sizes, but more on that in the frame details section. V10 Details • Frame: Carbon • Wheel sizes: MX (S, M, L), 29er (XL only) • Travel: 208mm, 203mm fork • VPP suspension • 62.7-63 degree head angle • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Reach: 419, 454, 474, 499mm (mid position) • Chainstays: 445, 451, 456, 461mm (mid position) • Weight: 16.5 kg / 36.5 lb (LG) • Price: $8,599 USD • santacruzbicycles.com As a carbon-only frame, the V10's price point isn’t low. The “S” kit starts at $6,799 USD while the X01 build costs $8,599. For riders looking to choose their own components, a frame kit checks out at $3,799 USD and includes a Fox DHX2 shock. Contents Introduction Frame Details and Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Descending Technical Report Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 37 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Riding this new V10 was like coming home - everything is where it should be and nothing is out of the ordinary. During the first outing, two corners into the track, I knew we were on. — Matt Beer Frame and Suspension Details On the surface, there are two significant visual changes to the frame, but tucked away are many more features that make this V10 a whole new bike. First, the twin tube seat mast adds strength to the relocated upper pivot, part of the equation when tweaking the kinematics. Then comes the dedicated wheel sizes. Size small, medium, and large sizes run on mixed wheels only, whereas the extra large frame uses 29” wheels front and rear, exclusively. Each frame size also receives cleanly executed adjustments in three key areas: reach, chainstay, and the head angle / bottom bracket. To accommodate riders situated in between frame sizes, interchangeable headset cups offer +/- 8mm of reach adjustment. There is a centered zero set of top and bottom cups and the 8mm offset pair which can be reversed. That offers a much smaller jump between frame sizes. The chainstays are size-specific but can also be adjusted fore and aft by 5mm from the neutral position. Unlike the headset cups, they don’t can't double up to reduce the amount of pieces needed, since the drive side requires three different derailleur hangers for each position. On the non-driveside, there are just two dropout chips, a centered one and another offset that can be flipped to give the shorter or longer positions. A discrete grub screw accessed from the inside of the dropout keeps the chip in place. The brake mount features three different positions to align the rotor, depending on the chainstay length chosen. Located at the top of the lower link, the shock mount sits in two pairs of centered or offset chips. Those raise or lower the bottom bracket and adjust the head angle in three arrangements. Although those numbers barely change on paper, the dynamic geometry or handling characteristics on the trail are noticeably different. Building on an already clean and solid chassis, Santa Cruz has integrated the fork bumpers and tidied up the cable routing with plastic external guides near the lower link. You'll also still find a sleek fender that guards the rear shock, finned chain protector, and bolt-on downtube guards. Underneath all of that comes revised kinematics - the greatest takeaway being more mid-stroke support. In our First Look , ex-World Cup racer and Santa Cruz engineer, Kiran MacKinnon, explains just how they achieve that. Geometry Thanks to this new fangled built-in reach adjusting headset, a trend that seems to be catching on amongst brands, riders previously stuck between sizes should now be much closer to their ideal reach number. The size large that we tested had a reach of 474mm and a 456mm chainstay in the neutral positions. There’s also a generous stack height for each frame, which seems to be a trend amongst Santa Cruz bikes in general. I wouldn’t say that it’s too high, but some riders choosing the short reach setting may find that they’ll be slamming the front end height or looking for a lower rise bar. As for the head angle, the middle position tips the fork back to 62.9°. The flip-chip on the lower link can push that up to 63° or down to 62.7. The BB drop measured from the front axle is 19mm, but varies 2mm up or down depending on the setting. One handcuff that riders choosing the XL frame size are faced with is the need to run a 29” rear wheel. Taller riders should have enough leverage to lean over that larger rear wheel and have enough clearance to avoid butt buzzes on the tire, however, some may still prefer a 27.5” rear wheel for increased maneuverability if given the choice. RIDING THE V10 Test Bike Setup The more adjustment options you have on a bike, the greater the chance of missing the mark on the setup. Thankfully, Santa Cruz started with up-to-date aggressive geometry and a neatly displayed suspension setup guide that is easy to find on their website. It may not be as comprehensive in terms of terrain selection as Commencal's Supreme V5 guide, however the middle geometry positions are an excellent starting point. Due to this bike being a little late in its inclusion in the group test, we sadly couldn't outfit it with the control tires. After building up the size large V10 and taking a parking lot spin, I immediately noticed how little 550 spring budged. I scoped out that rear shock set up guide and noticed that at 77 kg I landed right in the middle of the 500 and 550 spring weights. Swapping to the 500 spring gave roughly 25% sag and I instantly felt more comfortable. Throughout the test I rarely reached full travel or noticed places where the heavier spring would be more advantageous. Santa Cruz's advised shock settings start off with excellent numbers for baseline. I did open the high-speed compression circuit one click and added two to the low speed compression. I also toyed with the rebound, closing both red high and low-speed rebound clickers one notch from the suggested settings for the 500 lb/in spring. The Fox 40 is a fork that I’m familiar with, however, I decided to try Santa Cruz's suggestions for the Grip2 damper first. That meant pumping the fork to 76 psi and running the compression circuits closed more than I was used to. Although that made the front end very plush and track well, I wanted to keep the front end higher on heavy impacts. I installed a fifth volume spacer and increased the pressure to 87 psi. Next, the low-speed rebound was set -7 from closed and the high speed to -5. The low speed compression was set to -12 and the high-speed to -6. In terms of geometry settings and cockpit space, I placed the reach in the shortest setting to equal 466mm with a 5mm spacer below the top crown. The 35mm rise bars were cut to 780mm. Initially I placed the chainstay in the short setting and the lower shock mount to the slack setting. I found that whole combination to cause a tiny bit of understeer on flatter tracks, which admittedly, could have been a product of scaling down the rear spring rate. I moved the chainstay adjustment from the short to middle position of 456mm to place more weight on the front wheel. Later, I would place the shock flip-chip in the middle setting and found that to hold the bike up higher slightly in the sag. Descending Henry nailed it by saying the V10 is second best at everything. Don’t get us wrong, that’s not knocking it one bit, because it rides comfortably and confidently on any trail. This is a downhill bike with that Goldilocks quality about it: the frame is strong, but not too stiff, supple without being soggy, and progressive without being too kicky. The slacker head angle isn’t unwieldy either. A moderately long rear center matches the rear numbers well too. The Commencal Supreme may have that dancing rear wheel, but it didn’t bring about confidence on harrowingly steep sections in the same way as the V10. The Supreme does so by hugging the ground closely and doesn't rely on how far out the front wheel sits. The V10 had an attacking prowess to dive straight into those situations, like the M1, but with a more neutral balance and without the extra heft to get it moving on more mundane trails. And what about that added mid-stroke support that the team riders were ecstatic about? Well, it isn’t too dissimilar to the supportive suspension that the YT Tues brought into the equation. That really keeps the V10 from mushing into the travel when you want to pump out of corners or boost jump tracks. Chain clattering through the rear end of the bike is a negative attribute that we tuned into after spending time on all of these different bikes. Although some Santa Cruz Syndicate team riders are running O-Chain drivetrain damping devices (and we don’t see why you wouldn’t since it only calms this further) the V10 handles chain feedback well. Noise is also not a huge factor from the chain or anywhere else on the frame for that matter. Finding flaws in the V10 is no easy task. I’m inclined to say that the suspension caters to a slightly more dynamic rider due to the additional mid-stroke support. The rear end doesn't have quite the same degree of free-floating suppleness on tap like the Frameworks did, but at the same time, the V10 also doesn't call on such a soft top end to settle the bike in its stance. That dynamic geometry is where the special formula comes from. It's enough to hold you on the line while staying higher in the travel, yet still encourages attacking the spicy bits with poise. Technical Report Fox DHX2: Staying close to the parameters of the set up guide, I was impressed with the shock tune. Dialling back the compression may be favorable for some riders who have a forward weight bias. The performance of the DHX2 was good, until it wasn't. Further into the review the shock began to top out. This was particularly noticeable when Henry dialed in his fast rebound preferences. Reserve 30|HD AL wheels: Of all the aluminum wheels on test, they seem to fare extremely well. They were subjected to downhill tracks on the North Shore, Vancouver Island, and the Whistler Bike Park. I wouldn’t say that the V10’s frame is too stiff, but it’s on the higher side and these wheels bring a sense of comfort and tracking to the bike. SRAM Code RSC: What was once a staple on downhill bikes has now been overshadowed by the uber-powerful and mammoth-sized Maven brakes (these were installed by Santa Cruz prior to our single day of filming). The Code RSC still offers an agreeable lever feel with useful adjustments. You’ll still see them on select downhill racer's bikes, but for the average rider, the Code may be more aligned with the capabilities of trail bikes these days. Pros + Balanced, aggressive geometry + Suspension is supportive yet comfortable + Well executed frame adjustments and features + Not having a speciality is a speciality in itself Cons - Higher price point - Some XL riders may want MX wheels Pinkbike's Take How do they do it? Santa Cruz has knocked it out of the park with the latest V10. They've built a bike that has taken their team riders to the top step of World Cup podiums and makes weekend warriors feel like superheroes. Unlike some of the other bikes on test that have their moments of glory, the V10 is exceptional everywhere on the mountain. From jump trails to race tracks, the poise from the suspension and balance from the geometry work brilliantly together - it’s neither nervous or a total plow. That winning combo allows you to attack when you feel bold enough to let it bark without being glued to the ground at all times. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Field Test DH Bikes Santa Cruz Bicycles Santa Cruz V10 Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Santa Cruz Vala - The VPP-Free eMTB
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, santa-cruz-bicycles, santa-cruz-vala
article_text: The End Of VPP? Santa Cruz Vala Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/KdSWHIsK Copied Live 00:20 00:10 00:31 Skip The big news of the Vala's release was Santa Cruz's departure from their synonymous VPP suspension layout, but in many ways that buries the lede. The Vala is another solid bike from a the brand, regardless of what suspension design they chose to implement to make it work. The geometry, weight, and handling characteristics are all targeting a "normal bike" experience, and in some ways they've gotten quite close. Vala Details • 150mm frame travel, 160mm fork • Mixed wheels 27.5"/29" • C or CC carbon frame • 63.9° head angle • 600Wh battery • 85 Nm torque, 600 W max power • High/low and progression flip chips • Price: $7,299 – $12,799 USD • Weight: 48.08lbs / 21.8kg (XL bike) • santacruzbicycles.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Long story short, the Vala can handle just about anything you throw at it, but errs on the fun side of things, with proportions and weight meant more to mimic regular bikes than to maximize the monster truck e-bike characteristics. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details This is also Santa Cruz's first eMTB using the Bosch motor/battery system, and it implements the brand new CX Performance refresh to provide the power. With quieter operation, a lighter motor and batteries, and improved software elements, the new CX is certainly on the short list for best full-power systems on the market right now. While the new CX system has been designed around both a 600Wh and 800Wh battery option, Santa Cruz has chosen to make the Vala a 600Wh-only bike, with no option to run the larger package. It is compatible with the Powermore accessory battery, which provides an additional 250Wh of juice for longer missions. Bosch CX motor and a low-slung charging plug. Wireless control for the motor and shifting. C and CC frame options denote the carbon quality and weight, the latter being a bit lighter. High/low geometry. High/low progression. The Vala is a dedicated mixed-wheel bike, regardless of size. That doesn't mean they've done away with adjustability though, as there are two flip chips integrated into the frame. At the back of the rocker link is the High/Low mode adjustment, which slightly alters the frame geometry to suit rider preference and terrain. The lower shock mount flip chip allows you to switch between high and low progression, and all changes can be made easily trailside with a 5mm wrench. As with non-electric Santa Cruz bikes, the Vala comes in either C or CC frame options, with the latter incurring some weight savings and higher-end carbon. The CC bike also eschews any internal derailleur routing, like the new Bronson and Hightower, making it an electric-only bike as it stands. Three of the builds come with the C frame, and two with the CC. Geometry The Vala's geometry closely resembles the recently released Bronson, with a descent-focused demeanor that is meant to handle whatever you throw its way. The high/low geometry adjustment changes bottom bracket height by 4mm, and head/seat tube angles by 0.3°. I'll be referring to the geometry in the Low position, as that's how I've primarily ridden the bike. The 63.9° head angle is plenty slack for even the steepest trails around, and a fairly steep 77.2° seat tube angle keeps you between the wheels on all but the steepest climbs. The actual seat angle at pedaling height is a bit slacker than this advertised figure, but Santa Cruz is now publishing those figures so people get a more accurate sense of things. Reach figures range from 432mm to 522mm, with stack heights ranging from 626mm to 671mm. There are five sizes across the range, with nicely scaled jumps in sizing between each. The rear center lengths also vary by size, going from 440mm on the size Small to 451mm on the XXL. I'm a fan of the proportions Santa Cruz gives their bikes, with each size scaling in a way that rides well and feels balanced. That holds true with the Vala, as my XL fits like a glove and doesn't require any odd setup to get stack and reach suited to the bike's intended purposes. Leverage rate options. Anti-rise. Anti-squat. Suspension Design This may be Santa Cruz's first 4-bar bike, but they're employing many of the same tactics used on their VPP bikes to achieve the ride feel they wanted for the Vala. For the past few years, Santa Cruz has been trending towards lower and lower anti-squat figures for their more descent-oriented bikes, and the Vala is much the same. Though they say it was primarily a packaging issue, the different suspension plays a beneficial role here as well, as they can achieve more linear curves for things like anti-squat and anti-rise when compared to their VPP counterparts. As previously mentioned, this bike has a progression flip chip that allows you to change the leverage rate of the rear suspension to suit your preferences, opting between 26% and 29%. The lower starting ratio (Lo mode) will offer more support off the top, and a more consistent feel through travel, where the higher starting ratio (Hi mode) should feel a little more active at the top of the stroke, with more of a ramp up as you approach bottom-out. The difference between the two isn't gigantic, but it's worth playing with the two options to see which you prefer. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $11499 Travel 150mm Rear Shock FOX Float X Factory, 205x60 Fork FOX 38 Factory, Grip X2, 160mm Headset Cane Creek 50 IS Integrated Cassette SRAM XO T-Type Crankarms SRAM XO T-Type Chainguide OneUp Chain Guide Bottom Bracket N/A Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM XO T-Type Chain SRAM XO T-Type Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM XO T-Type Handlebar Santa Cruz 35 Carbon Bar, 800mm Stem OneUp Stem, 42mm -or- Burgtec Enduro Stem, 42mm Grips Santa Cruz Bicycles House Grips Brakes SRAM Maven Silver Stealth Wheelset Reserve 30|HD Carbon Hubs DT Swiss 350 Spokes DT Swiss Rim Reserve 30|HD Carbon Tires Front: Schwalbe Magic Mary, 29x2.5, Trail Radial Ultra Soft | Rear: Schwalbe Magic Mary, 27.5x2.5, Gravity Radial Soft Seat WTB Silverado Medium Fusion, CroMo SL Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post, 31.6, 120-240mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup The Vala was an easy bike to get up to speed on, with a bevy of familiar components and a geometry package that fits me well. At a certain point, I swapped out the Radial Trail casing front tire, as it felt a bit squirmy for the bike, especially compared to the Gravity rear. I also tried a Race Face Era handlebar in place of the Santa Cruz branded one, as I don't love the sweep of the SC ones. Santa Cruz's recommended suspension setup often works quite well for me, and that rang true here as well. I found I liked the Lo progression mode with around 202psi, and found the stock 0.4 cu. in. spacer worked well for most riding, shy of the heaviest g-outs. For more freeridey feature-heavy trails, I typically ran the bike in Hi progression, with a few more pounds of pressure in the shock. Lo progression and a 0.6 volume spacer was a happy medium, if I had to err on one setup for everywhere. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario The 160mm Fox 38 came with 3 volume spacers installed, and while I was able to use full travel here and there, I ultimately ended up running 2 spacers for the bulk of the test period, as I preferred the more linear feel at 106psi. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info The Vala goes well just about everywhere, with handling that isn't too boring on flatter trails, and capability that can push into some truly proper terrain. Generally, the bike feels ideal for doing just what you'd do on your average 150/160mm bike, but with a boost. Climbing A shocking revelation: the ebike climbs well. But there's more to it than just a motor - the Vala's handling characteristics bias more towards "normal" climbs than the goofy hard enduro climbs that can be a hoot on a full powered bike. For most use cases, this is a positive, as the Vala isn't a handful on climbs of all sorts, thanks to a wheelbase that isn't too extreme, fairly low build weight, and a comfortable seated position. The geo of the bike keeps you fairly centered, but it's not quite as central a spot as some more of the more progressive eMTBs out there. If your typical e-bike ride includes purpose-built climb trails and FSR climbs, then you'll be well served by the Vala. If you seek out more adventurous and challenging climbs, moto ruts, and otherwise inadvisable maneuvers, then some of the longer rear-center eMTBs out there do a better job of keeping the front wheel down when you're scraping up something heinous. A steeper seat angle could help here, but I think the seat angle of the bike fits the use case well, and feels nice and comfortable on flatter terrain - making a longer rear the better solution in my mind. Descending First and foremost, the Vala deserves a lot of praise for the suspension performance. The bike can be pushed well beyond what you'd expect of the travel numbers, and feels composed at the limits of that travel. I rode plenty of trails that regularly scare me, and didn't find the bike holding me back. On the flip side, mellower trails that can be a bit ponderous on a big eMTB were still fun on the Vala, as the geometry biases a bit towards all-around handling versus all-out speed. That geo feels mostly well considered, but I can't help but wonder how much more you could get out of the bike with a couple tweaks. As mentioned in the Climbing section, I think the rear center could be a good bit longer on this bike before you started feeling any detrimental effects. Sure, the shorter chainstay helps keep the bike lively when you're riding mellower trails, but I haven't been unhappy with the bikes I've ridden that push that figure well past the Vala's. For those looking to carry as much speed as possible on relatively low-angle trails (read: not vertical), there are better options out there. But for a mixed bag, where you're riding cruiser jump lines one day and feature-heavy tech the next, I think the Vala strikes a nice balance. While I do think that balance could be maintained while improving the overall stability, I understand why Santa Cruz settled on something a bit more neutral. Long story short, the Vala can handle just about anything you throw at it, but errs on the fun side of things, with proportions and weight meant more to mimic regular bikes than to maximize the monster truck e-bike characteristics. Vala vs. Bullit Part of the reason I ride e-bikes at all is to try to improve skills through the extra riding you can get, so in a way the Vala's sharper handling was a boon to this goal. I realize that's not everyone's intent with getting an eMTB, but hopefully you have a fairly accurate sense of why you're purchasing a very expensive motorized bike before you do so. If all-out capability is the goal, and you're looking to drag brake down the steepest trails around, Santa Cruz's new Bullit is probably the better option. I think the Bullit is a bit more location dependent, where the Vala will probably suit a wider swath of riders and terrain. Despite the longer travel, the Bullit doesn't add much weight, but the geometry is a bit more sprawled out, and the suspension feel biases more towards terrain that can really push a bike. Santa Cruz Vala Transition Regulator How does it compare? A few bikes come to mind when stacking the Vala up against the competition, but two will likely surface as the most relevant comparisons: the Specialized Levo 4 and the Transition Regulator. All three feature 150mm of rear suspension delivered by 4-bar suspension, all three rely on a full-fat eMTB motor to get you up the hill, and all three have fairly similar geometry on paper. The Vala and Regulator are nearly identical on the geometry front, with slightly longer reach on the Transition. Ultimately, build kit, suspension performance, and frame details are going to be the differentiating factors, so best to spin through Mike's review to get a sense of which will be better for you. For me, the user-adjustable geometry and kinematics are a point to the Vala's credit, as is the monetary value you get with a given build kit. If choosing between the Levo 4 and Vala, I'd opt for the latter. The motor and battery spec differences amounted to a smaller effect on trail than expected, and I far preferred the ride feel the Vala provided. The bike feels more balanced between the wheels, especially in hard cornering situations, where the Levo had a tendency to push the front if you weren't aggressively weighting the front end. This could be chalked up to a few different factors, be it the front / rear center geometry balance, the dynamic ride characteristics of the Genie shock, or simply the Levo's heavier weight. In the end, the Vala just felt more composed in a variety of trail situations, and kept things plenty fun. Which Model is the Best Value? Seven build options is a bit of a bloated lineup, especially when the spec differences are only vaguely dissimilar as you move through the models. Every single bike has a Float X shock (all with the same great tune), nearly all have a Fox 38 out front, and every model comes with some form of SRAM drivetrain and brakes. Optimizing for the best of those latter categories, I'd opt for the Vala 90, which comes with a mechanical Transmission drivetrain, Maven Silver brakes, and Reserve Alloy wheels. The suspension is as good as need be, and the finishing touches are all ideal for the use case. While $8,599 is very far from a steal, there aren't any wasted parts, and the bike should hold up to the abuse its sure to receive. Technical Report Schwalbe Radial Tires: It should be no mystery that we're fans of Schwalbe's new radial tire offerings - they offer a unique amount of grip, comfort, and durability without too much of a downside. I haven't gotten along with the Magic Mary very well in the past, but in a radial casing the classic tire starts to make more sense to me. I found the Trail casing to be a bit too light for the eMTB use-case, but the Gravity out back stayed on for the duration of the test. The Trail radials feel sweet on smaller bikes, but I found myself climbing so high in pressure to match the feel of the rear that it brought diminishing returns. A Gravity casing Shredda Gravity lived on the front for most of the test, and all was well. Bosch CX Race Motor Hardware: The motor and battery on the Vala gave me zero issues through the long duration of this test, which is astounding considering the failure rate I've had on eMTBs to date. The only hitch I encountered was a strange one, and hopefully secluded to the pre-production batch my bike came from. On one particularly foul mud ride, I noticed some play develop in the pedals, and upon cleaning the bike, found that the bushings that the motor mount bolts ride on had managed to escape their housing and disappear. Apparently the production versions have a better lip design to retain them, and after Santa Cruz sent a fresh set my way I had zero issues with the hardware working loose. OneUp 240mm Dropper Post: Not only does the 240mm post put the saddle completely out of your way on any sort of gradient, but this post just keeps working without issue. No play, noise, or slow return occurred through the long test period, despite some truly foul winter riding. Pros + Easy handling, adaptable performance + Impressively reliable frame and electronics + Excellent build kit Cons - Battery capacity will disappoint some (but a range extender is available) - Requires attention on awkward steep climbs - Very expensive Pinkbike's Take The Santa Cruz Vala is fun, adaptable, and does a surprisingly good job of simulating the non-motorized riding experience. It may not be the most capable eMTB I've ever ridden, but it doesn't shy away from speed or features, and retains a lively handling characteristic that pays dividends on mellower trails. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Santa Cruz Bicycles Santa Cruz Vala Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Saris' New Edge 2" Hitch Rack
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: SarisEdge https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/FT3qWM6K Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 07:15 Bike racks come in all shapes and sizes, but a common variety seems to be emerging amongst the mountain bike community. The fold up tray rack is pretty ubiquitous, with versions from most brands out there, both small and large alike. Saris already has one offering in this realm - the modular MHS system - but they're adding a second option with the Edge. This rack is fairly simple, with one arm holding the front wheel and a strap securing the rear, but there are some little details thrown in to try to set it apart. Edge Details • 80lb weight limit per tray • Fits wheels 24” to 29” x 3” or 27.5” x 5” • No frame or fender contact • Built-in cable lock • RV rated, 2" hitch • Weight: 67lbs / 30.4kg • Price: $1000 USD • saris.com The Edge has a very high load rating per tray, and adds compatibility with fatbikes and smaller-wheeled bikes to the mix. The 80 pound limit is well in excess of what most folks will need, but consider it extra assurance should you hit a nasty bump in the road with your 60-pound eMTB on the rack. Fits long bikes. The telescoping arm has some tricks up its sleeve, with a spring-loaded design that keeps things tight, and a built in detent to make sure the hook doesn't interfere with fenders, forks, or brake lines. The spring function keeps the arm tight to the tire automatically, and all you have to do is give it a solid push to secure things for the drive. For the rear wheel, things are more typical, but there's one added detail to improve the standard ratchet design. In addition to the very long ratchet strap, there's a sliding block that allows you to raise the rear tire by 2.5", for better clearance should your saddle interfere with the other bike's handlebars. In Use Loading / Unloading The loading process is the same as with any other one-arm rack, but things are made a bit easier by the spring loaded hook and limited range of motion on the arm's pivot. You can't over-clock the arm, it just lands in the same spot every time, right in front of a long fender. The spring helps keep things snug, and is extra handy when unloading the bike and dropping the arm down - the hook quickly returns to the shortest position out of the way. The built-in lock is a nice touch. Not the most secure, but good in a pinch. I find ratchet straps to be a bit annoying to use, as you have to thread them through the spokes every time you load the bike, making sure to not trap them down with the rear wheel when loading things. Unloading is fairly easy, as you just depress the plastic catch and yank the strap out. I haven't had too many occasions to use the rear wheel spacer on the Edge, so mostly it has remained off to the side as more of a tire chock. If you should need it, the block slides around pretty easily with two hands, and stays securely in position once ratcheted down. On and Off Road This is a Honda Fit folks, don't get too excited. The biggest hinderance to off road usage on the Edge is the lack of clearance, as the rack does nothing to lift the bikes above the hitch receiver. For taller vehicles, this might not be an issue, but every car out there benefits from more clearance out back, even if just for steep driveways. The anti-rattle tightening feature works well here, and keeps the rack securely in place in the hitch, without any loosening through the test period. I find two-arm racks to be more secure in true off-road situations, as the extra rigidity that comes from the second set of arms helps reduce how much the bikes can wag. The Edge lets the bikes flop a fair bit, but hasn't done anything scary in my time with it. Part of this extra movement is probably due to the extra tire clearance in the front wheel basket, where there's a fair bit of clearance for the tire to rotate without any support on the sides. When the rack is stowed upright in no-bike mode, there is a noticeable rattle between the rack and the main pivot point, as the body rocks back and forth in the detent. This probably won't be apparent on RVs and off the back of massive vehicles, but it was hard to ignore when driving around town in my smaller car. Stowing the rack in the 3/4 position mostly alleviates this, but that's not the cleanest solution if you're looking to keep the rack folded tight to the back of your car. Easy one handed deploy, usually a two hand stow. What Could Be Improved? The aforementioned rattle is an annoyance, but could be less of an issue for some users depending on the vehicle type. My bigger complaints come down to the finer details of the rack, and the potential durability issues that might come up. The overall chassis is sturdy, with a massive pivot point and beefy trays, but the arms have some play in them that is sure to develop further over time, as pretty much all telescoping hook armed racks are wont to do. The ratchet hardware is already showing some rust, and though it hasn't affected things in use yet, it might do so after a few more months of road grime immersion. I've noticed that after heavy rains the rack drains water for a while afterwards, meaning quite a bit can pool up in the trays if the rack stays on your car. It's possible that won't amount to much in the long term, but only time will tell. My last frustration is with the ratchet straps, namely the damage they can do to the rear rim of the bike. As your bike shimmies back and forth in the rack, the tightened ratchet strap constantly rubs on the location where installed, leading to noticeable scratching of the rim, especially in wet weather or when the bike is dirty. Some ratchet designs employ a slide-on boot to prevent this, but the Edge doesn't include that preventative measure. Plus, those boots are easy to lose as you use the rack, making them something of a half measure. Durability The Edge rack has been living on my car for the past two months, and aside from the concerns listed above, things haven't changed a bit. That's far from a long term test in the world of bike racks though, and time will tell how well the Saris survives. Saris offers a limited lifetime warranty, mostly against any defects in manufacturing, excluding rust, paint damage, and wear and tear. Pros + One hand opening, slick arm design + High load rating, RV approved + No frame contact + Holds large tires Cons - Cost is high for middling quality - Ratchet straps are fussy and can scratch wheels - Rattles when stowed upright Pinkbike's Take The Saris Edge packs a bunch of features into a fairly sleek package, but lacks some refinement when it comes to finish quality and elegance. The design of the front arm makes loading quick and easy, and easily avoids frame contact, but the ratchet strap is a little fussier and can scratch rims. Though it wouldn't be my top choice for a regular car, folks with extremely heavy e-bikes and an RV might find it ideal. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Schwalbe's Shredda Gravity Tires are a Loamer Cheat Code
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shredda tire review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DCyOw9FC Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:21 Schwalbe's recent release of their radial casing tires made quite the splash, with material properties and a ride feel that differs greatly from existing tire tech. Nested in that release was something wilder still: their new Shredda Gravity design. These are possibly the most aggressive tread we've seen on a mountain bike tire to date, with a comically deep tread depth and massive lugs. The Shredda has a front and rear specific tread design, with both tires molded with Schwalbe's Ultra Soft rubber in a 29x2.5", with a 27.5" option available for the Rear. Positioned as an eMTB-specific design, the Shreddas certainly have some moto influence, at least in the aesthetics department. Wanting to see how that grip shaped up on pedal bikes and their electric counterparts alike, I've been running both the front and rear Shreddas for a couple months now to see how they stack up. This has conveniently coincided with some of the wetter weather the Pacific Northwest has seen in years, as it turns out that's pretty much the ideal weather pattern for these. Shredda Front • Compound: Addix Ultra Soft • Casing: Gravity Pro Radial • Sizes: 29x2.5" only • Actual weight: 1494g • Price: $104 USD • schwalbetires.com Shredda Rear • Compound: Addix Ultra Soft • Casing: Gravity Pro Radial • Sizes: 29x2.5", 27.5x2.5" • Actual weight: 1509g • Price: $104 USD • schwalbetires.com Shredda Front. Shredda Rear. Shredda Front Tread The Shredda Front uses a pretty typical 2-2-2-2 center block pattern, with a consistent row of edge lugs, but it's the knob height that really sets this thing apart from just about anything else on the market. The lugs are massive, with enough support to keep them from folding over on anything firm. Every other pair of center lugs has a short bridge to help with braking traction and overall stability, but otherwise the knobs are interrupted. There is minimal siping, and not much else to mention - this thing is just about digging in and clearing soil, no matter how deep it gets. Shredda Rear Tread The Shredda Rear is a bit more involved, with a 3-2-2-3 center pattern and a consistent edge lug pattern with slightly varied spacing. The space between lugs is similar to the Front tread, but the knob height isn't quite as extreme. The 2-block pairs are joined with a bridge, and the 3-block sets are spaced wide enough to serve as a great cornering transition. It somewhat resembles the Magic Mary, but with a more open and aggressive pattern. Siping is similarly minimal to the Front, to keep the knobs stable. TESTING Download the Trailforks App It's hard to feature a testing area for the Shreddas, because all the trails where they shine are usually quite well hidden. Think steep, off-camber, and with the deepest soil you can conjure up in your mind. They also feel pretty happy in mud, but more on that later. Ultimately, they're a very location-dependent tread pattern, so don't go buying a set for your local hardpack tracks. PERFORMANCE The Shredda Gravity tires are a singular offering for now, coming only in Schwalbe's stickiest rubber and burliest Radial casing. Weighing in at about 1500 grams each, these are certainly not going to appeal to those concerned with keeping things spritely, but the mass pays dividends on the descents. The lack of a 27.5" rear variant is odd to me, considering the eMTB-specific claims Schwalbe is making and the fact that most long-travel e-bikes have mixed wheel sizes these days. It sounds like a smaller rear offering is in the works, so hopefully we'll see that before the deep winter soil dries up. * Editor's Note: as of publication, there is a 27.5" Rear option, rejoice. Despite being quite durable and supportive (once you've honed in on a slightly higher pressure), Schwalbe's new Radial casings are impressively pliant and comfortable, offering a similar feel to running lighter tires with inserts. On my non-electrified bike, I stuck to running 25 psi in the front and 27 psi in the rear during drier months, but have recently dropped both by about 2 psi for the very wet, very sloppy winter conditions we're currently enjoying. The additional weight and speed of an eMTB warranted a couple psi more than the pedal bike, but your mileage may vary. All testing has taken place on 30mm internal width rims, with no flats, leaks, or wobbles to report. ROLLING SPEED I mean c'mon, just look at them. These are probably some of the slowest rolling tires I've ridden, but they're not quite as heinous as I'd initially feared. The drag is in keeping with other mid-spikes and mud tires, with only a bit more of a rumble on hardpack and road surfaces. The rear tire (as a front) feels totally acceptable, and the front is just a bit behind that speed-wise. As a pair, expect to burn some extra watts, but that's the price you pay for digging deep. TRACTION Schwalbe's Ultra Soft rubber stacks up amongst the best when it comes to smearing grip, with predictable and acceptable loss in traction over wet rocks and roots. In the dry, the slow rebound and tacky grip impress just the same, especially if you have the larger contact patch that the Radial casing can offer. That said, the contact patch with the Shreddas is going to be somewhat minimized by the fact that you're dancing on spikes, as opposed to a more consistent and dense tread pattern like the Albert. That only really comes into play on hard surfaces like rocks and roots, and ultimately it just seems like the Shredda Front was designed to avoid those and aim for the patches of dirt between. The Shredda Rear does have a bit more contact patch to play with, and has felt predictable on slabs, root beds, and through rock gardens. Braking traction is as impressive as you'd expect, with the tires digging in no matter how loose the soil gets. A true mud spike might still be the better choice for sticky, slimy mud, but the Shreddas bridge that gap pretty nicely, and feel comfortable on firmer trails thanks to the soft casing. CORNERING The Shredda Front digs in like a rubber cleat, allowing for some pretty wild maneuvers anywhere there's soft dirt. With a very simple and linear pattern, there's a bit of a breakaway between the center lugs and the edge, but it never really lets you drift. There's also the matter of where you're likely to ride this tire, as it's really not for hard conditions or embedded trails. The Front can hold its own there, but feels a little vague compared to shorter-lugged alternatives. I'd reach for this when I'm riding the deepest loamers, and perhaps for sandy loose soil in some steep desert zones, but otherwise the Rear is possibly the better option. Despite being shoehorned as a back-tire specific design, the Shredda Rear has proved to be an excellent all-rounder for the front of the bike in soft conditions. The pattern corners perfectly in soft soil, and doesn't feel too vague or washy on harder-packed trails. I probably won't be running one all summer, but if I had to choose between the two this is the one I'd reach for as a front tire for any given ride. DURABILITY Most of my Shredda testing has taken place in the cold wet months of fall and winter, where rubber wear is at an all time low. That said, I have more than enough miles on the Front and Rear treads to say that they do hold up nicely, especially against undercut and knob shear, which were my initial concerns given the extreme design. If you were committed to the naming convention, and did run the Rear on the back of an eMTB, I'd expect the center knobs to diminish fairly quickly, as the Ultra Soft rubber wears away fairly quickly under braking and climbing miles. On the bright side, you could wear the tires well past the 50% mark before they started resembling other more traditional mid-spike tires on the market. On the casing front, Schwalbe's new Radial Gravity casing has been impressively durable, even through the initial trial period of seeing just how low I could run pressures. They saw plenty of rim hits during that period, but have remained unscathed. These tread patterns are definitely not designed for harder, rock-strewn trails, but my experience with the Radial casings tells me that they'd survive just fine should you choose to do so. Pros + Dominate the landscape + Extreme tread depth digs into deep soil + Excellent casing, sticky rubber Cons - Heavy, slow on hardpack trails - Front tread is squirmy on hard surfaces Pinkbike's Take The new Shredda Gravity might just be the most extreme tread pattern we've seen of late, but it's not all for show. If - and only if - you have the right conditions, these tires deliver incredible traction and predictability. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires Schwalbe Schwalbe Shredda Gravity Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Schwalbe's Magic Mary Radial Tires Deliver Top Notch Traction
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Schwalbe Magic Mary Radial Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/fnXLsjEM Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:13 It’s been nearly a year since Schwalbe unveiled their radial casing tires, a move that caused other tire manufactures to take notice. Changing the orientation of the threads in a tire's casing may seem like a relatively minor detail, but the difference in how the tire feels on the trail is significant, especially when it comes to the amount of traction. Most mountain bike tires are constructed with threads that run at a 45-degree angle from bead to bead. For the sticklers out there, Schwalbe’s tires aren’t a ‘true’ radial, where the threads are oriented at a 90-degree angle to the direction of travel, but the change in orientation is enough to have a dramatic influence on how the casing deforms when an obstacle is encountered. Magic Mary Radial Details • Sizes: 29 x 2.5" or 27.5 x 2.5" • Schwalbe Radial construction • Soft or Ultra Soft rubber compound • Gravity Pro (tested) or Trail Pro casing • Weight: 1406 grams (29") / 1295 grams (27.5") • Price: $108 USD • schwalbe.com At the moment Schwalbe is still the only company offering a mountain bike tire with this construction, but I wouldn’t be surprised if that changes sooner than later. The focus of this review is on the Magic Mary Gravity Pro casing tires - at this point I’ve ridden them in everything from snow to dust and all conditions in between, so the timing seemed appropriate to report on my findings. As the name suggests, the Gravity Pro casing is the burlier, downhill oriented option on the Magic Mary lineup - riders who want to save a couple hundred grams per tire can do so by choosing the thinner Trail Pro casing. No matter the casing, the Magic Mary tread pattern falls on the more aggressive side of the spectrum, with tall angled side knobs and a 2-2-2-3 center knob pattern. On my scale, the 29 x 2.5” Ultra Soft compound tire weighed in at 1406 grams, and the 27.5 x 2.5” Soft compound tire was 1295 grams. The tires are priced at $108 USD. Pressure Check It’s important to experiment with different air pressures when switching to radial tires - if you run your typical pressures there’s a good chance the tire will feel too soft and vague due to the difference in the way the tire deforms. I went up 2 - 3 psi higher than my usual DH tire pressures, settling on 22 up front and 24 in the back for my 165 pound weight. Those pressures are for trail riding - I typically add another couple pounds of pressure for bike park usage, where the speeds are higher and the trail surface tends to be firmer. With the thinner Trail Pro casing tires I’ve been running 3 - 4 psi more than I normally do on ‘regular’ tires. I wouldn’t recommend getting too extreme with the pressure increases - it’s worth starting with just a few pounds more than usual and seeing how it feels rather than running 40 psi and wondering why it feels like you’re riding on basketballs Conditions Report Wet Wet, slimy conditions are where the Marys excel. There’s a vast difference between how these handle wet roots compared to the non-radial version - the extra compliance makes them more predictable, and they’re much, much less likely to suddenly slide sideways. The traction that they deliver is due more to the mechanical properties of the casing rather than an extra-sticky rubber compound; while the Ultra Soft rubber isn’t as tacky as Maxxis’s MaxxGrip rubber, the way the casing molds over obstacles more than helps make up for that. I do think that there’s still room in Schwalbe’s lineup for an even softer rubber compound for riders who are willing to sacrifice some longevity for maximum grip (Super Duper Soft?), but as it is, the radial casing helps make the slightly firmer rubber a non-issue. The amount of grip available when conditions are grim is astounding. I’ve even swapped bikes mid-ride with a friend to confirm that I wasn’t imagining things, and I wasn’t - the way that the tire deforms allows it to find grip where other tires slip. The tires shed mud well, at least when it comes to the fir-needle heavy mud that we have here in the Pacific Northwest. They’re not as ridiculously grippy as the Shredda tires, but those are a very conditions-specific option - the Mary could be a year-round option, while the Shredda is best reserved for the grimmest of conditions. These tires have a muted feel that’s distinctly different from non-radial tires. The sensation is similar to the difference between a coil shock and an air shock - small bumps are erased, and there’s a glued-to-the-ground feel that’s truly confidence inspiring. To continue the suspension analogy, it’s almost like running a lighter compression tune - the difference is that dramatic. Dry / Loose The tall knobs that help dig into softer soil in the winter also deliver when things dry up and the trails get covered with 6 inches of fine dust. The bite isn’t quite as tenacious as Maxxis’ Highroller III, but it’s close. The Highroller has a more defined edge that’s easier to find when pushing hard into loose corners, but if there happen to be rocks or other obstacles hidden under the dust the Mary does a better job of muting those impacts. Hardpacked If your typical trail surface resembles a pumptrack I’d suggest going with something with a less aggressive tread pattern for better rolling speed. That said, even with those taller knobs and malleable casing the tires remained predictable - I took lots of laps on Whistler’s A-Line and Dirt Merchant trails and didn’t find the handling to be overly vague. The casing deforms more in the center of the tread, but the sidewall support remains, which keeps the handling very predictable; it never felt like the sidewall was going to collapse on me. Climbing Climbing on hardpacked trails or on asphalt does feel extra-draggy, though; that’s when the tire’s deformation is more noticeable. I could literally hear the knobs getting smooshed against the pavement on one particularly steep approach to the trails. The flip side is that climbing traction is excellent - most people aren't buying a DH tire for the uphill performance, but the Mary does a great job of digging in and holding on during steeper, technical climbs. Cornering I find the angled side knobs of the Magic Mary slightly visually jarring, but on the trail there’s good, predictable cornering traction - you’re not supposed to be looking down at your tires anyways... The Maxxis Assegai’s more rounded profile has a slightly more seamless transition when leaning the tire over, but the Mary has good grip and is free of any scary dead zones. What to pair it with? I don’t mind running the dual Magic Mary setup, but I’d like to see an updated radial version of the Big Betty or Eddy Current added to the mix - something with rectangular braking knobs would be a nice complement to the Mary up front. The Albert Radial is a good rear tire for all-round usage - it’s faster rolling, but still offers enough grip for most conditions. You could also run the Magic Mary radial up front and a non-radial tire as a rear - that's a similar tactic to running a softer front wheel and a stiffer rear. Price & Weight The Magic Mary’s 1406 gram weight is in line with what I’d expect for a DH tire, where weights are typically in the 1350 - 1500 gram range. The $108 asking price isn’t cheap, but tires across the board are pretty pricey these days - Schwalbe’s not an outlier when it comes to price. In fact, that’s less than a comparable option from Maxxis or Conti will cost. Magic Mary Gravity Pro Weight: 1406 grams Price: $108 USD Specialized Butcher Grid Gravity Weight: 1320 grams Price: $85 USD Maxxis Assegai DH Weight: 1480 grams Price: $116 USD Continental Kryptotal DH Weight: 1335 grams Price: $114 USD Durability I had zero flats during the test period, and that includes several days in the Whistler Bike Park. The wear rate has been commendable - all of the knobs are still in place, and the tires are still in good shape considering how many miles they’ve seen. I mentioned Schwalbe’s Ultra Soft rubber not being as chemically sticky as Maxxis’, but also seems to help it last longer. It’s hard to say how much influence the radial casing has on the overall wear rate, but I’ve been impressed by how evenly these are wearing. It's worth noting that the trail surface in my riding zone tends to be nicer to tires compared to somewhere that's covered with sharp, abrasive rocks; your mileage may vary. Pros + Very, very impressive level of grip + Mutes trail chatter + Good wear rate Cons - Draggy on smoother, hardpacked terrain - Pricey (but not as expensive as some competitors) Pinkbike's Take For riders looking for maximum traction, especially in wet conditions, the Magic Mary Radial should be on the short list. The grip is excellent, but the way that these tires mute the terrain is also very impressive - there's real merit to the radial casing hype. There aren’t many components out there that can truly transform a bike’s performance the way that these tires can. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires Schwalbe Schwalbe Magic Mary Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Scor 2030 - The Trail Bike You Didn't Know You Wanted
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: REVIEW Scor 2030 What would happen if you lifted the geometry figures from an enduro bike and applied them to a short travel trail bike? It's a question that's been asked (and answered) with varying degrees of success over the years, but the crew at Scor, the fun-focused offshoot of BMC, decided to find out for themselves with the new 2030. Spoiler alert: Scor's execution of the concept results in a bike that's an absolute riot out on the trail. The carbon-framed 2030 has 29” wheels, 120mm of travel paired with a 140mm fork, and a slack 64.5-degree head angle. There are five sizes in the lineup, all with chainstay lengths that are quite short. Mariano Schoefer, Scor's engineer, comes from a BMX background, and the stubby rear end was a conscious choice intended to make the bike easy to manual and flick around. Scor 2030 GX Details • Wheel size: 29" • Carbon frame • Travel: 120mm / 140mm fork • 64.5º head angle • 77.7º seat tube angle • 429mm chainstays (size M/L) • Sizes: S, M, M/L, L, XL • Weight: 29.9 lb / 13.6 kg (size M/L) • Price: $7,299 USD • scor-mtb.com There are three complete builds, with prices ranging from $5,199 to $9,299, and a frame-only option for $3,499 USD. I tested the $7,299 2030 GX model, which comes with SRAM Code RSC brakes, a 140mm RockShox Pike, Deluxe Ultimate shock, and a Bike Yoke Divine dropper post. It rolls on DT Swiss XM 1700 Spline aluminum wheels mounted up with a Maxxis Dissector / Rekon tire combo. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Tossing in an extra manual here, pumping a roller there, and maybe slashing a berm over there is a recipe for a good time, and on the Scor those maneuvers are very easy to execute. — Mike Kazimer Review: Scor 2030 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/njz2iB4C Copied Live 00:00 11:43 11:43 Frame Details The 2030 shares a similar aesthetic to the 4060, its longer travel sibling, including the low-slung top tube, but it's not a carbon copy (no pun intended). The lower link design is slightly different, and isn't as likely to get filled with mud, one of the only gripes we had about the 4060. That lower link clamps around the shock axle rather than threading into it, and the shock uses a bearing mount at that location to reduce the amount of breakaway force. There's a small storage compartment (the 'Stash Hatch') just in front of the shock that's accessed by turning a small dial. The actual compartment is on the smaller side, so you might not be able to fit a mega burrito or a rain jacket in there, but it can accommodate a small tube, mini-pump, and tire lever. The 2030 has internal cable routing that thankfully goes through ports in the side of the frame and not the headset, with internal guide tube that reduce noise and facilitates housing swaps. Along with the rubber downtube protector, Scor offers customizable frame protection stickers that are pre-cut to fit the 2030's top and downtubes. There's also rubber chainslap protection on the chainstay and seatstay, which proved to be very effective – the 2030 is a satisfyingly quiet bike right out of the box. Geometry & Sizing The 2030's 64.5-degree headtube angle is the number that stands out on the geometry chart, putting the 2030 on the slacker side of things for a bike in this travel bracket. The Canyon Spectral 125 and Chromag Darco are two bikes that come to mind that are a little slacker, with their 64-degree head angles, but the list of bikes with 65- or 65.5-degree head angles is much longer, and includes bikes like the Norco Optic, Santa Cruz Tallboy, and Commencal Tempo. As far as reach numbers go, Scor's five sizes and short seat tubes make it possible to size up or down depending on rider preference. At 5'11” I went with a size M/L, which has a 477mm reach. These days I'm typically happiest on bikes with reach numbers between 470 – 485mm; the 497mm reach and 650mm top tube of the size large would have stretched me out more than I wanted. For riders who aren't interested in going that slack, the headset cups can be rotated to steepen the head angle to 65.5-degrees. I'd highly, highly recommend giving the 2030 a try in the stock configuration first, but it's nice that the potential for a significant adjustment is there. The chainstays measure 429mm for the S, M, and M/L sizes, then increase to 432mm for the L and top out at 434 for the XL. Even with that tiny increase for the larger sizes they're still quite short for a 29” trail bike. Scor 2030 Suspension https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/SH8xUcjs Copied Live 00:00 00:09 00:09 Suspension Design The 2030 uses two co-rotating links to deliver either 120mm of travel with a 47.5mm stroke shock, or a 52.5mm stroke shock can be installed to increase the travel to 130mm. The progressive leverage curve flattens out slightly towards the end of the bike's travel, a design that can help make sure all of the travel is accessible, rather than locking precious millimeters away behind a wall of progression. Anti-squat values sit around 110% at sag, and drop off as the bike goes further into its travel. Specifications Price $7299 Travel 120mm Rear Shock RockShox Deluxe Ultimate Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate 140mm Cassette SRAM GX Crankarms SRAM GX carbon Rear Derailleur SRAM GX 12-speed Chain SRAM GX Shifter Pods SRAM GX Handlebar Scor carbon Stem Burgtec Enduro MK3, 35mm Grips Burgtec Bartender Brakes SRAM Code RSC Wheelset DT Swiss XM 1700 Spline Tires Maxxis Dissector / Rekon Seat WTB Silverado Seatpost BikeYoke Divine Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Scor 2030 Test Bike Setup My test bike showed up with a 160mm BikeYoke dropper post (retail models will have a 180mm post), so I swapped that out for a 200mm OneUp post in order to have more clearance in the steeps. I also ended up going with a 40mm stem and 35mm bars, rather than the 35mm stem and 20mm riser bars. The stock configuration worked well, but I ended up preferring the fit and feel of the bike with the slightly longer stem and higher rise bars. For the suspension, 180 psi in the RockShox Deluxe shock put me at 27% sag, and I ran 75 psi with one volume spacer in the Pike Ultimate. Condition were dry and dusty for most of the test period, ideal for the Maxxis Dissector / Rekon tire combo. That's a setup I'd happily run all summer here in the Pacific Northwest on a bike like this before switching to something with a bit more tread for the slippery fall and winter months. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Climbing The 2030 is more focused on the descents than some other bikes in this category (like the Yeti SB120 , for instance), but Scor's designers didn't forget that it needs to go uphill too. It's an energetic pedaler, free of any wallowing or mushiness while still delivering enough traction to keep that low-profile Rekon tire from spinning wildly. The perfect balance between efficiency and grip can be tricky to achieve, but the 2030 does it very well – it has the quickness you'd hope to get from a 120mm bike without any accompanying harshness. I preferred the middle compression setting (out of three) on the Deluxe shock the vast majority of the time, but the other two settings do make a noticeable difference – the full open position comes in handy when maximum traction is desired, and the firmer setting can be used to put even more pep in the Scor's step without resorting to the fully locked out position. Even with those short chainstays I felt centered and comfortable on the 2030. The steep seat angle helps prevent too much weight from ending up over the rear wheel, and the stack height isn't so high as to upset the balance, even after I'd installed higher rise bars. Descending My very first ride on the Scor took place up in Whistler, so I decided to start by feeding it some classic technical trails, with names like Korova Milk Bar, Rockwork Orange, and Wizard Burial Ground. They're full of steep rock moves and semi-awkward slower speed maneuvers, trails that reward precision over just trying to blast downhill as fast as possible. The Scor more than held its own, and any initial doubts I had about its capabilities quickly faded away as I worked my way downhill. The geometry plays a large part in how comfortable the bike felt in the steeps, but the suspension deserves a mention too – the Deluxe Ultimate shock is very well controlled, with a smooth ramp up at the bottom of the stroke. It's a trait we noticed with the Scor 4060 a couple of years ago – there's no harshness towards the end of the travel, which makes it easier to plop off a drop or push through a pointy rock garden without worrying about running out of travel. Yes, there are obviously limits – we're still talking about a 120mm trail bike here, and I'm not about to say it can go wheel-to-wheel with a full-on enduro bike. However, the fact that it can handle those more technical trails while also remaining entertaining on mellower ones is worth noting. The suspension opens up nicely at higher speeds, and it can deal with quick impacts very well. The concept of one bike being more 'fun' than another always annoys me a little, mainly because mountain biking's supposed to be fun no matter what. That said, tossing in an extra manual here, pumping a roller there, and maybe slashing a berm over there is a recipe for a good time, and on the Scor those maneuvers are very easy to execute. Speaking of recipes, there's been a lot of talk lately about the ideal chainstay length, and I've even seen equations floating around that are purported to be the key to making most perfectly balanced bike in the world. I'm not convinced that there's one magical set of geometry numbers out there, and the 2030 is a prime example. I do typically prefer bikes with longer chainstays, and yet I haven't had any instances on this bike where I thought that a longer back end would have improved things. Scor 2030 Canyon Spectral 125 How does it compare? The Canyon Spectral 125 occupies a similar space as the Scor, but it's even longer and slacker, which can make it more of a handful on slower speed trails. The 125 feels even more biased towards descending, while the Scor has more energetic trail manners, and a more refined suspension feel. The 125 ramps up very quickly, which can make if feel a little more harsh over repeated impacts compared to the 2030. The Spectral runs away with the cake when it comes to value, although it doesn't have an in-frame storage compartment or as much water bottle room as the Scor. Santa Cruz's Tallboy is a very close contemporary, both in terms of geometry and price. The wheelbase of a M/L 2030 is exactly the same as a large Tallboy, it's just the distribution of the front and rear centers that's different. The 2030 has a slacker head tube angle and shorter chainstays, which gives it a slightly more secure handling in steeper terrain. If forced to choose, I'd go with the 2030. There's just something about its overall handling and suspension feel that's very tough to beat. Which Model is the Best Value? Unfortunately, none of the models in Scor's lineup are what I'd call a great value. Take the model tested here, for example – for $7,299 I would have expected to see a wireless drivetrain or carbon wheels on the spec sheet. For the most part the build kits are well chosen, but the prices are higher than similar complete bikes cost from other companies, even excluding consumer-direct operations. If I was dead set on getting a Scor 2030 (which is a distinct possibility for anyone who manages to test ride one), I'd consider going with the $5,199 NX model with the plan of swapping out the NX drivetrain as soon as possible. There's also the frame-only route, which, considering how many deep discounts are currently available on components, could be the way to get a better build than the NX model for the exact same price. Burgtec Bartender grips, CODE RSC brakes. Maxxis Rekon rear tire. Technical Report SRAM Code RSC brakes: I'm glad that Codes are becoming a more common spec choice on trail bikes over the less-powerful G2 brakes. I would have liked to see HS2 rotors spec'd, though; the thicker rotors bump up the stopping power, especially considering the bike has 180mm rotors front and rear. Dissector / Rekon tire combo: No, these aren't the heavier enduro tires you might expect to find on a bike like this, but they're very fun and fast in dry conditions. I know there are some riders who can't look at an EXO casing tire without immediately flatting, but I didn't have any flats during the test period, even when riding some of Whistler's pointier trails. Frame hardware: It's too early in the Scor's life to consider doing a bearing swap, but I decided to disassemble the suspension linkage to get a better idea of how hard (or difficult) maintenance would be. Overall, it was a straightforward procedure, and everything came apart and went back together smoothly. The bearings all have metal dust covers, and everything was greased and spinning smoothly. The blind pivot hardware on the seat stays can't be accessed by an allen key without unbolting the shock, but there are wrench flats that can be used instead during a quick bolt check. Burgtec Bartender lock-on grips: These grips have been out for years, but they'd sort of flown under my radar until now. I ride without gloves a decent amount during the summer, and these proved to be a very comfortable option with loads of traction for those sweaty days, especially once they'd broken in. Pros + It's a better descender than most 120mm bikes out there + Very quiet, no cable rattle or excessive chainslap. + Adaptable thanks to adjustable head angle, ability to increase travel with different shock stroke. Cons - High price for complete bikes - Your enduro bike might start collecting dust. Pinkbike's Take The Scor 2030 is one of the most entertaining 120mm trail bikes that I've ridden in recent memory. It's my kind of trail bike, one that doesn't put up a fuss while climbing, and punches way above its weight when descending. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Scor Scor 2030 Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Scor 6080Z - Ready to Run Over Anything
author: Matt Beer
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, scor, scor-6080z
article_text: Scor 6080Z: Ready to Run Over Anything https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/6Ps7Bx6l Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 15:50 Scor’s newest e-bike, the 6080Z, is in a league of its own with a whopping 180 mm of rear wheel travel and chopper-like geometry. Boasting a 190 mm single crown fork, MX wheels, and Bosch’s Performance Line CX motor, it’s built to tackle the rowdiest downhill tracks without waiting around for an uplift. It’s certainly not light though. The aluminum frame, a first for Scor, and a drop-out 750 Wh battery stack up to weigh nearly 26 kg (57.3 lb). You can see why the prototype was dubbed “The Beast”. This metal monster truck made our 2024 eMTB of the Year nominee list with its ability to mask of that weight though. In the model line are just two build kits, which use a full SRAM gamut of drivetrain, brake, and suspension components. The entry price NX model goes for $5,499 USD and the GX for $6,999 USD. Compared to the other carbon bikes in Scor's catalogue, the 6080Zs are considerably cheaper, but the components list isn't without a few quirks for these downhill sluggers. Scor 6080Z Details • Aluminum frame • Motor & battery: Bosch Performance CX Line 85Nm / 750 Wh battery • Wheel size: MX • Dual-link suspension design • Travel: 180 mm rear / 190 mm front • 63-63.4º head angle • 76-76.4º seat tube angle • 435 mm chainstay (MX) • Sizes: S, M (tested), L, XL • Weight: 25.95 kg / 57.2 lb (size MD) • Price: $6,999 USD • scor-mtb.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Motor and Battery Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Range Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Technical Report Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The obstacles you can run into without regard on the 6080Z are downright laughable. — Matt Beer Frame Details The 6080Z is the first frame that the Swiss brand has built using aluminum tubing, but the lines are recognizably that of a Scor bike. In the past, all of their other bikes have have carbon frames. Without question, this adds some weight over a carbon alternative, like the 22.50 kg / 49.6 lb 4060Z we previously rode. Aimed at gravity seekers, the 6080Z is fit for bike park poundings based on its ASTM Level 5 classification and 130kg rider weight limit. The suspension pivots use a double-locking collet system that is bomber. Some patience is needed to reach a few of the bolts, though, including the flip-chip. The naming scheme for this beast, and all of Scor’s bikes for that matter, are derived from the rear suspension travel. The “80” denotes the greatest possible travel figure of 180 mm and the “60” showcases that 160 mm is also possible, but more about that in the suspension section. The letter “Z” tacked on the end signifies that this is an E-bike, in case the motor and sizeable downtube weren’t enough of a hint already. Built around a suspension design where the shock passes through a tunnel at the base of the seat tube, the total seat post insertion depth is limited to 205 mm. A 160 mm dropper is fitted to the MD and LG frames (GX build), which means you’ll likely need to raise and lower the post in the frame to achieve the optimal climbing position and descending clearance. The cable routing can be stubborn to feed through the base of the battery port when replacing either the brake hose or the dropper post housing. That choke point does not allow the cable housing slack to slide freely when adjusting the post height in the seat tube. Minimum seatpost insertion : S: 80 mm / M: 100 mm / L: 100 mm / XL: 100 mm Minimum seatpost insertion : S: 180 mm / M: 205 mm / L: 205 mm / XL: 205 mm The rest of the frame is well thought out with generous frame protection, an accessory mount under the top tube, and space inside the front triangle for a 650 mL water bottle. I added a smidge of rubber tape to stop the bottle from rattling against the top tube with this particular bottle cage. Finer details include an integrated chainguide and headset steering lock. Motor and Battery Details The 6080Z is built around the admirable Bosch Performance Line CX motor (4th gen.) and presents 85 Nm of torque and 600 W peak power to tap into. Adding to the bike’s descending prowess, the motor clutch is one of the quietest out there. Accounting for a chunk of the weight on the 6080Z is the 750 Wh battery. Removing the battery is straightforward but does require a key for the tumbler. Throughout the test, it remained in place without the slightest knock or rattle. Although the battery dock is robust and features a sturdy lock, it can take some elbow grease to fully slot it into place. Flipping the bike over and pressing hard was my preferred tactic, although that’s not necessary to charge it - there’s a plug on the non-drive side. The Bosch system uses a built-in top tube LED display that shows the battery life (5 bars total) and the assist level. New North American import rules (Reese’s Law,) meant that our bike shipped without the Mini handlebar remote. Bosch will have an updated solution to meet those requirements soon. The assist level can also be operated using the button on the top tube controller, but you won’t be able to use walk mode without the remote. Suspension Design All of Scor’s bikes use a compact suspension design consisting of two links that rotate in a clockwise direction. Those produce kinematic numbers that are very regular for an E-bike. The axle path has a touch of reward movement, but trends forward just after half of the travel is used. The leverage ratio is progressive with a small tick of regression at the very end of the travel. Scor says this gels with either the stock air shock or a coil damper, should you wish to go that route. In its stock form, this metal monster truck gets 180 mm rear travel from the 230x65mm shock. Reducing the stroke to 57.5 mm will limit the travel to 160 mm. Scor suggests matching this with a 170 mm fork, which would require a new air spring for the Zeb. As far as anti-squat goes, that starts around 135%, which is higher than most unassisted bikes (to compensate for the higher torque), but not too extreme. There’s enough support at 30% sag (around 100% AS) but that tapers off quickly from there. The graph looks odd because the anti-squat drops off to 0% after 145 mm of travel. Braking forces are met by a neutral anti-rise figure that starts at 100% and tapers off gradually. Leverage Ratio Anti-squat Axle path Anti-rise Geometry & Sizing Spread across four frame sizes, the 6080Z fits a decent range of riders from 155-200 cm, with the typical reach numbers of 439, 462, 489, and 517 mm. All sizes feature 435 mm chainstays and there are no adjustments to change that length. A key element of why the 6080Z rides lighter than expected is the geometry. The back half of the bike is on the short side and houses a 27.5” wheel. The head angle is also as slack as most downhill bikes. I’ll explain exactly how that plays into the handling as we get into the ride impressions. On the lower link, a flip-chip tweaks geometry a smidge. In the HI mode, the head tube sits at 63.4 and drops to 63 degrees in the LO position. This knocks the theoretical seat tube angle back from 76.4 to 76 degrees. That move also changes BB height by 6 mm and the reach by 4 mm. The only real outlier of the 6080Z’s geometry is the tiny head tube. At 96 mm tall, it's an odd pairing for a long travel fork and low-rise handlebar.
