Specifications Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $6999 Travel 180 Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate RCT | 230 x 65mm Fork RockShox ZEB Ultimate RC2 | 190mm Travel | 44mm Rake Cassette SRAM GX XG-1275 Eagle Crankarms Praxis Heavy Duty eCrank Chainguide Scor Bottom Bracket BOSCH Performance Line CX, 85Nm Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle 12 spd Chain SRAM NX Eagle 12 spd Shifter Pods SRAM GX Eagle 12 spd Handlebar SCOR Alloy Bar | 31.8mm Clamp | 800mm Wide | 20mm Rise | 5° Upsweep | 9° Backsweep Stem Burgtec Enduro MK3 | 31.8mm Bar Bore | 35mm Length | 0° Angle Grips Burgtec Bartender Pro Brakes SRAM Code RSC, SRAM Centerline | 6-Bolt, 220 F/200 R Hubs DT Swiss 370 | Ratchet LN Freehub | Straightpull Spoke Interface | Boost spacing Rim DT Swiss H 1900 SPLINE | 29 / 27.5" Mullet | 30mm Internal Width | Tubeless ready Tires Maxxis Assegai 29 x 2.5" 3C MaxxGrip Exo+ TR (F) | Maxxis Assegai 27.5 x 2.5" 3C MaxxTerra DD TR (R) Seat WTB Silverado Medium Cromoly SL Seatpost Bikeyoke Divine | S 125mm | M / L 160mm | XL 185mm Travel | Triggy Alpha lever Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE 6080Z Test Bike Setup At 178 cm, I’m caught in the middle between the medium and large frames. Due to the heftier weight of the bike, I chose to size down in favor of maneuverability. The 489 mm reach of the large would likely put me in an awkward, stretched-out riding position. I attempted to carry on with the stock 20 mm rise bar, but that felt too low with 30 mm of stem spacers underneath it. The next move was to keep the stem in the same position and install a 38 mm bar. This provided a more natural riding position and enough leverage to tip the bike into corners. For the suspension setup, I started with 30% sag (207 psi) as suggested, but was looking to take some weight off of the back wheel - likely the combo of the taller bar and short chainstay. Bumping up to 215 psi (27%) helped with that and also reduced bottom-outs. I typically ran the rebound 12 clicks from closed, the HSC 1 click “on” (from neutral on the Super Deluxe), and LSC 1 or 2 clicks in the same direction. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 38 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Up front, I set the 190 mm ZEB to 72 psi with one volume spacer and found that spring rate to work well from the get-go, but bumped that up to 74 psi at times. The rebound was on the fast side at 18 clicks from closed, the LSC at 0 or +1 clicks, and the HSC at +2. Routes and Range Download the Trailforks App Typical days out on the 6080Z would include multiple laps of the steepest downhill tracks in the Sea to Sky corridor, using a graded gravel road to get to the top again. When exclusively in Turbo mode, I’d expected to drain the 750 Wh battery after 12-1300 m of climbing. Toning down the assist level a few notches, it’s no problem to squeeze out 1800 m in Squamish’s Diamondhead zone. If you’re keen for more, the external battery dock lets you switch out for a fresh battery in less than 30 seconds. Climbing The Scor 6080Z is heavy, it has a lot of travel, and is built to light up downhill tracks so it must be a terrible climber, right? Surprisingly, it’s not as bad as you’d think. It may not be as an intuitive climber as other trail-oriented e-bikes, but I’ve been impressed with the lines it’s gotten up. With some foresight and muscle though, you can use that large amount of travel to your advantage. Smashing into obstacles faster and brute-forcing your way through technical sections is one way to get ‘er done. Under hard efforts on the pedals, the suspension holds the rider up well, so you can crank through tangly bits of trail without pounding your feet into the ground. Where it faults is when the grade shoots straight up. The slack angles do set you back, quite literally. The seat angle pushes the rider’s weight over the rear wheel, where the short chainstay doesn’t offer much leverage to counteract unintentional wheelies. For long road climbs or smooth climb trails, you’ll want to flick that climb switch to keep that upright riding position. The motor performance also wins back some points. It’s in those bursts of energy that the Performance CX Line stands out by delivering smooth power, even in Turbo mode. Descending Let’s not kid ourselves - lapping downhill tracks self-sufficiently is what the 6080Z is built for. This a beast of a bike that isn’t afraid of going fast, hucking huge features or mastering spicy rock slabs. When you point the 6080Z down the trail, you’re turning on autopilot to some extent. With gobs of travel at both wheels, grip and comfort are all time. The rear wheel gets out of the way for stutter bumps and the smooth progressive of the suspension takes care of hard landings. In the middle, there’s decent support, but as mentioned, aiming at the shorter side of the sag will help without undermining small bump compliance. The chopped-like geometry pushes the weight distribution off-center, in a somewhat positive way. The front wheel feels far away, giving you the confidence to plow into objects and not get bucked forward. This, combined with the weight also helps to keep your moment moving forward. All you have to do is point it in the right direction and let the suspension do the work. It’s rare to get bent out of shape on this bike. When you do need to change direction, in say tight, consecutive corners, it doesn’t act like a portly bike. While short chainstays aren’t known to be the best for high-speed stability, they work well with this behemoth and keep the rear wheel settled. It’s a similar characteristic I noticed on the Specilaized Levo SL as well. Although the bike jumps fairly intuitively, you’ll want a helping hand. Popping off the ground on benign gaps does take an extra effort due to the weight. This is where the higher spring rate in the rear shock and an extra click in on the compression circuit of the rear shock added balance. Another downside comes on flatter terrain. That should be no surprise. This is a unique bike with the intention of taking on downhill tracks. On shallow-angle trails, you will need to reevaluate the weight distribution, to a degree. Moving forward over the front wheel can eliminate any understeering and is why I spent the majority of the time in the HI setting. What's the Best Value? Aimed at park riders, the 6080Z is their first aluminum frame and that helps to keep the price lower, in comparison to their other carbon models. When it comes to choosing between the $5,499 NX or $6,999 GX build, I’d lean towards the more expensive option. With the bump up to the GX, you earn the Zeb fork and Super Deluxe in the Ultimate trim. That includes the Buttercup system in the fork and the Charger 3 damper. Most importantly, the rear shock includes a climb switch. Unless you upgrade the rear shock to include this function, you’ll want this feature for climbing anything except mellow roads. You’ll also receive more durable wheels from DT Swiss, stronger brakes, and a dropper post with a shorter insertion depth. There’s also the dropper post to consider again. The NX includes an X-Fusion Manic, which is a solid performer, except it loses 10 mm of travel and gains around 30 mm of insertion depth. This could be troublesome on the small and medium size frames. Scor 6080Z GX Devinci E-Troy GX LTD How Does It Compare? It’s been some time since I rode an e-bike with this much travel on tap. The Devinci E-Troy GX LTD has the same Bosch motor, mixed wheels and a similar head tube angle, but there's one main difference: suspension travel. The 6080Z has 20 mm more at both ends. That gives it a leg up on the downhills and soaks up big impacts with ease. On the E-Troy, the shock bottomed regularly, even with volume spacers mods, and had a tough time keeping up with what the bike itself was capable of. With that said, the E-Troy is built as an all-mountain eMTB. It’s made to handle trails of all kinds without being pigeonholed into one segment. Climbing is less problematic due to the 78.5-degree seat tube angle that lets you tackle steeper climbs. When it comes to cost, they’re nearly neck and neck and there isn’t much to separate them in the way of components. The 6080Z teams up with RockShox suspension while Devinci runs on Fox - both of the premium variety. Overlapping components include the Bosch 85 Nm motor, alloy bars and rims, and GX drivetrains. Neither one boasts about their weight though. These are hefty, yet burly e-bikes built for hard riding. It’s worth noting that Devinci makes this aluminum frame in their Canadian factory. Technical Report SRAM Code RSC brakes: As far as other components go, I try to keep review bikes as stock as possible. The 6080Z is a beast of a bike, though, and capable of handling some heinous lines. After a few holy-shit-I’m-not-stopping moments, I replaced the older Code RSC brakes to Maven Ultimates, along with thicker, grabbier HS2 rotors. Maxxis Assegai tires: These are superb tires, just not for this bike. In particular, the front tire. An EXO+ casing is far too flexible to resist folding in corners under the weight of this bike. A DoubleDown casing on the rear is adequate for some, but I’d still opt for something stronger. The 6080Z served as a test sled for revised WTB Tough Casing tires which were also in for review and did the job well. BikeYoke Divine dropper post: Another great product in the wrong place. Scor specs the 160 mm Divine on both the size medium and large 6080Z. If I had issues with the medium frame, then the rider on the large would likely be forced to find a longer post. Thankfully, a post like the 180 mm OneUp V3 Dropper offers more travel while fitting in that short seat tube. Installing a quick-release seat clamp is another, cheaper alternative. Pros + Changes direction quickly + Highly capable descender + Quiet chain, battery, and motor components Cons - Short dropper post - The brakes lack the power to slow down on the trails you want to ride this bike on - Weight - it takes intent to get this bike off the ground Pinkbike's Take Scor’s 6080Z descends like no other e-bike thanks to its attacking angles and beastly suspension numbers. In doing so, its optimal operating window narrows to committing types of trails. If that’s your style of riding and don’t want to rely on uplifts, the 6080Z offers decent value. You do pay that back with a hefty weight penalty and potentially a few component quirks. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Scor Scor 6080z Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Scott Gambler RC Review: A Race-Ready DH Bike That's Easy to Live With
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Scott Gambler review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/LxbUOQgI Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 13:35 If you’re familiar with Scott Bikes, you’d recognize the Gambler as their downhill bike even if it didn’t sport the brand’s badge. The “hidden” shock isn’t a gimmick, and the number of pivots isn’t a game of who has the most marbles; it’s the result of a carefully calculated suspension design. What the Gambler does boast is 210mm of travel and an assortment of adjustments, including shock rate, reach, chainstay, BB height/head tube angle, and wheel size. It’s been put through countless test laps thanks to team riders, engineers, and local heroes. That includes both racers and freeriders alike. Two models in the line are divided by a large price gap. The Gambler RC costs $9,299 USD, whereas the Gambler 10 is a softer blow on your wallet at $5,899. Savings on the Gambler 10 are attributed to an aluminum rear triangle and less performance-oriented suspension, however, after a few upgrades, it could be the more appealing option. Gambler RC Details • Carbon frame (alloy rear triangle on Gambler 10) • Wheel size: MX or dual 29" • 6-bar suspension design • 2-position progression adjustment • Travel: 210 mm (203 mm fork) • 62.5-63.5 degree head tube angle • 445-460 mm chainstay (high BB) • Sizes: M, L (tested), XL • Weight: 16.84 kg (37.13 lb) • Price: $5,899-9,299 USD / $7,999-12,499 CAD • More info: scott-sports.com The bigger questions to answer are, what’s all the fuss about with a ton of pivot points? What adjustments are right for you? And, how does all of that hardware hold up in the long run? In its element: Châtel Bike Park. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Descending Value How Does it Compare? Technical Report Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take So complex, yet so clean. The Gambler is adjustable at every corner, truly holding a setup that will appease any gravity rider. — Matt Beer You can turn both rebound dials at the back end of the Fox DHX2 by hand, thanks to the clicky cover. The sag-o-meter means you don't need a second set of hands to check your spring rate. UDH compatibility for the bolt-on drive-side dropout. Two pieces are included, altering the chainstay length by 15mm. A flip-chip and reversible brake mount are found on the non-drive side. A 29" wheel will only work in the long/low setting. Frame Details Scott doesn’t mess about with carbon construction - they’ve been playing that game for decades, so throw your preconceived notions about noise and stiffness out the window. The huge walls of the frame serve a purpose besides holding a unique aesthetic, too; they protect the shock and small eyelet bearings from debris and contaminants. Removing the lower shock cover is done by hand - no tools needed. In fact, a multi-tool is strapped to the inside of the cover. All of the pivots and the rear axle use T30 torx-head bolts. The torque values are etched on them, so there’s no excuse for stripped bolts. The unique two-piece lower link and press-fit bottom bracket shell combo threads together, carrying with it an integrated bashguard. When implementing a chainstay length adjustment, Scott engineers had to tackle the hurdle of the universal derailleur hanger to mount SRAM’s XX DH Transmission derailleur. The best approach was to offer a bolt-on 157mm dropout, one for each chainstay length. On the other side, a flip-chip is all that’s needed to move the axle position, and a reversible brake mount accounts for proper rotor alignment. The engineers learned that the laterally mounted brake adaptor on the previous Gambler model could flex under high frame forces. That caused braking consistency issues, so a top-loading mount was favored. In relation to brakes, riders who run their brakes moto-style (front brake on the right) aren’t forgotten about. Dual internal routes run the entirety of the frame for a quiet and sleek brake line. Suspension Design What’s the point of a 6-bar suspension design, anyway? By adding in those extra links and pivots, each aspect of the suspension can be tuned with further independence. In the Gambler’s case, the design allows for a high anti-squat for a downhill bike without needing to rely as heavily on chain dampers. The Scott DH Factory team often uses custom linkage, as each rider has their own preferences. The anti-rise is on the lower side, hovering around 60% throughout the travel. As a result, the rear wheel remains more active under braking. In turn, this can “lift” the rear wheel of the bike, making the geometry steeper. It’s a compromise that I’m not usually fond of, however, it was less apparent on the Gambler than on other bikes I’ve ridden with similar anti-rise values. At the forward shock mount lies a square flip-chip insert with four positions. Placing the bolt in the chip at the high or low end changes the progression between 25 and 30% overall. Each leverage curve is a smooth arc and gives a predictable ride. Team riders have even more linkage options. 0 seconds of 40 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/8WXVWn5w Copied Live 00:00 00:40 00:40 Less progressive. More progressive. Anti-squat. Anit-rise. Geometry & Sizing Woof! Where to begin explaining this puzzle? Let’s go with the easy pickings first. The Gambler comes in three front triangle sizes: M, L, XL. The size large I rode sat around 460-470mm. Even with all of the adjustments possible, it’s fair to say the Gambler is on the cusp on measuring shorter than other brands' typical reach numbers, but only by a few millimeters. To counteract some of that loss, Scott includes two headsets with the Gambler package: a set of centered, zero-offset cups and 6mm offset option to increase or decrease the reach. It’s tough to pinpoint the exact reach as that can hinge on other settings, because the reach can change by as much as 24mm in total on one frame size. Out back, two axle positions give a 15mm change, but four chainstay lengths are possible with the 27.5” rear wheel. In the high BB setting, the chainstays are shorter, 445 or 460mm. Moving to the low BB setting lengthens the chainstay by 5mm (450/465mm), but cuts the reach by 8mm. The 29” wheel only works in the long dropout setting and should be placed in the low BB position. That results in a head tube angle of 63.5 degrees, but retains the same 14 millimeters of BB drop as the low MX setting. I wouldn’t fret over getting the numbers exactly to your liking. Instead, I'd focus on how your style relates to the general setup. For example, freeriders will make the most of a 27.5” rear wheel, short chainstay length, high BB setting, and 25% progression setting. You could also experiment with the higher progression option, but keep in mind that you'll have to change spring rates and that the dynamic sag may vary. Two headsets let you dial in the reach with either +6, 0, and -6 mm moves. The four-way adjustable forward shock mount gives two progression settings and two head angle/BB options. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $9299 Travel 210 Rear Shock FOX DH X2 Factory Independent high, low comp., rebound, RVS custom tune, 250x75mm Fork FOX 40 Factory Grip X2 Air, Kashima HSC - LSC - HSR- LSR adjustable, 20x110mm 52mm offset, 200mm travel Headset Syncros-Acros Gambler Semi integrated, DH Reach adj +-5mm with cup, ZS56/28.6-ZS56/40 Cassette SRAM CS PG-720 DH 11-25 Crankarms Raceface Atlas DM36T Alloy Chainring DM, 165mm Chainguide MRP TR2 Upper Guide, SCOTT Gambler integrated Bashguard Bottom Bracket Raceface PF BB107 Double row Rear Derailleur SRAM X01 DH Short cage, 7 Speed Chain KMC X11 Shifter Pods SRAM GX Eagle Trigger, 7 Speed Handlebar Syncros Hixon 1.5 DH 35mm, 20mm Rise, 8°, 800mm Stem Syncros DH1.5 35mm, Direct Mount 50mm Grips Syncros AM lock-on grips Brakes SRAM Maven Silver 4 Piston Disc, metallic pads, 200mm rotors (F&R) Wheelset Race Face Atlas, 110x20 Boost front, 157x12 rear, 29"front, 27.5"rear, 28H, Tires Maxxis Assegai 29x2.5", DH casing, 3C Maxx Grip (front), Maxxis DHR II 27.5x2.5", DH casing, 3C Maxx Terra (rear) Seat Syncros Kaslo 1.5 Titanium Rails Seatpost Syncros DH 1.5, 31.6mm, Alloy 7050 Compare to other DH Bikes Appropriate brakes for Champéry descents. The Fox DHX2 coil gets the call for the Gambler RC build, but virtually all coil and air shocks were tested during the prototyping stage. Test Bike Setup As you can imagine by now, the amount of adjustability can be overwhelming. Scott leaves those choices up to the rider, but some guidance would be helpful. One crafty feature Scott carried over from other models is an integrated sag indicator. Measuring the sag would otherwise require precision eyeballs to work around the shock tunnel. Scott test riders rode every rear shock under the sun to make sure they all jived with the Gambler. The stock shock of choice is a Fox DHX2 Factory - a great damper, but a small inconvenience if the supplied spring doesn’t work for you. The progression and BB/head tune angle adjustments are independent from one another, however, changing leverage rates will likely require another spring change. Changing the chainstay length will also affect the leverage rate as you're lengthening the lever acting on the shock. I bounced between a 450 lb spring in the less progressive setting/short chainstay and a 475 when flipping the chips in the other direction. That usually spun the sag indicator around the common 30% mark in the progressive setting. The Fox 40 was set to 86 PSI with 5 volume spacers, trending towards more open rebound and compression damping than recommended. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 39 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Right off the bar, in Châtel, France, I swapped for a 30mm rise bar. By the end of the first day, I was super comfortable hanging it out on the bike through jump trails and in the tech. A few more changes were made as time went on. Back on home soil, in the Whistler Bike Park, I increased the reach to +6mm. The only option I didn’t experiment with was a 29” rear wheel, but you might see a bigger wheel make an appearance in another article. Download the Trailforks App The Gambler received a healthy dose of jumps, tech, and high-speed tracks. Conditions also varied from the mud of the Alps to late summer dust in Whistler. No matter what type of tracks I eyed up, the Gambler didn’t shy away from the task. The clay mud gets dragged onto the graphite-filled rock in Châtel. It's like goose shit on glass, but the Gambler held its course well. Descending Complex high-pivot contraptions may be mesmerizing in rough sections, but they can falter in other areas, or at least take some adaptation in riding technique. On the Gambler, it’s smooth sailing everywhere. That goes for any of the possible positions, too. 25 and 30% of overall progression are sweet numbers to work with. Neither figure is off in left field, nor does it make it difficult to set up the shock for. The change between the two is useful for matching a range of track types or riding styles. The less progressive option gives more support earlier on in the travel - ideal for flatter tracks or jump trails. For max traction in steep, slippery trails, which there were plenty of in the Alps, the 30% mode sits further into the travel at sag and dances over webs of roots, needing only the softest touch to initiate suspension movement. The suspension ramps harder and later, so you can understand the benefits of having two progression positions to choose between. Sliding down the Champery World Cup track takes the highest confidence. In the longest, lowest, slackest mode, the Gambler delivered exactly that. The bike settles in as you’d expect a race-type bike to do. I thought the 465mm chainstay might take more effort to throw around in the steeps, but that wasn’t the case at all. Funny enough, the low BB mode makes the chainstay longer than the reach. Even with this seldom-seen front-to-rear-center balance, it’s quite maneuverable. It’s possible that taller riders may wish for a longer and higher front end on the XL. Looking at the massive carbon surfaces, you might think that it’s extra-stiff or would vibrate you off the trail. I was pleasantly wrong about that assumption. Whatever methods the engineers applied here make for a strong, yet forgiving ride. So it’s not brutally stiff, but what about the noises? Surprisingly, the downtube doesn’t turn into a massive echo chamber either. Through the roughest sections of track, the chain can hit some part of the frame or lower skid plate. If you look at a few team members’ bikes, an STFU silencer attends to that. Rarely do they opt for a chain damper on the cranks, though. Every aspect of the Gambler is damn good. Collectively, the predictability, composure, and sensibility of the suspension sold me. It’s the type of ride that will always save you when you get into trouble, rather than bite back. Tracking well over the mud-caked roots. What's It Like to Live With? Scott made wrenching on the Gambler as straightforward as it could be. Compared to other bikes made up of one hundred pieces, bolt checks won't have to be made every lap, and shock adjustments can be completed with one set of hands. The concise manual explains cable routing management, settings, and a full list of spare parts, should you need one. After surviving major cleanups, the bearings are still rolling smoothly. The frame doesn’t retain water in the shock tunnel or crunch rocks in the linkage. Despite being blasted with copious amounts of mud, the covers stayed in place and didn’t rattle off after repeated use. The hardware is robust and the pivots have never developed any creaking. What's the Best Value? The full-carbon Gambler RC has all of the quality components you’d expect to find on a premium downhill bike. They’re identical, if not equivalent, to what you’d find on a high-end Santa Cruz V10 build. In U.S. dollars, the Gambler RC costs $300 more than a V10 X01. Neither top-end model is exactly a killer deal, but they feature proven components. The Gambler 10, on the other hand, is more enticing. It only costs $5,800 USD. Settling for an aluminum rear triangle, but keeping the carbon front half, is a fair trade-off. That leaves you with $3,400 set aside for spare parts, travelling, or upgrades. Speaking of, there are a few components I’d replace from the get-go. First on my list would be a Charger 3.1 fork damper, and possibly more honed DH brakes. I don’t have much experience with the Alex rims listed, but I’d run them until they were egg-shaped, then replace them with a reputable alloy option. I know what you’re thinking: why not build up a frame? Unfortunately, frame kits are only available for European markets. Scott Gambler RC: $9,299 USD, 16.84 kg (37.13 lb) size LG. Orbea Rallon DH: $8,299 USD, 16.36 kg (36.07 lb) size LG, no weights. How Does It Compare? Another hot bike with a matrix of setup choices is the Orbea Rallon DH. I mainly rode the Rallon DH in the lowest, longest, slackest setting to gain perspective from a downhill racer’s point of view. Both bikes exhibit a low center of gravity, yet they are decently light for a bike being put through the paces on the World Cup circuit. In race mode, the Gambler sets itself apart by sitting slacker with a longer chainstay length. I found the Rallon to be more disconnected in terms of balance. The stack height is generally taller, yet the front axle still feels underneath you - great for loading up into berms, but it didn't encourage me to attack down the steeps as confidently. Sifting further into the materials chat, the Gambler has a more even blend of flex front-to-rear. It’s neither uncomfortable nor noodley. On the Rallon, the front end is stiffer and produces some unwanted vibrations at high speeds or across braking bumps. Behind you, the rear triangle is softer. That can be helpful to maintain traction, however, some riders may find it to be on the more flexible side. Suspension-wise, the Gambler as a whole is much more composed, controlled, and quieter. There’s less energy loss when trying to pump and carry speed. The final point to take away is that the Gambler doesn’t confine your geometry choices. When purchasing a Rallon, you’ll have to decide between 442 and 450mm (which aren’t particularly long to begin with) and live with it. Similarly, the head tube only allows for an angle change (again, which isn’t that slack for a race bike), not a change in reach. You can also run a 29” rear wheel, which the Rallon DH cannot fit, and the interchangeable dropout lets you decide when and where you want to change the chainstay length. Technical Report Maxxis Minion DHR II, 3C Maxx Terra, DH casing: Say what? Yes, the high-end Gambler RC is not specified with the grippiest rubber available from Maxxis. I still hear complaints about tire prices from folks riding around on five-figure bikes, so this choice should save your tire from erasing knobs after a big day in the bike park. Unless you're attempting to tackle the gnarliest, steepest rock faces, it performs relatively well, even in the wet. Fox DHX2: The new mono tube layout of the DHX2 is a smooth operator when it comes to changing stroke direction. With the new design, both rebound knobs are located at the rear end of the shock and can be rotated without needing to break out any tools. I've yet to experience any top-out clunk on this generation either. Maven Silver Brakes: These are strong, consistent stoppers. On the most vertical tracks, there was no fading from the metallic pads and 200mm rotors. Being able to set up the lever throw and bite point equally on the fly is another big win. Pros + Very predictable suspension characteristics. + Low, stable center of gravity. + All of the adjustments you could ever want. + Tidy packaging given all of the technology packaged into the frame. Cons - Taller riders might find the XL too low and short. - A raw carbon finish would be more attractive, but I'm sure the marketing team would disagree (I'm grasping at straws here). Pinkbike's Take If it's not easy to tell, every time I parked the Gambler for the day, I left with a smile on my face. Part of that came from being a retired privateer racer; flashbacks to daily linkage rebuilds and constant bolt checks on complex frames had me worried at first. Those thoughts were put to rest quickly - the Gambler was put away wet and continued late in the season with minimal attention. It goes to show that the crew at Scott did their homework, putting the Gambler through equally polite abuse before signing off on the project. What's more is that there isn't anything weird happening at the linkage. It's not as mindblowing as some high-pivot bikes can be in specific instances, but it's composed, comfortable, and predictable. Those are the critical elements of a proper downhill bike. Job well done, I'd say. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews DH Bikes Scott Scott Gambler Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: SDG Tellis V2 Dropper Post - No Slop, More Drop
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Dropper posts could be argued as one of the most revolutionary changes made to mountain bikes. Nowadays, they're longer, more reliable, and even light enough to become standard equipment on cross-country bikes. SDG's new Tellis V2 touts an increased size range, with up to 230mm of drop available, plus a new style of clamping saddle rails. Arguably, the standout feature of the post is the patent-pending head, which lowers the stack height by 10mm and makes saddle installation a breeze. SDG Tellis V2 Details • Hybrid PTFE & copper keyways • Lower stack height w/patent pending head • 30.9, 31.6, 34.9mm diameters • 75-230mm travel (reduceable in 5, 10, 20mm increments) • Stanchion diameter: 26mm • Weight: 609g - 31.6x170mm • Price:$199.99-224.99 USD • More info: sdgcomponents.com SDG’s Tellis V1 Post has treated me well on various test bikes, but the V2’s performance and workmanship is said to have improved, thanks to numerous small but critical details. SDG say that the $199 USD Tellis V2 is smoother and more rigid thanks to a larger stanchion diameter and redesigned keyways, so we put some solid time on the updated version. The angled clamping bolts make seat installation a breeze. Construction and Features The new post head design features an independent saddle rail clamp with hardware that is easily accessible from the side. These clamps also allow for an infinite saddle angle adjustment (within a useful range) and are compatible with 7mm round saddle rails or 7 x 9mm oval rails. In addition to the 10mm of stack taken off the top of the clamp head, 5mm has been shaved off the bottom of the post, thanks to a redesigned actuator. Conveniently, the cable head still connects to the actuator for speedy installs. Three diameters of 30.9, 31.6, 34.9mm are available in 75, 100, 125, and 150mm travel configurations as well as the 170, 200, and 230mm options which have 30mm size gaps instead of 25mm. The longest travel 230 post is only available in 31.6 and 34.9 diameters and 26mm stachion diameter are used throughout. Each set post height is adjustable in travel by 5, 10, or 20mm due to the simple split bushing design, excluding the shortest post, which is limited to a 55mm total drop. The key to that quick travel adjustment is the updated split bushing design from IGUS. There’s also a Trelleborg main seal, which caps off the quadruple sealing system. The V2 continues to use the Wintek cartridge and is compatible with the current Tellis dropper lever, where SRAM Matchmaker, Shimano, I-Spec EV, and 22.2 bar mounts are available The price seems less, but it no longer includes the lever, cable or housing. Price and Weight Between the various diameters and travel lengths, the price of the Tellis V2 dropper ranges from $199.99 to $224.99USD, which doesn’t include the lever or cable. On my scale, the SDG Tellis V2 post (31.6x170mm) weighs 609g with the rail clamping hardware, but excluding the cable or lever. Installation Like any cable activated dropper post, finding the correct length of housing to use is always a bit of a guessing game, but once that is sorted, snugging up, any cable tension is straightforward. By connecting the cable barb at the actuator, you are free to secure the housing to the post, insert that into the frame and maintain tension on the cable while clamping it in place on the lever. This is much easier than guesstimating and pinching the snipped end of a cable to the tiny hardware at the bottom of the seat post that's found on some other brands. I’ve also found that levers that clamp the cable have less of a tendency to fray the end as well, meaning you can reuse the cable without any issues. It's worth mentioning again just how easy it is to install a seat on the Tellis thanks to the orientation of the bolts - there's much more room for a tool compared to the traditional mounting style, and it's one of those designs that makes you wonder why everyone doesn't do it this way. Performance Is there a noticeable difference between the Tellis V1 and V2 on the trail? I had the chance to ride them both back to back, as the V1 arrived on a test bike. Without a doubt the V2 is smoother and, most importantly, quieter when descending. One of the first things I noticed was the reduced noise or chatter on the V2, which is due to the copper/PTFE keyways. Before, rattles from the post head could resonate through the bike and fault an otherwise calm bike. Secondly, there’s smoother actuation as the stanchion glides very seamlessly. Even after riding through sloppy winter conditions for a few months, the Tellis V2 still feels silky smooth. Should something go sideways, SDG does have a gamut of replacement parts available through their dealer networks. I’ll admit, I did expect the new head clamp design to possibly slip under heavy loading. After clamping the bolts down to the recommended torque and a few seat bounces, there were no signs of slippage. They also avoided emitting any creeks or squeaks, despite the splashy conditions. SDG Tellis V2 OneUp V3 Dropper How Does It Compare? Drawing comparisons between another previously released post, the OneUp V3 Dropper, the critical areas that most riders look at when choosing a dropper post is the insertion depth. Measuring from the bottom of the collar to the end of the actuator (the portion that sits inside the frame) the OneUp V3 is 22.5mm shorter, and has a 6mm lower stack height, when comparing them both set to 170mm (OneUp 180 shimmed down 10cm). OneUp's V3 Dropper still has the longest dropper post on the market at a whopping 240mm, 10mm more than the Tellis V2. Weight wise, the Tellis V2 is in the respectable ballpark, but the OneUp V3 does chop off about 130g. At $55 less, though ($214.99 USD - SDG Tellis V2 vs. $269 - OneUp V3), weight may not be a factor for those looking to save a few bucks. On the warranty side, SDG offers a lifetime guarantee, whereas OneUp’s V3 post comes with a two-year warranty. Both posts use an actuator that holds the barbed end of the cable and feature ergonomic levers. OneUp’s option features a rubber thumb pad with colorized options. The SDG has a lighter action but the longer lever means pushing further to open the actuator. Each dropper features a black stanchion and body with discrete logos. Service and Durability I’ve been ragging on the Tellis V2 for more than six months, where it spent the most time aboard the Devinci E-Troy LTD smashing through winter slop. I’d also wager that seat posts on E-bikes are put through higher loads too since you can pedal across trail undulations faster, therefore leveraging the saddle more. When I pulled the post apart for an inspection, everything looked fairly clean, but one of the additional seals that’s part of their quad seal system seemed to have some material removed from it. SDG admitted that this particular post may have been rushed out to provide us enough time to put adequate miles on it and recognizes that the additional seal may have been damaged when removing the collar. They're confident that mass production won't see this issue as the same seal system has been working flawlessly for four years in their V1 post. Despite that minor quibble, no streaks were left on the post or excess grease was pumped out of there during operation and otherwise, the Tellis V2 has remained quiet, smooth and consistent over the test period. Changing the travel requires unthreading the lock ring collar by hand, sliding the bushing up and clipping the travel limiting spacer onto the post, much like other posts with adjustability. SDG recommends arranging that so that the split points anywhere except at the rear wheel (6-o'clock orientation). That procedure is straightforward to tackle, even for the greenest mechanics. No special tools are required to refresh the internals or adjust the travel either. Pros + Side-loading clamps allow for very easy saddle install and removal + Smooth operation with virtually no side-to-side play + Excellent performance to price ratio Cons - There are still other posts that offer more insertion depth with less stack height - Not exactly heavy, but there are lighter options out there Pinkbike's Take It's tough to nail all aspects of what consumers demand in a dropper seat post: lots of travel with increased insertion interruption, smooth operation, rattle-free keyways, and it has to be cheap and lightweight too. SDG has increased the performance of the Tellis V2 dropper considerably with the combination of slick materials used in conjunction with the improved keyways. They've eliminated the annoying rattle found in some dropper posts while keeping the movement extremely smooth. With an attractive price and the convenience of the easy saddle loading, the minor weight gain over the V1 can be shrugged off. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews SDG Tellis V2 Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: SeaSucker Talon Bike Rack - Does It Suck?
author: Seb Stott
article_text: The idea of a bike rack that sticks your bike to your car like something you'd use to hold your soap in the shower may sound like the best and worst idea the bike industry has cooked up. Seasucker's design makes use of rubber suction cups that hold the rack to the car surprisingly securely by creating a strong vacuum underneath. It's a concept that's been copied many times, but here we have Seasucker's longest-standing, most affordable, and most popular offering, the Talon, which carries just one bike. The Talon's fork mount attaches to the car with three suckers, and the bike clamps onto this with its front thru-axle, via adaptors called Huske plugs which are specific to each axle standard. Another suction cup with a Velcro strap holds the rear wheel. Seasucker Talon Details • Single-bike carrier but can be bought as a pair • Fits most vehicles • Adapters available to fit all major axle standards • Maximum bike weight 20kg/ 44 lb (with the front wheel removed) • Doesn't hold MTB front wheels • Price: $299 USD/£314 (+ $20/£26 per set of Huske plugs) • seasucker.com Before we get to how I got on with the Talon over the last year of testing, let's go over the (many) pros and cons of Seasucker's suction-based approach. The yellow Huske plugs push into the front wheel carrier and fit 15x110 mm axles; other plugs are available for most axle standards. Pros: To start with, it's universal. It fits on pretty much any vehicle provided it's not wrapped. You don't need specific cross bars or a tow hitch or even a boot/trunk lid that can accept a strap-on bike rack. If you change cars, the rack will still work. This also simplifies the buying process as there's no need to check compatibility with your vehicle(s) and no need to adjust it to fit. Secondly, it's very compact. You can leave it in the back of your car just in case you need it, or even take it on holiday in your luggage to use with a rental car. To remove the sucker, just pull on the tab at the edge to break the seal. Thirdly, it's very quick to take on and off. This is ideal for those who only occasionally need to carry a bike - once you're finished, just pop the rubber suckers off the car by hand, and put it somewhere clean until next time. Cons: Firstly, while installing the rack onto your car is straightforward, securing the bike to the rack is fiddly at best. The usual way is to stick the front wheel carrier to the car first, then lift the bike on top and slot the dropouts onto the tubular fork mount base. Then, with your bike balanced in one hand, install the axle through the fork and fork mount to clamp it in place. This isn't easy at the best of times, but if the bike is dirty, it's windy, or if the thru-axle isn't quick-release, it's awkward. There's a risk of dropping the bike onto your car. If you're using two Talons in parallel, one of the axles will need to thread in from the inboard side of the car, which is even more fiddly. If you want to stop the (dirty) rear wheel from touching your car, you also need to position the rear wheel carrier the right distance from the front one and rest the rear wheel on this while you install the fork in the fork mount base. Another approach is to start with the bike upside down, install the front wheel carrier onto the fork, then lift the bike and carrier onto the car right-side-up and secure the suction cups afterwards. This is fiddly too, but I think like there's less chance of the bike falling over than the first method, especially if you have a non-QR axle. Spring-loaded pumps create a vacuum in the suction cups. The orange band shows the vacuum pressure isn't sufficient or has escaped. This happened to me 10 minutes into a drive on one occasion. Secondly, while it is secure most of the time, there is a risk of the vacuum cups leaking causing the suckers to stop working. To avoid this, it's vital to ensure the suckers and car are clean and check the orange indicators regularly while driving. Thirdly, there is nothing to stop someone from stealing your bike. The same is true for most strap-on style racks, but most roof- or hitch-mounted racks have a built-in locking mechanism. Seasucker does have a solution to this, involving a cable lock and a steel anchor that slots inside the seam of the trunk, which can be locked with the car if you need to run to the bathroom on a road trip. I'm not sure I'd want to drive at high speed with a cable lock around my frame, so you may want to install the lock when you stop and then remove it when you resume the journey. Either way, it's a doddle to remove the Seacucker itself (this is one of its advantages) so I put the rack inside the car while out riding in case someone makes off with it. Fourthly, it doesn't hold the front wheel, so I tend to put that into the car, which isn't ideal especially if it's muddy or the car is full. Seasucker makes a Flight deck , to hold the front wheel next to the rear, but this isn't available for 110 mm MTB front hubs. Another option is to strap the front wheel to the frame, but that's hardly an elegant solution. Finally, it's expensive. In addition to the $299 / £314 for the basic rack, it's $20 / £26 for the Huske plugs , which are specific to each axle standard. So if you have a modern trail bike (15x110 mm), a gravel bike (12x100 mm) and an older trail bike (15x100 mm) like I do, you'll need three sets of Huske plugs. That's just to carry one bike at a time. If you want to carry two, you can either get a Talon two-pack ($519 / £522) or a Mini Bomber ($499 / £462) + Huske plugs. Swapping the plugs over isn't hard, but if you have different bikes there are loose parts to keep hold of.
