Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Advertisement SeaSucker wobble test by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 3,486 Faves: 0 Comments: 0 There is some wobble on a rough gravel road but not so much as other roof bar-mounted bike carriers. Performance I've been using the Talon occasionally for a year on a Tesla Model 3 and before that on a Honda Jazz/Fit. As mentioned, the rack works with almost any vehicle, but the taller the car, the more awkward it's going to be to fit. Don't be worried about the strength of the suction cups. They are claimed to withstand a pull force of 100 kg / 210 lb each and the bike feels secure when pushed from side to side. When driving, it wobbles from side to side a little if it's very bumpy but less than other roof racks I've used (especially the Thule Upride). It's reassuring that the Talon is rated to carry bikes up to 20 kg after removing the front wheel, which means it should work with some mid-weight e-bikes (if you can lift one onto the car!) The problem comes when air leaks under the rubber suction cup and the vacuum seal breaks. To avoid this, it's important to keep the cups scrupulously clean and wash the car roof before use. Even doing this as best I can, I once had the vacuum let go on one of the cups a few minutes into a journey. An orange indicator shows when this has occurred, and luckily my car has a glass roof so I could see the indicator while driving. But you need to be careful when installing it and check the orange indicator regularly on long journeys. It's a good idea to install the system in advance of setting off, so you can check the seal is holding long-term before hitting the road. Even then, I'd pull over and check the orange indicators at the first opportunity for peace of mind. On the other hand, there have been several occasions where I've left the Seasucker on the car for days without losing vacuum pressure, but I can't deny being a little nervous for the first few minutes of every journey. Pros + Universal - fits to almost any vehicle + Compact - easy to store or transport + Quick to take on and off a car + Surprisingly sturdy so long as he vacuum holds Cons - Awkward to install a bike onto the carrier - Doesn't easily carry MTB front wheels - Limited theft protection options - Far from foolproof - it's important to keep the suckers and car clean before installing and regularly check the vacuum seal remains unbroken - Expensive even as a one-bike system Pinkbike's Take The Seasucker Talon isn't the best solution for someone who's regularly carrying bikes around. If possible, I'd recommend getting a dedicated roof rack and carriers or a tow-hitch rack. Even a boot/trunk rack that straps onto the lid is a better option in many ways - it's easier to get the bikes on and off, the front wheel doesn't have to go in the car, they can carry different bikes without modification, and they're usually much cheaper. But so long as you're diligent with keeping the rubber suckers clean and checking the vacuum indicators, the Seasucker is secure and sturdy. It's quick to take on and off the car or swap between vehicles, and it's small enough to keep in the car, making it handy for occasional use. If it's the only way of getting bikes to the trails, it'll get the job done. — Seb Stott Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Bikeracks Seasucker Seasucker Talon Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: 10 Months With Shimano's GE9 Gravity Shoes
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shimano’s footwear team has produced some cult classics over the years, with the Stormtrooper-esque ME7 standing atop that heap. Those shoes were discontinued last year, replaced by this new model: the GE9. Aimed at the broad “gravity” market, they’re meant to hit for anything from trail riding to DH racing, with a relatively flat sole and features throughout. They carry over the stretchy gaiter from the ME7, implement a Boa ratchet, and have a simple Velcro closure at the cuff. With sizes from 38 to 48, two muted colorways, and a neutral/wide fit, the GE9s are meant to cover a wide range of rider preferences and needs. Shimano GE9 Details • Ultread sole • Carbon-reinforced midsole • BOA dial with plastic shield • Mesh ankle collar • Weight: 460 grams (size 45, per shoe) • Colors: black, olive • Sizes: 38-48 • $225 USD • www.shimano.com Shimano's Ultread sole. Works well with my slammed cleat position. Performance Sorry to anyone who was a megafan of the ME7s, but these are not going to be the direct replacement you're looking for. The GE9 fits and feels much more like a skate shoe, with a wider toe box and much more compliant sole. As a result, things are very comfortable right out of the gate, especially for someone like me with a wider forefoot. The updated Ultread sole is lower profile than the outgoing design, and works well with pedals that feature large platforms and pins. There isn't much sole contact unless you wind those pins out a ways, unlike some other shoes that have a deeper cleat channel to meet the pedal directly. I really like the BOA adjustment on these shoes, and had zero issues with durability, wear, or stickiness of the mechanism. There's probably something to the fact that Shimano integrated a little guard into the molding, protecting the dial from rock strikes and other impacts. The upper hook-and-loop strap never lost tack, and is a good way to fine-tune the security of the shoe. I was running it very loose for a bit last summer and actually popped out of my shoe in a crash - the shoe stayed connected to the bike. I'm conflicted about the stiffness of the GE9s. Their soft feel is part of what makes them so comfortable, but with a bit more rigidity to the sole the pedaling and cornering would be improved. Neither is horrible as is, but there's some compromise made to gain the walkability and all-day comfort. One thing to keep in mind is the width of the shoe, as I've seen some pedal/crank combos that seem too cramped to fit things perfectly. Most notably, Time pedals seem to bring the shoe in too close to the cranks, leading to some extra rubbing. I had no issues with this running either Crank Bros or SPDs. All-in, they're a great everything shoe, especially if you have wider feet or find yourself hiking a fair bit to get around in the woods. I'm happy to trade off some sportiness for a pair of shoes that feels like home, but I'm still on the hunt for my next Goldilocks pair. The one durability weak point. The upper strap's attachment loop eventually blew out due to crank rub. I simply cut a slit with a box knife and things have remained peachy. Durability I've been riding the same pair of GE9s every day for about 10 months now, and the durability is shaping up to be pretty good, with one exception. As you can see, there's plenty of wear on the sole, and some scarring on the uppers, but functionally everything has remained solid. I run my cleats far outboard to get a narrower pedaling stance, which in tandem with the wider shape of the shoe led to the strap loop wearing against some crank designs. That loop eventually blew out, but I cut a slot in the shoe itself and that system's been working for a couple months now. The sole stiffness seems to have softened a little over use, but that's to be expected from anything you're riding or walking in day after day. If anything they're just more comfortable for it. Pros + Very comfortable on and off the bike + Easy, effective adjustments Cons - Wide fit (and wide external dimensions around toe box) won't be ideal for everyone and all setups - Stiffness could be increased Pinkbike's Take Far from a reinvention of the ME7, the GE9 feels like a much different animal. Fairly wide and relatively soft, the shoe is supremely comfortable, and adjustable enough to fit a wide range of foot shapes. They feel stiff enough for hard pedaling, but conform nicely to wider platform clip pedals. Not the sportiest out there, but a good daily driver. — Dario DiGiulio My shoe of choice since last year's Field Test . Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Shimano Shimano Ge9 Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Shimano GF800 GTX Flat Pedal Shoes - Grippy & Ready for the Rain
author: Matt Beer
article_text: The GF8GTX has all of the features of a premium wet weather flat pedal shoe: sticky soles, Boa closure, ankle gaiter, and Gore-Tex. There aren't many similarities between derailleurs and shoes, but that hasn’t stopped Shimano from producing quality products in both categories for decades. One of their staples has been the MW5, a waterproof clipless shoe. Until recently, an equivalent flat pedal version has been hard to come by. Shimano has answered with the GF800 GTX, delivering all the bells and whistles needed in a waterproof flat shoe. It features a Gore-Tex shield throughout, an ankle gaiter to keep debris out, a Boa dial closure, and a specially formulated rubber sole. GF800 GTX Details • Boa dial closure • Gore-Tex gaiter ankle cuff • Ultread-GR • Added toe protection • Sizes: 38-48 • Weight: 433 grams (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $220 USD • shimano.com Shimano has worked diligently to ensure that the Ultread GF rubber compound remains flexible and grippy in cold weather when those materials tend to firm up. No laces to get clogged with mud. This Ultread rubber was designed to remain soft in colder weather. The sole is more flexible compared to previous Shimano flat pedal shoes I've tried. Price and Weight The GF800 GTX is Shimano’s exclusive waterproof flat pedal shoe in the lineup, and it doesn't come at an insignificant cost - $220 USD to be exact. But who can put a price on dry feet? A single shoe weighs 433g in size 42 or M8.5. Shimano includes a one-year warranty and free shipping on orders over $100. There’s also a free return and exchange program offered for that price. You also get the option of a fully black shoe or one with a splash of olive color. When lined up against a direct competitor such as the Five Ten Trailcross Gore-Tex, the Shimanos cost $20 more and weigh slightly more. They’re certainly not heavy, though, and I’d say the price is in the ballpark for a shoe with these features. The ankle gaiter has an elastic cuff to keep debris out but this soft material isn't exactly "waterproof". Overall, the shoe is not cumbersome as you might expect for a wet weather shoe, however, the toe has a stiffer rubber cap for protection. Fit The GF8GTX only comes in full sizes from 38 to 48 - no half sizes or wide options exist in this model. Shimano does add a note stating that if you previously used the ME line of shoes, size down. Otherwise, if you were running the AM models, keep the same size. Typically, I run a size 42/8.5 and found them to be a touch narrow and short, but extremely comfortable. Another note on winter riding is that you’ll probably choose thicker socks, so keep that in mind. Putting on the GF800 GTX's is a bit like sliding into those popular leather boots you see in every mountain town. The Boa dial makes them convenient to remove with cold or muddy hands and the elastic ankle cuff, which is neither too tight nor too loose, features finger loops to slip on easily. The entire lacing system relies on just one Boa cinch system with three lace loops per side of the shoe. Wisely, Shimano has placed the Boa dial slightly higher on the shoe to prevent it from snagging on low-lying objects and also included a small guard to shield the dial. Good coverage and breathable, but not the warmest for winter riding. Extra grip at the toe and heel edges work well for digging in when hiking up or down. Performance Grip is not the most important thing when it comes to clipless shoes, but for a flat pedal enthusiast, it is first and foremost. Thankfully, Shimano has made improvements from the GR9 shoe and specifically kept cold weather riding in mind for the GF800 GTX. Not only is the rubber designed to remain soft and sticky in cooler temps, but the sole of the shoe is also flexible enough to conform around the pedal slightly without giving up support underfoot. The GF800 GTX shoes aren't quite as sticky as Five Ten's benchmark Stealth rubber, but they're not far off, and I don't have any complaints about how well my feet stayed on the pedals. In terms of construction and protection, they meet a favorable middle ground. They're light, fairly breathable, yet still have a protective toe cap where it matters most. As for the rest of the shoe, I found the ankle area to be slightly shallow. Even though the sole is thin and wide, which is preferred for flat pedals, my heel sat high against the soft walls of the shoe. The retention and area that the laces capture is excellent however, especially considering there is only one Boa dial. Staying warm and dry is a crucial part of the equation here and the GF800 GTX does okay in those categories. Having enough overlap on your pants is critical in order to stay as dry as possible - the more, the better, because the upper material is not the tallest or 100% waterproof when submersed - it's more like splash proof. On rainy days, I'd end up with mildly wet feet shortly into a ride compared to something as impervious as the Fizik Artica X2 . The GF800 GTX shoes do a decent job of keeping water out without turning your feet into a total steam room though. The trade-off is that there isn't much insulation. Even with dry feet and wool socks, I'd end up with chilly toes on cooler days soon than I anticipated. All in all, I'd reach for these shoes when there's any chance of standing water and much prefer the dexterous, grippy GF800 GTX over the bulk of a waterproof sock in a regular flat pedal shoe. I tend to wear out shoes on the inside from rubbing the crank arm while descending, but the GF800 GTX shoes have been robust in gritty conditions. High performance rubber, whether it's in a tire or a shoe, tends to wear faster. These shoes have seen a lot of use this fall, but they're holding up well - most importantly, they're grippy. Durability Sticky rubber can degrade quickly at the sacrifice of increased grip, but so far that hasn't been the case with the GF800 GTX. They've latched on to pedal pins well without being totally shredded. I’ve noticed that I can be hard on the shoe panel that rests against the crank arms, particularly in winter months when gritty mud is prevalent. All of the materials have held up well without any signs of fraying at the seams. They've also staved off plenty of sticks while bashing through the underbrush and the Boa closures never accidentally disengaged. Pros + Flexible, thin and grippy sole clings to pedals in cold weather + Lightweight yet protective toe cap + Boa lace system is secure and shielded from impacts Cons - A deeper heel cup would provide further ankle support - Upper gaiter material is only water-resistant - "Cold-weather ready" refers rubber remaining soft, not so much the insulating qualities Pinkbike's Take Shimano has answered the call of flat pedal enthusiasts when it comes to an appropriately grippy and flexible shoe. The only small complaint has to do with the height of the ankle gaiter - an even taller cuff would improve the shoe's ability to keep water out. The GF800 GTX isn't cheap, but it hits the mark when it comes to comfort, grip, and overall performance, making it one of my top picks for wet weather riding. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Shimano Shimano Gr800 Gtx Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Shimano XT Linkglide - Here For a Good Time AND a Long Time
author: Henry Quinney
tags: reviews-and-tech, videos, reviews, drivetrain, shimano, linkglide, shimano-xt
article_text: Simple or Genius? Don't Overlook This 11-Speed Drivetrain | Shimano Linkglide Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/G87K7xs7 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 12:25 It has been a busy couple of years for the bicycle industry. A few years ago, like so many outdoor pursuits, it was ticking along in happy near anonymity then 2020 rolled around the corner, and... well, you know the rest. The push and pull of demand certainly hasn't only been felt within the cycle industry even if it is an area that feels like it's been hit very hard. However, as we moved forward into 2023 SRAM and Shimano, the biggest two premium drivetrain manufacturers by far were both busy readying the frontier for the new battle lines being drawn. SRAM received most of the plaudits for their T-Type drivetrains, but that's not to say Shimano hasn't been busy. Linkglide XT Details • 11-speed, 11-50 tooth cassette • Makes use of derailleur hanger • Focus on durability & smoothness • Linkglide runs through the whole Cues group • Steel cassette • Price: $343 USD • bike.shimano.com T-Type is wonderful, but it's currently only available on very high-end bikes. While GX plugged the gap and sits below X0 and XX, I would still consider it costly, even if the performance is very good. While much of the mountain bike world's attention was focused on SRAM's wireless efforts, Shimano delivered something arguably just as important - their LinkGlide-equipped drivetrains. All these drivetrains, which build up from their entry-level Cues groups all the way through to the XT drivetrain in question in this review, use technology that's all about interchangeability, streamlined product codes, durability, smoothness, and (not inconsequentially) improving the chances that your local bike workshop actually stocks the parts you need. To do this they've moved all of the LinkGlide drivetrain parts onto linear pull 11-speed spacing. This means that with some clever use of limit screws or phantom clicks before sinching the cable most capable mechanics will be able to make any one part work in conjunction with another—providing flexible upgrade paths for less expensive bikes. It also means they've been able to invest in the technology that will see the most amount of return because it will feature in so many products, and that technology is LinkGlide. LinkGlide utilizes taller teeth that are thicker at the base on a heavier steel cassette. The cassette, which does without the race-performance quick-shift of Hyperglide+, aims to offer the smoothest shift Shimano has ever made, even if not the fastest. This is valuable for several reasons. Firstly, it pairs up very well with Di2 Autoshift on e-bikes. Secondly, the focus on durability and robustness plays very well into the goals of Shimano's budget-friendly Cues drivetrains. And thirdly, although it's pure speculation, surely this lays the groundwork for the new Shimano Saint. I would be surprised, and quite frankly a little disappointed if it didn't. There is a Di2 version of the group, but that is currently only available on e-bikes. It's the LinkGlide technology that laid the necessary foundations for Shimano's FreeShift and Autoshift. Having ridden it, I was genuinely impressed by the performance. Normal bikes will have to make do with mechanical shifting, for now at least. Linkglide XT Details The Cassette Holds the Tech All too often you will see a new bike that has a fancy derailleur, a mid-tier shifter, and a bog-standard cassette. We've come to associate shift quality being something achieved thanks to great rear-mechs, but often the most eye-catching part of the drivetrain isn't the most important. This is definitely the case with LinkGlide. Most the novel technology is packed in the cassette. The 11-speed 11-50T cassette that I tested weighs 624 grams and features 11, 13, 15, 17, 20, 23, 26, 30, 36, 43 and 50T cogs. Initially, upon the release of LinkGlide XT there was some criticism of the weight of the cassette. That initial model weighed over 700 grams. Since then, Shimano has shaved around 160 grams off. That 160 grams also represents the weight difference when comparing the slimmed down cassette to a Hyperglide+ 12-speed 10-51T, cassette which weighs 470 grams. Not only is Hyperglide+ lighter but it also does so while featuring an additional cog. The range offered with Hyperglide+ is also larger (510% versus Linglide's 455%). All of this points to Hyperglide being the performance, premium option and it seems Shimano are very comfortable with that. Hyperglide+ is the technology that will give you a faster shift, greater range and lower weight. LinkGlide isn't about outperforming Hyperglide+ as an option for racers, but rather about catering to the needs of entry-level riders, e-bikers and riders who want a durable cassette that is smooth under power. In some ways, all three of these groups have a common enemy: delicate componentry that can't handle being treated poorly. Whether that's somebody on their first mountain bike who perhaps hasn't quite yet honed their shifting technique, e-bikers whose motors overpower drivetrains on their way through the gears, or core-mountain bikers who don't care much for race performance as such, but want something that will prove both of decent quality and cost-effective. How Have They Done This? LinkGlide uses a standard HG freehub body with 11-speed spacing, rather than the Microspline driver used on Shimano's Hyperglide+ drivetrains. While this does limit the size of the 11T (you cannot go any smaller due to the diameter of the body itself) it means that anyone that has a bike with CUES or just a bike with this style of freehub body now has the chance to incrementally upgrade their parts as they break or wear to a product line with quite a high ceiling in terms of performance. The teeth are thicker at the base and taller. The tooth profile also has a different profile compared to HyperGlide at their edge. This again is thicker and looks to have a large angled edge. Shimano's aim is to provide the chain with the "smoothest" path possible as it crosses between two cogs. They hope to remove "shifting shock", which is what they refer to the rider generating and then picking up chain slack when changing gears, and can sometimes be felt as a jolt through the feet. This is going to be very useful for e-bikes, where drivetrains can sometimes be put under a huge amount of strain under shifts. Shimano claims that this shift is 3 times smoother when featured in their internal comparisons. The cassette is also purported to be three times more durable. This is an impressive figure but it should be noted that these aren't independently verified, and goes off Shimano's data. That said, even if was merely twice as durable it would still be worth the weight trade-off and a slight reduction in range in my opinion. Chain Shimano claims that their chains aren't laden with the new technology like the cassette is, and are instead a much more conventional affair. Naturally, they say the system is optimized for a LinkGlide XT chain, but that it isn't strictly necessary. Oftentimes, the profile of the chain links can be similar or even interchangeable as you go to more expensive chains but the treatment or material changes. Derailleur & Shifter LinkGlide XT uses a shifter that differs from their other high-end offerings in only one meaningful way - you can only grab one upshift at a time. They clearly expect this system to get specced on a lot of e-bikes, but some will miss the double shift option. Apart from that, they're very similar. It's ergonomic with ribbed panels for more grip. It's available in I-spec or with a simple band that is tightened with a 4mm Allen bolt. Ours weighed 119 grams. The derailleur does see some small changes that will keep riders happy, while also including the features that people have come to expect from a Shimano Shadow mech - the relatively low profile, sealed bearings and a somewhat adjustable clutch that can be turned off with the flick of a lever. The smallest and largest cogs that this cassette is rated to work with are the 11 and 50T ones of the Linkglide cassette. It has a weight of 307 grams. There is a small amount of rubber protection on the underside of the mech body. Although this probably not going to save your mech if you hit something particularly hard, it should stop any chain-to-mech rattling. The mech uses a standard mech-hanger fitting. Performance I have found the shifting performance to be excellent. The slower shift isn't something you really notice, and it blends in with your riding. If I were picky, I would say it seems a shade slower going down the cassette into the smaller cogs rather than moving into the lower, bigger gears. It's very slight though. I think if you're somebody that pedals a reasonable cadence you probably won't notice it much at all. However, when on tech-climbs and trying to make a high-gear work I was thankful for the ability of the cassette to undertake unsympathetic shifts, if only because in those moments patience isn't always at the forefront of your mind. Speaking of which, while the shift might not be the fastest or the quietest, it is very robust. You really can just do what you want and it never misses a beat. Over the past year, I moved away from the XT chain I started with and went onto Deore 11 speed and the cheapest chain in my local bike shop, which happened to be a KMC. Again, the shifting remained very good. I did sometimes miss the double-click Multi Release you may well associate Shimano shifters with but you quickly adapt. The shifter is the one area that does feel that little less refined or premium compared to XT Hyperglide+. It's nothing huge, but there's just something about the ergonomics which feels that bit cheaper. Durability I've ridden this drivetrain. A lot. Climbing-wise about 100,000 meters of elevation gained over the summer. Whether it's been endless bike park laps over the summer or big days in Squamish, the durability has been excellent and it shifts just as well now as it did on the first day. I've changed the cable once, but it's been very solid. No bushing play or binding in the mech has crept in over time, nor has it developed any noise or slapping when descending. All in all, it has been excellent in this regard. During testing I used the SM-CRM85 12 Speed chainring, and it worked very well. Again, this uses steel teeth and looks to have stood up very well to use. Weight & Price I think this group set represents a great trade of between weight, durability, price, and performance. It may not be the best in any one category but for riders who want a compromise between all four this is a great option. It isn't as sleek as SRAM's Transmission but it's also vastly cheaper. Theoretically, you could get this cassette to last a very very long time indeed and just replace cables and chains as needed. If you paired this up to a steel chainring that could also be one less part to wear. All in all, the groupset costs around $350 USD. This isn't cheap but I do think it offers good value for something that has been so consistently impressive for nearly a year, never mind the fact it should theoretically offer that level of performance for a long time yet. SL-M8130 DEORE XT Linkglide Shifter - $66.99 RD-M8130 DEORE XT Linkglide Rear Derailleur , - $121.99 CS-LG700-11 Linkglide Cassette - 11SPD - $130.99 CN-LG500 Linkglide Chain - $22.99 Pros + Offers a smooth shift that requires little sympathy + Durable + Easy upgrade path + Fair pricing when considering durability Cons - Not as much range as Hyperglide+ or Eagle - Heavier than other options - Slower at shifting Pinkbike's Take For riders who feel slightly disenchanted with groupsets running into thousands of dollars with gizmos and gadgets sprouting out in every avenue, LinkGlide XT might just be the tonic. It's simple, effective and performs admirably. While it's not cheap, it's also not meant to be an entry-level setup. Rather, this sits atop the family of durability-focused parts. It's a premium level that offers excellent performance and should do for quite some time. While I wouldn't be itching to put it on an XC or lightweight trail bike, in some ways it seems tailor-made for enduro riders who want to get to the top, shred down, hose their bike down and throw it in the shed. I've loved how I've been able to wholly rely on it, and in the best part of a year, it's been nothing other than a reliable and consistent performer and is what I would recommend for people who just want to get on with riding and who have little interest in counting grams or chasing their fastest ever climb. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos Reviews Drivetrain Shimano Linkglide Shimano Xt Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: After 13 Years Shimano’s Saint Brakes Are Still on Top for Outright Power
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, big-brake-test, brakes, shimano, shimano-saint
article_text: Shimano Saint Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/Z7mBMf29 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 00:47 Shimano Saint Shimano's Saint brakes are something of an outlier in this group, but it's not because of their power, features, or design. The biggest differentiator is their age, with these old dogs clocking over 13 years of continued presence in the Shimano lineup. Why did we include them in the conversation around the best brakes on the current market? Because they still compete with the best of them. Saint Details • Mineral oil • Adjustments: Reach, free stroke • 2 pad options • 2x15mm, 2x17mm pistons • Price: $550 USD (pair) • Brake weight: 298g (front) • Rotor weight: 171g (203mm) • shimano.com BUY AT: Backcountry 2013 was the year that Apple released the iPhone 5s - many numbers ago by my calculations. That phone implemented the first 64-bit processor ever used in a handset, and had a timeless hardware design with slick features like a fingerprint scanner. Like the 5s, the Saints have all the critical features of modern brakes, just without some of the time-earned refinement and fancy touches that we've grown used to. Based on how much I like the Saints, it might be time to trade in my current phone for that older model. An interesting aside: while I was working on this, Apple released the first software update for the iPhone 5s in 12 years. Maybe they're listening, maybe I'm losing my grasp on reality. Regardless, it would follow that Shimano would release their Saint update to follow suit. Setup Shimano's funnel bleed is a simple procedure, but there are countless alternative tricks and methods that mechanics have developed over the years. Luckily, getting a decent feel is easy, and though you'll have to do some upkeep to keep them humming, it's not terribly involved. The only meaningful adjustment on the Saint lever is the reach adjust, standing prominently from the lever blade itself. Easy to turn on the fly, and features a large range. With a normal bleed, the lever doesn't bite super close to the bar, but has enough range for smaller hands. Livability: Like just about every ServoWave Shimano brake before the current XTRs, the Saint does have issues with a wandering bite point in some situations. Not all riders are bothered by this, and certain setup tricks can mitigate the issues, but ultimately it is part of the program. Feel Shimano's ServoWave lever cam has a very distinct feel to it, and the Saint embodies that sensation fully. A nose of resistance breaks you into the stroke, which then moves very freely until you hit the bite point. It takes some getting used to, but a squeezes gets you acquainted with the sensation. The Saints have quite a bit of free throw, and unfortunately the Free Throw adjuster does little (if anything) to alter this feel. As with so many brakes, there are some setup tricks to alter this feel, but that's best left to the forums and the comments. One defining characteristic stands out on the Saints, and that's just how touchy they are at the bite point. With very little modulation to speak of, metering the massive power is left up to the user's fine motor skills, and some well-timed braking points. Feel Ranking: Once you're past the initial point of resistance, the lever swings with light action, all the way through the large dead band and into the bite point. That bite point is firm and aggressive, requiring good technique. Water Bottle Pull Test: 597 g pull force (5th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power Though there are some competitive new alternatives in this space, the Shimano Saint is still the name many people think of when it comes to all-out power. That reputation is well earned, with the closest feeling to shoving a branch through your spokes as anything on test. As mentioned above, the finesse required to modulate power on the Saints will be a challenge for some riders, exacerbated in challenging trail situations. But for those that want all the power at once, and know when to demand it, these binary stoppers are hard to beat. On sustained descents where you're just pulling the brakes to reign in momentum, the power on tap is impressive, but it's the initial bite that leaves the strongest impression with these. Power Ranking: Turns out, nothing stops a Honda Fit quite like the Shimano Saint. These famously powerful brakes proved to be immensely strong on trail too, with one of the sharpest initial bites on test. Honda Fit Stop Test: 22.4 m / 73.5 ft (1st overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. Pros + Massive power + Distinct Shimano feel + Durable, simple, aggressive Cons - Large free strong deadband - Some wander in the bite point - Lack of adjustment beyond reach Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Big Brake Test Brakes Shimano Shimano Saint Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. title: Review: Shimano XT 8220 Brakes - More Consistency, Same Value
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Shimano XT Brake Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/bmdqgWsE Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 11:03 Shimano's XT level components have always been about high performance at a more reasonable price. They're not quite as light as the top-tier XTR parts, but share almost all of the same features. The XT 8220 model is the four-piston caliper brake - it's what you'll find on most trail and e-bikes sitting on showroom floors. The XT 8200 model uses the same lever but has a lighter, less powerful 2-piston caliper. The 8200 series master cylinders and calipers received a serious makeover with numerous mechanical changes. The main talking point is the low-viscosity mineral oil inside the system, said to improve the “wandering bite point" issues of the past. XT 8220 Brake Details • 2x15, 2x17mm pistons • Tool-free lever reach adjustment • Tooled free-stroke adjustment • Shimano low-viscosity mineral fluid • Non-ambidexterous master cylinders • Rotor diameters: 160, 180, 203, 220mm • Weight: 308g (front w/finned metallic pads) • Price: $265 per end (w/pads, w/o rotors) • shimano.com The redesigned master cylinder and parallel hose are very distinct. A small bolt at the front edge of the master cylinder adjusts the free-stroke (kind of). Pinkbike's Take Shimano has answered the cries of inconsistent brake performance by switching to a lower viscosity fluid, improving seal flexibility, and easing the bleed procedure on the XT 8220 brakes. What's more, the overhauled brakes have retained their value and power, while maintaining a respectable weight for trail and enduro bikes. One thing Shimano didn't deviate from was their Servowave lever actuation. It's unique and has divided consumers: some love the progressive feel, while others prefer a more linear power band. For those opposed to that functionality on previous brakes, new lever ergonomics and a wider range of adjustments could help to change their minds. — Matt Beer Details and Design The most identifying feature of the XT 8200 series brakes is the angle of the hose and master cylinder. Those now run parallel to the handlebar and are tucked out of harm's way. The hose does require trimming down to a minimum length to reduce tapping against the bar over bumps. The master cylinders are still individually shaped for either side of the bars, which can lower the chances of finding replacement bodies, but Shimano has a good reason. They say that this design allows for the bleed port to move to the highest point of the reservoir for the best bleed possible, something harder to achieve with an ambidextrous lever body. Shimano carries on with its Servowave technology in the XT 8200 levers. A cam at the lever pivot maximizes mechanical advantage on the master cylinder as the lever is pulled. This feature is the driver behind Shimano’s brake feel, which we’ll get into in depth in the Ride Impressions section. More tweaks occur within the lever itself. The tool-free reach adjuster is now housed in the lever blade to protect the dial in case of a spill. Each indent is noticeable, and the sweep range is large, so all hand sizes should be able to find their sweet spot. A second adjustment to change the pad contact, or free-stroke, as Shimano calls it, requires a 2mm allen key. This has more of an effect than the previous 8100 brake, but is still less noticeable compared to the range of adjustment offered by SRAM Maven or Hope Tech 4 V4s brakes. Two big updates are found on the caliper: the bleed port screw and the pad retention system. Bleeding the brakes no longer requires an open-ended wrench. Instead, a 3mm Allen key opens the bleed port, which is in a separate location from where the bleed syringe attaches. Shimano also worked to reduce the brake pad rattle in the caliper by creating an oval slot in the brake pads. Front = 308 with finned, metallic pads. Rear = 320g with pads. Price and Weight As I alluded to, XT components present a high performance for their price. The XT 8220 brakes cost $265 USD per end, which includes a set of metal, finned pads. Rotors and adaptors are not included, since those will vary depending on user preferences. The system comes pre-bled, except the lever body is not connected to the 1000mm (front) or 1700mm (rear) brake hoses, since most frames use internal routing. Included is one crush olive washer and needle fitting. The bleed kit, mineral oil, adapters, and rotors are sold separately. XTR is the cream of the crop MTB groupset from Shimano. XTR has always been lighter and more expensive, yet some components look similar to XT. An XTR 9220 front brake weighs 294g and costs $335 USD. I weighed the XT front brake (pads included) at 308g. That’s only 14 grams lighter than the XTR, yet they cost $70 more. So what gives? The 5-degree upswept on the XTR lever. Mono-block caliper for the top-tier Shimano brake. How are they different in terms of design and function from XTR 9220 brakes? The main difference between the XT 8220 and XTR 9220 calipers is that the latter is a one-piece unit, whereas the XT caliper is bolted together. The master cylinder pistons are the same size: 15 and 17mm. Regardless of being bolted together or not, the newer XT and XTR model calipers are stiffer than the outgoing 8120 and 9120 models. The XTR lever uses a funky blade with a 5-degree upsweep and rides on a bearing, instead of a bushing. Is the power any different between the new XTR and XT brakes? I would be hard-pressed to notice any gain in power or performance. Each system uses the same size master cylinder and caliper pistons, as well as brake pads. Specific model rotors exist, but they are interchangeable between brake systems. The biggest factor might come down whether you jive with the upswept lever, or if you care to spend the extra cash. The 3mm bled screw. The hose attaches to the port in front of the screw. Setup and Bleed Essentially, the system is bled from the bottom up using a syringe on the caliper to push fluid and air up the brake line. The process is approachable by a moderately confident mechanic and takes about 15 minutes, depending on how much air is present in the system and how familiar you are with the procedure. Bleeding the system follows the Shimano protocol found here . The only exception to the document listed is that the caliper uses a 3mm allen key to open and close the bleed port. It seems like a small switch-up, but the new split bleed port and screw make life much easier. You’re far less likely to slip the Allen wrench. One potential complication I found difficult to sidestep was attaching the hose to the caliper without introducing an air bubble. Squeezing the hose will help push the fluid to the very end and requires practice. Otherwise, most bleeds were successful and consistent. If you are experiencing wandering bite point issues, and this goes for any brake, it's likely improper oil volume, or trapped air is causing you problems. Revisit the bleed procedure and follow the instructions, or bring the bike to a professional. It’s stated very clearly and loudly on the packaging, but the new low-viscosity fluid is not backwards-compatible with the previous generation brakes. Doing so will render the 8100 series brakes useless as the fluid will degrade the seals. Shimano XT 8220: $265 USD per end. Hayes Dominion A4: $250 USD per end. How do they compare? The Hayes Dominion A4 could be considered one of the most valuable buys in the braking department. These check in at $250 per end and weigh 303g (w/pads, w/o rotor). What makes the Dominion a favorite choice for many is the extremely light lever pull. I listed this as both a Pro and a Con in our review of the brakes and stand by those statements. For some riders, they will appreciate how easy these are on the fingers, while others may struggle to sense where that bite point makes contact with the rotor during technical maneuvers. Like the XTs, once the pads do make contact with the rotor, they have a sensible and usable amount of power. However, I'd rank the Dominions slightly below in terms of maximum power and fade resistance. The metallic pad option will be your best bet for long and steep descents. Livability is simple with the Dominions. The bleed is straightforward in general, plus the dual ports on the caliper allow for isolated bleeding at this end. The lever adjustments are effective, though you still need an Allen key to change the pad contact (like the XTs). Ride Impressions If you were a fan of Shimano brakes before, then you’ll probably love the new XT 8220 even more. The lever adjustments are more meaningful, and the vulnerability of the lever body has been looked after. Both the reach adjust and free stroke have wider ranges to help balance the position and pull of the left and right levers. When it comes to power, the XT 8220s are a fine choice for enduro bikes and even e-bikes in most scenarios. More extreme use cases would be better suited to the Saint, where the power is incredibly strong, as we proved in our Big Bad Brake Test earlier this year. The lever actuation is fairly light, but still takes a small nudge to get moving. There’s also a large amount of deadband or free-stroke to work through. In that sense, they are still very “Shimano” in operation. By that, I mean the power can kick in suddenly. It takes some learning as to where in the lever stroke that power turns on. Once into the power band (holding pad-to-rotor contact), modulation is excellent, with the metallic pads. Various-sized rotors and pad compounds can be used to bridge the gap between all-or-nothing power. When bled according to Shimano’s guidelines, a fair amount of deadstroke exists. Shimano RT66, RT76, CL800 and their other high-end rotors are 1.8mm thick for the purpose of reducing friction, should a rotor be slightly out of alignment. The lever moves quickly through the deadstroke and ramps up the power as the lever is pulled closer to the bar. For riders wishing for even less free-stroke, thicker aftermarket rotors may be the way forward. The consistency or stability of the brake’s hydraulic actuation has been very well looked after with low-viscosity oil and revised seals. You can imagine how guessing when or where these brakes’ fine bite point kicks in could cause inconsistent results when trying to slow down. These XTs have remained consistent for long periods of time without a bleed, even through the cooler months of the year. Previous Shimano brake pads were known to rattle in the caliper. That problem has been diminished massively with the oval-shaped retainer pin, but not entirely eliminated. The pickiest riders will notice some noise and chatter. Shimano vs Galfer rotors and pads We’ve beaten brake talk to death in recent months, but I thought this experiment would shed some light on how thicker aftermarket rotors affect Shimano brakes. I put Shimano’s metallic, finned pads and MT905 rotors to the test against Galfer’s G1851 Advanced pads and the 2.0mm thick Wave rotor (DB034W), both in 203mm diameters. I conducted back-to-back laps with the Brake Ace sensors on Angy M (my go to test track). Shimano pads and rotors went first, followed by the Galfer bits. Keeping all else equal variables (track, conditions, bike, tires, and brake bleed), there were some pronounced differences. The largest factor was the decrease in free-stroke with the thicker Galfer rotor. That doesn’t come as a shocker. What was surprising was how I was using the brakes and their effectiveness. With the Galfer setup, the braking zones were longer - in other words, I spent more time braking. But I used the rear brake more. There were also fewer braking events (Galfer: 54 vs. Shimano: 64). I often caught myself starting to brake too early and pulled too hard with the Shimano pads and rotors, which explains why peak speeds varied more frequently with this setup. Who cares, right? Well, it depends on what you’re trying to achieve. A rough estimation of the start and finish shows that my times varied by less than one second. All of that tells me that my braking habits could turn on and modulate the power better than with the Shimano setup. Data aside, the Shimano setup had more power once the pads contacted the rotors, which I’d choose for freeride moves on super steep slab moves. If I were enduro racing, I might choose the Galfer setup for less chance of stabbing the brake accidentally. Run 1, Shimano XT, metallic pads and MT905 rotor. Run 2, Galfer pads and rotors. Pros + Compact lever redesign is less prone to damage from a crash + Snappier lever feel and more consistent performance from low-viscosity fluid + Improved bleed experience Cons - Difficult to minimize dead stroke - Dual-bar contact points take up more real estate than other single-clamp brake mounts Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Brakes Shimano Shimano Xt Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Shimano XT Di2 Review: Great Shifting, But Where’s the Clutch?
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Shimano XT Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/pdsZhhkn Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:44 After waiting for what seemed like an eternity, Shimano has levelled up to SRAM with a wireless electronic drivetrain. Shimano debuted its wireless Di2 technology with its gold standard XTR M9200 group . The functional aspects of that series have been passed down a step to the Deore XT Di2 M8200 group, sparing the exotic details and adding a couple of extra grams. Within the Deore XT Di2 M8200 series are brakes, wheels, and multiple drivetrain gear options. We’re focusing on the 12-speed wide range drivetrain, consisting of a 10-51 tooth cassette, long cage derailleur, plus an aluminum chainring and cranks. What about the chain, you ask? That and the bottom bracket are the only pieces of the puzzle that are carried over. The M8200 components are backward compatible with the older XT M8100 series. In fact, Shimano even sells an XT Di2 M8200 drivetrain upgrade kit, which includes a derailleur, shifter, battery, charger, and chain quick link for $860 USD. The full meal deal costs $1,280 USD, while a replacement derailleur on its own takes up a chunk of that sum at $570. XT Di2 Details • 11-speed Linkglide or 12-speed Hyperglide+ options • 10-51 or 9-45 tooth 12-speed cassettes • Wirelessly actuated • Universal derailleur hanger (UDH) compatible • Compatible with existing Shimano 11 and 12-speed components • Weight: 1,854g (SGS derailleur w/battery, shifter, 175mm cranks w/ 32T chainring, chain, cassette) • Price: $1,280 USD (derailleur, battery, shifter, cranks, chainring, chain, cassette) • bike.shimano.com UDH compatibility is much appreciated. The battery door latch and adjustment screws. Details and Design Derailleur In Shimano lingo, SGS means long cage, and GS mean short cage. Those derailleurs correspond to either a 10-51 tooth or a 9-45 tooth cassette. Both are of the 12-speed variety and only differ in cage length, which are replaceable. The cassettes use Hyperglide+ technology, which is compatible with Shimano’s Autoshift e-bike feature. Aside from the wireless actuation, the largest change to the XT M8200 derailleurs is found at the cage attachment pivot. Shimano moved away from a clutch to hold the cage steady and replaced that with a two-way spring. The design led to a more compact derailleur body with smooth, angular shapes, something that wasn’t possible with a protruding clutch cover. Shimano calls this Shadow ES technology, which means it’s slim and robust. But don’t try to stand on this one - the derailleur still uses a standard derailleur hanger as an engineered failure point. Moving to the dual-spring cage also helps the derailleur recover after a strike. It’s a function that Shimano calls the Automatic Impact Recovery Function - makes sense. The motor momentarily turns off, allows for the derailleur body to articulate out of the way, and quickly powers back on. More advantages are said to come in the form of improved shifting performance under load, less influence on suspension, and lower maintenance. Shimano nailed the shifter design. The smaller function button is used to begin trimming the derailleur alignment. Angle and sweep adjustments for each rubberized paddle. Shifter The shifter “switch,” as Shimano calls it, is highly customizable in terms of position, too. The housing slides along the bar clamp using either a band clamp or an I-Spec attachment. The paddles themselves can rotate independently 15 degrees on the lever axis, and can also be clocked the same amount on the shifter arm. All in all, it improves the experience over the limited range of movement that SRAM’s Pods offer. I prefer the clamped crank spindle opposed to the self-extracting style of other cranksets. It's not as pretty, but I do enjoy leaving the skin on my knuckles. And if you haven't figured it out already, a 5 and 8mm allen key doubles up to fit in the preloader nut perfectly. Cranks, Ring, and Chain Why put a vulnerable material in a high-impact zone? It beats me, and Shimano. They’ve produced aluminum cranks with their Hollowtech II technology for some time. Besides rock strikes, aluminum is more resilient than carbon. If you’re a duck-footed rider, your ankles will appreciate that all of the edges are smooth and round. I’m also a fan of the 24mm steel crank spindle compared to other brands' 28.99 or 30mm choices; the smaller diameter allows for larger bearings, which can last longer. In terms of crank length, the XTs come in four sizes: 160, 165, 170 and 175mm. These have a 176mm Q-factor and 55mm chainline. Chainrings ranging from 28-36 teeth mount directly to the crank arm with Shimano’s 16-notch lock ring. As mentioned, the chain is the only carry-over component from the M8100 series. There’s little to report on, in a good way. A 30-tooth ring has served well to keep the chain in place on two different test bikes. I haven’t spent quite enough time on this groupset to burn out a chainring. M8200 (left) vs the M8100 (right): the spider arm has fewer pieces and therefore fewer rivets. Cassette The M8200 cassette is lighter in color and weight than the M8100, but only by 7 grams. The most notable change is the redesigned spider that the cogs mount to. New spider arms and rivets are said to eliminate creaking, and that’s been my experience so far. You can see that the spider is now directly attached to the two outermost cogs with fewer rivets. There are a few more pieces to align than slapping on an XD cassette, but you get 12-speeds and a 510% range. Installation and Setup From the get-go, the default settings are great; however, you can dive deep into the shifter functions, speed, multi-shifts, and connectivity to cycling computers using Shimano’s E-Tube app. Basic trim adjustments are made by holding either the communication button on the derailleur or the shifter for more than 2 seconds. Once set to the adjustment mode, the shifter paddles let you trim the derailleur. This adjustment is global across the cassette (like a traditional cable adjustment) and not specific to each gear. Otherwise, the derailleur mounts in a traditional sense with high, low, and B-tension adjustment screws. I do wish Shimano added a cage lock feature to make wheel installation or removal more straightforward. Installing and removing the cranks is smooth and effortless. The non-driveside crank arm slides onto the spindle, which is permanently fitted to the driveside arm, where a set of pinch bolts locks down a preload nut. There’s no need to break out the cheater bar or use an impact wrench to free them. Batteries and Charging Replenishing battery juice is done by removing the battery from the derailleur and setting it on the charger, which is included in the group packages. A single press on the function button shows the battery’s percentage. Shimano says you can get 1,000 km of riding completed before you’ll need to recharge the battery. That’s not a very logical way to measure battery life since you could ride in one gear indefinitely - the number of shifts per charge would be more appropriate. Regardless, the battery lifespan is impressive since it doesn’t wake when shaken - you’ll need to press the shifter to get the system going. The shifter uses two CR1632 coin batteries and is said to last 4 years. Checking the status requires a press of both paddles simultaneously. A green light gives 11-100% and a red light shows 1-10% battery life. Price and Weight There’s no question that electronic components bring along a higher cost. The extra performance can be argued - in Shimano’s case, that’s more apparent as the new Shadow ES technology makes the derailleur more robust, however, that comes with a caveat that we’ll touch on later. What you really gain is the ease of installation and lower maintenance without cables. The hidden cost is weight. The XT Di2 SGS derailleur weighs 174g more than the mechanical version. Compared to SRAM’s equal wireless components, the silver-tier X0 Eagle Transmission, Shimano’s contender is heavier, but less expensive. I-spec shifter mount. SGS derailleur with battery. 170mm cranks with hardware. 10-51 tooth cassette with lockring (and 3g of plastic packaging). Chain retention has been solid so far. Performance So, the derailleur is intended to move out of the way, but does it wiggle around too much? In short, yes. Does it save the derailleur from munching hangers and cages? Sort of. I loaded up a Canyon Spectral CF with the full XT Di2 M8200 series. Compared to the SRAM GX Eagle T-type derailleur that the bike comes stock with, there was considerably more chainslap. On larger impacts, you can really feel the cage whacking back into place. Overall, there’s more noise from the XT derailleur. Is there any difference in the small bump performance between the dual springs on the XT versus the clutch on the GX? Even if there is the slightest improvement, I wouldn’t say it’s worth the increase in chainslap. I didn’t have the chance to compare the Di2 XT to the mechanical version. Both Dario and Mike have spent time on the GS versions of the XTR or XT Di2 derailleurs, relaying that there is less noise from the shorter cage. It’s a compelling option since you only lose 1.96% range moving from a 32-tooth ring with a 10-51-tooth cassette to a 30-tooth ring and 9-45-tooth cassette. You also save about 40g of unsprung weight and gain ground clearance from the compact drivetrain. A few scars from the trail and the garage. Speed and Shift Feel The shifter paddle actuation and shape are closer to a mechanical XT shifter. Each one has its own characteristic. The shifter paddles (X being closest to the rider, and Y being further away) have two stages to them. The X paddle has two detents, which can be felt with that assertive Shimano movement that’s almost mechanical in a sense. The Y paddle only has one detent; from the 1st to 2nd stage, meaning, it's light to the touch at first. For an electronic component, they can take a smidge more effort or distance to push and transmit a signal compared to SRAM's Pod buttons, but I can appreciate how this relates more closely to a traditional shifter. You confidently know when a shift has been made, and therefore, I made fewer accidental shifts on the trail. As far as the derailleur response goes, the shifts feel and sound very much like the mechanical XT derailleur, aside from the motor buzzing. Each gear change comes with that audible Shimano “cah-chunk” at the cassette. The shift speed can be adjusted from what I’d call comparable to mechanical XT, but also much faster. Within all of the settings, there is the possibility to play with where the multi-shift kicks in - at the first or second stage of the paddle detents, or turn it off altogether. However, the faster you set the multi-shift speed, the less reliable the shifts become. Pros + Decent value, if you're going the electronic route. + Compatible with existing Shimano components. + Highly adjustable shifter. + Excellent battery life. Cons - Derailleur and chain make a lot of noise. - Still relies on hanger alignment. - Less reliable shifts at speed or under power than SRAM Transmission drivetrains. Pinkbike's Take Shimano didn't reinvent the wheel with their wireless drivetrain, as SRAM did with Transmission; they cut cables and polished corners. The system still relies on the derailleur hanger to keep shifts perfectly aligned, which has its merits. The big sells are derailleur workmanship, personalized shifting, and less maintenance. Unfortunately, those highlights are overshadowed by a potentially distracting amount of chainslap. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. title: Review: Shimano XTR 9220 Brakes - Rebuilt for Refinement
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shimano XTR Brakes https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/26Y6pnHT Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:57 Shimano's brakes have become one of the more passionately divisive components in the bike industry - a fair share of riders view them as the pinnacle of what's available, while others don't have quite as much praise to dole out. Well aware of the chief frustrations out there, the team at Shimano has been hard at work refining their brake platform to match modern expectations. Though the architecture, ergonomics, and finer details may be brand new, the overall feel and function is still distinctly Shimano. Whether that's good news or bad depends a bit on how you ride and what feel you desire. XTR 9220 Details • 4-piston caliper • Tool-free lever reach adjust • Free stroke adjust • New mineral oil • New caliper seals • Weight: 295g (actual, front caliper w/pads, hose, and lever) • MSRP: $335 USD / $428 CAD / $484 AUS per wheel • www.bike.shimano.com A complete front brake with pads weighs in at 295g, which is about 18g more than the current XTR 9120 brakes. Pricing is quite high for these silver stoppers, clocking in at $335 USD per wheel. Details Though the XTR 9220 brake has been completely redesigned, the goal wasn't to drastically change how they felt or performed on trail. Ergonomics and consistency were the primary focal points here, with many small changes made to improve both fronts. I'll start with consistency, as that's the more hotly contested issue. The engineers, product managers, and athletes at Shimano were well aware of the wandering bite point issue that has plagued their previous 4-piston brake models, and wanted to be certain that they eliminated that issue in the updated series. By moving to a lower viscosity mineral oil, the brakes perform more consistently at operating temperature, and should experience little to no pump or fade as the system heats. I put the question to Shimano's North American Mountain Bike Product Manager Nick Murdick, so here's the scoop in his own words. What was addressed to fix the wandering bite point? It’s both the low viscosity fluid and new caliper seals. The key attribute of both is their consistent performance of their jobs. The fluid flows quickly and returns to the reservoir right away when the rider lets go of the lever, but more importantly it doesn’t speed up or slow down as conditions change. The seals rebound consistently to retract the pistons and push the fluid back towards the lever at the same speed every time too. Just changing one or the other doesn’t work, and can even make things worse. — Nick Murdick Unfortunately, Shimano is recommending that riders avoid using the new oil in the previous generation 9100/9120 brakes, as the seals are not compatible with the new fluid. The updated pad shape is backwards compatible. The oval pin hole and slight wedge shape both help to prevent rattling. No, the lever's not bent. Shimano gave the blades a 5° upsweep to better match hand angle in an aggressive position. Another detail that should be immediately apparent if you're giving the 9220 brakes a good ol' shop floor squeeze test is the slightly upwards angle of the lever blade. By angling the lever 5° up from flat, Shimano found that their ride testers were able to get a setup that better matched the ergonomics of their overall cockpit. Those who are picky about brake angle might find it a bit tricky to settle in on their preferred tilt with these, but it only takes a couple of attempts to end up at a happy spot - just bring a 4mm on your ride. Lever reach is adjusted in the same manner as the previous model, but the knob has been integrated into the lever blade to give the brake a slightly cleaner silhouette. That reach knob is a little trickier to fiddle with, but the tucked away position should keep it safer from damage. The knob has very tightly spaced click detents to make it easier to count clicks of adjustment. You may find that reach point different than past Shimano models, and that's due to the fact that they've moved the lever's pivot point 5mm closer to the bar, allowing the flatter lever blade to bite parallel to the bar in a more 'typical' reach position. Dimpled blade and a textured reach knob. The 2mm inside the lever body is for replacing the lever blade. The offset hose keeps things close to the bar. The somewhat unusual offset hose at the lever is meant to bring the hydraulic lines closer to the handlebar for tidiness, regardless of how you're routing your brakes through/on/around your frame. In some circumstances, the offset actually brings the hose too close to the bar, causing it to rub or tap while riding - being fussy about hose length is going to be critical here, if you want to maintain a silent and scuff-free bike. One upside to the new design is the reduced protrusion of the brake from the bar - hopefully this will keep them safer in the event of a crash. Little links housed between the brace and the clamp. This revised architecture was achieved thanks to a very interesting pull-link that actuates the master cylinder. Where most brakes use a simple linkage to translate lever pull to the pushrod inside the brake, the new XTRs use a longer linkage to pull the pushrod through its travel. This isn't critical knowledge for the daily use of the brake, but might come into play over long-term ownership, should you need to service the inner-workings of the system. Tightly spaced click detents make reach adjust a bit more measurable. Alleged free stroke adjustment, now done with a 2mm hex. Though the reach adjuster has a very wide range of adjustment, the Free Stroke's impact on the system is still vanishingly small. Yes, when adjusted correctly as part of a larger bleed, you can get a couple millimeters of movement out of the lever throw, but it's hardly up to par with how much adjustment you can achieve with other brake systems on the market. Performance I've been riding a set of the M9220 brakes for the past few months, through freezing weather and toasty temps alike, and can happily report that my chief complaints about the previous brakes have been addressed. No more wandering bite point, no more rattling pads*, and no pump or fade over long runs - as you'd hope. Like the previous brakes, the 9220 model is quite sensitive to the bleed you give them, so getting that perfect should be a chief concern. Yes, all brakes benefit from the best bleed possible, but given the very progressive nature of Shimano's leverage, you notice changes in that feel over the course of a run in ways you might not with a softer-biting brake. *In a cruel turn of fate, the pads started to exhibit some rattle only a couple days after I wrote this. I'm chalking it up to the braking surface quite worn down on the pads themselves, plus some very dry weather that does nothing to mask the little pings and pops that only come out of a bike in summer dust. Hopefully a fresh set is all that's required to alleviate this, but at least you can always run a non-finned alternative for guaranteed silence. That bite point feel is one of those intangible preference items that can be hard to translate, and might not even matter to some riders out there. For those that are picky about brake feel, rest assured that the unique power band Shimano offers hasn't really changed that much, but feels a bit easier to control than before. There's a slight nose of resistance as you break the lever into travel, but it then swings freely to the bite point, where things ramp up pretty quickly to max power. Photo: Lear Miller The Northwest can be a uniquely taxing place on brakes, with plentiful grip when the conditions are good, and plenty of long steep descents to push them to the limit. Even under extreme use cases, I've found the XTRs to hold up well to heat and resist fading, even if they do start howling towards the bottom of a massive run. I'd call this a significant improvement over the previous generation, which experienced a noticeable change in feel and power in similar use. Though Shimano says the power has been increased in the new XTR brakes, I don't find it to be a drastic difference over the previous model. You can still lock the wheels up whenever you want, but compared to the deep and accessible power in something like a Maven, they're really not that extreme. I personally prefer the feel and power curve of brakes like the SRAM Maven or TRP EVO Pro, but have been getting along with the XTRs well - it just takes a few rides to acclimate. Photo: Sterling Lorence Ultimately, I think my feel on the new XTR 4-piston brakes is this: if they were to come on a bike - especially a shorter travel bike - I'd be more than happy to run them, but I wouldn't necessarily go out of my way to get a set to replace many of the excellent aftermarket options that exist now. Brands like TRP, Hayes, Magura, and SRAM are all currently producing excellent brakes, so the competition is fierce. Nothing feels quite the same as a Shimano though, so if that's your jam then these will please. Matt Beer's Ride Impressions There’s nothing overly radical about the changes in performance of the new XTR brakes on the trail. The lever and caliper have been redesigned, but the way they operate still has that familiar Shimano sense - if you liked Shimano brakes in the past, then you’ll also find these will suit your taste too. The most noticeable changes come at the lever, both in ergonomics and actuation. The lever pivot itself is now closer to the bar and makes the XTR, dare I say it, feel somewhat “SRAM-ish” in your grasp. In terms of the actuation, the power ramps up more gradually than the past model, keeping that double-swing action, but without turning on and off like a light switch. A redesigned bite point adjuster has been introduced, however, the amount it influences the level is marginal. Pros + More power in classic Shimano feel + Consistent bite point + Novel and comfortable ergonomics Cons - Not as powerful as many competing options - Touchy feel won't suit all riders - Fussy bleed and minimal adjustment Pinkbike's Take Shimano did a great job of refining their system to eliminate their most significant problems, without changing the fundamental feel of the brakes. If you like the precise and sharp character of previous Shimano models, you'll love these. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Shimano XTR 9220 Brakes - Consistency is Key
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, big-brake-test, reviews, brakes, shimano, shimano-xtr
article_text: Shimano XTR Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/3ZAg2UEZ Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 01:12 Shimano XTR 9220 XTR grey remains a special color in the bike world, representing some of the fancier components one can bolt to their bike. Despite the consistent growth in popularity of aftermarket brakes, Shimano's finest offerings still have a serious fanbase, one that loves the distinct feel their brakes offer. The most recent update to the XTR platform saw revisions across the board, but that distinct feel remains unchanged. XTR Details • Mineral oil • Adjustments: Reach, free stroke • 2 pad options • 2x15mm, 2x17mm pistons • Price: $670 USD (pair) • Brake weight: 294g (front) • Rotor weight: 171g (203mm) • shimano.com BUY AT: Jenson The bridged lever design has been brought in-line with the handlebar, catering towards internal routing and tidy aesthetics. The lever body has been reinvented to accommodate that new form, with a pull-link actuated master cylinder, a new lever blade shape, and nicely integrated adjustments. In order to eliminate the wandering bite point their previous brakes had become associated with, Shimano implemented a lower viscosity mineral oil, as well as an updated seal design in the caliper. Setup Shimano's bleed process remains fundamentally unchanged, despite the move to their new Low Viscosity mineral oil - patience and a funnel are still your keys to success. Doing a basic bleed is as easy as it gets, but getting a perfect job done is still critical to maximizing performance here. That's true of all brakes, but something about the Shimano system seems to show a bad bleed more readily than other models on test. Once set, you have two adjustments on tap to change the feel of the brakes. The Free Stroke screw now uses a hex key as opposed to a Phillips head, but still does very little to alter the feel of the brakes. The reach adjust is a far more meaningful change, and is now housed cleanly into the lever body. The recessed dial has strong detents that keep it in place, but it is a bit harder to adjust one-handed if you're so inclined. One of the more atypical details on the new XTRs is the upswept lever blade, which has a 5° slant to it. This is meant to better match an aggressive descending body position, and requires a bit of calibration when setting up your lever angle. On trail, the difference fades away quickly, and the brakes feel normal. Livability: These may just be the easiest Shimano brakes to live with yet, thanks to the revised architecture and fluid choice that doesn't require constant attention to mitigate the wandering bite point that has plagued their system. The pads rattle less than they did as well, so you can leave the mastic tape on the shelf. Feel Shimano's Servowave lever cam has a very distinct feel to it, and that remains largely unchanged on this new generation of brakes. A slight nose of resistance breaks you into the stroke, which then moves very freely until you hit the bite point. It takes some getting used to, but now that the brakes are consistent in that end point, you can start to intuit where the contact will happen. There is quite a bit of free stroke on the XTRs, which is a benefit or detriment depending on your preference. Unfortunately, the Free Stroke adjuster does very little to alter this, so any modification is more dependent on cheeky bleed tricks to change the feel. The new XTR lever body moves the lever pivot point closer to the bar, with the goal of having the lever sit parallel to the bar at the bite point. This feels true for the vast majority of setup preferences, though those who like a to-the-grip bite point will probably prefer the ergonomics of some of the other brakes on test. Shimano is well known for the sharp bite point their brakes have, and that's largely still the case here. I'd call the initial come-on a little less binary than the previous generation or the Saints, but they're still fairly snappy compared to some of the more linear feeling brakes in the group. Feel Ranking: Once you're past the initial point of resistance, the lever swings with very light action, all the way through the large dead band and into the bite point. Water Bottle Pull Test: 823 g pull force (7th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power There is a moderate increase in power over the previous generation XTR brake, but I wouldn't call it a highlight of the revised system. The silver stoppers have plenty of power, but even compared to the Saints they're noticeably less aggressive. Because the power comes on quite quickly, you can get to that stopping force without pulling into the brake too much. Pulling just a bit past that initial bite point yields full power, and while the raw sum of that power may be less than others here, we both found it quite usable on trail. The XTRs also fared mid-pack in the illustrious Honda Fit stop test, proving their worth. Power Ranking: If you're after the most powerful Shimano brake, your best bet is still the Saint. The new XTRs have a good sharp bite, but don't feel quite as deep-feeling as other brakes on test. Honda Fit Stop Test: 28.2 m / 92.5 ft (6th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. Pros + Consistent bite point + Distinct Shimano feel + Good ergonomics Cons - Large free stroke deadband - Distinct Shimano feel - Lack of adjustment beyond reach Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Shimano's XTR Drivetrain Finally Goes Wireless
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shimano XTR vs SRAM Transmission https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/4uCeSH9r Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 23:21 Watch here for our semi-scientific comparison of the new XTR and SRAM Transmission. It's been seven years since Shimano's last major drivetrain launch. The world has changed a lot in seven years, but the bike industry seems to move a little slower, so things are less dramatic in our little bubble. In that seven year period, Shimano's primary competitor released a wireless drivetrain, then completely reworked their wireless drivetrain, then released a cable-actuated version of the same thing. Some tertiary players even got in on the wireless trend, yet Shimano stuck to their slow and steady approach. At long last, XTR Di2 is here, with a few bells and whistles to differentiate it from the pack. As to how it stacks up against the chief competition, read on. XTR Di2 Details • 12-speed, 10-51 or 9-45 tooth cassette • Wirelessly actuated • Mounts to standard derailleur hanger • Compatible with other Shimano 12-speed components • Groupset weight: 1735g • Price: $2030 USD (cassette, derailleur, cranks, shifter, chain, battery, charger) • bike.shimano.com When it was fresh. Photo: Lear Miller Though many of the XTR 9200 drivetrain's small details have been updated and reinvented, the core function of the groupset remains the same. The shifting quality is nearly identical to the outgoing 9100 group, and Shimano has been sure to keep the new components compatible with some of the core elements of the old, namely the cassette and chain.
