The lack of shift cable is ultimately the star of the show here, so let's dig in. Derailleur Price: $665 USD / $899 CAD / $1040 AUS 417 grams (long cage w/ battery) 413 grams (short cage w/ battery) As you can see, the XTR derailleur mounts to a standard derailleur hanger - nothing new on that front. The team at Shimano figured that maintaining the derailleur as the strongest element of the system was the right move, as hangers are readily available and inexpensive, should you need to replace one. One major change to the architecture of the M9250 derailleur is the omission of a clutch, instead opting for a dual-spring stabilizer that maintains an even tension throughout the gear range. The primary logic here was to reduce the form factor of that main pivot point as much as possible, giving the derailleur more of a wedge-shaped leading edge to prevent catastrophic impacts. There are two variants of the RD-M9250 derailleur to touch on here: SGS (AKA Long Cage), and GS (AKA Short Cage). Each corresponds to a specific 12-speed cassette, and will cater to a different sort of rider. The Long Cage variant is more in line with what we're currently used to, with a range of 10-51 teeth on the cassette. Short Cage ranges from 9-45t, and will appeal to those who want a smaller form factor and lighter weight at the expense of some top-end ease. Not all rear suspension designs play well with tiny chainrings, so factor that into your decision if considering your gearing and choosing between the two options. Both M9250 derailleurs use a carbon cage, with enough overlap to protect the pulley wheels from any strikes. The pulleys themselves are solid wheels, without any holes for sticks or debris to get jammed in. Solid pulleys, carbon cage. Battery hatch. The battery is housed within the main parallelogram, and is accessed through a door on the bottom of that structure. Beyond the benefit of keeping the battery out of harm's way, there's also a potential upside in how much larger that parallelogram itself is, as the pivots are spaced further apart, and the links get a bit beefier. In addition to being a more robust body overall, the derailleur features an automatic impact recovery function , allowing the derailleur to absorb an impact from a rock, tree, or your buddy's boot, then return to gear without skipping a beat. I've only had this happen on trail once or twice, which is a testament to the small form factor more than anything - I'm certainly not doing an amazing job of avoiding obstacles. In both of those instances, the derailleur let out a little yelp, then zooped right back to gear, taking maybe half a crank to return to its happy place. Primary function button and battery indicator. Standard adjustments and battery bashguard. The XTR derailleur retains the standard B-tension and high / low limit adjustment screws that we're used to, so expect to be using those to get the crispest shifting out of your new drivetrain. As is typical of other drivetrains with the same adjustment, checking tension and spacing on these bolts is critical to maintaining performance. That battery is rated at 305mAh, with a quoted range of 210 miles of riding. I've found the range to be quite impressive, but more on that later. Shifter Price: $220 USD / $298 CAD / $378 AUS 98 grams The XTR Di2 shifter is an impressively complex thing, with multiple adjustments and functions available to the end user. Most critically, it feels and functions like a regular shifter, with lever position and tactile feedback perfectly mimicking the analog alternative. Both the upshift and downshift levers can be set to one or two click shifting, thanks to a cam mechanism driven by each arm. The first of the two clicks is very light action, with a firmer detent to push into the second. Should you prefer to only get one shifter per push, you can limit that physically on the shifter, or digitally via the Shimano app. Lever adjustment. Function button. The rubberized paddles themselves are very adjustable, with 15° of fore-aft sweep, and 15° of rotation in each paddle. By simply loosening a 3mm at the end of the lever, you can get things adjusted to your liking. On the face of the shifter is a third button, which can be programmed to perform a variety of functions. In its stock state (and most usefully) it functions as a trim adjust feature. By holding the button for 3 seconds, you toggle into trim mode, and can click the shifter in the direction in which you'd like to adjust the derailleur. Hold for another 3 seconds, and you're back to shifting function. In my experience on trail, 3 seconds is an annoyingly long time, especially compared to the instant nature of turning a barrel adjuster, but the one-handed operation is nice. My one real gripe here is the lack of a trigger shift function, like that found on most of Shimano's mechanical drivetrains. I've always liked the ability to toggle gears with my forefinger on climbs, as I tend to choke up on the bars a bit when pedaling hard. Crankset Price: $310 USD / $406 CAD / $530 AUS 599 grams (170mm length w/ chainring) Shimano has two cranksets available as part of the larger XTR groupset, with XC and Enduro variants aimed at different user and weight targets. I've been testing the latter: FC-M9220. With 160, 165, 170, and 175mm options available, these hollow-forged cranks should appeal to a wider range of riders than before. Enduro-specific crank spindle. The little XTR insignia on the tip of the crank is a nice touch. They use the same chainring mount as before, though the new 0mm offset chainrings are key if you're hoping to hit the 55mm chainline target. Using an old 3mm offset ring will give you a 52mm chainline, where a new ring on an old M9100 or M9120 crank will give you 55mm. The M9250 crank uses a standard tension bolt and pinch clamp, like just about every other Shimano crank you've seen, save for last generation's XTR model. Chain & Cassette Cassette price: $500 USD / $675 CAD / $860 AUS 368 grams (10-51t) 323 grams (9-45t) Chain price: $85 USD / $101 CAD / $100 AUS 242-267 grams (depending on length) XTR Di2 uses the exact same chain as the previous XTR 12-speed group, and features a nearly identical cassette. The latter has a few updated details, with a lighter weight, stronger gears, and improved rivet structure, but the previous cassette will provide the same shifting experience. This little 9t cog and lockring combo is a fantastic bit of manufacturing engineering. And the corresponding tool is simple and high quality. Where the 10-51t cassette uses a standard cassette wrench for installation and removal, the 9-45t requires a new little tool to get the job done. This is critical to getting that very small 9-tooth cog to nest in the standard cassette layout, without adding more weight or complication. In my experience, it's been very easy to work on and live with. Installation & Settings The installation process for XTR Di2 is identical to the outgoing drivetrain, with a standard hanger, chain sizing procedure, and derailleur adjustments. Like the previous XTR 12-speed, the wireless generation is sensitive to setup, and benefits from a careful process. There are still engraved markings for checking B-tension, and the standard shop methods for fiddling in all the finer details remain true even when there's not a cable in play. That lack of a cable makes for an unsurprisingly more digital process, and in that realm Shimano has done an fine job of adding the XTR drivetrain to their E-Tube app. Despite the funny name and sometimes clunky interface, that app has yet to fail me when it comes to pairing to the drivetrain and toggling settings. I'm thankful for this, as the non-app pairing method (which involves holding a few buttons on the shifter and derailleur in sequence) has been spotty at best in my experience. Speaking of settings, you'll be able to change shifter orientation, alter the 3rd button function, and toggle multi-shift in the app. That multi-shift function even has 5 speeds to choose from, dictating how quickly the derailleur zips up or down the cassette when you hold the corresponding shift lever. I've set mine to dual-click only, with no multi-shift enabled, but to each their own. Photo: Sterling Lorence On the Trail If you've spent any time with the cable-actuated 12-speed Shimano XTR group, then you have a pretty clear sense of how the wireless version works. The shift quality is nearly identical, with a light and smooth quality under all but the highest pedaling loads. Speed of shifting seems to be the priority here, especially over multiple sequential shifts, dancing up the cassette as quickly as you request a shift. In those circumstances, I wouldn't say the shifting is seamless, but the cassette and chain are holding onto each other well enough to get to your desired gear as quickly as possible. In comparison to the current category leader in wireless drivetrains - SRAM's AXS Transmission - the speed differential is massive. Transmission takes its time getting up and down the cassette, but whether that's a problem or not really depends on the end user. Personally, I've never taken issue with the speed of Transmission's shifting, and have come to appreciate just how methodically and smoothly it drops into gear with each click. XTR is much faster, but doesn't feel as solid with each gear change, especially under very high pedaling load, such as when muscling up a technical climb or while riding an eMTB. Speed aside, the tactile quality of Shimano's new wireless shifter is superb, with light action and intuitive ergonomics that can easily be adjusted to suit your use case. It may look fairly standard, but the attention to detail here is impressive. I've found that as the miles racked up on the drivetrain, I was using the trim function quite a bit to keep the gearing perfect, as well as checking B-tension with some frequency. This isn't dissimilar to cable-actuated XTR, but does seem to be a fussier service item as you can't give the shifter that extra nudge to get into gear if things aren't syncing up perfectly - the downside of the digital. You can almost hear it. Photo: Sterling Lorence Quick shifts on quick trails. Photo: Lear Miller Shimano's decision to move from a clutch mechanism to the new dual spring design is a bit surprising, and while I can appreciate the longevity the springs are likely to bring to the table, the performance on trail is lacking. Chain slap is pronounced on this new derailleur, and while that's a bike-specific issue to some degree, the issue is still worth mentioning in comparison to other options out there. Deep compressions, harsh landings, and rough trails all elicit quite a bit of movement out of the chain and cage, causing a racket and a noticeable increase in chain movement. Luckily, this is greatly reduced if not eliminated when running the Short Cage version of the drivetrain, as the smaller system seems to generate less slack in general. As someone that has had issues with clutch mechanisms dying prematurely due to foul winter conditions, I can appreciate the simplicity of the spring. But if noise and chain flail are likely to bother you, I'd recommend opting for the 9-45t drivetrain over the 10-51t, or installing something like an O-Chain or STFU chain damper. Photo: Lear Miller With a bit less than 500 miles on the 10-51t drivetrain, it still feels too early to talk about extra long-term durability, though I have been impressed so far. The wedge-shaped derailleur stays out of the way, with only a few knocks to report. Those yielded no meaningful damage or change in shift quality, despite some scuffing on the plastic skid plates. The cassette has some shift scarring, but hasn't developed any rough shift spots or skipping gears. Like the outgoing 12-speed Shimano cassettes, the new one is prone to creaking if not kept clean and installed with just the right amount of grease. In most cases, this is a fixable issue, but on occasion they require a bit more attention than I'd like. I'll touch briefly on battery life, as its been quite impressive so far. Shimano programmed the XTR drivetrain to stay asleep until awoken by a click of the shifter, so the derailleur draws no battery while being transported on a vehicle, moved around the shop, or while sitting alone in a corner for a few weeks. As a result, the drivetrain has a noticeably longer battery life than AXS Transmission, in our experience so far. Finally, I want to touch on the Short Cage 9-45t drivetrain, or as I've been referring to it, the Small Block. I don't think this smaller gearing will be for everybody everywhere, but if your fitness and/or terrain allows it, I'd strongly consider this option. I've been running it on enduro and trail bikes alike, and have found the gearing acceptable for the long steep climbs we have in the Northwest. The major upside is the smaller form factor, which is far more clear of obstacles than longer-caged alternatives. The additional benefit of tighter shift spacing and reduced chainslap make for a pretty compelling option. Pros + Very fast shifting + Customizable and tactile shifter + 9-45t option is excellent for the right rider + Slimmer front profile avoids impacts & has sacrificial derailleur hanger Cons - Shifting under power not as smooth as competition - Pronounced chain slap - Still has a derailleur hanger Pinkbike's Take Shimano's new XTR Di2 drivetrain does a great job of bringing their 12-speed shifting performance into the wireless age, with a few compromises made to reach their goals. The overall speed and tactile experience will win some over, but the lack of composure in rough terrain is a sore point. For those looking to shift under maximum load, Transmission or Shimano's own Linkglide will be better options, but the quick shifting and variety of build kit options with XTR Di2 is worth considering. — Dario DiGiulio Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Drivetrain Shimano Shimano Xtr Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Sidi Dominator X Shoes - A Tougher Take on XC Footwear
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Sidi Dominator X Shoe Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/wsd0H8qB Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:23 I’ll admit, I’ve avoided XC shoes because they are impractical when you're not clipped in. A lot of my time spent on short-travel bikes takes me on far-reaching, multi-use trails where hiking is inevitable. Sidi offered up the Dominator X in the spring, just as I was getting back in the saddle after knee surgery. Since I was confined to gravel and tame trails, it sounded like the perfect time to review this type of shoe. According to Sidi though, the Dominator X are built for more than that - they're rugged, yet relaxed enough for long days in the saddle. Compared to their other race-oriented shoes, the carbon shank is more flexible and the volume inside is roomier. Two robust Nuun reels tighten up the Dominator X. They're a tad more clunky than Boa Fit System dials, however, it is possible to adjust them from the saddle. The sides of the shoe are hard-wearing, and the tread underneath is typical of a slim XC shoe. Dominator X Details • Dual Nuun quick-release ratchet dials • X3CC carbon composite sole • Stiffness index: 8 (scale of 5-12) • 2-year direct warranty • Sizes: 36-48 (half sizes in 37.5-46.5) • Weight: 759g (per pair, size 42) • Four colors: Deep Forest, Ice White, Black, Sand (pictured) • Price: $365 USD • sidi.com Pinkbike's Take A lot of XC shoes skimp on durability in favor of weight savings and breathability. Sidi used hard-wearing materials on the Dominator X, which still minimizes bulk without giving up much breathability or comfort. Wide-footed folks will appreciate the Dominator X's wide and flexible sidewall and stiff feel underfoot. My only letdowns with the Dominator X were that I couldn't move the cleats rearward enough, and that they lacked traction off the bike, especially on hard surfaces. — Matt Beer The Nuun dials can be tightened when the latch is pointing perpendicular to the shoe. The latch flips down to lock. To release the tension, you have to lift the latch, then pull up on the laces while pushing the button on the underside of the dial. Price At $369 USD, the Dominator X slots into a unique price point that I certainly wouldn’t call inexpensive, but they're also not the most expensive option out there. They sit between popular XC shoes from Shimano and Specialized. Shimano’s XC703 costs $260, while the next jump up is the mighty Sphyre shoe at $480. It’s the same story over at Specialized, where the sport category Recon 3.0 costs $279, and the S-Works Recon goes for $499. Shimano offers a one-year warranty on its shoes, whereas Sidi and Specialized boast two-year coverage against manufacturer defects. Sidi uses its own Nuun dial retention system with a polyethylene cable, which they call Tractis. Shimano and Specialized both license the Boa Fit System for the four models listed above. How that influences price, I'm not entirely sure, but it's always intriguing to see various approaches to a problem. Specialized S-Works Recon: $499 USD Shimano S-phyre SH-XC903: $499 USD Fit Two things stood out to me immediately when I slipped on the Dominator X: the arch support is moderately high, or higher than most gravity shoes I’ve worn, and the width around the toe box is more spacious than previous XC shoes I’ve tried out. Once the shoes broke in and my feet adapted to the high arch, they became softer and more comfortable. If I were spending the majority of my ride time in stiff XC shoes, I’d invest in a custom insole, though. In relation to the length, I found the size 42 to fit true to size with no other concerns to mention. The Dominator X paired well with Time Speciale 12 pedals. Performance These shoes haven’t been subjected to any hike-a-bike missions or torturous conditions, but so far, the construction is holding up well for this type of shoe, especially the aluminum Nuun dials. They lock down the release button, so if you do bump the dial while riding, the tension won’t back off. When I have hit a Boa dial off of an object, they’ve popped off, losing tension, but never broken. The toe cap construction is another area that’s built tough. The seams are covered in a hard-wearing TPU, and the firm toe is protective. The molded lugs are holding up well, and the replaceable toe spikes haven’t deteriorated too quickly, but holy hell, how are you supposed to walk anywhere without carpet in these shoes? The Dominator Xs don’t forgive misplaced clip-in attempts, nor do most shoes in this category, save for the S-Works Recon Evo and its small rubber pad. What bothered me more about the sole was the range of cleat adjustment. It is very forward-biased. Who pedals on their toes, or descends for that matter? Sidi claimed that they moved the cleat box further rearward from past models, but more range wouldn't hurt. Even for non-gravity-focused riding, I would prefer the cleats to sit further back. When I first looked at the short tongue, I was worried about the upper strap digging into my ankle tendon. For the most part, this wasn’t a concern, unless the strap was absolutely cranked down for max sprint efforts. I expected my foot to pull up and out of the heel cup when hiking uphill due to the relatively stiff sole, but the Dominator X’s hold is sufficient. The Dominator X is ranked at a stiffness level of 8, on a scale of 5-12. Go figure. Stiffness Sidi ranks the Dominator X with a stiffness of 8 on a scale of 5-12. I’m not sure I follow the logic behind that scale. Anyhow, that puts them right in the middle of the range, with many of their high-performance shoes nearing 11 or 12. I’d label the carbon shank under the shoe as plenty stiff. Almost more critical is how the stiffness transfers to the sides of the shoes. In that zone, the support changes from stiff to soft quickly. That could be described as too soft for some riders who are used to a tighter fit or more cohesive support. Sidi does stipulate that the Dominator X sits at a “7” rating on the recreational-to-performance scale (this time out of ten). Breathability I received these shoes in late winter and appreciated the extra space for thicker wool socks because air flows through these easily. Now that summertime temperatures are turning up, I'd rate them as moderately vented. Pros + Wide size range, including half sizes. + Wider fit may appeal to riders struggling to find other options in this category. + Nuun dials and outsole are made from robust materials. Cons - The most rearward cleat position may not be enough for some riders. - Traction off the bike is subpar. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Sidi Sidi Dominator X Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Silt's $400 Enduro Alloy Wheelset
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: Silt Enduro Wheel Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/fn96fdZp Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 07:12 In 2021, one of the first things I reviewed for Pinkbike was a set of Silt aluminum wheels. The brand was in its infancy at this stage, and while it would be a stretch to suggest it's a household name, it's grown in the intervening period, has increased its range, and even sponsored World Cup enduro and downhill riders. Silt does make carbon wheels, but the Enduro model I have on review is an aluminum rim. The hubs feature the same single disc star ratchet in a design which could be said to have drawn inspiration from DT Swiss' past successes with a similar product. Silt Enduro Details • Size: 29/MX • Intended use: Enduro • Rim dimension: 30mm inner, 35mm outer width • Hubs: Ratchet Drive System • Sapim D-Light J-bend Spokes • Weight: 2028 grams • MSRP: $400 US / $640 CAD • More info: siltmtb.com Technical Features This Enduro model builds on what made those wheels solid: easily serviced hubs with readily available parts, a strong alloy rim, and what I consider to be middling to decent value. They've also added a few new features. Firstly, the front now uses a two-cross pattern. Whether a wheel uses a radial, two-cross, or three-cross pattern will have an impact on how that wheel feels. For instance, the fewer crosses that a wheel has, the shorter the spokes will be used. This means that it can be, theoretically, stiffer. That said, the spokes on the third brace push against each other further away from the center of the wheel. This means that spoke tension isn't just about the individual spoke but how it works with or pushes against the other spokes on the wheel. To say that shorter spokes require less tension and, therefore, can result in a stiffer wheel could be true, but I have found in my experience that a two-cross wheel can be more compliant because it handles twisting forces less effectively. While we might imagine all impacts coming head-on to the rim, it's how the impact is received at the axle that affects the feeling in our body and hands. Anecdotally, because of this, I have found that three-cross wheels can be run at slightly lower than prescribed tension, meaning you can get something that does give compliance without also feeling washy through turns. Some, of course, will quote Young's Modulus and say that spoke tension doesn't affect ride quality. This is something I disagree with. When changing spoke tension, it's not the tension at the spoke we're concerned with but rather the preload on the rim. A rim will want to change shape any number of times through impacts, and it's this that preload affects, and subsequently, how comfortable or compliant a wheel is. The wheels are available with different ratchets, offering either 6.6 or 10 degrees of engagement. The asymmetric 6069 welded aluminum rim has a 30mm inner width and ramped rim bed that makes for particularly easy installation and removal of tires. The hubs use TPI bearings and a 6-bolt disc interface. The bearings have an full contact outer but non-contact inner seal to help them roll with less resistance. Sapim spokes and brass lock nipples are used throughout. A nice touch from Silt is the three-year warranty with a free first crash replacement. Should your luck still have not come in, then a further 50% crash replacement for all future alloy models. Ride Impressions The Enduro wheels are a nicely finished product. Subtle logos, a low profile rim, and a matte finish tick all the boxes for me. I have ridden these wheels since the spring, and they've seen a full complement of PNW rain and dust in equal measure. They've held up well, with no complaints in terms of longevity or reliability. So far, so good. I didn't know too much about these wheels upon receiving them. I normally try and know as little as possible about a product outside of how to set it up properly, and when I fitted some tires and went riding on the Enduro wheels, it was definitely in that category. Besides, how different can another alloy wheelset really be? When riding these wheels, something becomes very apparent. They're a lot more comfortable than the brand's previous efforts. On the tighter Squamish trails gave decent tracking and a lot of comfort. However, when really putting the bike through its paces, it started to feel quite vague, and it was hard to know exactly where they were underneath you. After going to the bike park, spoke key in hand, I ended up adding tension. I started focussing on the rear. At quarter turns, I eventually went up a total of half a turn to take the tension from around 20 to 25 KGF. This really helped through faster, higher-paced sections or squaring off turns. Still, though, the front didn't feel as direct as I would have liked. Again, at quarter turns, I experimented and, again, added a total of half a turn, taking the tension from 15 to 20 on the front. While very much in a window of acceptability, the front still did feel on the softer side. That said, it was comfortable and offered great tracking. On back-to-back runs, you do notice it, but when hopping on the bike or going for a ride, it is something you quickly get used to. The wheels stood up well to abuse. When a wheel is more compliant, I find it hard to really smash metal-on-rock and bottom out the tire. That was definitely the case here. That said, when they were hit, they stood up well and came out of the test relatively unscathed. I can see why Silt has gone down this road, and these wheels are markedly better rounded, if you'll excuse the pun, than the previous version. That said, to unlock their true performance, it is worth experimenting with tension. Pros + Compliant and comfortable + Subtle looks + Great warranty + Good value Cons - Not particuarly light, but within range - Soft with stock tension Pinkbike's Take The Silt Enduro Wheels offer a lot of what riders want - value, service features, reliability, and a product without any unwarranted fluff or hubris. After experimenting with spoke tension you can get these wheels working really very well, although they will still suit riders that prefer a wheel skewed more towards compliance than a direct feeling stiffer wheel. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Wheels Silt Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: Smith's Payroll Helmet Has Koroyd & A Crash Sensor
author: Matt Beer
article_text: When you take a look at MTB products around these days, it seems like every new-fangled piece of equipment is slapped with an e-bike rating. Smith’s new Payroll helmet might be guilty of that, but when you consider the higher average speed you travel on those bikes, more dome protection isn’t out of line. The Payroll is packed with safety features like the Aleck Crash Detection Sensor, a MIPS liner and that honeycomb-like Koroyd material to absorb impacts. And no, your eyes aren’t playing tricks on you - the Payroll is larger in volume than the other open-face helmets in Smith’s catalogue. Payroll Details • Aleck Crash Sensor • Koroyd and MIPS technologies • VaporFit retention adjustment dial • Eyewear storage • 19 vents • 6 colors • Weight: 414g (size M - verified) • Sizes: S, M, L, XL (51-65cm) • Certifications: CPSC, CE EN 1078, NTA8776 E-Bike and AS/NZS 2063 • MSRP: $220 USD • www.smithoptics.com All of that does bring the weight and price of the Payroll up above other mid-tier helmets. At 414g and $220 USD ($200 without Aleck Crash Detection Sensor) though, it’s still more affordable than the Forefront 2 (380g, $270 w/sensor), Smith’s premier MTB open-face helmet. Fit The Payroll comes in four sizes which span a huge range of heads, from 51cm in circumference, all the way up to 65cm. Based on the large outer volume and generous coverage around the lower occipital region of the skull, the Payroll offers a very secure fit, more secure than most other open-face helmets I’ve tried. The medium fit (55-59cm) might best fit towards the larger end of that range, but there’s ample adjustment on the VaporFit dial to snug it up. That can be done with just a twist of 270 degrees from fully open, plus the height has three positions too. Shape-wise, it’s closer to a round-fit, much like a Troy Lee Designs A3, compared to the mildly-oval fit of the Fox Speedframe. Inside, an Ionic+ antimicrobial lining placed on the MIPS liner cushions the fit and absorbs sweat well while offering enough airflow throughout. Those two layers give a buffer against the sharp Koroyd energy dissipating material. The Payroll's fit around the ears is adjustable, and the strap itself has a tidy finish with a rubber grommet, plus it’s also a reasonable length so you won’t get whipped in the ears by any extra material. The three-position visor can hold goggles when tipped all the way up or glasses can tuck into the two vents on the inside corners. Function In the age of apps, this one serves a valid purpose, especially while on solo rides. A mobile network is needed for any communications to be sent out to emergency contacts, however, it is possible to track the device's location while in service and the app is activated. The Aleck Crash Detection Sensor is simple to set up, charges on a USB C cable, and pairs like most Bluetooth devices. Following the setup instructions is straightforward and a simulated crash prompt completes the pairing. Thankfully, I didn’t put it through a real life scenario. Comfort and security are the mainstays of the Payroll, especially considering the size of the helmet. I did notice during heavy landings that the mass of the helmet can cause the MIPS liner to wiggle more than expected. That’s something that I could get used to though. Most importantly, it doesn’t make any squeaking noises. One preconceived notion I had before trying the Payroll was that it might not vent that well due to the Koroyd material, but that’s not the case. There’s actually great air flow through the top of the helmet, possibly because the Koroyd material only covers the ¾ side area, and the Ionic+ padding absorbs away a ton of sweat. Given all of the adjustments on the Payroll, none of them were tricky or fragile to reposition. The visor screws and chinstrap stayed tight, which isn’t always the case. In other areas concerning longevity, the EPS foam is capped by the integrated outer shell and the velcro sticky tabs are still holding the liner in place firmly. Smith Payroll - $200 USD ($220 w/Aleck Crash Detection Sensor) Specialized Tactic II - $110 USD (plus $29 for ANGi sensor) Compare Although the Payroll hasn’t undergone the widely referenced Virginia Tech helmet impact test yet, the EPS and Koroyd materials have been used and proven by Smith for ten years now. One helmet that has and ranks highly on that list (#4) is the Specialized Tactic II . Funny enough, this is Specialized’s budget-friendly helmet and costs $110 USD. The Tactic II can hold eyewear, but uses a fixed visor. It doesn’t have a radially adjustable chin strap and the sweat liner didn’t last as long as we’d hoped. In terms of safety features, the Tactic II is equipped with a MIPS liner and can hold an ANGi Sensor, Specialized’s equivalent crash detection sensor. An ANGi sensor costs just $29 and can be fitted to any helmet with its sticky foam tab. It isn’t rechargeable though and instead takes a CR2032 battery. Pros + Excellent coverage + Secure eyewear storage + Great venting/sweat management Cons - Higher volume shell is more susceptible to sliding around on MIPS liner - Competitors offer similar protection features for a lower cost Pinkbike's Take Smith has rolled out a comfortable new helmet with abundant protection and venting that's on par with most other helmets. The smart technology which can detect a crash is reassuring for people who often ride alone. Aside from the cost, which is on the higher side, the only downside we see is that the shell can move from an occasional heavy landing and may feel a tad strange at first. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets Smith Smith Payroll Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Some Pleasant Apparel Surprises From YT & Enve
author: Dario DiGiulio
product_name: Some Pleasant Apparel Surprises From YT &
article_text: We get a lot of apparel to test, but it's somewhat rare that a piece of clothing really changes your ride experience. I often err a bit too far on the run what ya brung spectrum, but recently a few items have made me realize how good some riding apparel can actually be. These are a few of those pleasant surprises, with more in store. For sizing reference, I'm 6'3", around 180 lbs., and look like one of the haunting War Boys from Mad Max. YT Gravity Pant YT is probably far from the first name to come to mind when you're thinking about riding apparel companies, but let it be known that you should bump them up a few places on that mental list. These are my new favorite riding pants, especially for rides that include more descending than pedaling. The Cordura fabric has been smartly specced to feature thicker durable patches where needed, and thin perforated breathable swaths where you're more likely to heat up. The three pockets are well placed, easy to open and close, and just big enough for the essentials without being silly. Gravity Pants • Cordura ripstop fabric • 3 zip pockets • Black colorway • Sizes: 28"-38", 32" tested • Quick-adjust ratchet closure • Hook and loop waist adjuster • MSRP: $129 USD • yt-industries.com The 2/3 leg zip seems odd, but is actually very useful for getting pads on, dumping heat on climbs, and doing some cool MC Hammer dances in the lift line. Zips up all the way to the quad, so you can put on your pads without stripping in woods. You can also tuck the lower half up into this funny lump. These are the best. Use these. For fine-tuning. Most of the pants I've tried recently have moved towards a ridiculously tight racer-y fit, which in addition to looking goofy just isn't that comfortable for pedaling and moving around on the bike. In order to get something to fit my 32" waist, everything below that belt line ends up being tighter than I want, especially in the quad/thigh area. These YT pants fit consistently the whole way down, with enough room to move and provide airflow. Big fan. It's also worth noting that they make an excellent Trail version of the pant, that uses a lighter weight fabric and simpler construction. Retail on those is $10 less, and they're the option I'd reach for if I were going out for a long pedal, or wanted some extra coverage for just trail riding or zipping around in the woods. The fit is similarly excellent, and the fabric breathes impressively well. Here's a blurry photo for reference. Enve Composite Long Sleeve Jersey Another company fairly new to the clothing world, Enve has released a surprisingly deep catalog of apparel and accessories to flesh out their otherwise resin-impregnated fiber lineup. Luckily, this jersey is a bit less stiff than a set of M7 bars, and has proven to be very comfortable for a wide weather window. It's not wool, but has a similar ease of use in terms of temperature - I've been happy in this when it's chilly just the same as when pedaling in the heat. Stink is somewhat helped by the Ionic+ side panels, which help extend the stank range a bit further than typical plastic jerseys. Composite Long Sleeve • 83% poly / 10% lyocell / 4% Ionic+ / 3% spandex • 3 colors • Sizes: S-XXL, L tested • Ionic+ anti-bacterial panels • Reinforced elbow and drop tail • MSRP: $90 USD • enve.com Comes in two colors other than invisible. The reinforced areas are nice for avoiding brush snags, and should hold up better than the stretchy fabric in a crash. I'm a fan of the cut, as it's loose enough to look like a good ol' shirt without feeling baggy. The fabric does a great job of keeping you dry and cool, which has a double benefit when it's drizzly out - remember: cotton kills. Enve Trail Pant It feels like pants are really taking over our previously shorts-heavy sport, and I think it's items like this that are to blame. Pants that are too damn comfortable are just hard to say no to, even when the temperature climbs as much as you do. While I still rock shorts on most hot days, these Trail Pants from Enve are a solid ankle-long option, with a light and stretchy fabric and well-placed ventilation. The side pockets are excellent and very easy to use while riding, and the metal snap closure is operable with gloves on. Trail Pants • 83% nylon / 17% spandex • 2 zip pockets • Black colorway • Sizes: S-XXL, M tested • Metal snap closure • Drawstring waist adjuster • MSRP: $175 USD • enve.com The drawstring tie waist adjuster seems unnecessary to me, so I just don't use it. If you're more in-between sizes, then it'll certainly help bring a larger size down to fit more snugly. Stretch cuffs are silly, but at least these actually work. Easy to open while riding. Big enough for snacks, tools, gloves. While the leg does taper down quite a bit, and they do have a cuff, the Enve pants fit very nicely and don't feel excessively tight. I prefer cuffless pants, just for ease of use and looks, but these have enough stretch to get on and off easily, and don't ride up your leg as you move around on the bike. For the price, I'd probably choose the YT Trail pants, but these are a very good option. If you want to show off your bulging calves, cool Ironman tattoo, or ankle socks, then you can get all three of these pants in a shorts fit. I've tried all three of those too and my feelings are about the same - just breezier. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Apparel ENVE YT Industries Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Sour Bicycles Double Choc - Exotic Normal
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, trail-bikes, sour-bicycles, sour-double-choc
article_text: Sour is a small brand with a growing presence in the mountain bike world, thanks to their diverse lineup and practical approach to each model they make. Whether it's XC or gravity, the Sour bikes have a nice balance of form and function, with the Double Choc representing their take on the quintessential all-mountain rig. With 148mm of rear travel and a 160mm fork, the Double Choc aims to tackle gnarly descents without taking too much away from the mellower end of the spectrum. As to whether it succeeded in that goal, read on. First, some details about what makes this bike unique. Double Choc Details • Travel: 148mm / 160mm fork • 29" wheels • Steel and aluminum frame • 64.5° head angle, 77° seat angle • 470-526mm reach, 634-656mm stack • 438-444mm chainstay length • Price: €3499 (frame only) • Frame weight: 3.8 kg (w/ hardware, no shock) • sour.bike The Double Choc is currently available as a frame only, with the option to add some components to the basket when ordering. Shocks, forks, headsets, and dropper posts are available from Sour, with a few options in each category. Like many smaller boutique offerings, the rest is up to you, so I did my best to put the Double Choc together with a build that felt like the norm for a bike of this sort. Mostly standard parts I'm used to, with a few choice items thrown in for flair. Sour Double Choc Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/BQvvCsJQ Copied Live 00:00 12:06 12:06 Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take The Sour is a bit hard to characterize here, as it does a pretty solid job everywhere - it's fun on flow trails, capable enough on challenging tech, and has an intuitive ride quality to it. I've ridden tons of bikes in this travel category, and would split them between those that punch above their travel numbers, and those that happily occupy that middle ground where a bit of everything is the ultimate goal. The Double Choc belongs to that second group, with plenty of comfort to tone down gnarly trails, but not quite as composed a feel as others. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details The Sour's simple steel silhouette hides a few nice details that make it easy to live with, and simple to work on. 3D-printed bolted cable routing makes any brake or drivetrain work a breeze, and allows for simple replacement should something break. The files are available directly from Sour, should you want to print your own. The steel front triangle is welded in Germany by the Sour team, but there's a quiet collaboration at play on the back end of the bike. The heavily-machined seatstay is manufactured by ActoFive, the German manufacturer known for their own fully CNC milled frames. This manufacturing method allowed Sour to keep the key tolerances tight on the rear linkage, and gives the bike a distinct look. You'll note that the build shown here has some ActoFive cranks as well, which tie together with the rest of the frame elements beautifully. As a package, the Double Choc is a pleasantly simple thing. There is a flip chip at the lower shock mount, altering bottom bracket height, head angle, and seat angle - as always, it's worth experimenting with both options before settling on one. The frame's hardware is robust and easy to access, with multitool-friendly interfaces. The one unusual element on the frame is the seatpost collar, which had a hard time keeping the 31.6mm post in place. I swapped the stock collar out for a Paul Components quick release collar, which alleviated any slippage. For those curious, the frame weighs 3.8 kilos, sans shock but including all the hardware. ActoFive rear end. Bolt-together rocker link. One of the best-looking headtubes in the game. Geometry in High mode. Geometry There are three sizes of Double Choc available, with the geometry decidedly skewed towards the tallest end of the spectrum. Like some other brands are want to do, the t-shirt sizing is essentially shifted a full size over, with the Medium's fit figures aligning more closely with a typical Large. I rode a size Large, which fits much like the standard XL. In the name of manufacturing efficiency, different frame sizes do share some common figures, with the 110mm headtube bridging M and L, and the 444mm chainstay length present on both L and XL. This isn't unheard of for smaller manufacturers looking to streamline the build process, though it may yield some compromise here or there. The geometry is adjustable, via a flip chip at the lower shock mount, with the Low position yielding a 0.3° slacker headtube angle, 0.47° slacker seattube angle, and 5mm lower bottom bracket. After some back and forth experimentation, I landed on the Low mode being the better fit for my use case. An interesting an somewhat frustrating asterisk to the geometry section: after a few initial rides, it seemed to me that the bike I was riding had a much steeper seat angle than advertised. Some measurements confirmed my suspicions, as the bike I was sent had a 79° seat angle, not the standard 77°. The folks at Sour confirmed that this was a one-off frame meant to experiment with some geometry concepts, and apologized for the confusion. I happened to like the steeper feel quite a bit, but it does alter my experience a bit when compared to the stock offering. Suspension Design The Double Choc's Horst link layout delivers 145mm of rear wheel travel when using a 210x55mm shock, and can be reduced to 136mm travel with a 50mm stroke shock. I exclusively ran the bike in the longer-travel mode, as it seems to be the bike's primary purpose. Anti-squat is fairly high on the Sour, landing around 120% at sag. That contributes to a firm pedaling feel, but might take away some composure on rougher sections of the descent. Anti-rise is rather low, ranging from the high 30s to low 60% range, giving the bike an active feel under braking, but pitching things forward slightly as you get on the brakes. Test Bike Setup The Double Choc is currently only sold as a frame, so the build kit will ultimately be up to you. My review bike showed up with a complete build, but over the course of testing I found myself experimenting with a wide array of parts to try to find the bike's happy place. In the end, a standard build prevailed: strong brakes, aluminum wheels, DoubleDown tires, and either a Manitou Mezzer or Fox 36 fork. Testing Info Testing took place mainly in Bellingham, Washington , and the surrounding area in the wide range of trail and weather conditions that accompany the winter and spring in the Pacifc Northwest. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 84 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Climbing The Double Choc climbs quite well, with a firm feeling under power and enough grip to scrape up some truly silly sections of singletrack. That latter element came as a bit of a surprise, but kicks in under harder efforts where you're standing up and stamping on the pedals to clean steeper climbs. In general, the feel is even-keeled and not particularly rushed, though I think you could easily speed things up with some fast-rolling tires. As mentioned before, this review bike came with a steeper seat angle than is stock, so my feelings are somewhat altered as a result. I got along with the very steep seat angle quite well, as it suited the steep climbs in my neck of the woods, and my general preference for a more upright pedaling position. The stock position isn't anything to scoff at, landing in a more typical place at 77° - having ridden plenty of bikes with that same figure recently, I think it would be just fine, and possibly preferable for those with more undulating terrain. The Double Choc isn't a particularly light bike, but it carries its weight well, thanks to that supportive pedaling platform and easy handling. It's not overly slack up front, so picking a line up technical climbs feels easy and intuitive. Over long days in the saddle, I found the Sour to be comfortable and easygoing, just a good solid bike to put in the miles on without much fuss. Descending The Sour is a bit hard to characterize here, as it does a pretty solid job everywhere - it's fun on flow trails, capable enough on challenging tech, and has an intuitive ride quality to it. I've ridden tons of bikes in this travel category, and would split them between those that punch above their travel numbers, and those that happily occupy that middle ground where a bit of everything is the ultimate goal. The Double Choc belongs to that second group, with plenty of comfort to tone down gnarly trails, but not quite as composed a feel as others. For the majority of riding, I think the suspension performance and handling work nicely together to deliver a feel that lets you get away with a fair bit, but reminds you when you start approaching the limit. Other bikes in the 140-160mm realm are more willing to let you run a bit past that line and really push things, but the Sour felt like it started protesting a bit earlier. Part of that may be due to the suspension feel, which has a supportive feel that can get a bit overwhelmed in very rough and chopped up trail. It's more than enough for most riding, mind you, just less composed through the feet when you're really doing something you shouldn't. The frame feel falls into a similar category, with a nice level of flex that takes the edge off chatter, but can feel a bit wiggly when you're stuffing it into corners and compressions. Those looking to push the Double Choc a little harder might benefit from an O-Chain, e13 Sidekick, or DT Swiss DF hub, mostly to take away some of that anti-squat effect through the rough. You'll still benefit on the climbs, but a bit of drivetrain separation may go a long way for those heels-down repeated compression situations. Frame flex is one of those things that falls more into the personal preference realm, where some folks love the feel of a softer bike, and others find it unnerving. The Sour is far from the wiggliest bike I've ridden recently, but when compared to something very stiff - like a Hightower - it's noticeable. Sour Double Choc Trek Fuel EX How Does It Compare? There are many bikes in the do-everything mid travel category, making comparisons both easy and overwhelming. I've spent a ton of time on similarly-positioned frames like the Specialized Stumpjumper and Santa Cruz Hightower, and would characterize those as very good bikes for riding just about anything you might encounter, including some very gnarly terrain at high speeds. Where the Double Choc sits is more in the capable trail bike niche, alongside things like the Trek Fuel EX. Forgiving enough, but not necessarily encouraging you to do anything dumb, these bikes both offer nice handling and a level of comfort that tames harder sections of trail without taking the fun out of easier and less demanding trails. Outside those characteristics, the Trek and the Sour are obviously quite different, appealing to vastly different customers and offering their own USP. The Sour has that boutique and handmade appeal, where the Trek has a practicality and built-in adaptability that will cater to a wider audience. Both solid options, just depends on who you are. Technical Report SRAM GX Eagle Drivetrain: It's been a while since I've had a review bike come in with a SRAM drivetrain that isn't some sort of Transmission. Turns out, the OG 12-speed kit still works well, though I do love shifting under max load like a dummy. The weaker clutch was a good test of Sour's chainslap protection, which did a pretty good job of quieting things down. Though the newer and fancier drivetrains do offer some improved performance, it's always nice to have a reminder that the slightly out of date options are still quite good. Tire Choice: I ran all sorts of tires on this bike, starting with some Schwalbe Albert Trai-casing radials. I don't entirely trust the Albert as a front tire, as the grip falls more into the "okay until it's completely gone" category. The Trail casings felt too compliant for this bike, so I ended up opting for a High Roller III / DHRII combo, in the Double Down casing. Solid without being too heavy, and possibly the most predictable tire combo I've used recently. Pros + Adaptable platform, works well everywhere + Very easy to live with, nice build details + Fun on wide range of trails Cons - Capability ceiling feels lower than some other mid-travel bikes - Caught between trail bike efficiency and enduro composure - No small sizes in the range Action photos: Finnegan McDermott Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Trail Bikes Sour Bicycles Sour Double Choc Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Spank 359 Downhill Wheels - Alloy Wheels Turned up to 11
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: When recently reviewing some E13 LG1 Race wheels, one name came up in the comments several times - Spank. Yes, I think there is a leaning towards alloy wheels within the Pinkbike community, and not without fair reasoning, but the kudos going to these wheels felt too intriguing to ignore. I might be going against the grain here, and indeed the feelings of the very community that I'm trying to serve, but I do quite like carbon wheels. Not just for their feel, or any one particular ride characteristic, but rather because they don't ding or dent. Spank 359 Vibrocore Details • Wheel size tested: 29/27.5" • Intended use: Downhill • Rim dimension: 30.5mm width, 19mm profile • Hubs: Spank Hex 3.5° engagement • Weight: 2199g total (actual) • MSRP: $349.99 front / $479.99 rear • More info: spank.com In fact, in my experience, the only way to guarantee imminent wheel failure is to wax lyrical about how reliable they are on a podcast and then wait for wheel failure to strike at the most inopportune times. However, when weight isn't so much of an priority you can build them both strong and heavy. Unburdened by the restriction of gram hunting, I've enjoyed great success with carbon wheels on downhill bikes. After testing the E13 wheels, and having a really positive experience, going to the alloy Spank models I wanted to not only compare ride quality but also longevity. Because, so far, a carbon wheel that has had no breakages or issues is quite a hard prospect to find fault with. That said, it isn't particularly difficult to find fault with the price tag that could be nearing $2,000 USD. Much in the same way that carbon wheels were once novel, I think you could argue that Spank's approach is also novel and features some fresh takes. Firstly, these wheels feature Vibrocore. This is essentially a pressurized foam inside the rim. Why? Well, to reduce and deaden vibration. It might sound trivial, but Spank is all in on this tech. It works in a way similar to how you might try and reduce echo in a room by putting padding on the walls. Secondly, they have no interest in making carbon wheels. Thirdly, in the same way some notable bike brands from Taiwan also make frames for customer brands, Spank does the same for other wheel brands. Claims, specs, and making rims for the great and the good are one thing, but let's look at these wheels in particular. Design & Specs At the heart of these wheels are the 102T, 6-pawl 3.5-degree Engagement Hex rear hub. The engagement is fast, and the hubs sing out in a characterful ring. They're less of a dull clack and are more of a high-pitched clicking. When going to more teeth on a ratchet, it can be the case that these teeth have to become shallower by their design. This can then mean that there is a risk of slipping or intermittent drive. To get around this, the freehub uses 6 independent pawls with 3 steps per pawl. This means there are 18 engagement points per click, and this should safeguard against any slipping. The hubs, front and rear, are J-bend. That, coupled with alloy rims probably says something about these wheel's intentions and the core mountain bikers these are being aimed at. The hubs can go between 150 or 157 mm spacing and the endcaps to change between were supplied. The wheels have an easy-to-interpret rating system. This rating puts in simple terms what the wheels are able to withstand. According to Spank these wheels will work well for a rider of any weight under heavy impacts. In fact, kudos to Spank for a well-sorted website and copy. I know it seems small, but all the information is clear and concise. In recent years, mountain bike websites have started to feel like you have front-row tickets to a David Copperfield light show. The simple clarity of the Spank website was something of a tonic when looking up tech specs. The wheels on test were 32 hole rims, with triple-butted spokes and brass nipples. Should you choose, these wheels are available without the Vibrocore foam for $20 less. Spank puts a lot of the details you would want or need on the hubs or rims, such as something like ERD. However, I wouldn't be opposed to them marking flange diameter too while they're at it. Test Setup These wheels have been used over the peak months of summer riding in Whistler Bike Park. While it hasn't been wet, it's certainly been dry, dusty, and rough. The wheels have been mounted to Specialized's Cannibal tires in 2.4", or my favorite, the 2.4" Maxxis DHR2. The 29er Cannibal tire weighs 1397 grams. It's well-damped and incredibly supportive. My experience with the tire is that it can feel wooden at higher pressures but when getting down to 25 psi and lower it really comes alive. I run the same pressures in both front and rear. When on the Maxxis tires, I would run 26 psi front and rear in the 2.4" width downhill casing. I do tend to find the Maxxis sidewall slightly less supportive for all-out ability at withstanding compressions, but I also find it a slightly more malleable and better tracking casing at lower speeds. Both are good but, much like the compression damping in your suspension, more damping is better at high speed and less can feel like it offers more grip at lower ones. These wheels were tested on a Santa Cruz V10. I love this bike. During testing, the difference in feeling when doing back-to-back testing with the E13 carbon wheels on the same tires was stark (during back-to-back I used the DHR2). I tried to experiment to see if I could show this with a BYB Telemetry kit but sadly I couldn't extract anything conclusive. I was hoping to see a difference in axle speed on high-speed chatter but it was just too difficult to replicate, as well as control line choice and pressures to within tolerance. The bikes came pre-taped with valves installed. Tire installation was a breeze. On the Trail These wheels offer a muted and controlled ride. Ultimately, they're not that stiff but it wouldn't be right to call them flexy either. I would say they're very middle of the road in this regard. They are noticeably less stiff than the E13 wheels and lack that sharp, precise feeling that a carbon rim can give, but so too do they forgo that bite-back you can get with a stiff wheel. While I did notice the flex, I wouldn't say it was light-years ahead in terms of radial flex or comfort through the big chunder. If mountain biking was just taking straight-on hits that only require radial flex then I think the carbon wheels do a great job. The difference is when the wheels have to manage both direct and twisting forces at the same time. These wheels handle torsional load very well and have a great amount of flex for keeping momentum when taking off camber hits, like when you are at risk of getting pushed off line or coping with harsh braking bumps and setting up for a turn Is the Vibrocore a silver bullet in terms of comfort? Truthfully I can't discern that. They do offer comfort and I would say that these are more comfortable than the LG1 Race wheels in some instances, but it's not universal - and on square edges when the bike is lined up on its y-axis, any benefit is negligible or non-existent. Plus, all the gains in comfort are bought and paid for with what's given away in steering precision, compared to a stiffer carbon rim. What this wheelset excels at is granting the rider less deflection through choppy terrain. Does that come with less precision? Yes, especially in high-load turns that want to chew you up and spit you out. But if you're a rider who likes fast, techy trails and loves getting yourself into trouble on rough trails, and then getting yourself back out of it again, it's probably a worthwhile trade-off. The 359s are not so razor-sharp as some stiffer wheels, and in the right situation that's not a bad thing. Durability These wheels have been great at taking a beating. There have been square hits, side-glancing hits, front-and-rear-all-at-once hits, and both wheels still spin true. I did have to retension the rear once, but when I did so the bike was still very much rideable. With a tire fitted at 25 psi, the spokes had a deflection reading of around 15, as opposed to the 20-25 that it should read. (I tend to measure spoke flex with tires installed at pressure as the compressing force of the inflated tire can change spoke tension quite significantly). It was an easy-to-remedy problem though and I think in keeping with my expectations of a downhill wheel. When driving the bike into compressions through the apex of catches on Whistler downhill, one of my favorite test tracks, the front has felt like it has hit nothing but pure rock. The ability of this alloy wheel to shrug off this style of hits, the type that in my experience usually ends up with a dented or buckled rim, is very impressive. The hubs sometimes squeal, which is annoying. Quickly pulling them apart remedies it though. Pros + Strong and reliable + Blends torsional and radial stiffness well + Comfortable through chunder Cons - Not the most responsive to steering inputs in high-load turns - Intermittent squeal from hub seals Pinkbike's Take The 359s blend some interesting features with the style of wheel that your average mountain biker loves. A simple and effective product that represents what the right blend of design, clear language, and familiar technology can achieve, all at a price around half of what a carbon wheelset might set you back. Is the Vibrocore revolutionary? I'm not sure it is, but I also don't think it has to be - it's more about maximizing the package of an alloy wheelset than trying to fundamentally reestablish what an alloy rim can or should do. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Wheels Spank Industries Vibrocore Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: Roval Control Alloy 350 6B XC Wheels
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: Roval Control Alloy Wheels https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/T78H7cn0 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 05:43 Last winter, I decided to build up an all-alloy XC bike. I don't have any particular allegiance to aluminum parts, nor am I against carbon for some ecological reason. Quite simply, I had just bought a Commencal Tempo frame, and most of the parts I had or wanted weren't carbon, so when filling in the gaps, it seemed like a fun quirk to pursue. For me, I don't like carbon cranks or bars, and while partial to carbon wheels, I ultimately am open to either as long it rides well. Roval Control Alloy 350 6B Details • Size: 29" • Intended use: XC/Trail • 19mm depth, 31 external, 27mm internal width • Hubs: DT Swiss 350 36T • Sapim D-Light Straightpull Spokes • Weight: 1650 grams • MSRP: $800 • More info: specialized.com Roval say these wheels were born on the World Cup, with the development and engineering of their race-ready carbon rims bleeding into these metal ones. They say, however, that this wheelset is a "trail-ripping, best-of-the-best, aluminum wheel," which is no mean feat. That said, many downhill riders still prefer alloy, and it's great to see Roval cater to riders of a similar disposition on the other end of the gravity spectrum. Technical Features The wheels used individually customized sleeves to bond the rim join, which they claim is not only "wildly over-engineered" but also helps reduce weight and eliminate creaking. Specialized also uses a customized shot peening process to create a stronger wheel. This is done by blasting the surface with small pieces of metal. This process remodels the surface of the component to a degree and bids to remove any potential points of failure, such as small imperfections, from the manufacturing process. The wheels are available in 29-inch only and use 28 straight pull spokes front and rear. The rims are a 6013 alloy and feature an asymmetric profile. Roval say they're 20% more durable. Compared to what, I'm not sure but hey, it sounds impressive. The wheels use DT Swiss 350 hubs with a 36-tooth ratchet, giving 10 degrees of engagement. While their single-ratchet start might no longer be the brand's flagship tech, it's still a personal favorite, if only for its simplicity and, in my experience, reliability. The wheels have a rider weight limit of 275 lbs or 125kg. When you buy the wheels, you have 90 days to register them to activate a lifetime warranty for the original owner. At 1650 grams, these aren't particularly light when compared to the modern crop of carbon rims. That said, you can also get lighter hubs. The latest generation of cutting-edge wheels can come in at around 400 grams less, which is huge in terms of racing. However, I would argue that the $1700 difference in price, give or take, is also quite large. There are mid-tier carbon wheels, but they could still be nearly double to save around 100 grams per wheel. In that context, these don't look too wayward. Ride Impressions The wheels found themselves at home on my Tempo, which I'd underforked to steepen it up, added some thin XC tires, as well as a few other touches to make it into the singletrack ripper I wanted. Yes, the Tempo is a very capable bike, but I was looking for an XC bike with comfort, more than a trail bike that's a bit livelier, and built it up as such. The Control wheels fit in line with this character. They're comfortable and absolutely light enough. That said, they don't feel particularly sharp or snappy. When under power or acceleration they do feel a bit soft. Then, when going downhill, it's very easy to send a lot of flex through them. A combination of a not particularly stiff bike, these wheels, and thin tires made something that's a very good place to be for hours at a time, as well as being a bike where you can get the tire to touch the stays if you really want to. I was very happy, though, and for every moment where you'd want something a bit more direct feeling, there'll be another where you snake through some rocks and feel the compliant rim find a way without passing on that harshness to your hands or failing to track. This is my comfort-inducing and confidence-inspiring cruiser, and the wheels play a big role in that. Pros + Comfortable + Good if not exceptional value + Great warranty Cons - Not particularly light - Easy to flex Pinkbike's Take Are these wheels going to win a World Cup? Probably not. Were they in any danger of that with me at the helm? Absolutely not. These wheels are reliable, comfortable, and decent value, and for me, that's more important. While not the stiffest through turns, I think they strike a nice balance in terms of feel and give a good tire profile to boot. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Wheels Roval Roval Control Alloy Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: Specialized Dissident 2 Helmet - Downhill Safety at an Enduro Weight
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Specialized re-entered the downhill helmet game with the release of their premium full-face, the Dissident 2. Their top downhill and enduro riders and racers have been using the sub-1000g helmet for over a year now. We've also spent a decent amount of time in the Dissident 2 on all forms of bikes and can appreciate its manageable weight, for riding all day long, audibility, and venting. With only one premium carbon model at $500 USD, we expected the best, but there are a few quibbles that keep the Dissident from earning perfect marks. Specialized Dissident 2 Details • MIPS Evolve Solution system • Scooped top vent • Fixed visor w/camera surface • D-ring closure • Includes two sizes of quick release cheek pads • Weight: 940 g • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Colors: Black/Carbon, White/Silver • Price: $500 USD • Certifications: ASTM F1952-15 DH certified • specialized.com Goggle strap under, over, or on the bump at the back of the helmet shell? Placing the strap above, as seen here, can put an uneven amount of pressure on the goggles. That could vary depending on the brand of goggle and individual fit. Dealer's choice: Loic Bruni prefers to place his goggle strap on the bump. Finn opts for the above option. Two sets of cheek pads and one Ionic head liner are included with each shell size. FIT The Specialized Dissident DH helmet offers a personalized fit with two sets of cheek pads included, allowing riders to tailor the comfort level to their liking. However, it's important to note that the helmet has a shallow fit on the crown of the head, which might not suit everyone. Compared to a size medium Ambush 2 half shell from Specialized, the fit is tighter, although it does break in over time. One potential drawback is that the goggle straps sit at a high angle, which may not put enough pressure on the top half of the goggle frame. The chin bar construction is remarkably thin. SAFETY SPECIFICATIONS For top downhill riders, safety is a serious concern and there's no doubt that Specialized went all-in on constructing a helmet that provides confidence, however the criteria isn't as involved as the moto world. The Dissident 2 meets the ASTM F1952-15 DH certified level and uses a 3-layer construction consisting of a carbon and poly-carbonate shell with an expanded polystyrene (EPS) liner. Five individually tuned pieces of EPS foam are molded into shapes that are said to be optimized for their location inside the helmet. Tucked away in the liner is the Mips Evolve Solution liner to dissipate rotational energy. That's topped off by a D-ring closure on the strap and quick release cheek pads. Shiny carbon, matte black and chrome should appeal to all fashion styles. WEIGHT In terms of weight, sub 1000g for a DH-rated helmet is impressive. The overall volume of the helmet might have something to do with the weight objective - it's significantly smaller than the Fox RPC or Leatt Gravity 8.0, which weigh 1285 and 1148 grams, respectively. The size, shape and weight is most similar to 100 Percent's Aircraft 2, another helmet that can be worn for extended rides in hot weather and still meet DH-race criteria. There are only two small over-brow vents along the side of the opening. The top of the helmet is flat and doesn't provide the most surrounding, deep-feeling fit. Exhaust ports aplenty. . By unscrewing four T9 bolts, the two halves of the grill are removable. VENTILATION Ventilation is a critical factor, especially one of this weight which will encourage riders to wear them on more occasions. The Dissident 2 provides decent venting, similar to the TLD D4, making it suitable for extended rides in hot conditions. When compared to Fox RPC and Leatt Gravity 8.0 helmets, the Dissident draws in more air around your brow and temples. It also sits a touch higher around the back of your head to exhaust the hot air. It doesn't quite match the airflow of enduro full-face helmets like the Smith Mainline or Fox Proframe RS, which prioritize breathability. A flat area on the sturdy visor offers purchase to mount a POV camera. The pull tab on the D-rings is so short that it's rendered useless while wearing gloves. PRICE Unlike the previous Dissident model, only a carbon version is available now, and that comes at a premium price of $500. The helmet does arrive in a fleece-lined, premium helmet bag with the extra set of cheek pads, but no spare visor is included. For this amount of money, I'd expect that the liner would stay silent, especially given the MIPS Evolve Solution name. I didn't experience any issues with creaking, but Mike Kazimer has this to say about his size medium Dissident 2, "The Dissident is light and comfortable, but I found the creak of the Mips liner against the inner foam to be very noticeable, and annoying." Pros + Extremely lightweight + Venting outperforms other popular competitors Cons - Mixed experiences with creaking Mips liner - D-ring pull tab is very short - Expensive, no composite options Pinkbike's Take Specialized has constructed an uber-light (and comfortable) DH-rated full face helmet that can be worn for more than just flat-out racing, thanks to its incredibly low weight and ability to exhaust hot air. A couple small quirks, like the short D-ring pull tab and funky goggle strap angle can become more familiar with time, but the noisy Mips liner on some helmets is harder to overlook. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets Specialized Specialized Dissident Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Specialized Eliminator / Butcher Tires - Great Performance at a Reasonable Price
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, tires, specialized, specialized-butcher, specialized-eliminator
article_text: Specialized's tire game has gone through some high and lows over the years, with performance and quality changing as they dialed in their designs. As of late, they seem to have found center, offering a solid lineup with good tread patterns and high quality compounds. In the center of the XC-DH spectrum they offer, we have two tires squarely aimed at the trail market, the Butcher and the Eliminator. Comically aggressive names aside, these tires have been a consistent friend of mine, and offer solid performance that I'll get into below. The Eliminator and Butcher both come in Specialized's Grid Trail and Grid Gravity casings, giving you the simple choice between the two based on weight, durability, and ride feel. There are also options between T7 and T9 rubber, with the higher number indicating stickier rubber used in the construction. The Eliminator uses a mix of the two, optimizing for rolling resistance and grip, as it is a rear-specific tire. Specialized Eliminator • Compound: T7 (Trail casing), T7/T9 (Gravity casing) • Casing: 60 TPI (Trail), 2x60 TPI (Gravity) • Sizes: 29" or 27.5" x 2.3" or 2.6" • Weight: 975g (2.3 Trail), 1270g (2.3 Gravity) • Price: $50 - $75 • specialized.com Specialized Butcher • Compound: T7 or T9 • Casing: 60 TPI (Trail), 2x60 TPI (Gravity) • Sizes: 29" or 27.5" x 2.3" or 2.6" • Weight: 1060g (2.3 Trail), 1350g (2.3 Gravity) • Price: $50 - $75 • specialized.com Specialized Butcher / Eliminator Tire Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/QrWJIKkk Copied Live 00:00 05:01 05:01 Butcher. Eliminator. Butcher Tread The Butcher's tread pattern is a classic do-it-all, similar to the staggered 2-2 lug designs many brands offer. Specialized gave the tire a consistent edge, without any staggering to break up the leading edge as you lean things over. The separated center lugs let the Butcher dig in a bit more in corners than something like a Maxxis DHR II, which unifies those two to offer a stronger braking surface. While both work nicely as front and rear tires, the Butcher errs a bit more towards the front, thanks to that independent lug design. Eliminator Tread The Eliminator biases towards speed, while still offering some braking bite and a strong edge knob to corner into. Those edge knobs have a bit of a sloping edge to them, which can feel washy in the wrong scenarios, but does catch well when leaned over. The small center knobs alternate in a 3-2-2-3 pattern, with gradually ramped leading edges to keep rolling resistance down. The closest proxy I've recently ridden would be the Maxxis Dissector, which is also positioned as a fast-rolling rear tire for drier conditions. TESTING Download the Trailforks App I've ridden the Butcher all over the world across a ton of varied terrain, and it's one of those tread patterns that works well just about anywhere. The Eliminator is a little more specialty, and seems best suited to faster trails where scrubbing speed is more important than shutting things down fully. Might as well make it a longer loop, as the fast rolling speed means that'll be just a bit easier. PERFORMANCE I've ridden both Grid Trail and Grid Gravity variants of these tires extensively, with time on both the 29" and 27.5" options - the latter only in the rear. My pressures for the Gravity casings tended to be 21psi in the front, and 23psi in the rear. When running Trail casings, 23psi in the front and 25-26psi in the rear tends to feel correct. While I've run the Butcher as a front and rear across a wide array of bikes, the Eliminator has only ever been a rear tire for me. With many sets of these tires in rotation, I have had the opportunity to mount them to a wide array of rims and see how consistent the casings are. The short answer? Very consistent, though larger than advertised. Both Butcher and Eliminator inflate to around 2.4" (61-62mm) across all 30mm rims, with a nicely rounded profile to them. I have not had any issues with wobbly casings, nor have I had a hard time inflating the tires on normal tubeless rims. ROLLING SPEED As a combination (Eliminator rear, Butcher front), these tires feel quick and efficient for trail and enduro bikes alike. You won't mistake them for cross-country tires, but they're certainly not slowing you down as much as something like a Specialized Cannibal, Maxxis Assegai, or Continental Kryptotal Rear. The tread patterns do a good job of bridging space while allowing for traction, and the T7/T9 rubber combo on the Eliminator saves some watts while you're rolling on that centerline. TRACTION Specialized has done a good job with their T9 rubber compound, which offers plenty of grip in wet and dry weather alike. Though it's not the absolute softest rubber out there, T9 tires do a good job of smearing on rocks and roots without any sketchy-feeling breakaway. Straight line braking is very good with the Butcher, both in the front and rear, with good bite in loose soil and no squirm on hard surfaces. The Eliminator shows its speed in loose terrain, struggling to hook up as well as meatier-lugged tires.
