That said, it does a good job in typical trail bike settings, where metering speed is more the game than shutting things down in the steepest, loosest chutes. CORNERING The Butcher is predictable and easy to intuit once you've found that breakaway point between the center and edge lugs. That point feels far less drastic than something like a Maxxis DHF, more closely resembling a DHRII in that sense. You know there will be a small give, followed by a secure bite, and it hooks up there every time. As a rear tire, you can kick it out when you want to and trust that it will hook up as the bike leans over. I like both the DHR and the Butcher, and would happily run either as a front or rear on just about any bike. The Eliminator is a little more exciting, with a quick breakaway point that catches further into the lean. This is a hard thing to explain in firm terms, but it feels like it wants to schralp corners more than cut a line across them. If your trails are tight, flat, and loose, it might feel a bit sketchy. If you want something fast that can break away, but catch you right before things go bad, it's quite fun. Overall, for the mix of deep soil and well-supported hardpack that I typically ride, it feels well balanced for a fast rolling tire. I prefer the Eliminator to the Dissector's flimsy-feeling side knobs, and find it more in line with something like the Aggressor, which wins out in tread durability and predictability. DURABILITY The durability of these tires is excellent, further increasing their strong value. The tread wears evenly and fairly slowly, with no unusual knob shearing or severe undercutting to report. Continental's Super Soft rubber seems to wear a little more evenly, but the Specialized tires hold up nicely over plenty of park laps and pedal miles alike. I've noticed that as these tires age, they seem to get a bit harder feeling - whether that's something to do with the rubber itself aging or the harder base rubber getting exposed, I don't know, but if you're looking for the most chemical grip then these are unsurprisingly best when fresh. The Grid Gravity casing feels very robust, and has surprised me many times with what it can survive at low pressures, even in sharp and jagged terrain. Rim hits, rocky chutes, and hard corners all seem to shake out nicely, with no flats or failures across the many Gravity tires I've installed on my bikes. I can't be quite as effusive about the Trail casing tires, which do feel a little prone to rolling at lower pressures, and can deform heavily in a good corner unless you're running very high pressure. This isn't unique to the Specialized casing, but is more just to do with the intended use case. I wouldn't recommend lining up for a long day of enduro racing with these, but they feel very well suited to general trail bike use. As with the Gravity tires, I've had no unusual flats. Pros + Butcher is a solid do-it-all tire, front or rear + Eliminator feels fast yet fairly capable + Consistent, reliable, and relatively inexpensive Cons - Stickier rubber does exist, though T9 holds its own - Gravity casing is fairly heavy, maybe too stiff for some Pinkbike's Take The Specialized Butcher is one of those tires I'd happily run on just about any bike, front or rear, rain or shine. It's consistent and predictable, and the price is right. The Eliminator is a bit more of a specialty proposition, with fast rolling tread and just enough traction to edge into harder terrain. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires Specialized Specialized Butcher Specialized Eliminator Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: 6 Months Aboard the Specialized Levo SL eMTB
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, emtb, reviews, trail-bikes, specialized, specialized-levo-sl
article_text: REVIEW: Specialized Levo SL WORDS: Matt Beer PHOTOS: Max Barron Levo Sl V3 0 seconds of 4 minutes, 31 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/oCoaUW8H Copied Live 00:00 04:31 04:31 More Videos 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close What do you get when you hide a battery and motor inside of a Specialized Stumpjumper EVO? The Levo SL, a sub-18kg eMTB with a carbon frame, 150mm of travel, and enough geometry adjustments to keep any style of rider satisfied. Stealthy looks aside, the Levo SL is intended to ride like an unassisted bike too. Compared to other 25kg-plus behemoths out there, riders should be able to pop off the trail without inflicting a hernia and have no trouble slowing down before the brakes catch on fire. Dubbed the “electric Stumpjumper EVO” by Mike Kazimer, it can theoretically tag along with full-powered eMTBs when climbing (at least in their lowest assist-modes) but it’ll cost more energy from you and the 320Wh battery. That's due to the upgrade from 35 to 50Nm on the new SL 1.2 motor. What’s the price tag on this transformable, lightweight eMTB then? Our S-Works model checks out at a cool $14,000 USD, which includes the extra 160Wh range extender. The Levo SL comes with Fox Factory suspension, premium SRAM wireless controls and Roval carbon components - Specialized left little to be desired on the showroom card. Specialized Levo SL Details • S-Works carbon frame • 150mm travel, 160mm fork • Specialized SL 1.2 motor; 50Nm torque, 320W power • 320Wh battery, 160Wh range extender (inc. w/S-Works) • Wheel size: Mixed or dual 29" • Travel: 150mm rear / 160mm front • 64.6º head angle • 75.8º seat tube angle • 432mm chainstays • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, S6 • Weight: 39.3 lb / 17.8 kg (size S4, S-Works) • MSRP: $8,000 - $14,000 USD • specialized.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take You'll have to get out a fine tooth comb to pick out flaws on this machine. Specialized has the entire Levo SL package covered from suspension performance, to motor demands, and of course the spec on the S-Works model is top notch. — Matt Beer The headset comes with two cups, a 1.0-degree and a 0 offset. This opens the doors to a matrix of geometry options. Changing the cups only requires a 5mm allen key and best of all, it never creaked once. The dropout pivot can also be changed to fit either rear wheel size without altering the geometry if desired. Details matter: the mud flap is mounted to the front triangle to shed mud and rocks. We did observe some squeaking when this area became caked in dust, but some silicone spray resolved that quibble. Another flip-chip rests in the shock eyelet for even more angle adjustments. Frame Details The Levo SL takes on classical Specialized shapes with straight legged, round tubes and FSR suspension. There’s also an abundance of geometry tweaks to be found throughout the frame which can hold a 27.5 or 29” rear wheel. Previously the Levo SL featured a frame-stiffening sidearm, bridging the seat tube and top tube. That member has been removed to increase access to the shock and eliminate the chance of bonking your knee on the brace. Inside the front triangle, a Zee side-load cage holds a common water bottle with grazing the shock reservoir. Aside from all of the possible geometry configurations, the new 1.2SL motor is the main talking point of the Levo SL. That’s been rejigged to produce 50Nm of torque, 15Nm more than the previous generation, and updated to tone down the noise levels. At peak power, it’ll pump out 320W, versus 565W of the full-size Levo. Inside the downtube you’ll find a 320Wh battery, which is removable, but you’ll have to partially unbolt the motor to do so. For most rides, slotting the 160Wh Range Extender in the bottle cage will be the play. If you’re embarking on unknown routes or all-day rides, carrying or stashing a second extender isn’t out of the question. One important consideration when using a Range Extender is that you’ll need to toggle the “deplete range extender first” option if you’re planning on ditching it along the way. Wisely, the charge port has a spring-loaded door and the Range Extender cord locks into place there. The SL 1.2 motor has been bumped up from 35-50Nm. Equally important are the noise levels; after 6 months of riding, the clutch remains calm and quiet while descending. Under load, it produces a low whine, but is not as stealthy as the TQ HPR50. The low-profile remote is easy to locate and change motor assist levels, but the walk-mode button can crowded by other controls depending on where you locate the lever clamp. Elsewhere, the finishing is well thought out with the MasterMind TCU which lies inside the top tube, lowering the chance of smashing any electronic controls. The remote is only as large as it needs to be with four buttons, although I’d like to see a wireless option in the future. Depending on where the dropper post remote is located, you may find it interferes with the walk-mode button. The Specialized App is similarly easy to navigate to change the MasterMind display and make other battery or motor tuning choices. Toggling the alarm and motor lock on gives you peace of mind and doesn’t come at any extra cost either, unlike the Bosch’s system. Between the chain routing lies the wavy chainstay protector, welcomed chainstay mud flap, and an integrated chain guide. Shocking, many eMTBs don't utilize a frame skid plate or have chain protection. The Levo SL has adequate ground clearance in this area, but I'd add a chainring mounted bash guard if this was my own baby. Suspension Design Specialized has been using the four-bar Horst-Link arrangement for decades now. The main pivot lies in line with the 32-tooth chainring but the rear axle sits on the seatstay. This allows for greater independence arranging the suspension kinematics. Likewise, the brake mount is also placed on the seatstay, reducing the compressive braking forces on the suspension. Specialized opted for a 210x55mm (210x50 on the S1 frame) Fox Float X air shock on the Levo SL. That’s been built to their unique “Rx Tune” damping requirements and delivers a low-to-medium amount of progression to the wheel rate - hard bottom-outs only occurred on grimacing impacts while the beginning of the travel is supple enough that finding more forgiveness from a coil shock would be questionable. Levo SL Suspension Squish 0 seconds of 12 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/IIs3V0Ay Copied Live 00:00 00:12 00:12 More Videos 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close Geometry & Sizing Specialized built six size front triangles for the Levo SL. The smallest S1’s tiny frame does limit the rear wheel travel to 144mm. Adaptability is a major talking point of the Levo SL and for good reason. There are three places to reposition hardware on the frame. Flip-chips are found along the chainstay and lower shock yoke, plus the head tube also captures an interchangeable upper cup system. All of the necessary components are included with the bike and can be made with the SWAT multi-tool hidden in the steer tube. The dropout pivot is offset to allow for quick changes between rear wheel sizes without altering the geometry. In the case of a 27.5” rear wheel, you have two choices; a 432mm chainstay length with a -29mm BB drop, or a longer, lower ride position at 442mm and -34mm. Specialized felt that these two lengths fit each half of the size range well, while keeping the nature of the bike on the sporty side. They still offer two options to customers of all sizes, however, I'd wager that riders on the S4 frame size and up will opt for the longer position, regardless of wheelsize. The angles can be tweaked further at the head tube with the use of two cups, a neutral or 1.0 offset option. In total, the head angle can range from 63.8- 65.7 degrees. Straight edges on the otherwise circular cups eliminate alignment errors or shifting in the frame At this time, Specialized doesn’t have the same handy Stumpy EVO geometry calculator tool available for the Levo SL, but they do explain the changes further on the support page . Furthermore, Specialized has an extensive user manual available for the Levo SL, and not just a basic one you toss in the bin when unboxing a new bike. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $14000 Travel 150mm Rear Shock Fox Float Factory Rx Tuned Fork Fox 36 Factory, 29", 44mm offset, Grip 2 Cassette SRAM XX Eagle Transmission 10-52T Crankarms SRAM Carbon Crankset Bottom Bracket Specialized 1.2 SL Custom R Rear Derailleur SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Flattop Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar Roval Traverse SL Carbon Stem Deity Copperhead 35mm Grips Deity Knuckleduster Brakes SRAM Code Stealth Ultimate, HS2 200mm rotors Wheelset Roval Traverse SL Carbon 29/27.5 Tires Butcher/Eliminator, Grid Trail, T9 compound Seat Bridge, 155/143mm, Hollow Ti-rails Seatpost RockShox Reverb AXS, 30.9mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Levo SL Test Bike Setup When I first laid eyes on the Levo SL, it arrived in the slackest position with the shortest chainstay setting. A 35mm length stem seemed fitting for my tastes, given the 44mm fork offset and head tube angle, but something about the steering threw me off. I trimmed the bars to 770mm and found that the 6 degree upsweep of the Roval bar (8 degree backsweep) needed to be rolled further back than I’d prefer. That placed my hands too far behind the steering axis with the short stem, so I played with a OneUp bar that was more familiar. I’d need to resort to a 50mm stem with the Roval bar. 25mm of spacers were kept underneath at all times. When looking at the suspension setup, I added a third volume spacer to the fork and set that to 106 psi.The damper settings were close to Fox's guidelines aside from the rebound running a few clicks further open. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 37 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Rear shock pressures ranged between 187-197 psi depending on conditions, trail choice and if I was carrying extra goods in a pack. The recommended sag is 13.75mm, but I ended up closer to 12mm most days. I also reduced the volume of air in the rear shock, switching the stock 0.2 spacer to a 0.4 after a few metal clangs on bottom-outs. I could see the benefit of a shock with a hydraulic bottom-out to provide softer stops at full-travel and avoiding the higher return forces of additional volume spacers. A 170mm Reverb AXS dropper post works fine for just about all situations for me, but on occasion, lowering it for mega-steep trails or jump tracks provided extra clearance. Throughout the test, I rode the Levo SL in what I’d consider the most likely configurations, including dual 29” wheels. Discussing the habits of each one would be a painful read, but having the ability to switch wheel sizes without skewing the geometry is reassuring. Climbing I like to think of SL eMTBs as the most lucrative way to save energy getting to the top while still enjoying the freedom of a lightweight bike on the descents. They won’t climb up walls like a full-powered e-bike but it's no sweat to gain more vert per hour than a non-assisted bike. The Levo SL grips the ground insanely well, however, the short chainstay and relaxed seat tube angle (in the more aggressive settings) do place the rider’s seated weight generously over the rear axle. When the grade does turn uphill sharply, you’ll have to be mindful about keeping your weight forward in the saddle. With that said, if you can avoid doing wheelies, the rear wheel sticks to the ground. I'd call it efficient but not that effective. In terms of ground clearance, the BB does ride fairly low, even in the steeper settings. You’ll want to use the climb switch, which is thankfully easy to reach, to avoid the pedal strikes. As stated, you can choose the geometry to shine on climbs - a 29” rear wheel, +1.0 head tube adjustment and high shock setting will do the best job of clearing obstacles if you’re want a more agile and reactive bike for pedalling through undulating trails. As for the motor haptics, the torque winds down and picks up again in a timely fashion. There’s no unexpected bursts of energy at the crank. Restarting on hill climbs isn’t impossible either. The motor clutch engagement is extremely quick too. If you wish to divert from the stock motor settings, there’s no shortage of pedal pickup and power delivery to play with in the app either. On that note though, I did notice that the battery life dropped substantially faster in “Turbo” compared to the “Trail” mode even though the outputs were fairly close. Descending Ironically, with all of the laborious hours that went into building this bike, I could sum it up in just two words; buttery smooth. From the rear suspension to the motor’s power delivery, the Levo SL is very pleasant to rider, and I'd like to think that would be the case for a rider of any level. In our Fall Field Test, we talked about frame stiffness quite a bit. It seems to be a common finding, but round tubes don’t send as many vibrations through the frame to the rider and that adds to the smooth ride of the Levo SL. The rear triangle absorbs off-camber hits and holds through chattery flat corners well, even more so in the longer chainstay setting. The Levo SL’s suspension has a floating feeling about it, eerily like a chainless ride. Almost everything about the rear suspension works seamlessly, whether keeping the wheels on the ground to slow down or pushing into the bike to gain speed. There are no irregularities in the travel such as progression spikes or mid-stroke wallowing. From start to finish, it's consistently smooth. If you’re a rider that stabs the brakes hard between consecutive bomb holes, you could find the bike’s balance to be mildly disrupted at times. On steeper trails where you’re fighting to release the brakes, you’ll appreciate how the wheels stick to the ground though. Normally, my first ride on a test bike ends with at least five negative points, but finding things to complain about the Levo SL was a chore. We got off to solid start, however, there was a peculiarity about the steering took longer than anticipated to debunk. I swapped out to the full 29er setup which helped and actually raised the BB. In the end, I stuck with the longer chainstay setting, even with the 27.5" rear wheel and chalked up the quirky turning habits to the low center of gravity and short chainstay, which gave a false sense of security on the steeper, chunky trails found on the North Shore. Frequently, I’d have to remind myself that this is a 150mm-travel bike and not to get too carried away with my downhill antics. In the slacker settings, the significant BB drop and low center of gravity add to the planted feeling of the Levo SL. The short chainstay and 27.5” rear wheel do slightly detract from its stability, but only when the trail becomes choppy and steep. Switching the chainstay into the long setting (27.5” wheel, shock flip-chip in high position, -1.0 cup) was my preferred arrangement. That provided the most balance and therefore the most traction for rallying through Squamish’s best handbuilt trails. After six months of hard riding, the Levo SL hasn’t missed a beat - not one loose bolt, creaking from the headset, or motor irregularities. It faced everything from blasting through muddy ruts and plinko-ing down blown-out moto tracks. Impressively, the motor clutch is still as quiet as day one as well and the wheels are as straight as an arrow. It wasn’t without a few minor quirks though. The derailleur mount did need to be retorqued a couple weeks into testing and extra slack in the TCU meant rearranging the cable internally to avoid rattling inside the top tube. What's the Best Value? Overall cost aside, when comparing the value of the models in the Levo SL line, the Expert is a sensible choice given it's still a carbon frame loaded with high-performance components - basically, you skip the extra pizazz of the S-Works and a few carbon bits. There is a slight difference in the frame layup of the Comp and Expert, versus the S-Works carbon finish itself, but is that extra weight savings really worth the extra dough? Likely not for most riders - that money could be best saved for spare tires and upgrades down the road. On the specifications side, you still receive the same Fox Performance Elite dampers but skip the flashy Kashima coating. Similarly, the brakes a minor step down to Code Stealth Silver, which use the new lever body style and include reach and bite point adjustments. Similarly, the drivetrain is the latest Transmission type from SRAM which eliminates the derailleur hanger and cables. Specialized Levo SL Trek Fuel EXe How Does It Compare? Trek and Specialized have each cooked up SL eMTBs that showcase their product development team’s skills - there’s little left to be desired if you’re in the market for one of these sub-18kg machines. The Fuel EXe and Levo SL don’t exactly line up against one another in their stock configuration, however, depending on how you set them up, there could be considerable overlap. The Fuel Exe has 10mm less travel at either end, but a 160mm fork isn’t out of the question. Out of the box, it arrives with dual 29” wheels. Again, swapping the stock parts for a 27.5” rear wheel will make it more “flickable”. Those two attributes point the Fuel EXe closer to a 60/40 split of up descending and climbing, versus the Levo SL’s 70/30 realm. A critical change for Specialized to keep up with the market meant bumping the SL 1.2 motor up 50Nm of torque. That matches TQ’s HPR50 now, however, Trek’s integrated battery holds 40 more watt-hours. One thing you don't want more of on an e-bike is noise. While the Levo SL motor acoustics aren't loud, it does have a slightly higher pitched whine compared to the TQ, particularly at higher RPMs. Both the Levo SL and Fuel Exe came in the flashiest trim available, however, the Trek slightly undercuts Specialized in terms of pricing. The Levo SL S-Works costs $1000 USD more than the Fuel Exe’s $13,999 USD price tag. House branded carbon bars, wheel are found on each bike, along with SRAM’s top-tier T-type wireless shifting and Code brakes. Tires are another item sourced within each brands’ garage. Specialized’s rubber is light years ahead of the plastic-like Bontrager treads, which were swiftly removed from the Fuel EXe. Another bonus point for Trek is the entry price. Aluminum Fuel EXes begin at $5,499 USD and still use the sought after TQ motor. Oddly enough though, only the aluminum Fuel EXe is equipped with a interchangeable headset cup to alter the angles which is an option that Trek’s customers spending top dollar would appreciate. Of course, this could be accomplished using aftermarket headsets if desired. Technical Report Fox 36 Factory: Before the burly 38 fork, the 36 served as the long-travel single crown for in Fox’s lineup. Between the two, there is a subtle difference in stiffness and how the air spring operates, but it still packs a punch. With the Grip2 damper and fool-proof volume spacers, it’s still a quality bit of kit for enduro and trail bikes. Code Stealth Ultimate Brakes: The master cylinder may look different on the outside, but internally, they use the identical parts to the previous generation Code RSCs. A reshaped lever has a rounder side profile which seems to be welcomed by all. Most apparent, the brake lines exit on an angle to tie into stem clamps for a neat and quiet setup. SL 1.2 motor: Bumping the torque from 35 to 50Nm may not seem like much on paper, but it’s significant on the trail. Grinding up climbs and lunging up tech moves comes with an added boost. It’s also much quieter than the first generation motor and most importantly, less whiny. Pros + Expectations meet price - neatly packaged features + Highly adaptable geometry + Stealth appearance and operation for an e-bike + Descends like a traditional bike Cons - No aluminum option creates an expensive cost barrier - Taller riders may find their center of gravity sits heavily over the rear wheel, making steep climbs tougher to negotiate Pinkbike's Take We’re not here to say that the Levo SL doesn’t cost a lot of money, but it does live up to everything we expected at this price point. Over the course of a 6-month review term, this bike has been put through the wringer and keeps coming back for more. Better yet, it still looks, feels and sounds like a new bike. As for the ride characteristics, the Levo SL handles more like a traditional bike and is quickly excitable on the trail - it’s neither a slug to pull through jumps, yet it doesn’t hold back at full pace either. In fact, I'd often forget I was riding an eMTB when descending. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech eMTB Reviews Trail Bikes Specialized Specialized Levo Sl Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Specialized's New Turbo Levo 4 Has More Power, More Battery
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: reviews-and-tech, emtb, reviews, trail-bikes, specialized, specialized-levo, levo-4
article_text: Specialized Turbo Levo 4 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/NtGDwbpv Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:05 Last year Specialized revamped nearly their whole mountain bike lineup - new versions of the Epic, Stumpjumper, and Status models were all launched, and a DH race bike was teased as well. Granted, there’s still no new Enduro, but I’ve stopped holding my breath on that one - hopefully the wait is worth it when it finally emerges. For 2025 the focus shifts to the motorized side, starting with the Turbo Levo. The new Levo picks up where the previous generation left off, and now has more power (thanks to a new motor), an 840 Wh battery, and there’s even in-frame storage. The 150mm of rear travel and 160mm fork are the same as before, and there’s still the wide range of possible geometry configurations that Specialized has become known for. There are four complete models, with prices starting at $7,999 for the Levo 4 Comp Carbon, and going all the way up to the S-Works model shown here, which retails for a whopping $13,499 USD. Specialized Turbo Levo 4 Details • Wheel size: Mixed • Travel: 150mm rear / 160mm fork • Carbon frame • Specialized S-Works 3.1 motor: 111 Nm torque, 720 watts peak output • Specialized 3.1 motor: 101 Nm torque, 666 watts peak output • Removable 840 Wh battery (range extender compatible) • Sizes: S2, S3, S4 (tested), S5, S6 • Weight: 52.3 lb / 23.7 kg (S-Works, S4) • Price: $7,999 - $13,499 USD MORE INFO Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through the affiliate link above. Learn more . You can also go the frame-only route, still something of a rarity when it comes to eMTBs, assuming the $7,499 asking price of the S-Works frame doesn’t make you spit out your coffee. Similar to what Bosch has done in the past by offering a Race edition motor, Specialized’s S-Works motor is more powerful than the ‘regular’ Specialized 3.1 motor found in the other three models, with a max torque of 111 Nm and max power of 720 watts, compared to the still-impressive 101 Nm torque and 666 watt power of the other models. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take On extra-techy sections of trail, the type with moves that slow speeds down to a crawl, the torque is especially impressive - pushing down on the pedals results in an instant surge of power, perfect for getting up and over rooty steps. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The Levo has an entirely new carbon frame, with the battery accessed by a door on the side of the downtube. In recent years we’ve seen more and more e-bikes released with batteries that are aren’t easily removed, and in many cases unbolting the entire motor is required to pull them out. That’s typically done to save weight, since there aren’t any extra grams from the latching mechanism of a door, and there's no need to reinforce the rest of the frame to compensate for that big port. In this case, Specialized has made battery swaps about as easy as possible - all that’s required is a hex key. An 840 Wh battery is the stock configuration, but there’s a 600 Wh option available for riders who want to save some weight (the 840 Wh battery is 9.3 lb), and there’s also a 280 Wh range extender that sits in the bottle cage. No matter which battery is installed, there’s room for storing a spare tube or other repair supplies in the head tube area. A small zippered bag is supplied, and a magnet holds it into place. You do need to pull the battery out to get to those goodies, so it’s not really where I’d put snacks; think of it more like the spare tire and jack that get crammed under the trunk of a car. It’s also possible to remove the internal battery entirely and run the Levo solely on the range extender. In that setting the motor power is reduced by 50%. You could also ride the bike with no batteries installed at all, but that’s a move reserved for masochists, since the total weight with that setup is still 43 pounds. 0 seconds of 14 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/B4dTiszi Copied Live 00:00 00:14 00:14 Motor There’s a bit of an arms race going on at the moment when it comes to eMTB motors - max torque and wattage outputs are increasing, as companies inch closer and closer to the limit of Class 1 ebikes. Regulations vary, but in general a Class 1 ebike must be pedal assist only, with a maximum assisted speed of 20 mph. The motor’s power must be limited to 1 horsepower, or 750 watts. With the Levo, the S-Works motor has a max output of 720 watts, and a max torque of 111 Nm. The previous levo had 90 Nm of torque and 565 watts of power, so the increase is substantial. Keep in mind that the increased torque and power does drain the battery more quickly, so it’s a good thing that 840 Wh is the stock option. 85 - 90 Nm of torque and 600 watts of power used to seem seem like the standard, until DJI arrived on the scene with their Avinox motor, which has 105 Nm of torque and 850 W of peak power, and a Boost mode that bumps those numbers up even further for a 30 second burst. There are rumors that Bosch will be increasing the output of their Gen 5 motors later this year, but that hasn’t officially been confirmed. As for the actual construction of the motor (it’s manufactured by Brose), it uses larger, full metal gears that have undergone a surface treatment to increase their durability. Care was taken to reduce the amount of noise and vibrations produced by the motor, and so far that seems to have paid off out on the trail - I didn’t find the whir of the motor to be overly loud or annoyingly whiny. Display / Modes Specialized’s Mastermind top tube display has always been one of the best, and that continues with the Levo. There’s a huge range of customizable screens, and Bluetooth devices can also be connected to it. The motor modes can easily be customized in the app, where there are presets that can skew the output to use less battery, or to provide maximum assistance for riders that want to cover the most distance in the shortest amount of time. Along with the Eco, Trail, and Turbo, and Auto modes, Micro Tune and Dynamic Micro Tune can be accessed from the remote. Dynamic Micro Tune has become the mode I use the most - it gives access to all the motor’s power when you pedal hard, but saves battery by doling out less power when you’re not mashing on the pedals. You can select the amount of assistance in increments of 10, so 10/100, 20/100, 30/100, etc… will show up at the top of the screen. The handlebar remote has a new shape, with a small toggle the sticks up out of the middle between the power and walk buttons. It took me some time to get used to the toggle - I still prefer the old Levo’s two button switch - but after a month of riding it’s feeling much more familiar. Playing with fire There’s a menu option on the Levo that’s going to raise some eyebrows, and rightly so. By pressing a few buttons on the controller, you can increase the Levo’s max speed limit to 28 mph, effectively turning it into a Class 3 e-bike, at least on the versions sold in the US. (Class 3 e-bikes are pedal-assisted and have a max assisted speed of 28 mph). A large Class 3 logo shows up on the display to let others know that you’ve unlocked the cheat code. It’s mainly designed for riders that have a paved road commute to the trailhead, and in that scenario it really is nice to have, since you don’t get that lurching sensation when the 20mph limit is reached. That said, I think Specialized are playing with fire by enabling this mode; it’s like leaving a bowl of Halloween candy out with a "Take one" sign - I’m sure there will be plenty of riders who use the mode in areas where it’s not allowed. It’s not going to affect average climbing speeds, since it’s rare that you’d be able to go up any sort of incline at 20mph, but on more rolling terrain it will bump up the average speed that riders can maintain. We’ll see if it sticks around - I’d vote that it gets removed, even if that means it takes a few more minutes of commuting to get to the trails. That little nubbin helps keep the range extender in place. A 280 Wh range extender is available aftermarket, bumping the total battery capacity up to 1120 Wh. Super Charger Specialized are also releasing a 12 amp “Super Charger” battery charger that can take the battery level from 0% to 80% in less than an hour, a handy feature for all the forgetful riders out there. Along with the fast charge mode, it also has an Eco mode that’s designed to help increase the lifespan of the battery. Geometry There’s no shortage of geometry possibilities with the Levo, but the base numbers aren’t that much different than the last Levo. The head angle can be altered by + or - 1-degree by swapping out the upper headset cup, the bottom bracket height can be raised or lowered via a flip chip at the rear shock mount, and the chainstay length can be changed by 9mm, from 435 to 443mm with the flip chips on the rear chainstay. The nice thing is that the adjustments can be done fairly independently, rather than having one change dramatically alter all of the other geometry numbers. Suspension Design The Levo 5 uses a 210 x 55mm shock to deliver 150mm of rear travel via a Horst Link layout. It's equipped with a piggyback version of the two-chamber Genie shock that debuted on the Stumpjumper last year - for a deeper dive on the inner workings of that shock, Dario Digiulio has the scoop here . The bike is also compatible with standard shocks as well. Specifications Price $13499 Travel 150mm Rear Shock Fox Float X w/ Genie Fork Fox Float 38 Factory, 160mm Cassette SRAM XX Eagle Transmission, 10-52 Crankarms SRAM XX carbon, 160mm Bottom Bracket Specialized S-Works 3.1 motor Rear Derailleur SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM XX Eagle FlatTop Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Race Face Era Carbon 40mm rise Grips Deity Lockjaw Brakes SRAM Maven Ultimate Wheelset Roval Traverse HD Tires Specialized Butcher Grid Gravity Seat Specialized Power Pro Mirror Seatpost Fox Transfer Neo Factory Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup 195 psi in the Fox Genie shock put me right at 30% sag, and I stuck with that amount for the duration of testing. I trimmed the bars down to 770mm, and the only other change I made was a swap to a 200mm front rotor on the SRAM Maven brakes, downsizing from the stock 220 setup. Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington, as winter faded away and hints of spring started to show up. That meant the bike saw plenty of muddy rides until the sun started to stay out long enough to create heroic conditions. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Download the Trailforks App Climbing I’ll start with the motor performance, since that’s going to be the big question on many riders’ minds. Is the extra ‘oomph’ noticeable? It certainly is, especially on really steep climbs - there’s no drop off in the level of assistance, just a consistent push to get you to the top. On extra-techy sections of trail, the type with moves that slow speeds down to a crawl, the torque is especially impressive - pushing down on the pedals results in an instant surge of power, perfect for getting up and over rooty steps. There’s absolutely zero delay, even when you’re starting from a standstill. The cadence required to make the most out of the motor isn’t wildly fast either - Specialized says it's around 80 rpm, and I didn’t feel like I needed to alter my usual pedal speed that much to maintain a good level of assistance. I didn’t have too many pedal strikes, but riders in rockier areas may want to swap out the fancy 160mm carbon cranks for something shorter, and made of metal. The overall speed of the bike is what really stands out - I knew I was getting places faster than usual, but it wasn’t until looking at my stats that I’d realize just how much vert I’d knocked out in a short amount of time. As always, range amounts will vary depending on rider weight and terrain - if you get Turbo drunk it’s possible to use all 840 watts in under two hours, but by staying in Trail mode a little more often (which is still very quick even in the stock setting) the range increases significantly. I was able to get around 23 miles of range and 4,000 vertical feet of climbing out of a full battery, and that’s using a mix of Turbo and Trail modes. As far as the overall climbing position goes, I don’t have any complaints - the slightly steeper seat angle was appreciated, and the higher rise bars help create an upright and comfortable setup. Descending The Levo and the Stumpjumper share similar geometry, and I’ve spent hours and hours on both bikes in the past, so it didn’t take long to get up to speed on the latest Levo. I tried a few different geo settings for experimentation’s sake and ended up preferring the long chainstay, low bottom bracket, and neutral head angle position - in that setting the front end didn’t feel overly sluggish on more rolling terrain, while still being slack to feel right at home in the steeps. There’s been plenty of questions about the Genie shock, as is typically the case whenever a company produces something that deviates from the norm. Realistically, the design is simply a way to add a little more tunability to the air spring’s curve and to increase the bottom out resistance available, even on a bike with a more linear leverage curve. The Levo comes with one volume spacer in the outer chamber, and I stayed with that setup - the shock and fork felt balanced, with a good level of support to keep the bike from feeling too mushy or wallowy. I didn’t have any harsh bottom-outs either, which isn’t all that surprising, since the shock’s design makes that almost impossible, but it is a handy feature to have when you’re plummeting back to earth aboard a 50+ pound machine. On that note, it’s probably time to discuss the Levo’s weight. Yes, there’s now an 840 Wh battery in that big down tube, but these days 52 pounds isn’t all that light. Look at the recently launched Norco Sight VLT - it’s 49 pounds with an 800 Wh battery, and that’s a high pivot design with an idler pulley. To be fair, that weight is with a Lyrik and trail casing tires, so it’s not exactly a totally even comparison. Or take the Transition Regulator, which comes in a 47 pounds with a 600 Wh battery - add a range extender onto that and you’re still looking at a sub-50 pound bike. Am I being picky? Maybe a little, but weight does make a difference in how a bike handles, and the Levo doesn’t feel nearly as maneuverable and energetic as the Regulator, despite having very similar geometry. It takes more effort to toss it around, and it feels more, well, e-bikey than some of the other options out there. Part of me wishes Specialized had given the Levo more travel - I’d be a little more lenient when it comes to weight if this was designed to be a DH smasher as opposed to more of an all-rounder. It’s approved for up to a 180mm fork - maybe we’ll see a longer travel version released that can take advantage of that capability, since the geometry is already pretty much there. Don’t get me wrong, the Levo’s no lethargic pig - it’s still a blast to plow through chopped up and rutted descents, and it does a very good job of making the most out of that 150mm of travel. Not surprisingly, it has a lot in common with the previous model, with nice and neutral trail manners, at least if you ride with a little more aggression than you would on a non-motorized bike. Specialized Levo 4 Transition Regulator CX How Does It Compare? I had the Transition Regulator on hand at the same time as the Levo, so let’s start with that comparison. Both bikes have 150mm of travel with a 160mm fork, although the travel can be increased on the Regulator by running a longer stroke shock. The Regulator doesn’t have as many geometry options as the Levo, but I have a feeling that the settings most riders will end up at will be pretty similar between the two. The Regulator does have longer chainstays - 448 or 450mm vs 443mm for the Levo in the long setting. Other than the slight geometry differences, it’s the motor, battery size, and weight that separates the two - the Regulator uses Bosch’s gen 5 motor with a 600 Wh battery and checks in at 47.25 pounds, while the Levo’s Specialized motor ant 840 Wh battery puts it at 52.3 pounds. All the stats and numbers aside, out on the trail I felt more comfortable on the Regulator - it’s easier to toss around thanks to that lower weight, all while still maintaining excellent traction. I prefer the slightly longer chainstays as well - they add an element of centered stability that’s not quite as accessible on the Levo. The Levo’s suspension is calm and composed, but even with that fancy Genie shock the Levo’s suspension never felt quite as supple and ground hugging as the Regulator. On paper it looks like the Specialized motor should blow the Bosch out of the water, but that’s not really the case out in the real world. The Specialized motor does an excellent job of maintaining a consistent level of assistance on steep climbs, and the extra torque is noticeable in situations when you’re almost stalled out and need to put the power down to keep moving forward, but the Bosch is no slouch in that department either. I’ll put it this way - I didn’t feel like the Specialized motor allowed me to get up anything that I couldn’t also climb with the Bosch motor. Overall, the Levo and the Regulator are approaching the task of creating an all-mountain eMTB from two different angles. The Levo has a bigger battery capacity, a higher max power output, and a wider range of geometry options, while the Regulator saves weight at the cost of battery size and ease of battery access. For me, the Regulator ticks more of the boxes - it’s closer to the dream of a 40ish pound, full-power eMTB with enough battery for big rides. The Amflow PL Carbon is also a part of nearly every eMTB conversation these days, and for good reason - it’s a 44 lb bike with an 800 Wh battery and a motor that puts out 105 Nm of torque with 850 watts of peak power. To be fair, that weight is for a lightweight build with Fox 36; it'd be a few pounds heavier with the same parts as the Levo 4. At the moment, it’s not easy to obtain that bike in the US, but several companies will be announcing bikes that use the DJI motor in the coming days - we’ll get a real-world comparison going as soon as possible. Which Model is the Best Value? At the risk of sounding like a broken record, the best value is always going to be a bike without a motor - Stumpjumper 15 alloy frames are currently on sale for $1,500. Digging into the spec sheets of the Levo 4, the Levo 4 Expert model is the one that catches my eye. It has Fox's Performance Elite suspension, Maven silver brakes, Roval alloy wheels with DT Swiss 370 hubs, and SRAM's wireless GX Transmission drivetrain. It's a solid build kit that doesn't need any immediate upgrades. Granted, for $9,499 you'd hope it'd be fully dialed. Value probably isn't the term I would use here, but that build kit is the closest to how I'd build up a bike of my own if I was trying to maximize the performance without going fully bankrupt. Technical Report Fox Transfer Neo dropper post: The Neo post’s response time is incredibly fast - it feels instant, and a slight push of the trigger is all it takes to lower or raise the seat. It’s held up well to plenty of mud and grit, and I’ve managed to remember to charge the battery often enough to keep it from dying out on the trail. That’s really my only tiny nitpick (other than the post's sky high price) - I wish the it was hardwired to the main battery somehow, so that there was only one battery to charge. Otherwise it’s currently the best wireless dropper post that I’ve used, a list that includes the newest RockShox Reverb AXS, KS Lev Circuit, and the TranzX EDP01. Butcher Grid Gravity Tires: It’s great to see proper tires spec’d on a full power eMTB - the higher speeds and heavier overall system weight mean that DH tires are the way to go to prevent flats or vague handling. Specialized’s T9 compound isn’t the absolute stickiest, but it holds its own when things are wet and slimy, and the tread pattern is easy to get along with in a variety of conditions. SRAM Maven brakes: Mavens are ideal for slowing down big e-bikes. Sometimes, almost too good - after having a couple of unwanted front wheel slides I swapped out the 220mm rotor for a 200 in order to tone things down a little. I’d rather have too much power than not enough, and for most riders the 220 / 200 combo will be fine, but if you find yourself overbraking, swapping to a smaller or thinner rotor should do the trick. Pros + Instant power delivery, and plenty of it + 840 Wh battery as the stock configuration + Very adaptable, whether that's geometry or battery size Cons - Heavier than expected - Deserves more travel - Class 3 option shouldn't be a feature Pinkbike's Take I spent a lot of time on the previous Levo, and back in 2021 I called it ‘the new benchmark.” At the time, it was a much more refined offering than the other options on the market. Four years later and the eMTB landscape isn't as desolate looking - it’s as if a sleepy village with one restaurant morphed into a bustling metropolis with no shortage of good food options. That makes it harder to stand out, and even with the extra torque and power the Levo 4 doesn't tower over the competition. Still, the new motor is very impressive, as is the bike's adaptability - for riders who want an e-bike that can be turned into almost anything, the Levo could be the one. — Mike Kazimer Video / Action Shots: Doug Jambor Posted In: Reviews and Tech eMTB Reviews Trail Bikes Specialized Specialized Levo Levo 4 Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Squidworx Modular Flat Pedals
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Most pedal companies these days have a composite model in their lineup, which typically comes in at a lower price point, but none of those options have the same level of customization as the Squidworx Modular Pedals. Two replaceable composite wings or cages attach to the main aluminum body to make up the platform. This allows for the most vulnerable part of the pedal platform to be replaced when damaged, exchanged for a different sizes or even one of the various colors, by using the costly materials in this area. Squidworx Pedal Details • Aluminum main body, steel axle, nylon composite cages • 3 modular platform sizes • Dual cartridge bearings with full length bushing axle system • 14 pins per side, 5.5 or 4mm heights available • Colors: 12 various colors for MD platform, black only for SM & LG • Area: 98, 108 or 116mm x 97mm width • Thickness: 12mm • Weight: 458g per pair (419g claimed) • Price: $129.95 CAD • squidworx.bike Replaceable pins double as the fixing point for the composite cages which comes in three lengths and various colors. Details 14 replaceable pins are scattered across either side of the platform - 8 on the aluminum portion of the body and 3 on each composite cage. All the pins thread into an aluminum barrel, instead of the pedal body, making them easy to replace. Each set of composite cages are held in place by four of the pin sets closest to the axle. Squidworx offers 5.5mm and 4mm pin options, and customers can choose to have all of the pins the same size, to have a concave option with taller pins around the perimeter and shorter ones in the center, or a convex option with taller pins in the middle. You’ll need a 5mm socket or wrench on either side to unthread the pins. This is not included with the pedals, but is available on the Squidworx website for $5 CAD. The small, medium and large size wings make up platform length of 98, 108 and 116mm while they each measure 97mm wide. In terms of height, the pedals are basically flat and measure 12mm in thickness. The medium wings are available in various colors, while the main aluminum body is available in just silver or black. For the small and large wings, they are only offered in black. As for the internals, they use a chromoly axle with a long DU bushing and two cartridge bearings at the outer end. Squidworx includes the bushing tool required for a full rebuild with the pedal set. Price and Weight Composite pedals are known for being significantly cheaper than CNC’d aluminum options, however, the mixed materials and replaceable features push the Squidworx pedals to $130 CAD. Regardless of the pedal body size or colour option though, the price remains the same. Replacement cages of varying sizes or colours are available for $5 each so changing the size of a set of pedals will cost $20. $35 will also get you a new set of internals, including the axles or a singular aluminum replacement body. Our size medium Modular pedals weighed in at 458g per set, slightly above the claimed 419g. That's 18 grams more than OneUp's Composite pedals, and 110 grams more than Crankbrothers' Stamp 1. The medium platform measures 108mm L x 97 W x 12 H. Performance Every flat pedal rider has their own preference when it comes to the level of grip they’re looking for and that can largely vary depending on the shoes they prefer. I’ve found through testing numerous pedals that a completely flat surface is not necessarily the most desired shape for off-road riding in general, even with the stickiest shoes. There is a balancing act between the stiffness of the shoe, the concavity of the pedal and the grip of the pins. If the shoe is too flexible, and the concavity is too deep that can lead to fatigue at the arch of your foot. Conversely, an ultra stiff shoe match with a flat pedal tends to feel numb and doesn’t hold your foot in place even if the rubber is grippy and the pins are tall, which is the case of the Sqiudworx Modular pedals (size MD tested). I played with removing two sets of pins, hoping that would allow the remaining pins to dig into my shoes more, but the difference was negligible. There’s also a small bump in the main portion of the pedal body to make room for the DU bushing, which elevates the inside of your foot off the pins. You can see polishing effect of the shoe in this area. If you’re currently using aluminum pedals and frequently destroy pedal pins or the body itself, then the replaceable cages of the Squidworx might be worth a look. I don’t see many riders purchasing one size and then switching to another, however, mountain bikers do love experimenting with tech equipment and customizing colour schemes, so these could be for the tinkering type. Inside, there's a massive DU bushing and two cartridge bearings (note: this one is brand new). Durability Our time spent on these pedals was throughout the summer months in dry and dusty conditions yet there were a few splashy rides thrown into the mix as well. Internally, the sealing system has shielded the elements well with the grease still looking fairly fresh. As for the pins, they’ve held up well and haven't needed to be replaced. The same goes for the nylon cages, which haven’t seen much damage - it’s not like I tried to go out and hit rocks. The pins dig into the shoe well, but being able to tune the concavity with the pins would help, meaning lowering the height of the middle pins, or raising the outside ones would be ideal. OneUp's Composite pedals may not have the swap-able pieces of the Squidworx Modular pedals, but they have a tenacious grip, come in two sizes, and cost half the price. Comparison I’ve never complained about a pedal being too grippy, although it’s obvious when there is a lack of traction. Even with the stickiest stealth rubber shoes and the taller 5.5mm pins around the perimeter, I found the completely flat profile of the Squidworx pedal to lack the connective feeling for riding at full pace. Looking at options from other brands, Chromag’s Synth pedal has a similar size platform to the medium Squidworx size but offers increased concavity. The Synth’s also comes in a wide range of colours cost and cost $65 CAD - half the price of the Squids. OneUp offers two sizes of composite pedals as well. The OneUp Composite pedals come in a rainbow of color options, cost even less at $54 CAD and have a ton of grip with their threaded pins. Although the Synth’s are not adjustable in size, they do weigh 70g less per pair. Again, this is where the thickness doesn’t mean as much as it seemingly does on paper. The concave shape provides a better grip and supports my theory on the middle pins not playing much use in grip. The Synth is just 3 millimeters thicker at the axle but tapers upwards towards the fore and aft rows of pins. In terms of area, the length is about the same as the medium size Squidworx, although the Q-factor is 10 millimeters wider on the Synth. This could mean that you will avoid rock strikes when leaning into turns, or sneaking through narrow gaps with the Squidworx. Pros + Cages and pins can be easily replaced if damaged + Replacement cages on a fixed body is a creative way to offer three sizes in one Cons - Not ideal if you like a high-grip or very concave pedal - Expensive - there are full composite pedals on the market for half the price Pinkbike's Take The Squidworx Modular Pedals use a crafty method of replacing parts and offering low-cost platform size changes. With that said, the initial investment is on the higher side for a pedal that contains composite components. In terms of traction, they're adequate for general trail riding, however, there are grippier, more concave options out there. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Pedals Squidworx Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: SRAM's New Eagle 90 Mechanical Transmission - No Batteries Required
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: SRAM Eagle 90 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/2SsOEeO1 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:34 A couple of years ago the rumor that SRAM was going to fully eliminate cable-actuated derailleurs from their catalog started to spread. It seemed far-fetched at the time, but then several frames were released without any ports for derailleur housing and it all started to seem more believable. Thankfully, those rumors weren’t true, as the launch of the new Eagle 90 and Eagle 70 drivetrains confirms - both are cable-actuated, with no batteries to be seen. The 12-speed drivetrains are based on the design of the existing wireless Transmission drivetrains, including the use of the Full Mount interface, where the derailleur clamps around both sides of the frame rather than using a traditional hanger. If a frame is universal derailleur hanger compatible it should also work with the new mechanical Transmission derailleurs. Eagle 90 Details • 12-speed, 10-52 tooth cassette • Cable actuated • Full Mount design • Compatible with other T-Type components • Groupset weight: 2085 grams • Price: $670 USD (cassette, derailleur, cranks, shifter, chain) • sram.com The Eagle 90 drivetrain is the main focus of this article, but the Eagle 70 components are worth a mention, since those will likely be showing up on more entry-level mountain bikes in the near future. The Eagle 70 cassette has the same 10-52 tooth range as Eagle 90, but it’s designed to work with an HG driver body rather than the XD body used on the higher end options. That’s great to see, since it means riders won’t need to upgrade freehub bodies or potentially buy a new wheel to get a wider gear range. The Eagle 90 drivetrain uses the same cassette and chain used by wireless GX Transmission; it’s the derailleur, shifter, cranks, and chainring that are new. The entire Eagle 90 groupset (including cranks) is priced at $670 USD, and weighs in at 2085 grams. Derailleur Price: $190 USD 395 grams You can buy two Eagle 90 derailleurs for the price of one wireless GX Transmission derailleur and still have enough money left over for a burrito, which seems like a great reason to skip the batteries to me. The weights of the two groups are about the same too - the weight of the cable and housing balances out the weight of the battery and tiny motor on the wireless groupset. SRAM claims that the Eagle 90 derailleur is the strongest mechanical derailleur they’ve ever produced, but if something does break it’s fully rebuildable. And by fully rebuildable I don’t just mean the pulley wheels and cages - nearly every single component can easily be replaced, and SRAM will offer different kits depending on which part needs to be swapped out. Taking the derailleur apart is quick and easy - with minimal practice I could fully disassemble and reassemble it in under 10 minutes. Shifter Price: $50 135 grams The Eagle 90 shifter body's profile is slightly longer than what we’ve seen in the past, with a look and housing path that’s designed to help it blend in with SRAM’s newest brakes. The forward thumb trigger is nice and large, and provides a positive perch for shifting to a harder gear, while the larger lever can be used to shift up the cassette two gears at a time. SRAM’s previous mechanical systems allowed for a larger jump with one push of the lever, but they also weren’t really designed for shifting under load, where with Transmission you can mashing down on the pedals as hard as you want and still shift. The cassette design also comes into play here - there’s a chance that shifting up the cassette more than two cogs at a time with one push of the lever could create less-than-smooth shifting due to the locations of the shift ramps. Cranks Price: $155 825 grams The Eagle 90 cranks are made from forged aluminum and use SRAM’s 8-bolt chainring mounting pattern. There’s not a whole lot to comment on, but the steel direct mount chainring is nice to see - that should last longer than an aluminum ring at the cost of some extra weight. The cranks are offered in five lengths - 155, 160, 165, 170, and 175mm, a range that should cover riders who have hopped on the ‘shorter is better’ bandwagon, and those who prefer the more traditional lengths. There are also e-bike specific crank arms for Bosch and Brose motors that go down to 150mm in length. Chain & Cassette Cassette price: $250 445 grams Chain price: $50 285 grams Eagle 90 uses the same Flattop chain and 10-52 tooth cassette that are already used for the wireless GX Transmission. Of course, higher end T-Type chains and cassettes are also compatible with Eagle 90, for those riders who want to shave some grams and potentially improve longevity while still sticking with a cable-actuated system. Installation The setup steps for mechanical Transmission are quite simple, and follow a four word order of operation: prepare, hang, tighten, route. Prepare involves finding the correct chain length and setup position for the derailleur based on SRAM’s online calculator. Hang is just like you’d expect - the derailleur is mounted to the frame, and then it’s put into the correct position to continue that setup. On the wireless version, that position is achieved by clicking the shifter to move the derailleur, but it’s a little different with the cable-actuated version, since at this point the cable hasn’t been installed. In order to move the derailleur into the right position either a 5 or 6mm allen key is slotted into a hole on the underside of the parallelogram. The size of the allen key determines which cog on the cassette the derailleur will line up with. After that, it’s simply a matter of tightening it down and then installing the cable like you would with a typical derailleur, except that there’s no B-tension or limit screws to worry about. It’s a fairly simple procedure, and SRAM has videos and instruction manuals that clearly illustrate the required steps. On the Trail Solid and stable are the two words that best describe the Eagle 90’s shifting - each click results in a defined ‘ca-chunk’ as the chain moves up or down the cassette, and other than some small cable tension adjustments (done with the barrel adjuster on the shifter) on the first two rides I haven’t had to touch anything. The speed of those shifts is quick, but it’s nothing earth-shattering - I’d still give Shimano the edge when it comes to how fast the chain can go from one end of the cassette to the other. Some of that comes down to the shifter design - the Eagle 90 shifter can move the chain two cogs up the cassette with one lever push, and down the cassette one cog at a time, while Shimano’s shifters allow the chain to go up four cogs at a time, and then down two with one lever or paddle push. SRAM seems to be placing the priority on consistency rather than outright speed, which makes sense if you’re advertising a drivetrain’s ability to shift under heavy loads, but I still feel like there’s a way to make a super fast and stable drivetrain - maybe someday we'll see an Eagle 100 option added to the catalog. The cassette's shift ramp design is also part of the overall shift speed equation - those ramps help keep each shift precise and well defined, but they also have an affect on just how fast the chain can move from one cog to another. As far as the ergonomics of the shifter itself, the thumb lever and trigger are easy to reach and intuitively positioned, although I think there's room for improvement on the shape of the lever. I was spoiled over the summer when I tested Vivo's F3 shifter - that super expensive option and its array of thumb paddle options made me realize how much potential there is when it comes to the shaping of shift paddles. There are dimples to help with traction on the Eagle 90's sweep lever, but those don't match the textured rubber pad that Shimano uses on their XT shifters, or even the ridges on the SLX shifters. Eagle 90 feels a little more basic than XT - granted, it's also less expensive, but it'd be nice to have the option to select a higher end shifter and stick with the same derailleur. Historically, that's been a great way to improve the feel of a drivetrain without needing to spend a ton of money upgrading. Unfortunately, the cable pull ratio of the Eagle 90 derailleur isn't the same as SRAM's previous drivetrains, so it's not as easy as digging a nice older X0 shifter out of the parts bin and returning it to service. I’ve been very pleased with the strength of the derailleur’s clutch - it’s smooth while also providing enough resistance to keep chainslap to a minimum. Compared to the wireless Transmission derailleurs, the Eagle 90 seems even quieter - so far I’ve been enjoying the sounds of silence as far as chain related noises go. The Eagle 90 drivetrain has been on my Raaw Madonna for a little over a month, which is enough time to discuss the shifting performance, but still too soon to go over long term durability. It’s held up very well so far, but I’ll report back with an update and more comparisons after it’s been dragged through more mud and smacked into some stumps. Pros + Solid, stable shifting + Easily rebuildable derailleur + Strong clutch mechanism (so far) Cons - Not the absolute fastest shifting performance - Shifter ergonomics are okay, but not outstanding. Pinkbike's Take I'm glad SRAM didn't fully drop cables from their lineup, especially since the new Eagle 90 drivetrain is a shaping up to be a solid, workhorse drivetrain, one that you can install and pretty much forget about for miles and miles. I would like to see a higher end shifter added into the mix in the future, one with a little more texture on the paddles and maybe a lighter action, but as the beginning of what's hopefully a return to a full line of battery-free drivetrains, Eagle 90 is a solid step. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Drivetrain SRAM Sram Eagle 90 Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: SRAM Maven Base Brakes - Light Lever Action & Lots of Power
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Maven Base https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/PF3aPzuE Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:24 SRAM launched their new Motive XC / trail brakes earlier this year, but another brake was released at the same time that didn’t get nearly as much fanfare - the Maven Base. Even on SRAM’s own website it’s not immediately visible - it takes an extra click to find it after viewing the Maven Ultimate, Silver, and Bronze options. The thing is, for many riders, there’s a good chance this is the Maven brake they really want, with lots of power, very light lever action, and a reasonable price. Maven Base Details • 4-piston caliper. 18mm pistons • Mineral oil • DirectLink lever design • Tool-free reach adjust • Weight: 387 grams (actual, front caliper w/pads, hose, and lever) • MSRP: $205 USD per wheel • sram.com Let’s start with what sets the Maven Base apart from the three other brakes in the Maven lineup. Rather than using two 18mm and two 19.5mm pistons like the original Maven trio, the Base uses four 18mm pistons. That downsizing is due to the other differentiator - the fact that the Base uses a DirectLink lever design, rather than a SwingLink lever design. With the DirectLink lever, the power is more linear, meaning that it remains consistent from the beginning to the end of the lever pull; with SwingLink, the power (leverage) ramps up the further the lever is pulled. It’s the same lever design used on the aforementioned Motive brakes. One of the confusing things about the Maven Base brakes is that they’re slightly more expensive than the Bronze model, at $205 vs $185 per wheel. Realistically, I think SRAM could have given them a different name all together, maybe call them Super Codes or something, to help get them out of the Maven shadow. Installation I’m glad that SRAM made the switch to mineral after so many years of espousing the superiority of DOT fluid. On paper DOT does have some advantages, but mineral oil is less likely to damage a frame's paint job, and it also doesn't absorb water, which should allow for longer service intervals. Of course, you still definitely shouldn’t drink the stuff, and wearing gloves and eye protection is always a good idea when dealing with brake fluids - if you read the MSDS sheets for DOT fluid and mineral oil you see that care should be taken when dealing with either one. Bleeding the Maven Base brakes is an easy procedure, especially if you’re familiar with SRAM’s bleed procedure. Attach a syringe to the caliper with SRAM’s Bleeding Edge fitting (everything has a special name), and thread the other into the lever. Push fluid from one end to the other until you don’t see bubbles, then close off the port at the caliper. Next, squeeze the lever a few times and then pull up gently on the syringe to remove any remaining air bubbles. Lightly push on the syringe plunger to equalize the system, then unthread it, keeping a rag nearby to clean up the excess fluid that’ll be present when it’s removed. SRAM’s instructional video does a good job of going through the steps. Piston Massaging There is one step that wasn’t really necessary with SRAM’s previous brakes - the piston massage . It’s a funny name for a procedure that’s absolutely necessary, unless you want to end up writing late-night forum posts about your inconsistent brake lever feel. Trust me - this step is important. Luckily, it’s easy to do. SRAM even has a $4 chunk of plastic for this specific purpose, or you can use two Centerline rotors. With the pads removed, put the spacer into the caliper, and then squeeze the lever until the pistons contact the spacer. Remove the spacer, and carefully push each piston back into its home with a plastic tire lever. Be gentle - if you get wild you run the risk of popping a piston entirely out of its seat, and then you’ll be sad and there will be mineral oil everywhere. Do this a few times and the pistons should be moving smoothly, and the feel at the lever should be consistent. If you're not comfortable pushing on the pistons, or a little short on time, a pad advance procedure can also be useful. This involves inserting the thinner side of the included plastic pad spacer between the brake pads and squeezing the brake lever (you'll need the wheel removed for this step). Next, insert the thicker side of the spacer, which will push the pads further back into the caliper. Re-install the wheel, squeeze the lever a few times, and make sure everything's operating properly. Performance I installed the Maven Base brakes on a Santa Cruz Bronson, where they took the place of a set of Maven Ultimates. I’ve gotten used to the power of the Mavens in the months since they came out, but I still tend to de-tune them, running a smaller rotor size or going to the thinner Centerline rotors over the thicker HS2. I love steep trails, and powerful brakes, but since I’m on the lighter side, at 160 pounds, and I don’t need the same amount of stopping power as someone that’s 30 or more pounds heavier. For those bigger riders, that extra power is going to be very welcome, and could mean that automatically going to the largest diameter rotors around won't be necessary. When the Mavens first came out there was a lot of online hand-wringing and theorizing about the harder-to-pull lever. There’s no denying that the initial force required to pull the lever on the regular Mavens is slightly higher than it was on the Codes, but I think some of those initial complaints were related to an early production run - I’ve been on at least six different sets of Mavens in the last year and didn’t have any lever-related issues as long as they were bled properly and the piston massage was performed. That said, for riders who prefer a lighter lever action, the Maven Base brakes are the way to go. It takes minimal force to get the lever moving, and while the Hayes Dominions still take the cake when it comes to an effortless initial pull, the Base brakes aren’t far off. There’s a light initial action, and then a very defined point where the pads contact the rotor. The feel is closer to what you’d expect from Shimano’s brakes, with a sharp, crisp bite as the pads make contact, but the overall amount of power is noticeably higher than Shimano's XT or XTR brakes. There’s not as much modulation from the lever compared to the SwingLink-equipped Mavens, but I haven’t had trouble switching between the two brakes - a couple pulls and my brain re-calibrates to tell my fingers how much force to exert. With the Base brakes, max power can be reached quickly, and then it doesn’t change throughout the lever stroke. With the SwingLink levers there’s a bit more modulation; the power hits a little deeper into the pull than it does with the Mavens. That means the Base brakes could take a little getting used to if you’re a brake dragger, but again, it’s pretty intuitive. The consistency and power of the Base brakes has been impressive - I’ve taken them on plenty of steep, sustained trails and the lever feel has remained the same from top to bottom. On one particular brake-burner of a traiI was able to get them to howl, but the stopping power remained the same, and that trail is one that’ll make almost every brake make some noise, whether it’s Shimano, SRAM, or TRP. I wouldn’t hesitate to put these on an e-bike or a DH bike - there’s enough power for any scenario. Organic pads are the stock configuration, but as always, for riders in wetter areas metallic pads are the way to go. Personally, I’d be happy if organic pads went away across the board - I eventually end up to swapping to metallic pads on every brake that comes, and the performance always increases. It is another way of tuning the feel, though, and between pad compound and rotor size / thickness there’s a decent level of customization available. Pros + Very light lever action + Powerful and consistent + Reasonable price Cons - More of an on/off feel than a dimmer switch - No version with pad contact adjust Pinkbike's Take The Maven Base brakes eliminate any of the lever-related gripes that surround the SwingLink-equipped Mavens, all while delivering an immense amount of power and consistency. I’d love to see a higher end option with a pad contact adjust, but that’s a minor detail - as it is, these brakes nail it when it comes to price, power, and overall performance. Highly recommended. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Brakes SRAM Sram Maven Base Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: SRAM's New Maven Brakes - The Big Brake
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, brakes, sram, sram-maven, dario-digiulio, mike-kazimer
article_text: REVIEW: SRAM Maven Brakes WORDS: Dario DiGiulio / Mike Kazimer PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio They say power is infinitely corrupting, but that quote doesn't really specify anything about slowing bikes down. In the name of that pursuit, SRAM pulled out all the stops to create the strongest mountain bike brake they've ever made: the Maven. It's meant to compete with all the strongest options on the market, and aims to best them. Parts of the system may look familiar, but the overall architecture has been overhauled to provide more power, resulting in a much larger overall footprint you see here. All the power in the world is for naught if you can't control it, so the team at SRAM focused on giving the Maven a familiar lever feel and modulation characteristic. Maven Details • 4-piston caliper • Tool-free lever reach adjust • Contact adjust • Mineral oil • Organic or sintered pads • HS2 or Centerline rotors • Weight: 366 grams (actual, front caliper w/pads, hose, and lever) • MSRP: $189-300 USD per wheel • www.sram.com A complete front brake with pads weighs in at 366 grams, which is about 68 more than the current Code Ultimate Stealth. Pricing is higher than the Code as well, with three tier options - Ultimate, Silver, and Bronze - coming in at $300, $265, and $185 USD respectively. SRAM Maven - The Big Brake https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/dHh32vEr Copied Live 00:00 09:55 09:55 Four bolts for power. Pads are now only accessible from the interior of the caliper. Details Thanks in part to the Eddie Van Halen splatter paint job, the calipers of the Mavens really draw the eye. They're noticeably larger than just about anything else on the market, and according to SRAM that's core to their function. All that extra mass houses significantly larger pistons, with two 18mm and two 19.5mm phenolic plastic pad pushers per caliper. The hefty shape also doubles as an effective thermal mass, taking longer to heat up and cool down, and ultimately delivering more consistent performance across long descents. The levers and SwingLink arrangement have been made to feel familiar to that of the Codes, but are optimized to work with the increased power and rigidity of the Maven. This is apparently why we won't be seeing any carbon levers for the Maven Ultimates, as those sported by the current Code Ultimates are too flexible to provide the feel they wanted out of the new brakes. You'll see cast aluminum levers on both the Ultimate and Silver models, and a stamped aluminum variant on the Bronzes. The Mavens sport a new pad shape - dubbed the XL - which come in two compounds that Code users will find familiar. The organic option is meant to provide a stronger initial bite and quieter performance, while the sintered offers better heat resistance and better wet weather stopping power. While I found myself exclusively using the sintered pads in Codes, I've gotten along well with both compound options on the Mavens, regardless of weather conditions. More on that later in the ride impressions. One key detail to the Maven system is hidden from view, and that's the fluid within them.
