article_text: 6D ATB-3 helmet review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/SmWzJGjC Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 07:37 Rebuilding a helmet after a crash sounds like a odd concept, but that’s exactly what 6D is touting with their premium ATB-3 gravity full-face helmet. It’s not as black and white as it sounds - there is an inspection process, which I’ll get into, but it’s the reason 6D partially justifies the high cost of the ATB-3. The ATB-3 comes in two basic finishes, clear carbon or white, for the tune of $769 USD. For those seeking a brighter look, there’s the LS version. Three striped designs in white, bronze, or neon yellow tack on an extra $30. ATB-3 Details • Rebuildable ODS system • 3K carbon weave outer shell • Omni-Directional Suspension • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL, XXL (53-63cm) • Colors: Carbon, White • Weight: 1,157 g (actual, size M) • Price: $769 USD • 6dhelmets.com Under the 3K carbon-woven shell, the focal point of 6D’s safety tech is the ODS (Omni-Directional System). This cup and cone layering of EPS and EPP shells is connected via isolation dampers, which absorb impacts 3-dimensionally. MIPS-equipped helmets use a slip plane to dissipate rotational forces, but can’t absorb hits to the noggin the same way that the ODS can. Pinkbike's Take The ATB-3 is an excellent helmet, no question about it. The ODS system is one of the most logical approaches to helmet safety and it’s proven to score high in Virginia Tech Rankings. Adding to the redesign, 6D managed to drop the weight and volume compared to the prior generation ATB. Limiting factors will be cost and fit, which boil down to personal decisions. 6D makes a case in terms of value with their rebuildable technology. It’s also possible to mix and match the helmet shell size with different liners to tweak the fit, you’ll just have to buy the pad kits separately. — Matt Beer FIT It’s always great to see a wide range of sizes offered in any product, whether that’s a helmet or a bike. In theory, that should allow the consumer to have the best fit possible without compromises. The ATB-3 is available in 6 sizes, starting at a circumference of 53cm for the XS and moving up to 63cm for the XXL. I typically wear a size Medium (57-58cm) full-face helmet. While the circumference of the ATB-3 was true to size and fit without pressure points, one area was still off. When I pulled on the ATB-3 was how high the helmet sat on my head. It was almost concerning how much of my jawbone was exposed. I requested a thinner liner and 6D sent one normally found in the size Large helmet. That led to a comfier and more secure fit, though it still felt a shade too high. I’d also classify the ATB-3 as a “close talker”. By that, I mean that the chin bar rests closer to your mouth. It’s not too claustrophobic, but the Troy Lee Designs D4 and Fox Rampage RS give more breathing room. The LS colorways go for $799 USD. PRICE & WEIGHT Cost is always a constant consideration, even for safety gear. The ATB-3 tops the TLD D4 Carbon by $30, but touts that rebuildable function. How realistic that option is will depend on the severity of the crash, since it's possible 6D may deem the helmet unrepairable. Even if a rebuild is possible, it isn’t free. 6D charges $132 for this service, plus you’ll also be out of a helmet for a few days. The clear carbon seen here, and a gloss white finish, each cost $769 USD. If you’re looking to spice up your life with more color, the LS model costs $30 more and comes in three striped colorways: neon yellow, white, and bronze. For the high cost, I do wish that 6D provided a second pad set to customize the fit on the spot. A padded helmet bag to protect your investment, as opposed to just a sock, wouldn't go astray either. Looks can be deceiving. While the ATB-3 isn’t slim or light, it shaves off more than 200g from its predecessor. I weighed a size medium at 1,157g, on par for a gravity full-face helmet, however, much lighter options exist. Slipping under the 1,000g mark are the Troy Lee Designs D4 Carbon (941g, size large) and the Specialized Dissident 2 (940g, size medium). Omni-Directional Suspension. The safety sandwich covers high and low-speed impacts. "Damping towers". SAFETY SPECIFICATIONS The ATB-3 is made up of two halves. A multi-impact outer EPP layer is fixed to the carbon shell. That outer half is connected by the ODS damping towers to the replaceable EPS layer, which sits closest to your head. The beauty of the dual-shell design and ODS is that it's said to be safer than a traditional helmet at low-velocity impacts, while having everything in place to handle severe impacts. Aside from the integral parts of the helmet, you'll find more thoughtful functions. The cheek pads sit on perches that detach readily with a pull tab, should the helmet need to be removed in an emergency. Extra padding is placed where your jaw or sternum may come in contact with the perimeter of the helmet. Cut-out sections near the clavicle and cervical spine provide space to avoid crunching bones. Lastly, the visor also shears off in case of a scorpion move. The ATB-3 exceeds the following ratings: CPSC EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063, ASTM 1952-15, and ASTM 2032-15. REBUILD PROCESS Right, so how do you go about rebuilding a helmet? Aren't they purpose-built to break, dissipate energy, and save your head? Totally, but 6D takes a unique approach with their ODS layering. That inner EPS liner is the critical point of the rebuild process. If the rider has been in a crash and believes the helmet can be repaired, they start by filling out a form on 6D's website. Next, they send it in for an evaluation. Derek Radel, from 6D, explains the ins and outs of the process, if deemed repairable. The process itself is fairly straightforward. We remove the chin bar, pull four pins, and extract the damaged EPS liner. From there, we thoroughly inspect the shell and all remaining components of the ODS tech to ensure everything is in perfect condition. If those components pass inspection, a new EPS liner is installed, functionally and from a safety standpoint, you’re left with what is essentially a new helmet. The cost for an EPS rebuild is $132. — Derek Radel A rebuild isn't free. It costs $132 USD, plus the shipping to and from 6D, but it is cheaper than the crash replacement (typically around 30% less MSRP) discounts offered by other brands. VENTILATION During the majority of the time spent reviewing the ATB-3, the temperatures remained below 20°C, so I can’t speak to what it’s like to wear on extremely hot days. What I can say is that the helmet wouldn’t be my first choice to race enduro with. The ventilation is acceptable for a gravity-based helmet, however, there are higher performers out there in that area, like the Bell Full 10. RIDE IMPRESSIONS There’s no denying the size of the ATB-3. It’s similar to the previous Fox RPC helmet in terms of volume. With that said, the balance is well sorted and it isn’t tiring to ride laps all day. I’m always stoked to find a D-ring closure that has a magnetic clip for the dead end of the strap. I also appreciate when the strap is long enough to loosen up, but leave threaded between the rings. That way, I can remove the helmet in a pinch without totally undoing the strap. As I pointed out, the chinbar sits somewhat closer to your face than I’m used to, and that impedes the field of view. I didn’t have a chance to try the ATB-3 with 6D’s own Max goggle, though. The frame on these goggles is designed to sit against the cutout of the helmet and offer the widest field of view possible. It’s a known fact that MIPS-equipped helmets can creak, even after a few days of ownership, as the liners rub together while bouncing down the trail. After a surprising amount of time spent on downhill bikes this spring, the ATB-3 remains quiet. The ODS system never made a peep. Pros + Unique ODS 3-dimensional impact protection. + Rebuildable inner EPS shell. Cons - Depth of shell is shallow. - Chin bar may sit too close to some riders' faces. - A premium helmet comes with a premium price. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Helmets 6d 6d Atb 3 Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: The Adventure Dog Channel - Training the Ultimate Trail Dog
author: Lexi White
article_text: Photo by Katie Bennett Most anyone who owns both a dog and a mountain bike has dreamed of being chased down the trail by their best furry friend. There’s something special about spending the day pedaling in the woods with your pooch that goes way beyond giving them some exercise. If you’ve ever been out for a ride with your dog, you know the pure joy that comes from watching them run at full tilt to keep up with the bike as you rip down some of your favorite singletrack together. Taking a water break and hydrating from the same bike bottle. Sharing your trail snacks and taking in the views at the top of a climb. High fiving in the parking lot after another awesome ride. It’s just the best. The Adventure Dog Channel by Molly Avrin Details • 195 total vides, 20 bike videos • Avg Video Length: ~6:30 • Total Time Commitment: ~8 hours (for me) • Training hiking, biking, skiing, joring & more • 1x/week members only group virtual session • Price: $36/month or $380/year • petesadventurepack.com But trail dogs can be a controversial subject amongst mountain bikers. With lots of complaints around unruly, uncontrolled dog mayhem on trails that pose a real safety hazard, along with the unmitigated piles of poop that inevitably don’t get picked up, it’s easy to understand why trail dogs often get a bad rap in the MTB community. Much of this controversy could be avoided with a little more time spent on training our trail dogs and giving them the skills they need to hit the trails with us safely. I won’t downplay the time and effort that goes into doing that training, but if you’re going to take your dog out with you on trails that are shared with other riders, making sure you’re able to ride safely is the least you can do, for them and for your dog. Photo by Katie Bennett The Adventure Dog Channel by Molly Avrin Luckily for those interested in turning their four-legged best bud into a responsible trail dog, there are resources available that claim they can help you chase down that goal. Working with a professional trainer in your local area is one way to get knowledge and advice if you’re looking to train a well-mannered trail dog, but if you don’t have access to someone in your area who has experience working with dogs and bikes, you’re not out of luck. Molly Avrin of Pete’s Adventure Pack has created an in-depth digital resource for all things trail dog training – the Adventure Dog Channel – and you can access it from anywhere in the world with monthly and yearly membership options. I've seen, bought, and consumed many different online dog training courses (dog nerd alert!), but I've never run into one that focuses on training your dog in the rules of adventure sports, so this course felt quite unique. A monthly membership will run you $36/month and annual memberships go for $380. So who is Molly, and should you trust and pay her to help you train your dog? Molly runs Pete’s Adventure Pack, a professional dog training business out of Salt Lake City, UT where she offers everything from one-on-one private sessions to full day training camps and group adventure sessions. Molly has over 17 years of experience working with dogs of all types and temperaments and a lifetime of experience adventuring in the outdoors. She says that the Adventure Dog Channel is the culmination of what she's learned with step-by-step training programs for everything from the foundational training basics to more advanced techniques for teaching dogs how to safely ride, ski, hike, and camp. What’s In the Channel The Adventure Dog Channel spans many topics, starting with all the most basic, foundational dog training and life skills a dog would need to be a good companion, let alone a good trail bud. From leash work to recall, the channel offers a lot of upfront guidance on how to work with your dog on basic training before bothering to teach them more advanced skills like how to run next to a bike, which is a nice addition as many of those skills dovetail into the bike work. The Basics Courses: Leash Work Place Yes Work Thresholds Crate Car Recall First Steps E-collar & Transition Off Leash For the dogs who already know the basics pretty well, you can skip right to the fun stuff and dig into the more adventure-focused course, although I’d wager most people and pups would benefit from at least a small refresher on basic skills. There’s surely a few tips in the series for tuning up dogs of all training levels. From there, the channel covers nearly any outdoor activity you could think of doing with your dog. Adventure Courses: • Hiking • Biking • Camping • Backcountry Skiing • XC Skate Skiing • Pulling Sports (Skijoring, Canicross) • Climbing • Horses • Paddleboarding The courses are structured in a series of 2-10 minute videos that all build on each other within the different course topics. The bite-sized nature of the videos within each course make the programs easy to follow and give you small steps to progress through without getting overwhelmed by too much information at once. After you’ve watched and worked through one of the videos, you can mark it as complete and move onto the next. Some videos give you enough to work on that you may not come back for the next one for a few days, but you can easily pick right back up where you left off. For the sake of this review, we’ll stick to the Biking Course content, but in addition to the already-massive library of videos, new content is continuously added to the channel based on requests and feedback from the community. Speaking of community, another really valuable benefit that comes with your Adventure Dog Channel membership is Virtual Group Sessions. Held at least once a week, Molly sends out a Zoom link and invites anyone in the channel to join for live Q&A to help you troubleshoot any issues you’re having while you work through the courses. I think this is one of the best components to an online learning course, especially as it relates to dog training. Every dog is different, and accomplishing anything cool with your dog is rarely as easy as watching a handful of videos and expecting your sessions to go exactly as it was laid out online. Having the opportunity to talk things through, get questions answered, and hear the experience of other people working on the same things really brings it all together for me. Bike Course Breakdown I dove into all of the content on the Adventure Dog Channel throughout summer and applied the techniques in the courses to see if I could help my dog, Murray, become an even better trail dog citizen. Murray and I do a lot of adventuring together, from biking and skiing, to working as a Canine SAR team, so we’re always looking for new ways to sharpen our skills. When you and your dog both hit the same feature and they do it with more style. Murray and I love riding together, but we admittedly hadn’t done too much formal training around it. Since he’s a pretty highly trained dog to begin with, he sort of just coexisted well with me on the trail, and we didn’t think too much about the specifics. So with that in mind, we started from scratch with Molly’s bike videos and applied each step as if we knew nothing about riding together. The bike course has 18 videos, with 2 new videos around introducing puppies to bikes, so currently 20 videos total. 20 videos might sound like a lot, but most of them are 5 minutes or shorter, so the overall watch time isn’t too bad. Starting with leash drills and bike drills in a parking lot and ending with an off-leash single track ride, the course takes you through each step of the process, building up to the ultimate goal of riding safely and sending it on the trail with your dog. It might be tempting to skip some of the parking lot drills and head straight to the trail work, but I think it’s worth it to do your best to stick to the actual program and skip as few steps as possible. Even if you breeze through some of them, just ticking the box is worth it to make sure you’re both sharp before moving on. For me and Murray, practicing riding together on leash was a new experience. We never did that before hitting the trails, and I was reluctant to go through the tediousness of taking steps back. But at the end of the day, making sure those pieces were in place didn’t slow us down much and helped as we got deeper into the course. In addition to the dog training components, Molly offers up some advice on what gear to bring for your dog, choosing good trails for dog rides (directional, low-traffic, well within your skill limit), and general trail etiquette expectations for trail dogs. Some Gear Tips to Consider: • Water • Collapsible water bowl • Poop bags • Slip leash • Dog first aid kit with extra bleed stop (for broken toe nails) • Treats • You can also put a bear bell on your dog if you want to hear where they are behind your bike - this is especially helpful so you make sure you do not let them get too far behind you. I always put a bear bell on Murray when we ride together, and it’s super helpful for keeping track of where he is without having to have eyes on him all the time. It’s also great for other trail users who can hear the bell coming and aren’t blindsided by a dog coming in hot around a corner or at an intersection. I also like this collar from Nite Ize that has a retractable leash built in. It's pretty handy if you find yourself needing to leash up unexpectedly on the side of the trail, walking through the parking lot, or any number of other reasons. Don't forget to hydrate and take breaks on big rides with your dog. Time Commitment Doing anything cool with your dog is going to take training time. There’s really no way around putting in the extra hours to get the results you want. That said, the amount of time it will take to complete this course and have a bonafide goodboy trail dog is going to be different for everyone and every dog. Some dogs will come in totally green and start from scratch. Others will be able to breeze through the first few steps and jump right into the fine tuning. Some dogs will struggle with random components for reasons that are hard to predict and explain (that’s where the virtual group sessions come in handy). Generally speaking, I would go into the journey with a slow and steady mindset. Rushing through things is the perfect recipe for frustration and failure, so take your time and enjoy the process. At the end of the day, it’s just more time spent with your favorite little buddy anyway, so it’s all good. Who Should Join If you’ve been fantasizing about sharing your favorite sport with your favorite pal, but have limited access to local trainers or want a more convenient approach you can tackle on your schedule, give the Adventure Dog Channel a look and consider putting in the extra work to make sure you, your dog, and all the other riders out on the trail are safe when you’re out there riding together. Everyone will find different aspects of the channel valuable, depending on the level of training they and their dog already have, but no matter what place you’re starting from, there are helpful tips and tricks for all dog owners. You could even passive-aggressively buy a month-long membership for your friend who always brings their dog with them and ruins your ride. And if you’ve more or less mastered riding with your dog, but are interested in introducing them to any of the other activities on the list of courses included, the membership would likely be well worth your time. Photo by Katie Bennett Pros and Cons Pros + Progress at your own pace on your own schedule + Learn from anywhere, no need to be local to an area or trainer + Covers a wide range of sports/activities + Virtual group sessions add a lot to the online learning experience Cons - Training independently without real-time oversight and guidance can be difficult for less experienced dog people Pinkbike’s Take The videos in the Adventure Dog Channel are thorough and easy to follow, offering practical and approachable steps to training a solid adventure dog. Having the option to work through a program on your own time without having to worry about scheduling with an in-person trainer is convenient and affordable, especially because the virtual group sessions still give you the opportunity to get live feedback. There’s no one right way to train a dog to do anything, but the lessons outlined in the channel are a great place to start and should give most people and dogs a strong foundation to build on. And whether you jump into this particular product or not, training your dog to have the skills it needs to know what to do and how to behave on a ride means you can both focus less on keeping track of each other and more on having as much fun as possible. Which is why we’re out there in the first place, right? — Lexi White Posted In: Reviews and Tech Tutorials and Guides Reviews Traildog Author Info: Lexi White Durango, CO, United States Member since Aug 29, 2022 1 article

title: Review: The Airdrop Edit MX - No Frills, All Thrills
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: REVIEW Airdrop Edit MX WORDS: Jessie-May Morgan PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson It's a pretty big deal for Airdrop to finally launch a bike with a 29" wheel, having remained true to the 27.5" wheel for much, much longer than almost any other mountain bike brand. The demand could no longer be ignored, however, and many a rider (myself included) will have been rather pleased to see Airdrop launch the Edit MX at the end of last year. The design team never denied the rollover advantages of the 29" wheel, they simply prefer a smaller wheel for its faster, more playful handling. True to that, a dedicated 27.5" Edit frame is also available. What we have here is a 161mm travel enduro bike with a familiar four-bar Horst-link design. The shock stroke can be bumped from 62.5mm to 65mm to deliver 167mm travel. Edit MX Details • Aluminum frame • Rear Wheel Travel: 161mm / 167mm • Fork Travel: 160-170mm • Wheel size: MX • Head Angle: 64º • Seat Angle: 78.1º (at 700mm) • Chainstay: 433mm (S1/2) / 438mm (S3/4) • Weight: 15.75 kg (34.7 lbs) • Price: £5,499 (MX Works, reviewed) • 5 Year Warranty + Lifetime Accidental Damage Policy • airdropbikes.com With an intention-appropriate, modern set of geometry figures, and a warranty recently upped to five years, a starting price of £1,799 for the frame and shock is sure to put the Edit MX on many a rider's shortlist for their next bike. That raw aluminum finish doesn't hurt, either. Airdrop Edit MX Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DK8qzATf Copied Live 00:00 05:28 05:28 Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Also available in Sean Grean The Edit shines on more flowing technical trails. Careful line choice and well-timed weighting and unweighting will reward any rider on any bike, but on the Edit those rewards feel a bit sweeter. — Jessie-May Morgan Construction & Features The Edit MX comprises a custom-butted 6061-T6 alloy tubeset combined with machined pieces making up the one-piece rocker, the lower shock mount, dropout and brake mount, BB shell and the yoke where the chainstays meet the front triangle. Airdrop manufacture frames in small batches, so its economical for them to use machining instead of forging. There's nothing particularly ornate about the machining, but on the underside of the chainstay yoke you can see where little troughs have been machined into the aluminum to run the cables along, out of harms way; they are exclusively externally routed (save for the dropper). Airdrop have two distinct swingarm lengths giving a 433mm rear-center on the S and M frames, and a 438mm rear-center on the L and XL frames. That extra length is introduced with the tubes themselves, not on the machined parts that make up the yoke and the dropout at either end. The frame runs a ZS44/56mm headset, the BB is 73mm threaded, the rear brake mount is positioned for a 200mm rotor with no adaptor required, and there's a UDH dropout that brings compatibility with SRAM T-Type drivetrains. There are also ISCG tabs for the mounting of a bash guard and chain guide. It takes a 34.9mm seatpost, the girthiness of which is preferable in the context of the very long travel dropper posts this frame can work with. Sure enough, a 240mm dropper post will easily find a home on the larger frame sizes. Drive side stay protection is on the more conservative side, but entirely sufficient with little noise from chain slap to report. The main pivot bearing is very exposed to mud thrown from the rear tire, and most riders will want to fashion something around here to ward off the worst of it. A number of brands go to the effort of bolting on some kind of mud flap at this location for that very reason. Of course, there are no mounting points for that on the Edit frame, but there are a number of aftermarket solutions out there. Geometry & Sizing The Airdrop Edit MX is available in sizes S1-S4, with reach figures of 430mm, 455mm, 475mm and 500mm, respectively. Chainstay length does vary across the frame sizes, but certainly not enough for the rear-center length to be considered genuinely proportional to the growing front triangle sizes. The S1 and S2 frames run a 433mm chainstay, while the S3 and S4 frames get a 438mm stay. The Edit MX gets a modern but not overly progressive set of geometry figures. They are also fixed; there is not a single solitary flip-chip to speak of. With the recent flurry of highly-adjustable frames of this genre, it is kind of refreshing to see a brand just say, "this is what we like, we hope you like it too". It can be fun and sometimes educational to play around with geometry, especially when the flip-chips deliver meaningful changes to stay length and angles, etc. But, it's arguably more marvelous to go ride and not have to think about all the "what ifs". A 64º head tube angle is coupled with an effective seat tube angle of 78.1º (at a saddle height of 700mm). As you will see in the geometry chart, Airdrop go to the trouble of specifying a range of effective seat tube angles pertaining to defined saddle heights, which I reckon is the better way of communicating it. Meanwhile, some manufacturers report the effective seat tube angle for an average saddle height for each frame size, which would be fine if only they also published what that average saddle height is. Sigh. A majority straight seat tube, uninterrupted by pivot hardware, welcomes long travel dropper seat posts. Airdrop have a super useful chart on the website that gives you a good idea of what length OneUp V3 you could run in the context of your preferred frame size and saddle height. Suspension Design A four-bar Horst-pivot linkage articulates the rear triangle relative to the front triangle, delivering around 161mm or 167mm of rear wheel travel dependent on the stroke length. With a 205mm x 62.5mm shock (as tested), rear wheel travel is communicated as 161mm, which gives an average leverage ratio of 2.57. The rate remains relatively high through the beginning stroke to sag, with most of the progression coming thereafter. Through the mid-stroke, the leverage ratio is a little higher than we see on some other horst-pivot bikes of this category, and it lends itself well to the linearity of a coil shock. Airdrop do not offer the Edit with an air shock right now, but they say the kinematic works well with one and they intend to offer that as an option in the near future. In the climbing gears, anti-squat is just shy of 100% around sag. That indicates the Edit will not get sucked into its rear wheel travel under pedal-induced accelerations, with the shock remaining relatively still as you spin away up a climb. Anti-rise, the term used to describe how a bike's suspension will behave under rear braking, sits quite consistently low at 25-35% through the full stroke. That is a fair bit lower than most Horst-pivot utilizing bikes of this category. The value indicates that, while braking, the rear-end will have an increased tendency to extend (come up out of its travel), rather than compress, as compared to other bikes with higher anti-rise values. Within an acceptable range, the level of anti-rise seems to be a matter of personal preference. With low anti-rise, the suspension should stay higher in its travel where leverage ratios are higher and its easier to compress the shock, something that in theory should give the bike improved small bump sensitivity. On the flip-side, it also keeps the bike's BB high, which can contributing to pitching sensations on steep slopes, necessitating more support from the fork to keep the front-end high. Higher anti-rise bikes are better able to preserve geometry under braking, but the rear suspension can feel less supple. An extreme example of that comes in the form of the Auckland Cycle Works Marra - there, anti-rise never drops below 100%, and sits around 170% at sag. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $5716 Travel 161mm (205mm x 62.5mm) Rear Shock Ohlins TTX Coil Fork Ohlins RXF 38 m.2 Cassette SRAM XS-1295 10-52 T-Type Crankarms SRAM X01 Eagle T-Type 165mm Rear Derailleur SRAM X01 AXS T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM AXS POD Ultimate Handlebar Burgtec Enduro Wide Enduro Stem Burgtec 35mm Grips Burgtec Bartender Pro Lock-on Brakes SRAM Code Ultimate Wheelset Reserve 30 Aluminum Hubs Hope PRO5 Tires Michelin Wild Enduro MS Seat WTB Volt Fusion Seatpost OneUp V3 Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Riders with big feet may consider running pedals with a longer spindle for heel clearance at the stays RIDING THE Airdrop Edit MX Test Bike Setup I tested the Airdrop Edit MX Works in S1, with a 430mm reach. The Ohlins TTX shock, fitted with a 320 lbs/in spring, gave me a sag of 30% fully kitted. For the Ohlins RXF 38 m.2 fork, I stuck with the manufacturer's recommendation of 171 PSI in the ramp chamber, and 80 PSI in the main chamber. I felt no need to deviate from that. The shock has three high speed compression damping settings, with 16 clicks of LSC adjustment and 7 clicks of rebound. A notable amount of force is required to move the LSC dial, which is a bit of a pain. That said, I consistently ran the shock open on compression and rebound. That was mostly the case for the fork, too, but I did feel the need to wind on some compression damping for the fork on the steepest, roughest tracks to keep the bike from feeling too pitched. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 130 lbs / 59 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan A 35mm stem was paired with a 30mm rise Burgtec Ride Wide Enduro bar, cut to my preferred 740mm. I like a relatively low bar height for a short effective stack height, as a general rule. I slammed the stem to its lowest position on the steerer. I probably could've benefited from an even lower bar height, but was limited by the rise of the bar and the headtube length (it is a little taller than you get on most other small bikes). I was spoiled with the ability to run a 180mm travel dropper on this one, give me all the clearance I could ever wish for. I switched out the Burgtec saddle for a dinner plate from SQlab. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place over the last three/four months in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, on the enduro and downhill tracks around Innerleithen. Trail conditions were largely very wet, but with some slightly drier, greasier days on the hill as things began to dry out. The bike was sent to me with Michelin Wild Enduro MS tires. I didn't get on well with the stiff, heavy casing, finding that the lack of suppleness translated to poor traction over wet rocks and roots. At 20 PSI, the rear tire also burped air and sealant consistently, something I tend to experience less of with tires of a more supple casing. Airdrop were happy for me to switch these out for a Maxxis Assegai (EXO+), DHR II (DD) pairing for the rest of the test period. This Trailforks route includes a bunch of trails that I used for the review. Climbing The steep seat tube angle of the Edit MX is comfortable on a long climb. It biases the rider's weight forward sufficiently to do away with any concerns of unintended front wheel lift when the trail gets extra steep. That's because it naturally pushes more weight onto the rider's hands, which can be uncomfortable on very long, flat sections. The seat angle isn't as steep as some bikes, and that's a good thing. I think Airdrop have found a good balance in that most riders should be accommodated for. It's worth noting that at a saddle height of 800mm, the effective seat angle is still a healthy 77.7°. Riding consistent gradient forest road climbs, I was happy enough with my saddle positioned centrally on the rails. So, there is scope to effectively steepen the seat angle if I need to winch up very steep technical singletrack to get to my favorite trails. On the flip-side, there is scope to effectively slacken the seat angle if my rides were to all of a sudden be more undulating. The Edit MX is a sufficiently proficient technical climber, thanks to that steep seat tube angle. Spinning up the forest road, there is small amount of movement from the shock but nothing that is remotely concerning. Descending I was comfortable on the Edit MX from the very first ride, which entailed riding some of the more technical and challenging trails that I might ride on a regular basis. That in and of itself is a notable positive, and a clear indicator of a bike that is both appropriately spec'd and fits well. The 430mm reach paired with a 35mm stem gives a nice compact cockpit that places me in a commanding position from which I can be sufficiently dynamic to effect quick changes in weight distribution, grip and direction. My overall impression of the Edit MX is very good. It's not often that I get on quite so well with a test bike on its first outing. The 64° head angle isn't quite as slack as some modern enduro bikes, but I think that unless the vast majority of your riding is on trails that are better-described as DH race tracks, or trails that are cut to go almost straight down the fall-line, 64° is as confidence-inspiring as most riders would ever need. The front-to-rear balance of the bike is such that I don't feel the need to over-exaggerate weighting of the front wheel, as I might with the longer front-centre that a slacker head angle would bring to the table. Rear wheel traction is plentiful in all but the most challenging of circumstances. When the trail pitches down a rock- and root-strewn fall line, and consistent braking is required to manage speed, the bike has a tendency to feel a little tall in the rear-end. The back end can feel a bit light and more liable to deflection. I find myself sitting further back in an attempt to keep weight down through the back tire, and just generally riding with a bit more caution in those sections. Counteracting that slightly, I felt some benefit to winding on some compression damping at the fork, keeping the front end a little higher on the steep sections. The suspension can feel a little harsh and jarring with square-edged impacts. It remains composed though, with no bucking sensations as it rebounds from deep in the travel. When travelling fast over mellower-gradient, rough ground, the Edit MX doesn't feel quite as calm as some mid-pivot bikes like the Hope HB 916 where the axle path is considerably more rearward. That's not to say it feels vulnerable; I still feel sufficiently confident on the bike to push on through these choppy sections. On the plus side, the consistently shorter rear-end of the Edit MX has a little more life to it, making it a bit more chuck-able on more flowing trails that allow for a more playful, care-free riding style. The Edit shines on more flowing technical trails. Careful line choice and well-timed weighting and unweighting will reward any rider on any bike, but on the Edit those rewards feel a bit bigger. Airdrop Edit MX Marin Alpine Trail XR How does it compare? Riders aren't exactly drowning in options for quality, well-considered, sensibly-priced enduro bikes right now. But, the Airdrop Edit MX and the Marin Alpine Trail (reviewed recently ) stand out as two bikes that can be accurately described as such - and both are sold with a 5-year warranty. I've had the benefit of having both of these bikes in to review simultaneously, and have jumped back and forth between the two a few times. To my mind, both are great bikes and I'd happily recommend either. The Airdrop pips the Marin on playfulness, and feels a little more rewarding on flow trails, as well as being easier to manage on slower-speed trails with really tight turns. The Marin (in its stock geometry setting), on the other hand, is more comfortable to ride when the track gives way to the fall-line. All that said, the Marin is easily the more versatile of the two, with scope to meaningfully alter BB height and chainstay length, as well as head angle. I cannot reasonably finish this comparison without pointing out that, while you can get a proper size water bottle on every size of the Airdrop, you cannot fit one on the small Alpine Trail (an unforgiveable oversight for many). On value for money, the Airdrop wins, but only just. The Deluxe model which retails at £4,399 ($4,573) fetches you the Rockshox Zeb Ultimate fork, Super Deluxe Ultimate coil shock (with your choice of spring), a SRAM GX Eagle group, a OneUp V3 post, WTB Proterra i30 Tough wheels, SRAM Code Silver brakes and a Burgtec finishing kit. Meanwhile, the Marin Alpine Trail XR which goes for £4,445 ($4,599) is built with the same Rockshox Ultimate suspension components and drivetrain, SRAM DB8 brakes, Marin's own-brand wheelset and finishing kit, and a TranzX dropper. But, if you value having plethora geometry adjustments, the Marin of course wins hands down. The Airdrop's ride feel compares favorably to some other MX enduro bikes, too, including the Vitus Sommet CRX and the YT Capra Core 3. Note that Airdrop's USD pricing is based on current exchange rates, so actual price will vary as of the date of publication Which Model is the Best Value? The Edit MX Works I tested is excellent on value. I think the £5,499 ($5,716) price tag is more than fair, especially when you consider the quality of the Ohlins suspension components and Burgtec finishing kit, the OneUp V3 post and, last but not least, the Reserve HD 30 Alloy wheelset with Hope PRO5 hubs and a lifetime warranty on the rims. Next down the price list is the Deluxe model (detailed above in the "How does it Compare" section), retailing at £4,399 ($4,573). I'd steer clear of that one if you're lightweight (≤60 kg), as the Charger 3 damper on the RockShox Zeb Ultimate doesn't give sufficient rebound speed for good, reliable traction. Otherwise, it seems like good value for money with powerful brakes, an adjustable length dropper from OneUp and Burgtec bits in the cockpit. That said, the most affordable one is the Edit MX Luxe, priced at £3,699 ($3,845). That's a tremendously attractive price point for a solid performing bike with a capable set of geometry figures. It gets the RockShox Zeb Select fork, Super Deluxe Select+ coil shock, a SRAM GX drivetrain, SRAM Code Bronze brakes, and WTB Proterra i30 Tough wheels with a WTB Verdict / Judge tire combo. Folk with good inventory kicking about might consider a frame and shock only. Starting price there is $1,870 with the RockShox Super Deluxe RC2T coil. Frame and Ohlins TTX coil goes for $2,078. At the very top end you can buy the frame with the new EXT Storia Lok V3 coil shock for £2,299 ($2,487). Technical Report Ohlins RXF 38 m.2 & TTX Coil Shock : The fork and shock performed really well and the manufacturer's pressure recommendations for the fork were spot on. I did run the compression and rebound fully open on both, however, so I do wonder whether there's improved performance to be had from a lighter damper tune for light riders. SRAM Code Stealth ULT : Plentifully powerful, and the tool-free reach and bite point adjustments are nice to have, though the latter seized up early on. Burgtec Bartender Pro Lock-On Grips: A reasonable length for small hands, but I found the rubber to be too hard and bouncy for my liking. Pros + Very capable on fast, technical, challenging terrain + Well-balanced + Good value for money + Two permissible travel configurations + Low standover and room for long travel dropper seat posts Cons - Can feel tall and a little unsettled while braking on steep terrain - Main pivot bearings are very exposed - No geometry adjustments Pinkbike's Take The Edit MX is no frills, all thrills. Straight out of the box, this is a well-rounded bike with carefully considered components that will bring much satisfaction to those who just want to ride without overthinking at every turn. I found it wasn't as easy to ride as some enduro bikes of similar stature, but it can be adapted to over time. The Edit MX is capable of handling some very challenging and intimidating terrain, with a geometry that imparts confidence on its rider to do so. And, the excellent value for money is undeniable. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Airdrop Edit Mx Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: The Arctos Epic is a Unique High-Volume Hip Pack
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Arctos Epic 14.4 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/8gYQK5yA Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 04:52 For those who enjoy long days out on the bike, the problem of how to carry food, water, and gear can have an outsized effect on the success and ease of a given objective. As a result, there always seem to be novel approaches to this prompt, from on-bike baggage to the infinite variety of backpack-type bags on the market. A new addition to the list of options is the Epic 14.4 from Arctos Packs, which features a massive capacity for both storage and water, with a lumbar-style weight carry and shoulder straps for stability. Arctos Epic 14.4 Details • 14 liter total storage • 4 liter water storage • Oversized YKK zippers • Material: 210D ripstop nylon • Weight: 1142g/2.5lb w/ bladder assembly • Price: $175 USD • arctospacks.com The Epic 14.4 features 14 liters of gear capacity, with a dedicated 4 liter water bladder. Weight is kept as low as possible, with the shoulder carry only involved in stabilizing the pack and holding the water hose. The design is reminiscent of a wildland firefighter's pack, which employs a similar carry method to distribute weight and allow for easy upper-body movement. Fit & Packing I'm around 6'3", with what I'd call a narrow build. Typically hip packs end up being mid-torso packs if they're not cinched down tight enough, and they're just as liable to rotate around my waist if not loaded enough to keep the back planted. All that in mind, the fit of the Arctos pack was lower than I initially anticipated, sitting below the waist and directly above the hip joint. This is their recommended setup, as is a loose fit on the shoulder straps. Finding a comfortable fit was easy for my 32" waist and taller torso, but I wanted to see how it fared on a different body. My girlfriend is about 5'5", and she found the pack to sit a bit too high on the hips, and took up more room than desired. Addressing that fit issue, Arctos already has an Epic 10.3 pack in the works, with 10 liters of storage and a 3 liter bladder. The smaller overall size should work better for smaller bodies, though the low-slung position might not suit folks with big butts. Five zippered pockets, one slide-in stash pocket. The easy-access hip pockets are huge. Packing the Arctos Epic was easy, thanks to the relatively massive volume. As I'll get to in the comparisons, there are some larger items that won't fit in the pack, but standard tools, jackets, and food will easily fit in the main pockets and side stashers alike. Compared to other hip packs and backpacks I've ridden with, the Epic feels like a crammer's dream, with big, uninterrupted pockets you can stuff without too much thought. Those who like to dial in their internal organization might be bummed at the lack of options here, but there's room for bags-within-bags if you're wanting to keep em' separated. Four liter bladder and mega pocket. This fairly narrow opening hides another very large pocket. Small soft pocket for glasses, crystals, lizards. Foam standoffs and a rigid back panel keep things in place. Ride Impressions The 14 liter nugget that is the Arctos Epic can get pretty heavy, with 4 liters of water and whatever gear you might be packing, but you wouldn't know it once you've strapped it on. That was the most notable first impression I had of the pack, and it continued to impress through the test period. The large side pockets allow you to distribute the load well over your hips, and once dialed in the fit keeps the weight squarely settled down low. Wide webbing, thick materials, and well-placed compression straps keep things fairly well secured, with the shoulder harness handling the side-to-side. I never found the weight to be too low or rearward on climbs, which was a pleasant surprise given how the pack feels when standing upright. Like any pack, you could put all the weight in the wrong place and screw up the balance, but I found the Arctos spreads the base water weight very well, thanks to the rectangular bladder. Very steep trails are where hip packs can get the most out of sorts, sliding around and jumping up the body as you move and groove. I found the Arctos sat still better than most, though the pack did shift more than the vest-style packs I've come to use more. The shoulder straps keep the pack from jostling side to side, but the pack can still shimmy up when all your weight's on your hands and the catch berm at the bottom is your only goal. Of course, that's not the most typical use case out there, so it's key to mention that when you're ripping around upright, the Epic 14.4 feels stable and comfortable, with a secure fit that stays in place off jumps and drops alike. Sweat seems to be a big reason people ditch backpacks and vests, but in my mind it's more relocation than elimination. Hip packs - especially those with large capacity - are just as warm, it's just lower on the body. The Arctos has a decent footprint on the lower back, so expect some perspiration there. The foam risers do a good job of keeping most of the shell fabric from making contact, but nothing quite compares to the packless feel. The Mission Workshop Hauser 14L, with a full set of Trail Boss tools inside. The Arctos Epic 14.4 in attack position. Comparisons My go-to pack for short and long rides alike is the Camelback Chase Vest. The size is ideal for my use cases, and I've never found the fit of it to be uncomfortable or cumbersome. It can require some creative packing to get a full day's worth of gear inside, but rarely am I left wanting for more room. That said, there are certainly circumstances where a bigger pack is warranted. On those days, I usually reach for the Mission Workshop Hauser, a 14 liter backpack with a more traditional silhouette. The Chase Vest weighs around 460 grams with a 3L bladder, and the Hauser weighs about 950 grams with the same bladder. My trusty little Chase vest. The Henty Enduro pack. The Hauser is the closer proxy to the Arctos in terms of storage capacity and overall weight, so that'll be the primary comparison here. The most notable difference between the two is the orientation of the storage - vertical for the Hauser, horizontal for the Epic. The Epic keeps the weight low and away from your head, with no interference with your helmet on steep trails and features. The Hauser does push against the back of the helmet if you have the fit cinched down, and carries the weight in a generally higher spot on the back. My primary use for larger packs is to haul trail tools, namely those from Trail Boss. The segmented handles and heads are perfect for toting in and out of dig days, and have been in heavy use this fall. This is where a traditional pack is hard to beat, as the Arctos simply can't fit long rigid items, despite the 14 liter capacity. Though I haven't used it myself, the Henty Enduro pack came to mind when I first saw the Arctos design. RC reviewed one of these a while back, and was quite a fan of the water capacity and comfort. The key differences come down to storage capacity and weight, as the Henty's 5 liter capacity is a good deal smaller than the Arctos' 14, but the 550 gram weight also halves that of the Arctos. Pros + Nothing else offers this capacity and carry position + Much more stable than hip pack + Carries weight well Cons - Could use more internal organization - Pockets are cavernous, but won't fit large tools - Looks are sure to be polarizing Pinkbike's Take For my purposes, the vest I use on most rides has become a reliable companion, with a fit that works well for me. That said, if I lived somewhere where water were more scarce, I can imagine the Arctos coming into steadier rotation, thanks to the more comfortable weight carry it provides. Three liters in the vest can get cumbersome, four isn't really an option, and gear storage is pretty minimalistic. Again, that program works for me, but if you're someone who likes to bring a ton of items on a given ride, then the Epic 14.4 is worth a closer look. — Dario DiGiulio Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Packs Arctos Arctos Epic 144 Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Chromag Throwdown Review: Too Slack or Just Right?
author: Matt Beer
article_text: The lines between bike categories are constantly blurring. Lately, long-travel, aluminum freeride bikes have been on the rise, be it enduro, downhill, or park bikes. Before we get any further, let’s define what a “park bike” is. Park bikes come in various forms, from short travel, like the Zink Vacay, to the cushy Devinci Chainsaw. They focus on hitting jumps and features with confidence, whereas enduro and downhill are tailored to their specific forms of racing. That’s not to say that a custom-built version of an enduro or downhill bike couldn’t be reshaped into park bikes, but it will take some component modification, like a smaller rear wheel, or possibly making the head tube angle steeper. In fact, some variants of the bikes mentioned are available from the factories in those forms. Throwdown Details • 170mm travel, 170mm fork • Wheel size: 29" front, 27.5" (mixed) • Aluminum frame • Dual-crown compatible • 63.5° head tube angle • 435mm chainstays (S-M/L), 442mm (L, XL) • Sizes: S, M, M/L (tested), L, XL • Weight: 17.5 kg / 38.5 lb (measured, M/L w/Eagle 90 build) • Price: $5,250 USD / $6,950 CAD (as tested) • Frame only: $2,750 USD / $3,450 CAD • chromagbikes.com The Chromag Throwdown enters the game with a big, honking frame with 170mm of travel front and rear that’s built Whistler-tough. It’s essentially a choppered-out enduro bike capable of handling lapping the bike park all day long. At the same time, the Throwdown can still pedal to those secret stashes which a downhill bike would have a tougher time accessing Chromag Throwdown Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/W6SmFt7W Copied Live 00:00 16:18 16:18 Contents Introduction Pinkbike's Take Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Technical Report Pros & Cons If you regularly ride a chairlift or shuttle, but love the simplicity of one bike, then the Chromag Throwdown is a quality choice. The comfort of the suspension and sturdy frame gives the calmness of a downhill bike without killing the buzz on jump trails. It wouldn't be my first choice as a do-it-all enduro bike - there are more well-rounded choices out there, but it can get the job done, just at a slower pace uphill. With that said, the parts on the Eagle 90 build make it a worthy contender for anyone seeking out this type of niche bike. — Matt Beer Frame Details If you’re familiar with Chromag’s full suspension bikes, you’ll recognize the Throwdown from a mile away with the huge rocker link and prominent head tube badge. It’s not a light frame to begin with. The Throwdown frame moves the needle to 4.5 kg (9.9 lb) for a M/L, a few hundred grams more than the Raaw Madonna R, and like that bike, the bearings and hardware never missed a beat throughout the review. As I mentioned in the First Ride, the Throwdown is the first adult-size bike from the Whistler-based company to use an aluminum front triangle. The features follow Chromag’s no-B.S. ethos. That means external cable routing from top to bottom, water bottle bosses for supplies, and zero adjustment points - minimal faff, as they say. On that note, the rear wheel size is limited to a 27.5”. Two areas that could use refinement are the cable clamping zone behind the head tube gusset and the short gap along the chainstay protector. Otherwise, the chainslap protector does an incredible job of taming noises, even over the worst braking bumps. Suspension Design Following suit with the frame’s functionality is the four-bar, Horst-link suspension layout. Chromag’s choice of pivot points gives a very supple, cushioned ride that has no irregularities. The result is a predictable, comfortable ride in all trail scenarios. When it comes to pedalling performance, the Throwdown behaves as you’d expect for a long-travel park weapon. There’s some bob when sprinting, but it holds fairly still when buckled in on long road climbs. Chromag specifies the Vivid air shock on the Throwdown, however, a coil shock is said to pair nicely too. Huge impacts are softened by the Vivid and its adjustable hydraulic bottom-out (ABO) feature. I found the tune to match decently well, but looked for additional support once I learned how the Throwdown handles and speeds increased (more on that later). Geometry I was stoked to see that Chromag didn’t shortchange riders when it came to finding their ideal size. They make all of their full-suspension frames in five sizes, from size Small, all the way up to XL, including the seldom seen M/L. It’s worth noting that Chromag’s sizing may be larger or have a longer reach number than you’re used to. I’ll talk a lot about the balance of the Throwdown because that’s what separates it from the Lowdown, Chromag’s enduro bike. Both bikes have slack head tube angles and long reach numbers for their frame size labels, though the Lowdown is a smidge longer (490mm). It’s the same story at the back of the bike. The Throwdown chainstay is 435mm (442mm on the L and XL frames), 5mm shorter than the Lowdown. The drop-down to a smaller wheel size compounds the effect of the short, snappy rear end. On the geometry chart, the head tube angle reads 63.5, but it seems slacker on the trail. Everything checked out when I measured the head tube angle using an iPhone angle finder (however precise that may be). Specifications Specifications Release Date 2026 Price $5250 Travel 170 Rear Shock RockShox Vivid Ultimate Fork RockShox Zeb Select+, 170mm, 44mm offset Headset Cane Creek 40 ZS44/ZS56 Cassette SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Crankarms SRAM 90 Transmission 165mm, 30-tooth ring Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA Rear Derailleur SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Chain SRAM GX Eagle 90 12 Speed T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM Eagle 90 Transmission Handlebar Chromag OSX 35, 25mm rise Stem Chromag HiFi BSX 35, 35mm length Grips Chromag Format Brakes SRAM Maven B1 Bronze w/Centerline rotors 200mm F&R Wheelset Chromag BA30 V2 w/R6 hubs Tires Maxxis DHF 29x2.5" DD, Maxx Grip (f)/DHR II 27.5x2.4" DD, Maxx Grip (r) Seat Chromag Trailmaster Seatpost OneUp V3, S 150mm; M 180mm; M/L 210mm; L 210mm; XL 240mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup Chromag’s sizing chart places me (178cm) smack dab in the middle of the elusive M/L frame. At 485mm, the reach is on the upper limit of what I can ride; however, once I placed 20mm of spacers under the stem, the cockpit felt more within reason. I followed Chromag’s suspension setup guide, and found the air pressures for my weight (78kg) to be on the softer, plusher side. I found that the rear shock rode better when inflated to read 27% sag, while the fork remained at 14% sag. After fiddling with the air pressure and ABO feature on the Vivid, I added a third volume spacer to the air can. That provided the resistance I needed as the shock neared full travel. In the saddle zone, I switched out a few components. The M/L review bike came with a 180mm dropper post, which usually fits me well, but additional clearance was needed, so I installed a 210mm dropper. Chromag did state that it's possible to contact their customer service team when ordering a bike and request this change. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 39 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r I also added an Aenomaly SwitchGrade, which some keen-eyed readers picked up on in the First Ride article. I found that the sheer size of the Trailmaster saddle could get in the way of the Throwdown's descending and jumping purposes. Testing Info: During most of my rides on the Throwdown, I set out with the intent to hit the steepest trails or tackle serious features. Although I didn't cover the exact route on the map below with the Throwdown, the trails listed encompass the terrain I did ride regularly. These are where Throwdown thrives - places that you want a downhill bike on the descents, but couldn't be bothered pushing one up to the entrance. Download the Trailforks App Climbing I’ll kick off the climbing chat by stating that the Throwdown climbs as expected for a monster truck. I didn’t expect it to breeze uphill at a blistering pace. Sticky tires and a hefty weight of 17.5 kg (38.6 lb) mean a slow and steady approach on road climbs. One way to make the most of the uphill performance in a bike of this nature is to prop it up for the rider with a steep seat tube angle, which the Throwdown has. 78-degrees drives your legs straight down onto the cranks, rather than slouching off the back of the saddle. The seat tube angle is a big win for this bike, as the short chainstays have a tendency to wheelie when trying to winch up small punches. The 27.5” rear wheel doesn’t help either. It has a tendency to hand up easier than 29er and doesn’t offer as much traction. Climbing becomes more tedious when the trail tightens up. As I pointed out earlier, the head tube angle seems slacker than stated, and that calls for strong arms and core strength to keep the front wheel on track up sharp switchback corners. Big travel helps out in the traction department as the suspension glues itself to the ground. A+ there. It doesn’t bob too much for a 170mm travel bike, but the ground clearance is low. I had a hard time cleaning climbs that I can tackle on other bikes of this stature. I’m really kicking the Throwdown when it comes to climbing, because it’s certainly not its forté. I’d rank it down next to other big-travel enduro bikes, like the Devinci Chainsaw and Norco Range. Luckily, each of those bikes absolutely mows rocks when it's time to turn around and head down. Descending Right, time for the fun stuff, going downhill. A lot of enduro bikes get overwhelmed when faced with the speed and impacts of a bike park. Sometimes they rattle you to death, or the braking bumps eat them alive. Not on the Throwdown, though. The comfort and confidence to charge in the nastiest pinball sections rival most downhill bikes. Bump absorption is the Throwdown’s finest factor. I found a similar characteristic with the Reazon, Chromag’s 135mm travel steel trail bike. But this struck me as a surprise - I didn’t expect that quality to be reflected in the Throwdown’s aluminum frame. There’s very little bump or chain feedback transmitted through the rider’s feet. In fact, I’d argue that flat pedal riders could have an easier time keeping their feet attached because of that. I found the rear suspension had a tendency to ride deep and tag the bottom often. Ramping up air pressure, increasing the air spring progression, and closing low-speed compression tamed that habit. It helped even out the support and balance on flatter trails. Otherwise, it’s far too easy to fall behind the rear wheel and fold the rear tire in a corner. Even with those changes, the handling of the Throwdown can be funky on mellow tracks. You’ll want to exaggerate how much you place on the bars, or potentially face sliding the front tire. The same goes on jump lines, like Dirt Merchant, for example. It borders on too slack to ride up steep lips and tuck the long wheelbase into short landings. That said, specific landing zones aren’t always needed on a bike with this much cushion. All of that makes it ironic for Chromag to call the Throwdown a park bike, because I’d peg it as more of a freeride bike: the faster and gnarlier, the more confidence and control you have. Think of plunging down chundery chutes, death gripping berms full of holes, or lining up the steepest slabs. It’s the type of bike that is capable of huge features. It saved my ass more than once. What's the Best Value? There are four ways to purchase a Throwdown: two enduro builds, one pure gravity kit, or a frame only. The last option offers moderate value too, but I have a tough time believing you could match the price tag on the Eagle 90 component spread. The “Park” build, as Chromag calls it, with its dual-crown fork and narrow-range drivetrain, comes in at a reasonable price, but appeals to a very specific type of rider. It's not quite a downhill bike, yet you likely won't be travelling uphill much unless you're walking. For those reasons, I'd award the best value to one of the uphill-friendly versions: the Eagle 90 build. This delivers everything you need in a hard-charging 170mm-travelbike, without the extra fluff. It's stocked with strong brakes, tough wheels, and stellar suspension. Performance-wise, the components give you the versatility of an enduro bike with RockShox’s cushiest suspension features. ABO is found at both ends, though the mechanisms operate in separate ways. The Vivid delivers a hydraulic bump-stop, while the Zeb Select+ gives you the elastomer bottom-out control. Plus, you can do X-ups with a single crown fork. Shifting duties are carried out by a SRAM 90 T-type derailleur without the hassle of charging batteries. In the braking department, SRAM Maven Bronze brakes have been highly rated by all of Pinkbike’s editors. Those could be beefed up with larger, thicker rotors if needed. Eagle 90: T-type life without the batteries. Raaw Madonna R Fox Factory Kit: $5,690 USD / $7,690 CAD. Weight: 17.7 kg / 39 lb (measured). Santa Cruz Nomad 90 : $6,099 USD / $8,299 CAD. Weight: 35.15 lbs / 15.94 kg (size M). Devinci Chainsaw GX 12 : $4,299 USD / $5,299 CAD. Weight: 16.3 kg / 35.9 lb (Size M). Transition Bottlerocket : $4,699 USD / $6,499 CAD. Weight: 39.4 lb / 17.9 kg (size L). How Does It Compare? The Throwdown reminds me a lot of the Devinci Chainsaw for two reasons: the geometry and the uphill-to-downhill performance. They're both made from aluminum, can handle 190mm dual-crown forks, and have 170mm of rear wheel travel. One trick that the Chainsaw has over the Throwdown is the ability to carry either a 27.5" or 29" rear wheel. When it comes to geometry, they're cut from the same cloth with very slack, stretched-out front centers, and they don't really come to life until you pick up the pace or push into very steep terrain. Pivot placement is what separates them the most, though. The Chainsaw has a high-pivot suspension, which produces a rearward axle path. Even though the static chainstay measurement of the Chainsaw seems short on paper, it lengthens as it settles into the travel. That makes it a hair more balanced at speed, but also slightly harder to pop off the ground. For some riders seeking composure over reactivity, it could be exactly what they're looking for in a freeride bike Another popular bike that I see in bike parks, that isn't a downhill bike, is the Santa Cruz Nomad. I know it's not aluminum, but the head tube angle and travel numbers are in line with the Throwdown. The carbon construction has pros and cons. On one hand, you get in-frame storage and save weight. The downside is that the Nomad's tubing can't handle a dual-crown fork. It's also less comfortable in the park (though I haven't ridden the Nomad V7 in the bike park). Carbon also makes the Nomad more expensive, which means you'll have to spend $2,000 to get an equal fork and brakes as the Throwdown Eagle 90. Lastly, the Raaw Madonna R is a close relative of the Throwdown with a utilitarian ethos: an aluminum frame and four-bar, Horst-link suspension design. The Madonna R can hold either rear wheel size thanks to a flip-chip at that lower shock mount. Even with the smaller rear wheel installed, the handling is a world away. Raaw opted for a steeper 64-degree head tube angle, finding stability from a tall stack height and longer chainstay. The result is an even weight distribution across the axles that handles better than the Throwdown at low speed, though the margin for error when blitzing bike park berms is tighter. Technical Report Chromag OSX35 25mm rise handlebar: The long and low front center left me maxing out the stem height on the steer tube. The 25mm rise bar isn't that low, but it's lower than by today's standards. Swapping a handlebar isn't the end of the world, but I'd like to see a 35mm rise as stock equipment, or a longer steer tube - you can always cut it down, if needed. SRAM Maven B1 Bronze: When SRAM debuted the Mavens, I was proud of them. Finally, a brake that was too strong (for some riders). Although there are ways to tune the power with rotor size, diameter, shape, and pad material, they launched the Maven B1s with a modified lever pivot design and 4 x 18mm pistons per caliper, instead of 2x 18mm pistons and 2x 19.5mm pistons. Chromag chose these for the Throwdown. Metallic pads and 200mm Centerline rotors work just fine for the most part, but I wouldn't have said no to more power. HS2 rotors would quickly boost the power if that's what you need. Chromag R6 hubs and BA30 V2 rims: The R6 hubs are a newer piece from Chromag. They target faster engagement and longer service intervals. During my time on them, they functioned perfectly, never missing a beat or producing any popping noises. In terms of the hoops, the BA30 V2 rims are seriously strong. I didn't need to touch the spokes once during the review. They shook off any potential dings without a worry. Pros + Extremely comfortable. + A confidence-building bike. + Good value in Eagle 90 build kit. Cons - Geometry may not align with what some riders consider a "park bike". - Cable routing under the head tube gusset could use some refinement. Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Chromag Bikes Chromag Throwdown Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 610 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: The CounterSycle Tuned Mass Damper
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: CounterSycle Tuned Mass Damper Review.mp4 0 seconds of 6 minutes, 42 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/JAPWGLpr Copied Auto 270p 1080p 720p 406p 270p 180p Live 00:00 06:42 06:42 More Videos 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close A new category of bike component quietly hit the World Cup DH circuit in 2024; the Tuned Mass Damper. While new to mountain bikes, this is not a new technology in general. It is a very well-understood technology that has proved incredibly useful in improving stability in ships, skyscrapers, and even F1 cars. So much so in the latter that the FIA banned the use of TMDs after it became evident that Alonso's Renault was benefitting from a big competitive advantage over cars without one. But, what on earth is it? What is a Tuned Mass Damper? A TMD is a device that reduces vibrations in a structure by counteracting its own resonant frequency. Every structure in existence, from a skyscraper to a human body to a mountain bike, has its own resonant frequency. In skyscrapers, the TMD inside it is tuned to resonate at that same frequency. As the skyscraper oscillates in the aftermath of a large gust of wind, or an earthquake even, the weight inside the TMD moves counter to the skyscraper's direction of sway - out of phase with it - and in doing so, it reduces the maximum amplitude of the sway. CounterSycle Details • Applications: DH & trail riding • Main performance claims: Improves traction and reduces forces that reach the rider • Mounts to the steerer tube (Enduro) • Dimensions: 112mm long, 34mm diameter • Weight: 550g (DH) / 380g (Enduro, tested) • Price: $275 USD (includes choice of bracket) • countershox.com In F1 cars, TMDs were advantageous because they worked to ensure the car was in an aerodynamically favorable position more of the time. As the car's chassis was deflected away from its most aerodynamically favorable position, by acceleration, or a bump on the road for example, the TMD positioned somewhere between the two front wheels was able to bring the nose down earlier, making the car faster. The CounterShox CounterSycle For the avoidance of any doubt, there is no hydraulic damping going on inside the CounterSycle tube - Seb measured the temperature of it immediately after a run of the DH track, and it was the same temperature as our static weight control. Inside the tube a brass piston is oscillating between two springs. The frequencies of vibration it is tuned to attenuate are in the region of 8-10 hz (not the higher frequency vibrations that we associate with a buzzing in the bar). Mounted vertically via the steerer tube, the CounterSycle's weight is displaced inside the unit when your front wheel hits a bump. As the force is transmitted through the fork and the frame accelerates upwards, the weight actually moves down relative to the tube, out of phase with it, as the lower spring compresses. The designer, Nathan Looney, tells us that the unit is directional. He says that in order to avoid bottom-out clunk, the lower spring is twice as long as the upper spring. The upper spring serves only to 'catch' the brass weight as it is thrown upwards by the lower spring's rebound, pushing it back onto the lower spring to reset. CounterShox market the CounterSycle TMD as a device to improve chassis stability, improve traction, and reduce rider fatigue through allowing the bike's suspension to work more efficiently. They have had a similar unit in production for motocross for a few years now, but only recently have they turned their attention to mountain bikes. Impressively, their debut entry went straight to the pointy end of the sport; a significant proportion of the World Cup DH field equipped their bike with the CounterSycle last season, including Amaury Pierron, Myriam Nicole, and Loris Vergier. Installation Getting the CounterSycle set up is fairly straight-forward once you've figured out the clearance above and below the unit. The mount consists of a bracket with two clamps; one that clamps to your steerer tube as a 5mm spacer, the other clamping the TMD. Both Seb and I had to mount the TMD offset of center to allow it to clear the bottom of the stem. I don't foresee any insurmountable compatibility issues with it, though riders with a very short head tube who like to run a very low stem position will want to be mindful of the clearance between the base of the TMD and the arch of the fork at full compression. Our TMD was 117mm long and mounting it in its lowermost position (with the clamp high up on the shaft) leaves 107mm of the TMD sitting below the clamp. You can swivel the clamp on the steerer tube so it's not in the path of the arch and there is a pinch bolt on the steerer clamp to prevent rotation, but it wouldn't be impossible for the mount to rotate on the steerer while you're riding (or crashing), with the resultant risk of the arch smashing into the base of the TMD - so, checking that clearance is advised. If there is a clearance issue, running the TMD will necessitate positioning the stem higher up the steerer, perhaps leaving the rider wanting to maintain their original bar height with a different rise bar. It's also not impossible that the TMD would damage the frame in a bad crash so it's probably a good idea to fashion a bumper for your headtube. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info It is rare to test a product that we actually know so little about, and even rarer to test a component of an entirely new category. To make this as fair a test as possible, and to eliminate the risk of suffering from the placebo effect, Seb and I conducted double-blinded back-to-back testing of the CounterSycle against an almost identical control provided by CounterShox. The control was very similar in weight to the real TMD (366 g versus 380 g), and it looks the same, but it doesn't have a suspended weight inside it. The only way to know which one you are holding is to shake it; you can feel the weight inside the real TMD being displaced. There is a very quiet noise associated with its movement but you can't hear it while riding. To test the unit, we spent a day lapping a downhill track that we are both very familiar with - myself on a Marin Alpine Trail and Seb on a Privateer 161 . Conditions were dry, and the ground was hard pack and rough, and a little loose in places. The track consists of big swooping berms, fast wash-board sections, steeper sections that require very hard braking on rocky ground, big compressions and a couple of small jumps. Armed with two real TMDs and two control units, we went about the day swapping the units for one another, blinded on any given run as to which of the two was bolted to the bike. After each run, we guessed which of the two units we were running before removing it and shaking it to find out if we were correct. As we were randomly selecting unit each time, we didn't get a straight 50/50 split between runs with the TMD and runs with the control. The Results I completed eight runs of the downhill track, the first three with the real TMD - knowingly. Thereafter, I was blinded to which of the two units - real TMD or Control - was bolted to my bike. Following my fourth run of the day, I reported that the bike felt more planted but also more harsh, and that the bumps more jarring than they had felt on the previous runs. I guessed that, with the notable difference in ride feel, my unit had been switched to the Control. I was correct. On my fifth run of the day, I reported that the bike felt more lively and that the rougher sensations I had experienced on the previous run were smoother in comparison. With that change, I guessed that my fifth run of the day had been with the real TMD. I was correct. Thereafter, I incorrectly guessed that my 6th and 7th run were with the Control unit, reporting those rougher, jarring sensations again, and more fatigue. My final run of the day was by far the worst, and a little erratic in places. Comparatively, the runs felt very rough and harsh, and I felt a little unstable at times. I guessed correctly that I was on the Control unit that time. Of the five runs wherein I was blinded to which of the units I was running, I guessed correctly three times, and incorrectly two times. As I'm sure you can appreciate, it is hard to draw a firm conclusion about any purported performance benefits of the TMD from this data set alone. I was unable to consistently perceive a ride feel difference between the real TMD and the Control, and therefore I can't - at this stage - recommend that anyone spend $275 USD of their hard-earned cash on one. However, my initial positive reports of an improved ride feel have been enough to pique my interest such that I'd like to continue testing it. Any further testing I do with the CounterSycle will be under the same blinded back-to-back conditions for the avoidance of any confirmation bias. — Jessie-May Morgan Science is hard. We decided to randomly pick either the real TMD or the control for each run because, with an alternating "ABAB" approach, you know it's going to be different each time, so if you guess right once, you guess right every single time. The trouble with making it random is that there's a chance you get the same one many times in a row, reducing the number of times you get to experience the direct comparison. This is what happened to me - I happened to pick up the TMD four times in a row. In hindsight, a better method might have been to randomly pick one of the options (TMD or control) for one run, then pick the other for the second run, then repeat as many times as possible. As the day came to a close, I decided to use my last two runs this way, picking one at random, then swapping to the other option for the last run. Taken together, of the five runs where I didn't know which I was riding, I guessed correctly four times, although I only got to switch between the control to the TMD twice, and I guessed correctly on both occasions. When I switched from the control to the TMD at the start of the test, or the other way around at the end of the test, I'm fairly sure I had less hand and arm fatigue towards the end of the run with the real TMD. I was doing full runs quite consistently, and my hands were flagging by the end each time.
