It's impossible to be sure with the limited number of back-to-back comparisons, but it's enough to make me think there might be something in it. What's the takeaway? If you're looking to leave no stone unturned to minimise hand/arm fatigue, I'd say the CounterSycle is worth a shot. But if there is a benefit, it's pretty subtle - most people would be better off focusing on optimising their suspension setup, tire pressures and handlebar choice first. Otherwise, it's hard to recommend spending the $275 asking price based on our testing so far. — Seb Stott Pros + Possible small improvement in comfort and control Cons - Very expensive - 380 gram weight penalty - Could force an increased bar height for some Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Accessories Countershox Countersycle Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Fox Podium Fork - Heavy, Expensive, Upside-Down, & Excellent
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Fox Podium https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/6HjIH5k4 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 15:05 Inverted forks have a long and meandering history in the mountain bike world, but they've rarely succeeded on a large scale in anything but a dual-crown application. There have been plenty of boutique offerings for folks looking to try the upside-down life, but Fox is now entering this space with their newest fork, the Podium. The Podium isn't a replacement for the 38, so much as an attempt to make the lowest-friction fork they could. In fact, the initial concept wasn't even an inverted design, it's simply what they settled on to achieve the goal. Podium Details • 160 or 170mm travel (150mm OE only) • 29" (44mm rake) • Grip X2 damper with specific tune • Glidecore air spring • Steel 20x110mm Boost axle • Weight: 2695g (170mm) • Price: $2,000 USD / $2,679 CAD / €2,399 EUR / $3,349 AUS • ridefox.com Details The Podium's most obvious detail is the inverted architecture. Large, 47mm upper tubes are machined from 7000-series aluminum, as is the generatively-designed crown. The tapered steer tube fits to a frame no differently to a standard fork. Axle to crown heights are equivalent to a 38 of the same travel, so bike fit and geometry shouldn't change if you were to swap from one to the other. The stanchions are 36mm in diameter, with most of the fork's fore-aft stiffness coming from those beefy uppers. Aftermarket availability is limited to 160mm and 170mm travel variants, with a 150mm option available for manufacturer spec only. Similarly, you'll only be able to buy the Kashima-coated Factory edition aftermarket, as the black-stanchioned Performance Series Elite is also OE-only. Beyond the stanchion coating, the only other differentiator between Factory and PSE is the stanchion guard material: Factory spec gets carbon fiber, PSE gets thermoplastic. The primary reason we're not seeing Podiums with more than 170mm of travel comes down to weight - adding any more travel necessitates higher chassis stiffness, and that meant adding even more weight to an already heavy fork. By flipping the fork on its head, a few targets that typically require careful optimization become more easily achieved. Unsprung mass is reduced significantly, as the lowers of a traditional fork are replaced by the lighter stanchions of the Podium. Oil immersion is a constant thanks to gravity, and precludes any need for bypass channels in the design. Air release buttons are still useful, as the Podium can still build up plenty of casting pressure with altitude or weather changes, but those are now found at the top of the crown, tucked into the adjusters. The brake mount is 200mm native, and can take up to a 230mm rotor. The brake housing routes through a series of 3 guides to make sure it stays quiet and out of the way as the fork cycles. There isn't a fender in production for the Podium yet, but Fox is promising one by the fall. The current design mounts into the lower hole of the steer tube, like an old THE fender. Torsional stiffness is a common complaint with most inverted forks, and unsurprisingly became a key detail for the team at Fox to get right. In pursuit of matching the feel of the 38, they ended up with a 20mm steel axle that uses a DH hub standard, eliminating any unwanted flexibility. In fact, the option that ended up making it to production wasn't the stiffest design they tested, but was the one preferred by test riders. To fit the beefier axle standard, you'll need a different front hub than you'd run in other single crown forks, or in some cases just a new set of end caps. Fox's sister company Race Face is offering end cap kits to adapt some of their existing Atlas and Vault hubs, detailed here . The last internal detail to touch on comes down to the bushings, and more specifically the amount of overlap they've achieved inside the Podium. For the numbers people, that increase clocks in at 32% more bushing overlap than the 38 and 7% more than the 40, with 175mm spanning the upper and lower bushings. The point here is to better brace the fork under load, and keep things moving smoothly despite heavy compressions, rough terrain, and braking loads. The engineering team at Fox chose not to implement a sliding trombone bushing, as they found that reducing the distance between the axle and the first bushing had a greater impact on overall friction. Coupled with the very stiff uppers assembly, and you've got a very precise fork that feels smooth in extreme situations. Damper & Air Spring The Podium piggybacks on Fox's recent release of their Glidecore air spring, which uses elastomer rings to decouple the air spring from any chassis flex that might occur during riding. This is meant to isolate the spring's motion, and prevent any binding under hard riding. Pressure adjustment now takes place at the bottom of the fork, as does volume spacer adjustment. I prefer flipping the bike to adjust pressure, as the air valve can spit a bit of oil if you connect and disconnect a pump with the wheel on the ground. Even when flipped, I'd recommend being careful and having a rag on hand to clean up any oil that might bubble up - you're awful close to the brake rotor, after all. Featured in that inverted air spring is a new volume spacer, with a unique design relative to any of Fox's other forks. The Podium's spacers are flat little 5cc donuts, with an o-ring in the center hole to keep them secured to a post on the air cap. The smaller increment allows for more fidelity in volume adjustment, and the cassette-style tool access means you won't be wanting for any proprietary tools. I've been happy with 99psi in the air chamber, and 2-3 volume spacers on the stack. The new spacer. Kebab situation. The Podium runs a Grip X2 damper, with a unique tune to match the lower friction of the fork's chassis. It features heavier compression tunes help control the easier rate at which the fork can go through travel, and a rebound tune to match the lower unsprung mass and reduction of sliding friction. In comparison to a Fox 38 with the X2 damper, I'm running the Podium's clickers a bit more closed off. From closed, I'm sitting at 2-4 clicks of low-speed compression (LSC), and 2-3 clicks of high-speed compression (HSC), with the former of the two changing a bit more depending on terrain and trail style. The rebound is where things differ a bit more. Where I'm usually a good bit faster than suggested on the 38, I've been erring slower on the Podium. Currently, my rebound settings are 3-4 LSR and 1-3 HSR, again from closed. I'm curious how the current rebound tune will scale as rider weight and corresponding air pressure goes up, but only time will tell. Speaking with some of the team at Fox, it sounds like folks have settled all over the map on rebound settings, but it's worth noting that a few people in my weight range were similarly closed on high-speed rebound. For me, the more closed end of that spectrum has been preferable, but that's only relevant to this fork, on this bike, in the places I've been riding it. According to Fox, a few aftermarket tunes will be available for the damper - any changes there can be made at a Fox service center. If you were to warrant such a thing, having to make a fairly major edit to a $2,000 fork would be quite annoying, but hopefully that's not necessary. Price & Weight These are likely to be two of the main sticking points with the Podium, so I figured it was worth doing a bit of comparison to some other similar forks on the market. Fox Podium : 2695g | $2,000 USD. 150-170mm travel. Fox 38 : 2381g | $1,249 USD. 150-180mm travel. RockShox Zeb : 2324g | $1,159 USD. 150-190mm travel. Fox 40 : 2745g | $1,849 USD. 190-203mm travel. Push Nine One : 2790g | $2,250 USD. 140-170mm travel. Intend Flash : 2350g | $2,263 USD. 160-190mm travel. The latter two forks remain untested, but are worth noting as they're two of the more prominent inverted single crown options on the market. Obviously the combination of price and weight has little to do with how well a fork performs, but it's good to get a relative sense of how things stack up. Durability Two months of ride time isn't long enough to comment on the long-term durability of a suspension product, but I can touch on my experiences so far. The primary concern is sure to be the relatively exposed stanchions, as the inverted design puts them closer to harm's way. On this front, the stanchion guards have done their job admirably, with no damage to the stanchions to report. In fact, the guards themselves are pretty unmarred, despite having a satin coated carbon finish that readily shows marks. Obviously freak accidents happen, but so far it seems the rocks and debris have been kind. The brief I received on the service procedure sounds fairly standard, and perhaps even easier than a standard fork. The lack of a combined lowers assembly means you can service one leg at a time, minimizing oil loss and focusing on one task at a time. I have yet to perform this myself, so I'll report back once the job is done. We'll see how this shakes out in time, but I have a theory that the fork will ingest less contaminants, as the seals are pointed down and tend to collect less grit and grime. Otherwise, the Podium is pretty standard, with no other maintenance tasks to keep in mind. One little detail to keep an eye on is the front brake hose routing, as the line needs to be set with enough slack for the fork to cycle without any binding or bending - just pay attention when first installing and check every now and then. Ride Impressions I've been riding the Podium consistently for a couple months now, and also had the opportunity to spend time on a copy of the fork earlier this past winter. In both cases, the fork has been nothing short of impressive, with a level of grip and sensitivity that can almost be hard to get used to. That grip comes without much of a penalty, especially once I honed in my damper settings. I found that the Podium benefits from heavier damping adjustment, and can ride on that damper to provide support. There were instances early on where I thought I'd stiffen the spring to achieve the desired feel, but was able to hit those targets through damper adjustment. The most unique sensation provided by the Podium comes into play in rough terrain under heavy braking - something plentiful in the Sea to Sky. The big fork feels like it's moving through travel where others would start to halt a bit, keeping the wheel on the ground and comfort in the hands. In loose, rough, trashy bits of track, I've found you can keep a bit less weight on the hands and let the fork work to keep things in check. By no means is this a profound difference from a Fox 38, but subtle gains add up in situations like these. Stiffness I'll touch briefly on stiffness, as it has been anything but an issue over the testing period. Torsionally, the fork feels very similar to a 36 - while I'm sure there are some subtle differences that could be picked up in machine testing, I'm left feeling like they both keep my wheel going where I want it to, without any surprises. Fore-aft, the Podium feels more like a DH fork, holding shape through successive hits and big one-offs impressively well. Comparing Fox's Options Riding the Podium in the bike park feels like an ideal use case, as does pedal-access terrain where you'd potentially opt for a downhill bike. The 170mm single crown is excellent, but still won't convince you that it's a 200mm Fox 40, which still takes the cake for just trucking through the roughest terrain. Having ridden the 38, Podium, and 40 all within the past week, I think they all have their places. The 40's is obvious, but the line between the 38 and the Podium is a little more blurry. If pure bump-absorbing performance and grip are your ultimate metrics, then the Podium is the winner. If a balance of weight, price, and performance are more equally weighted, then I'd opt for the 38. The 38 isn't some wimpy lightweight option, and still feels like an excellent option on all manner of gnarly trails. Climbing & More Besides the additional weight, there's not much that the Podium does to impact climbing performance. Sure, improved bump absorption plays a role on the way up as well, but not nearly as much as it does on the way down. I wouldn't go putting the Podium on an otherwise light trail bike, as the added weight might impact the character of a snappy bike. That said, I do think there will be some interesting shorter travel builds that implement the 150mm Podium. I imagine the Podium will be a common sight on all sorts of eMTB builds, particularly for those looking to perfect their moto cosplay, but I'm more curious to see which pedal-powered mountain bikes choose to feature the extra 300-400g of fork. For me, it feels like a perfect fork for the Frameworks Enduro I've been riding, matching the heft and descending capability of the bike very well. Jessie-May's Take For those curious as to how the Podium performs under a smaller and lighter rider, we have Jessie-May Morgan's thoughts on a 160mm Podium she's been riding. Her first impressions are as follows: I rode the Podium fork at 160mm on a Yeti MTe on a mix of bike park and enduro trails around Leogang and Saalbach Hinterglemm. The day amounted to 4,350 meters of descending, or 14,000+ feet. I mention it because that's the biggest day of descending I've bagged since I tore a rotator cuff tendon. It was also the most comfortable I've been on a bike since that accident. Its suppleness did not leave me wanting. I'll not be drawing final conclusions from a single ride, and will continue to test the fork at home, with a full review to come on how this fork performs for lighter riders (I weigh 57 kgs or 126 lbs). I ran 66 psi in the air spring with two volume spacers. I went to full travel twice, feeling a distinctly metallic clunk at the bottom. That said, there is room for a heap more spacers in there to dial in the progression. As for the damping, I ran more compression damping than I typically would on the Fox 38, settling on a low speed setting close to the middle of the range, with the rebound close to fully open on the high and low speed adjusters. Throughout the stroke, the fork felt incredibly smooth and traction was ample. In high speed berms I felt nothing untoward with the steering, and any concerns over a potential lack of torsional stiffness were, for me on that day at least, unfounded. — Jessie-May Morgan Pros + Stellar downhill performance + Best in class grip + No stiffness compromise Cons - Very expensive, very heavy - 20x110mm wheel requirement - Damping range might be too limited for some Pinkbike's Take The Fox Podium isn't for everybody. The weight, cost, and limited travel offerings make it something of a specialty item, but boy is it good. For the intended purpose, it may just be the best single crown fork I've ridden, taking the category one step further. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Suspension Fork Fox Factory Fox Podium Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: The Liv Pique Advanced 29 is a Thoroughbred XC Race Bike
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: REVIEW Liv Pique Advanced WORDS: Jessie-May Morgan PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson The latest version of the Liv Pique arrived at the back end of 2023 as a lighter, longer travel cross-country bike with a more aggressive geometry - fit for evolving XCO courses. The most notable update was the switch from Maestro to the FlexPoint Pro suspension layout, a move that allowed Liv to shave a considerable 297 grams off the frame weight. In conjunction, rear wheel travel was increased to 115mm paired with a 120mm travel fork, as compared to the 100mm at each end of the predecessor. The result is a modern XC race bike complete with remote lockout that weighs in at a very competitive 10.1 kg (22.26 lbs) in a size small. To anyone wondering, this bike is not the same as the Giant Anthem. If Giant doesn't release something very similar to this within the next 12 months, I will eat my hat. Pique Advanced 29 0 Details • Carbon frame • 115 mm travel rear, 120 mm fork • 29" wheels • 67° head angle • 76° seat angle • 406, 420, 432, 445mm reach • 435mm chainstays • Sizes: XS, S, M, L • 1,468 gram frame (claimed, size S) • Actual weight: 10.1 kg (22.26 lb) w/o pedals, S • Price: (29 0) £11,999; (29 1) $7,800; (29 2) $5,499 • liv-cycling.com Though they share the FlexPoint Pro suspension and have similar XC racing intentions, the two bikes are really quite different. The Liv Pique has 15mm extra travel and a slacker head tube angle amongst other geometrical differences. And, considering it is primarily (though not exclusively, of course) aimed at women, the sizing is based around shorter riders in the region of 150 - 181 cm. Liv Pique review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/pQQv5BK9 Copied Live 00:00 08:06 08:06 This is the third generation of the Liv Pique, designed to be competitive on World Cup XCO tracks for the likes of Jenn Jackson, your 2024 Canadian National Champion. She has since moved to the Orbea Fox Factory Team, leaving Marin Lowe, Tyler Jacobs, Linda Indergand, and Ronja Blochlinger as the 2025 Liv Factory Racing team members. The exact lineup varies from country to country, but there are only two models available in the US just now; the $5,400 Pique Advanced 29 2, and the $7,800 Pique Advanced 29 1. The Advanced 29 0 shown throughout is available in the UK, Canada and Europe. Regardless, all models have the same carbon frame, a dropper seat post, 29" x 2.4" tires, and 3-position remote lockout. Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take I am happy enough hunting out the natural little trails gaps on it, but its enthusiasm doesn't necessarily match mine every time. The Pique is more of a wheels-on-ground affair, prioritizing efficiency over bump-eating compliance. — Jessie-May Morgan Frame Details Like many a XC frame manufacturer, Liv has chosen to make the swingarm of the Pique much lighter with the switch to flex stays. As compared to the Maestro layout of the predecessor (a dual-link affair), the latest generation Pique is missing one set of pivot hardware. The flex in the seatstay takes away the need for a set of bearings, making a large contribution to a massive 297 gram weight saving. The claimed frame weight? An astonishingly competitive 1,468 grams in a size small (w/o shock). For comparison, the Cannondale Scalpel frame (M) weighs 1,980 grams for the Series 1 frame without shock, and 1,780 grams for the highest end Lab 71 frame. Full disclosure: the weight given to us by Liv is for an unpainted frame, but does include the UDH hanger, and all pivot and shock hardware. P.S. Liv Factory Racing's Tyler Jacobs has her small built up to 10.66 kg / 23.50 lbs Additional weight saving comes from the use of a new carbon layup that is both lighter, and you guessed it, stiffer. Liv say the new bike has a 10% increase in pedaling stiffness and a 6% increase in torsional stiffness over the previous iteration that they attribute to the straight top tube and wider, square-shape redesign of the downtube and chainstay. Liv is one of few manufacturers that has steered clear of thru-headset cable routing, a decision that will disappoint very few. To my mind, that kind of carry-on should be confined to performance-oriented road bikes. Regardless, many modern XC bikes have gone fully integrated so it's nice to see Liv going to the easier maintenance route. The Advanced 29 0 model I tested has the SRAM XX SL T-Type AXS drivetrain, but the frame does have ports for cables should you ever wish to switch to mechanical. Cables are not guided throughout, and the cable for the shock lockout can rattle a little. The brake hose is routed externally along the chainstay - sensibly located guides mean the hose doesn't rub the stay as the bike is pushed through its travel. The rear TyreWiz developed a fault early on, flashing red at all times even when the tire pressure was within the pre-set range The chainstay protection is lacking in this key area leaving the frame vulnerable to paint chips, and the undesirable noise of chain slap The Pique has two sets of bottle bosses in the front triangle to fit two proper size water bottles, even on the small frame. The dropper post is 30.9mm and the size small gets a 125mm drop while medium through XL get 150mm of drop. Other details include 2.4” tires on 30mm internal width rims, a press-fit bottom bracket, and the Zipp 1ZERO HiTOP SW wheels on the Advanced 29 0 model come with TyreWiz real-time pressure sensors that flash red when the pressure sits outside your pre-defined range. Geometry The Pique's geometry is a complete overhaul, bringing it up to date with the latest modern cross-country machines. The head tube angle is 1.5° slacker now at 67°, and the seat tube angle is 2° steeper at 76°. The reach numbers have grown by 5-7mm depending on the frame size, with the size medium getting a 432mm reach. That's very short for a size medium, but here it's important to remember that Liv's target market is considerably shorter than the target market of most other manufacturers. On that note, the jumps between frame sizes are really quite small with reach increments of just 12-15mm between sizes. That compares to most other bikes where the reach increments are in the region of 25-30mm. Liv's target rider height range of 4ft 11" up to 5ft 11" (150 - 181 cm). On the short end, their XS has a reach of 406mm, and at least by that metric is one of the smallest 29" wheel XC bikes that money can buy. When catering to smaller riders, standover does become a point of discussion. Though it is not a metric that is relevant to how the bike feels when you're actually riding it, it is always nice to know that if you do need to take the occasional dab through technical terrain there is clearance to do that without fear of smacking yourself off the top tube. On the small that I rode, the standover is 767mm, which is only just about workable for my 30" inseam. It is actually a fair bit taller than the standover height on the size small Scott Spark RC, Cannondale Scalpel, Trek Supercaliber and Orbea Oiz. If that is a metric that is very important to you, know that most other brands will offer you more clearance in that department. I will say that the 445mm reach (with a 70mm stem) sounds a bit cramped for a rider at the upper end of that spectrum, but at 163cm tall I can't speak from experience. I can say that the recommended size small with a 420mm reach and a 60mm stem is appropriate for me and I had no issues with the fit. It's a little surprising to see that Liv has not afforded the Pique size-adjusted chainstays . The stays are a consistent 435mm across the four-strong size range, meaning the front-to-rear weight distribution - a key metric that impacts ride feel - will vary a fair bit between frame sizes. At this kind of price point we do see other brands like Cannondale and Yeti giving each frame its own size-adjusted swingarm. For the sake of comparison, here are some other modern XC bikes and their head angle / seat tube angle / reach (size S) / chainstay length. The head angle still sits on the steeper end of the spectrum, but the Pique is fairly middle-of-the-road elsewhere. Liv Pique Advanced 0: 67° / 76° / (R) 420 mm / (CS) 435 mm Juliana Wilder: 67.1° / 75° /(R) 412mm / (CS) 431mm Specialized Epic 8: 65.9° / 75.5° / (R) 420 mm / (CS) 435 mm Orbea Oiz: 67°/ 76.5° / (R) 425 mm / (CS) 432 mm Scott Spark RC: 67.2° / 76.6° / (R) 411 mm / (CS) 437.5 mm Yeti ASR: 66.5° / 75.5° / (R) 420 mm / (CS) 435mm Cannondale Scalpel: 66.6° / 75.5° / 425mm (R) / (CS) 434mm Suspension Design The Pique uses a single pivot flex stay with a linkage driving the 165mm x 45mm Trunnion shock to deliver 115mm of rear travel. They call it their FlexPoint Pro suspension platform, making the hard-to-substantiate claim in their marketing copy that it is the most active, lightweight XC suspension system on the market. All three models benefit from a remote lock-out for both the shock and fork, and all sizes and models are equipped with the very same custom compression damping tune on the Fox Float SL shock. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $11999 Travel 115mm Rear Shock Fox Float SL Factory 165mm x 45mm Fork 120mm Fox 34 Float SC Factory FIT4 Cassette SRAM XX CS 1299 SL T-Type Crankarms SRAM XX SL T-Type with AXS Power Meter, Chainguide Giant MRP Chain Guide Rear Derailleur SRAM T-Type XX Eagle AXS Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Ultimate Handlebar Giant Contact SLR XC, Carbon, 35mm Stem Giant Contact SL XC Grips Liv XC Pro, 32mm Brakes Shimano XTR, 180mm and 160mm rotors Wheelset ZIPP 1ZERO HiTop SL 29 Tires Maxxis Aspen, 29" x 2.4" - EXO, MaxxSpeed Seatpost Fox Transfer, 30.9mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Spec pricing in GBP, not USD as shown RIDING THE Liv Pique Advanced 29 0 Test Bike Setup The Liv Pique Advanced 0 comes with Giant's Contact SLR XC carbon bar that I cut down from its stock 760mm to 740mm. It is a 35mm clamp, flat, 9° backsweep affair, paired with an 8° rise (or drop) 60mm stem on the XS and S (70mm on larger sizes). I spent a heap of time riding a 35mm rise bar on this bike to take weight off an injured shoulder, but have since gone back to the Giant bar and find it to be perfectly acceptable. I ran the stock Maxxis Aspen tires (EXO, 120 TPI) throughout the test period; as a dry conditions tire, they left a lot to be desired in soft conditions but they are insanely quick on dry hardpack terrain. I rode the bike with 112 psi in the Fox Float SL Factory EVOL rear shock, which put me around 27% sag, and I ran the recommended 62 psi in the Fox Float Factory 34 SC fork. Tire pressures were 17 psi front and 20 psi rear. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Aboyne, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 127 lbs / 57.5 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan Download the Trailforks App Testing Info My testing of the Liv Pique took place over an extended period of time, and in two main locations. It has seen the sculpted trails of the 2023 World Championships XCO course in Glentress, and also more natural riding around the eastern edge of the Cairngorms National Park. More recently, I have enjoyed the efficiency of the Pique on longer rides that largely consisted of rough gravel-like terrain - terrain that would be completely horrible to ride on an actual gravel bike - interspersed with natural, unpredictable rocky singletrack that absolutely warrants the use of a full suspension bike with high volume tires. Climbing The seated pedaling position on the Liv Pique was initially a little alien to me, having spent most of the last few years riding longer travel bikes. The seat angle is slacker than most bikes I ride (though not most XC bikes), which I appreciate has good merit on a bike that is capable of taking a rider over long distances quite comfortably, and for relatively little effort. I found myself offsetting the rearward bias with a forward saddle position to get my hips in a more ergonomically familiar position. That was of great benefit on steep climbs, helping to keep the front end down - here I also appreciated the 32-tooth chainring. On longer flatter rides I chose to put the saddle back to a neutral position to take the weight off my wrists. Zipping along on gentle gradient fire roads, the bike is at its best in the middle of the three compression settings. It will bob quite considerably otherwise, so that middle shock position is good for keeping the bike from oscillating high in its travel while keeping it supple enough to manage some of the chunkier gravel. I only used the full lock-out for tarmac commutes, and yes, it is very very firm. That third position of the remote lock-out also firms up the fork massively. There was no scenario where I felt that desirable, so I backed off the tension on the barrel adjuster such that the second press of the remote only locks out the shock. I find it's best to keep the fork supple especially when climbing steeper rocky bits. Allowing the fork to compress a little allows the pitch of the bike to be more manageable, holding more weight at the front. This push-to-lock system is preferable to the push-to-unlock system that other brands employ That middle shock setting is preferred for out-of the-saddle efforts, too. In the open setting I find the suspension platform isn't as firm as it could be when laying down power in bigger gears. The remote lock-out lever on the bar is a push-to-lock affair, which I absolutely prefer over the push-to-unlock variation (Orbea, I am looking at you). The presence of a dropper lever and a remote lock-out always results in competition for handlebar real-estate, and the Liv Pique is no exception. I positioned it inboard of the dropper lever, as it's more important to me to have fast and reliable access to that over the lock-out. I kind of wish someone would make an electronic remote for the lock-out; it could be much smaller than the stacked levers we have here, and you wouldn't have to worry about a cable (just batteries instead). On tight singletrack, the ergonomics of a 60mm stem took a little getting used to. It makes the steering feel a little slower than the the 67° head tube angle would suggest. Once I'd familiarized myself with the setup, navigating tight turns while climbing got a little easier, but never felt totally natural. Over small bumps, the rear end is nice and supple. While the rear smooths out the chatter making things nice and comfortable, the fork has trouble matching that small bump sensitivity. It performs well when I'm out of the saddle, weighting the bar, but in the seated pedaling position the fork struggles to get moving into its travel. When climbs got more technical and required more than just pure power to get up and over obstacles, the Pique's traction was good. In the open shock setting, the rear wheel is able to move up and out of the way over consecutive hits without feeling wallowy. A lot of riders will want to switch out the Aspen for more grip in soft conditions, though. Descending The Pique is a whippet on mellow, undulating singletrack and long, grueling fire road climbs. I have no qualms with its kilometer-eating efficiency, but it isn't the most confidence inspiring XC bike when navigating rough descents. The head angle isn't quite as slack as some more modern XC bikes, but I wouldn't say that's what holds it back necessarily. While the rear offers plenty of support in fast, compressive berms, its hesitancy to give up too much travel can make it feel overwhelmed in choppy sections. It navigates flowing sections of smooth trail with finesse but it is easy to get carried away with that, barreling into less predictable sections with more speed than the bike can manage drama-free. The bike can get bounced around, leaving me feeling vulnerable and having to reset my expectations. At 25% sag and no volume spacers I never used all 115mm of rear wheel travel; the ramp in that small volume shock is considerable. The system as a whole has quite a lot of lateral flex. It's hard to point a finger at any one component, but that flex I feel when landing jumps and riding through rooty sections will be the sum of the light casing Aspen tire, the compliance in the remarkably light Zipp 1ZERO HiTOP SW wheels and the swingarm itself. It takes some getting used to. Compliance will be important in traction and comfort, but I would argue that too much can make a bike feel unsettled. The 2-piston XTR brakes are a contributing factor here in limiting the bike's descending capability, not offering a great deal of stopping power. These days, four-piston brakes aren't just for long-travel bikes; the Cannondale Scalpel gets that more powerful option as stock on most models, and we regularly see World Cup XCO athletes taking the weight penalty to have more control on the descents. Given the right track, the Pique can be an absolute joy to ride fast. I find its handling to be quite precise, and am confident pushing the bike into support for quick changes in direction. It's not overly playful, though - which is perhaps exactly what you might want from a XC race bike. I am happy enough hunting out the natural little trails gaps on it, but its enthusiasm doesn't necessarily match mine every time. The Pique is more of a wheels-on-ground affair, prioritizing efficiency over bump-eating compliance. Orbea Oiz Liv Pique Advanced 0 How Does It Compare? The latest Orbea Oiz came well over a year before the 2024 Liv Pique Advanced with a geometry that is not dissimilar. I rode it over three days at the launch event in Spain, and have some thoughts on how it compares to the Liv. Both bikes rely on a flex pivot to deliver their rear wheel travel; the Oiz has a smidgen more than the Pique, at 120mm. It does have the edge in terms of descending performance with more of a playful overall ride feel that gives me a little more confidence in rough terrain. It wouldn't be fair to say that one is outright a better bike than the other, but I have a good idea as to which one I'd choose for what. To my mind the Pique is more of a thorough-bred race bike, and Liv has clearly taken every opportunity to maximize the rider's power-to-weight ratio with this particular build. If money were no object, and my intention with the bike was to ride XC marathons, or other endurance-style events, I'd probably pick the Liv. If I was looking for something that was just as fast, but wanted to take it onto rougher trails as a bit of a downcountry bike occasionally (sharp intake of breath) I'd probably go for the Oiz - its frame is rated for use with a 130mm fork, making it that little bit more versatile on the trail end of the spectrum. As far as pricing goes, Orbea offers more models (eight!) to choose from and is likely to satisfy a wider range of budgets. Looking at the carbon options only, Orbea also has the edge with the value proposition. There is no direct like-for-like spec comparison to be made, but the Orbea Oiz pricing starts at $4,999 USD with the Pique starting at $5,400 USD. While the Liv is very light, the Orbea is a little lighter at a claimed 1,453 grams in a size medium , without the shock, versus the Pique's claimed 1,468 grams in a size small. For those who care: the Oiz does have thru-headset cable routing, while the Pique does not. I'll throw in the Santa Cruz Tallboy here for an extra comparison. That might seem odd to some, but the truth is that (rightly or wrongly) people often make purchasing decisions based on travel numbers, and the Tallboy and the Pique aren't so different there. The Tallboy has a little more with 120mm rear wheel travel with a 130mm fork, and it is pitched as the downhiller's XC bike. Having ridden it earlier this year, I can say that statement is easily justified. It was the biggest lesson of all on why one shouldn't judge a bike on its travel numbers. On the trail, the two bikes couldn't be more different; the Tallboy will trounce the Pique on any descent thanks to its slacker head angle and more supple, calm feeling suspension platform. That said it is hefty in comparison to the Liv Pique, with a frame that is almost a kilogram heavier at 2,830 grams. That should tell you all you need to know about the intentions of these two bikes. If you want to go fast and far, choose the Liv. If you want to have a blast riding trails, go for the Tallboy. Which Model is the Best Value? The first comment I wish to make: If Barbie became a XCO superstar tomorrow, surely this is the very bike she would choose? I kind of love it, and I kind of hate it at the same time. Two things can be true. Indisputably, the pink and gold sparkly colorway is polarizing. Alas, you can only get it if you choose to part with £11,999 for the Pique Advance 29 0 reviewed here. Similarly, you can only get this bike in the more understated Raw Carbon/Chrome, or Golden Haze/Carbon/Chrome if you are happy to settle for the more financially attainable Advanced 29 1 , or Advanced 29 2 models, respectively. Pique Advanced 29 1 - $7,800 Pique Advanced 29 2 - $5,400 To that end, the Pique Advanced 29 2 is going to be your best bang for buck. Claimed weight is still very respectable at 11 kg in S. You get the same lightweight carbon frame, Fox Performance level suspension components with the 3-pos remote, a Shimano XT mechanical drivetrain and Giant's XCR carbon wheels, and you get an adjustable length dropper seat post (110-140mm) giving more clearance to those who have the legs for it. I would absolutely spend any spare cash I had on upgrading the brakes to the 4-piston Shimano SLX. Final note: While the Advanced 29 0 model tested here asks you to pay attention to the status of five batteries, the base model Pique has none. Joyous. Technical Report Maxxis Aspen tires: These are a highlight, but only really in the context of dry hard pack riding. They look scary with their minimal tread, and they very much are in soft conditions, but they impressed me with how much grip they offered in their intended conditions. They are incredibly fast rolling, making very light work of long stretches of gravel between trails. Fox 34 SC Fork: The fork tracks well and I found it to have a good range of usable damping adjustment but its fore-aft stiffness is poor. It flexes visibly under hard braking sending vibrations up to the rider. Remote Lock-Out: I had an issue with the cable routing on the shock's remote lock-out wherein it was locking itself out when the bike was pushed deep into its travel. It seems to be an issue of cable being fed up into the downtube in compression and then getting tugged on the rebound. It caused the plastic cable end-cap to crack. Replacing it with a metal Jagwire one and putting a cable tie onto the cable where it exits the frame solved the issue. Shimano XTR 2-Piston Brakes: These leave a lot to be desired in terms of power, and I expect a lot riders will want to upgrade to a more powerful 4-piston offering, just as many World Cup XCO racers do. A number of other XC bike manufacturers opt for the small weight penalty of a 4-piston caliper, and I think that's going to be the right decision for most riders. Liv XC Pro 32mm Foam Grips: While negligible in weight these grips aren't very comfortable. I find holding onto the squishy foam on long descents is quite fatiguing for my hands and forearms. Pros + Efficient climber + Capable on flowing descents + Comfortable for very long rides + Reliable components Cons - Expensive - Not the most composed on rougher descents - Not the quietest Pinkbike's Take The Pique Advanced 29 0 is a no-holds-barred high-end XC race bike that will delight weight weenies who don't mind charging lots of batteries. Most of the spec has proven reliable and fit for purpose, and there is no denying the thrilling kilometer-eating capacity of this thing. It is fun to ride on smoother trail center terrain but can quickly become a bit of a handful when things get choppy. If I was putting my hand in my pocket for this bike, it would be for its long-distance comfort rather than its capacity to inspire great confidence on technical descents. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews XC Bikes Liv Liv Pique Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: The Norco Fluid VLT 140 is a Modest Overachiever
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: REVIEW Norco Fluid VLT WORDS: Jessie-May Morgan PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson Norco entered the lightweight eMTB market earlier this year with the all-new Fluid VLT, a Bosch SX-powered trail bike with a 400 Wh battery and a dedicated MX wheel configuration. It comes in two formats. First, there is the weight-watcher: the C1 130 model has 130mm of rear wheel travel, a 140mm fork, a full carbon frame, an in-line shock and a host of componentry selected primarily to keep weight to a minimum - it weighs a claimed 18.1 kg (39.9 lbs) in S3. Then, there is the more performance-oriented 140mm travel option, of which there are three models to choose from. The lightest C1 option (reviewed here) runs a full carbon frame and weighs 18.76 kg (41 lbs) in S2. The more affordable C2, C3 and C4 models are a little heavier, pairing a carbon front triangle with an aluminum rear-end. Norco Fluid VLT Details • Carbon frame • Wheel size: MX • Fork Travel: 150mm • Rear Travel: 140mm • 64.5º head angle • Motor: Bosch SX • Battery: 400 Wh (+250Wh Range Extender) • Sizes: S1-S5 (S2 tested) • Warranty: Lifetime • Weight: 18.76 kg / 41 lbs (S) • Price: $9,999 USD (Fluid VLT C1 140) • norco.com Like the well-received Norco Fluid trail bike, the Fluid VLT delivers its rear wheel travel via a traditional Horst-link suspension platform. That's in contrast to the longer-travel, full-powered Range VLT that goes all-out with an idler-equipped high-pivot suspension platform. Editor's Note: The Norco Fluid VLT 140 C1 reviewed here is not spec-correct. The bike was supplied with SRAM Level SLV brakes and a TranzX RAD+ dropper post, but the correct components are SRAM Code SLV brakes and a OneUp Components dropper. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Fluid VLT C1 130 is the only shorter-travel model; it retails at $11,499 USD, the additional cash demanded by the presence of a Crank Brothers Synthesis Carbon Enduro wheelset with Industry Nine 1/1 hubs, Ultimate level Rockshox suspension and a SRAM XX/SL group The Norco Fluid VLT 140 is remarkably wieldy, with calm, balanced and supportive suspension with excellent traction that bestows a confidence and reassurance that is not easily matched. — Jessie-May Morgan Like most other eMTB motors, the Bosch SX suffers from a clutch-based rattling noise that is frustratingly audible on the trail. The notable, rattle-free exception is the TQ-HPR50 Frame Construction & Motor System The Fluid VLT 130 and 140 eMTBs are all built around the Bosch Performance Line SX system, a 55 Nm drive unit paired with a non-removable 400 Wh battery. A 250 Wh PowerMore range extender is an optional extra, boosting capacity up to a maximum of 650 Wh. Norco forgo the heads-up Kiox or Purion displays, opting instead for a relatively clutter-free cockpit with only the bar-mounted remote that allows you to switch between assistance modes (Eco, Tour, eMTB and Turbo) and activate walk-mode, which can be somewhat helpful in the event of a forced dismount on a steep slope. Despite its sub 2kg weight, the Bosch SX motor actually has the capacity to deliver the same 600 W peak power of the meatier 85 Nm CX motor employed by the Norco Range VLT. However, you have to pedal faster to convince it to give you that. While the 2.9 kg CX motor will give you 600 W at a cadence of just 70 rpm, the Bosch SX asks you to spin at 100 rpm before it will dish out the big watts. This can sometimes make one feel a bit like a hamster in a wheel, but those with a naturally high pedaling cadence wont be fazed. The mud-flap on the lower link prevents build up in the gap between the chainstay bridge and the front triangle, but isn't wide enough to protect the main pivot bearings Chainstay protection is adequate Happily, Norco have opted not to run the cables through the headset. They enter aft of the headtube at wee recesses that, aside from looking neat, also reduce stress on the cables and hose as they enter the frame. Adequate frame protection is provided on the drive-side chainstay, though it's difficult to know whether it does much to quieten chain slap over the noise of the motor's clutch rattling as you descend. While some eMTBs wire the SRAM AXS derailleur into the motor system, Norco opted for a battery-operated derailleur The charging port is well-sealed. It can be tough to flick open, but at least that means its incredibly unlikely to open while you're riding along All sizes of the Fluid VLT can take the 250 Wh range extender, or a water bottle; the S2-S5 frames take a 750ml, while the S1 front triangle can only accommodate a 620ml bottle when used with a side-entry cage. Norco will soon have available an adaptor that will allow or the mounting of a standard cage. An extra set of bosses on the underside of the top tube offer a secure mounting point for tools. ISCG tabs are provided for a chain guide and a bash guard. Geometry & Sizing The Norco Fluid VLT 140 and 130 models are available in fives sizes, accommodating riders from 5ft 1" (155cm) to 6ft 5" (195cm) tall. Norco use their Ride Aligned system to size riders to an appropriate frame, using height only. On the 140 models, reach figures are 422.5mm, 447.5mm, 472.5mm, 495.5mm, and 522.5mm, respectively. The rear-center length grows modestly with the increasing size of the front triangle; the chainstay lengths are 432mm on Size 1 & 2, 436mm on Size 3, 440mm on Size 4 and 444mm on Size 5. This is a semi-proportional approach to sizing, with the larger frame sizes still subject to a greater rearward weight bias than the smaller ones. The Fluid VLT sees a slightly slacker head tube angle than the Fluid trail bike, at 64.5°. The bottom bracket is 10mm higher at 350mm, which is high even compared to other eMTBs of this category. The effective seat tube angle is 77° for the S3 (measured at HT height), getting progressively steeper with increasing frame size. That will go some way to offsetting the naturally more rearward bias of the seated pedaling position that comes with increasing saddle height. Suspension Design The 140mm travel Norco Fluid VLT delivers its rear wheel travel through a Horst-link suspension platform, with a 185mm x 55mm trunnion mount shock. All 140 models are provided with piggyback-equipped air shocks, save for the most affordable C4 140 which gets an in-line shock. The average leverage ratio is 2.54. That's delivered in a very progressive manner. Norco no longer publish detailed kinematic information as they once did. Instead, they have provided us with the following information: On Leverage: The Fluid VLT 140 has an overall progression of 27% with starting leverage between 3 and 3.1 dropping to 2.3 at full travel. We carried over some learning from the Fluid pedal bikes we launched previously where we moved to slightly longer stroke lengths to achieve our wheel travel targets. This allowed us to increase progression while keeping the average leverage in a suitable range to keep shock pressures below max pressure for heavier riders. We’ve found this level of progression allows for a good balance of initial sensitivity, support and bottom out control either with high volume air shocks like the Fluid VLT as well as coil options if a customer wanted to swap to something aftermarket. On anti-squat and anti-rise: We took a similar approach to the Fluid VLT as we did on the Fluid pedal bike in defining anti-squat, where we consider the combined contribution of anti-squat, leverage curve and damper tune to the pedaling characteristics of the bike. We’ve found through ride testing that getting more support from the leverage curve and damping circuit helps to support the rider under acceleration and lets us reduce anti-squat while still having a bike that pedals well. I think this is especially true on e-bikes where the motor smooths out torque delivery and reduces the peakiness of the acceleration generated through the pedal stroke. It can be a bit of a different feel to bikes with high anti-squat that may have a bit more get up and go under power on smooth trails but we’ve found our approach to give a good balance between efficient feel on smooth trails while providing traction for technical climbing. The Fluid VLT has a pretty typical Horst link anti-rise characteristic which is relatively consistent through travel sitting at approx. 50-60% depending on the centre of gravity height used. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $9999 Travel 140mm Rear Shock Fox FLOAT X2 Performance Elite, 185mm x 55mm Trunnion Fork Fox 36 Performance Elite, 150mm Crankarms Praxis Carbon Bosch, 165mm Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 Eagle AXS T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar One Up Carbon 800mm, 20mm Rise Stem Norco SL, 40mm Grips WTB Wavelength Grip Brakes SRAM Code Silver Stealth, Centerline Rotors (200mm F, 180mm R) Wheelset Crank Brothers Synthesis Alloy Tires Continental Kryptotal Fr (Trail), Continental Xynotal (Trail) Seat Fizik Ridon Seatpost One Up Travel Adjust Dropper, 31.6mm, 125mm (S1), 150mm (S2), 170mm (S3), 200mm (S4, S5) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Norco Fluid VLT Test Bike Setup At 163cm tall, I fall between the S1 and S2 sizes. Despite sitting closer to the S1, Norco supplied me with the S2 with a 447.5mm reach and a 40mm stem. That is a little longer than I would normally choose to ride, but I haven't felt that the bike is necessarily too long. Sure, it's not the most compact, but it doesn't feel unwieldy in flat corners, which is where an ill-fitting bike normally reveals itself. I used the Norco Ride Aligned platform to set the bike up, initially. I plugged in the following information: 163cm tall, 59 kg, Frame Size S2. Then, there's a sliding scale for "Suspension Feel" - which I set to the middle between Soft and Firm. For "Trail Grip," I selected Low to account for the soft, slippery conditions that were rapidly developing, and selected Mixed for "Trail Type," as opposed to Tech or Flow. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 130 lbs / 59 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan The Ride Aligned suggestion was a 740mm bar (my preferred width), with a 20mm rise, 15mm of stem spacers, with 18 psi in the front tire and 20 psi in the rear tire. That's a little lower than I'd normally choose to run with a Trail Casing, especially on an e-bike, but I went with it. The bike was shipped with the dropper set to 150mm, but I was able to increase that to 170mm to give plentiful clearance. Also provided are suspension setup specifics; 69 psi in the fork with 1 volume spacer, and 151 psi in the shock with 0 volume spacers. It also tells you the exact number of clicks to run on the rebound and compression dials. The suggested setup gave me 27% sag on the shock, with a damping setup that is firmer and slower than I usually settle on, but again I went with it, setting the bike up exactly as suggested. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing of the Fluid VLT C1 140 took place over the last month in the Tweed Valley, Scotland. Trails comprised a mix of flowing bike park, DH tracks and hand-cut natural trails, with intermediate greasy conditions turning to full slop-fest. Climbing The Bosch SX motor will feel natural to riders with a relatively high pedaling cadence. It only produces the peak power of 600 W around cadences of 100 rpm. You can't be lazy with it; if you don't make a concerted effort to spin quickly, the motor's assistance dies down considerably. On a regular bike, I have a tendency to grind my way up a fire road, rarely choosing to spin up in the 52T cog. I quite like that the Bosch SX motor forces me to increase my cadence; it makes riding an e-bike feel more like exercise, as compared to some of the full-powered eMTBs that permit you full torque and full power even when your cadence drops to 50 rpm. The high cadence demand of the Bosch SX motor limits its potential for technical climbing. When climbs are littered with rocks and roots, care must be taken in the timing of pedal strokes. To maintain the higher end of the SX motor's power output, you basically have to throw caution to the wind, spinning quickly in the hope that you'll not catch your pedal on something. Norco have seemingly accounted for that somewhat with a relatively high bottom bracket (350mm) and shorter 165mm cranks. Still, I was glancing pedals off the trail's chunky bits too often for my liking. To my mind, a motor that delivers a higher torque at lower cadences (like the EP801 RS of the Orbea Rise) can outperform the Bosch SX motor in some scenarios where a lot of assistance is required but the timing of pedal strokes must be carefully considered. As ever, there are pros and cons. With the low torque at low cadences, the rear wheel is much less likely to break traction and spin out when the ground is soft or loose. The Bosch SX motor is great in that regard, allowing you to manage the assistance when navigating climbs with lots of switchback corners. The power delivery isn't quite as smooth as some. There is hardly any discernable lag - when you back off on the pedals, the motor backs off its assistance almost immediately. That's experienced in the rear suspension as pronounced pedal bob, most noticeable when pedaling along the flat in the middle or bottom end of the cassette. I found that the threshold lever on the Fox X2 shock did little to eliminate that. That said, the pedal bob is less pronounced when pedaling up a climb in the larger cogs of the cassette. The S2 has a seat tube angle of 76.75°, giving an effective (or horizontal) top tube length of 593mm; that is a fair bit longer than most bikes I've ridden recently (with the exception of the Pivot Shadowcat ). In the seated pedaling position, I felt my weight was too far off the back. Sliding the saddle forward on the rails effectively steepens that seat tube angle, giving a much more comfortable position for climbing. At least in respect to the seated pedaling position, I believe the S1 frame would have given a better fit with a much shorter effective top tube length. That said, for a bike of this category, sizing decisions are, as they should be, made on reach, given that is the fit number that impacts descending performance the most. Ultimately, I think the seat tube angle of the Fluid VLT is a little on the slack side. Even though they get steeper as the frame sizes get larger, I'm not confident it'll be enough to give the tallest riders a comfortable pedaling position for climbing very steep terrain. Arguably, a steep seat tube angle is more important on an eMTB than it is on a non-motorized bike, given that riders are far more likely to be pedaling up very steep climbs thanks to the free torque that's on tap. Descending The Fluid VLT felt like a very composed descender straight off the bat. Rear wheel traction is nothing short of superb, smoothing out trail chatter with no drama, allowing me to look further ahead to anticipate the next corner. I was really impressed by how balanced the setup was, and can say that the Ride Aligned suggestions were an excellent starting point for me. That said, as I got up to speed on familiar trails, I felt the shock was packing down over long rooty sections. That was easily resolved by opening up the rebound damping to allow the shock to return faster after compressions. The progressivity of an air shock combined with the linkage's 27% progression delivers a bike doesn't give up its rear wheel travel very easily. Departing from the Ride Aligned setup, I backed off the compression damping on Low and High speed in a bid to encourage use of more travel. That went some way to giving me more millimeters, but ultimately I had to increase the sag to 30% to get even close to full travel. The X2 shock does have a generous bottom out bumper, which was likely why I never used all 140 mm of travel. In that regard, I do wonder whether a coil shock with a hydraulic bottom-out adjustment may be a better option for lighter riders. Before testing, I had some reservations over the length of the reach, but after riding a few trails I was satisfied it was more than manageable, and I felt no need to reduce the stem length. Sure, the bike is not as compact as some of the enduro bikes I have tested recently, and that's noticeable. It would have been interesting to try the S1 for comparison's sake, but realistically the fit was not something I felt was holding me back from riding the bike in a competent fashion. A major limitation was the SRAM Level SLV brakes. They are simply not powerful enough for a 140mm trail bike, let alone a 140mm eMTB. It's difficult to ride a bike to the full system (bike+rider) potential when stopping distances are much longer than one is accustomed to. Luckily, the C1 140 will actually be supplied with SRAM Code SLV brakes. I know these brakes well; they are far more powerful, and thus far more up to the task. When braking hard on steep, choppy terrain, the bike remains neutral and predictable. The geometry is maintained and traction is plentiful, with no unwelcome pitching sensations to report. The Fluid VLT is one of the more playful and chuck-able eMTBs I've ridden to date. It is far more responsive in my hands than either the Canyon Spectral:ON (a 23kg full-powered MX eMTB) and the Canyon Neuron:ON fly (a lightweight Bosch SX powered 29" eMTB). The suspension is also immeasurably more composed and confidence-inspiring in every riding scenario. Early on in the test period, I was very comfortable riding the Fluid VLT at good speed on familiar downhill tracks. In many riding scenarios, the bike rides as though it has more than its 140mm rear wheel travel. As compared to other lightweight eMTBs, I found the Fluid VLT to be altogether more flattering to ride. While largely impressive, the Fluid VLT isn't immune to the drawbacks associated with a 4 to 5 kg weight penalty introduced by a motor and battery. When a section of track is simultaneously steep, twisty and riddled with steps in short succession of one another, the bike feels clunky, cumbersome and deeply unflattering. The brakes will be a contributory factor here, but I feel the weight of the battery loading the front-end is largely responsible. This is only enhanced with greasy conditions; small mistakes come with bigger punishment, and it's much harder to recover composure after sliding off line. In these very technically challenging sections of trail, one can feel like one's skills have regressed by several years. This experience is not unique to the Fluid VLT; every eMTB I have ridden to date produces this feeling of vulnerability, and I am yet to ride one that imparts the confidence that permits the care-free riding style needed to progress. To finish on a positive note, when remaining in my comfort zone on fast, flowing, technical enduro and downhill tracks, the Fluid VLT performs very well, making it easy to have a lot of fun. It performs really well in the bike park, with heaps of mid-stroke support to prevent the bike from folding in fast, compressive berms, and it's nice and predictable off the lips. Canyon Neuron:ON fly How does it compare? I don't have an exhaustive list of comparable eMTBs with which to compare the Fluid VLT. After all, there aren't many lightweight 140mm travel eMTBs with a dedicated MX wheel configuration knocking about. The two most comparable eMTBs I've ridden in recent months include the Canyon Neuron:ON fly, and the Orbea Rise LT. It compares favorably to both, but for different reasons. The Fluid VLT easily trumps the Canyon Neuron:ON fly on performance. Its suspension is far more balanced, supportive and generally composed. It is far more capable and fun to ride on a variety of terrain. The Norco is the more expensive of the two - you get higher-end spec for your money with Canyon - but it will always be difficult to compete with the direct-to-consumer giants like Canyon and YT. Something worth noting is that while Canyon offer a 6 year frame warranty, Norco offer a lifetime warranty. Albeit, that's less of a biggie when it comes to eMTBs, as most of the motors only have a two year warranty. Between the Orbea Rise LT and the Norco Fluid VLT, it's not so easy to decide which is the better performer. Both are good in their own right, but I haven't yet had the opportunity to compare them on the same trails. The Rise LT has slightly more travel, pairing 150mm in the rear with a 160mm fork, and it runs 29" wheels. It also runs the torquier Shimano EP801 RS motor that delivers higher torque from a much lower cadence. I'd say the Orbea has the capacity to be a more competent technical climbing machine than the Fluid VLT, while the Norco is the more playful, chuck-able bike on the descents. The C3 140 goes for £5,999 / 6,499 € / $5,999 The C2 140 goes for £7,999 / 7,999 € / $7,999 Which Model is the Best Value? If you can make your peace with a 600 gram weight penalty, I'd say the Fluid VLT C2 model is probably the best value of the three 140mm travel models available. A combination of spec differences and an aluminum rear triangle contribute to that weight penalty but crucially, neither are likely to negatively impact that bike's performance in a major way. For $7,999 USD ( 2,000 USD cheaper than the C1 reviewed here) you get the same shock and, instead of the Fox 36 Performance Elite fork, you get a RockShox Lyrik Select+. While the former has more external adjustments of damping (low and high speed), the latter is known to me as a very capable fork that will satisfy the needs of the vast majority of riders. It gets the TRP Trail EVO brakes with 200mm rotors, which I know to be adequate (and importantly, more powerful than the Level SLV brakes mentioned here). It also gets a SRAM T-Type AXS drivetrain of the GX flavor, and the easily adjustable TranzX RAD+ dropper post. The C2 runs the same Continental tires, but on a WTB KOM Trail wheelset. Going to the most affordable model - the $5,999 USD C3 140 - there is the risk of reduced performance in a number of areas. It is the heaviest with a claimed weight of 20.3 kg in S3 and it gets a RockShox Psylo fork with an in-line shock and fewer external adjustments for fine-tuning of damper performance. It also comes with a mechanical Shimano Deore 12 speed group, and a fixed-length dropper seat post from TranzX, meaning that some riders will immediately want to upgrade to something with more drop. Technical Report Fox 36 Factory w/GRIP2 Damper: The fork performed really well, and I felt I had a sufficient range of damping adjustment to adapt the fork to different trails. SRAM Level SLV Brakes: Performance of the bike was held back by the SRAM Level brakes. These were not spec-correct, however. The C1 140 model reviewed herein will actually be supplied to customers with the SRAM Code Ultimate brakes, which are far more powerful and therefore appropriate. Level brakes are provided on the C1 130 model. WTB Wavelength Grips: I'm not a fan of the flared outboard portion of these grips. It leads to premature hand a wrist fatigue for my small hands. At 140mm long, they are also unnecessarily long for small hands. I optimize my cockpit setup for braking position, positioning the levers such that I can rest the outboard edge of my hand on the outboard edge of the grip. The closeness of the SRAM Stealth master cylinder to the bar prevented perfect positioning of the Bosch bar-mounted remote. In order to access it to switch between modes, I have to take my hand clean off the grip. Continental Trail Casing Tires: I really liked the Continental Kryptotal Fr front tire. However, the Xynotal lacked sufficient bite for climbing in soft, wet conditions. The Trail casing tire was easily punctured. In the rear at least, a tougher Enduro casing tire would have been the more appropriate option. TranzX RAD+ Adjustable Dropper Seat Post: Another component that is not spec-correct. The C1 140 model reviewed herein will ship to customers with a OneUp post. Regardless, the TranzX post is the more easily adjusted of the two. It takes less than 30 seconds to shim it down in 5mm increments over a 30mm range, at the trailside without the use of tools. Pros + Excellent performance-to-weight ratio + Very capable and playful for a mid-travel eMTB + Norco Ride Aligned provides a well-balanced suspension setup + Plenty of seat post insertion depth for long travel droppers + Lifetime warranty (on frame) Cons - Seat tube angle could feel too slack for some - Motor's high cadence demand limits potential for technical climbing - No aluminum option Pinkbike's Take Norco has a reputation for delivering short-travel bikes that can be ridden hard, and the Fluid VLT eMTB does not depart from that. Its composed, well-balanced and supportive suspension bestows a confidence one might normally associate with a more aggressive, longer-travel bike. It's not the lightest SL eMTB money can buy, but it's fair to say it has one of the best performance-to-weight ratios I've experienced to date. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech eMTB Reviews Norco Fluid Vlt Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: The Orucase B2 MTB is Tiny, but Not For Everyone
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Orucase B2 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/e6NHnDw1 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 11:54 Flying with a bike is a generally unpleasant experience, but one I'm pretty familiar with. I take a bike on just about every work trip, and there have been plenty of flights this year. Accompanying me on all of these has been the Orucase B2 MTB, the smallest bike case on the market. For those who value packed space above all else, the B2 is hard to beat - but it might not be for everybody, considering how involved the packing job can be. That said, the small size can pay dividends when it comes to navigating airports, trains, and tiny rental cars, so the work might just be worth it. Orucase B2 MTB Details • Fits XL MTB • Stows into 1/3 size • 1680 Denier Ballistic Nylon Exterior • Aluminum base rails and oversized wheels • Backpack or roller carry • Weight: 18 lbs / 8.16 kg • Price: $649 USD • orucase.com One bike, to go. Fit & Features The Orucase B2 MTB is the larger of the two cases the company offers, with the standard B2 oriented more towards road bikes and smaller frames. The case's overall size is as small as can fit a mountain bike frame, with room to the sides for wheels and components. Orucase quotes the case as fitting an XXL frame, but I'd give a more conservative estimate, as my XL bikes have fit quite snugly. This fit ultimately depends on your bike's geometry, as chainstay length, reach, and stack all play a critical role. If you're on the fence about fit, shoot them an email, or consult my short list of confirmed fit bikes below. I've taken the following bikes on trips with me without fit issues: Santa Cruz Tallboy, XL | Rocky Mountain Altitude, L | Specialized Stumpjumper 15, S5 | Frameworks Enduro, L. High quality wheels. Hard bottom and a durable handle. The whole case packs away into a tidy little bundle. For me, the most critical feature of the B2 case is how small the case itself can be when broken down. Unlike traditional bike cases, the B2's internal armor can be removed, folded down, and stowed for easy storage at home while traveling. This is key for folks like me who live in small homes and often find themselves in tiny cars in foreign lands. Even with a full bike inside, the case fits into the back of compact vehicles and is negotiable on public transportation. It's not the easiest, but it is definitely easier to deal with than a full size case or bike box. If weight is the primary concern, the B2's 18-pound figure does clock in at about a pound less than the average large bike case, but still weighs quite a bit more than a cardboard box and packing material. Well-protected rotor storage. Cinch straps to keep the load tight. The side panel armor zips off and folds up. Lightly padded wheel bags are included and easily fit an inflated 29x2.5" tire. There's ample protection built into the case, but Orucase does offer a bike armor kit for those who want extra protection. It costs $129 USD, and adds some secure padding to the frame and bulky components. Once the bike is packed into the case, you can cinch down the load with the exterior compressions straps, assuring nothing will be able to jostle around too much. For carry options, there are two drag handles on the front of the bag, and a pair of oversized wheels to keep things moving as you run after the bus you missed. If you'd rather keep the bike on your back, there are deployable backpack straps that tuck into the case's outside panel. I preferred the wheel method personally, as I usually have one or two other on-body bags to deal with while carrying the case. Packing and Traveling I've flown with the Orucase B2 quite a bit, with nothing but good results to report. The case is secure, safe, and well protected, and I've had no damage occur despite some long journeys with plenty of plane exchanges and other modes of travel thrown in for good measure. Like any bike bag, your success on this front entirely comes down to how you pack it. I've got my methods, and have had great luck using knee pads, jackets, and and shop rags to make sure no loose parts are touching each other or getting knocked around. Keeping things tight is usually the best method to prevent real damage, and the B2's compression straps help a lot on this front. Looking at the packing procedure as a whole, the Orucase is far from a thoughtless job. I'd call it a difficult procedure, relative to your average bike case or the classic cardboard box. Because the case is essentially the size of the frame from dropout to top of headtube, you'll have to remove the following to get it to fit: wheels, rear derailleur, fork, stem/handlebar, front brake, pedals, and fender. Far from full disassembly, but pretty involved if you're not a confident mechanic. For me, the process of packing a bike took about 30-40 minutes until I felt good about throwing it to the baggage handlers, but that time has crept down a bit as I've honed in on my methods. For folks who want this small packed space, but don't have the skills to build a bike with limited tools, I'd consider getting in touch with a shop at your destination to see if they can handle the unpacking and repacking for you. Again, I had no problem with this process, and didn't mind the few extra minutes it took. For reference, packing something like an EVOC usually takes me about 10-15 minutes, and a carboard box is about the same, if a bit longer. All tucked in and ready to go. Hard to say if the patch helps the odds, but I'll take what I can get. Comparisons Most bike cases are essentially the same. They occupy a large space, can't break down much smaller than their full form, and weigh around 20 pounds. Cardboard boxes come in all shapes and sizes, and can be cut to perfectly fit your bike, but sourcing one on the backside of a trip can be difficult depending on what the circumstances of your trip are. For what it's worth, most World Cup racers travel with cardboard boxes, as the weight factor is paramount, and the convenience of being able to toss the cardboard once you've landed is pretty nice. All that said, the Orucase is what I'll continue to use for the foreseeable future. The packability of the empty case makes taking it along during your travels a lot easier, and the security of the well-packed packed bike is on par with the best cases out there. The small overall size makes sweet talking airline employees a little easier, and somehow allowed me to fly with my 41 lb Frameworks, the 18 lb case, and a healthy dose of gear without exceeding the overweight baggage limit. Your mileage may vary, but I've had good luck here. Orucase B2 MTB. The standard EVOC bag. Because of the Orucase's more freeform approach to packing, you'll have to get creative with your approach, but it can be just as secure as more traditional bike bags. The EVOC bag I'd been using previously is certainly easier to pack, and will would probably fit larger bikes with more ease, but for my use case the B2 feels ideal. Pros + Overall size is small as possible + Safe and secure when packed well + Collapsible case is excellent for travel and storage Cons - Not that light, despite size - Hard to pack, compared to competition - Won't fit the largest frame sizes Pinkbike's Take The Orucase B2 isn't the easiest bike bag to pack, but the overall size is best in class, and the minimalistic approach still offers great protection for the bike. Add to that the collapsible design, and you have an item that suits certain people - like me - very well. For those who don't want an involved and creative packing process, I'd look elsewhere, but this is the travel case I'll be using for the foreseeable future. — Dario DiGiulio Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Orucase Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: The Pivot Shadowcat is Niche & Nimble
author: Jessie-May Morgan
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, trail-bikes, pivot, pivot-shadowcat, jessie-may-morgan
article_text: REVIEW Pivot Shadowcat WORDS: Jessie-May Morgan PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson Pivot introduced the Shadowcat in 2022, adding it to what is now a vanishingly small group of bikes to put 27.5" wheels on a mid-travel platform. A configuration that would have been fairly standard 10 years ago is now a rarity, thanks to the rise of 29" wheels and the broad appreciation for their improved rollover capability. Nevertheless, as niche as it now is, the complete 27.5" wheelset still has its place in the lineups of some big brands. These smaller bikes are oft pushed as fun-focused, with less flipped on its head to actually mean more. The Pivot Shadowcat, with its 160mm fork and 140mm of rear wheel travel, is no different, with words like "agile," "lithe," and "feline creative mayhem" littering the marketing copy. Pivot Shadowcat Details • Carbon frame • Travel: 140mm / 160mm fork • Wheel size: 27.5" • 65.8º head angle • 76º seat tube angle • 430mm chainstays • Sizes: XS, S, M & L • Weight: 27.6 lb / 12.5 kg (size S) • Price: $8,899 USD (Pro XT/XTR w/ Carbon Wheels) • pivotcycles.com It all sounds incredibly attractive, and I can't deny I was very excited to give this one a shot. Alas, I shan't pretend I didn't miss the rather more carefree riding style that a 29" front wheel allows, but happily there's a bit more to the Shadowcat than its numbers alone. The Shadowcat is offered in no fewer than eight complete builds, with prices ranging from $6,399 USD to $11,399 USD, all of which get the same custom-tuned Fox Float DPS Factory shock. I tested the $8,899 USD Pro XT/XTR w/ Carbon Wheels, which comes with Shimano Deore XT brakes, a 160mm Fox 36 with the FIT4 damper, and a 150mm Fox Transfer Factory dropper seat post. It rolls on Reynolds Blacklabel Trail Pro carbon wheels with an Industry Nine Hydra hubset, mounted with Maxxis Dissector 2.4" tires in the EXO casing. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Credit: Finlay Anderson The Shadowcat was at its best when I slowed things down a little, instead of just trying to ride fast everywhere. Its quick, responsive handling and poppy suspension encouraged me to seek out little hops and alternative lines off the main rut, which was a great deal more pleasurable than just trying to plow down — Jessie-May Morgan No thru-headset routing here, nor is there on any bike in the Pivot range. Construction & Features The Shadowcat is lightweight, with our size small test bike tipping the scales at just 12.5 kg (27.6 lbs). Like all Pivot frames, the Shadowcat gets a size-specific carbon layup. So, for each frame size, the orientation and positioning of the carbon fibers is subtly different, and the resulting tube sizes are appreciably different. The aim is to give all riders, no matter their chosen frame size, the same ride quality. They say that torsional stiffness and vertical compliance should “feel” the same whether you're a 5’2” rider on an XS or a 6’5” rider on an XL. While some Pivot trail bikes run a SuperBoost swingarm for increased stiffness, the frame designers did not deem that necessary here. Five sets of Enduro MAX bearings articulate two short aluminum links relative to the front triangle. All are of a considerable diameter that lends itself well to durability.
