The Dreadnought is the option for someone who wants maximum descending capability at the expense of pedalling efficiency and comfort, whereas the Ritual is more versatile, lighter on its feet, and geared toward fun across a wide range of scenarios, while still having enough travel to get you out of trouble in a pinch. Pros + Extremely fun in a wide array of conditions. + No mechanicals or issues, quiet and reliable. + Light weight and responsive. Cons - No adjustability. - Short chainstays can be a con for some. - More pedal feedback than some designs. Pinkbike's Take Following the Rascal SL’s success in our Field Test, the Ritual had a high bar to clear. While categorized as an enduro bike, that label doesn't quite capture its versatility. It manages to pair the climbing efficiency and low weight of a trail bike with enough travel to handle heavy terrain. By prioritizing a responsive feel and manageable weight over pure high-speed stability, it works across a much broader range of trails than most 160mm bikes. There is some pedal feedback to navigate, but for anyone not focused purely on race times, it’s a negligible trade-off for a bike that handles almost any terrain with ease. It’s a capable, all-day platform that just happens to be a blast to ride. — Stephane Pelletier Photos: Liam Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Regions in Article Cumberland trail network Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Revel Bikes Revel Ritual Stephane Pelletier Author Info: Stephane Pelletier Cumberland, BC, Canada Member since Sep 30, 2008 461 articles Based in Cumberland, BC, Stephane Pelletier is the Content Manager at Pinkbike, overseeing events, tech, media, and gear reviews. He got his start in bike shops at age 14 and competed in slopestyle before working in industry brand marketing roles. With 18 years of experience and an unrelated Geology degree from the University of Calgary, he pairs deep tech knowledge with a passion for the sport. Off the clock, he’s usually riding, surfing, snowboarding, or on his moto.

title: Review: The SR Suntour Durolux 38 EVO Fork is a Budget Performer
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: Suntour Durolux 38 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/42ckrs6p Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 04:18 Riders are perhaps most familiar with SR Suntour primarily as a manufacturer of OEM suspension. The brand is prolific in that regard, but in recent years we've seen it push harder than ever into the high-performance segment, sponsoring riders like Tom Pidcock and Pauline Ferrand-Prevot on the XC side, with Anna Newkirk, Jack Reading, Roger Viera and Davide Palazzari having recently flown the flag on the gravity side. The Durolux 38 EVO reviewed here is the latest addition to SR Suntour's line of enduro and freeride forks, positioned as a mid-range option. This 38mm stanchion fork has a different chassis but the same self-equalizing air spring as is seen on the more expensive Durolux 38 R2C2 and RC2 forks. In place of a four-way adjustable damper cartridge is the RC+ damper with only low-speed compression and rebound externally adjustable. It is not a fully-sealed affair, and is better described as a semi-open bath damper. According to Suntour, the damper architecture on this one allows for extra oil flow for high speeds and hard impacts. Importantly, it is several hundred dollars more affordable than 38mm stanchion options from Fox, Rockshox, and Ohlins (when they aren't heavily discounted, that is). SR Suntour Durolux 38 EVO • Intended Use: Enduro • Travel: 160-180mm (170mm tested) • Axle-to-crown: 590.5mm • Wheel size/offset: 29" - 44mm • Damper: RC+ PCS • Adjustments: Low-Speed Compression, Rebound, Air Pressure & Spring Volume • Quick Service Ports • 38mm Stanchions • Brake Mount: 203mm direct • Weight: 2.34 kg (actual, uncut 270 mm steerer) • Warranty: 2 Years (fork), 1 Year (damper) • MSRP: $800 USD • srsuntour.com Technology & Features The Durolux 38 EVO fork weighs in at 2.34 kg with an uncut (27 cm) steerer, making it a fraction lighter, but not meaningfully so, than the latest Fox 38 and RockShox Zeb forks. Like those, it is home to 38mm stanchions, with magnesium lowers and a hollow forged aluminum crown. Similarly, it is positioned as the brand's stiffest single-crown fork, designed for freeride, bike parks laps, and the abuse that can be expected to be dished out an EDR race. It is 29" only with a 44mm offset, and is available in 160mm, 170mm and 180mm travel options. Changing the travel requires replacement of the air spring, but these are relatively affordable at $48 USD. Still, it's not free, and Manitou continue to win hard on that front with the internal travel adjustment system on the Mezzer enduro fork. Suntour make the EVO in two versions; there's the lighter hollow crown option discussed here, or there's the extra strong one for e-bke use that has a solid crown. Perplexing to me is the brake mount is sized for 203mm rotors. There's nothing to stop you from running a 200mm rotor as I did, but you will get slightly less pad contact. To my mind, given how prolific SRAM brakes are, it would make better sense for the brake mount to be sized for 200mm rotors, allowing riders to run an adaptor or spacers for any rotor bigger than that. The Durolux 38 fork has a slightly taller ride height than its competitors, owing to its longer axle-to-crown (A2C) measurement. At 170mm travel, the A2C is 590.5mm, versus the 583.7mm A2C of the equivalent Fox 38, and the 586mm A2C of the RockShox ZEB. Inside, we have the EQ air spring. It is relatively straightforward with a single positive and negative air chamber, with a self-equalizing port that allows the negative spring pressure to automatically calibrate. The progressivity of the air spring - how much it ramps up toward the end of its travel - is tunable with rubber volume spacers. The fork is supplied with three volume spacers installed, but you can increase that to a maximum of 6. The detents for the LSC and Rebound adjustment are barely perceptible The Durolux 38 EVO fork runs on their RC+ damper. It is not a fully sealed cartridge, owing to the existence of a blow-off port. If the oil level rises inside the cartridge during use, excess oil can be purged from the Internal Floating Piston (IFP) through that port. The piston itself is spring-backed, and the bleed hole helps maintain consistent IFP pressure. The cartridge and lower bath both use the same 5wt oil. SR Suntour say their RC+ damper actually has increased oil flow versus the sealed cartridge R2C2 PCS and RC2 PC dampers in their more expensive forks, allowing it to better manage high-speed impacts while remaining very supple off the top. It has just two dials; one for adjustment of low-speed compression (15 positions), and another for adjustment of rebound (26 positions). The compression shim stack does have capacity to distinguish between high- and low-speed movements, but only the low-speed can be adjusted externally by the rider. The compression and rebound circuits are separate thanks to a check valve on the shim stacks that prevents back-flow of the damper fluid. The Durolux comes with a bolt-on fender of modest dimensions. It's fine; it doesn't flap about, and it protects the fork seals nicely, but ultimately it is bettered by bigger fenders from brands like Mudhugger and RRP. When will for manufacturers make us proper size bolt-on fenders? On the lowers just south of the wiper seals there are quick service ports, or QSPs. These function just like the bleed ports on Fox forks, and the pressure relief valves on Rockshox forks, allowing the rider to release pressure build up in the lowers, something that occurs when the fork heats up, or when there's a big change in altitude. Pressure build up can increase the force required to get the fork moving into its travel, negatively impacting sensitivity. On the aforementioned, the valves are tool-free, but you'll need a 2.5mm Allen key to open the valves on the Suntour fork. The QSPs can also be used to inject oil into the lowers, replenishing the foam rings under the wiper seals that keep everything sliding smoothly. Happily, Suntour have done away with their proprietary axle system used on the previous Durolux 36, replacing it with a standard thru-axle. Unlike the 38mm options from from Fox and Ohlins, the Durolux fork does not have a floating axle. It is fixed, and does not self-align with the hub spacing. That's not really a deal-breaker for me, but it remains true that a floating axle allows the fork legs to perfectly self-align independently of the hub width tolerances. The axle used by SR Suntour, Rockshox, and many others, squeezes both fork legs together against the hub, which can introduce opportunity for increased friction if the hub tolerance is a way off. On this, Suntour say, "The Durolux EVO chassis is made with a new machining process that enables precise alignment of the bushings, axle, and brake bosses to a new standard. This ensures that the lower case is kept within the tightest tolerances in the industry for better sliding and axle mounting performance and durability". Suntour opt for a two-piece axle over a one-piece axle that would be more expensive to manufacture. I did run into an issue with this. The bolt-head is threaded into the axle body and fixed into position with thread lock. On my fork, the adhesive bond created by the thread lock expired such that when I tried to unthread the axle to remove the front wheel, the bolt-head itself unthreaded from the axle, leaving the axle fixed in the dropout. It can be removed from the opposite side with a 6mm Allen without damaging anything or using a proprietary tool. It could leave a rider out on the hill without the ability to install an innertube in a hurry. Though far from unsolvable, it is a point of pain that could be avoided with the use of the more expensive one-piece axle. Setup The Durolux 38 EVO is relatively straight-forward to setup, owing to the existence of a single positive air chamber, with only low-speed compression and rebound adjustments on the table. I ran the fork at 43 PSI, as is suggested for my 58 kg. Initial rides saw the low-speed compression and rebound adjusters set to the fully open position. The fork is supplied with 3 (of a possible 6) rubber volume spacers in its air spring. I incrementally removed these, one at a time, until there were none. Performance The small bump sensitivity of the Durolux EVO impressed immediately. The fork is very supple off the top, pushing into the early part of its travel with ease. The fork has seen in excess of 20 rides on the Marin Alpine Trail, including some warm, dry, dusty laps back in June of last year, though most of the testing has taken place over winter in much colder, wetter conditions. The small bump performance of the fork remains very impressive, making the Durolux 38 EVO one of the most comfortable enduro forks I have tested to date. After experimenting with the range of low speed compression settings, I settled on 4 clicks from fully open. This was, for the most part, enough to keep it riding higher in its travel, reducing the pitching sensation that can make one feel unduly vulnerable through slow, stepped sections of trail. Having removed all three rubber volume spacers, such that the positive air spring was in its highest volume state, I was able to compress it beyond 160mm of the 170mm travel available. The air spring still has a relatively pronounced ramp toward bottom out, and I never actually found the end of the travel, having put in some reasonable efforts on the steeper granite slabs of Aboyne. For the most part, I ran the rebound fully open. Even in very cold test conditions (0-5°C) the fork tracked well through rock gardens without noticeably packing down. In the slightly warmer, wetter (and much sloppier) conditions of late (10°C) I found the rebound a little fast, with the fork becoming a bit more difficult to manage. I have since found it beneficial to run a couple of clicks of rebound damping, helping to keep things calm and tracking smoothly. How does it compare? I've ridden a lot of enduro forks and, for my weight and riding style, the Suntour ranks near the top of those I've tested recently. In back-to-back testing, its sensitivity and support is equivalent to that of a freshly serviced RockShox Zeb with the Charger 3.1 damper (tuned for light rebound). Differences between the two forks were largely imperceptible in the colder conditions I have been testing them. The most appreciable difference was in the noise; the Rockshox fork is very quiet, while the Suntour makes an easily audible scooshing noise when it rebounds from deep in the travel. Sure, the RC+ damper in the EVO fork lacks the adjustability of more expensive dampers like the R2C2 damper from SR Suntour, the GRIP X2 from Fox, and the Charger 3.1 from Rockshox. But, that didn't prevent me from finding a setup that works well on a wide variety of terrain. If anything, it was nice to keep things simple with one less thing to over-think. Sales prices notwithstanding, the Fox 38 Factory ($1,249 USD) and Zeb Ultimate ($1,159 USD) are far more expensive than the Suntour Durolux 38 EVO, which is one of the most affordable enduro forks money can buy at just $800 USD. Perhaps it isn't fair to compare those top-of-the-line forks with Suntour's mid-range option, but it seems justified especially when the Suntour fork is, in my hands, offering comparable comfort and support to the Zeb Ultimate. Still, it's considerably cheaper than the Performance Elite version of the Fox 38, and $96 cheaper than the lower-end Zeb Select fork. Finally, while RockShox formally offer $30 shim kits for fine-tuning of compression and rebound damping, Suntour do not. They do however offer shim stack tuning as part of their $109 fork service and cartridge rebuild at SR Suntour service centers. They also stock all shims necessary for fine tuning, and can supply these alone for the home mechanic who is confident in doing the work. Pros + Excellent small bump sensitivity + Comparable performance to much more expensive competitors + Great value Cons - Not the quietest - No external adjustment of high speed compression and rebound - Detents on LSC and Rebound knobs aren't easily perceived Pinkbike's Take The Suntour Durolux 38 EVO is a compelling option for all enduro riders, whether on a budget or not. While it isn't without its quirks, its small bump sensitivity is very impressive, and its overall performance is equivalent to that of considerably more expensive 38mm forks. Chapeau. — Jessie-May Morgan Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Forks SR Suntour Durolux 38 Evo Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Yeti MTe - A Mid-Power Contender with TQ's New HPR60 Motor
author: Jessie-May Morgan
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, trail-bikes, yeti, yeti-mte
article_text: Yeti MTe Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/ZlLM59Ln Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 14:37 There has been a gaping hole in Yeti's lineup until today; the MTe is their first lightweight mid-power e-Bike and it is powered by the new TQ HPR-60 with, you guessed it, a maximum torque of 60 Nm. There are two batteries to choose from; a 290 Wh and a 580 Wh, as well as a supplementary 160 Wh Booster Pack. Like the full-powered 160E, the MTe uses their Six-Finity suspension platform to deliver slightly less rear wheel travel (145mm), paired with a 160mm fork. There are three solid build options to choose from - I have spent the last 6 weeks testing the lighter T4 XX model with the 290 Wh battery, while Kaz has been riding the heavier (but not very) T3 model with the 580 Wh battery, and we both have lots to share. Yeti MTe Details • Carbon frame • Travel: 160 F, 145mm R • 29" or MX • 64° head angle • Reach: 430, 460, 480, 505mm • Chainstay length: 449mm • TQ HPR-60 Motor • 290 or 580 Wh battery (160 Wh Range Extender, $600) • Actual weight: (T4) 17.2 kg / 37.9 lbs w/290 Wh battery - Small • Actual weight: (T3) 20 kg / 44.1 lbs / w/580 Wh battery - Large • Price: $14,300 (T4 XX, tested) / $12,650 (T3, tested) / $9,850 (C2) • Lifetime Warranty • yeticycles.com Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The MTe was at its best on faster tracks where you're committing to the sculpted berms and support offered by the main line. It carries speed tremendously well and feels really quite flattering through rhythm sections and consecutive berms where you're low to the frame and pumping to generate speed. Carrying that speed out and into rock gardens, the suspension feels excellent, tracking the ground in a composed fashion. — Jessie-May Morgan TQ's new color display shows how many watts the rider is inputting alongside how many watts the drive unit is supplementing that with Frame Details & Motor The Yeti MTe is first out the starting block with the new TQ HPR60 motor. The original HPR50 motor was lauded for being very quiet on the trail, but in terms of torque and power it has been the back-of-the-pack option for a few years - even amongst its mid-power competitors, the Fazua Ride 60 and the Bosch SX motor. That is semi-rectified today with the HPR60 now offering a maximum torque of 60 Nm, and a peak power output of 350 W. So, it is still the lowest power option out there (Fazua is 450 W, and Bosch SX is 600 W), but on the flip-side that should offer a decent advantage in terms of range. The actual peak power available will depend on which battery you choose: the lighter 290 Wh battery will limit your maximum power to 250 W, while the 580 Wh will allow use of the full 350 W. It is merely a question of amperage, not a limitation written into the software. The battery is removable for charging off-the-bike, or you can charge on-the-bike using the very well-sealed port on the non-drive side. It's a closed downtube and the battery slides out from the bottom. The motor integrates cleanly with the carbon frame, and importantly for Yeti it leaves enough clearance in key areas for their Sixfinity layout. For most riders the packaging is decent; it leaves clearance for a piggy-back shock and a water bottle, and a long enough straight seat tube to make use of a long travel dropper. That and the 20 kg weight of the T3 model with the bigger battery is quite impressive, especially considering the use of a 6-bar suspension platform and the adjustability that Yeti are bringing to the table with it. The small suffers a little on the packaging front; the T4 model I tested fits a 550ml bottle, but the T3 model with a piggy-back shock is going to run into problems. That said, Yeti says their 15oz (425 ml) Hot Lap bottle will fit no bother. It's a Yeti so of course the frame is carbon. For the first time on a production bike they are adding Vectran into their carbon layup to enhance the frame's impact resistance. It is something they have been doing with their Special Projects DH bike for a while. A wheel-chip between the rocker and the seat stay allows you to switch out the stock 29" rear wheel for a 27.5", correcting for the bottom bracket height reduction and preserving the geometry. Then, there are four possible locations for the lower shock mount. Two flippable flip-chips give four leverage curves to choose from, allowing riders to run the suspension's progression at 12%, 14%, 22% or 25%. It would be an aftermarket component, but there is also scope to adjust the reach and/or head angle, something that Yeti has confirmed would not void the frame warranty. The bike is super quiet owing to excellent protection along the stays, and guided internal cable routing that is secured at the ports. The internal carbon tubes aren't quite full length; they stop shy of the exit ports at the headtube to allow for a regular or moto brake setup. A mini-fender at the lower link is a nice touch for keeping some trail debris away from the bearings, of which there are sixteen. Ouch. There are no surprises elsewhere; the MTe has boost spacing, UDH for that T-Type compatibility, a wee top guide for chain retention, and it looks sick. Mike Kazimer has been testing the Yeti MTe T3 model in a size large with a 580 Wh battery - it weighs 44.1 lbs / 20 kg Geometry The MTe has the longest chainstay length of any Yeti in their current lineup at 449mm. While they afford their unassisted bikes a frame size-specific rear-center length, all four sizes of the MTe get the same chainstay length. The actual length isn't wild for the larger frame sizes, but it's quite a standout number on the geometry chart if you're considering the small with a reach of 430mm. The smaller sizes will have less of a rearward weight bias than the larger frame sizes, and I think it makes good sense to offer that 35mm rise bar so everyone has scope to get their hands higher should they feel the need. The head angle is a sensible 64° given the bike's intentions, and the seat tube angle is an effective 77° - not the steepest, and it wouldn't surprise me to learn that taller riders want their saddle biased forward on the rails. The T4 XX model gets 165mm cranks, while the more affordable ones get 160mm cranks. I think e-Bikes benefit from shorter cranks, and there's good justification for going down to 150mm as Transition has done with the Regulator. There are no geometry adjustments available per se, but the headtube can take a reach/angle set without voiding your warranty. The frame is also rated for use with a fork up to 180mm. All models and sizes are shipped with a complete 29" wheelset, but a 27.5" rear is also doable. A flip-chip at the seatstay-rocker pivot adjusts for the difference in BB height when switching between wheel sizes. It's a super easy job - it takes a few minutes and the washers and chips don't fall out of the frame, so you don't need five hands to reassemble. Love that. Yeti MTe Suspension Articulation 0 seconds of 8 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/FsIweJca Copied Live 00:00 00:08 00:08 More Videos 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close Suspension Design The MTe delivers its 145mm of rear wheel travel through the Sixfinity suspension platform, damped by a 55mm stroke shock. Unlike other six-link designs like the Specialized Enduro, here the rear axle path and thus the anti-rise and anti-squat character is determined by all 6 bars - not four. This allows the engineers more fine control over those parameters while still allowing them independent adjustment of the progression. The actual rear wheel travel differs dependent on the progression setting; it is 145mm increasing a touch to 148mm in that most rearward 25% setting. A second set of lower shock mount chips allows for the 14% and 22% settings. The T3 X0 and C2 Eagle 90 models are shipped in the 14% setting. No matter which bike you buy, the second set of chips are provided so everyone has those four settings on the table. Yeti run the very same shock tune across all models; it is not tuned specifically for any one of those progression settings. It is also the very same damper tune on all frame sizes. They say the external adjustments on the Float and Float X rear shocks should be sufficient to accommodate a wide range of rider weights as well as the wide range of leverage rate change that is possible. The shock's damper will be working hardest in the lowest progression setting; 12%, where the shock pressure will be lowest and the speed of the shaft movement will be greatest (thereby generating the highest damping force). The 'inflection point' refers to the point at which the lower link switches from rotating clockwise to anti-clockwise Looking at other aspects of the kinematic, Yeti has tuned the Sixfinity platform with a fairly consistent anti-rise character throughout the stroke. It never strays far from the 60% mark, indicating the suspension will be allowed to extend under braking to recover from repeated hits. Anti-squat is on the higher end of the spectrum sitting north of 100% up to and a fair bit beyond sag. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $14300 Travel 145mm Rear Shock Fox Factory Float Fork 160mm Fox 36 Factory, GRIP X Headset Cane Creek 40 Cassette SRAM XX Eagle T-Type Crankarms SRAM XX, 165mm Rear Derailleur SRAM XX Eagle AXS Transmission Chain SRAM XX Eagle Flat-Top Shifter Pods SRAM AXS POD Handlebar Yeti Carbon 35mm rise, e-routing Stem Burgtec Enduro, 50mm Grips ODI Elite Pro Brakes SRAM Motive Ultimate Wheelset 29" DT SWISS EXC 1501 Carbon Hubs DT SWISS 240 DEG Tires Maxxis Minion DHF/DHR II, EXO, MaxxTerra Seat WTB Solano SL Titanium Seatpost Rockshox Reverb AXS, 175mm (S) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC You have to hold down the up button on the controller to engage walk mode. It's not so easy to keep it depressed whilst pushing up a steep climb, making the feature unusable to me in most scenarios. On another note, I prefer the SRAM Pod shifter mounted separately to the brake - combined with the MX clamp as seen here, there is a compromise to be made between brake lever angle and shifter position - especially for those with small hands. Test Bike Setup I have been testing the lightest variation of the MTe; the T4 model in a size small with the 290 Wh battery. It weighs a very respectable 17.2 kg or 37.9 lbs, which incidentally is actually a touch lighter than my staff ride enduro bike - the Marin Alpine Trail, which doesn't have a motor or a battery. Like all models and size options, this one arrived with a complete 29" wheelset. It comes with the shock positioned for 12% progression - flipping this one would send it to the opposite end of the spectrum to give 25% progression. Included in the box is an alternate shock mount that gives the 14% or 22% progression settings. I initially set up the bike with the recommended 30% sag (16.5mm on a 55mm stroke), with the Float shock compression and rebound dials set to fully open - I added a little rebound damping later on to keep the rear end calm. With the Fox 36 and its GRIP X damper I opted for the manufacturer's recommended settings, which I am pleased to report do work very well for me. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Aboyne, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 127 lbs / 57.5 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan Testing Info I've ridden the MTe on pure flow trails, fast enduro tracks, slow and steep techy ones, and chunky downhill tracks. All of the testing has taken place in dry, flattering conditions. The T4 model I tested is shipped in the 12% progression setting with a complete 29” wheelset, and that is how I have ridden it most of the time. I'm at home on the 430mm reach, 50mm stem and the 35mm rise bar on the small, and I'm delighted with the 175mm dropper seat post - that's generous for a small. I struggled to use more than 50mm stroke in the 12% or the 25% progression settings with the stock 0.2mm spacer in the shock. Removing that allowed me to use all of the travel without any harsh bottom-outs. I'll get the question of range out of the way first; on the first discharge cycle I got just 13.17 km and 859 meters (2,818 ft) of elevation gain, using only the most powerful mode. On another ride where I switched between the middle and highest assistance settings I managed 20 km and 982 meters (3,220 ft). The range extender doesn't fit the small. I'd like to see Yeti offer the 360 Wh battery, it could be a good compromise. Mike Kazimer, on the MTe T3 model in a size large with the 580 Wh battery, was able to rack up 5,700 vertical feet and 27 miles (1737 meters / 43.5 km) with some battery left over, using a mix of the full and partial power modes. Climbing The MTe gives a comfortable seated pedaling position - it isn't overly steep but coupled with the long chainstays it is enough for me to feel confident sending the bike up steep pitches without too much risk of looping out. Running a lower rise bar and pushing the saddle forward on the rails would improve the bike's uphill handling further - if ever I was to go Munro-bagging aboard this thing with the poorly-educated ambition of cleaning the technical climbs. Yeti has a custom tune on the TQ HPR60 wherein the most powerful mode is tweaked to give the full 60 Nm of torque from a low cadence, something that should prove to be advantageous on trials-like sections as it should, hypothetically, reduce the risk of catching a pedal. There is no appreciable over-run on this system though, and you'll still need to look to the full-power options for that trump card. The system is very quiet, and the motor sounds more like the purring of a cat (albeit at a higher pitch) than the classic whine you get with most other drive units. The decibels increase the more you ask of the system, but for the most part it fades into the background - very socially acceptable on a mixed group ride. In terms of assistance, it does still leave a little to be desired as compared to the Fazua Ride 60. While both motors have a top-end torque of 60 Nm, the Fazua motor has a 12-second boost function in Rocket mode that takes the peak power to 450 W. I won't knock it too hard, though. It takes the edge off tough sections of track without asking for too much cadence, and its reduced power will naturally be beneficial for range. If you're after a pure shuttle machine, look elsewhere. This is best used as a ride extender for those who like to pedal. Descending The bike is very quiet descending, too. That is a treat on any bike, but especially so on an e-bike. Most motors have a 'clack' coming from the clutch that arises from the oscillations in the upper length of chain, but the TQ HPR-60 motor is silent in this regard. Elsewhere, the comprehensive stay protection on the drive-side nigh-on eliminates noise from chain slap. I am yet to hear a single rattle from this thing; chapeau. The MTe isn't as playful as others; the Norco Fluid VLT is standout in that regard. The Yeti's traction is however excellent, and I find it rides through rough chop much better than I might have expected a 145mm travel bike to. It has a stable, consistent and reliable feel that we've those long chainstays to thank for. The downside there; I found it wasn't so easy to unweight the front wheel at short notice. Being a short rider, I do generally have a preference for shorter chainstays. A short rear-center is especially useful when riding slower speed tracks where hunting out weird lines is fun. That said, I can appreciate the benefits of a more balanced weight distribution offered by a longer rear-center on faster, rougher sections of track. It carries its weight fairly well for an e-Bike. The steepest most committing tracks did highlight the need for extra compression damping on the fork to keep the bike from pitching too violently on steep slab-to-dirt transitions, and the Grip X damper on the Fox 36 did not let me down. The SRAM Motive brakes are sufficient, but I can see a lot of riders benefiting from an upgrade to the more powerful Maven brakes that could make things more comfortable when braking late. To be fair, the T4 model gets a 200mm rotor up front but it's a 180mm in the rear - upgrading to larger rotors would probably be a good idea before forking out for more expensive brakes. Out of the box, the stock configuration of the MTe T4 model is 12% progression with complete 29" wheelset. I did some back-to-back testing on one of the steeper, rougher local tracks, switching between the lowest and highest progression settings (correcting for sag) just to see if one suited me and that track better than the other. The difference in ride feel was clear – the ramp effect in the 25% setting was quite aggressive – it still felt predictable and calm, but came up on very firm support on those medium to larger hits which felt a little too aggressive at times. At least on that terrain I preferred the consistency of the stock 12% setting. There's plenty more testing to do here, though. The 12% progression setting can feel a little firm over repetitive hits on soft ground (think loamy braking bumps), and I have a suspicion that one of the higher progression settings could give a more supple feel in those scenarios. I look forward to doing more back-to-back testing. The MTe was at its best on faster tracks where you're committing to the sculpted berms and support offered by the main line. It carries speed tremendously well and feels really quite flattering through rhythm sections and consecutive berms where you're low to the frame and pumping to generate speed. Carrying that speed out and into rock gardens, the suspension feels excellent, tracking the ground in a composed fashion. I recently switched out the 29" rear wheel for a 27.5". I had no issue with clearance on the 29", but I think the smaller rear wheel makes the bike's handling a little quicker in rhythm sections and on twisty flow trails where the turns are closely spaced. I've had a lot of fun on the MTe, fun that I am yet to have on a full power e-Bike with a big battery. If you're put off by the on-paper performance numbers of the TQ motor but enamored by the 120 Nm torque of the latest DJI and Specialized motors then this probably isn't the bike for you just now. It's easy to see why riders gravitate towards those ultra-powerful systems, but I continue to be more interested in mid-power options of which the Yeti MTe is a very well-executed example. Photo: Eric Mickelson Mike Kazimer's Ride Impressions of the Yeti MTe T3 Historically, I haven’t been that impressed by most mid-power e-bikes - they always seemed to pale in comparison to their full-powered siblings, and they didn’t seem to offer that much of an advantage over a regular bike. With the MTe, things are a little different. For one thing, the ride quality is top notch, for any bike, not just for an eMTB. The level of grip is excellent, and there’s still plenty of support to keep the bike from feeling overly mushy on firmer, faster trails. It'll hold its own on rougher, steeper trails too - this isn't some high-strung trail bike that gets flustered when things take a turn for the technical; instead, it has handling closer to what you'd expect from an enduro bike on the descents. Then there’s the way that it sips on the battery, making it possible to knock off decent sized rides without running out of juice - I was able to get close to 6,000 vertical feel of elevation gain over the course of 27 miles, and that’s spending most of the time in the highest level of assistance, and without fully draining the battery. Plus, range anxiety is less of a thing with the MTe, since if the battery does die you’ll be pedaling a 44-pound bike home, which is hard, but not nearly as hard as it would be on a 54+ pound behemoth. The speed and power that the TQ puts out fit the overall character of the bike nicely. I started thinking of this as my adventure bike, the one I’d grab to explore rarely frequented trails, or when I wanted to maximize the length of a ride without feeling like the whole thing went by in extra-extra fast forward. The bulk of my time on the MTe was with it in the stock, full 29" configuration, but I recently swapped out the rear wheel for a 27.5" one, and that's the setting I prefer - the rear wheel is further out of the way in the steeps, and the motor helps minimize the amount of difference the smaller wheel size makes on the climbs. I’d gravitate towards a full-power bike if blasting up a fire road and down a gnarly descent was my main goal, but the range and silent operation of the MTe meant that this was the test e-bike I kept grabbing, even when there was a faster, more powerful option sitting right next to it. Jessie-May will discuss that sky high price tag, which unfortunately will put this bike out of reach of most riders; I’d love to see a non-motorized version of the MTe hit the market - the Sixfinity suspension design’s performance is extremely impressive. - Mike Kazimer Photo: Eric Mickelson How Does It Compare? It may not surprise you to learn that, at 57 kgs or 126 lbs, I have struggled to enjoy many e-bikes, finding the added weight unwieldy and cumbersome. The Yeti MTe is a bit of an exception on that front, coming in at a weight that is similar to my non-motorized Marin Alpine Trail. That weight is distributed differently, of course. Concentrating a considerable amount of weight in the downtube is always going to have an effect on handling, and I do feel that on the MTe, but it is absolutely one of the better handling e-bikes I’ve ridden to date – up there with the Norco Fluid VLT, and the Orbea Rise LT. The TQ motor isn't as powerful as the Bosch SX on the Norco, or the Shimano EP801 RS on the Orbea, and it should therefore offer more range for the equivalent battery size though I wouldn't be able to back-to-back test that theory. The downside is that 350 W (250 W on the 290Wh) will limit the bike's prowess on steep climbs. If you're looking for a bike that excels on the front, the Rise LT is where it's at. The most affordable model of the Yeti MTe goes for $9,850 USD The most affordable model of the YT Decoy SN 29 goes for $6,499 USD The Yeti is the lighter of the two however, and its suspension has that buttery-smooth edge over most bikes I've tested. It feels like it would be the more capable, faster bike on most descents. It would also be my preference over the far more affordable YT Decoy SN 29 I tested recently; the two bikes have a similar set of geometry and travel numbers, but the YT runs the more powerful Fazua Ride 60 motor and a 480 Wh battery. I've had more fun riding the Yeti which may be no surprise given it is considerably lighter - yes, the battery is smaller, but with the reduced power the difference in range might not be all that big in the end. Regardless of range let's not forget the most important thing: it doesn't matter how many times a bike can get you up a hill, if it's not the one you want to ride down it, you've pedaled the wrong one to the top. With budget considerations, know that the Yeti does have the pricier build options of the lot, though that C2 Eagle 90 build offers pretty good value when you compare it to spec offered elsewhere. Which Model is the Best Value? I won't compare the $14,300 USD T4 XX build to the other two; with its 290 Wh battery and weight-conscious spec it is a different kettle of fish. The price tag puts this model out of reach for most riders and to be honest, it is a little perplexing. For that money, there's surely justification for offering the customer the 290 Wh and the 580 Wh battery? Discuss. Moving onto the 580 Wh builds, without a shadow of a doubt the C2 Eagle 90 Transmission model is the best bang-for-buck, retailing at $9,850 USD - still far from a budget option but it's a solid build all round, with powerful SRAM Maven Bronze brakes, the mechanical Eagle 90 Transmission, DT E1900 alloy wheelset and Schwalbe's Radial tires. It's not the lightest with a claimed weight of 20.09 kg / 44.3 lbs, but it is certainly no brute. This one has the Performance level suspension from Fox. The $9,850 USD C2 Eagle 90 model offers the best value for money UK customers should know of another option; the C2 Eagle 90 Factory will go for £9,499. It's the same as the C2 Eagle 90 seen above, only with Fox Factory level suspension components. Technical Report Fox 36 Factory Fork - GRIP X I find this fork very smooth and comfortable, and it offers a good usable range of damping at this lower end of the rider weight spectrum. TQ HPR60 Motor The motor has very socially acceptable levels of noise when climbing and it is perfectly silent while descending. Bravo. Unfortunately on two of the discharge cycles the battery management system was misleading, reading 12% before going straight to zero. We are awaiting comment from TQ on this issue. This issue was only experienced with the 290 Wh battery. RockShox Reverb AXS: It's super nice to have a 175mm drop seat post on a small frame, and this new electronic one from SRAM is great. It has a very intentional small amount of squish when positioned anywhere below full extension, which SRAM says makes things more comfortable when climbing very steep technical sections. I can't say I noticed it at all. It is however plenty fast enough to actuate and there's no play whatsoever - love that. Hopefully they get this wired into the main battery system sooner rather than later so we haven't got that extra battery to worry about. DT Swiss 240 DEG Hub: After 100 km or so the freehub starting skipping, and within a few hundred meters it failed. Opening up the hub, there was no damage to the ratchets or springs but one of ratchets was getting stuck down into its recess and the spring force wasn't enough to push it back to engage. It's not clear if this arose as a result of too much grease inside the mechanism, or whether that ratchet is tilting in its seat with friction preventing it from returning to engage. Pros + Really fun on a wide variety of trails + Traction-plentiful suspension that's adjustable + 29" and MX compatible + Quiet up, silent down + Good range with the 580 Wh battery Cons - Lower max power when equipped with smaller battery - Sixteen frame bearings to maintain - Very expensive - Issues with the battery status readings Pinkbike's Take Torque and power to one side, let's not forget the most important thing: it doesn't matter how fast or how many times a bike can get you up a hill, if it's not the one you want to ride down it, you've pedaled the wrong one to the top. The Yeti MTe hammers this home with an enviable performance-to-weight ratio. It's never going to be a super-shuttle when compared to any full-powered affairs, and anyone looking for a power stage-winning race bike will need to look elsewhere. The MTe is a fine example of a lightweight e-bike, and those with deep pockets will delight in the fantastic performance of the Sixfinity suspension. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Photos: Pete Scullion Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Yeti Yeti Mte Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Yeti Goes Long With the Ultra-Adjustable LT
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Yeti is often associated with their race bikes, and for good reason. The SB160 might be the most successful single model of enduro race bike to date, with a dominant pedigree preceding it. In keeping with that heritage, the brand's new long-travel chassis aims to stay quick between the tape, but add some latitude for a wider range of setup options. The LT is a huge step for Yeti on a few fronts. Not only is it the most adjustable production bike they've made to date, but it features geometry you'd hardly expect from a traditionally conservative brand, and even marks the departure from their synonymous Switch Infinity suspension layout. Yeti LT Details • Travel: 160mm / 170mm fork • 29" or MX wheels • Carbon fiber frame • 63.5-64° head angle, 77-77.5° seat angle • 435-510mm reach, 620-650mm stack • 439-475mm chainstay length • Price: $8,400 - $10,200 USD • Actual weight: 36.6lb / 16.6kg (XL, stock X0 build) • yeticycles.com I owned an SB160 for a couple years, and recently spend a lot of time on the Bosch-powered LTe. Both are obvious precedents for this bike, and represent two very impressive takes on their respective genres. As a result, I came into the LT test with very high expectations and excitement. Many of those expectations were met, but some remained elusive - dig in to learn more about the many faces of the LT. The Best Cornering XL Bike I've Ridden | Yeti LT Review 0 seconds of 9 minutes, 48 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/TtW6QzPv Copied Live 00:00 09:48 09:48 More Videos 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:25 Range of Motion Project 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East Close Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Pinkbike's Take A bike this adjustable can be hard to pin down, but the LT's core trait shines through just about every permutation. Speed and stability are the primary goals, achieved through some very progressive geometry and a uniquely supportive suspension platform. It may not be the comfiest way down the hill, but it's certainly one of the quickest, and may just be one of the best-cornering XL frames I've ridden to date. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details As you might expect, the LT is a fancy carbon machine, with some well-considered details and clean lines. Hiding within that polish is a massive amount of adjustment, but I'll touch on those elements more in the geometry and suspension details. Rear wheel size can be altered without affecting either of those elements, thanks to a flip chip at the seatstay / rocker link intersection. That said, there is a special third option in that adjustment, but more on that later. Yeti implemented their easy internal routing on the LT, with guided tubes running your brake, dropper, and shift lines all through the frame, with clamps at entry and exit to keep things quiet. Within the downtube armor, there's a small hatch to help route the dropper post, as well as a clever little stash spot for a Tile or Airtag, should you want to keep track of your hefty investment. In keeping with Yeti's precedent over the past few generations of bikes, the frame alignment is top notch, with easy access to all pivot hardware to keep things in check. Despite a massive volume of riding so far, the pivots on my LT have stayed impressively secure, with only one torque check required after the initial break-in. New to Yeti is the inclusion of in-frame storage, which they're referring to as the Cavity. It features a very nice molded door with a roller cam mechanism to tighten the hatch, keeping things secure and quiet even with a full bottle on top. For the traditionalists out there, there's an accessory mount on the underside of the top tube. Where Yeti tends to offer their bikes in either a standard carbon layup or a lighter, more expensive to manufacture Turq layup, the LT will only be available with the latter build quality. The difference here comes down to weight, with the Turq frames offering the same stiffness and durability as a standard layup, but with a more efficient structure. To improve durability in high-impact areas, the frame features plies of Vectran, a high strength liquid-crystal polymer that can withstand more blunt force than carbon. The Cavity. Nice little bag. Clean graphics, a cable clamp, and a classic badge. Geometry Looking at Yeti's SB-series bikes from the past few years, their approach to geometry has typically fallen into the appropriate but conservative category. Most of the dimensions revolved around the middle of the size range, with chainstay length, stack, and headtube length all scaling slightly as you moved up or down in the size range. Coming into the LT review, I half expected the same approach, but was pleasantly surprised when I was met with one of the most progressive carbon frames I've ridden to date. 29, MX, or MX Slack. Like so many elements of the LT, there are many adjustments to be made here, so I'll try to touch on each option as we go through the geo chart. First off, the simple things: 64° head angle in 29" and MX modes, 63.5° in MX Slack mode. The seat tube angle follows suit, at 77.5° in the standard wheel size settings, and 77° in Slack. The other key change with that setting is the bottom bracket height (and resultant stack change), with the MX Slack position dropping the BB from 350mm to 344mm. Though the seat tube angles might not seem to be the steepest on paper, the high anti-squat pedaling platform keeps you quite high in travel, yielding a steeper-feeling seated position than you might expect. The actual seat tube angles also get steeper as the sizes get larger, so those with a mile of seatpost are better positioned. 29" rear wheel, for scale. Moving to the back of the bike, and that's where things get really exciting. The LT's chainstays grow significantly with each size, ranging from 439 to 465mm across the range. Beyond that, every bike ships with a +10mm bolt-on dropout, that can take you even further if desired. Compared to the SB160's 2mm change per size, this is a stark difference, and should actually provide a balanced feel for the full range of sizes. On top of all the baked-in adjustments, the LT also utilizes a ZS44/56 headset to allow for some aftermarket adjustment of head angle and/or reach. With the relatively high bottom bracket, the LT's stack heights are a bit lower than other contemporary bikes in the 160-170mm range. Headtubes are fairly short, so plan on a stack of spacers or higher rise bars to bring that front end up if that's your preference. Ultimately, the bottom bracket height is the better means to control this fit, so dropping the bike in the MX Slack mode will be your best bet if a roomier standing position is the desired outcome. Suspension Design The headline here is Yeti's move away from their Switch Infinity layout, which has been a core element to their pedal bikes for a few generations now. The LT adopts the Sixfinity six-bar design, utilized on Yeti's eMTB models for some time now. They initially implemented the Sixfinity layout due to the packaging constraints a motor and battery bring about, but quickly found that they could achieve some desirable outputs with that more complex linkage. With the development of their Special Projects DH bike, the concept was pushed even further, leading to the implementation you see here. As is clearly a theme with this bike, the suspension features a 3-way leverage adjustment, similar to that seen on their MTe and LTe e-bikes. A set of interchangeable chips at the lower shock mount allow you to set the bike in 15, 20, or 25% progression modes, with a setup calculator to help find a happy starting place in each position. Worth noting, there isn't a setup recommendation for volume spacers in the shock, so you'll want to sort through that on your own to find the sweet spot. Three leverage rates, one frame. Yeti did build upon their previous designs to arrive at the LT's suspension outputs, but the bike is very much a beast of its own when it comes to the little details. When compared to the LTe - a bike that we lauded for its incredible suspension performance - the LT has much higher anti-squat, and lower leverage across the three options, crossing over at the 25% mark. To cut back on that anti-squat's impact on suspension feel while descending, the LT does ship with the DT Swiss Degrees of Freedom rear hub, which allows the user to adjust the deadband between 0°, 10°, and 20° options. 10° felt like a nice do-all position, with less of a detrimental impact on climbing, and a quieter feel on the descents. Anti-squat, LT versus LTe. The SB160 sets the precedent for much of the LT's performance, with 17% progression, a similar starting leverage to the LT's 20% position, and fairly high anti-squat as well. The LT differs both in adjustability and when it comes to anti-rise, where Yeti chose to stick to a lower value throughout travel, freeing up the suspension under braking and reducing any squat that may occur. Anti-rise, LT versus SB160. Beyond the kinematic goals, durability was a driving factor for the move to the six-bar layout. Though there are more bearings throughout the linkage, the bracing and load distribution between each of those pivot points is greatly reduced, hopefully yielding longer lifespans for those constituent parts. According to Yeti, this has been the case with the utilization on their eMTB and DH platforms, dating back to the 160e release in 2021. No notes. One final suspension detail to touch on is the fork compatibility. Yeti is on board for dual-crown forks aboard the LT, all the way up to 190mm. As someone who can't leave well enough alone, I gave it a try just to see how things went. As you'd probably expect, the front end of the bike was very comfy, but felt a bit at odds with the 160mm of frame travel. Even at the lowest insertion point, the dual crown does rake out the head angle a fair bit as well, so expect a higher bottom bracket and head angle in the 62-63° range. For those looking for something a bit less extreme, you can run the LT with single crown forks from 170mm up to 190mm. Specifications Release Date 2026 Price $10200 Travel 160mm Rear Shock Fox Factory Float X2 Fork Fox Factory 38 Grip X2 170mm Headset Cane Creek 40 Cassette SRAM X0 T-Type Crankarms SRAM X0 T-Type Chainguide MRP AMG SL Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 T-Type Chain SRAM X0 T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Yeti Carbon 35x780mm 35mm Rise Stem Burgtec Enduro MK4 35x42.5mm Grips ODI Reflex Brakes SRAM Maven Silver Wheelset DT Swiss EXC1700 EVO DEG DF Hubs DT Swiss DEG DF Tires Schwalbe Gravity Pro Albert Soft / Schwalbe Trail Pro Magic Mary Ultra Soft Seat WTB Solano Chromoly Seatpost Fox Transfer 31.6mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup Beyond the wide range of adjustments baked into the LT's frame, there are still the ubiquitous adjustments you can make to any bike to better tailor things to your specific use case. Though the stock build kit is quite good, I did make a couple tweaks to dial in my preferences. The stock tire setup (Trail Radial Magic Mary, Gravity Radial Albert) is fine, but felt undergunned for the bike's potential. For me, the Trail casing is far too squirmy for use on a long-travel bike that can transfer so much force to the front wheel. I bumped up to a Gravity casing Mary up front, and swapped in a Gravity casing Tacky Chan out back for better braking and cornering performance. Beyond that, a slightly higher-rise handlebar (40mm) and a bigger chainring (32t) was all it took to get things feeling right. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 84 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario A note on Yeti's setup calculator : it's quite good. I found the shock pressures to be a great starting point, and only deviated from their damper settings a little bit. I tended to run a more open LSR adjustment, and was within 1 or 2 clicks on HSR, LSC, and HSC. Despite the many adjustments on tap, I often found myself close to the middle of the adjustment range, with room to play. Testing Info The LT saw a huge range of terrain, joining me on a roadtrip through the BC interior and Sea to Sky corridor. From a few big days at the Sun Peaks bike park to some big laps in Whistler and Pemberton, it wasn't wanting for variety. Beyond that, the majority of miles were racked up at home in Bellingham, on jump trails, steep lines, and plenty of fast singletrack. For those jonesing for every last bit of tech chat, this video is for you. I run through a variety of setup parameters, my thoughts on how they differ, and a few tweaks I've made along the way to get the LT running just right. Yeti LT Tech Chat 0 seconds of 13 minutes, 58 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/CGAY7MSK Copied Live 00:00 13:58 13:58 More Videos 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close Climbing Yetis are often associated with a supportive and intuitive climbing feel, and that holds true on the LT. Despite a very large footprint and fairly heavy build weight, the big rig feels even keeled under heavy efforts and spinning alike, efficiently using only as much travel as need be to keep your efforts translating forward. Despite having a firm feel and very high anti-squat, the rear wheel maintains surprisingly good grip, preventing burnouts or sketchy moments when trying to clean tech climbs. That could partly be due to the radial tires, which do an impressive job of conforming to tricky terrain, but regardless it performs well. With a relatively high bottom bracket and 165mm cranks, pedal strikes aren't much of a concern on the LT. Add to that the supportive suspension, and you can spin through some pretty junky terrain without much worry. That should suit most riders, especially those who don't want to have to make powerful moves through tricky sections to avoid placing a pedal wrong - something more often required on lower-slung bikes. Though the seat tube angle might not seem like the steepest on paper, I found the LT to be very comfortable for seated climbing, both on lower-angle climb trails and heinously steep pitches alike. The latter is helped by the long rear center, which reduces your need to shift weight over the front of the bike to keep the wheel pinned down. Again, the high anti-squat plays a role here, keeping your weight higher in travel and reducing unwanted sag over the rear axle. From a race perspective, I think the LT does a good enough job of staving off too much fatigue over the course of a long day of pedaling. After racking up plenty of average 3-to-4,000-foot climbing days, and one particularly spirited 8,000-foot day, I can attest to its efficiency, despite weighing north of 37 pounds as I have it built. Sprinting efforts are surprisingly well-rewarded, with a snappy return of energy that you don't always get on a bike in this travel range. Descending Surprise, surprise, the big long Yeti is quite fast. That attribute shouldn't be too much of a shock to people, given Yeti's focus on speed between the tape and results on the world stage. That said, the LT doesn't necessarily feel like a race bike first and foremost. Yes, it can comfortably carry as much speed as you're willing to tolerate, but the adjustability, body position, and overall stoutness of the bike feel more multifaceted than something like the SB160. The common thread is there, but each setting does alter things pretty significantly. I'll start with geometry. This might just be the best-cornering XL I've ridden to date, thanks in no small part to the long chainstays. Even in the short mode, the XL's 465mm stays never leave you wanting for front wheel grip, regardless of the geo flip chip position. In the MX Slack mode, the longer 475mm stays give you a massive wheelbase, and a stability on high-speed trails that few single crown bikes I've ridden can match. That MX Slack mode ended up being my overall preference, thanks primarily to the lower bottom bracket position and resultant bump in stack height. When set in MX or 29" mode (yielding a 64° head angle) the LT's weight balance feels a bit more front-biased, making the long chainstay mode a bit less usable in steeper terrain. It still works, but you have to consciously shift your weight backwards, or risk overriding the fork and doing some emergency pushups. I could see folks racing the LT on flatter tracks preferring the 29" short position, as the rollover and pedal clearance make carrying speed on awkward trails a bit easier, but the tradeoff on steep pitches and big features left me wanting for that MX Slack body position. Moving to the suspension performance, and you open up an entirely different can of worms. Where the geometry can lull you into a rewarding sense of security, the suspension feel keeps you honest and reminds you where you are on trail. It's not a punishing ride, but the LT does provide a fair bit of feedback through the feet on rougher sections, metering out travel efficiently to deal with the hits as they come, as opposed to erasing them entirely. I've come to like this sensation when I'm feeling fresh, as you get a good sense of what the bike is doing underneath you, but it isn't the numbing, sticky feel you get out of some long-travel enduro bikes. My primary setting throughout testing has been the 20% progression mode, which felt like the best balance of small-bump erasure and big-hit support of the three options. With the stock shock tune, the 25% mode felt like it used too much travel too often, dipping through the fist half of travel quickly before catching you later in the stroke. I think some shock fiddling could pull more performance out of this setting, so I'll be spending more time on that front. On the other end of the range, the 15% mode has a surprisingly nice ride feel to it, offering a very consistent platform to push through, and still avoiding any harsh bottom-outs on jump trails and natural terrain. The dynamic body position does settle a bit higher there, adding to the already tall-feeling bottom bracket height in the stock geometry mode. As it stands, if I had to choose one combination of all the LT's factors, it would be this: MX Slack geometry and wheelsize, +10mm chainstay position, 20% progression, 10° deadband. I'll be experimenting more over the rest of the summer, so we'll see if and how that changes. Mike Kazimer's Ride Impressions I tend to prefer mixed wheels on longer travel bikes - I like that wheel out of the way on the steeps, and I find the slightly smaller rear wheel to be a little easier to whip into a corner. That’s the configuration I focused on during my time on the LT: mixed wheels, and I worked my way through pretty much every single setup possibility. I only changed one parameter at a time in order to avoid getting too lost in the sauce. By the end I felt like I’d gone full circle and settled on the stock mixed wheel setting with the chainstays in the neutral setting. Descending is clearly the LT’s focus, but the rear suspension performance while climbing is worthy of praise. The shock seems impressively immune to rider weight shifts while climbing - seated or standing, it just keeps doing its thing without any excessive wallowing, and while still providing enough traction to keep it tractoring up and over the rough stuff. Even though it’s billed as an enduro race bike, the LT felt more like a bike park smasher, or something for riders that want maximum stability at speed. It’s a bike that requires strength and attention to really get the most out of it. That’s no problem for a brick of a human like Richie Rude, but for someone like me, who’s ever-so-slightly less jacked (I'm 5'11" and 165 pounds), the LT can feel like a lot of bike, and it’s not really what I’d grab for trying to speed through tight European switchbacks. I rode it on several trails in Whistler that have been used as Enduro World Series stages in the past, and while there was no shortage of straightline speed, I had to pay more attention than usual to ensure I was in the one in charge and not the bike. Tight corners are obviously still possible, but they do take more pre-planning; the LT prefers making fast, big arcs rather than quick and snappy turns. I spent a bunch of time on the new Santa Cruz Nomad this year, and there’s a very noticeable difference going from that bike to the LT. The Nomad is a really easy bike to get along with, a slack, 170mm bike that still manages to remain maneuverable at slower speeds. With the LT, the sweet spot is a little harder to achieve, at least it was for me - I felt immediately comfortable with the Nomad, while there was more of a learning curve with the Yeti. Mike's Notes: It felt like I was hitting the rear derailleur way more than usual, even though the chainstays are just 5 millimeters longer than the aforementioned Raaw Madonna that I have a ton of time on. For whatever reason, the derailleur felt like it was exactly in the line of fire from every rock or stump that slightly stuck out into the trail. The LT is impressively quiet. There was a moment on an especially rough trail in Whistler’s Creekside zone where I realized just how silent the bike was - other than the electronic “bzzt, bzzt” of the rear derailleur there really weren’t any other sounds. Yeti LT Frameworks Enduro Yeti LTe Orbea Rallon How Does It Compare? There aren't too many bikes that hit all the same notes as the LT, but there are a few in the ballpark that are worth touching on. Typically, the very long rear center figures featured on the LT's larger sizes are more associated with smaller-scale boutique bikes (something like the Vessel Enduro ), or a high-pivot bike that grows through travel (anything from Forbidden ). The same goes for the level of adjustment baked into the LT - major manufacturers tend towards simplicity on most fronts, where smaller outfits will opt to pack more variables into a frame to widen its remit. The LT's closest competitor in my orbit is the Frameworks Enduro, which has long been a personal favorite when it comes to steep trails, big hits, and rough descents. The two offer very different ride feels, particularly on the suspension front, with the Frameworks erring on a softer, more comfortable ride, and the Yeti giving more feedback and platform through the stroke. Both work quite well, but the feel will definitely suit different riders. Depending on the day, I fall into either camp - sometimes you want to push into the bike as hard as you can, sometimes you want to take a calmer approach and let the bike work for you. My mind keeps going back to Yeti's LTe and its' remarkably even-keeled rear suspension, which set a very high bar for combining comfort, traction, and support for a single-crown bike. That character wasn't quite met on the LT, but the purview is different and the engine has proven a bit less powerful. Some souped-up version of the LT, with lower anti-squat and higher leverage, could potentially hit the same feel, but I can't speak to what I haven't ridden, and it could very well be the motor and battery weight that make the bigger difference. The last note comes to the Orbea Rallon, another carbon superbike with some forward-thinking (albeit less so) geometry and a rich set of adjustment features. The Rallon's character is much less aggressive-feeling than the LT, with a softer suspension feel and the chassis flex to match it. For those looking for a more forgiving ride for freeride and big-mountain lines, the Rallon could be the easier bike to set up for the task. Mike Kazimer's Comparisons Yeti LT Raaw Madonna V3 I kept mentally comparing the LT to the Raaw Madonna, since the Raaw is still my geometry benchmark for this category. The Madonna’s combination of a high stack, low bottom bracket, and long(ish) chainstays makes it perform very well on steep, rough trails. I’ve used the analogy before, but it feels like you’re piloting an off-road scooter due to the very upright position it creates. On the Yeti LT the stack is 35mm lower lower, the chainstays are longer by 5 - 15mm, and the bottom bracket height is taller. Those create a ride feel that’s less similar to the Madonna than I expected. The LT is harder to get off the ground, and I never quite got that “in-bike” cornering sensation that makes the Madonna so fun to ride. Technical Report DT Swiss DEG DF Hubs: DT Swiss' anti-kickback hub is very simple, and makes it easy to bracket back and forth between the 0° setting and the 10/20° deadband options. This allows you to get a feel for whether the pedal slop is worth the descending benefits, or whether they even feel like benefits for your riding. Compared to the e*thirteen sidekick, the DEG DF feels a bit clunky, but it gets the job done without adding much complication. Compared to the DT Swiss hubs of old, their new DEG ratchets seem to have a far worse track record when it comes to durability, with more issues stripping or seizing than their legacy designs. The Yeti's haven't provided any issues, but it's worth keeping a spare set around if you're heading way out into the backcountry. Schwalbe Gravity Pro Tires: Schwalbe has been iterating on their radial casings since release, and the newest version is certainly the best yet. A bit sturdier than before, they balance the compliance and traction you get from a radial casing with a bit more stability and composure in corners and heavy compressions. I've been running an Ultra Soft Magic Mary up front (the new rubber is great) paired with a Soft Tacky Chan out back (the updated tread pattern is more predictable) and am loving both. There are a ton of great tires out there right now, but this combo is currently a personal favorite. Frame Hardware: After owning the SB160 for a couple years, the only real complaint I had about the longevity of that bike had to do with the Switch Infinity link. It didn't play nicely with the Northwest's wet and silty soil, and required more attention than I'd like. The bearings, on the other hand, were impressively robust through slop and dust alike. Luckily, the LT's linkage is just bearings, and has alignment and sturdiness that bode well for the bike's long-term ownership. After about 70,000 feet of bike park descending and weeks' worth of pedaling, the bike is creak free and still nice and tight. Pros + Progressive geometry with meaningful adjustment + Feels fast and supportive + Durable, quiet, easy to work on frame Cons - Suspension lacks comfort compared to other Yeti 6-bar bikes - Stack height / BB drop could be pushed further - Adjustment complication may overwhelm some Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Yeti Yeti Lt Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 490 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: 2023 Transition Smuggler - The Little Ripper
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: REVIEW Transition Smuggler WORDS: Dario DiGiulio PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio / Eric Mickelson The newest addition to the Transition Bikes catalog comes in the form of a long-awaited refresh, breathing new life into the Smuggler name. With 10mm more rear travel than the prior model, as well as a host of frame and geometry updates, the 2023 Smuggler fits squarely into the trail bike shaped hole none of us knew was in Transition's lineup. With 13 unique models now filling out their range, Transition seems to offer something that caters to every niche in the sport, but the Smuggler might just be the bike with the widest reach of the bunch. With a 130/140mm travel layout, 29" wheels front and rear, carbon and aluminum frame options, and a few solid build kits, the Smuggler hits the sweet spot for most riders in most places. I've been testing the Carbon GX AXS build for the past few months, and for the most part it's been a great ride. Smuggler Details • 29" wheels • 130mm frame travel, 140mm fork • 65° head angle • 430-535mm reach (485mm size L) • 435 or 440mm chainstays, size dependent • 78° seat tube angle, size dependent • 5 sizes available, L tested • Weight: 31.6 lb / 14.3 kg • Carbon Frameset: $3,699 USD • Complete: $3,999-$8,499 USD • transitionbikes.com More on that below, after some more information about the bike. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Smuggler hits a sweet spot right in the middle of my preferred types of riding, with a great climbing characteristic, lively suspension feel, and surprising downhill abilities for a shorter-travel trail bike. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details The outgoing Smuggler was the last bike in the lineup to harken back to the swoopy curvy days of Transition, with the new model landing in the angular aesthetic that marks the brand's new era. Alonside the change in form are a few updates to the frame's details, namely in the cable routing and headset department. Where the prior Smuggler Carbon had external brake routing, the new model snakes the rear brake through the front and rear triangle. The headset has also gone the way of integration, with drop-in bearings replacing the pressed headset cups. This was done to facilitate the use of the new internal cable guides that terminate at the headtube of the bike, as those are apparently incompatible with the press-in aluminum cups seen on most other bikes. The integration is clean, but I'm sure some will bemoan the loss of aftermarket options there. I wish I could say the updated cable routing simplified things, but it turned out to be quite the opposite. Where most carbon bikes are now employing fully guided tube-in-tube cable routing, the Smuggler stops a step short of that convenience. The routing ends a bit in front of the bottom bracket, with the carbon tubes' raw ends accessible via a big cutout in the back of the bottom bracket shell; I've dubbed this opening the Loam Cupboard. From there, it's a bit of a free-for-all through the stays, though those are easy enough to route if you start from the back. Ultimately, this means you'll have to remove the lower pivot hardware and swing the stays up in order to start fishing the cable up into the guide tubes, at which point they'll pop out of the headtube. That Loam Cupboard does the job of getting the cables through the bike, but it also does an excellent job of funneling mud and debris into the frame. Every time I pulled the bottom bracket, I found a hardened plug of dirt crammed all around the BB area. I tried to shield it with some mastic tape above the stay, but it seems to get from the sides and below, and at a certain point you don't want to wrap every exposed gap. My advice here would be to make sure you use the plastic tube that connects the two bottom bracket cups, it'll go a long way in keeping crud from collecting and killing those bearings. I asked the folks at Transition about this issue, and they're well aware of it, enough so to design a little fender that shrouds the area over top of the hole. Dirt and water might still accumulate from underneath, but hopefully this would do a bit better than my mastic tape fix. This hole sits behind the chainstay bridge, but there's enough of a gap for plenty of trail debris to accumulate. There are no flip chips, no geometry adjustments, no extra gadgets or features - just a well-rounded bike made to ride up and down hills. Stick a water bottle in the frame, accessories on the top tube mount, and get out the door. Geometry & Sizing The do-it-all trail bike can be a hard geometry to nail, as any desire to bias towards one characteristic can derail the overall balance of the package. Arriving at something that climbs well, descends confidently, and keeps things fun is tricky, but I think the Smuggler toes that line very well. The 65° head tube angle is becoming a pretty typical figure for modern trail bikes, and I wouldn't want it any other way. That keeps the handling sharp and responsive, without sacrificing a reasonable amount of confidence in steeper terrain. For the majority of riding, this is plenty slack, and pairs well with the shorter travel forks seen on trail bikes. Transition was once on the sharper end of pushing reach numbers, but now their figures are pretty much in line with other contemporary brands. 485mm for a size Large felt bang-on to me, and it's nice to see the stack numbers growing somewhat proportionally with each jump in size. The effective seat tube angle of the Smuggler changes with each size in the range, but the center of that fairly tight spectrum is the size Large's 78° figure. This is much steeper than the trail bikes of even a few years ago, and it's a trend I'm fully in favor of. The seated position feels nicely central and upright, with good weight balance and handling over steep and mellow terrain alike. That upright feel is partially helped by the low bottom bracket, with a drop of 35mm across sizes. Transition fell short of fully size-specific chainstay lengths for the Smuggler, instead splitting two different lengths across the size range. Small and Medium frames have a 335mm long chainstay, while the Large through XXL get the 440mm option. This feels well suited to the bikes in the middle of the bike fit bell curve, hitting good balance points where the majority of riders sit, but those on the larger frames might find the front center to rear center balance getting a little off. The Small's reach is actually shorter than the rear center, which should make for a truly middle-of-bike feel. One small asterisk to the geometry chart is the subtle difference between the alloy and carbon frames, as the former has a longer seat tube on the size Small, and slightly longer chainstays across the board. Transition Smuggler Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/bofK52Rp Copied Live 00:00 00:19 00:19 Suspension Design The Smuggler's 130mm of travel is delivered by a simple Horst link layout driving a 210x50mm standard-mount shock. Where the majority of Transition's bikes use a trunnion mount, I was happy to see this standard mount and a beefier rocker link, as this tends to bode well for shock longevity. The rather progressive rising rate of the bike's leverage curve makes it compatible with both air and coil shocks, though you'll note that all of the stock builds come with the Float X, as that's the shock tune that the Transition team felt best matched the character of the bike. The setup information states that the bike can be run anywhere between 25 and 35% sag - technically true of most Horst link bikes - but I found my happy spot was right around 30-31% sag, or 15mm of shock compression. If you want to run the Smuggler in the long-travel 140mm configuration, it's as simple as removing the travel spacer from the shock and airing things back up. Lots of folks who are doing this change then run the bike with a 150mm fork, pushing the little bike a little more into the burlier end of the spectrum. For me, the shorter travel arrangement felt ideal and more than capable enough, so that's where it remained. It's worth noting that Transition has had some issues with the stock Float X the bikes are coming with, namely in the form of a knock in the rebound / topout portion of the cycle.
