By no means dangerous or even that critical to performance, but it's quite annoying to have the shock thocking away as you rally over bumps. Apparently Fox has a quick fix, and it's just a matter of replacing one sticky shim in the stack to get things back to normal. My test bike had this issue right off the bat, but after a couple hours in the shop the Float X was quiet once more. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $7299 Travel 130mm Rear Shock Fox Float X Performance Elite, 210x50mm Fork Fox Float 34 Grip 2 Performance Elite, 140mm Headset FSA NO.42/48/ACB Cassette SRAM XS 1275 T-Type (10-52t) Crankarms SRAM GX AXS Transmission Chainguide N/A Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM GX AXS Transmission Chain SRAM GX AXS Transmission Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM GX AXS Transmission Handlebar ANVL Mandrel Alloy 35 Stem ANVL Swage, 40mm Grips ODI Elite Flow Brakes SRAM Code Silver Stealth Wheelset Raceface Aeffect R Hubs Raceface Trace 28H Spokes Raceface 2.0/1.7/2.0 Rim Raceface Aeffect R Tires Maxxis Assegai EXO+ 2.5 / Maxxis Dissector 3C EXO+ 2.4 Seat SDG Bel Air V3 Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE SMUGGLER Test Bike Setup Most of my time on the Smuggler was as you see it, with the stock parts you'd get on the GX AXS build, but as with many long term testers I eventually began to play with the setup and tweak things here and there. The main change was in the wheel department, where I swapped the stock Race Face Aeffect hoops for some carbon wheels I've come to rely on. Suspension, drivetrain, and brakes stayed the same, and tires changed with wear and the shift in seasons. I quickly found a happy place with the suspension setup on the Smuggler, with around 180psi in the shock and 95-100psi in the fork, depending on the terrain and my mood. You can long-shock the Smuggler to land around 140mm of rear travel, but I opted to keep things stock for the duration of the test. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 180 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Climbing Back to the geometry for a moment, as the seated position on this bike feels perfect for a bike meant to climb, traverse, and move quickly over varied terrain. An unforseen upside to that upright position is how easy it is to transition (ha) out of the saddle in technical sections of trail. For those that climb heavy - sitting down and not shifting weight over obstacles - there may be more suspension movement than you want over bumps. I think this bike really rewards an active riding style up and down the hill. For me, the support and grip offered by the Smuggler was perfect; I felt like I was riding high in the travel while spinning, without losing too much grip when standing up and stamping on the pedals. Overall the climbing character is active, but it suits the nature of the bike well - you can simply leave the shock open and rip around, catering your riding to the terrain as opposed to making the bike adapt to take up slack. Despite the grip offered, the bike feels light and zippy when you're simply trying to spin up a forest road. While singletrack climbs felt like the Smuggler's preferred terrain, it's clearly just as happy to motor up dirt roads. When things do get tight and squiggly, the centered position within the wheelbase allows you to arc around corners a little more easily than bikes that put you in a further rearward position between the wheels. Descending My first ride on this bike wasn't at home in Bellingham, despite the proximity to the headquarters it came from. I actually first hopped on the bike up in Whistler, to check out some old janky scary trails I'd heard about in the Valley. Luckily my initial setup was pretty close to correct, as things were pretty full-on from the get go. To my pleasant surprise however, the Smuggler took it in stride, working hard to keep the wheels on the ground through some old chopped out tracks. This came to exemplify the rest of my time on the bike, as one that could punch pretty far above the assumed pay grade so long as you're able to cash the checks. For a light feeling bike that climbed so well, I figured there would be a larger tradeoff on the descents, but the Smuggler can handle proper terrain if you're willing to be active on the bike and choose lines wisely. It's far from a long-travel enduro rig, but it's equally far from a pedal-only cross-country machine. On more buff, flowing terrain, the Smuggler was an absolute hoot. It's one of the most intuitive-feeling jumping bikes I've ridden, arcing perfectly off lips without any unpredictable changes in the suspension feel. That predictability also makes it easy to pop onto the rear wheel, jib off small features, and carve around turns, as the rear wheel always seems to be where you want it. Only in very rough terrain does the back end get a bit lost - and that's when you're pushing things well beyond the typical trail bike realm. It may lose some shape, but luckily the relatively short overall travel makes for a compressed position that isn't too compromised. All that positive ride quality comes with a bit of a price, namely in the form of noise and premature wear. The GX AXS-equipped bike felt like a best-case scenario in terms of noise mitigation, but it still wasn't a silent ride. As I mentioned earlier, there was the initial issues with the Fox Float X knocking on rebound - that was fixed - but there was still a good deal of frame noise on rougher sections of trail. Most of this came down to chainslap and cable rattle, so one could probably chase down all the little sounds and come away in silent bliss. Chainslap might just require a beefier chainstay protector, as the stock one is hard and flat compared to some of the better options out there. The cable rattle was mostly centered around that Loam Cupboard, where the cables have a few inches to play before snaking back into smaller tubes. That plus the dirt that collects down there made for some in-frame noise that would come and go. I've been riding this bike since earlier this summer, and the first set of bearings lasted me about 2-3 months of dry riding before needing service and a subsequent replacement. Luckily, I had a set at home that fit the spec, but it would incur an annoying cost right off the bat if you were a new owner. The bearings could definitely use better sealing and more robust hardware to deal with the forces you can generate cornering this speedy little whip. Transition Smuggler Santa Cruz 5010 How does it compare? Don't bite my head off here - I know these aren't identical bikes. But they feel like the two of the top contenders if I were looking for a shorter travel trail bike right now. Both are very fun on mellow trails, capable when you push them beyond their purview, and have simple layouts that don't require too much fuss. The mullet wheel layout on the 5010 might give it a leg up in the turns, where it feels more natural and seamless to initiate breakaway and slap into a catch right when you want to. The Smuggler's full-29" setup gives it more ability to truck over rough sections of trail, and might carry a bit more speed when pedaling along at a clip. If I had to choose one to own long term, it would be the 5010. The frame feels more robust, the bearing life proved to be much better, the cable routing is a breeze, and you get the added benefit of internal storage. While the Smuggler might be a more adaptable base frame - you really can go long-travel XC or lightweight all-mountain with a build - the 5010 felt like a very well sorted trail bike for the trails I regularly ride. The 5010 beats out the Smuggler on price as well. Equivalent builds are a little bit cheaper, and the fancy carbon framekit comes quite close. It's also worth mentioning the new YT Jeffsy here, as the pricing on those models falls well below either of these, and the ride quality is similar if a little more all-mountain. I could be swayed by the overall value of an alloy Smuggler, but having not ridden one yet I can't say for sure. Which Model is the Best Value? As I just alluded to, the pricing on the Smuggler is up there with the most premium brands on the market. Though the ride quality on offer here is up to par, the frame itself might be a step behind. Overall, I think that Transition's builds are excellent in terms of the parts on tap, but there were a couple sore spots I'll touch on in the Tech Report below. If I were buying one of these sporty joints, I'd probably go with the Alloy NX or Carbon GX build. As I said before, I haven't ridden the Alloy frame, but assuming the only difference is about three pounds of weight and some slight variation in ride feel, I could get on just fine. The cash saved would probably be spent on a fork upgrade, as I really do love the performance of the Fox 34 for bikes in this range. The carbon GX is mostly just a frame upgrade as most of the parts carry over, including the NX shifter and Code R brakes, but at least you do get a 34 with that kit. As a range, I'm really happy to see consistent shock, tire, and dropper post spec across all of the Smuggler's pricepoints. Usually details like that are overlooked in service of flashier component choices, but ultimately those are some of the most critical parts to enjoying that bike long term. Technical Report SRAM Transmission GX AXS Drivetrain: I'm closing in on a long-term review of the GX AXS gruppo, but suffice to say it was totally unproblematic for the duration of this test. I clipped more than a couple rocks with the lower cage, had to do some creative Knipex work to get things back to normal, but still it shifts excellently under power. Compared to the old AXS drivetrain that initially came on this bike, it's a massive improvement in terms of shift quality and overall bike noise. The prior derailleur's weak clutch let things clap around far too much, causing a racket. Raceface Aeffect R Wheelset: Soft and comfortable, but not my choice for the long haul. I found these wheels to be just fine for hard riding, so long as you kept a careful eye on their tension and trueness after most rides - but over time things just degrade with rims this soft. Strangely, I also found them a bit harder to mount tires to, as if the overall diameter were too small for looser-fitting tires like those from Maxxis or Bontrager. SRAM Code Stealth Silver Brakes: You'll note that I have the older-style Code RSCs on my test bike - that was a small spec discrepancy that occurred in the handoff. Luckily, the new Silver model is functionally the same as the outgoing RSC, save for the positioning of the master cylinder. I like the new position (except for the weirdly inward-facing hose direction), but have no issue with the prior model's ergonomics and performance. OneUp 210mm V2 Dropper: Transition did a great job of maximizing dropper clearance on these bikes, with plenty of clearance and insertion depth for the longest-travel droppers for each size. Small bikes get a 150mm, Mediums a 180mm, and Large/XL/XXL get the full 210mm. Pros + Lively, energetic, and composed ride quality + Comfortable to ride, easy to get up to speed on + Impressively capable in serious terrain for such a sporty feeling bike Cons - Premature bearing wear, dirt can get in around bottom bracket area - Suspension may be too active on climbs for some - Not the best value compared to equivalent bikes Pinkbike's Take The Smuggler hits a sweet spot right in the middle of my preferred types of riding, with a great climbing characteristic, lively suspension feel, and surprising downhill abilities for a shorter-travel trail bike. Sadly, the frame quality doesn't feel like it's quite on the same level, but I might be willing to deal with that long-term frustration for the joy of the ride. It's been a long time coming, but I'm happy to see the Smuggler waving the little-bike flag once again. Complaints aside, I'm definitely a fan. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Transition Transition Smuggler Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Transition's New Regulator CX eMTB
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, trail-bikes, transition, transition-regulator-cx
article_text: The Regulator is the newest addition to Transition’s eMTB lineup, essentially an electric version of the most recent Sentinel. Billed as an “all-mountain workhorse” the Regulator is available in two different configuration - the CX model, which uses Bosch’s full power, 85 Nm Performance CX motor and a 600 Wh battery, or the SX model with Bosch’s 55 Nm SX motor and a 400 Wh battery. Sizes M-XL of both models are compatible with Bosch’s PowerMore range extender, a downtube mounted external battery with a 250 Wh capacity. The CX and SX both roll on mixed wheels, and have 150mm of rear travel with a 160mm fork. The geometry is identical between the two models, although the CX does have a flip chip on the lower shock mount, and the SX doesn’t. The difference really comes down to power and weight - the CX has a more powerful motor, a bigger battery, and a burlier fork than the SX. On my scale it weighed in at 47.25 lb, a very reasonable number for a full power eMTB. Regulator CX Details • Wheel size: Mixed • Travel: 150mm rear / 160mm fork • Carbon frame • 64 or 64.5° head angle • Chainstay length: 448mm • Bosch Performance CX motor (5th gen) • 600 Wh (range extender compatible) • Sizes: S, M, L, XL • Weight: 47.25 lb / 21.4 kg (size L) • Price: $8,999 USD • More info: transitionbikes.com We’re starting to see the SL and full power categories merge, and I have a feeling that trend is only going to continue as battery capacities increase and motor performance improves. It’ll be interesting to find out which version ends up being more popular. Personally, if I’m going to ride a bike with a motor I want as much range and power as I can get. Otherwise, I’d rather be on a regular bike and not need to worry about battery life at all, other than my own, and that’s something a quick stop at the nearest convenience store can easily fix. Transition Regulator CX https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/YpO3ufQG Copied Live 00:00 08:07 08:07 Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take I was impressed with how easy it was to place the bike exactly where I wanted - the descending position is centered and upright, and it really does have that ‘in the bike’ sensation, as cliche as that term may be. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The Regulator’s carbon frame casts a familiar shadow, and it’s really just the larger downtube and motor bulge in front of the bottom bracket that gives it away as an eMTB. Transition only made the thru-headset cable routing mistake once, on the original Repeater. On the Regulator, the cables are routed through ports on each side of the head tube. Each port is wide enough for two cables, so a clean setup should be possible no matter which side you run your rear brake lever on. Motor / Display The Regulator CX uses Bosch’s 5th generation Performance CX motor, which puts out 85 Nm of torque and has a max output of 600 watts. That’s paired with a 600 watt hour battery enclosed in the downtube - removing it requires dropping the motor, which isn’t that hard, but it’s also not something you’d really want to do with any regularity. Bosch’s minimalist System Contoller display is mounted in the top tube, where different colored lights indicate the motor mode and battery level. Each illuminated bar is equivalent to 20% charge (there are five of them), and it’ll change color when the level drops by 10%. The motor modes are controlled by a wireless remote located on the left side of the bars. Those mode can easily be customized in Bosch’s Flow app, allowing riders to fine tune the amount of assistance each one provides. The charging port is located underneath a plastic hatch on the non-driveside. Other details include room for a water bottle (or a range extender) inside the front triangle, a new chainslap protector, and Transition’s own upper chain guide. Geometry The Regulator’s numbers closely mirror those of the non-motorized Sentinel - the head angle, reach, and chainstay lengths are the same between the two models. A flip chip on the Regulator CX’s lower shock mount can be used to change the head angle from 64.5-degrees to 64, which also drops the bottom bracket by 5 millimeters and slackens the seat angle by .5-degrees. There are 4 sizes, S - XL, with reach numbers ranging from 425 - 510mm. The S and M sizes have 442mm chainstays, and the L and XL sizes have 448mm chainstays. Suspension Design The Regulator uses a 205 x 60mm trunnion mounted shock to deliver 150mm of rear travel. For riders looking for a little more squish, a 205 x 65mm shock will bump up the travel to 160mm. 27% shock progression means that both air and coil shocks should work well with this design. Specifications Price $8999 Travel 150mm Rear Shock RockShox SuperDeluxe Ultimate Fork RockShox Zeb Ultimate, 160mm Headset FSA No.57E Cassette Shimano XT Crankarms SRAM S1000 (150mm) Chainguide Transition integrated top guide Bottom Bracket Bosch Performance CX motor / 600W battery Rear Derailleur Shimano XT 12-speed Chain Shimano XT Shifter Pods Shimano XT Handlebar OneUp Carbon Stem ANVL Swage (40mm) Grips ODI Elite FLow Brakes TRP DHR-Evo Wheelset DT Swiss H1900 Hubs DT Swiss Hybrid 370 Tires Schwalbe Albert Radial Seat SDG Bel Air V3 Lux Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post T Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup 175 psi in the RockShox SuperDeluxe shock put me right at 30% sag, and I stuck with that amount for the duration of testing. I ran 69 psi (nice) in the Zeb Ultimate, with the compression adjustments set in the middle of the range, and rebound 9 clicks from closed. Schwalbe's radial casing tires require a little more pressure than non-radial tires to keep them from feeling too squirmy; running 3-5 psi more than I would in a regular tire does the trick for me. In this instance that equated to 23 psi front / 25 psi rear. I used the Alberts for most of the test period, but I did try out some Shredda tires when conditions were especially grim. The traction those provide in loose, muddy conditions is borderline ridiculous, although the extra drag is noticeable when climbing, even with a motor to help out. Other than trimming the bars to my preferred 770mm width there's really nothing else to report - the Regulator is a simple, no fuss bike to get up and running. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington during the heart of winter. That meant the bike was fed a steady diet of mud, along with a stint where the trails froze solid and were covered with a few inches of snow. There were some perfect dirt days in the mix too, but overall conditions were on the wetter side of things. Climbing These days approximately 30 - 40% of my rides are on e-bikes. I still love non-motorized bikes the most, but when the days are short and the weather is foul it’s nice to be able to bundle up and cover a ton of ground without much effort. The Regulator is a great bike for exploring - it’s not some big behemoth that only works well flying up fire roads and then descending gnarly trails. Instead, it’s impressively quick handling without feeling twitchy, especially in the high geometry setting. If you’re the type of rider who enjoys getting up weird, technical climbs almost as much (or maybe more) than descending, the Regulator fits the bill. Several factors combine to make this bike such a great climber. First, there are those teeny-tiny 150mm cranks. Short cranks mean you’re less likely to smack a pedal, so you can keep pedaling and motoring uphill even in chopped-up terrain. They may look a little funny, but the performance is worth it, at least as long as there’s a motor involved. This was a smart spec choice by Transition, and I’d expect other companies to follow suit, at least once more product managers get to try them out. The Bosch motor is part of the equation too, with a very smooth power delivery. There’s a bit of an arms race going on at the moment, as motor manufacturers try to up their systems’ torque, but that extra output needs to be controllable; otherwise the bike will want to spin out or spit the rider off. The CX motor response well to a variety of pedalling speeds, and you don’t need to pedal wildly to hit the sweet spot. The majority of my time was spent in the eMTB mode (indicated by a purple light). That mode adjusts the level of assistance dynamically depending on rider output, automatically switching between Trail and Turbo modes. There's plenty of power for getting up silly steep sections, and the whir of the motor is unobtrusive - the pitch wasn't annoying to my ears, and I’d consider myself pretty sensitive to noise. Other factors include the Schwalbe Albert radial casing tires, which work great in pretty much all conditions other than deep mud, well-balanced geometry, and suspension that feels like traction is the main priority. The Regulator is fairly eager to enter its travel, but that’s part of what allows it to stick to the ground and claw its way up slippery, slimy sections of trail. I'm typically someone that ends up preferring slacker to steeper geometry if a bike has a flip chip, but in this case the steeper setting ended up being my favorite. There's only .5-degrees between the two positions, so it's not a super dramatic difference, but the steeper position did make the bike feel more energetic and easier to handle, especially on tighter trails. The nice thing is that the swap only takes a couple of minutes - it's worth doing some experimenting to see which one feels best for your local trails. Descending I tested the Sentinel as part of our Fall Field Test last year and wasn’t totally blown away by its performance. It did what it was supposed to, but there were times when it felt longer than I expected, and I didn’t totally click with its handling. It’s a different story with the Regulator - the smaller rear wheel and the extra weight around the bottom bracket give it a much different riding characteristic despite having identical geometry to its non-motorized sibling. The overall suspension feel is nice and plush, with a good level of sensitivity off the top to absorb those chattery sections of trail. It’s planted when it needs to be, but it also retains a good deal of maneuverability. One of my favorite local trails has a series of tight switchbacks followed by a sections with a series of steep drops a couple of bike lengths apart, basically like a set of dirt stairs designed by Salvador Dali. It’s not something you can just sit back and hold on through and hope for the best - good brake control and plopping down into the right spot is key. The Regulator took it all in stride, and I was impressed with how easy it was to place the bike exactly where I wanted - the descending position is centered and upright, and it really does have that ‘in the bike’ sensation, as cliche as that term may be. Bigger drops and hits were handle with out issues, although I’d probably run a little less sag for drier, faster conditions - 30% felt good in the slop, but a little more support would be good for hitting hardpacked berms and jumps. The bike as a whole is very quiet - there weren't any clacking noises from the motor, and the internal cables all stayed satisfyingly silent.. There was a moment when it seemed like Transition was going to continue down the longer and slacker road forever, but their recent bikes have bucked that trend, in some cases getting a little steeper than before. With the Regulator, this geometry recipe works very well - there’s more to making a bike handle well than just a super slack head angle, and I’m pretty convinced that that 64ish-degree head angle is a good place for most trail and enduro bikes to be, at least when that number is complemented by a fairly high stack and longish chainstays. What’s It Like to Live With? So far the Regulator has held up well - all the hardware has remained secure, and the bike is running as quietly as it did on day one. I pulled the plastic motor cover off and was pleasantly surprised to see that it wasn't packed full of mud - there are only a few small slits where mud can enter, and the inside of the cover is smooth, which keeps the mud from packing up, unlike what I experienced with the Orbea Wild . My one gripe has to do with the seatpost clamp - it doesn't do its job very well, and had a tendency to let the post slowly slide down over the course of a ride. Some carbon friction paste helps, but I'd be inclined to swap it out for a nicer clamp. Oh, and I'd also like to see a more robust charging port cover. It's a common refrain with most eMTBs these days - the charging port covers tend to be a little flimsy and not very well sealed from the elements, and the Regulator is no exception. Transition Regulator CX Orbea Wild How Does It Compare? Let’s start by pitting the Regulator up against its big brother, the Repeater. The Repeater has 170mm of travel and SRAM’s Powertrain motor powered by a 720 Wh battery. In that matchup, I vastly prefer the Regulator. It’s 6 pounds lighter, has a better, more tunable motor, comes with mixed wheels as stock, and feels much more manageable on the trail. Compared to the Orbea Wild that I recently reviewed, the Regulator is more of an all-rounder, where the Wild does best on properly steep terrain. The Wild does have the edge when it comes to flat-out blasting down the fall line; that's where the extra travel and slacker head angle comes in handy, but that difference would narrow if you increased the shock stroke length on the Regulator, and maybe bumped up the fork travel to 170mm. When it comes to pricing between the two, the Orbea has a slight advantage - you can get the Wild M10 with an XT drivetrain, 600 Wh battery, and a Fox Factory 38 fork for $8,600. The Wild does have thru-headset cable routing though, so that point goes to the Regulator. Which Model is the Best Value? There are only two models of the Regulator, but neither one of them presents what I’d call a screaming deal. As always, if you’re truly on a budget sticking with a regular bike is the way to go - you can get a well spec’d alloy Transition Sentinel for $4,500 USD. As for the two models offered, the XT build certainly isn’t cheap, but it does have a very solid spec for the price, with top of the line suspension and great brakes, and that’s the one I’d recommend The Deore build is decent, with a Deore drivetrain and brakes, and a RockShox Domain fork, but seeing those parts on a bike with a $7,000 price tag is a tough pill to swallow. We’re all going to be pulverized by a giant meteorite one of these days, so you might as well splurge and get the nice bits on the XT model. Or at least that’s one way to justify it. Technical Report 150mm cranks: I mentioned them earlier, but it’s worth another round of praise for these stubby little cranks. The additional ground clearance is a huge benefit - e-bikes make it possible to pedal in places you normally wouldn’t, so the less chance there is of smacking a pedal on a rock or root the better. I’m not as sold on super short cranks for non-motorized bikes, since the difference in pedalling feel is more pronounced when there’s no motor to help, but I don’t see any reason why all eMTBs shouldn’t have them. TRP Evo Pro brakes: I spent the first half of the review period with TRP's DH-R Evo brakes, and then they were swapped for the Evo Pro brakes that the Regulator will come with. I didn't notice a huge difference in power (there's plenty) or brake feel (they have a nice smooth ramp up and aren't overly grabby); it's mainly the improved lever adjustability that's the difference. I would suggest swapping the stock organic pads for metallic ones if you're planning on riding in wet conditions - the metallic pads last longer and are more consistent when it's pouring rain. Schwalbe Albert radial tires: I get a little nervous any time I see a ‘Trail’ tire spec’d on a full-power e-bike, but I didn’t run into any issues with the Alberts. They’re still a 1150 gram tire, so it’s not as if Transition spec’d tires with casings made of tissue paper. The Radial casing lives up to the hype, and the increased traction makes it easier to relax when heading into slippery, off-camber roots. I also spent some time with Schwalbe’s Shredda Gravity tires installed. The increased drag was noticeable on the climbs, but on the flip side the traction in wet, muddy conditions is ridiculous. Going with heavier more DH-oriented tires does change the character of the Regulator slightly, but it still maintains most of the easy handling that I enjoyed in its stock configuration. Bosch PowerMore range extender: The PowerMore external battery is priced at $525, or $2.10 per extra watt hour. That's a lot of money, but having that extra range is very nice - 850 Wh is enough juice to rack up some serious vert. If you're usually just going out for an hour or two it's probably not worth it, but any longer than that and it's worth flipping some couch cushions to try and find enough change to add one on. Pros + Great handling on a variety of terrain + Full power at a reasonable weight (for an eMTB) + Short cranks unlock impressive climbing abilities Cons - Some riders may want larger internal battery capacity - No XXL, and no range extender option for size S Pinkbike's Take E-bikes are evolving rapidly, and the Regulator CX is a prime example. The weight is very reasonable, the motor is smooth and quiet, and there's plenty of power for quickly zipping up steep climbs. It may not be the flashiest option on the market, but bells and whistles (or idler pulleys and high pivots) aren't necessary to create a bike that's tons of fun to ride just about anywhere. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Transition Transition Regulator Cx Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: TranzX EDP01 Wireless Electronic Dropper Post
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Wireless electronic dropper posts have been in existence since 2017, when Magura's Vyron post debuted. RockShox released their Reverb AXS dropper two years later, and in the years since it's become the most commonly seen housing-free option. There's just one problem – there's still no 200mm version, and no clear answers as to if / when we'll see one. That hole in the marketplace is slowly starting to fill with alternatives, including the TranzX EDP01 reviewed here. EDP01 Dropper Details • Travel amounts: 150, 170, 200mm (tested) • 30.9 or 31.6mm diameters • Weight: 767 grams / remote: 52 grams • Two-year warranty • MSRP: $499 USD (includes battery + remote) • More info: na.tranzx.com The EDP01 is available in 31.6 and 30.9mm diameters with either 150, 170, or 200mm of drop. The post is powered by a rechargeable 7.4 volt battery, and the remote uses a CR2032 battery. TranzX claims the post can be activated 6,000 times before needing a charge, although my fear of being stuck out on the trail with a non-functioning dropper caused me to charge it every handful of rides just to be safe. INSTALLATION There isn't any performance aspect of a wireless dropper post that makes it a must-have over a cable-actuated version, although the ease of installation does count for something. There's no cable and housing to deal with, and getting it up and running is as simple as putting the post in the frame, pairing it with the remote, and hitting the trail. TranzX touts the post's short insertion depth, and while it is low at 290mm, the total length of the EDP01 from the bottom to the rails when fully extended is 545mm, while a 200mm OneUp dropper measures 530mm – the distance from the bottom of the collar to the seat rails is longer on the EDP01, due to the motorized mechanism. That means that to achieve the same seat height, the TranzX would need to be inserted further into a frame than the OneUp. The post uses a one bolt design to clamp the seat into the rails, with another bolt at the front of the post that's used to adjust the seat angle. It's a convenient system, and I found it to be quicker to use than some of the more fiddly two-bolt options out there. The remote comes with a standalone split clamp, and it's also compatible with SRAM's Matchmaker clamp design. Trans X EDP01 Dropper Post Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/hzcQHqL2 Copied Live 00:00 00:18 00:18 PERFORMANCE Initially, the EDP01's return speed was on the slower side, that is until I increased the air pressure in the cartridge to 350 psi (the maximum recommended) via the air valve on the bottom of the post. That helped speed things up, and while it still doesn't extend as quickly as a RockShox AXS, that faster return rate was an improvement. The effort it takes to lower the seat is fairly average – it's not as silky smooth as a BikeYoke, for example, or even the aforementioned Reverb AXS. The good news is that it didn't develop any squishiness during the test period – the cartridge is still going strong. TranzX also sells replacement cartridges separately, and should an issue arise they can be swapped out in a matter of minutes. They may look and function similarly, but TranzX is adamant that the cartridges are not manufactured by Wintek, the company that makes the internals for many other dropper posts. The remote lever is easy to access, and while it may not be the most elegant looking component, I don't have any complaints about its functionality. As for the run time of the post, I did have the battery die on me partway through a ride. I'd left the battery installed during a three-hour drive with my bike on a vertical hitch rack, and then forgot to check its status before my next ride. It's a good reminder to take the battery out when driving longer distances; otherwise the road vibrations can potentially cause it to constantly try to pair with the remote and run the battery down, at least I'm assuming that's what happened. That was the only hiccup I experienced, although I'd be tempted to purchase a spare battery to bring along on mega-rides, just in case (they're available for $49 USD). It's wishful thinking, but it sure would have been nice if the EDP01 battery was compatible with SRAM's AXS batteries, and vice versa. ISSUES After a few months of use the post started making a knocking noise when the seat was lowered and I went over chattery sections of trail. I eventually narrowed it down to the nut that attaches to the cartridge at the base of the post. That had loosened slightly, and was allowing the cartridge to move up and down and make a racket. I snugged it down, and it's been smooth sailing since. Pros + Very easy installation + No need to worry about cable routing or contaminated housing + Easy to disassemble for basic servicing Cons - Going wireless isn't cheap - Not the fastest return speed - Heavier than cable-actuated posts, even when factoring in the weight of cable + housing Pinkbike's Take For riders dead set on a wireless dropper, the EDP01 could fit the bill. It's still more expensive (and heavier) than the sea of fully functional cable-actuated options out there, but it's also not as pricey as a RockShox Reverb AXS, and it's available with 200mm of drop. I am glad of its existence, mainly for the ease that I can install it when a review bike inevitably arrives with a dropper that's too short. That said, I'm not entirely convinced that needing to remember to charge a battery is worth the tradeoff for a cleaner-looking cockpit. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Trek Fuel EX+ Review: The Mid-Power eMTB That’ll Make Most Riders Happy
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, trail-bikes, trek, trek-fuel-plus
article_text: Trek Fuel+ EX Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/VqFlhK65 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 17:54 Trek's approach to the lightweight eMTB is simple: take a successful mountain bike, and add a motor. Of course, that process isn't so simple in practice, but at least it makes the bikes a little easier to understand. In this case, we have the electrified version of the brand-new Fuel platform, which encompasses three sub-model options depending on build kit and link choice. Though I've been testing the EX variant, there are many different permutations one can create from the base frame, by using the rocker link and shock mount kits that Trek is selling alongside the bike. Fuel+ Details • 145-160mm frame travel, 150-170mm fork • MX or full 29" • Aluminum or carbon frame • 63.4-64.5° head angle • TQ HPR60 motor • 580Wh battery • 60 Nm torque, 350W max power • Price: $6,000 – $11,700 USD • Weight: 43.24lbs / 19.61kg (model tested) • trekbikes.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The mid-travel lightweight eMTB isn't necessarily the segment of the electric mountain bike market that gets people stoked, but something like the Fuel+ EX might be the best choice for a lot of riders out there. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details Trek's new Fuel platform is simultaneously very simple, and very complicated. There is a common base frame shared between the EX (tested), MX, and LX sub-models, with the rocker link and lower shock mount determining which of those three options you end up with. To throw some sand in the engine, there's also a secret menu containing more than thirty permutations of all those rocker, link, and shock combinations, making for a very wide array of options for those that want to experiment. Instead of drowning in choice, I opted to stick to the bog-standard Fuel+ EX, exactly as it comes from factory. Without getting into all the possible permutations of the Fuel+, here are the broad overviews of the three sub-models. The Fuel+ EX is a 29" bike with 145mm of frame travel and a 150mm fork. It uses a 205x60mm shock. Large geo: 485mm reach, 64.5° head angle, 77.3° seat angle, 443.5mm chainstay length. The Fuel+ MX is an MX bike with 150mm of frame travel and a 160mm fork. It uses a 205x60mm shock. Large geo: 481.5mm reach, 64.2° head angle, 77.0° seat angle, 440.4mm chainstay length. The Fuel+ LX is a 29" bike with 160mm of frame travel and a 170mm fork. It uses a 205x65mm shock. Large geo: 473.2mm reach, 63.4° head angle, 76.3° seat angle, 443.5mm chainstay length. Those who want to parse through all 32 secret menu permutations can do so here . The Fuel+ does away with the headtube angle adjustment that the previous model touted, instead opting for a standard press-in ZS headset. If angle adjustment is desired, aftermarket options exist, but generally it's nice to see Trek moving away from the proprietary parts required to make the previous system work. The frame features internal cable routing, accessory mounts on the top tube, and room for a water bottle or range extender on all sizes. The small models do feature a curved top tube to accommodate all of these features. All sizes feature a continuous straight seat tube, for maximum dropper post insertion and adjustability. Motor & Battery Trek has been using TQ's HPR motors for a while now, so the integration is well-developed and seamless. There is no motor or battery rattle to report, and the remote/display functions were intuitive and easy to use. I've mentioned it before, but the charging port and associated hardware feels uniquely high quality on TQ's system, which bodes confidence for long-term durability and utility. Even the charge estimates are quite accurate, which is nice if you're tying to squeeze a little more life into the battery before heading out to ride. For mid riders. The full smiley completes for the high folks. Yes, there's also an eco mode. As well as a few other bits of info in the menu. Trek's choice to spec a 580Wh battery is one of the best assets of this bike, as it takes the range to impressive levels, without increasing the weight to a point where it feels unmanageable. Sure, a smaller battery would mimic the ride feel of a bike even better, but I haven't been impressed with the range those smaller cells offer. The 580Wh feels like a great balance, and gives this bike a lot of utility. Battery Issue: During testing, I had two occasions where the battery percentage misrepresented the actual range. While draining the bike from 100%, the battery would deplete as normal to about 10-13%, at which point it would die as if it had none left. Turning the bike off, on, yelling, playing hard to get, nothing seemed to alleviate the issue. I warrantied the battery through the local Trek shop, and it turns out there was a software issue with the specific battery in this bike. I haven't replicated the issue with the replacement unit, so hopefully all is well. The Fuel Flap. Geometry Bad news for those striving for simplicity - thanks to the rocker link and lower mount kits, there are three geometry tables to reference here. Luckily, the differences aren't too extreme, with only a little over a degree in headtube angle and about 12mm of reach separating the full span of configurations. Let's take a size Large Fuel+ as an example. The EX head angle is 64.5°, the MX is 64.2°, and the LX is 63.4°. The EX seat angle is 77.3°, the MX is 77.0°, and the LX is 76.3°. The reach and stack figures are as follows: EX , 485mm reach, 638mm stack. MX , 482mm reach, 641mm stack. LX , 473mm reach, 647mm stack. The EX and LX chainstays are 443.5mm, while the MX is shorter at 440.4mm. Is this confusing? Yes. Are there compromises in some of the geometries to get the link system to work? Also yes. But do you generally arrive at a geo table that makes sense for most of the bike's use case? Luckily, yes. To me it feels like the EX model is the primary in the lineup, with the other builds following suit. All three scale nicely per size, with stack heights growing much more proportionally than before. Chainstay lengths don't scale as well as the other figures, but do grow by a few millimeters per size to keep up with the front center growth. The EX bike should suit most riders in most places, with neutral geometry figures that delivers easy handling and a capable enough stability. Suspension Design Trek is using their classic ABP layout here, implementing a co-rotating pivot around the rear axle to deliver simple curves and suspension performance that is relatively unaffected by braking forces. As there are three models on tap here, there are also three distinct suspensions to talk about. Instead of getting into the weeds with each possible iteration of the links and shock mounts, I'll touch on the three main offerings. The Fuel+ uses a lower shock mount flip chip to adjust progression, with More or Less indicating the overall shock progression. All three builds use either a 205x60mm or 205x65mm shock to deliver the 145-163mm of rear wheel travel, with the final travel figures dependent on which setup you land on. There are also some secret menu options - I've included the likeliest use cases below, with the stock options highlighted in bold. Fuel+ EX : 145mm travel, 205x60mm shock, High progression 21.3%, Low progression 16.4%. Fuel+ EX Long-Stroke: 156mm travel, 205x65mm shock, High progression 21.9%, Low progression 16.6%. Fuel+ MX : 150mm travel, 205x60mm shock, High progression 22.1%, Low progression 17.9%. Fuel+ MX Long-Stroke: 163mm travel, 205x65mm shock, High progression 23.0%, Low progression 18.3%. Fuel+ LX : 160mm travel, 205x65mm shock, High progression 24.2%, Low progression 19.1%. There are both air and coil shock build options available, so folks should be able to find a combination of factors that suits their use case best. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $10700 Travel 145mm Rear Shock Fox Factory Float X, 205x60mm Fork Fox Factory 36, 150mm Cassette Shimano XT 12-speed Crankarms e*thirteen Alloy Chainguide e*thirteen Rear Derailleur Shimano XT Di2 Chain Shimano XT Shifter Pods Shimano XT Di2 Handlebar Race Face ERA Carbon Stem Bontrager Grips Bontrager Brakes Shimano XT Wheelset Bontrager Line Comp 30 Tires Maxxis Minion DHR/DHF, EXO+ Seat Bontrager Seatpost Bontrager Dropper Post Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Fuel+ EX Test Bike Setup I was initially meant to test the MX variant of the Fuel+ platform, but after just one ride on that bike it became apparent that there was a critical assembly issue that needed to be addressed. The problem ended up being an out of tolerance spacer in the rear linkage, causing quite a bit of play while riding. I only note this to say: if something seems wrong, it's always best to go back to the shop and ask. Once I took delivery of the Fuel+ EX, things were smoother. The Shimano-centric build kit was fine, save for one critical detail. Trek chose to spec Organic pads with the XT brakes, which are quiet and functional in dry weather, but fail to function once things get wet. I swapped them out for metallic pads, and proceeded happily. The suspension package was a very familiar baseline, which made setup very quick and easy. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 190 lbs / 86.2 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info I rode the Fuel+ EX everywhere. The shorter-travel platform is fun on mellower trails, but capable enough to tackle some proper terrain. Thanks to the excellent battery range, I was able to explore some zones I rarely poke into, just to see what the view looked like from the top of that hill. Climbing It should be a surprise to nobody that a 43lb mid-travel eMTB climbs well. The motor plays an obvious role in that character, but it's not the only factor at play. Trek did a good job of managing the build kit here, keeping the Fuel+ EX light without sacrificing much in the way of capability. The EX variant's geometry is the best suited to climb-heavy riding, with the steepest seat angle of the bunch. This is one of the main compromises in the do-everything frame approach Trek took here, as the effect of increasing fork travel only slackens the seat angle further as you go to the MX and LX versions of the bike. Luckily, the EX's 77.3° seat angle felt steep enough for all the riding you'd expect to do on a trail bike, even at a fairly high saddle height as I had to run it. The pedaling platform feels even-keeled enough to keep you from dipping off the back of the bike too much, and stays composed in technical sections of trail. As far as the motor feel goes, the TQ HPR60 falls right between the timeless power of a human body and the ludicrous speed of modern full-power eMTBs. You're not oblivious to the boost provided by the bike, but it's far more normal feeling than the 100Nm+ alternatives. Those who like using the motor to scrape up heinously steep and tricky climbs will probably want to look elsewhere, but for folks who want to augment their experience with more power and speed, this could be it. Realistically, I think this motor and battery combo makes the best case for eMTB use to date. It's nearly silent, the aid provided is substantial without getting extreme, and it facilitates more laps / easier climbs / quicker rides as effectively as just about anyone needs. I know people love full-power bikes, but there's something really compelling about this take on the lightweight eMTB, even if the segment isn't massively popular. Descending Trek tends to do a very good job of delivering on the prompt for a given bike's intended purpose, without letting it stray too far outside the lines. While the Fuel lineup does get a bit rangy as a whole, the EX itself is a tidy, fun, and capable trail bike. The fact that it's electrified feels like a secondary element in some ways, as the overall weight and handling errs more on the bicycle end of the spectrum. You still benefit from the extra heft that comes with the integration of a motor and battery, but the Fuel+ has an ease of handling that full-power bikes rarely achieve. Despite a build kit that doesn't prioritize weight, you get a bike that feels lively and engaging even when you're not plowing. This 43lbs / 19kg mark feels like a sweet spot in balancing that calmed feel you get from the eMTB and the more familiar feel of a bike without a motor. As mentioned before, it seems to me that the EX was the primary option in the new Fuel+ lineup, with the geometry shifting shorter and taller as you increase fork travel to achieve the MX and LX models. That shift should work for the steeper terrain you'd expect to ride those bikes on, but many users might end up benefiting from a different size in the lineup to hit their ideal figures. For instance, I opted for a Large EX, and was happy with the 485mm reach; if I was shopping an LX, I may opt for an Extra Large, as the reach figures shrink significantly with that modification, putting the Extra Large at 498mm. Obviously, reach isn't the only figure of note, but it exhibits the trickiness of sizing the Fuel+, particularly if you think you might be swapping between variants. The only on-board adjustment the Fuel+ has is the progression adjustment at the lower shock mount, and I found myself preferring the more progressive of the two options in just about every situation. The feel isn't radically different between the two, but the more active feel of the higher leverage position was nice, without taking away too much from the platform the bike provided for pumping and jumping. That said, I think the majority of riders will probably be best served by the Fuel+ EX, relative to the other options in the family. The 145mm travel is plenty for most trail systems, and the bike feels intuitive and capable on a wide variety of terrain. Those looking to exclusively ride gnarlier trails will be better suited by the LX or the Slash+, but I found the EX to be a solid platform on steeper trails - you're just relying more on the handling than the displacement and maximum stability. Trek Fuel+ EX Trek Slash+ Santa Cruz Vala Yeti MTe How does it compare? All four of these bikes are viable options for those looking for something mid-travel or light weight in the eMTB world. There are plenty more to choose from, but these are on the short list. Slash+ : If you're getting a lightweight eMTB to smash out laps on steep trails, this is the one to buy. This bike still sits at the top of my list for lightweight eMTBs, and remains one of the best descending bikes I've ridden to date. Vala : If you're not sure about the 60Nm output, and feel like you'd prefer a full-power experience, the Vala would be my pick. The Vala aims at a similar segment, but does so with the Bosch CX motor and battery system. That 600Wh capacity and 100Nm/750W power output adds about 5lb to the overall weight, but the Vala is equipped with burlier parts to match. Generally, it feels like a much more capable option, despite being close on travel. MTe : Call it an upgrade pick . The MTe has the same frame travel as the Fuel+, but benefits from Yeti's excellent Sixfinity suspension system, which is easily one of the most composed and capable feeling we've ridden to date. The two are close on weight, even with the 580Wh battery, so it's ultimately a choice on subtle geometry differences, price, and brand. Fuel+ EX : The Fuel+ is probably the bike most people should ride, but the capability and specialization of other options here will probably draw folks away. The Fuel platform is highly adaptable, but you can't swap between the EX/MX/LX options without a fair bit of work. The EX will suit the broadest band of riders, with easy handling and a happy medium feel to it. Which Model is the Best Value? Trek's lineup has seven variations on the Fuel+ EX right now, all priced between $7,700 and $11,500 USD. Shimano or SRAM, Fox or RockShox, the options are all fairly similar, with the choices coming down to which tier of component you want. If it were me, I'd probably opt for the Fuel+ EX 9.7 ($8,700), which has a solid balance of budget parts that still work nicely, all mounted to the fancier carbon frame. Having not ridden the alloy version, I can't speak to the performance, but the weight increase is substantial - you'd lose out on some of the normal bike feel that the lighter builds achieve. Technical Report Shimano XT Brakes: I'm happy to report a consistent and dependable bite point on these new Shimano brakes, thanks to the revised design and lower-viscosity mineral oil they're using. One detail I wasn't able to get dialed in to my liking was the free stroke, which always felt unusually long. I was able to get used to it, but would have preferred less travel in the lever to get the pads to activate. Maxxis Minion DHF/DHR: It's been a while since I've had a review bike come with a DHF up front. A funny shift, considering how ubiquitous they were not too long ago. Luckily, the tire is still great if you trust it, hooking up nicely at a strong lean angle and clearing mud well. I think the neutral feel of the Fuel might cater towards an easier to handle tire for some newer riders, but maybe the DHF will teach good technique. Fox 36 Fork: I'm a big fan of the new 36. It offers a comfortable ride, plenty of support, and adjustment that allows for a reasonably wide range of preferences. It felt like a perfect pairing for this bike, matching the capability well, even at the bike's upper limit. Folks looking to turn the EX into the LX might want a bigger fork chassis, but only because the 170mm travel on that version of the bike narrows your choices down. Pros + Silent motor, excellent battery range + Open-ended build options with link kits + Neutral handling is intuitive and fun Cons - Battery reliability could be a concern - One-frame-for-all system has inherent compromises - Limited size range Pinkbike's Take The mid-travel lightweight eMTB isn't necessarily the segment of the electric mountain bike market that gets people stoked, but something like the Fuel+ EX might be the best choice for a lot of riders out there. The power output is usable, the range is excellent, and the ride quality feels neutral and fun on a wide variety of terrain. There are more exciting options out there, but this one gets the job done well. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review.
