Those wanting to run a Transmission drivetrain will have to run a minimum rear center of 465mm, as the 460mm option can't accommodate the direct-mount derailleur. Gizmomaxxing. A well-executed floating brake arm. Since taking the Vessel in for review, Jack Harle - the man behind the brand - went and updated the frame to incorporate a few new elements. The next generation of frames will feature a bolt-in bottom bracket armature that will allow for gearbox compatibility, or a standard drivetrain layout as you see here. He also beefed up the stiffness of the frame, while still allowing for the feel to be adjusted using the bolt-in bridge at the seatstay. All those extra bits and bobs do add to a fairly hefty frame weight when it's all said and done, with the build you see here tipping the scales at 41.12 lb / 18.7 kg. With a shock and seatpost, the frame weighs 15.8 lb / 7.17 kg, so while lighter builds are possible, you'll be starting from a very high base. The new frame layout: a more robust connection between the front and rear triangles, a bolt-in bracket housing the BB / gearbox, and some updated details on the idler and floating brake arm. Geometry with a 180mm fork. Geometry The geometry chart you see above is representative of the bike on test, but Vessel doesn't offer stock geometry in the traditional sense. Instead, customers are given the choice between a few figures on some variables (head tube angle, head tube length, wheel size), and a freeform entry on others (reach, chainstay, bottom bracket drop). Obviously there are still material constraints, but my understanding is that the limits are fairly wide here - you can really spin up the bike of your dreams. For those who don't have specific numbers in their heads, Jack has a contact form to help determine the best figures to suit your use case. Any information and feedback on past bikes you've ridden would be key to sussing out the right combination of factors. The custom geometry configurator . The bike I have wasn't the result of this custom geometry process, but is instead Jack's personal rig that he let me use for the long-term review. I would have pushed the numbers around here and there, but the overall proportion and fit of the bike feels well-sorted for the purpose. The Vessel's rear center length can be altered from 460 up to 480mm, in 5mm increments. That is done via the bolt-on dropouts mentioned earlier, and the floating brake arm has enough adjustment to accommodate the range. As you'll see in the suspension information, the rear center grows by 24mm through the full range of travel. One of the only details that can't easily be changed on the geometry chart is the seat tube angle. There are too many other mounting points contingent on the seat tube being in a specific place in space, so modifying it too heavily would require a more significant redesign for each frame. Luckily, the 80° position feels appropriate for the type of riding you should expect to do on this bike. Pedal yourself up the hill, all in purpose of going down as fast as possible. Not for rolling terrain, not for your wandering ventures - get up, get down. Suspension Design The Vessel uses a 230x65mm shock to deliver 170mm of rear wheel travel. The shock is actuated by a linkage-driven single pivot layout, with a fairly high main pivot. The floating brake arm adds complexity to the braking system, increasing the design control over how the bike loads dynamically under braking forces. There are four lower shock mount positions on tap, with varied leverage to suit use case and preferred feel. Starting from the forward-most position, the options are 20%/23%/27%/30% total progression. According to Jack, the majority of the leverage change is in the beginning of the travel, with the end rates all aligning quite close, so spring bottom-out force doesn't change significantly. 0 seconds of 18 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/BtzqOXWy Copied Live 00:00 00:18 00:18 At around 185lbs, I was happiest on either a 475 lb spring in the 23% setting or a 500 lb spring in the 30%. The 27% crossover worked with both, but would have been ideal with something like a Sprindex to settle on a happy medium spring rate. As you'd expect, the higher progression modes have a more active dynamic feel, and tend to sit deeper in travel at a given spring rate. Though specific data on anti-squat was not provided, I can guesstimate it as feeling fairly high when pedaling seated, with a firm feel underneath you, and very little chassis bob to speak of. The floating brake arm's purpose in life is to achieve a lower anti-rise figure, and the bike rides with that character - active and balanced front and rear, with very little braking squat to speak of. Jack quotes that figure as being around 75%, though that is ultimately dependent on shifting figures like center of gravity. Axle path is the final piece of the Vessel puzzle, with the high pivot layout providing quite a bit of rearward growth through travel. At 24mm total growth through the full stroke, you can expect quite a bit of front wheel grip, as well as a rear wheel that readily gets out of the way of any in-plane hits that you might push through on your way down the hill. RIDING THE Vessel Enduro Test Bike Setup The Vessel came to me as a frame, with a few parts strapped to it here and there. Building it up didn't feel like a chore, thanks to the full external routing, and standard parts compatibility. Jack provided a 500 lb spring for the shock, which worked well for me in the 30% progression position, but I ran my own 475 lb spring in the lower leverage positions. Over the course of the test, I ran three different forks. A 170mm Fox 38, a 170mm Fox Podium, and a 180mm RockShox Zeb. The 180mm fork feels the most correct and balanced from an overall bike perspective, but the Podium matched the feel of the frame in a very nice way - if only there was a 180mm spring available for that fork. Jack shipped the 460mm and 470mm dropout options with the bike, and I tried both in a few different situations before settling on the shorter option as my preferred all-rounder. More on that later, but it just felt like a more balanced package with the shorter starting length. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 84 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info The Vessel feels best used on rides where you're spinning up a road grade to access fast and natural trails. I found it adaptable to tighter, more feature-heavy terrain, but had the most fun on trails where you could let the speed pick up and open the corners to carry momentum. Climbing Frankly, I don't think anybody is considering this bike for the climbing performance. That's good, because the Vessel Enduro doesn't love that task. It'll get you up the hill for every lap, but expect a bit more effort and budget some time for the commute to the top. The anti-squat feels very high, which reduces any chassis bob to a minimum, and provides a very firm feel to push the pedals into. Given that, the real detractor when it comes to the climbing performance is the overall weight, and the extra drag from the high pivot's required idlers. By no means is it the worst pedal performance I've experienced, but compared to other 170mm bikes I'm riding right now, it's notably more labor-intensive. Luckily, the Vessel comes with that 80° seat tube angle, which keeps you upright and comfortable for your spin up the hill. That same angle might make the bike feel awkward on rolling and traversing terrain, but again you'd be wrong to consider one of these for that purpose. The rear wheel traction delivered by the suspension is so-so, as that supportive feel detracts from some of the stickiness that can help get you through challenging tech climbs. A bit more body English is required to navigate those moments, but I found it perfectly capable of picking through tricky climbs, so long as you have the energy. Descending On this front, the Vessel really lives up to the moniker, delivering you to the bottom of the hill at a rapid speed. It's worth breaking bike down into a few contingent parts - each element works in concert to deliver the final product, but it's worth highlighting them in their own right. First, suspension performance. The Vessel feels exceptionally high-pivoty, with very little feedback coming through the feet, even on very rough, square-edged hits. The back wheel moves rearward quite readily, a sensation that does require some adjustment to use to your advantage. Folks who tend to ride quite forward-biased will want to consciously shift weight towards the rear axle, letting the bike weight the front wheel for you. I found this intuitive after a couple rides every time I got back on the Vessel, but some people might not like having to adapt. The four progression modes allow for a wide range of feel differences in the suspension, and I found two ideals for my different use cases. The 30% position (w/ 500 lb spring) felt best for steeper trails, rough terrain, and all-out traction, with a more sensitive top end and plenty of bottom-out support. On flatter trails, jumps, and for generating speed, the 23% setting (w/ 475 lb spring) had a slightly more predictable feel, and offered more platform for pumping and popping off lips. The geometry is a tricky thing to talk about, as ultimately that's up to the customer to determine. As delivered, I think Jack arrived at a solid place for the steep, fast riding you'll want to do on this bike. If I were ordering one for myself, I'd consider a slightly longer reach, primarily for climbing comfort, and higher stack for even more in-bike feel. The 460mm chainstay length felt very balanced for the front triangle as-is, but I'd consider bumping things longer if I were to lengthen that front center at all. Remember, that back end grows a lot (24mm) through travel, so you're never hurting for front wheel traction. If anything, some people will find that the bike requires a bit too much arm strength to maintain a good body position through repeated hits on steeper trails, where the bike tends to pitch forward a bit. Part of this is due to the lower anti-rise value, but I'd take that tradeoff any day, as the active braking characteristic and lack of brake squat is something I definitely prefer. Finally, we have the chassis element. The Vessel is a uniquely compliant bike, offering quite a bit of wiggle to keep your wheels dancing through awkward and challenging terrain. For my neck of the woods, the bike feels a bit unnerving at times, loading up and springing back in heavy corners and off bigger features. That eats away at the predictability for me, but does give back when you're just playing passenger and letting the bike plow along a line. From the sounds of it, the updated frame should address this by offering a stiffer median feel, opening up the seatstay tuning option to suit different people in different places. Vessel Enduro Kavenz VHP16 Forbidden Dreadnought Trek Slash How does it compare? Four long-travel high pivot bikes, all with their own responses to the prompt. I've ridden all four of these bikes, and though sizing is a bit varied across the models, the ride characteristics are the biggest differentiator. Slash : I'd call this one of the more normal-feeling high pivots I've ridden, maintaining a predictable feel through the feet while still giving that extra bit of top-end comfort through rough terrain. The braking performance is similar to the Kavenz and Vessel in terms of staying active and avoiding too much squat. We didn't award this Bike of the Year for nothing, it sets a very high bar. Dreadnought : The geometry is the star of the show here, and that show is hyperfocused on stability at speed. I rode the Nought as a full-29" bike in the +0 chainstay position, giving it a starting rear center of 460mm that grows 25mm through travel. The suspension performance isn't as sensitive as any of the other bikes, though the chassis does feel composed on big hits and through rough sections, as long as you stay off the brakes. As a whole the bike doesn't feel as well-rounded as some of the others here, but if keeping the front wheel glued to the ground is your main objective then it's quite good. VHP16 : I think the modularity of the VHP16 straddles the gap between the Slash and Dreadnought nicely, keeping things fun on a wide variety of trails and retaining an excellent suspension feel. Competing with the Slash on this front is a major accolade, as the Trek really holds its own on rough descents. The livability of the Pinion integration is a different matter entirely, but with a normal drivetrain configuration the VHP is up there with the best of them. Vessel : This is the most labor-intensive climber of the bunch, but makes up for some of that deficit with the composure on the way down. The flexibility of the frame might be too extreme for some, but it does reap rewards when you shut the brain off and let it run through choppy sections of trail. Like the Kavenz and the Dreadnought, the geometry is fairly adjustable thanks a modular dropout system, though the Vessel takes things a step further with the fully custom front triangle, and the adjustable suspension kinematic. Which Model is the Best Value? Considering the bespoke nature of the Vessel frame, I think the value is quite good. Like with any custom item, you get a fair bit of say in the final product, and theoretically that will yield a higher utility for whatever your intended purpose is. $3,300 USD isn't cheap, but few other options allow for the level of customization on tap here. Technical Report We Are One Triad Wheelset: I've had these wheels for years, and have not treated them nicely. That said, they've taken that abuse without complaint, only requiring the occasional re-tension and true. The Hydra rear hub is on its second axle, and while I'd prefer to swap to the vastly improved Hydra 2, these aren't slowing me down. I'd be keen to try the updated version of these wheels, but this generation still feels like a premium and robust product. Microshift Advent MX Drivetrain: This bike has served as the test platform for the new Advent MX drivetrain so far, and I've been generally impressed. High pivots with idlers aren't always the easiest bikes to get shifting nicely, but the Advent group has done a good job of holding up and staying in gear. One notable positive is just how quickly you can shift up to the easier gears, with two rips of the shifter getting you from the bottom to the top in no time. Fox Transfer 240mm Dropper: Having 240mm of drop is ideal for a bike like this. Getting low and rearward felt key to unlocking the peak performance, and deleting your seat is a key part of that. The Transfer delivers a smooth and light actuation, holding up very well to winter slop. My only complaint is the consistency of the return, as the Transfer still likes to stop a few millimeters shy of full top-out every now and then. Pros + Composed, confidence-inspiring suspension feel + Custom geometry to suit your exact goals + Meaningful adjustments, well integrated Cons - Chassis flex - Frame details could use refinement - Very heavy Pinkbike's Take The Vessel Bike Project Enduro is not a well-rounded bike. It feels like a device built for a specific task, and that task is going downhill on rough, natural trails very fast. The very compliant frame feel and weight will turn some off, but for the right rider is offers a very impressive ride. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Action photos: Eric Mickelson Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Vessel Bike Project Vessel Enduro Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Virginia Tech's Top 10 Safest MTB Helmets Ridden & Rated
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, ridden-and-rated, helmets, 6d, bluegrass, fox-racing, poc, specialized, sweet-protection
product_name: Virginia Tech's Top 10 Safest MTB Helmets Ridden & Rated
article_text: Virginia Tech's Top 10 MTB Helmets https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/qhWtUAWU Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 16:56 What makes a helmet “safe”? Is it thicker material, more coverage, or slip planes? From a general physics perspective, more material (considering it’s the correct type) will dissipate the most energy. We’ve seen examples of that theory before - remember the Troy Lee Designs D3 that Cam Zink wore at the 2013 Red Bull Rampage? That was a special use-case, though, plus the average consumer isn't likely to ride around with an enormous bobble-head-like helmet either. That’s not exactly practical, plus extra inertia from a heavier helmet can have other negative effects as well. Helmet manufacturers and other head trauma experts, such as MIPS, have pushed the development of safer equipment in a realistic package. We’ve seen other forms of safety features, especially slip planes, but also designs like 6D’s Omni-Directional System (ODS), and the implementation of variable density materials. Each helmet manufacturer touts their design work, but one entity, Virginia Tech (VT) has been the consumers’ go-to reference to check "how safe a helmet is", or at least, where it stacks up in comparison to other helmets on the market. We’ve rounded up the current top-10 MTB-specific helmets and listed them by their Virginia Tech ranking. Some of those are half-shells, others are ¾ open-face designs, and there’s even one lightweight enduro full-face that made the list. Included is their overall test score, certifications, and technical details. We've also spent time in each one and highlighted the pros and cons. Just want to know which ones we like best? Read our conclusions here . Certifications EN1078: This is a European safety standard for helmets used during cycling, skateboarding, and roller skating. CPSC 1203: The U.S. safety standard for bicycle helmets for persons aged 5 and older, issued by the Consumer Product Safety Commission. AS/NZS 2063: The Australian/New Zealand safety standard for bicycle helmets. NTA 8776: Is a Dutch standard certification given to high-speed e-bike helmets. These are designed to provide increased protection over traditional bike helmets for e-bikes, which reach speeds up to 45 km/h (28 mph). They offer increased coverage and can withstand higher impacts than traditional cycling helmets. How Virginia Tech Conducts Tests The ratings are conducted at their laboratory, independently of the manufacturer. The results are gathered from the data taken from over 24 impact tests conducted from a drop tower. Virginia Tech positions the helmet to receive medium and high energy impacts on 6 locations, measuring the linear acceleration and rotational velocity sustained. The forces are cross-referenced against concussion risk and given a value. Those are added up for an overall score. The lower the score, the fewer concussions the rider would experience, and therefore, the higher the helmet ranks. You can read more specifics about Virginia Tech's testing here . Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through the affiliate links below. Learn more . #1: POC Cularis BUY AT: Backcountry (USA) Before the Cularis came out, POC was quite far down the Virginia Tech ranking list with the Tectal in 90th, and unfortunately, the beefier-looking Kortal wasn't tested. It was clear that POC had some catching up to do if they were to be more visible on the VTech ranks. Well, they hit a home run with the Cularis as it claimed top spot. It pipped the previous best MTB helmet on there, the Fox Speedframe RS, by a few tenths in scoring. From the outside, and at a glance, there's nothing remarkable about the Cularis - it's quite an average-looking helmet. The coverage is less in terms of EPS foam compared to the Kortal, but that clearly doesn't matter as much when it comes to the impact testing results. Cularis Details • VT score: 7.1 • Fidlock chin strap closure • EPS and polycarbonate shell construction • Mips Air Node • Weight: 360 g • Sizes: S (51–54 cm), M (55–58 cm), L (59–62 cm) • Price: $260 USD • Certifications: EN1078, CPSC 1203, AS/NZS 2063 • poc.com Oddly enough, it doesn't receive the Dutch E-bike safety certification (NTA 8776), which deems helmets appropriate for use over 45km/h. The Cularis uses the newer, slim profile Mips Air Node slip plane built into the sweat liner - no more sharp plastic edges there. It's not the most sweat-absorbing liner, but it does away with any squeaky or sharp plastic inside. It also has a deep fit similar to the Kortal. Glasses storage is found in the forward vents, which feature a fuzzy material to grip those eyewear arms. I wouldn't call it secure, but it does the job if you want to stash them while climbing. The price is getting up there at $260 USD, but should you pick up one of these helmets, you can rest easy knowing it's number one in Virginia Tech's books. You can read more about the Cularis in this full-length review by Seb Stott . The Cularis is a mix of POC's Kortal and Tectal in terms of looks. Even from the inside, the Cularis doesn't show signs of anything unique. Lots of vents up top, yet still the highest rating. The sunglasses' vent storage works well enough to keep them in place uphill. Pros + Secure, deep shell fit. + Mips air node doesn’t have sharp plastic edges. Cons - Doesn't receive NTA-8776 (e-bike) rating. - Air Node liner doesn't have the best sweat absorption. #2: Fox Racing Speedframe RS BUY AT: EVO (USA) The Speedframe RS is the premier half-shell helmet in Fox's lineup. Prior to the POC Cularis, it sat in first place on the VT ranking, and is still a very worthy contender (as are any of the 5-star helmets). It's the only half-shell helmet to use Mips Integra Split, which uses a three-piece, dual-density EPS foam, like Fox's Proframe RS enduro full-face. An inner and outer shell can rotate like one giant Mips slip-plane. The inner shell is even made up of two halves. There's no shortage of features on the Speedframe RS either. The sweat liner is one of the best; the Ionic+ material absorbs sweat well and doesn't irritate my skin, even after long, arduous days. Out back is a BOA Fit System dial to adjust the tension on the fly, while up front is a sunglasses holster. The rubber grippers under the 3-position visor hold them tight enough to descend confidently without them flying out, but locating that stash zone is trickier than it appears. Speedframe RS Details • VT score: 7.23 • Mips Integra Split Dual Density Protection System • BOA Fit System • Fidlock chin strap closure • Ionic+ head liner • Sunglasses storage • Weight: 400g • Sizes: S (51–55 cm), M (55–59 cm), L (59–63 cm) • Price: $319.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com In terms of fit, it's shallower than Speedframe Pro and 50g heavier. Cost-wise, it's the most expensive at $319 USD, a whole $100 more than the Pro model. The shell is made up of dual-density EPS foams. The inner shell can rotate and is made up of a front and rear half. This bullseye is hard to hit with sunglasses. Once in place, they're locked, though. Pros + Only ½ shell helmet on the market with Mips Split Integra. + Dual EPS construction. Cons - Pricey. - Eyewear storage is tricky to find. #3: Fox Racing Speedframe Pro BUY AT: Jenson (USA) Fox has three new helmet models under the Speedframe heading: a standard model, the Pro, and the RS mentioned above. This one shares the same styling and exterior features of the RS, like the eyewear stowage zone, BOA Fit System, and Fidlock buckle, but the main difference is the shell construction The Pro opts for a Mips Air Node slip plane, opposed to the advanced-looking Integra Split. You still receive the benefit of dual-density EPS foams inside the shell for absorbing variable forces. It's not an inexpensive helmet, however, in my eyes, the Speedframe Pro is a decent value at the higher-end market. Speedframe Pro Details • VT score: 8.1 • Mips Air Node Protection System • BOA Fit System • Fidlock chin strap closure • Dual-density EPS • Sunglasses storage • Weight: 350g • Sizes: S (51–55 cm), M (55–59 cm), L (59–63 cm) • Price: $219.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com It's worth noting that the fit is slightly deeper than the Speedframe RS. It's lighter and cheaper, too, yet still receives a score of 8.1 on the Virginia Tech ranking scale. Like the Speedframe RS, the visor has three positions. And a BOA Fit System to dial in the tension. The Speedframe Pro has dual-density EPS, like the RS, except it uses the simpler Mips Air Node. The eyewear storage is identical to the RS model. You'll likely need two free hands to store your glasses. Pros + Dual-denisty EPS. + Good value in the high-end price range. Cons - Eyewear storage is tricky to find. - Air Node liner doesn't have the best sweat absorption. #4: Bluegrass Jetro BUY AT: All4Cycling Here's a helmet you don't see too frequently, but then it goes and lands in #4 on the VT rankings. The Jetro is one of three 3/4 helmets on the list and is a solid lid, if you're into that look. It uses Mips Evolve Core for the slip plane, a Fidlock buckle chin strap closure and comes in three sizes. The weight is reasonable at 595g, and the venting is respectable, given the coverage. Bluegrass's own "Safe-T Heta" fit system has a big range with vertical snap adjustments and an easy-to-reach dial. The padding is very comfy and stays in place well when pulling the helmet on and off - something that can be an issue with other helmets on this list. Jetro Details • VT score: 8.41 • Mips Evolve Core • Fidlock chin strap closure • Additional material around critical impact zones • Safe-T Heta retention adjuster • Weight: 595g • Sizes: S (52-56), M (56-5 , L (58-61) • Price: 250 EUR • NTA 8776 certified • met-helmets.com I found a variety of goggles to work with the Jetro, plus there's even sunglasses storage in the vents up top. The Safe-T Heta retention system. Cushy and secure cheek pads along with a Fidlock buckle. For a 3/4 helmet, the venting is decent. Pros + Great airflow for a ¾ helmet. + Secure cheek pads. Cons - Shallower fit than the Fox Dropframe Pro (2023). - Sunglasses fit in vents but aren't totally secure. . #5: 6D Alterra BUY AT: Jenson (USA) The only full-face helmet making the top 10 MTB helmets in the VT ranks is 6D's Alterra. 6D might be best known for their larger volume moto, BMX, and downhill helmets, but they've managed to pack their advanced safety technology into a lighter-weight package. Omni-Directional System is similar in some regard to Mips Integra Split, but absorbs impacts in a 3-dimensional sense, not just rotationally. That's proven to score well in VT's testing, too. On top of that, the chinbar is reinforced with carbon fiber and earns certification for downhill racing. For a lightweight enduro full-face helmet, it also grants a ton of airflow, even with goggles installed. The fit is a bit roomy in some of the rearward areas, but that could be tuned with extra padding. A Fidlock buckle is handy for pulling the helmet off when taking a break at the top, and then back on before dropping back in. Alterra Details • VT score: 8.5 • Fidlock chin strap closure • Omni-Directional Suspension (ODS) • EPP and EPS construction • Weight: 719 g (actual, size M) • Sizes: S (53-56), M (56-59), L (59-62) • Price: $395 USD • ASTM F1952 DH and NTA 8776 certified • 6dhelmets.com Back in the spring, we reviewed the Alterra in depth and were thoroughly impressed with the ventilation and safety tech built into the helmet, given its weight. The recess and padding on the lower edges aim to save clavicles. From the back, the helmet has a bit of a sloping, wide Porsche coupe appearance to it. The padding on the chin strap moves out of the way and the buckle can rest against your jawbone. There's no debating the convenience of a Fidlock helmet buckle. Pros + Impressive venting for a lightweight fullface + ODS system is silent Cons - Chin strap padding rotates to side - Additional padding may be needed to refine fit #6: Specialized Tactic 4 BUY AT: Specialized (USA) What's a helmet that only costs $59.95 USD doing in the Top 10 list? How did Specialized do it? And why would you spend more than that, given its score of 8.55 on the VT scale? Those are all very good questions. Making up the Tactic 4 are a polycarbonate shell, an EPS foam construction, and Mips Evolve Solution - pretty par for the course, yet it's #6. The coverage is great and the weight remains well under 400g. Some answers where the costs were cut lie in some of the less critical materials, and the adjustments. Tactic 4 Details • VT score: 8.55 • Mips Evolve Solution • Breakaway visor • Standard buckle chin strap closure • EPS construction • Weight: 380g • Sizes: S (51-56), M (55-59), L (59-63) • Price: $59.95 USD (on sale from $119.99 • NTA 8776 certified • specialized.com The sweat liner isn't the most absorbent, and, in a previous longer-term review, it delaminated quickly. The chin strap uses a standard clasp buckle, which we can live with; however, the TriFix ear straps aren't adjustable. This can cause the helmet to sit further forward on your brow than you may like. The retention system is a smooth, accessible dial, though, and that offers vertical adjustment, which may alleviate that for you. The best execution of eyewear storage. Dial in the fit. The TriFix strap isn't adjustable. The biggest downside of the Tactic 4 is the liner. This one is intact, but in our previous long-term test, this fell apart quickly. Pros + Unbeatable safety rating for the price. + Best execution of eyewear storage. Cons - Liner delaminates at seams quickly. - TriFix strap is not adjustable and may cause the helmet to sit forward. #7: Sweet Protection Trailblazer Mips BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist (USA) Back in the summer of 2022, Sweet Protection sponsored our Quebec Trail Bike Field Test and sent out Trailblazer Mips helmets, so I've spent a considerable amount of time in this lid . After that test, I continued to wear the helmet because it was comfy, secure, and promoted a ton of airflow. The 4-piece variable elasticity shell technology and Mips Evolve System stack up to give the Trailblazer an 8.69 score on the VT scale, which is nothing to scoff at - it even beats out another one of Sweet Protection's heavy-duty, enduro helmets. Trailblazer Mips Details • VT score: 8.69 • Mips Evolve System, 4-piece shell • 360 Occigrip Fit System w/height and width adjustment • Eyewear storage in front vents • Weight: 325g • Sizes: S/M (53-56), M/L (56-59), L/XL (59-61) • Price: $200 USD • CPSC 1203, CE EN 1078 certified • sweetprotection.com I don't have too many gripes with the Trailblazer Mips, but one of them was the malleable visor. It's not the end of the world, but it can easily sit lopsided. For a helmet that costs $200, that shouldn't be an issue. I'd also appreciate a Fidlock buckle at that price point. The eyewear storage is less refined than their Bushwhacker 2Vi helmet, but it's acceptable. Yellow marks the Mips liner. The flexible visor is durable, however, this one doesn't have any notches to keep it lined up. No Fidlock buckle here. Pros + Great airflow + Fit retention system has height and width adjustment Cons - Would expect Fidlock closure given price tag - Visor can easily be distorted and sit crooked #8: Fox Racing Dropframe Pro (2020 version) BUY AT: REI (USA) If the helmet shape doesn't cause you to raise an eyebrow, then perhaps the model year might. This is the older model Dropframe from 2020. Yes, there's a newer version out now, and looks aside, they are quite different, however, that doesn't detract from its safety ranking. This uniquely shaped helmet from Fox is light for a 3/4 open-face helmet at 485g. Despite coming in four shell sizes, I found the medium to have some pressure points in the upper back half of the shell. The features are on the basic side, too. There's a standard buckle, standard Mips slip plane, and no retention system at all - it relies on padding alone. Dropframe Pro (2020 version) Details • VT score: 8.85 • Standard clip buckle closure • Dual-density Varizorb EPS construction • Weight: 485g • Sizes: S (52-54), M (54-56), L (56-5 , XL (58-60) • Price: $179.99 USD • Certifications: CPSC, EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063 • foxracing.com If nothing else, the Dropframe Pro 202 can be scooped up for some killer deals, and that doesn't detract from the fact that it earns its way into the Top 10. Angular lines and a fixed visor. No BOA Fit System here. Regular old Mips for Princess Leia. And a standard buckle. Pros + Light for a ¾ shell helmet. + Dual EPS foam. Cons - No rention system. - Tight for a medium with small pressure points on top, back corners (there are 4 sizes, however). #9: Fox Racing Dropframe Pro (2023 version) BUY AT: EVO (USA) Landing right behind the 2020 Dropframe Pro is the 2023 version. It's large and in charge at 630g, but there's a ton a features packed into this helmet. Inside the Dual-density Varizorb EPS shell construction is an improved Mips Evolve Core slip plane with an Ionic+ antimicrobial liner. There's also a handy goggle strap recess and a GoPro mount under the 3-position visor. Most notable is the addition of the BOA Fit System, which wasn't featured on the 2020 model, so you can literally dial in the security of the helmet. With that said, the number of shell sizes has been slimmed down to just three, however, the helmet does come with two extra pad kits: one thinner, one thicker. I found the fit to be snug, yet very comfy. The venting isn't as great as the Jetro, and it's best saved for e-biking or cooler weather. Dropframe Pro (2023 version) Details • VT score: 9.03 • Fidlock chin strap closure • Mips Evolve Core • Dual-density Varizorb EPS construction • Ionic+ antimicrobial liner • BOA dial • Weight: 630g • Sizes: S (51-55), M (55-59), L (59-63) • Price: $279.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com Given the lower levels of ventilation and weight, I'd be more inclined to spend a bit more and go for the 6D Alterra - I like my pearly whites. There is less chance of ending up with a Jerry goggle strap position thanks to the indentations. Lots of vents, but still not the best in terms of ventilation. Mips Evolve Core slip plane and Ionic plus liner. The cheek pads can dislodge when you try to pull the helmet on by the straps. Pros + Snug/comfy fit of a full-face. + Easy to reach BOA Fit System dial. Cons - Cheek pads can dislodge when pulling on the helmet too easily. - Warmer than the previous model. #10: Sweet Protection Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist (USA) Rounding out the Top 10 MTB Helmets in the Virginia Tech Rankings is the big sibling in the half-shell lineup from Sweet Protection. The Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips is a long name with a lot of details packed in. What is 2Vi exactly? This is the name of the technology Sweet Protection gives to the multi-density EPS construction spread throughout the helmet. It's not just randomly placed, but positioned purposefully in common impact zones. The shell is large in volume on the outside and which leads to a deep-fitting helmet. The M/L weighs 430g, has adequate venting, and enough adjustment to suit most head shapes. Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips Details • VT score: 9.05 • Mips Air • 2Vi multi-density EPS construction • 360 fit system • Fidlock closure • Eyewear storage in front vents • Weight: 430g • Sizes: S/M (53-56), M/L (56-59), L/XL (59-61) • Price: $250 USD • Certifications: UKCA, NTA-8776, EN1078:2012, A1:2012, CPSC 1203\ • sweetprotection.com Under the visor are rubber pads in the forward vents to hold glasses tightly. Unfortunately, I found that the visor can shift positions and rattle on rocky descents or head-slapping drops. A precise fit, but the sunglasses storage is solid. The Mips Air liner is as comfy as... well, air. Vertical and lateral occipital adjustments with the 360 Fit system. The visor can jiggle and make noise when charging down the trail. Pros + Snug, wrap around fit + Dual EPS foams in key areas Cons - Visor can rattle in mid position - Excessively long dead-end chin strap loop (I cut mine to avoid choking) Overview Top Choice: Fox Speedframe RS Before the POC Cularis, the Fox Speedframe sat in the top spot of the Virginia Tech ranks for MTB helmets. The Speedframe holds a number of features that make its shell construction more complex with Mips Integra Split, and that seems to have paid off. Fox reworked the shape of the helmet inside, too. In the past, the forehead area didn’t work for me, but now it’s very comfortable. The venting is top-notch, the liner wicks sweat well, and the forward vents hold sunglasses securely enough to ride with. Best Value: Specialized Tactic 4 How could you ignore this helmet? It lands in the top 10 MTB helmets and is currently on sale for $59.95 USD. My favourite part, aside from the excellent safety-to-price ratio of this helmet, is how easy it is to locate the glasses holster - you can place them with just one hand. That’s something I haven’t been able to do with any other helmet (besides the Specialized Ambush). As for that liner, I bet you could retrofit the higher-end Ionic+ fabric from the Ambush helmet into the Tactic with some arts and crafts. Honourable Mention: 6D Alterra Shapes aside, the Alterra is an incredible helmet. Its unique impact protection design, based on Virginia Tech’s testing, proves its worth. For a full-face, it also breathes and vents exceptionally well. Add in the relatively low weight and reasonable price, and you can see why it deserves an honorable mention. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Ridden And Rated Helmets 6d Bluegrass Fox Racing POC Specialized Sweet Protection Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Vittoria Mazza Enduro Race Tires - A Compound Too Far?
author: Henry Quinney
article_text: Maxxis, Schwalbe, and in more recent times Continental, have a firm grip on the high-end tire market. In fact, it's to the extent that their model names become almost ubiquitous. Tires from other brands will often be described as a Minion copy or a Magic Mary, whether or not it flatters or they're happy with the comparison. In recent years, Vittoria have been making an earnest push into the high-end mountain biking sector. Whether through new models in the range, updating casing or compounds, or sponsoring some of the world's biggest events, they're certainly giving it a go. Mazza Race Details • 29 or 27.5" in 2.4 or 2.6" • Exceptionally soft Graphene & Silica compound • Reinforced casing across the breadth of tire • Actual weight: 1340g (29") • Price: $89.99 USD • vittoria.com It's been several years since the release of the Mazza, and the Mazza Race uses the same tread pattern and spacing. Instead, the key difference is in compound and casing. This tire uses a 60 TPI casing. This uses thicker threads in a lower quantity, and it represents something of a departure from the idea that has been perforated in recent years that a higher TPI will give a better tracking and a more supple tire. As for the compound, it's just the one throughout. Vittoria has a history of eking the life out of their soft rubber by using graphene. This goes even further by using both graphene and silica. The rubber is incredibly soft, to say the least. The Mazza Race has a reinforced layer across the entire tire and a further additional protective insert that sits across the tread of the tire itself, as well as an anti-flat insert on the bead of the aramid bead. All you need to know. Details So what is it? Is it a Minion? Well, there are elements of that. However, I would say this a more of a Minion and cut spike hybrid. Simply put, there is a lot of canvas on show with Mazza. This might be good for mud shedding and loose surfaces, but a more open tread can introduce a vague feeling when initiating turns, as well as being more vulnerable to rim damage or punctures. Compared to the standard Mazza, the Mazza race has a slightly more open tread. The tires aim to negate that in two ways. Firstly, these tires are exceptionally soft. Each lug is like a gummy bear that's been left in the sun. At the start of the test, I wondered if these would be prone to being ripped straight off the canvas but I can report no such incidents. As for rim damage, the design of the sturdy single-ply sidewall aims to protect against that eventuality, not least by being relatively portly. At 1340 grams each, the weight of these tires combined with the incredibly soft compound, might not be an enticing prospect for all-day pedalers. Curiously, Vittoria don't provide any details on what that means in terms of the durometer of the rubber. These tires aim to establish themselves as the highest-performance option for gravity-focused riders in the range. A range that includes the 900-gram Trail casing (29x2.4"), and the 1300-gram 2-ply Enduro casing. It also represents something of a departure for the brand in terms of the technology utilized. Normally, Vittoria tires are known for their 4C makeup. This combines, you guessed it, four different rubber compounds throughout the tire, to tailor each knob, be it center or side, to the task at hand. They can then have firmer bases with softer outer layers. When this is combined with relatively complex siping patterns, it can give tires that hook up, don't deform too much, and roll well. In the past, I have been very impressed with 4C tires that I have used from Vittoria in a variety of casings and tread patterns. The Enduro Race tire uses one super-super-soft compound throughout. The compound used is softer than any of those features in the 4C models. Minion-esque, but a little better at penetrating deeper, softer dirt in my opinion. Performance Let's start with the main point of these tires. They're intended to be exceptionally grippy for true enduro trails, and do they deliver? This tire has exceptional grip under braking on sheet-like rocks or slabs. However, I would say that the same thing that can make it excel in those situations is the same thing that can undermine them in other instances. The Mazza Race, thanks to the softness of the rubber, can offer levels of grip and deforming at slow speeds that other tires can't come anywhere near to. If you want something that will give consistently high levels of grip then these are very impressive. The problem is when the speeds are higher and there are more inputs or twisting forces going through those exceptionally soft lugs. At higher speeds, especially through turns, it can feel like the lugs just twist or roll. While this isn't the worst feeling ever, and it can give a very positive feeling in medium-load turns, it can also quickly breach a threshold and the tire goes from conforming to squirming. On the front, I have no complaints and I didn't feel I was ever getting the drawbacks of the soft compounds, while still enjoying the positives. On the rear, however, it was a thin line between carving a turn and feeling like you were going to rip the tire off the rim. If you could isolate these attributes away, I would say that it's a very confident turner across the tread, going from edge to edge consistently. It's an okay braker, but compared to something like an Assegai or Krypotal Front it lacks the large braking paddle that's made up of center, transfer and edge knobs. The softness of this tire could make it a very good candidate for the front and it would pair excellently with a 4C Enduro Mazza on the rear. So, is it simply too grippy? It's so hard to say. The element of squirming only came when leaning, so was far more present in higher-speed bike park trails with built-up banked turns with positive cambers. The rest of the time, on more natural trails where there isn't the same lateral load going through the bike, the tire did give a very good grip. For me though, I think you choose to run a 1300-gram tire for a reason, and that reason is support. For all the grip on the Mazza Race, it excels in places where you don't need such a heavy tire in the first place. Some of this instability should also be laid at the door of the sidewall. Vittoria, for this tire, has included a APF (anti-pinch flat) insert around the rim but it is markedly thinner than what you might find in other tires. Typically this insert is an inch-or-so long piece of material that sits between the plys of rubber in the sidewall and not only helps support the tire when under extreme load, but also helps protect the rim in harsh compressions. While it is present with the Mazza, I question its effectiveness at helping protect the rim and stabilize the tire. Cutaway of the Mazza Race sidewall, left, and a Maxxis Doubledown casing. To try and demonstrate this, I cut up two tires. On the left you can see a cutaway of the Mazza Race, and on the right of a Maxxis DHR2 Doubledown. You can see the effects of the butyl rubber insert in the Maxxis tire means that it mainly flexes where the sidewall ends and the tread begins. On the Mazza, which instead has a thinner and more flexible insert, it doesn't have this inflexion near the tread and I believe is a less robust option because of it. Note - the red part of the Vittoria tire is the anti-pinch flat insert; you can see the dark grey insert of the Maxxis if you look closely. Whilst the pictures might not look too dissimilar, the sidewalls feel markedly different when in your hands. A comparison I would make would be suspension with a low spring rate and low compression damping. Yes, it might grip at lower speeds but as soon as you start to go faster you'd gladly trade some suppleness for some consistency and control under larger hits. The wear on the rear after 2 full days of park riding and a few trail rides. Durability & Reliablity You measure some tire's lifespan in weeks, others in days. The Race compound on the Mazza's mean you can measure it in runs. You also don't have to look too far to see where the squirming sensation is coming from as each lug seems to deteriorate as much from the base as it does the edge. A few days in the park riding classic Creekside enduro trails will use up any tire, but that's not to say I wasn't taken aback but just how fast the Mazza Race's showed signs of wear. The tracks, which included trails such as Delayed Fuse and Misfire, are often used in Enduro races at a world series and regional level - and for good reason. If you looking for the style of feature or section you'd find on an enduro world cup race track, it's probably going to be in there. During testing, I typically ran either 26 or 27 psi, which would be matched front and rear. There were also issues present within the test that can't be ignored. Typically, I wouldn't say I break many wheels. Or rather, when I do I can point to one large, Hail Mary style incident. During the first five rides on the Mazza Race tires I broke two carbon wheels. I can only speculate as to whether each rim would have failed with a different model on, but I have sliced tires on both rims previously and the rim has come away unscathed. It should be noted though that the tyre received no visible damage, and in the second instance still held air, although I did fit a fresh one when moving to a new wheel. There was also an incident where the tire blew off the rim under medium compressions on the exit of a turn at 27 psi. I was surprised at this, to say the least. It's not something I've had happen for a very long time, and also not something that I have suffered with other tires on the same rims. It was terrifying and frustrating in equal measure, as I was left trying to slow the bike down in wet slippy tech. You could, I suppose, run extra tape to tighten up the fit of the tire. While I'm not against this, it's more of a racers trick and I don't think it's a reasonable expectation with new tires in 2023. I think, and it's only my opinion, but these tires would be better with the added security of a thicker butyl insert. Yes, they would be heavier for it but the sheer grip delivered by this soft compound merits a sidewall that can keep up. The red hot patch might not be to everyone's liking but it's grown on me. Pros + Incredible grip on rocks and slabs + Penetrates loose dirt and mud Cons - Soft compound wears very quickly - Squirming under load - Came off the rim while riding Pinkbike's Take I have always enjoyed Vittoria's 4C Mazza Enduro tire, in fact, it's one of my favorites. With this Race model, they haven't quite hit the same heights. I love the Mazza Enduro, and the consistency it offers in a range of situations - be it turning, braking, harsh compressions or rim protection. Sadly, with the exception of crawling down rock slabs at very low speeds, the new Race tire is inferior in every regard, including weight. The Mazza Race isn't without merit, and if you want something exceptionally grippy to put on the front then this would be a good candidate, but I think to make the most of the Mazza family it would be worth mixing with a 4C Enduro model on the rear. — Henry Quinney Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires Vittoria Vittoria Mazza Author Info: Henry Quinney Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Jun 3, 2014 362 articles

title: Review: Vivo Enduro Derailleur & F3 Shifter
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, drivetrain, vivo, vivo-enduro-derailleur, vivo-f3-shifter
article_text: While wireless electronic drivetrains have been stealing the spotlight lately, Vivo, a small New York-based company, has been toiling away on their version of the ultimate cable-actuated derailleur. Simply called the Enduro Derailleur, it's machined from 7075 aluminum (even the pulleys are aluminum), with titanium pivots and hardware. The $350 derailleur is designed for 12-speed, 10-52 tooth drivetrains, and is ideally paired with Vivo's $250 F3 shifter. I'll dig into the price / weight comparisons later on in this review, but needless to say, small batch drivetrain components are not cheap. Enduro Derailleur & F3 Shifter • 7075 aluminum, titanium hardware • Designed for 12-speed, 10-52 cassettes • Derailleur weight: 331 grams • Shifter weight: 128 grams • Enduro derailleur: $320 USD • F3 shifter: $250 USD • Available: February 2025 • vivocycling.com The F3 shifter is also constructed with a healthy dose of machined aluminum and titanium hardware – the only plastic bits are the 3D-printed paddles, barrel adjuster, and lower shifter housing. There's a huge level of customization available with the shifter; Vivo says there are over 30,000 possible permutations. The derailleur and the shifter are assembled in the US, but the machining and 3D printing takes place at mutiple locations, some in the US, and some in China. Vivo Enduro Derailleur & F3 Shifter https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/I2BX807W Copied Live 00:00 03:37 03:37 Details Enduro Derailleur The Enduro derailleur looks absolutely amazing – all that polished aluminum makes me happy, and doesn't require any help from Dario and his oven cleaning chemicals . Cartridge bearings are in place at all the pivots, and there's also one for the pulley that the cable runs through. One thing that's missing is a built-in button to lock the derailleur cage forward for wheel removal, similar to what SRAM uses, or a way to release the clutch, the way that Shimano does. Instead, a small rod is included that's inserted when the cage is rotated forward to keep it out of the way. In a pinch, a multi-tool, or even a small stick could be used to accomplish the same thing. That small aluminum rod also serves as a way to ensure the B-tension is set properly. The clutch tension is adjustable, but to get to it you'll need to remove three of the tiniest bolts you've ever seen (I'm still looking for one that I dropped in the grass while filming the video for this review. Dang it). They also require a 1.3mm hex wrench. That's extremely small, and it's not a size most home mechanics will have, at least not unless you're a budding horologist. The derailleur is compatible with SRAM shifters, but not Shimano, due to a slightly different cable pull ratio. F3 Shifter The F3 shifter can be customized in all sorts of ways thanks to the liberal use of 3D-printed parts. There's a huge library of shift paddle, barrel adjuster, and back plate shapes to choose from. That aspect of it is intriguing, but it does give the shifter a slightly more homebrewed feel – the texture of those parts doesn't seem quite as finished as the smoother, more polished parts on a SRAM or Shimano shifter. PRICE / WEIGHT There's no getting around it, the Enduro derailleur is one expensive piece of bike jewelry. The weight is reasonable, but it's not a featherweight; it's three times more expensive than a Shimano XT derailleur and it weighs 45 grams more. F3 Shifter: 128 grams / $255 Enduro derailleur: 331 grams / $320 XTR shifter: 130 grams / $137 XTR derailleur: 240 grams / $274 XT shifter: 133 grams / $63 XT derailleur: 286 grams / $119 SRAM XX1 shifter: 112 grams / $179 SRAM XX1 derailleur: 269 grams / $323 SRAM XX Transmission shifter: 52 grams / $200 SRAM XX Transmission derailleur: 463 grams / $650 On the Trail I installed the Enduro derailleur on my Raaw Madonna, since that's the bike that sees the roughest terrain, increasing the likelihood of it seeing some solid bounces and smacks. Installation was simple, you'll just want to make sure to run the cable along the correct path (Vivo includes a photo that shows the correct orientation). I ran the derailleur with the F3 shifter for the entire test period. On the trail, the shifting was excellent – the F3 shifter has a light, crisp action. It doesn't take much effort to shift, and there's a very positive 'click' each time the lever is actuated. I'd say the actual feeling when shifting is closer to how SRAM's mechanical shifters feel, but with a slightly lighter lever action. The orientation of the thumb lever wasn't totally ideal for me; I tried a couple different thumb paddles, but didn't find the Goldilocks configuration I was searching for. I would have preferred if it was a little more parallel with the bars; despite all of the customization potential there's no way to adjust the actual thumb lever angle. This is me being extra-picky, though; for most of my rides I didn't give the paddle position a second thought. The derailleur hasn't needed any adjustment after my initial shakedown ride, and it hits all the gears exactly as it should. It's also nice and quiet - the friction clutch mechanism is effective and has remained smooth for the entirety of the test period. I did increase the amount of tension slightly from the stock setting in order to reduce the amount of chainslap, a procedure that was slightly more difficult than I'd expected due to the aforementioned use of screws for ants. T-Type Hack For a portion of the test period I ran the Enduro derailleur with a SRAM T-Type chain and cassette, mainly to confirm my suspicions that batteries aren't necessary to have a functioning T-Type drivetrain. As it turns out, I was right (recent shots of a mechanical T-Type derailleur from SRAM attest to this too) – the shifting was just as quick (or quicker when it comes to multi-shifting) and I didn't miss having to remember to charge a battery at all. The key to making this hack possible is grinding some of the plastic off the back of the UDH hanger to create enough room for the chain. This does make wheel installation a little more difficult, since the lip that the axle sits against isn't as prominent, but it's not the end of the world. Of course, this isn't endorsed by SRAM at all, and I'm sure they would recommend against it. I also swapped the aluminum pulley wheels for the thinner, plastic SRAM pulleys in order to keep the chain from hanging up. I'd love to see Vivo come up with some sort of direct-mount version of this derailleur; that would really help it stand out from the other mechanical derailleurs currently on the market. DURABILITY The derailleur has held up well so far – the pivots are free of any slop, and it's still functioning perfectly. I've luckily avoided any massive direct hits so far, but the construction seems quite solid – there aren't any tiny plastic bits to be seen. It's also designed to be rebuildable with standard tools when the time comes to give it a refresh. Pros + It looks amazing. + Shifter has a huge range of customization options + Light action, crisp shifting Cons - Expensive - Clutch adjustment requires very small hex key Pinkbike's Take The Vivo Enduro derailleur works very well, and it's also gorgeous. For some riders, that's going to be all it takes for it to find a spot on their bike, price be damned. It's an impressive achievement, and it's great to see more homegrown options released to compete with the drivetrain behemoths. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Drivetrain Vivo Vivo Enduro Derailleur Vivo F3 Shifter Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Vorsprung Telum Coil Shock - Canadian Made & Custom Tuned
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: After years of offering shock tuning services, along with manufacturing their own line of fork upgrades, Vorsprung Suspension decided to create a shock of their own. A three year development process resulted in the Telum, a Canadian-made, custom tuned, twin tube shock. The overall design and resulting list of features aren't wildly different from what's currently offered from other manufacturers, but the smart details and the ability to revalve the shock without pulling it entirely apart help it stand out from the crowd. There's also the fact that each shock is custom tuned for the bike it will be installed on, and for the rider. Vorsprung Telum Details • Adjustments: low speed compression, high speed compression, rebound • Adjustable hydraulic bottom out • Climb switch • Weight: 971 g (with spring, 205 x 65mm) • Made in Canada • MSRP: $1,200 USD • vorsprungsuspension.com That level of customization does come at a price – the Telum retails for $1,200 USD, making it one of the more expensive options on the market. Vorsprung Telum review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/v7wxvyNr Copied Live 00:00 09:21 09:21 Adjustable rebound, high and low speed compression, and hydraulic bottom out. The climb lever creates a firm but not totally rigid pedalling platform. Details Mentioning that the Telum's design isn't a radical departure from the norm isn't meant as a slight – after all, there's no need to start from scratch when there are decades of previous developments to pull from in the moto and mountain bike worlds. The Telum uses a twin tube design with a solid, 22mm diameter main piston. As the shock compresses, the piston pushes oil through the damping circuits in the reservoir bridge, and then it recirculates between the inner tube and the outer tube, back behind the main piston. One of the reasons Vorsprung went with the solid main piston design was to allow for the shock to be revalved without being disassembled. With their Rapid Revalve design it's possible to externally adjust the force required to flex the internal shims, which enables significant changes to the level of damping without a full overhaul. It's worth noting that a revalve isn't something that most riders will need to do unless they're switching the shock to a different bike or had a significant change in weight or riding style. The shock has 18 clicks of low-speed compression, 12 clicks of high-speed compression, 18 clicks of rebound adjustment, and 18 clicks of hydraulic bottom-out, along with a Sprindex coil; that provides a wide range of possible configurations. All of the adjustments are done with either a 3 or 6mm allen key – there aren't any tool-free adjustments, other than the lockout lever. The hydraulic bottom out kicks in for the last 15mm of the shock's stroke. Steve Mathews, Vorsprung's founder, has put together four videos that do an excellent job of explaining each and every detail, free of any unnecessary marking fluff. Those videos are worth a watch if you want to take an even deeper dive into the inner workings of the Telum. Ordering Filling out a detailed questionnaire is part of the purchasing procedure for the Telum. Along with the typical rider weight and bike model information, riders are asked about their riding style, aggression level (on the bike, not in life), strength to weight ratio, plus typical jump size and terrain steepness. It may seem a little excessive, but it's similar to getting fitted for a custom suit (or at least I assume so – I've never actually owned a suit, bespoke or otherswise). Just like you wouldn't expect a tailor to measure one arm and call it good, the more information Vorspung can gather the greater the chance that the shock they ship out will need as little setup time as possible. Ideally, it can be installed and ridden upon arrival without needing any adjustments. The information you provided is saved in Vorsprung's Tuning Hub database, as is the exact tune that the shock is shipped with. All of that information is accessible by the rider, which can come in handy if you decide to try a different tune. In my case, I noted my preference a balanced setup optimized for steep terrain at a variety of speeds, and rated myself advanced / expert with a 'very athletic' strength to weight ratio. For my 160 lb weight I ended up with a 475 lb spring, although the beauty of the Sprindex design is that that can easily be changed by turning the collar. Performance When the shock arrived I did exactly what Vorsprung suggested – I installed it, checked the sag (27%), and did a quick bounce to make sure the settings didn't feel too wacky before heading out for a shakedown lap. That initial ride went well, free of anything that would cause me to pull out my multi-tool and start turning dials. I've been able to try a wide range of excellent shocks on my Raaw, so it wasn't a mindblowingly different ride experience compared to what I was used to, but it was very quiet, very calm, and free of any annoying top-outs. Given the choice, and if it doesn't negatively affect the performance, I'll take a silent shock over a noisy one every single time. Three months later the shock is still as smooth as when it arrived, and I haven't had to make any drastic setup changes – I'm still within a click or two of the original recommended setup. The best shocks fade into the background, and the Telum sits firmly in that category. It enters its travel smoothly, and then remains nicely composed when dealing with repeated sharp impacts. The hydraulic bottom out feature works well, and there isn't any harshness to speak of at the end of the stroke. Many of my favorite rides involve an hour or so of grinding up a steep fire road, which gave me plenty of time to think about the shock's lockout. The Raaw is a pretty calm pedaling bike even with a shock fully open, but the level of support that the Telum provides without being jarring is commendable. It's the style of lockout I prefer – the shock can still move to take the edge of rougher sections of road or trail, but it's also firm enough to provide a more supportive pedaling platform. If you do forget to flip the lever to open before descending it's not the end of the world – a blowoff feature prevents any damage to the shock, and since it's not a full, super firm lockout it's not an unbearably harsh ride. What about the Revalve feature? I haven't had a need to go down that route, but it's nice to know it's possible. It does require one additional tool, but the steps are simple to follow, and it doesn't require getting covered in oil or having access to a vacuum bleeder. How Does It Compare? I've had a wide range of coil shocks mounted to my Raaw Madonna over the last 12 months, including an Ohlins TTX22, RockShox Vivid Coil Ultimate, and the Push SV Eight. Each shock has their own slightly different feel out on the trail, but with a little experimentation I was able to find a good setup with each option. If I had to rank them, the Vorsprung would be one step from the top, the spot currently occupied by the SV Eight. There's just something about that SV Eight that keeps me coming back to it – it feels slightly more sensitive than the Telum, and it's a little livelier overall, a sensation I prefer. I can achieve a feeling that's similar with the Telum, but it's not identical. At the end of the day, both shocks are excellent, and I'm certainly not being held back by either one. Prices (shock + spring): Push Elevensix = $1,600 (spring included) Vorsprung Telum: $1,200 (spring included) EXT Storia: $1069 + $130 = $1199 Push SV Eight: $865 + $95 = $960 Ohlins TTX22: $845 + $100 = $945 Fox DHX2: $679 + $130 = $809 RockShox SuperDeluxe: $619 + $39 = $658 Pros + Excellent custom tuning service + Very quiet & extremely composed in rough terrain + Wide range of adjustments + the ability to revalve without disassembly Cons - Expensive (but it is fully custom tuned for each rider) - No tool free adjustments Pinkbike's Take Imagine if a band came out with a greatest hits collection as their first album. That's essentially what Vorsprung has done with the Telum – this isn't a tentative step on the long road to perfection. Instead, it's a fully realized, extremely well executed option, one that can go toe-to-toe (or eyelet to eyelet) with anything else on the market. It's quiet, consistent, and the custom tuning helps ensure that it's fully dialed right out of the box. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shocks Vorsprung Vorsprung Telum Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: We Are One Arrival 170 - An Enduro Race Bike That Can Climb
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Review We Are One Arrival 170 WORDS: Matt Beer PHOTOS: Max Barron You're likely familiar with the We Are One backstory by now - the company has made a name for itself crafting premium carbon frames and rims in Canada. Three frames exist under the Arrival name, with either 130, 152, or 170mm of rear travel. This boutique frame is subtle with minimal paint and branding but stands out nonetheless. We're focusing on the Arrival 170 UDH MX, which is what I’d call version 1.5 of their enduro race bike, since the geometry and kinematics haven’t changed, but the entire rear triangle has been redesigned to accept SRAM’s Universal Derailleur Hanger. A second welcomed addition is a flip-chip built into the seatstay pivot to accommodate a 27.5” rear wheel while maintaining the full 29er geometry. The frame comes in three sizes and uses a dual-link suspension, yielding 170mm of travel. That's paired with a 170mm fork which rests at a sub 63.7-degree head tube angle, making it plenty aggressive, but not crazy-slack. We Are One Arrival 170 Details • Carbon frame, made in Canada w/ lifetime warranty • Travel: 170mm front and rear • Wheels: 29" front and rear (MX compatible) • Super Boost 157mm rear hub spacing • 63.7° head tube angle • 76.6° seat angle (L) • Sizes: M, L, XL • Reach: 445, 472, 497mm • Chainstay: 447mm (XL: 450) • Weight: 14.6 kg / 32.2 lb (as pictured) • Price as shown: $8,299 USD • Frame only: $5,499 CAD / $4,299 USD (w/ RockShox SuperDeluxe Coil) • weareonecomposites.com Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Arrival 170 is cut out for enduro racing. It's plenty capable downhill, but isn't as single-minded towards descending as other long-travel bikes that can be cumbersome in flatter terrain. — Matt Beer Frame Details The Arrival frame now accepts a UDH and features a flip-chip at the seatstay pivot to maintain geometry if swapping between 27.5 and 29" wheels. The front and rear carbon triangles can be built into three unique bikes: the Arrival 152 on which the platform is originally based, Arrival 130, and the 170. The biggest version requires its own set of suspension links, a longer shock, and a fork of equal travel. The upper plates are braced by a carbon bridge, saving 50 grams over the aluminum version of the past. Glancing over the frame, We Are One has all the usual areas that cause complaints covered. On the rear triangle, there is healthy layering of rubber to silence chain slap, as well as BB and downtube protection. This high-end build uses few cables. When the brake hose enters the downtube, foam tubing eliminates rattling noises - internal guides were omitted in favor of superior carbon compaction. Other areas worth investigating are the lack of ISCG tabs and accessory mounts. We Are One does offer a proprietary chainguide, however SRAM’s X0 Transmission cranks carry their own bashguard, relieving the frame of any forces from rock strikes. We know not everyone loves 157 Super Boost rear wheel spacing (probably because they already own a 148 Boost wheel or frame), but zoom out and look at what We Are One have implemented with the Arrival's chainline (55mm) - they're paired the wider 157mm spaced hub with the 148mm crankset (168mm Q-factor). By combining these components, they've reduced the angle of the chain when in the lower gears - arguably, the place you spend the most time. Who wouldn't welcome less drivetrain wear? Smart. Overall, the frame is well thought out with room inside the front triangle for a water bottle and a small fender to protect the linkage from debris. Another worthy mention about the build quality is the high tolerance on the hardware which is apparent when switching the Arrival between rear wheel sizes. All of the threaded components along the frame feed into female chips that can be replaced if damaged, excluding the BB shell. Geometry & Sizing We Are One evolved the Arrival 170 to cater to enduro racing, making the wheelbase longer and the head angle slacker than the 150. Those measurements land in the ballpark of what we’d expect; aggressive for bike park speeds but not unwieldy when nose-picking around 180-degree switchbacks. The headtube sits in what most would consider the slack range at 63.7-degrees while the seat tube rests at a reasonably steep 76.6. When slack head tubes and mega-steep seat angles meet, they can cramp the seated position and put a ton of weight into the handlebars. The Arrival 170 leaves enough space without placing so much weight over the back wheel that you’ll loop out. Our size large frame stretches to a 472mm reach at the front half and a 447mm chainstay for the rear end. The medium frame uses the same chainstay length, but that grows by just 3mm on the 500mm-reach XL frame. When scoping the vertical measurements though, the 100mm headtube and 20mm BB drop add up to a low stack height of 624mm. A low-rise 20mm bar doesn’t help a forward weight bias, but that’s easy enough to change to suit your style. Another figure to point out is the seat tube length and dropper post height. The seat tube length measures 445mm, about 15mm longer than a Santa Cruz Nomad. The size large was provided with a 170mm RockShox AXS post which has a bulkier clamp head and therefore a taller stack height than most mechanical droppers, which is something to keep an eye on if you require more clearance in that area. Suspension Design The Arrival 170 uses its own set of links that differ from the 130 and 152 travel models. It also uses a longer 205x65mm stroke shock. We Are One Arrival 170 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/bEEs50zg Copied Live 00:00 00:17 00:17 We Are One hasn’t disclosed kinematic graphs, the anti-squat and anti-rise are likely high on this dual-link design due to its resistance against pedal bob and neutral braking characteristic. On the leverage side, the Arrival 170 uses longer links than the 130 and 152. That calls on a 205x65mm stroke shock. This build came with a RockShox Super Deluxe Coil which was tuned for We Are One. You can also choose the air version of that shock as well as a Fox Float X2. Off the top, the SuperDeluxe Coil moves with a light action and then quickly ramps up to a firm mid-stroke for support. Overall, the Arrival is quite progressive. Rarely did I hit the end of the travel. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $8299 Travel 170 Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate Coil Fork RockShox ZEB Ultimate 170mm Headset Industry Nine ZS44/ZS56 Cassette X0 Eagle Transmission Crankarms X0 Eagle 170mm Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA 73mm Rear Derailleur X0 Eagle Transmission Chain X0 Eagle Transmission Flattop Chain Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar We Are One Da Bar 20mm Stem We Are One Da Stem 45mm Grips ODI SDG Lock-On Brakes SRAM Code Ultimate Stealth, w/ 200mm F, 180mm R rotors Wheelset We Are One Convergence Triad 30mm Seat SDG Bel Air 2.0 Seatpost RockShox Reverb AXS 170mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC SRAM's T-type derailleur bolts straight to the UDH mount. Da Bar and Da Stem combine to make Da Package. The Arrival came with a 20mm rise bar and 45mm length stem, but I favored a shorter, higher setup. RIDING THE Arrival 170 Test Bike Setup Since their launch, there have been some critical misunderstandings surrounding how to set the sag on the Arrival. The 130 and 170 are designed to ride at a typical 30% sag, whereas the 152 travel version rides at a lower 23-25% sag. Two triangles, three rear travel figures, various shock strokes - I can see how confusion could develop, however, We Are One has a detailed shock setup guide on their website . Adding sag gradients to the rocker links might have eliminated that issue. A 475 lb spring was supplied with the bike, which was much too firm. I settled on a 425 spring, which after one turn of preload on the coil spring sat perfectly at the suggested 30% sag number. The shock tuning guide mentioned above also includes damper settings, which is always a welcomed starting point. Specifically, they suggest the rebound, high and low-speed compression be placed in the middle positions, while the hydraulic bottom-out should be 75% of the way closed. I didn’t stray far from there throughout testing. To counteract the slight change in dynamic position that the 27.5” wheel seemed to inflict, I added a half turn of preload, plus an extra click of LSC, depending on the grade of the trail. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 36 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r For the fork, I’d bounce between 83-86 pai in the 170mm ZEB with the compression clickers nearly in the middle. The HSC was turned one more click open and the LSC one more closed (from their middle position) - rebound would vary with spring rate and trail type, but ten clicks from closed was the norm. I mentioned the low 20mm-rise bars and opted for a 35mm rise Da Bar. Shortly after, I swapped out the 45mm long stem for a 35mm as I found the steering swung more intuitively for my taste. Next, I switched the front tire for the MaxxGrip compound of the Maxxis Assegai and installed a Schwalbe Big Betty rear tire with the Soft compound, Super Gravity, since the 27.5" rear wheel supplied for the test didn't include any rubber. Stiffer wheels and more supportive downhill casing tires found their way onto the Arrival to cross-compare along the way too. Lastly, the 180mm rear rotor was replaced for a 200 to dissipate heat more effectively. You could easily be fooled that this Arrival has 170mm of rear wheel travel because it climbs exceptionally well, providing gobs of grip and pedalling efficiency. Climbing Let’s get straight to the point, the Arrival 170 is hands down the best climbing long-travel bike I’ve ridden yet. Honestly, I could be fooled on the uphills if you told me it only had 150mm travel. Given that I’ve ridden the 152mm travel version of this bike, is it any better at climbing? I’d be inclined to say yes. There’s less BB drop, so you gain some crank clearance, the chainstays are longer for more balance, and the increased travel makes for unrivalled traction. Furthermore, the support doesn’t melt away when you hammer down on the pedals. Spinning up is equally as pleasant thanks to the comfortable seat tube angle that’s neither too relaxed nor too steep. In fact, the Arrival came with me on multiple backcountry days where I’d normally grab a 120mm bike. Its efficiency made me question, why not have the safety of more travel on these types of rides when you’re absolutely bagged by the time you get to the downhill segments? On those transitions, though, I was drawn back to the dropper post constraints. I’d love an extra 10mm of drop from the Reverb AXS and jostled with the perfect height. On long or heinously steep climbs, I’d need that extra bump up. Conversely, I’d have to lower it back down for descending. The Arrival 170 is cut out for enduro racing. It's plenty capable downhill, but isn't as single-minded towards descending as other long-travel bikes that can be cumbersome in flatter terrain. Descending By the time you read this, we will have wrapped up the 2023 Enduro Bike Field Test where the Arrival 170 would have gone toe to toe against similarly matched machines. In that test, there was a clear differentiation between enduro “race bikes” and others that we’d never want to put against the clock, yet still felt confident descending on. Where would the Arrival 170 slot in? After spending a good chunk of the late summer on the stealthy bike, I can confidently say that it’s best suited to enduro racing, and less freeride smashing. Sure, the travel, angles, and sturdiness are there, but it requires a little more poise and control at high speeds and in steeper zones. A bit of that comes down to the suspension. The light action tracks and carries speed across stutter bumps incredibly well, however, the early progression requires a strong rider to time compressions precisely on steep, chunky trails.
