On occasion, I’d experience an unexpected “spike” as if I’d bottomed out, particularly when on the brakes. I bet with a tiny bit more BB drop, the high-speed stability would become more predictable. The sense of standing “on the bike”, as opposed to “in the bike”, comes back around. It doesn’t always appear to settle in. Basically, the Arrival will do the work but you can’t casually hang on - you have to work for it. There’s a trend amongst the We Are One’s bikes because we hinted at similar findings on the 152. Switching between 27.5 and 29” rear wheel sizes presented a phenomenon that I’ve noticed with other bikes that are convertible, like the Giant Reign. In the MX setup, the bike rode lower and slacker, although on paper, the geometry is preserved. The sensation is backed by the fact that the position of the handlebar appeared to be rolled further back than before. The 447mm chainstay made the most sense with the smaller wheel too, finding a balance of sportiness and cornering stability. Either way and as expected, I could ride more aggressively with the smaller wheel behind me. Given the flip-flopping back and forth between wheel sizes and throwing other shocks on there, I was highly impressed with the craftsmanship of the frame - creaks and groans from re-mounted hardware were never a worry. Aside from the foam tubing slipping slightly and leading to a minor cable rattle, the Arrival 170 is incredibly quiet all around. We Are One Convergence Triad 30mm internal width rims are said to be 33% stronger and 18% more damped than their previous generations of carbon hoops. No complaints here. Technical Report We Are One Convergence Rims: We often hear about riders favoring aluminum wheels over carbon for their compliance. The Convergence rims are the third-generation of carbon hoops that We Are One has manufactured. Their unique shape allows the nipple to optimally align with the angle at which the spoke enters the hubs. 28 spokes are used up front and 32 out back, regardless of the rear wheel size. They were anything but harsh and stood up impeccably, barely needing the turn of a spoke key. SRAM X0 Transmission: All of our tech editors are well-versed on the Transmission drivetrains by now and agree that the shifting is SRAM’s best work yet. However, I’ve had to re-position and re-torque the direct mount T-type derailleur more than once. This component requires a high torque to hold it in place; 35Nm to be exact. An extra tug on the torque wrench was needed for it to stay put. RockShox Super Deluxe Coil: RockShox has come a long way with their new generation of shocks, winning the 2022 Pinkbike Suspension Product of the Year award with the Super Deluxe Coil. Dollar for dollar, the features built into the shock, such as the hydraulic bottom-out, are unrivaled. This tune in particular brings outstanding suppleness and control to the Arrival. Which Model is the Best Value? If you’re looking at a We Are One Arrival, you probably didn’t stumble across it by mistake and were already aware of the high cost, but this isn’t one of umpteen thousand catalogue carbon frames pumped out of a factory overseas. The dollar figure is high, but so too is the component level and frame quality - those are hard to beat. Adding to its value is how adaptable the Arrival can be. The two triangles serve as a multi-bike platform (as discussed in the Frame Details section above) - the 170 can even tout a 190mm dual crown fork. Similarly, the 152 accepts up to a 170mm fork. Between the link kits and rear wheel changes, there are a ton of options. This complete Arrival 170 SRAM X0 AXS Transmission build that I tested has sold out, but a GX AXS kit rings in at $7,599 CND / $5,919 USD. If you back up a bit and strip it down to the frame, an Arrival 170 frameset checks out at $5,499 CAD / $4,299 USD with the Super Deluxe Coil shock. That’s basically the same price as a Santa Cruz Nomad, without a shock. Factor in that the Arrival is made in North America and the value is impressive, if you’re looking at premium bikes. According to We Are One, they’ll continue to build limited edition runs of Arrival UDH MX frames. We Are One Arrival 170 UDH MX Santa Cruz Nomad How Does It Compare? During Shock Week , I spent plenty of time riding the Whistler bike park on the Nomad. Santa Cruz’s long-travel mixed-wheeled bike has what I’d consider near-perfect geometry that’s worthy of freeride antics, yet still excels as an enduro race bike. At a later date, I took the Arrival in the park as well. Sailing over jump trails, I never felt as surefooted. You have to ride the Arrival with more focus, whereas on the Nomad, you can get away with pointing it in the right direction and holding on. Numbers-wise, the head angle, reach, and wheelbases are virtually identical to the Arrival 170. The major difference comes from the BB drop and stack height. 10mm doesn’t sound like a lot but it does bring a secure nature to the Nomad when pointed downhill. For 170mm travel bikes, they’re both alive at low speed, however, the Arrival wins out when it comes to generating speed by pumping through the trail and its astute climbing abilities. As for the technical features of these bikes, each frame has a few aces up its sleeve. The Nomad offers in-frame storage. On the Arrival, there aren’t even accessory mounts under the top tube. Tools and such are left to be strapped to the outside of the frame. Then there are the flip-chips. Like most Santa Cruz bikes, the Nomad has a small adjustment to tweak the angles and BB height minutely on the lower link. Over on the Arrival, the toggling seatstay pivot restores the fixed geometry when switching between rear wheel sizes. As for the frame quality and workmanship, both brands build sturdy, durable bikes with lifetime warranties. Up, down, all around - it's hard to find a more versatile 170mm travel bike. Yes we love it, but a couple small quirks do exist. Pros + Frame craftsmanship is on par with (or above) premium brands + Light on its feet for a 170mm-travel bike + Small bump sensitivity equates to impressive traction up and downhill + Incredibly versatile across a variety of terrain making it a killer enduro race bike Cons - Requires more focus to descend steep, rough tracks compared to other “smashy” enduro bikes - Suspension can firm up unexpectedly when deeper in the travel - Three frame sizes limit riders at either end of the height spectrum Pinkbike's Take Someone recently asked me, "Are there any bikes you actually like?", possibly poking fun at my highly-critical nature. Of course those exist. It's impossible to create a bike that perfectly suits all aspects of riding, but if you're looking for a long-travel bike that has nearly all corners covered, the Arrival 170 is one of my top picks. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes We Are One We Are One Arrival Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Whyte Elyte 150 Works - 'One of the Best Handling E-Bikes'
author: Seb Stott
tags: reviews-and-tech, emtb, reviews, enduro-bikes, whyte, whyte-e-lyte-150
article_text: REVIEW Whyte Elyte 150 Works WORDS: Seb Stott PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson The niche of "SL" e-bikes continues to grow in popularity, and it's easy to see why. They offer a ride feel that's very similar to a regular mountain bike on the descents while still providing a boost on the way up. New lightweight motors from Fazua, TQ, and Brose have driven this trend, but now Bosch has thrown its hat in the ring with their new SX motor, which claims 600 Watts of peak power while weighing just 2 kg. Impressive numbers. Whyte have taken full advantage of this with their Elyte. By building the whole frame out of carbon fiber, a material they haven't used for a few years, Whyte claim the lightest model weighs as little as 16.4kg (36.1 lb). That's pretty impressive for a bike with up to 600 W of power and a 400 Wh battery. Whyte Elyte 150 Details • Full-carbon frame • Wheel size: 29" front & rear • Travel: 142mm rear /150mm front • Bosch SX motor, 55 Nm, 600W peak • 400 Wh battery (+ 250 Wh range extender) • Head tube angle: 64-64.6° • Reach: 435, 460, 485, 510mm • Chainstay: 450mm • Weight: 19.5 kg / 43 lb (actual, XL) • 4-year frame warranty, lifetime warranty on bearings • Pricing: £9,999 (includes range extender) • whytebikes.com The Elyte is available in two main flavors: the Elyte 140 and 150. Confusingly, the name refers to the fork travel, so the former has a 140 mm fork and 135 mm of rear travel, while the latter has 150 mm up front and 142 mm at the rear. Both use the same frame, just different forks and shocks. The 140 probably exists to show off the most impressive headline weight, with light-duty parts and a hefty price tag. The 150 makes the most sense thanks to the extra travel, more robust components and lower price, so that's what we've tested. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take The Elyte is one of the best-handling e-bikes out there. It's stable on the steeps, maneuverable through tight turns and doesn't mind being picked up over obstacles and chucked about on the trail. — Seb Stott Frame Details The Elyte is designed and built around the Bosch SX motor. It provides a modest 55 Nm of torque, but if you spin your legs fast enough it boasts a 600 W peak power output, which is up there with "full-power" motors like Shimano's EP801. Torque is the rotational force the motor applies to the chainring, while power is the torque multiplied by the rotational speed (cadence). It's the power that dictates how fast you can ride up a hill, not the torque. So, to get so much power with relatively little torque, you have to spin quite fast. You won't get anywhere near the peak power unless you use a higher cadence than most riders are used to. Cleverly, Whyte have specced stubby 155 mm cranks in all sizes, which naturally result in a higher cadence as well as extra ground clearance. A 400 Wh internal battery sits inside the enclosed downtube and isn't designed to be removed in a hurry. A 250Wh Bosch Powermore range extender is included with the top-spec model (tested) and can be fitted to the bottle bosses nearest the motor, giving a total of 650 Wh of energy, putting it in line with many full-power e-bikes. The range extender weighs 1.5 kg, bringing the total weight to 21 kg. When riding, the range extender and main battery drain simultaneously and maintain a similar percentage, so you can't drop off the range extender once it's spent leaving you with a full main battery. The long, straight downtube means a second bottle can be fitted in front of the range extender. Whyte say a 450 ml Fidlock bottle will fit in sizes M-XL, but I was able to fit a regular 750 ml bottle in my Xl test bike. If you're planning to exert yourself more than the motor, you can mount two bottles instead. There's an integrated sealed seat collar. Not a flip-chip per se, but there's an offset bushing in the shock eyelet to adjust the geometry. The electronic derailleur is powered by its own AXS battery rather than a wire from the main battery. AXS derailleurs powered by the main battery make sense in theory; proponents say there's a reserve in the main battery for a few hundred shifts after the motor "dies". However, Whyte say they decided to have a separate battery for the derailleur because this reserve doesn't always work, leaving you suddenly without power or gears. And with a bike like this, you could keep riding for a long time without assistance so long as the gears work. The downside is you'll need to charge the derailleur battery every few rides. The frame features cable ports for "traditional" internal cable routing, but the headset has room to run the cables through the headset if you prefer - I can recommend a good psychiatrist if that's you. There's plenty of rubberized frame protection plus an aluminum skid plate under the motor, which gets some use thanks to the low-hanging motor position, which is designed to keep the center of gravity as low as possible. The unidirectional carbon frame is category-four rated, which loosely means it's designed for enduro-style riding but not bike parks. It's only tested for forks up to 150 mm, so you can't fit anything longer without voiding the warranty. Suspension Design A classic Horst-link layout with a clevis-driven shock is familiar from other Whyte bikes. This time, the shock eyelet contains offset bushing hardware that can adjust the bottom bracket height by 8 mm and the head angle by half a degree. It's probably a simpler and lighter solution compared to a dedicated flip chip, and there is the option to install regular hardware to get the geometry in the middle. Anti-squat, shown in a 50-tooth sprocket. Leverage Anti-squat, which determines how firm and efficient the suspension feels under power, is pretty average for an e-bike, with around 100% at sag. That should mean minimal pedal bob without the suspension becoming unresponsive when pedalling hard. The leverage curve is moderately progressive throughout, so it should be supple at the start of the stroke and supportive towards the end. Whyte say it plays nicely with coil shocks. Geometry & Sizing Whyte have always favoured low bottom bracket heights and the Elyte is no exception. I measured the BB on my test bike at 335 mm off the ground in the high setting and 326 mm in the low. Even for a short-travel bike, that's very low indeed. The 155 mm crank arms will give up to 15 mm more pedalling clearance than "standard" 170 mm arms. My measurements also come out slacker than claimed, with a head angle of 64 or 63.5-degrees depending on the setting, and an effective seat angle measuring 76.1 or 75.6-degrees at my pedalling height. It's normal for bikes to measure up slightly differently from the geometry chart, which is why I take these measurements. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $12663 Travel 142 mm Rear Shock Fox Float X Factory EVOL, lockout, 210mmx50mm Fork Fox 36 Factory, Grip2, 150 mm Headset FSA No. 69/56/SRS Cassette SRAM XS 1295, T-Type, 10-52T, Crankarms Hope E-Bike Cranks, 155mm length, 36T chainring Chainguide Integrated top guide Bottom Bracket Bosch Performance Line SX, 55 Nm Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM XO T-Type AXS, 12 speed Chain SRAM XO T-Type, 12 speed Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod, right, MMX Handlebar Race Face Next R Carbon, 20mm rise, 35mm bore, 800mm width Stem Whyte Trail, 32mm length Grips Whyte Enduro lock-on Brakes Hope Tech4 V4, 200 mm floating rotors Hubs Hope Pro 5, six bolt, 32h rear, 28h front Spokes DT Swiss New Champion 2.0mm, brass nipples Rim Whyte TeXtreme carbon Trail rims Tires Maxxis Minion DHF, EXO TR MaxxGripp 29in x 2.5in front; Maxxis Dissector, EXO+ TR MaxxTerra 29in x 2.4in, rear Seat Fizik Terra Aidon, 145mm wide Seatpost Whyte drop.it II Adjust, 30.9mm (S/M 140-170mm travel; L/XL 170-200mm travel), MMX Paddle remote lever Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Elyte Test Bike Setup I found the suspension easy to set up on the Elyte, sticking with 30% sag, compression generally open and rebound just on the faster side of halfway. The Fox 36 Grip 2 is familiar to me, so I set it to 95 psi with compression and rebound both a few clicks from fully open. I did find the bar height way too low on my Xl test bike, so I soon swapped out the 20 mm rise handlebar for a 35 mm rise OneUp handlebar along with a 40 mm stem and ODI grips. This made it much easier to move the bike around and feel comfortable riding hard. Tire pressures hovered around 22-23 psi front and 26-27 psi rear. Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 31 Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 37" / 93cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, at Glentress, Cademuir and Innerleithen. The Whyte felt at home on all but the most bike-parky features of Innerleithen's Matador trail, but was in its element on the tight, steep tech of the Golfy. Climbing The Bosch SX motor likes to spin fast, so you do find yourself consciously increasing the cadence to get the most support. The 155 mm cranks make it easier to do this, as shorter cranks lend themselves to higher RPM due to the smaller pedal circle circumference. If you pedal fast, there is plenty of power available when zipping up steep fire roads, even enough to keep up with full-power bikes at a push. But on technical climbs, the SX motor has noticeably less oomph when the cadence drops, making it hard to regain momentum when you lose it. When the trail kicks up in a crux move and speeds plummet, leaving you struggling to turn the cranks over, the motor seems to stall, and provides no assistance until the cadence builds to a certain level, which is less than ideal. Also, the seat angle is a little too slack with the flip chip in the high setting and even slacker in the low position (which I preferred for descending). This makes it harder to maintain a good weight distribution on steep climbs. Despite the short cranks, pedal strikes were occasionally an issue in the low mode. Throw in the Dissector rear tire and the Elyte isn't the best for clearing technical climbs. On the other hand, the moderate weight and fast-rolling tires make it impressively efficient. I managed around 1,000 m of climbing from the 400 Wh battery with maximum assistance in winter conditions, or around 1,600 m with the range extender. Even with the motor off, it's surprisingly easy to keep up with regular bikes until the gradient gets to the point where the 36-tooth chainring becomes limiting. Descending On the first ride with the stock low-rise bar, I felt like a passenger. I found it hard to push the front tire down into holes to maintain grip and to lift it up and over obstacles. Swapping to a higher bar made all the difference. Despite the longish chainstays, I found it remarkably easy to lift the front wheel and bunnyhop over roots or fallen trees, while also being able to maintain consistent pressure on the front tire through turns. One of my local trails has a knee-high boulder on a moderate climb, which I find very hard to get enough speed to clear on an unassisted bike, and impossible to get over on a 25 kg full-fat ebike. The Whyte hit a sweet spot with plenty of power to build speed, without feeling stuck to the ground, allowing me to clear this familiar obstacle easier than any other bike I've tested. The point is, the Elyte has comparable peak power to a full-fat e-bike while being almost as easy to manual and hop as a regular enduro bike (just not with the stock handlebar). I started testing in the high setting which is how the bike ships. In this setting, the bike is far from high or steep, but on occasion, I did find the steering less predictable than I'd like when entering sharp, complicated turns. I was reluctant to try the low setting because of the already low bottom bracket and relatively slack seat tube, but I'm glad I did. This took the handling to another level on the steeps. The lower bottom bracket and slacker head angle make it easier to chuck it into awkward turns and carve around them with confidence. I did scrape the frame's skid plate on one awkward rock step, but the short cranks mean this setting isn't ridiculous in terms of ground clearance. The downside of the low setting is mostly on the climbs and mostly in the seat angle, but the grin-inducing stability and "chuckability" on steep downhill tech are worth it in my view. Whyte's lead engineer told me he runs a neutral bushing for in-between geometry, which might be a good compromise for all-round riding. The suspension is remarkably supple on small and medium bumps, fluttering over the chatter amazingly well for the suspension travel. This is perhaps due to the shock tune, which Whyte say has minimal preload on the compression valving and a linear rebound tune. It does lose ground to longer-travel bikes on big hits and drops, where the limits of the suspension travel can be felt. It's a shame that the frame is not officially rated to take a 160 mm fork because that could deliver a slacker head angle without the bottom bracket being quite so low (perhaps in with a neutral shock bushing) while also giving a little more cushioning in the front. Nevertheless, the Elyte is one of the best-handling e-bikes out there. It's stable on the steeps, maneuverable through tight turns and doesn't mind being picked up over obstacles and chucked about on the trail. What's the Best Value? The Fox suspension and Hope brakes were some of the key components of the Works model, so it's hard to recommend downgrading to the SRAM-equipped RSX model with its inline shock and Code Bronze brakes. It may be 20% cheaper, but the RSX model doesn't include a Range extender (and you're going to want one of those if you're the type of person who enjoys fun). Whyte Elyte 150 Works (£9,999) Scott Voltage eRide 900 Tuned (£10,099) How Does It Compare? The Bosch CX motor might be in the same SL category as the TQ’s HPR50 found on the [PI=https://www.pinkbike.com/news/first-ride-scott-voltage-the-electric-genius.html]Scott Volatge[/PI], but they are very different to ride. Although the Bosch lacks low-cadence torque, it’s way more powerful, to the extent that swapping to the Scott feels a bit like going back to a regular mountain bike. The Scott’s smaller battery means the range is similar if not slightly less, but on my scales, it’s only 0.25 kg lighter. The Voltage has a better climbing position for technical climbs, but on the descents, I preferred the Whyte. With its lower BB, shorter cranks, shorter stem and slacker head tube, it felt more intuitive and confident on steep and technical trails. The extra travel on the Scott is handy when things get gnarly, but I think the Whyte’s rear suspension and shock tune are better judged, with similar suppleness and more predictable support. Despite generally preferring longer-travel e-bikes, I'd much rather have the Whyte. Technical Report Hope Tech 4 V4 brakes: I’ve put a lot of miles on two sets Hope’s most powerful stoppers, and they are a big improvement over the Tech 3s. There’s plenty of power and an intuitively linear lever feel that makes it easier to judge braking force on slippery descents. But both sets had a lever feel/free-stroke that changed slightly over the test period, going from ultra-sharp to having a little too much free-stroke for my liking even with the adjuster wound fully in. They're still solid brakes, but I’m not blown away by the Tech 4s as some journalists are - I don’t think they’re as solid and consistent as a Hayes Dominion or a SRAM Code with sintered pads and a HS2 rotor. 155 mm cranks: These should be standard on electric bikes. SRAM Transmission: I’m not convinced Transmission is necessary or worth the premium on a mechanical bike, but on an eMTB the decisive shifting is appreciated. It didn’t skip a beat throughout testing - except when I forgot to charge it. Pros + Confident yet agile handling especially on steep and tight descents + Impressive suspension performance + Bosch SX motor gives powerful support and range with the included extender + Fits a water bottle and a range extender Cons - Not the best for steep and technical climbs - Stock cockpit is too low for the XL size - It's ten grand Pinkbike's Take The Whyte Elyte is the best-handling eMTB I’ve ridden. The geometry adjustment and short cranks make it possible to experiment with geometry that I would previously have considered too low, and it pays dividends on steep and twisty terrain. It’s relatively easy to pop a quick manual or hustle through flatter sections too. The Bosch SX motor offers impressive power and range compared to other SL e-bikes, but don’t expect to get up the trickiest sections as easily as full-power eMTBs. If you want to quickly dispense with climbs rather than tackling the most technical way up, and then have maximum fun on the way back down, this could be the bike for you. — Seb Stott Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech eMTB Reviews Enduro Bikes Whyte Whyte E Lyte 150 Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: Wolf Tooth Components Ripsaw Aluminum Flat Pedals
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: Wolf Tooth Components have added a new flat pedal offering to their range, named the Ripsaw. Thin at the edges and with a pronounced convex profile, it sits in stark contrast to the Waveform, the equivalently-priced but thicker, concave option. While concave pedals are by far the most common design, allowing one's foot to sink into the center portion and forcing the pins to bite into the rubber as it does so, some appreciate the benefits of a convex profile. Wolf Tooth state that the Ripsaw is designed for riders who prefer a mid-foot riding position, where the ball of the foot is in front of the pedal spindle and the arch of the foot aligns with the convex arch in the center of the pedal. Ripsaw Details • Weight: 383g (pair) • Area: (L) 118mm x (W) 102mm • Front edge thickness: 7mm • Outboard edge thickness: 12mm • Thickest Point: 16mm (inboard edge of axle) • Axle material: Stainless Steel • Pins per side: 12 • Pin height: 4mm • Color: Black / Raw Silver / Ultraviolet Purple • Price: £180 / $199.95 USD • Five-year warranty • wolftoothcomponents.com Details While the Waveform pedal is available in two sizes, the Ripsaw is one size only - it is 118mm long and 102mm wide. That's slightly longer and slightly narrower than OneUp Components Aluminum pedal (115mm x 105mm). I will compare and contrast the two pedals throughout this review as they are another notably convex option for those that subscribe to the mid-foot position. The CNC machined aluminum body accommodates 12 replaceable, rear-loading pins per side. They have a 3mm Allen head and, in all but one case for the most inboard pin, the head is recessed into the pedal body where it is less vulnerable to damage. Unlike the pins on most modern flat pedals, they are threadless. I measure them at 4mm tall. No washers are supplied to shim down the height, but available separately are shorter 3mm pins that one might want to install for various reasons; perhaps along the front and rear edges to maximize the convex profile, or at the center in an attempt to reduce it. At $199.95 US, the Wolf Tooth Components Ripsaw pedals are some of the most expensive flat pedals on the market that do not have titanium axle. Weighing in at 383 grams for the pair, they are also some of the lightest, heavily machined to give a competitively thin profile that may entice those who ride rock-strewn terrain and are prone to regular pedal strikes. At the outboard edge, the convexity measures 2mm (per side) The convex profile is accentuated at the inboard edge where it is raised to accommodate the axle For those interested, the Ripsaw pedals are actually slightly thinner (7mm) than the OneUp pedals at the front and rear edges, which is arguably where it matters most. The difference is 1.3mm, though, so we are absolutely splitting hairs here. Helping to keep the weight down is a stainless steel axle that does not run the full width of the pedal body. Internally, an Igus bushing and its carrier cartridge, three sealed bearings, a compression washer, and a lip seal keep the pedal body spinning on the axle. Every single component of the Ripsaw pedal is available as a replacement part. Wolf Tooth pride themselves on that serviceability and the customer's right to repair. The process of completely refreshing the internals does require use of some non-standard tools, however. The refresh kit will set you back $22 USD, with the necessary bushing removal and install tools available for another $22 USD. On Trail Performance For context, my shoe size is a UK 5 (EU 37, US 7). I tested the Wolf Tooth Ripsaw pedals exclusively using my go-to shoes; the FiveTen Freerider Pro. With that combo, almost all pins see some action on the sole of the shoe, with only the more rearward inboard pin left out. Riders with smaller feet may want to consider a pedal with a narrower platform (like the Crankbrothers Stamp 1 Small). I get on well with them myself, but there's no hard and fast rule when it comes to pedal size. The convex profile is clearly felt underfoot, with a raised bump discernible. The foot-pedal connection was a bit of a mixed bag, and I have some contrasting feelings about the Ripsaw. While the connection doesn't feel the most secure (I'm more accustomed to concave pedals), I actually had no issues with the connection over 3 days riding the Orbea Rise LT on dry, loose, chunky terrain. I got none of that frustrating foot creep - you know, when your foot edges forward on the pedal, millimeter by millimeter - and no discernible discomfort or 'clawing' sensation while riding the rough tracks. Versus the OneUp Components Aluminum pedal, the Ripsaw would be my preference. That's largely because the Ripsaw lacks the massive inboard bearing bump that you can see on the OneUp pedal. My foot has a tendency to get hung up on that bump at the inboard edge, compromising the connection. Credit: Jérémie Reuiller I can't fault the design for the comfort and level of grip it provides while descending, but I'm not a fan of the feel while climbing. My foot has a tendency to rock front to back while climbing on the Ripsaw pedal, pivoting about the central raised portion. Slight differences in how much my ankle is flexed throughout the pedal stroke can mean that I'm either pushing against the rearward half of the pedal, or the front half of the pedal. The raised central portion is most noticeable while climbing, possibly because one has fewer, and less exciting, distractions. I'm very aware of it, and I found it would bother me on longer rides, with slight discomfort developing under the ball of my foot. Given that I found the profile less than ideal while climbing, the Wolf Tooth Ripsaw pedals are quite a long way off the top of my list of best flat pedals. They won't be replacing my go-to Crankbrothers Stamp 1 pedals any time soon - a concave, composite option that is notably more affordable at $59.99 USD. Durability I put in around ten rides on these pedals, on various enduro bikes and eMTBs, around half of which were in wet, muddy conditions. After seven rides, one of them developed a small, but nevertheless detectable, amount of play between the axle and body. It is less than 1mm, and I can't feel it while riding. A few rides later, the other pedal developed this play, too. I opened up one of the pedals to inspect the internals. There's little evidence of grime getting past the seal, but a rusted outer race on one of the cartridge bearings demonstrates water ingress. The bearings are still spinning very smoothly, however. The bushing appears to be seated inside its carrier as intended. I reassembled the pedal, tightening the retainer nut onto the end of the axle, and the cartridge into the pedal body to the specified torque values. Checking for play once more, I can confirm it is still present. On this issue, Wolf Tooth have stated that they are yet to experience any such issues with the Ripsaw pedals. We are told that if a customer were to experience this issue, Wolf Tooth Components would either issue them with a replacement set of pedals under warranty, or supply a refresh kit free of charge. Pros + Lightweight + Five-year warranty + Thin profile offers good ground clearance + Good grip while descending Cons - Very expensive - Convex profile can become distracting and uncomfortable on long climbs - Could be too narrow for riders with feet larger than UK 7 (US 9.5) Pinkbike's Take The Wolf Tooth Ripsaw flat pedals are a thin, relatively lightweight option for gram-counting riders that are concerned with ground clearance. The pronounced convex profile offers uncompromised grip for a consistent foot-pedal connection while descending, but the raised central portion can be distracting and a little uncomfortable during a long climb. At $199.95 USD, they are not inexpensive, and I would have expected a great many more play-free rides out of them for that money. — Jessie-May Morgan Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Pedals Wolf Tooth Ripsaw Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: WRP Zero Drag Hub Internal Kit
author: Matt Beer
article_text: WRP Zero Drag Kit Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/L95XytaF Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:59 Mic Williams is one clever guy, a problem solver with the tools and know-how to improve great products even further. One of his greatest creations is the least visible: the Zero Drag kit for DT Swiss hubs. The Zero Drag kit is a double-clutch system that retrofits into popular DT Swiss hubs to virtually eliminate any rolling friction from the freehub mechanism. In fact, it’s attractive enough for World Cup racers, such as Troy Brosnan, who took the win with one aboard his bike at Mont St. Anne in 2024. Details • Easy installation • Decreases rolling resistance • Fits DT Swiss Star Ratchet and EXP Ratchet hubs • Weight: 9g • Price $379 AUD ($429 for Star Ratchet hubs) / (Approximately $248 - $280 USD) • williamsracingproducts.com Design The Zero Drag kit is made up of a series of springs, washers, and clutch plates to decrease rolling resistance by disengaging the forward-direction clutch. This shift occurs when the rider backpedals slightly, letting the wheel freecoast, where the wheel can spin in the reverse direction while the cassette “floats”. When enough force is put on the chain by the cranks, the forward drive will re-engage. The Zero Drag kit is not intended to reduce pedal kickback; however, it can have a positive effect on chain feedback, as we’ll see later on. It’s also not intended to handle the forces of freestyle riding, where a dedicated freecoaster hub may be beneficial. Another notable point is that the Zero Drag kit may not work as designed on an e-bike. The motor clutch will inhibit the rider from being able to backpedal the chain and disengage the hub clutch. The Zero Drag kit comes in two configurations: one for DT Swiss Star Ratchet hubs and another for the newer Ratchet EXP-style. Both DT hubs have the option of a 36 or 54-tooth clutch, however, the Zero Drag kit only comes in a 36-tooth option at this time. Despite the Zero Drag kit having a greater number of parts than the EXP clutch internals, it's actually lighter than the stock internals, but only by a marginal amount (9g vs 11g). The Zero Drag kit fits DT Swiss Star Ratchet and Ratchet EXP-style hubs. Clear and concise instructions for the tiny bits. The clutch plates on the left faces inward against a matching gear, which is fixed in the hub shell. These are the stock EXP Ratchet internal pieces. And the Zero Drag components. Installation This may be the component with the highest ease-of-installation-to-performance ratio. First, the only tools needed are the necessary wrenches to remove your rear wheel. Next, you can pull the freehub body off with the cassette still in place and remove the existing clutch and spring (Star Ratchet internals have two of each). WRP has also loaded all of the parts of the clutch onto a dummy axle, which allows you to transfer them in the proper order. Simply, remove the backing plug and while keeping tension on the Zero Drag components, use the freehub body to push the assembly onto the hub axle. The hub end cap should pop into place when all of the parts are correctly aligned. WRP does state that excess amounts of grease on the clutch plates can prevent the system from engaging properly. I exclusively rode this on my personal enduro bike, but there are plenty of applications for this kit, from downhill racing to road cycling. The hub of choice was a DT Swiss 240 Ratchet EXP. The gear cluster arrives ready to install on this dummy axle. An extra O-ring and clutch plate are pictured. The dummy axle fits into the hub axle and allows all of the pieces to slide on in order. Price Depending on which hub you choose to start with, there are two price points. Each kit achieves the same benefits. If you have an Ratchet EXP-style hub, the Zero Drag kit will cost $379 AUD, whereas the WRP internals for the more common Star Ratchet hubs are an extra $50 ($429 AUD). Considering that’s about the same price as a stock DT Swiss 350 (in Australian coin), free speed is a hefty investment. Like WRP’s other components, the Zero Drag Kit is for the savvy tinkerer who loves to add niche aftermarket components (and can afford it), squeezing out the very last drops of performance from their bike. Performance “Zero drag” may be in quotes on the product page of this component because some amount of friction will be there. That force only comes from the hub bearings so the wheel rolls incredibly freely and silently. There’s something about a stealthy hub that makes you think you're riding smoother than you probably are, but you know what? I’ll take it. As for reliability, the Zero Drag kit has withstood riding through sloppy winter conditions and enduro racing in the dust. The system never had any hesitancy to start freecoasting or re-engage from that state. On one or two rare occasions, I did experience the odd “pop” from the clutch plates. This never affected performance, however, it did set off an alarm bell in my head. WRP explained that this misfire can happen when the tips of the opposing teeth happen to line up exactly and are then placed under load. Subsequently, they will slip ever so slightly into the next phase with an audible clunk, catching without issue. This is not particular to the WRP Zero Drag kit, but more the DT clutch design itself. Rolling Resistance Timed Test To find out the claimed benefits of the WRP Zero Drag kit over the stock DT Swiss Ratchet EXP clutch, we set up a simple test to measure how long it took a wheel to come to rest from 20mph. An e-bike was used to measure the wheel speed. The wheel was spun above 20mph, the timer started when the wheel reached 20mph and then stopped when the wheel came to rest. The same wheel was used for all ten trials and torqued to 12Nm each time it was installed. The brake pads were also removed to eliminate any possible friction. Zero Drag Kit Trials • 1:41.34 • 1:41.50 • 1:42.02 • 1:42.12 • 1:43.65 • Average: 1:42.13 DT Swiss Ratchet EXP Trials • 0:55.10 • 0:55.98 • 0:56.14 • 0:56.67 • 0:57.03 • Average: 0:56.18 In the end, the WRP Zero Drag kit consistently proved to roll for a much longer time, averaging a time of 1:42.13 - nearly twice that of the stock hub internals. WRP on the left, DT on the right (the screenshots are not intended to focus on chain movement). On Trail Comparison On the trail, it’s tough to perceive the exact gain in efficiency, but doing back-to-back laps with and without the kit, there is a difference in chain feedback. Although that's not the intention of the system, it was worth a deeper look. What you can distinctly tell is when the clutch is disengaged, since it rolls silently. This happens nearly instantly when you pass over the first bump on the trail - there’s no need to initiate a backpedal, at least on the Santa Cruz Nomad we used to review the Zero Drag. While it’s nearly impossible for even the most dedicated and clever riders to pinpoint pedal kickback, I can say that I do feel that the system calms down chain feedback. It’s not totally eliminated, but there’s less chaos happening at your feet. Pros + Less resistance than stock DT Swiss internals + Fast and simple install + Silent on the trail Cons - Speed isn’t free - it’s an expensive upgrade - Only 36-tooth clutches available at this time Pinkbike's Take Cyclists are always looking for incremental gains in efficiency, particularly racers, and especially those focused on uphill performance. After our time on the WRP Zero Drag kit, it's clear that it rolls faster than the stock DT Swiss Ratchet EXP-style clutch internals, which are already highly acclaimed. The workmanship of the tiny parts inside the kit have held up when smashing the pedals in an enduro race and bursting up technical climbs, too. Although it's not totally clear how much the Zero Drag kit mitigates chain feedback (which it's not intended to do), it does "free" up the chain tension to a noticeable degree. With that said, I'd steer towards another source to damp the chain if that's what you're after. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Hubs Wrp Wrp Zero Drag Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: WTB's Revised Tough Casing Tires
author: Matt Beer
article_text: WTB is increasing the performance of their enduro tires by making them more durable and stickier. These changes will be available in four of their most aggressive tread patterns: the Judge, Trail Boss, Verdict, and Vigilante. The new SG1 layup adds extra support and security to the top of the casing and nearer to the bead. A rebalanced Tri-Tec rubber compound is said to work better in wet conditions while also lasting longer. All four tread patterns are available in 29 or 27 .5” diameters, excluding the Vigilante (29” only), with black sidewalls only. SG1 Tough Casing Tire Details • Improved durability with new SG1 casing • Reformulated Tri-Tec compound • Available in High Grip or Fast Rolling compounds • Verdict 29x2.5" weight: 1525g • Vigilante 29x2.5" weight: 1653g • Judge 27.5x2.4" weight: 1360g • Price: $89.95 / €74.50 / £80 (all Tough casing variants) • wtb.com All new SG1 Tough casing tires will retail for $89.95 USD, which is less expensive than most competitors’ gravity options, but there is a catch - there is an added weight penalty for the durability. WTB Updated Tires https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/WmCgmHu8 Copied Live 00:00 03:05 03:05 Specifications WTB has some of the least flashy sidewall hot patches and simplest tire tech naming schemes in the game. They have two compounds, "High Grip" or "Fast Rolling," and two casings, "Light" or "Tough". WTB states that their "acceptable tolerance for weight variation is 10%." Claimed weight: Vigilante 29” – 1575g Judge 27.5” – 1455g Verdict 29” – 1390g. Features SG1 casing The SG1 technology is an addition to the “Tough” casing options. The 60 TPI carcass is still tubeless-ready and comes folding, plastic-free packaging. There are two main changes, the extra layering under the tread in the center of the tire and a taller Apex shield around the bead which is now 15mm versus 5mm. With all of those robust additions to the SG1 line of tires, there is a considerable increase in weight. The new SG1 Vigilante 29" I received had a weight difference of 383g compared to the older model in the same casing. Oof. The new Verdict wasn’t too far off of that delta either, while the 27.5" Judge saw an increase of 210g. Tri-Tec rubber compound By the name, you can guess where this is going. Both the High Grip and Fast Rolling compounds use three different rubbers of varying amounts. The softest is on the side knob, then there’s a slightly more durable one in the center, and those are backed up by a firmer base. Since WTB changed the casing name, I’m surprised they didn’t change the compound name too because although it’s still made of three rubbers, the formula is different. They say they’ve tweaked the natural versus synthetic rubber ratio and rebalanced the carbon and other reinforcing agents to give more grip in wet conditions and somehow last longer than before. Setup Installation was straightforward for all three tires on a multitude of rims. They had no trouble seating with a regular pump and never leaked any sealant through the sidewall. Primarily, these tires were tested under the weight of an e-bike, but I also put them on a regular enduro bike to see how much that added weight affected the perceived efficiency and suppleness. I ran the Vigilante and Verdict on the front between 21 and 23 psi, and the Judge on the back wheel at 25 to 27 psi. The most time was spent on the Verdict doing back-to-back comparisons between the old and new versions. Ride Impressions Verdict The open tread pattern of the Verdict makes it a great candidate for loose and muddy conditions. The rows of square knobs shed mud well. There is a large gap between the center and shoulder lugs. Each square knob has a small sipe, or relief, to allow it to conform on harder surfaces. Although the Verdict has excellent bite in soft conditions, especially when you get the side knobs to bite into off cambers, committing to leaning it over on hard pack services can be a little bit more daunting. It’s also not the best tread design in terms of braking performance. Due to the profile, the center knob stand taller than the shoulder knobs which may provide less contact when braking in a straight line. They are also more square rather than rectangular so there’s less to dig in. I’d advise you to keep this one in the front and pair it with a Judge out back. Judge Speaking of that tire, the Judge holds a similar appearance to other popular rear specific tires on the market. It has alternating square and rectangular blocks pairs down the center and very supportive side knobs that are spaced closer to the center row than the Verdict. I only had the chance to try the Judge on the rear. Braking performance is stellar on just about every surface, whether you are upright or leaned over. Vigilante As for the Vigilante, it’s a great all-arounder that’s very predictable in mixed terrain. Compared to the Verdict, it doesn’t have quite the same bite in deep mud or soft conditions, but it’s a little bit less squirmy on hardback surfaces. That also means it’s more consistent when leaning into turns, particularly hard packed berms. Support, grip, and durability WTB has made a huge leap forward with the performance of their gravity oriented tires. There’s ample support even under the forces of an e-bike, but the casing is also decently active for use with enduro bikes. I’d say they’re closest in comparison to the feel of a Schwalbe Super Gravity casing. They’re not quite as supple as a Maxxis DH casing, though. It’s not so much the casing stiffness that’s an issue, but the overall weight of the tire is considerably heavier than before. If you’re pedaling to the top of the hill, you’re going to feel that extra weight penalty very quickly. The new SG1 casing and reformulated Tri-Tec rubber makes these tires far superior to the old, especially on wet rocks and roots. In terms of reliability, I never experience any punctures or burping despite my best efforts with the heftier e-bike. I can’t speak too much to the long-term durability of the rubber just yet because I’ve only had them for a few weeks, and while doing back-to-back comparisons with the old tires the trail conditions have been on the soft side. Pros + Improved grip in wet conditions + Extra shielding doesn't impact suppleness + Versatile tread patterns + Lower price than much of the competition Cons - Some of the heaviest enduro tires out there - Not as compliant as some competitors' gravity casings (which are lighter) - Vigilante is not available in 27.5" diameter Pinkbike's Take The revisions that WTB made to their new tough line of tires will definitely be appreciated by eMTB and gravity riders who demand a resilient tire. The improvements to the rubber compound are welcomed, especially when trail conditions get wet. Whether the large weight penalty will be worth swallowing is another question though. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires WTB Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: XFusion's New Vengeance 38 Fork
author: Matt Beer
article_text: X-Fusion Vengeance Fork Review.mp4 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/tjbOvEyv Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 06:41 The push for longer, lower, slacker and heavier bikes has put more stress on the front end, leading to suspension manufacturers redesigning fork chassis. The best course of action to fight flexing forks has been stouter crowns and larger diameter stanchions. XFusion is the latest amongst the major suspension manufacturers to bring a stiffer, 38 mm stanchion fork to the table. The Vengeance 38 aims to compete against the Fox 38 Factory and RockShox Zeb Ultimate, which have had a stronghold in that realm since their introductions. The Vengeance is built with simplicity in mind but doesn't fall short of the latest selling features. The Finecut HLR damper is an easy-to-understand, three-way adjustable damper. On the spring side, the air spring is straightforward to tune and change the travel between 160, 170, or 180 mm. Vengeance 38 Details • Wheel size: 29 inch • 38 mm stanchions • 160, 170, 180 mm internally adjustable travel • Finecut HLR damper • Air spring w/volume spacer tuning • Tapered steer tube • 15 x 110 mm axle spacing • Axle to crown: 596 mm (180mm travel) • 44 or 51 mm offset • Rotor Size: 180-220 mm • Colors: blue, sand (black OE) • Weight: 2464 g (uncut steer tube) • Price: $869 USD • More info: xfusionshox.com At $869 USD, the fork hits a lower price point than the top-tier models from Fox and RockShox. The cost savings does come with extra weight. The Vengeance is roughly 100 g heavier than those competitors mentioned above, yet still respectable at 2,464 g. Chassis Sticking to the simple theme, the Vengeance only takes one move to tighten the axle - there’s no pinch bolt on the lowers to clamp the axle in place. The axle spacing is 15x110 mm Boost. The lower legs are designed around 29” wheels, although I'm sure some freeriders will fit a smaller wheel. Our 180 mm travel test fork measured in with an axle-to-crown length of 596 mm. And, in case you missed it, the aluminum stanchions measure 38 mm in diameter. Further features of the chassis include Neutra valves, ABS mounts, and a plastic brake hose guide. The Neutra valves release air built up over time to make the fork more sensitive and are actuated by loosening a 2 mm grub screw on the back of the lower casting. Air Spring The air spring on the Vengeance self-adjusts to balance the positive and negative pressure. Volume spacers allow for modifications to the amount of progression delivered by the air spring. Opening the air spring top cap requires a flush or chamferless 28 mm socket. It has a max pressure of 140 psi and ships with 2 volume spacers installed. A maximum of 4 can be installed, which are available separately. Unlike some popular forks on the market that use fixed-length air spring assemblies, the Vengeance 38 is internally adjustable between 160, 170, or 180 mm of travel. To change the travel, you will have to remove the lower legs and relocate the travel positioning spacers to the desired arrangement. No extra parts are needed which is a welcomed convenience. The air spring uses plastic volume spacers to adjust the progression which thread together like RockShox. Internal spacers can be rearranged to set the travel to 160, 170, or 180 mm. Damper The Vengeance uses the XFusion’s latest Finecut HLR damper with three straightforward adjustments hinted at in the name (high-speed, compression, low-speed compression, rebound). This is a closed cartridge system with an IFP (internal floating piston) system to handle the damper fluid displacement. The rebound adjuster is found at the bottom of the right leg and the high and low-speed compression knobs are located up top (gold and blue knobs). Each knob is smooth to turn, aside from the high-speed adjuster, which takes a little more effort, but I wouldn't say it's too stiff. As for how much influence each click has, that differs depending on what aspect of the damping you’re changing. Each click of the high-speed compression is more pronounced than the low-speed. Both offer a decent range of adjustments. The rebound, however, could have a wider range as we’ll discuss in the next section. Price and Weight At $869 USD, the Vengeance aligns with the RockShox Zeb Select RC ($896 USD). Both of those are cheaper than the least expensive aftermarket Fox 38 model - the Performance Elite Series ($1,149 USD w/GripX2 damper). The Vengeance 38 comes in at a weight of 2,464 g. That’s a little more than the premium level Fox 38 Factory (2,381 g) and RockShox Zeb Ultimate (2,324 g), but those forks cost a few hundred dollars more. Service and Warranty All XFusion suspension products include a 2-year warranty against material defects and workmanship. This is only applicable to the original owner and the service intervals found here must be respected. At 25 rides, they recommend removing the lowers to be cleaned and lubricated. Once the 100-ride mark hits, you’ll need to replace the seals. This is something you can do at home with standard tools, but removing the damper calls for a proprietary hex tool. Setup With the fork positioned at 180 mm of travel, I set the sag to 10% of the travel. That equated to a pressure of 96 psi. Depending on the conditions and trails of choice that day, moving up or down a few psi made a difference in ride height and progression. The volume spacers make progression adjustments straightforward and less finicky than a dual-chamber system, as found in EXT and Ohlins long travel forks. I found the amount of progression from the two spacers to be best suited for the 180 mm travel fork. I started with the compression wide open and quickly closed the low speed considerably to achieve more support. In the end, I found that setting the low-speed compression in the middle of the range and increasing the air pressure provided the best support. Two clicks of high-speed compression have a very noticeable effect on the damping. There are eight in total. I’d stick to this dial set 5 clicks from closed. For the rebound, there are far more clicks, but each one isn’t as noticeable. The range could also be wider. Even with the pressure in the high 90’s, I ran the rebound fully open at times. Lighter weight riders who like a fast rebound setting may not be able to achieve that since the air spring will return to full travel slower and at a lower pressure. Settings Rebound: -20-26 (from closed, 26 clicks total) High-speed compression: -4 (8 clicks total) Low-speed compression: -7 (14 clicks total) Pressure: 94-99 PSI Volume spacers: 2 Performance I'm of two minds about the Vengeance 38. In some instances, it’s smooth and comfortable. At other points on the trail, there are a couple of quirks. The air spring rides deep in the travel but had a less refined feel. By that, I mean it was more reluctant to break into the start of the travel, would then fall through the middle, and then ramp up as expected at the end. That translated to the fork feeling plush over mid-size bumps when the front wheel was weighted and on the ground. For larger, repeated drops and steps down chunky trails though, that extra breakaway force followed by the diving sensation through the mid-stroke could change the pitch of the bike undesirably. Closing in the low-speed compression helped to a degree, but then added more resistance against breaking the fork into the travel smoothly. Adding high-speed compression had a considerable effect on the support, again helping this sensation, but could be overbearing when halfway closed. Three spacers were beneficial at holding the fork up higher in the middle of the travel but proved to be too progressive - to the point where I wasn't achieving full travel. Although it's a much more complicated and expensive air spring design, this is where a dual-chamber would allow the rider to fine tune the mid-stroke support. In addition, when the rebound was adjusted to my preferred settings, the fork had a tendency to top out occasionally. This occurred when popping off the ground for small gaps and rhythms, but not necessarily on successive bumps. Closing the rebound didn't entirely remedy that issue. Pros + Deeper ride height provides comfortable tracking. + User-friendly adjustments at a decent price. Cons - Fast rebound setting causes top-out. - Air spring doesn't offer the most mid-stroke support. Pinkbike's Take The Vengeance 38 fork is a less expensive alternative in the enduro and freeride segment that is comfortable, but it isn’t without a few hiccups - the nature of the progression is less intuitive than those found on comparable 38mm stanchion forks. The damping range is also an interesting one. One click of high-speed compression makes a considerable difference, but the low-speed compression and rebound have a less pronounced change. My preferred rebound settings also caused the fork to top-out. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews X Fusion Shox X Fusion Vegeance Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: The Yeti SB165 Is Still a Pedalable Park Bike
author: Mike Kazimer
tags: reviews-and-tech, videos, reviews, enduro-bikes, yeti, yeti-sb165
article_text: REVIEW Yeti SB165 WORDS: Mike Kazimer ACTION PHOTOS: Eric Mickelson The SB165 is the longest travel option in Yeti's lineup (excluding the protoype DH bike that Richie Rude has been racing on this season... fingers crossed that makes it to production someday). There's no reason it couldn't be used in an enduro race, although at its core it's really more of a freeride / big mountain machine, a bike with more of a focus on navigating gnarly terrain than going as fast as possible between the tape. As the name indicates, the SB165 has 165mm of rear travel, which is paired to a 170mm fork. It'll accept up to a 190mm fork, and its approved for use with a dual crown fork, just as long as the axle to crown measurement is at or below 607mm. Yeti SB165 Details • Wheel size: 29" front / 27.5" rear • Carbon frame • Travel: 165mm / 170mm fork • 63.5º head angle • 76.9º seat tube angle • 437mm chainstays (L) • Weight: 34.6 lb / 15.7 kg (L) • Price: $9,200 USD • yeticycles.com There are four different complete models, with prices starting at $6,300 USD for the C2, and going up to $9,200 for the T3 version shown here. Highlights of the T3 build include a Fox 38 Factory for with a Grip2 damper (the bike was launched before Grip X2 came out), a DHX2 coil shock, SRAM Code RSC brakes, an X0 T-type wireless drivetrain, and DT Swiss EX1700 aluminum wheels. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take When it comes to the overall suspension performance, the SB165 does a great job of predictably handling impacts of all sizes. The small chatter is nicely muted, and there's plenty of ramp up for those bigger hits. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details Over the last few years an increasing number of brands have implemented various levels of adjustment into their frames, everything from a simple flip chip for different rear wheel sizes, to frames that allow almost every geometry figure and the amount of shock progression to be adjusted. You won't find any of that with the SB165 – other than the ability to run a longer travel fork, which would slacken the head angle even further, there aren't any adjustments to been seen. There's not any frame storage either, for that matter. I actually don't mind the what-you-see-is-what-you-get approach – it's kind of nice being able to hop and a bike and not have those "Would this be better in X mode" thoughts rattling around your head. That said, it's still worth noting that the constant tinkerers out there may find themselves wishing for more chips to flip. Yet offers the SB165 in two flavors of carbon – Turq and C. It's similar to what Santa Cruz does with their CC and C frames, where one is lighter and more expensive, and the other is slightly heavier and slightly less expensive. The concept of having two different layups for bikes other than full-on XC race rigs is still a little strange to me – I'd be curious what the sales split ends up being between the two options. Other details included tube-in-tube internal routing (even for a cable actuated drivetrain), a threaded bottom bracket, generous chainslap and down tube protection, plus plenty of room for a water bottle inside the front triangle. The 165 was designed with coil shocks in mind. The SB165's Switch Infinity system. Geometry The SB165's geometry isn't all that different from the previous model, with the exception of that 29” front wheel. The head angle still sits at 63.5-degrees, although now that's with a 170mm fork vs. the 180mm fork on the prior model, and reach numbers range from 430mm for a size small and go up to 505mm for a size extra-large. The chainstay length varies depending on the frame size, but there's only a six millimeter difference from the smallest to largest size – that's closer to the size difference you'd expect to see between each size in order to think about claiming they're size specific. No matter what, the chainstays are on the shorter end of the spectrum. The head tube length is on the shorter side too, which means the stack heights aren't all that high, although Yeti does spec' the bike with a 35mm rise bar to keep the front end from feeling too low. Suspension Design The SB165 uses Yeti's Switch Infinity suspension layout, which uses a translating pivot that moves upwards in the beginning of the travel, and then downwards deeper in the travel (that's what the two Kashima-coated rails above the bottom bracket are doing). The SB165 has a 22% leverage rate change, with a linearly progressive change for the first two-thirds of the travel, and then a more pronounced ramp up for the last third in order to increase the bottom out resitance for bigger hits. That leverage rate change should allow the bike to work well with both air and coil shocks. A grease fitting is in place to help keep things moving smoothly, and Yeti recommends a lubrication interval of every 40 hours. Specifications Price $9200 Travel 165mm Rear Shock Fox DHX2 Factory Fork Fox 38 Float Factory Grip2 Headset Cane Creek 40 interated Crankarms SRAM X0 Transmission 30T 170mm Chainguide MRP AMG SL bash guide Rear Derailleur SRAM X0 Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM X0 Eagle Transmission Flattop Shifter Pods SRAM AXS pod controller Handlebar Yeti carbon 35 x 800 Stem Burgtec Eduro 35 x 50mm Grips ODI Elite Pro Brakes SRAM Code RSC Wheelset DT Swiss EX1700 30mm Tires Maxxis Assegai EXO+ / Minion DHR II DD Seat WTB Silverado Seatpost Fox Transfer post Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE YETI SB165 Test Bike Setup I only made a few small changes to the SB165 to get it to better suit my needs. The first was swapping the 50mm stem for a 40mm one. That's mainly down to personal preference, but for my 5'11” height and overall dimensions I'm typically more comfortable with the handling of a bike with a 40mm stem vs. something longer (or shorter), at least if we're talking about enduro / freeride bikes. With that swap made, I trimmed the bars to 770mm and was ready to roll. I've spent a lot of time on the Fox 38 Grip 2, so getting that setup only took a matter of minutes – I ran 82 psi, and the rebound and compression set to my personal preference. For the DHX2 I ran a 450 lb spring, which put me just under 30% sag. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Yeti SB165 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/3zjEwWvW Copied Live 00:00 06:22 06:22 Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington, and the surrounding area over the course of the spring and summer, with conditions tending to be on the drier and sunnier side of things, although there were more than a few soggy rides. The loop above would be a good way to get acquainted with the 165's handling before venturing off in search of even steeper, rougher tracks. Climbing At its core, the SB165 is a bike that's best suited for grinding up a logging road and then bombing down something steep and rowdy. Many of my rides on it involved exactly that – I'd flip the climb switch on the DHX2 to firm things up and then comfortably cruise to the top of whatever exciting trail was on the menu that day. The climb switch is easy to reach, and since it doesn't fully lock out the shock it's usable even on rougher climbs, since the shock can still move enough to provide traction. The movement that occurs when the shock is open has to do more with rider weight shifts and the sensitivity of the coil shock more than anything else – it stays fairly calm if you're good a staying seated and spinning circles, but there is more movement when you start to muscle it around. The seat angle is steep without being extreme, which means that the climbing positions remains comfortable even when you're not trying to scale a vertical wall. I am glad Yeti spec'd a higher rise bar and left enough room for a decent stack of spacers under the stem – the 107mm head tube length is a little stubby, and with the stem slammed and lower rise bars the fit of the bike wouldn't have been as comfortable. On more technical singletrack climbs, the SB165 does feel like a big bike, but it's not so slack and sprawling that it feels totally out of its element during those mellower moments. The short chainstays help with maneuverability, at least to a certain extent, but the also make it a bit harder to keep the front end down on really steep ascents. Once your weight gets back past a certain point the wheel wants to lift, and it takes more body movement to maintain balance compared to a bike with longer chainstays and a steeper seat tube angle. Compared to other bikes in this category, the SB165 isn't as energetic as the Ibis HD6 or the Propain Tyee, but it has more support for those out-of-the-saddle efforts than the Rocky Mountain Slayer. On really steep climbs I prefer the geometry of the Raaw Madonna, but for all-round usage the SB165 gets the nod. Descending The SB165 falls into the no-fuss category when it comes time to descend. It's one of those bikes that doesn't ask that much from its rider; you don't need to try to break the sound barrier in order to have a good time. Of course, it does feel best blasting down steep trails, but it doesn't feel overly lethargic when things mellow out a little bit. The slack head angle and overall wheelbase keep it stable at the higher speeds encountered in a bike park or similar setting, although the shorter chainstays prevent it from having the same unflappable level of calm that a bike like the Raaw Madonna possesses. I'll get into the handling differences between those two specific bikes in a bit, but the main takeaway is that it's easier to let the back end break traction on the SB165 compared to the Raaw. The SB165 doesn't feel quite as balanced front to back as the Madonna, or Commencal's Meta V5 for that matter. Yeti says that the short rear end was chosen to complement the smaller rear wheel, but more and more I'm convinced that going slighly longer with the chainstays on a mixed wheel bike is the way to go. That said, I try not to be too dogmatic with my geometry ideals – my job would be pretty boring if all bikes ended up with the exact same geometry. For some riders, the ease with which the SB165 can slap through a corner or loft off the lip of a jump will be strong selling points, and I get it. After all, if this is a freeride bike then it might as well make those freeride flicks nice and easy, right? (Just kidding - I'm actually very glad the trend of randomly sliding out the rear wheel on straight sections of trail to get 'style' points seems to have died off). The short back end can also be a benefit when navigating through tight, awkward turns, since there's less bike behind you to worry about. When it comes to the overall suspension performance, the SB165 does a great job of predictably handling impacts of all sizes. The small chatter is nicely muted, and there's plenty of ramp up for those bigger hits. I was happy with the coil shock, although I do wonder how the SB165 would feel with a larger volume air shock – I feel like that would help it lean into its bike park / big jump tendencies. Yeti SB165 Raaw Madonna V3 How Does It Compare? As that old, strange saying goes, “There's more than one way to skin a cat,” and putting the SB165 up against the Raaw Madonna V3 is a perfect example. Both bikes are focused on descending, and both have a 170mm fork with 160+ millimeters of rear travel, but there are some significant geometry differences. The front end height is a big one – the stack on the Madonna is 664mm vs 630mm on the SB165. That's due to the Madonna's tall head tube, and its low bottom bracket. The Madonna also has a longer wheelbase, at 1284mm vs the Yeti 1266 thanks to its 450mm chainstay length. Those differences are very clear on the trail – cornering on the Madonna reminds me of carving on skis – it's more about tipping the bike as one unit. It felt like that I was steering more with my feet on the Madonna, and with my hips on the the SB165. I really enjoy the calm nature of the Madonna, but that may dull the edges a bit to much for some riders. The SB165 is more eager to get off the ground, and its nature is a little more drifty / slappy, compared to planted and composed. As for price, the Madonna's aluminum frame and shock is $3,258 USD, and the carbon Yeti goes for $4,800. The Yeti frame is going to be lighter, but whether that's worth the extra $1,600 is between you and your wallet. Which Model is the Best Value? Realistically, if you're on a budget, Yeti isn't the best company to gravitate towards. $9,200 is a lot of money for a freeride bike, and you're not even getting carbon wheels for that price. A Nomad with carbon wheels and a similar / better spec is almost the same price, and Santa Cruz isn't exactly known for focusing on affordability. That said, if I had to choose, I'd probably go with the $6,300 C2 model, and consider upgrading the fork damper once my savings account recovered from that sizeable hit. Or, there's also the option of going with the previous version, the full 27.5” SB165. Those bikes are on sale at a significant discount – you can save thousands of dollars if you're not fussed by the smaller front wheel. Technical Report I mentioned most of the quibbles I had with the build kit in the First Ride article on the SB165, but I'll briefly go over them again here. Centerline rotors: Running a 220mm front rotor helps maximize the power available, but SRAM's newer, thicker HS2 rotors would have been a better choice – those provide a noticeable bump up in power and consistency. Maven brakes wouldn't have been out of place either, but those weren't available when this bike was launched. Silverado saddle: There's a new WTB Silverado on the market, but the one on the SB165 isn't it. The dimension almost look comical, especially compared to the shorter and slightly wider options that are becoming more common. I swapped it out for a Nukeproof Horizon saddle (RIP) and was much happier. Tire spec: The SB165 was initially spec'd with an EXO+ tire front and rear. That's since been updated, and there's now a DoubleDown casing rear tire. I'm a fan of that setup for most conditions here in the Pacific Northwest, and riders that want the most flat protection can always swap out the front for something beefier. Livability The SB165 is an easy bike to keep up and running, as long as you don't totally neglect that Switch Infinity system. All of the hardware has remained secure, and there haven't been any mystery creaks to chase down. The cable routing is well thought out, and I didn't have any excessive rattling to deal with. Overall, a pretty pleasant bike to live with – it's the mountain bike equivalent to a roommate that cleans up after themselves almost all of the time and doesn't hold multi-day ragers, at least not without asking first. Pros + Easy handling, especially when it comes to berms and jumps + Smooth, predictable suspension performance + More versatile than numbers might suggest Cons - Some of the spec isn't the latest & greatest - Expensive, even compared to direct competition - No geometry adjustments or frame storage Pinkbike's Take Yeti tout the SB165 as being a do-it-all big bike, and that's exactly what it is. For riders that want to ride lifts and slash berms one day and then go for a big pedal to access some steep and spicy lines the next, it certainly fits the bill. It's not going to be a bike for everyone – the price is one factor, and riders that need maximum adjustability, or who prefer longer chainstays will want to look elsewhere, but there's certainly room in the world for a long travel machine that doesn't require reaching ridiculously high speeds to come alive — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Videos Reviews Enduro Bikes Yeti Yeti Sb165 Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Yoshimura's New Chilao SS Flat Pedals
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Yoshimura dipped their toes into the mountain bike market a couple years ago with their unique two-in-one ENDH stem and the Chilao pedals. They’re back with a new CNC’d component, but instead of expanding the line they’ve refined their flat pedals with the Chilao SS. On the outside, the pedal is nearly identical to the original Chilaos. Inside though, there’s much more at play - or better yet, no play. “SS” stands for “static spring,” which is the critical part of the new patent-pending internal bearing preload system. The spring eliminates any lateral play between the pedal body and the spindle, no matter how worn out the spinning parts become. Yoshimura Chilao SS Details • 6061-T6 aluminum machined in USA • Patented static spring preloader • Triple outboard bearings • 10 removable, 4mm tall pins each side • Colors: YoshiKote, pewter, black and Ice • Sizes: 110mm L x 107.25 W - LG (tested), 100mm L x 95.5 W - SM • Thickness: 14mm • Weight: 364g (actual) • Price: $229, ($219 SM) USD • yoshimuracycling.com The Chilao SS will still be available in two platform sizes and four colors. Price-wise, and like most products these days, the cost has increased from $200 to $229 (size large), but Yoshimura believes that pedals shouldn’t be a disposable item. In fact, they’re backed by a limited lifetime warranty. The cutaways show the new Chilao SS on top with its patented static spring preload system, V-Ring dust seal and triple cartridge bearing system. Details Hopping back to the internals, the static spring is really the bread and butter of the pedal now. The majority of flat pedals feel great until they break in. Then it’s a constant battle to keep them spinning just enough so they don’t bind, but also don’t resonate when you give them a kick. The SS aims to solve that issue permanently by keeping the bearings under a predefined load which is independent of the spindle lock nut torque. This design also minimizes side load on the triple cartridge bearings, single bushing, and seals if the pedal body is impacted. Furthermore, the Chilao SS dons a new V-Ring seal that sits nicely in a machined seat, making it fool-proof to remove and reinstall without incident. The body and end caps are machined from 6061-T6 while the pins are 7075-T6. A proprietary alloy steel is used for the spindle itself and then cerakoted for ultra-longevity. All of those parts are machined, laser etched, and anodized in Chino, California, USA. Dimensions Like the original Chilao, the SS comes in the same two platform sizes; (110mm L x 107.25 W) for the large and (100mm L x 95.5 W) for the small. They use 10 pins per side whereas the small only holds 7. All M4 pins are the same length at 8mm and machined with a 3mm allen key head which stand a much better chance of staying square, versus a tiny 2mm, dirt-caked grub screw. Both pedals measure 14mm thick at the leading and trailing edges. Additionally, that new spindle lends to a 3mm wider pedal Q-factor. Price and Weight With all of the clever workings tucked away, the Chilao SS’s pedal body is the nearly same shape and size, however, Yoshimura whittled away an extra 18g over the original pedal. That brings the size large Chilao SS to an even 360g per set, making them second lightest against the rest in our flat pedal group test last spring , only outdone by 5DEV’s Trail/Enduro. As for the price, it’s not easy on the wallet. All of those smart intervals may mean extra longevity, but it comes at a steep price of $229 USD ($219 USD - size small platform).
