Those numbers place them close to the top of the price range from that test, only to be outdone by the Canadian-made, North Shore Billet Daemons. Included in that price are 5 replacement pins and the limited lifetime warranty that excludes wear items, such as bearings or pins. Replacement bearing kits go for $15 USD. Performance Another unique tidbit on the Chilao design is the angled front row of pedal pins for a claimed enhanced grip. To allow for a thin pedal body underfoot, Yoshimura has opted to cut down that middle bridge as much as possible, but leave the dreaded axle bulge on the inner and outer area over the spindle. Yoshimura says that this cradles your foot, but I often found that to be a deterrent to my foot staying in place if my foot needed to be repositioned on the trail, particularly in wet conditions. I tend to ride slightly duck footed and with my feet towards the outer edge of the pedal. When your foot is centered perfectly, there's an admirable grip with a thin, controlled feel. After heaps of time on various flat pedals, I’ll take a taller platform overall provided there is still decent concavity. It’s worth mentioning that the grip did seem to be a smidge more than the original Chilao. That could be down to the extended Q-factor on the SS, the reworked pins, or a combination of the two. Durability The Chilaos showed up two months ago which isn’t enough time to judge their seemingly bulletproof design, but so far, they still feel the same as day one. Those slick internal workings have lept the grease looking fresh and the bearings spinning smoothly. I did give one corner a good blow that resulted in a snapped pedal pin, but thankfully, as designed, that sheared off cleanly. The broken piece wound out cleanly and the new one installed without a hitch. Comparison To decipher what pedals are truly grippy, I’ll often reach for less-tacky shoes. The two main factors that bring together a solid pedal are threaded pins and true concavity. Along with the Yoshimura Chilao SSs, I’ve spent a solid amount of time on the Wolf Tooth Waveform pedal bodies. Each one offers a decent amount of concavity in the X and Y axis. Similarities could be drawn between the shapes of each, but I don’t believe there’s any copied homework going on here. There’s only so many ways to cut out a block of aluminum. Hands down, the Chilao SS offers more grip with their threaded pins, but only when your foot is perfectly centered. It comes down to that raised area around the inner and outer axle port on the platform and that’s marginally taller on the Chilaos in respect to the overall concavity. Pros + The ideal pins; threaded for max grip, snap cleanly, and are removable by a sizeable allen key from the protected side + Static spring retained low-friction, but not overly free spinning pedals, throughout test period Cons - Raised outer edge on end cap requires perfect foot placement to achieve maximum traction - Quality comes at a higher price Pinkbike's Take Although it's still early to confidently evaluate the long-term durability of Yoshimura's novel Static Spring system on the new Chilao SS, they've evolved an already sound pedal. The grip and control is spot on when your foot is in that ideal groove, but that darn axle bump means there are other pedals out there with a uniform concavity that outshine the Chilao SS by a hair. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Pedals Yoshimura Yoshimura Chilao Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: YT Decoy SN MX Core 4 CF – Less Power, More Handling
author: Ralf Hauser
tags: emtb, reviews-and-tech, reviews, enduro-bikes, yt-industries, yt-decoy-sn
article_text: REVIEW YT Decoy SN MX Core 4 CF WORDS: Ralf Hauser PHOTOS: Ale Di Lullo, Karin Stifter With 170mm of travel in the front, 160mm in the rear, and Fazua's Ride 60 motor system, the Decoy SN – or Supernatural – is expanding YT's Decoy family. The "MX" in the model name reveals that the Decoy SN runs on a mixed wheel setup, with a 29" front and 27.5" rear, without the option for changing the rear wheel to another size. Looking at YT's current lineup, the Decoy SN is aimed at the enduro category and therefore comes closest to the character of the non-motorized Capra, although in terms of geometry and features, the Decoy SN is clearly an evolution of that model. Form language is an important part of the package, and YT's Decoy SN is not only nice to look at but does an excellent job of concealing that it's an e-bike. From the drive side, you have to look twice to spot any sign of the Fazua Ride 60 motor. The motor’s positioning, especially the way it extends into the downtube, is a smart solution and has been cleanly integrated with YT's skid plate seamlessly connecting to the down tube bash guard. Decoy SN MX Core 4 CF Details • Wheel size: 29"/27.5" Mixed • Rear wheel travel: 160mm • Ultra Modulus carbon frame • 64.2°/63.9° head angle • Chainstay length: 442mm/443mm • Core 4 weight, size L (w/o pedals): 20.72 kg/45.58 lbs. • 12 x 148mm rear spacing • Sizes: S-XXL • Colors: Core 4: Field Grey, Black Magic Bolt • Price: Core 4 $8,499 USD (same for EUR and GBP) More info: yt-industries.com It's the sum of all things that make the Decoy SN stand out: it shreds, it jumps, it pops, all without needing much time to get used to. — Ralf Hauser Contents • Frame &amp Motor Details • Geometry & Sizing • Suspension Design • Specifications • Test Bike Setup • Climbing • Descending • How Does It Compare • Which Model is Best? • Technical Report • Pros & Cons • Pinkbike's Take Cable management done right. Inside, the lower ports feature directly connected carbon-molded guides. The Ride 60 motor is playing hide and seek. Frame & Motor Details Made from ultra high modulus carbon fibers, the frame alone, without the motor, battery, and shock, weighs 3,038g in size L. The Decoy SN's frame is tested to ASTM Category 4 (enduro) standards. The cable routing enters the frame through the sides of the head tube, using co-molded inner tube bonding inside for easy cable installation. The lower two entry ports are directly connected to the carbon-molded guides, but since the top ports are open in the head tube area, you can easily swap the rear brake cable from the left to the right-hand side if required. The link is also made from carbon fiber. Well-protected rear end with soft chainstay and seatstay guards as well as a protective film on the sides. A thick seatstay bridge adds to the stiffness of the rear end. There's no cover, but due to its alignment and spacing I never had issues with little rocks getting caught between the chainstay bridge and the main frame. The bike comes with a YT bottle cage that holds a full-size water bottle, though the maximum capacity varies with different frame sizes. On the size L, I was able to fit a 750ml bottle, which came close to the shock but caused no issues. Two extra mounts on the underside of the top tube offer the option to mount accessories. The Crankbrothers TS2 Tube Stash + kit, which holds a spare tube and tire lever with valve tools, is included with the Core 4 spec and can be purchased aftermarket for other models. YT says they have no issues with shock durability and their shock yoke due to implementing a short enough version (60mm) to prevent putting massive extra stress on the absorber. In fact, even Fox, who doesn’t allow their coil shock to be used on certain bike models with long yokes, gave them a thumbs up on their design. The bottom bracket measured 5mm lower than on paper. But the e13 cranks are nice and short at 160mm in length. Bash guard and slide plate going hand in hand. It's hard to make out the motor in the design. Post mount 200 brake mounts. Found the decoy. In addition to the soft chainstay and seatstay protectors, the bike comes with a clear protective film on its sides – a nice detail. There's also a clear protective film in the midsection of the downtube area, above the thicker frame protection in the lower section. The YT Decoy SN relies on Fazua's Ride 60 system, which delivers a maximum torque of 60Nm and is coupled with an integrated 430Wh battery. The motor weighs 1,960g, and the 430Wh integrated battery weighs 2,200g. The charge port is located in the lower seat tube area and is protected by a sturdy magnetic cover that doesn’t rattle. That's how any charging port should look. Rattle-free thanks to a magnet and nicely sealed. The ring controller is a good concept but could be sturdier. Five lights on the Fazua LED hub equals vague information about your battery charge. This little opening covered by the plug is in preparation for Fazua's range extender – once they bring it to market. The cooling fins of the motor are not enclosed for better heat management. Geometry & Sizing Five sizes are available, ranging from S to XXL. The short seat tube across the range allows riders to select a size based on their riding style rather than being limited by height. Seat tube lengths start at a short 390mm for size S, with size XXL measuring 460mm. Add an extra 5mm to those measurements due to the seat collar. The seatpost insertion depths are as follows: S: 285mm, M: 290mm, L: 310mm, XL: 325mm, and XXL: 340mm. A change of 0.3 degrees of the head tube angle and 0.2 to 0.3 degrees (depending on the frame size) of the seat tube angle is minimal. The most noticeable change is the 4mm drop in bottom bracket height. This is especially important because, with the Continental 2.4" tires used, the actual measured bottom bracket height is 339mm in the regular setting and 335mm in the low setting. This makes it 5mm lower than the numbers suggest on paper. Additionally, the head angle measures slightly slacker than indicated in the charts. Suspension Design The V4L system is based on a four-bar linkage layout with a Horst link and a shock yoke. V4L suspension layout with a Horst link and yoke. The leverage curve starts at about 3.03, gradually decreasing to 2.16 without any erratic changes throughout the travel. This results in a 28.7% progression overall. The initial 3:1 ratio at the start of the stroke improves suppleness, while the higher support in the mid-stroke slightly tapers off towards the end of the travel. The average leverage ratio is 2.46:1. Leverage ratio Anti-squat remains above 100% within the sag window for all gear combinations. At 30% sag – the recommended value – the range varies from 110% (34/10 gearing) to 133% (34/51 gearing). In theory, these values should extend the suspension rather than compress it when pedaling. Anti-rise Anti-squat Anti-rise stays below 100% at all points, starting around 78% at the beginning of travel and nearing 60% at full travel. This lower level means braking has relatively little impact on suspension performance, keeping it active. The narrow range helps the bike remain predictable under braking. YT states that their setup caters well to both coil and air shocks, which is reflected in their choice of components across different spec levels. Specifications Specifications Price $8499 Travel 160mm Rear Shock Fox DHX2 Factory Fork Fox 38 Float Factory Grip X2 Cassette SRAM GX Eagle Transmission 10-52T Crankarms e*thirteen Helix Race / 160mm, 34T Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM GX Eagle Transmission Shifter Pods SRAM Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar Renthal Fatbar 35 / 40mm rise Stem Renthal Apex 35 / 50mm Grips ODI Elite Motion 2.1 Brakes SRAM Maven Silver Hubs Crankbrothers Synthesis E-Bike Alloy Tires Continental Kryptotal FR 29x2.4, SuperSoft / Continental Kryptotal RE 27.5x2.4, Soft Seat SDG BelAir 3.0 Seatpost YT Postman V2 Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE YT Decoy SN Core 4 Test Bike Setup I fall into YT's recommendation for a size M frame. However, with the short seat tube on the Decoy SN, I was able to go for a size L, allowing me to ride my preferred reach of 475mm without compromise. YT recommends setting the sag for the Decoy SN at 30% while seated. In my case, that required a 300 lbs spring, which worked perfectly. I was close to the recommended damping settings, with one click slower on the high speed rebound (HSR 6) and slightly faster on the low speed rebound (LSR 15). I ran the low speed and high speed compression almost fully open (LSC 15, HSC 7), staying within the window of recommended settings. I set up the Fox 38 with Grip X2 damping at 20% sag by the end, down from 15%, which Fox considers its plush setting. I used two volume spacers during most of the rides but removed one toward the end of the testing period to experiment further with the settings. While the air pressure was slightly lower than chart recommendations to achieve the 20% sag, the damping values were close to the guidelines, though I ran the high speed rebound slightly slower – LSR 14, HSR 6, LSC 18, HSC 8. Ralf Hauser Age: 46 Location: Vienna, Austria Height: 5'6” (168cm) Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @nr22bikes YT spec'd the Decoy SN with a 50mm stem, feeling it offered a good balance with the 44mm fork offset. To each their own, and it's a quick and inexpensive fix, but that opinion doesn’t align with my personal preference. To me, longer stems come from a road bike context, and the longer the stem, the more the ends of the bars are offset from the steer tube axis when turning, which ultimately impacts balance. While a 50mm stem isn’t exactly long, I’m more accustomed to the direct and predictable steering that a shorter stem offers. After some back and forth, I stuck with a 32mm stem for most of the testing period. Although there’s nothing inherently wrong with YT’s provided bottle cage, I swapped it for a Fidlock system, as I’m used to grabbing my bottle with my right hand and find less fiddling involved when removing and replacing the bottle. I also switched out the SDG saddle for an SQlab model after spending more hours than necessary in discomfort. Testing Info After its initial launch on bike park tracks in Saalbach and Leogang, Austria, most of the testing took place in the sunny and dry conditions of Finale Ligure, Italy, with some additional time spent on my home trails around Vienna, Austria. Climbing YT refers to the Fazua system as a mid-support solution rather than calling their bike a light e-bike, and I have to agree with their assessment—at least when comparing it to something like the TQ HPR50 or Specialized SL motor. The power that the Ride 60 motor delivers feels like real support, more than just a gentle push from an invisible helping hand, especially in its highest setting. That’s not to say it matches the power of a full-power e-bike, but if you’re a reasonably fit rider, you can keep up with your buddies on full-power motors—unless they’re deliberately trying to drop you. A major advantage of Fazua’s Ride 60 is its low noise emissions. There’s no annoying whine, and even pushing the motor doesn’t significantly increase the noise level. After several hundred kilometers of riding, the motor's sound hasn’t changed, which is something I’ve noticed happening with motors from other manufacturers. The Decoy SN’s steep seat tube angle puts you in an efficient pedaling position, which is also advantageous on steeper inclines. However, I couldn’t resist pushing the saddle forward on its rail, as I prefer the benefits of a really steep seat tube. Ride Modes / Motor Performance I’m not a fan of the names for the ride modes, so I’ll refer to Breeze as Low, River as Medium, and Rocket as High mode. I suppose I can live with the name of the Boost function, which delivers a maximum of 450 watts for 12 seconds (or until you stop pedaling). The system shuts down fairly quickly when taking a break. While it's not a huge issue, it does become tedious to go through the startup procedure more than once during a ride. The startup process takes a few seconds, but after a five-minute break, it takes longer, and the bike starts in 'No Support' mode. For breaks shorter than five minutes, it starts back up in its lowest riding mode. An upcoming software update will make it start in the lowest riding mode all the time. It would be nice to have the option via the app to choose which support mode the bike starts in or adjust the shutdown time, but Fazua currently has no plans to implement this feature. I like the general concept of the ring controller, but Fazua could have made it sturdier. The plastic components appear a bit flimsy and the actuation of the ride modes could be more defined. On a few occasions, it didn’t register an input on the first try. Like any other motor, you can find reports of failures, but throughout the testing period, covering nearly 500 kilometers and encountering some extremely hot days (over 40°C) with the sun beating down on long climbs, I didn’t encounter any issues with the Ride 60 motor or battery, including heat management. I don’t know how the motor handles prolonged wet conditions but it's sealed according to IP54 standards. If you ever run into problems with the motor, since YT is a direct-to-consumer brand, your first point of contact would be YT’s Customer Care section on their website. From there, the team will assist you. YT customers also have access to Fazua’s worldwide Premium Dealer network, which YT’s customer service can facilitate. The Fazua Ride 60 is one of the less power-hungry systems on the market, making its range with a relatively small 430Wh battery quite impressive. A 90kg rider should be able to cover around 1,200 meters of elevation in its highest ride mode. With a balanced mix of the different ride modes, it’s possible to reach over 1,500 meters of elevation. Once Fazua's range extender battery is released – now delayed until 2025 – you'll be able to enjoy even longer days in the saddle. The Decoy SN is already prepped to work with the range extender. While YT saves weight with the non-removable battery, it’s something to consider if you can't charge the bike where it's stored, or during road trips where the charging situation is unknown. Obviously, swapping out a battery during a ride loop isn’t an option either. On the majority of climbs, even those with technical sections full of loose rocks, I had no major issues with the system getting me to the top, as long as I was using the correct gear. The bike doesn’t perform as well if you're powering into an uphill section in too high of a gear, something a full-power motor might be able to overcome. Thanks to the high sensitivity of the suspension and the coil shock – possibly due to the kinematic setup – you’ll notice a fair amount of bob from the suspension when rolling on asphalt or smooth fire roads. Adding more low-speed compression helps, but I’m not a fan of the ride quality when overdoing it, so I regularly used the platform lever on the shock for extended climbs in these conditions. However, on trails or rougher fire roads, I never felt the need to tame the suspension. I loved how the rear wheel’s grip let me plow over obstacles without disrupting my pedal stroke. As expected, the Fazua Ride 60 system isn’t ideal for tackling highly technical uphill sections. While Boost mode is helpful in short segments, it doesn’t do much for extended technical climbs. In fact, when the Boost cuts out after twelve seconds in the middle of a critical obstacle – which happened to me a few times, as I enjoy tackling uphill challenges beyond simple fire road climbs – it can be frustrating. Additionally, the two-second delay before Boost activates can be tricky to time perfectly. On unknown tracks, you might not even have time to initiate it when you need it. Fortunately, Fazua has announced a new controller for 2025, which will allow Boost mode to be activated via an extra button – a welcome improvement. Since the Ride 60 system lacks an overrun feature, it’s also more challenging to clear tricky rock slabs without risking a pedal strike, especially with the Decoy SN’s low bottom bracket, which is even lower than the listed specifications. With relatively short chainstays, the Decoy SN isn’t a class leader in keeping the front end glued to the ground on steep climbs, but this doesn’t mean it’s a poor climber by any means. In fact, I was surprised by the types of trails I could ascend with the Ride 60’s support, even though it required more effort and skill than a full-power motor and sometimes turned extreme uphill challenges into a hit-and-miss struggle. When dismounting becomes necessary, the Ride 60’s walk function could use significant improvement. Its slow initiation and execution led me to abandon it after a few tries, opting to push or carry the bike myself instead. As with many e-bikes that feature only five bars to indicate battery charge, I find Fazua’s LED Hub to be an annoyingly inaccurate source of information. When there’s only one bar left, it’s unclear if you have 20% or 2% charge remaining, making it a guessing game whether you’ll make it home if you add in that little extra loop or not. Reading YT's slogan to "ditch the display and free yourself from distractions," I don’t really get what they’re trying to say. I never considered knowing how much battery I have left a distraction, apart from the fact that if I want to know the exact charge I have to pull out my cell phone to look up Fazua's Rider app that can show the exact value. Now that's a real distraction. You could also opt for displaying the info on a Garmin device or similar, but that would clutter the cockpit somewhat. Just give us a display in the top tube that shows the percentage, it really isn't that hard. At least, if the battery does run out, the system weight of the Decoy SN did not make it an absolute horror to pedal without motor assistance. I tried once, not necessarily by choice … The app also acts as a hub to download different ride mode presets (which works really well) of how the power unfolds for the different ride modes. You also have the possibility to fully customize each ride mode. While the Fazua Ride 60 feels quite natural even in the most powerful settings – I spent most of my riding time using the downloaded Mountainbike Power profile – you have the opportunity to sacrifice some initial power and acceleration for some truly smooth engagement of the power band. Descending It took YT a few extra years to shorten their seat tube lengths in relation to reach, but they've finally reached a point where smaller riders can size up a frame or two if they want to, without having to compromise much on seatpost drop. Except for the size S frame (42mm), all other frames have a 52mm difference between reach and seat tube length. However, that difference alone wouldn’t matter much if the seatpost insertion depth was lacking, but luckily, the Decoy SN has plenty of room. On the size L, you can fit the latest version of the YT Postman with 200mm of drop and smooth engagement, all the way down to the collar. With a head angle of 64.2-degrees in the regular setting and 63.9-degrees in the low setting, which measured about 0.4 degrees slacker, the Decoy SN’s numbers are on the slack side but neither number makes the bike feel like it’s pushing into extreme territory. As a package, the geometry feels nicely balanced. After gathering a good amount of riding impressions, I switched to the low geometry setting and spent most of the testing time in that configuration. I frequently encountered technical trails with tricky rock slabs to navigate on both climbs and descents, where I would have appreciated a few extra millimeters of bottom bracket height. Even with the thankfully short 160mm e*thirteen Helix cranks and being extra cautious, pedal strikes with the lower than claimed bottom bracket height were inevitable. That said, I preferred the slacker head angle for the added stability at speed even if the difference is not that big to begin with. The bike feels highly planted while cornering, regardless of which geometry setting you’re in. Thanks to YT’s forward-thinking design, the zero stack 56 headset cup on top means you can install an angle set. If the Decoy SN were my personal bike, I’d consider running the regular geometry setting for a higher bottom bracket, then use an angle set to bring back the slacker head angle. YT is known for injecting a certain amount of playfulness into their bikes, whether it's a trail bike or a World Cup downhill machine. The chainstay length reflects that philosophy. Although, 442mm (or 443mm in the low setting) on all sizes is what their longest size-specific chainstay on the current Capra 29 measures, so you could say the Decoy SN is on the longer side of YT’s spectrum. Overall, for size L, the Decoy SN has a nice front-to-rear center balance that allows it to carve into tight, snaking turns but won’t scare you when the downhill gets fast and rowdy. I wouldn't mind a slightly longer rear center for some extra weight on the front, but it's hard to fault the SN's character. For sizes XL and XXL, it’s reasonable to assume that the weight bias shifts more onto the rear wheel. When I first hopped on the bike, it was immediately apparent that the Decoy SN is stiff and precise in its line choice, yet it finds a good middle ground by being compliant enough in the right places to avoid feeling harsh. I’m also impressed by how the Decoy SN’s kinematics strike a balance between hugging the ground in almost any situation and staying playful, making it relatively easy to pop off obstacles when you want to get the bike airborne. The constant rate of the suspension curve likely plays a role in that. It’s quite hard to bottom out the rear suspension, yet it feels like you’re making good use of the travel in just about any situation. I can only recall two instances during the entire test period where I bottomed out the rear end hard, but in both cases, it was well deserved. I took the SN on some seriously rough tracks and never once felt concerned about the suspension's ability to handle what was ahead. The tune YT chose for Fox’s DHX2 coil shock is probably another big factor for the rear suspension’s smooth performance. It handles both fast and slow hits with equal poise and rarely gets flustered. What stands out about the bike’s versatility is that, despite its capability on tough terrain, it never felt out of place on mellow trails. In fact, its playful nature translated well to flowy trails too. At 21.1 kg (46.42 lbs.) with pedals, the Decoy SN might not be the lightest mid-power e-bike out there, but it certainly feels light compared to some of the heavier e-bike options when riding it. Generally speaking, most e-bikes in the 23 kg range handle pretty well—they don’t push into corners too aggressively and can be maneuvered without feeling like they’re fighting you. However, the magic number seems to be right around where YT's Decoy SN sits, or below, as it’s hard to find situations where the extra weight becomes a negative on the downhills. If anything, it makes the bike feel nicely planted in corners and through rough sections. Lifting the front wheel over a ledge at the top of a steep chute feels natural, as does initiating a drop when you can only roll to the edge slowly. When you speed into a tight, winding trail, it’s hard not to grin at how easily the Decoy SN can be thrown from side to side beneath you, with plenty of standover height for free movement. The ability to run posts with a long drop – something still not standard on many bikes today – greatly enhances that freedom of movement. One of the standout features is how quiet the bike is on the downhills. For one, the motor doesn’t rattle at all, which is a big win in my book. The cables are well managed, with internal guides that suppress any noise, and the soft chainstay protectors, with air bubbles for extra cushioning, do an excellent job. YT mentioned that they intentionally equipped the bike with the GX Transmission derailleur, even on their top model, because its battery sits on top of the derailleur, rather than on the backside like it does with the XX and XO models, resulting in less bounce. While I haven’t spent enough time on those higher-end models to form a strong opinion, it makes sense in theory. Also, I’d pick the GX’s more protected battery placement over the pricier options any day anyway. The loudest part of the bike is the I9 hub’s freewheel, but even with its many engagement points, it’s still one of the less obnoxious freehub sounds out there to deal with. I fully agree with YT’s decision to run a higher stack height with the 40mm riser bar. The bike’s modern geometry supports a higher front end without much compromise, offering that extra bit of confidence when the trail gets steeper. How Does It Compare YT Decoy SN Trek Slash+ I haven't ridden a bike from another manufacturer in the same riding category with a similar motor type, but I have ridden the TQ HPR 50, and Dario gave a pretty clear picture of how the new Slash+ feels, so we decided to compare these two models head to head. Looking at the numbers, both bikes use mixed wheel setups, but the Trek Slash+ offers a more adjustable geometry than the Decoy SN. The headset can accommodate a +/- 1-degree or 0-degree cup, which almost completely isolates the adjustment from the rest of the geometry. Additionally, a flip chip at the shock mount allows for altering the overall rear end progression. With a mid-high pivot, 170mm of travel at both the front and rear, and the ability to push the head angle below 63 degrees, the Slash+ is massively composed at tackling the roughest trails. On the other hand, the YT Decoy SN should have the edge in terms of liveliness and all-around versatility. The biggest difference between the two bikes, however, is the motor: the Slash+ features the TQ HPR 50. Its 10Nm less torque compared to the Decoy SN’s motor is more noticeable on the trail, than the number suggests. While the power transfer feels very smooth and natural, you’ll need to put in more effort to climb hills and navigate steep technical sections. On the plus side, the Slash+ comes with a 580Wh battery, so it's going to go further than the Decoy SN with its 430Wh battery. While the Fazua Ride 60 motor is impressively quiet, the TQ HPR 50 reigns as the king of stealth, making the Slash+ the closest thing to a regular bike in terms of noise. Plus, the display integrated into the top tube provides the exact battery percentage and other key data. Which Model is the Best Value? It feels like prices for bikes from direct-distribution manufacturers have come down a bit in recent months, and fortunately, the Decoy SN is no exception. The Core 4 flagship model, equipped with Fox Factory suspension, isn’t exactly cheap, but when you consider the spec list and compare it to similarly equipped bikes on the market, it’s much easier on your wallet. With RockShox ZEB and Vivid Ultimate air suspension, the Core 3 likely offers the best value for money, coming in at $1,000 less than the Core 4. The frame is identical to that of the top model. Although I haven't had a chance to ride the new S1000 Eagle Transmission derailleur, I doubt there will be a noticeable difference compared to the GX derailleur from the Core 4. My only gripe is that while the TRP DH Evo brakes are reliable, they don’t deliver the same massive stopping power as the SRAM Mavens. At $6,499, the Core 2 still has a a smart spec, with a mechanical Shimano XT derailleur and no-nonsense tires and wheels. Minimal geometry adjust thanks to a flip chip on the yoke. Crankbrothers Synthesis wheels can take a real beating. Technical Report Adjustments The flip chips – one at the top and one at the bottom of the yoke – are small and a bit fiddly to work with, but since you’ll likely only change the setting once or twice to find your preferred setup, it’s not a big deal. That said, swapping them on the trail might not be ideal. In my one attempt, I was hindered by the linkage, which didn’t allow me to turn my wide multi-tool at all (unbolting the shock in the front and compressing the rear end solved that issue but I won't try to repeat that ordeal). Crankbrothers Synthesis wheels: I didn’t encounter any issues during the test period, even though rough rock sections were a near-daily occurrence, and I did hear the rim bottoming out on rocks once in a while. On top of that, their damping characteristics are top-notch. Continental Kryptotal tires The tread clearly resembles a competitor's 3-2-3 pattern, and I’m totally fine with that. The use of the SuperSoft compound in the front is a welcome choice, especially now that it’s available with Conti’s lighter Enduro casing, which is quite sturdy. I didn’t have any issues with defects while running my usual air pressures in both the front and rear. Given how impressed I am with the minimal wear, even after serious abuse on sharp rocks and lots of hardpack riding, I’d personally switch to the SuperSoft compound in the rear as well for a bit more traction everywhere. I had a few instances where the rear tire slid out on sharply angled rock slabs, and I could do without moments like that. Maven SLVR brakes: The Mavens are always predictable and ready when you need them. At the same time, they offer great modulation. So, if someone suggests they have too much power, I’d counter that they simply need to learn to pull them less at lower speeds or on surfaces that require a gentler touch. I was initially concerned that the higher force required to initiate the brake lever might be a drawback with SRAM’s new stoppers, but I hardly gave it a second thought during most of my rides. Occasionally, on long and consistently steep technical trails, I wished for less strain on the lever, but overall, the significant braking power more than makes up for the extra breakaway force and helps alleviate fatigue far more than the extra effort required at the lever. Continental Kryptotal FR with Enduro casing, now also with SuperSoft compound. Massive brake power, very good modulation. Pros + Dialed supportive suspension. + Modern geometry with short seat tube. + Great all-round capabilities. + Quiet and strong motor for its size. + Great spec. Cons - Battery not removable. - Lower than listed bottom bracket height needs extra attention. - No display with real battery percentage. Pinkbike's Take YT says that the Decoy SN is the best enduro bike they've ever made. A daring statement, but when taking the motor support out of the equation after having ridden the latest generation of Capra, I agree. The Decoy SN is a bike that you never have to second-guess, regardless of the trail conditions. With its supportive suspension it does well in rough conditions while remaining reactive enough to not act like a bore on smoother tracks. It's just flat-out fun when carving turns, although its low bottom bracket height requires extra attention in rocky sections. Adding the motor back into the equation, the Fazua Ride 60 may not rival a full-power system, but it delivers enough punch to offer solid support on climbs, with enough range for longer days in the saddle. While the bike’s overall weight isn’t record-breaking, it’s low enough that it doesn’t negatively impact the ride quality. Overall, if you're satisfied with, or even seek the compromise, of a bit less power and a more natural ride feel, the Decoy SN should be high on your list of contenders for a good time on just about any kind of trail. — Ralf Hauser Posted In: eMTB Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes YT Industries Yt Decoy Sn Author Info: Ralf Hauser Vienna, Austria Member since May 10, 2010 72 articles

title: Review: Zerode G3 - A Belt Driven, Gearbox Equipped DH Bike
author: Matt Beer
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, dh-bikes, pinion, zerode, zerode-g3
article_text: Review Zerode G3 WORDS: Matt Beer PHOTOS: Riley McLay Kiwis are known for doing things a little differently, and Zerode Bikes is a prime example of that. They've been designing frames in New Zealand for over a decade. However, the philosophies of Rob Metz, the founder of Zerode Bikes, date back to 2001(check out their history here ). Their first production frame, the G1 downhill bike, was reminiscent of, yet more attainable than another obscure Kiwi creation; the Lahar M8 , which also used a high-pivot design and internally geared hub. Bring it back to the present day and you’ll find the G3, an evolution in the Zerode G-series. The mixed-wheeled, aluminum chassis continues to use a high-pivot suspension design but the drivetrain has been streamlined. Replacing the bulky hub and dual chain transmission is a belt driven, 6-speed Pinion gearbox. G3 Details • Aluminum frame • Travel: 200 mm, 203 mm fork • Wheel size: Mixed • 142x12mm single speed rear hub • Pinion C.Line 6-speed gearbox • 63-degree head angle • Chainstays: 440mm (static) • Reach: 435, 460, 485mm • Weight: 19.0 kg / 41.9 lb (size MD - tested) • Colors: red, grey, raw (brushed) • Price: $10,300-$10,795 NZD • Frame kit: $6,000 NZD (shock, gearbox, drivetrain, shifter) • Smart.Shift upgrade - $675 NZD • zerodebikes.com Ultimately, the G3 is geared towards riders who have asked for increased security and silence from their drivetrain, whether that's a racer-type or simply a rider who loves lapping bike parks. Adding to that derailleur-free bliss, 200mm of travel arcs rearward to smooth out square-edge hits. Contents Introduction Frame Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Specifications Setup Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take There's a magical sense about the G3's rearward axle path and derailleur-free drivetrain that helps it float through the choppiest trails. — Matt Beer G-series Evolution The unpainted steel G1 prototype. And the production G1 which was released in 2011. A G2 evolved to work with dual 27.5" wheels in 2013. Fifteen years later, the high-pivot, gearbox traits live on in the G3. Frame Details For what appears complicated to work on, the G3’s aluminum frame is surprisingly easy to service, however, there are some specifications that are pointing out. The rear hub uses the older standard of non-Boost with a width of 142mm and a 12mm diameter axle. The Pinion belt cog also runs on a single speed driver to maximize the hub flange, so finding a replacement wheel in a pinch will be less likely than the common sizes of 148 and 157mm of today’s standards. Zerode has also kindly allowed for the rear brake line to be run neatly into either side of the top tube, depending on which side you prefer to run your rear brake on. If you opt for the frame kit, you will want to pay attention to the 203 mm post brake mount. The wire runs through the downtube to the gearbox and battery while the cable actuated version exits just above the crankset. Adjustments on the G3 are limited. There are no alternate chainstay lengths or shock progression flip-chips, however, the reach could be increased by using an aftermarket +/-10mm headset in the full ZS56 head tube. The built-in bumpers are substantial in thickness and even limited the turning radius of a Fox 40. Zerode covers their frames with a lifetime warranty for the original owner and has tons of literature available on their website that explains how to care for the gearbox and belt. If Rube Goldberg designed a downhill bike. The G3 exclusively uses a Pinion C.Line 6-speed Gearbox and Gates carbon belt, although you can choose between a mechanical or electronic shift system. The Pinion Smart.Shift uses a dual action thumb paddle with similar ergonomics to a TRP shifter. The charge port connects next to the shifter. Separate wires are shrink wrapped together and run internally through the downtube. A 142mm-wide DT Swiss 350 Hybrid single speed hub keeps the rear triangle width to a minimum. Drivetrain The 6-speed Pinion C1.6 gearbox is standard equipment on the G3, however there’s a choice between mechanical or electronic shifting. The 6-speeds give a 295% range with ratios starting at 2.86 and ending at 8.44. Each step is said to be a 24.3% change. One of the largest deterrents to the mechanical system is the need to stop pedaling in order to change gears. The second is the twist shifter. The C1.6i Smart.Smart gearbox upgrade takes care of both of those drawbacks. A sensor in each of the 165mm long crank arms takes care of the semi-automatic shifting by changing gears at the 6 and 12-o’clock crank positions when the torque instantaneously hits zero. The ergonomic dual push button shifter has rubberized paddles that take little effort to tap through the gears. The Pinion app connects by Bluetooth, allowing for quick calibrations, shifter direction and further tuning to be personalized. Pinion says the battery nestled inside lasts 20,000 shifts, or 100 hours. In less than 3 hours, the battery can reach full charge. The connector is located next to the shifter and runs parallel on a wire, but is wrapped together with the shifter wire. Regarding the Gates carbon belt, those should last 30,000km, roughly three times as long as any chain. The Gates carbon belt has a molded central ridge to hold it in line with the middle of the grooved cogs. Removing the rear wheel requires unbolting the rear axle and then lifting the belt off of the lower pulley to give enough slack in the system. As for the efficiency, Zerode acknowledges studies on their website here that state there can be efficiency loses in gearboxes and drivetrains, however, those were tested again chain and derailleur drivetrains in ideal conditions. We’ll weigh in about how that stacks up in the real world later in the ride impressions. All of Pinion’s C-Line gearboxes are covered by a 2-year (optional to upgrade to 3) warranty and require an oil change every 10,000km. Their detailed FAQ page with even more info can be found here . Zerode's G-series downhill bikes have always been frames designed around a high-pivot design. The floating chainstay arm pulls on a rocker link that pivots around a large bearing centered in the flat braces of the interrupted seat tube. Suspension Design Keeping form with its predecessors, the G3 uses a rate controlled, single pivot suspension design. The main pivot placed very high on the main frame and the floating chainstay pulls on the short rocker link, quickly creating an extremely progressive leverage rate. Not only does the rate change very fast from 3.3 to 2.4, but it remains around that lower leverage for the last 50mm of travel. What sets the G3 apart from most other high pivot bikes which use an inverted Horst Link, such as the Forbidden Supernought and Norco Range, is that the seatstay runs uninterrupted to the axle. Instead, the dropout pivot is placed on the chainstay. This provides a completely rearward arcing axle path but doesn’t allow for the same degree of freedom to tune the ant-squat or anti-rise and the aforementioned bikes. The anti-squat is fairly neutral, hovering around 100% in the sag window. On the other hand, the anti-rise is very high, even for a high pivot bike. That number aligns to about 166%. While some critics will claim that this firms up the suspension under braking, this did not seem to be the case on the trail. Reaching the high speed rebound dial of the Fox DHX2 means unbolting one end of the shock and cycling the suspension. It’s not a huge time commitment, or an adjustment that is often changed, but it does take an extra step over a fully-exposed damper. All of the shock bolts and pivots are easily accessible, including the lower rocker point, thanks to a bored hole in the shock tunnel. The G3 has a progressive leverage ratio ramps up quickly. Geometry The sizes of the G3 are limited to three: small, medium, large. At 435, 460, and 485 mm in reach, those reach measurements could be considered standard, but may exclude riders above the 188cm/6’2” mark, or thereabouts. A short 105mm head tube is used across all frame sizes, so it wouldn't be surprised to see riders swapping out the 25mm rise handlebar for a higher perch. Offsetting the G3's other irregular frame specifications, its geometry is contemporary for a downhill bike. A 63° head angle is par for the course and a -4 mm BB drop, from the rear axle, makes it low without scraping the ground. At static, the chainstays measures 440 mm for all three sizes and continue to grow as the bike goes through its travel. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $6050 Travel 200 Rear Shock Fox DHX2, 250x75 Fork Fox 40 Factory 29, Grip2, 29 Headset Cane Creek zs56/zs56 Cassette Pinion C1.6 - 6 speed gearbox Crankarms Pinion 165mm Chainguide Custom skid plate Bottom Bracket Pinion Rear Derailleur N/A Chain Gates belt Shifter Pods Pinion Smart.Shift Handlebar Title 35, 800x25mm Stem Title DM 35, 46mm Grips Zerode Brakes Hayes Dominion A4, 200mm F/R Hubs DT swiss 350 Spokes DT Champion Rim DT Swiss FR541 Tires Maxxis Assegai / DHR ll Seat Title JS1 Seatpost Title 31.6mm Compare to other DH Bikes The aluminum frame of the Zerode G3 is finished with alloy Title AH1 handlebars and DT Swiss 540 rims. Fox Factory suspension, Hayes Dominion brakes, and Maxxis tires round out the complete build kit. The beauty of gearboxes is that you can change gears without pedalling. What's even more appealing, Pinion's Smart.Shift new-age gearbox can now handle shifting under load. RIDING THE G3 Test Bike Setup All of the components, aside from the drivetrain, were familiar to me, expediting the setup process, and downloading and connecting the Pinion app to the gearbox took no time at all. I set the shifter to the traditional direction; the lower lever moved to easier gears and the higher lever to higher gears. After thinning out to 78kg (including my downhill kit) following the NZ MTB Rally, Zerode suggested 25-50% sag and starting with Fox’s recommended base settings in " Table B ” for the 425lb spring on the 75mm stroke DHX2 shock. I managed to hit the 25% sag number with half a turn of preload on the coil and remembered how Sam Blenkinsop had setup his G3 for Red Bull Hardline . Although he’s roughly the same weight, he’s been known to run a firm rear end and was running a 500# spring. Given the impacts on that track are way gnarlier than what I’d be taking on, I figured I’d lean into his preferences for the G3. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 37 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r The high-speed compression was set 6 clicks open from a total of 8 when closed and the low-speed compression sat 13/16 clicks from closed. On the rebound side, the high-speed rebound was 4/8 open and low-speed at 7/16. From there, the Fox 40 was set to 84 psi with my usual preferences, a hint less compression and rebound damping than Fox’s suggested settings. Given the low bar and stack height, I raised the front end height by sliding the stanchions down in the crowns. On the handling front, I personally would have preferred a shorter offset fork. At head angles of 63 degrees, I find 52mm offset crowns make the front wheel flop unpredictably part way through tighter turns. Download the Trailforks app here. Descending I spent a week thrashing an Ibis HD6’s around the Queenstown bike park before jumping on the Zerode G3. I’m not knocking the enduro bike’s performance, since it’s a totally different type of bike with less travel, however, it was almost comedic when I dropped into the first rough section of trail on the G3 - the high-pivot suspension and calm belt drive simply floated down rattly brake bumps. This wasn’t my first ride on a high-pivot downhill bike, or a belt-driven bike either, but when you put the two together, it’s astounding how it mutes the chaos of a beat down, blown out bike park. Sure, there’s still the usual caveats of that rearward axle path. You’ll need a few laps to adjust to the rear wheel returning to its resting position as you launch off of jumps, but with a balanced suspension setup, it becomes as natural as any other suspension type. The only place I found that it became strange was steep, tight, bermed corners (think of trails like Angry Pirate in Whistler or KY in Queenstown). In those instances, where it’s tough to totally drop the rear brake, the chainstay extends. This can feel like you’re being stretched or pulled slower through the turn. Opinions vary as to what the ideal amount of anti-rise actual is, but I didn't have any complaints about how the Zerod behaved under heavy braking. The higher anti-rise level means that the head angle is better preserved since the fork also undergoes compression while braking. Because of this, I found my confidence grew and I felt safer to hit those turns faster and faster, finding more and more traction. The aluminum frame and wheels help the G3 track exceptionally well through rough spurts, dissipating any vibrations that might be sent through the chassis. Literally, the only sounds you can hear are the tires rolling on hard packed surfaces, and sadly, the brake pads jiggling in the calipers. Recognizing that there may be a more centralized weight distribution from the gearbox might be a confirmation bias, but the one thing that is clear is that the rear wheel acts as if it’s chainless, obviously. Perhaps it’s better to say that it is unimpeded by the tension of a derailleur clutch or feedback from a bouncing chain. The light, calm feeling of the belt that wraps around the single cog on the rear wheel is incomparable to any other bike I’ve ridden with a derailleur. Learning how the gearbox bangs between cogs also takes a second. There’s no clutch in the system or a chain to ease the gap between gear reductions. It slams into place instantly, and with authority, at least most of the time (I’ll get into that below). Technical Report Title Bar and Stem: The G3’s headtube isn’t the tallest and I’d wager most riders would prefer a higher rise bar. Handlebar bends come in all flavors. These are 5 degrees up, 9 degrees back. The backsweep of the AH1 alloy bar felt even greater than that, with a seemingly late bend. The 46mm long DM1 stem has a massive 86mm clamping width and a thoughtful cross brace on top of the crown to shrug off any thoughts of twisting in a tumble. Hayes Dominion A4 Brakes: Hayes Dominion brakes have a nice and light lever action with a tool-free adjust. After coming off of the newly released SRAM Maven brakes, I missed the savage power compared to the somewhat numb powerband of the Dominions. Furthermore, the brake pads rattling in the caliper killed the otherwise stealthy ride of the G3. Drivetrain: I’ve ridden Pinion’s mechanical gearboxes before and disliked the two most apparent traits of the system: the twist shifter and the requirement to stop pedaling when changing gears. The electronic trigger shifter is more conveniently arranged and safer compared to the twist shifter, which requires easing up on your grip. It takes a short amount of time to adapt to the location of the buttons compared to the natural position of a SRAM or Shimano shifter. As for the precision and timing of the shifts, again, it’s an improvement over the mechanical system. Occasionally, the shifts lagged, only to clunk into place unexpectedly a few moments later. Zerode did preface that Pinion is working to optimize the location of the sensor in the non-drive side crank arm. Banging through the gears always felt solid, like a derailleur's upshift, except in both directions. The gap in the 6-speed gears are noticeable, and I felt the steps between each one were greater than a 7-speed Sram X0 DH setup, particularly between the two highest gears. Elsewhere, the pick up speed of the hub's clutch and gearbox don't add up to the most responsive crank inputs either. A small tab below the rear cog dissipates regular oscillations in the belt caused during pedalling. At some point, possibly in transit, while riding, or loading the bike onto carriers, the plastic piece was compromised and caused the belt to skip on the cogs when placed under tension. The absence of a derailleur doesn’t mean this unique drivetrain was without issues, though. On day four with the G3, a sudden sense of skipping gears started causing a racket. The hub clutch was inspected and then the gearbox was recalibrated; it turns out that the small tensioner that hides below the rear cog became weakened. This led to the belt skipping on the cogs when the crank arms reached maximum torque. That may have been bumped during shipping. Striking this critical component would be extremely difficult while riding and I don’t recall bumping this at any time. No exterior damage or scuffs were visible either. Zerode identified the problem once the bike was returned. They’re working to increase the integrity of this guide and the depth of the bolt that holds it in place. Which Model is the Best Value? Deciding on the best value for your money with the G3 involves two main decisions: mechanical or electronic gearbox, and complete build or frame only? If you’re after the electronic Pinion Smart.Shift unit you’ll have to cough up an extra $650 NZD or settle for the mechanical twist-shifter. Is it worth the extra cash? Considering that the Smart.Shift automatically meshes the gears, regardless of how much torque is on the cranks, it’s a huge leap forward in gearbox technology. You also move away from that old school, twist shifter system, making it a no-brainer in my mind. There’s just one complete build kit option for the G3, nearly identical to the one featured here - the only out-of-spec component on this build versus the listed parts were the Hayes Dominion brakes - Magura MT7s and TRP DH-Evos are the regular starters in the stopping department. Beginning at $10,300 NZD, it’s a solid choice of parts for hammering downhill tracks with a tough wheelset, powerful brake sets, and quality suspension. For those wishing to build one from the frame up though, that’s an option too. The $6,000 NZD frame kit includes all of the necessary gearbox and drivetrain components (cranks, cogs, belt, shifter), plus a Fox DHX2 shock. Keen to match up the dampers? Zerode sells the G3 frame kit with a Fox 40 Factory for $7,800 NZD. The complete build kit with the Smart.shift would be my pick. The components are all of excellent value, plus a conventional drivetrain won't transfer over and it's likely you'd need to build the G3's specific 142mm rear wheel. Zerode ships their bikes directly from New Zealand, so for some consumers, the exchange rate could be of benefit but they’ll have to factor in taxes, shipping and the usual customs duties. Estimates on shipping would be $985 NZD for complete bikes or $775 for framesets. Taxes are expected to be in the range of $500-$750 NZD. To keep things simple for US customers, Zerode has committed to approximate landed costs of: $4,325 USD for the frame kit, $5,525 USD - frame kit with a Fox 40 Factory, and $7,245 USD will score the complete bike. Those prices exclude the Smart.Shift system. Zerode G3 Antidote Darkmatter How Does It Compare? Picking a downhill bike to compare to the G3 took me a second because it's one-of-a-kind. The only other close rival would be the Gamux DH-prototype with the same drivetrain and (single) high-pivot design. We’ll have to wait to swing a leg over that one though. A closer competitor would be the high-pivot Antidote Darkmatter. Obvious contrasting points are the frame materials, drivetrain, and overall weight. The Darkmatter, a.k.a. the carbon spaceship, is 5.5 kg lighter than the G3. Zerode’s aluminum DH rig is a hell of a lot more comfortable. The frame's alloy tubing damps more vibrations and then there’s that uninterrupted start to the suspension. A low unsprung weight and rubberized carbon belt take the G3’s suppleness and silence to a level that can’t be attained with a chain and derailleur. Sprinting off the line is not the G3’s forte. Whether it’s the high-pivot, belt drive, gearbox, or a total loss of that system. Ever try to run on a sandy beach? Ok, maybe it’s not that bad, but there’s a serious amount of effort sapped from the pedals versus the direct power driven to the ground on the Darkmatter. Pros + Smart.Shift gear changes and trigger shifter are a massive improvement over the mechanical system. + Suspension absorbs braking bumps and square edges brilliantly. + Calming ride quality with the absence of a bouncing chain or tension from a derailleur's clutch. Cons - Acceleration is not its strong point. - Could be too progressive for some rider's tastes. - Finding replacement parts for the drivetrain and outdated rear hub size could be a challenge. Pinkbike's Take The G3 lands near the top of my list for downhill bikes that deliver superior grip and have the ability to delete square-edge impacts. Just be prepared to cough up some power when you get on the pedals - it's not the lightest or most efficient bike. The unconventional drivetrain has its advantages and disadvantages; it's less susceptible to damage and wear, but you'll want to keep the necessary proprietary parts on hand if you're traveling to races or road tripping to bike parks. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews DH Bikes Pinion Zerode Zerode G3 Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Zoceli Narum - A Czech-Made Steel Trail Bike
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: REVIEW Zoceli Narum WORDS: Dario DiGiulio PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio / Eric Mickelson Zoceli is a small one-man Czech bike manufacturer, with each bike from the 4-model catalog designed and fabricated in Brno by Martin Saida. The Narum is the trail bike of the group, with a steel front and rear triangle, 135mm of rear wheel travel, and beautifully packaged twin-link suspension. As a smaller outfit, Zoceli can only offer frames for the time being, to be built up as you see fit for each bike's purview. This allowed for some leeway when it came to the Narum's review, but also lead to some indecision on how best to outfit the bike. Those questions were answered as I put the bike through its paces, in and around my home trails in Bellingham. Narum Details • Steel frame • 27.5", MX, or 29" wheels • 125 or 135mm frame travel, 140-160mm fork • 65° head angle (140mm fork) • 455-515mm reach • 435mm chainstays • 80° seat angle • Weight: 3,700g frame, no shock • Build weight: 34.38lbs / 15.55kg (140mm fork) • Frame Price: € 2,800, 71,000 CZK • zoceli.cz This video file cannot be played. (Error Code: 102404) Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Be it flowy or natural, when the trails aren't too steep or massively rough, the Narum is keen to get off the ground and cut corners as tight as you're willing to try. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details There's more to the Narum's frame than just some skinny steel tubing, thanks to a few well thought out details that bring it to the next level. It's rare to see guided internal cable routing on a production steel bike, but the Narum features sleek tubes through the length of the front triangle. The custom headtube allows for an oversized lower bearing for increased durability, and casts a unique shadow. The machined 7075 aluminum links are connected by a bolt-on bridge, and house the bearings within the link for ease of service. The Narum features a UDH dropout and a 180mm post mount in the rear, a threaded bottom bracket, and plenty of clearance for a long travel dropper post. The frame is built to accept 30.9mm droppers, and a ZS44/ZS56 headset. Fully guided steel tubes. Drain hole. The lower shock mount features a clever little through-frame drain hole to avoid any water pooling, and the frame's internals are ED coated to keep the Columbus tubeset from rusting. Customers have their choice of many powdercoat options, from color to texture, and can also choose between silver and black anodized links. While this bike came with internal routing (the stock option), you can also opt for external routing should you prefer it. That certainly would have been my selection, if given the choice. Stiffness. Strength. Geometry & Sizing The Narum features three separate geometry tables depending on what wheel size you plan on running. Full 27.5, full 29, or MX, each feature their own rear ends to keep most geometry the same - only changing chainstay length and BB drop. I only rode the bike with 29" wheels front and rear, so I'll speak to that geometry here. The numbers that make the Narum are modern throughout, with a bias towards handling and agility over all-out stability. A neutral 65° head angle is right in the ballpark for many trail bikes these days, though the 80° seat angle is an outlier in how upright it is. Reach figures are in line with some of the industry's larger players, like Santa Cruz and Specialized, with the size XL I tested landing at 495mm - pretty ideal in my book. One unusual point is the stack height, which is consistent across every size in the range - likely as a result of that custom headtube, which measures at 120mm for everything from the M to the XXL. Rear center lengths are also consistent at 435mm for each size. Bottom bracket offset is 34mm below the axles, keeping the bike roomy and low slung. Suspension Design Martin at Zoceli has implemented a dual link layout for the Narum, with two short links driving the 185x55mm shock. With a fairly progressive leverage rate, Little Pinky works well with air and coil shocks alike, breaking into travel easily and ramping up smoothly towards the end of the stroke. The anti-rise figures add to that, landing right around 100% at sag and tapering off from there. Leverage ratio. Anti-rise. Anti-squat. Pedal kickback. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $3114 Travel 135mm Rear Shock RockShox Vivid Coil Ultimate Fork Fox Factory 36 Grip X2 Headset Chris King NoThreadSet Cassette SRAM XO AXS T-TYPE Crankarms Ignite Components Catalyst Chainguide SRAM XO AXS T-TYPE Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB Pedals Crank Brothers Mallet E Rear Derailleur SRAM XO AXS T-TYPE Chain SRAM XO AXS T-TYPE Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Handlebar RaceFace Era Stem Burgtec Enduro MK3 Grips ODI Longeck Brakes Hayes Dominion A4 Wheelset Roval Traverse SLII Hubs DT Swiss 240 Spokes DT Swiss Aerolite J-Bend Rim Roval Traverse SLII Tires Specialized Butcher / Eliminator Seat WTB Silverado Seatpost BikeYoke Revive 3.0 Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Narum Test Bike Setup As this was a frame-only review bike, I was left to fill in the parts kit with components I had on hand. I'd say it built up into a pretty high end bike overall, with some of the best, brakes, wheels, and suspension I've used lately. You'll notice two different forks in the shots here - that's a simple byproduct of experimenting with which travel setup and damping characteristic worked best with the bike. I rode the Vivid Coil for nearly the entire test period, and found a neutral tune worked pretty nicely with the Narum. I played with both a 140mm 36 and 160mm Lyrik throughout the testing period, and found that both suited the bike nicely, including the geometry changes they incurred. As it stands though, the 140 fork is closest to the intended build, so that's what I'll be referencing in the ride impressions unless noted otherwise. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Download the Trailforks App Testing Info As a great middle-ground trail bike, the Narum can be hard to shoehorn into one ideal loop. This one came to mind, with high speeds, fun side hits, and corners that reward speed. The bike's geo works well on steep climbs, and keeps mellower-grade descents from feeling too lethargic. Nothing too crazy, just some good ol' trail bike riding. Climbing The climbing characteristics of the Narum are mostly defined by its unusually steep seat tube angle, jutting 80° up straight out of the bottom bracket. The seated position is very upright, and puts you squarely between the wheels despite the mid-length rear center. I've found bikes with seat angles in this range - particularly those that reflect the same angle in their actual STA - to be more even-keeled when it comes to pedal bob and bodyweight shifting on the climbs. That proved to be true with the Zoceli, which motored up climbs with little fuss, keeping you high in the travel and free from too much seesaw action. Thanks in part to the coil shock and higher starting leverage, the Narum has good grip at the rear wheel for technical and loose climbs. You won't be fooled into thinking it's in the same realm as some longer-travel enduro bike in that sense, but for a mid-travel trail bike the grip is good. Weight is a fair concern when it comes to a bike with steel tubes tip to tail, but Martin at Zoceli has done a pretty good job of keeping things from getting out of hand. At 3,700 grams for the frame, the Narum is far from featherweight, but comparable to plenty of aluminum trail bikes on the market from much bigger brands. Descending There's only so many ways you can skate around using the word fun in a review, so I'll just rely on it as the catch-all here. This bike offers a great time on trails of all sorts, especially when the terrain isn't too extreme. Be it flowy or natural, when the trails aren't too steep or massively rough, the Narum is keen to get off the ground and cut corners as tight as you're willing to try. The neutral head tube angle feels steeper than I expected of a 65° figure, but doesn't lead to things feeling sketchy. If anything, that's the element of this bike that keeps corners fun and precise, with snappy handling that changes direction easily. The shorter rear end plays a role here too, preferring to slash into a turn rather than arc around the wider faster line. I wouldn't call it a terribly fast bike, though the suspension does a good enough job of handling bumps to keep the speeds up. It just errs on maneuverability over stability, and feels plenty fun at lower speeds. Manuals, bunny hops, and weird little side hits are the name of the game, with a tighter wheelbase than we're used to seeing on trail bikes these days and a supportive suspension that rewards some body English. As Zoceli doesn't offer a specific shock tune for the bike, there's a bit of a gray area in terms of how the bike will perform with the shock a given customer might end up using. I was perfectly happy with the stock tune on the Vivid Coil I ran, but there's always some curiosity as to how a bike would perform with some internal work. Zoceli Narum Transition Smuggler How does it compare? Fun, short travel 29ers are some of my favorite bikes to ride around home, and these two both manage to deliver a great time on an adaptable platform. The Smuggler feels like the more capable bike between the two, with a suspension characteristic that allows you to push the bike a bit further than you'd expect from a 130mm travel rig. Zoceli's Narum, on the other hand, keeps handling a bit sharper and more reactive, which might be preferable to folks in flatter areas or who value quick and tight turning over stability. For my neck of the woods, the Smuggler ended up being a bit more versatile, as I could push it into gnarlier terrain without adding too much zest to my life. Technical Report Internal Routing: Early on in the review period, the Narum had a persistent rattle that I couldn't quite diagnose, until I isolated it to the internal cable routing. Like many bikes that use tube-in-tube routing without any sort of cable clamp at the entry or exit, you could hear the lines in the Zoceli's frame on chattery terrain and on hard landings. I dripped some Dumonde liquid grease into the ports and let it gum up a little bit to stop the cables from clattering. Not the most elegant solution, but it's worked well so far and the noise hasn't returned. Chain Slap Protection: As stock, there is none. I started of with some mastic tape on the chainstay, but still found it a bit clattery. A strip of STFU's stay guard did the trick, quieting the chain slap down and adding a cool spiky look to the stay. Frame Hardware: The alignment on this frame was damn near dead-on, and the rear linkage allows for minimal flex - contrary to what you'd expect of a steel bike. That said, the combination of those factors and the nature of the frame hardware can elicit some funny creaks and pops if not clean and tightened properly, leading to a bit more upkeep than I'm used to on the linkage members and bolts. The bearings are still in great shape, but to keep things silent you'll need to be attentive. Pros + Fun, unique, and easy to ride + Not pushing the boundaries of what a trail bike can be + Loves to carve a good turn Cons - Frame noise requires upkeep - On the heavier side for a shorter travel, fun-focused bike Pinkbike's Take There seems to be a persistent trend towards making trail bikes as capable as possible, but the Zoceli Narum keeps things fun without going extreme. Not trying to be an enduro bike, the Narum has an intuitive handling characteristic, feels exciting on a wide variety of trails, and does so in a truly unique and elegant package. It's not without some idiosyncrasies, this steel trail bike stands out from the crowd and is a solid offering from a very small manufacturer. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Zoceli Zoceli Narum Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: RockShox Vivid - Shock Week 2023
author: Dario DiGiulio
tags: reviews-and-tech, reviews, shock-week, shocks, rockshox, rockshox-vivid
article_text: RockShox Vivid Ultimate Shiny and new, the RockShox Vivid is the freshest shock in this week's lineup. The Vivid name was long dormant in the RockShox catalog, so it's fitting that the reintroduction of that model came with a host of new features and the highest level of refinement their team has put into a shock to date. There are some significant performance claims being made here, primarily around the coil-like performance that the Vivid is supposed to deliver. If true, it could very well be the new benchmark for downhill air shocks. For all the pertinent details, head over to the First Ride article from the initial launch. Vivid Ultimate Details • 2-position Threshold lever • High-speed compression - 5 clicks • Low-speed compression - 5 clicks • Hydraulic bottom-out - 5 clicks • Low-speed rebound - 20 clicks • Weight: 738 grams • Price: $699-729 USD • rockshox.com RockShox Vivid Air | 2023 Shock Week https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/nF8Kmdjr Copied Live 00:00 11:32 11:32 Setup In keeping with the entirety of the current RockShox lineup, the new Vivid is relatively painless to set up, thanks to the limited clicker positions, sag gradients, and ease of on-trail adjustment. The 5 clicks of high and low compression damping offer distinct differences between each spot, making bracketing single clicks fairly easy to grasp. Thanks to the handy little 3mm knob attached to the rebound adjuster, you can make any needed compression adjustments on trail without needing extra tools. We were aiming for about 18-20mm of sag on the Nomad, which for my riding weight of about 180 lb resulted in 215 psi in the air can. My rebound was usually either 7 or 8 clicks from closed, and I typically ran the LSC and HSC in the middle setting. Hydraulic bottom out was +1 from center. I found the LSC was the adjuster I was most prone to changing for different tracks, though the middle position strikes a pretty ideal balance on the Nomad. Big knurled LSC knob. Climbing The Vivid's Threshold lever provides a very firm platform, just shy of a full-on lockout. I like this binary-feeling approach to a shock lockout for two reasons: One, it really shores things up for paved climbs and smooth fire roads; Two, you know pretty quickly if you accidentally left it on for the descent. Because it's so firm, I don't ever use it on trail, but if you subscribe to the Henry Quinney method, then your shock should actually be softer for the climbs anyway. The Vivid is sensitive enough to hum through all the littlest bumps on your way up trail, and has excellent grip in loose and steep terrain. HSC and Threshold. HBO up top. Descending Put succinctly, the Vivid looks and feels like a RockShox Float X2. Take that as a compliment, because the best-in-class sensitivity of the X2 had pretty much been unchallenged until now. Not merely matching performance, the Vivid also offers some distinct RockShox features that are unique to their lineup. One attribute that really stands out from the crowd is just how quiet the shock is, hardly making a peep even under great duress. That quiet performance fades away nicely, but there's no mystery that the Vivid is working very well underneath you. We were hugely impressed with how well the shock performed on our test Nomads, and it has handled very different kinematics with equal composure. Some bikes might benefit from the external control of high speed rebound, which is present on the competing X2, but you could make a vice-versa argument for the adjustable hydraulic bottom out that the Vivid has and the X2 lacks. Ultimately the two are neck and neck, and most of the decision will come down to brand preference, which of those two settings you'd rather have, and whether you want every possible click in the range (X2) or relative simplicity of setup (Vivid). Time will tell if the Vivid's durability is up to the 100-hour service standard they've laid out, but if it proves to be that burly, then that might be the detail that puts it ahead of the Fox shock in competition for best beefy air shock. As to whether it's a true competitor to an actual coil shock, the jury is still out, though the competition may be close there as well. We're planning some side-by-side comparisons for the long term review, so stay tuned for that. In the meantime, I'm willing to say that the increased tunability and lighter weight might put the Vivid above a coil shock on the right bike, for the right person. Gotta love the sag gradient. Rebound knob. And cheeky little 3 mil. Pros + Silent performance + Effective and distinct adjustments + Hydraulic bottom-out will complement some kinematics very well Cons - Lack of high speed rebound adjustment - Fitment may prove tricky on some frames Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shock Week Shocks RockShox Rockshox Vivid Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Staff Rides: Seb Stott’s Pivot Firebird
author: Seb Stott
tags: reviews-and-tech, staff-rides, reviews, pivot, pivot-firebird, seb-stott
article_text: Pivot Firebird Staff Ride https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/beSjWRDw Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 12:48 STAFF RIDES Seb Stott's Pivot Firebird If you want a big, burly enduro bike that could happily be your only bike, the Pivot Fiebird pretty much nails the brief. Its DW4 suspension offers as-good-as-it-gets pedalling performance for the category, while it has plenty of travel and well-judged geometry for descending. Its ride feel is composed and confident on the gnarly stuff without feeling lethargic on mellower terrain. While my riding impressions haven't changed too much since my initial review , I am now more confident in saying this is one of the best all-round enduro bikes out there. I've been riding this bike since last May, using it as a test bench for new parts and a base to experiment with. Let's get into what I like, what I don't and what I've changed about the Firebird. Pivot Firebird Details • Wheel size: 29" or MX compatible • 170 mm rear travel(measured, long chainstay setting), 170mm fork • 63.8° head angle, 76.5° seat angle • Size specific & adjustable chainstay • Three bottle bosses & downtube storage • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • Actual weight (stock): 16.1 kg / 35.5 lb • Actual weight (as it sits now): 17.1 kg / 37.7 lb • Lifetime original owner frame warranty & bearing replacement • Price: $6,499 to £13,699 ($8,699 as tested) • pivotcycles.com What's good Suspenion I've come to think the DW4 linkage could be the best all-around system there is. Thanks to the co-rotating links, it's got a solid amount of anti-squat and anti-rise right the way through the travel, which means the bike doesn't pitch as much when braking or pedalling and responds consistently. It feels stable and composed, but without becoming harsh under braking. It's got a well-judged progression curve, too, offering plenty of support without making the end-stroke abrupt or harsh. I've stuck with the frame's more progressive setting, which plays nicely with both coil and air shocks. Anti-squat. Anti-rise. It doesn't erase bumps as completely as some bikes, but it does a great job of taming the chaos when you really need it, without moving around too much and upsetting the chassis. I measured the usable travel on my test bike at over 170 mm in the long chsainstay setting (5 mm more than claimed), which helps explain the capable feel when things get spicy. Best of all, it pedals really efficiently for such a long-travel bike, which makes the Firebird more rewarding on climbs or mellower terrain - and partially covers for the slack seat tube. Geometry (mostly) I've ridden plenty of longer bikes, and I've ridden plenty of shorter bikes. My current thinking is that somewhere around 1,310 mm wheelbase and 510 mm reach is ideal for me, at 191 cm / 6'3". Much longer, and the bike starts to feel more unwieldy with steeply diminishing returns in terms of confidence and stability. It's been said that the Firebird has a particularly rearward weight bias, but it's about average with the chainstay set to the long setting. It measures 446 mm in XL. In my view, the downsides start to outweigh the advantages when chainstays get much longer than that - at least for the steep tech trails I enjoy. Frame details The frame seems well-made and thought-through. There's sensible cable routing, no cable rattle, good tire clearance and really generous heel clearance.
