I don't tend to use internal storage often, but the Firebird's downtube door is secure, and the ability to fit two bottles inside the front triangle is a nice bonus. Many of my rides are too long for just one bottle, but doable with two. Reliability While I should caveat that this has been far from my only bike this past year, I've been impressed with how the Firebird's held up. Despite many muddy rides and regular washing, there's been no creaking, rattling or bolts coming loose. And unlike some bikes, it doesn't collect water after washing. Parts-wise, I've become increasingly impressed by SRAM Transmission over the years. While I'm not a fan of having more batteries to charge, I'm now in the habit of carrying a spare. And the shifting reliability is very impressive; even if you shift through multiple gears unsympathetically on steep climbs, cover it in mud, or scrape it off tree stumps, it changes gear crisply virtually every time. The Maven brakes have had two bleeds and a piston massage. The latter is essential to get them to work at their best, which I think explains the mixed reviews. While they can be fussy, the power and control are superb. But I must say that the Hayes Dominions on my staff ride last year had zero maintenance. A simple modification or a cry for help? You be the judge. What's not so good Seat tube angle Pivot were aiming for a 77° effective seat angle, according to their geometry chart, but at my saddle height, it measures 76.5°. Either way, I think it could be steeper. The supportive suspension makes up for the (relatively) laid-back position to such an extent that I'd still call it a good climber, but having tried it with the Fair Bicycle offset saddle clamp , which steepens the angle to 78.5°, Pivot missed a trick by not making it this steep from stock. Turns out, having a steep seat angle and efficient suspension is a winning combination. I had previously tried the high flip-chip setting to improve the climbing position, but I preferred the low setting for descending. My feet felt too high relative to the front contact patch on steep descents in high mode. It's worth pointing out that the flip chip doesn't fully compensate for wheel size changes, so if you want to run it as a mullet, the seat angle will be slacker still. SuperBoost (sort of) I don't consider SuperBoost a deal breaker, but it's been frustrating not to have the option to swap out some of the other enduro/trail wheels I have lying around. (Is it just me who has multiple wheelsets lying around?) On the other hand, the ability to share wheels between your enduro and DH bike could be handy for some people. It's also worth saying that you can adapt a 148 mm wheel to fit a 157 mm frame (with spacers and a re-dish) but not the other way around. Plus, I've subjected the rear wheel to several clumsy landings, and it remains true and even-tensioned, so maybe there's something in it. Having typed all that, I'm not 100% sure this should be in the dislikes section. Price It's $8,699 as tested (not including upgrades). Enough said. What I've changed • The first change was from the 210 mm travel Fox seatpost to a 240 mm OneUp V3. I just prefer more drop. • Peaty's Monarch knurled grips replaced Pivot's own. I find them more comfortable, and I like to keep grips consistent between bikes. • I swapped the WTB Volt for a Fizik Aidon eMTB saddle, which I find gives a more forward position for steep climbs. • Fair Bicycle offset saddle clamp, as mentioned • I tried 40, 45 and 50 mm stems, but settled on 45 mm. The 50 mm stem that comes stock on the XL felt too stretched out for me on steep tech descents. • I tried out a Fox Live Valve coil shock for my own curiosity and because Pivot sells it as an upgrade. Is it worth the money? No. It's impressive, but this bike simply doesn't need it to avoid pedal-bob, and if it's set closed-biased enough to reliably "prop up" the rear suspension on steep climbs (to make up for the seat angle), it occasionally stays locked when I want it open. On a fire road with occasional bumps, the suspension feels like it's ratcheting up on the smooth bits, then jolting down when you hit a small bump and the shock opens up. I'd prefer a remote manual lockout to hold it locked for a sprint or steep climb, then leave it open the rest of the time. The bike works well with a coil shock. As you'd expect, it's slightly more sensitive than the Float X2, but less tuneable and around 250 g heavier. I setted on 500 lb/in for my 86 kg weight, which is slightly firmer than Fox's recommendation. • I fitted a Level Of Future handlebar for testing. I'm still not sure how much difference it makes to hand/arm fatigue, but I prefer its 40 mm of rise to the stock handlebar's 35 mm. • I've also fitted a mudguard and a stickier front tire for winter (the Firebird came with the Soft, not SuperSoft compound from Continental). All these changes have added a full kilogram to the Frebird's weight. But thanks to the offset seat clamp, it climbs even better than stock. Back when it was new last March. Is it a good bike? While it's frustrating that the seat angle is slacker than I'd like, the Firebird remains one of my all-time favorite enduro bikes. The offset seat clamp is a conspicuously ugly solution, but it does work. What you can't change is a bike's frame construction, sizing, and suspension kinematics, and Pivot nailed those. The heart of the bike is the suspension, which is so well-rounded as to create one of the most versatile bikes around. It feels predictable and rewarding on a wide range of terrain, from self-powered singletrack to burly shuttle laps. It was a close contender for Pinkbike's Bike of the Year 2025 , but while we decided that the uncompromising Orbea Rallon is more exciting, it's precisely the Firebird's vanilla versatility that makes it great. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Staff Rides Reviews Pivot Pivot Firebird Seb Stott Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Can MTB Brakes Stop a Honda Fit? We Tested 10 Four-Piston MTB Brakes
author: Matt Beer
article_text: The Big Brake Test - Pinkbike's Biggest Brake Test Ever https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/H4pOrS8i Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 32:20 Tires matter just as much as suspension and frame geometry, but without solid brakes, it's all worthless. Brake control starts at the brain, analyzing what your eyes see and how your fingers react at the levers, so what makes "good brake" comes down to more than just sheer power. By our standards, a decent gravity brake should deliver consistent, efficient power that's easily accessible, without costing a fortune, either. Below, you'll find all of the brake specifications, compelling test stats, category standouts, our top three brake recommendations, and links to the full reviews. Test Lap and Setup Dario and I cooked up a lap through the Whistler Bike Park that consisted of varied terrain and was repeatable. Within that course, the trails covered fast sweeping corners, sharp roll-overs, and a chunky rock garden. The only thing missing was an absolute brake-scorching sustained descent, something rarely found in a bike park. Each brake was set up as the most powerful gravity brake based on the manufacturer's recommendations (at the time of testing). That consisted of the thickest rotor options on all accounts and mostly metallic pads. The only exceptions were Magura and TRP, which recommended organic pads. Rotor diameters varied between 200 and 203mm based on the manufacturer's specifications. The pads and rotors were bedded in using the same procedure on flat ground before any test laps began. Brake Ace helped out by loading up both of our bikes with front and rear sensors. Brake Ace uses a strain gauge sensor built into the adaptor to measure data ten times a second. The sensors measure front-to-rear brake bias, temperature, average power, major braking events, GPS location, and more. Brake Ace sensors delivered our data. Download the Trailforks App Lever Pull Force Test There's a lot of debate around how hard it is to pull a lever, so we set out to put that argument to rest. We realize that lever setup is personal, dependent on hand size, and can affect lever force. To keep it consistent, all of the brakes were set up to my preferred position. Next, the lever was clamped vertically on a handlebar, and a string was taped to the finger purchase near the end of the lever. The lower end was tied to two water bottles and zeroed on the scale. Dario then wiggled the brake rotor between the pads while I poured water into the hanging bottles. When the rotor could no longer fall freely, the bottles were weighed. As a result, the Hayes Dominion came away on top with the lightest lever engagement, followed by the Trickstuff Maxima in second, and the Hope Tech 4 V4 in third. We also used the average power derived from the Brake Ace data to compare it with the lever-pull force results from the lab. This is where the Trickstuff Maximas show their might. They produced the highest lever pull force-to-power ratio. Car Stop Test Over the course of this big review, the most critical test was stopping a car with bicycle brakes. In fact, we're kind of surprised it worked at all. For this test, Dario towed me behind his Honda Fit, and I attempted to stop the train over ten rounds with each brake. On our cone marker, Dario stopped accelerating and coasted through at 20 km/h. I applied the brakes after passing the cone until the car stopped. There are some caveats: keeping the tow strap tight was daunting to manage, both in terms of entry speed and avoiding any front wheel tangles. I also did my best to apply the brakes consistently while maximizing traction. All things said, the result was consistent with what we found on the trail. The Shimano Saint brakes came out on top by an authoritative margin. Following next were the SRAM Mavens and the Trickstuff Maxima. At the bottom were the Lewis LHP+U4 and the Magura Gustav. Category Highs and Lows Control Best: Hope Tech 4 V4 The Hope has a long lever that rides on a bearing pivot. It’s very smooth, but it can take more lever travel to get into power. The difference between the Tech 4s and the Trickstuff is that the power turns on earlier and in a more linear fashion. If you’re a fan of short levers or immediate grabbing brakes, then adapting to the Tech 4 V4’s long lever travel and smoother modulation may take some time to learn. Once you figure it out, though, they offer supreme control. Worst: Shimano Saint/XTR Shimano brakes all function in a similar way; the short, hooked levers need to travel a fair distance of dead space before the pistons touch the rotors. In sections where you need to brake suddenly, the pads can clamp down on the rotors with a solid “cah-chunk”. If you grab too much lever, it can lock the wheel when you least expect it, or not pull enough. Figuring out where that point in the lever travel is tricky, as they both deliver power quickly once engaged. Shimano does admit that their brakes have a higher degree of deadband with their Ice Tech rotors, but that keeps them running drag-free more often. It’s worth noting that I’ve found thicker rotors to alleviate that deadband and guesswork. Weight Lightest Brake: Formula Cura 4 The Formula Cura 4 brake was the lightest on test at 260g. Despite the caliper being very slim, it holds four 18mm pistons. That doesn’t include an adjustable banjo out of the box, though. Heaviest Brake: SRAM Maven The biggest looking brake takes the cake for heaviest too; that’s SRAM Maven, which weighs in at 367 grams, due in large part to that beefy caliper. Lightest Rotor: Trickstuff Trickstuff wins the competition for lightest rotor, at 155g. The Cura 4’s rotor comes in 41 grams heavier at 196g. Heaviest Rotor: The Maven’s HS2 rotor sits mid-pack at 199g. It’s seriously overshadowed by the 2.3mm thick Lewis Heavy Duty rotor at 268g. Power Most Powerful: Shimano Saint We had a feeling that it would be a hard-fought battle between the Shimano Saint and the SRAM Maven, coming into the Car Stop Test. A few others had moments of glory, but the results spoke for themselves; the Saint has immense power. The Saint brakes stopped Dario’s Honda Fit 2.5m sooner than the Mavens. Based on the average power recorded by the Brake Ace equipment, the Trickstuff Maxima’s 636 W edges out the Saints' 611 W. But, keep in mind that the Maxima dropped down to 4th place in the Car Stop Test - the Saints stood atop that list by a wide margin. Ultimately, it’s the user’s ability to tap into that raw power that makes us award the Saint with top honors in this category. Least Powerful: Lewis LHP+U4 When we take into consideration the Car Stop Test results, weak average power, and our ride impressions, it’s no surprise that the LHPs landed at the bottom here. No matter how hard we pulled on the lever, they lacked the friction and power attained in other brakes at lower lever forces, too. Liveability Best: SRAM Maven When SRAM first launched these brakes, there were mixed reports of wandering bite points (personally, I didn’t find that to be the case). Since then, general opinions have changed. SRAM also added a how-to video to their YouTube Service channel. The bleed procedure is less complicated than other brakes, in part, due to the Bleeding Edge tool. SRAM’s vast dealer network sourcing spare parts means you’ll be more likely to find the parts you need, whether you’re wrenching at home or on the road. The tool-free dials make matching one brake to feel like the other, dead simple. The pad contact dial doesn’t change lever reach position either. On top of that, there are endless ways to build a Maven brake with two pad compounds, three rotor sizes, and, more recently, two new lever SwingLinks. Worst: Trickstuff Maxima Bleeding the Maximas requires a special type of oil (bionol) that you likely won’t find at your local shop. The bleed screw on the master cylinder is a small M4 thread, a piece not found in a universal bleed kit. The oily procedure is lengthy, difficult to achieve a solid bleed, and not easy to replicate between front and rear brakes. Value Best: SRAM It took a while for SRAM to deliver with a set of big stoppers, but they hit the mark, and then some. The power is massive, should you decide to bulk up on rotors and metallic pads, but the price isn’t outrageous. A tamer setup can be achieved too with smaller diameter discs and organic pads, or, as mentioned, the less progressive SwingLinks. Adding to the tuning options, the ease of ownership and the ability to source spare parts. On paper and from our point of view, the power-to-price of the Mavens is an unbeatable gravity-based brake. Worst: Trickstuff If the price alone doesn’t stand out as a hurdle, then the low liveability of the Maximas should. Bleeding them is a pain. There’s a ton of power, but that doesn’t come quickly - it’s buried deep at the bottom of the lever engagement. That would be more accessible, except the brake lacks any adjustment outside of the lever throw. Personal Picks: Amongst the seven other brakes on test, there are a bunch that sit in the middle ground that are great. After extensive tests, laps, and chit chat, it doesn't surprise me that Dario and I ended up with the same three brakes we'd most likely recommend. If we were spending our hard-earned dough, here's how they'd line up. Dario's Picks 1. SRAM Maven Power and adjustment were my main reasons here. Getting the bite point to hit exactly where you want it and having the power come on quickly were easily achieved. 2. TRP EVO Pro The consistency and simplicity of this system make them an easy choice for all sorts of riders. The revised lever has a nice level of adjustment, but even the older model feels competitive and high performance. 3. Hope Tech 4 V4 The sensitivity and controllable power curve of the Hopes put them in contention for my all-out favorite, but a couple of nitpicks kept them from the top. Great feel, easy adjustment, big fan. Matt's Picks: 1. Hope Tech 4 V4 The characteristic that won me over here was the immediate yet gentle rise in power. Basically, the earlier in the lever pull you can reach the meat of the power, the more efficient you will be at braking. But it can’t be an overwhelming surge - it has to arrive smoothly in a way you sense the friction. The Tech 4 V4s do just that. Deeper down, extra power is gained gradually. Helping position the English brakes as my top choice is the build quality, found in the two-way lever adjustment dials, the one-piece caliper, and Hope’s extensive spare parts catalogue. Oh, and the reusable hose fittings. A new thread-on bleeding cup doesn’t cut down time in the workshop, but it does help to produce an extra crispy bite point. 2. SRAM Maven Silver I like that SRAM hit the limiter with the potential power of a fully loaded set of Mavens. I’ve yet to hear any complaints about them lacking power. The lever action is also a basic action; pull a bit to get power, pull the bit further and get it all. They don't have to turn tires into dirt daggers, though. The immense power can be toned down substantially with the organic pads - As Dario pointed out, the tool-free adjusters allow you to dial in the bite point and reach without compromise. Being under the SRAM umbrella, parts for Mavens and knowledge about the brakes are widespread, something that only Shimano can compete with. 3. TRP Evo Pro The Evo Pro makes a strong case for being the most “normal” brake here. The revised lever shape from the previous DHR Evo fits more hand sizes than before and now includes tool-free reach and bite point adjusters. Powerwise, it’s not comparable to the Maven or Saint, but a short amount of deadband makes it an effective brake. Price, weight, and liveability all fall in the middle of the road, making it tough to ignore. Individual Reviews: Formula Cura 4 Trickstuff Maxima Lewis LHP+U4 Hayes Dominion A4 • BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist Hope Tech 4 V4 • BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist Magura Gustav • BUY AT: Jenson Shimano Saint BUY AT: Backcountry Shimano XTR • BUY AT: Jenson SRAM Maven Silver • BUY AT: Thunder Mountain TRP Evo Pro • BUY AT: Thunder Mountain Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Big Brake Test Reviews Ridden And Rated Brakes Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: The Crankbrothers Stamp EVO is a Comically Large Flat Pedal
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: There may be more ways to design a flat pedal than there are to skin a cat, and the market reflects that. Every brand has a slightly different take, with small differences here and there to try and stand out from the crowd. Boldly standing out on merit of size alone is the new Crankbrothers Stamp EVO, which may just be the largest flat pedal you can buy. These huge aluminum steps have a few unique features, but their primary calling card is going to be the size. With two options to choose from, small-footed folks have a variant too, but I'll be focusing on the Large version today. Stamp EVO Large Details • Weight: 464g (pair) • Pins per side: 13 • Usable platform dimensions (W x L): 114 x 129mm • Internals: 2 plastic IGUS bearings • Two pedal colors, four plastics colors • MSRP: $200 USD / EUR / GBP • crankbrothers.com Stamp EVO Pedals https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/s2nzM8w5 Copied Live 00:00 07:11 07:11 Small. Large. Features Both Large and Small Stamp EVO use an aluminum body with plastic bashguards on the outboard side, allowing for aesthetic repair and color accents, should you please. Those plastics come off easily once you've removed the outer corner pins, and will keep the aluminum from getting marred when you lay the bike down. The Large EVOs are 114mm wide, and 129mm long (AKA: huge), giving them ample room to stomp around on. Crankbrothers recommends a minimum shoe size of a US 10 to run that pedal, with the small fitting US 3-10. I wear an US 11.5 (45 for the rest of y'all), for visual reference. With 13 grub screws per side, you can wind some in and out to alter the relative height. One key exception is those outside corner pins, which have to be set in the middle, as they go all the way through the pedal to hold the plastics on. The guts - a full parts breakdown can be found here . Photo from Crankbrothers. The internals are simple and serviceable, with two IGUS bearings holding the spindle in place. I don't have nearly enough time on the pedals to comment on durability, but so far they're spinning smoothly and are free of play. At the leading edge, the pedal is 15mm thick - the pin at that leading edge is 23mm end to end. The middle of the pedal is 12mm thick, with a 17mm end-to-end pin towards the outside edge. Concave, but not extreme. Plastics doing their job. Comparisons The obvious comparison here is the previous high water mark for gigantic flat pedals: the Pedaling Innovations Catalyst . Very long, but relatively narrow, these have been the primary option for folks searching for the most for the past decade. The Stamp EVO matches the length within a millimeter, and adds 20mm of usable width. Pedaling Innovations. Cardboard proxy compared to Stamp EVO. Tectonic. Compared to Stamp EVO. The Tectonic Alibi has a similar length at 128mm, but a narrower width at 108mm. That shape feels effective without adding too much bulk, and matches my foot size well. Once the whole forefoot is accounted for, I don't find myself hunting for more surface area on a flat. I'm sure folks with bigger feet might warrant more standing room, but they'll have to chime in below to flex their big dogs. Closing Thoughts Riding with the Stamp EVOs has been uneventful, save for the occasional person pointing and commenting on my gigantic pedals. The grip is just fine - not stellar, not horrible. With the shoes I'm used to riding in, I'd say they feel slightly less bitey than options with sharper/smaller diameter pins, be they threaded or straight-sided. Part of the issue could be how spread out the surface area of the shoe is, and the fact that there are times where your foot isn't even in contact with all of the pins at once. For folks who like a single locked-in position, these probably aren't your best fit. For those on and off the bike a lot, or who like to reposition often, the jumbo Stamps might be worth trying out. Because there's so much surface area to stand on, it often feels like you've landed in a pretty decent spot, even if it's not the perfect position on the pedal. I bet you're wondering: Dario, they're so big, don't you hit things on trail? Yes, yes I do. As the plastic guards can attest, the edges of the pedals feel like they contact more random trailside obstacles than the average, so keep your technique clean. Those scuffs and contact moments haven't amounted to anything too scary, but it's something to be aware of, especially if you're doing a lot of rocky, tight, awkward riding. As to the guards themselves, I think they warrant existing on a pedal this big, only because they will probably pick up more scars than the average in a shorter period of time. I don't mind my parts showing some wear and tear if it feels earned, but in this case you may be happy to have a refreshed set of plastics now and then. I'm curious if these appeal to the folks out there with massive feet, but for me I'm pretty satisfied with the scale of pedals as they are. Those Tectonic Alibis are still my personal favorite, so maybe that's big enough in my book. This pains me to look at. The Crankbrothers Stamp EVO retails for $199.99 USD / EUR / GBP, and you can learn more at their website . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Pedals CrankBrothers Crankbrothers Stamp Evo Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: The Good, The Bad & The Ugly: Pinkbike Tech Editors' Preferred Handlebars
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: The DMR O'Dub Bar in 31.8 mm and a 50 mm rise is the most compliant handlebar we have tested to date. I've had a bit of an obsession with handlebar design over the last few years, investigating claims of vibration damping and compliance , and trying to tease apart any relationship between the two. The vast majority of the bars we tested offer a typical 8° backsweep and 5° upsweep, with very few deviating from those numbers. This makes good sense, given that most trail and enduro bikes come with a bar of those dimensions. It's funny then that the handlebar I now run is a dramatic departure from what is considered standard fare. I have my reasons, spelled out below. Despite key geometry features changing over the last decade (reach has grown and effective top tube length has shortened), handlebar geometry geometry hasn't changed much - at least in terms of sweep. Should it have? Probably not. I'm not sure there's justification for any changes on trail and enduro bikes, given that we spec cockpits with downhill handling at the top of the list of priorities. Bar rise is increasing, though. Many riders are appreciating the benefits of a higher stack, and what's more, high-rise bars tend to be more compliant than flatter ones . In the end, it comes down to personal preference. Here's what us tech editors are choosing to ride just now. My contribution to this piece ticks all of the boxes. It's good (in a small way), bad in most ways and, incredibly ugly. Geoff is a 31.8 mm aluminum piece from budget brand Planet-X; he boasts a 45-degree backsweep, a plethora of hand positions, and a generous amount of real estate for accoutrements - ultra endurance bike-packers, rejoice. Alas, I cannot be accurately described as such. Jessie-May Morgan Position: Tech Editor, Keeper of Small Zoo Height: 5'4" / 162.6 cm Wingspan: 164 cm Current Handlebar: Geoff Clamp diameter: 31.8 mm Material: 6061 aluminum Width: 720 mm Rise: 13 mm Backsweep: 45-degrees My reason for running this de-reacher handlebar on a trail bike arises out of misfortune. I have had a chronic shoulder issue for almost two years, resulting from a partial thickness rotator cuff tear. The following months and years have seen my shoulder reposition itself to a more internally rotated position. From this position, it is sore to move in a few directions. I am in the process of retraining it to sit more rearward, in a more externally rotated position, from which point it feels strong and, importantly, pain-free. My theory is this: by positioning my hands splayed out, my elbows naturally tuck inwards, and my shoulders naturally rotate outwards. It is solving a very niche problem while being horrible in most other ways, including: • There is no good position for the brake levers • Handling is terrible • It's heavy The silver linings? It's immeasurably better than not riding at all, and it is yours for just £29.99. Like the Jones bars it mimics - which are considerably pricier, and also available in carbon and titanium, if you care - Geoff was made with the intention of offering improved comfort to those who are riding very long distances - like, Silk Road Race distances. The logic is that the severe backsweep allows the angle at the wrist to be straighter, which is meant to be more comfortable. That works well for some people, and not for others - I think I fall into the second camp, as my wrists start to complain after only 5 km. That said, they are conditioned for a mere 8-degrees of backsweep, which is typical for trail and enduro bikes. For technical trail riding, they are not - not that I've done much of that lately. Steering is wildly sluggish, the only benefit of which is that it really forces you to be more mindful about anticipating the trail ahead, and accentuates that feeling of needing to direct the bike through the hips. It's quite the education. As my shoulder rehab progresses, I'll be looking to switch to something a little more handling-friendly, and a little less jarring to look at - 16-degree backsweep, for example. I've been experimenting with higher-rise bars recently. In my new position, I’ve had the pleasure of being able to test multiple bikes, which has been an absolute joy, but one thing became very apparent: cockpit matters a lot. I’d almost say cockpit setup can create one of the most distinctive ride characteristic changes of anything on the bike. Try hopping on a friend’s bike for part of a lap, and you’ll see. I personally can really feel when the sweep, brake lever position, and rise aren’t what I’m used to. Stephane Pelletier Position: Content Manager Height: 6'3" / 191 cm Current Handlebar: Deity Highside Clamp diameter: 35 mm Material: 6061 aluminum Width: 780 mm Rise: 50 mm Backsweep: 9-degrees Upsweep: 5-degree As such, I’ve got a few sets of these Deity Highside bars kicking around that I typically swap from bike to bike, especially if I’m not getting along with the stock bars too much. They’re marketed as a dirt jumping and slopestyle handlebar, but I’ve found them to work just fine across other disciplines, too. So here it is: 50mm rise, 35mm bar clamp diameter, and cut down to 780mm. I’ve also played around with the same bar in a 31.8mm equivalent, and I may actually prefer the slight compliance gains from the narrower diameter. Other than the higher-than-normal rise, it’s a pretty standard shape most riders would be familiar with, although it does feature slightly more backsweep at 9 degrees, alongside the typical 5-degree upsweep. Readers who follow some of my ramblings will know I typically like to size down on bikes, or at least lean toward the smaller end of my frame size spectrum. This stems from my dirt jump background and a general penchant for fun over outright speed. I like a bike to be a bit more manoeuvrable and playful, rather than soled focused on all-out stability and speed. One unfortunate byproduct of this strategy is that smaller bikes can sometimes feel cramped, and they also typically don’t come specced with especially tall bars. Going with the higher rise, although slightly reducing effective reach, does give me a higher stack and longer distance between my feet and hands. For me, it puts me in a really comfortable and confident riding position. I’ve swapped to these trusty Highsides on a few bikes and felt a noticeable improvement in confidence and stability almost immediately. Other notable setup details include that I typically have the bars rolled slightly forward, partly to keep the reach as long as possible and partly to line up the flat part of the bar parallel to the ground. I’m not sure what angle I run my brake levers at; I’ve never measured, but I basically line up my arms in a neutral riding position and set them up so I have minimal bend at the wrist. I’d say around 45 degrees if I had to guess. Also worth noting, I’ve still been running the funny little silicone brake lever covers on this particular ride. I picked them up at Sea Otter, and, partly because I didn't want to take them off and partly because they actually feel quite good, they’ve stayed on. Although I do wish they had given me a black set instead. You can learn more about those in my recent Check Out article. I've gone all over the map when it comes to handlebar options, trying a wide array of sweep, rise, and width options before settling on what feels like home for me now. Both of my favorites have one distinct feature to them: as little backsweep as possible. I like the handling feel, the ergonomics, and the ease of finding a good roll that this facilitates. Aluminum is a perk, the shot-peened finish keeps controls and grips in place without too much torque, and the 31.8mm bore wins core points with bar-heads everywhere. Dario DiGiulio Position: Tech Editor, shelf reacher Height: 6'3" / 191 cm Wingspan: 199.5 cm Current Handlebar: Renthal Fatbar / Pembree GCS Clamp diameter: 31.8mm Material: 7000-series aluminum Width: 770mm Rise: 40mm Backsweep: 7 degrees Upsweep: 5 degrees Renthal is the tried and true when it comes to combining all of these factors into one consistent product, but Pembree is a welcomed newcomer hitting all the same targets. For my generally preferred rise of 30-40mm there are a few options between both brands, and I'd call the feel quite similar between the two. It's hard to capture bar roll in a way that portrays the feel, but I aim to have the face plane of the handlebar in parallel with the head angle of the bike. Relative to the norm, my bars tend to be rolled back a little bit. I've run 770mm width for as long as I can remember, and would rather run narrower than wider if I had to settle elsewhere. If ever I ride a set of 800mm bars, I'm surprised at how slow and awkward the handling can feel. Like anything, you adapt pretty quickly, but I'm happy sticking with my tried and true setup. With a 40-45mm stem, the flatter profile of these bars seems to hit a sweet spot for the handling characteristic I want out of most bikes. More backsweep is something I can adjust to, but if I want a bike to feel like home quickly, popping a set of Renthals on is a quick way to settle in. Bikepacking side note: I've tried a few alt bars for extremely long days / months in the saddle, and I think I'm still happy with normal sweep figures even for that use case. I rode a set of Jones bars across the country, and my wrists were unhappy the whole time. I ran some SQ Labs 12° bars on the Colorado Trail, and found them comfortable and normal enough handling-wise. At this point, a set of the bars you see here with a set of inner-barends provides enough variety in hand position to keep the hands and wrists happy. Maybe my body has just morphed to fit these shapes over time, but I find them perfectly ergonomic. Handlebars, like all touch points on the bike, are a personal preference. Heck, I even find that they can vary from “perfect” to “just okay” depending on the bike. Bar roll also plays a massive, well, role in how the bike handles. On enduro bikes, I tend to like higher-rise bars with less backsweep. Usually, I roll them to a neutral (grips appear level with the ground) or a slight positive position. It likely has to do with the interactions between the head tube angle and fork offset. Higher average speeds on downhill bikes equate to smaller bar movements. In this case, I might prefer a bar with more sweep or a mild negative roll, likely due to the longer crown offsets specified on those bikes, and more subtle steering inputs needed to turn a downhill bike. Matt Beer Position: Tech Editor, recycling manager Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Wingspan: 176 cm Current Handlebar: Race Face Era (780mm version) Clamp diameter: 35mm Material: Carbon Width: 770mm Rise: 40mm Backsweep: 8 degrees Upsweep: 5 degrees Some of my favorites over the last few seasons have been the OneUp V2 Carbon and Race Face Era handlebars. Both bars are made from carbon, which doesn’t inherently make them stiff. In fact, Jessie-May Morgan’s article on handlebar compliance , these two options ranked in the upper half of the sample pool, in terms of measured deflection. The cool thing about each one of these bars is that both OneUp and Race Face change the stiffness, depending on the chosen width. OneUp has two stock width options (760 and 800mm) while Race Face has three (760, 780, and 800mm). I trim both the 780mm Eras and the 800mm OneUp’s down to 770mm for the Nomad. On some shorter travel bikes, I might go as narrow as 760mm, depending on the fit of the bike. Each bar is designed around a 5-degree upsweep and 8-degree backsweep, which I find to be the most comfortable and controlled for my riding technique. Height-wise, I’ve been rocking the 35mm rise from OneUp and the 40mm rise from Race Face on my Nomad. On the downhill bike, I’ve been enjoying the Burgtec Ride Wide Enduro 30mm rise bar , which has a 31.8mm bar clamp. They’re on the softer side, but I can’t say it’s ever put me off kilter or caused any concerns. My go-to DH width is 780mm, but again, that can change plus or minus 5mm based on the overall fit of the bike. The Ride Wides have a 9-degree backsweep, which balances out 52mm fork offsets well for me. When a 48mm offset is spec'd, I find that an 8-degree backsweep reacts best. I wouldn’t consider myself overly picky when it comes to handlebars. The right amount of picky, sure. Carbon or aluminum is fine with me, and the same goes with 35mm vs 31.8mm diameters as long as they're not bone-jarringly stiff. I ran 780mm width bars for years, but after a couple of Field Tests where the other test riders were running 770mm I decided to switch, purely to make swapping bikes between easier during those group tests. For me, 770mm works well for most bikes, and then I’ll go down to 760mm for XCish bikes. Side note: I'm a big fan of higher rise bars and shorter stems on short travel bikes - it's an easy way to turn a serious XC race machine into something that encourages goofing off. Mike Kazimer Position: Editor in Chief Height: 5'11" / 180 cm Wingspan: 184 cm Current Handlebar: BikeYoke Barmate Clamp diameter: 35 mm Material: Carbon Width: 770 mm Rise: 42 mm Backsweep: 9-degrees Upsweep: 6-degree I have gone higher with my typical rise over the last few years, and nowadays I’d pick something around 40mm if I had to choose. That’s the rise amount I’m running even on bikes with a relatively tall stack like my Raaw Madonna - I just run fewer spacers than usual under the stem to keep the front end from feeling too high. On the Raaw I’m currently running a BikeYoke BarMate with a 42mm rise, 9° backsweep, and 6° upsweep. BikeYoke recently decided to take a slightly different route when it comes to handlebar measurements, which is worth a read ( here ). It's an interesting concept, which entails measuring "stack" rather than "rise." Stack is measured differently from the way that most brands measure rise - it’s from the center of the bar to the center of the out edge of the bar, as opposed to rise, which usually gets measured from the center of the bar to where the flatter part of the bar begins. I do think it makes things more confusing for the end consumer. Take the bars I’m running - they have a stack of 65mm, which makes people think that they’re extra-high-rise bars, at least when browsing through pages of options online, when they’re not - they have a more typical 42mm of rise. I like BikeYoke’s concept, but it’s going to be tricky to convince other manufacturers and riders to adopt it as the default measuring method. A 42mm Burgtec Enduro stem holds the Barmate Carbon bars in place. That pattern in the center of the bar is a titanium mesh that BikeYoke says helps distribute the clamping forces evenly. Side profile. Not a French-flat brake lever angle, but not pointing directly at the ground either. As for the bars themselves, they’re nice and comfy, and devoid of any flashy logos, which I like. I run them close to vertical or maybe slightly rolled back, but not by much. Other bars that I’ve been getting along with recently include Race Face’s 40mm rise Era carbon bars, and PNW Components Loam Carbon bar in a 38mm rise. Where to Buy Ready for some new bars? Want to hop on the higher rise bandwagon? Click below and add them to your cart. ( We may earn an affiliate commission on things you buy through these links. Learn more. ) Race Face Era, 40mm rise: $140.99 (25% OFF) at REI PNW Components Loam Carbon, 38mm rise: $119.99 (25% OFF) at REI Deity Highside, 50mm rise: $84.99 at Jenson Renthal Fatbar, 40mm rise: $107.85 at Performance OneUp V2 Carbon, 40mm rise: $127.99 (20% OFF) at Backcountry Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Editors Choice Handlebars Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: The Good, The Bad, and The Ugly: What Fenders Pinkbike's Editors Actually Use
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: We review a lot of kit here at Pinkbike. In fact, sometimes it can feel like a constant merry-go-round of helmets, gloves, tires, or if we're extra lucky the latest bikes. It often transpires that we spend little time on the things we actually like most and more time trying to understand why things don't fit, work or feel as good as the manufacturer insists they should. Sometimes those preferred items aren't the flashiest, like the lowly fender here. While it might not inspire lust like other components, fenders are undeniably critical, especially for those of us who preside in wetter climes. Nobody likes mud in their eye, but we do have preferences over how we avoid such things. MuckyNutz MugGuard Long There's not too much one can say about a plastic fender - they're one of those simple utilitarian items that either does or doesn't work. In this case, it works very well. I went looking for a low-profile fender with as much coverage as possible, and landed on the MuckyNutz as the one that fit my needs best. Dario DiGiulio Position: Tech Editor, glasses guy Preferred features: Easy swap between bikes, huge coverage Chosen fender: MuckyNutz MugGuard Long Price: $30 USD In order to keep the massive extrusion stable, the MugGuard relies on 6 zip ties or removable bands to hold things in place. The lower mounts on the fork legs are longer than typical fenders, and the double row is key to keeping the fender centered and away from the edge knobs of your tire. Speaking of those removable bands, MuckyNutz ships each fender with a roll of somewhat elastic hook and loop material that you can cut to length to affix your fender. Though I'm using standard zip ties here, I've come to prefer the removable nature of the alternative, as you can more easily swap the fender around between bikes or remove it for travel. The only real downside to this massive mud guard is a material aversion to holding stickers very well. Because what are fenders for if not to provide a swath of sticker real estate? I'm sure a more thorough alcohol scrub or even some sanding would help, but the pebbly texture does help shed muck, so I'll leave it alone for now. I don't imagine I'll be running this titanic fender once things dry out, but the slightly smaller MugGuard does make a great all-year item, and is less finicky to center than the long version. I'd love to use the stock Fox option, but I've had bad luck with the durability of those - especially the XL. The lower loop-style mounts always seem to break under regular usage, which results in a floppy, noisy fender. Fox Mud Guard I run a fender year round, rain or shine - to me, a fork looks like it's missing something if one isn't installed. I also hate getting stuff in my eyes, whether that's mud in the winter or dust in the summer. I'll often run a cheap, flexible plastic fender simply because that's what I have lying around, but that's not my preferred option. Mike Kazimer Position: Gear Director, Preferred features: Umm, that it works? Chosen fender: Fox Mud Guard Price: $25 USD In my ideal world, all forks would come with a fender designed specifically for that model, one that offers good coverage and easily bolts on, no zip ties required. That's finally starting to happen, and over the last few years Fox and RockShox started making fenders that integrate well with their forks. It's Fox's regular-length fender that's shown here, which does the trick, but I prefer the XL option - as the name implies, it offers more coverage on both sides of the fork arch. The shape works well, and I like that it leaves room next to the stanchions for mud to escape through, rather than creating a shelf that could potentially drag debris into the seals. Fox XL Mud Guard Next to lock-on grips, mud guards could be one of the most practical wet-weather additions you could make to your bike, since holding onto your bike and being able to see where you're going are mildly important. Fox's XL Mud Guard for the 36, 38, and 40 (different width but the same idea) is my favorite fender out there right now mainly for its security, shape, and fixing points. Matt Beer Position: Tech Editor, recycling manager Preferred features: Doesn't use single-use zip ties, mounts securely without rattling Chosen fender: Fox XL Mud Guard Price: $25 USD Fox make a short and long version, but if you're going to have a mud guard, you may as well go all in with the XL size. Both fenders use four fixing points; two bolts at the back of the arch and one to each of the bleed valve nuts, doing away with any bothersome noises while riding. Another bonus point is that the fender doesn't rely on single-use zip ties. Nearly all of the longer travel forks on the market have had small mounting points drilled into the back of the arches for years now, but Fox was the first to offer a factory-spec fender (although it's still an aftermarket purchase and rarely provided with bike). That means it's specifically shaped to Fox forks only, but it does come with the benefit of shedding mud from the seals, as Mike pointed out, and it also avoids sand-blasting the bottom of the fork arch, like the flexy zip tie versions can induce, since it's held firmly in place. Due to the overall insignificance of the 92-g weight and sleek appearance, I haven't found any reason to remove it in summer months either - just be mindful of how it integrates with some tailgate pads and bike racks which hold the bike vertically by a front wheel basket. For such a simple, lightweight piece of plastic, they are highly effective. I found this fender in a bin in my garage, I like the way it looks, and it's simple and effective. I don't remember where it's from, and I love that there are no clues. No event name, no brand name, no writing at all. Perfect. (Excessive branding is my least favorite thing about the bike industry, so if a little artsy-looking plastic thing on my bike can give me a break from that, I'll gladly take it.) Alicia Leggett Position: Making that up day by day Preferred features: Simple, effective, aesthetically nice, unbranded Chosen fender: ? Price: ? This fender is a little smaller than some others I've liked in the past, but it still does its job well. That's mainly what there is to say about its function. I put it on and from that point on, my tire has flung less mud toward my face. The biggest decider is that I'm in a life moment of being really into Mexico. I've been putting way more effort than ever before into improving my Spanish, which is structured in a way that makes so much more sense than English (admittedly a low bar for sense-making). I'm a little bit in love with Mexican culture, which I think falls into the Venn diagram overlap between logical and sexy . Throw in more creativity than I see in the US, delicious food, and lots of my own great memories? I'll call it a win. Could I have been neater with my zip tie cutting? Totally. Does that keep me up at night? Not at all. There are plenty of fenders out there that make marginal gains toward a tiny bit more effectiveness (see the rest of this article), but really, the simplest version also works just fine. My biggest complaint is that I don't love single-use zip ties. If I were planning to move this fender around (or even if I were more principled), I'd replace the zip ties with little Velcro strips, but really, this fender is staying on my personal bike, so that's a really, really minor gripe. Bonus points for matching some of my home decor. A mud guard is a year-round essential in Scotland, owing to the gloriously moist conditions we both enjoy and complain about in equal measure. Dry conditions do occasionally arise, but never for long enough that I would actually bother removing the mudguard. After all, there basically are no downsides. Jessie-May Morgan Position: News Writer & Technical Editor at Pinkbike & Velo Preferred features: Plain, comprehensive coverage, easy to swap between forks Chosen fender: Mudhugger EVO Long Price: £27.50 (*£30.99 for bolt-on) My go-to is the Mudhugger EVO Long, of the Velcro strap-on variety, compatible with the latest Fox 36 and 38 forks, and the latest Rockshox ZEB (maybe others, too). The bolt-on version looks better (see Sarah's), but the Velcro one is probably the easiest and fastest to swap between bikes. That, and its massive coverage, make it the best option for me. I wouldn't necessarily recommend it, but the coverage on this one is so good you can often get away with not using any eyewear even in the most sloppy conditions. The Velcro straps are a little abrasive, hence my incredibly careful application of Gorilla tape (that I'm sure will be appreciated by many) to the legs and arch. They'll also wear out after a winter of riding. Mudhugger sell replacement straps (4 for £4.99), or you can zip-tie. Pure glam *More aesthetically pleasing ways of mounting the Mudhugger EVO are available, see below Mudhugger EVO Bolt-On Bike fenders are actually the best. Especially this time of year, I find it hard to ride without one! This Mudhugger EVO Bolt-On fender is my go-to for wet-weather rides and I've been impressed with how well it works. Occasionally, I'll notice it when a little rock gets scooped up into it and makes a noise before it flies out the back, but the fender always shoots them back down onto the trail. It's also quiet even on chunky descents. I wouldn't recommend it if you have peanut buttery or clay-like mud since that will build up and cause the wheel to stop moving, but for the riding I do around Squamish, it's perfect. Sarah Moore Position: Content Manager Preferred features: Keeps mud out of my eyes Chosen fender: Mudhugger EVO Bolt-On (Long) Price: £30.99 GBP While I made a brief exception right after I got laser eye surgery and the doctor expressly told me not to get dirt in my eyes, I almost never wear glasses when mountain biking. I know I should, they're a great piece of safety gear, but in cooler weather, I fog them up, and in warmer temperatures, I sweat so much onto them that I have to wipe them every stop. I can usually manage goggles in the bike park as long as I make sure I don't put them on until the second before I drop in, but for the rest of the time, I rely on my fender to keep trail debris out of my eyes. It also keeps my face and clothes a whole lot cleaner, so even if you do ride with glasses, a fender is a worthwhile purchase. In the summer, I'll generally ride a shorter fender that bolts to my fork like the one Kaz has in this article or just a cheap plastic fender that is attached with zip-ties. We had some Marsh Guard x Pinkbike ones at Crankworx a couple of years ago and I had a stash for while, but I think I've made my way through all of them. I've never actually made my own fender out of a Dollar Store placemat, but I might have to try that this summer! I think mountain bikes should come with front fenders fitted, so I was pleased to see the latest Scott Ransom and Voltage do just that. The Syncros Trail 2 Fender fits Fox 36 and 38 forks and is easy to remove and re-install. It also doesn't cost much to buy on its own ( I found it online for under £10) and is pretty light at 63 g. I would like more coverage, though, as some spray can come up vertically from the trailing part of the wheel. Seb Stott Position: Tech editor, professional pedant Preferred features: Quiet, easily-removable, good coverage Chosen fender: Syncros Trail 2 Fender Price: £9.50 Otherwise, I tend to strap a Mudhugger or similar mudguard on when things get muddy. For wet weather, I like the longer version that extends almost a quarter of the way around the wheel for best coverage, but I will happily keep the shorter version on all year round. If I have some, I like to use reusable zip ties so it's easier to remove and re-install on another bike when needed. These seem to be easier and longer-lasting than the Velcro straps that sometimes come with mudguards. A hastily-installed Mudhugger with slapdash zip-tie installation, but it's done the job without fuss for several years now. Here's a mudguard secured to a non-Kashima fork with reusable zip ties - how thrifty! Posted In: Reviews and Tech Editors Choice Reviews Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly: What Grips Pinkbike Editors Actually Use
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: It's hard to think of anything more mind numbing to write or read than a grip review. As such, we haven't published many of those historically, despite the dizzying array of rubber tubes you can buy at any given bike shop. Test bikes tend to come with a similarly wide variety, leading to plenty of tests-within-tests, and allowing us to try plenty of different options to inform our choices. We do of course try new intriguing options on the market, but suffice to say they won't get as much press as some hot new trail bike or an electronic exoskeleton. Cutting to the chase, here are the grips we each choose to run and why. Most grips feel a bit overly complicated to me. There's a lot of fuss over a few millimeters of rubber that will rub away under your sweaty palms after a little while, and ultimately end up feeling like a warm wad of rubber, which is pretty much what my favorite grips are. The Longnecks don't have a top, bottom, or side - if you can manage to slip the bar into them and tighten a single 3mm bolt you're done with installation. Dario DiGiulio Position: Tech Editor & used tire hoarder Grip: ODI Longneck Lock-On Glove Size: XL Key Element: Unidirectional pattern Price: $30 USD Easy. Somewhat durable end piece and a MUSA stamp. I tend to ride without gloves whenever I can, so my hands have developed some nice callouses that fit the shape of these nicely. Familiarity being the ultimate performance enhancer, I'll be sticking with these for the foreseeable future. These also seem to be ideal for gloveless riding due to the increased moisture wicking of the ribbed texture. Durability on the Longnecks is excellent, thanks to the rubber ribs wearing evenly and slowly. I've managed to stretch a set an entire year, swapping bike to bike for daily riding, only retiring them after the end plugs were too busted to stay on. My key to longevity is simply rotating the grip when a bald spot develops, and boom : you have a fresh surface under the palm. I've mindlessly grasped plenty of other colorful options in bike shops, but so far nothing matches the shape and tack of these simple beauties. Plus, they come in the only color they need to: black. As a contact point, grips can make or break your ride experience. I'm a glove guy 95% of the time as I find that they increase the friction without needing to clench the grip with more force. My grip of choice these days is the Ergon GDH Team, which bears an uncanny resemblance to the ODI Elite Pro, my previous favorite. Both remind me of the features that I loved about the underrated, but now retired, ODI Intense grip . Matt Beer Position: Tech Editor & dishwasher re-organizer Grip: Ergon GDH Team Glove Size: MD Key Element: Tapered, raised palm pad Price: $39.95 USD I don't want to call the GDH Team grips a direct copy because there are major differences - the main one being the rubber is softer and tackier. I've noticed that the combination of the rubber's tackiness and waffle patterns lower the chance of slipping a grip when wet compared to mushroom-style patterns too. For shapes, the top features a raised palm pad that matches the curve of your hand as you make a fist around the handlebars. Underneath, you'll find the popular waffle pattern that stops short of the inner thumb area to avoid unnecessary rubbing. A flange also wraps part way around to protect your hand from biting into the grip clamp while leaving room to reach the dropper post and shift levers. There's also a trick replaceable plastic end cap which I first thought was hokey. Integrating into the end of the grip not only makes them replaceable, but it protects the grip's plastic sleeve and the end of the handlebar itself - a quality function if your carbon bars taper to a thin diameter at the outer reaches. A single clamp locks them down with a 3mm allen key head which is much less prone to stripping out. Color-lovers are out of luck though as these clamps only come in a neutral gun metal finish. I'd consider myself moderately picky when it comes to grip choice – I'm not beholden to only one specific make and model, but a grip needs to meet certain criteria in order to earn a permanent spot on a bike. First, it needs to be reasonably thin. I have big hands, but that doesn't mean I want it to feel like I'm holding onto a Coke can for hours at a time – a diameter around 30 millimeters works best for me. Since I'm constantly swapping between bikes I typically stick with lock-on grips over push-ons; it makes things a lot easier, and I don't need to waste time messing around with hair spray and safety wire. Mike Kazimer Position: Cycling Gear Director Grip: Peaty's Monarch Glove Size: XL Key Element: Soft rubber, thin diameter Price: $31.99 USD I go gloveless a fair bit, especially during the summer, so I prefer a grip with at least some raised bits of texture to keep my sweaty palms from slipping off. Ideally the sweet spot for the textured portions is fairly large – I hate needing to stop and re-orient a grip a to find the ideal position. The textured portion on the underside of the grip comes in handy on extra-humid or rainy days. There's a knurled or a mushroom pattern to choose from. My current favorite grips are the new Peaty’s Monarch lock-on. I snagged a couple of pairs earlier this year, one with a knurled pattern and the other with a mushroom pattern that feels similar to the Longnecks that Dario chose. I like them both, but if I had to choose I'd pick the mushroom pattern for the extra comfort it provides. The rubber is nice and soft on both versions, and the 30-32mm taper feels perfect. They're sort of like a mash-up of the DMR Deathgrip and the Burgtec Bartender, two other grips that are on my shortlist of favorites, so it's not really surprising that I've been digging these. I'm not too worried about grip color, but for riders who want to color match everything there are five rubber color options, and 12 different lock ring colors to choose from. SQlab 70X 2.0 Pro The Raaw Jibb LT that I'm testing at the moment arrived with Ergon GD1 grips, which are just fine. I'd swap them out if I was having an awful time with them, but they're a decent fit and the rubber texture is good for riding without gloves, which I will do occasionally throughout summer. Most of the time I ride with gloves that have a very thin palm - I find gloves with padding horribly uncomfortable, as they seem to generate a hot spot underneath the padded sections and make me feel somewhat disconnected from the bike's handling. Jessie-May Morgan Position: Tech Editor & News Writer Grip: SQlab 70X 2.0 Pro Glove Size: S Key Element: Raised palm, soft, slow rebound rubber with a thin(ish) diameter Price: $34.95 USD The levels of comfort they provide can't match the SQlab 70 X 2.0 Pro grip, though. Like just about every other product made by SQlab, they are designed with ergonomics in mind (and the name is too long). The 70X grips are available in three sizes; S, M and L. All are 139mm long, but have different circumferences. At 1/3 of the length, they measure 98mm, 103mm, and 100mm respectively. SQlab provide this sizing guide to help you determine which one is best for you. I have small hands, so use the 70X grips in size small. The fit is excellent. Low and behold, I don't get on well with oversized grips marketed at those with big hands (though it was interesting to try) - the experience is too akin to trying to hold onto a pair of toilet rolls. Needless to say the arm pump is savage. I'd say there's justification for making the small 70X grips shorter, as there is still a fair bit of unused grip on the inboard side, but the feel of the grip in my hand is very good. Looking at the side profile, you can see that the rubber is much thicker where the grip interfaces with the rider's palm. The extra material is said to help damp vibrations. On the front side of the grip, extra rubber is added to give the grip a shape that is complementary to the rider's fingers as they wrap around the grip. If you look at the shape your fingers assume in this position, it isn't round. Of course, I noticed none of this while I was actually riding. In fact, I forgot about the grips entirely. And that's exactly how it should be. If a product can fade into the background, chance are it's probably performing perfectly well. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Editors Choice Reviews Grips Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Value Field Test: Giant Stance - A Great Leap Forward
author: Sarah Moore
tags: reviews-and-tech, field-test, reviews, trail-bikes, giant, giant-stance, mike-kazimer, sarah-moore, 2024-value-field-test
article_text: Giant Stance Review: A Giant Leap Forward | 2024 Value Bike Field Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/EF4OMNbx Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:50 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST Giant Stance 29 1 Words by Sarah Moore; photography by Tom Richards Giant sent us their previous version of the Stance back in 2020 and it was far from being best on test at the time with its conservative geometry and flexy frame. Now, however, there's a new Giant Stance in town, with a rear thru axle, updated geometry, and slightly more travel. The new model is two degrees slacker than the previous generation and there's 125mm of rear travel which is controlled by Giant's 'Flexpoint' suspension design, a link-driven single pivot that relies on the flex of the frame to allow the suspension to go through its travel instead of a chainstay or seatstay pivot. It's a common suspension platform for lightweight cross-country models since it does away with all of the suspension hardware. Giant Stance Details • Travel: 125mm rear, 140mm front • 29" wheels • 65.5° head tube angle • 76.5° seat tube angle • Reach: 445mm (M) • Chainstay length: 440mm • Weight: 34 lb / 15.3 kg • $2,899 CDN (not yet available in US) • More info: giant-bicycles.com There are also a whole lot of Giant branded components, including saddle, grips, cockpit, wheels, dropper, and even the fork, that keep the Stance's price at a very reasonable $2,899 CDN. We're not sure what the US pricing is yet because the US market is still trying to sell through the previous generation models before adding this one to the line up, but it's safe to say it will be available in the near future. There's a version with 27.5 wheels and then the 29er version that we have here on test. The bike comes in four sizes, Small through XL. The aluminum frame with its 29" wheels has a 140mm Crest 34 SL fork and a Suntour Raidon R rear shock to take the edge off things and a Giant Contact Switch AT dropper for when things point downhill. As for the tires, there is a 2.5" Maxxis Minion DHF front tire and a 2.4" Maxxis Dissector rear tire, both tubeless-ready with EXO casing. The stoppers are Shimano BR-MT420 4-piston with 180mm rotors front and rear and the drivetrain is SRAM's SX Eagle. Compared to the previous model of the Stance, there have been some significant geometry changes in addition to the 5mm more rear travel and 10mm more travel on the fork. Our test bike had a 65.5° head tube angle which is a full two degrees slacker than the previous generation. That's paired with a 76.5° seat angle and 440mm chainstays across all four sizes. Reach on the Stance is 445mm on the size Medium and 470mm on the size Large. That's a full 16mm longer than the previous generation. FIELD NOTES Climbing How does this thing climb? The climbing position on the Stance is upright and comfortable and it's easy to wind up tight switchbacks on the Stance and to pick your way purposefully through technical sections of trail. While we don't look too much at weight when evaluating budget bikes, it's worth mentioning that the Stance climbs lighter than its weight would suggest. It's the lightest of the full suspension bikes we had on test this time around at 34 lb (15.3 kg), but that isn't really saying much. However, it doesn't sit too deep in its travel and so while it's not exactly light for a 125mm travel bike, it has a responsiveness to its pedalling that makes it feel like a lighter bike. While it's not exactly snappy on climbs like a cross-country bike, it's no slouch either. There's no lockout for when you hit paved roads or long sections of fire road, but the Flex Pivot suspension is a relatively firm and efficient-feeling pedalling platform. That being said, it's not at the expense of traction and when things got wet, slippery, and steep, the Stance provided good traction. Descending Compared to some of the other full-suspension bikes in the Giant lineup with similar travel, the reach on the Stance is about 10mm shorter, so it puts you in a bit more of an upright position when descending. It feels like the Stance is targeted at a more beginner rider, unlike a bike like the Jeffsy that's aimed at the same rider as its more expensive carbon counterpart. That being said, the reach isn't overly short and it feels at home on the trails you'd expect a light trail bike to. With its 125mm rear travel, the Stance isn't the bike for smashing through rough sections of trail at speed, and when things start to get technical on the descents, you have to be careful with line choice. It really shines on fast, flowy trails and is most in its element when popping over things and flying through berms. It has good traction on lighter technical trails as well and is an easy bike to get along with on a relatively wide range of descents. The Crest fork works, and while I hit the end of the travel on the rear shock a couple times, overall the rear suspension worked well. Components Let's have a run down of what Giant has hung off the Stance frame. First off, when setting up the cockpit, you'll notice that it's a bit hard to get the dropper post lever in the right place with the slide-on grips and the SX shifter. This is an issue we've had before with budget bikes and while it's relatively minor, it would be nice to be able to get the dropper post lever in an easy-to-reach spot for humans with regular length thumbs. It's cool to see that Giant uses the same Contact Switch dropper post across their line of bikes and so you'll see the same dropper post on higher-end Giant models as on the Stance. While it worked well and it's great that it's adjustable by 30mm since I found I needed to make it a touch shorter than its full 140mm length to pedal the bike, it would be nicer if the bend in the seat tube didn't limit that insertion and I could run it at its full 140mm. Another downside was that the rear shock loses more than a couple of PSI when you take off your pump and so it's difficult to get an accurate reading. That, paired with the fact that there's no O-ring, makes set-up trickier than it needs to be. The house components, including the Crest fork which was perhaps the component with the biggest question mark around it, worked well. The four-piston brakes also work well and allow you to feel confident about getting up to speed knowing that they'll actually slow things down when you need them too. In addition, it was really nice to see good tires on this bike, the Maxxis Minion and Dissector is a good combination and appropriate for the majority of trails. Who's It For? The Giant Stance is a great bike for someone getting into riding. It's not for the sender who is just looking for a budget bike, it's really aimed at a beginner rider. That's no bad thing, and the mild and easygoing manners of the Stance will likely feel slightly more intuitive to a learner than the raked-out long-low-slack angles of a modern enduro bike. Pros + Great to see the upgrades compared to prior model - this one is a lot better. + House branded components work well and help keep that price low + A great bike for someone getting into riding Cons - Some of the basic ergonomics aren't as good as they could be - Better dropper insertion would be nice to see Pinkbike's Value Bikes Field Test is presented by Ride Concepts Posted In: Reviews and Tech Field Test Reviews Trail Bikes Giant Giant Stance Mike Kazimer Sarah Moore 2024 Value Field Test Author Info: Sarah Moore Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 30, 2011 1,624 articles Pinkbike Content Manager

title: Video: Do You Really Need DH Casing Tires?
author: Charlie Coquillard
article_text: Tire Casing Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/jQHD5lUQ Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 10:37 I’ve been running downhill casing tires all summer, and honestly, I haven’t had a single flat. They’re heavy, sure, but after months of bike park laps and rocky descents, I’ve started to wonder: do I actually need all that extra protection? Could a lighter trail or enduro casing survive the kind of riding I usually do? So I decided to find out. The Setup For this test, I ran three versions of the Continental Kryptotal in the soft compound, each with a different casing — Trail, Enduro, and Downhill. Same tread, same compound, same wheels, same pressures. The only variable was how much protection and stiffness each tire offered. The test took place at Paganella Bike Park in northern Italy, which has just about everything: flow trails, rocky traverses, and rough black descents. It’s the kind of place that finds weaknesses in both riders and equipment pretty quickly. The bike was a Mondraker Superfoxy with 170mm up front and 160mm at the rear — the same bike I’ve been riding with downhill casings all summer. Since I already knew how the downhill casing performed: bulletproof, predictable, and basically indestructible, I decided to focus the test on the Trail and Enduro casings instead. To keep things consistent, I ran the same track repeatedly: a mix of flow and rocky sections. Nothing extreme, but enough rough stuff to give the tires a proper workout. It’s the kind of trail where a casing’s limits show up naturally, without needing to ride on the edge. I set up every tire the same: 22 psi up front and 26 at the rear. I’m 210 lb (95.3 kg), for the record. Trail Casing I started with the lightest of the bunch. Straight away, you can feel how supple the Trail casing is. You get more grip, more comfort, the tire feels softer, but the confidence disappears the moment the trail turns rocky or fast. Within minutes, I was pinging off edges, feeling the rear tire bottom out on the rim through sharper hits. It’s manageable for mellow terrain, but once the speed picks up or the rocks get square, you spend more time worrying about line choice than actually enjoying the ride. Even if it felt soft on rough stuff, it rode quite nicely. The issue came in fast berms and anything that involved putting G-forces through the bike. I could feel the bike squirming under me and the tire folding under my (rather high, I concede) weight. Then came the scary moment: on the landing of a small jump, my front tire folded under me. I landed slightly sideways and it just couldn’t take the load, almost sending me over the bars. That was enough for me to end that test. While I didn’t manage to flat the tire, its inability to hold when pushing hard in a turn — due to the flexibility of its sidewall — made it a no-go for me. Yes, increasing the air pressure would have helped add some support, but the thinner sidewalls do make it easier to fold over, even at higher pressures. Enduro Casing Swapping to the Enduro casing immediately felt better. There’s a touch more damping through the carcass, and the sidewalls hold up far better in corners. It’s a nice middle ground, you still get decent trail feel without the constant fear of destroying your rim or folding it in a corner. I’m not the most subtle rider nor the most in touch with my bike, and even if it felt a little less sturdy than a downhill tire, I was riding with confidence. Definitely a huge improvement from the Trail casing. However, I could still hear my rim pinging from time to time, which doesn’t happen except in extreme cases on a DH carcass. Ultimately, this Enduro casing didn’t hold. I landed a bit harshly in a compression, too close to a rock, and got a cut in my sidewall, which, as you probably know, is pretty hard to fix. Takeaways I went into this thinking maybe I’d been overdoing it, maybe I could save a bit of weight and pedal a bit faster on lighter casings. But for how and where I ride, downhill casing still makes the most sense. If you ride mostly smoother singletrack, or your trails don’t serve up constant sharp hits, you can get away with Enduro casings, they’ll feel livelier and climb better. But once the terrain gets rough and speeds pick up, that extra protection isn’t overkill in your opinion. From my perspective, puncture resistance and sidewall strength is where you gain the most by going for a downhill tire, and I’d rather drag those heavy rubbers than get even one single puncture on a day out. As for the Trail casing — for someone my weight and my type of riding, I’d only consider them for cross-country or very light trail use. On top of the risk of puncture, you’re also running the risk of blowing the tire off the rim if you’re getting into a corner a bit hard.
